One should not be disturbed by the "spike" in justifiable homicides that have resulted from Stand Your Ground laws. The alternative would have been a "spike" in murders of honest people. People are defending themselves and their families; that's a good thing. Shelly Fischman, Harpers Ferry, W. Va. l The editorial on Stand Your Ground laws expressed understandable concerns about that law in Florida and other states.   But perhaps the editorial staff has overlooked some potential benefits of this trend to allow greater use of guns when one feels threatened. Possession of guns is now allowed in many public places, including national parks, churches and bars. It is just a matter of time before guns are allowed on the floors of state legislatures and even the floor of  the House and Senate. Soon there will be a federal "Stand Your Ground" statute, and I have no doubt that some senators and representatives will swear that they believe their lives are threatened by colleagues across the aisle. When this Second Amendment option begins, there will be no need for term limits. Brian Saxe, Warrenton, Va.

95.text: GUN-5431 PRIMARY WITNESS RULING DUE IN GUNMAKER CASE A judge is expected to rule today on whether a deposition from a gun-industry whistleblower should be heard in a landmark lawsuit. The decision comes in an unprecedented case against gunmakers brought in Brooklyn Federal Court by the families of gunshot victims. The gun manufacturers' lawyers oppose having former Smith & Wesson marketing executive Robert Hass' 150-page deposition read to the jury, saying he should testify in person or not at all. But lawyers representing the families of six people slain by gunfire and one shooting survivor said Hass, who has heart trouble, is too sick to come to court. "His doctor has given us a letter indicating the stress of the trial would be too much for his health," said plaintiffs' attorney Denise Dunleavy. She said there is ample precedent for Judge Jack Weinstein to let the jury hear the deposition of an ill witness. Hass, a Harvard MBA who lives in Connecticut, joined Smith & Wesson in 1978 and worked at the company for 11 years. He contacted plaintiffs' lawyer Elisa Barnes in 1995 after reading about the case, according to Dunleavy. The groundbreaking lawsuit contends that gunmakers and distributors are liable for creating an underground market for illegal handguns by oversupplying Southern states that have lax gun laws. It charges that the excess guns are then illegally channeled to states with tough gun laws, such as New York.

101.text: GUN-10176 PRIMARY NEW GUN LAW IS MEANT TO PROTECT FLORIDIANS I would like to remind the writer of the letter "Gun law needs provision to let people know who's packing heat" (Wednesday) that the right to carry a concealed weapon in Florida has been around for many years without incident. This provision rescinds the duty to retreat or run away from danger if confronted outside the home. If a concealed weaponholder uses his or her firearm for defense, the person still would have to meet the burden of imminent death or great bodily harm. The writer's statement, "As parents and grandparents, we are entitled to protect ourselves and our children from harm," is just what concealed-weapons permit holders want to be able to do. We gun owners appreciate his support in this aspect and also support his idea of identifying concealed weapons-holders when on the street. This way, criminals will avoid us and go after people such as this letter-writer. RAY JOHNSON Riviera Beach

104.text: GUN-2993 PRIMARY Day One: Brady law halts metro area's gun-buying spree 'Panicked' buyers beat deadline, deplete stocks And on Monday, they rested. After a weekend of record gun sales, metro gun dealers said Monday that gun purchases dropped dramatically on the first day the Brady law required criminal background checks for all gun buyers. "Gun sales? Today?" Harry Clark, owner of Fred's Gun and Tackle Shop in Lilburn asked. Over the weekend, buyers desperate to acquire handguns before Brady took effect Monday depleted Clark's firearms stock. "Now everybody's looking at rifles and shotguns," he said. People buying rifles and shotguns are not subject to criminal background checks required for those buying handguns. "Everybody panicked," Clark said of the weekend buying spree. "I don't know what they thought was going on. You'd think Noah only had a few spaces left on the ark and everybody was trying to get a place." At Shooters in Duluth, store manager Robert White said gun sales were slow after "they went through the ceiling over the weekend." But Brady didn't hold up a gun sale White made Monday morning to a customer who held a Georgia permit to carry a concealed handgun. The Brady law doesn't require a waiting period for owners of handgun permits. "I don't know how it's going to work around here," he said of the mandated background checks. "But if we don't hear anything in five days, they get it [the gun] anyway."

102.text: GUN-1217 PRIMARY DEMOCRATS ATTACK BUSH CRIME BILL FOR LACK OF GUN-CONTROL MEASURES Congressional Democrats, citing a record U.S. murder rate in 1990, criticized President Bush's crime bill Tuesday for failing to include gun-control measures. Gun control proponents on Capitol Hill pushed their own crime-fighting proposals certain to draw fire from the gun lobby, including a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases and a ban of semiautomatic "assault" weapons. The renewed campaign for gun control came as the Senate Judiciary Committee reported a record number of murders in the United States, 23,200 homicides in 1990 based on FBI statistics. Bush called on Congress last week to approve his crime bill and other domestic proposals within 100 days. The president's package, sent to Capitol Hill Monday, would apply the federal death penalty to cover more violent crimes, toughen criminal appeals procedures and increase penalties for crimes involving firearms. Tuesday, Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Joseph Biden Jr., D-Del., introduced his own crime bill that includes the president's proposals and adds provisions to ban the manufacture and sale of assault weapons and boost aid to state and local law enforcement by $1 billion. House Democrats are pushing a bill to create a national seven-day waiting period for the purchase of handguns. The measure, known as the Brady Bill for the former presidential press secretary, James Brady, who was gunned down during the 1981 attempt to assassinate Ronald Reagan, is expected to reach the House floor before

105.text: GUN-1709 PRIMARY LEWIS IS RIGHT: BANNING SEMIAUTOMATICS WON'T HELP A letter writer's response to Florida's Republican Congressman Tom Lewis' letter on the National Rifle Association and gun control appears utopian and shortsighted. The statement "If the George Hennards and Patrick Purdys can't find semiautomatic weapons to purchase, they would not have the weapons used repeatedly in mass murders" is sophomoric. A few weeks ago, it was reported that thousands of semiautomatic weapons, along with fully automatic ones, were stolen from a police academy. The armed forces have so many millions of weapons stolen, including machine guns, that they can't keep track of them. These weapons are available on the street, and anyone wishing to avoid a waiting period, background check or any other regulation, can get one. When anything is prohibited, be it alcohol as in the `20s or drugs today, the criminal world will fill the gap. Dr. Steven L. Schwartz Stuart\

100.text: GUN-11549 PRIMARY 6,000 Bullets With the ease of downloading a song, anyone with a computer and a credit card can order thousands of bullets and shotgun shells on the Internet, along with tear-gas canisters and speed loaders. They can get the same high-capacity ammunition clips that infantry soldiers use. They can even get bulletproof vests and SWAT helmets. All without fear of a single background check. No one is paying attention to whether buyers have criminal histories or mental-health records. No one is monitoring bulk sales of ammunition to see who might be building an arsenal. Even after a young man in Colorado buys 6,000 rounds by mail order and uses them to commit mass murder, it is the rare politician who proposes to make the tools of terror slightly harder to obtain. When he was campaigning for office in 2008, Barack Obama vowed to reinstate the assault weapons ban that had expired in 2004. That would have prohibited the AR-15 rifle used in the Colorado theater shooting on Friday, along with the large 100-round magazine attached to it. But as president, Mr. Obama has made no attempt to do so. Mitt Romney banned assault weapons as governor of Massachusetts and undoubtedly saved many lives, but now he opposes all gun control measures. He never repeats what he said in 2004 when he signed the ban: ''Deadly assault weapons have no place in Massachusetts,'' he said. '' They are instruments of destruction with the sole purpose of hunting down and killing people.''

103.text: NA-43942 PRIMARY Worst Week in Washington The assault-weapons ban left the political stage with a whimper this past week. Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid (D-Nev.) signaled the end Tuesday when he announced that the ban (as well as a ban on high-capacity ammunition magazines) would be stripped from the gun bill and offered only as an amendment on the Senate floor. Reid added that the assault-weapons ban had only about 40 votes. The news - while not entirely unexpected, given the fraught politics of gun measures for Democrats running in places like South Dakota, Montana and Arkansas in 2014 - hit Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) hard. "I very much regret it," she said of the near-certain defeat of the assault-weapons ban. "I tried my best, but my best, I guess, wasn't good enough." For Feinstein, the defeat was personal. Her political career is scarred by gun violence; she became mayor of San Francisco after the 1978 assassinations of Mayor George Moscone and City Supervisor Harvey Milk. After the murders of 20 children in Newtown, Conn., in December, Feinstein, who has long worked within the Senate to tighten gun-control laws, insisted that now was the time to reauthorize the assault-weapons ban that had expired in 2004. When she introduced the legislation in late January, she acknowledged the uphill fight to passage but added, addressing the public: "There is one great hope out there, and that is you. Because you are stronger than the gun lobby."

106.text: GUN-20269 PRIMARY Youth gun deaths decline 35% in four years The number of youths killed by gunshot wounds in the USA decreased 10% in 1998 compared with the year before, double the decrease of gun deaths among the population at large. The drop continues a four-year trend. Between 1994 and 1998, gun deaths for Americans under age 20 declined 35%, according to mortality data released by the federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. In 1998, 3,792 youths died from gunshots, compared with 4,223 in 1997. In 1994, the number of young victims was 5,833. Translated, that means an average of 10 youths a day died of gunshots in 1998, vs. 16 a day in 1994. Health and Human Services Secretary Donna Shalala says the decline shows that "violence prevention programs are working," but "we still have far to go to protect our young people." Gun deaths include homicides, suicides and accidents. Gun-related homicides among young people declined 14%, and accidents dropped 14%, but suicides decreased only 1.7% from 1997 to 1998. CDC spokesman Jeff Lancashire says a number of possible factors, including a better economic climate, may have led to the decreases. High-profile shootings, like the Columbine slaughter, also may have contributed, he says. "There might be more public awareness about safety." Naomi Paiss, a spokeswoman for Handgun Control, says she is optimistic that tougher gun laws in several states have contributed to the decline.

characters: Health and Human Services Secretary Donna Shalala

107.text: NA-43977 PRIMARY A gun deal is within range WHEN HE ran for the Senate in 2010, then-West Virginia Gov. Joe Manchin (D) released a campaign ad in which he loaded, aimed and fired a rifle, showing off his endorsement by the National Rifle Association (NRA) before shooting a hole through an unpopular bill. Now Mr. Manchin is one of the architects of a major new compromise on federal gun rules  - approval of which is the least Congress can do following the country's spate of mass shootings. If everything goes according to plan, the Senate will begin debate Thursday on the first major federal gun legislation to have a real chance in two decades. Before the announcement of Mr. Manchin's proposal, drafted with Sen. Patrick J. Toomey (R-Pa.), the prospects of 60 senators agreeing even to begin that debate were murky. With the compromise plan on the table, the chance that the country will finally get some up-or-down votes on basic gun measures looks better. The Manchin-Toomey plan would amend a base bill that does three big things - cracks down on gun trafficking, toughens school security and expands federal background checks of would-be gun purchasers. That last required bargaining. Mr. Manchin and Mr. Toomey agreed that background checks should be mandatory in all commercial gun sales - including over the Internet and at gun shows. That's good. At least 40 percent of private gun sales currently take place without such scrutiny. The nation's 4,000 yearly gun shows, in particular, have been a notorious hole in the background-check system. But the deal would not require checks in noncommercial, personal transactions. It also tasks licensed gun dealers - not the feds - with keeping records of the checks they facilitate, heading off criticism that the new system would create a national gun registry. We see little reason why all purchasers can't be subject to background checks, but the deal is still a good one.

108.text: GUN-7654 PRIMARY GOP, NRA REFUSE TO SEE THAT GUN CONTROL WORKS After watching some of the coverage of the Million Mom March, I have yet to hear a satisfactory answer from members of the National Rifle Association and the Republican Party as to why they have consistently opposed legislation such as the Brady Bill and other gun-control bills. It seems clear that they are way out of step with the majority of the American populace. Incidents of violent crime have declined for the eighth straight year, and this year saw a record low for murders of police officers. Those are trends we should all be thankful for and pledge to help continue by voting for gun-control measures that protect the safety of citizens while still respecting their constitutional rights. J. SINGLETON Riviera Beach

109.text: GUN-30188 PRIMARY In down market, gun sales are booming 'Assault weapons' sell briskly as some anticipate tighter restrictions under Obama. Deer hunting season, which opened Saturday in Missouri, is always a busy time for gun dealers. But buck fever alone may not account for the surge in the sales of firearms and ammunition since Barack Obama was elected president. The reason, dealers and their customers here say, is a concern that an Obama administration will stiffen gun control. "Some of my customers have predicted that another Clinton-type gun ban is coming," said Denis Dennis Jr., owner of Denny Dennis Sporting Goods in Fenton. He was referring to the bill that President Bill Clinton signed in 1994 that outlawed the sale, manufacture and importation of 19 types of so-called "assault weapons" and ammunition magazines that contain more than 10 rounds. The federal law expired in 2004. Dennis, 46, said sales of such guns had about doubled at his store in recent weeks, with an even greater jump in sales of magazines that hold up to 20 rounds. Other gun stores here have reported similar increases. And national statistics from the FBI appear to point to a connection between higher sales and Obama's election. Through October, the bureau conducted about 9.7 million background checks nationwide on people purchasing guns, an 8 percent rise over the first 10 months of 2007. For the week of Nov. 3-9, however, the bureau conducted 374,510 checks - a 49 percent jump over the same week last year. (One person may buy multiple firearms with only one background check, thus the statistics are not a precise statement of gun sales.)

114.text: GUN-533 PRIMARY CLEARLY, A BILL OF RIGHTS TYPO Having just finished studying the American Constitution and the Bill of Rights, I am astounded to find a glaring error that absolutely no one has discovered. I bring it to your attention. The Second Amendment states that it shall be the right of the people to keep and bear arms. In 1792 there were few schools, and Americans, in their newly found federation of states, were extremely poor spellers. Unfortunately, in their haste to establish the great democracy which it is, the Founding Fathers misspelled the word bare. What they intended to do was to allow their fellow countrymen to wear short-sleeve shirts, something that is allowed virtually everywhere. Because of a typesetter's error, however, a monster was created and today, 200 years later, the country is still paying for this mistake and thousands of Americans are killed annually because of the misuse of guns. Stewart L. Martin West Palm Beach\

110.text: GUN-2705 PRIMARY Tough concealed-weapon law urged City Councilman Ed Thomas yesterday delivered, as promised, a bill to toughen Denver's concealed-weapons law. What Thomas' bill can't do, though, is make it mandatory for Denver cops to arrest people carrying concealed weapons. So he has turned to Police Chief Dave Michaud for help. Thomas, who served for 22 years on the Denver police force before he was elected to the city council last month, said Michaud has committed to a policy that requires Denver police officers to make an arrest when confronting a person carrying a concealed gun. The policy is similar to that of domestic-violence cases in which officers are required to make an arrest when there is evidence of physical abuse. The mandatory arrest procedure would not apply to concealed weapons such as knives or brass knuckles, although those types of weapons fall under the city's existing concealed-weapons law. "My specific interest is geared toward the kid with a gun in his belt," Thomas said. Michaud, who held a press conference to explain the policy, said it always has been police procedure to make an arrest when an officer confronts a person carrying a concealed gun. But, he said, some police officers haven't always followed that policy and have issued summons to appear in court instead. "If you're carrying a gun, we consider that a very serious violation," Michaud said. There are circumstances when it is lawful to carry a concealed gun, Michaud said. For example, when a person is on the way to a firing range or a hunting trip and the weapon is not loaded.

111.text: GUN-9582 PRIMARY State Senate overrides gun law veto The Illinois Senate moved Tuesday to override Gov. Rod Blagojevich and support a law that would allow people who own guns illegally to avoid punishment if the weapon is used to stop a home invasion. The motion to override Blagojevich's veto now goes to the House. The gun bill pertains mainly to the few communities in Illinois that have passed local gun bans, but it has touched a broader nerve with both sides of the gun debate and became a centerpiece of Illinois' U.S. Senate campaign fight. The bill would specify that if a person owns a gun in violation of local ordinance, and that ownership comes to light because the person uses the gun to stop a home invasion, that fact can be offered as a defense against the ownership charge. The case stems from a case last year in Wilmette, Ill., in which a homeowner shot and wounded a burglar, using a gun that the homeowner possessed in violation of Wilmette's local gun ban. The homeowner was charged with violating the ban -- punishable by a fine -- creating a national uproar among gun-rights advocates. The Legislature earlier this year passed a law allowing an affirmative defense in local gun ban violations for defendants who had acted in self-defense. Blagojevich vetoed the bill, saying it amounted to a license to ignore local law. Both the Senate and House need to repass the bill by a three-fifths majority to override that veto, which the Senate did Tuesday with a 40-18 vote.

115.text: GUN-20231 PRIMARY Society in the cross hairs Even in a nation soaked with guns and violence, the murder Tuesday in Michigan of a 6-year-old girl by a 6-year-old boy with a .32-cal revolver provoked unique outrage. The destruction of two young lives -- count both children as victims -- speaks directly to the realities of communities saturated by firearms. Neither age nor innocence is shield enough. The same message was delivered in a different manner Wednesday in Pennsylvania, where a gunman shot five people, killing at least two. No children were harmed, but it was close. The shooter holed up in a building containing a day-care center. Again, the nation's gun culture imposed itself on our children. By rejecting even the most obviously useful gun controls, lobbies such as the National Rifle Association implicitly argue that individual gun privileges are worth the attending public havoc. But even the NRA must cringe at the thought of applying that argument to children. Yet who is doing the dying? Gun violence claims roughly 5,000 children under age 18 every year. In the past week alone, the dead have included a 15-year-old shot in Texas, a 12-year-old shot in Wyoming and a 6-month-old baby shot in New Hampshire. Tuesday night, President Clinton called again for mandatory gun safety locks -- an indisputably worthy cause. But it wouldn't have prevented Michigan's monstrous moment. The firearm was stolen, and thieves have little regard for gun-safety conventions. Likewise, it seems unlikely any particular new law could have thwarted Pennsylvania's psychotic.

113.text: GUN-8996 PRIMARY WAR ON TERRORISM: Gun show loopholes aid terror, ad claims Washington --- Supporters of a bill that requires stricter background checks at gun shows are touting the measure as a way to combat terrorism. In an ad that appeared Monday in the Capitol Hill publication Roll Call, three mug shots of suspected terrorists appeared with these words beneath: "Terrorists are exploiting the gun show loophole." Of the three men, one is from the terrorist group Hezbollah, another had ties to the Irish Republican Army and the third is a suspected al-Qaida operative. They reportedly tried to buy weapons illegally at gun shows in Florida, Texas and Michigan. "Foreign terrorists --- buying guns here in America," reads the ad, sponsored by the nonprofit Americans for Gun Safety. Andrew Arulanandam, spokesman for the National Rifle Association, called the the ad "crass, political opportunism." He said senators sponsoring the bill are trying to take advantage of the Sept. 11 attacks. "The very notion that a state-sponsored terrorist groups like Hezbollah are buying their weapons one gun at a time at a gun show is absurd," said Arulanandam. "They get millions of dollars from countries like Iran, and they have plenty of weapons." The ads initially focused on gun safety but switched to terrorism after Sept. 11. Americans for Gun Safety said the switch is legitimate. "Of course our ads have changed since Sept. 11. What hasn't changed in America since then?" said spokesman Matt Bennett.

112.text: GUN-5767 PRIMARY House Defeats Gun Control Bill; Angry Democrats, Republicans Blame Each Other for Stalemate The drive for expanded gun controls abruptly collapsed in the House yesterday, raising doubts that Congress will pass any gun measures this year and prompting a round of angry finger-pointing over which party was to blame for the stalemate. A deeply divided House voted 280 to 147 to reject the first major package of gun proposals to come before it in five years. The bill included new rules for background checks at gun shows, a ban on the importation of large-capacity ammunition clips and a requirement that safety locks be sold with new guns. In the end, a strange coalition of Democrats and moderate Republicans favoring gun control and conservatives on the other side of the issue combined to kill the measure. Many Democrats decided they were better off with no bill after pro-gun forces managed, late Thursday, to substantially revise part of the bill dealing with gun shows. And hard-liners skeptical of the efficacy of gun control could not live with any new restrictions at all. Lawmakers and White House officials insisted there is still a chance that Congress will enact gun legislation later this year, and they noted that the House may yet accept some of the gun control provisions -- including child safety locks and a ban on serious juvenile felons ever owning guns -- that the Senate recently approved. Still, leaders in both parties wasted little time in pinning blame for yesterday's developments and maneuvering politically over an issue that could have a potent -- if uncertain -- impact on next year's congressional and presidential elections.

116.text: GUN-11461 PRIMARY Letters to the editor Gun laws need to be changed This is regarding the question of whether gun laws should be changed after the Aurora, Colo., massacre. At the federal level, we need a ban, such as California's, on high-capacity ammunition magazines and military-style semi-automatic assault weapons like those that made the attack in Aurora so deadly. We need a federal law requiring background checks every time a gun is sold -- including at gun shows. Too many Americans die because our present policies keep it too easy for criminals and other dangerous prohibited persons to purchase firearms. Unfortunately, too many politicians, especially Republicans, aid the gun trafficking. We must pass federal laws that help shut down gun dealers who consistently violate gun laws and repeatedly "lose" dozens of guns out the back door. Readers can help. Ask the presidential candidates to tell us what they plan to do to prevent gun violence and have your friends do the same. See this link:www.wearebetterthanthis.org . Griffin Dix Kensington Soldiers pay for our freedoms I am a combat veteran of the Korean War. Although a truce was signed in 1953, the conflict has never officially ended. How does a Korean combat veteran view today's world? Surely, he must recall freezing in the Korean winter, vastly outnumbered by an attacking force, wounded from being too long on the line, emotionally scarred from isolation as a POW, and surrounded by the death and carnage of combat.

119.text: GUN-346 PRIMARY GETTING DOWN TO CASES ON LEGISLATING GUN CONTROL To the Editor: As one with mixed emotions about gun control, and recognizing the principal hypothesis of the proponents of gun control to be that there is a positive correlation between the legal availability of handguns and the level and character of crime in the United States, I suggest that a wise next step is to enact quite stringent gun control laws at the Federal level but have a finite expiration date for such laws. he period must be enough to permit testing of the above hypothesis. Such legislation should require the gathering of necessary data and, if the link between gun availability and violent crime is proved, I would expect Congress to have little hesitancy in passing more permanent gun control legislation. Should the hypothesis be false, Congress could then focus on other solutions to the universally acknowledged problem of violent crime.   AARON J. GELLMAN Wyncote, Pa., April 14, 1981

117.text: GUN-8768 PRIMARY San Mateo County votes to ban guns San Mateo County supervisors voted unanimously Tuesday to ban guns on county-owned property -- a move all but certain to draw a legal challenge. An attorney for two gun dealers who have run shows at the county's Expo Center said they will file suit after the ordinance takes effect in mid-January. But this suit would focus primarily on the First Amendment, not the Second. In the view of Don Kilmer, attorney for gun dealers Russell and Sallie Nordyke, the new rule would violate the speech rights of gun enthusiasts because the county doesn't like their culture and the message of gun shows. ''I think that's repulsive,'' he said. Supervisor Mike Nevin, who proposed the new law, said he opposes the signal the county sends when it hosts gun shows: ''That's not the role of county government to be selling weapons. It's the wrong message for the next generation of Americans.'' San Mateo County hosts only a few gun shows a year; the last one yielded a $13,000 profit, Nevin said. Supervisor Mark Church said the law does not outlaw gun shows or limit speech rights -- gun fans could still gather on county property to discuss guns, only the weapons could not be there, he said. The law lets police, concealed-weapon permit holders and a few other professionals, mostly in security, take guns onto county property.

118.text: GUN-5692 PRIMARY Does Faith Have A Public Role?; Gun Is No Threat To the Editor: A June 20 Week in Review article gives the impression that it is outrageous that a measure for tighter controls on a long-range, semiautomatic, .50-caliber sniper rifle never made it to the House of Representatives' floor. This is the kind of thing that makes members of the National Rifle Association, like me, resist silly gun control measures. One kind of long-range semiautomatic rifle, the Barrett rifle, at 28 pounds and $6,800, is ludicrously unsuitable for criminals. You offer no evidence of any crime involving this gun. I have personally handled one, and found it ungainly, unbearably heavy and uneconomical. It is a good candidate for mounting on a truck. The purported ownership of this kind of gun by various groups -- terrorists, religious cults and drug traffickers -- shows their bad judgment of guns. What did they actually do with these guns except store them in the cellar? DANA F. GEIGER Port Washington, N.Y., June 21, 1999 http://www.nytimes.com

120.text: GUN-4686 PRIMARY Montgomery Gun Plan Picks Wrong Target I yield to no one, Mr. Chairman, in my desire for reasonable gun control. Ban assault weapons? You bet; they're made only to kill. Make would-be buyers pass a firearms course and wait a week while their bona fides is checked? No big burden. Restrict handgun purchases to one a month? Seems like plenty. That said, I'd like to introduce Art Harris, gun dealer and victim of gun-phobia run wild. Art sells shotguns and rifles, although you'd never know that as you enter the Gentleman Hunter, the shop he owns. There's nary a weapon in view, only items popular with the sporting set: leather-clad flasks, $ 358 sport coats, $ 125 shooting waistcoats, hats, boots and books on dogs, fishing and cooking game. Dominant colors are charcoal, forest green and brown. The wing chairs -- offered for relaxation, not purchase -- are maroon and comfortable. It's a tweedy, Englishy place. The weapons are in the back, in a vault that's down a hallway and behind a 1,000-pound steel door. The cheapest is $ 795, the dearest, $ 50,000. Art sells no handguns. Not one. And he sells no ammunition. None. He figures you can get ammunition anywhere; his place is meant to be special. He wanted not a gun shop, but a boutique for those with money who like to hunt game or shoot clay targets, activities that are perfectly legal. And what better place for such a business than Bethesda, where there are persistent reports of comfortable incomes.

122.text: GUN-3545 PRIMARY High court trims anti-drug gun law The Supreme Court ruled unanimously yesterday that a federal law giving drug traffickers an extra five years in prison for using a firearm can be invoked only when a defendant actively employs the weapon. The court reversed convictions in two District of Columbia cases in which the firearms were locked away during the drug dealing. In one case, police found a pistol inside a bag in a car trunk. In the other, an unloaded derringer was discovered in a footlocker in a bedroom closet. The decision was a defeat for the Justice Department and its campaign against illegal drugs. It is rare for this conservative court to side - unanimously - with criminal defendants over the government. But the decision in Bailey vs. United States, written by Justice Sandra Day O'Connor, made plain that the court thought federal prosecutors went beyond the bounds of the law. The Justice Department had argued that both cases were covered by a 1988 law that requires a minimum of five extra years in prison for anyone charged under a statute making it a crime to "use or carry" a firearm during a trafficking crime. But O'Connor rejected arguments that mere possession of a firearm is enough to merit the extra charge and longer sentence. She said the law requires evidence the defendant actively used the weapon in the commission of a drug crime.

125.text: GUN-2672 PRIMARY Denver gun rule will hit wrong targets Denver's city council continually hears citizen cries that the government must DO SOMETHING to stop crime. The pressure is so intense that, when a proposal crops up that seems to DO SOMETHING about crime, it's impossible for politicians to reject it. So it was with the hubbub over penalties for people who tote concealed guns. Some gun-control laws make sense. It's logical to run computer checks on gun sales so crooks don't purchase pistols. It's important to keep guns out of kids' hands - it's hard enough to teach teenagers to drive a car safely without direct adult supervision, much less to handle a firearm. But the new Denver rule on concealed weapons doesn't accomplish any objective in the so-called war on crime. In fact, it has less practical value than the city's new curfew law. Contrary to opponents' claims, the rule doesn't change how a concealed firearm can be carried. It just stiffens the penalties for doing so. The first offense is a $ 500 fine, the second $ 750 and the third $ 999. Violators must spend any jail time in addition to, rather than concurrently with, any other jail term. The eight council members who voted for the measure probably hoped it will deter crooks from packing pistols, but in reality it won't prevent the crimes that really concern citizens. Wife beaters don't have to reach for a concealed revolver to slay their spouses - a kitchen knife will do the job. Drive-by killers who risk life in prison or death by injection won't be cowed by a $ 500 fine.

124.text: GUN-5654 PRIMARY Barnes sells out to gun lobby Gregory Ferrell should have begun his sophomore year at Southside High School last week. Instead, his family buried the 15-year-old, killed in broad daylight by a stranger bearing a grudge and an AK-47. Roy Barnes probably didn't have much time to consider Gregory last week. He was busy capitalizing on his endorsement from the National Rifle Association, barnstorming the state with the organization's chief lobbyist. The endorsement was a sweetheart deal between the Democratic gubernatorial candidate and the rabid gun lobby. The NRA earns the undying devotion of the likely next governor of Georgia, and Barnes inoculates himself from accusations by GOP opponent Guy Millner that he's "too liberal for Georgia." The losers are the Georgia kids dying from gun violence. Gregory's death came two weeks after 17-year-old Levi Simon, an honor student hours from leaving for college, was gunned down in suburban Atlanta as he left a "sweet 16" pool party. The NRA opposes simple child protection efforts, including trigger locks and parental responsibility laws, because it alleges that these measures mask a hidden agenda to suppress gun ownership. To put the NRA's hysteria in context, its position on child safety issues is tantamount to the American Automobile Association suggesting that infant car seat laws reflect a plot to force drivers to surrender their automobiles. The NRA is playing politics with children's lives, and Barnes is helping.

127.text: GUN-10024 PRIMARY Pointing the Trigger Finger LAWYERS, GUNS, AND MONEY One Man's Battle With the Gun Industry By Carol X. Vinzant Palgrave. 228 pp. $29.95 By now, the gun control debate has become predictable. Advocates point to the laxity of gun laws and the high percentage of gun victims in the United States; opponents maintain that gun rights are important for self-defense and recreation. After a brief crest in favor of gun control in 1993 and 1994, which saw passage of the Brady Bill and the ban on assault weapons, such strictures now seem to be political poison. Some blame gun politics for Democratic defeats in the 2000 presidential race (with the loss of key states like West Virginia) and in Congress in 1994 and 2002. Just weeks ago, Congress passed special legislation to shield the gun industry from litigation. In "Lawyers, Guns, and Money," Carol X. Vinzant focuses on an individual gun victim and the gritty facts of the gun industry. She is far from impartial. To her, the National Rifle Association is evil and manipulative, and gun control advocates are heroic and courageous. But Vinzant's attention to one victim  -- and to the cold reality of the gun industry's economics  --  allows her to move beyond the familiar generalities. On Dec. 7, 1993, a lawyer named Tom McDermott happened to be on a Long Island Rail Road commuter train when Colin Ferguson opened fire with a Ruger 9mm pistol. He killed six people and wounded 19, including McDermott. A former prosecutor working for an obscure state agency, McDermott found his life upended. He was particularly enraged that Ferguson had been able to use a 15-bullet clip, which allowed him to keep shooting without reloading. Searching for a way to fight back, McDermott became a consulting lawyer in litigation against gun manufacturers. He soon discovered what he believed to be a suspicious insurance company that supported low-end gun companies manufacturing cheap, often defective handguns. Frequently at his own expense, he began an investigative crusade, showing up unannounced at offices across the country, in the Caribbean and in Central America.

128.text: NA-44214 PRIMARY After Newtown shooting, mourning parents enter into the lonely quiet After Newtown shooting, mourning parents enter into the lonely quiet They had promised to try everything, so Mark Barden went down into the basement to begin another project in memory of Daniel. The families of Sandy Hook Elementary were collaborating on a Mother's Day card, which would be produced by a marketing firm and mailed to hundreds of politicians across the country. "A difference-maker," the organizers had called it. Maybe if Mark could find the most arresting photo of his 7-year-old son, people would be compelled to act. It hardly mattered that what Mark and his wife, Jackie, really wanted was to ignore Mother's Day altogether, to stay in their pajamas with their two surviving children, turn off their phones and reward themselves for making it through another day with a glass of Irish whiskey neat. "Our purpose now is to force people to remember," Mark said, so down he went into his office to sift through 1,700 photos of the family they had been. The Bardens had already tried to change America's gun laws by studying the Second Amendment and meeting with President Obama in the Oval Office. They had spoken at tea party rallies, posed for People magazine and grieved on TV with Katie Couric. They had taken advice from a public relations firm, learning to say "magazine limits" and not "magazine bans," to say "gun responsibility" and never "gun control." When none of that worked, they had walked the halls of Congress with a bag of 200 glossy pictures and beseeched lawmakers to look at their son: his auburn hair curling at the ears, his front teeth sacrificed to a soccer collision, his arms wrapped around Ninja Cat, the stuffed animal that had traveled with him everywhere, including into the hearse and underground.

123.text: GUN-10847 PRIMARY Proposed gun legislation targeted by RCND By KEITH UPCHURCH kupchurch@heraldsun.com; 419-6612 DURHAM -- Gun owners could legally bring concealed weapons into restaurants and neighborhood parks if bills before the N.C. General Assembly pass, and that has plenty of people concerned, including the director of North Carolinians Against Gun Violence. Roxane Kolar, who leads the anti-gun violence advocacy group, told those at a Thursday luncheon roundtable meeting of the Religious Coalition for the Non-Violent Durham that bills to expand the places where concealed weapons are allowed are "gaining traction" with the new Republican leadership in the Legislature. N.C. House Bill 111 would allow gun owners to bring their concealed weapons to city and neighborhood parks, and into restaurants, including those that serve alcohol, she said. Another proposal, N.C. House Bill 63, would require employers to let employees keep guns in their cars in parking lots, including church lots. And a third bill, N.C. House Bill 650, would let people keep guns in their cars in school parking lots. "We think this isn't really the time or the place [to pass these bills]," Kolar said. "There are a lot more important things to focus on right now, like the programs that keep our kids in school, prevent violence and give people jobs. That's what really matters." Kolar also wonders why there's a sudden rush to pass these bills. "There's not a rash of shootings at our public parks or restaurants," she said. "In fact, violent crime is down."

characters: Religious Coalition for the Non-Violent Durham

121.text: GUN-8568 PRIMARY SENATE VOTES TO END GUN BUYBACK PROGRAM ; OPPONENTS SAY IT DID NOT TAKE WEAPONS FROM HANDS OF CRIMINALS The Senate voted Thursday to back President George W. Bush's plan to kill the government's gun buyback program, handing a victory to gun-rights forces. Senators voted 65-33 against a proposal by Sen. Charles Schumer, D-N.Y., to provide $15 million for the program, which was established less than two years ago by former President Bill Clinton. Bush's administration announced last month that it was ending the program, saying there was no proof that it was taking guns from criminals. Missouri's two senators, Christopher "Kit" Bond, a Republican, and Jean Carnahan, a Democrat, voted to end the buyback. Illinois Sens. Dick Durbin, a Democrat, and Peter Fitzgerald, a Republican, voted to continue the program. Under the plan, local police departments received up to $500,000 to buy guns in and around public housing complexes for about $50 each. The weapons were then destroyed. "Someone is alive today because of this program," Schumer said. Opponents said the program was a failure that siphoned money that public housing authorities could better use to upgrade housing or to help the homeless or others. "Do they take away the semiautomatic and the .38 used in commission of crimes? Absolutely not," said Sen. Larry Craig, R-Idaho. The vote was not a clear referendum on the Senate's sentiment on gun issues. To pay for his amendment, Schumer would have taken the money from funds provided to public housing authorities for anti-drug efforts, a program some lawmakers were reluctant to raid.

131.text: GUN-8831 PRIMARY Townsend Attacked on Gun Checks; State's Enforcement Negligent, Ehrlich Says Rep. Robert L. Ehrlich Jr. ripped his gubernatorial rival yesterday for Maryland's failure to check the criminal backgrounds of hundreds of people applying to buy guns, taking the offensive on an issue that has dogged his campaign for weeks. Ehrlich's campaign also unveiled a sharply negative television ad that criticizes Lt. Gov. Kathleen Kennedy Townsend (D) and her broader record on crime and gun control. Staff members said the ad would begin airing today in Baltimore and in the Washington region tomorrow. In an interview, Ehrlich (R) said the state's refusal to help the FBI perform background checks on an estimated 500 people with criminal records this year was "just ridiculous. It's just negligence, very simple negligence that can add up in a very costly way." Townsend said that the problem had been resolved this summer and that Ehrlich should focus instead on measures to improve gun safety, such as expanding a ballistic fingerprinting program. "I think he's just criticizing gun enforcement because he doesn't want to have gun laws that work," she said. Ehrlich's decision to go on the attack was an attempt to shift the debate on gun control, a volatile topic that until now has hamstrung his standing among many liberal and moderate suburban voters whose support he needs to win. For weeks, Townsend has run television commercial ads of her own that slam Ehrlich for his legislative votes to effectively legalize the sale of certain so-called assault weapons and cheap handguns known as Saturday night specials. He has also taken heat for comments he made last month when he said he would review Maryland's strict gun control laws and possibly get rid of ones found to be ineffective.

126.text: GUN-10073 PRIMARY Houses Passes Ban on Gun Industry Lawsuits; Bush Vows to Sign Bill That Contradicts D.C. Law; Opponents Call for Action The House yesterday voted to shield companies that make and sell firearms from lawsuits by the victims of shootings, sending the legislation to the White House and handing the nation's gun lobby a paramount victory it has sought for years. The House's 283  to 144 vote, less than three months after the Senate approved identical legislation, delighted President Bush, who portrayed it as part of the administration's drive to "stem frivolous lawsuits" and said he will sign it into law. Leading proponents of gun control immediately vowed to challenge the law's constitutionality. Congress's decision has particular relevance for the District, the only place in the country with a law that explicitly allows victims of crimes involving semiautomatic weapons to bring legal claims. In April, the D.C. Court of Appeals upheld the constitutionality of the city's 15-year-old law, and the Supreme Court this month declined to hear the case. Supporters and opponents of the legislation said the law would halt pending District government litigation trying to win compensation from gun manufacturers for medical costs and other expenses associated with shootings and several claims by local victims and their families. The pending suits include a federal lawsuit brought against Bushmaster Firearms Inc. by the relatives of Pascal Charlot, a victim of the 2002 rash of local sniper shootings. The legislation is intended to cut off an avenue that gun-control advocates have used in recent years to exert leverage on the firearms industry, trying to curb the sale of weapons to criminals by holding it financially responsible for crimes. The National Rifle Association and other gun enthusiasts have complained that the expense of fighting lawsuits put manufacturers and gun stores on shaky financial ground, regardless of who wins the cases.

129.text: GUN-6762 PRIMARY White House plans 'all-out'assault on guns The Clinton administration, convinced that Congress has badly underestimated the public appetite for new gun regulations, is planning an " all-out offensive on guns" in the coming year, the Washington Post reported today. The plan will include extra spending by the Justice and Treasury departments for various gun control measures, the Post said, quoting a " senior White House official" whom it did not name. It underscores a growing fervor for gun regulation in the administration, which last week announced it was joining forces with several cities that have sued or threatened to sue the gun manufacturing industry over safety and distribution issues. The Post said the goal is either to force Congress to adopt new gun control measures --- including several it rejected this fall --- or pay a heavy price in the 2000 House and Senate elections. ''We believe if we keep the pressure on, we can bring the industry to an agreement and make it as difficult as possible for Congress to go home without doing anything,'' the White House official was quoted as saying. Administration officials planned to hold a meeting today to be run by chief of staff John D. Podesta and including White House domestic policy chief Bruce Reed, deputy treasury secretary Stuart Eisenstadt, Deputy Attorney General Eric Holder, vice presidential counsel Charles Burson, presidential adviser Joel Johnson and Housing and Urban Development Secretary Andrew Cuomo.   Podesta ''is going to ask them to come up with a series of actions to build pressure on the gun industry for an agreement and intensify the pressure on Congress to break the logjam on the gun bill and provide the president with a series of executive actions he can take in the weeks and months to come,'' the Post's source said.

135.text: GUN-1261 PRIMARY Another shooting puts hole in NRA cause Thanks to the ready availability of guns in this society, a traffic disagreement last week on Interstate 285 that should have produced no more than shaken fists and mouthed obscenities escalated into a small- scale, deadly war. One person was shot dead, another wounded. Something else was wounded a bit: the National Rifle Association and its campaign against any reasonable sort of restriction on gun purchasing and ownership. Everytime some 4-year-old kid shoots his sister with a gun found under his dad's pillow, everytime a 15-year-old guns down a schoolmate with a semiautomatic pistol, everytime a distraught mother buys a pistol and runs home to kill her retarded son and then herself, as happened recently in DeKalb County, the NRA's position is weakened. That fact can be demonstrated in myriad ways. For one, membership in the NRA is slipping. It has fallen almost 400,000 nationwide from its peak of the mid-1980s, and the rate of decline seems to be increasing. Another, more subtle sign of trouble is found in a nasty little war for leadership of the NRA. A group of pro-gun activists, zealous even by NRA standards, has challenged the group's current leadership on grounds it has not been rabid enough. In the manner of zealots everywhere, the hard-liners have concluded that if their position is losing national support, the cause must be inadequate faith on the part of those who were hired and elected to advance it. They cannot conceive of the idea that the argument itself might be faulty.

130.text: GUN-260 PRIMARY PISTOL LIABILITY IS ISSUE AT TRIAL David Clancy was a 15-year-old ninth-grade student in Amarillo, Tex., six years ago when a pistol being handled by a fellow student in a high school parking lot accidentally discharged. A single bullet severed Mr. Clancy's spine and left him a quadriplegic. Mr. Clancy, who is now restricted to a motorized wheelchair, is the plaintiff in the first lawsuit to go to trial among 80 involving the purpose of pistols. The $43 million suit filed by Windle Turley, a Dallas lawyer, asserts that pistols such as the $19 weapon that crippled Mr. Clancy are, by their nature, unsafe, hazardous products and that the manufacturers and sellers should be liable for injuries or deaths that result from them. The suit by Mr. Turley has touched off the other pistol lawsuits around the country, which are being closely watched by both gun-control advocates and groups that oppose curbs on gun ownership, such as the National Rifle Association. Both sides say that if many of the suits are successful they could have a significant impact on the costs and availability of pistols in the United States. Andrew Kendzie, a spokesman for the National Rifle Association in Washington, said: ''What Turley and others of his ilk are trying to do is to win in the courts what they fail to achieve legislatively and politically.'' Mr. Clancy's lawsuit names as defendants the Zale Corporation, which owned the store where the gun was sold, and the designer and maker of the Armsco .22-caliber weapon of the type known popularly as a ''Saturday night special.''

132.text: GUN-10630 PRIMARY On guns, it's time to be like Mike THIS TIME the line of mourners is outside the Claude R. Boyd-Spencer Funeral Home in West Babylon, on Long Island, maybe 30 miles from the place in Cypress Hills, Brooklyn, where Officer Peter J. Figoski, a hero of his city, was shot dead early Monday a week ago by another nobody with a gun. This time the mayor of New York, Michael Bloomberg, who fights as hard against illegal guns as any politician of his time, joins that line in West Babylon with the policemen who kept walking through the doors of the Boyd-Spencer Funeral Home all weekend long. Police Commissioner Ray Kelly was in that line on Saturday afternoon. Kelly, who got the call on Figoski at 2:15 Monday morning and was on his way to the hospital about 15 minutes later. This time the cop shot, the cop down forever, is Figoski, shot after a two-bit robbery in the night, shot with the Ruger 9-mm. that somehow ended up in the hand of Lamont Pride, a small-time career criminal who now moves into the big-time by killing a policeman out of the 75th Precinct. So another senseless death gets everybody talking about the plague of illegal guns all over again, makes reasonable people wonder all over again why so many politicians in this country genuflect in front of the NRA, and all the other idiots who try to tell you that anti-gun legislation is some threat to our way of life.

133.text: GUN-5035 PRIMARY MAN SENTENCED UNDER FEDERAL GUN LAW; CONVICTED PREVIOUSLY FOR WIFE-BEATING, WAUSAU RESIDENT WAS TURNED IN BY EX-WIFE FOR HAVING GUNS Turned in by his ex-wife for possessing guns, a man was sentenced Tuesday to 11 months in prison under a federal law that prohibits people convicted of beating a spouse from owning firearms. Kirk J. Lewitzke, 37, of Wausau was the first person in Wisconsin and one of about six in the nation to be convicted under the 2-year-old law, U.S. Attorney Peggy Lautenschlager said. Lewitzke also was the first person who went to trial challenging the law, Lautenschlager said. The rest pleaded guilty. The sentencing by U.S. District Court Judge Barbara Crabb came 11 months after federal agents raided Lewitzke's house and found six firearms that he says belonged to friends. Lewitzke was convicted in February. Bruce Rosen, Lewitzke's attorney, said he will appeal the conviction. Leaving court, Lewitzke and Rosen would not respond to a reporter's questions. During the hearing, Rosen had complained about media coverage of his client since his conviction, arguing that it could affect Lewitzke's sentencing. In an article Sunday in the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel, Lewitzke said his ex-wife turned him in out of retaliation over their marriage, which ended in divorce nine years ago. "My ex-wife must be in seventh heaven now," Lewitzke told the newspaper. "This is exactly what she hoped to accomplish. She hates my guts." Lewitzke, a maintenance supervisor at a packing plant in Merrill, said he feels he did nothing wrong.

134.text: GUN-7371 PRIMARY Gun-show checks supported Coloradans want background checks at gun shows, and a poll  shows they will overwhelmingly support such a requirement in the  voting booth. A Denver Post/9 News/KOA poll of 600 likely voters shows that  83 percent of Coloradans support 'closing the gun-show loophole,'  as supporters call the measure they want on the November ballot. 'Clearly Coloradans see this as a common-sense proposal,'  said Dick Wadhams, spokesman for Gov. Bill Owens. Owens pledged to support gun-control groups' efforts to put  the gun-show measure on the ballot after his fellow Republicans  defeated it in the Legislature. The Columbine High School massacre and other shootings since  then have taught people that it's too easy for the wrong people to  get guns, said Tom Mauser, who started lobbying for gun control  after his son Daniel was killed at Columbine. 'It wasn't just the killings at Columbine,' said Mauser,  director of political affairs for the gun-control group SAFE  Colorado. 'It was people learning what it was about and where the  guns came from.' All four guns used at Columbine were bought at the Tanner Gun  show in Adams County. The woman who bought three of the guns,  Robyn Anderson, said she sought to avoid a background check so she  wouldn't have to give her name to the vendors. The National Rifle Association contributed to the defeat of  the gun-show measure in the Legislature, but a spokesman for the  gun group said he wasn't surprised to hear that Coloradans support  it.

136.text: GUN-7981 PRIMARY SEVEN GUN MAKERS DROP LAWSUIT AGAINST HUD AND CITIES * The suit challenged a policy of favoring Smith & Wesson because it agreed to various safety rules. Seven gun makers and a firearms group dropped their lawsuit Friday against the Department of Housing and Urban Development and 39 other government officials and municipalities, including St. Louis, over a program favoring manufacturer Smith & Wesson. The gun makers said they were dropping the suit because a new Republican administration was about to take office and is thought to be unlikely to support the program. A HUD spokeswoman disputed that, saying the real reason the manufacturers dropped the suit was that they expected to lose. The lawsuit was filed in April over a plan to give preferential treatment to Smith & Wesson when buying guns for law enforcement agencies because the gun maker had agreed to install gun locks on its weapons and block gun sales at gun shows without background checks. Smith & Wesson implemented those measures in exchange for a promise that a lawsuit against it would be dropped. Any other gun maker that agreed to the plan also would be given preferential treatment. "It is clear that the election of George W. Bush changes the dynamic regarding this pattern of government abuse coached by President (Bill) Clinton and quarterbacked by" HUD Secretary Andrew Cuomo, said Robert T. Delfay, president and chief executive of the National Shooting Sports Foundation, a Connecticut-based firearms industry group.

140.text: GUN-11346 PRIMARY With guns blazing Great good luck to Mayor Bloomberg, who hopes to turn his fortune into national political leverage and that leverage into saved lives. Bloomberg aides announced that Hizzoner is doling out $10 million to $15 million in an effort to tip as many as a dozen races in the House and Senate. He is motivated not by an interest in seeing Democrats or Republicans rule Washington, but by three concerns: better schools, in which teachers are evaluated more professionally and parents have more choice; gay rights, so same-sex partners everywhere can legally marry, and, most of all, gun control. When a man like Bloomberg says "gun control," some hear "left-wing liberal," but that's wrong. The mayor is rightly disgusted at both sides. In the second presidential debate at Hofstra University, Mitt Romney flatly said, "I'm not in favor of new pieces of legislation on - on guns and taking guns away or making certain guns illegal." Rhetorically, President Obama was a bit better, saying, "We've got more to do when it comes to enforcement," and adding in support for the assault weapons ban. For that alone, the National Rifle Association has invested $1.5 million a week in anti-Obama television commercials in the swing states of Florida, Virginia and Wisconsin - and another $800,000 in online ads. But Obama has failed to move the needle as President, and not just because of GOP opposition. He has refused to lead.

138.text: GUN-8977 PRIMARY VERSTEEG IGNORED STUDY ON DEFENSIVE USE OF GUNS In the May 28 Post, Jac VerSteeg turned his column into an assault on Attorney General John Ashcroft's opinion on the Second Amendment ("Justice takes aim on the public"). According to Mr. VerSteeg, the right to bear arms is not an individual right but a collective one. He then recited a litany of examples of gun violence to attempt to prove his point. One example he failed to cite was the work of Florida State University Professor Gary Kleck, which challenges gun-control advocates' claims that guns are a public health problem. Mr. Kleck found that arms are used defensively more than 2 million times a year, dwarfing the violent uses by criminals. In fact, he states that more than 25 times as many lives are saved by a gun for each life lost because of a gun. Since the advent of the concealed-weapons permits, there has been a 69 percent decrease in death rates from multiple shootings, according to Mr. Kleck's and John Lott's book More Guns, Less Crime. In the First, Second, Fourth, Ninth and 10th Amendments, the word people means all citizens, not just militia members. GEORGE FAAS Belle Glade

137.text: NA-40488 PRIMARY Gun violence panel focuses on parental neglect, other woes Toy gun buy-back program and summer job initiative emerge from three-hour discussion, are small steps toward reversing epidemic. ST. LOUIS o It was billed as a forum on gun violence. And that it was - minus a single mention of background checks, semi-automatic weapons and high-capacity magazine clips. The free-wheeling and brutally honest discussion that unfolded in a Midtown church Friday night instead focused on the core reason St. Louis records more than 100 firearms-related homicides each year. Mental health, parental neglect, the decline in urban education, Hollywood, video games, music and even sartorial choices - no topic, save gun control, was off the table for a panel of law enforcement professionals, jurists and academics on a dais at Washington Tabernacle Missionary Baptist Church . "To say this is an epidemic is to understate it," said former St. Louis Police Chief Dan Isom, the moderator for the three-hour "Newtown to Our Town" conversation. The panelists and the 125 people in attendance learned of two new programs organizers view as incremental measures in the larger effort to reverse the epidemic. One, a private-public partnership called STL Youth Jobs, aims to provide employment to 500 young city residents this summer. The other, headed by the host church , will buy back toy guns to "bring awareness to the impact that a culture of violence has on our children." The panelists were unsparing in identifying the problems that each year lead to the deaths or incarceration of thousands of young African-Americans.

characters: St. Louis Police Chief Dan Isom

141.text: GUN-3252 PRIMARY VENDORS, FANS SIGN PETITION PROTESTING BAN ON GUN SHOWS Hundreds of women, men and children roamed up and down the aisles where 200 vendors were showing their wares. They stopped long enough to taste a bit of venison jerky, ogle war memorabilia or handle a gun. Perhaps the most popular booth at the show, however, was located beneath the American flag where members of the Gun Owners of California and the San Mateo County Republican Assembly circulated petitions against San Mateo County's ban against all gun shows at the Expo Center. More than 1,000 gun enthusiasts paid $6.50 a ticket to attend the Crossroads Gun Show at the center Saturday. Each person was greeted at the front door with a blue flier announcing the San Mateo County Board of Supervisor's Tuesday decision to ban all gun shows at the center starting in 1996. The flier also explained the county's tentative plan to reimburse the Expo Center for roughly $100,000 in lost revenue from the gun shows. At 1 p.m., midway through the first day of the show, 1,000 signatures had been collected, said Penny Ferguson, member of the Republican Assembly. With the petition signed, shoppers pushed into the showroom where the mood was much like a swap meet or a car show.

characters: San Mateo County Board of Supervisors

139.text: GUN-30162 PRIMARY Ruling prompts gun-law challenges Felon cites Supreme Court, says weapons are for self-defense. Twice convicted of felonies, James Francis Barton Jr. faced charges of violating a federal law barring felons from owning guns after police found seven pistols, three shotguns and five rifles at his home south of Pittsburgh. As a defense, Barton and several other defendants in federal gun cases argue that last month's Supreme Court ruling allows them to keep loaded handguns at home for self-defense. David Chontos, Barton's court-appointed lawyer, and other criminal defense lawyers say the high court's decision means federal laws designed to keep guns out of the hands of people convicted of felonies and crimes of domestic violence are unconstitutional so long as the weapons are needed for self-defense. So far, federal judges uniformly have agreed these restrictions are unchanged by the Supreme Court's landmark interpretation of the Second Amendment. A court hearing in the Barton case is set for late this month. The legal attacks by Chontos and other criminal defense lawyers are separate from civil lawsuits by the National Rifle Association and others challenging handgun bans in Chicago and its suburbs as well as a total ban on guns in public housing in San Francisco. People on both sides of the gun control issue say they expect numerous attacks against local, state and federal laws based on the high court's 5-4 ruling that struck down the District of Columbia's ban on handguns. The opinion by Justice Antonin Scalia also suggested, however, that many gun control measures could remain in place.

143.text: GUN-3395 PRIMARY GUN BAN FELL VICTIM TO OVERKILL PRO-GUN HOUSE MEMBERS VOTED TO KILL THE PHILA. ASSAULT-WEAPONS BAN AND THEN VOTED TO REFUSE A RECONSIDERATION VOTE. It was like pumping a second bullet into a dead person. Not only did pro-gun House members vote to kill Philadelphia's ban on assault weapons Tuesday night, they made certain that opponents did not get a chance to change anyone's mind. Less than a half-hour after the House got the 136 votes necessary to override Gov. Casey's veto of House Bill 185 and overturn the city gun law, the House bucked tradition and voted to refuse a reconsideration of the vote. "Reconsideration votes are normally a courtesy extended regardless of people's passions or positions," said Tim Potts, an aide to House Speaker H. William DeWeese (D., Greene). But the House voted 107-92 against reconsidering the override, effectively sending the measure to the Senate, which promptly voted to override and thus rescind the city assault-weapons ban. While there's no guarantee that advocates of the assault-weapon ban would have persuaded House members to change their minds, with the reconsideration blocked, no one will ever know. The procedural tactic was the coup de grace for the pro-gun forces, who waged a months-long campaign in the legislature to get rid of the assault- weapons bans. The whole episode has angered Philadelphia City Councilman Angel Ortiz, sponsor of the city ordinance. "While the rest of the country is heading into the 21st century, the Pennsylvania legislature is going back to Dodge City," Ortiz said. "I thought there were some politicians with guts out there. Some Philadelphia politicians obviously don't represent the people of Philadelphia; they represent the National Rifle Association interests."

146.text: GUN-1505 PRIMARY Don't blame the NRA The May 15 letter headlined "Officer's killing tragic" seems to be placing the blame for Officer Randy Schipani's death on the National Rifle Association and the "system". The NRA has nearly 3 million paying members, as well as millions of hunters and gun owners who agree with it on gun-control issues. The NRA can and has swung elections. The "system" didn't give the NRA its power; it went out and got it. The letter writer is obviously ignoring the fact that no gun-control law could have prevented Brad Perry from having the gun used to shoot Officer Schipani. The letter writer is correct in pointing out that the problem is violence. Violence has become widely accepted in our culture as a way of solving problems, and we need to find ways to change that. But guns do not cause violence any more than matches cause arson. JOHN H. CAMPBELL Atlanta

144.text: GUN-11604 PRIMARY Judge sides with San Mateo County over guns in parks A San Mateo County Superior Court judge on Monday dismissed a lawsuit challenging an ordinance that prohibits people from bringing guns into county parks, but gun-rights advocates promise to appeal. Judge V. Raymond Swope ruled in favor of the county, finding its ban on firearms and dangerous weapons in parks does not violate state law. The Calguns Foundation, based in San Carlos, argued people with permits to carry concealed guns should be allowed to bring them to parks. Deputy county counsel David Silberman said Swopes recognized the county's authority to oversee its property and make it safe for residents. "The judge got it right," Silberman said. "Even though folks have a permit to carry a concealed weapon, the county has the ability to put in place reasonable regulations which limit the ability of a person to bring guns into county parks." Calguns claims the county ordinance interferes with the rights of citizens who have concealed-weapon permits under state law. A person with a licensed weapon could use it as protection against animals such as mountain lions, the nonprofit maintains. Don Kilmer, attorney for Calguns, said the case will now head to the 1st District Court of Appeal in San Francisco, although the simplest solution, in his view, would be for the county to amend its ordinance to exempt people with concealed-weapon licenses. "I don't think it mattered who won in the trial. Whoever lost was going to appeal," said Kilmer. "We'll let the court of appeal decide who's correct here."

142.text: NA-40474 PRIMARY Fair or Foul: Filibuster compromise; gun control gone wild FAIR: There are a lot of people unhappy about the filibuster compromise reached last week by U.S. Senate leaders Harry Reid, D-Nev., and Mitch McConnell, R-Ky. The far right is mad anytime the word "compromise" is used. On the left, folks think Mr. Reid should have used his Senate majority to make it much harder for the minority party to block the president's appointments and the majority's will. We wish the deal would have included the requirement for a "standing filibuster," which is what works pretty well in the Missouri Senate. But the very fact that Mr. Reid and Mr. McConnell are starting the new Congress finding some common ground is worth applauding. Republicans have found a bit of sanity post-election, refusing, so far, to hold the nation's economy hostage over the debt ceiling. The GOP is moving in the right direction on immigration. Mr. McConnell is willing to work with Mr. Reid to improve the ability for the Senate to function. Hopeful signs all. FOUL: Missouri state Sen. Maria Chappelle-Nadal, D-University City, has filed a bill that would punish parents for not keeping their firearms away from children. The spirit behind Senate Bill 124 is a good one. But Ms. Chappelle-Nadal goes too far, and contributes to pushing the gun control debate off the rails, by proposing that all parents who own guns will have to notify local schools simply that they are gun owners. Such an invasion of privacy would clearly be unconstitutional. Opponents of any reasonable gun control laws are pointing to this provision as the overreach that it is. It's not as bad as the Republican proposal to criminalize enforcement of federal gun laws, but it falls into the same category: Bills aimed more at drawing publicity than actually solving a problem.

149.text: GUN-11873 PRIMARY Why Gun 'Control' Is Not Enough In the wake of the school massacre in Newtown, Conn., and the resulting renewed debate on gun control in the United States, The Stone will publish a series of essays this week that examine the ethical, social and humanitarian implications of the use, possession and regulation of weapons. Other articles in the series can be found here. ~~~ Americans are finally beginning to have a serious discussion about guns.   One argument we're hearing is the central pillar of the case for private gun ownership: that we are all safer when more individuals have guns because armed citizens deter crime and can defend themselves and others against it when deterrence fails.   Those who don't have guns, it's said, are free riders on those who do, as the criminally disposed are less likely to engage in crime the more likely it is that their victim will be armed. There's some sense to this argument, for even criminals don't like being shot. But the logic is faulty, and a close look at it leads to the conclusion that the United States should ban private gun ownership entirely, or almost entirely. One would think that if widespread gun ownership had the robust deterrent effects that gun advocates claim it has, our country would be freer of crime than other developed societies.   But it's not.   When most citizens are armed, as they were in the Wild West, crime doesn't cease.   Instead, criminals work to be better armed, more efficient in their use of guns ("quicker on the draw"), and readier to use them.   When this happens, those who get guns may be safer than they would be without them, but those without them become progressively more vulnerable.

145.text: GUN-9190 PRIMARY The N.R.A.'s List To the Editor: Bob Herbert (column, Oct. 13) seems to take exception to the National Rifle Association's list of "unfriendly" people and organizations. Like any other organization, the N.R.A. has those who oppose its mission, and like other organizations, it lets its members know who those individuals and groups are. I will be happy to demonstrate a good reason civilians should have arms. It's called the Bill of Rights. As a gun owner and N.R.A. member, I see nothing fanatical about defending our constitutional rights. If anything, I see the N.R.A. as too willing to compromise on gun ownership. If there are groups that don't like the Second Amendment, let them introduce an amendment repealing it. The problem with that is the fact that when gun rights meet American democracy, gun rights generally prevail. CURTIS L. RUSH Coldwater, Kan., Oct. 13, 2003

148.text: GUN-20283 PRIMARY Companies that ban guns put on defensive Ronald Honeycutt didn't hesitate. The Pizza Hut driver had just finished dropping off a delivery when a man holding a gun approached him. Honeycutt wasn't about to become another robbery statistic. He grabbed the 9 mm handgun he always carries in his belt and shot the man more than 10 times, killing him. Honeycutt faced no criminal charges, because prosecutors decided that he acted in self-defense. But the 39-year-old did lose his job: Carrying a gun violated Pizza Hut's no-weapons rule. "It's not fair," says Honeycutt of Carmel, Ind., who has found another pizza-delivery job and continues to carry a gun. "There is a constitutional right to bear arms. If I'm going to die, I'd rather be killed defending myself." Employers have long banned guns from the workplace as part of a violence-prevention strategy, but those policies are being tested as states pass laws making it easier for residents to carry concealed guns -- in some cases, crafting legislation that strikes down employers' attempts to keep guns off company property. That means employers, who have traditionally shied away from such politically charged issues as gun control, are filing lawsuits to preserve their no-guns-allowed rules. Gun owners are also fighting back, boycotting companies that ban guns or fire workers for having them. "Are we promoting open firefights in the parking lot?" says Paul Viollis, president of Risk Control Strategies in New York. "For legislation to permit employees and contractors to bring loaded firearms to work in vehicles is blatantly irresponsible."

151.text: GUN-8278 PRIMARY LAW AIDED COP SHOOTER License loophole led to gunman's 20-yr. stockpil e The death-wish gunman who shot four cops in a Brooklyn gunfight was able to legally buy weapons for 20 years because of a loophole in city licensing laws, according to officials and gun-control advocates. Arthur Alalouf, 47, shot dead after a six-hour siege Saturday, acquired permits for many of the shotguns and pistols in his arsenal in the 1980s. Privacy laws and the city's licensing procedures mean that nobody checked Alalouf's mental health status as he had the licenses renewed year after year. During that time, he began taking an anti-depressant and earned a reputation as a dangerous crank in his Gravesend neighborhood. Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and other gun-law advocates consider the failure to update someone's mental health status a dangerous shortfall in licensing laws. "The method by which people are checked for mental illness has more holes than Swiss cheese," Schumer said. "We often don't know who has a mental infirmity that would prohibit someone from using a gun . . . " Karen Pakstis, assistant commissioner of the Police Department's license division, said cops check for arrests, warrants, orders of protection and driving records when someone applies to renew a gun permit. As for mental history, "there is no repository for us to continue to update that information," she said. Felons and people hospitalized for mental illness are automatically denied pistol permits. First-time applicants must pass a personal interview and must allow the city to check their records with the state Mental Hygiene Department.

152.text: NA-45961 PRIMARY Ohio Woman's new gun permit comes in handy A disabled 64-year-old woman who said she got her license to carry a concealed weapon in June to protect herself, repeatedly fired her gun at an intruder during an attempted home invasion robbery overnight, local Middletown police said. No one was hurt, and the intruder, a 21-year-old woman, is under arrest and facing several charges including aggravated burglary and drug abuse, police said. "I can shoot good. If I'd a had my glasses on, I probably would have shot better," Mabel Fletcher said. "But who thinks about putting on their glasses when they're getting broken into?" Officers arrived at her apartment in the 900 block of Kensington Street just after midnight when they responded to a report of shots being fired. Fletcher told them an intruder came into her home while she was sleeping. Her dog, a white bichon frisé named Benji, woke her, so Fletcher scrambled in the dark for the 9mm Glock handgun she bought in February. She keeps it in the nightstand next to her bed. Fletcher, who has had a stroke,  said she confronted the intruder with her gun as the woman came into her bedroom. The woman kept advancing,  so Fletcher squeezed off a round in self-defense. The intruder fled out a back window. The Cincinnati Enquirer

155.text: GUN-11128 PRIMARY 'GUNSHINE STATE' ; How NRA attained dominance in Florida Gun rights supporters and gun control activists alike see Florida as one of the nation's gun-friendliest states. Its reputation as the "Gunshine State" is rooted in politics, culture and the seemingly irresistible force of Marion Hammer, a soft-spoken grandmother who parlayed her gun rights advocacy in Florida into becoming the first female president of the National Rifle Association. The Feb. 26 shooting death of unarmed Trayvon Martin by neighborhood watch volunteer George Zimmerman in Sanford has cast a national spotlight on Florida's first-in-the-nation "stand your ground" law, which allows individuals who feel threatened to use deadly force to defend themselves in any public place where they have a right to be. But that law is just one of a slew of pro-gun laws that Florida has put on the books in the past 25 years.
