It started in 1987 when Florida became the first state to create a "shall issue" concealed weapons license. The law, which took the NRA several years to get passed, requires Florida to issue the licenses to anyone who hasn't committed a felony, is 21 or older, and meets certain other requirements that gun control advocates say are too permissive. Previously, laws limited the permits to certain professions or allowed counties to decide who could get them. Florida's law became a model for the nation and helped Hammer, the NRA lobbyist in the state, to become the organization's president from 1995 to 1998.

147.text: GUN-10783 PRIMARY 4 border states face new rule on gun sales The Obama administration implemented its most aggressive and controversial gun measure to date Monday when it ordered dealers in four Southwestern states to report multiple sales of semiautomatic rifles to the federal firearms bureau. The rule, which had been opposed by the National Rifle Association and many members of Congress, takes effect immediately and is meant to stem gunrunning to violent Mexican drug gangs. It requires about 8,500 dealers operating in the border states of California, Arizona, New Mexico and Texas to alert authorities when a person buys within five days two or more semiautomatic rifles greater than .22-caliber with detachable magazines. Deputy Attorney General James Cole said the rule will help the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives "detect and disrupt the illegal weapons trafficking networks responsible for diverting firearms from lawful commerce to criminals and criminal organizations." NRA chief lobbyist Chris W. Cox said the rifle association will take the firearms bureau to court. "ATF and the administration lack the statutory authority to do this, and the NRA will file suit as soon as ATF sends the first demand letters" to dealers, Cox said. The administration has been loath to take on the gun lobby, and one anti-gun group, the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence, has given Obama a failing grade on gun violence. But pro-gun activists seized on the decision, made by the White House Office of Management and Budget, to accuse the administration of trying to take attention away from a bungled ATF anti-smuggling operation.http://www.washingtonpost.com/investigations/atf-director-testifies-on -bungled-gun-operation/2011/07/06/gIQAhy3d0H_story.html

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives
NRA chief lobbyist Chris W. Cox
Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence
White House Office of Management and Budget

153.text: GUN-459 PRIMARY Senate Again Votes to Ban Assault Weapons for 3 Years The Senate today narrowly reaffirmed its decision to impose a three-year ban on certain types of semiautomatic assault weapons. By a vote of 50 to 48, the lawmakers beat back an attempt by Senator Phil Gramm, Republican of Texas, to remove the ban from an omnibus crime bill. The action broke a three-week deadlock that had stalled final passage. Today's reaffirmation of the three-year ban on the sale and manufacture of nine types of domestically produced semiautomatic pistols, rifles and shotguns is the third approval from the senators in a little more than a month. Debate Shifts to House ''Three times,'' said Jeff Muchnick, legislative director of the Coalition to Stop Gun Violence, which has urged the Senate to uphold the ban. '' I don't know how many more times they want to vote on it, but it looks like we're going to get it.'' Gun control opponents, including the National Rifle Association, say the battle over assault weapons will now shift to the House, where a bill that would ban these firearms permanently has been approved by the Judiciary Committee. ''We feel confident in the House that this legislation will go down to defeat,'' said Wayne LaPierre, executive director of the rifle association's Institute of Legislative Action, who expressed disappointment at today's Senate vote. Mr. Gramm, a staunch opponent of gun control, had sought to replace the assault weapon ban with stiff mandatory sentences, including the death penalty, for Federal felonies committed with firearms and for some drug offenses. But Senator Dennis DeConcini, Democrat of Arizona, who sponsored the ban, attached an amendment to Mr. Gramm's proposal reinstating the original restrictions, eviscerating the Texan's proposal.   By STEVEN A. HOLMES WASHINGTON, June 28 - The Senate today narrowly reaffirmed its decision to impose a three-year ban on certain types of semiautomatic assault weapons.

154.text: GUN-2939 PRIMARY LETTERS FROM THE PEOPLE Will Concealed Weapons Curb The Crime Wave? The lead March 13 editorial, "Fewer Guns, More Realistic Sentences," targets, once again, the wishes of honest Missouri citizens who want the ability to protect themselves from criminals while outside their homes.   Unfortunately, the Post-Dispatch editorial staff pays no attention to what has happened throughout the rest of the country. Thirty-five states now allow honest citizens to carry concealed self-defense weapons. None of the terrible results forecast by anti-gun fanatics and closed-minded media has taken place. In fact, states like Florida show a reduction in crime and a tiny number for licensees losing their permit. The Post-Dispatch and others often quote law enforcement officials who are against armed honest citizens. What they don't print is the fact that street cops say they have no fear of armed lawful citizens, only armed criminals.   Even Rep. Richard Gephardt, formerly very anti-gun, now says that people should have the right to carry concealed weapons. The Post-Dispatch has printed FBI statistics showing that private citizens kill nearly as many criminals as the police. What is not reported is that more than 2.5 million criminal acts each year are prevented by armed victims! According to another article in the Post-Dispatch, the Justice Department has stated that crime victims are much safer when they defend themselves.   Just showing a firearm to the attacker results in the ending of that criminal act in most cases.

150.text: GUN-11655 PRIMARY After public outcry, Pleasant Hill to review home gun sales PLEASANT HILL -- Under pressure from residents, city leaders agreed Monday to hold a workshop to review home gun sales. "I think this should be discussed at the highest level," said Councilman Michael Harris. He wanted to talk about residential and commercial firearms and ammunition sales, review police data on existing gun shops and look at other cities' ordinances banning home gun sales. The rest of the council, however, wanted to restrict the workshop to home sales only; Councilman Jack Weir voted against even having a discussion. The controversy began when members of the Contra Costa chapter of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence learned that an online firearms business is operating from a Pleasant Hill condominium. In October, the city approved Rico Tedjakusuma's request for a home occupation permit for an office for LNC Arms, an online firearms business. Tedjakusuma listed a condo on Scottsdale Road in the Tres Lagos North development as the business address on his permit application. Tedjakusuma is a federally licensed firearms dealer and has passed criminal background checks by the Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco and Firearms and the state Department of Justice, according to Pleasant Hill police Lt. Dan Connelly. Tedjakusuma's home occupation permit is for a home office only, and it prohibits him from selling or delivering guns to customers at his residence. Pleasant Hill's zoning code doesn't require public notice or a public hearing for a home occupation use as an office, so the council never reviewed Tedjakusuma's proposal.

characters: Contra Costa chapter of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence
Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco and Firearms
Pleasant Hill police Lt. Dan Connelly

156.text: GUN-8702 PRIMARY This Time, No Million To March Over Guns; Anniversary Effort Is a Local Affair A year ago, it drew Hollywood stars and hundreds of thousands of women to an unprecedented national protest against gun violence. Yesterday, the Million Mom March returned to the capital, but as a shadow of its former self. In part, that was by design. Organizers decided this year to hold marches across the country, aimed at influencing local legislation, rather than a national event. But yesterday's protests also demonstrated the difficulties facing the Million Mom March -- whose new slogan is "from a march to a movement" -- and gun-control advocates in general. "I actually expected more [people], but we did this with no money. Considering we had no money, we did pretty well," said Claudette Perry, director of the Million Mom March office in Washington. She spoke after about 200 protesters marched from Seventh Street NW on the Mall to Freedom Plaza, chanting: "What do we want? Gun control! When do we want it? Now!" While last year's event featured celebrities including Rosie O'Donnell, this year's Mother's Day rally emphasized local political figures, including D.C. Mayor Anthony A. Williams (D), and gun-control advocates. A similar rally, sponsored by the Million Mom March Northern Virginia chapter, took place in Alexandria's Market Square. Organizers estimated 500 people turned out for an afternoon's worth of speeches by elected and religious leaders and victims of gun violence. Last year's Washington march was the biggest ever against gun violence, with organizers estimating they drew 750,000. Speakers at the time hailed it as a turning point in the debate over gun control. Since then, leaders say the Million Mom March has become a grass-roots group with about 230 chapters nationwide.

157.text: NA-41264 PRIMARY 'We've got to make them afraid of us' Mike's $50M pledge to fight NRA IN HIS FIRST major political investment since leaving City Hall, former Mayor Michael Bloomberg is taking aim at one of his favorite targets - the NRA. Bloomberg plans to spend $50 million of his vast fortune to bankroll grass-roots efforts to build a national network dedicated to passing stricter gun-control laws in America, The New York Times reported Tuesday. Bloomberg said the game plan calls for absorbing the gun control groups that he has funded - Mayors Against Illegal Guns and Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America - into an umbrella group known as Everytown for Gun Safety. They will focus enrollment efforts in 15 states, including such pro-gun strongholds as Texas, and seek to land a million new backers this year. The strategy takes a long view of how to produce change on gun law reform, and is designed to beat the National Rifle Association at its own game: developing the political clout needed to get lawmakers to back the group's agenda - and to punish those who don't. A main focus of the grass-roots drive will be to bring women, particularly mothers, into the fold - aÌ? la the advocacy group Mothers Against Drunk Driving. "You've got to work at it piece by piece," Bloomberg told The Times. "One mom and another mom. You've got to wear them down until they finally say, 'Enough.' "

characters: Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America

161.text: GUN-497 PRIMARY LETTERLINE Why Johnny won't return a form: With all the speculation about why the census forms are slow in coming, I wonder why there has been little attention paid to illiteracy. Not long ago, all kinds of statistics pointed to a staggering number of people who can't read. Maybe part of the census problem is that recipients don't know it from junk mail. Robert H. Donahugh Youngstown, Ohio Protect gun rights: A recent editorial called for a ban on assault rifles. You made the assertion that a ban would save lives. Just across the river from your offices is a prime example of how futile and counterproductive bans on firearms are. In Washington, D.C., there has existed a virtual ban on the private ownership of just about any type of firearm for the past decade. Yet in our nation's capital exists the highest murder rate in the nation. Clearly, the only result of such bans on the private ownership of firearms is a populace that is unable to protect itself from the violence fueled by the drug pushers. The Second Amendment was created by our forefathers for one reason: To provide the individual the means to protect himself and his constitutional rights from an oppressive government and, by doing so, prevent an oppressive regime from coming to power in the first place. Steve Putnam Mojave, Calif.

158.text: GUN-5385 PRIMARY NATION IN BRIEF State Blocks Atlanta Gun Liability Suit ATLANTA -- After heavy lobbying by the National Rifle Association, Gov. Roy Barnes yesterday signed a bill blocking Atlanta's liability lawsuit against gun manufacturers. The law, which goes into effect immediately, makes Georgia the first state to prevent a city or county from filing product liability suits against gun makers. The law reserves that right for the state. Barnes, a Democrat, said the state has the right to force Atlanta to avoid the costs of pursuing its lawsuit. Atlanta filed its suit against 17 gun makers last week as the city was the host of the nation's largest gun show. As with similar cases in Chicago, New Orleans, Bridgeport, Conn., and Miami, Atlanta accused the industry of negligently designing guns with inadequate safety devices and sought reimbursement for the costs of gun-related violence. The city plans to move forward with its lawsuit and see how the court responds, Mayor Bill Campbell said through a spokesman. "Now it's in the hands of the courts to take the appropriate action and determine . . . which side will prevail," spokesman Nick Gold said. "We don't feel it's constitutional for the state to tell the municipalities who they can and cannot sue." The bill was supported by the NRA's lobbying muscle. The organization, which says it has 93,000 Georgia members, backed Barnes in the 1998 election.

162.text: GUN-10952 PRIMARY In Firearms Research, Cause Is Often the Missing Element Ultimately, the conundrum in firearms research comes in establishing what scientists call ''causality.'' An analysis, for instance, of the 62 most urbanized counties in the United States conducted for The New York Times by Daniel Webster, co-director of the Johns Hopkins Center for Gun Policy and Research, found that higher rates of gun ownership correlate with higher rates of per capita homicide, after adjusting for residents' ages. On average, counties in the top quartile when it comes to the prevalence of guns -- determined by a proxy that academics use based on the ratio of firearms suicides to overall suicides -- had an age-adjusted homicide-rate that was 63 percent higher than those in the bottom quartile. But as anyone familiar with basic statistics knows, correlation does not equal causation. Dr. Webster pointed out that most of the counties in the bottom quartile in gun ownership, with generally lower per capita homicide rates, are in states like New York, New Jersey and Massachusetts, which have strict gun control policies. ''Cities that have gone the furthest to keep guns out of the hands of dangerous people have the lowest homicide rates,'' he said. A prominent exception is the District of Columbia, which has an elevated homicide rate and lower levels of gun ownership. But Dr. Webster said this could be explained by how easy it is to buy guns in the surrounding region.

159.text: GUN-3280 PRIMARY SUPERVISOR PROPOSES BAN ON GUN SHOWS Supervisor Tom Huening will call on his colleagues Tuesday to ban gun sales and shows at the San Mateo County Exposition Center. He proposes an outright ban beginning in 1996 on all firearms shows at the center in San Mateo. To make the change, the county would have to amend a contract with the center's operators. Gun shows provide the Expo Center about $100,000 in annual revenue, according to a staff report. To make up for the lost money, the county would give the Expo Center $100,000 in the first year of the ban and $80,000 in 1997. Huening, who could not be reached for comment, said in the report that county facilities should not be used to promote the use or sales of guns. Sandy Cooney, western regional director of Handgun Control Inc., said some gun show dealers sell guns without proper background checks and waiting periods. Bob Delfay, president of the National Shooting Sports Foundation, said only .00006 percent of all ammunition used in the United States in 1992 resulted in a violent or accidental shooting or firearms death. He said the statistic was calculated at a meeting last month of firearms industry representatives and crime experts at the congressional Office of Technology Assessment in Washington, D.C. Delfay said 35,400 firearms-related deaths were reported in 1992. The National Shooting Sports Foundation is a Connecticut-based trade group whose 1,200 members include major firearms companies and sporting goods manufacturers.

163.text: GUN-30203 PRIMARY IT'S NOT THE GUN, IT'S THE CRIMINAL USING IT In response to the two letters to the editor dated April 22, "Gun-control opponents bear measure of blame," and "It's time to federalize gun control, oversight," as a former police officer, prosecutor and police firearms instructor, gun control is not an answer to the Virginia Tech tragedy or crime in general. Gun control never has been effective. In fact, those states with the most restrictive gun-control statutes have much higher rates of violent crime -- Washington, D.C., New York City, and Camden, N.J. It is time to stop blaming the gun and put criminals behind bars. Law-abiding citizens should be allowed to carry concealed weapons. The Virginia tragedy would not have occurred had the shooter been faced with an armed potential victim. If the letter-writers and proponents of gun control would sit in a criminal court for a day, they would observe defendants with long histories of crimes committed; however, by and large in major cities, criminals are released over and over due to overcrowding in prisons. If either of the writers is from major cities like New York City, maybe they should return to that city with its restrictive gun-control policies and high crime if they'd feel safer than in Florida. Or spend some time observing the criminal justice system. Maybe then they will decide to apply for a gun permit. MITCHELL COHEN Jupiter

160.text: GUN-31231 PRIMARY Let the District Have Its Gun Law On Jan. 11, the DC Appleseed Center for Law and Justice  filed a brief in the Supreme Court, asking the court to uphold the District's gun control law  in the case of District of Columbia v. Heller. Our brief was co-sponsored by the D.C. League of Women Voters, the D.C. Chamber of Commerce, the Federal City Council, DC for Democracy and the Washington Council of Lawyers. All of these local organizations have one key interest in common: They believe that important public policy questions affecting District residents should be decided by local elected officials, not by the federal courts. In Heller, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit  struck down the District's ban on handguns on the grounds that any law banning any single type of "arms" is, necessarily, a violation of the  Second Amendment right to bear arms. We believe  this is wrong. Nearly every state has a law banning some type of firearm, such as  assault weapons or  sawed-off shotguns. And those laws vary not only as to the kinds of arms they ban but in many other ways as well. There are hundreds of  state and local gun control laws. Each of those measures was enacted by local elected officials in response to local needs and desires. And each of those local governments  is called on to balance people's desire to possess weapons for self-defense against the obligation to protect public safety.

characters: DC Appleseed Center for Law and Justice

164.text: GUN-6556 PRIMARY LETTERS TO THE EDITOR Are new laws needed on gun control? I was appalled at the Feb. 11 editorial backing legislation allowing St. Louis and Kansas City to pass more restrictive gun control laws. Has anyone at the Post-Dispatch considered the consequences of a patchwork of firearms laws? I can picture driving through St. Louis, with my perfectly legal firearm, recently deemed illegal by St. Louis city. I get a speeding ticket on the highway, and during a search I find that the firearm, locked in my trunk, is now illegal. The police arrest and charge me, all because I was unaware of a change in the law in a community where I do not live. The assumption is that St. Louis or Kansas City can create some additional laws that will decrease firearm crime. Under current state law, it is already illegal to carry a concealed weapon, to brandish a weapon, to be drunk and carry a firearm, to carry a functioning firearm within reach while driving, for a felon to posses a firearm and, in St. Louis, to carry a firearm openly. What more do you want? Does anyone really think that a felon (who is already breaking the law by possessing a firearm) is going to worry about an additional law prohibiting a certain firearm? All this legislation will do is allow municipalities to further restrict and limit law-abiding citizens in a way that will have no impact on firearm crimes.

168.text: GUN-662 PRIMARY Gun lobby just can't admit that guns are dangerous It has been a generation since the Legislature recognized that it    could do something to stop children from suffocating in abandoned    refrigerators. Children who might have died are parents themselves now,    thanks to a law that punishes people who abandon refrigerators and    freezers without removing the doors - and which punishes them even more    severely if a child's death results. The Legislature has now acknowledged that guns are at least as    dangerous as refrigerators. But it's no thanks to the gun lobby that the new law was passed. Marion Hammer and her robots in the Legislature blocked its passage for    two years and kept chipping at it last week despite the prospect of    certain defeat. It takes a lot more than three dead kids in a single    week to shame them. You have to wonder if anything could. Here's a lobby that has insisted ad nauseam that there's nothing    wrong with guns that couldn't be fixed by punishing the people who    misuse them. So what does it say when just such a bill comes along? That it would be too cruel to jail parents who are grieving over the    loss of their children. In other words, don't punish anyone. By the same reasoning, the drunken driver who kills someone    shouldn't go to jail because he might be a parent who killed his own    child. Or at least there ought to be an exemption for parents. But you    never heard the gun lobby saying that while the drunken driving laws    were being strengthened. You didn't hear the automobile lobby saying it    either.

165.text: GUN-30852 PRIMARY Oh, That Past One of the divertissements and disillusions of the primary season is watching the presidential contenders double back in denial of their own local political roots as they play to the biases of their parties' national nominating base. A prime example occurs today when Rudolph Giuliani, former mayor of New York -- where the murder rate made him a chief advocate of banning assault weapons on the home front -- seeks the blessing of the National Rifle Association. As he approaches his speech in Washington before the N.R.A.'s lock-n-load zealots, the Giuliani campaign denies that its contender is trying to revise and amend his well-documented past as a fervid City Hall lobbyist for federal gun controls. ''What works in New York doesn't necessarily work in Mississippi or Montana,'' the Giuliani campaign now declares, presenting the candidate as a true adherent of states' rights when it comes to the gun mayhem taking 30,000 American lives a year. (We're sure he'd rather not remember that President Bill Clinton lauded him for helping pass the now-defunct national ban on assault weapons.) Better not look for Mr. Giuliani, so sensitized to the red-state G.O.P. base, to repeat any of his past warnings that 60 percent of the crime-making guns in his city came from southern states brandishing porous gun controls. In a politically eerie coincidence, the multicity lawsuit previously led by then-Mayor Giuliani, accusing the gun industry of liability for the runaway gun culture, is being argued this week in federal court.

166.text: GUN-20270 PRIMARY Research shows Brady Act cut some suicides A new study says the Brady Act, a federal law requiring background checks and waiting periods for handgun buyers, likely has reduced the suicide rate of older Americans. But the law, enacted in 1994, has had no apparent impact on the overall rates of suicides or homicides, according to today's issue of The Journal of the American Medical Association. The study's findings provoked strong words Tuesday on both sides of the gun-control debate. The National Rifle Association said the research shows that gun regulations such as the Brady Act have no effect on crime. Advocates of stricter gun laws said the study is not an appropriate measure of the act's success. According to the study, the suicide rate among people 55 and older fell 6%-9% in the 32 states whose laws changed under the Brady Act. The decrease showed up only in those states instituting waiting periods. States that enacted instant checks, or states that already had some type of handgun regulation in place, showed little or no drop in suicides among seniors. "It's the waiting period that matters. And it's the opportunity to delay the (suicide) impulse that seems to be important," says Philip Cook, a public policy professor at Duke University and co-author of the study. Cook and fellow researcher Jens Ludwig of Georgetown University analyzed data from the National Center for Health Statistics from 1985 through 1997.

167.text: NA-43841 PRIMARY Congress reacts to Obama's gun control proposals Much initial reaction to President Obama's proposals to limit gun violence is breaking down along predictably partisan lines, with Democrats expressing support and Republicans adopting a tepid wait-and-see approach. But even some Democrats are taking a cautious stance. Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid (D-Nev.) predicted recently that Obama's most ambitious proposal -- an outright ban on assault weapons -- has almost no chance of passing the Republican-led House. Michael Steel, a spokesman for House Speaker John A. Boehner (R-Ohio), said Tuesday that House committees of jurisdiction will review the president's recommendations. "And if the Senate passes a bill, we will also take a look at that," Steel said. Rep. Carolyn McCarthy (D-N.Y.), a longtime gun control advocate whose husband was killed in a mass shooting on the Long Island Railroad in 1993, called on the the Democratic-controlled Senate to consider and pass a single comprehensive measure including most, if not all, of Obama's proposals. "We know that a lot of Republicans in the House will probably vote for the background checks, a lot of them will vote for the mental illness [proposals]," she told CNN moments after Obama's speech. "So those are things that they will vote for. If we start taking them one by one, the chances in the House will be more difficult." Senate action on a comprehensive measure "might give us some strength, that might  give some members of Congress the spine to do the right thing," McCarthy said.

characters: Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid

169.text: GUN-30763 PRIMARY F.D.I.C. Post Seems Remote For Official In New York This much is known: As of last week, it was assumed in Washington and on Wall Street that the White House would nominate Diana L. Taylor, the New York State banking supervisor -- and companion of Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg -- as the new chairwoman of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation. Associates said she had told them the nomination to the F.D.I.C. was in the offing, the White House had signaled to some staff members on the Senate committee that approves such appointments that her nomination was being prepared, and F.B.I. background investigators were calling her friends and colleagues. This much is not known: What exactly changed in the last few days to scuttle the expected nomination, which now seems unlikely. One senior aide on the Senate Banking Committee, who, citing the sensitivity of the matter, spoke on the condition of anonymity, pointed yesterday to the National Rifle Association. The mayor is singling out the group for criticism as he begins a new campaign for tougher national gun laws. '' Our understanding is that the nomination was going to be sent to the Senate this week,'' the aide said. When it was not, the aide said, his office questioned the White House, other Banking Committee staff members and the Pataki administration. '' The reason we were given is that the N.R.A. had a bee in its bonnet about Bloomberg's position on guns and wanted to try to send him a message,'' the aide said.

171.text: GUN-5705 PRIMARY The Culture Wars Rage On I guess it's too much to ask the House members to vote their consciences when it comes to the gun problem and the resultant tragedies in our society ["House Votes to Weaken Senate Gun Show Checks," front page, June 18]. I know that in today's world money usually speaks louder than words. Little did I know, until now, that money -- namely, the NRA's -- can speak louder than actions. Clearly, Littleton and the other school tragedies -- not to mention what happens every night on the mean streets of Washington, Detroit, the Bronx and Camden -- should have taught a lesson. Sadly, the lesson remains unlearned in the House. GLENN HAVINOVISKI Fairfax I am appalled that the Republican-controlled House refused to enact a three-day waiting period for those purchasing firearms at gun shows. Rep. Bob Barr of Georgia, an NRA member, thinks that posting the Ten Commandments in schools will do more to reduce violence than taking guns out of the hands of criminals who make purchases at gun shows. Maybe these lawmakers should post their own Ten Commandments: (1) Thou shalt not make it easier for others to kill. (2)Thou shalt not be in the pocket of the NRA. (3) Thou shalt not make senseless statements about the Second Amendment. (3) Thou shalt not prevent cities from filing suits against the gun industry.

170.text: GUN-31329 PRIMARY On Voting Rights, A Missed Opportunity Once again, the perfect has become the enemy of the good. I refer to the demise of the D.C. Voting Rights Bill ["D.C. Vote Supporters Defer Fight in Congress," Metro, June 10]. City officials, angry that Congress would add a rider to the bill gutting the District's gun laws, dug in their heels and demanded the rider be removed. Meanwhile, Democratic leaders in Congress did not want to move the bill forward without support from our locally elected leaders. The fact is, ever since the Supreme Court's decision in District of Columbia v. Heller, pro-gun majorities in Congress have been looking for a chance to interfere with the city's gun laws. They will do so whether as part of a voting rights act, our annual appropriations bill or some other legislative vehicle. It increasingly appears that we will end up with no gun laws and no vote, thanks to the "my way or the highway" attitude of our local leaders. BOB DARDANO Washington

173.text: GUN-11952 PRIMARY Newtown shootings change tenor of debate on gun violence WASHINGTON o Ann Wagner skipped holiday events Sunday evening to tune in to the memorial service for the Newtown massacre victims, where President Barack Obama spoke of the nation's obligation to prevent more such violence. Wagner, a newly elected U.S. House member from Ballwin, lives in a home with more than a dozen weapons and describes herself as "a big Second Amendment supporter." Yet she believes that a number of issues related to the shootings - including potentially stronger gun control laws - must be aired in a national dialogue seeking ways to prevent another tragedy like the shootings at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn., that took the lives of 20 children and six adults. "I watched things unfold on Friday and have been moved to tears several times since," Wagner said. "If we don't seize this moment, it's really going to be a travesty." In the past, Republicans such as Wagner have been loathe to endorse discussions that venture into the topic of stricter gun laws. Democrats, too, have been reluctant to advance the gun control debate out of fear of reprisals from the gun lobby. But the horror that unfolded in rural Connecticut this holiday season appear to have changed the tenor of the debate, emboldening proponents of stronger gun laws. One key will be whether the outrage ebbs with the passage of time and the arrival of other urgent issues in Congress. And some voices are calling for a measured response - one that doesn't focus exclusively on gun control.

175.text: GUN-4649 PRIMARY DEADLY WAVE OF WEAPONS FROM THE GUN-SHINE STATE Kenneth Herndon, a burly 250-pounder with a pit bull tattoo and a criminal record to match, could be a poster boy for Florida gun shops, authorities say. Although on probation, under indictment and twice convicted, Herndon was able to buy at least 31 guns because he had obtained proof he was a law-abiding resident of the Sunshine State. He did that by presenting phony New York identification. Herndon, 24, of Queens, was arrested Thursday, four days after a gunman opened fire atop the Empire State Building with a pistol he bought in Florida. Ali Abu Kamal, a Palestinian in the United States on a visitor's visa who was living in a motel, got a .380-caliber Beretta at a Melbourne gun shop with a temporary Florida identification card. Last Sunday, on the 86th-floor observatory of the city's renowned skyscraper, he shot seven people one fatally and then killed himself in a rage against "enemies." Law enforcement officials said the Herndon and Kamal cases illustrate the disparity in attitude between New York and Florida. "With Florida, there is no concern about violence; it's profit motive," Police Commissioner Howard Safir said. "They couldn't care less." Florida is a primary source state for illegal firearms in New York City, "and it has been forever," said Dan Rather, chief of the Manhattan district attorney's firearms trafficking unit. "The lesson is how compliant or eager some Florida dealers are to make sales in questionable situations," Rather said.

174.text: GUN-149 PRIMARY Guns in Florida: This Week It Becomes a Lot Easier to Bear Arms Legally A new gun law goes into effect this week in Florida, where violent crime is about as common as it is anywhere, and it will immediately be a lot easier to carry a weapon legally. Under the new law, which supersedes more restrictive local ordinances, the state can grant virtually anyone a permit to carry a hidden pistol, knife, club or tear-gas gun. Guns in plain view will carry no restrictions at all. The increase in violence here helped persuade legislators to liberalize the rules. ''Victims of crime must also have rights,'' said State Representative Ronald C. Johnson of Panama City, the law's chief sponsor. But many of those in charge of combatting that increase, including the state Attorney General and urban police chiefs in South Florida, have been among the law's most adamant opponents. Only in Florida National lobbyists on both sides of the issue say the law is one of the most liberal in the country; they are watching closely to see if there is any effect on crime statistics. A change either way could influence gun control efforts in other states. Only Florida has a law ''in which a license to carry a concealed gun is given by the state,'' said Sarah Brady, vice-chairman of Handgun Control Inc., a Washington-based lobbying group. '' We believe that this kind of decision should be made on a local level where people know one another rather than applying to a state office.''

176.text: GUN-30903 PRIMARY The Drug Cartels' Right to Bear Arms The hypocrisy grows all too gruesome: The Justice Department pronounced the Mexican drug cartels ''a national security threat'' this week, even as American gun dealers along the border were busily arming the cartels' murderous gangs. Mexico complains that American dealers supplied most of the 20,000 weapons seized last year in drug wars in which 6,000 Mexicans died. A vast arms bazaar is rampant along the four border states, enabled by porous to nonexistent American gun laws. Straw buyers can pick up three or four high-powered war rifles from one of more than 6,600 border dealers and hand them off to smugglers. They easily return to Mexico, where gun laws are far less permissive. Licensed dealers routinely recruit buyers with clean criminal records to foil weak laws and feed the deadly pipeline, according to a report by James C. McKinley Jr. in The Times. The countless unlicensed ''gun enthusiasts'' free to deal battlefield rifles at weekend shows, thanks to loophole-ridden laws, are a second source. The federal government is allowed to only trace weapons used in crimes and has no idea of the full scope of the border trade, which accounts for 9 out of 10 recovered weapons. One dealer exploited the lack of federal controls by packing up his California shop, where laws were tougher, and moving to the lenient Arizona border. He is accused of selling hundreds of AK-47 rifles to the cartels before he was finally arrested in a sting by undercover agents. He's more the exception. At best, 200 agents work the border expanse where gun smugglers operate as a ''parade of ants,'' in the words of one frustrated prosecutor.

178.text: GUN-8544 PRIMARY National Briefing Rockies: Utah: G.O.P. Allows Guns At Its Gathering A year after Republicans fought back an effort in the Legislature to ban guns in churches and schools, party leaders have ruled that guns will be allowed at the Republican state convention except when Vice President Dick Cheney delivers the keynote address. Under a compromise drawn up by State Attorney General Mark L. Shurtleff, delegates to the convention, to be held tomorrow in Sandy, outside Salt Lake City, will be asked to keep their weapons in storage lockers while Mr. Cheney speaks. The Secret Service had expressed concern about the presence of guns, but an earlier decision to ban them from the convention entirely had angered many party members.

characters: State Attorney General Mark L. Shurtleff

179.text: GUN-10870 PRIMARY The Guns of Academe Los Angeles BY Monday, Gov. Jan Brewer of Arizona must decide whether to sign a bill partly lifting her state's ban on guns on college and university campuses. Gun advocates insist that will make campuses safer by discouraging mass killers and giving students the ability to fight back. Gun control proponents warn the law will lead to more lethal violence. Both sides are probably wrong. Gun violence at colleges and universities -- there are fewer than 20 homicides on campus per year -- will probably not be affected much, one way or another. What is really at stake is America's gun culture. Colleges and universities have long been gun-free zones. In 1745, Yale adopted a policy punishing any student who ''shall keep a gun or pistol, or fire one in the college-yard or college.'' Today, most universities, public and private, prohibit anyone but authorized security and law enforcement officers from bringing guns onto campuses. Arizona would join Utah as the only states to require public colleges to permit guns on campus, but Texas and eight other states are considering similar laws. Many find the idea of students with guns shocking. They fear that undergraduates are too young to handle firearms responsibly and that the presence of guns will lead to the deadly escalation of minor disagreements. Others worry about the volatile mix of guns and alcohol. Glocks don't belong at a frat party.

180.text: NA-46270 PRIMARY Readers write A gun purchase is harder than voting I must take umbrage with a recent letter to the editor. The writer makes a broad statement: "There are more restrictions on voting than there are on owning a gun" (Readers write, May 4). Owning a firearm is not complicated or restrictive. It takes a dose of common sense and a thinking mind and carries grave responsibilities. Purchasing a firearm, though, is a complex dance with county, state and federal laws. Registering to vote is not complicated or restrictive, either. While there are conditions that must be met with each process, registering to vote is fairly painless. You can register when you renew your driver's license, online at the library, by mail or in person. To legally purchase a firearm in Georgia is more difficult. While you can register to vote via different methods, purchasing a firearm must be done in person. Even if you order a firearm from out of state, it must be transferred though a federally licensed Georgia dealer. JAMES VAN VALKENBURG, SNELLVILLE Let's put job funds loss in perspective What a great piece on the theft of money that was meant to be spent on training for jobless people ("City squandered job-training funds," News, May 4). It involves the usual suspects who brush it off by saying mistakes were made. It makes me sick to see these unscrupulous people preying on those who truly need job training. How can these people live with themselves?

177.text: GUN-9771 PRIMARY MIKE: PUSH LAWMAKERS ON GUN BILL MAYOR BLOOMBERG wants your help to fight the gun lobby. Last week, the mayor blasted a bill that would bar the city from getting federal data tracing the sale of guns. The city wants the data for a suit against the gun industry. "Call, write or E-mail Congress and tell them to stand up to the gun lobby and protect our city," Bloomberg said in his weekly radio address, aired yesterday on 1010 WINS. City lawyers believe the data will show that some dealers sell most of the guns to criminals and that the industry generally turns a blind eye to the problem. In May, a Brooklyn federal judge ruled the city should get access to the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives records. The bill, which would allow release of the data to prosecutors only for criminal investigations, is sponsored by Rep. Todd Tiahrt (R-Kan.). It was approved by a wide margin last week by the House Appropriations Committee.

182.text: GUN-30697 PRIMARY Open-carry picnic set at St. Croix County Fairgrounds Glenwood City Police Chief Robert Darwin said he's not overly concerned about hundreds of people coming to the St. Croix County Fairgrounds on Aug. 23 armed with loaded sidearms. "I don't have a problem with what they want to do," he said. "They have the lawful right to do that. "My concern is how the general public will respond," Darwin said Wednesday. "I hope they will respect it, since it is lawful. The people coming to this are not the kind of people we need to worry about." Dave "Ole" Olson said he and his wife, Annette, organized the "Open Carry Picnic" from noon to 4:30 p.m. at the fairground primarily to inform people of their Second Amendment right to openly carry sidearms. He plans to hold another event in a few months in Hudson related to First- and Second-Amendment rights, with a nationally-recognized guest speaker. "This is important to me because it has to do with our liberties," he said. "Our passion is liberty and freedom. In my thinking, the country is going in the wrong direction and people need to know that they have the right to carry a gun." Olson said the effort is not sponsored by any group, and said he is spending about $2,000 of his own money for food to handle about 400 people, including barbecue beef sandwiches, beans, coleslaw and other food items.

185.text: NA-42713 PRIMARY What Gun Lovers Think IN most respects, Dan Baum is a political liberal. But he has always had a thing for guns and has just written a book, ''Gun Guys: A Road Trip,'' which is part gun country travelogue and part meditation on what it means to be a gun lover at this particular moment. Baum, who lives in Colorado, agreed to come to New York, where he grew up, to debate the issue with me. JOE: Let's start here: Connecticut just passed what may be the toughest gun law in the country, which includes restrictions on magazine capacity and an assault rifle ban. Sounds good to me. DAN: I'm not one of those gun owners who says, ''You can't ever infringe my rights.'' My orientation is safety. How are we going to live more safely? And, to me, the impulse to ban guns and large-capacity magazines is rooted in a delusion -- that somehow if we ban them, we'll be rid of them. That might have been a good idea 75 years ago, but it's too late. There are 300 million guns in the country. JOE: In your book, though, you make a very different argument for not banning assault weapons. You argue that very few people are killed with them. DAN: That is true. They've been used in these big high-profile mass killings, no doubt about it. But there were no assault rifles at Virginia Tech or Fort Hood.

181.text: NA-42665 PRIMARY Movie Exception For New Gun Law ALBANY -- Six weeks after approving an expanded ban on assault weapons, Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo and lawmakers are considering whether to amend the measure to add an exception -- for Hollywood. Mr. Cuomo said Wednesday that while he and lawmakers did not want assault weapons on New York's streets, they did not intend to ban their use on film shoots, and that concerns had been raised that the new measure could put a damper on the state's growing film and television industry. ''Should you be able to use these types of guns in movies? The answer is yes,'' Mr. Cuomo said in a news conference at the Capitol. '' We do spend a lot of money in this state bringing movie production here.'' Mr. Cuomo, a Democrat, added that fake assault weapons might already be permitted under the new gun laws, but that the film industry wanted clarity. '' People want certainty, and there's no reason not to make a change like that to give an industry comfort, especially when it's an industry that we want to be doing business in this state,'' he said. Advocates of gun rights are pushing for more substantive changes to the law, or even its repeal. Protesters plan to rally at the Capitol on Thursday, in opposition to the new laws, passed soon after the school massacre in Newtown, Conn.

184.text: GUN-8343 PRIMARY Gun Groups Fight Ban on Hidden Arms Around the country, gun rights groups are pressing what supporters call a deliberately gradual effort to ease state laws restricting the carrying of concealed weapons. The National Rifle Association and Gun Owners of America say their efforts are no greater this year than before. But they say the Sept. 11 attacks may have helped their cause. "People feel the need to protect themselves and their loved ones," said Randy Kozuch, the rifle association's chief of lobbying in the states. In the 1980's, at least 40 states prohibited concealed weapons, the rifle association said. Six states, all in the Midwest, prohibit concealed weapons. A dozen others allow them with restrictions. After years of efforts to pass a law allowing people to carry a concealed weapon, Missouri legislators put the issue on the ballot in 1999. The rifle association spent $3.8 million campaigning for the proposal, but it failed because of opposition in St. Louis and Kansas City. Supporters are back before the Legislature, though Gov. Bob Holden, a Democrat, has threatened to veto any bill resembling the 1999 effort.

187.text: GUN-7638 PRIMARY DUELING STATISTICS ON GUN CONTROL, BOTH SIDES MISFIRE DURING the run-up to the Million Mom March, organizers and opponents flooded the airwaves with statistics about guns. For every figure advanced, another was set up in opposition. Commentators queried numbers proffered by their adversaries while accepting unquestioningly data in their favor. The debate has become polarized; each side portrays the other as little more than liars, and its own data as the superior weapon in the battle for public opinion. But statistics are simply the fog of war, blinding people to the underlying reality and turning serious consideration of the issues into a no-man's land. If we want to advance, we have to blow that fog away. The most abused statistic in recent weeks has been the much-quoted figure of "12 (or 13) children killed every day by gun violence." As the Washington Post pointed out, that figure only holds true if you include older teens, aged 15-19, in the figure. Most of those are killed because of their involvement in distinctly un-childlike activities like drug crimes. Using a more usual definition of children (14 or below), even the Centers for Disease Control admit that only 1.7 children a day die from gun violence. The figure goes even lower (to 1.3) if you exclude suicide, where the victims have a range of lethal options for killing themselves besides using guns. Quoting a figure 10 times the size of one fitting the definition of the problem more closely could be described as disingenuous; calling a 19 year-old drug dealer a "child" is definitely stretching the point.

186.text: GUN-9000 PRIMARY Taking away our guns isn't a solution Re: Right to carry guns killing thousands, May 16 letter Editor: If I were to accept the writer's distorted presentation of handgun fatalities around the world, one might have reason to pause and think that the United States was the most barbarian country. If, however, one was to dig a little deeper, some very different conclusions would emerge. Let's take Japan, for example. Handguns are banned there. There is a very different culture, and there are no constitutional protections for anyone. Since all handguns are illegal, it's logical that handgun fatalities are quite low. But, if one takes an overall violence fatality rate, adding the total homicide rate from all causes to the suicide rate from all causes, that result is almost equal to the U.S. rate, according to data from the International Journal of Epidemiology 1998, comparing available data from 1993-1994. Applying the same approach, Switzerland shows a 1994 rate of violent death as 22.80 per 100,000 population (the only accurate method of comparison) while the United States shows a 1993 rate of violent death as 18.57 per 100,000 population. Aren't the Swiss quite civilized? Also, remember that the Swiss enjoy a gun culture whereby automatic weapons are commonplace as each citizen is armed at home in a sort of militia-type defense system (which seemed very effective in keeping any World War II belligerents from crossing the borders).

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not explicitly mentioned)

188.text: GUN-1030 PRIMARY How house voted on 7-day gun wait Here is the 239-186 vote Wednesday in which the House passed the Brady bill. It would establish a 7-day waiting period before handgun purchases to allow local police time to check buyers' backgrounds; 21 states with tougher handgun laws would be exempted. The bill is named for former White House press secretary James Brady who was shot in the 1981 attempted assassination of President Reagan. '' Yes'' indicates the House member voted for the bill, ''No'' means against. An ''X'' indicates a member did not vote. (Defeat for National Rifle Association, 1A, 6A) ALA. 172.text: GUN-2080 PRIMARY LETTERS FROM THE PEOPLE Is Brady Bill Gunning For Trouble?   February 1993 was the bloodiest month for law enforcement in recent memory; 13 federal, state and local law-enforcement officers were fatally shot during that month. In comparison, 64 officers were murdered with firearms in all of 1992.   As law-enforcement officers, it is our job to protect the citizens of the United States, but who is protecting us? The laws of this country should be protecting those who enforce them - not the criminals whose easy access to guns has turned even the most routine of police responsibilities into ones fraught with deadly possibilities.   The Brady bill has again been introduced in Congress. This common-sense lifesaving legislation requires a waiting period of five business days and mandatory background check before the purchase of a handgun.   Last year, the Brady bill passed both the House and Senate but was ultimately blocked by former President George Bush. This year is different; the climate has changed, and more people are getting involved in the effort to make a sensible national gun policy a reality.   The passage in Virginia of the one-per-month limit on handgun purchases and the unanimous vote by the New Jersey Senate to uphold the state's ban on assault weapons, despite fierce lobbying by the National Rifle Association, represent a monumental break from the past. The actions of lawmakers on the state level should echo loudly through the halls of Congress.   The recent gun battle in Waco, Texas, also has pointed out in dramatic fashion the need for federal gun control. A fanatical sect was able to stockpile an arsenal of weapons.   Until we have a strong national gun-control policy, the domestic arms race will continue to spiral out of control and more cops - and more innocent civilians - will die. The Brady bill must be the cornerstone of this policy.   Every major law-enforcement organization in the country has called for passage of waiting period legislation. Ninety-five percent of the American public supports the Brady bill. A recent USA Today/CNN/Gallup Poll showed that even 88 percent of gun owners support the Brady bill, and President Bill Clinton has promised to sign the bill if Congress passes it.   The Brady bill was first introduced in Congress in 1987. Since that time, more than 138,000 Americans have been killed by handguns, more than were killed in World War I, Korea and Vietnam combined. Before members of Congress recess for the Memorial Day holiday, they should pause to think of those who could have been saved by a sensible national gun-control policy, then they should not hesitate to act quickly to pass the Brady bill.   Col. Ronald A. Battelle Chief of Police St. Louis County Police Department Col. Clarence Harmon Chief of Police St. Louis Metropolitan Police Department The Post-Dispatch editors and editorial cartoonist Tom Engelhardt are again distorting the facts to try to deceive the people into thinking that the availability of inanimate guns are somehow the cause of the current wave of "deadly violence." The March 26 editorial cartoon should have had at the top of its "deadly violence" monument not a gun but the entertainment media, which glorify violence, the news media, which publicize and sensationalize violence, and the liberal government, which creates false expectations of something for nothing and then refuses to deal harshly with those who break the law.   The cause is not the guns, and the violence will continue until the media stops its constant exploitation of it and the liberals stop deceiving the people into believing in an easy life without personal responsibility.   Robert Brandon Creve Coeur Remember Matthew Jackson, 10; Jerome Williams, 19; Tony Hill, 15; Gregory Brown, 19; Tony L. Hall, 17; Rodney Carter, 15, and Iretha Fox, 31? They are seven area residents who were victims of fatal gunshots during an 18-day period in March.   Legalizing concealed guns is not the answer to safety in our state or our country. We pay men and women to be law-enforcement officials. Then, when they attempt to protect us, we ignore them.   As Gregory Freeman presented in his Feb. 12, column, "Handguns Kill Their Owners," on this issue the law-enforcement officials agree. Those who either missed this article or have forgotten its message should read it. Guns kill only if there is a "hand" at the trigger. Concealing these "hands" can only lead to more deaths. Let's stop now.   V.W. Murphy Creve Coeur

183.text: GUN-30413 PRIMARY Tom Ferrick Jr. | Think politicos can aim high? It was supposed to be a meeting of the minds. It ended up being an ambush. The half-dozen anti-handgun advocates who gathered at the Hatfield House in Fairmount Park last month thought they were there to meet two pols: State Sens. LeAnna Washington and Vince Fumo. Washington was chief sponsor of a bill these advocates wanted passed - a law to limit handgun sales to one a month in Pennsylvania. The goal: to cut the flow of handguns to the streets through straw buyers who purchase the weapons at gun shops and then peddle them at a huge markup to bad guys. Fumo is a die-hard supporter of the National Rifle Association. But he had advanced an idea that he said could get Washington's bill moving. That idea: amend it to make the one-handgun-a-month ban apply only to Philadelphia and "sunset" the law after five years - after which it would be repealed, unless renewed by the legislature. Bryan Miller of Ceasefire N.J. and one of those present at the meeting told me that the group didn't like Fumo's idea, but thought it wise to meet him in the hope of opening a dialogue with the influential senator. Who asked you? The meeting began, as scheduled. Then a funny thing happened. Other guests began to appear. Uninvited guests. The list included Jerry Mondesire, local head of the NAACP; State Rep. Dwight Evans; and Councilwoman Donna Reed Miller.

189.text: GUN-2263 PRIMARY GUNS AND GOVERNMENT Firearms dealers say business booming Scared buyers: Fear of crime and of more laws are considered the reasons sales are climbing. Gun lovers won't have to bite any bullets before Feb. 28, when the key provisions of the Brady Act go into effect. Until then, it's business as better-than-usual at gun shows around the state, such as one last weekend at the Cobb County Civic Center in Marietta. More than 6,200 gun enthusiasts showed up to buy, sell and trade rifles, handguns and paraphernalia - what they see as sporting goods or self-defense tools, what others see as implements of destruction. Promoter Matt Eastman, who puts on 29 such shows a year, said a normal show in past Januarys might have drawn 4,500. Most attributed the increased turnout to recently enacted gun legislation. "Record crowds all over the country are buying guns because they are scared," Eastman said - scared of becoming crime victims, scared of what they see as a trend toward enforced disarmament, scared of a world in which people who live within the law can't get a gun but people who pay no attention to laws don't have that problem. Said a dealer from Kennesaw named David, who declined to give his last name: "Right now, because of this Brady Bill, they're waiting at the door" - and it was 28 degrees outside as they waited. The five-day waiting period for buying a handgun, one of the central elements of the Brady Act, won't be a reality until Feb. 28, and even then it won't be a huge thorn in honest gun buyers' sides. But some see it as a step toward greater restrictions, said Eastman - "bans on semiautomatics, 10,000-percent taxes on ammunition, restrictions on the number of guns you can own . . . It's ludicrous what they're proposing."

191.text: GUN-3844 PRIMARY A Role for B.A.T.F. The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms is under attack by the gun lobby. In March, the National Rifle Association took out full-page advertisements in national newspapers saying the agency "deserves public contempt." N.R.A. supporters in Congress have gone even further. Representative John Dingell has called its agents "jackbooted American fascists" and Representative Harold Volkmer has called the B.A.T.F. "One of the most Rambo-rogue law enforcement agencies in the United States." The agency -- which has primary responsibility for enforcing Federal firearms regulations -- has had its share of problems, including inexcusable errors leading up to the tragedy at Waco, Tex. But neither these mistakes nor the rantings of the gun lobby should obscure two important truths. First, America needs more regulation of guns, not less, and better, not weaker enforcement of existing regulations. Second, responsible leaders in Congress, especially in the light of inflammatory comments by their irresponsible colleagues, must work to dispel the growing mythology that pictures the B.A.T.F. as the agent of some nefarious Government plot to subvert the Constitution and rob Americans of their traditional liberties. This effort, to be credible, needs to be accompanied by the recognition that the B.A.T.F. is badly in need of internal reform. Waco was merely the most spectacular in a series of lapses in which the B.A.T.F. became too aggressive and publicity-conscious. Since Waco, B.A.T.F. Director Stephen Higgins has been replaced by John Magaw, who has begun to address the problems that led to Waco and other disasters. He has also taken on the agency's woeful record of sex and racial discrimination.

192.text: GUN-11813 PRIMARY Obama Vows Fast Action Pressing for Gun Control WASHINGTON -- President Obama declared on Wednesday that he would make gun control a ''central issue'' as he opens his second term, promising to submit broad new firearm proposals to Congress no later than January and to employ the full power of his office to overcome deep-seated political resistance. Leading House Republicans responded to the president's pledge in the aftermath of the Connecticut school massacre by restating their firm opposition to new limits on guns or ammunition, setting up the possibility of a bitter legislative battle and a philosophical clash over the Second Amendment soon after Mr. Obama's inauguration. Having avoided a politically difficult debate over guns for four years, Mr. Obama vowed to restart a national conversation about their role in American society, the need for better access to mental health services and the impact of exceedingly violent images in the nation's culture. He warned that the conversation -- which has produced little serious change after previous mass shootings -- will be a short one, followed by specific legislative proposals that he intends to campaign for, starting with his State of the Union address next month. ''This time, the words need to lead to action,'' Mr. Obama said. '' I will use all the powers of this office to help advance efforts aimed at preventing more tragedies like this.'' At an appearance in the White House briefing room, the president said that he had directed Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr. to lead an interagency effort to develop what the White House said would be a multifaceted approach to preventing mass shootings like the one in Newtown, Conn., last week and the many other gun deaths that occur each year.

characters: Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr.

195.text: GUN-2668 PRIMARY Brady law rules hearing draws blanks Gun dealers deny being notified LAKEWOOD - Imagine throwing a party and having no one show up. That's sort of what happened to Colorado Department of Public Safety and Colorado Bureau of Investigation officials yesterday. No one showed up at a public hearing on their proposed formal rules for handling criminal background checks on prospective purchasers of handguns as required by the Brady gun-control law. That's because they weren't aware of the hearing, a number of firearms dealers said. Although state officials said an announcement was enclosed in the bills for background checks that were mailed out in mid-June to more than 600 firearms dealers across the state, an informal survey of a dozen Denver-area gun shops failed to turn up anyone who recalled getting a notice about the hearing. "There's absolutely nothing about any hearing in my bill, absolutely not," said David Jewell, owner of Scottie's Guns & Militaria in Denver. "I would have gone. I was one of the people who testified at the initial hearings. We like to be involved in any hearings." Others were equally adamant that they hadn't received the notice. "This is the first I've heard of it," said Tony Didamo, owner of the Foothills Shooting center in Lakewood. "I certainly would have gone. I have a few points I want to bring up." The federal Brady act - named for former White House press secretary James Brady wounded in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Reagan - requires background checks for handgun purchasers.

190.text: NA-43970 PRIMARY Support widens for gun control Prospects for a bipartisan deal to expand federal background checks for gun purchases are improving with the emergence of fresh Republican support, according to top Senate aides. The possibility that after weeks of stalled negotiations senators might be on the cusp of a breakthrough comes as President Obama and his top surrogates will begin on Monday their most aggressive push yet to rally Americans around his gun-control agenda. Even though polls show that a universal background-check system is supported by nine in 10 Americans, the president has been unable to translate popular support for the measures into legislative momentum on Capitol Hill. But in a move that could draw other Republicans as well as Democrats from conservative states who have not yet backed Obama's agenda, Sen. Joe Manchin III (W.Va.), a key Democratic broker, has spent the past few days crafting the framework of a possible deal with Sen. Patrick J. Toomey (R-Pa.). Manchin and Toomey are developing a measure to require background checks for all gun purchases except sales between close family members and some hunters, which addresses concerns of some conservatives, according to the aides, who spoke on the condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to talk publicly about the talks. Spokesmen for Manchin and Toomey said only that the senators are talking to many of their colleagues about gun legislation and could not confirm details of their discussions.

194.text: GUN-10973 PRIMARY Virginia Tech victim's message on guns: Enough already "Yeah, yeah, yeah," he would hear, one navy blue suit nodding to another. They would listen to what Colin Goddard had to say, shake his hand, then open the door for the next Washington lobbyist or constituent. See, Goddard doesn't really warrant a second glance on Capitol Hill. He's a tall, well-spoken, broad-shouldered 25-year-old with a good suit and purposeful handshake. Plus, the arguments he was peddling on sensible gun control had been heard before. But as Goddard was giving his earnest, wonky spiel about banning the kind of magazines that Jared Loughner allegedly used to spray gunfire in Tucson or requiring background checks on people who buy weapons at gun shows, those listening didn't know there were three bullets painfully worming their way through his body. If he wriggled in his seat too much, it hurt. And if you touched his skin in a certain spot, you could feel the outline of one of the 9mm hollow-points poking through. So after trying to play it straight for a while - just another young climber doing his time in the marbled halls of Congress - Goddard realized that he had to speak up about why he cared so deeply about this issue. "The whole dynamic changed once I told them," Goddard said. He told them what it felt like to be in French class at Virginia Tech on April 16, 2007, conjugating verbs one minute, then trapped in what looked like a bloody war movie the next.

193.text: NA-45843 PRIMARY New takes on the week's top talkers Overzealous gun control does little or nothing to prevent gun crimes, and  the hackneyed phrase "guns don't kill people, people kill people" is painfully true. The toxic mix of high profile, senseless killings and the mentally ill can be reduced only by the mentally ill being held and treated in mental institutions. The real crime behind the severe problem of gun violence and mental health is caused by reactionary government regulators, social meddlers and the ACLU. If it were not for them, mental health care would not have been eroded  in recent decades.   If it were not for the  self-inflicted social, political and economic sickness infecting our society, innocent lives would not have been  senselessly taken or destroyed. Surely, any finger-pointing to be done should be directed at zealots who ignore the fundamental rule "first, do no harm." Daniel B. Jeffs Apple Valley, Calif. I read with interest the opinion piece by Gabrielle Giffords and Mark Kelly urging common sense solutions to gun violence. Unfortunately, rather than outlining  solutions, they  resorted to  attacks. While I can certainly understand their  emotions, considering the attack Giffords  suffered, if they are seeking to influence public policy, they  should suggest specific policy changes. They indicate that NRA members do not necessarily agree with the policy stances of  NRA leadership. Speaking as an NRA member, I can say that  the NRA has a powerful voice. Four  million gun-owning members  believe  the NRA protects their constitutional right to keep and bear arms.   Giffords and Kelly also said that they are not coming to take my guns, and that I can still hunt.   The Second Amendment is not about the right to hunt. It recognizes the God-given, natural right of self-defense and defense from tyranny.

197.text: GUN-4478 PRIMARY Guns Sold in Maryland Counties Need Locks Maryland's two most populous counties have become the latest municipalities around the country to adopt laws requiring that the sale of a handgun include a safety lock for the trigger. With similar measures passed this week, Prince George's County and Montgomery County -- both of which border on the District of Columbia -- followed Connecticut, Massachusetts, Chicago and more than a dozen cities and counties in California that have enacted similar laws in recent years as a way to reduce the number of inadvertent shootings in the home by children. "This is a growing trend," Robert J. Walker, of Handgun Control, an advocacy group, said of the gun-lock measures. "If you look at where the gun control debate is headed, one of the prime areas is safety of handguns. This is the beginning of an attempt to focus on ways of reducing accidents, suicides and even homicides." Safety locks are relatively simple and inexpensive devices that attach to a handgun's trigger, blocking its use. They are removed by a key or a combination. More sophisticated devices are available. Mr. Walker said that lawmakers in New Jersey were reviewing a bill that would require a safety mechanism to be part of the gun, allowing only the user, wearing a magnetic ring or a comparable device, to pull the trigger. How effective the blocking devices are remains unclear. Mr. Walker said that because the first of the measures was passed so recently -- two years ago in Connecticut by the State Legislature -- there is no reliable evidence of its effectiveness.

198.text: GUN-3988 PRIMARY Woe at the NRA FOR YEARS now, Americans who own and use legal firearms for hunting, sports and other accepted activities have been parting ways with the one-note, any-gun-is-great captains of the National Rifle Association here in Washington. The leadership's reflexive defense of anything with a trigger has backfired, leaving the organization with a declining membership, serious financial problems, no presidential candidate to endorse and a loss of clout on Capitol Hill. We are able to contain our grief. A sizable group of dissident members, led by a former NRA board member, even has concluded that the NRA lobbyists' fervent fascination with cheap Saturday night specials, assault-style weapons and other tools of the criminal trade has been missing the mark. According to the Associated Press, the NRA has laid off 30 of its approximately 400 employees in recent weeks, suspended production of a weekly cable television program and shifted staff in its Fairfax headquarters to create rental space and bring in new revenue to help meet the payroll. From a peak of 3.5 million members last year, the NRA rolls have dropped by at least 400,000. The big setback came when police -- officers and members of every leading law enforcement organization in the country -- parted ways with the NRA lobbyists who were opposing a ban on assault weapons, trying to kill the Brady Bill waiting period (which has worked to weed out criminals attempting to buy handguns) and fighting one-handgun-a-month purchase limits that have curbed interstate gunrunning. Gradually, politicians who once feared the NRA or welcomed the organization's generous campaign contributions discovered that there is a political life after bucking the NRA in favor of public safety. They were hearing from more and more constituents -- firearms owners included -- demanding a stop to the all-too-free flow of firearms to criminals.

196.text: GUN-7357 PRIMARY Va. Moves To Limit Firearm Lawsuits; Local Governments Couldn't Sue Makers Virginia's General Assembly approved a measure today that would ban local governments from suing gun makers and distributors for the costs of firearm violence, making it the latest of more than a dozen state legislatures to adopt the prohibition. The bill, introduced in Virginia and elsewhere at the behest of the National Rifle Association, was approved in the Senate today without debate by a vote of 32 to 8. The measure passed the House last month and will now be sent to Gov. James S. Gilmore III (R), who is a champion of gun rights and has appeared in an NRA advertisement. A Gilmore spokesman said the governor had not yet indicated a position on the issue. Fourteen states have adopted similar measures, according to the NRA, and such a bill awaits a governor's signature in one other state besides Virginia. The measure has been introduced in about 20 other legislatures. It has not been introduced in Maryland, but the NRA says it is considering doing so. The push in state capitals by gun rights advocates comes in response to a growing number of lawsuits filed by cities against gun makers and distributors. Some 29 cities and counties including the District, Chicago and New Orleans have filed such suits. Some plaintiffs argue that the gun industry is partly responsible for urban violence and should reimburse localities for police work, medical costs and other expenses related to firearm use.

199.text: GUN-7150 PRIMARY SMITH & WESSON UNDER FIRE GUN CONTROL SMITH & WESSON, the only gun manufacturer to agree to basic safety measures, has become a pariah in the gun industry. The National Rifle Association says the company has "run up the white flag of surrender." A major gun wholesaler that has handled Smith & Wesson products for two decades says it will quit distributing the company's weapons. And many retailers say they'll stop selling Smith & Wesson guns. The industry backlash has been so severe since March 17, when Smith & Wesson agreed to new safety measures, that attorneys general in New York, Connecticut and Maryland are investigating the industry for antitrust violations. The swift and vicious retaliation against Smith & Wesson shows how threatened the industry is by efforts to impose reasonable safety standards for gun manufacturing and ownership. The deal was just common sense. The company agreed to equip handguns with trigger locks and to refuse to sell to dealers who don't run criminal background checks. In return, a coalition of local, state and federal government agencies agreed to drop civil liability lawsuits against the gun maker. The agreement is the first real break in the united front of gun makers and owners long accustomed to blocking tougher gun laws. Because it is such a threat, the remaining gun makers are eager to shoot down Smith & Wesson. For this reason, it's important that gun-safety advocates, including responsible gun owners and government agencies, support the company. That's where St. Louis comes in, with a proposal by Mayor Clarence Harmon that the city deal only with companies, like Smith & Wesson, that are making safety efforts.

201.text: GUN-1935 PRIMARY Gun-control backers cool to Miller plan Ban targeting minors falls short, critics say Gov. Zell Miller's plan to ban handgun possession by minors Monday drew tepid approval from gun-control proponents who want tougher measures. The National Rifle Association, which has blocked most gun restrictions in Georgia, seemed ready to accept it. In a six-point plan to combat gun violence in schools, Miller wants to outlaw possession of handguns by juveniles under 18. But the measure would exempt those teenagers who use guns for target practice, hunting or are under the direct supervision of a parent. The proposed legislation, modeled after a law passed in Colorado, also would make it a felony for adults to sell or furnish minors under 18 with handguns. It is a federal misdemeanor to sell guns to those under 21, but it is not against Georgia law for children to possess guns. "I don't think this concept is wimpy, but I don't think it's the whole enchilada," said Gwen Fitzgerald, spokeswoman for the Center to Prevent Handgun Violence. Seventeen states and the District of Columbia prohibit the possession of handguns by juveniles, she said. A final draft of the bill will be ready in a few weeks, said Miller, who contacted the NRA, among others, before he unveiled his plan. The NRA endorsed Miller in his 1990 campaign for governor. "I hope that the NRA would support it . . . or certainly not fight it," said Miller, who is attending the Southern Governors' Association conference in Richmond.

202.text: GUN-30461 PRIMARY Mayors aim to keep guns in safe hands Michael R. Bloomberg is the mayor of New York City The horrific murders in Lancaster County last week remind us of the tragic consequences that guns can cause when they fall into the wrong hands. Although the gun used in these killings was purchased legally, the fact is that most guns used in murders are purchased illegally. We can do something to stop those tragic crimes - and shame on us if we don't. Recently, at the invitation of Mayor Street, I joined a bipartisan delegation of Pennsylvania mayors, as well as Trenton Mayor Doug Palmer, on a visit to Harrisburg to push for tougher laws against illegal guns. Why does the mayor of New York City care about Pennsylvania's gun laws? Because about 90 percent of guns used in crimes in our city come from out of state, including many from Pennsylvania. Taking illegal guns off New York City's streets requires us not only to strengthen law enforcement efforts within our city, which we have done - gun arrests are up 14 percent this year - but also to prevent illegal guns from flowing into our city in the first place. Mayors across the country are facing the same dual challenge. That's why this year, with help from Mayors Street and Palmer, we created a national coalition of Mayors Against Illegal Guns. More than 100 mayors - from both political parties - have joined us, including Pennsylvania mayors from Allentown to Williamsport.

204.text: GUN-2916 PRIMARY Colo. Springs may ease weapons law Changes on concealed arms sought COLORADO SPRINGS - The city council has voted to consider changing the local concealed-weapons ordinance to assure law-abiding motorists that they won't be jailed for carrying a weapon needed for self-defense. City staffers and gun owners suggested a change in the law earlier this month, and the council voted unanimously Tuesday to seek an even less-restrictive ordinance. "The concept of making everybody happy with the law is going to be very difficult," said Police Chief Lorne Kramer. Under the existing law, a person can carry a weapon - including a firearm, knife, brass knuckles or blackjack - in a car openly, but not out of sight. Until last year, a judge would dismiss a charge of carrying a concealed weapon if the motorist could prove that the weapon was needed for self-protection.
