The council dropped that provision, saying too many people were using the law to justify weapon possession while driving. Gun owners argued that the old law effectively legalized their right to carry a concealed weapon in a vehicle. More than 100 people rallied at city hall last month to support a change. The compromise proposed earlier would have restored the self-defense provision if a motorist could show that he was headed to a specific place or going about everyday activities. Those caught loitering or driving about aimlessly wouldn't get the automatic dismissal. On Tuesday, Mayor Bob Isaac said he had received many complaints from gun owners and now agrees that the compromise didn't go far enough.

200.text: GUN-315 PRIMARY False Alarm Against Gun Control In the eyes of the National Rifle Association, David Moorehead, a gunsmith and disabled Vietnam veteran, is a hero for his service at home as well. Agents of the Treasury Department's Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms spent a lot of time and money in 1976 prosecuting Mr. Moorehead for owning a machine gun. But the weapon turned out to be an M-14 rifle, which he kept as a souvenir. And at his acquittal, a Federal District Judge apologized for the shoddy treatment he received at the hands of his Government. A new film produced by the rifle association depicts the bureau's treatment of Mr. Moorehead as routine. Representative John Dingell appears in the film to call the bureau's agents ''Gestapos.'' The movie aims to persuade Congress to abolish the agency. We assume Congressmen are discriminating enough to know propaganda when they see it. With virtually no control over guns in America, there is need for an agency that tries to make the best of a bad situation. In the two years since Glenn Dickerson became its director, the bureau has shifted attention from individual violations to the thriving illegal interstate traffic in guns. Its major targets are violent gangs, hate groups, narcotics dealers (who often trade guns for drugs) and organized crime. During a recent 18-month period, two-thirds of its arrests were of persons with criminal records.

characters: Treasury Department's Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

205.text: GUN-5131 PRIMARY IN BRIEF; Handgun Renewals Under the recently enacted count Handgun Record-Keeping Accountability Act, all pistol licenses must be registered by April 1, 2000, and all permits must be renewed every five years thereafter to reflect changes in an owner's status or address. In an effort to expedite the license-renewal process and avoid a last-minute rush, County Clerk Leonard N. Spano is asking Westchester residents to apply for their renewals now by sending him a self-addressed, stamped envelope, which will be returned with the renewal application and instructions. Mr. Spano said he would follow up on his initial request by mailing applications to gun owners who do not respond. With more than 63,000 pistol licenses on file and with many thousands probably no longer valid, the County Clerk said it was "highly unfeasible to mail out applications to everyone at the outset." Mr. Spano's office will issue plastic-coated cards with the handgun owner's picture and a fingerprint as well as a list of the owner's licensed guns and date of renewal to qualified gun owners. The self-addressed envelopes should be mailed to the Office of the County Clerk, Pistol Division, 110 Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard, White Plains, New York, 10601.    ELSA BRENNER

206.text: GUN-8709 PRIMARY GOV. VENTURA STANDS FIRM ON CONCEALED GUN BACKING THAT SUPPORT BEHIND BILL'S SUCCESS SO FAR, SAYS OPPONENT MOE Gov. Jesse Ventura on Wednesday restated his support for wider access to concealed weapons permits, aligning himself with those who believe they are close to winning a major change in state weapons law. Ventura, who has made few comments about the issue since it began moving through the Legislature, made it clear he hasn't wavered in his support now that the bill is close to final passage. "I support it," said Ventura, a gun owner who took out a concealed-weapons permit himself after he took office. "I think it's the case that over 30 other states have it. It does not turn you into the Wild West show that it's perceived." The Republican-controlled House passed the bill last month, and the Democratic-Farmer-Labor-controlled Senate is expected to take it up on the floor next week. The bill would end the current concealed weapons permit system, which gives wide discretion to police chiefs to determine who has a job-related or public safety need to carry a loaded handgun in public. Under the new bill, backed by the National Rifle Association but opposed by the state's police organizations, permits must be issued to anyone who passes a criminal background test, does not have a history of serious mental health problems and receives required weapons training. Applicants would not have to show a need to carry a loaded weapon in public.

207.text: GUN-6241 PRIMARY MAN CHARGED IN SHOOTING AT ST. PAUL GUN SHOW The man who discharged a handgun at a St. Paul gun show, wounding four people and prompting the St. Paul City Council to ban gun shows on city property, was charged Thursday with a felony. Terrance Stafford, 49, of Minneapolis was charged with reckless discharge of a firearm for taking a .22-caliber pistol off a display table, loading a loose round into it and firing it at the floor at the show Saturday morning at RiverCentre. The round splintered and pieces struck four people. One required hospital treatment. Stafford told police he believed the bullet was a dummy round and was surprised when the weapon fired. In filing the charge, Ramsey County Attorney Susan Gaertner said, "This was a very dangerous and irresponsible act. It is fortunate that no one was more seriously injured, but that does not diminish the seriousness of the offense." On Wednesday, the St. Paul City Council voted 4-3 on a resolution banning gun shows on city property.

208.text: GUN-2271 PRIMARY LETTERS Beginning of the end So Tom Teepen accepts Treasury Secretary Lloyd Bentsen's latest attack on the U.S. Constitution in the guise of crimefighting. It bothers Teepen that there are more licensed gun dealers in America than there are gas stations, but he does not even attempt to explain why he thinks that's a problem. Would it be a problem if there were more daily newspapers than movie theaters? The mere number of license holders is of no importance. The underlying problem with the anti-gun hysteria is that it claims to be concerned with crime but in fact is based on a disregard for law. When the Justice Department, the Treasury Department and the president advocate ripping up the Constitution of the United States, guns are the least of our problems. Rights from trial by jury to freedom of the press could be next. This should scare the bejesus out of every citizen. ANTHONY TRAURING Decatur Wisdom of forefathers Isn't it about time all of us Georgians paused and took an unemotional, analytical look at this flag issue that's beginning to cause a dangerous division among citizens? Our state flag was designed and adopted in 1879 by Confederate soldiers who had just fought a divisive war. However, instead of selecting a divisive symbol of that war, they chose to adopt a state flag representing a new era. In 1956, some members of the state Legislature chose to express their anger over Brown v. Board of Education by adding a Confederate symbol to the state flag. If the Confederate soldiers who designed the original state flag had wanted a Confederate symbol on the flag, they would have put one on it.

203.text: NA-40458 PRIMARY REACTION TO (GUN CONTROL) PROPOSALS "I have no illusions about what we're up against or how hard the task is in front of us. ... We should do as much as we can, as quickly as we can. And we cannot let the perfect be the enemy of the good." - Vice President Joe Biden "I am committed to ensuring that the Senate will consider legislation that addresses gun violence and other aspects of violence in our society early this year." - Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid, D-Nev. "Attacking firearms and ignoring children is not a solution to the crisis we face as a nation. Only honest, law-abiding gun owners will be affected and our children will remain vulnerable to the inevitability of more tragedy." - National Rifle Association "There's an extraordinary disconnect between what the American public wants - including gun owners and NRA members - and what our elected officials are doing about it." - Dan Gross, president of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence "Nothing the president is proposing would have stopped the massacre at Sandy Hook. ... Rather than sweeping measures that make it harder for responsible, law-abiding citizens to purchase firearms, we should focus on the root causes of gun violence and keep guns out of the hands of criminals and the mentally ill." - Sen. Marco Rubio, R-Fla. "There's a slow-motion Sandy Hook going on in every community in America. ... The center of the gun debate should not be Washington. It should be the streets of America, where ... there's a much deeper consensus than there is in the halls of Congress."

characters: President of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

209.text: GUN-7713 PRIMARY "THESE KILLINGS HAVE TO STOP" : PARTICIPANTS VOW TO BE MORE POLITICALLY ACTIVE IN WASHINGTON: Inaction by Congress on gun proposals is decried Tens of thousands of mothers, grandmothers, daughters and other women and men filled the National Mall on Sunday to decry gun violence and rally for stricter gun control laws. With tragic stories and angry chants, with posters and T-shirts, participants in the "Million Mom March" sent an unequivocal message to Congress that they want sweeping new gun laws, including licensing, registration and limits on firearms purchases. "These killings have to stop," said Gail Thorson, whose son was killed by a gunman at the Empire State Building three years ago. "We've had enough of politics as usual. We will insist that our children's lives be put before the special interests." Among the crowd were more than 50 women, men and children from the St.  Louis area. "I have two small children, and I want to make the world safer for them," said Kirstin Ware, 31, from Chesterfield. She was one of two organizers for the state's delegation to the rally. "It's time that mothers take charge and show their power as women and as mothers," she said. President Bill Clinton helped kick off the march, telling a group of women at the White House not to be "deterred by the political mountain you have to climb." Ware and another organizer from Missouri, Jeanine Emch, attended the event at the White House.

212.text: NA-46151 PRIMARY Gun group files suit against Carrollton The gun rights group GeorgiaCarry.org and one of its members have filed a lawsuit claiming that a pair of Carrollton local ordinances that prohibit firearms at parades and on the city's planned 16-mile Greenbelt Trail violate state law and the state constitution. The suit brought Thursday asks the Superior Court in Carroll County to declare the two ordinances void. John Miller, a Carrollton resident who is one of the plaintiffs, said he wants to be able to carry a firearm for his protection without fear he will be arrested for violating an ordinance that carries a punishment of a $1,000 fine and six months in jail. According to the suit, GeorgiaCarry.org tried unsuccessfully to discuss the matter with city officials in January. In February, according to the suit, Mayor Wayne Garner told reporters he would not put discussions of the gun ordinances on any City Council meeting agendas and would instead " 'let a judge tell me that we need to do that.' " The suit said Georgia law prohibits local governments from regulating where or when guns can be carried. The suit also said the state constitution says only the Legislature can "prescribe the manner in which arms may be borne" as long as state laws don't infringe on "the right of the people to keep and bear arms." Carrollton city officials did not respond to a telephone message seeking comment.

210.text: GUN-1910 PRIMARY Clearwater mayor joins in call to ban gun sales Clearwater Mayor Rita Garvey is joining her counterpart in St. Petersburg in calling for a local ban on gun sales. "If it's possible, I would definitely  say we should go for that," Garvey said after reading news reports of St. Petersburg Mayor David Fischer's proposal to ban gun sales in his city.   "There should be some kind of local control." Clearwater City Attorney Al  Galbraith, in a response late Friday to Garvey's request for information on a  gun ban, said state law forbids local governments from regulating gun sales.   That power is reserved for the state. But Garvey said she wants Galbraith to  draft a proposal for state legislators to grant an exemption for Clearwater.   Fischer had suggested a similar plan. Fischer, who made his proposal Wednesday  to ban gun sales in St. Petersburg, said he welcomed Garvey's support. He said  it was the first official support he has received from an elected leader of  another city. He has received about 70 calls to his office, most opposing his  idea. UPDATE FORMER PINELLAS JAIL ADMINISTRATOR HIRED IN DADE. Former Pinellas County Jail administrator Charles Felton has been appointed head of the Dade County Corrections Department. Felton, 49, once nominated to be a U.S. marshal by former President Bush, will begin Monday, according to the Miami Herald.   Felton retired from the Pinellas County Jail position in January 1992. He  became a marketing consultant for Pride of Florida Inc., a company that uses  prison labor to make goods. At one time, Felton was the superintendent at the  Cook County Corrections Department in Chicago. Dade's Corrections Department  has had ethnic troubles in the past and those troubles will be among the first  Felton must tackle. Felton beat out two assistant jail directors in Dade  County for the job. The former jail director, Lonnie Lawrence, left the  department after an uproar over declining morale in the department and the  death of a mentally ill inmate, according to the Herald. Dade County Manager  Joaquin Avino selected Felton to head the department earlier this week.

211.text: GUN-9908 PRIMARY Senate Votes to Require Safety Locks on Guns The Senate voted Thursday to require handgun manufacturers to sell weapons with child-proof locks. The measure was added to a bill the Senate is considering that would grant immunity to gun makers and dealers against civil lawsuits. Senator Barbara Boxer, the California Democrat who sponsored the amendment, described the 70-to-27 vote as ''a very strong signal'' that lawmakers would be willing to further amend the bill, despite pressure from President Bush not to do so. Gun makers have argued that legislative action is unneeded because they are already voluntarily moving to produce weapons with the locks. The Senate passed a similar safety lock measure in 1999. But the gun bill to which it was attached did not make it out of the House because of disputes about other provisions, including how to regulate gun shows. On Thursday other parts of the Senate's debate over legal immunity for gun dealers and manufacturers turned into a political free-for-all. Democrats tried to use the measure as a vehicle to extend unemployment benefits, and the majority leader, Senator Bill Frist of Tennessee, tried to use it to renew parts of a landmark civil rights measure. Both efforts were unsuccessful. The unemployment benefits extension, sponsored by Senator Maria Cantwell, Democrat of Washington, failed on a procedural motion when it fell two votes short of the 60 needed for the Senate to take it up. The civil rights amendment was withdrawn when Mr. Frist realized he did not have the votes to tack it onto the bill.

216.text: GUN-10090 PRIMARY The shoot first state? Ads warn about new gun law It's definitely not your typical tourist pitch. "Thinking about a Florida vacation?" the advertisement asks. "A new law in the Sunshine State authorizes nervous or frightened residents to use deadly force." Ads, fliers, billboards and a Web site are part of a campaign by a national gun control group to warn visitors about Florida's "Stand Your Ground" law that takes effect Saturday. The law gives broad legal protections to a person who is attacked not only at home, but "any other place where he or she has a right to be," and removes a common-law duty to retreat in the face of attack. A person being attacked will have "the right to stand his or her ground and meet force with force," not only at home but in public places. The law also gives the shooter immunity from criminal and civil charges unless the victim is a police officer. The Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence says its goal is to alert travelers, not scare off tourists who are vital to the state's economy. "We think it's reasonable to tell people that they should avoid passionate confrontations when they come to Florida," said Peter Hamm, the Brady campaign's communications director. "This was a dangerous solution to a problem that doesn't exist. The jails are not full of people who acted in self-defense. This crosses the line of reasonableness."

213.text: NA-42286 PRIMARY March 13 letters to the editor Second Amendment still remains relevant In his Feb. 21 letter, Alfred H. Buchta criticizes a letter from reader Alexander Montalvo and suggests using "present-day common-sense" interpretation of the Constitution. He implied other amendments have their limitations, why shouldn't the Second? The Second Amendment has already been limited, multiple times. The point is, we shouldn't continue to constrict this right based on the senseless acts of criminals and the mentally unstable. His straw-man argument about owning nuclear arms is a ridiculous exaggeration. While more than 230 years old, the principles contained within, such as the ability to protect ourselves from tyranny, will never become outdated. They're inalienable rights, not privileges. They're not given by man so they can't be taken by man. Are those in power so evolved now that they're not susceptible to becoming corrupt? Keep in mind that some proponents of gun control in the Senate hold unrestricted weapons permits. Restrict the law-abiding but exempt themselves? Are they more important than we? Remember, they are people, too, and "public servants," not aristocrats. Christopher DeFonte San Ramon Hacienda Restaurant gone but not forgotten How sad to witness the demolition of the Hacienda Restaurant at Macdonald and San Pablo avenues in Richmond. Fortunately, I happened to be there to take pictures before the final demolition earlier this month. Tony Carrico, the owner, founded and operated the restaurant successfully for more than 56 years. He passed away in June.

218.text: NA-41194 PRIMARY Sicko sends mayor ricin THREATENING letters laced with poisonous ricin were sent to Mayor Bloomberg in New York and to his anti-gun group in Washington, police said Wednesday. But Bloomberg vowed he wouldn't let the incident stop his group from fighting gun violence. "There's 12,000 people that are going to get killed this year with guns . .. and we're not going to walk away from those efforts," he said at a museum gala Wednesday night. The letters were postmarked from Shreveport, La., a police source said. The letter to Bloomberg did not reach its intended target, but the one sent to the head of Mayors Against Illegal Guns was "opened more casually," NYPD spokesman Paul Browne said. "In both letters there was this suspicious substance, a pink-orange oily substance" that was initially thought to be some kind of organic food product, he said. Nobody was hurt, but three members of the NYPD Emergency Service Unit who handled the letter Friday came down the next day with mild diarrhea - a symptom of ricin exposure. In Washington, the group's director and other people who touched the letter "remain asymptomatic," Browne said. Both the NYPD Intelligence Division and the FBI Joint Terrorism Task Force are investigating. Browne said he believes this is the first time a New York City elected official has been targeted with a ricin-laced letter. Whoever sent the poison made threats to Bloomberg "with references to the debate on gun laws," Browne said.

214.text: GUN-1572 PRIMARY UNDER THE GUN No one involved in the heated debate over allowing Missourians to carry concealed weapons is concealing the emotions involved. Law enforcement officials are strongly opposed to the bill; the National Rifle Association and other gun groups are just as strongly in favor. The House is scheduled to vote on the proposal today. It should resist the vocal support for this ill-conceived bill and prevent more weapons from being put on already-dangerous streets.   Since Rep. Joseph L. Driskill of Doniphan attached the concealed-weapon proposal to a crime bill last week, both sides have been campaigning hard. The NRA worked the telephones vigorously to win first-round approval for the plan, claiming that criminals already are heavily armed and law-abiding Missourians have the right to fight back on even terms. That prospect naturally alarms police and sheriff's departments that have their hands full dealing with the guns now in circulation. James Nelson, agent in charge of the St. Louis office of the FBI, called the plan ''a prescription for murder and family tragedy.'' A rising murder rate is enough to scare anyone who lives or works in a high-crime area into feeling the need for self-defense. But even with the safeguards that Rep. Driskill put into the bill - permits given only to those over 21 who have no felony conviction and have passed a firearms safety course - the spread of concealed weapons would be bad news. Parents who arm themselves against a perceived threat are sending their children a message that is already reinforced too often in real life and in fiction - that guns are a handy equalizer and violence can best be combatted with more violence. Instead of encouraging vigilante action that would only escalate gunfire in dangerous neighborhoods, the House should pass a far more responsible piece of legislation. Rep. S. Sue Shear wants to make sure that any gun in a home where children live is either fitted with a trigger lock or stored safely away from little hands and curious minds. She also wants to set up gun-free zones around public schools.   The NRA, which professes to be so concerned about working for a safe society, is doing its best to block the Shear bill from even reaching the House floor. If it is truly sincere about reducing the threat of fatal shootings, it will stop working to put more guns into inexperienced hands and instead try to keep them away from children.

219.text: GUN-7221 PRIMARY MOST PREFER TOUGHER GUN LAWS, POLL SEZ WASHINGTON - A new poll in the increasingly bitter debate over gun control found that a clear majority of Americans wants more controls. The poll, by Zogby Associates, also found that few Americans, 27%, believe accusations by the National Rifle Association that President Clinton tolerates some violence to win support for gun controls. The NRA has supercharged the debate over new gun controls - stalled in Congress since last summer - by claiming the President has not enforced existing gun laws and that Clinton has "blood on his hands." The poll indicates that the NRA is losing support and that 64% of Americans favor tougher controls on guns. Clinton pressed the debate yesterday by firing off an angry veto threat to Sen. Orrin Hatch (R-Utah), who had threatened to strip gun control measures from a juvenile justice bill that is now being considered. "Let me be clear: I will not sign juvenile crime legislation that fails to move forward in our efforts to make guns safer, and to keep them out of the hands of children and criminals," Clinton wrote to the chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee. Clinton wants the bill to include mandatory child safety locks on guns and a 72-hour waiting period for background checks at gun shows. Democrats intend to bolster their gun control credentials today when Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and Rep. Carolyn McCarthy (D-Nassau) unveil a bill to finance more investigators and prosecutors to crack down on rogue gun dealers.

215.text: GUN-3805 PRIMARY Q&A on the News; Do you have a question about the news - local, national or internation?; Colin Bessonette will try to get an answer for you. Call 222-2002 on a; Touch-Tone phone and follow the instructions. On Access Atlanta, the; AJC's online service, jump: Q&A to ask a question or read hundreds of recent Q&A answers. Q: Former President George Bush has dropped his membership in the National Rifle Association. I'm curious: Are Bob Dole and Newt Gingrich members of the NRA? -Marceline Haver, Atlanta A: Dole is a member; Gingrich is not. The official responses from their respective offices: Dole: "Senator Dole has already stated he has concerns over the tone of some of the rhetoric in the NRA letter," said Nelson Warfield, press secretary of the Dole for President Campaign, referring to a recent NRA letter calling federal agents "jack-booted government thugs." Warfield continued: "President Bush certainly has a right to his decision. While Senator Dole respects President Bush very much, he has no plans to drop his membership in the NRA." Gingrich: "Speaker Gingrich was a member of the NRA at one time, but he didn't keep up his membership, so he's no longer a member," said spokesman John Cox. "But he firmly believes in the importance of the Second Amendment." The Second Amendment to the Constitution: "A well- regulated militia being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed." Q: How do we submit ideas to the governor's commission on privatizing state government? Are they welcomed? -Mark Webber, Marietta A: Yes, input from the public is welcome, said Rick Dent, spokesman for Gov. Zell Miller. Mail your ideas to: Joe Tanner, Executive Director, Governor's Commission on Privatization of Government Services, Governor's Office, State Capitol, Atlanta, Ga. 30334.

217.text: GUN-11820 PRIMARY Inquirer Editorial: State gun laws are too weak Judging from the refusal of top Pennsylvania Republicans to entertain tougher gun-violence measures, news must travel slowly to Harrisburg. State legislators in the capital seem a million miles from grief-stricken Newtown, Conn., scene of the mass murder of schoolchildren on Friday. "Gun control is not going to stop madmen," says Gov. Corbett through a spokesman. State Rep. Daryl Metcalfe (R., Butler), who heads the House state government committee, vows that he won't "allow the left to use this horrific act to advance their gun-grabbing agenda." Fortunately, elected leaders in Washington, and other states, seem to be better in touch with the national mood. They are showing welcome signs that it might be possible to take action to reduce opportunities for the deadly mass shootings that have bloodstained the very innocence of childhood. President Obama has asked Vice President Biden to coordinate efforts to curb gun violence in the wake of the school shootings that claimed the lives of 20 first graders and six adults. Philadelphia Police Commissioner Charles H. Ramsey is on Biden's panel. Among the moves under consideration are reinstating a ban on assault weapons, reinstituting a needed ban on large-capacity ammunition clips, and taking more steps to keep weapons from the mentally ill. Clearly, the most sweeping change must come from Washington. But Corbett and his legislative colleagues will need to get in step to safeguard Pennsylvanians.

characters: Philadelphia Police Commissioner Charles H. Ramsey

224.text: NA-42469 PRIMARY Ruben Rosario: Gun debate moves to state Capitol next week There's going to be a gun show next week at the state Capitol. No, not that kind of gun show. As it also is taking place in virtually every state legislature in the nation, the Minnesota House Public Safety Finance and Policy Committee will hold three days of public hearings next week on a slew of firearms-related bills. Now, these hearings won't have nearly the same drama, emotion or controversy as those held this week in Washington, D.C., and Connecticut, site of the Sandy Hook Elementary School massacre Dec. 14. At the Connecticut hearing, for example, debate swirled following the testimony of a parent of one of the 20 children killed in the school massacre. An edited MSNBC TV excerpt appeared to suggest the man was interrupted by shouts about the Second Amendment from gun-rights advocates in the audience. The full version showed that some audience members responded after the parent asked aloud whether someone at the hearing could explain why civilians need to own assault-type weapons and high-capacity magazines. Some thought the response to the parent's rhetorical question was still distasteful, and the committee chair threatened to clear the room if there were more comments from the audience. We'll see what Minnesota Nice brings to the local proceedings. For some reason, I don't think the pros and cons over whether scopes should be allowed on muzzleloaders, as one bill proposes, will generate as much spirited discussion as say, bans on assault-type weapons or universal background checks.

221.text: GUN-30252 PRIMARY MIKE GUNNIN' FOR NRA. His fortune could provide real ammo in tough battle. HE'LL ROUND UP MAYORS & MAY USE OWN CASH IN PUSH FOR U.S. GUN LAWS MAYOR BLOOMBERG IS getting ready to spend his political capital - and quite possibly his own cash - to try to stop the flow of illegal handguns into New York from other states. Bloomberg has been mostly mum on how he plans to wage his long-shot crusade, other than to say he hopes to build a coalition of big city mayors at the outset. Boston, Chicago and Los Angeles - all of which have seen increases in gun violence in recent years - are considered likely allies. But the biggest wild card, experts say, is whether and to what degree Bloomberg chooses to use his fortune, estimated at $6 billion, to battle the National Rifle Association. "I think money could make a huge difference," said Zach Ragbourn, a spokesman for the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence. "If you get into a shouting match with the gun industry, you better have a big bullhorn - and that's the kind of thing money can buy." The Brady Campaign became the No. 1 lobbyist for handgun control in the United States by spending $238,000 to sway Congress since 2002. By comparison, the NRA has spent about $6.5 million since 2002 on lobbying Congress, federal filings show. Both sums pale in comparison to the $80 million Bloomberg spent this year on his own reelection, or even the $7.5 million he spent to promote a single ballot question in the city in 2003.

220.text: GUN-12151 PRIMARY BEEP IF YOU AGREE THE DAILY NEWS' campaign for gun control and an assault weapons ban has spread to all corners of the city - and to all five borough presidents. The leaders of Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, Staten Island and the Bronx all signed the News' petition Thursday in the wake of the Newtown, Conn., massacre. "When first-grade classrooms become shooting ranges, it is time for the NRA to stand down and for all of us to stand up, and that's why this petition is so important," said Manhattan Borough President Scott Stringer. "It gives us the opportunity for average citizens to express ourselves on one of the most important issues of our time." Bronx Borough President Ruben Diaz said his borough faces a crisis when it comes to gun violence. "This is a common sense first step toward sensible gun laws, and I am proud to support it," he said. Brooklyn Borough President Marty Markowitz said "an astounding 500 people" were shot in Brooklyn in 2012, "nearly 100 of them fatally." "It is time to end America's love affair with guns and instead aim for a safer, more peaceful future," he said. Since The News launched the petition drive calling for comprehensive gun control and an immediate ban on all assault weapons, 80,000 people have signed it. Queens Borough President Helen Marshall and Staten Island Borough President James Molinaro also signed on.

223.text: GUN-9728 PRIMARY Reject gun foes' efforts 'to violate federal law' The National Rifle Association released a statement in late 2001 supporting Attorney General John Ashcroft's decision to destroy information from criminal background checks on gun purchasers within 24 hours rather than in 90 days. Here are excerpts: Once again, shameless gun-ban extremists are using the specter of terrorism to promote their anti-gun agenda. The gun-ban lobby and numerous anti-gun editorialists have all launched a coordinated attack on Attorney General John Ashcroft. This time, they object to him obeying the law. Specifically, Ashcroft's Department of Justice has stated that the records generated by the National Instant Check System that do not result in an individual being denied the ability to purchase a firearm cannot be used for anything other than what the Brady Act--- which established NICS --- allows. The gun-ban community, however, insists that Ashcroft violate federal law by allowing non-denials through NICS to be used in a fishing expedition for possible connections to the attacks of Sept. 11. In reality, gun-ban groups are using the issue to promote their agenda to expand NICS toward a national registration scheme of all law-abiding gun buyers. While the gun-ban advocates demand federal law be violated --- shamelessly invoking "homeland security" to advance their agenda --- Ashcroft has stood his ground and correctly stated the Brady Act forbids using records on transfers that are not denied through NICS for anything other than auditing the system. The act, as passed in 1993, even includes language that calls for the destruction of all records of transfers that are not denied, although the law does allow for the retention and use of records on transfers that are denied.

222.text: GUN-6983 PRIMARY A plan to swap guns for gifts; A merchant's innovative idea seeks to benefit charities while reducing the number of weapons While politicians debate the merits of gun control, a Conyers Olde Town merchant has crafted his own plan to help keep guns off the street. Garvin Haynes, an owner of the Richmond House and Renaissance Pointe shops, is launching what could be the nation's first privately financed program in which a firearm is exchanged for a gift certificate or a donation to charity. "There are lots of people who aren't gun people who acquire guns as gifts, or maybe they inherit them," Haynes said. "They don't know what to do with them. They don't want to sell them to a pawnshop because they'll end up on the street. This would give them an alternative." Haynes is willing to put $ 2,000 in a pool to start the program. He hopes to attract funding from various civic groups. He plans to have the program running by August. Haynes plans to offer a $ 50 gift certificate to his stores for each gun turned in. The gun owner could request instead that a $ 25 donation be made to the owner's favorite charity. Or, absent any particular choice of charity, the donation would go to the restoration of the historic Depot in Conyers. According to Haynes' plan, the guns would be given to the Conyers Police Department for disposal. David Bernstein, spokesman for Handgun Control in Washington, said Haynes' approach is a novel one.

227.text: GUN-5753 PRIMARY GUNS AND SCHOOLS: THE SCENE -- CAPITOL SKETCHBOOK; A Congresswoman Is Defiant in Defeat, and the G.O.P. Is Anxious "Let me go home," came the weary plea to the House from Representative Carolyn McCarthy as she watched her gun-control measure go down to defeat in the middle of the night, beyond the waking notice of most Americans. "Let me go home," she repeated, teary-eyed in the early morning, transfixing the House in her moment of exasperation at the bare-knuckled political machinations that befell her cause. Far from quitting, the second-term Congresswoman was vowing to work at her new career until some progress was enacted on her signature issue of gun control. Mrs. McCarthy, the Nassau County Democrat who became a citizen-politician after she was widowed by a gunman in a random shooting on the Long Island Rail Road in 1993, was free to go home at 2:40 P.M. today. That was when the effort by House Republican leaders to pass a minimalist gun measure in place of her own amendment collapsed in a furious round of Republican chagrin and cross-party recriminations. Democrats and Republican moderates, crying sham, rose up to defeat the Republican leadership's measure almost 2 to 1. The thwack of the gavel left the House drifting off in an inconclusive hubbub as members wondered how the nation would deal with their inaction in the face of the schoolhouse shootings. "I'm re-energized by this," Mrs. McCarthy said as she left the Capitol, far from tears. She seemed every bit the resilient professional politician as she vowed to go to the voters with a message that the National Rifle Association had won, gutting the issue of gun control to the point that not even a nominal measure could pass the House as political cover for the Republicans.

225.text: NA-40010 PRIMARY LAW CLOSES LOOPHOLE IN GUN SALES TO MENTALLY ILL Florida Gov. Rick Scott on Friday signed a bill to close a loophole in firearm sales to some mentally ill people, a controversial measure that has divided gun rights activists. Scott assured conservative supporters in his signing letter that he is a "strong supporter of the Second Amendment" and has "signed legislation protecting the privacy of firearm owners and stopping local governments from overreaching in the regulation of firearms." But Scott said "reasonable parameters on firearm purchases must be set forth in state law to ensure public safety." "Mental health and second amendment advocates worked together to produce this bill that does not affect persons voluntarily seeking mental health exams or treatments but rather closes a loophole in current law that could potentially put firearms in the hands of dangerous, mentally ill individuals who are a threat to themselves or others as determined by a court," he wrote. Florida law has allowed people who voluntarily commit themselves to a mental institution to buy a gun once they are released. The new law requires that before agreeing to voluntary treatment under the state's Baker Act, individuals receive written notice that if treated, they may be prohibited from buying a gun or "applying for or retaining a concealed weapons or firearms license" while they're deemed a danger. Their names are then added to a national database of people prohibited from buying a gun. People involuntarily committed are already added to that list.

228.text: GUN-2686 PRIMARY CONFERENCES TAKE DIFFERING SIGHTS ON FIREARMS THE ABA GATHERED TO DISCUSS GUN VIOLENCE. THE NRA'S FOCUS WAS CRIMINAL VIOLENCE. The National Rifle Association was not to be outgunned. When the American Bar Association scheduled this week's First National Conference on Gun Violence, the NRA drew up a dueling First National Conference on Criminal Violence to start a day earlier. The timing was no coincidence, said NRA spokesman George Walker. The ABA is "focusing on guns," he said, "and we are focusing on criminals." Richard Kuh, chairman of the ABA's criminal justice section, said his organization wanted to take guns from criminals and end the "ready access to guns that criminalizes so many young people." The conferences were pumped with hyperbole and sometimes contradictory data. Guns were called "flesh-eating killers" by an ABA speaker; another person showed slides of shot-up bodies and body parts. One ABA speaker suggested that gun violence should be considered a public- health crisis, the polio epidemic of our time. In contrast, the NRA held a session titled: "Criminals are not micro-organisms." NRA speakers called those who commit crimes with guns "predators" and "monsters." Attended mostly by law enforcers, victims' rights supporters and local crime fighters from around the country, the NRA conference focused on the erosion of moral values and effective punishment of the convicted. It has been a tough year for the NRA, what with the passage of the Brady gun-control law and Congress nearing a ban on assault weapons. The biggest name on its agenda was former Attorney General William Barr.

229.text: GUN-1924 PRIMARY Ban on gun sales proposed by mayor of St. Petersburg Mayor David Fischer said Wednesday he will seek to ban the sale of guns - all guns - within city limits. "Society has run amok," the mayor said in a speech and subsequent  interview. "We've got to take a stand." The mayor's plan will be difficult to implement. State law now regulates  the sale of guns and ammunition in Florida, "to the exclusion of all existing  and future county, city, town or municipal ordinances or regulations . . ." Fischer said he will seek to have the city exempted from existing state  laws so the city can draw up its own regulations. As the mayor envisions it,  the local law would be a model of simplicity. It would ban the sale of all  guns without exception. "All guns. I'm tired of trying to determine 1-inch, 30-inch, how many  rounds a minute. It doesn't make a difference. Everything. Just guns." Fischer said a number of recent events convinced him to take action. "I was down in Dade County a few weeks ago, and I learned they were  finding more guns in middle school than they were finding in high school. "I saw a Time magazine article recently reporting that kids were buying  guns out of a car trunk in Omaha. "Two weeks ago I went to the Mahaffey Theater and saw a sign advertising a  gun show at the arena. I told the manager no more - not in this city.

226.text: GUN-6057 PRIMARY CLINTON PRESSES HOUSE DEMOCRATS TO PASS MORE GUN RESTRICTIONS * The president tells group that they "shouldn't settle" for the Senate's measure and should raise the legal age for owning handguns. President Bill Clinton urged House Democrats on Friday to press for more gun restrictions than those passed by the Senate, including raising the age for owning handguns and assault-style weapons, congressional and administration aides said. "We shouldn't settle" for the Senate-passed restrictions, Clinton told the lawmakers in a private meeting, according to these officials, who demanded anonymity. In addition, Clinton was said to have told the lawmakers, Democrats should insist on measures that would raise from 18 to 21 the legal age for possessing and purchasing handguns, semiautomatic assault-style weapons and high-capacity ammunition magazines. But by late Friday, Democrats had not yet agreed on what proposals they should seek. Some House Democrats, according to congressional sources, wanted to pressure Republicans into accepting a limit of one handgun purchase per month, a proposal Senate Democrats abandoned because of the unlikely chance of getting enough votes. Democrats insisted, however, on House action by Memorial Day on the juvenile justice bill and its new gun restrictions approved by the Senate Thursday. Majority Republicans, anxious to let the issue cool, refused to budge Friday from their plans to hold committee hearings on the matter next week with no floor debate until mid-June. "We're going to make sure that we do this in a reasonable, rational and responsible way," said John Feehery, spokesman for House Speaker Dennis Hastert, R-Ill.

230.text: NA-42749 PRIMARY GE Capital Cuts Gun Shop Purchase Loans GE Capital, General Electric's lending arm, recently stopped offering consumer financing programs to retailers whose main business is selling guns, in response to the ''industry changes, new legislation and tragic events.'' The move, said a GE Capital spokesman, expanded on a policy established in 2008. That year, GE Capital stopped offering new consumer financing for purchases of guns from gun shops, but existing relationships with gun shops were grandfathered in and those programs continued. Fewer than 75 retailers are affected by the recent policy change, a fraction of 1 percent of gun retailers in the United States, said the spokesman, Russell Wilkerson. The terminated financing programs, as a business, were ''immaterial'' to the company, he said. The lending cutoff applies only to retailers whose primary business is selling guns, Mr. Wilkerson said. GE Capital is still offering financing to other retailers who sell many different kinds of merchandise, including guns. Those retailers range from Walmart to sporting goods chains. The change in GE Capital's lending policy to the gun stores was first reported on Wednesday by The Wall Street Journal. The move by GE Capital comes in the aftermath of the school shooting in Newtown, Conn. General Electric, the giant industrial and finance company, is headquartered in Fairfield, Conn., and some employees have children in the Sandy Hook elementary school, where the tragedy occurred. Adam Lanza, the Sandy Hook gunman, was the son of Peter Lanza, an executive at General Electric.

233.text: GUN-7424 PRIMARY Reader responses: GUN CONTROL: Confiscation is ultimate goal Thank you for your editorial encouraging me to let our state officials know how I feel about Senate Bill 308. The bill is just as misleading, just as disingenuous, as your editorial ("Selling out to the NRA," Feb. 22). The comparison of gun deaths in the United States to other industrialized nations is silly. The truth is that accidental gun deaths among children have declined by 50 percent over 25 years. Children's accidental deaths from drowning are six times the number of deaths from guns, while accidental deaths from fires is five times the number from guns. Many more children are killed in bicycle accidents than by gun accidents. The "save the children" mantra is in reality a disguise for the ultimate goal, which is to take all guns from law-abiding citizens. MICHAEL MORTON Morton, a sales executive, lives in Acworth.

231.text: GUN-865 PRIMARY ' CLEVELAND SHUFFLE' SNEAKS WEAPON BAN PAST NRA The Cleveland City Council caught the National Rifle Association off guard as it "snuck through" a ban on semiautomatic assault weapons Friday. Meanwhile, a gun manufacturer dropped a lawsuit against a similar measure in Los Angeles, leaving the NRA to fight it on its own. "We have no obligation to inform the NRA of what we are going to do," said Cleveland City Council President George Forbes after Friday's unanimous vote to ban the sale or possession of certain semiautomatic rifles, carbines, shotguns and handguns. "What you see is what I call the `Cleveland Shuffle,' " Forbes said. "While they were watching California . . . we were passing an ordinance that is going to be very effective." Dozens of states and cities have been considering limits on the weapons after the Jan. 17 killings of five schoolchildren in Stockton, Calif., by a man with an AK-47 rifle. The gunman, Patrick Edward Purdy, 24, shot himself to death with a pistol. Congress has been considering a proposal by Sen. Howard Metzenbaum, D- Ohio, to ban the sale of AK-47s, Israeli-made Uzis and other similar weapons that have become the guns of choice among drug dealers and gang members across the country. President Bush, an NRA member, said Thursday that he was "strongly opposed" to such a ban. The Los Angeles City Council unanimously voted Feb. 7 to immediately ban the sale of assault weapons and to prohibit citizens from owning such guns unless they are rendered inoperable. The ownership ban is to go into effect

232.text: GUN-1744 PRIMARY Autopsy of Man Who Killed 23 In Texas Finds No Drugs or Tumor The gunman who killed 23 people at a cafeteria here last week and then killed himself did not have narcotics or alcohol in his system, nor did he suffer from an aneurysm, according to an autopsy report released today. The report leaves the authorities still struggling to determine a motive for the killing rampage by George J. Hennard Jr., said Sgt. Michael DeHart, a spokesman for the Killeen police. Mr. Hennard's father, a Houston physician, suggested to the police last week that his son's violent outburst might have been the result of an aneurysm, a weakening in the wall of a blood vessel that leads to bulging that could then either rupture or cause pressure to build in the brain. Call for Tougher Gun Laws Also today, Sergeant DeHart discounted a published report that the police had found in Mr. Hennard's home a ticket stub to the movie "The Fisher King," which includes a scene in which a gunman shoots several patrons in a New York City restaurant. About 70 miles south of here, in the state capital of Austin, the Killeen killings led to a call this weekend to strengthen Texas's gun control laws, which state officials acknowledge are among the most lenient in the nation. Lieut. Gov. Bob Bullock said on Sunday that the Legislature should adopt a gun control law based on that in Virginia, which is generally considered by gun control advocates as the best state law now in effect.

235.text: GUN-30734 PRIMARY Lock and Load Thirty-thousand Americans are killed by guns every year -- on the job, walking to school, at the shopping mall. The Supreme Court on Thursday all but ensured that even more Americans will die senselessly with its wrongheaded and dangerous ruling striking down key parts of the District of Columbia's gun-control law. In a radical break from 70 years of Supreme Court precedent, Justice Antonin Scalia, writing for the majority, declared that the Second Amendment guarantees individuals the right to bear arms for nonmilitary uses, even though the amendment clearly links the right to service in a ''militia.'' The ruling will give gun-rights advocates a powerful new legal tool to try to strike down gun-control laws across the nation. This is a decision that will cost innocent lives, cause immeasurable pain and suffering and turn America into a more dangerous country. It will also diminish our standing in the world, sending yet another message that the United States values gun rights over human life. There already is a national glut of firearms: estimates run between 193 million and 250 million guns. The harm they do is constantly on heartbreaking display. Thirty-three dead last year in the shootings at Virginia Tech. Six killed this year at Northern Illinois University. On Wednesday, as the court was getting ready to release its decision, a worker in a Kentucky plastics plant shot his supervisor, four co-workers  and himself to death.

236.text: GUN-9485 PRIMARY MISSOURI HOUSE PASSES BILL ALLOWING CONCEALED WEAPONS; IT RESTRICTS WHO CAN GET PERMIT, WHERE GUNS CAN GO Carrying concealed weapons would be legal under a bill that won final approval Thursday from the Missouri House. The bill restricts who could have a concealed weapon and where a permit holder could carry it. The measure passed on a 108-33 vote and now moves to the Senate for more debate. The bill has many safeguards. For example, those who get the permit from their sheriff could not be a felon or have been convicted of a misdemeanor involving a crime of violence in the past five years. Applicants would have to go through eight hours of safety training and a background check. Permits would cost $100 and be valid for three years. Concealed weapons could not be taken into casinos, bars, prisons, or police stations but could be carried into schools, churches and child care facilities with proper approval. Members of the Legislature would be allowed to carry concealed weapons in the House and Senate chambers. The sponsor, Rep. Larry Crawford, R-California, said the bill has some features that make gun-rights supporters skittish. He said one of the criticisms of Proposition B was that there was no central repository of information on who would have had concealed weapons permits. Under his bill, the state Department of Revenue would keep the information and would issue a new drivers or nondrivers license with a "concealed carry endorsement."

238.text: GUN-8671 PRIMARY It's ladies' day on the firing range; An event sponsored by a national group offered women a chance to learn about and fire weapons.< Kaitlin Ebner, a Millersville University student, came to shop for a birthday present - a semiautomatic pistol for self-defense. Her mother, Janet Ebner, was there for a revolver. Debbie Bond, a mother of two from Atlantic City and a first-time gun-wielder, did a little target shooting. "It was awesome," she said, holding a shredded cardboard target. Yesterday was Ladies' Day at the Lower Providence Rod and Gun Club, where about 80 women got some basic lessons in firearm use, archery and fly casting. The daylong event was sponsored by the local chapter of the Second Amendment Sisters of Pennsylvania, part of a national organization of nearly 5,000 members who promote self-defense, parental responsibility, and the constitutional right to bear arms. Teenagers and seniors alike, many with little or no experience handling firearms - got help in everything from selecting weapons to choosing a stance while firing. "More women today are tired of being victims of crime," event organizer Jim Bestider said, adding that women are at a greater risk of being attacked by men, but that women are also more intimidated by guns. Other chapters around the nation sponsor similar activities. Michigan members of the Second Amendment Sisters organize a "shop n' shoot": one day at an outlet mall, the next at a shooting range. The national organization, which is not affiliated with the National Rifle Association, held the Armed Informed Mothers' March on Mother's Day as a counterprotest to the Million Mom March against gun violence. It is estimated that 12 million women nationwide own or have access to guns.

237.text: GUN-6266 PRIMARY EDITORIALS Gun laws a tough sell Gov. Bill Owens and Attorney General Ken Salazar have formed what could be a potent bipartisan partnership in support of new controls on guns and youth violence. Now they need to work with legislators to accomplish their goals. Already on the books are federal gun-control laws that lack the proper enforcement and penalties to make them true consequences. Colorado needs to do a better job. In the just-released findings of their June Summit on Youth Violence, Owens and Salazar recommend giving Colorado's district attorneys the power to prosecute gun violators to assure more swift penalties. But if the punishment turns out to be no more than a $ 200 fine, what good is it? Owens and Salazar have a series of excellent suggestions to close the gun-show loophole, a gap in federal and state laws that allowed Columbine High School shooters Dylan Klebold and Eric Harris to obtain the guns used in the worst school shooting in U.S. history. Among specific recommendations: Require background checks for firearm purchases at gun shows. Raise from 18 to 21 the age for purchasing firearms at a gun show. Ban "straw purchases" of firearms, when one person buys a weapon for another at a gun show. Include juvenile criminal records in gun background checks. Rep. Doug Dean, R-Colorado Springs, who wants to ease current gun laws, says some of the gun-control measures are going to be a tough sell to the Legislature. We hope he's wrong. They are reasonable measures, many of which simply bring Colorado to the level of some other states.

239.text: GUN-5406 PRIMARY ARMING TO FIGHT VIOLENCE Palm Beach County commissioners on Tuesday did what politicians up the line should do. They ignored the few but predictable alarmists and unanimously approved a gun-control ordinance. The ordinance closes a loophole that had made it easier for criminals to buy guns. Under the old law, licensed dealers had to conduct background checks and adhere to a waiting period, but unlicensed dealers could bypass both. At gun shows all over Florida, such unlicensed dealers sold all comers deadly weapons from their "personal collections." The gun-show loophole never made sense, but the Legislature feared the National Rifle Association too much to close it. Florida voters, however, had no such fears and voted by more than 2-to-1 in November to give counties the power, if the commission agrees, to require a criminal check and waiting period for all gun sales on property open to the public. The knock on the local ordinance is that it does too little. Obviously, it would be better to have stricter gun-control laws applied statewide or nationwide. But Palm Beach County is making a start. The impact is greater because Broward and Miami-Dade counties also have closed the loophole. If Martin and St. Lucie counties will act, South Florida can extend the safety zone up the east coast. A few from the Second Amendment fringe told Palm Beach County commissioners that the new ordinance was a terrible infringement on their rights. Those tired squawks don't work anymore. It is stupid to sell guns without proper controls. The National Rifle Association, hijacked by radicals, has lobbied for stupidity. Voters, and an increasing number of responsible gun owners, have become smarter than that.

234.text: GUN-10141 PRIMARY Gun-Control Battle Heats Up in District; Leaders Fight Repeal of Limits A coalition of District political, labor, business and religious leaders took aim yesterday at U.S. senators seeking to repeal the city's gun restrictions, with some accusing the senators of using D.C. residents as pawns to further their own political ambitions. At a news conference at the District's Shaw Junior High School, some of the local leaders threatened to work for the defeat of Sens. Kay Bailey Hutchison (R-Tex.) and George Allen (R-Va.), who are spearheading a Senate bill to abolish various D.C. gun laws, including the city's 29-year-old ban on private ownership of handguns. Hutchison is weighing a 2006 bid for governor. Allen faces reelection in 2006 and is contemplating a 2008 presidential bid. "To those who seek to use the District of Columbia as a platform to run for governor of their state . . . you might find residents of the District of Columbia knocking on doors in Texas and educating folks about your work," said Joslyn N. Williams, president of the Metropolitan Washington Council of the AFL-CIO. The Rev. Lionel Edmonds, head of the Washington Interfaith Network, said leaders of the 60 denominations represented in his group would reach out to organize like-minded clergy in Virginia and Texas. Others appearing at the news conference included Del. Eleanor Holmes Norton (D-D.C.)  --  who said repeal would increase a D.C.  homicide rate that is at a 20-year low  --  School Superintendent Clifford B. Janey and relatives of shooting victims. The anti-gun activists announced the formation of the National Coalition for D.C. Democracy and Safety to lobby against the Senate bill and a similar House measure, which were introduced last month.

240.text: GUN-5734 PRIMARY Mr. DeLay's Power Play That was one embarrassing week for Dennis Hastert. When the House blocked tough gun control legislation and then could not muster the votes for the pathetically weak measure that was left, the Speaker was nowhere to be found. It was bad enough to have to explain why his promise of "common sense" gun control legislation flamed out as the House surrendered to the gun lobby. Had he appeared in public, he also would have had to explain why he surrendered control of the House to Tom DeLay. George W. Bush and others, take note. Mr. DeLay -- fund-raiser, friend of lobbyists and nasty culture warrior -- has become the main voice of the G.O.P. in Congress. The gun control issue is instructive for those studying the contours of politics in Washington as it approaches the most competitive election year in a long time for both Congress and the Presidency. For all that, the Republican Congressional leaders are oddly passive. While the country yearns for centrist politics -- something that helps to explain the success of Mr. Bush in the Presidential field -- the top Congressional Republicans have yielded to the fire-breathing ideologues who drove the House to impeachment last year and seem determined to make their hatred of the life choices of many Americans the center of their strategy. As a result, the skill that Trent Lott, the Senate majority leader, brought to the impeachment trial earlier this year has deserted him. Mr. Hastert is the accidental House Speaker who owes his job to Mr. DeLay and has all but handed over the Speakership to him. While Mr. Hastert sputters platitudes about inclusiveness, Mr. DeLay has called the shots all year on campaign finance reform, Kosovo, the budget and now guns.

241.text: GUN-6708 PRIMARY NO END IS IN VIEW FOR ILLINOIS GUN-BILL IMPASSE Legislators have been talking for weeks, but there's little sign that anyone has been listening. A dispute over the proper penalty for illegal gun possession drags on at the Illinois Capitol. After nine days of special sessions in three weeks, Senate Republicans and supporters of GOP Gov. George Ryan can't even agree on what the proposal before them actually does. On Friday, Sen. John Maitland, R-Bloomington, said, "Let's just back away, enjoy the last part of the holiday season, come back after the first of the year and begin working on this." The Senate has twice rejected a bill backed by Ryan that would make illegal weapons possession a felony while giving first-time offenders probation and a chance later to have their arrest record cleared from the books. Republicans say they still have concerns about how it would work and want assurance that people who make an innocent mistake aren't haunted by a serious criminal charge. Democrats say the Senate Republicans just don't get it. "This is not a felony conviction that's expunged. It's a dismissal of a felony charge, and the arrest record is expunged," said Sen. John J. Cullerton, D-Chicago, a key negotiator on the bill. "It's not even a 'not guilty.' It's like, 'I'm sorry we charged you. We're going to forget we charged you.'" After the bill fell five votes short of the constitutional majority it needed, Ryan said he would not give up on having the felony-charge provision restored. That's the way it was in a massive anti-crime bill that the Illinois Supreme Court invalidated for technical reasons on Dec. 2, prompting Ryan to call a special session.

242.text: GUN-5917 PRIMARY The Dealers; In Many Crimes, Trail Leads To a Tiny Fraction of Stores One percent of the nation's gun dealers sold nearly half of the guns used in crime last year and many of those businesses show up year after year as major sources of crime weapons, according to a new study of federal firearms data. The study conducted by the office of Sen. Charles E. Schumer (D-N.Y.) marks a new phase of a growing effort to hold firearms dealers responsible for gun violence. Schumer's office used government data to locate sellers of weapons traced in crimes nationwide between 1996 and 1998 and found a pattern of "gun store recidivism" -- repeated crime-gun sales by a small number of stores. The study also found that Virginia ranked third in the nation as a source of weapons used by criminals in other states and was the leading out-of-state source of firearms for crimes committed in New York State. Some gun dealers may have shown up simply because they conduct a high volume of sales, the report said, but "others are likely to be stores that criminals and straw purchasers have discovered are lax with state and federal gun laws." Schumer's report did not take into account guns linked to crimes as a percentage of a dealer's total gun sales. The federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, which supplied the data to Schumer, did not dispute the findings in the report but said they took no part in the analysis.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

243.text: NA-42792 PRIMARY Disruptions: Smart Guns Can't Kill in the Wrong Hands Gun owners and advocates are fond of saying, "Guns don't kill people, people kill people." This might be a more useful aphorism: Smart-guns don't kill the wrong people. Technology exists, or could exist, that would make guns safer. The idea of a safe gun might seem to be the ultimate oxymoron: guns are designed to kill. But something missing from the gun-control debate that has followed the killing of 20 children and six adults at an elementary school in Newtown, Conn., is the role of technology in preventing or at least limiting gun deaths. Biometrics and grip pattern detection can sense the registered owner of a gun and allow only that person to fire it. For example, the iGun, made by Mossberg Group, cannot be fired unless its owner is wearing a ring with a chip that activates the gun. But you would be hard pressed to find this technology on many weapons sold in stores. "The gun industry has no interest in making smart-guns. There is no incentive for them," said Robert J. Spitzer, a professor of political science at SUNY Cortland and the author of four books on gun policy. "There is also no appetite by the government to press ahead with any kind of regulation requiring smart-guns." Why can we open our front doors with our iPhones and have cars that drive themselves, but we can't make a gun that doesn't fire unless its registered owner is using it?

248.text: NA-42810 PRIMARY No Easy Path for Obama's Gun Safety Agenda WASHINGTON - The fact that President Obama would make an all-out push for new gun restrictions seemed all but inevitable from the moment he delivered tearful yet resolute remarks to the nation in the hours after the Newtown, Conn., school massacre last month. On Wednesday, he will begin that effort, vowing executive action where possible and calling on Congress to adopt tough new bans on assault weapons, limit the size of ammunition clips, prohibit gun trafficking and expand background checks on gun purchases. Flanked by gun control advocates and school children from around the country, Mr. Obama is expected to summon the nation's outrage on behalf of action to prevent more massacres and to reduce the level of gun violence by focusing on how to keep guns out of the wrong hands. "Members of Congress, I think, are going to have to have a debate and examine their own conscience," Mr. Obama told reporters at a news conference this week. "We're going to have to come up with answers that set politics aside. And that's what I expect Congress to do." But once the event is over, there is little else about the effort that is inevitable. Lawmakers, advocates and administration officials agree that the president's gun agenda faces steep political hurdles, including a well-funded opposition that has repeatedly threatened retribution against any politician that backs new gun laws.

246.text: NA-41188 PRIMARY FIGHTING FOR FALLEN Newtown kin push gun reform Struggle brings them strength - and pain WASHINGTON - When she's far from the media spotlight, away from questions about the Newtown massacre and her mother's murder, Erica Lafferty struggles. There are days when the daughter of Sandy Hook Elementary School Principal Dawn Hochsprung is inspired to continue her fight for tougher gun control laws. Other days, she concedes, she doesn't even want to get out of bed. "Some days I'll say, 'Let's take on Congress!' " she said. "On others, well, it's just different." In the months since Newtown, Lafferty has become a high-profile proponent for gun control. Last Tuesday, she dramatically confronted Sen. Kelly Ayotte (R-N.H.) at a town hall meeting for voting against expanded background checks. On Friday, she made national news again by vowing on the "Today" show to chain her body to the Sandy Hook school if the town tries to tear the building down. But talks with her and other Newtown family members underscore the unavoidable emotional complexity of their lives. They should not be caricatured as just "gun control activists" or "relatives of Sandy Hook Elementary victims" because there are many other components to who they are. Lafferty hasn't returned to her job as a college admissions counselor in Waterbury, Conn. Speaking to the Daily News last week after several national TV appearances, she said she's just "not in a place where I can focus on anything but this" - namely, pushing the cause.

244.text: GUN-7184 PRIMARY THE NATION; Bringing Lawsuits to Do What Congress Won't FOR more than three decades, gun-control advocates have pressed lawmakers for stiffer measures, but the gun lobby ensured that they got nowhere in Congress. Then about two years ago, two dozen cities and counties nationwide began suing gun producers to recover the costs of treating gun shot victims. The result: Faced with the lawsuits and the costs of litigating them, Smith & Wesson, the nation's largest firearms maker, recently agreed to make changes in the way it makes and markets handguns. Similarly, cigarette makers had long frustrated public health activists who sought national legislation aimed at restricting how tobacco is sold, especially to children. Congress proved unwilling to buck the tremendous lobbying power of the industry, and nothing happened. But four years after state attorneys general nationwide and wealthy plaintiffs' lawyers joined forces to sue the tobacco companies, the companies reversed field and sued for peace. These two cases are emblematic of a shift taking place in how Americans attempt to govern their own society. Over the last few years, the legal system has begun to overtake the legislative process as the vehicle to resolve contentious debates, particularly over unpopular products, when Congress is unwilling to act. Both the tobacco and firearms litigation, for example, were highly organized assaults on specific industries. Previously, lawsuits had been brought by individuals against both industries with little financial success or effect on company policies.

249.text: GUN-9409 PRIMARY Gun Firms On Verge Of Winning New Shield; Liability Bill Reflects Industry, NRA Clout The gun industry, demonstrating its resurgent influence over Washington politics, is on the cusp of convincing President Bush and Congress to protect it from pending and future lawsuits. Under pressure from the National Rifle Association and a lesser-known organization funded with $ 100 million from gun manufacturers, Bush and a majority of lawmakers are on record supporting significant new legal protections for companies that make and sell guns. The legislation would prevent victims of gun crimes from making civil claims against companies that manufactured, imported or sold the weapons. The NRA calls the legislation a prudent way to prevent companies from going belly up simply because a criminal used their gun illegally. But critics say the measure would allow some gunmakers who misplace caches of weapons -- or dealers who sell guns to felons -- to escape civil penalties. Victims of the Washington area sniper, for instance, might be prohibited from suing the controversial gun dealer in Tacoma, Wash., who supplied the Bushmaster rifle used in some of last October's shootings if the bill becomes law, according to legal experts. Some gunmakers and distributors, facing numerous lawsuits nationally from victims and gun control groups and the possibility of losing hundreds of millions of dollars through jury verdicts and legal costs, worry they could be driven out of business if the cases persist. In a bipartisan political retreat from gun control, more than two-thirds of House members recently voted for the gun bill; 52 senators have sponsored it, and, if it passes the Senate, Bush plans to sign it into law. Opponents are scrambling to persuade at least 41 senators to filibuster the bill, but odds are stacked against them.

245.text: GUN-6539 PRIMARY Owens supports putting gun proposals on ballot Gov. Bill Owens says he'll back gun-control groups if they  decide to take any of his gun proposals to the ballot in November. 'I'll support any bill I supported in the Legislature as an  initiative,' Owens told editors gathered Thursday at the Colorado  Press Association Convention luncheon. ' As Yogi Berra used to say,  'It ain't over till it's over.'' Owens had proposed a five-point gun-control package as part  of his response to the Columbine High School massacre. Three parts of the package - requiring background checks at  gun shows, raising the minimum age to buy a handgun at a gun show  from 18 to 21, and requiring safe storage of guns in the home -  have been defeated at the hands of his fellow Republicans. But Owens has shown support for a Republican-sponsored  alternative to 'safe storage' legislation, though it falls short  of his original goal. The bill, HB 1274, expands the kinds of  people who can be charged with a crime for giving a juvenile a  handgun. The other two proposals still under consideration tinker with  existing law and are supported by the National Rifle Association. SAFE Colorado, a gun-control lobbying group, has said it  plans to try a ballot initiative on requiring background checks at  gun shows, which it calls 'closing the gun-show loophole.' The group is still deciding whether to try to take other  proposals to the ballot. But it is welcoming Owens' support.

250.text: NA-41228 PRIMARY PARK SCOPE Web weapons seller caught in tony B'klyn nabe A BROOKLYN man was charged Tuesday with selling guns out of his tony Park Slope apartment, where a rifle and two shotguns were found, authorities said. Hannibal Smith, 29, previously had a limited federal firearms license, which allowed him to sell gun parts, like triggers or magazine attachments, outside the city. But he sold fully assembled weapons in an online store run from his Warren St. building on a prime brownstone block, state Attorney General Eric Schneiderman alleged. "Gun dealers in New York must have permits, and they must abide by our local and state law - laws that are tough but fair and that were enacted to ensure that those who live in our communities are safe," Schneiderman said. "Selling unregistered guns illegally, particularly assault weapons, in Brooklyn is illegal and dangerous." A search by federal agents a couple of months ago uncovered two Mossberg 12-gauge shotguns and a .22-caliber Ruger rifle in Smith's home, even though he had no permit for them, court papers said. His limited license was subsequently revoked. Smith also allegedly shipped two Waffen Werks AK-74s to Florida. The semiautomatic rifles had a conspicuous pistol grip and a threaded barrel that fits a flash suppressor, which are features that define them as assault rifles, prosecutors said. Even people who have federal firearms licenses must comply with state laws and New York City's stringent gun regulations, said Joseph Anarumo, special agent with the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives.

247.text: NA-42671 PRIMARY Senators Bearing Arms Whenever talk turns to gun control in Congress, lawmakers feel compelled to mention their love of weaponry. ''I'm probably one of the few who have a pistol range in my backyard,'' said Senator Patrick Leahy of Vermont on Thursday, as he led a meeting of the Judiciary Committee on gun legislation. ''I have an AR-15,'' said Senator Lindsey Graham, referring to the nation's best-known assault weapon. ''I'm not going to do anything illegally with it,'' Graham added. There were no audible sighs of relief from the audience, but I am sure everybody was glad to have the reassurance. People, do you think Congress is actually going to do anything about gun violence in the wake of the Newtown shootings? Judiciary is going to vote on two big proposals next week: a ban on assault weapons and an expansion of gun purchase background checks. If the Democrats stick together, the bills can pass on a party-line vote. But to go any further, they need Republican support, and there wasn't a whole lot of it in evidence this week. Senator Dianne Feinstein, the chief sponsor of the assault weapons ban, seemed less than optimistic. '' I want to thank those who are with me,'' she said. '' I don't know that I can convince those who are not, but I intend to keep trying.'' She looked exhausted. At one point, she referred to Richard Blumenthal of Connecticut as ''Senator Delvanthal.''

252.text: NA-43820 PRIMARY Poll: Democrats favor Booker over Lautenberg in New Jersey Make sure to sign up to receive "Afternoon Fix" every day in your e-mail inbox by 5(ish) p.m.! EARLIER ON THE FIX: The Fix's 8 favorite Richard Nixon photos Cuomo, Hickenlooper leading charge on state gun control measures Joe Biden's on a roll Joe Biden and NRA have long history of antagonism Deval Patrick, Barney Frank and the politics of Senate appointments Jimmy Fallon on President Obama's inaugural theme (video) 5 senators to watch in gun control debate WHAT YOU MIGHT HAVE MISSED: * Vice President Biden said Thursday he sees an emerging consensus around "universal background checks" for all gun purchasers and a ban on high-capacity ammunition magazines. Biden, who is leading an Obama administration working group tasked with coming up with ways to curb gun violence, will present his team's recommendations to President Obama next Tuesday. The consensus Biden described is among gun-control advocates and law enforcement officials he has met with during the past week. * The National Rifle Association panned its meeting with Biden on Thursday, saying, "We were disappointed with how little this meeting had to do with keeping our children safe and how much it had to do with an agenda to attack the Second Amendment." The gun rights group indicated it would have nothing more to do with the Biden's task force. * Another poll suggests Democratic voters in New Jersey prefer Newark Mayor Cory Booker (D) over Sen. Frank Lautenberg (D). Among Democrats and Democratic-leaning independents, Democratic voters prefer Booker over Lautenberg 42 percent to 20 percent, according to a new Fairleigh Dickinson University PublicMind survey. Booker recently announced that he is exploring a Senate run, even as Lautenberg -- who turns 89 this month -- hasn't yet said whether he will run for reelection in 2014.

256.text: GUN-30193 PRIMARY MODERN COURT STUCK WITH MUSKET-ERA THINKING In regard to the Supreme Court decision on the Second Amendment and the law banning handguns in Washington, D.C., I feel that the court did not understand the intent of the Founding Fathers when they wrote this amendment more than 200 years ago. They wrote this amendment based on the technology that existed at that time. Guns were limited in their destructive ability compared with modern arms. They were single-shot weapons, hard to reload and prone to misfiring due to bad or wet gunpowder. If the Founding Fathers had to deal with pistols that could fire a dozen rounds in a few seconds or assault rifles that could fire 600 rounds a minute or machine guns that could fire 1,000 rounds a minute, I am sure that they would have written an amendment limiting the ability of individuals to possess such weapons. The Constitution is a wonderful document, but the court tried to make believe that weapons have not changed over the centuries. It is a shame that this amendment has not changed and that the court makes decisions that would shock the Founding Fathers. MICHAEL COHEN West Palm Beach

254.text: GUN-6060 PRIMARY Jumping the Gun Your May 8 front-page article "Gun Groups to Support White House Proposals" was ill-timed and inaccurate.
