While representatives of the National Shooting Sports Foundation and the American Shooting Sports Council did, indeed, meet with White House representatives on May 4 to discuss gun control issues, it was premature to suggest that agreement was reached on any issue. At the May 4 meeting, White House and industry representatives discussed a list of possible legislative proposals. In several cases, we indicated we "could" support the proposal under discussion, but no commitments were made. Indeed, all present agreed "the devil would be in the details" of drafting the proposed legislation. Your article made much of the fact that the NRA was not present at the May 4 meeting. This is only because its representatives were not invited. The NRA's participation at the May 10 White House Strategy Session on Children, Violence and Responsibility, however, was a major topic of discussion. The NRA had indicated that it would accept an invitation, and we urged that NRA executives be invited. There was obvious reluctance, but the White House people indicated they would take our comments into consideration. Our meeting with the White House was represented as, and was conducted as, preparatory to the May 10 strategy session. We were, therefore, surprised and disappointed when the Clinton administration proceeded to introduce its list of anti-gun proposals on May 7. As a result of this breach of cooperation, combined with the White House's refusal to invite NRA executives to the strategy session, we informed the White House that we would not participate in the session.

251.text: GUN-7368 PRIMARY Who's to Blame When Kids Kill Kids? The March 1 editorial "Lethal Gunfire in Grade One" correctly criticized Congress for inaction on reasonable gun control. Legislation that would reduce the nation's plague of school shootings has passed both the House and Senate in nearly identical juvenile justice bills. Both bills have provisions for tightening juvenile access to guns and for preventing school violence and student drug use. Only a minor difference between the House and Senate versions on gun control provisions is holding up this legislation; members of Congress seem content to play politics during this election year. Meanwhile, school shootings and drug use rage out of control across the nation. DeFOREST RATHBONE Great Falls Concerning the March 1 editorial about the shooting at the elementary school: The Post loudly attacks these isolated incidents and the item used in the crime, rather than condemn those responsible. In this case, the responsible parties were the school system, which let such a violent child (one who stabbed a classmate with a pencil) remain in class, and the local justice system, which did nothing to arrest those violating local and state laws at a house known to have both illegal narcotics and guns present. The same government entities that want to gain greater control over people's lives--the schools and the courts--won't be held culpable when they mess up. KEN STILES Centreville Typically, The Post blamed guns for the recent shooting death of a child.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not explicitly mentioned)

255.text: GUN-8598 PRIMARY STILL THE LAW IN KENNESAW; Times change, but world famous Second Amendment statement remains in place after 20 years. "... every head of household residing in the city limits is required to maintain a firearm, together with ammunition therefor." EXCERPT FROM 1982 KENNESAW ORDINANCE, SEC. 34-1 John Nunez and his family moved to Kennesaw from New Jersey nine months ago. Friends and relatives rib the sales manager about whether he's complied with the city's famous ordinance. Does he own a gun? "No," he said. "Does a water pistol count?" It's not a matter of philosophy, Nunez said. He likes handguns. But he has three kids in the house under the age of 5. The gun law heard 'round the world has entered its 20th year. It still reads the same: The head of every household in Kennesaw must have a firearm and ammunition. Unless he or she doesn't want to. But little else has stood still. The little town of 7,000 has exploded into a city of 22,000. The young, personable mayor who ordered up the gun law abruptly vanished. The police chief who was assigned the task of explaining the law --- and coping with the international army of journalists who stormed the railroad town in its wake --- is now a writer of mystery novels in Nebraska. Dent Myers, a self-described racist and unreconstructed Confederate, made a second career from the gun law, posing in front of his downtown shop with the .45-caliber pistols he keeps strapped to his hips.

258.text: GUN-6461 PRIMARY Guns: The Limits of Federal Power Former senator Bill Bradley's comments on how he would control guns may be "common sense" in his mind, but they appear to overstep the bounds of legitimate federal action [op-ed, July 9]. Some complain that cars are registered and their drivers licensed, so why not guns? Vehicles are registered at the state level. If states choose to register guns and license gun owners, they are perfectly within their rights. For the federal government to create a national registry of guns and require the licensing of every gun owner, however, is not within its constitutional powers. If Mr. Bradley truly wants to control guns, he would do well to enforce current laws, including the Brady Law, and encourage gun owners to take lessons on gun safety and proper gun handling. Those who use guns to commit crimes often commit other crimes to obtain their guns. Federal licensing and registry of guns and their owners will not prevent any of these crimes. Enforcement of existing laws will. JOSEPH COLETTI Alexandria

253.text: GUN-2073 PRIMARY NEW LAWS CHANGE OLD BUSINESS Peter Cutelli has organized gun shows in the St. Louis area for nearly 10 years, but the region's firearm laws have changed the scope of his business - at least on the Illinois side of the river.   "No, I don't do shows over there any more," Cutelli said.   Cutelli said some Missouri firearm exhibitors find the paperwork of Illinois' stricter gun laws more trouble than a sale is worth.   "You have to have an Illinois gun buyer's card, then there is a waiting period - even for long guns - and then there's that 900 (telephone) number providing for an instant check," Cutelli said.   "Illinois residents can buy guns at (a Missouri) show, but they can't take the guns with them," Cutelli said. "The weapons have to be shipped to an Illinois dealer and picked up there." Illinois residents obtain their Firearm Owner's Identification card from the state police. A card holder must be 21 or older and never been convicted of a felony. Card holders cannot be mentally retarded, or ever been a patient in a mental hospital or a current drug addict.   Illinois gun dealers must withhold delivery of handguns for 72 hours and rifles and shotguns for 24 hours. There is no state registration of firearms, but some cities, including Chicago, require it. Missouri's firearm laws are less strict, but they still place some restrictions on the sale of handguns, Cutelli said.   People wanting to buy a handgun in Missouri must get a "permit to purchase" from their local sheriff's office. "In St. Louis County, it takes about a week" to process the request, Cutelli said. Like in Illinois, the Missouri gun buyer must be 21 or older, without a criminal record or a record of drug addiction or mental problems. The $10 permit is valid for 30 days.   Also, anyone buying a firearm at a gun show or retail outlet must fill out a federal gun purchase form, which is kept by the gun dealer.   Cutelli stressed that shows follow strict safety procedures, with off-duty St. Louis police officers at entrances, checking firearms to make sure they are unloaded. Off-duty plainclothes police also move through the hall.   "Security is one of my biggest expenses," Cutelli said. "It runs about $5,000 per show." "We have never had any trouble," Cutelli said, as he knocked on wood.   Cutelli said tighter federal and local gun laws have prompted some exhibitors to stop selling certain types of weapons, such as military-style assault rifles and handguns.   "And some guys just don't feel right in selling handguns," Cutelli added.   And there are other uncertainties.   "The Congress could pass a law and shut down gun shows in a minute," Cutelli said.   To counter this possibility, Cutelli is expanding the focus of his shows.   "We call our (exhibit) the St. Louis Weapon Collectors Gun and Knife Show," he said. "But we are thinking of renaming it something like 'Sportsman Expo as sponsored by the Gun and Knife Show.' "

257.text: GUN-10008 PRIMARY TIME FOR ALBANY TO GET TOUGH ON GUNS The Daily News' Stop Crimes Against Cops Campaign will take a big leap forward Wednesday when the Legislature returns to Albany to take action. And act it must. Kudos to Gov. Pataki for interrupting the lawmakers' holiday preparations and summoning them to Albany to deal with a true crisis - a rash of shootings in the city since June that has killed two officers and wounded nine more. Pataki is urging lawmakers to beef up penalties against thugs who threaten or attack cops and traffic in illegal guns. The Assembly and Senate should move quickly to refine these proposals - in public negotiations - and deliver them before Christmas. They will make the perfect gift to New York's Finest, and to the grateful citizens who rely on the police for protection. Proposals from Pataki and the Senate's majority Republicans closely track the goals laid out by the Daily News after the recent murders of Detectives Dillon Stewart and Daniel Enchautegui, heroes who pursued their attackers even after taking bullets to the heart. Pataki and the Senate want to restore the death penalty for cop killers and sharply increase prison time for anyone who threatens or attacks an officer of the law. They would also stiffen the punishment for trafficking in illegal firearms, taking deadly weapons out of thugs' hands and making the streets safer for cops and citizens alike.

259.text: GUN-4612 PRIMARY Gun Ban for Domestic Offenders Is Too Broad To the Editor: As a prosecutor with a heavy domestic violence docket, I can appreciate the danger of permitting gun ownership for those who have committed misdemeanor domestic-violence offenses. Yet your March 20 editorial "Don't Rearm Domestic Abusers" and the Federal law barring such ownership assume that all who have been convicted of domestic violence acted with excessive and egregious violence. In the wake of the Nicole Brown Simpson murder, state legislatures expanded domestic violence laws. In Ohio, for example, the law was amended so that police must adhere to a preferred arrest policy. An officer who fails to make an arrest from a domestic abuse call is required to compile a lengthy report justifying the decision. Many officers fear civil action for failure to make an arrest. The result has been an exponential surge of domestic violence cases premised on heated, mutual shouting and drunken, mutual shoving. Before Ohio amended its law, these cases never made it to criminal court, since police could exercise arrest discretion, separate parties and allow a cooling-off period. Essentially, Ohio law, and its new counterparts, have created a new class of criminals, now subject to the Federal gun ban. Surely Congress should consider these changes, and the level and degree of domestic abuse, before broadly banning gun possession. Domestic abusers who cause physical harm present a significant risk of gun violence and are rightly denied possession; it is excessive to bar gun ownership for less weighty offenders.

261.text: GUN-3939 PRIMARY SUBURB OUTLAWS GUN SALES The commercial sale of firearms and ammunition is now illegal in this northern Milwaukee suburb. The Village Board unanimously approved an ordinance prohibiting such sales. It acted Monday night after a public hearing at which no one spoke either for or against the measure. No business in the community now sells either firearms or ammunition. The board previously outlawed the sale of guns or ammunition in pawnshops, jewelry stores or resale shops and enacted an ordinance requiring all guns in the village to be disabled by a trigger lock or locked or encased when not in use.

260.text: GUN-7055 PRIMARY Gunmaker 'clarifies' landmark pact WASHINGTON - Smith & Wesson, America's largest manufacturer of handguns, has issued a 'clarification' of its landmark pact with the federal government that would effectively eviscerate much of the gun controls trumpeted by the Clinton administration. Smith & Wesson's interpretation - posted on its Web site - has forced the company back into talks with the administration that could lead to a protracted court battle. Administration officials dismissed Smith & Wesson's interpretation as more of a public relations gambit than a genuine change of heart, but said they would be willing to go to court to force Smith & Wesson into compliance. 'The deal speaks for itself,' said Neal Wolin, general counsel of the Treasury Department, referring to the agreement Smith & Wesson reached March 17 with federal, state and local officials as a way to settle lawsuits it had faced related to gun violence. 'I think the language of the deal is clear on all these points.' Contrary to the government's interpretation, the gunmaker says now, its agreement would compel Smith & Wesson dealers to impose background checks and other gun-sale restrictions only on buyers of Smith & Wesson products. The government maintains that under the agreement, dealers who wish to sell Smith & Wesson's products must impose such restrictions on buyers of all guns. Moreover, the company says, the criminal background checks Smith & Wesson agreed to for gun-show sales would not apply to guns sold by private citizens, only to those sold by licensed dealers. Gun-show sales by licensed dealers are already subject to background checks.

263.text: GUN-6633 PRIMARY Read case and decide Possession was limited to gun clubs. Possession of long guns meeting specified parameters and antiques was allowed. The law was appealed on Second Amendment grounds and the appellant lost. The Supreme Court refused to hear a further appeal. This law affected thousands of Americans' ability to possess certain types of guns, and the Supreme Court looked the other way. The ruling in Quilici and the Supreme Court's action are not consistent with any claim that the Second Amendment protects the right to personal ownership or possession but are consistent with a state's right to have a well organized militia. I recommend that those interested in this issue look at this case themselves. It can be found on the Internet at www.abanet.org/gunviol/cases/quilici.html. If you don't have access to the Net, we have three local law schools whose librarians can help you find a copy to review. So the citizens of Morton Grove were denied what is commonly called the right to bear arms. The Supreme Court did not lift a hand to change it. Read the case and decide for yourself. CONNIE PADGETT Chapel Hill

262.text: NA-46195 PRIMARY Facts back up support for ban About a month after a mass shooting in Newtown, Conn., left 20 schoolchildren and six adults dead, President Barack Obama rolled out a package of gun-control proposals during a speech with Vice President Joe Biden. The package included initiatives such as an assault-weapons ban that requires congressional approval, along with 23 executive actions that the president can implement on his own. In presenting the package, specifically the portion dealing with the assault-weapons ban, Obama made a point of conjuring past President Ronald Reagan's stance on the same issue. "Weapons designed for the theater of war have no place in a movie theater," Obama said during the speech. "A majority of Americans agree with us on this. And, by the way, so did Ronald Reagan, one of the staunchest defenders of the Second Amendment, who wrote to Congress in 1994, urging them --- this is Ronald Reagan speaking --- urging them to listen to the American public and to the law-enforcement community and support a ban on the further manufacture of military-style assault weapons." Evoking past presidents is a frequent practice by politicians. Unfortunately, sometimes the context and the content of the recollections are incorrect. PolitiFact Georgia decided to check the accuracy of Obama's statement, as well as whether most Americans support a ban on military-style assault weapons. Obama's plans drew immediate and intense reaction from supporting groups such as the Coalition to Stop Gun Violence, as well as opponents such as the National Rifle Association.

265.text: GUN-5997 PRIMARY A Gun Bill in the House THE MAILBAGS and e-mail baskets of House members continue to reflect strong public pressure for protection from gunfire. They have forced at least some Republicans to change their tunes by at least a note or two. GOP leaders in the House -- while not exactly stampeding toward serious regulation of America's outlandishly loose gun markets -- have budged a bit. On Tuesday they announced support for the modest measures included in the legislation passed by the Senate. Congressional enactment of this package would be more symbolic than substantive, but the change in congressional climate is a start. More members are finding out that they can stray from the hard line of the NRA gun-pushers and live politically to tell about it. House Speaker Dennis Hastert and Judiciary Committee Chairman Henry Hyde are in no huge hurry to move or improve the Senate bill, however. Supporters had pressed for action this week, before the House takes off on its 10-day holiday recess. But Chairman Hyde said he hopes to get a bill to the House floor by mid-June. Unless the Republicans have some major legislative surgery in mind, they shouldn't postpone action beyond that time. The likelihood of improvements in the bill over a longer period is small, the opportunities for legislative mischief greater. One danger is that the House package could wind up loaded with "juvenile justice" provisions that have proved controversial in the past. Rep. Bill McCollum, chairman of the Judiciary crime subcommittee and a loyal soldier of the NRA lobby over the years, is talking about new punishments of juvenile offenders that could alter the bill enough to thwart any hopes -- or reason -- for prompt enactment. "In debating the pros and cons of gun control," Rep. Hyde said, "we cannot avoid our responsibility by ignoring the deeper cultural and spiritual problems evidenced by the violent conduct of the accused juveniles in Littleton and Conyers." But to move gun safety measures with some sense of urgency is not to ignore other responsibilities.

264.text: NA-44119 PRIMARY Newtown didn't change the politics of guns The decision this week by Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid to drop the assault weapons ban and a ban on high-capacity clips from the broader Congressional effort to curb gun violence sent an unmistakable message: The murders of 20 children in Newtown, Conn., in late 2012 has not changed politics as much as many people thought it might. That's a hard political truth to hear for many Americans who viewed what happened in Connecticut as a moment when the conversation about guns in America changed. President Obama pledged action. Vice President Biden chaired a White House task force to recommend legislative and executive solutions to curb gun violence. Longtime gun control advocates like New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg (I) and California Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D) insisted that this time -- past mass murders involving guns had not moved the needle on a desire for stricter gun laws -- was different. And public polling suggested -- and continues to suggest -- that large majorities favor many of the provisions put forward by Biden's task force. Nearly six in 10 Americans support banning assault weapons in the latest Washington Post-ABC News poll, and large majorities back expanding background checks to cover all purchases. A smaller majority -- though still a majority -- favor a ban on high-capacity clips. And yet, as Newtown disappeared further in the political rearview mirror, the same politics that had turned guns into a dormant issue on the national political stage for much of the 1990s and 2000s began to take hold.

characters: New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg

267.text: NA-44348 PRIMARY Five things to watch on Election Day in New York and Colorado Note: We originally posted this item on Monday, 9/9. We are re-posting it today as voters go to the polls. Voters head to the polls in New York and Colorado  on Tuesday to cast ballots in some high-stakes races that are not to be missed. From fresh clarity about the gun control debate to whittling down the possibilities of who will get the top job in America's most populous city, here are the five biggest things to watch: 1. Will Colorado voters oust either, both, or neither of the Democrats facing a recall election? Colorado state Senate President John Morse and state Sen. Angela Giron face recall elections Tuesday in a campaign that has shaped up as referendum on gun laws. Both voted for new gun-control measures this year, setting the stage for the opposition to seek to remove them from power. Outside money has poured in, mostly on the anti-recall side. New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg (I), one of the country's most vocal gun control advocates, and the National Rifle Association, the nation's largest gun rights group, have each spent six figures, telling you everything you need to know about what's at stake in Colorado. The fundamentals (money, party tilt of the districts) favor the Democrats. But early voting turnout figures in Morse's district, different rules for mail-in ballots and the general unpredictability of turnout in recall elections mean Republicans have a chance to shake things up.

characters: Colorado state Senate President John Morse

271.text: NA-42688 PRIMARY Bloomberg's TV Blitz on Guns Puts Swing Senators on the Spot The commercial is an unambiguous appeal to gun owners: a middle-aged hunter, shotgun in hand, vows that he will fight to protect the Second Amendment. But in a sensible, father-of-the-house tone, he also urges voters to support comprehensive background checks, ''so criminals and the dangerously mentally ill can't buy guns.'' The man behind the advertisement is not known for his kinship with the gun crowd: Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg, the nation's fiercest advocate of restrictions on firearms since the December rampage at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. Determined to persuade Congress to act in response to that shooting, Mr. Bloomberg on Monday will begin bankrolling a $12 million national advertising campaign that focuses on senators who he believes might be persuaded to support a pending package of federal regulations to curb gun violence. The ads, in 13 states, will blanket those senators' districts during an Easter Congressional recess that is to be followed by debate over the legislation. In a telling sign of how much the white-hot demands for gun control have been tempered by political reality, Mr. Bloomberg's commercials make no mention of an assault weapons ban once sought by the White House and its allies, instead focusing on the more achievable goal of universal background checks. ''You don't want to lose everything in the interest of getting the perfect,'' Mr. Bloomberg said in an interview, acknowledging his disappointment over the apparent unlikelihood of an assault weapons ban, but insisting he is resolved to push the legislation through at a time when its prospects are uncertain.

266.text: GUN-4128 PRIMARY GUN CONTROL BY CITIES WOULD SEND A MESSAGE TO STATE THE federal government won't do it. The California Legislature won't do it. So up and down California, cities are trying to deal with a problem that ought to be handled at a higher, broader level: gun control. On Tuesday night, the San Jose City Council will take up a proposal by Councilwoman Margie Fernandes to ban the sale of Saturday Night Specials, the shoddy, easily concealed handguns that are used in the vast majority of gun crimes. Fernandes wants San Jose to join a movement that began in West Hollywood and is spreading to the Bay Area, as she describes above. The National Rifle Association has sued West Hollywood, claiming cities do not have the authority to enact gun control. The legal issue is murky, so City Attorney Joan Gallo recommends that San Jose not implement a ban until the West Hollywood case is decided. With that stipulation, we think the council should adopt Fernandes' proposal. Even if local bans are ruled illegal, cities at least can send a message to the state Legislature, perhaps providing political cover to lawmakers who have been afraid to buck the powerful NRA. And if the bans are upheld, the result could be a real decrease in gun-related crime - if enough cities participate. Stopping the sale of junk guns in San Jose won't do much to curb crime here if gang members can just drive to Campbell or Milpitas for cheap guns.

268.text: GUN-9795 PRIMARY GROUP BLASTS ASSAULT WEAPONS/ MILLION MOM MARCH URGES RENEWAL OF BAN, REMOVAL OF LOOPHOLE Nick Wilcox wore a button with a picture of his late daughter and spoke of the satisfaction of joining other gun control advocates Sunday at a rally to urge renewal of a federal ban on assault weapons. "It's always gratifying to be with kindred spirits, and that's what this is," said Wilcox, whose 19-year-old daughter, Laura, was one of two workers at a mental health department office in California shot to death in 2001 by a patient. The assailant used a 9 mm semiautomatic handgun with an illegal 3 0-round clip, Wilcox said. "I don't believe assault weapons belong on our streets. If people want to play with such tools, they should join the military," said Wilcox, who came from Nevada County, Calif., with his wife, Amanda, for the second "Million Mom March." The demonstration, attended by about 2,000 people, was much smaller than the original one, which took place on Mother's Day four years ago. Speakers at the "Halt the Assault" rally reminded the crowd to "Remember November!" as they spoke mainly of a need to renew a ban on assault rifles that is to expire Sept. 13. "We are working very hard in Iraq to get AK-47s off the street, to get Uzis off the streets. The president says we're fighting the war on terror by doing that," said Rep. Chris van Hollen, D-Md. "What about the terror right here on our streets at home?"

269.text: NA-44134 PRIMARY Conn. Gov. Dan Malloy: NRA's LaPierre like 'clowns at the circus'; The Connecticut governor hits back against the NRA head Wayne LaPierre. Connecticut Gov. Dan Malloy (D) on Sunday compared National Rifle Association head Wayne LaPierre to "clowns at the circus." "Wayne reminds me of the clowns at the circus. They get the most attention, and that's what he's paid to do," said Malloy on CNN's "State of The Union." LaPierre suggested Thursday that the new gun control laws passed in Connecticut wouldn't have prevented the mass shooting at a Newtown school late last year. The new regulations, he said, wouldn't deter criminals from illegally obtaining guns and would only impose further burdens on law abiding gun owners. Malloy defended the laws he signed, saying they are "probably a little tougher on everybody." The new laws include a tougher assault weapons ban and new restrictions on large capacity ammunition magazines. "The gun that was used to kill 26 people on December 14 was legally purchased in the state of Connecticut, even though we had an assault weapons ban. But there were loopholes in it that you could drive a truck through," Malloy said.

270.text: GUN-10994 PRIMARY In Tucson, gun dealers say show must go on TUCSON - At the "Crossroads of the West" gun show Saturday, University of Arizona junior Kiely Katz opened her plaid Burberry shoulder bag, took out a wallet shaped like a Japanese animated cat and plunked down her credit card for a $549 Glock 31  semiautomatic handgun. "I've been wanting one for a while; I've been shooting since I was little," Katz, 21, told Steve Zacher of Glockmeister, a gun dealership in Mesa, Ariz. The company's slogan -  Got Glock? (the question mark replaced by a gun barrel) - was written on the display case near "Team Glock" baseball caps. Katz was among the thousands of patrons who streamed into the Pima County Fairgrounds, a 10-mile drive southeast of Tucson, exactly one week after 22-year-old Jared Lee Loughner allegedly opened fire on a crowd outside a Tucson grocery store, killing six and wounding 13. He allegedly used a semiautomatic Glock 19. The rampage has reignited the national debate about gun laws. Critics have called for stiffer regulations, while gun rights defenders counter that the shooter was an outlier whose reckless behavior should not restrict responsible firearm owners. "The events at the Safeway store were tragic and unprecedented, but they weren't about lawful gun ownership," said Bob Templeton, the president of Crossroads. "It was about a mentally ill person who gained access to a firearm he shouldn't have."

272.text: GUN-5006 PRIMARY Governor Pataki and Gun Control Last week's deadly school massacre in Oregon should make it harder for the nation's lawmakers to ignore the urgent problem of children having easy access to guns and ammunition. That certainly ought to be the case in New York State, where pending legislation to keep loaded guns out of the hands of youngsters presents the Legislature and Gov. George Pataki with a test of political and moral leadership. For the fifth consecutive year, the Democratic-led Assembly has approved a sound bill that would require adults either to store loaded guns in a place inaccessible to children or to use gun locks that make weapons inoperable. Other key provisions would hold an adult owner criminally liable in the event a child obtains an improperly stored gun. By enacting the measure, New York would join 15 other states that have already passed similar laws, and help build momentum for national action by Congress. But first the bill must clear the Republican-controlled State Senate, where the gun lobby often prevails over common sense. The recent rash of school shootings seems to have softened Senator Dale Volker, chairman of the Codes Committee, where the legislation has stalled in the past. He is said to be considering lending his support to a version that contains sanctions, albeit weaker, on adult gun owners who fail to store their guns safely. This is a moment when gubernatorial leadership could make a big difference. But as the session races toward adjournment, Governor Pataki remains oddly aloof. He has not yet announced a position on the bill, much less begun to rally his fellow Republicans to the task. Mr. Pataki's hiding may please the National Rifle Association. But it badly misreads the national mood and the views of concerned New Yorkers.

273.text: GUN-8253 PRIMARY Gun Safety 101 TODAY'S DANGEROUS facts of life include guns, and health courses in schools ought to teach children at least a little something about the hazards of firearms in their midst. In Maryland, two state lawmakers who differ sharply on the need for more gun controls do agree that gunfire--like drug abuse or HIV infection--is enough of a threat to public health that firearm safety should be addressed in the classroom. Democratic State Sen. Barbara A. Hoffman of Baltimore, who supports tighter gun controls, and Republican Del. Carmen Amedori of Carroll County, who doesn't, say they will push next year for gun-safety training to be taught in all Maryland public schools. A good idea, but given the heavy cross-fire of opinions about guns in America, settling on appropriate course content is not easy. Sen. Hoffman says she would oppose direct adoption of the National Rifle Association's gun safety program. The general content of the package is straightforward enough: how to stay safe when you find a gun and what to do about it. But the NRA and other lobbies for or against gun controls ought to be kept at a safe distance from public school classrooms, which can be turned into platforms for political propaganda. Teachers should not be urging students either to join or shun target shooting facilities. The programs ought to make children aware of existing firearms laws in their home districts without loading the presentation. When a nationwide poll this week showed 90 percent of high school students favoring registration of all newly purchased handguns and the licensing of handgun buyers, an NRA spokeswoman questioned whether the students were aware of the "tens of thousands of gun laws already on the books." Include that kind of loaded line in a safety course and another group could argue that teachers also should tell kids about all the loopholes and differences in state and local laws that allow gun traffic to flourish.

274.text: GUN-6436 PRIMARY Teens talk guns with elite of D.C. Colorado lobbyists hailed by the chief WASHINGTON - The student lobbyists from SAFE Colorado left the White House on Thursday with an assignment. In a private meeting with the more than 90 students, President Clinton challenged them to expand their membership nationwide and come back to Washington next year in even greater numbers. "They have come to Washington to hold our feet to the fire and to make their voices heard," Clinton said. "They are plainly impatient with the lack of action on the important legislation before Congress. "For the past three months the gun lobby has been calling the shots on Capitol Hill," he added. "Now it's time for Congress to listen to the lobbyists who truly matter." At a news conference later, 17-year-old David Winkler of Denver, one of the student organizers of the trip, vowed that the youngsters "will not go quietly into the night." "We are the students of today, the voters of next year and the leaders of tomorrow. We will be heard." Not everyone wanted to listen, however. The students' overtures to several congressmen opposed to gun-control legislation were sternly rebuffed. Colin Cunliffe, 18, of Littleton attempted to engage Rep. Bob Barr, R-Ga., a member of the National Rifle Association and an opponent of gun control, in the Capitol. The congressman walked away, refusing even to shake Cunliffe's hand. Meanwhile, Winkler approached Rep. John Dingell, D-Mich., asking for two minutes of his time. "He said, 'I'm busy. Write me a letter,"' Winkler said. "He wouldn't even give me his card."

276.text: GUN-3393 PRIMARY Barr, Darden sticking to their guns in 7th District race Five-year-old testimony and a modern-day endorsement have 7th District congressional candidates scurrying to prove who's the best friend of the gun lobby. Former U.S Attorney Bob Barr, whose bumper stickers proudly proclaim "protect the 2nd" Amendment right to keep and bear arms, is under fire from incumbent Rep. Buddy Darden, who says Barr waffles on the gun issue. But Barr hotly denies the charge, insisting that Darden is a "sore loser" because he lost the endorsement of the National Rifle Association and the Police Benevolent Association. "They're flailing around, trying to find a way to divert attention," Barr said. "They're distorting the truth." The feuding has exploded the gun issue, one of the key issues in the race so far, into one that reaches far beyond the confines of the 11- county west Georgia district. State legislators who attended a Senate Special Judiciary Committee meeting nearly five years ago say they came away with no doubt that the then-U.S. attorney favored legislation prohibiting the sale and transfer of assault weapons, a measure similar to a federal firearms policy established by the Bush administration. "Oh no, I recall him being in favor of it," said former state Sen. Donn Peevy, who chaired the committee. "He was the most notable witness we had." The official minutes of the meeting list Barr as a supporter, as did bill sponsor Sen. David Scott (D-Atlanta) and then-Sen. Roy Barnes (D- Mableton), who voted against the bill.

278.text: NA-43088 PRIMARY Minority Gun Owners Face Balancing Act, Weighing Isolation and Stigma of Violence INDIANAPOLIS -- Standing in a small booth surrounded by displays for rifles, pistols, holsters and other firearm accouterments, the Rev. Kenn Blanchard signed copies of his book ''Black Man With a Gun: Reloaded.'' Amid the sea of thousands of white faces that descended on this city for the National Rifle Association convention in late April, Mr. Blanchard, an N.R.A. member since 1991, offered his reasoning for why he was one of the few black visitors. ''We still culturally have a fear that we're going to be that lone guy out, and you don't want to be the lone guy out,'' he said, estimating that one in 100 people at the convention was black. '' The exposed nail gets hammered.'' With his blog and podcasts, Mr. Blanchard is an avid proponent of gun rights and founder of the Maryland Tenth Cavalry Gun Club, a national pro-gun organization for African-Americans. And he was one of very few African-Americans comfortable enough with the N.R.A. to be hawking wares here. At a time when gun issues are volatile nationally and sales are increasing, minority gun owners -- whether black, Asian or Latino -- may feel that their weighing of the practical pros and cons of gun ownership comes up against the conservatism and unyielding stances of the N.R.A. and some other gun advocates. Mr. Blanchard said it could be a difficult balancing act.

275.text: GUN-20261 PRIMARY Marchers tell Congress: Mind your mothers Thousands of parents rally for gun control WASHINGTON -- They sang This Little Light of Mine with children's troubadour Raffi, and when a speaker asked why their plea for gun safety should be heeded, they chanted, "Because I said so." Hundreds of thousands of concerned parents -- 500,000, organizers claim -- gathered on Mother's Day at the foot of Capitol Hill to demand that Congress pass stricter gun laws. They came with strollers and diaper bags, in baseball caps and T-shirts, for the Million Mom March, the largest rally ever for gun control. This was no radical protest. This was mom and sometimes dad, asserting a political voice at a family rally on the National Mall that featured a "Children's Village" with clowns and jugglers and a tent for breast-feeding. "This is mom power," said Rep. Carolyn McCarthy, D-N.Y., whose husband was killed in a shooting in 1993 that also seriously injured her son. "We are going to make a difference.  . . . This is a first step. This is not where it ends." March organizers say they will form a grass-roots group to press for political change, especially the licensing of gun owners and the registering of every handgun. Nationwide, thousands gathered at dozens of other rallies, including an estimated 1,500 in Los Angeles and more than 4,000 along the downtown Chicago lakefront. The Washington event had the most celebrity sizzle. It hummed to music by Melissa Manchester, Melissa Etheridge and Emmylou Harris and heard from Susan Sarandon, Courtney Love and Reese Witherspoon.

characters: celebrities (e.g. Raffi, Melissa Manchester, Susan Sarandon)

279.text: GUN-1653 PRIMARY GUN CONTROL: DO WE NEED IT?; THE DEBATE Cynthia Tucker, editor of The Constitution's editorial pages: The Georgia Legislature must act now to pass gun-control legislation. The host of the 1996 Olympics Games cannot be asked to tolerate the rising crime rates. Ronnie Garrison, secretary of the Bighorn Hunting Club: Gun control hasn't worked in the past, and will not work in the future; educating society on problem-solving without violence will work better than banning guns. Mark Newman, Atlanta attorney: Waiting periods and a verification system are needed to cut off the easy access to guns currently enjoyed by felons and the deranged. Georgians should lobby legislators to pass the gun bill currently in the General Assembly. Reuben Greenberg, Charleston, S.C. police chief, says a waiting period would be fine but he's not in favor of prohibiting sales of guns or confiscation of weapons. The problem is not guns, but a cultural ethos of violence. YOUR IDEAS: What do you think about gun control? Let us know by faxing your thoughts and ideas to 404-526-5611.

277.text: GUN-8906 PRIMARY Perdue to NRA: 'Choose wisely'; Group hints it will back Barnes again Augusta --- After receiving a broad and public hint that he wouldn't get the group's much-desired endorsement, Sonny Perdue cautioned the National Rifle Association on Saturday to "choose wisely" in the governor's race. In his debut speech as the Republican nominee for governor, Perdue also suggested that he and Gov. Roy Barnes shoot it out --- with shotguns, at clay targets. The winner would walk away as the NRA's choice. "I'm serious about the challenge," Perdue said after his speech. The Barnes campaign declined comment. The rifle association endorsed Barnes, a Democrat, in 1998 --- as it had Zell Miller in 1990 and 1994. On Friday, at the outset of a two-day unity meeting of the state GOP, Perdue got a clear indication that the NRA's string of Democratic endorsements for governor probably would continue. In a 10-minute speech before a ballroom full of GOP delegates, the chief Washington lobbyist for the gun group put the weight of its national membership of 4 million behind Saxby Chambliss in the U.S. Senate race against Democratic incumbent Max Cleland. Not once did Charles Cunningham, the NRA's director of federal affairs, mention Perdue. On Saturday morning, speaking before the same group, the Republican nominee appealed for unity, condemned the policies of Barnes and gave his warning to the NRA. "I issue a call to the National Rifle Association to choose wisely, based on principles and not politics," he said.

281.text: GUN-12189 PRIMARY Dec. 27 Readers' letters Once again, it's people, not guns, who kill "60 Minutes" interviewed this past Sunday the school nurse who cowered under her computer desk and could see the legs and feet of the gunman who later committed the atrocities. What if she had a handgun concealed under her desk every morning with a strong magnet and locked away every night? She could have ended the massacre before so many lives were changed forever. Guns don't kill. People do. They can be defensive as well as offensive. In her account she was mere inches from the shooter and could have shot him in the leg, nonlethal, and stopped what later happened when he left her office headed for the schoolrooms. Tran Nguyen Santa Clara It's reasonable to limit the type of weapons I am stunned to see the NRA's glib solution for gun control to be armed guards on public school property. This seems to be an act that imprisons the innocent, leaving freedom for the criminal. While our greatest concern at this time is the protection of children from harm, what about all of us? What about protection at movie theatres (Aurora, Colo.), college campuses (Virginia Tech), malls, grocery stores? It seems that we'll need a militia to offer protection from the few who are unstable and choose guns to express themselves. Gun control needs to be addressed with much more careful assessment of those who choose to keep weapons for personal use and the way they intend to use them. I don't understand why a sport hunter must have an automatic weapon that acts as a weapon of war. People have a right to protect themselves but not as an aggressor in a friendly society. I think it is reasonable to limit the type and number of weapons that an ordinary citizen may keep for hunting and personal protection, and it is the permit holder's responsibility to secure the weapon away from any other person.

282.text: GUN-1567 PRIMARY Would have been a first for Ga. Lobbyists for the NRA opposed the measure because it would have been the first firearms regulation to pass in Georgia, he said. "The NRA might be a very strong political group, but they can't restore life to anybody who's been killed," he said. "Even the people who belong to the NRA have to realize that safety steps have to be taken." About 200 children in Georgia have been accidently killed with guns in the last 10 years, according to Handgun Control, a group a founded by Sarah Brady, the wife of former White House press secretary James Brady, after he was wounded in the assassination attempt on former President Ronald Reagan. Mrs. Brady Wednesday deplored the defeat of the Georgia gun bill. "I feel sorry for those who . . . ignored the pleas to save other kids' lives and capitulated to the gun lobby," she said.

280.text: GUN-7048 PRIMARY The Killers: Lessons Along a Trail of Death To the Editor: Re "Hole in Gun Control Law Lets Mentally Ill Through" (front page, April 11): Several New York counties have found one solution to the problem of identifying applicants for gun licenses who have a history of serious mental illness without violating their civil rights. New York State law requires completion of a character reference form. Columbia, Jefferson, New York and Suffolk are among the counties that conduct personal interviews with referees, spouses, neighbors and others. Family, friends and fellow workers are the people most likely to know if someone should not own a gun. If a thorough background check is carried out, it is far less likely that guns will get into the hands of deeply disturbed, potentially dangerous individuals. NANCY F. REGALADO New York, April 11, 2000 The writer is secretary of New Yorkers Against Gun Violence. http://www.nytimes.com

283.text: GUN-3540 PRIMARY FEINSTEIN GETS GRAPHIC OVER GUN BILL S.F. MASSACRE PHOTOS SHOWN IN SENATE DEBATE His murderous rampage over, Gian Luigi Ferri lay on his back in a San Francisco skyscraper's stairwell. The poster-size photograph displayed on the Senate floor Monday showed his face frozen in a haunting death mask streaked in sweat and blood. At San Francisco's Pettit & Martin law firm, his victims' arms and legs poked out gruesomely from beneath desks in the crime-scene photos. Everywhere are images of shattered glass, shattered picture frames, shattered lives. "This is what it looked like," Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., told her colleagues as she used an unusually graphic presentation of never-before-seen photographs from the 101 California St. massacre to fend off a new attack on the assault-weapons ban she championed last year. "'These are the shattered windows - the bullet holes through the windows. And then we get to the victims," Feinstein said, pointing to the huge photographs as a national C-SPAN audience watched. Feinstein recounted the 1993 slayings that left nine dead and six wounded as she pledged "to wage the mother of all filibusters" if Republican lawmakers deliver on their promise to dismantle the assault-weapons law. "The story of every victim of an assault-weapon shooting that we can find will be told," she vowed. Senate Majority Bob Dole, R-Kan., last spring called the weapons-ban repeal one of his chief legislative priorities for 1995. A vote to repeal the ban was set for May but was sidetracked when a terrorist bomb blew up the federal building in Oklahoma City on April 19.

284.text: GUN-3682 PRIMARY Crimes Involving Handguns Rose in 1993, Report Shows About 1.3 million people in the United States faced assailants armed with guns during 1993, and the use of semiautomatic weapons by juveniles rose, the Justice Department said today. Of the victims of rape, sexual assault, robbery and aggravated assault by offenders carrying a firearm, 86 percent, or 1.1 million, said the weapon was a handgun, the department reported. These figures show an 18 percent increase from 1992 in the number of people who suffered handgun crimes, according to Handgun Control Inc., a group that lobbies for the regulation of handguns. Firearms were involved in 70 percent of the 24,526 homicides in 1993, the Justice Department's Bureau of Justice Statistics said. Handguns were involved in four of five crimes committed with guns. Despite the overwhelming predominance of handguns in crimes committed with firearms, they represent only about one-third of the 223 million firearms manufactured or imported for sale in the United States from 1899 to 1993, the government report said. But the bureau said that more than half of the new guns added in 1993 were handguns. From 1973 to 1993, more than 40 million handguns were produced in the United States. The report comes while the Republican-controlled Congress is considering legislation to weaken the Brady Law, which requires a waiting period before the purchase of a handgun, and to eliminate the 1994 Federal ban on 19 assault weapons.

289.text: GUN-2765 PRIMARY Washington Talk; Day's Work in House Takes Week in Senate The contrast between the Senate and the House of Representatives has rarely been so stark. On Thursday, the House had two and a half hours of crisp debate on whether to ban semiautomatic assault weapons. Almost every seat was filled. Most representatives spoke for a minute or two. No one spoke for more than five. Then, before 5 P.M., they voted. The excitement built as the running tally was registered on the electronic scoreboard overhead. After 15 minutes, the opponents of the ban were ahead, 214 to 213. Then a Congressman switched, and the proponents were ahead. Two more voted yes. One voted no. And that was it. The bill was approved by the narrowest of margins, 216 to 214, and the House went on to other matters. In the Senate, meanwhile, the business was a bill that would prohibit members of Congress from accepting gifts and meals and expense-paid trips to fancy resorts. Senator Carl Levin, the Michigan Democrat who was the bill's floor manager, explained why it was needed. A recent poll that he cited time and again had asked this question: Which do you think really controls the Federal Government in Washington? These choices were the President, Congress or special interests. The responses, Senator Levin kept saying with a flourish, were these: The President, 7 percent; Congress, 22 percent; special interests, 50 percent.

288.text: GUN-4762 PRIMARY Handguns can help to save lives, too Re: Our handgun epidemic, by Richard Karl, Dec. 1. The writer asks the question, "What possible reason could there be for us  to be armed?" I suggest he watch the 6 p.m. TV news reports - any day, any  channel. Crime has become a major concern of U.S. citizens. No one is immune. Since the adoption of the concealed weapons law in Florida through 1995,  FBI Uniform Crime Reports show an amazing 27-percent drop in handgun murder  rates in Florida. A recent study by Professor John R. Lott Jr. of the  University of Chicago law school, which analyzed the FBI's crime statistics  for all 3,054 counties in the United States, shows that in the 31 states which  have adopted "shall issue" concealed weapons laws, murders have been reduced  by 8.5 percent, rapes by 5 percent, aggravated assaults by 7 percent and  robberies by 3 percent. Florida has issued more than 300,000 concealed weapons permits to date. Unquestionably, a significant number of these permit-holders are women. A gun is the only effective means of defense against a predatory male for most  women. Does the writer of this article really feel that these women are  "inadequate personalities that can only be made whole by carrying a gun"? The statement "Studies have found that household firearms are more likely  to cause the death of a family member than of intruders" assumes there has  been no self-defense unless the criminal is killed. It is this kind of faulty  logic which is promulgated by the Centers for Disease Control that the NRA  rightly objects to.

285.text: NA-44013 PRIMARY Schumer to move ahead on background checks Continued disagreements over whether to keep records of private gun sales prompted Sen. Charles E. Schumer (D-N.Y.) to move ahead Wednesday without the support of the Democrats and Republicans he's been meeting with for weeks in hopes of striking a deal to expand the national background check system, with limited exceptions. Schumer said he will reintroduce a proposal mandating background checks on all gun sales, private or commercial, on Thursday at a Senate Judiciary Committee hearing, where senators will begin debating and voting on legislation put forward in the months since the mass shooting at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. For weeks, Schumer has been locked in negotiations over a new background check bill with Sens. Tom Coburn (R-Okla.), Mark Kirk (R-Ill.) and Joe Manchin III (D-W.Va.). Early on, Schumer agreed to change his original proposal to permit limited exceptions for the transfer of firearms between family members and close friends. But aides familiar with the talks said Schumer and Coburn repeatedly locked horns over whether to keep records of private gun sales, a provision that Democrats believe is necessary to fully enforce any new background check law. Coburn and other gun rights advocates think that mandating recordkeeping could place an undue burden on gun owners or be perceived as the beginning of a national gun registry. One Schumer aide credited Coburn for negotiating "in complete and utter good faith" but added that "there is an honest difference of opinion" over the issue of recordkeeping. "As much as we wish to earn Senator Coburn's support, Senator Schumer is not prepared to negotiate away the recordkeeping requirement in its entirety, lest it make the law unenforceable," said the aide, who was not authorized to publicly discuss the talks and so spoke on the condition of anonymity.

287.text: GUN-658 PRIMARY ID CARD AMONG GUN-CONTROL OPTIONS WASHINGTON - Compelled by Congress to craft a nationwide plan to try to keep guns out of the hands of felons, a Justice Department task force published on Tuesday a 40-page option package that includes an idea vehemently opposed by the gun lobby: a national gun owners' registration card.   The options study, published in a 40-page chunk of the Federal Register distributed Tuesday, outlines dozens of variations on two major approaches.   One would target potential gun owners by requiring some form of pre-sale certification that they have no criminal record. The other would target the nation's 270,000 gun dealers, requiring them to check with police agencies that would be equipped to search criminal records quickly.   Besides a gun owner's card, the variations include one of the broadest citizen-identification proposals possible: a requirement that all Americans, not just potential gun owners, be issued a ''smart card,'' a standard identification card, such as a drivers license, that would be encoded with electronically imprinted identification information, including fingerprints.   Such a nationwide identification scheme has been long opposed by virtually every civil liberties group in the nation as a violation of privacy.   Under the proposal, listed as Option B-2, the identification card would be updated with any information that disqualified a person from buying a gun. Gun dealers would run a prospective customer's card through a device to ''read'' the information.   The Justice Department task force insisted in its report that it was trying to be ''assiduously neutral'' and was making no recommendations. The task force noted that it was ''keenly aware'' of public concerns about protecting privacy, as well as concerns about protection from criminals.   The options' costs range up to $10 billion a year for systems that would amass the scattered, incomplete data in state and federal criminal records to make it quickly available when one of the 7.5 million annual gun purchases is about to occur. Other options included are telephone checks of records or live scans of fingerprints by gun dealers.   The options are a result of an effort by Rep. Bill McCollum, R-Fla., last year to head off legislation that would have required a seven-day waiting period before a pistol could be purchased. Foes of the waiting period, led by the National Rifle Association, were widely charged with trying to delay gun controls by requiring the Justice Department to produce a list of options.   James Baker, chief lobbyist for the NRA, said his group has always opposed and continues to oppo se any national registration scheme, viewing it as the first step toward gun confiscation. Baker, who had argued that a waiting period would be too expensive, too difficult to implement and ineffective, said Monday night that the NRA considered a variation of the national identification card ''the most attractive'' option at first reading.

290.text: NA-42285 PRIMARY March 11 letters to the editor Colonial America and the militia In a recent letter in the Times by Gabby Talkington, the writer expressed amusement at some interpretations of the Second Amendment. But his own letter is filled with more than a little high comedy. He artfully quoted the Second Amendment: "The right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed." However, he left out the part that refutes his argument. The Second Amendment is quoted as follows: "A well regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free state, the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed." Like most Second Amendment absolutists, he leaves out the part that, indeed, states that gun ownership may be regulated. The militia was a big deal in Colonial America. It did not mean a bunch of guys who shoot on the weekends. It meant an organized paid military unit that provided a badge of office. By banning militias, the British struck a blow at an organ of local government and the right of self-government itself. Despite the myth, our freedoms were not won by the Minutemen. They were earned by the professionals trained by Baron von Steuben and the Marquis de Lafayette. Harold Franklin Richmond Medicare for all would be best Soon many Americans will be shopping on the newly created health care exchanges.

286.text: NA-45930 PRIMARY Senate appears ready to move on guns; New background check deal brings chamber closer washington For the first time in years, gun-control advocates may have some legislative victories over the next few days as the Senate cleared key hurdles to getting a vote on a broad package of new gun rules. They  say it is still a long way from becoming law. Nearly four months after the tragedy at Sandy Hook Elementary and two decades since President  Clinton signed the last major gun-control law, the Senate is poised to take up a gun bill that would expand background checks, beef up a national criminal database and crack down on straw purchases of firearms. The Senate first has to clear a 60-vote threshold to end a threatened filibuster by conservative Republicans, but that seemed more likely Wednesday after a bipartisan deal to expand background checks for most commercial gun sales. President Obama said the background check deal brokered by Sens. Joe Manchin, D-W.Va., and Pat Toomey, R-Pa., represents "welcome and significant bipartisan progress." The Manchin-Toomey amendment is more friendly to gun buyers than Obama's proposal. It would close loopholes for gun shows and Internet purchases but exempt most private transactions. In some areas, it would expand gun rights, allowing for easier transport of guns across state lines and accepting a state concealed-carry permit in lieu of a background check. "I don't consider criminal background checks to be gun control. It's just common sense," Toomey said. "If you pass  you get to buy a gun. It's the people who fail that we don't want having guns."

292.text: GUN-1418 PRIMARY In Connecticut, Other Issues Help Foes of Gun Control As in New Jersey, the National Rifle Association maintains a political presence in the state capitals of New York and Connecticut. In New York, through its legislative fund, the N.R.A. Political Victory Fund, the association contributed more than $100,000 to legislators and party campaign committees between Jan. 12, 1989 and Nov. 28, 1991, according to State Board of Elections records. From 1986 to 1991, more than 120 members of the State Legislature received N.R.A. contributions. Using a sophisticated computer network and effective money-raising methods, the association has helped to stave off most gun-control legislation in New York in the last few years.   The most obvious example of the N.R.A.'s success has been the Legislature's refusal, year after year, to enact a ban on assault weapons, as Gov. Mario M. Cuomo would like. In Connecticut, the N.R.A. has been bolstered by other political factors, including many lawmakers' dislike for Gov. Lowell P. Weicker Jr., a gun-control proponent, in its efforts to block stricter gun laws. In June, when Governor Weicker called a special session of the General Assembly to consider new gun laws, the association sent a member of its Washington staff to bolster efforts of local sportsmen's groups. The session ultimately passed several measures, including broadening the two-week waiting period for handgun purchases to cover many purchases of rifles and shotguns as well, but it failed to pass many of Mr. Weicker's proposals. Lawmakers, however, were quick to say it was some members' annoyance at the tight scheduling of the session, their failure to rally behind one proposal and their reluctance to back Mr. Weicker -- not just lobbying by the N.R.A. -- that drove many to vote no.

294.text: GUN-30643 PRIMARY A look at Thursday's Supreme Court ruling on guns A LOOK AT THURSDAY'S RULING . . . For the first time, the Supreme Court ruled that there is an individual right under the Second Amendment to possess a firearm unrelated to service in a militia, and to use a firearm for a lawful purpose such as self-defense in the home. The 5-4 decision made clear that the Second Amendment right is "not unlimited," citing gun regulations that have been permitted by the courts. The ruling applied to the District of Columbia, but did not address whether the Second Amendment right would undercut state and local gun regulations across the country. That issue will now fuel other lawsuits, including one expected to be filed today in San Francisco opposing gun laws in that city. . . . AND HOW IT AFFECTS CALIFORNIA A look at some of California's gun control regulations that could be challenged: A ban on semiautomatic assault weapons with military features Limits on the ability to carry concealed weapons Safety requirements on handguns, including a state-certified locking device and child safety locks Restrictions on the sale of "junk" handguns, or Saturday night specials Restrictions on the sale of large-capacity, rapid-fire ammunition

295.text: GUN-1813 PRIMARY Observer; Sass With Bile In recent weeks this column has spoken harshly of the National Rifle Association and sympathetically of Senator Edward Kennedy. These pieces have produced a high volume of mail that is interesting for its earnest and sometimes vituperative passion. One knows that barrels of angry letters will be the punishment for derogatory remarks about the N.R.A. Intemperate mail from supporters of the gun lobby is so inevitable that I rarely beat this drum. This is not because I can't take the heat, but because the issue is so hopeless. Guns are out of control in this country, wanton murder is destined to be our lot for decades to come and, like it or not, there isn't much anybody can do about it. Still, a man ought to cry out once in awhile about life's nastiness, no matter how futile the cry may be. When the N.R.A. is criticized the response time of letter writers seems almost as fast as Wyatt Earp's draw. As if bugled to battle by a central alert system, excited defenders of the right to bear arms, no matter how destructive, instantly dip pens into poison, and it's Katy bar the door. This time, however, a lot of the letters were notably civil. Some were even persuasive. Maybe the gun lobby's recent small political defeats have not only had a wholesome effect on manners, but also persuaded members that sound argument does more for any cause than billingsgate can.

291.text: NA-42573 PRIMARY The Bid to Stop Gun Trafficking Instructed by President Obama to find ways to curb gun violence after the Connecticut school massacre, a working group led by Vice President Joseph Biden Jr. appears ready to recommend a package of proposals that go beyond reinstating the expired ban on assault weapons and high-capacity magazines. Mr. Obama has already expressed support for a bill being prepared by Senator Dianne Feinstein, Democrat of California, that would provide a more effective ban on military-style assault weapons than the law that lapsed in 2004. The Biden task force, according to a report in The Washington Post, is considering a broader range of other measures backed by key law enforcement leaders and by Mayors Against Illegal Guns, the group started by Mayor Michael Bloomberg of New York. These measures include making background checks universal for all gun buyers, creating a national database to track the movement and sale of firearms, expanding mental health checks, and increasing penalties for carrying guns near schools or giving guns to minors. Even this sensible list could be enlarged and improved. Notably missing is a concerted crackdown on illegal gun trafficking. The task force's final recommendations, which are due to be released by the end of the month, should include a measure to stem the illegal gun trade and make it easier for law enforcement agencies to investigate and prosecute gun traffickers and the straw buyers and rogue dealers who enable them.

293.text: GUN-5646 PRIMARY EQUAL TIME FOR VIOLENCE SPEAKERS FINAL FORUM TONIGHT IN SUNNYVALE Organizers of the last of four community forums sponsored by the Santa Clara County Violence Prevention Council say the rules will be the same for all speakers. "It'll be an open meeting," said Danielle Kelly, an aide to county Supervisor Pete McHugh, host of tonight's meeting in Sunnyvale. "If there's any limit on comment, it'll be the same for everybody." Such a limit would be determined by moderator Andre Burnett, Kelly said. Burnett, an outreach coordinator with Social Advocates for Youth, also will be a speaker. The community forum two weeks ago resulted in a lawsuit being filed against Santa Clara County Supervisor Blanca Alvarado, a gun control proponent, by the National Rifle Association. The rifle group claimed that its members were denied the same length of time to comment that other audience members were given. One legal expert predicted the lawsuit would be dismissed because the forum was a political event, not an official proceeding. The lawsuit came as Santa Clara County supervisors are preparing to vote Oct. 27 on a controversial series of gun control measures proposed by the violence prevention council. Among them are a proposed sales tax on guns and ammunition and a ban on residential gun sales by "kitchen table" dealers. The council was launched by the board of supervisors and the public health department to create a county action plan to cut violence. The forums are aimed at getting community input, an essential ingredient in an action plan.

characters: Santa Clara County Violence Prevention Council

297.text: GUN-2684 PRIMARY Council OKs fines for guns Meeting on concealed weapons packed The Denver City Council passed 8-1 an ordinance adding mandatory fines for people convicted of carrying a concealed handgun and requiring any sentence for such a conviction to be served consecutive to any other sentence, rather than concurrently. Before a packed, basted and baking crowd of gun advocates and residents concerned about crime on their streets, the council conducted a public hearing on a bill introduced by new councilman Ed Thomas, a retired Denver policeman, that fell far short of what many of them wanted to debate. The non-air-conditioned council chamber was filled with hot air before anyone started speaking, and the crowd of nearly 100 sweated out the hour-long hearing. Representatives from the Firearms Coalition of Colorado and the Libertarian Party, and others, made impassioned pleas against the law banning the carrying of concealed weapons, and Council President Dave Doering interrupted more than one speaker to remind them that the concealed weapon law wasn't under consideration - it was already on the books. The hearing was for an ordinance to amend that law to make mandatory fines of $ 500 for first offenders, $ 750 for second offenders and $ 999 for any subsequent offenses for anyone convicted of carrying a concealed weapon, if that weapon of a firearm of explosive device. It also requires any jail term imposed for such a conviction be served in addition to, rather than along with, any other jail term imposed. That means if someone is convicted of multiple crimes, one of them being carrying a concealed handgun, the sentence for the latter must be served consecutively with any other sentence, not concurrently.

299.text: GUN-7256 PRIMARY UNDER LEGAL SIEGE, GUN MAKER AGREES TO ACCEPT CURBS Smith & Wesson, the nation's oldest and largest manufacturer of handguns, agreed today to accept a wide array of restrictions on the way it makes, sells and distributes hundreds of thousands of handguns each year in exchange for ending some lawsuits that had threatened to bankrupt it. The agreement represented a major splintering in the powerful alliance of gun makers and owners that has thwarted tough gun laws in Washington and state capitals for years. And it opened a new avenue for regulating the firearms industry without action from Congress, where partisan gridlock has stalled even modest gun-control legislation in recent months. Under the agreement, which was immediately criticized by other gun manufacturers, Smith & Wesson will place a second, hidden set of serial numbers in all its new guns to make it harder for criminals to scratch away those identifying marks. The company promises to sell with each new handgun a small lock that prevents the trigger from being pulled, something Smith & Wesson was already doing. And the agreement requires, within three years, "smart-gun technology" that will allow each of its new handguns to be fired only by authorized users. But the broader impact of the agreement will depend heavily on whether other major manufacturers follow Smith & Wesson, which experts say produces about one-fifth of the 2.5 million handguns sold nationwide each year. Today, the president of the largest trade association for gun makers issued a statement denouncing Smith & Wesson for breaking ranks.

298.text: NA-40525 PRIMARY Nation briefs Gun background checks in sight Two influential senators, one from each party, are working on an agreement that could expand background checks on firearms sales to include gun shows and online transactions, Senate aides said Sunday. If completed, the effort could represent a major breakthrough in the effort by President Barack Obama and his allies to restrict guns following last December's massacre of schoolchildren in Newtown, Conn. Sens. Joe Manchin, D-W.Va., and Pat Toomey, R-Pa., could nail down an accord early this week, said the aides, who spoke on condition of anonymity to describe the private talks. With the Senate returning today from a two-week recess, the chamber's debate on gun control legislation could begin as soon as Tuesday, though it might be delayed if the lawmakers need more time to complete a deal, the aides said. The potential deal, which aides cautioned still might change, would exempt transactions between relatives and temporary transfers for hunters and sportsmen, they said. Fracking rules plan angers both sides o A new plan to strengthen standards for fracking is creating divisions among environmentalists and supporters of the oil and gas industry. Some environmentalists say the plan is too soft and meaningless, while some in the industry complain it goes too far. Others say the Pittsburgh-based Center for Sustainable Shale Development threatens extremists on both sides of the fracking debate.

301.text: GUN-6466 PRIMARY Gore Unveils Crime-Fighting Plan, From Right and Left Vice President Al Gore outlined his plan for fighting crime today, essentially delivering two very different speeches in one. On the one hand, he called for gun control measures that go beyond anything President Clinton has proposed, requiring photo licenses for all new handgun owners and banning cheap handguns. But he also sounded a number of conservative anticrime themes, saying he would support longer sentences for all crimes committed with guns and for those who commit crimes in front of children. He pledged to work with states that want to stop plea bargaining on crimes committed with guns. A program he calls "Stay Clean to Stay Out" would send parolees who fail mandatory drug tests back to jail. In an address at police headquarters, surrounded by uniformed officers who made the Vice President look unusually loose, Mr. Gore also pledged to push for a Federal law establishing "gang-free zones" with curfews on individual gang members and a ban on "gang-related clothing." After giving Mr. Gore a Fenway Park T-shirt for his newborn grandson, Mayor Thomas M. Menino called the Vice President a "visionary" and a "friend of American cities" who with the President had helped push crime in Boston to its lowest rate since 1971. Recalling a speech Mr. Gore made at a St. Patrick's Day event two years ago, Mr. Menino, a Democrat, added, "They say he's wooden! Huh! I wish I was as wooden as he was that morning!"

300.text: NA-44102 PRIMARY Everything you need to know about the Senate's gun bills; A review of the proposals under consideration -- and their prospects for final passage. The Senate Judiciary Committee begins the process Thursday of debating four proposals to limit gun violence, the first formal consideration of legislation introduced in the wake of the mass shooting in December at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. The committee is split between 10 Democrats and eight Republicans -- meaning the Democrats should be able to approve most of the legislation on a party-line vote. 1.) Assault Weapons Ban of 2013: Lead sponsor: Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.). Senate aides privately call this bill "a liberal's dream come true," and the proposal's ban of almost 60 specific military-style weapons and related parts is considered a bridge too far for moderate Democrats -- many of whom face reelection next year -- and all Senate Republicans. The proposed ban goes much farther than the federal ban on assault weapons that expired in 2004. Feinstein's bill, which has 21 Democratic cosponsors, would ban the sale, transfer, manufacturing or importation of more than 150 specific firearms, including semiautomatic rifles or pistols that can be used with detachable or fixed ammunition magazines that hold more than 10 rounds and have specific military-style features, including pistol grips, grenade launchers or rocket launchers. It excludes more than 2,250 firearms used for hunting or other sport, but would require background checks for the sale or transfer of grandfathered weapons and would bar the sale or transfer of large-capacity feeding devices owned before the bill's enactment. Current assault weapon owners also would need to safely store their firearms. Unlike the original federal ban passed in 1994, the new ban would be permanent.

302.text: GUN-5056 PRIMARY Carolyn McCarthy's Story ; Laurie Metcalf Plays the Congresswoman In "The Long Island Incident" (Sunday at 9 on NBC), Laurie Metcalf plays Carolyn McCarthy, whose life was forever changed when a disturbed man boarded a commuter train at New York's Penn Station and opened fire, killing six, including her husband, and wounding 19, among them, her 26-year-old son Kevin. Metcalf, best known for her Emmy-winning portrayal of wound-up Jackie on "Roseanne," met McCarthy halfway through filming. "Carolyn and Kevin came to the set," she said. "I felt like I had already met her." Metcalf had seen McCarthy interviewed and watched home movies of Kevin's recovery. "She was a nurse before all this happened so she put a lot of this on video to help him see the progress he made," said Metcalf. She said the movie should assist McCarthy's ultimate goal: "She wants to get her message about gun control out to more people." Two years after the shooting, McCarthy learned that the U.S. representative from her district was voting to repeal major gun control legislation. She decided to run for Congress on a platform that included a ban on assault weapons, and was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives.

296.text: GUN-10824 PRIMARY Expand concealed carry rights Thanks Paul Valone, for your concealed firearms column. Gun rights legislation always brings letters predicting gun violence off the chart, as Valone referred to. Few, if any, of these incidents have occurred. Permit holders have shown responsibility since 1995. Restaurant robberies have occurred here. Domino's manager Matt Howerton was killed in a robbery. Fast food restaurants have been robbed, one of them not far from my house. Opponents worry about armed citizens drinking, which is already disallowed, no change. Can anyone recall a permit holder caught drinking? It would have been headline news if so. People have also been robbed in parking lots. A woman was murdered in Duke Park about the time I moved here. I recall concerns of drug dealers hanging out in parks. Bike riders have been assaulted on the ATT bike trail. A human body was found in Eno River Park. What is our latest count of parole absconders? Remember the Carson, Mahato and King murders? DAVID RUSSELL Durham Rockwood flood I read with great interest the article about flooding at the Rockwood Building on University Drive after Friday's rain. I had a shop there (the only one in the building below ground level) from 1986-1997. Numerous times I had to rush over there during the night to get fixtures with merchandise off the floor before they were ruined. When Hurricane Fran hit us, the water was two feet deep in my store but everyone else had minimal damage.

characters: victims of crime (e.g. Carson, Mahato, King)

308.text: NA-44154 PRIMARY Who's voting for the Manchin-Toomey amendment? (UPDATED); Supporters of a bipartisan background check plan appear on the verge of earning enough support. Updated 5:55 p.m.                       Tuesday: Sufficient support to ensure final passage of a bipartisan plan to extend the gun background check program to include most commercial sales is at risk, based on the latest vote count. The amendment will require at least 60 votes to be added to the underlying gun bill. Based on analysis by The Washington Post, only 22 of the 100 Senate votes are in play: The 16 Republican senators who voted last week to proceed with debate and six moderate Democrats who face difficult reelections in 2014 or represent rural states with strong gun cultures and would face strong political pressure at home for supporting new gun-control legislation.
