461.text: GUN-781 PRIMARY VIRGINIA TO BEEF UP LAW ON GUN SALES; BILL REQUIRES ID CHECKS ON ASSAULT RIFLE BUYERS Legislators who passed the nation's first law requiring instant background checks on people wanting to buy certain guns say they hope to end Virginia's reputation as a supplier of firearms to drug dealers. The deadline for signing the bill is midnight today; Gov. Gerald Baliles has said he supports the measure and is expected to sign it. It would take effect Nov. 1. Debate on the bill saw weapons drawn in the halls of the General Assembly. The sponsor of the measure took a Soviet-designed AK-47 assault rifle to a Senate committee hearing, saying he wanted to show that dangerous guns can be sold to anyone who has the money. The measure awaiting Baliles' signature is designed to keep assault rifles and handguns with barrels 5 inches long or less out of the hands of convicted and wanted felons. It sets up instant background checks on resident purchasers; non-residents may have to wait up to a week while the checks are made before they can take home a gun. The system will be similar to that used by merchants checking the validity of a credit card. Gun dealers, after checking a purchaser's identification, will call a toll-free number operated by the State Police. In minutes, an officer at a State Police computer terminal will determine if the purchaser has a criminal record. Meanwhile, in Arlington, Va., the International Association of Chiefs of

characters: International Association of Chiefs of Police

464.text: NA-42598 PRIMARY Sweeping Limits on Guns Become Law in New York ALBANY -- Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo signed into law a sweeping package of gun-control measures on Tuesday, significantly expanding a ban on assault weapons and making New York the first state to change its laws in response to the mass shooting at a Connecticut elementary school. Mr. Cuomo signed the bill less than an hour after the State Assembly approved it by a 104-to-43 vote on the second full day of the 2013 legislative session. The State Senate, which had in the past resisted more restrictive gun laws, approved the measure 43 to 18 on Monday night. ''I am proud to be part of this government, not just because New York has the first bill, but because New York has the best bill,'' the governor, a Democrat, said at a news conference. '' I'm proud to be a New Yorker because New York is doing something -- because we are fighting back.'' The expanded ban on assault weapons broadens the definition of what is considered an assault weapon and reduces the permissible size of gun magazines to 7 rounds, from 10. It also includes provisions to better keep firearms away from mentally ill people and to impose stiffer penalties on people who use guns in the commission of crimes. Gun-rights advocates denounced the measure. The New York State Rifle and Pistol Association said New York gun owners ''should be ashamed and afraid of our state,'' and the National Rifle Association said, ''These gun-control schemes have failed in the past and will have no impact on public safety and crime.''

characters: New York State Rifle and Pistol Association

459.text: GUN-214 PRIMARY POLICE GROUPS REVERSE STAND AND BACK CONTROLS ON PISTOLS In a sharp departure from the past, the nation's major police associations are fighting for controls on pistols, adding a new element to the long, bitter struggle over regulation of firearms. The police lobby has been active in recent months at both the national and state levels and is currently pitted against the National Rifle Association in opposition to a bill before Congress to reduce Federal regulation of interstate gun sales. In the past, the nation's law-enforcement officers remained mostly in the background on issues related to gun control. While some big-city police chiefs testified for more controls, much of the rank and file, according to all accounts, embraced many of the goals of the rifle association, the chief lobby opposing controls. Police influence on legislation was therefore muted. An example of the change is provided by the Fraternal Order of Police, which says it has 170,000 members in 43 states. Its national president, Richard A. Boyd of Oklahoma City, said that a few years ago the union supported the National Rifle Association in its efforts to roll back controls contained in the Federal Gun Control Act of 1968. Dispute Over Bullets Now, however, the police group is actively opposing those efforts, in a coalition with the National Sheriffs Association, the International Association of Chiefs of Police, the National Organization of Black Law Enforcement Executives, the National Troopers Association and two research and policy groups, the Police Executive Research Forum and the Police Foundation.

characters: International Association of Chiefs of Police
National Organization of Black Law Enforcement Executives

469.text: GUN-4415 PRIMARY MAKING IT WORK; Guns on the Run "SOME guys like to bowl on Tuesday nights," says Donald Passantino, lining up the sights of his .22-caliber pistol as if it were a 9-iron. "We shoot guns. It's a real family thing to do and the kids enjoy it." Mr. Passantino, 47, is a retired marine mechanic from Flushing and the president of the Stuyvesant Rod and Gun Club in Middle Village, Queens, one of the few remaining gun clubs and target ranges in the city and a bastion of Constitutional scholarship. The 170-member club is located behind a small, white door on Dry Harbor Road down a flight stairs and in a basement that smells of stale gunpowder, mildew and old coffee grounds. In the anteroom is a panel desk, a lectern where speeches are given about proper hunting procedures and the latest laws concerning the gunner's Second Amendment rights. The walls are lined with plaques of appreciation from various firearms groups, and a framed copy of Article II of the Bill of Rights is displayed prominently: "A well regulated militia being necessary to the security of a free state, the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed." Al Buro, a 42-year-old disabled and out-of-work security guard from Woodhaven, can recite it like the Apostles' Creed. "The founding fathers drafted the Second Amendment so an abusive and tyrannical government could not impose their will on people," said Mr. Buro, a man who wears a National Rifle Association cap, a beard and smokes strong cigarettes. "But, somehow, anti-gunners have made us out to be the criminals. It's like being a smoker."

466.text: NA-41077 PRIMARY Handful of Dems could nix new law DEMOCRATS desperately trying to pass an assault weapons ban in the Senate have come up against an unlikely roadblock - members of their own party. At least six of the 55 Democratic senators are wary of the ban or have simply shot it down, which means the proposal would be defeated unless the legislators - all from rural states - have a change of heart. Max Baucus and Jon Tester of Montana, Mark Begich of Alaska, Heidi Heitkamp of North Dakota and Joe Manchin of West Virginia all oppose the ban. Maine Sen. Angus King, an independent who usually leans left, is another naysayer. "I don't believe that we need to pile on new laws and suddenly that solves all the problems," Begich said this month. Democrats need a 51-vote majority to pass the measure, and 60 votes to break a Republican filibuster. Vice President Biden, who has been fervently pushing gun control in the wake of the Newtown, Conn., massacre, said it will take "persuasion and information" to garner enough support to implement the White House's sweeping package outlawing military-style weapons. "We have an obligation to act - not wait," Biden told reporters after a private round-table discussion Friday. The day before Biden's statements, Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) unveiled bold new legislation banning sales of assault weapons. Some of the lawmakers skeptical of the gun ban appear onboard with other parts of the proposal, such as forbidding high-capacity magazines and requiring more thorough background checks.

467.text: GUN-6746 PRIMARY The gun pipeline In Colorado and 46 other states, it is legal to sell an unlimited number of handguns in a single transaction to a customer who passes a background check. Some of the guns are collectibles, others are gifts, but many of them end up in the hands of criminals. In a typical week, American firearms dealers sell 4,000  handguns to people buying at least two - and sometimes dozens - at  once. Some customers are collectors investing in rare weapons they  hope will increase in value. Some are buying gifts for Christmas,  the peak season for multiple-handgun purchases. But others who buy handguns by the handful from licensed  dealers provide what law enforcement experts call a persistent  source of 'crime' guns in the United States. They shop for cheap,  semiautomatic pistols that can be resold on the streets for  profit, either in cities and states where handgun permits are hard to get, to juveniles or to people who wish to avoid a criminal  background check. In Colorado, The Denver Post identified nearly 100 handguns  seized by law enforcement agencies that could be traced to adults  who made multiple purchases in the Denver and Colorado Springs  areas alone. The guns were used in homicides and armed robberies,  the shooting of a federal agent and the near-fatal beating of a  retired Lakewood teacher. Dozens were taken from juveniles, one  from a 13-year-old cocaine dealer. In Illinois, the white supremacist who shot 11 people during  a three-day rampage in July was armed by an illegal gun dealer who  bought 65 cheap handguns from one store. In California, more than three dozen handguns used in  homicides and other crimes were traced to four people who bought  253 guns from a suburban gun shop and supplied them to Los Angeles  gang members.

468.text: NA-40008 PRIMARY GUN-RIGHTS GROUP SAYS BILL WOULD CRIMINALIZE LEAVING GUN AT HOME WITH SOMEONE ELSE FOR MORE THAN A WEEK Says a gun bill before the Senate would make it a federal felony to "leave town for more than seven days, and leave someone else at home with your firearms." Arizona Citizens Defense League on Wednesday, April 10th, 2013 in an email to supporters * * * THE RULING: HALF TRUE As gun legislation works its way through the U.S. Senate, gun rights groups warn that some proposals in the original bill could create accidental felons. One such email warning, from an Arizona group, says, "You would be committing a federal felony if you leave town for more than seven days, and leave someone else at home with your firearms." Could hosting a house-sitter become a crime? Could your unsuspecting roommates face a felony? The bill, known as the Safe Communities, Safe Schools Act of 2013, faces votes on a series of amendments, most prominently one proposed by Sens. Joe Manchin, D-W.Va., and Pat Toomey, R-Pa. So all bets are off on the bill's final language. Still, as the debate unfolds, we were curious: Does Senate Bill 649 make felons of vacationing gun owners with roommates or house guests? Gun transfers As introduced in the Senate in March, S. 649 requires background checks for all gun sales, with a limited set of exceptions. And it's not just sales that are subject to the law - but gun "transfers." In general, it would be illegal to give your gun to someone else without seeking a federal background check.

465.text: GUN-1019 PRIMARY Brady Bill scores big win; House gives an easy OK to 7-day wait for guns WASHINGTON - In a stunning victory Wednesday night for gun control advocates and a defeat for opponents led by the National Rifle Association, the House approved 239-186 the Brady Bill to establish a seven-day national waiting period for handgun purchases. After intensive lobbying and personal appeals by James and Sarah Brady, the House preserved the waiting-period measure from an early death as the NRA sought to replace it with an alternative proposal. Just prior to the Brady Bill vote, the House voted 234-193 against the NRA-backed substitute, which called for a national system of instant computer criminal background checks for handgun buyers. The margin of victory surprised both cheering supporters and glum opponents of the Brady Bill. After months of fierce lobbying, both sides had predicted a closer vote. The Georgia delegation split evenly. Voting for the measure were Democrats George "Buddy" Darden, Charles Hatcher, Ben Jones, John Lewis and Roy Rowland. Voting against were Democrats Doug Barnard, Ed Jenkins, Richard Ray and Lindsay Thomas, and Republican Newt Gingrich. Mr. Brady, a former White House press secretary who was permanently disabled in the assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan 10 years ago, cheered from the gallery as the measure was approved. The last time the House considered the bill, in 1988, it was defeated 228-182. Some lawmakers attributed the outcome to public impatience with violent crime, heavy-handed tactics by the 2.6 million-member NRA, and the powerful emotional appeal of Mr. Brady and his wife, Sarah.

472.text: GUN-8759 PRIMARY No Retreat in the War on Guns The Post reports [front page, March 28] that "the modern gun control movement is in retreat." I've been involved with gun control since a day 26 years ago when one of my parishioners was gunned down by a kid whose buddy gave him a gun so he could "go out and get some money." I've thought about retreating, but God and my conscience won't let me. I've got to stay on the front lines: * Informing people that 30,000 Americans die by gun violence every year. * Weeping with those who have been devastated by gunfire. * Trying to convince Virginia legislators that no one should be able to carry guns onto school property, into recreation centers or bars. * Hastening the day when Americans realize they have been deceived by a mammoth campaign of disinformation from the gun lobby. * Praying for an outbreak of common sense in the House and Senate. Our country started counting gun assaults and deaths in 1933. Since then, more than 1.4 million children, women and men have died from gunfire -- more than double all battlefield deaths in all our wars since 1775. Poor parenting and glorified violence in the media bring terrible consequences, but children of other nations watch the same programs, play the same video games and also suffer from poor parenting. Our kids are 12 times more likely to die from a firearm than children in 25 other industrialized nations combined. Easy access to guns must be seen as part of the reason.

471.text: GUN-5584 PRIMARY NRA CALLS ON HESTON TO FIGHT AMENDMENT REGULATING GUN SHOWS Moses championed the Ten Commandments, but the 13 revisions are another story. In a statewide campaign that kicks off today on some West Palm Beach radio stations, actor Charlton Heston refers to his movie role as Moses to argue against a change to the Florida Constitution that would allow counties to regulate gun shows. Other National Rifle Association ads question the need for all 13 groups of revisions on next Tuesday's ballot. ''As Moses, I led my people to the promised land, but that was all in the movies,'' Heston, the NRA president, says in a 60-second spot. '' I'm Charlton Heston. I'd like to talk to you about real life and real crimes.'' Responded Miami-Dade State Attorney Katherine Fernandez Rundle, after hearing the script: ''He sounds less like Moses, who stood for the truth, and more like the character from Planet of the Apes. He's from outer space.'' The NRA radio spot is just one of many advertising campaigns hitting their peak with one week until Election Day. Only in Florida are voters presented with a proposed overhaul of their state constitution every 20 years. In 1978, voters rejected every amendment. But this year an unprecedented amount of money has gone into advertising for and against the four proposed amendments and nine proposed constitutional revisions. Critics slam 'Willie Horton' ad Florida's 67 counties, for example, have raised nearly $ 4 million, including $ 265,034 from Palm Beach County, to argue for a change that requires the state to pay more for its own court system.

470.text: GUN-5315 PRIMARY MOVE TO JAIL OFFICIALS WHO SUE GUN FIRMS GETS FIRST APPROVAL The gun lobby's attempt to throw city and county officials who dare to sue them in jail passed its first legislative test Monday, clearing a House committee 3-2. The measure would make Miami-Dade Mayor Alex Penelas and any county employee who helps him sue gun makers a felon who could receive as much as five years in prison. '' That hits pretty close to home,'' said Jess McCarty, an assistant county attorney working on the lawsuit for Miami-Dade. McCarty asked the House Government Operations Committee to defeat the bill but, failing that, to at least remove the provision creating a third-degree felony. '' Obviously if the legislature passes it, we'll stop with the lawsuit,'' he assured committee members. National Rifle Association lobbyist Marion Hammer cited a similar law passed and signed into law by the Georgia legislature and governor. he mayor of Atlanta defied it and said he would continue his lawsuit. ''This is exactly why you need penalties. Otherwise the law is meaningless,'' Hammer said. The Miami-Dade lawsuit, similar to a handful of others around the country, seeks compensation from gun manufacturers for the costs the county has incurred treating the victims of gun violence. Hammer - whose group drafted the bill at the request of Rep. George Albright, R-Ocala - called the suits ''frivolous'' and ''perverse,'' designed only to take money from legitimate businesses. '' These lawsuits are nothing more than extortion,'' she said.

475.text: GUN-31316 PRIMARY The Numbing Toll of So Many Guns The phrase "sensible gun laws" ["13 Dead in New York," editorial,  April 4] contains an oxymoron. There is nothing sensible about guns. The Post was correct in saying that "guns amplify the damage that can be done." Guns are plentiful in the United States, and as a result, mass killings occur at an alarming rate. People are becoming immune to news of this senseless carnage, which in many cases does not even rate a front-page story. The people behind these heinous crimes may be insane or possibly just plain evil. But the fact remains that  if guns were not available to such unstable individuals, we would have fewer of these types of tragedies. We need to stop drinking the National Rifle Association's Kool-Aid and start thinking for ourselves. It is pretty straightforward: More guns mean more violence, and fewer guns mean less violence. Forget sensible gun control legislation, which is code for no meaningful restrictions at all. Enough already; let's get these guns off the streets. Serious gun control measures will not eliminate the types of tragedies that took place in New York last week, but they will reduce them. It is criminal not to try. NATALIE ROY Arlington

474.text: GUN-8780 PRIMARY Court: Citizens have no right to own guns; FEDERAL PANEL IN S.F. ADDRESSES 2ND AMENDMENT IN UPHOLDING STATE BAN ON ASSAULT WEAPONS A federal appeals court in San Francisco on Thursday found that individual citizens do not have a constitutional right to own a firearm, cutting to the core of the modern debate over gun control and repudiating the gun lobby's most cherished legal argument. Weighing California's attempt to regulate rapid-fire assault weapons against a legal right established in the days of musket fire, the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals jumped into the increasingly heated legal debate over the true intent of the Second Amendment's protection of a right to bear arms. The opinion is starkly at odds with a federal appeals court ruling in New Orleans last year that the 211-year-old amendment does give individuals the right to bear arms. For that reason, it could give the U.S. Supreme Court its first opportunity in decades to clarify what has long been an unsettled question in constitutional law. In upholding California's most recent assault weapons ban, the appeals court reached back into history to conclude that the founding fathers crafted the Second Amendment solely to enable state militias to protect themselves if the federal government grew too powerful. ''The historical record makes it equally plain that the amendment was not adopted in order to afford rights to individuals with respect to private gun ownership,'' Judge Stephen Reinhardt wrote for a three-judge panel. The 3-0 decision, which drew immediate and harsh criticism from the National Rifle Association and other gun groups, also conflicts with the Bush administration, which last year attracted widespread attention when Attorney General John Ashcroft took a public position that the Second Amendment gives individuals the right to own a gun.

473.text: GUN-5168 PRIMARY Police can't say how felon got gun license Authorities aren't sure how Anthony Joseph DiPaolo was able to obtain a  federal license to sell firearms and a state license to carry a concealed  weapon. Because he has a criminal record dating back to the early 1970s in New  York, it is illegal for DiPaolo to own a gun, officials said. But that didn't  stop the Tampa pawn shop owner from applying for the permits and stocking his  shelves with hundreds of guns for sale. Authorities stumbled onto his record during another investigation. Early Thursday, DiPaolo, 49, was arrested and charged with being a felon in possession of a firearm. "We think he is just one who slipped through the cracks," Hillsborough sheriff's Sgt. Ed Peeler said. "We hope that it is very rare." Tampa police and Hillsborough sheriff's deputies spent the day raiding DiPaolo's two businesses, City Pawn shops at 3901 E Hillsborough Ave. and 901  W Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Blvd. Officials seized 540 guns worth about $ 200,000 including pistols, rifles,  some assault-type weapons and a few grenade launchers, Peeler said. The  businesses will remain open. Detectives conducting another investigation came across DiPaolo's name  several months ago and realized he had a felony conviction, Peeler said. In  1974, DiPaolo was convicted of drug possession and sentenced to three years probation. When authorities arrested DiPaolo at 6 a.m. at his Carrollwood home at 4401 Hudson Lane, he proclaimed he had been cleared of the 1974 charges, but Peeler said that is not true.

478.text: GUN-9198 PRIMARY CARRYING A GUN MEANS SHOULDERING RESPONSIBILITIES * As courts consider the constitutionality of Missouri's concealed carry law, training courses are preparing those who will carry weapons. Terry Ganey, who covers Missouri government and politics, took a course to learn how the training will work. My uncle, Woodrow Vogt, used to carry a chrome-plated revolver in his belt while managing his corner confectionery at 8th & Ohio in East St. Louis. He carried it for protection, a deterrent against armed robberies. The memory of Uncle Woodrow with his pistol came to mind last weekend as I took an eight-hour concealed weapons training course, one of the prerequisites to getting a permit to carry a gun under Missouri's new law. When Tim Oliver, the course instructor, stuck a plastic model of a gun into his belt, it reminded me of Uncle Woodrow's carrying style. Oliver called it "the Mexican holster," and said it was not the way to carry a gun. "Unless you're a real expert gun-handler, don't do that," he advised. "Use a holster. Part of safe carry is keeping it concealed. If it's not concealed, someone else can get it from you." Thirty-two people -- 22 men and 10 women -- listened attentively. They were among the first group of Missourians who began the process of applying to county sheriffs around the state for permits to carry concealed handguns. Passage of the course is one of the requirements to get a concealed carry license, but implementation of the law has been suspended for now by a court.

477.text: NA-43595 PRIMARY Rubio: Obama 'doesn't have the guts' to admit Second Amendment opposition Make sure to sign up to receive "Afternoon Fix" every day in your e-mail inbox by 5(ish) p.m.! EARLIER ON THE FIX: Don't look now but the 2016 race is already underway The long (and sometimes turbulent) history of the NRA and the GOP President Obama has the public on his side on gun proposals Why Republicans have no incentive to compromise A cool diagram of the 113th Congress Is it fair to call them 'assault weapons'? 'The Daily Show' on an ATF leadership void (video) What the next two months will tell us about Congress WHAT YOU MIGHT HAVE MISSED: * House Republicans are weighing a plan to raise the nation's debt ceiling for just a few months, which could shift debate over the matter into March, when Congress will face other significant fiscal decisions, Rep. Paul Ryan (R-Wis.) said Thursday. * Sen. Marco Rubio (R-Fla.) said in a Wednesday night interview that Obama "doesn't have the guts" to admit he opposes the Second Amendment. "If he doesn't want the Second Amendment to be in the Constitution or if he wants to reform it, then have the guts to admit that," he said. Rubio also said he supports background checks for gun purchases, but the matters should be left to individual states. * Vice President Biden renewed the Obama administration's call for action on gun control, telling the U.S. Conference of Mayors that the issue is "more urgent and immediate" than any other facing the country. "I know we don't have absolute unanimity in this ballroom - nor do we in any ballroom - but we all know, everyone acknowledges we have to do something," Biden said.

476.text: GUN-3918 PRIMARY Safety top concern in concealed gun debate RALEIGH -- Safety was the top concern of state residents, a police chief and lawmakers debating a bill approved in a House committee Thursday that would legalize the carrying of loaded and concealed handguns. Bill backers said that criminals would fear for their own safety whenever they attacked a victim who could be armed. Opponents said more people would be killed by accident, either because of a bad shot or because children will find more idle guns. ``I find it absolutely appalling that a state in the Bible Belt would resort to mass arming of its citizens with concealed weapons. It is written that for all that take the sword shall perish with the sword,'' said the Rev. Chester Debnam Jr. of Raleigh. But Paul Milligan of Raleigh said he worries about his safety whenever his job calls him to fix heating and cooling systems in the middle of the night. He said he often is alone in unfamiliar or dangerous neighborhoods where police officers cannot guarantee his safety. ``Police have no requirement to protect the individual. None whatsoever. In addition, how can you expect the police to be there?'' Milligan said. Lawmakers agreed with Milligan in approving the bill, which now goes to the House Finance Committee, which must consider the $85 fee sheriffs would charge to investigate and process the permit to carry a concealed handgun.

480.text: GUN-5737 PRIMARY LATEST SALVO IN SHIFTING POLITICS OF GUN CONTROL LAWS END UP BEING WATERED-DOWN VERSIONS OF LEGISLATION / THAT WASN'T AMBITIOUS TO BEGIN WITH. LAST WEEK'S INITIATIVE COLLAPSED. With more than 100 squeezes of the trigger of his Chinese-made AK-47 assault rifle, a 24-year-old California drifter and drug addict named Patrick Edward Purdy set off a national movement to ban assault weapons. His attack in a schoolyard in Stockton, Calif., on Jan. 17, 1989, left five children dead and 30 others wounded. Although Purdy had a long arrest record for theft, drugs and other offenses, and had some history of mental illness, he had no difficulty buying the arsenal he used to such devastating effect. The ease with which he purchased his weapons, the innocence of his victims, and the unmitigated savagery of the attack helped fuel a gun-control movement that culminated in 1994, when President Clinton signed legislation banning the manufacture and sale of 19 specific assault guns. Dozens of other firearms that meet specific tests for lethality and ease of concealment also were banned. But the ban, like so much else in the convoluted, shifting politics of gun control, turned out to be less than meets the eye. Although Clinton said the measure would go a long way toward taking guns out of the hands of criminals, it left untouched hundreds of thousands of assault weapons that were in private hands before the ban. There is even evidence, contained in a Justice Department study released in March, that the law increased the number of assault weapons in private hands because manufacturers boosted production of targeted weapons for sale to collectors before the ban went into effect.

479.text: GUN-9471 PRIMARY Dems, police chiefs urge Owens to veto state gun measure Bill pre-empts local authority A group of police chiefs and prosecutors joined Democratic  lawmakers Sunday in urging Gov. Bill Owens to veto a bill that  would override local gun laws. Senate Bill 25 passed the state House on Friday and now awaits the  governor's signature. The bill would prohibit local governments  from making gun control laws more restrictive than the state's and  would abolish local registries of gun owners. At a press conference on the steps of the state Capitol, Rep.  Andrew Romanoff, D-Denver, said the issue is not gun control, but  local control and the rights of cities and counties to make their  own decisions. Flanked by law enforcement leaders from around the  state, he said local authorities know better than the state  government which gun laws their communities need. 'I am proud to stand with the peace officers of this state, who  risk their lives every day to protect ours,' Romanoff said. 'I  voted against Senate Bill 25 because I believe it jeopardizes these  leaders' ability to protect their communities from gun violence.' Fountain Police Chief John Morse said the bill would interfere with  successful gun laws and send the wrong message to local  authorities. Fountain was voted an all-American city last year, he said, proving  the government there can make good decisions on its own. 'Declaring things matters of statewide concern is basically  telling local governments 'You're inept to handle problems; we'll  handle them for you,'' Morse said.

482.text: GUN-4565 PRIMARY SCHUMER, NRA SWAP POTSHOTS WASHINGTON In the opening volley of a 1998 political shootout, the National Rifle Association yesterday blasted away at anti-gun Rep. Charles Schumer (D-Brooklyn) as a "headline grabber" exploiting gun issues to win a Senate seat. Schumer, who has led the gun-control fight in Congress, quickly fired back, declaring the NRA a fringe group out of touch with New York values. "I wear the NRA's scorn like a badge of honor," he said. The war of words started when NRA Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre issued a statement trashing Schumer gun-control initiatives as political PR stunts. LaPierre said Schumer, who wants the job of U.S. Sen. Alfonse D'Amato (D-N.Y.), proposes "one meaningless bill after another in a purely self-serving effort to see his name in the newspaper." Schumer, however, shot back that the NRA has opposed "all common-sense gun-control laws from banning cop-killer bullets to the assault weapon ban."

483.text: NA-43003 PRIMARY The Gun Report: September 19, 2013 How did a coffee shop become a lightning rod for gun policy? In August, Second Amendment supporters carried their firearms into a Starbucks in Newtown, Conn., for "Starbucks Appreciation Day," prompting that location to close five hours early. "We recognize there is significant passion surrounding the topic," read a sign posted on the front of the store. "However, we believe people should be sensitive to our community." The purpose of the stunt was to thank the company for allowing open-carry firearms in their stores, in accordance with local and state gun laws. But a few months prior, while waiting in line at a Starbucks in St. Petersburg, Fla, a woman accidentally shot her friend in the leg when she dropped her purse, which held a fully loaded .25-caliber semi-automatic handgun. Yesterday, Starbucks chief executive Howard Schultz attempted to diffuse the issue by writing an open letter asking customers to please leave their guns at home, unless they are authorized law enforcement personnel. The reason, he wrote, was because "we believe our store partners should not be put in the uncomfortable position of requiring customers to disarm or leave our stores." He also asked that "Starbucks Appreciation Days" come to an end. "The presence of a weapon in our stores is unsettling and upsetting for many of our customers," he wrote, which undermines the relaxing atmosphere he is trying to create.

481.text: GUN-2688 PRIMARY Guns and Family Abusers ONE IMPORTANT measure being considered by Congress as it tries to shape an anti-crime bill for final passage is a gun safety proposal that deserves solid support. Introduced last year by Sen. Wellstone and Rep. Torricelli, the proposal would prohibit ownership of firearms by individuals deemed to be domestic abusers. Both the House and the Senate have approved the measure, but the Senate version calls for greater, more effective action. It would ban ownership of guns by those convicted of abusing spouses or children, or those who have restraining orders issued against them for committing domestic violence. The House version would only prohibit possession of a gun by people who are being restrained by a court order from abusing an intimate partner. That is too narrow a measure, given the dimensions of these violence problems across the country. Domestic violence is the leading cause of injury to women in the United States between the ages of 15 and 44. As Rep. Torricelli noted last fall, this means these incidents are "more common than automobile accidents, muggings and cancer combined." Firearms are used by abusers in 7 percent of these incidents; that may not seem significant, but it comes to nearly 150,000 cases a year. Sarah Brady of Handgun Control, leading campaigner for the Brady Bill named after her and husband, Jim, cites the obvious logic of enacting the Senate-passed proposal to expand the category of people who should be banned from buying guns: "Clearly, individuals with a history of domestic abuse should not have easy access to firearms." The congressional conferees who understand this should not hesitate to vote for the Senate version.

484.text: GUN-1431 PRIMARY NATION IN BRIEF N.J. governor vows to fight legislators for weapons ban New Jersey Gov. Jim Florio says he will veto the Legislature's attempt to gut the nation's toughest ban on assault weapons. The Legislature voted Monday to make most assault weapons legal again but to increase penalties for drug crimes committed with a gun and to ban all firearms near schools. The vote was 28-9 in the Senate and 47- 19 in the Assembly. In 1990, New Jersey banned the sale or possession of semiautomatic firearms capable of holding more than 15 rounds. Most owners of such weapons had a year to sell or disable them. Mr. Florio, who has 45 days to act on the bill, said he will veto it and seek to put the issue before voters in a non-binding referendum. Mr. Florio, a Democrat elected in 1989, considered New Jersey's law one of his crowning achievements. Republicans who won control of the Legislature in 1991 have been undoing many of the laws passed during his first two years, including large tax increases. Overriding a veto requires 27 votes in the Senate and 54 in the Assembly. KANSAS: 9-year-old boy storms off in family car. A 9-year-old boy who had squabbled with his father about cleaning his room took off in the family car with a neighbor chasing him at speeds up to 70 mph, police and witnesses said. The ride ended Monday when the boy, with his 7-year-old sister in the car, slammed head-on into another car stopped at a busy intersection. Police said there were no serious injuries. On the way, the 4-foot-tall boy zoomed through traffic lights and stop signs, brushed a startled road surveyor, hit two cars and almost ran over his mother, who jumped in front of the car to try to stop him. "For a young kid, he was driving pretty good," said the surveyor, Richard Mehl.

485.text: GUN-10297 PRIMARY Monica Yant Kinney: 'A lost cause,' Rendell laments about Pa.'s gun issue In the waning days of Gov. Rendell's administration, Pennsylvania legislators tried to trap him in a no-win decision. Conservative legislators attached a controversial measure expanding citizens' right to shoot in self-defense to a no-brainer bill strengthening reporting requirements for sex offenders under Megan's Law. The officials ramrodded the odd bill to passage without debate, hoping Rendell would be more afraid of risking children's lives than his own reputation on the gun issue. Blessedly, Rendell refused, calling the messy matter both unconstitutional and unseemly in a veto message days after Thanksgiving. Pennsylvania already has a "castle doctrine" allowing residents to fire a gun without fear of arrest if attacked at home. There's no need, the governor said, to throw open the castle doors and let folks take aim at any perceived threat, anywhere. Whatever happened to walking away? In a conference call with reporters Monday, Rendell acknowledged the futility of his decision. Come January, Tom Corbett, conservative Republicans, and their NRA handlers will rule Harrisburg. Hold onto your holsters. As he lamented Pennsylvania's bloodstained past and future, I thought about the time Rendell got so emotional about gun violence that he shed tears. Since he seemed to be in a reflective mode, I asked him to sum up his eight-year quest for a safer state. "An abject failure," Rendell replied bluntly. "A lost cause." Guns for everyone! Despite the tears and tirades, Pennsylvania remains one of the easiest places in the nation for criminals to get guns, thanks to lax laws that practically encourage straw buying and trafficking.

489.text: NA-41804 PRIMARY Casey stands by his support of assault-weapons ban WASHINGTON - The Newtown, Conn., shooting and the images of its young victims were so wrenching that Pennsylvania Sen. Bob Casey had a change of heart. Long a pro-gun Democrat, Casey reversed his position in late December, saying he would support bans on assault weapons and high-capacity gun magazines. He said he knew he was opening himself to criticism by shifting his position less than two months after winning reelection. The criticism may still come - the National Rifle Association expressed its anger this week - but the two measures Casey backed appear doomed. Casey said Thursday he did not rue his decision. "I have no regrets at all. I thought a lot about it," he said in an interview. "It's frustrating when you think there is momentum behind a policy change that you favor, but you have to prepare yourself for that." Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid (D., Nev.) said this week he would not include the bans on assault weapons or high-capacity clips in the gun bill Democrats will soon bring to the floor. They don't have enough votes. But Reid said Thursday he would soon bring up a plan that includes expanded background checks - which gun-control groups have labeled their top priority - and Casey said he would support that measure. The bans on so-called assault weapons and clips holding more than 10 rounds will also be offered on the Senate floor as an amendment. Casey said he would still vote for them even if they fall short.

488.text: GUN-1793 PRIMARY Incumbent Wins Police Group's Election Fraternal Order of Police President Dewey Stokes, a strong proponent of gun control, won reelection to a two-year term at the biennial convention in Pittsburgh. Stokes received 1,681 votes to 781 cast for his opponent, Newark detective Tom Possumato, who opposed the high-profile stance the FOP has taken on gun control under Stokes. "I feel good about what we have been able to do, including our work on the gun issue," Stokes said. The 225,000-member FOP heavily lobbied Congress last spring for the "Brady bill," which calls for a seven-day waiting period for prospective gun buyers. And the group supports the crime bill pending before Congress, Stokes said. Possumato had received backing from the National Rifle Association and the Falls Church-based Law Enforcement Alliance of America, a national organization of 8,000 police officers.

486.text: GUN-2981 PRIMARY U.S. STIFFENS LICENSING FOR FIREARMS DEALERS In a major step to curb the number of firearms dealers in the United States, the federal government has begun requiring new applicants and those renewing their licenses to submit photographs and fingerprints and is asking them whether they are complying with local and state laws. Currently, only machine gun dealers - a tiny fraction of the 266,000 licensed firearms dealers - are required to produce photo IDs and fingerprints. The federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, which regulates the gun industry, began mailing out the new four-page application form and a three-page questionnaire last month to new applicants and those wanting to renew their licenses. The two documents replace the previous two-page application form that asked few questions about the planned business and required virtually no proof of the applicant's identity. Last year, the federal agency began providing local and state agencies with the names of new firearms licensees. Now, the new forms - and the prospect of five years' imprisonment for lying on the application - will help the agency share more precise information with local officials, the firearms agency said. Richard Gardiner, the National Rifle Association's counsel, says the new requirements are illegal because they go far beyond the scope of the 1968 Gun Control Act, which broadly defined license requirements. He accused the Alcohol,Tobacco and Firearms bureau of "acting almost like a bunch of criminals. Please quote me on that."

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

487.text: NA-41725 PRIMARY Media couple put their beliefs into action time and again Terry Rumsey fights for gun safety. Before that, it was mental health reform. And reducing homelessness. And fair voting laws. And ending the death penalty. "The thread between all the work I've done is a belief in nonviolence," said Rumsey, 59, of Media. His current crusade is universal background checks on gun sales. Rumsey and Robin Lasersohn, 48, his wife and business partner, created Delaware County United for Sensible Gun Policy after the 2012 Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting in Newtown, Conn. Movita Johnson of Lansdowne is a member. Her son, Charles, was shot and killed in Germantown in 2011. "They complement one another and have so much energy," Johnson said of the couple. "They pull you into the project." Rumsey clashes with some in his battles, such as State Rep. Stephen Barrar (R., Delaware). In January, protesters both for and against increased gun control gathered outside Barrar's office, and Barrar and Rumsey exchanged words. Barrar has said he does not believe more laws will stop criminals from using guns. Sitting in the Springfield Friends Meeting House, where Delco United meets every month, Rumsey and Lasersohn finished each other's sentences. They spoke softly, contemplative yet assertive. Cross-legged, a long peppered braid swept over her shoulder, Lasersohn said people don't have to be leaders to make an impact. She and her husband weren't, at first. Rumsey grew up in Chester. It was a "hard town," Rumsey said, but he was raised in a working-class neighborhood where gun violence was not the norm.

490.text: GUN-4121 PRIMARY LAUTENBERG SEEKS BAN ON CONCEALED FIREARMS< THE SENATOR'S BILL WOULD SUPERSEDE THE LAWS OF 31 STATES. < HE AND THE NRA IMMEDIATELY TRADED FIRE. Surrounded by crime victims and gun-control advocates, U.S. Sen. Frank Lautenberg (D., N.J.) said yesterday that he would introduce federal legislation to prohibit most Americans from carrying concealed handguns. The legislation would effectively override laws in 31 states that make it easy to obtain permits to carry concealed handguns. States such as Connecticut, Pennsylvania, Florida and Texas allow nearly all non-felons to obtain gun permits for concealed weapons without showing need. Vermont is the only state where anyone can carry a concealed weapon without any gun license or permit. During a Statehouse news conference, Lautenberg said security guards and others whose jobs require gun protection would not be affected. The federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms would grant permits to qualified carriers, he said. "Common sense tells you that there are more than enough dangerous weapons on the streets," said Lautenberg, who attacked the National Rifle Association for pushing so-called right-to-carry laws nationwide. "More guns in the hands of private citizens will not make that citizen safer," said Rev. Jack Johnson, chairman of New Jersey Citizens To Stop Gun Violence. Lautenberg said his bill would preempt legislation by state Sen. Gerald Cardinale (R., Bergen) that would allow most New Jerseyans to carry concealed handguns. Cardinale's bill would allow qualified gun owners to claim "personal protection" as a justifiable need for obtaining the permits without offering any evidence.

491.text: GUN-7054 PRIMARY Hundreds protest at gun-rights rallies Advocates on two sides trade insults As thousands filed out of a gun-control event led by  President Clinton on Wednesday morning, a gun-rights activist  tossed a pamphlet to a high school student attending the Downtown  rally. The girl wiped her behind with it and threw it over her  shoulder at more than 200 protesters chanting, 'Guns Save Lives.' The clashing gun-rights advocates and opponents illustrated  the dramatic debate over gun laws in the state at a time when  Clinton said 'the country is looking very closely at Colorado.' Hours later, in front of the state Capitol's west steps, a  9-month-old boy in camouflage overalls chewed on his baby seat,  oblivious that he was the focal point of a separate, 120-person  gun-rights rally. Two signs leaned on his baby seat: 'Let my  parents protect me' and 'Mommy doesn't call 911.' Protests around Denver on Wednesday contrasted sharply with  the pep-rally spirit inside the Colorado Convention Center, where  Clinton pledged support for a proposed state ballot initiative  that would mandate background checks for people buying guns at gun  shows. Some 220 gun-rights supporters made sheep noises and  heckled the invitation-only crowd of 3,000 gun-control supporters as they filed out of the convention center. Despite the shouting, the protest remained peaceful. Police  said they made no arrests. 'If you really want to cut crime, you have to arm everybody!'  Larimer County resident Steve Zeigenhagen shouted at students.      Justin Segall, a 16-year-old junior at Denver's East High School,  snapped back, 'So I guess that means we should just have everybody  shooting everybody.'

494.text: GUN-30049 PRIMARY STOP CRIMINALS' ACCESS TO GUNS Fight firearms in the schools Sept. 3 letter to editor Letter writer Arthur Hayhoe may unwittingly be moving closer to the NRA's position on one issue than he may care to acknowledge. He writes, "Guns in schools and colleges is simply another more-guns solution the NRA continually pushes, instead of restricting criminal access to handguns that would make a difference." How does Mr. Hayhoe's fight against allowing students with concealed weapon licenses to carry handguns on campus restrict criminal access to handguns? If a student with a Florida license to carry a concealed weapon or firearm is permitted, through an act of the Florida Legislature, to carry a handgun on campus, how can that student be considered a criminal? Does Mr. Hayhoe consider a person with a concealed weapon or firearm license who carries a handgun to be a criminal? He was partially right. Now, we just have to get him to work harder in his attempts to "make a difference." Please, heed your own wise words in this regard and make a difference by working harder on restricting criminal access to handguns, instead of working to restrict the Second Amendment rights of your law-abiding neighbors. Frankie Steinbeck Jr.,New Port Richey Re: Alcohol sales downtown in New Port Richey Alcohol sales can help revive city Wake up people! It's not about to drink or not to drink, it is about revitalization, making changes. I haven't heard any positive feedback on how to save our dying city, only pessimistic views on how beer and wine sales will tarnish our family-friendly environment. Get real!

493.text: GUN-8324 PRIMARY LAW AND ORDER Illegal gun sales being investigated Federal agents are investigating a ring of college students who were buying firearms in Georgia and reselling them illegally in New York. After a monthlong investigation, three students from Georgia Southern University in Statesboro were approached by agents of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, said Mark Potter, assistant special agent at the Atlanta office. The students bought 50 firearms and federal agents were able to confiscate 16 of them, Potter said. Potter did not release the names of the students, who have not been charged. The investigation is still under way in Georgia and New York. Agents want to locate the missing firearms and file charges against others involved in the crime, Potter said. DEKALB COUNTY: Sketch released in attempted kidnapping DeKalb County police have released a sketch of a man wanted for attempting to kidnap a 7-year-old student walking home from Pine Ridge Elementary School in Stone Mountain on March 19. The boy's 10-year-old brother was able to ward off the suspect. Police described the man as a 30-year-old African-American, about 5 feet 10 inches tall with a scar on his right arm and a discolored tooth on the left side of his mouth. The boys told police the man was driving a two-door, dark green car with no hubcaps. Anyone with information is asked to contact DeKalb school police at 404-292-7343.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

492.text: GUN-11405 PRIMARY Colorado shooting prompts gun bills in big states SACRAMENTO, Calif. - Democratic leaders in three big states have used this summer's mass shooting in Colorado to push bills that would crack down on assault weapons and ammunition sales, rekindling a debate that has not gained much traction in Congress or on the presidential campaign. In Illinois, Gov. Pat Quinn proposed that his state enact a strict ban on assault weapons, similar to California's. New York lawmakers have proposed wide-ranging legislation that would limit weapons purchases. California Attorney General Kamala Harris and the Democratic state Senate leader back a bill that would make it more difficult and time-consuming to reload assault weapons. The chairmen of public safety committees in California's Assembly and Senate co-authored a bill that would require dealers to report purchases of large quantities of ammunition to law enforcement authorities. The suspect in the July 20 Colorado shooting, James Holmes, legally bought 6,000 rounds of ammunition online without raising authorities' attention. He had four weapons, including an assault rifle, on him after the rampage that killed 12 people and injured 58 at a midnight movie screening. "California sets the pace for the country. If there's no action in Congress, we better do something here and hope it catches fire in other states," said state Sen. Leland Yee, a San Francisco Democrat. who authored the legislation that would slow down the process of reloading an assault weapon with a new magazine.

495.text: GUN-5835 PRIMARY Gore Takes On NRA at Cities Convocation; With Gun Control Measures Blocked, Vice President Seeks Support of Mayors With his official presidential announcement just two days away, Vice President Gore came to this southern metropolis on a typically muggy June day to speak of community, faith, livability and guns, the latter being the dominant domestic issue in the nation's capital. A little after noon, Gore took to the podium at the Fairmont Hotel ballroom, outside the French Quarter, where more than 300 city executives from around the country had gathered for the national meeting of the U.S. Conference of Mayors. Although he was speaking before a bipartisan group, Gore confidently recited his party's line on gun control, denouncing the National Rifle Association and urging the House to pass legislation approved by the Senate. "This week we face a test," he said. "Will we let the NRA call the shots in Washington, or will we cross party lines to make our children safer? Will the Congress close the gun show loophole to help protect our children, or will we stand by as the carnage continues?" The vice president reminded the audience of what was perhaps his most politically fortuitous moment of recent weeks -- the day he cast the tie-breaking vote in the Senate to approve gun control legislation. "Now it's time for every parent, every citizen, every elected official, every national leader to stand up and be counted," he said, a line aides suggested was meant to highlight the silence of some GOP presidential candidates on the subject. "Don't duck. No more delays, no more fuzziness and no more excuses."

498.text: NA-40465 PRIMARY Obama seeks research on effect of media, video games on gun violence Congress had barred CDC inquiry. GUN CONTROL PROPOSALS NEW YORK o Hollywood and the video game industry received scant attention Wednesday when President Barack Obama unveiled sweeping proposals for curbing gun violence in the wake of the Newtown, Conn., school shooting. No connection was suggested between bloody entertainment fictions and real-life violence. Instead, the White House is calling on research on the effect of media and video games on gun violence. Among the 23 executive measures signed Wednesday by Obama is a directive to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and scientific agencies to conduct research into the causes and prevention of gun violence. The order specifically cited "investigating the relationship between video games, media images and violence." The measure meant that media would not be exempt from conversations about violence, but it also suggested the White House would not make Hollywood, television networks and video game makers a central part of the discussion. It's a relative footnote in the White House's broad, multipoint plan, and Obama did not mention violence in entertainment in his remarks Wednesday. The White House plan did mention media, but suggested that any effort would be related to ratings systems or technology: "The entertainment and video game industries have a responsibility to give parents tools and choices about the movies and programs their children watch and the games their children play." The administration is calling on Congress to provide $10 million for the CDC research.

496.text: GUN-3740 PRIMARY `Check your weapon at door' under Senate bill RALEIGH -- Businesses could take a lesson from saloons in the Wild West and post a sign telling patrons to keep their concealed pistols outside under a Senate measure unveiled Tuesday. ``I guess it would be like the Wild West. You could check your weapon at the bar,'' said Sen. Fountain Odom, D-Mecklenburg, who later expressed his frustration with allowing expanded gun permits. A Senate Judiciary committee approved thoroughly rewriting a bill that passed the House allowing more law-abiding citizens to carry concealed handguns. ``Quite frankly, it is very radically different from what came to you from the House,'' said bill sponsor Rep. John Nichols, R-Craven. While local governments would be barred from enacting local laws on concealed handguns, the Senate version allows pistols to be prohibited in several locations. Hidden handguns would be forbidden in banks, jails, government buildings, bars or parks. Any business or residence could outlaw concealed pistols by posting a sign at the entrance. Senators battled over a provision that would make people who carry concealed handguns without going through the permit process guilty of a felony after a first warning. Odom and others who wrote the Senate version argued that if law-abiding citizens are allowed to carry guns, it will be mainly criminals wearing weapons without a permit. Lawful citizens who want to carry pistols also need a strict sanction to prod them to follow the process for a gun permit.

497.text: GUN-12105 PRIMARY At Halftime, Costas Put Spotlight on Guns. By Morning, the Spotlight Was on Him. Watch on Youtube. Bob Costas recognized that he was likely to be walking into a minefield with his commentary during halftime of the Sunday night National Football League game on NBC. Prompted by the horror of a  murder-suicide carried out the day before by a Kansas City Chiefs football player, Mr. Costas quoted approvingly, and extensively, from a sports column that decried Americans' easy access to guns. But Mr. Costas says he has been given the freedom by NBC to editorialize on subjects related to football and sports - views that the network neither specifically endorses nor opposes. And he was convinced, he said in a telephone interview on Monday, that "it was likely that these two people would not be dead" if there hadn't been a gun available that made it easy to take a life in a moment of anger. After a flight overnight Sunday back from Dallas, where the Cowboys hosted the Philadelphia Eagles on Sunday night, Mr. Costas said he woke to "a zillion text messages and phone messages" about his commentary. Most of them were supportive, he said, but there was also a torrent of harshly critical comments from defenders of gun ownership, whether online or on TV shows like "Fox and Friends" on the Fox News Network. Some of those critics called for NBC to fire him. In the Monday interview, Mr. Costas said, "I am emphatically not backing off from anything I said." But he noted that in the commentary he had quoted from a column posted on the Web site of Fox Sports by the writer Jason Whitlock. Since he was not able to reach Mr. Whitlock before going on the air on Sunday, Mr. Costas said he did not feel it proper to edit or add extensively to those comments.

499.text: GUN-11396 PRIMARY Aug. 22 letters to the editor Gun-control stats tell different story I find it interesting that so many pro-gun-control letter writers are proposing more onerous gun laws or cite inaccurate statistics to justify their proposals. Here are the government statistics that can be found using National Instant Criminal Background Check System (2001-2010) and Department of Justice FBI Uniform Crime Reporting Stats (2001-2010): Murder rates have declined from 5.6 per 100,000 of population in 2001 to 4.8 per 100,000 of population in 2010. This is a 14 percent decline in the national murder rate. During this period, there has been a 60 percent increase in background checks for firearms purchases. Simply stated, the media and gun-control narrative is conclusively refuted by the statistical data. It would be better for intelligent people to get their facts straight before making incorrect assertions about gun ownership. Gary Buffon El Cerrito Customers paying for Chevron's mess So let me see if I got this right. Chevron's incompetence and negligence endangers millions and sends thousands to the hospital. One would think this incident would be a terrible thing to happen to a corporation. However, as we have already seen, gasoline prices have increased by almost 50 cents per gallon, which will result in increased profits for Chevron (already on track to rake in about $30 billion in profit this year), as well as the entire oil industry.

characters: National Instant Criminal Background Check System
Department of Justice FBI Uniform Crime Reporting Stats

500.text: GUN-714 PRIMARY COWARDICE ON GUNS; BUSH, LEGISLATURE FAIL TO ACT ON ASSAULT WEAPONS The cowardice of the Bush administration and the Florida Legislature regarding assault weapons is disgusting. President Bush on Monday outlined a get-tough program to "take back the streets." He spoke of more prisons and longer sentences. But, when it came to assault weapons, the firearm of choice for the drug lords, he would do nothing aside from banning imports. All that amounts to is protectionism for U.S. manufacturers . . . not that they need it, inasmuch as they already dominate the market. The president's plan would expand the categories of people who may not own firearms. But, without a waiting period and a background check, there is no way to stop them from buying weapons. Meanwhile, the Florida Senate's Judiciary-Criminal Committee was engaging in its own capitulation to the gun lobby. These fearless lawmakers wouldn't even approve the ban on large ammunition holders that was the House Criminal Justice Committee's cop-out. Instead, they voted to create a study commission to find out what the state's law officers already have told them, that assault weapons are easily defined and have no legitimate purpose. It's becoming clear that the only way Floridians will get anything meaningful in the way of gun control is through a constitutional amendment placed on the ballot via petitions. This week's events should give new impetus to "Cool It! Florida," spearheaded by Rep. Ron Silver, D-North Miami Beach, a drive to place a statewide seven-day waiting period on the 1990 ballot.

501.text: GUN-20194 PRIMARY Dems hope Bush stance backfires SAN ANTONIO -- Detective April Titus drives around this city famous for an 1836 shootout and wishes it were not so easy for criminals and kids to get enough guns to re-enact the assault on the Alamo. San Antonio's 500 licensed gun dealers are required by federal law to conduct criminal background checks before a sale. But every weekend, there are flea markets where people can buy guns without a background check. At least twice a month, there are heavily advertised gun shows that Titus calls "open markets for felons" because sales are unregulated. Titus' job with the San Antonio Police Department is to trace guns used in crimes, particularly by teens. Since George W. Bush was elected governor in 1994, violent crime has decreased in San Antonio and across Texas. But Titus sees no end to criminals' easy access to guns. "The ability of juveniles and felons to walk into gun shows and purchase guns is a huge crime problem," she says. "There are days when I feel like I'm just spinning my wheels." Democrats hope Titus' frustration will become Republican presidential candidate Bush's liability. They say his record -- he has backed tougher penalties on criminals who use weapons but opposes mandatory gun registration and other controls -- will trouble voters in key suburban areas and in states such as New York and California, where most voters support gun controls. He doesn't think it will.

502.text: GUN-9453 PRIMARY Ehrlich Seeks Compromise on Gun Bill; Measure Targeting Felons Is Criticized by Advocates on Both Sides of the Issue Maryland Gov. Robert L. Ehrlich Jr., scrambling to salvage some of his legislative agenda, tried yesterday to forge a compromise on his signature crime-fighting initiative to toughen punishment for felons caught with guns. But Ehrlich's efforts to attach a gun-control measure to his Project Exile bill met with resistance from advocates on both sides of the gun debate. Ehrlich (R) met at the governor's mansion yesterday with National Rifle Association chief Wayne LaPierre and a few gun-rights lawmakers to try to save his legislation, which would raise the maximum sentence for gun-toting felons from five years to 10. The tougher sentences are key to the Project Exile approach, which also involves working with federal prosecutors to exile gun offenders to federal prisons in other states. The governor has secured an agreement from the U.S. attorney to handle more gun crimes. But to pass new state sentencing legislation, he needs support from gun-control advocates in the General Assembly. In particular, Senate Judicial Proceedings Committee Chairman Brian E. Frosh (D-Montgomery), is holding up Ehrlich's bill until the governor agrees to tougher gun-control measures. Ehrlich said he plans to meet with Frosh in addition to the NRA. "There's concern on all sides of this issue," Ehrlich said. A loss on the gun crimes bill would represent another setback for a young administration struggling to chalk up a single legislative victory in the Democratic-controlled General Assembly. At the same time, the stalemate presages the difficulty gun-control advocates face with a Republican occupying Government House for the first time in more than three decades.

508.text: GUN-3836 PRIMARY At the Bar; In the constitutional amendment on the right to bear arms, one clause defines a cause. These 12 words were engraved in red stone near the entrance to the National Rifle Association headquarters, which used to be near the White House: "The right of the people to bear arms shall not be infringed." (The group last year moved to suburban Virginia, a state with more lenient gun laws, which has the side effect of making it easier to bring guns to the N.R.A.'s headquarters for use on its practice range. The 12 words still adorn many of the association's pamphlets.) The language is from the Second Amendment to the Constitution and serves as a mantra to people who insist that the Government may not take away their guns. Yet, the basic flaw in relying on those words alone is that they constitute only half an amendment. The full text says: "A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed." It is the first clause that has been used by opponents of a broad reading of the amendment to assert that the Constitution does not guarantee each citizen the right to own a gun. So, what is a well-regulated militia? More important, does the language of the Second Amendment mean that the right to bear arms exists only in conjunction with such a militia? In other words, is it a right guaranteed to individuals by the Constitution?

505.text: GUN-3499 PRIMARY Don't stop with guns; ban assault turtles, too They can take away my assault turtle from me when they pry my cold dead fingers from around its shell. Or something like that. The politicians, in the crime bill that may be voted on in Congress today or Friday, are trying to fight crime by stopping law-abiding citizens from owning assault rifles. I guess I missed something in the translation; I thought crime was committed by the lawless and not the lawful. Since by definition only the lawful will obey the law, then only the lawless won't. I feel safer already. But if Washington really wants to protect us, it should ban assault turtles. In a Pittsburgh suburb a few days ago, a woman was chased around her apartment by her boyfriend, wielding a snapping turtle which he was trying to coax to, well, snap. The cops arrived just in time. Society was spared the carnage, this time. But what about the next assault-turtle attack? Will we be so fortunate? Can any of us truly feel safe? It must be just a question of time until Congress bans assault turtles. What reason could a law-abiding citizen have which would justify the ownership of such a potentially lethal weapon? They are good for one thing and one thing only. What if some little innocent child got hold of dad's assault turtle? The results are almost too terrifying to contemplate.

507.text: GUN-1150 PRIMARY Brady Bill Will Help Illegal Gun Runners To the Editor: In "A Real Response to Violent Crime" (editorial, March 22), you use the case of Ruben Floyd, an illegal gun runner who sells guns on the street, to justify the Brady bill's seven-day waiting period and background check between purchase and delivery of guns. You acknowledge that Mr. Floyd's activities break the law. You also criticize the National Rifle Association proposal for an instant point-of-sale background check on the ground that poor record keeping makes that impossible. You ignore the obvious conclusions: (1) since the waiting period applies only to sales through legal outlets, Mr. Floyd's illicit sales of guns on the street are not touched; (2) if a waiting period succeeds in stopping improper sales from legal outlets, there will be more demand for Mr. Floyd's services, not less (Attorney General Dick Thornburgh reported recently that five out of six crime guns were bought illegally, and this would merely make it six for six); (3) if the mere presence of guns creates crime, then crime should be lower in the more restrictive states than in the more permissive ones, but it's higher, and (4) if the record keeping is as bad as you suggest it is, then the seven-day background check required by the Brady bill is not feasible either. ERIC S. H. CHING Mountain View, Calif, March 25, 1991

503.text: GUN-786 PRIMARY TIGHTENING ILLINOIS GUN LAWS Since 1968, the Illinois Firearm Owner's Identification Card has been effective in limiting the number of guns in the wrong hands. Of the 200,000 card requests received each year by the Illinois State Police, at least 10,000 are rejected because the individual failed the criminal or mental illness background check. Still, even the best-enforced gun laws will have loopholes, and that is why the Illinois General Assembly should seriously consider the new bill by state Sen. William Marovitz and Senate President Philip Rock.   The bill would place stricter regulations on handgun purchases by requiring a separate Handgun Owner's Identification Card and a more detailed record check. The handgun buyer would have to fill out an application at a local police agency rather than mailing in the request. This should prevent criminals from using aliases. Also, the number of handguns and their serial numbers would have to be reported.   In addition, the applicant would have to waive the right of confidentially to allow a check of the records of both public and private mental institutions.   At present, only state records are reviewed. This loophole could allow individuals - like Laurie Dann, who shot and killed an 8-year-old boy - to buy a handgun because they were treated at private and not public facilities.   Speaking for the bill, Chicago Police Superintendent LeRoy Martin noted that half of the 14,000 firearms his department has recently confiscated could not be traced. The Marovitz-Rock bill is aimed at those who illegally sell or rent to criminals guns that were purchased legally. The bill should also help police in tracing stolen handguns.   Though far from a cure-all, the legislation would make it more difficult for the wrong people to obtain handguns in Illinois. What is Missouri doing?

506.text: GUN-4401 PRIMARY IT'S HIGH NOON IN WASHINGTON STATE: GROUPS FACE OFF OVER GUN RIGHTS A gun-control measure on the state ballot this fall has turned into a Western shootout in a state where nearly one out of five residents owns a pistol. On one side is the state medical association, law enforcement leaders, religious groups, the state's largest teachers union and Microsoft chairman Bill Gates, among other Seattle-area business leaders. They back the measure, which would require handgun owners to pass an exam or take an eight-hour safety course to get a license. The proposal also would require trigger locks on all handguns sold or transferred in the state. On the other side are gun rights groups, such as the National Rifle Association, and police groups, which say the measure is an invasion of privacy, an infringement of the right to bear arms and a smokescreen for a hidden agenda: registration and eventual confiscation of handguns. "What they're doing is basically using children and hiding behind a phony handgun safety ballot title," said Alan Gottlieb, chairman of the Bellevue-based Citizens Committee for the Right to Keep and Bear Arms. "These people don't want education. They want to make it very hard for people to buy a handgun or use one, and that's exactly what this initiative does." If voters approve the measure on the Nov. 4 ballot, Washington will have one of the strictest gun laws in the nation. Only a dozen states require some sort of handgun permit or license, and just two - Massachusetts and Connecticut - have passed laws requiring trigger locks.

504.text: NA-45915 PRIMARY Why does Congress keep protecting rogue gun dealers? Outrage over the elementary school massacre in Newtown, Conn., raised hopes that Congress would finally break free of the gun lobby's grip and pass sensible measures to reduce gun violence. Three months later, however, those hopes are fading. The plan to ban assault weapons and large-capacity magazines? On life support in the Democrat-controlled Senate. Prospects for other important measures, such as universal background checks? Dicey in the Republican-controlled House. If anything, Congress seems headed in the wrong direction. This week, with little attention or discussion, lawmakers passed a half-dozen gun provisions, most of them designed to please the National Rifle Association by handcuffing the agency that enforces the nation's gun laws. The impact is to make it more likely that guns will get into the hands of criminals. The worst of these "riders," tacked onto a must-pass spending bill to keep the federal government open until this fall, ensures that no federally licensed gun dealer ever has to conduct an inventory -- a basic practice in just about any other industry.   If a particular dealer's business model involves selling guns off the books to people who can't pass a background check, it's awfully convenient to be able to tell federal inspectors that no one had any idea guns were lost or stolen until inspectors showed up and asked to see the records. The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives can inspect most dealers only about once every five years. And in a given year, inspectors find that dealers can't account for about 20,000 guns. The rifle used by the  snipers who terrified the Washington, D.C, area in 2002 was stolen from a gun store in Tacoma, Wash., that had "lost track" of 238 guns over three years, according to a report by the Center for American Progress.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives

509.text: GUN-8160 PRIMARY WEST PALM'S MAYOR PUZZLED BY BATTERY OF NRA CARDS Mayor Joel Daves has a bull's eye on him, and he doesn't know why. In the last week, more than 2,000 postcards have poured into city hall criticizing his "favorable treatment to Smith & Wesson when making firearm purchases." The cards are signed by National Rifle Association members who hail from all parts of Florida, such as Auburndale, Pensacola and Jacksonville. Daves said he doesn't know what triggered the barrage. The city doesn't have a purchasing policy that favors Smith & Wesson, the gun manufacturer that enraged the NRA this year when it agreed to install gunlocks on all the firearms it sells. Puzzled, Daves called Police Chief Ric Bradshaw Monday. "I'm getting a lot of hate mail and I'm trying to figure out what I did," Daves explained to Bradshaw. Bradshaw said it might be related to the county's intention to pass its own law that would require trigger locks on guns. But NRA member Albert Tobin, a machine operator who lives in Davie, said he sent in his postcard because the NRA asked him to send it to six politicians who support gun control, and Daves was on the list. Tobin admitted that he knew very little about Daves and that he couldn't recall the names of the five other politicians who received his postcards. The NRA didn't return phone calls Monday, leaving Daves to wonder how he was on its list.

515.text: GUN-3635 PRIMARY CONCEALED-HANDGUNS MOVEMENT OUT OF TOUCH Though a Post-Dispatch Show-Me Poll reveals that residents in all parts of Missouri are deeply disturbed about crime, they reject any notion that allowing citizens to carry weapons would ease public anxiety over crime or make streets safer. The poll's key finding should serve as a warning to Missouri legislators who have pushed repeatedly for a law to make it legal for residents to carry concealed weapons. Missourians, the poll shows, are taking several steps to protect themselves: 58 percent say they have added home security devices; 35 percent have begun keeping guns in their homes and 21 percent report carrying Mace or pepper spray. Most, however, still have strong reservations about carrying concealed weapons. Sixty percent of St. Louis area residents questioned in the poll said they were against concealed weapons laws. That view was shared by 56 percent of those polled in outstate Missouri. Moreover, Missourians favor laws making it more difficult to buy guns. More than 60 percent of St. Louis area residents favor tougher gun-control laws, as do 54 percent of outstate residents. These results show that, in Missouri at least, the gun crowd is seriously misguided in its approach to crime. In addition, statistics show that violent crime, particularly murder, is declining. In New York City, for instance, handgun murders were down by more than 40 percent during the first half of this year. Police say the drop probably stems from crackdowns on gangs for gun violations. That's one of the most effective answers to street crime; fewer guns in the hands of gangs mean fewer murders.

512.text: GUN-30262 PRIMARY ILLEGAL WEAPONS BIZ SHOT FULL OF HOLES AS FEDS COLLAR 10 THE PARKING LOT of a Brooklyn Costco was a supermarket for illegal guns - only the bad guys didn't know they were selling the firearms to undercover agents, authorities said yesterday. The transactions took place, conveniently enough, across the street from the Sunset Park office of the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, officials revealed. Ten people in New York and South Carolina have been charged in the interstate gun trafficking operation - smuggling firearms to the city and selling them to undercover agents, authorities said. The investigation netted up to 70 guns. "We were able to get everybody from top to bottom," including people who bought, transported and resold the guns, said Special Agent William McMahon, head of the ATF's New York Field Division. In some instances, the alleged gun traffickers told undercover agents they would take payment for the guns in cocaine, officials said.

510.text: GUN-5592 PRIMARY 'Smart Guns' Setting Off Debate: How Smart Will They Really Be? The 9-millimeter semiautomatic looks like an ordinary pistol, evil and menacing to gun haters, strangely beautiful to gun fanciers. But it is no ordinary weapon; pull the trigger and nothing happens. Only when a shooter puts on a special wristband, containing a tiny radio transmitter, does a squeeze of the trigger make the hammer snap down hard, making the gun every bit the lethal weapon it appears to be. The pistol is a computerized "smart gun," designed to fire only for its owner. And its maker, Colt's Manufacturing Company, sees it as the firearms industry's reply to one of the strongest criticisms of gun control advocates -- that guns too often fall into the wrong hands, those of children or criminals, usually with horrible consequences. Colt's hopes, naturally, that the new product will help it survive what has become a long decline in the gun market.
