But critics say the new law will mean the end of local gun shows. And, they say, it won't keep guns off the streets because enthusiasts will simply buy them on the Treasure Coast. "This is just the same old crap liberals use to make themselves feel good," said George Russum, president of the members council of the Palm Beach County chapter of the National Rifle Association. Under the county's current law, someone who passes a background check can purchase a rifle and receive it on the spot. The new measure has broad support among commissioners. Commissioner Carol Roberts said recent high-profile shootings, such as the massacre at Columbine High School and last month's murder of seven people at a Fort Worth church, highlight the need for stronger gun regulations.

566.text: GUN-7083 PRIMARY Hole in Gun Control Law Lets Mentally Ill Through With the voices inside her head ordering her to kill, Lisa Duy walked into Doug's Shoot'n Sports here to buy a Smith & Wesson 9-millimeter semiautomatic pistol. For Doug's, the biggest gun store in Utah, the transaction was routine. The manager, Dave Larsen, called the Utah Bureau of Criminal Identification to run a background check on Ms. Duy, and the agency quickly approved the sale, having found no record of felony convictions or mental illness. In the basement shooting range, Mr. Larsen showed Ms. Duy, 24 and unemployed, how to hold and fire the heavy stainless-steel gun. The only thing unusual, he thought, was that as she left the store, headed for the bus stop outside, she was wearing the shooting-range ear protectors he had lent her. Less than two hours after leaving Doug's, on Jan. 14, 1999, Ms. Duy, ear protectors still in place, walked into the studios of television station KSL a few blocks from the Mormon Temple and began firing her new weapon. She shot more than four dozen times in all, killing a young mother and wounding the building manager. What the background check had been unable to detect was that Ms. Duy (pronounced dwee) had a long history of psychiatric problems, had in fact been found to be suffering from paranoid schizophrenia and, only a year before the check, had been committed to a mental hospital by a judge after threatening to kill an F.B.I. agent, an encounter that sprang from her delusions that the station was broadcasting information about her sex life.

565.text: GUN-11735 PRIMARY The gun lobby speaks AFTER THE NEWTOWN, Conn., massacre, the National Rifle Association promised to "offer meaningful contributions to make sure this never happens again." Friday, the gun owners and manufacturers lobby called for stationing an armed guard in every school in the country. "The only thing that stops a bad guy with a gun is a good guy with a gun," said the organization's executive vice president, Wayne LaPierre. "Would you rather have your 911 call bring a good guy with a gun from a mile away or from a minute away?" This is not a fresh idea; the NRA responded to the mass shootings at Virginia Tech in 2007 and at Columbine High School in Colorado in 1999 with similar comments. It is not unreasonable where local schools feel they need armed guards. But the notion of thousands upon thousands of additional gun-carrying guards roaming school halls strikes us as impractical and unwelcome. Where would these new guards come from, would they really be trained and what additional margin of safety would be achieved? Do local police think this is a good idea? Mr. LaPierre's simplistic slogan about stopping a bad guy with a gun ignores the reality that mass shootings are usually scenes of mayhem and crossfire, not a movie lot where a hero stops bad guys in their tracks. Would an armed guard have stopped Adam Lanza, or would the guard have been mowed down by Mr. Lanza's semiautomatic rifle, as were the school principal and other courageous teachers and school officials?

568.text: GUN-2010 PRIMARY High-rise murder survivors form gun-control group The survivors of last week's mass shooting at Pettit & Martin law firm  returned to work this week - and immediately began pushing for stricter gun  control laws. When employees arrived Tuesday at the 48-story office tower, an impromptu memorial of flowers and cards greeted them outside. A team of four counselors was on hand to help employees cope. "I expect it to be a long, long time before people come to terms with any  of this," Richard Climan, a lawyer with the firm, said before heading up to  his office Tuesday. "It's virtually impossible to put a senseless deed like  this into any perspective." The gunman, Gian Luigi Ferri, 55, opened fire with semiautomatic weapons at  the firm on Thursday. Eight people were killed and six wounded before Ferri  killed himself as police closed in. A frustrated businessman, Ferri, 55, left a rambling letter blaming food additives, Pettit & Martin and others for his woes.   The last service for a shooting victim was held Wednesday. The city planned a  public service for Friday. Many of the firm's employees have found the best therapy was in trying to prevent such a tragedy from happening again. Tuesday, they formed a new gun-control group called Legal Community Against Violence. Within hours, the firm had received dozens of calls from other law firms and companies offering to help.

567.text: GUN-8321 PRIMARY He's the NRA The bumper sticker on the back of his blue Dodge pickup hints at John Gluck's passion: "Charlton Heston is my president." So does his tag: LIFENRA. But John Gluck is just as likely to preach about safety as he is about the importance of the Second Amendment. He locked up his guns long before laws required adults to do so. The 70-year-old former police officer used his handcuffs to bolt his service revolver to an eye hook in his closet, safely away from his children's reach. Four Gluck children grew up without incident. All around him, people are watching the nightly news and finding more and more reasons to put restrictions on gun ownership. In Colorado, two heavily armed teenagers carry out a massacre. In West Palm Beach, a boy shoots and kills his teacher on the last day of school. Hillsborough County State Attorney Harry Lee Coe III sits down under an overpass and takes his life with a handgun. Restrict firearm ownership, gun-control advocates say, and you prevent many of these deaths. Although Gluck doesn't agree with everything the NRA says, his views on the Second Amendment are not much different from those of Heston, the man who once said Vice President Al Gore would have to take his guns from his "cold, dead hands." But Gluck says the criminal and irresponsible use of a firearm - not the gun itself - is what hurts people.

569.text: GUN-20107 PRIMARY Global 'gun control' won't stop violence Commentary writer Ted Fishman makes the usual call for what essentially would be international "gun control" ("America's war on terrorism overlooks small-arms trade," The Forum, Monday). But his ideas are heavy on rhetoric -- with such phrases as "overwrought fears of American gun owners" and "the world's most lethal trade" -- and light on common sense. He calls for "indelibly marked weapons" but doesn't explain just how manufacturers are supposed to create those "indelible" marks. His claim that "small arms have been the weapons of choice in 47 of the 49 largest conflicts since 1992" is a bold statement of the obvious. Just out of curiosity, I'd like to ask Fishman what were the weapons of choice before 1992. There is also this statement: "Assault rifles and rocket launchers are now made lightweight and idiot proof, perfect for child soldiers." Would Fishman prefer that soldiers of any age be equipped with heavy, complex weapons? Finally, there was one glaring omission in Fishman's column: the slaughter of hundreds of thousands of Rwandans -- by machete. Too bad there weren't "indelible marks" on those weapons. If so, according to Fishman's logic, a human tragedy of inhumane proportions might have been averted. Bill Estes Jr. Mount Hermon, La. NFL and gambling USA TODAY sports columnist Jon Saraceno's comparison of police inspector Louis Renault's hypocrisy in the classic Casablanca nightclub scene with that of the NFL is on the mark ("NFL on gambling: All talk, no action," Keeping Score column, Sports, Friday).

571.text: GUN-2328 PRIMARY Capitol Hill's big guns take aim at NRA The Clinton administration's proposal to increase gun dealers' license fees could carry with it enough political appeal to overcome opposition from the National Rifle Association. Indeed, Congress has already begun tightening the screws on gun dealers. Half a dozen proposals were introduced last year requiring dealers to abide by local gun ordinances, provide more personal information to the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, and chip in a larger share of the costs of processing applications and inspections. The Senate has cleared several measures requiring firearms dealers to provide photos and fingerprints as part of the licensing process and to immediately report thefts and losses of guns to the ATF. "I suspect hearings will be held and we'll have to hear from the industry," said Rep. Bill McCollum, R-Fla., who favors a more comprehensive approach to fighting crime. "Something would be likely (to pass), if you had to predict." Treasury Secretary Lloyd Bentsen, the latest Clinton Cabinet member to speak out against violence in the nation's streets, said Tuesday he'll ask Congress to raise gun dealer licensing fees to $ 600 a year. Last November, Congress raised the fee to $ 200 for three years - $ 66 a year. Before that, a license cost $ 10. Bentsen said the increase would eliminate close to 80% of current dealers. The idea is to give the ATF more discretion in issuing gun licenses, to rid the streets of dealers who illegally sell guns they've obtained legally and to cover the bureau's administrative costs, estimated by the ATF at $ 580 per license.

570.text: GUN-6877 PRIMARY WORKPLACE SHOOTINGS HEIGHTEN DEBATE OVER GUN CONTROL PROPOSALS IN CONGRESS; LEGISLATION HAS IDLED AS REPUBLICANS TRY TO COME UP WITH COMPROMISE Spurred by deaths in two workplace shootings this week, Vice President Al Gore and congressional Democrats accused Republicans of stalling new gun restrictions. Republicans replied that enforcement is the solution, not more laws. "How many tragedies does it take before the members of the Republican leadership bottling up this legislation get the message?" Gore asked Thursday as he noted the workplace killings in Honolulu and Seattle. "The leadership of the majority, the Republican Party, in the Congress today is frustrating the will of the American people by refusing to take up legislation that the American people support." Republicans have been unable to reach a compromise between gun restrictions passed by the House and Senate. They have accused the vice president of being part of an administration that has presided over an increase in mass shootings. Actor Charlton Heston, president of the National Rifle Association, also criticized the Clinton administration Thursday in testimony before Congress. "This administration, as a policy, is putting gun-toting felons on the streets in record numbers," Heston told the House Government Reform criminal justice, drug policy and human resources subcommittee. Heston said the White House lacks "the time or the spine" to enforce current laws. "Why does the president ask for more federal gun laws if he's not going to enforce the ones we have?" Heston said. The legislation in question has been stalled since early summer, but the dispute flares up with each new mass shooting.

572.text: GUN-2587 PRIMARY Parents of Slain Japanese Student to Meet Clinton With Anti-Gun Message Masaichi and Mieko Hattori, whose teenage son was shot to death while an exchange student in Baton Rouge, La., 13 months ago, are to bring their crusade against gun violence in America to a meeting with President Clinton at the White House today. "We came here to present to President Clinton the wishes of all those who signed these petitions," said Masaichi Hattori, 46, who works for a manufacturing company in Nagoya, Japan. Since their son, Yoshihiro, died, the Hattoris have collected 1.7 million signatures in Japan on a petition to Clinton "to reassess the easy availability of guns" and "help end the senseless yearly slaughter of thousands of Americans and foreign visitors." "The life of my son will never be back," Mieko Hattori, 45, said in an interview yesterday. "But I don't want his death to be in vain. I know so many people suffer here the same grief which we had to endure. I don't want to see more agony. I like America very much. That is why we are working for this endeavor." On Oct. 17 last year, Yoshihiro Hattori, 16, known as Yoshi, had been invited to a Halloween costume party in Baton Rouge but mistakenly arrived at the home of Rodney Peairs. Peairs testified later that the youth appeared to be a grinning, potentially crazed intruder brandishing a weapon, actually a camera.

573.text: NA-44202 PRIMARY Giffords and Kelly hope to revive gun debate with fly-around tour of key states Former congresswoman Gabrielle Giffords will try to revive the contentious debate over the nation's gun laws next week with a fly-around tour of states whose senators rebuffed popular opinion and voted against expanding background checks. Giffords and her husband, astronaut Mark Kelly, plan to swoop into Alaska, North Dakota, Nevada and New Hampshire to hold public events orchestrated to pressure key senators to switch their votes to support expanding background checks to more gun buyers. They also will visit Maine and North Carolina, where senators took political risks to vote for the background checks bill this spring. The couple's "Rights and Responsibility" tour will run from July 1 to 7 and include public events in a different state each day. The tour is designed to make the case to elected officials that the Second Amendment rights to bear arms demand greater responsibility -- and that, in each state, a majority of voters support expanding background checks. "I've been around guns my whole life, and I know that as an American, my right to own a firearm goes hand in hand with my obligation to be a responsible gun owner and to do my part to make sure guns don't fall into the hands of criminals or dangerously mentally ill people," Kelly, a retired Navy captain who flew combat missions in the Gulf War, said in a statement.

574.text: GUN-7088 PRIMARY READER RESPONSES: GUN CONTROL: Waiting period not needed Tom Teepen's latest canard is that the NRA is against the concept of gun checks ("NRA's whining sets new record for hypocrisy," April 2). The foundation of the original Brady legislation was the notion that an arbitrary waiting period would lower crime and would have prevented John Hinckley from his attempt on President Reagan's life. (Never mind the fact that Hinckley had purchased the gun some six months before.) The NRA has long supported the instant background checks, a goal easily handled with computers. And that is the same reason why it did not support yet-another arbitrary waiting period of 72 hours for gun show purchases. Can you imagine a shopper presenting his credit card to a merchant and being told to wait three days for the computer to verify his credit? Why Clinton shot down reasonable legislation including a 24-hour instant background check for gun shows, trigger locks and lifetime bans on guns for violent teenagers is beyond me. RALPH WALLACE Wallace, of Lilburn, is a technical sales consultant.

576.text: GUN-9482 PRIMARY IGNORING THE WILL OF THE VOTERS CONCEALED WEAPONS PROPONENTS of concealed guns didn't play dead long. They dusted themselves off and came up with a flimsy, new argument for selling a concealed weapons law to Missourians, just four years after voters defeated the last one. The new argument is that concealed guns will somehow make us safer in the wake of the attacks of Sept. 11, 2001. That's why House Speaker Catherine Hanaway, R-Warson Woods, says she backed the concealed weapons law that the Missouri House passed Thursday, after she opposed a similar measure that was defeated by voters in 1999. "There's just a different climate than there was before Sept. 11," she says. Ms. Hanaway is right about the different climate. But she's too smart to think that a citizen packing heat is going to plug a terrorist. It's preposterous for a lawmaker to imply that a concealed-carry law would have made Americans safer on Sept. 11, or now. New York was among states with a concealed-carry law on the books in 2001. Overall, there has been no sharp rise in violent crime in Missouri. When Attorney General John D. Ashcroft pointed this year to the sharp nationwide drop in gun violence, he didn't credit concealed weapons laws that were in effect in most states. Rather, he pointed to aggressive federal gun prosecutions and record numbers of convictions. In addition, recent studies have questioned the research of law professor John Lott, who has claimed that concealed-gun laws made states safer.

575.text: GUN-3198 PRIMARY ROUND OF DEBATE AIMED AT GUN BILL; SENATE WEIGHS CONCEALED WEAPONS Senate Majority Leader J.B. "Jet" Banks strapped on two toy pistols Wednesday to illustrate his belief that "everybody you meet is going to be loaded" if the Legislature legalizes concealed weapons. With that, Banks, D-St. Louis, opened debate on one of the hottest issues of the legislative session: whether sheriffs should issue permits so that people can carry concealed weapons. A bill sponsored by Sen. Harold Caskey would let people 21 and older carry concealed guns if they demonstrated handgun safety and met other criteria. Caskey, D-Butler, said that 38 states have such laws - and that most have lower crime rates than Missouri. Vermont, with a wide-open gun law, has the least crime and is the safest state, Caskey said. Missouri is the 14th most dangerous state because criminals know citizens here are unarmed, Caskey said. "This lets our good citizens carry a weapon to defend themselves," he said. In an orchestrated attack, opponents of the bill began offering a raft of amendments to tighten it. Debate is expected to continue today. One amendment adopted Wednesday increased the penalty for carrying a concealed weapon into a building posted as off-limits to hidden guns. Under the bill, bar owners, public bodies and others could put up signs barring people with gun permits from bringing their weapons inside. The amendment makes violators guilty of misdemeanors instead of infractions.

579.text: GUN-1800 PRIMARY Police Group's Leadership Election Seen as Showdown Over Gun Control; Challenger Targets FOP President's Strong Support of 'Brady Bill' As president of the national Fraternal Order of Police, Dewey Stokes has become one of the nation's most effective advocates of gun control. Last spring, for instance, he threw the weight of the 225,000-member organization behind a waiting period for gun buyers, and helped in the successful campaign to get such a measure passed by both the Senate and House. "It's what my membership wants," Stokes said of his anti-gun policy. "And what I think is right." For more than a year, however, Tom Possumato, a New Jersey detective, has mounted an all-out bid to unseat Stokes as head of the organization of police officers and one of his issues has been Stokes's anti-gun stance, which he says is too stringent. "A lot of policemen are coming up to me and saying Stokes doesn't speak for us on that issue," said Possumato, who says he is not opposed to gun control but thinks that each state should be allowed to makes its own policy. This week, 3,000 officers will convene in Pittsburgh for the biennial convention of the FOP, and the contest between the two veteran officers is widely viewed as a showdown on gun control. Stokes, a 50-year-old Columbus, Ohio, policeman now in his second two-year term as FOP president, has sent out only one letter notifying members that he is up for reelection.

581.text: GUN-3834 PRIMARY LETTERS FROM THE PEOPLE Is Militia Movement Threat To U.S.? There has been a lot of talk about the militia movement in the wake of the bombing in Oklahoma City. Some call for giving the FBI and other law-enforcement agencies increased authority to infiltrate and investigate radical groups of both the right and left. Others want to maintain the status quo with regard to investigative authority. I side with the latter group, although I can understand the frustration of those who would turn up the heat on paramilitary groups. My true frustration, though, is with those who defend the militia's existence by insisting that they are simply self-defense forces, organized to protect the people's rights in the event the central government infringes on them. They point to the word "militia" in the Second Amendment to justify this assertion and seem astounded that I can believe that the militias referred to in the Second Amendment were for the purpose of defending frontier society against incursions by hostile neighbors. Or that the words "well-regulated" in the amendment indicate that the right to keep and bear arms might not be absolute. What really floors them, though, is my contention that the Constitution provides a number of mechanisms for the people to "petition the government for a redress of grievances," none of which includes armed force. There is only one crime explicitly defined in the Constitution. It occurs when two or more people conspire together, then commit at least one overt act, with the aim of overthrowing the government. That, minus the overt act (so far) sounds like a good description of the militia movement. The name of the crime they plan to commit in the name of patriotism is treason. Charlie Robinson Oakville

577.text: GUN-4698 PRIMARY IF ENOUGH CITIES BAN CHEAP GUNS, MAYBE THE LEGISLATURE WILL LISTEN WHEN San Jose passed a law banning the sale of shoddy, inexpensive handguns last year, it stood alone in the South Bay. Now, police chiefs and other officials from all over Santa Clara County are planning to write local gun control rules that all 15 cities in the county can adopt and enforce. San Jose's "Saturday Night Special" ban and other local laws affecting gun sales have been adopted largely out of frustration that the state will not pass reasonable gun controls. The gesture was worth making, but it was mostly that - a gesture - as long as it was easy to cross into an adjacent city to get around the laws. A strategy session for police chiefs, city attorneys and elected officials is planned Feb. 20 to brainstorm about gun laws. San Jose Mayor Susan Hammer and Jim Beall, chair of the county supervisors, are encouraging the effort, along with the county Department of Health. Los Gatos Police Chief Larry Todd, who chairs the committee on firearms issues for the state police chiefs association, has been urging cities to adopt uniform gun laws. Contra Costa and Alameda counties have done it. San Mateo and Los Angeles counties are talking about it. State legislators are far removed from the misery that results from the ready availability of cheap handguns. But city police and elected officials know all too well the connection. If enough cities act, perhaps the Legislature will be stirred to look beyond the gun lobby and act in the interests of the people.

585.text: NA-44655 PRIMARY Shifting focus to the human side of the gun debate When my daughter was 14, she accompanied me to an FBI shooting range in Quantico, Va. An FBI SWAT team leader showed us how to fire a Heckler & Koch MP5 submachine gun. His safety regime was strict. The first time my daughter took a shot, in semiautomatic mode, there were four hands holding the gun - hers and his. That experience, in 1992, came to mind after the tragic accident last week at a gun range in Arizona. A 9-year-old girl lost control of the Uzi submachine gun she was firing and fatally shot the instructor standing next to her. I can understand why so many people were appalled, but there are other lessons in the tragedy besides the common refrain: Don't give a little girl a submachine gun. A cellphone video released by police shows just how fast the fun turned fatal. It's over in a flash. You have to watch closely to see what went wrong. Pay attention - that's a lesson in itself and one not easily learned in our distracted, impatient, multitasking society. Giving my daughter car keys for the first time was far more worrisome than letting her fire a gun. Important lessons about gun safety are also likely to be missed because of how we "debate" gun control vs. gun rights in this country, the winner being the side that makes the most political hay out of a killing before the TV cameras move on to something else. The discourse this time was arguably worse than most because of how unfairly some have treated the families.

578.text: GUN-174 PRIMARY WASHINGTON TALK: BRIEFING; Split in the Gun Lobby The traditionally monolithic gun lobby, one of Washington's most powerful, is undergoing a siege of interservice rivalry. In recent months various pro-gun factions, usually united against virtually all restrictions on access to firearms and ammunition, have divided over such questions as bans on ''cop-killer'' bullets and further sales of machine guns to private owners. President Reagan signed a bill Aug. 28 banning the sale, manufacture or importing of ammunition that can penetrate police flak jackets. The ban was strongly opposed by the Gun Owners of America and the Citizens Committee for the Right to Keep and Bear Arms, but the National Rifle Association remained neutral, which some gun-lovers took as a virtual endorsement. The most militant also say the N.R.A. acted too late to stop a provision of the recently revised Gun Control Act that restricted sales of machine guns to those already licensed and in existence. Now gun owners and dealers are disputing over another revision in the Gun Control Act, which beginning next month will eliminate record-keeping for most ammunition sales and eliminate the ban on mail-order sales. The National Association of Stocking Gun Dealers and the National Association of Federally Licensed Firearms Dealers are lobbying for a return to tighter regulation. That makes some other pro-gun factions furious. ''This is nothing more than greed showing its ugly face again,'' fumed an editorial in The Gun Owners, the newsletter of Gun Owners of America. The group maintains the dealers really want the Government to reduce the competition.

characters: Citizens Committee for the Right to Keep and Bear Arms
National Association of Stocking Gun Dealers
National Association of Federally Licensed Firearms Dealers

587.text: NA-45918 PRIMARY Laws need waiting period more than guns; Good legislation takes time for deliberation After New York state hastily passed its post-Newtown gun law, it quickly became obvious we need a waiting period for laws more than for guns. After all, the idea behind waiting periods for guns is that people intent on doing something rash would "cool off" if they had to wait a few days before getting their hands on one. Laws are dangerous, too, and legislators seem prone to the same fits of hysteria as the gun owners those lawmakers want to regulate. Now New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg, a tireless champion of gun restrictions, has noticed the problem, too. The ban on selling magazines larger than 7-rounds, a major feature of the New York law, turns out to be unworkable. For starters, nobody makes 7-round magazines, and the law doesn't exempt police from the rule. Starting April 15, when the law goes into effect, it would turn the state's police into criminals for carrying their usual Glocks with the standard 10- and 15-round magazines. Bloomberg observed: "We've just got to start to thinking a little bit more about the implications of things before we rush to legislate." Well, yes. Of course, it's a bit rich to hear this from Bloomberg, who had a public relations organization on call waiting for a mass shooting (which turned out to be Newtown) so he could rush gun control through at just the time emotions were most likely to cloud people's thinking.

582.text: GUN-2150 PRIMARY VA. HOUSE REJECTS GUNS COMPROMISE; TOUGHER MEASURE ENDORSED INSTEAD The Virginia House of Delegates rejected Monday night a compromise handgun- control bill endorsed earlier in the day by Gov. Douglas Wilder, instead embracing a tougher measure that Wilder had abandoned because he feared it would be rejected by the General Assembly. Minutes later, a Senate committee endorsed the compromise version, setting up a potential confrontation between the two houses over the dominant issue of the 1993 legislative session. Though there was considerable confusion about what form the final bill would take, Monday night's action leaves little doubt that lawmakers this year will adopt some limit on handgun purchases in Virginia. "It's a nice situation to be in," said U.S. Attorney Richard Cullen, a Republican who helped craft the compromise. "Obviously, it appears there's going to be meaningful limitation." The original bill would have limited handgun purchases to one a month. The compromise would allow Virginians to buy more than one handgun in 30 days, but only with police approval. Wayne R. LaPierre, chief executive of the National Rifle Association, said after the House vote that "if you listen real closely, you'll hear the criminals laughing. This won't work."

583.text: GUN-6144 PRIMARY AL GORE MAKES PLEA FOR STIFFER GUN LAWS HE IMPLICITLY SLAMS BUSH WHILE EARNING DAVIS' ENDORSEMENT In the wake of this week's Texas church shootings, Vice President Al Gore called for stiffer gun laws Friday in an attempt to draw a stark contrast with the views of GOP front-runner George W. Bush. Although he didn't name the Texas governor, he asked a group of Santa Monica high school students: "How can we allow guns in churches? Why don't we have a zero-tolerance for guns in schools? Why don't we have a nationwide ban on junk guns, assault weapons?" Gore posed his questions in a state where Democrats just enacted some of the nation's toughest gun-control laws and the GOP has found itself increasingly out of step with public opinion on the hot-button issue. Almost two-thirds of Californians say they would choose a candidate who supports stronger gun control over one who opposes it. With the state's economy humming, gun control could prove to be one of the pivotal issues in the presidential contest in California. Wrapping up a two-day visit to California, Gore also received the endorsement of Gov. Gray Davis and appeared at a gay event in San Francisco before heading to a $200,000 fundraiser at the home of a real estate mogul in Los Altos. Davis, who was traveling with Gore, said, "From the right to the left, people want sensible gun-control legislation in California, and anyone who wants California's vote has to come to terms with that." Davis signed five gun-control bills this year, including a sweeping ban on assault weapons.

580.text: NA-42961 PRIMARY Rules to Require Equal Coverage for Mental Ills WASHINGTON -- The Obama administration on Friday will complete a generation-long effort to require insurers to cover care for mental health and addiction just like physical illnesses when it issues long-awaited regulations defining parity in benefits and treatment. The rules, which will apply to almost all forms of insurance, will have far-reaching consequences for many Americans. In the White House, the regulations are also seen as critical to President Obama's program for curbing gun violence by addressing an issue on which there is bipartisan agreement: Making treatment more available to those with mental illness could reduce killings, including mass murders. In issuing the regulations, senior officials said, the administration will have acted on all 23 executive actions that the president and Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr. announced early this year to reduce gun crimes after the Newtown, Conn., school massacre. In planning those actions, the administration anticipated that gun control legislation would fail in Congress as pressure from the gun lobby proved longer-lasting than the national trauma over the killings of first graders and their caretakers last Dec. 14. ''We feel actually like we've made a lot of progress on mental health as a result in this year, and this is kind of the big one,'' said a senior administration official, one of several who described the outlines of the regulations that Kathleen Sebelius, the secretary of health and human services, will announce at a mental health conference on Friday in Atlanta with the former first lady Rosalynn Carter.

characters: Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr.

586.text: GUN-8934 PRIMARY State Senator's Defense of Efforts on Gun-Control Legislation Ricochets State Sen. Timothy R. Ferguson (R-Carroll), a lifetime member of the National Rifle Association and regular opponent of gun-control legislation, is facing criticism from an unlikely source: the NRA. Greg Costa, the NRA's lobbyist in Annapolis, said in an interview that Ferguson "is trying to hoodwink the people in his district" with a letter sent to supporters. In the letter, Ferguson says he and other gun advocates did "the best we could do" to fight the Maryland Responsible Gun Safety Act of 2000. Costa disagrees. "Ferguson negotiated what he called a compromise," Costa said. "To me, it was a capitulation." For his part, Ferguson said he is "as pro-gun as you can get without going over the edge." "Every vote I make in Annapolis is a pro-gun vote," Ferguson said. "I'm the one that gets eaten up by the liberals for having my name on gun bills." In a Democrat-dominated legislature and in a state that has not been shy about passing gun-control measures, adamantly pro-gun legislators are few. But in Ferguson's district, which covers conservative and rural areas in Carroll and Frederick counties, conventional wisdom says he who fights hardest against gun control wins the most votes. In the Sept. 10 Republican primary, Ferguson faces David R. Brinkley, a two-term member of the House of Delegates, and David P. Gray, the president of the Frederick Board of County Commissioners.

584.text: NA-41162 PRIMARY Checks could have stopped massacres WASHINGTON - Background checks would not have prevented Adam Lanza's shocking school shooting in Newtown, Conn., four months ago, but tougher screening might have stopped last year's massacre in Aurora, Colo., and others, details of the cases and pro-gun control groups suggest. A University of Colorado, Denver, psychiatrist alerted the school's behavioral threat assessment team about accused Aurora shooter James Holmes, 24, but they did not act and he was not legally ruled mentally unfit to buy or own a gun, according to a January report by Mayors Against Illegal Guns on mass shootings. Virginia Tech gunman Seung-Hui Cho, who killed 32 and wounded 17 in April 2007, had been legally ruled mentally unsound, but state interpretation of federal law allowed him to purchase guns because he had never been hospitalized, according to reporting on the case. Reforms enacted in the following months aimed to seal the loophole. This past Oct. 21, Radcliffe Haughton, a 45-year-old ex-Marine, killed his wife and two others, wounded four and then killed himself in Brookfield, Wis. Haughton could not have passed a background check due to domestic violence charges, but found his murder weapon online from a private seller, Brookfield police said last year. Columbine High School shooters Eric Harris and Dylan Klebold were both too young to buy firearms from licensed dealers before the April 1999 massacre. They purchased three with cash at a gun show, aided by an 18-year-old friend, and bought a fourth privately from a seller who had in turn purchased it at a gun show, reports show.

588.text: GUN-11096 PRIMARY Romney seeks to reassure NRA, gun enthusiasts Former Massachusetts governor Mitt Romney was in St. Louis on Friday seeking to reassure the nation's largest gathering of gun owners that he would be an aggressive defender of gun rights if elected president - and that President Obama would be the opposite in a second term.           In an address to about 65,000 attendees of the annual meeting of the National Rifle Association, Romney pitched himself as a staunch defender of the right to bear arms. But as he works to pivot from primaries spent wooing conservatives to a general-election battle for independent votes, Romney spent most of the speech talking more broadly about economic and religious freedoms, using constitutional language that would appeal to NRA supporters to address a wider range of issues. "This president is moving us away from our Founders' vision. Instead of limited government, he is leading us toward limited freedom and limited opportunity," Romney said. "As president, the Constitution would be my guide, and the Declaration of Independence my compass." Romney's address came just three days after former U.S. senator Rick Santorum (Pa.) suspended his campaign, leaving Romney without any real challenge for the Republican nomination. According to Romney's campaign, the NRA speech was the first in a series of addresses the former governor will deliver in an attempt to crystallize the differences between him and Obama. Romney spoke about expanding the economy by reducing regulations and taxes, and he accused Obama of infringing on religious liberties.

590.text: NA-43018 PRIMARY The Gun Report: November 5, 2013 Guns are now allowed in public libraries in Seattle, thanks to a state supreme court ruling holding that cities have no right to ban guns in public places such as parks and libraries. The board of the library system said it did not have a choice but to remove its longstanding ban on firearms. Dave Workman, a gun rights advocate, told KOMO News that there's no reason guns shouldn't be allowed in libraries. "It's public property," he said. "Carrying a firearm is fundamental, individual civil right." But Pete Holmes, a city attorney, said he hopes that someday cities will be permitted to create their own gun controls. "It's just wrong, and it is something that can be fixed at the state level," Holmes said. Guns are still banned in schools and courthouses in Washington State. Here is today's report. -Jennifer Mascia A man who walked through a Paramus, N.J., mall firing a weapon and sending thousands of people running Monday night shot and killed himself early Tuesday. Richard Shoop, 20, entered the Garden State Plaza at around 9 p.m., shortly before the mall was scheduled to close, and roamed the hallways before taking his own life. No customers or employees were injured. - The New York Times 12-year-old Dario L. Datis shot and killed himself at a home in Lower Milford Township, Pa., Monday morning. "Any time a child loses their life, it is very difficult," said Leah Christman, the district superintendent.

593.text: GUN-5092 PRIMARY Editorials; No sport in assault guns An executive order issued this week by President Clinton brings hope that a million fewer military-style assault weapons will reach American streets. Clinton's directive plugs a dangerous loophole that allowed gun makers in other countries to get around the U.S. assault-weapon ban. Of course, the National Rifle Association responded to Clinton's order with well-rehearsed outrage and threatened to push Congress to undo the directive. If Congress is truly concerned about threats, it should ignore the gun lobby and heed the escalating public frustration with high-powered guns that kill a maximum number of people in minimum time. In the wake of the schoolyard rampage in Arkansas, lawmakers should think twice before donning pistol lapel pins and rhapsodizing about how rapid-fire machine guns protect our children. Although none of the firearms on Clinton's banned list were used in the Arkansas attack, military-style assault weapons constitute a daily threat to children and adults. Indeed, it was another wholesale slaughter of children that led to the ban on imports of assault weapons. In 1989, President George Bush banned imports of AK-47s and other semiautomatic assault rifles after a gunman opened fire with an AK-47 on a crowded schoolyard in Stockton, Calif., killing five children. Eventually, 43 types of weapons, including AK-47s, were banned, unless they met a sports exemption. To circumvent the ban, firearm manufacturers simply altered the appearance of their weapons to give them a sporty look.   Importers obtained permits to ship in 600,000 modified guns last year, and applications were pending for an additional million. Clinton's executive order stops 58 types of weapons.

592.text: GUN-2325 PRIMARY An opportunity for gun control Building on the long-delayed enactment of the Brady bill last year, the White House has begun 1994 with a new salvo aimed at getting handguns off the streets. The Brady bill imposes a five-day waiting period for a handgun purchase and raises to $ 200 from $ 30 the initial cost of a three-year federal license to deal in guns, with a $ 90 fee for renewal of a valid license. Now Treasury Secretary Lloyd Bentsen says the administration will ask Congress to increase the license fee to at least $ 600 per year, a move the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms estimates will discourage about 80 percent of the  nation's 250,000 licensed gun dealers from renewing. It's an excellent move, with growing public support, and Congress should  pass it. Most of the dealers who would fall by the wayside are, as Bentsen put  it, buying and selling out of their kitchens. Their only real objectives are  to be able to get guns they want for themselves at wholesale and to ship  firearms across state lines. At the moment, ATF has only 250 agents assigned  to inspect gun dealers and assure they are abiding by the law. That makes any  uniform, cohesive enforcement impossible. To help the process further, the administration will propose expanding the  ATF inspection force and seek sweeping improvements in record keeping to  enable better tracking of individual firearms. Legislation also would require  licensed dealers to abide by state and local ordinances and to share with ATF  agents information that might help trace weapons involved in crimes. Neither  of the latter provisions is required now, although a similar proposal was  passed by the Senate in anti-crime legislation pending in Congress.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

591.text: GUN-31260 PRIMARY Justices to Decide if State Gun Laws Violate Rights; Court, Which Reversed D.C. Ban, Will Look to Local Level in 2nd Amendment Case The Supreme Court set up a historic decision on gun control Wednesday, saying it will rule on whether restrictive state and local laws violate the Second Amendment right to gun ownership that it recognized last year. The landmark 2008 decision to strike down the District of Columbia's ban on handgun possession was the first time the court had said the amendment grants an individual right to own a gun for self-defense. But the 5 to 4 opinion in District of Columbia v. Heller did not address the question of whether the Second Amendment extends beyond the federal government and federal enclaves such as Washington. Most court observers say they think that the five justices who recognized the individual right will also find that the Second Amendment applies to state and local governments, a move that could spark challenges of state and local laws governing gun registration, how and when the weapons can be carried, and storage requirements. The court will hear a challenge of handgun laws in Chicago and the neighboring village of Oak Park, Ill. It was filed by Alexandria lawyer Alan Gura, who successfully argued the Heller case. He said the Chicago ban is "identical" to the one found unconstitutional in the District. The announcement came as the court prepared for its new term, which will officially begin on Monday. Justices sifted through more than 2,000 petitions accumulated through the summer and selected 10 to hear.

589.text: GUN-10189 PRIMARY Florida Expands Right to Use Deadly Force in Self-Defense Gov. Jeb Bush signed a bill on Tuesday giving Florida citizens more leeway to use deadly force in their homes and in public, a move that gun-control groups and several urban police chiefs warned would give rise to needless deaths. The measure, known as the ''stand your ground'' bill, lets people use guns or other deadly force to defend themselves in public places without first trying to escape. Floridians already had the right to defend themselves against home intruders under what is known as the castle doctrine, but until now, they could not do so in public. The National Rifle Association lobbied hard for the bill's passage, and Wayne LaPierre, the group's executive vice president, said it would use the victory to push for similar measures elsewhere. The bill's sponsor, Representative Dennis K. Baxley of Ocala, said it would curb violent crime and make citizens feel safer. ''It's a clear position that we will stand with victims of violent attacks when the law is in their favor,'' said Mr. Baxley, a Republican. '' People want to know we stand on the side of victims of crime instead of the side of criminals.'' Governor Bush, a Republican, said he supported the measure because when people faced life-threatening situations, ''to have to retreat and put yourself in a very precarious position defies common sense.'' But John F. Timoney, Miami's police chief, called the bill unnecessary and dangerous. Chief Timoney, who has successfully pushed his police officers to use less deadly force, said many people, including children, could become innocent victims. The bill could make gun owners, including drivers with road rage or drunken sports fans who get into fights leaving ball games, assume they have ''total immunity,'' he said.

594.text: GUN-5206 PRIMARY LETTERS, FAXES & E-MAIL COLORADO'S LENIENT JUDICIAL SYSTEM HELPS CRIME FLOURISH Boston reduced murders in the 24-and-under age group by more than 60 percent without new gun laws. We in the Denver area have the laws; we have enforcement. We lack a credible threat ofpunishment. "The Boston Gun Project" began in 1994 as a study by Harvard University's JFK School of Government. The study found that fewer than 1 percent of Boston youths were in gangs, but they were responsible for at least 60 percent of murders. Further, 75 percent of gang murders and victims had lengthy criminal records averaging 10 crimes each. Applying common sense, Boston focused on violent crimes in that 1 percent. Officials made the threat of punishment credible. Freddie Cardoza, 24, had a long history of violent felonies. He was found with a 9mm cartridge, and a U.S. district judge ultimately imposed an enhanced sentence, 19 years and seven months, no parole. The sentence was well publicized. Murders among that age group have dropped by two-thirds since then. If Matthaus Jaehnig were in prison because of his record of violent offenses, both he and Denver officer Bruce Vander Jagt would be alive. Others with long records have been apprehended, tried, convicted and then returned to society. Law enforcement must be extremely frustrated to see good police work thwarted by a lenient judicial system and a scare about overcrowded prisons.

595.text: GUN-1917 PRIMARY PANEL BACKS CITY-FOCUSED GUN CONTROL Calling firearm violence an urban epidemic, a St. Louis aldermanic committee wants state legislators to exempt the city from state firearms laws, thereby allowing the city to pass more stringent gun controls. On Thursday, the committee unanimously approved a resolution to that effect introduced by Alderman Mary Ross, D-5th Ward. Ross said she would soon ask elected officials in St. Louis County and Jackson County, which includes Kansas City, to back the resolution. If state lawmakers back the legislation, Ross said, the city would be exempt from state laws that ban a county or city from passing measures that are more strict than state laws. Ross long has supported gun control and has unsuccessfully tried to pass laws requiring gun owners to have trigger locks for firearms and ammunition sellers to keep detailed inventory of their sales. Such provisions now are illegal because they exceed the limits placed on guns by state law, Ross said. Ross said her resolution is aimed at lessening the resistance historically offered by rural lawmakers on gun-control measures. "By this method, we are not asking rural areas to change anything," Ross said. "But our problems are quite different than theirs. In rural areas, people hunt rabbits. In cities, people hunt people." Ross also stressed that her proposal was not aimed at sportsmen or collectors. Dr. William Kincaid, the city's public health director, said that gunshot wounds now are the leading cause of death of people 15 to 19 years old.   "We as a community have a problem," he said. "And we have to begin taking steps to solve it."

597.text: GUN-3524 PRIMARY Gov. Glendening on Handguns GOV. GLENDENING has proposed a strong, sensible and sorely needed set of handgun and assault-weapon measures including training, background checks, licensing and a one-gun-a-month limit on purchases. Nothing in these proposals applies in any way to hunting rifles or shotguns -- an important point already being blurred by opponents of any serious efforts to curb firearms violence. Like law enforcement authorities on the front lines, Marylanders should welcome these proposals to get serious about the marketing of lethal, concealable weapons. Gov. Glendening is alert to the concerns of gun owners and enthusiasts. But more and more threatened Americans are rejecting the NRA tactic of calling almost any effort to regulate gun sales a restriction of freedom. "Freedom in America is not found in a misinterpretation of the Second Amendment of the Constitution," the governor said. "Anyone who believes that our liberty depends on people going around armed to the teeth with Uzis and handguns does not know what freedom is really all about." Gun violence is not confined to the inner cities or to gang warfare, the governor notes, nor is it "about drug dealers killing drug dealers. This is about good, decent people -- including children and seniors -- being shot and killed by guns." According to State Police Superintendent David Mitchell, Marylanders who own guns are 47 times more likely to harm a family member than a criminal with that weapon. Isn't that reason enough to require that those who buy firearms understand how to use and store them?

600.text: GUN-10448 PRIMARY Another victory for the bulletproof NRA Even with Democrats in control of Washington, the National Rifle Association usually gets what it wants. Long regarded as one of the most powerful lobbying groups in town, the GOP-friendly NRA has had a remarkable series of political victories in recent months that underscore its continuing influence on Capitol Hill -- no matter which party holds the reins and no matter what policy issues are involved. The latest example came this week, when House Democrats announced a deal exempting the NRA and a handful of other large nonprofit groups from donor disclosure requirements in a proposed campaign-finance law. In exchange, the NRA agreed to back down from fomenting opposition to the bill. In addition, the gun-rights group helped push legislation through the Democratic Congress last year that lifted a decades-old ban on carrying concealed weapons in most national parks and wildlife refuges. It also effectively scuttled congressional representation for the District  by supporting pro-gun legislation attached to the bill; rebuffed attempts to stop the destruction of background-check records; and has blocked legislation to forbid gun purchases by people included on the government's terrorist watch list. The reason for the group's recent successes? "It's a well-known fact that it's bad politics and bad policy to be on the wrong side of the gun issue," said NRA spokesman Andrew Arulanandam, adding that lawmakers are responding to strong support for Second Amendment rights among voters.

599.text: GUN-20051 PRIMARY Poll finds wide support for mandatory gun-safety standards WASHINGTON - Americans want some form of firearms regulation, according to a poll published today in The New England Journal of Medicine. The survey was sponsored by several universities and organizations, including the Johns Hopkins Center for Gun Policy and Research and the National Opinion Research Center at the University of Chicago. In the survey, 1,200 people, including 338 gun owners, were questioned about legislation they would like to see: -- 88% want firearms child-proofed so small children can't fire them. -- 71% favor a law requiring that firearms be personalized. Known as smart guns, personalization prevents guns from being fired by an unauthorized person. -- 73% support a law requiring that all new handguns be equipped with indicators that tell whether the weapon is loaded. -- 82% favor magazine safety locks for all new pistols. Bullets can remain in the firing chamber even if the magazine is removed; the lock would prevent firing in those cases. Among the gun owners included in the survey, 80% supported child-proofing, 59% wanted a law requiring that firearms be personalized, 60% wanted load indicators and 75% wanted magazine safeties. The appearance of the survey in one of the nation's most prestigious medical journals is part of a trend in recent years by medical professionals seeking to have firearms seen as a public health issue. Dennis Henigan, general counsel at Handgun Control in Washington, said the survey shows "broad public support for idea that guns should be regulated as dangerous consumer products and should be subject to safety standards just like other dangerous consumer products."

characters: Johns Hopkins Center for Gun Policy and Research

598.text: GUN-11059 PRIMARY Florida: Task Force Is Chosen To Consider Gun Laws Gov. Rick Scott on Thursday named the members of a task force that will examine the state's gun's laws, including the expansive and controversial Stand Your Ground law, which has been cited in the Feb. 26 shooting death of Trayvon Martin by a neighborhood watch volunteer. The 17-member panel is being led by Lt. Gov. Jennifer Carroll; the vice chairman is the Rev. R. B. Holmes Jr., pastor of Bethel Missionary Baptist Church in Tallahassee, one of the state's largest churches. The task force will report back to the governor and the Legislature. Ms. Carroll said the group includes a mix of law enforcement officials, lawmakers, neighborhood watch leaders, defense lawyers and prosecutors, among others. She said it would begin its work on May 1 and hold public meetings around the state. '' We are not walking into this with any preconceived notions,'' Governor Scott said. This is a more complete version of the story than the one that appeared in print.

601.text: GUN-5807 PRIMARY House Endorses Tough Terms for Youth Gun Crime; House Rejects Crackdown on Entertainment Media The House yesterday approved tough mandatory penalties for young people convicted of using a gun in a crime but rejected new curbs on the entertainment media, as lawmakers began work on a wide-ranging package of measures aimed at curbing youth violence. The action came during an emotional, day-long House debate over juvenile justice legislation that has assumed center stage on Capitol Hill. The debate will continue today when lawmakers consider a series of closely contested measures on gun control, including competing plans to require background checks at gun shows. At stake yesterday was a juvenile justice bill that had stalled for two years until the Littleton, Colo., school shootings in April. The basic measure would provide an extra $ 1.5 billion to the states to help combat juvenile crime, but lawmakers worked though the evening on a series of amendments offering a variety of additional approaches to the problem. Final passage of the overall bill is not expected until today, or possibly Friday. On one of the most closely watched amendments, lawmakers approved 249 to 181 a plan by Rep. Bill McCollum (R-Fla.) to impose stricter penalties on juvenile offenders. Under the measure, the maximum penalty for juveniles convicted of illegally possessing a firearm would be increased to one year, while those convicted of possessing a firearm with the intent of taking it to school would face a maximum of five years. Also, juveniles as young as 14 could be tried as adults.

602.text: GUN-1042 PRIMARY 7 - DAY WAIT FOR GUNS SUFFERS SETBACK The National Rifle Association and its allies have been given an opportunity to block a long-awaited House vote today on a bill to create a national seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases. Under procedures adopted by the House Rules Committee, an alternative bill must be defeated before there can be a vote on the seven-day waiting period. The alternative, to be offered by Rep. Harley Staggers Jr., D-W.Va., calls for instant background checks of handgun purchasers. Staggers' bill is supported by the NRA. The Staggers proposal seems virtually certain to come up first, since only the approval of Staggers himself would cause it to be withdrawn from early consideration. Both sides said Tuesday the vote on the Staggers bill, once it reaches the House floor, is too close to call. "If I had to bet all my money, which isn't much, I wouldn't know what to do. It's that close," said Rep. Charles E. Schumer, D-N.Y. The Staggers amendment is "a ruse, it is simply intended to kill Brady," said Schumer. In its key decision on sending the two bills to the House floor, the Rules Committee voted to follow standard rules during floor debate. That means that if the House first passes the computer checks, supporters could force a vote on the seven-day waiting period only through a complicated series of parliamentary maneuvers that is given little chance of success.

607.text: GUN-30092 PRIMARY THANK THE NRA FOR 'GUNLANDIA' We still live, fearfully, in 'Gunlandia' - Guest column April 19 James Pettican's description of Florida as Gunlandia describes exactly what the National Rifle Association has been allowed to do in Florida, since I became involved in those issues in 1985. It started with guns in cars and spread to concealed handguns, then to guns in businesses and today to guns in schools. But it wasn't just more guns in more places that turned Florida into Gunlandia, it was also a campaign by the NRA and our Republican Legislature to cripple law enforcement's ability to track and arrest criminals before they committed crimes and by not allowing the passage of a single law that would prevent a gun from falling into the wrong hand. If that wasn't bad enough, the Legislature allowed the NRA pre-emption law to sail through, canceling all city and county gun control laws, leaving local law enforcement without a single statute to control guns and the mayhem they create in our communities. That is the main reason Florida is one of a half-dozen states that have unrelenting systemic gun violence. But in Gunlandia, the NRA and our Legislature have given us the right to shoot back at armed criminals. What more could a citizen ask for? Didn't we want more guns everywhere and more ill-trained and poorly vetted but armed people next to us at schools, at the workplace and at public outings?

603.text: GUN-20162 PRIMARY Ricochet from gun chat hits O'Donnell An ad agency that has done paid and pro bono work for Rosie O'Donnell's children's foundation severed the relationship Thursday, charging the talk host with "self-serving" tactics in her standoff with Tom Selleck on her Wednesday show. O'Donnell grilled Selleck about the National Rifle Association, for which he did an ad, and gun control. Selleck was unhappy, and so was Thomas J. Coyne III, 42, whose Coyne Communications has worked with O'Donnell for about two years, as recently as her Star Wars benefit Sunday in Chicago. He sent a letter Thursday to the For All Kids Foundation, saying O'Donnell "is entitled to any viewpoint she may have, or tries to create for television ratings," but he said she abused and exploited public airwaves. Coyne told USA TODAY Thursday he has no connection with the NRA, any gun lobby or with Selleck, but he said in the letter O'Donnell had "blindsided" Selleck, who behaved "in a truly professional manner." The letter said Coyne did not support O'Donnell's "immature thinking" with which he said she tried to influence her audience. O'Donnell apologized to Selleck on her Thursday show, as she did when he appeared Wednesday. Both Selleck and the show said he knew she was going to bring up gun issues. Coyne, whose Morristown, N.J., agency also works with Land Rover and Sony, earned $ 78,900 for his work with O'Donnell. He says O'Donnell is "hypocritical" because Kmart, for which she does ads, is a major seller of sport guns. O'Donnell spokeswoman Lois Smith said O'Donnell's goal is to make guns safer. She said O'Donnell and Kmart executives have discussed gun issues, and Kmart respects the star's views. Kmart hasn't sold handguns in two decades and has procedures like background checks in place.

604.text: NA-41035 PRIMARY PS .38 SPECIAL S.I. group votes for armed guards STATEN ISLAND residents voted Monday night for armed guards in schools, but the city's top education official said he'll shoot down the initiative. "It's not happening. Plain and simple ," said Schools Chancellor Dennis Walcott. "I can't be any clearer than that," he added, echoing Mayor Bloomberg's longstanding position in favor of gun control, not gun-toting guards. Walcott said armed guards are not needed: "We have safe schools." In the wake of the school shooting in Newtown, Conn., last month, the National Rifle Association called for arming school guards. Following the gun group's lead, Marlboro, N.J., voted last week to arm its school officers. The vote Monday by the Staten Island Community Education Council - approving its own controversial proposal to station 300 to 500 retired police officers at local schools - was symbolic because Bloomberg and Walcott can veto the council's recommendations. "It doesn't mean you will have guns in your school," Sam Pirozzolo, the education council's president, told about 60 parents before the 8-to-1 vote, with one abstention. "We are ignored by the Department of Education quite often." Michael Riley, who introduced the measure, said he knows nothing - not even armed guards - will guarantee absolute safety. "But we need to do something," he said. The proposal triggered a few outbursts from angry parents, but no major incidents disrupted the meeting. Most parents backed the measure. "We are not talking about guns in schools. We are talking about school security," said Nancy Rooney, 52. "Why do we need to repeat Columbine and Virginia Tech?"

606.text: NA-46223 PRIMARY Universities and the 'active shooter' scenario Now that the Washington version is over, it's time to dip back into a gun debate in which you might have more invested --- the one in Georgia. Before state lawmakers left Atlanta last month, they preserved a bill in a vat of amber --- ready to be taken out and given final passage when they reassemble in January --- intended to expand the list of places that concealed weaponry can be carried by those over the age of 21. Included on that list, for the first time, would be the campuses of public universities. The Board of Regents is dead set against this. Gov. Nathan Deal intends to negotiate some sort of compromise in the next several months. A few days ago, I received a furtive call from a ranking campus cop. "Parents don't understand what's about to happen," he said. "Things have changed. They've rewritten the book." So there was a book. That was something to track down, along with the fellow who wrote it. He's John Kane, an affable retired police lieutenant from Sacramento. Fifteen minutes of conversation turned into 40, and by the end, this much was clear: Set aside, if you will, the philosophical debate over whether private firearms belong on a university campus. You're still left with a potentially dangerous clash of tactics between Second Amendment enthusiasts and a newly aggressive police approach to dealing with crazed gunmen. It's something that state officials --- not to mention parents --- are obliged to take into account.

596.text: GUN-7217 PRIMARY For the Record Here's how some major bills fared recently in Congress and how local congressional members voted, as provided by Thomas's Roll Call Report Syndicate. NV means Not Voting. HOUSE VOTES GUN CONTROL For: 218 / Against: 205 Members urged a House-Senate conference committee to act within two weeks on a juvenile crime bill (HR 1501) that requires criminal background checks on buyers at weekend gun shows. The two chambers differ on whether to allot 72 or 24 hours for the computerized checks of police records. The bill began moving through Congress last April, soon after a massacre at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo. It cleared both chambers but has been stalled since August. A yes vote urged prompt action on a post-Columbine gun bill. MARYLAND Yes No NV Bartlett (R) * Cardin (D) * Ehrlich (R) * Gilchrest (R) * Hoyer (D) * Cummings (D) * Morella (R) * Wynn (D) * VIRGINIA Yes No NV Davis (R) * Moran (D) * Wolf (R) * Bateman (R) * Bliley (R) * PROPERTY RIGHTS For: 226 / Against: 182 The House passed a bill (HR 2372) making it easier for plaintiffs to win claims that government has illegally confiscated their property. The "takings" bill is an outgrowth of the GOP's 1995 Contract With America. In part, it gives developers and homeowners more tools to fend off restrictive zoning. The Constitution entitles property owners to "just compensation" if their holdings are confiscated.

609.text: GUN-10014 PRIMARY Police Deaths And Stalemate On Gun Laws LEAVE it to Albany. Two police officers are dead, killed by bullets from illegal guns, and the leaders in our state capital got caught playing politics as usual. Because if you pare away the emotions and the side issue of Assembly Speaker Sheldon Silver's unfortunate remark that ''I don't go to cops' funerals,'' what you've got here is classic Albany politics, but applied to a matter that has gone beyond the realm of the ordinary. Mr. Silver not only got himself into big trouble with his statement (which he tried to clarify by saying he did not consider funerals to be settings for photo ops), but has been accused of blocking legislation that would strengthen the penalties for the sale and possession of illegal guns. In fact, that is precisely what the Democratic speaker is doing: blocking legislation favored by the Republican-led Senate. But as always in Albany, there is a calculated objective. And this time, there is also an odd twist -- because Mr. Silver, portrayed as soft on criminals in a tabloid campaign, is pushing for a measure that would do more to regulate guns than the Senate version he's thwarting. That's hard to realize because the media-averse Mr. Silver seems not to realize he could use a communications makeover. He is opposing the Senate bill, actually Gov. George E. Pataki's bill, to get more. He wants not only tougher penalties for the sale or possession of illegal guns, but tighter restrictions on licensed guns -- on their sale in gun shops and at gun shows. For instance, he wants gun shops to open their records to law enforcement officials if one of their guns might have been used in a crime.

605.text: GUN-4466 PRIMARY Supervisors Reject Fingerprinting of Gun Applicants; Many Residents Oppose FBI Checks, as Sought by Law Enforcement Officials After nearly two hours of impassioned lobbying by Prince William residents opposed to a measure that would allow county police to fingerprint applicants for concealed weapons permits, the Board of County Supervisors struck down the measure last night. In a 4 to 4 vote, supervisors rejected a police department recommendation that applicants submit to state and FBI fingerprint checks. "We're being asked to impose our will on the good people of Prince William County without a just cause," said Supervisor E.S. "Ed" Wilbourn III (R-Gainesville). "I will vote with the facts and oppose the ordinance." "This is feel-good legislation that doesn't accomplish anything," echoed Supervisor Michele B. McQuigg (R-Occoquan) minutes before she also cast a no vote. State legislation passed earlier this year leaves it up to local officials to decide whether to continue to fingerprint weapons permit applicants. Until recently, Prince William law enforcement officials have sent applicants' fingerprints to the Virginia State Police for analysis, although that is less thorough than the FBI's national database check. Last month, the board rejected emergency legislation that would have allowed county police to fingerprint applicants until yesterday's public hearing was held and a vote taken. As a result, the county issued permits for more than three weeks without checking applicants' prints to see whether the prospective permit holder was a convicted felon. Last night's vote means that practice will continue.

608.text: GUN-4797 PRIMARY GUNS AND POLITICS; Some issues are dead losers for conservatives In an ad for San Francisco area Democrat Ellen Tauscher, Steve Sposato, whose wife was killed in a massacre at a San Francisco law firm in 1993, attacked incumbent Rep. Bill Baker for voting to repeal the assault weapon ban earlier this year. "This is the same guy I invited into my house to spend about 15 minutes with my 2-year-old. And he declined to do that," Sposato says. "I was hoping that he would see how an American dream turns into a shattered family when the weapons he's trying to protect are used the way they're designed to be used to kill a lot of people." Tauscher spent $ 140,000 to air the ad. She won. In a San Francisco newspaper before the election, Baker said that if he lost "it will be over the assault weapon ban." In New York, Rep. Dan Frisa, who represented the heavily Republican district where the Long Island Railroad massacre occurred in December 1993, lost to Carolyn McCarthy, the widow of a victim of the massacre. McCarthy, whose son was also seriously wounded, announced for the race after Frisa voted for repeal of the assault weapons ban. In Missouri, 20-year veteran Rep. Harold Volkmer of Missouri, an enthusiastic supporter of the National Rifle Association, was defeated. In 1988, 1991 and 1993, Volkmer led Democratic opposition to the Brady Act, which mandates a waiting period before purchasing handguns. He also voted against the ban on some assault-type weapons in 1994, introduced repeal legislation in 1995 and voted for repeal in 1996.

611.text: GUN-6432 PRIMARY THIS LAWSUIT OVER GUNS OUT OF LOBBYISTS' RANGE The National Rifle Association sees the utmost danger in everything - except guns, of course. So, naturally, an NRA mouthpiece predicted disaster if the NAACP's lawsuit against gunmakers succeeds. The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People says gunmakers and dealers market their weapons in ways that encourage illegal gun ownership. Dealers should restrict the number of guns sold to one person, the NAACP suit says, and dealers whose wares routinely end up at crime scenes should have their licenses lifted. '' If that theory of law is upheld in America," hyperventilated the NRA's Wayne LaPierre, "they'll never manufacture another car, and the pharmaceutical industry would be on the chopping block." The NAACP lawsuit could scare automakers and drug companies out of business? Not likely. Cars and medicines are safer because of the kind of common-sense government regulation the NRA claims would crush freedom. And the NAACP wants regulation, not money. Benjamin Nathaniel Smith's racist shooting spree justifies that demand. After being refused by a licensed dealer who performed a background check, Smith got his guns from a man who had bought 65 weapons over two years. A limit on sales to individuals would cut down on such illegal street sales. Others like Smith also can buy weapons at gun shows, which, thanks to lawmakers such as U.S. Rep. Mark Foley, R-West Palm Beach, can exploit loopholes exempting many sales from background checks. The NAACP, like many cities and counties that also have sued, wants the courts to order those loopholes closed.

615.text: NA-42737 PRIMARY A Senate in the Gun Lobby's Grip WASHINGTON SENATORS say they fear the N.R.A. and the gun lobby. But I think that fear must be nothing compared to the fear the first graders in Sandy Hook Elementary School felt as their lives ended in a hail of bullets. The fear that those children who survived the massacre must feel every time they remember their teachers stacking them into closets and bathrooms, whispering that they loved them, so that love would be the last thing the students heard if the gunman found them. On Wednesday, a minority of senators gave into fear and blocked common-sense legislation that would have made it harder for criminals and people with dangerous mental illnesses to get hold of deadly firearms -- a bill that could prevent future tragedies like those in Newtown, Conn., Aurora, Colo., Blacksburg, Va., and too many communities to count. Some of the senators who voted against the background-check amendments have met with grieving parents whose children were murdered at Sandy Hook, in Newtown. Some of the senators who voted no have also looked into my eyes as I talked about my experience being shot in the head at point-blank range in suburban Tucson two years ago, and expressed sympathy for the 18 other people shot besides me, 6 of whom died. These senators have heard from their constituents -- who polls show overwhelmingly favored expanding background checks. And still these senators decided to do nothing. Shame on them.

610.text: GUN-10975 PRIMARY New gun laws not the answer, industry leaders say at show New gun laws not the answer, industry leaders say at show LAS VEGAS - Since the Jan. 8 shooting rampage in Tucson, gun-control advocates have called for greater restrictions on firearms. But here at the Sands Expo and Convention Center, where the world's largest annual gun trade show opened this week, firearms industry representatives say that would be a misguided response to the crime that killed six people and wounded 13 others. The more than 55,000 attendees from the United States and 100 other countries are focused on promoting firearms, networking and doing business - and preventing what they believe would be an erosion of their gun-ownership rights. "What happened in Tucson was not a failure of gun-control laws," said Lawrence Keane, general counsel of the National Shooting Sports Foundation, which is sponsoring the gun show. "This was a failure of the mental-health system." Jared Lee Loughner, the 22-year-old who faces murder charges in the shooting that apparently targeted Rep. Gabrielle Giffords (D-Ariz.), displayed increasingly erratic behavior in the months and days leading up to the crime, including outbursts at a YMCA and disruptions in classrooms and libraries at Pima Community College. The Shooting, Hunting, Outdoor Trade Show and Conference (SHOT Show) comes at a time when people are buying guns at "near record levels," said Steve Sanetti, the president of the shooting sports foundation, the trade association for what he called a "$28 billion a year industry."

614.text: GUN-6736 PRIMARY Program to control crime, not guns, right on target IN ONE OF the more positive developments on the crime front, the Clinton administration has announced a new system under which it will assemble a database of firearm "fingerprints." This is not the techno-wizardry handgun opponents want in which "smart" guns identify their owners' fingerprints before firing. Rather, the Federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms will develop a computer database of the distinctive markings each gun leaves on shell casings when it is fired. Police departments and the administration believe this can help speed information at crime scenes when the only physical evidence is shell casings. Glock, a major gun maker, is cooperating with the government in its effort to assemble the database. "As long as this is aimed at crime control, not gun control, we will support it," said Glock general counsel Paul Januzzo. If that's the objective, so will we. Already, the makers of a constitutionally protected product --- guns --- are under fire by 28 governments, including Atlanta, and the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development. Unable to control crime in their jurisdictions, they want to shift responsibility to gun makers. One of their concerns is that serial numbers on guns are too easily obliterated. This new gun "fingerprint" could make that issue moot. They also want universal registration of gun owners. Recording the firing signature of every firearm would create a universal registration of guns, if not of gun owners. The latter is of little use in tracking criminals anyway, since they usually steal their weapons or otherwise acquire them illegally.

characters: Federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

613.text: GUN-7336 PRIMARY Columbine panel ducks gun question 24 House members urge more police in schools WASHINGTON - A bipartisan congressional panel that has been  studying youth violence since the Columbine High School shootings  called Thursday for placing more police officers in schools and  installing early-intervention programs to weed out potentially  dangerous students. But the 12 Republican and 12 Democratic lawmakers dodged what  they acknowledged was the most controversial issue of all: guns in  the schoolhouse. 'If we talked about guns, that would be all we could talk  about,' said Rep. Martin Frost, D-Texas, co-chairman of the group.   'And we would come out with a split decision.' That issue could be  addressed elsewhere, the legislators decided. With the gun issue on the shelf, the 24 lawmakers - who  included gun-rights advocate Rep. Bob Barr, R-Ga., and gun-control  advocate Rep. Jim Greenwood, R-Pa., the 'Bi-Partisan Working Group  on Youth Violence' agreed on a number of approaches to prevent  youth violence. Many of the recommendations call for local and  state governments to act, and almost all call for spending more money on schools. For example, the panel called for greater access to childhood  health and mental health programs for at-risk children. It also  urged parents and community groups to play a bigger role in  schools, saying some children need help because they 'may lack an  ideal support structure at home.' Rep. Tom Tancredo, R-Colo., who represents the Columbine  area, said he doubted that the report would stir great interest in  Washington because it lacks the word 'gun' and 'there is no  bombshell in it.'

617.text: GUN-12177 PRIMARY GUN LAWS; Gun makers adapt to restrictions Federal and state officials across the country are considering new gun control legislation in the wake of the school shooting in Newtown, Conn. No one knows exactly what it will look like. But there is one near-certainty: The gun industry will spin the legislation into profits. Not only will new laws spur scarcity and demand, but the industry will alter current firearms to fit new restrictions and offer new products that stimulate the consumer's interest.
