"These are companies that have shareholders and owners that want to meet consumer demand," said Bob Barr, a former Republican congressman from Georgia and gun industry supporter. "These companies will look for ways to keep their business viable." A precedent already exists. The 1994 Federal Assault Weapons Ban outlawed semi-automatic rifles with cosmetic features that resembled military rifles. The law also banned magazines for pistols and rifles that held more than 10 rounds. After a fevered sales period before the law took effect, gun manufacturers quickly began selling redesigned products that were legal but no less effective. Glock Inc. was particularly nimble in adapting to the new climate. In August 1994, the pistol maker, which has its United States offices in Smyrna, introduced a subcompact pistol with a magazine that held only 10 rounds. The smaller model shoots a round just as large as its full-sized cousins. But the slimmed down pistol is marketed as the perfect concealed-carry handgun. All of the firepower of the original, with none of the bulkiness.

612.text: GUN-6465 PRIMARY LETTERS, FAXES & E-MAIL RIGHT TO BEAR ARMS SPELLED OUT Ben Gelt and David Winkler feel that the current gun debate in our country is out of control because "extremists" on both sides seem unwilling to compromise ("Taking the gun message to D.C.," July 2 guest commentary). If unwillingness to compromise my rights under the Constitution makes me an extremist in the eyes of these two naive young men, then so be it. If they would spend as much time studying the history of how this nation was founded as they have on organizing anti-gun rallies, they would find that George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Benjamin Franklin and John Adams were labeled as "extremists" in their time. I believe those great patriots and statesmen, who gave us the Bill of Rights where the Second Amendment resides, would approve the words of a modern patriot and statesman, Sen. Barry Goldwater, who reminded all Americans that "extremism in the defense of liberty is no vice." Gelt and Winkler claim that gun laws should be "clear and concise, much like the U.S. Constitution." Well, boys, the right to keep and bear arms, and the prohibition of infringement thereof, is right there in the U.S. Constitution. As another great American, Casey Stengel, said, "You can look it up!" ANTHONY J. FABIAN Aurora  BETTER LATE THAN NEVER Kudos to the "Reinstate gun checks" editorial (July 1) for prodding the governor into ordering the Colorado Bureau of Investigation to do background checks on gun purchases. Better late than never, except for those three little Castle Rock girls. I wonder if those committee members, who decided they could save money by eliminating CBI background checks, are sleeping good now?

618.text: GUN-5572 PRIMARY History checks for gun buyers begin Nov. 30 WASHINGTON - A national system of instant background checks for gun buyers will begin Nov. 30 in time for a crush of holiday purchases. Federal officials anticipate customer complaints as they break in the routine. "Although it's not been easy, we are on schedule," Attorney General Janet Reno told her weekly news conference Thursday. She announced the system will be operating nationwide on the date set by the 1993 Brady Act, which established federal background checks for handgun purchasers. The new instant system, operated by the FBI and state governments, will encounter two crushing burdens from the day it replaces voluntary checks on handgun buyers made since 1993 by state and local police and sheriffs: The new law will double the number of checks because it requires background approvals not just for handgun buyers but also for those who buy long guns and shotguns. December is the busiest month of the year for gun sales, because hunting seasons coincide with Christmas buying.

616.text: GUN-6782 PRIMARY Barr says HUD lied about firearms suit; Georgian claims official had told Congress the housing agency would not take legal action against gun makers. Rep. Bob Barr is calling for an investigation into the federal government's plan to take part in a suit against gun makers. In particular, Barr wants to know whether a Housing Department lawyer lied to Congress about taking such legal action. In August, Gail Laster, counsel at the Department of Housing and Urban Development, told a House panel that the department "does not plan to bring any action on its own against the gun industry." Barr, a board member of the National Rifle Association, contends that Laster's statment amounts to "misleading, if not lying" to the House Government Reform Committee's subcommittee on criminal justice. Barr has asked the chairman of that panel, Rep. John Mica (R-Fla.), to investigate whether Laster acted on her own or whether this is part of a " department policy to lie to Congress." "This is a perfect example of the way the Clinton administration works," Barr said Thursday. "These people come up here and they are basically thumbing their nose at Congress. They're saying, 'We're going to tell you whatever we want. We're not going to tell the truth, and then we're going to go back and do whatever we want.' That is wrong." The HUD officials have a starkly different memory of Laster's April 4th testimony. Laster did tell the panel that the federal housing agency had no plans to file suit against the gun industry. But she did not rule out that a suit would be filed by one of the 3 million people who live in public housing developments across the country or by a local housing authority.

characters: Department of Housing and Urban Development

621.text: GUN-1468 PRIMARY Handgun Prohibition Sen. John H. Chafee's June's 10 column [op-ed] correctly stated that the proliferation of handguns is one of the greatest problems facing our nation today. Ideally, legislation will be passed that restricts handguns to law enforcement officers and, perhaps, to licensed recreational facilities. But such legislation must do more than prohibit handgun possession by all others. A gun-control law must also provide means for minimizing the illegal trade in guns that would otherwise surely increase due to its enactment. As with drugs, if the supply of handguns is restricted, the incentive to deal illegally in guns grows, with the possible result that only those people that the law is meant to protect us from have guns. Therefore, any law banning handguns must provide a continuous stream of resources if it is to be successful. The only shortcoming with Sen. Chafee's proposed legislation (at least as he described it) is that it will not implement a program to interdict the illegal supply of guns. Given the great costs already incurred because of handguns, investing these resources is not only necessary but also would likely result in a substantial net gain to society. BILL McCARRON SCOTT KINNEY Washington Sen. Chafee fails to point out that of the 66 million handguns in the United States, less than four-tenths of one percent are misused yearly. Because of this misuse, he wants to ban the other 99.6 percent of handguns that are owned by more than 30 million law-abiding American citizens. Sen. Chafee never once asked why these Americans are buying handguns at the rate of 2 million per year. Surveys in 1978 by Decision Making Information, confirmed by a 1978 Caddell survey, found that 58 percent of all handguns, owned by about 21 million Americans, are owned for protection. The same survey says that of all handguns, 13 percent, or 4.6 million, have actually been used for protection.

619.text: GUN-10749 PRIMARY Bloomberg Responds to Weekend of Shootings Mayor Bloomberg on Wednesday blamed last weekend's spate of violence on what he said was a national scourge of "young, disaffected people with guns." He said there were remedies, however, including stricter gun regulation and social programs like his new initiative to help black and Hispanic young men. Mr. Bloomberg said he had "spent half of last night'' on the phone speaking with local leaders across the city asking them to propose solutions to address the violence. A total of 67 people were shot between Friday and Monday and 13 of them died. Mr. Bloomberg said that violence was also caused by "an educational system that hasn't included everybody," a condition he suggested that his young men's initiative would attempt to redress. The initiative will include, among other things, assessing city schools based on the academic progress achieved by black and Hispanic male students. Mr. Bloomberg is helping to finance the $130 million program with $30 million of his own money. The billionaire investor George Soros has also donated $30 million, and the city is providing the balance. "We're going to do everything we can to long-term stop" the problems that lead to violence, Mr. Bloomberg said. "But short term we're not going to walk away from this." Mr. Bloomberg, who has been a prominent advocate for stricter gun laws through a group he founded, Mayors Against Illegal Guns, also noted the shooting on Tuesday at an IHOP in Nevada, which left five people dead. The shooting was carried out with an AK-47 assault rifle.

620.text: GUN-8172 PRIMARY For Lieberman, Guns Are No Easy Target; Senator Sometimes Aids Conn. Industry Vice presidential candidate Joseph I. Lieberman (D-Conn.) has a long record of support for gun control, making him a convincing advocate for a key plank in the Democratic platform. But when it comes to defending the interests of the firearms companies that make pistols, rifles and shotguns in Connecticut's "gun valley," Lieberman has sometimes acted like just another home-state senator defending his region's economic interests. He has helped keep one controversial gun off Congress's list of banned assault weapons. He voted to repeal a far-reaching District of Columbia law allowing victims of assault weapons violence to sue gunmakers. He defended the industry against a Democratic-led effort to prevent gun manufacturers facing liability lawsuits from declaring bankruptcy. And gun company representatives left a meeting with Lieberman last year convinced that he was on their side in opposing lawsuits by cities seeking to hold manufacturers liable for gun violence. Lieberman's spokesman says that is wrong but that the senator has "some sympathy" for the companies' plight. Lieberman declined to comment directly. His complex relationship with Connecticut weapons manufacturers is but one aspect of the pro-business record that has set him apart from many in his party. But with Vice President Gore strongly supporting increased pressure on the gun industry to make weapons safer and less accessible to criminals, Lieberman finds himself to some extent caught in the political cross-fire, pulled between his long-time support for a home-state industry and his current alliance with a candidate who has gone on the offensive against it.

623.text: NA-41146 PRIMARY Gabby's anti-gun ad hits Times Sq. GABBY GIFFORDS and Mark Kelly are going big in Times Square with a larger-than-life push to stem gun violence. The former Democratic congresswoman from Arizona, who narrowly survived a January 2011 shooting that left six people dead, will appear alongside her ex-astronaut husband on a huge billboard smack dab in the middle of the Crossroads of the World. The powerhouse pair hope the high-profile advertisement - with the message "ENOUGH. We need your help to stop gun violence" - will put pressure on Congress to pass gun control measures. The billboard, going up Friday, will urge passersby to text "Solutions" to "90975" to weigh in on the controversial firearms issue. "What other issue is supported by 90% of Americans?" Kelly said Thursday in a statement to the Daily News. "Universal background checks keep us all safer. And we need everyone's voice to be heard so Congress takes action, now." The top-shelf space at the Bow Tie Building was donated to Americans for Responsible Solutions, the political action committee created by Giffords and Kelly earlier this year. "We hope everyone who sees the billboard, in one of the most prominent intersections in the world, will see Gabby and Mark's call to action and tell their own representatives . . . how important it is to enact universal background checks," said Pia Carusone, executive director of the PAC.

622.text: NA-43578 PRIMARY In arguing for firearms restrictions, Obama points to Constitution In making his case Wednesday for tighter controls on gun ownership, President Obama turned to the document most often cited by firearms advocates in defense of gun rights - the Constitution. By doing so, Obama sought to turn a perceived political weakness - his image as an aloof intellectual - into a strength, and, at the same time, to turn a perceived strength of gun advocates - the constitutional right to bear arms - into a potential weakness. Citing a series of mass shootings, Obama listed several amendments, as well as the defining phrase of the Declaration of Independence, to argue that the right to bear arms should not compromise other rights. "We have the right to worship freely and safely - that right was denied to Sikhs in Oak Creek, Wisconsin," Obama said at a midday event. "The right to assemble peacefully - that right was denied shoppers in Clackamas, Oregon, and moviegoers in Aurora, Colorado." Obama added that "that most fundamental set of rights to life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness" were "denied to college students at Virginia Tech and high school students at Columbine and elementary school students in Newtown, and kids on street corners in Chicago on too frequent a basis to tolerate." "All the families who never imagined they'd lose a loved one to a bullet, those rights are at stake," he said. "We're responsible."

626.text: GUN-3946 PRIMARY A Life Saved, a Life Lost: Gun Issue Gets Personal Sharon-Jo Ramboz was a teen-ager when, home alone one day, she was beaten and left for dead by a man who had broken into the house. She was emotionally devastated and dropped out of school. Years later, in 1989, after she was married and had children, another man broke into her home, in suburban Maryland. This time she was ready. "I calmly walked to our closet where the firearm was stored," she testified today at a Congressional hearing on the proposed repeal of last year's ban on 19 varieties of assault weapons. "I took my Colt AR-15 semiautomatic rifle" and "inserted the magazine while I was in the closet. "I walked to the top of the stairs. Then I pulled back the bolt and, letting it go, chambered a round." That act, which loaded a bullet for firing, produced a sound loud and distinctive enough that the intruder uttered an expletive and fled. Mrs. Ramboz, 36, testified that the rifle had saved her life and those of her three children, whose pictures she displayed on the witness table. It is one of the types of assault weapons whose manufacture and sale were banned by the anticrime law enacted last year, a provision that Republicans are trying to repeal. Sitting behind Mrs. Ramboz in the audience today was another woman, Byrl Phillips-Taylor, from a rural county outside Richmond. Like Mrs. Ramboz, Mrs. Phillips-Taylor was wearing a red suit and white pumps. And like Mrs. Ramboz, she had an experience that year with one of the weapons that are now outlawed.

624.text: NA-42947 PRIMARY Colorado Lawmakers Ousted In Recall Vote Over Gun Law COLORADO SPRINGS -- Two Colorado Democrats who provided crucial support for a slate of tough new gun-control laws were voted out of office on Tuesday in a recall vote widely seen as a test of popular support for gun restrictions after mass shootings in a Colorado movie theater and a Connecticut elementary school. The election, which came five months after the United States Senate defeated several gun restrictions, handed another loss to gun-control supporters. It also gave moderate lawmakers across the country a warning about the political risks of voting for tougher gun laws. The recall elections ousted two Democratic state senators, John Morse and Angela Giron, and replaced them with Republicans. Both defeats were painful for Democrats -- Mr. Morse's because he had been Senate president, and Ms. Giron's because she represented a heavily Democratic, working-class slice of southern Colorado. In an emotional concession speech, Mr. Morse called the loss of his seat ''purely symbolic'' and defended the record of the last legislative session as ''phenomenal.'' ''We made Colorado safer from gun violence,'' he said afterward, as his supporters trickled away from a hotel ballroom here in his district. '' If it cost me my political career, that's a small price to pay.'' For advocates on both sides, the stakes in Tuesday's elections were far bigger than the fates of two state politicians. As money and national attention poured into Colorado, a state of hunters that has been stained by two mass shootings, the races became a symbol of the nation's bitter fight over gun control, with one side bolstered by Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg of New York and the other by the National Rifle Association.

627.text: GUN-3596 PRIMARY GRAMM LETTER HELPING NRA QUESTIONED Sen. Phil Gramm, a Republican presidential candidate, is allowing the National Rifle Association to use his name and title to solicit contributions and new members on "U.S. senator" letterhead. The gun lobby drafted, paid for and mailed a Gramm letter seeking donations as a "special birthday gift" for the NRA's chief lobbyist. Gun-control groups denounced the strategy as ethically questionable. But aides to the Texas lawmaker defended it as a common fund-raising technique. Gramm's undated letter praises the NRA's executive vice president, Wayne LaPierre, as "my friend ever since I came to Congress." Noting that LaPierre's 45th birthday is next month, Gramm asked recipients to give a friend or relative a gift membership in the NRA "as a special birthday gift to Wayne." He called on supporters to help the NRA, whose membership has declined, to recruit 100,000 new members by year's end. Gramm's letter was sent to NRA members on stationery marked "Phil Gramm U.S. Senator" with an eagle seal on top. The envelope also bears a "Phil Gramm, U.S. Senator" legend. However, despite its official appearance, the letterhead is not actually Senate stationery and the seal is not an official one. Both the gun lobby and Gramm's office said the letter was produced and mailed by the NRA.

625.text: GUN-4216 PRIMARY LETTERS; GUN LAWS; Applicants screened Two weeks ago I was angered by Cynthia Tucker's diatribe alleging the expected wholesale killing of police if the clarifications to the concealed weapons law were enacted. Last week I was outraged by the editorial "Governor 1 for 2 on gun vetoes." The lead paragraph bluntly stated that Gov. Zell Miller "signed into law a worse bill that makes it easier for everyone to carry concealed weapons." Everyone? The law clearly applies only to those individuals who have been or will be granted a weapons permit in this state or those issued in other states. The Georgia Bureau of Investigation conducts a background investigation of all applicants, and only those who pass this investigation are issued permits.   I assume other states have similar procedures to ensure that the criminal element is not vested with this right. Backing wrong nuts DANNY DOUGLAS, Cartersville In a letter to the editor, Congressman Bob Barr (R-Marietta) said let's " not attempt to find spurious vehicles to advance the anti-gun agenda." Two weeks earlier, the paper published a chart showing Barr as the only member of the Georgia congressional delegation to vote against continuing the farm support for peanut farmers. Barr supports the sale of Uzis, AK-47s and other street-sweeper- style assault weapons while opposing aid for the state's second-largest agricultural product. Sounds like the only nuts the congressman is interested in are the gun lobby's right fringe and the militia movement.

628.text: GUN-1853 PRIMARY Two-thirds favor banning assault rifles School, neighborhood violence shakes up attitudes on firearms Ronald Roberts, a 56-year-old Richmond County resident, owns a shotgun, a hunting rifle, a handgun and two other firearms. He also supports banning the sale of assault weapons and establishing a system of instant background checks for gun purchasers in Georgia. The National Rifle Association, the organization Roberts once belonged to, has vigorously opposed legislation that would accomplish those ends. "What's the purpose of [assault weapons] unless you're in a tunnel fighting the Viet Cong?" said Roberts, adding that "it probably wouldn't hurt" if all guns were registered. Re-evaluating attitudes Guns in schools, the Rodney King riots and the fear of crime in their own neighborhoods are leading Georgians to re-evaluate their attitudes toward guns, said those who participated in an Atlanta Journal- Constitution/Georgia State University poll. Nine in 10 support instant background checks and two-thirds favor banning semi-automatic assault weapons, according to the survey completed last week. More than 70 percent support making it a felony for minors to have guns and for adults to give them firearms. The poll, which has a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3 percentage points, showed that support for these proposed measures was widespread, crossing the lines of political affiliation, gender, race, age and geography. And there is some evidence that support for gun control measures is growing, particularly among white Georgians. "The NRA people are pulling people's chains when they say you need these [assault weapons] for hunting," said Jim Crawford, 40, a survey respondent from Moultrie who owns three guns, including a 12-gauge shotgun. Four years ago, a survey found 57 percent of Georgians in support of an assault rifle ban, with no significant difference between blacks and whites. The latest survey, which found two-thirds of Georgians favoring the ban, saw support among whites had grown to 69 percent from 55 percent in 1989. Blacks were unchanged at 58 percent.

629.text: NA-42970 PRIMARY Sheriffs Refuse To Enforce Laws On Gun Control GREELEY, Colo. -- When Sheriff John Cooke of Weld County explains in speeches why he is not enforcing the state's new gun laws, he holds up two 30-round magazines. One, he says, he had before July 1, when the law banning the possession, sale or transfer of the large-capacity magazines went into effect. The other, he ''maybe'' obtained afterward. He shuffles the magazines, which look identical, and then challenges the audience to tell the difference. ''How is a deputy or an officer supposed to know which is which?'' he asks. Colorado's package of gun laws, enacted this year after mass shootings in Aurora, Colo., and Newtown, Conn., has been hailed as a victory by advocates of gun control. But if Sheriff Cooke and a majority of the other county sheriffs in Colorado offer any indication, the new laws -- which mandate background checks for private gun transfers and outlaw magazines over 15 rounds -- may prove nearly irrelevant across much of the state's rural regions. Some sheriffs, like Sheriff Cooke, are refusing to enforce the laws, saying that they are too vague and violate Second Amendment rights. Many more say that enforcement will be ''a very low priority,'' as several sheriffs put it. All but seven of the 62 elected sheriffs in Colorado signed on in May to a federal lawsuit challenging the constitutionality of the statutes.

630.text: NA-41255 PRIMARY Son of a gun, Plax can relate WHEN PLAXICO Burress first heard that Knicks guard Raymond Felton was arrested on gun charges, he knew how "unfortunate" it really was, since he learned all about New York City's strict gun laws the hard way a little more than five years ago. The former Giants receiver spent 20 months in prison after shooting himself in the leg with an unlicensed gun in a Manhattan night club in November of 2008, and torpedoed Big Blue's chances that season of winning back-to-back Super Bowls. "It may not be easy but I believe that (Felton) will get through it,"Burress said in an interview on ESPN's "Outside the Lines" on Tuesday afternoon. During the "OTL" interview, Burress was shown at a speaking engagement talking about gun violence prevention, and he said, "I no longer own a firearm. I don't want to be associated with them." "I had been a gun owner since 2000," Burress said. "To be honest, at that time I didn't see anything wrong with owning a firearm at the time because it was done the right way. I had a license to carry the firearm, it was registered in the state of Florida. But coming here to New York City, not being aware of the laws or what could have happened to me if something of that nature happened, I don't think I would have made the same decision."

633.text: GUN-3616 PRIMARY ISLETON STOPS CHARGING $150 FOR CONCEALED-WEAPON PERMITS The city has been asked to stop charging $150 for permits to carry concealed weapons because state law sets the fee at $3. A regulatory fee that exceeds the cost of processing the permit amounts to a "special tax" and thus requires a two-thirds approval from voters, Deputy Attorney General Paul V. Bishop said in a letter to the police chief. Isleton, a city in Sacramento County, also charges $50 annually for permit renewal. The fees supply about a third of the city's yearly budget. The city projected earnings of $141,000 from gun permitting this fiscal year. The budget was passed Aug. 2 on a split vote because some felt relying on gun-permit money would be risky. The police department's budget was increased from $100,000 to $180,000 to pay for patrol car rentals and reserve officer salaries. The department also wanted to hire a narcotics officer. "I knew it was coming," said Councilman Carl Hendren, one of the dissenters. "Our city attorney told us three months ago what we were doing was illegal."

631.text: GUN-1004 PRIMARY Senate panel to take up Brady Bill; Gun measure likely to become part of Bush anti-crime package WASHINGTON - Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Joseph R. Biden Jr. (D-Del.) pledged Thursday to gain his committee's approval "as soon as I can" for an anti-crime package that includes the House-passed Brady Bill's seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases. President Bush has vowed to veto any gun control measure that reaches his desk independently of a series of anti-crime measures the White House has sought from Congress. The House passed the Brady Bill as a stand-alone measure Wednesday night. White House press secretary Marlin Fitzwater said, "Our position is the same on the veto, but we'll have to wait to see what the Senate does." Mr. Biden said there was "room for compromise" between conservatives and liberals on Mr. Bush's other anti-crime proposals, to which the Brady Bill could be annexed. That would open the way for swift Senate action and a House-Senate conference to resolve differences before Mr. Bush's suggested deadline of June 15. When the House approved the Brady Bill 239-186, it had the support of 60 Republicans and 179 Democrats. That was 56 more Republicans and 40 more Democrats than supported it three years previously. The Senate has never voted on the bill, named after James Brady, the White House press secretary who was disabled when he was shot during an assassination attempt against President Ronald Reagan in 1981. Brady Bill proponents acknowledge that their measure isn't perfect. It would not require police agencies to investigate the backgrounds of would-be gun buyers during the waiting period. But they say it would prevent gun sales to some felons and perhaps deter murders of passion.

632.text: GUN-10066 PRIMARY Spineless lawmakers keep giving in to the gun lobby Scientists say that a jellyfish is made up mostly of water, lacking heart, brain or bones, and that the largest of them is called the lion's mane, with a bell 8 feet wide. But what do they know? The largest, I say, is the U.S. Congress. The Florida Legislature is close behind. Like their nautical cousins, they go with the wind and are dangerous to anything in their way. The gun lobby has finally cowed the Congress into protecting firearm manufacturers and dealers from being sued even in state courts for carelessly selling guns to people like the Washington snipers. It will be fun to watch how the Roberts court deals with this assault on federalism. Florida, meanwhile, has a new state law saying it's okay to shoot to kill if you think you're in danger, not just in your home but anywhere. This doesn't forgive you for blowing away a police officer, but there's a catch. The officer must have "identified himself or herself." What if there wasn't time? Most legislators knew the bill went too far but no senators and only 20 House members, all of them Democrats from very safe districts, dared to vote against it. The gun lobby wants now to make it a crime - a felony, mind you! - for business people (or anyone else) to try to keep guns off their property if the weapons are otherwise lawful and locked in a car.

635.text: GUN-4039 PRIMARY It Took Superman to Rescue This One An appearance by two people formerly associated with the Republican Party saved the first night of the Democratic National Convention from virtually complete disaster. "Jim, we must have made a wrong turn," anti-gun activist Sarah Brady joked to her husband from the stage at United Center in Chicago. "This isn't San Diego." The crowd cheered, of course; the symbolism was sublime. Jim Brady had been press spokesman for Ronald Reagan when he was seriously injured in an assassination attempt on the president. As her husband sat by in a wheelchair, occasionally offering a comment of his own ("and a big-bear thumbs-up"), Sarah Brady delivered an effective speech on behalf of gun control and against the National Rifle Association. In addition, actor Christopher Reeve, seriously injured in a horse-riding accident, moved the crowd in the hall with a touching, though overlong, speech of his own. From his wheelchair, Reeve began on a light note in a halting voice. "Well, I just have to start with a challenge to the president," the former "Superman" star said. "I've seen your train go by, and I think I can beat it." Reeve defined "family values," a favorite catch phrase of the Republicans: "I think it means we're a family, and we all have value." The appearances so close together of the Bradys and Reeve had the unsavory aura of blatant emotional manipulation, like a Jerry Lewis telethon.

636.text: GUN-9235 PRIMARY Guns Used in Crimes To the Editor: Re "Illegal Guns and Liability" (editorial, Sept. 22): That most guns used in crimes in New York City were originally purchased out of state is only part of the story showing culpability of the gun industry. Studies of Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms data show that only 1 percent of dealers are responsible for more than 50 percent of illegal guns, yet the gun industry continues to supply these dirty dealers. Selective lawsuits that seek changes in industry behavior are necessary. Federal gun industry immunity legislation must be rejected, and City Council legislation that would establish a code of conduct and target lawsuits against those who contribute disproportionately to the city's illegal gun problem should be passed. JACQUELINE A. KUHLS New York, Sept. 23, 2003 The writer is director of public policy, New Yorkers Against Gun Violence.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

634.text: GUN-10212 PRIMARY Letters to the editor People's rights should supercede gun rights In a shooting rampage that resurrected memories of the Columbine school shootings in April 1999, a Minnesota teen killed nine and then himself last Monday. Eight of the 10 deaths occurred on school grounds -- five students, a teacher, a security guard, and the gunman. A dozen others were injured. It was the worst school shooting since Columbine and the worst of the multiple-shootings in the last few weeks. A total of 26 were killed by guns in Indiana, Ohio, Texas, Los Angeles, Chicago, Atlanta, Wisconsin and Dallas. Here in North Carolina, a Durham man allegedly killed his pregnant cousin and her twin brother. A Randolph County man killed himself after killing his wife. A similar incident occurred in Pilot Mountain. In 2002, 30,242 people died from firearm injuries, including 1,136 in North Carolina. Meanwhile, the NRA and other gun advocate groups have continued to assault our gun laws. A Senate bill (S. 397) would rob gun victims and their families of legal rights and give special protection to the gun industry and reckless gun dealers. For example, the D.C. sniper victims would have had no recourse against the gun dealer that "lost" the gun used by the snipers if this legislation had been in effect. Until people's rights become more important to our officials than gun rights, we can expect a continued level of gun violence that threatens our children, our communities and our nation.

638.text: GUN-6068 PRIMARY Support grows for gun control WASHINGTON - Two-thirds of Americans in a new poll, including three out of four women, say increasing restrictions on guns is more important than protecting the rights of Americans to own them. Support for gun control in the poll by the Pew Research Center for the People & the Press was slightly higher than in a Pew poll in December 1993. The percentage of people who feel television news contains too much violence also grew. The latest Pew poll was taken prior to Thursday's school shootings in Conyers, Ga. Three-quarters of Democrats in the poll said gun control is more important than protecting the rights of gun owners, an increase from the two-thirds who felt that way six years ago. Meanwhile, just over half of Republicans, 53 percent, said gun control was more important, compared with 47 percent six years ago.

637.text: GUN-11099 PRIMARY Sen. White attacked by group as anti-gun The state's most strident gun-rights group claims a Republican state senator who has had a concealed-weapons permit for 15 years is "anti-gun." Dudley Brown, director of the Rocky Mountain Gun Owners, mailed a letter to voters in Senate District 8 attacking Sen. Jean White's record on gun issues. "You and I are only one or two votes shy in the Colorado State Legislature of losing our right to keep and bear arms," Brown claimed. White countered with her own letter, saying her father taught her to "cherish" the Second Amendment and was thrilled when she obtained her concealed-carry permit. White, of Hayden, is competing with Rep. Randy Baumgardner of Cowdrey for the GOP nomination in the newly drawn northwestern Colorado Senate district. The gun group backs Baumgardner. They will be nominated for the ballot during one of multiple Republican assemblies being held today at the Colorado Convention Center. There will be nominating assemblies for other legislative races, district attorney seats and six of seven congressional districts. These assemblies involve districts that include multiple counties. To make the ballot outright, a candidate must get more than 30 percent of the delegate vote. Those who get between 10 and 30 percent can petition onto the ballot. Candidates falling under 10 percent are out of the race. The primary is in June. Brown said his group is involved in several legislative assemblies today to make sure the "most solid pro-gun candidate is nominated."

641.text: GUN-6937 PRIMARY GOP panned for gun-control inaction Clinton cites Columbine shootings WASHINGTON - Saying the shootings at Colorado's Columbine  High School were 'not an isolated incident,' President Clinton  Friday chastised the Republican congressional leadership for  failing to complete action on new gun-control laws. It was the second day in a row that the president, stung by  the Senate's rejection of a nuclear weapons test ban treaty,  lashed out at the GOP leadership. It likely won't be the last time  that the president and his party attempt to link Republicans with  inaction on gun legislation, White House aides said. The president's harsh words and invocation of the Columbine  shootings were seen here as a preview of a major Capitol Hill  event Democrats have planned for Tuesday, the day before the  six-month anniversary of Columbine. Democratic members of Congress  have invited 350 teenagers, most of whom have been involved in  programs to curb violence, to the event. Clinton, who will speak at what Democrats call 'the  Congressional Teen Summit on School Violence,' evoked the memory  of the Colorado shootings Friday as he appealed for Congress to  end its stalling on gun legislation. 'It has been six months since Littleton now,' the president  told a sympathetic White House audience. ' Congress has had more  than ample time to analyze and act on the elements of this  problem. 'They have had more than enough time to recognize that one of  the biggest problems of intentional and accidental violence  against our children is the appalling ease with which young people  can gain access to guns.'

644.text: GUN-9160 PRIMARY The Democrats and Gun Control Democratic presidential candidates are distancing themselves from gun control issues for political reasons, an Oct. 26 article suggests ["Democratic Hopefuls Play Down Gun Control," front page]. Citing the party's past embrace of gun control as a factor in numerous electoral losses, the story notes there are large numbers of gun owners in many 2004 swing states. The fear seems to be that sticking to one's political guns, so to speak, would lead to further losses at the polls. Perhaps the more moderate position most Democratic candidates have adopted is indeed inspired by political calculus and little more. It would be nice to think, however, that facts and principle had something to do with it. Maybe the candidates became aware of the estimated annual 2.5 million instances of defensive firearms use in the United States, or perhaps they read the Centers for Disease Control report that found gun control laws had little effect on crime, or maybe they researched the growing body of constitutional scholarship that demonstrates the country's founders sought to preserve an individual right to keep and bear arms. Possibly the candidates came to see that abrogating the second tenth of the Bill of Rights by extraconstitutional means threatens every right enshrined in the document, or perhaps they realized the way gun control adherents and many media outlets frame the debate is fundamentally unfair.

640.text: GUN-1025 PRIMARY GUN PROPAGANDA WAY OFF TARGET Why do the media so despise the Second Amendment to the U.S. Constitution? I am referring to a syndicated cartoon in the April 30 Palm Beach Post by Jim Borgman of the Cincinnati Enquirer. I am a law-abiding citizen who happens to enjoy the sports of shooting and hunting that were taught to me by my father, and taught to him by his father. The propaganda by the majority of the news media suggests that I am a crazed animal who is undeserving of my constitutional guarantees. People such as Jim Borgman and Editorial Page Editor Randy Schultz seem to feel that we should abdicate all of our constitutional guarantees and allow the government to decide what is best for us. When propaganda such as this begins to convince the public that it should trust those in office to know better what rights we truly need, I become afraid. Floyd A. Berus West Palm Beach\

646.text: NA-44263 PRIMARY The party line between Bloomberg and Schumer NEW YORK -- Chuck Schumer and Michael Bloomberg both believe in gun control. But only Schumer believes in political parties. That contrast  is at the heart of Schumer's recent criticism of Bloomberg's efforts to run ads against Democrats who voted against background checks. Schumer said in an interview with Time that the effort was not "effective." What he really means, of course, is that Bloomberg using his vast fortune to punish Democrats is politically certifiable and undercutting of Bloomberg's very own anti-gun agenda. The chances of a Republican Senate doing anything on gun control is approximately nil. By endangering some of the very Democrats that Schumer helped elect as architect of the current Democratic majority, Bloomberg is only guaranteeing the death of the issue he cares about perhaps most deeply. Bloomberg and those close to him argue that a Democratic majority is useless if it doesn't actually do anything on the issue, and that giving gun-control opponents a pass just because they belong to the Democratic Party is the actual madness. Schumer's criticism, and the appeal of other Senate Democratic leaders, has not changed Bloomberg's mind. At an event in Queens Thursday morning, I asked Bloomberg about Schumer's critique. "I think at the moment we are running more ads against Republicans than Democrats but let me just point out this is not a partisan issue," he said. "If you vote to continue the carnage on our streets I don't care what party you're in. To bring in partisanship when you are talking lives, lives and deaths and most of these are kids, just to me it doesn't make any sense. Chuck is perfectly capable of expressing himself."

642.text: GUN-2046 PRIMARY Verdict in Death of Student Reverberates Across Nation Three days after Rodney Peairs walked out of a Louisiana courtroom a free man in the shooting death of a Japanese exchange student, the case is reverberating from radio talk shows to newspaper editorial pages, raising difficult issues of law, justice and race in a nation with more than 200 million guns and where 4.2 million new guns are sold each year. Legal experts say that in cases like those of Mr. Peairs and Bernard Goetz, the New York subway gunman whose case became a cause celebre in 1984, jurors tend to sympathize with otherwise law-abiding people confronting what they believe is a threat in an increasingly violent society. "I think it was a fair verdict," said Karl Lavergne, a Baton Rouge construction worker who said he owned several guns, "It's unfortunate it happened. But I feel like in the days and times we live in now, when you see someone come to your door, that's the first thing you think of." Legally, the case turned on the ability citizens have virtually anywhere in the nation to use force to protect their homes from perceived threats. But to gun control groups and many citizens, the case has become a nightmarish glimpse of the fraying of the social fabric in an overly armed nation obsessed with crime. They argue that the case is a potent argument for limiting the availability of firearms that kill 65 Americans every day.

639.text: NA-43097 PRIMARY Do California's Gun Laws Prevent Gun Deaths? Do gun-control laws prevent gun deaths? Proving causality is tough, especially in the United States, which has long had an effective federal ban on gun research, engineered by the gun lobby and sustained by congressional majorities. But available data shows a clear link between strong gun laws and less gun carnage, and that's good enough for me. In the latest gun-control ranking of the states by the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence and the Law Center to Prevent Gun Violence, seven of the 10 states with the strongest gun laws in 2013 also had the lowest rate of gun deaths: Hawaii, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, New York, New Jersey, Connecticut and California. Conversely, many states with the weakest gun laws had the highest gun-death rates. The rankings are based on a state's performance in thirty gun-policy areas, including background checks on gun sales, reporting lost or stolen firearms, reducing risks to children, permitting concealed weapons in public places and prohibiting dangerous people from buying guns. California ranks first for the best gun laws and ninth for the lowest rate of gun death. Two new California state laws, signed this month by Governor Jerry Brown, will likely ensure its first place standing and improve its ninth place rank. One of the laws closes a loophole in California's "Unsafe Handgun Law." Under state law, handguns sold and manufactured in California must have safety features like loaded chamber indicators; meet specified drop safety tests and include micro-stamping technology to help solve gun crimes. But the law long exempted single shot handguns, a loophole that let gun dealers get around the standards by selling semiautomatic weapons that had been temporarily altered to fire one shot. After the sale, those weapons were easily converted back to semi-automatic form.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence
Law Center to Prevent Gun Violence

647.text: GUN-653 PRIMARY Residents must demand strict gun laws Enough is enough  We have to stop the senseless slaughter and maiming    of our children by guns. Daily, the numbers rise, and Floridians are    confronted with the results of stupid and insufficient laws and    lawmakers, paranoid and macho self-defense advocates, a National Rifle    Association out of control, and worst of all, good residents who can    look past the blood and the tears and see some "rights" being    threatened. As is their practice, our state legislators spent a busy two    months being wined and dined by lobbyists disguised as constituents. They were asked to do something about semi-automatic weapons, the tool    of choice for mass murders. Well, gee, gosh folks, you see it's really    difficult to decide which semi-automatics are people killers and which    are hunting weapons, you know, in case you want to take down an entire    herd. So, we'll just let this one go for another year while our newly    formed commission looks this question over. As the children fell, the paranoids among us still failed to see    the difference between being victimized, a sad but realistic fact of    life, and the unnecessary deaths of innocent children. Protect    yourselves, your property and your pride, by all means, but please give    a thought to that gun and what it might do. A law is not the answer    n n here; good common sense and a realistic weighing of the pros and cons    of weapon possession is.

645.text: GUN-9382 PRIMARY BUCKING THE NRA ASSAULT WEAPONS PRESIDENT George W. Bush surprised many when he decided to buck the National Rifle Association and throw his support behind a renewal of the ban on assault weapons. The NRA has made this issue one of its key legislative priorities. Mr. Bush isn't giving up much by siding with the anti-gun lobby on this issue. The Washington Post reports that GOP House leaders are thinking of not renewing the ban by dropping the issue and not bringing it to a vote. If Mr. Bush is sincere about wanting a ban, he should insist that these GOP leaders take up the issue. There are other real tests of Mr. Bush's commitment to rational gun-control laws. One is what he allows his attorney general, John Ashcroft, to do about federal background checks. Mr. Ashcroft wants to h ave federal background checks on gun sales destroyed after 24 hours. This would thwart efforts to track down suspected criminals, such as those behind the deadly attacks in the D.C. sniper case. Moreover, Mr. Ashcroft has offered a curiously literal interpretation of the Second Amendment's guarantee of gun ownership rights. Last December, a three-member panel of the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled unanimously that the amendment protects only the right of states to organize and maintain militias. Mr. Ashcroft thinks it protects an individual's gun rights.

643.text: GUN-277 PRIMARY Domestic Disarmament Even supporters of San Francisco's new law banning handguns say it is only symbolic and that its effect on crime will be minimal. The truth of that remains to be seen, but in the meantime, the symbolic value of such a law should neither be disparaged nor lost on Washington. The law gives San Franciscans 90 days to get rid of their guns and then would punish violators with fines up to $500 and jail terms up to 30 days. It marks the most dramatic advance of a growing domestic disarmament movement. In recent years, New Jersey, Connecticut, Hawaii, Massachusetts, New York and the District of Columbia have tightened gun restrictions. And forces for stronger gun laws have attracted support for their campaigns in places like Louisville, Ky., and Dade County, Fla., where such activity once was unthinkable. Tiny Morton Grove, Ill., gave the movement a major victory by approving a ban on handguns and prevailing over an important court challenge to it. Berkeley, Calif., also approved a ban on handguns. And in November, the entire state will vote on a referendum that would require registration of concealable firearms. Research shows that in some places the restrictions are followed by drops in gun crimes, but the effect of local gun control is often eroded by the easy availability of guns in neighboring jurisdictions. More than 20,000 gun laws are already on the books, and they vary widely. In that sense, a new proliferation of local laws amounts to a powerful argument for national legislation and makes clear its political feasibility. The next move is up to Congress.

648.text: GUN-3578 PRIMARY SENATE BACKS REPEALING LOCAL GUN LAWS; BILL GOES TO CONFERENCE COMMITTEE A bill to repeal most local gun laws that are stricter than state law was passed 23-9 Thursday by the Wisconsin Senate as hunters in blaze-orange clothing watched from a visitors' gallery. The bill could nullify local ordinances that regulate the firing of weapons within city limits, forbid short-barreled handguns, and require trigger locks and similar safety devices on weapons in storage. A Milwaukee ordinance, requiring handgun customers to wait seven days before taking possession of their purchases, would remain in effect until Nov. 30, 1998, under an amendment the Senate approved on a voice vote. The amendment means Assembly Bill 69 must return to the Assembly for consideration. Rep. DuWayne Johnsrud, R-Eastman, the bill's chief sponsor, said he reluctantly agreed to the amendment but predicted the Assembly would approve it. Other communities intending to adopt waiting-period ordinances like Milwaukee's might qualify under the amendment but only if they act before Gov. Tommy Thompson signs the bill into law. Regulations that could survive include state laws forbidding concealed weapons, carrying weapons in state and local government buildings, carrying or shooting a weapon while intoxicated and possessing a gun while in a tavern. The state requires a two-day waiting period for handgun purchasers. Supporters of the bill wore blaze-orange jackets, sweaters, hats and vests before they jammed into the Senate gallery as debate began. Blaze-orange garments are worn by hunters to avoid being accidentally mistaken for wild game in the woods.

649.text: GUN-6107 PRIMARY FULTON COUNTY NOTES Resolution supports handgun measure Fulton County has gone on record supporting pending federal legislation that attempts to control access by juveniles to handguns. The commission voted 4-2 recently to adopt a resolution, introduced by Commissioner Michael Hightower, to support a law making its way through Congress. If approved by the Republican-controlled Congress, the measure would raise the age at which a person can possess a handgun from 18 to 21, expand background checks and require a three-day waiting period. The county resolution was only the latest measure sponsored by Hightower with an eye toward tightening gun regulations. A few weeks ago, he asked his colleagues to support a measure, which was approved 4-3, to allow the county attorney to study whether Fulton could join the city of Atlanta's lawsuit against handgun manufacturers. The decision is pending. He also pushed through a resolution urging county gun owners to use safety locking devices on guns to prevent accidental firings. Commission Chairman Mike Kenn and Bob Fulton, both Northside Republicans, voted against Hightower's latest effort. "Even Republicans on the Hill are looking for responsible gun control," Hightower said. WORKING TOGETHER: Fulton County commissioners are forming partnerships with other agencies to deal with many of the county's urban woes. The commission recently gave its OK to form a partnership with Atlanta and DeKalb County to tackle the region's problems, and also endorsed a plan to collaborate with the Fulton health board to improve its AIDS treatment and prevention programs. Both approvals were contained in legislation introduced by Hightower.

650.text: GUN-9043 PRIMARY TAKING AIM AT ANTI-GUN RULINGS In a historic reversal of U.S. gun policy, the Justice Department argued to the Supreme Court this week that Americans have a right as individuals to own guns. The stand is contrary to a 1939 high court interpretation of the Second Amendment that the right to "keep and bear arms" applies only to citizens who are part of a "well-regulated militia." The new position - that the Constitution "broadly protects the rights of individuals to own firearms" - appears in a footnote to briefs prepared by Solictor General Ted Olson that were filed late Monday. At issue are two cases, one in Texas and one in Oklahoma, in which the defendants are asking the Supreme Court to rule federal gun laws unconstitutional. The Justice Department opposes the appeals for other reasons but is using the two cases to advance its new interpretation of the Second Amendment, which was elaborated last year by Attorney General John Ashcroft in a letter to the National Rifle Association. "While some have argued that the Second Amendment guarantees only a 'collective' right of the states to maintain militias, I believe the amendment's plain meaning and original intent prove otherwise," he wrote. Gun-control advocates were enraged at what they saw as an attempt to conceal the new gun politics. "This action is proof positive that the worst fears about Attorney General Ashcroft have come true," said Michael Barnes, president of the Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence, which promotes gun control. "His extreme ideology on guns has now become government policy."

characters: Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence

653.text: GUN-11560 PRIMARY What Colorado tells us about guns I was appalled to see The Post's editors cowering at the feet of a deranged individual who committed an irrational act of violence and calling once again for increased gun control ["Senseless," editorial, July 21].I spent 30 years in uniform defending the freedoms that we all enjoy here in the United States. With that freedom come responsibilities and risks. I have a God-given right (or, if you're a nonbeliever, a natural right) to protect myself and my family from harm. Criminals are called "lawbreakers" for good reason. All the laws on the books have failed to prevent criminals from acquiring and using illegal weapons. Therefore, what possible rational basis is there for imposing more restrictions on gun ownership by law-abiding citizens? These would only create more "gun-free" environments for criminals to exploit at will. Aurora, Colo., was just one more example of "When seconds count, the police are only minutes away." I, for one, refuse to be a victim. John D. Edgar, Stafford l We are often advised not to vote for a presidential candidate on the basis of a single issue. After another horrific mass shooting, I am tempted to ignore that advice and vote for whoever is most willing to fight to pass some sane and stricter gun-control laws. A candidate who has the guts to do that probably also has the guts, and the intelligence, to handle the other problems he or she would have to face as president.

651.text: GUN-1914 PRIMARY REGION IN BRIEF Mayor's cry in St. Pete: Ban gun sales The mayor of St. Petersburg, Fla., wants to ban all firearm sales within the city limits. "Society has run amok," Mayor David Fischer said, noting that he would need to get the city released from state laws regulating sales of guns and ammunition. "We've got to take a stand." He decided to stand up, he said, after learning that more guns were being found in middle schools than high schools in Dade County, home of Miami, and after seeing the results of a report that said youths were buying guns out of a car trunk in Ocala. Fischer plans to lobby for support from residents and state legislators, and he hopes the movement will spread to other cities. What would he ban? "All guns. It doesn't make a difference. Everything. Just guns." -SUITS UNLIMITED: Separate lawsuits relating to the Sunset Limited train disaster have been filed by the families of two Mississippi employees of Amtrak: John Wilson, 62, of Lyons and Ronald Quaintance, 44, of Jackson, who were among those killed in the Sept. 22 crash. The suits each seek damages of more than $ 50,000 for wrongful death, among other claims. The defendants in the suit include CSX Transportation Inc., which owns the track and bridge; Andrew Stabler, the pilot of the towboat MV Mauvilla, which apparently struck the bridge just before the crash; and Warrior and Gulf Navigation, the owner of the towboat. Meanwhile, members of the towboat crew are giving their account of the bridge collision to the National Transportation Safety Board.

652.text: GUN-10879 PRIMARY Where Md. gun bills die Despite the tragedy in Tucson and a rash of deadly shootings this year in Prince George's County, almost every gun-control bill proposed in Maryland has died - again - in a committee controlled by a Washington area defense lawyer whose firm has become highly successful at helping clients avoid lengthy prison terms for alleged gun crimes. Del. Joseph F. Vallario Jr.'s committee has quashed in recent weeks a bill to cut from 20 to 10 the number of rounds allowed in clips for semiautomatic weapons. It sidelined a measure to require gun dealers to track sales of firearms. And it shelved a bill, stemming from a fatal shooting last year at Johns Hopkins Hospital, to enhance penalties for carrying a gun onto a college campus or into a hospital. For a second time in three years, Vallario (D-Prince George's) also did not allow his committee to vote on a bill that police and prosecutors in Prince George's and in Baltimore said was a top priority for reducing homicides: a mandatory 18-month prison term for anyone convicted of carrying a loaded gun without a permit. In those three years, court records show, the chairman of the state's House Judiciary Committeewas the defense attorney of record at his Prince George's County law firm for nearly 30 defendants facing gun charges. Several would have had a hard time avoiding significantly longer prison terms had the mandatory-minimum bill that died in Vallario's committee three years ago become law.

654.text: GUN-5370 PRIMARY CHILD SAFETY THE ISSUE; Georgia Legislature offers no resistance to gun lobby Georgia made it look easy for the National Rifle Association. And now the state will again be held up to the nation as the sterotypical home of the good 'ol boys. A bill the NRA will try duplicate all over the country rolled through the Georgia Legislature on money-greased wheels. It prohibits local governments from suing gun manufacturers on a products liability theory. It's aimed at Atlanta, one of four cities that have filed such lawsuits. Atlanta's suit is limited to the failure to add safety locks to protect children, which may have a better chance of withstanding constitutional scrutiny. In Congress and in many states, it's mostly Republicans who side with the gun lobby. But in the South, the NRA gives heavily to Democrats too. The gun lobby's support for Georgia's top Democrats in the last election paid off big time. Newly elected Democratic Gov. Roy Barnes, a lifelong NRA member, received generous contributions from the lobby. So did Lt. Gov. Mark Taylor. But even state legislators who sided with the NRA for sincerely held policy reasons were taken aback by the lobby's unprecedented tactics. The morning of the final Senate vote on the bill, two NRA lobbyists were invited to address the Democratic Senate caucus. Old-timers say lobbyists have rarely, if ever, been invited to speak to a party caucus, and never in memory on a pending bill. The lobbyists reminded Democratic senators of the healthy financial support they had received.

655.text: GUN-2100 PRIMARY Poll: Owners favor gun laws Gun owners are siding with gun control advocates in the push for new restrictions on firearms, a new USA TODAY/ CNN/Gallup Poll shows. Fifty-seven percent of gun owners favor stricter gun laws, 36% want no change and 6% want less stringent laws. Among non-gun owners, 82% favor stricter gun control vs. 13% who want no change. Gun owners strongly oppose an outright ban on handguns _ a move favored by non-gun owners. But otherwise, gun owners come down solidly in favor of gun control. Among gun owners: 88% support the Brady Bill requiring a seven-day waiting period to buy a handgun. 60% favor a total ban on the possession of assault weapons. The New Jersey Senate upheld a similar law Monday. 60% favor limiting individuals to one gun purchase per month. Virginia's Legislature approved a one-handgun-a-month restriction last month. The poll reports that guns are found in about half of U.S. households, with some estimates as high as 200 million firearms in private hands. The survey finds gun control support strongest in cities, in the East and among women. More than one-third of those polled say they fear gun violence at home and at work. The National Rifle Association charges the poll is tilted to get pro-gun control answers. "You can get anything you want out of a poll. The questions were geared to get pro-gun control results," says NRA executive vice president Wayne LaPierre.

657.text: NA-42270 PRIMARY E.J. Dionne Jr.: Stand your ground laws are supposed to solve problems, not create them The law is supposed to solve problems, not create them. Laws should provide for as much clarity as possible, not expand the realms of ambiguity and subjectivity. Laws ought to bring about the practical results their promoters claim they'll achieve. And at its best, the law can help us to live together more harmoniously. By all these measures, "stand your ground" laws are a failure. These statutes make the already difficult task of jurors even harder. They aggravate mistrust across racial lines. They appear to increase rather than decrease crime. We should not have had to go through another racially charged trial in Florida to learn this. Writing in The Washington Post, Mark Berman offered a succinct account of the facts of the Michael Dunn case that has aroused so much legitimate passion. "In November 2012, Michael Dunn shot 17-year-old Jordan Davis in a Jacksonville, Fla., gas station parking lot. Dunn had approached a Dodge Durango holding Davis and three other teenagers and asked them to turn down their music. ... An argument developed, and Dunn fired 10 times at the vehicle, including multiple shots fired as it pulled away. "Davis died almost immediately after he was hit. ... Dunn, who was in town for a wedding, returned to his hotel and drove back home to Brevard County the following morning; he was arrested later that day." Dunn said he saw a shotgun in the SUV, but there was no evidence of one.

656.text: GUN-6682 PRIMARY COLO. GOVERNOR CHANGES STANCE ON GUNS Last April, Gov. Bill Owens was sitting in his office, ready to sign legislation to expand the right to carry concealed firearms. Then gunshots rang out at Columbine High School. The Republican governor didn't sign the measure on his desk, and asked lawmakers to take all gun legislation off the table, which they did. Now, nine months later, Owens has come out in support of gun-control measures. And despite some opposition from Owens' party, which controls both houses of the state legislature, the measures have a pretty good chance of passing. Columbine has clearly changed the politics of gun control in Colorado, a Western state that has long cherished the Second Amendment. "If you look at before Columbine, the legislature was looking at laws to make at least concealed weapons and some other aspects of guns easier," political pollster Paul Talmey said yesterday. "Whereas, today they are looking at a whole series of bills that would regulate guns, gun purchases." Owens said Columbine forced officials to "look at what we have on the books and ask ourselves, 'Are there areas where in fact, given this new examination, we do have some loopholes?' And in fact, I think there are." Two teenage gunmen stormed Columbine High School in suburban Littleton on April 20, killing 12 fellow students and a teacher before committing suicide. In the aftermath, Owens did his homework on weapons laws.

658.text: GUN-6495 PRIMARY How I Would Control Guns It's time to stand up to the National Rifle Association and enact common-sense gun control. Consider this: Every day in America, 13 children are killed by guns -- not injured, not hurt, but killed. Every day we suffer tragedy the equivalent of the one that took place at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo. While Washington is counting the political winners and losers from the recent gun "debate," parents are frightened that their child may be the next victim. Consider this: State agents at a gigantic gun show in California recently spent $ 4,000 in a matter of hours buying illegal assault weapons and machine guns and were offered a flame-thrower without once being required to submit to a background check. Consider this: California is expected to pass and sign into law a ban on Saturday night specials -- cheaply made handguns -- but Congress won't even consider it. Consider this: Eighty-five percent of Americans think handguns should be registered, but the Clinton administration won't propose it. I believe it's time to take bold steps to protect our children, families and communities. Here's what America must do: First, ban the distribution, sale and manufacture of all Saturday night special handguns. Second, require registration for every one of the 65 million handguns in this country. Third, require licensing with a safety course for every person who owns a handgun. Fourth, prohibit gun dealers from selling guns in residential neighborhoods. Fifth, insist on mandatory trigger locks for guns.

659.text: GUN-30141 PRIMARY Gun-buying checks, use of deadly force are OK'd in House measure The Missouri House on Tuesday moved to close a legal loophole similar to the one that allowed the Virginia Tech gunman to pass a weapons background check despite having mental health issues. The proposal would give the Missouri Highway Patrol more access to mental health records. It was packaged into a measure that would allow Missourians to use deadly force to defend themselves from intruders in their home. The bill was approved by the Missouri House 154-6. The legislation began in the Senate. The bill now returns there for consideration of House changes. Under the bill, any mental health records and files used in court proceedings could be sent directly to the Highway Patrol. The Highway Patrol and federally licensed gun dealers in Missouri report all firearms purchases to the FBI's National Instant Criminal Background Check System. "This type of information is helpful and probably would have been a useful tool if it would have been at place for the folks at Virginia Tech," said Capt. Tim Hull of the Highway Patrol. The deadly force bill is SB62 & 41.

660.text: GUN-2819 PRIMARY Assault guns in Clinton's sights / Congress is prodded for a ban President Clinton is taking aim at assault weapons. The White House has launched an all-out effort to win support for Thursday's House vote on a bill that would halt production and sale of 19 assault guns and copycat models. It also would limit detachable magazines to 10 rounds. The bill would outlaw rapid-fire weapons like the AK-47 and Uzi but exempt hundreds of hunting and recreational rifles. Similar provisions are in the Senate's crime bill. The White House says it's about 20 votes shy. "It's going to be tough and it's going to be close," Treasury Secretary Lloyd Bentsen said Sunday on NBC's Meet the Press. Bentsen said assault guns can carry as many as 90 rounds of ammunition. The law governing the hunting of migratory birds limits firearms to three shotgun shells, he said. "Why should we have laws that protect ducks more than human beings?" he asked. Clinton, who has made fighting crime a priority, says Congress can be prodded toward tougher gun controls in the wake of last November's passage of the Brady law. That measure, the first gun controls approved by Congress in more than 20 years, set a five-day waiting period before the purchase of handguns. To woo crucial votes of suburban Republicans and Southern Democrats, the administration is readying a public-relations blitz: -- Personal lobbying by Clinton of 76 House members.

662.text: GUN-10501 PRIMARY Gun rights rallies Dozens of Second Amendment rights activists holstered their handguns and slung unloaded rifles over their shoulders Monday at a gun rights rally in northern Virginia, while hundreds of like-minded but unarmed counterparts converged in the nation's capital. Signs reading "Which part of 'shall not be infringed' confuses you?" and bright orange stickers saying "Guns save lives" dotted the crowd at the Washington Monument. Across the Potomac River in Alexandria, former Alabama Minutemen leader Mike Vanderboegh told the crowd armed confrontation should be reserved only for instances of the government threatening people's lives. Mark Potok, spokesman and director of publications and information with the Southern Poverty Law Center, was online Monday, April 19, at 2:30 p.m. ET to discuss today's armed rally, politics and gun rights. ____________________ Mark Potok: Hi, Mark Potok here with the Southern Poverty Law Center to discuss the gun rallies around DC today and the rise of the antigovernment "Patriot" movement. I'm looking forward to answering your questions. _______________________ Chicago, Ill.: Lots of people have been stocking up on guns and ammo since Obama was elected. Just to play that out -- what do they plan to do with that stuff? Presumably they think they might have to shoot somebody, but who? Under what circumstances? What do they think might happen? I mean this literally -- I don't stock up on milk and toilet paper when it snows, for example, because there's no causal connection between snowfall and calcium deficiency (or GI problems). What exactly are these people afraid of? Thanks.

664.text: GUN-2550 PRIMARY Next, comprehensive reform of gun laws The gun lobby got sucker-punched by the U.S. Senate last weekend. It couldn't happen to a more deserving bunch. For seven years, gun advocates have thwarted the supersensible Brady Bill, which calls for a national waiting period on handgun purchases. Through a mix of political intimidation, political contributions and perverse constitutional reasoning, gun lobbyists were able to convince Congress to ignore the nine out of 10 Americans who support that idea. But suddenly, after two days of filibuster, the Senate abruptly adopted the Brady bill. The House has already acted, so all that remains is to do some slight tinkering in a House-Senate conference, and then it's off to the White House for President Clinton's signature. That's not the end of welcome gun control news, though. As part of the anti-crime bill adopted last week, the Senate agreed to ban the manufacture and sale of 19 types of assault-style semiautomatic weapons. Although these weapons constitute fewer than 1% of all guns in private hands, they figure in nearly 10% of all crime. The bill also bans some types of ammunition and restricts gun sales to, and ownership by, juveniles. These ideas are worthy, but they can't do the whole job. Waiting periods and background checks keep criminals from buying guns from legal dealers. Banning certain types of anti-personnel weapons and ammunition will keep those guns and bullets from growing more common and commonly lethal.

667.text: NA-42139 PRIMARY Tri-Valley letters: Isla Vista killings show access to guns still too loose Killings show access to guns still too loose The recent tragedy in Isla Vista occurred across the street from where my daughter lived for two years while attending UCSB and, for me, was the last straw. Some of my dearest friends own guns. They happen to be very responsible people, and I wouldn't want to deny them the right to own guns for hunting or self-defense, but something has to change. While the Second Amendment could be updated, repealing it is not the answer. But these tragedies have not had the effect I would expect in a civilized society. Instead of working to make us safer, our government, in response to Newtown, Virginia Tech, etc. has, in many cases, only made gun ownership and access easier. Had my child been killed in one of these incidents, and I saw how our nation responded, I would have gone crazy. The collateral damage of children and innocent people dying is not worth the right for almost anyone to get a gun as easily as possible. What to do? Start with making guns at least as difficult to get as a driver's license. Add rigor and time to the process. Require gun owners to register in a national database. Use technology to link weapons to owners. We can do many things TOWARD reducing gun violence. All of these suggestions might not have prevented Isla Vista, but if we continue doing nothing, we're actually making matters worse. As Richard Martinez pleaded, "Stop this madness -- we don't have to live like this!"

663.text: GUN-4785 PRIMARY NATION IN BRIEF; Justices to hear Brady Act case For seven years, James S. Brady and his wife, Sarah, fought for passage of national legislation to regulate the sale of handguns. They watched a bill requiring buyer-background checks die, get revived, cause a congressional standoff and finally, in 1993, become the first major gun control law in a quarter century. The Brady Act, named for the former press secretary who was disabled in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan, was immediately challenged by local sheriffs, who must perform the background checks, as an unconstitutional infringement on their power. Now, the most controversial gun control bill of the era has reached the Supreme Court in Washington, just as the justices are questioning whether Congress is imposing too much of its will on local authorities. Oral arguments on the case are set for Tuesday. Attorneys for Richard Mack, sheriff of Graham County, Ariz., and Ravalli County Sheriff Jay Printz in Montana say in their written filings that Congress has no power to force them to be part of the gun control policy and refer to a 1992 ruling that Congress "may not compel the states to enact or administer a federal regulatory program." MILITARY Unexploded bombs A slight temperature change, a good breeze or even shadows can set off explosions at the target ranges in Fort Bragg, N.C. That's because tens of thousands of dormant bombs - some the size of golf balls, some weighing 1,000 pounds - litter the scorched land after 75 years of constant bombardment. As a result, the military has begun trying to get rid of as many unexploded bombs as they can find on the base's 6,000 acres, The Charlotte Observer reported today. Last week, Marines and soldiers found 1,500 dud or unexploded bombs.

661.text: GUN-8515 PRIMARY Anti-gun letter was without substance Re: Gun programs already offered in our schools, Oct. 9 letter. Editor: Letter writer Arthur Hayhoe asks a lot of questions but delivers no substance. He should be answering questions such as his connection in the Sarah Brady group, how his organization is funded, and how is the money  spent? Just as Jesse Jackson's organization needs to do some accounting. Another question he should answer is whether he is paid for his anti-gun efforts and, if so, how much? His anti-gun efforts were recently rejected by the Polk County commissioners just as they were here in Pasco County. He has said that the Second Amendment and the Minutemen were a myth. I suggest he get a lesson in history. He opposes the Eddie Eagle Gun Safe program. He attacks NRA programs he evidently knows nothing about. He either forgets or does not know that there are U.S. senators and congressmen along with state elected officials who are NRA members. John F. Kennedy was an NRA member. The NRA is the world's premier gun safety organization. The "Protect the Second Amendment" license tag is not an NRA project. It is a grass roots project just as the freedom effort in 1776. We are volunteers fighting for something we believe in. What and who are you really, Mr. Hayhoe? Bill Bunting, president Second Amendment Club of America

665.text: GUN-2785 PRIMARY The Gun Minority Flexes Muscle Foreigners cannot understand it. Neither can Americans who live in cities where neighborhoods are terrorized by guns and gangs. How can it be that the overwhelming majority of Americans say they favor stronger Government restrictions on firearms, yet Congress even has trouble controlling civilian possession of rapid-fire weapons originally designed for military and police use. The answer is that Americans who oppose gun control tend to feel much more strongly about the issue than than do those who favor it. Politicians read polls showing that 80 percent of Americans favor outlawing assault weapons, but they doubt that very many of their constituents who are part of that 80 percent will vote for or against them solely on the basis of their vote on the issue. On the other hand, many politicians feel sure that their position on gun control will be the main factor determining how their constituents in the 20 percent minority will vote in the next election. Showdown in House "If you polled my district, it would be a tossup," said Representative Jim Chapman, a Democrat from rural East Texas. "But all the calls are against it." The matter will come to a head Thursday, when the House votes on whether to prohibit the manufacture, sale or possession of 19 types of weapons that were designed fully automatic weapons for military use but semiautomatic models of them are increasingly the weapons of street violence.

668.text: GUN-8818 PRIMARY Scolded author quits Emory An Emory University history professor has resigned after an outside academic panel issued a report condemning his research for a book that went to the heart of the national controversy over gun control. Emory officials said Friday that Michael Bellesiles' resignation is effective Dec. 31. They also released for the first time Friday the panel's 40-page report, dated July 10, and a statement from Bellesiles disputing the panel's findings. Bellesiles, who said he had enjoyed his 14 years at Emory, continued that he "cannot continue to teach in what I feel is a hostile environment." He said he will, instead, focus on his next book. Bellesiles wrote "Arming America: Origins of a National Gun Culture," an award-winning book that claimed early Americans did not possess or use firearms nearly as much as is popularly believed. The book, published in 2000, won the 2001 Bancroft Prize, the highest prize in the field of American history. Bellesiles contended that because guns were too expensive and hard to maintain, they did not become widespread among the American population until after the Civil War. Critics, including gun rights advocates and scholars, accused him of bias and sloppy research.
