Officials at Emory appointed an outside panel of scholars this spring to investigate the charges surrounding the book. Bellesiles has been on paid leave from Emory throughout the fall semester. The report said panelists could not determine whether Bellesiles falsified probate records from various locations around the country, as some critics have charged. But the panel did conclude that Bellesiles falsified a table detailing gun ownership because he omitted, without telling readers, gun counts in 1774-76.

666.text: GUN-835 PRIMARY HANDGUN OPPONENTS LAUD NEW POLICY A representative of two Missouri groups seeking tighter controls on handgun purchases applauded on Thursday St. Louis Sheriff James W. Murphy's decision to stiffen requirements for gun permits.   The representative, Eugene P. Schwartz, said that the measures were a strong step toward reducing what he called widespread deaths and injuries from handguns.   ''I think Sheriff Murphy should be commended,'' said Schwartz, chairman of the advisory board for the Missouri Council to Control Handguns and the Missouri Council on Firearms Safety.   Last month, Murphy enacted three procedures dealing with the issuance of handgun permits.   The sheriff initiated a seven-day waiting period for gun permits. Previously, permits often were issued immediately to applicants who had submitted to a records check by city police and had displayed proof of residency from the city Election Board.   Murphy also required that applicants submit two letters of recommendation from reputable residents - such as ministers or business people - who could vouch for their character. Sheriff's officials said a similar policy was in effect about a decade ago but was dropped after a few years.   The sheriff's office also is making checks of Missouri and national criminal records before issuing a gun permit. Previously, most record checks listed only convictions in St. Louis or St. Louis County.   Schwartz said Thursday that the waiting period was ''absolutely essential'' to screen applicants adequately.   Schwartz said that he wanted to see handgun sales reduced. He said that many Americans, including a growing number of children, have access to handguns. He said that that trend had led to large numbers of deliberate and accidental shootings and that many of those shootings occurred in homes.   ''It has become a symbol of power, rather than something just for sport,'' he said.   Schwartz said handguns make poor defensive weapons because they are difficult to aim in stressful situations. '' Even the police (sometimes) have problems shooting them properly,'' he said.   Schwartz said shotguns, which disperse several pellets from a single shell, are better choices for self-defense and are not so easily concealed. '' We have no problem with long guns,'' he said.   Also Thursday, a local spokesman of the National Rifle Association said that he had got 17 calls from people opposing the new measures. The spokesman, Willis Corbett, had criticized the new procedures.

characters: St. Louis Sheriff James W. Murphy

673.text: GUN-20196 PRIMARY Latest senseless slaughter screams for serious gun debate Throughout Thursday afternoon, the bad news from Atlanta worsened. By early evening, at least 12 people had been shot dead and another dozen injured in a gunman's rampage through the affluent Buckhead section and a southern suburb. Mark Barton, reportedly a distraught stock trader, was identified as the suspect. According to Atlanta's mayor, Bill Campbell, he used two powerful handguns. This is part of the true horror of these mad outbursts. Even in the first hours, as the body count was mounting and the descriptions of mayhem were beginning to unfold, it was obvious that the killings could not have been so extensive with any common weapon other than a firearm. No knife, club, axe or frying pan could ever be so deadly. Likewise it was obvious, once again with sorrow, why the country and its lawmakers need to bear down on the firearm debate. Each such shooting invites a string of self-righteous sound bites about the need to control guns or the right to bear them. But while lawmakers fiddle, guns continue to kill. There was no way of knowing immediately how the suspect acquired his weapons. Maybe he bought them legally, for his own pleasure and protection. But maybe he took advantage of the poor quality of current controls. Maybe he cheated the background check. Maybe he bought them from a reckless dealer. Or stole them unlocked from a neighbor. Other multiple slayings -- in Colorado, Illinois, Arkansas -- were empowered thus.

669.text: GUN-7366 PRIMARY Gun Curbs Reduce Youth Slayings, Study Says; President to Cite New Justice Dept. Report in Urging Congress to Enact New Controls Juvenile homicides and suicides decline when youths have less access to guns, according to a new Justice Department study that President Clinton will cite this morning when he urges congressional leaders to enact new gun control laws. White House sources said the study will help rebut the claims by gun advocacy groups that guns don't kill people, but people do. "Guns kill. In many cases, guns kill our children," says the report, "Kids and Guns," compiled by the Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention. The study found that juvenile homicides not involving firearms have remained fairly steady over the past two decades, at about 1,000 victims a year. But the number of juvenile homicides involving guns has changed significantly, from fewer than 600 in 1984 to about 1,700 in 1993 to just under 1,200 in 1997. Because many such killings are youth-on-youth crimes, the report said, juvenile access to guns--especially handguns--played an important role in both the sharp rise and the subsequent and somewhat less dramatic decline in gun-related juvenile homicides in the 1980s and 1990s. "The major legacy of the growth in juvenile homicides from 1987 through 1993 is that it increased the proportion of juveniles killed by firearms," the report says. Criminologists disagree on explanations of the steady drop in gun-related homicides commited by juveniles beginning in 1994. The report notes, however, that there was no similar decline in non-gun homicides by youths.

characters: Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention

671.text: GUN-4768 PRIMARY Why the Brady Law Is Constitutional This week, the Supreme Court heard arguments that the Brady law violates states' rights. The law imposes a five-day waiting period on handgun purchases to allow checks of criminal records. While most law enforcement organizations support the law, some county sheriffs oppose a provision that requires them to do the background checks. They object, as Justice Antonin Scalia puts it, that the Brady law "makes the states dance like marionettes on the fingers of the Federal Government." The sheriffs' argument should be rejected. The Brady law asks state and local law enforcement officers to make only reasonable and minimal efforts to aid in insuring that guns stay out of criminal hands. It makes sense to ask local sheriffs to check such things as state arrest records. It's not clear why states' rights advocates think a Federal bureaucracy would be any better. Background checks do not invade states' rights any more than Federal laws requiring local law enforcement authorities to report missing children or traffic fatalities. The reason is that, in the Brady law, the Federal Government has not commanded the states to make any policy. That sort of command, the Supreme Court has held, indeed would violate states' rights. In a 1992 decision, the Court held that Congress could not order the state of New York to pass laws regulating disposal of low-level radioactive waste or to take title to such waste itself. In an opinion by Justice Sandra Day O'Connor, the Court held that such a law wrongly "commandeers" the state's legislative process. To solve a national problem, Congress may not pass the buck to the states, because "state officials may bear the brunt of public disapproval," while Federal officials enjoy a free ride. Lines of electoral accountability are blurred: Voters won't know which set of bums to throw out.

670.text: GUN-6024 PRIMARY EDITORIALS Campbell, Allard misfire On the heels of yet another frightening school shooting, the Senate did the right thing by passing stronger gun-control laws, but we're disappointed in the continued lack of support for such measures by Colorado Republican Sens. Ben Nighthorse Campbell and Wayne Allard. The Conyers, Ga., shooting Thursday, in which a 15-year-old boy walked into his suburban high school with two guns and opened fire, injuring six students, most likely provided incentive on the Senate floor. The just-passed amendment requires background checks for all sales at gun shows. It also would require people reclaiming pawned guns to undergo background checks. The vote on the measure was split 50-50 and it took Vice President Al Gore's vote to break the impasse. Both Allard and Campbell voted against the amendment, claiming it will create a burden on law-abiding gun enthusiasts. Campbell said the measure forces gun sales further into the black market; Allard said the people are not served by passage of more gun laws when emotions are high. Allard voted in favor of the overall gun-control package, but against the crackdown on unregulated sales of firearms. Campbell voted no on both the full measure and the key amendment. The full package, which would ban importing high-capacity ammunition clips and deny any juvenile convicted of a felony the right to purchase a gun for life, would also pump $ 5 billion over five years into fighting juvenile crime and studying the impact of entertainment industry violence on the young.

672.text: GUN-7785 PRIMARY Mothers on the March; For Arundel Moms, Anti-Gun Rally Inspires Deeply Personal Response Laurie Hanley is doing it to let her kids know that Mommy and Daddy are in control. Tabetha Barncord is joining it in memory of her brother and for the sake of her mother. Donna Platner is doing it because she knows all too well how easily accidents can happen. And, of course, they all want to protect their children. Hanley, Barncord and Platner are just three of the hundreds of Anne Arundel mothers who are preparing to march on Washington this weekend to show Congress their support for handgun registration and licensing, safety training and trigger locks. About 200 county residents have signed up for Sunday's Million Mom March, said Hanley, the county's march coordinator, and organizers expect many to arrive with friends or family members, she said. In addition, Hanley said she is learning almost daily of other local groups, such as churches and schools, that will be busing down their own contingents, all in support of "common-sense" gun control laws. "You can't get more basic than protecting your children," she said. "This is something that hits a nerve or pushes a button. There are a lot of people who feel passionate about this--like me." Hanley was the first to sign up for the march in January, after reading about it online. The appeal was simple, the Odenton mother said; she wants to protect her 4-year-old and 14-month-old from the violence that has claimed so many of this country's youth in recent years.

674.text: GUN-5757 PRIMARY Pataki Orders Gun Sellers At Shows to Check Buyers Gov. George E. Pataki said today that he would require a background check for anyone who buys a firearm at a gun show held on state property. The Governor's comments came on the same day that the House of Representatives defeated, 280 to 147, a package of gun-control legislation. Under the current Federal laws, collectors and hobbyists who sell firearms at gun shows are not required to conduct criminal background checks on buyers, but licensed dealers who sell guns at the shows are required to conduct the checks. Mr. Pataki said his order "closes the loophole that exists under Federal law." The Governor's aides said he planned to submit a bill to the State Legislature if the efforts to regulate the sale of weapons at gun shows failed in Congress. His aides did not elaborate on those plans, and it was unclear how such a move would be received in the Legislature. Mr. Pataki's comments came in response to questions by reporters about a gun show scheduled for this weekend at the Empire State Plaza, a state-owned complex near the Capitol. The Governor's aides said there are only a handful of gun shows held on state property each year. This weekend's show, sponsored by a group called the New York State Arms Collectors, is expected to draw several thousand people over two days. Roughly 150 gun dealers have registered to attend the event, according to state officials.

677.text: GUN-8661 PRIMARY Finding Offshoots For Profit POLITICAL battles over gun ownership may rage, but there's one place where there's a consensus that guns are not a growth industry: the gun industry. Production for domestic and overseas handgun sales declined 52 percent between 1993 and 1999, according to the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. The number of licensed gun dealers has fallen to 104,000 from a high of 284,000 in 1992. Twenty years ago, according to the National Shooting Sports Foundation, 1 in 14 Americans was a licensed hunter. Today, it's 1 in 18. "The long-term prospects," said Tom Diaz, an analyst at the Violence Policy Center in Washington, "are bleak." Well, gun companies are, above all else, companies. And they're going to the mattresses. Not over the right to bear arms, though. The buzzword of the moment is diversification. Smith & Wesson, which started selling guns in 1852 and became a fixture of popular culture through Clint Eastwood's use of its .44 Magnum revolver in "Dirty Harry" movies, has opened retail stores where customers can buy the gun maker's logo on jewelry, gym bags, mountain bikes -- even teddy bears. (The company, whose sales plummeted after it pledged to install safety devices on its guns, was sold last month by its British owner, Tomkins P.L.C., to the Saf-T-Hammer Corporation, a start-up in Arizona that makes trigger locks.) Firearms from Fabbrica d'Armi Pietro Beretta S.p.A., the 475-year-old Italian arms manufacturer, can be found at the Beretta Gallery on Madison Avenue in Manhattan, a boutique with a facade of hand-chiseled stone extracted from quarries in Italy. But they are on the mezzanine. The main emphasis is selling other high-end items that seek to build on Beretta's patrician image: books on African game hunting, Beretta-branded sportswear, hand-painted porcelain and engraved knives.

679.text: GUN-8326 PRIMARY Who's who; Faces in the news Clinton praises Brady NEW YORK - Former President Clinton praised the efforts of gun control activist Sarah Brady at a party in Manhattan to celebrate publication of her new book, "A Good Fight." Clinton said Tuesday that Brady's 20-year campaign to keep guns out of the hands of criminals has "given the gift of life to countless thousands and thousands of Americans." Brady's husband, former White House press secretary James Brady, was wounded in the attempted assassination of President Reagan in 1981. The Bradys began a campaign for legislation restricting handgun purchases, which culminated during the Clinton administration with the signing of the Brady Law. Spears cites security LONDON - One day after fans booed Britney Spears at the London premiere of her film, her spokeswoman blamed the brief appearance on security concerns. About 3,000 fans had waited outside the Odeon Cinema in Leicester Square to see Spears on Monday night, some of them for hours. When Spears turned up an hour late, she was quickly whisked into the theater and did not sign autographs. Spears briefly appeared later on the theater's balcony before the screening of "Crossroads." But that didn't appease many of the fans, who were standing behind barriers below. Allen: Still film NYC LOS ANGELES - Woody Allen has definite ideas about how to film New York City without the World Trade Center as part of the skyline.

675.text: GUN-5164 PRIMARY COMMISSION SETS SIGHTS ON GUN CONTROL AMENDMENT Despite an intense lobbying barrage, the National Rifle Association and other gun-owner groups failed to shoot down a proposal Tuesday to change Florida's constitution so local governments can regulate gun sales. In many urban counties, the proposal, if approved by voters, probably would mean an end to the virtually unregulated flow of firearms at gun shows, which are exempt from background checks and waiting periods that apply to retail gun shops. The Constitution Revision Commission gave preliminary approval to a plan to allow counties and cities to impose a waiting period of three to five days and conduct background checks on any sale of firearms. Jubilant sponsors hugged each other and said it would help reduce violence in homes, streets and schools. Rural counties might not want it, they said, but the seven largest counties, including Palm Beach, are home to more than half the state's 2,059 deaths from handguns annually. Eighty-one percent of victims under age 24 are shot in those seven counties. ''This deals with saving lives,'' said sponsor Katherine Fernandez Rundle, Miami-Dade County state attorney and commission member. '' It's a way to prevent a lot of senseless violence from occurring.'' Gun lobbyists disagreed. ''This is a smokescreen,'' said NRA national President Marion Hammer, who is based in Tallahassee. '' This amendment would allow the local sheriff to investigate people's mental health records (as part of a background check). It would prevent private sales. It would mean if you wanted to trade guns with a friend or a neighbor, you would have to wait up to five days.''

676.text: GUN-6574 PRIMARY Background-check program on gun purchases advances Gov. Bill Owens' push to reauthorize the state's  criminal-background checks on gun purchases sailed smoothly  through the House Judiciary Committee on Tuesday. The committee endorsed the program on a unanimous vote,  brushing aside concerns of the National Rifle Association, which  objects to a part of the legislation that could block gun sales to  a small number of people even though they're eligible to buy a gun. 'It was easier than I thought,' said Rep. Steve Tool, a Fort  Collins Republican who is the House sponsor of the measure. It has  passed the Senate and now must go to the House Appropriations  Committee before it can go to the full House. The committee also endorsed a bill by Rep. Shawn Mitchell,  R-Broomfield, allowing illegal possession of a gun to be an  aggravating factor that prosecutors can use in trying to get  murderers sentenced to death. The bill, HB 1234, won on a 10-3 vote. The background-check program is expected to cost state  taxpayers about $ 1 million a year. It used to be paid for with a  fee on each gun purchase, but Owens has chosen to underwrite it  with tax dollars. The Insta-Check program was started in 1994 to avoid the  five-day waiting period on gun purchases that otherwise would have  been imposed on gun purchases by the federal Brady law. Last year, a Senate committee decided not to renew the  program, opting to go with the federal government's instant check  system. At the time, senators did not ask which system is safer.

678.text: GUN-4691 PRIMARY GUNS IN RIGHT HANDS PREVENT CRIME The emotional comments in recent letters favoring a ban on gun ownership in the wake of a shooting at a West Palm Beach public school amaze me. Professor Gary Kleck of Florida State University, formerly anti-gun, says that violent crime has been reduced 40 percent by honest citizens carrying concealed weapons. Another study spanning 15 or more years, by Dr. J. Lott and David Mustard and released last August in the University of Chicago Journal of Legal Studies, concludes that citizens allowed to carry concealed handguns deter violent crimes to the tune of 1,570 murders, 4,177 rapes and 60,000 aggravated assaults fewer each year, at a savings of $ 6.214 billion. Non-emotional research statistics supplied by the FBI Uniform Crime Report have shown, contrary to Sarah Brady's Handgun Control Inc. propaganda, that the armed citizen prevents violent crime. It is a tragedy when anyone, especially a child, is harmed. When that happens, we must search out the cause. You would not give a child the keys to a car or a chain saw to play with, nor should a child be allowed access to a lethal weapon. Daniel Herrmann Boynton Beach

681.text: GUN-1111 PRIMARY Import of Fake M-16s Banned; Customs Cites Safety Threat in Japanese Toy Guns While Congress, the White House and the gun and anti-gun factions wrangle over the issue of gun control, the U.S. Customs Service has decided that hundreds of toy M-16 rifles pose such a safety threat that it has banned their import. The imitation rifles, manufactured in Japan and destined for an unnamed firm that sells replicas to gun collectors, were prohibited from passing through Customs because they failed to meet regulations that enable police to distinquish them from the real thing and because they possibly could be converted into a weapon, the service said. "The resemblance of the so-called toy to the real thing is uncanny," Customs Service Commissioner Carol B. Hallett said at a news conference this week. ". . . The consequences arising from the use of imitation weapons are not amusing and pleasurable -- they are serious, and, in many cases, fatal." Hallett said the Customs Service will work with police to reduce accidental police shootings of individuals carrying fake guns "by working to keep look-alike firearms outside our borders." The Coalition to Stop Gun Violence, a gun-control group, endorsed the action. "It's not a bad thing, it's just seems their priorities are a little skewed," said Jeffrey Muchnick, legislative director for the coalition. "It's not the toy guns that are killing people." With the exception of BB guns and antique replicas, toy guns manufactured in the United States and abroad must contain a bright orange plug in the barrel, which allows police to tell it is a toy. The guns in question had the orange plug, but the coloring could be scraped off, the Customs Service discovered.

characters: Customs Service Commissioner Carol B. Hallett

682.text: GUN-322 PRIMARY ILLINOIS TOWN FACES LAWSUIT AFTER LIMITING PISTOL USE This quiet village of 23,000 people decided recently to ban the sale and possession of pistols, and with that decision it thrust itself into the center of a long and acrimonious national debate. ''We felt gun control would have to be a grass-roots effort, as with child labor and pollution laws, and wanted to send a message to other villages and towns that they could enact such ordinances,'' said Don Sneider, one of the six trustees of Morton Grove, which is a few miles northwest of Chicago. ''We felt that maybe this would be a way to get the message to the state and to Congress,'' he said. A Quick Reaction The village passed the two ordinances June 8. Before the end of the next day, Victor D. Quilici, a competition pistol shooter and lawyer, had filed suit challenging the ordinances on the ground that they violated the Bill of Rights provisions of both the Illinois and United States Constitutions. The National Rifle Association, a long-standing foe of gun control, has since contributed $2,500 to Mr. Quilici in support of the lawsuit, and association attorneys are arranging more financial and legal assistance, according to a spokesman, John Adkins. ''We are focusing our attention on Morton Grove,'' Mr. Adkins said, ''because their actions exemplify what we believe is the first step toward banning all gun possession.''

683.text: NA-42763 PRIMARY Gun Makers Saw No Role In Curbing Improper Sales The Glock executive testified that he would keep doing business with a gun dealer who had been indicted on a charge of violating firearms laws because ''This is still America'' and ''You're still innocent until proven guilty.'' The president of Sturm, Ruger was not interested in knowing how often the police traced guns back to the company's distributors, saying it ''wouldn't show us anything.'' And a top executive for Taurus International said his company made no attempt to learn if dealers who sell its products were involved in gun trafficking on the black market. '' I don't even know what a gun trafficker is,'' he said. The world's firearms manufacturers have been largely silent in the debate over gun violence. But their voices emerge from thousands of pages of depositions in a series of liability lawsuits a decade ago, before Congress passed a law shielding them from such suits in 2005, and the only time many of them were forced to answer such questions. Much of the testimony was marked confidential, and transcripts were packed away in archives at law firms and courthouses around the country. But a review of the documents, which were obtained by The New York Times, shows the industry's leaders arguing, often with detachment and defiance, that their companies bear little responsibility, beyond what the law requires, for monitoring the distributors and dealers who sell their guns to the public.

684.text: GUN-6136 PRIMARY Putting Your Money Where Your Politics Are Large percentages of Americans favor limits on handgun sales, according to most polls. But how strong are those feelings? If you deplore pistols, would you own a mutual fund that invests in companies that produce them? That question is being asked by the Calvert Group of mutual funds in Bethesda. Calvert offers eight funds that specialize in "socially responsible investing"--buying stocks that do no harm. Its Web site (www.calvertgroup.com) contains a search service called "Know What You Own," based on data developed by the Investor Responsibility Research Center in Washington. You can enter the name of your U.S. or international mutual fund and find out if it owns any one of eight publicly traded, civilian gun or ammunition manufacturers. Here's the research center's U.S. list: Blount International, Olin Corp. and Sturm, Ruger & Co. The international companies: Creditanstalt AG, IMI PLC, SIG Schweizerische Industrie, SNC Lavalin Group and Tomkins PLC. (Tomkins also trades in the United States.) At latest count, 249 mutual funds own at least one of these stocks, Calvert spokeswoman Elizabeth Laurienzo told my associate, Dori Perrucci. The largest number of deaths resulting from injuries in the United States is associated with motor vehicles, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in Atlanta. The fatality count came to 43,591 in 1997 (the last year for which data is available). Guns came in second, with 32,436 deaths (suicides, homicides and accidents). Handguns have been blamed for nearly 75 percent of the body count.

characters: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

685.text: GUN-31430 PRIMARY OUR OPINIONS: A militia of one; Second Amendment ruling will let loose a fusillade of challenges to sensible gun laws Few sentences in the English language have been parsed more intently than "A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed." On Thursday, the U.S. Supreme Court settled the argument over the definition of the Second Amendment, ruling that the right to bear arms extends beyond states to individual gun owners. Written by the Founding Fathers at a time when the country had no standing army and a firearm was a muzzle-loaded musket, the Second Amendment has now been broadened to uphold gun ownership for personal defense as well as national defense. The 5-to-4 landmark decision states that the Second Amendment protects an individual right to possess a firearm for self-defense within the home. The ruling overturns a Washington, D.C., law that essentially banned handguns in homes. But the ruling does not immediately change much, especially here in Georgia, where gun rights have always been sacrosanct. In his majority opinion, Justice Antonin Scalia wrote that the constitutional right to bear arms disallows "the absolute prohibition of handguns held and used for self-defense in the home." However, he also wrote that "it is not a right to keep and carry any weapon whatsoever in any manner whatsoever and for whatever purpose." Likely anticipating that the gun lobby would seize on his ruling as a means to attack virtually any gun law, Scalia cautioned that "the court's opinion should not be taken to cast doubt on long-standing prohibitions on the possession of firearms by felons and the mentally ill, or laws forbidding the carrying of firearms in sensitive places such as schools and government buildings."

680.text: GUN-3158 PRIMARY Study Links Rise in Killings to Relaxed Gun Laws The number of people killed by guns rose after laws making it easier for people to carry concealed weapons went into effect in four of five urban areas that were studied by the University of Maryland, researchers reported today. The university's Violence Research Group studied homicides committed by gun and by other methods before and after the weapons laws were relaxed in three Florida cities, Jacksonville, Miami and Tampa, and in Jackson, Miss., and Portland, Ore. The average monthly number of homicides by gun increased 74 percent in Jacksonville, 43 percent in Jackson, 22 percent in Tampa and 3 percent in Miami, the study said. In Portland, this type of homicide declined by 12 percent, the researchers said. The study also found that while homicides by gun mostly increased after the less-restrictive laws were adopted, homicides committed by other means remained steady. A University of Maryland criminologist, David McDowall, said: "While advocates of these relaxed laws argue that they will prevent crime, and suggest that they have reduced homicides in areas that adopted them, we strongly suggest caution. When states weaken limits on concealed weapons, they may be giving up a simple and effective method of preventing firearm deaths." Alaska, Arizona, Tennessee and Wyoming relaxed their laws on concealed weapons in 1994, as did Idaho and Montana in 1993. The legislatures in Colorado, Texas and Virginia are working on measures to ease restrictions on concealed guns, and the Governors of Virginia and Texas have indicated their willingness to sign such legislation. In Colorado, El Paso County recently adopted that state's most lenient policy on concealed weapons and was flooded with applications for permits.

689.text: GUN-3383 PRIMARY HAROLD L. VOLKMER THE FLOOD OF 1993 may have smeared mud over Midwestern flood plains, but it helped polish the legislative reputation of Rep. Harold L.Volkmer, D-Mo. Long known for his Rambo-esque defense of assault weapons and other National Rifle Association guns of choice, the nine-term congressman from Hannibal, hit the bull's eye last year with his bill to expand federal programs to promote buyouts of flood-prone property. "Volkmer took the lead on a bill that has helped shift federal flood policies more toward clearing off flood plains," says Scott Faber, flood plain expert with the American Rivers, a conservation group that sought changes in flood policy. Volkmer's straight-shooting on the buyout bill did not prevent him from getting caught in the congressional crossfire over the crime bill and its ban on semi-automatic assault weapons. The Missourian fought a losing battle to kill the assault-weapon ban and the bill. While he acknowledges that few Missouri hunters regularly use assault weapons when they stalk rabbits or deer, Volkmer contends that "some of those banned weapons aren't as powerful as my own .30-06" semiautomatic rifle. Because it holds only five rounds of ammunition, his rifle is not among the guns banned by Congress, Volkmer says. An NRA official once called Volkmer the "Cadillac" of gun-backers in Congress. He has bagged hefty campaign contributions and expenses-paid trips from the NRA, and, since 1987, he has been a board member of the Firearms Civil Rights Legal Defense Fund, which has worked closely with the NRA to help defend some gun owners.

688.text: GUN-10872 PRIMARY COMMON SENSE IS NO MATCH FOR GUN LOBBY The news was huge. A new law would allow people to roam Florida packing pistols on their hips, for all the world to see. The shopper next to you at the grocery store could saunter down the aisle sporting a .45. Disney World would have to post signs at the gate saying NO GUNS ALLOWED. A headline warned this about the state of our state: The Wild West? Yes. Prosecutors and police tasked with handling the resulting mayhem did not like the idea. Politicians wrung their hands about the fate of tourism should Florida's unofficial motto become: the Gunslinger State. So when all this happened here 24 years ago, the so-called Wild West Loophole in a new gun law was hastily closed. And that was that. So you would think. But here we are in the New Wild West, where apparently nothing made right can't be made wrong all over again. A pending bill embraced by those who believe there's no such thing as too many guns would make it okay for anyone with a permit to carry a concealed weapon to carry that weapon in the open. Again, police are appalled and prosecutors alarmed. Even some gun rights advocates have said this is not a good idea. And maybe it's the ringing of gunshots, but Tallahassee seems to have gone deaf. Big supporters of the Second Amendment - heck, even Polk County's uber-law-and-order Sheriff Grady Judd, self-professed advocate of the National Rifle Association - have explained the potential problems as patiently as a parent talking to a child:

687.text: NA-41735 PRIMARY Pa. Senate closes session with flurry of activity HARRISBURG - The state Senate closed out its 2013-14 session by passing more than a dozen pieces of legislation, many of them crime-related, including bills covering local gun ordinances, domestic violence, and animal cruelty. Most of the bills approved Thursday are headed for Gov. Corbett's desk, but others must receive final votes in the House, which returns for its last voting day Monday. Among the most controversial pieces of legislation moved by the Senate was a measure to give legal standing to "membership organizations," including the National Rifle Association, to sue municipalities if they enact gun laws stricter than state law. Philadelphia is one of 30 municipalities that have enacted ordinances mandating the reporting of lost and stolen weapons over the last several years since efforts failed to win passage of state gun-control laws. The bill passed, 32-14, after a contentious debate led by Philadelphia lawmakers, who said it would intimidate local municipalities trying to find ways to deal with gun violence. The bill, which also awards legal fees and damages to the plaintiff without proof of injury, will be considered by the House on Monday. The Senate also moved a fast-tracked bill prompted by convicted Philadelphia cop-killer Mumia Abu-Jamal's commencement address to a Vermont college two weeks ago. The "Revictimization Relief Act" would allow crime victims to petition courts to halt activities of a felon causing them "mental anguish." Democrats voting against the bill said it unfairly limits free-speech rights.

686.text: GUN-7168 PRIMARY EDITORIAL Pumping up gun bill The Senate Judiciary Committee deserves praise for doing a  180-degree turn and strengthening a bill that will allow local  police and prosecutors to enforce federal laws banning people from  buying guns for criminals and minors. The committee, which had earlier voted to make it harder for  local law enforcement to take action against straw purchases,  voted again, reversing their original decision. The Columbine High School massacre, in which Dylan Klebold and  Eric Harris killed 12 students and a teacher before turning the  guns on themselves, has been used as an example during the push  for this bill. But the fact that 18-year-old Robyn Anderson was  never charged for buying three of the long-barreled guns used in  the April 20 shooting spree really wouldn't have changed under  this bill. Straw purchases already are illegal under federal law when  guns are bought from federally licensed dealers, but enforcement  by the U.S. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms' agents is not  as aggressive as we would like it to be. The measure would broaden  enforcement by allowing local police and prosecutors to enforce  the federal ban on straw purchases. So even if this type of law would not have made a difference  in the aftermath of the Columbine shooting, it's still a good and  valid law-enforcement tool to have in place. State Sen. Bryan Sullivant, R-Breckenridge, and Rep. Richard  Decker, R-Colorado Springs, are the co-sponsors of HB 1214. The  bill is supported by the National Rifle Association.

characters: U.S. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

690.text: GUN-8580 PRIMARY Brady Law works as it is As Bert Lance used to say, "If it ain't broke, don't fix it." The Brady Law background check system for handgun purchases isn't broken. To the contrary, it has stopped gun purchases by 600,000 lawbreakers. So why does Attorney General John Ashcroft want to weaken the law? The obvious answer is that he was doing the bidding of the National Rifle Association, giving the gun lobby what it failed to win in court. Law enforcement agencies had been retaining gun-purchase records for auditing purposes for up to 180 days (the time limit dropped to 90 days last month). But Ashcroft has decided the records should be held for only one business day after the sale. The attorney general claimed the one-day limit was sufficient for law enforcement purposes and would protect the privacy of legitimate gun owners. One day is not enough time for the FBI to audit records to make sure checks were performed properly. Among other things, they try to determine if a gun buyer won approval by using a false identification. This system gives applicants more than the benefit of the doubt - so much so in fact that the Department of Justice estimates about 45,000 applicants with open arrest records slipped through the cracks and purchased guns between April and December of 2000. The less time officials have to verify an applicant's background, the more mistakes will be made.

692.text: GUN-1995 PRIMARY Good reason for gun sales This "issue" is being blown out of proportion by gun-control advocates. Because of that, I find myself in a defensive mode far too often lately - defending a course of action which may result in equipping my officers with much-needed equipment in an attempt to stay on an almost-even keel with the bad guys and girls. Politicians often run on "anti-crime" or "law enforcement" platforms, but, once elected, the police community gets slam-dunked with empty promises and even emptier pockets. We have to revert to whatever means are available to best equip the patrol officer faced with enforcing the law. Police are taken for granted because we have been doing the job year after year with little or no support from the bureaucrats. Here in Greenfield, the town government supports the selling of seized weapons to help buy equipment. We are not selling guns in open bidding. I am going to take the high bid from a federally licensed dealer for the entire collection, 90% of which is hunting-type weapons, such as shotguns, and the remainder is collector weapons. Assault weapons, sawed-off shotguns or weapons used in the commission of any criminal act will be destroyed. We have taken some heat for our "idea," but we have not seen anyone come forward with deep pockets to equip my officers with necessities like a bullet-proof vest for responding alone to a domestic complaint or helmets for our special-response unit when dealing with a hostage situation.

694.text: GUN-10794 PRIMARY Don't blame the NRA The June 26 editorial "A better gun agency" reported that the Obama administration and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) surveilled straw gun purchases that took place in Phoenix and then "lost track of" 2,500 such weapons, "some of which have now been traced to criminal activity south of the border." Despite this scandal, The Post editorial board absurdly concluded that the ATF needs more power and that National Rifle Association (NRA) members are to blame for the ATF's weakness because they're too "paranoid" about losing their Second Amendment rights. Most reasonable people would process these facts and alarming lack of competence and conclude that the ATF needs less power and that the NRA should be commended for its vigilant pursuit of the truth surrounding the ATF's gun-running operation. But not The Post. As for gun owner "paranoia," there would be less reason for worry if The Post ceased its anti-Second Amendment campaign. After all, the paper railed against the Supreme Court's 2008 decision affirming Americans' individual right to keep and bear arms, and  it advocated for the draconian laws that robbed D.C. residents of their Second Amendment rights. Chris W. Cox, Fairfax The writer is executive director of the National Rifle Association Institute for Legislative Action.

696.text: GUN-4791 PRIMARY The Brady Law Prevents Crime John Lott is wrong to claim that the Brady law and the assault-weapons ban have not reduced gun crimes ["What Deters Criminals?" letters, Oct. 31]. Further, his suggestion that making it easier to carry concealed handguns dramatically reduces murder, rape and aggravated assault is not substantiated by his research. His previous claim that his research provides "direct empirical evidence" that the Brady law is associated with an increase in aggravated assault is as flawed as his study. Waiting periods and background checks are designed to block felons and other prohibited purchasers from the easiest, most direct route to the broadest selection of handguns -- the retail market. And we know these interventions work. The Brady law, requiring a five-day waiting period and background check, has stopped more than 100,000 prohibited purchasers (including 72,325 felons, or 85 felons a day) from walking out the door of a gun shop with a handgun. Analysis of the FBI's crime data provides new evidence that the Brady law is working. Between 1985 and 1993, the use of guns in violent crime rose significantly. However, since implementation of the Brady law, the percentage of crimes committed with a firearm has dropped more than the concurrent decline in the violent crime rate. In fact, since enactment of Brady, the overall proportion of aggravated assaults involving a firearm has fallen by 8.8 percent, while gun use in robbery and murder has fallen by 3.3 percent and 2 percent respectively.

695.text: GUN-7365 PRIMARY Why have gun laws if criminals ignore them? Re: When will we learn to amend gun laws?, March 3 letter Editor: When will Elizabeth Booth of Holiday, and others who think like she does, learn that the criminal by his very definition will not obey any gun laws! As I understand the situation, there are at the present time over 20,000 gun laws on the books in the United States. Show me any criminal who obeys the law and I will show you an idiot! The gun used to kill that 6-year-old girl was, according to the St. Petersburg Times, a stolen weapon. All the gun laws in the world will not stop the criminal from stealing guns. Let's face it, gun laws are for honest people. Carl J. Morgan, New Port Richey Bearing arms is a God-given right Re: When will we learn to amend gun laws? Editor: The hysteria fanned by the media, self-serving politicians and people that have adopted a "government will take care of us" mentality continue to distort facts and obfuscate the obvious in their pursuit to erode our most basic of liberties. It defies logic and common sense that any citizen of this country could actually believe that any portion of the Constitution of the United States and the Bill of Rights is archaic or invalid. If we use the same logic people have presented here against the Second Amendment, one could argue that people subscribing to Judeo-Christian beliefs and ethics are behind the times for believing in the Ten Commandments and/or portions of the New Testament.

691.text: GUN-10430 PRIMARY High court leaves Philadelphia leeway on gun laws While solidly affirming an individual's right to bear arms, the U.S. Supreme Court left a bit of wiggle room on Monday for states and cities, such as Philadelphia, to impose some restrictions on firearms. But in the 5-4 ruling against Chicago's blanket ban on handguns, the high court did not lay out a framework on exactly which less-restrictive gun-control laws might pass muster under the Second Amendment. "There's not a whole lot of guidance" about what will constitute "reasonable regulation," said University of Richmond law professor Carl W. Tobias. "That just means, I think, we're going to see a lot of litigation." In Pennsylvania there has been a widening movement by towns and cities, including Philadelphia, to pass ordinances that require gun owners to report lost or stolen handguns. Earlier this month, the state Supreme Court affirmed Philadelphia's law mandating the reporting of lost or stolen guns when it refused to hear a National Rifle Association challenge to a lower-court decision. Joe Grace, executive director of the gun-control group CeaseFire PA, said he believed such "reasonable restrictions" ultimately would be permitted. "We believe that reasonable steps like requiring the reporting of lost or stolen guns to police will be held to be constitutionally permissible if it ever gets there," Grace said. Kim Stolfer, chairman of Firearms Owners Against Crime, said he did not believe the reporting requirement would survive scrutiny by the nation's highest court.

693.text: GUN-20213 PRIMARY Suits threaten our freedoms The separation of powers has stood as the cornerstone of our democracy. It is the concept that keeps this country strong and free from the dregs of tyranny. Yet, as we enter a new millennium, powerful special interests seek to overturn this idea, and, with it, our nation's Constitution and the freedom it guarantees. More than the Second Amendment is at stake when cities and states file lawsuits against gun manufacturers. The entire Constitution and the principles underlying it are, too. In this country, the power to make laws and regulate business rests with Congress and state legislatures. These harassment lawsuits are nothing more than backhanded attempts to circumvent this process and use the courts to regulate businesses. That's why a Cincinnati judge recently threw out that city's lawsuit against gun manufacturers, ruling that it is the responsibility of Ohio's legislature to regulate gun makers. If these special interests want to change gun laws, they should make their case before the elected representatives of the people. To do otherwise would set a terrible precedent: holding manufacturers of a product responsible for how others misuse it. Guns are meant for self-defense, sporting and hunting -- not for committing crimes. Rather than waste taxpayers' money going after law-abiding businesses, government would be better served enforcing the 40,000 gun laws on the books and arresting criminals who commit gun crimes. They are the ones responsible for the costs of gun violence, and we make them pay by locking them up.

697.text: GUN-9185 PRIMARY RICHMOND HEIGHTS BANS HIDDEN WEAPONS FROM PUBLIC BUILDINGS Richmond Heights plans to post signs in all its public buildings to tell people that even if they have a permit to possess concealed weapons, they may not bring them inside. Those who enter with guns may be asked to leave, and, if they refuse, could be arrested for trespassing, according to two bills approved recently by the Richmond Heights City Council. The council approved the bills in response to the new concealed-weapons law the Legislature approved Sept. 11. The bills were timed to take effect Oct. 12 or whenever the bill would become law. Otherwise, says City Attorney Kenneth Heinz, people with permits could legally take their guns to council meetings. The new law gives sheriffs the power to issue permits to people older than 21 to carry certain concealed weapons if they meet certain criteria, including training in the weapon's use. The law, however, bars municipalities from assessing punishments for violators. Heinz said that under the new trespassing ordinance, the municipality can ask persons with concealed weapons to leave; then if they refuse, they would be guilty of trespassing and be subject to a fine of up to $1,000 and jail sentence of up to three months. Councilman Tim Gallagher, 1st Ward, said, "I don't see where this would prohibit guns from being carried to A.B. Green Field or the basketball courts." Gallagher suggested adding language to cover that.

698.text: GUN-8838 PRIMARY Stand behind ballistic fingerprints It is beside the point to argue whether a nationwide database for tracing bullets to gun owners could have stopped the mystery sniper terrorizing suburban Washington. The fact remains that a tracing system could have added a vital piece to the puzzle. President Bush should stand squarely behind a national registry of these so-called ballistic fingerprints. The issue is not gun control, but protecting the public when guns are involved in a crime. The White House sent mixed messages last week about whether it would support such a fingerprinting system. The White House spokesman first said that Bush had doubts about the technology, but later said the president had ordered a review of a ballistic fingerprinting system. Guns, when fired, leave a distinct mark on a shell casing. This is how investigators are able to determine whether bullets were fired from the same gun. The practice is used by police to process crime scenes, but they need a greater ability to trace a bullet to a gun and the gun to its specific owner. The National Rifle Association questions the science, derides fingerprinting as a "scheme" akin to gun registration and says the process is inherently flawed because the gun's signature marking of a shell casing is altered over time by repeated firing. Some ballistic experts disagree. Congress and the administration should not wait for legislation that is acceptable to the NRA. Public safety is not a special-interest issue. We also have uninsured motorists with suspended licenses driving up and down the streets. Does that mean the government should do away with licensing laws for drivers?

701.text: GUN-4767 PRIMARY LETTERS, FAXES & E-MAIL New gun control law unconstitutional, ill-conceived Rescind retroactivity It is horrible to hear that Denver's finest have been accused of the same activities they have vowed to protect us from, but what is even more horrible to hear is that these men are being punished by a retroactive law. One cannot be punished for something he or she did years ago by a law that went into effect Sept. 30, 1996. The U.S. Constitution states clearly that no ex post facto law shall be passed. If these men were involved in a punishable crime after Sept. 30, they may have their guns taken, but they cannot be punished if the offense was prior to Sept. 30. This is a bad law that needs to be re-examined and re-written so as not to punish retroactively. CHAD PARKER Westminster Rights violated Here are a few thoughts on the domestic abuse/right to arms law. Never before in the history of this country has a misdemeanor conviction denied an individual the right to arms. If the crime was serious enough to deny access to arms, the crime would've been charged as a felony. One also cannot vote oneself back in time to reclassify a whole sector of the population without a new hearing in each case! Not too long ago our omnibus crime bill promised to put 100,000 new police on the street. Are we now to have them gobbled back up off the street by another new law that violates as many individual rights as the last one?

699.text: GUN-7675 PRIMARY GOALS MATCH NRA'S: Moms should redirect efforts at enforcing existing gun laws Could it be that the moms who converged on the Mall in Washington on Sunday and their most hated organization, the National Rifle Association, want the same thing --- enforcement of the nation's gun laws? They'd probably rather fight than recognize their common ground, but the truth is that enforcing the laws we have on the books would go a long way toward tackling gun violence. The other aim of the moms --- the licensing and registration of handguns --- will do little to get the guns out of the hands of criminals. Enforcing existing laws would do plenty. Moms learned that with their fight against drunken drivers. It has always been illegal to drink and drive; trouble was existing laws were not adequately enforced. Drunken drivers were too often slapped on the wrist and allowed back in the driver's seat. Moms, angry and intolerant of the carnage, formed Mothers Against Drunk Driving, which has been responsible nationwide for tougher laws against drunken drivers and tougher enforcement of exisiting laws. Of course, they can have the same influence on gun violence. They can insist that laws against illegal possession and placement of firearms be enforced. They can organize and lobby for passage of measures such as state Sen. David Scott's bill to hold adults responsible for keeping guns away from kids. And through their new organization, they can strike out at elements of our culture that tend to glorify guns and violence: TV programs, rap music, violent movies. Together, they can reach out to mothers whose family members are victims of crimes --- and reach out to mothers whose children are the criminal perpetrators. They can develop programs that help young, at-risk mothers raise peaceful, law-abiding young men. They can set a moral tone that demonizes violence and gun use as much as, in some circles, it has been celebrated. Just as they single-handedly changed attitudes about drunken driving, they can do the same with a nation that has become inured to gun violence.

702.text: GUN-6634 PRIMARY Until the Next School Shootings NOT ALL weapons are equipped with silencers, but Congress doesn't hear any gunfire unless the guns go off at a school. Almost forgotten now are last year's vows to enact tougher gun controls after a singularly awful rash of terrorism in schools, summer camps, office buildings, houses of worship and other places where people gathered. Everyday shootings, including of thousands of children each year, don't count for much on Capitol Hill. So though Congress has returned to work, its members seem unlikely to return to any of the gun legislation they were so busily introducing (and diluting) when the headlines were full of gun violence. When the House last toyed with legislation, most Republicans were scrambling to join one of two camps: the do-littles or the do-nothings. With guidance from NRA lobbyists who already had helped reduce a Senate bill to scraps, House leaders had narrowed the gun-legislation debate to definitions of "gun show" and of who should be considered a vendor when it comes to requiring background checks on purchasers. Any stronger suggestions--to curb gun-running, for example, with limits on the number of handguns that anybody may buy in a given year--were given short shrift. And the most sensible proposal, to ban handguns and assault-style weapons, gets no hearing. How sad that it may require another tragedy to prod Congress to take any action at all.

700.text: GUN-8283 PRIMARY Webb rallies Democratic mayors on gun control LOS ANGELES - Denver Mayor Wellington Webb led a group of  Democratic mayors Sunday in a pledge to make gun-control  legislation the focus of national and local Democratic campaigns. The gun-control debate in Colorado has sharpened since the  April 20, 1999, massacre at Columbine High School in Jefferson  County. The shooting has become both a benchmark and a rallying cry  for gun-control advocates such as Webb, who intend to speak out at  the four-day Democratic National Convention. 'Many people think that gun violence is only an inner-city  issue,' Webb said. ' What Columbine did was change that instinct.   It showed you can be white and living in an upscale neighborhood,  but gun violence can still happen in your children's school.' Webb and mayors from Gary, Ind., Minneapolis and New Orleans  gathered before a wall bearing a list of 4,001 names, all people  who had died from gunfire starting with Columbine and ending April  1. The names were drawn from 100 American cities, the largest  being Chicago and the smallest Bedford Heights, Ohio. New Orleans Mayor Marc Morial said the 'Wall of Gun Deaths'  stood as a symbol of the collective resolve of Democratic mayors  in big cities to fight for stricter gun control. He said they  would have to work to ensure that Republicans don't make firearm  control a peripheral issue in the presidential election. 'I don't remember a wall like this up in Philadelphia. I  don't think they (the GOP) would allow us to put a wall like this  up at their convention,' Webb said.

708.text: NA-45860 PRIMARY President's initiatives won't stop mass killings; Too many guns are already out there President Obama's anti-gun proposals -- including universal background checks, an assault weapons ban and curbs on high-capacity magazines -- will do more to reduce everyday gun violence than prevent mass shootings. Unfortunately, there are already many extraordinarily lethal weapons in circulation. Moving forward will feel like having to bail out the Titanic with a paper cup. And, without minimizing  the well-meaning groundswell in the wake of the elementary school massacre in Newtown, Conn., it is sad and ironic that it took the violent deaths of so many children and teachers to get us to this point. When Obama ran for the presidency in 2008, he had a strong gun control platform, but then disappointingly did nothing during his first term. He even hesitated during the 2012 election season to deal affirmatively with the gun issue, including his non-committal response to a debate question about the assault-weapons ban. Violent crime impact Many of the president's proposals for gun control could very well take a large bite out of violent crime. But in terms of lessening the incidence of mass murder -- the very kind of event that prompted the Obama administration to be so proactive -- at best it will be a nibble. Mass killers are unwavering in their determination to carry out their mission; there is little that we can do to prevent them. If unable to purchase the necessary firepower, they will resort to stealing or borrowing a weapon, one of the millions in circulation, to achieve their objective.

705.text: GUN-11497 PRIMARY Bloomberg Endorses Republican in Heated Massachusetts Senate Race Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg, one of the most outspoken defenders of Wall Street, has sided against one of the financial industry's biggest critics in a hotly contested Senate race in his native Massachusetts. Mr. Bloomberg has agreed to host a fund-raiser at his Upper East Side town house for the re-election campaign of Senator Scott P. Brown, a Republican, who is being challenged by a fiery consumer advocate, Elizabeth Warren, a Democrat who is also a professor at Harvard Law School. Mr. Bloomberg, through a spokesman, said his endorsement was not about Wall Street at all, but about his desire to reward Mr. Brown for voting against his party and the National Rifle Association on a gun control measure. ''If you take a tough stand and buck party orthodoxy that helps the City of New York, the mayor would like to support you,'' Mr. Bloomberg's spokesman, Stu Loeser, said Thursday. Mr. Bloomberg has long been a supporter of more restrictive gun laws, and founded, along with Mayor Thomas M. Menino of Boston, the group Mayors Against Illegal Guns. Mr. Bloomberg has been emphasizing the need for gun control in the wake of the mass shooting last week in Aurora, Colo. Mr. Brown has generally received high ratings from gun rights organizations and has not pushed for renewing a federal assault-weapons ban or for tightening restrictions on gun shows. But Mr. Loeser said the mayor had been won over by the senator's ''tough stand'' opposing a measure that would have required states to honor concealed-weapons permits issued by other states.

704.text: GUN-31182 PRIMARY Ready, Aim, Raffle; A tasteless drawing to assist gun scofflaws in Fairfax THE VIRGINIA Citizens Defense League, a trigger-happy group that regards the National Rifle Association as slightly spineless on gun rights, will hold a raffle tonight in a Fairfax County government building. The door prizes include a hunting rifle, a semiautomatic handgun and ammunition -- more than $2,000 in merchandise in all. The league's goal is to assist Virginia firearms dealers alleged to have skirted the state's already lax laws on gun purchases. So, a month after a deranged gunman killed 32 people at Virginia Tech, a Virginia group is coddling scofflaw gun dealers in the same state. This sorry story originated with a reasonable attempt by New York City authorities to take action against several dozen gun stores, in Virginia and elsewhere, that sold firearms later used in the commission of felonies in the Big Apple. The city sent pairs of private detectives to six of the stores to make "straw" purchases, in which one selected and paid for a weapon, and the other filled out the paperwork, thereby disguising the true owner's identity. The gun-loving Defense League decided that the gun stores were victims -- not the New Yorkers shot, pistol-whipped or mugged in New York -- and cooked up the idea of the raffle. (The drawing was originally scheduled for April 19, three days after the Virginia Tech killings; it was postponed because the group knew it would be inflammatory.)

703.text: GUN-8501 PRIMARY NRA Report Doesn't Back Earley Or Warner -- The National Rifle Association's newly published report card on Virginia political candidates makes no endorsement in the governor's race, a neutral stance that Democrat Mark R. Warner's campaign hailed as a turning point in their race against Republican Mark L. Earley. In the November issue of the American Hunter, a monthly magazine arriving at Virginia households over the next few days, the NRA gives a letter grade to all statewide and legislative candidates except Earley and Warner, both of whom have spent months courting the politically muscular gun rights organization. Earley running mates Jay Katzen and Jerry W. Kilgore received an A-plus and an A, respectively, in their bids for lieutenant governor and attorney general. The NRA said in the magazine and through spokesmen tonight that it might still mail a gubernatorial endorsement letter to its 100,000 Virginia members before the Nov. 6 election, but strategists for both Warner and Earley said they were uncertain how much impact such a late communication would have. Warner campaign manager Steve Jarding said he doubted that the NRA would follow its published neutrality with a strongly worded endorsement of either candidate. "We've reached out to all groups in this campaign, including hunters and gun owners, and we endorse Second Amendment rights," Jarding said. "This is further evidence that the positive campaign is working." Christopher J. LaCivita, Earley's senior campaign strategist, said he held out hope that a formal NRA endorsement was imminent. "I sure would hope so, considering on two different occasions in debates Mark Warner showed he did not know what the NRA did, let alone what the positive impact of the group has been."

706.text: GUN-7507 PRIMARY TWIN CITIES DRAW NATION'S CARTOONISTS; VISUAL PUNSTERS GATHER TO SHARE GOSSIP, LISTEN TO A FREQUENT TARGET Charlton Heston - actor, NRA president, American - delivered a sermon from Minneapolis' Marriott Hotel to a ballroom full of editorial cartoonists with this commandment: Thou Shalt Honor the Second Amendment. Speaking Thursday at the Association of American Editorial Cartoonists' convention, being held in the Twin Cities, Heston took the editorial cartoonists to task for what he called "cultural bigotry" against advocates of gun ownership. "On newsstands all around the country, it seems I'm one of your favorite targets," Heston told the cartoonists. "So let me take a few shots of my own." Heston pointed to a cartoon showing George W. Bush allied with a demonic-looking gun owner. "Your cartoon Beelzebub is labeled the 'gun lobby.' Ha. Ha," Heston said. But he said if the devil figure had been labeled the nation of Islam or Scientologists or Jehovah's Witnesses, "suddenly these funnies wouldn't seem so funny anymore." Another cartoon displayed by Heston showed an NRA spokesman asserting that the solution to the "gun crisis" is earplugs. "That might be funny. But it's false," he said. "If anybody's been wearing earplugs, it's the Clinton-Gore administration." Heston asserted that gun ownership has prevented crime, that the National Rifle Association has been a leading gun safety educator, that gun accident rates are low. But he said editorial cartoonists instead have focused on a proposal for an NRA theme restaurant in Times Square.

707.text: GUN-11745 PRIMARY Gun debate rages near Connecticut store where Nancy Lanza bought AR-15 EAST WINDSOR, Conn.    Customers at the Sky Diner in East Windsor on Friday didn't know much about the neighboring gun store that was raided by federal agents the night before. But they did know where they stood on gun control: Like the rest of the nation, very far apart. The television behind the counter flashed with news of funerals and the debate about the reasons why Adam Lanza chose to kill 20 children and six faculty members at Sandy Hook Elementary School with the AR-15 rifle police say his mother, Nancy Lanza, bought at Riverview Gun Sales in the same sprawling shopping plaza. Among those with eyes trained to the TV, was a man named Aaron, who would not give his last name, "for obvious political reasons." Aaron had gone to the gun shop to browse, but, turned away, settled for breakfast and heated discussion. What did he think the country should do about guns in the face of violence? "Absolutely nothing," he said. It is schools that need to change by putting armed guards and metal detectors, he said, echoing suggestions the National Rifle Association made Friday. Denielle Hitchcock, a waitress and mother of young children, argued back. "I don't think people with mental health problems should be able to get guns," she said. Aaron urged her, "You've got to think logically, not emotionally."

709.text: GUN-720 PRIMARY Assault rifle ban sets up showdown President Bush's hard line against assault rifles puts him on a collision course with the USA's powerful gun lobby. In unveiling his anti-crime package Monday, Bush said he'll make permanent a temporary ban on imports of semiautomatic assault weapons found ''unsuitable'' for hunting. He also urged Congress to ban the importation, manufacture or sale of gun magazines holding 15 or more rounds. ''We must not allow deadly weapons to fall into deadly hands,'' he said. Now, the focal point of the battle moves to Capitol Hill, where backers of bills restricting U.S.-made assault guns say Bush has helped their cause. ''He did what he could do. Now we have to take it from here,'' says Rep. Pete Stark, D-Calif. But National Rifle Association lobbyist James Jay Baker vows an all-out fight. NRA ads against assault weapons bans have run in major newspapers and more will follow. '' We'll take our case to anyone who will listen,'' says Baker. He says the gun proposals would do little to combat violent crime, but admits having Bush - an NRA life member - on the other side makes his job tougher. Larry Pratt of Gun Owners of America, says members ''feel betrayed'' by Bush's ''public relations stunt'' and predicted political troubles for him. ''People who never before joined a group like ours are writing and calling in droves because they think their president has sold them out.''

713.text: GUN-2533 PRIMARY Filibuster bluster . . . The Senate impasse over the Brady bill has less to do with gun control than politics. Many Republicans who supported the filibuster that originally prevented the bill from coming to a vote were, contrary to their words, perfectly comfortable with the most controversial aspect of the legislation, the five-year provision for a five-day waiting period for a handgun purchase. Some of the Democrats who fought to pass the bill were less concerned with a legislative victory than with hanging Republicans for the failure of the measure, which has broad popular support across the country. When the Senate failed to muster the votes to cut off debate on the bill  last week, Majority Leader George Mitchell of Maine declared the measure dead  and blamed the Republicans. By all accounts, Minority Leader Bob Dole of  Kansas, who led the opposition, doesn't object to the bill's substance. But  Dole wants to make another run for president in 1996, and if he's going to be  successful, he has to do well in the GOP primary in New Hampshire, a state  where gun control is anathema. If Dole allowed Mitchell's pronouncement of the demise of the Brady bill to stand, he saddled his party with the blame for its defeat; if he helped the bill, his actions could come back to haunt his aspirations. Dole compromised. He worked out an arrangement in which the five-day waiting period would remain in force for just four years, with a fifth year to be tacked on only if the attorney general deemed it necessary.

712.text: GUN-4385 PRIMARY TEXANS REMEMBER ALAMO WITH SIGNS BANNING GUNS A reminder from the Catholic Church in Texas: Thou shalt not carry a weapon inside. And remember - no guns at the Alamo. It is now permissible under a new law to carry concealed handguns in most places in Texas. However, they still aren't allowed in churches, hospitals, bars and a few other places. "Blessed are the peacemakers," reads a sign posted at one church in San Antonio. "Please no guns permitted on these premises. Property of the Archdiocese of San Antonio." The Alamo, scarred by bullets from the 1836 battle for Texas independence, has always had a policy forbidding guns, said Bobby Boothe, chief of security. It will post signs at its five entrances to specifically ban concealed guns.

710.text: GUN-3617 PRIMARY Appeals Court Backs Proviso Of Gun Law A Federal appeals court today reversed two lower-court decisions that had struck down part of the law requiring a waiting period before buying a hand gun. The appeals panel ruled that contrary to the district court rulings, the Federal Government could require local law-enforcement agencies to check the records of people seeking to buy pistols. The 2-to-1 ruling by the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit, in San Francisco, applies to the nine Western states in the Ninth Circuit. The ruling overturned decisions by Federal district judges in Montana and Arizona declaring part of the law unconstitutional. Judges in Vermont, Mississippi and Louisiana have issued similar rulings. The law, which took effect in March 1994, requires a five-day waiting period before anyone may buy a pistol. During the wait, the local authorities are supposed to determine whether the prospective buyer has a felony record, a history of mental illness or drug use or other problems that would make the sale illegal. Two sheriffs in Montana and Arizona, backed by the National Rifle Association, sued to overturn the law. They argued that requiring local officials to check records violated the Constitution's 10th Amendment, which protects state and local governments from certain types of Federal interference. But the appeals court said the law's requirement was a minor burden that the Federal Government could impose.

characters: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

714.text: GUN-20126 PRIMARY NRA sues to keep gun purchase records away from FBI WASHINGTON -- The National Rifle Association filed a lawsuit Tuesday and asked for a temporary injunction to stop the FBI from keeping gun purchase records required under the new national instant background check system. At issue is a plan by the FBI to keep the sales records for six months to make sure the instant background checks are being conducted properly. The records include the buyer's name, sex, race, date of birth and state of residence. The lawsuit filed in federal court names Attorney General Janet Reno as the defendant. It said the plan to hold records constitutes "an illegal national registration of gun owners." The Brady Law, which set up the check system, calls for all records to be destroyed once a transaction is approved. But it does not specify when. "If the FBI maintains records on lawful gun owners, whether for 24 hours or 24 years, it is doing so in violation of federal law," said Rep. Bob Barr, R-Ga., a former federal prosecutor. Justice Department officials said they plan to file a response to the lawsuit next week. No date has been set for a hearing on the request for an injunction. "We are confident that our policy of keeping the records for a very limited period is consistent with the federal law," Justice Department spokesman Gregory King said. The national instant check system began operating Monday, opening to delays and problems. Although the computers shut down for 20 minutes Tuesday, FBI spokesman Paul Bresson said the system was "running a lot smoother."

711.text: NA-42058 PRIMARY Tammerlin Drummond: Zimmerman verdict shows insanity of Florida gun laws Let's set aside the issue of race for just a moment in the Trayvon Martin case. Strip away the hyperbole on the left and the right and cut to the facts. Here is what the not-guilty verdict in the George Zimmerman murder trial tells us. In the state of Florida, a grown man can stalk a teenage boy whom he thinks "is up to no good" in his gated community. He can disobey a police dispatcher who tells him not to follow the 17-year-old. When the teen, who happens to be visiting the man's neighbor, gets upset that some stranger is following him and the two wind up in a brawl, the man can pull out a concealed gun and shoot him dead. Then he can claim self-defense even though he was the one who provoked the confrontation. Walk away scot free with the law on his side. On Saturday, a jury of six women found Zimmerman not guilty of second-degree murder. A number of legal experts -- among them civil rights attorney John Burris -- have said the prosecution failed to make its case. A legal finding of not guilty, however, does not absolve Zimmerman of the fact that he took the life of a teen who was simply returning from the store with a box of Skittles. A teen who would be alive today if the neighborhood watch member and wanna-be-cop had not initiated a confrontation, then escalated it by pulling a gun.

715.text: GUN-30069 PRIMARY GUN-RIGHTS GROUP SAYS HEALTH CARE BILL COULD HARM GUN OWNERS The Baucus health care bill "could be used to ban guns in home self-defense." Gun Owners of America on Friday, October 9th, 2009 in an alert to members * * * The Ruling: FALSE The Gun Owners of America has recently taken aim at an unlikely subject: health care reform. In the last few weeks, GOA has sent out several action alerts to members criticizing the health care reform bill that passed the Senate Finance Committee last week. The bill, authored by Finance Chairman Max Baucus, D-Mont., was supported by all of the committee's Democrats, as well as Republican Sen. Olympia Snowe of Maine. It will now be merged with another Senate health care bill and go to the full Senate. On Oct. 9, 2009, the day before the Finance Committee began voting on amendments to the Baucus bill, GOA sent an alert to members titled, "ObamaCare Could be Used to Ban Guns in Home Self-Defense." The conservative group speculated about the impact of the health care bill on gun owners, hypothesizing that, in a bid to control spiraling health care costs, the administration will target people who have "excessively dangerous" behaviors that officials believe raise the cost of health care. The group warned that gun ownership - particularly keeping "a loaded firearm for self-defense" - is likely to be high on that list. The memo accuses Health and Human Services Secretary Kathleen Sebelius - who would have significant influence over any new health care policy - of having a "well-established antipathy to the Second Amendment" because she vetoed concealed-carry gun legislation when she was governor of Kansas.

717.text: GUN-393 PRIMARY NEWARK SHOOTINGS STIR CALL FOR GUN LAW NEWARK THE recent slaying of a Newark police officer during a bank robbery and the shooting, six days later, of another officer during a liquorstore holdup have prompted officials here to call for strict handgun controls and for mandatory prison sentences for those convicted of using a gun while committing a crime. The respective calls came from Mayor Kenneth A. Gibson and Police Director Hubert Williams.   ''I have always been a fighter against the ownership of guns by private citizens,'' the Mayor said in an interview. '' Most people who have them don't know how to handle them. The person usually getting shot is the friend or relative of the person who brought the gun into the house.'' Strict gun controls, such as those in Japan, can work, he said.   ''In Japan, there is an absolute prohibition with no escape clauses,'' Mr. Gibson explained. '' They have crime problems, but you don't see anyone getting killed by guns.'' The Mayor said that opposition to gun controls based on the constitutional right to bear arms was faulty.  '' The constitutional right to bear arms was intended for a militia,'' he said. '' It was never intended for every citizen to have a gun in his night table.'' The Mayor said that he had been a member of a rifle-pistol team while in the armed forces, and still fired a weapon occasionally at a range. Although there have been threats against Mr. Gibson's life, Bernard Moore, a spokesman for the Mayor, would not say whether Mr.  Gibson carried a gun.

720.text: GUN-265 PRIMARY IN THE NATION; TALE OF TWO INITIATIVES ''Costs too much ... Diverts police ...  Violates rights ... No on 15.'' Thus reads a billboard on the freeway into town, and it exemplifies the kind of buzz-word opposition being mounted to both of the most important initiatives on the California ballot -one for a bilateral, verifiable freeze on production and deployment of nuclear weapons, the other for tighter controls on the numbers, ownership and use of handguns in the state. In both cases the opposition has attempted to confuse the issues, obscure the facts, sow doubts and stimulate fears: with handgun control ''you can't protect yourself''; a nuclear freeze ''ties the President's hands.'' Neither assertion is true - the freeze is in no way binding on President Reagan; a handgun in the house can be more dangerous than a burglar - but both are effective. In the case of Proposition 15, the gun lobby's campaign of fear and obfuscation may well have succeeded. The initiative was favored by 64 to 30 percent in the first California Poll on the subject; in the latest, published today, it trails by 41 to 49 percent. One strong reason is the $5.2 million raised and spent by the opposition - including $2.6 million contributed by the National Rifle Association and about $700,000 from various gun manufacturers.
