Sponsors of Prop. 15 have been able to spend only about $1.6 million, and $600,000 of that was required just to get the proposition on the ballot.

716.text: GUN-1724 PRIMARY GUNS // Liability legislation under fire Restricting assault weapons through lawsuits may prove more popular with voters than the courts and Congress. Washington, D.C., voters Tuesday approved a law that would make assault weapon companies liable for injuries caused by the weapons. Supporters hope that big damage awards will stop assault weapon sales. Similar bills failed this year in the Illinois, Minnesota, New York and Oregon legislatures. ''The goal is to make selling these guns unprofitable,'' says Jeff Muchnick of the Coalition to Stop Gun Violence. Congressional opponents Wednesday introduced a bill to overturn the D.C. law, which takes effect Dec. 23. If that bill doesn't get out of committee - controlled by pro-gun-control Congress members - the law could be overturned when the D.C. budget goes to Congress next year. Congress last month rejected an assault weapon ban. ''The (D.C.) law is ridiculous,'' says Mike Solo, of Intratec, an arms maker. '' It puts every firearm in jeopardy and wouldn't stop there. What's next? Football helmets?'' A company making a legal and non-defective product should not be liable for the actions of others, Solo says. He says less than 1% of assault weapons are used in a crime. ''This type of law ... is an effort to control a serious social problem in a backhanded way,'' says University of Denver law professor Arthur Best. Says Rev. H. Beech Hicks, a backer of the law: ''It is more than an issue of constitutionality, but (one) of conscience.''

719.text: GUN-3354 PRIMARY IN ELK COUNTY, GUN OWNERS AND SINGEL FACE OFF OVER BANS TEMPERS FLARED. GUN OWNERS CALLED SINGEL SOFT ON CRIME. HE STOOD BY HIS SUPPORT FOR A FEDERAL GUN BAN. Democratic gubernatorial hopeful Mark Singel went toe-to-toe here yesterday with 15 angry members of the Elk County Militia, a group of gun owners upset over his position on assault weapons. The group confronted the lieutenant governor as he entered a political dinner. Before it was over, Singel got into a shouting match with a man who said Singel had violated his oath of office for supporting legislation that conflicts with the Second Amendment right to bear arms. The group also accused him of not being tough enough on people who break the law, a reference to Republican Tom Ridge's assertion that Singel has been "soft" on crime as chairman of the state Board of Pardons. "When are you going to quit coddling those criminals?" asked Joe Gruesu, a board member of the militia, which is part of the Pennsylvania Gunowners Association. "Don't be telling me I am soft on crime just because some Republican candidate puts it on TV," Singel said. He added that while he is "not for additional gun control," he believes the federal ban is necessary. "You may not see it up here, but we've got kids killing kids in Pennsylvania," he said. For Singel, the stopoff served as a marked reminder of the differences in the state's political and geographic landscape. The visit to Elk County - arguably the most rural area of Pennsylvania - followed morning stops at African American churches in Philadelphia.

721.text: GUN-8014 PRIMARY Clinton pins Gore loss on NRA WASHINGTON - President Clinton said Tuesday that his  administration's advocacy of gun control measures had cost Vice  President Al Gore 'at least' five states in the election and  suggested that Colorado illustrated Gore's difficulty with the gun  issue. Speaking to CBS-TV's Dan Rather in his first interview since  the election, the president credited the National Rifle  Association for turning out anti-Gore voters because of the gun  issue. 'I don't think there is any doubt that, in at least five  states I can think of, the NRA has a decisive influence because  they disagreed with our attempts to close the gun-show loophole  and have child trigger locks, safety locks and ban large-scale  ammunition clips,' the president said. 'You know, presumably, some people voted for him (Gore)  because we were for those things,' he told Rather. 'But one of the sad things about all gun safety legislation  is that people tend to vote for the issues, but, when they're  voting for candidates, the 'antis' tend to be more intense than  the 'pros.' 'I mean, if you look at Colorado, which is basically a  Republican state now, the vice president lost there, but closing  the gun-show loophole passed 70-30 (percent),' he said. Clinton did not name the five states, and White House  spokesmen were not able to say whether the president believed  Colorado was one of them. Clinton said he was at peace with himself over the end of his  presidency.

718.text: GUN-137 PRIMARY Dealer in Bronx Indicted in Plot To Resell Guns A 35-year-old Bronx man was indicted yesterday in connection with what prosecutors called a scheme to resell at least 1,000 handguns he had ordered legally by mail as a federally licensed dealer. Most of the weapons ''have disappeared into the back alleys and streets of the Bronx, if not elsewhere,'' said the Acting Bronx District Attorney, Paul T. Gentile, in announcing the indictment. He said the suspect, David Taylor, of 531 East 143d Street in the Mott Haven area, had used ''the laxity of the Federal gun laws'' to arrange for the c.o.d. shipments of handguns to his apartment. Mr. Gentile said the shipments were carried out ''with the help of the United States Government and without the knowledge of the New York City Police Department.'' The scheme, Mr. Gentile told a news conference in his office, ''is only the tip of the iceberg in a grand jury investigation which we have begun into the most incredible violation of this city's gun laws that I or anyone else has ever heard of.'' $30 and No Record When Mr. Taylor was arrested last month, 112 handguns were found in his apartment. Mr. Gentile said. They ranged from ''small and easily concealed pistols and revolvers to large and sophisticated and extremely dangerous 9-millimeter semiautomatics,'' the prosecutor said. Mr. Gentile, who took office after the death of Mario Merola in October, said that under the Federal Gun Control Act of 1968, anyone with $30 and no record of a felony conviction could obtain a license to purchase firearms through the mail.

722.text: GUN-7208 PRIMARY Enforcing gun laws is answer, NRA says; Control advocates call stand hypocritical As the gun debate and the push for stronger gun controls have heated up in recent weeks, the National Rifle Association has begun fighting back by focusing on enforcement of gun laws already on the books. The theme resonates in NRA President Charlton Heston's defiant ads on television, in public remarks by other NRA officials and supporters, and in multiple essays and press releases on the group's Web site. "All the polls I've seen, going back years and years, indicate that Americans expect and want enforcement of laws that prohibit felons from carrying guns, prohibit drug dealers from carrying guns, prohibit violent felons from even trying to get them," NRA spokesman Bill Powers said. "And yet those laws are never enforced or hardly ever enforced." Powers said the NRA has pressed the issue for several years. Now, he said, the issue "has reached a broader spotlight and is being discussed at the national level," and that is a "positive thing for all Americans." Gun control advocates say the NRA's stress on enforcement is hypocritical, because the group has opposed legislation that would strengthen enforcement. President Clinton has been pushing for laws to require safety locks on all handguns, ban imports of large-capacity ammunition clips and require 72-hour background checks for buyers at gun shows. The House and Senate have passed separate versions of a bill that includes gun control measures, but have not reached a compromise.

724.text: GUN-9742 PRIMARY Time is running out GUN CONTROL IF PRESIDENT GEORGE W. BUSH is serious about throwing his political weight behind the move to get Congress to extend the ban on military-style assault weapons, he better hurry. After this week, Congress will have just four working days to act before the existing ban expires Sept. 13. What President Bush does will say much about whether Candidate Bush was sincere during the 2000 presidential campaign when he promised to support renewing the ban. Aside from antagonizing the leadership of the National Rifle Association, Mr. Bush would seem to face no long-term political fallout from working to extend the ban. A majority of Americans support a renewal of the ban, according to a poll by the Consumer Federation of America. In Missouri, for example, the CFA poll found that 67 percent of likely voters were in favor of extending the ban. Some critics have attacked this poll, saying the CFA has ties to the left. But the CFA says its survey of 1,000 adults was conducted by Opinion Research Corporation International and had a margin of error of plus or minus 3 percentage points. In any case, the public is getting nothing but buck-passing from GOP leaders. House Speaker Dennis Hastert, R-Ill., has said he won't move the weapons ban extension to the House floor without the president's support. Mr. Bush, meanwhile, says he's waiting for Congress to act. Meanwhile, The Washington Post reported that House Majority Leader Tom DeLay said the measure won't be taken up because Mr. Bush never told Mr. DeLay personally that he wanted the ban renewed.

726.text: GUN-11157 PRIMARY Responsible gun laws in Virginia? Yes, it could happen In December, New York police officer Peter Figoski, a 22-year veteran of the NYPD and a father of four, was shot to death while confronting a robbery suspect in Brooklyn. The Ruger 9mm pistol the suspect used to kill Figoski was sold in Virginia to a man who later moved to Queens but claimed that he "lost" the weapon while being evicted from a Virginia apartment in 2009. The man never thought to inform law enforcement that he had "lost" the weapon, and police in New York suspect that, in fact, the gun was illegally sold and trafficked to New York. Gun trafficking between Virginia and New York has caused countless tragedies over the years.            Barely two months later, Virginia Gov. Robert McDonnell (R) fulfilled a campaign promise and signed the repeal of one of the few anti-trafficking measures we had in Virginia, the ban on purchasing more than one handgun a month. This law was on the books for 19 years and was originally passed because Virginia was the No. 1 source for crime guns in New York and elsewhere - remember the "iron pipeline"? The one-handgun-a-month law had been a target of the gun lobby for almost two decades, and with the Republicans in control of the General Assembly, it was doomed. But the law's repeal does not relieve Virginia's policymakers of the obligation to stop the flow of illegal guns to neighborhoods near and far. The good news is there are effective policies that work, research makes clear: States that require background checks on all gun sales and require the reporting of lost or stolen guns, among other things, have a lower rate of guns trafficked to criminals.

727.text: GUN-1670 PRIMARY Montgomery Gun Bill Backed by Curran; Attorney General Urges Similar Measures Montgomery County's proposed child gun-safety law passes legal muster and should be voted into law, Maryland Attorney General J. Joseph Curran Jr. said yesterday. "What we are saying to you is go ahead and pass your Montgomery County law," Curran said at a news conference at Takoma Park police headquarters. "Make it as strong as you want. And I urge my friends in Prince George's County to do the same." The proposed law, which has been endorsed by seven of the council's nine members, would impose a civil penalty of $ 500 on gun owners who do not keep their weapons locked or out of reach of children. Curran's opinion had been requested by backers of the bill who feared the proposed legislation would violate state law, which prohibits a county from passing its own gun-control laws. Among the exceptions to the law is one permitting county regulation of firearms "with respect to minors." In his eight-page opinion, Curran argued that because the intent of the Montgomery County law was to keep firearms away from children, it was "specifically authorize[d]" by that exception. "If you choose to own a gun you have an obligation to store it safely so that a young child or a teenager cannot find it and cause a tragedy," said council member Derick Berlage (D-Silver Spring), who introduced the law. A law almost identical to the Montgomery proposal is already in effect in Baltimore. The Prince George's County Council will consider similar legislation next year, according to its legislative officer, Ralph Grutzmacher.

723.text: GUN-11959 PRIMARY Will Sandy Hook become tipping point on gun laws? Jill Lawrence, National Journal: "The latest slaughter of innocents and those who tried to protect them may not turn out to be a tipping point for the country. But President Obama has left no doubt that it was a tipping point for him.    Not that Obama mentioned gun control in his speech at a memorial for the 20 children and six adults killed in a shooting rampage in Newtown, Conn. He didn't.    It was one of the highest profile moments of Obama's presidency, and he didn't waste it for fear of igniting anger among gun-rights lobbyists and enthusiasts. He used it to start the process of winning hearts, minds and support for a sea change in gun politics." Amy Davidson, The New Yorker: "It is a mark of how deep America's gun pathology is that (the president) would sound radical -- a speech without any specific policy proposals, whose greatest force came by way of implication, by drawing a line between 'Tucson and Aurora and Oak Creek and Newtown and communities from Columbine to Blacksburg before that.' But in the context of a memorial service, when he might have just spoken about mercy and faith, the speech was a point of departure.   We'll see what happens. Obama still has to do something other than speak." Eleanor Clift, The Daily Beast: "California Sen. Dianne Feinstein got things going when she said   that she would introduce an assault-weapons ban on the first day of the new Congress in January.   The question now is whether Democrats will back Feinstein and whether any moderate Republicans will join them.   The school shooting in Columbine, Colo., in 1999, shocked the nation and spawned books and movies, but didn't change gun laws. The shooting of Rep. Gabby Giffords two years ago shook up the Congress, but didn't make any impact on gun policy. This time could be different."

730.text: GUN-8336 PRIMARY EDITORIAL Sane gun bill in sight Has Hades has frozen over? Can the legislature truly be close to  a compromise on passing a sensible statewide concealed-weapons law  that will end the crazy-quilt of local ordinances and whims that  has heretofore passed for policy? Only last month, the Senate Judiciary Committee, voting along  party lines, killed Republican Sen. Ken Chlouber's proposal that  would have required concealed-carry permits to be issued to anyone  over 21 who passed a background check, completed a gun safety  course, and paid a fee of about $ 100. Chlouber had unsuccessfully championed his 'shall-issue' bill  for years. It passed in April 1999 only to be withdrawn because of  the Columbine tragedy. The bill's chances diminished sharply when Republicans lost  control of the Senate. Senate President Stan Matsunaka, D-Loveland, and House  Speaker Doug Dean now seem close to a compromise that makes sense  to us: Law enforcement will be able to deny permits if there's  documentable proof of instability under a so-called 'naked man'  provision. That would apply to people who have done weird things but avoided being charged with crimes. Dean says he'll support that provision if there's a way to  appeal denials. Rep. Al White, R-Winter Park, is set to introduce the bill  in the House. Chlouber wants to carry it in the Senate, but  Matsunaka has strongly suggested that it would be 'helpful' to  have it carried by a Democrat in the upper chamber.

728.text: GUN-2186 PRIMARY The Guns of Virginia It's common lore on the sidewalks of New York: If you want a gun, go to Virginia, where weak laws and willing dealers allow almost anyone to acquire firearms. A recent study of guns seized at crime scenes found that one in four in New York had come from Virginia; in Washington D.C. the figure was one in three. Even in Batman comics, denizens of Gotham City swap drugs for guns in Virginia. For years Virginia's government has knuckled under to local gun dealers and the National Rifle Association. But publicity about gun violence now prompts Gov. Douglas Wilder to push new laws. His concern makes more glaring than ever neglect at the Federal level, as Congressional leaders have hesitated to push aggressive gun laws and Presidents have refused to sign them. Governor Wilder proposes a state law limiting handgun sales to one per customer per month. That would prevent bulk purchases by out-of-staters bent on selling the guns back home. He would also require expanded record-keeping on gun purchases and prohibit juveniles from possessing handguns. As for those who evade the law, Virginia enforcement officials have reached an agreement with their New York counterparts to cooperate on gunrunning investigations. The need for strong action is obvious. An article in the current Atlantic magazine documents how lax gun regulation in Virginia and elsewhere increases the general risk of senseless terror. In 1988, a 16-year-old named Nicholas Elliot carried a fearsome Cobray M-11/9 handgun to school. Insulted by another student, he pulled the gun from his backpack and began firing. He killed one teacher, wounded another twice and forced a third to run a zigzag pattern through the schoolyard as he fired bullet after bullet at her back.

733.text: GUN-644 PRIMARY VOICES/Do gun-control laws save lives? Not many. Gun laws probably save a few lives, but they won't change much. There are already too many guns out there that can't be taken away, and people are still going to kill with guns. A gun law is not going to save a person who buys a gun legally and then accidentally shoots himself at home. Leo Davis, 65 Retired millwright Denver, Colo. I believe they do. Gun-control laws won't stop people from being murdered, but they will cut down on the number of people killed with guns. States should make it harder for people to get guns. Right now, everybody can buy a gun, especially those AK-47s. Those should be banned totally. Alfredo Gaussoin, 30 Interpreter for deaf San Diego, Calif. I don't think the laws themselves save lives. People do. It's the irresponsibility of the gun handler that causes the problems. They're the ones who pull the trigger. Stricter controls are necessary because of the simplicity of acquiring guns. But banning all types of weapons is unnecessary. Ruby West, 71 Homemaker Lexington, Ky. Yes, because some states make it harder for just anyone to own a gun. California has banned AK-47s, and the president has banned AK-47 imports. If guns aren't available, you won't have many gun deaths. Also, if guns are going to be around the house, parents should teach their kids about gun safety.

725.text: GUN-4865 PRIMARY THEFTS ARE KEY SUPPLY OF ILLEGAL FIREARMS MTN. VIEW GUN SHOP IS SOURCE OF CONCERN The illegal gun trade has come calling at a leafy corner in downtown Mountain View. Eddy's Sport Shop, a community institution that has quietly catered to Peninsula sportsmen for nearly half a century, in recent years has been inadvertently supplying the black market with weapons ranging from semiautomatic pistols to shotguns, various authorities and reports say. Since 1995, the store has been broken into four times, resulting in the theft of 33 guns that have turned up from the Mexican border in Brownsville, Texas, to the Los Altos bedroom of the man who escaped from the Palo Alto courthouse July 17. Today, the boarded-up front door at Eddy's still bears testament to the latest break-in and highlights the vexing problem of illegal weapons that flow into communities nationwide from even the most innocuous-looking businesses. Illegal gun trafficking today is a multimillion-dollar business that spans the continent. The federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms estimates that every year 10,000 guns are stolen from licensed gun dealers nationwide. The number of missing guns from all sources annually totals closer to 100,000, according to the ATF, which tracks the illegal weapons trade. "Stolen firearms by their very nature are destined to become crime guns," warns the ATF in a recent publication. Some guns disappear in spectacular thefts that net hundreds of weapons. In 1994, for example, two employees of Lorcin Engineering, a Southern California gun manufacturer, were arrested for taking more than 2,000 handguns that were later sold on the streets. Most of the guns have never been found.

characters: federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

729.text: GUN-30810 PRIMARY Virginia Tech: The Killer, the Guns, the Anguish To the Editor: ''Officials Knew Troubled State of Killer in '05'' (front page, April 19) reports that Cho Seung-Hui was allowed to continue as a student at Virginia Tech despite the fact that he had been determined to be mentally ill by a doctor and according to a judge's order presented an imminent danger to himself or others. So why was Mr. Cho allowed to buy a gun, given his obvious mental health problems? If this country is not yet ready to outlaw guns outright, then at the very least stringent safeguards and background checks urgently need to be put in place that deny easy access to guns to people with severe mental imbalances.   Mathias Frisch College Park, Md., April 19, 2007  To the Editor: Re ''The Silence of Politicians'' (editorial, April 19): While I commend your editorial urging politicians to confront what you call ''the runaway gun problem,'' I see the only workable solution to putting a stop to more and more Columbine and Virginia Tech-type disasters is not for politicians to admit there is a problem but to repeal and rewrite the Second Amendment to the Constitution. While it's true that even the whisper of gun control causes politicians to run for cover, I believe that a groundswell can be built by those of us -- and we are in the majority -- who are appalled by what the rest of the civilized world sees as our Wild West heritage.

731.text: GUN-5288 PRIMARY Guns Sold Without Checks In Md.; Police Scramble To Find Firearms Dozens of handguns were sold to convicted criminals and others barred by law from buying them after Maryland State Police fell weeks behind in completing required background checks, officials said yesterday. The guns were among thousands that Maryland dealers sold blindly in recent months as the state police struggled to overcome a backlog they blamed on problems with a new computer and a new federal requirement to run gun purchases by the FBI. After discovering that at least 54 of those weapons were sold to people barred from buying them, police have scrambled to recover the guns. But they acknowledged yesterday that 10 of them have not been retrieved. It was unclear why police officials allowed the backlog to build up for so long or why the new computer system was installed during the holiday season, when gun sales generally peak, and just as the federal regulation took effect. State Police Superintendent David B. Mitchell said that after learning of the problem two weeks ago, he assigned extra employees to work through a backlog of about 1,500 background checks. He said he also ordered an internal investigation "to find out how this happened and to make sure that it can never, ever happen again." "I'm very disappointed in our performance. It's just unacceptable," he said, adding that the backlog was finally eliminated last week. "There will be accountability on this, trust me," he said.

732.text: GUN-338 PRIMARY The Region; Gun Control Voted - The Hard Way A Connecticut gun control bill, voted out of committee the day after President Reagan was shot, got Senate approval last week after the attempted assassination of Pope John Paul II. The measure was passed when a senator who had been against it switched her vote ''in consideration of the violence that has taken place against a man of peace.'' Governor O'Neill has not said if he would sign the bill, previously approved by the House. It mandates a one-year minimum sentence for those caught carrying an unlicensed handgun, except under certain ''mitigating circumstances.'' The New Jersey State Senate debated a much tougher gun control measure favored by Governor Byrne and sponsored by Senate President Joseph P. Merlino. But the bill, which would ban handgun sales, was withdrawn from consideration until after the June 2 primary when it became clear that it lacked sufficient support for passage.

735.text: GUN-10761 PRIMARY Cases piling up seeking Supreme Court's clarification of Second Amendment rights A funny thing has happened in the three years since gun-rights activists won their biggest victory at the Supreme Court. They've been on a losing streak in the lower courts. The activists found the holy grail in 2008 when the Supreme Court's 5 to 4 decision in                 District of Columbia v. Heller             said the Second Amendment guaranteed an individual right to own a firearm unconnected to military service. The court followed it up with                 McDonald v. Chicago              two years later, holding that the amendment applies not just to gun control laws passed by Congress but to local and state laws as well. The decisions were seen as a green light to challenge gun restrictions across the country, and the lawsuits have come raining down - more than two a week, according to the anti-gun Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence. But it is the Brady Center that is crowing about the results. "Three years and more than 400 legal challenges later, courts - so far - have held that the Supreme Court's ruling in Heller was narrow and limited, and that the Second Amendment does not interfere with the people's right to enact legislation protecting families and communities from gun violence," the center said in a report optimistically titled "Hollow Victory?" Even those challenging gun restrictions acknowledge that the courts have been unwilling to expand upon the basic right that most people agree Heller bestowed: the ability to keep a handgun in one's home for self-defense purposes.

734.text: GUN-11496 PRIMARY Candidates Cower on Gun Control At a moment when the country needs resolve and fearlessness to reduce the affliction of gun violence that kills more than 80 people a day, both presidential candidates have kicked away the opportunity for leadership. On Wednesday, reacting to the mass murder in Colorado last week, Mitt Romney and President Obama paid lip service to the problem but ducked when the chance arose to stand up for their former principles. That's not terribly surprising in the case of Mitt Romney, who has built an entire campaign around an avoidance of specifics and a refusal to take unpopular positions. The governor who once showed mettle by banning assault weapons in Massachusetts told Brian Williams of NBC News that he now believes the country needs no new gun laws and no government action at all. ''Changing the heart of the American people may well be what's essential,'' Mr. Romney said, though he provided not a clue on how he plans to reach that heart and help reduce the nation's tolerance of violence. He didn't even seem to understand the gun laws that are in place, saying the Colorado shooter ''shouldn't have had any kind of weapons.'' In fact, all of the shooter's purchases, including an assault rifle, were perfectly legal in the state. Though Mr. Romney expressed faith in the federal requirement for background checks before buying a gun, he didn't acknowledge how porous the federal system is -- largely by allowing unchecked sales at gun shows -- and how much more effective tighter state regulations have been in restricting trafficking in places like California.

737.text: NA-46254 PRIMARY GUEST COLUMN; Gun bill is a reversal for Ga. public safety Columbine, Sandy Hook, Virginia Tech: These are names we now associate with a horrifying image --- students fleeing schools as their peers are gunned down by disgruntled loners or, more frighteningly, by their own classmates. Price Middle School and Ronald E. McNair Academy are institutions much closer to home where we recently experienced near misses. A January 2013 shooting at Price wounded a 14-year-old boy. Last August, a gunman barricaded himself in McNair offices and fired at police; miraculously, no one was hurt in that ordeal. House Bill 875 increases the possibility that Georgia students may once again be subjected to such dangers. The bill, if it becomes law, increases the chances of violence by expanding the list of public places where a concealed firearm can be carried. The right to bear arms, as stated in the U.S. Constitution, is part of our Bill of Rights. The majority of gun owners in Georgia are law-abiding citizens who own firearms for sport or protection. My opposition to HB 875 is not about that right, but about the accompanying responsibility, in particular our responsibility as lawmakers to keep all citizens safe. The bill would allow concealed weapons to be carried in bars, houses of worship and even areas of the airport preceding TSA-secured screening stations. HB 875 also would expand the list of people allowed to carry a firearm on a school campus, and makes carrying a firearm on elementary school property a misdemeanor.

738.text: GUN-6818 PRIMARY ATF UNVEILS ONLINE TOOL TO HELP COPS TRACE GUNS USED IN CRIMES Widening the war against illegal gun trafficking, the federal government Tuesday announced the start of a high-tech system that officials said would speed efforts to track the sources of guns used in crimes. The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms said the new system would give police in the field instant access to computerized records of more than 1 million guns used in crimes. Bureau officials said they expect it to be used to more quickly identify sources of guns used in crimes, as well as regions with high concentrations of illegal weapons sales. "The more information that we have on 'crime guns,' the more productive we become," said ATF Director John W. Magaw. "The more quickly we follow leads, the more likely it is that we will solve a crime." What makes the new system so useful, ATF officials said, is that now they are able to tell almost instantly whether someone shows up repeatedly as the source of a crime gun or whether a particular gun store has been the source of many such weapons. By identifying such patterns, they said, they can more efficiently target their investigations. The announcement of the new gun-tracing initiative, called Online Lead, marks the latest step in a government effort to target illegal weapons traffickers. The ATF began building a database of guns seized in crimes about five years ago. But until now, agents in the field were not able to get access to the information on their own. They had to telephone the ATF's crime-gun-analysis unit in Washington or wait for the unit to mail them computer disks containing the information.

739.text: GUN-10279 PRIMARY Metro Briefing  New York: Manhattan: Council Passes New Gun Laws The City Council voted 43 to 2 yesterday to pass legislation that would make it easier for crime victims to sue gun manufacturers and dealers who do not follow strict guidelines for distributing and selling their weapons. The bill, which is similar to measures passed in San Francisco and Washington, would establish a code of conduct for the manufacturers and dealers that is intended to help reduce illegal gun trafficking. The gun industry opposes the measure, but Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg said that he intended to sign it. The Council also passed bills to ratchet up penalties for those who violate the city's assault weapons ban, and to increase the minimum age for permits for rifles and shotguns to 21 from 18.    Winnie Hu (NYT)

736.text: GUN-5465 PRIMARY System misses the mark In Colorado, one popular way to celebrate peace on Earth and goodwill toward men is with a shiny new firearm. Happiness is a warm gun, remember, and more than 9,000 Coloradans rang in the holiday season this month with improved firepower. God bless us, everyone. One good thing about the trend is that at least this year, fewer guns were sold to spouse abusers, drug addicts and convicted felons. Beginning Nov. 30, the Brady Act provisions requiring criminal background checks went into effect for people buying rifles, shotguns and other long guns, as well as handguns. That date also marked the launch of the new FBI national instant check system, a computerized database designed to give licensed gun dealers approval to sell a gun or orders to put the sale on hold within two minutes. It includes information on anyone with a criminal record or with a history of domestic violence, or anyone judged mentally incompetent or under a restraining order. People who have renounced their U.S. citizenship, have been dishonorably discharged from the military or who are illegal drug users also are identified. It's an enormous database and, suffice it to say, the system isn't exactly a well-oiled machine yet. "The first couple of days, there were hideous and lengthy crashes," said Susan Kitchen, Colorado Bureau of Investigation's agent in charge of the statewide gun-buyers' background check program.

741.text: GUN-7058 PRIMARY A Closer Look at Rampage Killings Next week marks the first anniversary of the shootings at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo., which left 15 dead and 23 wounded. The massacre, carried out by two Columbine students, generated intense debate about access to guns, the effect of violence in movies and video games, and the failure of schools and families to spot signs of murderous rage in the teenage gunmen. Yet the Littleton incident was only one of 13 rampage attacks last year, and multiple murders in schools and workplaces have become common news items. A team of New York Times reporters and researchers, in examining the nature of these attacks and the individuals involved, compiled a detailed database on 100 such cases over the past 50 years. The series of articles published in The Times this week based on that research offered several new insights. Although such killings account for only one-tenth of 1 percent of all homicides, the series confirmed the public perception that they appear to be increasing. It also found that rampage killers share several traits. Most are better educated than typical murderers, are more likely to have military experience, and are far more likely to kill themselves. Cultural influences on this group, such as violent entertainment, seemed to have little impact. Instead, the most common factor was serious mental health problems. About half had received formal diagnosis of mental illness, often schizophrenia. More than half made threats, and a third had histories of violent behavior. Many never received treatment for mental disorders or were not monitored to keep them on their medication. Most of their rampage attacks were not sudden, impulsive acts but the culmination of years of rage, depression and mental illness. Often the failure of families, co-workers and even therapists to deal with warning signs led to catastrophic consequences.

746.text: GUN-11557 PRIMARY July 25 letters to the editor Theater should make sure doors are closed Regarding the bloody chaos that occurred in Aurora, Colo., I agree with the focus on better gun control; however, we have to be concerned with all those automatic weapons already out there. In addition, how about placing some responsibility with the cinema where the massacre took place? Shouldn't it be the responsibility of the theater to be aware if an exit has been opened and left ajar before and during the performance? It would be in their best interest to possibly thwart this type of incident with better security, and it could also prevent people from sneaking in these exits and not paying for a ticket. Len Rogers San Leandro Some brilliant people can be wired wrong About the event in Colorado, I suggest we look at it as one person losing it all. It could be somewhat similar to someone losing it all and committing suicide. Consider how many people are on Earth, and expect some to lose it all. Another idea to consider is that very brilliant people are mentally wired strongly and there can be problems with those types of personalties. They probably lack the ability to disregard an idea or forget things. They probably lack emotional ability to accept many possibilities. They think in terms of yes or no. Frank Jakubowsky Oakland NRA offers usual lame excuses

742.text: GUN-6253 PRIMARY LETTERS, FAXES & E-MAIL GUN LAWS NEED TO BE ENFORCED  Before all of you good citizens pat yourselves on the back after getting the CBI check back on line, ask yourselves the following questions: Did the CBI send an officer around to the 88 rejected buyers to see why they wanted to buy a gun when they had a restraining order or some other criminal record preventing them from buying a gun? Did the CBI send an officer around to any rejected buyers with a felony record to arrest him, or her, for committing a federal felony by trying to buy a gun? If the CBI didn't do either of these actions then they are accomplishing exactly nothing. This is what the FBI does when they reject a felony gun buyer. Where is the follow-up, arrest, trial and jailing of felons trying to buy guns? Not! Gun laws without enforcement are no laws at all and accomplish nothing. STEPHEN E. BROWN Trinidad  WHICH PASS TO TAKE? Re: "The routing of Interstate 70: a landslide decision," Ed Quillen column, Aug. 3. I note that Quillen is again on the pass-road warpath. He rejects the wisdom of the current I-70 corridor over the previously existing Loveland Pass route, in favor of an obscure route over or through Arapahoe Pass, north of Boulder. Ironically, the Colorado Legislature - usually not one of Ed's favorite institutions - anticipated his motion more than three generations ago, appropriating funds in 1904 for just such a road over Arapahoe Pass. Fortunately it was never completed, since this road, now a hiking trail, would have bisected the present Indian Peaks Wilderness Area.

745.text: GUN-6812 PRIMARY News in Brief The Richmond, Va.-based United Network for Organ Sharing has run the transplant system under an HHS contract since 1986, when the national system was created. It is now operating under a one-year contract extension. The network is in the middle of a bitter high-profile fight with HHS over who has the authority to set transplant policy and what the policy should be. Gun program blocks 160,000 potential buyers WASHINGTON - More than 160,000 people were prevented from purchasing firearms during the first year of computerized instant criminal background checks on would-be gun buyers, the Justice Department said Tuesday. Nearly three out of four of them were convicted felons. Combined with the 312,000 handgun purchases barred during the manual system in effect from Feb. 28, 1994, through Nov. 30, 1998, the Brady Act has barred a total of more than 470,000 prohibited people from acquiring firearms, the department said. State and local authorities did background checks during the old system. Under the National Instant Criminal Background Check System that began Dec. 1, 1998, about half the checks are done by the FBI and the remainder by states. Since the computerized system began, rifles and other long guns, as well as handguns, have been covered. Of the prospective buyers denied firearms in FBI checks, 72 percent were known felons, 12 percent were convicted of domestic violence, 4 percent were abusers of illegal drugs, 3 percent were subject to domestic violence restraining orders and 3 percent were fugitives from justice.

740.text: GUN-9589 PRIMARY Guns "R' Us club settles in Re: E-mail goof leaves party red-faced, Oct. 31 C.T. Bowen column. Editor: Bill Bunting was not after state Rep. Tom Anderson because he didn't want to drop mail for the president. Bunting is angry at Anderson because he was one of the very few Republican House delegates in Pasco who abstained from voting on one of Bunting's high priority gun lobby bills, House Bill 149, now enshrined as section 790.333 of Florida statutes. It makes us, the people, pay to clean the lead pollution from the gun ranges in Florida. Anderson, to his credit, told me at a candidates debate that he thought people should clean up their own mess. Except for Anderson, our entire legislative delegation -  Ken Littlefield, Victor Crist, Mike Fasano and Heather Fiorentino -  voted to stick us with the bill. The e-mail comments of Alan Levine ("I don't know what is going on, but Bill is capable of sometimes being fairly extreme and it worries me that he may do something that draws attention") and Karen Unger, spokeswoman for Gov. Bush ("Oh, yeah, Bill can be bit nutty") are hardly a revelation to those of us who have watched Bunting turn the Pasco County Republican Party into his own private Guns "R" Us club. He uses the party to support whatever the gun lobby in Florida wants, regardless if it diminishes public safety or makes us pay for it.

749.text: NA-41199 PRIMARY Mike urged to holster attacks TOP SENATE Democrats, including Chuck Schumer, want Mayor Bloomberg to lay off his attacks on Senate Democrats who voted against background checks for gun buyers. With the mayor bankrolling attack ads against senators from both parties who opposed the gun control measure, Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-Nev.) told reporters Thursday that he spoke to Bloomberg this week "to remind him that a Republican-controlled Senate is a sure sign that we'll never, ever get anything done" on gun control. Schumer (D-N.Y.) knocked Bloomberg in Time magazine. "Frankly, I don't think Bloomberg's ads are effective," Schumer told the magazine in an extensive article published Thursday. "The mayor of New York City putting ads against people in red states is not going to be effective." But a defiant Bloomberg said Thursday he isn't changing course. "This is not a partisan issue," he said. "If you vote to continue the carnage on our streets I don't care what party you're in."

744.text: GUN-5517 PRIMARY CHECKS ON BUYERS OF LONG GUNS BEGIN NEXT WEEK / GUN DEALERS ANTICIPATE DELAYS AS A NEW PHASE OF A FEDERAL GUN-CONTROL LAW GOES INTO EFFECT. Gun dealers are bracing for an onslaught of surprised hunters come Monday, the start of deer-hunting season and the day a new phase of a federal gun-control law takes effect. Pennsylvania and other states will begin conducting background checks on people who want to buy rifles and shotguns, and some of the state's 1 million hunters may have to wait days to get their weapons. Currently, background checks are required only for handguns. Although the provision to expand the checks was part of a Brady gun-control law that went into effect in 1994, many sportsmen do not know about it. "They never really paid attention to what the NRA [National Rifle Association] told them," said Mike Protz, who runs Hallstead Electronics & Sporting Goods in Hallstead, Pa. He said that rifles and shotguns represented the bulk of his firearms sales, and that each sale on or after Monday would result in more paperwork and potential delays. Hunting season and the weeks leading to Christmas tend to be the busiest for gun dealers. "I don't think anybody is looking forward to that crunch," said Protz, who is firearms chairman of the Pennsylvania Federation of Sportsmen's Clubs. Each check will cost dealers $2, a fee they likely will pass on to customers. An additional $3 will be collected for each gun sold. Federal law bans gun purchases by people convicted of or under indictment on felony charges, fugitives, the mentally ill, those with dishonorable military discharges, those who have renounced U.S. citizenship, illegal aliens, illegal-drug users, and those convicted of domestic violence misdemeanors or under domestic violence restraining orders. State laws add other categories; for Pennsylvania, that includes repeated drunken driving and some juvenile offenses.

743.text: GUN-1564 PRIMARY OFFICERS RIP MISSOURI GUN PROPOSAL ... POLICE, SHERIFFS, TROOPERS UNITE Law enforcement officers held a series of news conferences around Missouri Friday to denounce a legislative proposal that would allow people 21 and older to get permits to carry concealed weapons.   In St. Louis, several area police chiefs said the streets would become more dangerous for both police and the public if the proposal is passed.   ''This legislation is being pitched to people as a quick fix,'' said St.  Louis Chief Clarence Harmon. '' In no terms has this proven to be the case. I think it's regressing to the Wild West era.'' Harmon was among about 75 law enforcement officials who gathered Friday in front of a police memorial statute downtown to oppose the amendment to an anti-crime bill before the Missouri House.   The police chiefs of St. Louis County, Maryland Heights and Hazelwood were among those who denounced the proposal. The presidents of both the city and county police boards also spoke against it. '' This is bad legislation,'' said Maryland Heights Police Chief Neil Kurlander. ''Common sense tells you that if there are more guns on the street, there will be more violent crimes, not less.'' Added Washington County Sheriff Ron Skiles: ''This will set our society back 125 to 150 years.' ' In Jefferson City, Col. C.E. ''Mel'' Fisher, superintendent of the Missouri Highway Patrol, said that law enforcement was united in its opposition to the measure, concerned about the safety of both the public and officers.   Law enforcement officers would have trouble ''sorting out the good guys and the bad guys'' when all carried guns, Fisher said. Pat-down searches would become routine because everyone would be a potential threat, he said.   The proposal would lead to more violence, Fisher predicted. '' Do we really want people carrying concealed weapons at our sporting events, rock concerts and other large gatherings?'' Fisher asked.   Fisher was joined by eight other officers at a morning news conference in Jefferson City. In addition to St. Louis, similar events were planned in Kansas City and Springfield.   The House may take a final vote on the gun proposal next week. Under the measure, permits would not be issued to convicted felons, people convicted of drug offenses, people who have been committed to a mental hospital, and people who are ''publicly known to be habitually intoxicated'' or have at least two convictions for driving while intoxicated.   In St. Louis, Fisher and Harmon said some of the restrictions would be hard to enforce. Sheriffs would have no way of knowing whether a person had spent time in a mental hospital, they said. In addition, ''drug dealers who have not been convicted could get permits,'' said Fisher.   The Missouri Sheriffs Association voted 74-7 to oppose the proposal, said Cass County Sheriff Homer Foote, president of the association.   Under current law, only specific groups, such as law enforcement officers and judges, can carry concealed weapons. Others can carry firearms so long as they are not hidden.   The House approved the concealed-gun proposal last month by attaching it to an anti-crime bill.   Legislators have said they are receiving record numbers of calls from constituents who favor the proposal. The lobbying is being coordinated by the National Rifle Association.

characters: people who have been committed to a mental hospital
people who are publicly known to be habitually intoxicated
people with at least two convictions for driving while intoxicated

752.text: GUN-902 PRIMARY THE RIGHTS OF THE PEOPLE One need not feel secure from the possible use of the concealable handgun evenif the National Rifle Association's $4 million campaign in Maryland to weaken handgun legislation failed. With the NRA's financial support to both our senators, one can see why our legislators are not supporting any legislation to fight handgun sales, suicides, accidents and family members killing each other in domestic quarrels. Compare the more than 10,000 annual deaths from handguns in the United States with the only three children who died through the use of lawn darts over the past 18 years. But our senators supported action to ban lawn darts because the Consumer Product Safety Commission felt it was necessary. But our senators would not support permitting the same Safety Commission to include within its function a study of the ''hazardous substance'' of the deadly handgun.   Eugene P. Schwartz University City

747.text: GUN-6795 PRIMARY PA. SENATE APPROVES BILL ON FIREARMS / TRIGGER LOCKS WOULD BE SOLD WITH GUNS. CITIES WOULD BE UNABLE TO SUE GUNMAKERS. A HOUSE VOTE IS THE NEXT STEP. The state Senate yesterday approved a mixed-bag bill on gun control that would require dealers to sell trigger locks with firearms but also prevent Philadelphia and other cities and school districts from suing gun manufacturers. The bill, approved 42-7, would not require that the trigger locks be used. The measure still would require a final endorsement in the state House of Representatives. Floor debate over the bill focused on the lawsuit ban, aimed mainly at Philadelphia and Pittsburgh. Public officials in those cities have said they are contemplating lawsuits against gunmakers to recover the costs incurred because of gun-related violence. The suits would be patterned after the lawsuit that led to a settlement between state governments and tobacco companies. The Senate had originally debated the lawsuit ban as part of an amendment passed last month but never given final approval. By incorporating the provision into a wider-ranging gun measure already approved by the House, senators gave the other body a choice between accepting the lawsuit provision or rejecting the bill entirely. The bill would not prevent gun manufacturers from being sued over such things as product-liability matters, but specifically bans suits over lawful design, manufacture, marketing or sales of firearms. State Sen. Vincent Fumo (D., Phila.) argued in favor of the bill, saying the lawsuit ban would prevent Philadelphia from being exposed to legal action taken by gun manufacturers who would contend that such lawsuits were filed only as a harassment tactic by the city.

748.text: NA-41033 PRIMARY PS .38 SPECIAL S.I. parents want armed guards STATEN ISLAND residents are gunning for armed guards in schools, but the city's top school official said he'll shoot down the initiative. "It's not happening. Plain and simple ," said Schools Chancellor Dennis Walcott. "I can't be any clearer than that," he added, echoing Mayor Bloomberg's longstanding position in favor of gun control, not gun-toting guards. "We have safe schools." In the wake of the horrifying school shooting in Newtown, Conn., the National Rifle Association called for armed guards at schools. Some towns, including Marlboro, N.J., have followed the gun group's lead. And Monday, Staten Island's Community Education Council was poised to vote on its own controversial proposal to station 300 to 500 retired police officers at local schools to perhaps thwart another school shooting. Parents supported the move. "They should do it. Definitely. (After) what happened in Connecticut, we need security in schools," said Maha Riz, 34, of Heartland Village, who has two children in the fourth and sixth grades. Riz was referring to the massacre at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, where Adam Lanza mowed down 26 people with his AR-15 assault rifle. Danielle Sclafani, 33, of Rossville, doesn't want to see that happen again. "Whatever needs to be done for the safety of our kids, I'm for," said Sclafani, whose 4-year-old son attends preschool. "It's a great idea." Jose Garcia, a carpenter with an 11-year-old daughter in sixth grade, also endorsed the by-any-means necessary approach to school security.

751.text: GUN-6204 PRIMARY NUMBERS KEEP GUN-LAW VIOLATORS AT LARGE / AN ODD CHORUS OF PRO- AND ANTI-FIREARM FORCES IS CALLING FOR TOUGHER ENFORCEMENT. Every day, a computer at the sprawling FBI crime information center in rural West Virginia kicks out the names of 100 or more convicted felons and others who, though barred by law from owning a gun, tried to buy one anyway by lying on their firearms application. In most instances, the federal government knows where to find these gun-law violators, and has in its possession evidence that could lead to their convictions. But this is one gun law that the government rarely prosecutes. The Clinton administration says it does not have the staff to pursue many cases, and is instead focusing on more serious crimes, but that assertion has triggered criticism from Republicans and pro-gun groups. "It takes a lot of nerve to bang your fist and demand tougher juvenile gun laws while doing nothing to enforce the ones that already exist," said Rep. Bill McCollum (R., Fla.), a former federal prosecutor who has held hearings on the issue. "I must point out that doubling the size of the criminal code will not matter if the Clinton-Gore administration refuses to vigorously enforce these laws." The administration counters that the gun-control law has accomplished its main purpose by preventing felons from buying guns legally. The issue figures to play a central role in the congressional debate later this month on proposals by the White House to restrict gun sales to juveniles and to impose background checks on all sales at gun shows.

750.text: GUN-20033 PRIMARY Ban on abusers carrying guns divides coalition WASHINGTON, D.C. -- A law that bans anyone with a domestic abuse conviction from carrying a gun threatens to fracture what has been a powerful coalition of gun control advocates, domestic abuse groups and law enforcement. The nation's largest police organizations say the law unfairly punishes officers because they can lose their jobs if they have to give up their weapons, and they want the law changed to let officers keep their guns. But advocates for domestic abuse victims and some other officers ask why law enforcers should be exempt from a law crafted to protect battered spouses and their families. The sides were far apart Wednesday as the House subcommittee on crime heard proposed changes to the law, which covers misdemeanor domestic abuse convictions and is retroactive from Sept. 30, 1996. The Fraternal Order of Police, the nation's largest police organization, wants the law to cover police but doesn't want it to be retroactive. The National Association of Police Organizations and the International Brotherhood of Police Officers both support giving law enforcement an exemption that would allow officers to carry their weapons on duty. Advocates for victims and the National Black Police Association say the law should be left alone. Police officers "ought to be above and beyond the standard we want to set for our society," says Ronald Hampton of the National Black Police Association. "We should hold police to a higher standard, not a different standard."

753.text: NA-41047 PRIMARY The will of the people What more could Washington need? What more than 126,952 petitions urging the most reasonable restrictions on ownership of weaponry that is designed for mass killing and that has been put repeatedly to full lethal use? What more than the horrifying massacre of 20 children and six adults that prompted the Daily News to invite readers into a campaign for a ban on assault rifles and high-capacity ammunition magazines, as well as for more comprehensive gun controls? Response was immediate, sustained and overwhelming. Petitions have poured in from New Yorkers and from citizens around the country who have joined The News' family of readers through the paper's digital edition. This passionate flood bespeaks a truth that National Rifle Association leaders have so effectively bullied out of the halls of power: Americans ache for gun controls even as they respect the Second Amendment. Americans are desperate to stop the madmen of Sandy Hook, Aurora, Columbine, Virginia Tech, Tucson and so many other killing fields, past and future, from getting their hands on battlefield-style firearms. Americans pray for an end to the trafficking in illegal handguns that routinely kill in ones and twos and threes every day. Visibly devastated by Sandy Hook, President Obama assigned Vice President Biden to develop far-reaching gun control measures. Biden is due to issue a report by Tuesday, setting the stage for a perversely tough battle.

756.text: GUN-6151 PRIMARY Advocates Of Gun Bills Plan TV Ads With Congress locked in a pitched battle over gun control, a leading advocacy group for tighter restrictions will show a new series of television advertisements featuring the talk-show host Rosie O'Donnell and the actress Susan Sarandon. The group, Handgun Control Inc., will broadcast 30-second advertisements in a two-week campaign beginning on Friday in the Washington area, and in nine swing Congressional districts on Tuesday, urging viewers to call legislators and demand tougher laws. The first week of advertisements will cost $100,000, said Naomi Paiss, a spokeswoman for the group. The campaign was scheduled several days before the fatal shootings on Wednesday at a Fort Worth church, Ms. Paiss said. A string of similar multiple shootings, including one at a Colorado high school last spring, has galvanized the gun debate on Capitol Hill. The timing of the new advertisements is meant to coincide with a final legislative showdown on gun control this year. House and Senate negotiators are trying to hammer out new legislation, but lobbyists on both sides see little room for agreement on the pivotal issue of stricter regulations of gun shows. Handgun Control has run radio spots during the Senate and House debates on gun bills earlier this year, but the organization decided to make one last push with television. "We need to reach as many people as quickly as possible," Ms. Paiss said, explaining the group's first television advertisement campaign in three years.

755.text: GUN-3109 PRIMARY NEW GUN LAW ; Miller signs bill as criticism builds Gov. Zell Miller has signed a new state gun law that he says will keep criminals from buying handguns but that gun control proponents decry as the nation's "most extreme example" of efforts to gut gun laws. "This piece of legislation is going to keep handguns out of the hands of criminals while allowing law-abiding citizens to purchase guns," Miller said Wednesday as representatives of the National Rifle Association watched him sign the bill into law. But Sarah Brady, chairwoman of Handgun Control Inc. and wife of former White House press secretary Jim Brady, who was shot during the 1981 attempt on Ronald Reagan's life, said that, in passing the new gun law, Georgia's legislators "have, in effect, sent an invitation to criminals urging them to come purchase guns in Georgia." "I am shocked that a state which is preparing to host the Summer Olympics next year would risk scaring off millions of tourist dollars just to appease the gun lobby," she said. The new law, which takes effect Jan. 1, requires instant background checks for gun buyers and eliminates local waiting periods imposed on gun sales by 10 Georgia communities. The bill was portrayed by legislative supporters and the NRA as a substitute for the federal Brady law, which requires a waiting period of up to five days to conduct a criminal background check on handgun purchases.

759.text: GUN-6110 PRIMARY CANDIDATE, NOT GUN ISSUE, LOST IN CALIF. When Marta Macias Brown announced last month that she would campaign for Congress as a gun-control candidate, and would paint her chief rival as a tool of the gun lobby, her staffers decided to focus heavily on working-mom voters, whom they nicknamed "women with lives." On occasion, Brown staffers would say aloud, "I wonder what the women with lives are thinking right now?" The answer became clear early yesterday, here in a working-class district 55 miles east of Los Angeles, in the first test of whether gun control will be an issue in the 2000 congressional races. Women with lives were not sufficiently motivated to go to the polls and vote for Brown on the basis of her antigun crusade. So she lost a squeaker to a state senator, Joe Baca, whom she had derided as a poster boy of the National Rifle Association. The result suggested that perhaps gun control might not work as an issue, despite all the recent carnage and all the support in polls for new restrictions on weaponry. But analysts profess to see a silver lining for gun-control supporters. In Tuesday's special election to fill the seat of her late husband, liberal George E. Brown Jr., who died in July, the numbers were so close - 11,689 to 11,201 - that Marta Brown has refused to concede, citing 1,750 absentee ballots that had yet to be counted. But those tallies, due by tomorrow, are not expected to change the result.

757.text: GUN-2716 PRIMARY National Rifle Association shoots back USA TODAY got a taste this week of what members of Congress and other government officials face when they tackle gun-control issues. A stream of more than 215,000 pre-printed, pre-addressed postcards flowed in from members of the National Rifle Association. Cards also went to major TV networks. NBC received "thousands." They are part of an effort by the 3.4-million-member NRA to raise money for a campaign to defend "firearm freedoms." USA TODAY's editorials support tough gun laws including registration of guns and licensing of owners. "Quit attacking my constitutional right to keep and bear arms," wrote Carole J. Mahnke, Brenham, Texas, in an attached letter. "If this constitutional right is abolished, the right to free speech and the right of a free press is sure to follow." "Go get their ass," E.S. Secrease, Coalinga, Calif., advised the NRA. "My wife and I would like to add our voices to those of other readers urging you to rethink the anti-gun news thrust of USA TODAY, and do a real service to this country by championing productive crime-control measures," said Bradford and Doris Cummings, New Bern, N.C. "To solve a problem, you must attack the cause," wrote Robert Tinsley, Albuquerque, N.M. "Guns are a symptom. By attacking that symptom, you are in danger of depriving me and others of a basic right of humankind: the right of self-preservation."

761.text: GUN-9664 PRIMARY Kerry Faults Bush for Failing to Press Weapons Ban Senator John Kerry used the expiration of the assault-weapons ban on Monday to broaden his attack on President Bush over crime and security. Mr. Kerry said Mr. Bush had failed a ''test of character'' by not pressing Congress to extend the ban and by cutting financing for community police officers. It was the second time in four days that Mr. Kerry chose to focus on the assault-weapons ban, a departure from months in which he mainly mentioned guns only to talk about being a lifelong hunter. Many Democrats consider gun control dicey for their party -- a priority for their urban base but an issue that costs votes in rural precincts and among blue-collar union members. But Mr. Kerry, the Democratic presidential nominee, hammered away on the issue, accusing Mr. Bush of leaving the nation less safe. ''Today George Bush chose to make the job of terrorists easier and to make the job of America's police officers harder, and that's just plain wrong,'' Mr. Kerry said, playing up his long-ago experience as a prosecutor to an audience of police officers and crime victims. Mr. Kerry said the president ''chose his powerful friends in the gun lobby over police officers and families that he promised to protect.'' He added, ''He failed the test of leadership by saying that he supports an assault-weapons ban but then doing everything in his power to keep the Republicans from sending it to him.''

760.text: NA-42805 PRIMARY Obama Set to Announce Plans to Address Gun Violence 1:30 p.m. | Updated  President Obama will formally unveil his plans for reducing gun violence at a White House event Wednesday just before noon, standing side by side with gun control advocates, White House officials said on Tuesday. The president is expected to announce a push for new laws to restrict the availability of guns and to embrace a series of executive actions that he can take without seeking congressional approval. The White House press secretary, Jay Carney, said Mr. Obama will be flanked by children from around the country who had written to Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr. in the wake of the massacre of school children in Newtown, Conn. in December. "He intends to take a comprehensive approach," to reducing gun violence in the country, Mr. Carney said in a briefing with reporters. Gun control was not on the president's agenda for his second term, but became a top priority in the hours after the Newtown shooting. With the event at the White House, Mr. Obama will kick off what lawmakers predict could be a bitter and drawn-out fight over a new assault weapons ban, limits on the high-capacity magazines that are used by those weapons, and a dramatic expansion of the background check system for gun purchases. Mr. Carney said that the president will call upon Congress to pass that legislation, including a push to "close the very big loopholes" in the background check system for gun purchases.

characters: Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr.

758.text: GUN-854 PRIMARY VOICES/ Do you think we need stronger gun-control laws? Yes. We need to make it as hard as possible for people to get guns. No automatic weapon should even be sold to lay people. The Arizona Legislature is presently trying to pass a bill that would allow citizens to carry a concealed weapon under certain circumstances. The police have enough trouble. Shirley Fifer, 58 Benefits administrator Montpelier, Ohio The laws are strong enough. Perhaps part of the problem is that law enforcement agencies are understaffed and not able to enforce everything. Of course, I live in a rural area where we don't have much exposure to the crime found in big cities. But I do believe that we need more police officers. Raymond J. Buffington 71, business owner Muskogee, Okla. Yes, we definitely need stronger gun-control laws. It's too easy for most people to get guns these days. With the money and a driver's license, I can go out in Muskogee right now and buy a truckload of guns from pawnshop to pawnshop. There is no seven-day waiting period. Anyone can buy a gun here. Jane Garrett, 47 Homemaker West Terre Haute, Ind. No. The laws just need to be enforced more. Stronger gun-control laws are not going to keep guns out of the hands of criminals. They would just make it harder for law-abiding citizens to get guns. If that happened, I would be apt to purchase a gun under the table just like the criminals. Carlo Russo, 36 Physician New York, N.Y. I definitely think so. It's the guns that kill, not people. If you don't allow people with guns, then it would be more difficult for them to kill. In Europe, it's almost impossible to get a gun. You have to justify the need for one. We may have to lose a little of our right to own a gun for the sake of society.

762.text: GUN-10437 PRIMARY The right to bear arms, but . . . ; The Supreme Court's decision isn't a license to overturn all gun-control laws. The city of Chicago bans private citizens from keeping handguns in their homes. So, too, did the District of Columbia, before the Supreme Court's 2008 ruling, in District of Columbia v. Heller, that the District law violated the Second Amendment guarantee of the right to keep and bear arms. Should the Chicago law be treated any differently, as a constitutional matter, than the District ban, because of the District's unique status as a federal enclave? Or, to put the question in legal terms: Is the Second Amendment among the protections in the Bill of Rights that also constrain state and local governments? Acting on the final day of the 2009 term and splitting 5 to 4, the Supreme Court said Monday that it is. The court's decision in McDonald v. City of Chicago is both correct and potentially troubling. It is correct because the general tendency of the court has been to find that the amendments in the Bill of Rights -- guarantees of free speech and freedom of religion, protections against unreasonable search and seizure, prohibitions on cruel and unusual punishment -- apply to state and local governments through the 14th Amendment's due process clause. Some scattered and relatively minor provisions in the Bill of Rights -- such as requiring a jury trial in civil cases or prohibiting excessive fines -- are not deemed to have been "incorporated" by the 14th Amendment, but most are. Having decided that the Second Amendment protects an individual right to bear arms, it would have been odd for the court to determine that state governments can infringe on that right.

765.text: GUN-3879 PRIMARY Court says gun-ban law went too far The Supreme Court on Wednesday struck down a federal law banning the possession of guns near schools, telling Congress it invaded the power of the states by passing the law in 1990. The 5-4 ruling, one of the most significant constitutional decisions in years, could spark challenges to a broad range of federal laws - from environmental to criminal - that are unrelated to interstate commerce or other areas of federal jurisdiction. The decision also could energize Republican efforts to return power to the states. "This is a not very gentle reminder from the court that there are limits to congressional power," says constitutional expert Alan Morrison. He forsees a quick challenge to the federal law criminalizing marijuana possession. The Gun-Free School Zones Act was passed to curb the rising tide of firearms in schools. Like many federal laws, it was justified as part of Congress's constitutional power to regulate interstate commerce. But Chief Justice William Rehnquist said the law "has nothing to do with commerce or any sort of enterprise" and steals state power away. Laws in more than 40 states banning gun possession near schools are unaffected by the ruling. Justice Stephen Breyer read an impassioned dissent, arguing that Congress was justified in passing the law: "In today's economic world, gun-related violence near the classroom makes a significant difference to our economic, as well as our social, well-being."

763.text: GUN-5453 PRIMARY Tampa examines details of joining anti-gun litigation The city of Tampa isn't sure if it's going to join the coordinated legal assault on the gun industry. Though facing pressure to take action, the city attorney said he needs to sift through several legal issues before making a decision on filing suit.    Last fall New Orleans and Chicago sued gunmakers and gun shops, accusing them of irresponsibly making and selling weapons that ultimately fell into the hands of criminals. The lawsuits, modeled on successful litigation against tobacco companies, aim to reform the gun industry and recoup millions of dollars spent on police and health care.    But Florida is a state where guns are controlled at the state level, not city by city, and that may be a high hurdle for Tampa to join the anti-gun lawsuits. Unlike Chicago, which has a citywide ban on handguns, local governments in Florida are limited by the state Constitution on how much they can regulate firearms. It took a constitutional amendment in November before counties were allowed to mandate a waiting period for people who buy weapons at gun shows.    "The state of Florida has pre-empted cities to control weapons," said City Attorney Jim Palermo. "That might be a serious obstacle because it's not clear if Tampa, as a city, can engage in this type of litigation."    Miami-Dade County, though, looks like it's going to test the waters. The county has hired a legal team to research the issue and plans to file a lawsuit against local gun sellers by the end of the month, said Maggie Fernandez, a spokeswoman for Mayor Alex Penelas.     Palermo said his legal staff will pay close attention to the Miami lawsuit.    Bob Buckhorn, the law-and-order council member who led the charge against rave parties last year, is hoping Tampa can find a way to file its own lawsuit.    "People are tired of blood literally flowing through the streets," Buckhorn said.    But Buckhorn, a pistol owner, was quick to add that he doesn't want Tampa to go as far as Chicago's handgun ban.    "This lawsuit would be about gun violence," he said. "Not gun control."

764.text: GUN-2571 PRIMARY SENATOR: BRADY BILL'S CHANCES GOOD; STALLING TACTICS NOT EXPECTED TO WORK The Senate sponsor of the Brady bill gun control measure expressed confidence Thursday that it would be law by Thanksgiving, even as Senate action on a separate $22.3 billion crime bill remained stalled despite strong bipartisan support. ``We feel very good about the situation,'' said Sen. Howard Metzenbaum, D-Ohio, at a news conference with Sarah and James Brady, the bill's namesake who was wounded in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Reagan. The Brady bill requires a five-day waiting period and a background check on all would-be handgun buyers. The waiting period would be phased out when background information is sufficiently computerized to enable instant checks, but checks would then be done on buyers of both handguns and long guns. The Brady bill is scheduled for Senate action immediately after the chamber acts on a far-reaching crime bill, but action on the crime bill is stalled because of maneuverings over the Brady bill. Some Republican senators want to keep open the option of filibustering Brady. To prevent that, Senate Minority Leader Bob Dole might offer a more restrictive version of the Brady bill as an amendment to the crime bill, which cannot be filibustered, according to an earlier bipartisan agreement. Negotiations to resolve the situation began late Wednesday and continued through Thursday. Brady bill supporters want the measure handled separately so it can proceed through a conference with the House and get to President Clinton's desk before Congress recesses next week for the year.

766.text: GUN-11619 PRIMARY When 'Self-Defense' Violates Civil Rights The United States Commission on Civil Rights is right to order an investigation into whether racial bias might be a factor in the rise in homicides connected to notorious Stand Your Ground laws. The shooting death of Trayvon Martin, an unarmed black teenager, in Florida has put into question how police enforce this kind of statute. Law enforcement groups like the Association of Prosecuting Attorneys, which have opposed these laws, have long cautioned that they would have a ''disproportionately negative effect on minorities.'' Florida was the first in the nation to put public safety at risk with its 2005 Stand Your Ground law, and a score of states have followed suit. In the Martin case, the police initially accepted the self-defense claim by George Zimmerman, who is Hispanic, even though a 911 dispatcher had cautioned him not to follow the teenager. The state eventually charged Mr. Zimmerman with second-degree murder but only after a public furor over the irresponsible law and the potential racial factors involved. Data show that justifiable homicides have increased by 50 percent in states with these lethal laws. Michael Yaki, a member of the civil rights commission, has properly asked that the cases involving Stand Your Ground laws be analyzed to see if there is racial bias in accepting a claim of justifiable homicide when the victim is a minority. The commission's investigation should examine the multiple dangers to public safety wrought by undoing traditional self-defense principles. In place of the wise duty to retreat, these laws grant gun owners easy license to shoot to kill on the mere claim that they ''reasonably believed'' they were in danger. This is an immunity greater than that afforded police officers who shoot in the line of duty, the prosecutors association has pointed out. There can be no justifying the public mayhem legalized by Stand Your Ground. These laws should be repealed, and the civil rights inquiry should help make that point.

characters: United States Commission on Civil Rights

768.text: NA-43648 PRIMARY O'Malley details proposal for tighter gun-licensing rules Maryland Gov. Martin O'Malley's ambitious gun-control package would require citizens who move to the state to register guns purchased elsewhere and make it illegal for residents younger than 21 to purchase or own registered firearms or ammunition. Those restrictions were among new details the governor and his staff released at a news conference Friday as they put the final touches on several gun-control measures that have gained national attention as some of the most aggressive outside of President Obama's proposals in the wake of last month's school shootings in Newtown, Conn. O'Malley (D) will seek a broad assault-weapons ban, new licensing requirements for gun owners and a proposed expansion of reporting mental-health problems to get guns out of the hands of those considered dangerous to others. The details of the governor's plan were first reported Monday in The Washington Post. When asked by a reporter, O'Malley said he was also open to discussions with lawmakers about stationing more armed police officers in Maryland public schools - the response, pushed by the National Rifle Association, to public outcry over mass shootings. Flanked by police chiefs from across the state, O'Malley called his gun-control package his top legislative priority for the 90-day session of the Maryland General Assembly. His agenda also includes getting the state's death penalty repealed. On Friday, he released the rest of his legislative proposals. Many had been previously publicized, including expanding early voting and  incentives for construction of an offshore wind farm.

767.text: GUN-5657 PRIMARY Mauser leads call for special session Dad of slain student urges passage of gun laws Tom Mauser, father of slain Columbine High School student Daniel Mauser, said Tuesday that most of the lessons learned from the worst school shooting in U.S. history have to do with fixing families and communities. What politicians can do, he said, is pass stricter gun laws, and Mauser went to the Colorado Capitol to urge them to do just that. "We ask our leaders here and in Washington to address what issues they can, to pass measures that get guns out of the hands of children," he said. Mauser spoke at a rally staged by the Colorado Coalition Against Gun Violence intended to increase pressure on Gov. Bill Owens to call a special session to pass several gun-control laws. Most of the proposals would be aimed at keeping guns away from children. Daniel Mauser was killed April 20 when two student gunmen at Columbine used four guns to kill 12 students and one teacher; 23 others were injured in the assault. Tom Mauser was joined by family members of five other Coloradans killed by guns in calling for a special session.
