"I will never forget the horror I felt when I had to say goodbye to my brother," said Kelly Banes, who lost her 14-year-old brother, Carl Midland, to a handgun accident in Glenwood Springs in 1994. "What will it take for our political leaders to stand up? (Owens') decision could be the difference between life and death."

770.text: GUN-6347 PRIMARY GUN LAWS ARE NOT THE ANSWER I have been disappointed to hear people talk about the tragedy in Atlanta as being another gun control issue. Here is a person who apparently was very distraught, definitely needed medical help, who chose to bludgeon his immediate family to death with a hammer, and is suspected of two other deaths with a machete-like tool. But all people talk about are the guns. Should we start outlawing hammers and machetes as well? The real truth is that man is a tool user. How we choose to use the tool is really the issue. Millions of people own guns and hammers. This man chose to kill, and he used a variety of tools. Even if he didn't have any guns, at least three people would still be dead. That is no less a tragedy than the destruction he wrought later. And proves that guns are not the issue. Michael Shirley Saratoga IT'S too bad that Joanne Jacobs (Opinion, Aug. 2) has had her myth shatteredby evil doers. "Shane" is actually alive and well in millions of gun owners across the United States. Each and every gun owner knows and understands that being able to defend one's self and others is what made this country strong, and it is what makes us the defenders of freedom in the world today. The National Rifle Association is the foundation that gives strength to the Shanes of today. Legal and registered guns give our society the strongest foundation of any nation in the world. Yes, just like in the old West, a few people have lost their way and gone over to the dark side and caused harm. But the potential for prevention of violence is in all of us.

775.text: GUN-1786 PRIMARY READERS BARE OPINIONS ABOUT BEARING ARMS Let's talk some more about our right to keep and bear arms. Should we rewrite the Second Amendment to make sense, or leave it as it is, a sacred anachronism? When I wrote recently that our "right to bear arms" has more to do with a state's right to conscript a militia, than an individual's right to defend himself, I was promptly answered by several readers who like the Second Amendment just the way it is written. Me, I'd rewrite the Second Amendment and not mention militia. See if you like how this reads: The right of the people to be secure in their homes being fundamental, the right to keep personal weapons shall not be infringed." And then the nation could enjoy a huge semantic shouting match as to what constitutes infringement. We could all have a jolly time deciding where abridgment ends and infringement begins. At this moment you cannot buy a machine gun without registering it and paying a stiff licensing fee. Is that abridgment or infringement? AN INVISIBLE MILITIA In my view, requiring the licensing and registering of firearms is prudent public policy and not infringement. Several readers wrote to say that each citizen with a gun is a member of an invisible militia safeguarding us against military dictatorships. "The intent of the Second Amendment," writes David Zarnowski of Delray Beach, "was to provide parity. The instrument of tyranny is a centralized government with an army at its command. When considering the term `arms' the context is any light infantry weapon . . . We are to have access to any firearms that will ensure a balance between the citizenry and central authority."

772.text: GUN-8060 PRIMARY LIEBERMAN COUNTERS PRO-GUN LOBBY On Charlton Heston's heels, Democrat Joseph Lieberman assured Wisconsin hunters on Thursday that he and Al Gore would not take away their guns if elected to the White House. Three days ago, movie star-turned-political activist Heston had urged members of the National Rifle Association in Waukesha to "pull the trigger on tyrants" and vote for George W. Bush next week. Heston, NRA president, called the Nov. 7 presidential election "the most important election since the Civil War." Lieberman, the first Jewish vice presidential candidate on a major party ticket, asked people not to be "fooled" by Heston, the former actor most famous for playing Moses. "In a manner of speaking I'm familiar with Moses. ... You might consider Moses a friend of mine," Lieberman told about 1,000 people packed inside a steamy union hall. "Charlton Heston is no Moses." "Al Gore and I respect the Second Amendment right to bear firearms. That's in the Constitution. No hunter, sportsperson or law-abiding citizen will lose any rights they have under our proposals," Lieberman said. Wisconsin is one of several states the NRA is targeting this year with time and money because of a large number of gun owners. State NRA membership has grown by 30,000 in the last year to a high of 110,000, according to the group. Gore supports licensing future handgun buyers and expanding background checks, while Bush favors fewer restrictions on gun ownership.

769.text: GUN-10054 PRIMARY Arming Criminals A move is underway in the Illinois legislature to make life easier on gun runners and armed criminals. Of course, that's not quite the way its supporters put it. They say it's an effort to free law-abiding gun owners from the intrusive gaze of government. But crooks would love this bill. The legislature last spring passed a bill that would force the state police to destroy records of gun purchases shortly after the sales are made. Gov. Rod Blagojevich vetoed the bill along with two others loosening gun controls in Illinois. The Illinois State Police run mandatory background checks on gun buyers. That's where they get the purchaser database that has become useful to law enforcement. Put yourself in the shoes of a police team planning a raid of a suspected drug dealer or meth-maker. Wouldn't it be nice to know if the suspect had been stocking up on weapons? If the state destroys its records, that's one less tool the good guys have at their disposal. Felons often get guns from people who can buy them legally. It's easier to put dangerous middle-men in jail if prosecutors can show records of them buying large numbers of guns. Yet, the National Rifle Association is now pressing the legislature to override the governor's wise veto. The opposition relies on the irrational train of thought that holds that recording gun purchases will lead to confiscating guns. It's like arguing that auto registrations are a step toward confiscating cars. In a nation in which one of three homes contains a firearm, neither will ever happen.

771.text: GUN-4512 PRIMARY Court Voids Background Check Of Gun Buyer Under Brady Law The Supreme Court yesterday struck down a key portion of the Brady gun control law, ruling 5 to 4 that Congress overstepped its powers by trying to commandeer the resources of states to carry out federal law. Issued on the last day of a tumultuous term, the decision imposed another restriction on the power of the federal government, a recurring theme of the court's rulings in recent years. Voting 5 to 4, the high court invalidated a provision requiring local sheriffs to check the backgrounds of gun buyers, part of a broader law inspired by James S. Brady, who was disabled in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan. The law marked the first major effort by Congress to regulate firearms in two decades, and passed in 1993 after seven years of fractious debate. "Congress cannot compel the states to enact or enforce a federal regulatory program," Justice Antonin Scalia wrote for the court, relying on an important 1992 precedent but also narrowly reading the Constitution's grant of congressional powers. In dissent, Justice John Paul Stevens read aloud for nearly 20 minutes his statement protesting the majority. If Congress believes the Brady Act "will benefit the people of the nation . . . we should respect both its policy judgment and its appraisal of its constitutional power," he said. The decision is likely to have limited impact on gun control; most states already require background checks and the federal government is developing a nationwide screening system to do the work that currently is left to local law enforcement. But it holds great significance for how Congress writes laws affecting social policy.

773.text: GUN-3966 PRIMARY Potshots at the NRA The In the Loop column of Oct. 14 was the most recent of several pieces in your paper that characterized the NRA's modest staff reduction as evidence of "serious budget problems" and, by implication, our impending demise. Yet when you report on Fortune 500 companies trimming personnel, you characterize such actions as "streamlining the work force." Currently, the NRA membership stands at nearly 3 million. This is indeed a reduction from our 125-year historic peak of 3.5 million members, but it is our success rather than failures to which the fluctuation should be attributed. Even disregarding the natural attrition that accompanied our dues increase of 40 percent in July 1994, our enormous success in electing our friends and defeating our foes in the 1994 congressional elections led many gun owners to believe that the threat to their freedoms had waned. Remember, even Bill Clinton acknowledged that the NRA was "the reason that the Republicans control Congress." More recently, U.S. News & World Report noted that Handgun Control Inc. is considering a name change and "is said to be worried that it is losing the public relations war to the National Rifle Association." U.S. News reported that the organiztion believes "the NRA has successfully convinced many voters that gun control does not work." Firearms owners see the NRA as their safety net against government encroachment on their rights. Indeed, the president's more strident campaign rhetoric is reenergizing our grass-roots membership base. No doubt we can all look forward to reading about it in your paper, perhaps under the headline "NRA Membership Increases to Record Levels" or "NRA Declares Overwhelming Electoral Victory Again."

774.text: GUN-4413 PRIMARY NEW JERSEY DAILY BRIEFING; Guns an Issue in Senate Race James S. Brady, who was wounded in a 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan, and who inspired a Federal law mandating a waiting period for handgun purchases, will travel here today to endorse Ilan Plawker, a Democratic candidate for the State Senate in the 39th District and a fellow gun-control advocate. Mr. Brady will be joined by Jake Locicero, whose daughter, Amy Federici, was killed in a 1993 shooting spree on a Long Island Rail Road commuter train, said Doug Dodson, Mr. Plawker's campaign manager. Mr. Plawker's opponent, Senator Gerald Cardinale, a Republican, recently sponsored a bill to allow individuals to carry concealed handguns. Senator Cardinale said his bill would require background checks that would prevent people with criminal records or a history of psychological problems from obtaining and carrying weapons.

777.text: GUN-30102 PRIMARY LIST YOUR GUNS ON YOUR 1040? NO -- THIS BILL NEVER PASSED You must list all your guns on your 2010 tax return. Chain e-mail on Sunday, November 1st, 2009 in an e-mail circulated to many people. THE RULING: PANTS ON FIRE * * * It's a scary time to be a gun owner in the United States - at least if you believe some of the claims made by gun-rights groups and spread in chain e-mails. We recently investigated a claim made by Gun Owners of America(http://www.politifact.com/truth-o-meter/statements/2009/oct/20/gun-owner s-america/gun-rights-group-says-health-care-bill-could-harm-/) that a version of a health care bill currently in Congress "could be used to ban guns in home self-defense," and rated it False. We gave the National Rifle Association a Pants on Fire for making a similar claim(http://www.politifact.com/truth-o-meter/statements/2008/sep/23/national-ri fle-association/yes-and-outlaw-apple-pie-as-well/) during the 2008 election. So we were skeptical when a reader forwarded us a chain e-mail that claims the following: "Now 'all Guns' must be listed on your next (2010) tax return! As if we didnt [sic] have enough to get upset about! If you have a gun, I hope it isn't registered! Senate Bill SB-2099 will require us to put on our 2009 1040 federal tax form all guns that you have or own. It will require fingerprints and a tax of $50 per gun." The e-mail continued: "This bill was introduced on Feb.. 24, 2009, by the Obama staff. BUT... this bill will only become public knowledge 30 days after the new law becomes effective! This is an amendment to the Internal Revenue Act of 1986. This means that the Finance Committee has passed this without the Senate voting on it at all. Trust Obama ? ...... you must be kidding!"

776.text: GUN-3086 PRIMARY Violence fires legislative debate The Colorado Capitol became a crucible yesterday, as lawmakers struggled with ways to stem violence through a torrent of hotly contested legislation. "There's no question violent crime has forced a new level of concern in our community, and I think it's terribly important our legislature grapple with some of the issues regarding crime," said Ray Slaughter, director of the Colorado District Attorneys Council. Throughout the building, fiery debates raged on violence issues: requiring firearms to be safely stored, curbing stalking and loosening restrictions on concealed weapons. "The gun-control bills are well-intentioned, but bills like them don't work anywhere in the country. The concealed-weapons bills will help victims defend themselves in some instances," said David Kopel, a gun-law expert with the Independence Institute. "But it wouldn't be realistic to expect these laws are going to cause a major drop in crime rates," he added. After the full House gave final approval to a measure by Rep. Doug Friednash, D-Denver, that would allow courts to hand down the death penalty in cases of multiple murders, committees in both chambers handled a flurry of bills designed to fight crime. "We all realize we need to do something," said Rep. Mike Salaz, R-Trinidad. Most notably, lawmakers renewed the debate surrounding concealed weapons, taking testimony on the final two bills designed to loosen restrictions on getting concealed-weapons permits. Meanwhile yesterday, the House Judiciary Committee unanimously approved bills that would increase penalties for the crime of stalking - HB 1045 by Rep. Celina Benavidez, D-Denver - and create a central registry listing people restricted by court-issued restraining orders - HB 1090 by Rep. Debbie Allen, R-Aurora.

778.text: GUN-4604 PRIMARY No Guns for Wife-Beaters Last September, President Clinton signed into law one of the most important bills of rights for women and children in recent years: the domestic violence gun ban. It is dedicated to the simple principle that wife-beaters and child abusers should not have guns. As the author of the law, which makes it illegal for any person convicted of domestic violence to possess a gun, I know firsthand how tough the battle against the National Rifle Association was to get the bill passed. But in the end, it passed Congress -- and by a vote of 97 to 2 in the Senate -- because even members supportive of the NRA agreed that the lives of women and children should not be held hostage to gun-lobby politics. But The Post [editorial, March 27] took issue with the fact that the law prohibits gun possession only by those convicted of a felony or misdemeanor for domestic violence, and not by those who, according to The Post's description, committed a "similar assault on a disabled neighbor, co-worker or in-law." I couldn't agree more with The Post that gun ownership by anyone convicted of a violent crime -- no matter what the relationship of the convict to his or her victim -- should be banned and the law should be strengthened. But until we can overcome the NRA's opposition, we certainly should not weaken the law.

780.text: GUN-9377 PRIMARY LAW'S AUTHOR COULD BE DENIED PERMIT TEEN CRIME MIGHT WEIGH AGAINST HER The state representative who wrote the law expanding Minnesotans' ability to gain handgun permits was arrested as a teenager after she and a companion stole money from two stores. The incidents occurred 35 years ago, but Rep. Lynda Boudreau admitted Friday that her local sheriff could deny her a handgun permit under the new law because of her juvenile conviction. The fifth-term Republican from Faribault informed her colleagues of the incidents during a brief speech on the House floor Friday. "Things we do throughout our past, even in your juvenile years, can be brought up many, many years later," she said. "I feel I have compensated for the crime I committed. ... I hope people will look to my productive community service in the last 35 years and not to the foolish mistake I made when I was 16." The story was first reported on KMSP-TV Thursday night. But on Friday, even those who opposed her during the session's gun debate said the incidents had little relevance. "To me it's character assassination. It doesn't add anything to the discussion," said Bill Gillespie of the Minnesota Police and Peace Officers Association, which opposed Boudreau's bill. Said House DFL Minority Leader Matt Entenza: "There has to be a focus on policy rather than events that are ancient history." Boudreau is the mother of three and a Realtor. She has worked in the past as a police reserve officer and a public health worker.

779.text: GUN-11139 PRIMARY California's tough gun laws could not prevent East Oakland tragedy SACRAMENTO -- California's gun laws are the toughest of any state in the nation, but they could not prevent this week's East Oakland shooting that left seven people dead. And short of banning semi-automatic weapons, it's unlikely that any new laws could prevent someone from going on a shooting spree with the weapon that authorities say One L. Goh used to kill students at Oikos University. Even some of the most ardent gun-control advocates believe that's the case. "It wasn't a failure of laws," said Amanda Wilcox, who along with her husband, Nick, lobbies for the California chapter of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence. "I just don't see how our gun laws could have stopped something like that." Police say that Goh, a 43-year-old South Korean immigrant, used a legally purchased .45-caliber semi-automatic pistol. Police say they believe the gunman had four fully loaded magazines, each with eight rounds of ammunition. Under California's assault-weapons ban, the maximum number of rounds allowed per magazine is 10. In addition to the seven people police say Goh shot and killed, three others were injured, and several people were shot multiple times. Because there were more than eight shots fired, police are presuming Goh reloaded the gun. Authorities reportedly have copies of the receipt for the weapon Goh purchased at a Castro Valley gun shop, but they have not divulged details other than to say what caliber gun it was and that it was a semi-automatic.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

781.text: GUN-10483 PRIMARY Taking new aim at D.C. gun control; Bills appear in Congress after similar legislation derails voting rights bid Legislation introduced in Congress on Tuesday would weaken the District's gun-control laws and restrict the D.C. Council from regulating firearms. The effort comes a week after congressional officials, responding to similar pro-gun language in a D.C. voting rights bill, dropped the legislation, which would have secured a voting seat in the House for the District. The Senate version, sponsored by Sen. Jon Tester (D-Mont.) and Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.)  is intended to make it easier to buy guns and ammunition in the District and to repeal local registration and firearm storage requirements. The sponsors say it would ensure that the District complies with a landmark 2008 Supreme Court decision that overturned the city's decades-old ban on handgun possession. "Some may ask why a senator from Arizona and a senator from Montana would introduce legislation that impacts the District of Columbia," McCain said in a statement. "It's simple -- we believe that residents across this country should be able to exercise their constitutional right to have access to firearms to protect themselves." Joshua Horwitz, executive director of the Coalition to Stop Gun Violence, called the legislation "political grandstanding" that does not take into account that the D.C. Council passed laws last year to comply with the court ruling, which were upheld in March by a federal judge. The city has already repealed a ban on semiautomatic pistols, he said, and allows residents to keep loaded guns in their homes. Most troubling to gun-control advocates, Mayor Adrian M. Fenty (D) and members of the D.C. Council are the legislation's proposed restrictions on local control of firearms.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not directly mentioned)

784.text: GUN-1620 PRIMARY Don't allow lobbyists to shoot down gun bill Remember the horror of the Perimeter Mall shooting in April 1990? A madman named James Calvin Brady took a gun and fired into the crowded food court at lunchtime, killing one and injuring four others. Back then, there was a lot of public anguish over our lax gun laws. Any lunatic or convict can wander into a pawnshop or gunshop and buy a gun with few questions asked. All the gun purchaser has to do is say he's not a criminal. That's proof enough. Back then, our politicians promised us some small amount of protection. At the very least, they said, they would pass a law requiring an instantaneous background check of all would-be gun purchasers. At the very least, they said, they would try to weed out convicted felons and those who were so unstable they had, at least once, been involuntarily committed to a mental institution. Gov. Zell Miller got on board during his gubernatorial campaign. He said he would support a system in Georgia similar to that used in Virginia, where instantaneous background checks are in place. After all, it's hard to object to a procedure that would not be any more intrusive than the credit card checks that consumers are routinely subjected to in department stores and even gasoline stations. State Sen. David Scott (D-Atlanta) managed to get a bill mandating instantaneous background checks through the Senate last year. He has been waiting for the House to consider the legislation.

786.text: GUN-3011 PRIMARY No more gun laws needed USA TODAY's call for applying to firearms ownership licensing, testing, insurance and other measures required of motorists fails the reason test and definitely fails the law enforcement test. First, automobile deaths are, for the most part, accidents caused by a number of circumstances - intoxication, negligence, bad weather, even mechanical failure. The primary deterrent to automobile-related deaths is that negligent drivers are held responsible for their behavior. But criminal violence is not an accident. It is deliberate human behavior committed by those who have decided that the wages of crime are worth far more than the potential insignificant punishment. However, let's examine USA TODAY's approach. -- Require firearms training just as you require driver education? Will you have "shooter education" in every school, a "Department of Firearms" bureaucracy in every state, underwritten by taxpayers? -- Register all guns and yes, the government will have a list of all legally owned guns. But what would a drug lord pay for a list of homes that contains firearms? Are we sentencing firearms owners to burglary, robbery, even physical harm if the list gets into the wrong hands? And are criminals actually expected to register their guns? -- Require insurance of all gun owners. Fine idea if the carnage of gun violence is caused by people who buy insurance. But are criminals actually expected to buy insurance? To stop gun violence, we must stop criminal violence. Here's how to start:

785.text: GUN-10253 PRIMARY Q&A Q: News reports have mentioned a bill in the Legislature that would allow anyone with a concealed weapon permit to carry a firearm into a restaurant. I thought it was OK to carry a concealed weapon into a place as long as alcohol wasn't served there. Can you clarify? --- Max Smith, Roswell A: As explained by Russ Willard of the Georgia attorney general's office, the first caveat is that in order to have this apply, you must have a concealed carry permit issued according to state law. Second, current law forbids the carrying of any firearm, explosive compound or knife designed for offensive or defensive purposes into a public gathering, regardless of whether you have a concealed carry permit. The Georgia Code lists several events and locations considered to be public gatherings, including any establishment that sells alcoholic beverages for on-premises consumption. If passed, House Bill 193 would allow an individual with a concealed carry permit to carry a firearm into a restaurant that serves alcohol if the restaurant derives at least 50 percent of its annual gross sales receipts from food sales, Willard said. Q: A 3,106-carat diamond was discovered in 1905 at the Premier mine in Pretoria, South Africa. It was the largest diamond ever found. Where is it now? --- Brenda Robinson, Ellenwood A: It isn't intact as a single stone.

782.text: GUN-9437 PRIMARY SIDING WITH THE NRA GUN CONTROL AFTER the sniper and terrorist attacks in Washington, one might hope that lawmakers would want more legal weapons to shoot down illegal guns. Instead, Washington is adopting new laws and rules making it harder to crack down on guns. The House has passed a bill to protect gun makers and dealers from lawsuits, such as one filed by relatives of two victims of the sniper. Meanwhile, Attorney General John D. Ashcroft wants federal records of gun sales destroyed after 24 hours, even though that could make it harder to track down suspected terrorists. Last week the House approved the bill to protect gun manufacturers and dealers from lawsuits growing out of the criminal use of firearms they make or sell. The White House and legislators who supported the bill argue that makers and sellers of a legal, non-defective product shouldn't be unfairly punished when criminals use their guns to commit crimes. The National Rifle Association agrees, of course. But why shouldn't gun makers and dealers be held accountable for the harm of their products when the gun makers knowingly ship weapons to unscrupulous dealers who funnel them to criminals? Consider the sniper shootings around Washington last year. The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms couldn't find federally required sales records for the rifle used in the killings. The weapon was made by Bushmaster Firearms Inc., and came from Bull's Eye Shooter Supply, a gun store in Tacoma, Wash. During a previous audit of Bull's Eye, ATF inspectors said they were unable to account for 160 guns.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

783.text: GUN-31105 PRIMARY District Gun Bill Goes to Council; Officials Anticipate More Legal Action On Weapon Types Within weeks, District residents could legally keep handguns in their homes under emergency legislation that goes to the D.C. Council today, as officials try to comply with the Supreme Court ruling rejecting the city's handgun ban. But District officials said yesterday that they are braced for the possibility of more legal wrangling as they try to respect the high court while maintaining the strictest controls possible. Residents could begin applying this week for handgun permits in a process that requires a written examination, proof of residency, good vision and ballistic testing. Applicants also would have to pay a fee and agree to fingerprinting and criminal background checks. The legislation does not lift restrictions on semiautomatic handguns, a move that will probably land the District back in court, according to the lawyer who successfully challenged the gun ban. Announcing the regulations yesterday, Mayor Adrian M. Fenty was clear about what might be ahead. "We think we have struck the delicate legal balance," he said during a news conference. "While we will have lawsuits, we think we stand on solid legal ground." Alongside Fenty (D) were Acting Attorney General Peter Nickles, D.C. Council Chairman Vincent C. Gray and several council members, including Phil Mendelson (D-At Large). The legislation was originally crafted by Mendelson, who chairs the Committee on Public Safety and the Judiciary and who worked with Nickles in recent days to revise and strengthen the bill.

characters: D.C. Council Chairman Vincent C. Gray
Committee on Public Safety and the Judiciary

787.text: GUN-3294 PRIMARY Bill Planned to Ban New Bullets A New York Congressman who was a main sponsor of the crime bill enacted last fall said yesterday that he would seek a ban on all ammunition that can penetrate bulletproof vests. Representative Charles E. Schumer, Democrat of Brooklyn, made the announcement in response to a news report that two new types of handgun ammunition. The new products are Rhino-Ammo, capable of producing a baseball-sized wound, and the Black Rhino, which can pierce armor. They are made of carbon-based plastics, polymers, which shatter into razor-sharp shrapnel upon impact. The ammunition, made by Signature Products Corporation of Huntsville, Ala., has prompted criticism from police and gun control advocates. David Keen, chief executive of Signature, said the ammunition he is about to introduce is "a strictly defensive round." Mr. Schumer called Mr. Keen's position "ludicrous."

788.text: GUN-2542 PRIMARY House backs campaign reform; Brady bill delayed Just when Congress thought it might be adjourning for the year Monday, along came the Brady bill, the controversial handgun control bill that has dogged members for seven years.   On Saturday, the Senate approved a version of the bill after last-minute  changes that ended a GOP-led filibuster. But Monday, Democrats in House and  Senate negotiations stripped away many of the provisions that had pleased  Senate Republicans. So Monday night they blocked a vote on the bill. Democrats vowed to press ahead for final passage of the compromise bill -  even if lawmakers have to return to Washington after Thanksgiving.   In other matters, the House: Approved a plan to remake campaign finance laws. The vote was 255-175. The  plan would place a $ 600,000 voluntary spending limit on congressional races  and would bar candidates from taking more than $ 200,000 in contributions from political action committees. House members now must negotiate with the Senate, which has approved its own bill. Rejected a measure to cut federal spending by $ 90-billion over five years, largely by trimming Medicare. The vote was 219-213. By 272-163, members approved an alternative plan cutting $ 37-billion, mainly by trimming the federal bureaucracy. Was considering compromise legislation to extend jobless benefits to the long-term unemployed. The bill was cleared Saturday by the Senate. Was considering a bill to provide a final $ 18-billion to clean up the savings and loan fiasco.

754.text: GUN-1381 PRIMARY MOST IN SURVEY WANT NO CHANGE IN GUN LAW Two-thirds of state officials and candidates for office who responded to a survey favor keeping Missouri's law on concealed weapons the way it is.   So says the Coalition Against Concealed Guns, which sent surveys to 315 legislators and candidates for state office; only 54 responded.   State law now permits people to carry guns, but not to conceal them.   Exceptions to the ban on concealment are made for police, judges and process servers.   Twenty-two percent of the survey's respondents said they favored broadening the law to allow more people to carry concealed weapons.   Sharon Polster, the coalition's chairwoman, said she was disappointed that so few legislators and candidates answered the survey. She said their positions were important because the issue may surface in the Legislature next year.   Earlier this year, the House approved a bill that would have allowed people 21 or older to ask their sheriff for a permit to carry a concealed weapon.   Police strongly opposed the measure, and the bill's sponsor, Rep. Joseph Driskill, D-Doniphan, shelved it. He said he would work with police on a compromise for next year's legislative session.   On Monday, Driskill said he was undecided whether to reintroduce the measure. He said he had raised the issue to ''give to people who have the need the right to defend themselves.'' Polster called concealed weapons a safety threat and ''a kick in the face to law enforcement.'' The Coalition Against Concealed Guns is an umbrella group for 10 groups, including the League of Women Voters, Missouri Police Chiefs Association, Archdiocesan Commission on Human Rights, Missouri School Boards Association and Missouri Council to Control Handguns. The handgun-control council conducted the survey. The results follow; an asterisk denotes an incumbent, no asterisk denotes a candidate: Favor Broadening Law: Phil Tate*, D-Gallatin, 3rd House Dist.   Everett Brown*, D-Maryville, 4th House Dist.   Dick Franklin*, D-Independence, 53rd House Dist.   Dean S. Hodge, Libertarian-Bridgeton, 80th House Dist.   Jan Polizzi, D-St. Louis, 97th House Dist.   John E. Griesheimer, R-Washington, 109th House Dist.   Jim Froelker*, R-Gerald, 111th House Dist.   Delbert Scott*, R-Lowry City, 119th House Dist. Gene Lang*, R-Warrensburg, 121st House Dist.   Merrel Breyer, D-Richland, 148th House Dist.   Ken Fiebelman*, D-Salem, 150th House Dist.   Floyd Self, R-Sedalia, Sen. Dist. 21.  Favor Current Law: Cindy Ostmann, R-St. Peters, 14th House Dist. Helen Schnare, R-St. Charles, 15th House Dist.   Del Robertson, R-Columbia, 23rd House Dist.   Millie Humphreys*, D-St. Joseph, 27th House Dist.   Karen McCarthy*, D-Kansas City, 38th House Dist. Matt O'Neill*, D-St. Louis, 66th House Dist.   Fran Brady*, D-Jennings, 69th House Dist.   Sheila Lumpe*, D-University City, 72nd House Dist.   Mike Begley, D-Florissant, 76th House Dist.   Robert Gravlin Jr., R-Florissant, 77th House Dist.   Patrick O'Connor, D-Bridgeton, 79th House Dist.   Don Marshall, R-St. Ann, 80th House Dist.   Ilene Ordower, D-St. Louis, 82nd House Dist. Matthew McCormic, D-Manchester, 93rd House Dist.   Clarence Wohlwend*, R-St. Louis, 97th House Dist.   Ken Meyer, R-St. Louis, 98th House Dist.   Bob Lindsley, D-Arnold, 101st House Dist.   Roger D. Fleer, D-Washington, 109th House Dist. Bill Gratz, D-Jefferson City, 113th House Dist.   Deleta Williams, D-Warrensburg, 121st House Dist.   Dale O'Neal, D-Carthage, 126th House Dist.   Riley Denton, D-Republic, 134th House Dist.   Mike Schilling, D-Springfield, 136th House Dist. Wayne Goode*, D-Normandy, Sen. Dist. 3.  Marjorie Reinhart D- St. Louis, Sen. Dist. 15.  Walt Mueller*, R-Kirkwood, Sen. Dist. 15.  Joe Moseley,D-Columbia, Sen. Dist. 19 Undecided: Charles Redecker, D-Warrenton, 19th Ho use Dist.   Irene Treppler*, R-St. Louis, Sen. Dist. 1.  Francis Flotron*, R-St. Louis, Sen. Dist. 7.   Public Should Vote: Carl M. Ward, D-Chesterfield, Sen. Dist. 7.

793.text: GUN-1073 PRIMARY Georgians can lead the way on Brady bill Two years ago, the position of Georgia's 10 congressmen on the Brady handgun-control bill might have been easy to predict: Two for, eight against. No more. A telephone poll of the delegation Tuesday reveals a surprising uncertainty among several who would have opposed the bill outright in the past. If that trend is also occurring in other congressional delegations, the Brady bill may stand a good chance when it comes up for a vote next week. The biggest obstacle to passage is a so-called "alternative" to the Brady bill backed by that champion of sane gun laws, the National Rifle Association (NRA). The Staggers bill, named after sponsor Harley Staggers (D-W.Va.), calls on the federal government to conduct immediate background checks of would-be handgun purchasers, instead of the seven- day background check proposed in the Brady bill. The Staggers bill sounds great, except that it's unworkable. One of the NRA's criticisms of the Brady bill has been that adequate background checks can't be done in seven days. So how can the NRA argue for doing it instantaneously? The U.S. Office of Technology Assessment predicts such a system may be as much as 10 years away. The Staggers bill is not meant to bring sanity to our nation's gun laws, it is meant to provide cover for congressmen who want to toe the NRA line but still be able to say they supported background checks. Voters should not be fooled.

790.text: NA-42858 PRIMARY N.R.A. Chief Says He Will Counter Bloomberg's Gun Control Campaign The chief executive of the National Rifle Association said Sunday that his organization would lead a national campaign against efforts by Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg of New York to persuade Congress to adopt stricter gun controls. The mayor and the rifle association executive, Wayne LaPierre, appeared separately on the NBC News program "Meet the Press" on Sunday. Mr. Bloomberg, a billionaire and a registered independent, said he was spending $12 million on advertising in support of pending federal legislation to curb gun violence. In reply, Mr. LaPierre, said, "He can't buy America.'' "He can't spend enough of his $27 billion to try to impose his will on the American public," Mr. LaPierre said. Referring to Mr. Bloomberg's well-known campaigns against smoking and junk food, he said: "They don't want him in their restaurants, they don't want him in their homes, they don't want him telling them what food to eat. They sure don't want him telling what self-defense firearms to own.'' Mr. Bloomberg formed a "super PAC'' last year to donate to candidates and causes that he supports. The causes include gay rights and tougher gun laws. Mr. LaPierre said that gun owners would make up a political counterweight to the mayor. "We have people all over, millions of people, sending us $5, $10, $15, $20 checks, saying, 'Stand up to this guy that says we can only have three bullets,' which is what he said,'' Mr. LaPierre said. " 'Stand up to this guy that says ridiculous things like the N.R.A. wants firearms with nukes on them.' I mean, it's insane, the stuff he says.''

792.text: GUN-7608 PRIMARY PONDER MOMS' IDEAS; HOW CAN ANY ONE WORK? The media can't stop fawning over the Million Mom March. But anyone capable of reasoning can understand that the emotionally charged efforts of these "moms" are misguided. We have so much gun control on the books that heaping more onto the pile cannot accomplish anything. Let's look at what the "moms" want: Register gun owners? That's an invitation to the government to use, at a later date, the compiled data on gun owners to confiscate their guns. Mandate trigger locks? How can you possibly enforce the use of them? License gun owners? "We license drivers," they say, "so why not license guns?" Since the goal is safety, and the gun-haters make the comparison, are they claiming that licensing drivers in any way prevents them from misusing their cars? JEFFREY BUDZYNSKI West Palm Beach

789.text: GUN-6941 PRIMARY Gun dealer surrenders firearms license TEC-9 sale to teens may bring criminal charges The gun dealer who sold a TEC-9 assault weapon to two  teenagers at a Denver-area gun show after the Columbine High  massacre has surrendered his federal firearms license and may face  criminal prosecution. Terry Kern, an Adams County gun store owner, sold the TEC-9  in June to Corey Tucker and David Winkler, two boys who wanted to  demonstrate how easily they could buy an assault weapon privately  at a gun show even after the Columbine shootings, which left 15  people dead and 23 wounded. All four of the guns that seniors Eric Harris, 18, and Dylan  Klebold, 17, took to Columbine High had been sold privately last  year by unlicensed vendors at Tanner gun shows in Adams County.   One was a TEC-DC9, a variant of the notorious TEC-9 pistol that  was banned from manufacture by the 1994 assault weapons law. Tucker and Winkler obtained their TEC-9 at the first Tanner  show after the massacre. At a news conference held with law enforcement officials and  gun control advocates, they announced they had bought a TEC-9  assault pistol legally, for $ 600 cash. The sale was legal because  federal regulations permit unlicensed vendors at gun shows to sell  guns to teenagers. What they didn't know was that Kern had a federal firearms  license - and could not legally sell them a handgun, with or  without a criminal background check.

796.text: GUN-10565 PRIMARY Buzz and bullets: Gun fans cheer Starbucks' policy Dale Welch recently walked into a Starbucks in Virginia, handgun strapped to his waist, and ordered a banana Frappuccino with a cinnamon bun. He says the firearm drew a double-take from one customer, but not a peep from the baristas. Welch's foray into the coffeehouse was part of an effort by some gun owners to exercise their rights in states that allow people to openly carry firearms. Even in some "open carry" states, businesses are allowed to ban guns in their stores. Some have, creating political confrontations with gun owners. But Starbucks, the largest chain targeted, has refused to take the bait, saying in a statement that it follows state and local laws and has its own safety measures in its stores. "Starbucks is a special target because it's from the hippie West Coast, and a lot of dedicated consumers who pay $4 for coffee have expectations that Starbucks would ban guns. And here they aren't," said John Bruce, a professor at the University of Mississippi who is an expert in gun policy. The fight heated up in January when gun enthusiasts in California began walking into Starbucks and other places to test state laws that allow gun owners to carry weapons in public. Some were spontaneous, with one or two gun owners walking into a store. Others were organized parades of dozens of gun owners walking into restaurants with their firearms at their sides.

794.text: GUN-5227 PRIMARY THE 1997 ELECTIONS: REFERENDUMS; Oregon Stays With Its Law On Suicides Oregon voters refused yesterday to repeal the country's only law allowing doctor-assisted suicide, first passed three years ago but never put into effect because of a series of legal and political challenges. With more than half the state's precincts reporting, the repeal effort, one of more than 50 ballot initiatives decided across the country in the day's voting, was going down to defeat by a ratio of better than 3 to 2, according to The Associated Press. Backers of the failed repeal effort, among them the Roman Cathiolic Church and a number of anti-abortion groups, indicated that they would continue the fight by filing more court challenges to the law. In another major initiative vote, in Washington State, stricter gun controls were soundly defeated by a 2 to 1 ratio after gun advocacy groups, notably the National Rifle Association, outspent control advocates by better than 3 to 1. The control proposals would have required gun owners -- at least one of every five Washington residents -- to pass an examination or take a safety course to get a gun license. Also proposed was a requirement that would have forced gun dealers to equip all new guns with trigger locks. Gun advocates, who viewed yesterday's vote as a major victory in a long, hard national fight, fought the proposed legislation by warning that the real, hidden goal of control advocates was to eventually register and then confiscate all handguns.

791.text: GUN-10109 PRIMARY Some feel wounded by Salazar gun vote Senator backs bill to limit suits against gunmakers Washington - Colorado Democratic Sen. Ken Salazar is wholeheartedly backing a ban on lawsuits against gun manufacturers and sellers by crime victims, leaving gun-control advocates in the state feeling betrayed and gun-rights forces pleased. Salazar has joined nine other Democrats and 51 Republicans in co-sponsoring the gun-lawsuit ban. Fourteen Democrats voted yes on the bill Friday; 29 voted no. The Democratic support assured passage of the measure Friday on a 65-31 vote. Colorado Republican Sen. Wayne Allard also voted yes. The House has not yet acted on the legislation but approved a similar measure last year. "I was floored," Tom Mauser, president of Colorado Ceasefire Capital Fund, said of Salazar's vote. "It's especially hurtful to me because I have defended Ken Salazar to people." Mauser, whose work on gun issues was spurred by his son Daniel's death in the 1999 Columbine High School massacre, said Ceasefire members expect to confront Salazar about the bill when he holds a town meeting in Colorado. "I have heard from a number of our supporters that they're not going to support him anymore. They feel betrayed," Mauser said. Salazar supported gun-control measures as state attorney general in the wake of the Columbine High shootings. He grew up with guns on a ranch and drew crucial support in his Senate campaign from rural areas of Colorado, where gun rights are popular.

797.text: GUN-11529 PRIMARY Readers write CHICK-FIL-A Thanks to Cathy family for a courageous stand I applaud Dan Cathy and his Chick-fil-A family for their courageous stand in not compromising their faith for the "almighty dollar." Why should he have to endorse something which contradicts his faith, just to gain a few customers who choose not to eat at his restaurants because he doesn't go along with their way of thinking? Thank you, Mr. Cathy and Chick-fil-A. It is so refreshing to see someone stand firm, and not compromise their faith in the face of these puny threats. MARY ELLIOTT, MCDONOUGH GUN CONTROL Easy access leads to senseless killing It is time to do something about guns. No other civilized nation in the world is so saturated with guns as the United States. For some reason, we equate gun ownership with freedom, independence and personal rights. As long as any Tom, Dick or Harry can get easy access to any kind of gun or assault weapon they want, we will continue to have senseless killings and slaughters like Columbine, the theater massacre in Colorado, the slayings at Fort Hood and Trayvon Martin. Enough! Guns are not a right in my book --- and if you throw the Second Amendment at me, I say, let's change it. WILLIAM A. JORDAN, LAWRENCEVILLE GUN CONTROL Limit the capacity of ammo magazines The Colorado shooting tragedy has brought the gun-control cheerleaders out of the woodwork again.

795.text: NA-44663 PRIMARY D.C. Council to vote on gun law nobody seems to like; Secret Service weighed in after a judge struck down city's ban on carrying firearms, lawmaker says. The D.C. Council is poised to pass legislation Tuesday allowing the carrying of concealed firearms in public, but virtually no one involved is happy about it - not the lawmakers voting for it, not the attorney who brought the lawsuit forcing them to do it, and not the federal agency charged with protecting the president and foreign dignitaries. The vote comes less than two months after a federal judge struck down the city's long-standing prohibition on carrying weapons in public, saying it does not comply with Supreme Court precedent. The judge's ruling is under a temporary stay, giving city leaders time to fashion a new gun carry law that meets constitutional muster. But even the tightly restrictive draft legislation unveiled last week by Mayor Vincent C. Gray (D) and D.C. Council Chairman Phil Mendelson (D) is giving council members heartburn. Several said Monday they would vote for it only grudgingly - knowing that should the city fail to pass a new law, the judge's ruling would wipe out a broad swath of the city's restrictive gun statutes when the stay expires next month. "We don't want to be in contempt of court, and we believe that we can accommodate the court with the emergency law and still appeal the decision," said Tommy Wells (D-Ward 6). "But frankly, I am reluctant for the District to be the vehicle by which the gun laws in America are systematically weakened or eviscerated."

801.text: GUN-10725 PRIMARY Oct. 6 Readers' letters Second Amendment merits a 28th When in the course of human events it becomes self-evident that mankind has changed sufficiently to warrant alterations to the amendments to the U.S. Constitution (Article V), we have belatedly acted to make said changes. Now we are engaged in a great civil debate testing whether the Second Amendment, or for that matter any amendment, should long endure. Should individual citizens have the right to carry weapons of mass murder, or not? Just as the 21st Amendment altered the intent of the 18th (Prohibition), so too ought there be an amendment to limit the ability of citizens to arm themselves to the teeth with a wide variety of semi-automatic and automatic weapons. The ready availability of AK-47s to the public now calls for change. Tap Merrick Sunnyvale Just a thorn in the government's side Hans Waagen (Letters, Oct. 4) called it a "ludicrous distortion" to insist that the government should have granted Anwar al-Awlaki his right to a trial. I call this statement preposterous. The U.S. Constitution grants even traitors the right to a trial with a minimum of two witnesses. Waagen insists that just because the government says al-Awlaki was a terrorist, this makes it so, as if the government had never, ever before trumped up charges against an inconvenient or annoying thorn in its side. And that's exactly what al-Awlaki was: a thorn. He had a strong opinion, and he was not afraid to voice it to the world. The last I heard, that was not a crime.

802.text: GUN-8370 PRIMARY Adversaries battle over guns, visions One of the staunchest gun control advocates in Florida has an arsenal that could stock a small militia. There it is, spread across the floor of his doublewide mobile home. A handgun. A Tec-9 semiautomatic. An M-16 rifle with a bayonet. Several Uzis. Okay, so most of them are water guns. But you get the point. At least Arthur C. Hayhoe hopes you do. All of these weapons, the for-real versions, are available at gun shows throughout Florida for anyone to buy legally, he says with a mixture of disbelief and disgust. He wears a white shirt and a yellow tie. Through wire-rimmed glasses, the 69-year-old bachelor stares disapprovingly at the stockpile on the floor. Hayhoe is the executive director of the Florida Coalition to Stop Gun Violence. The coalition's headquarters consists of a small office in Hayhoe's home, down the hall from the shelves of classic movies and classical CDs. Here, in the cramped space surrounded by books about the Second Amendment, he runs his crusade to "keep guns from falling into the wrong hands," as he puts it. It's in this office where he recruits for the coalition, whose 50 or so current members have teamed with other gun control coalitions around the state, such as the Million Mom March. In here he bangs out numerous letters to newspaper editors each week and plans where to travel next to argue for tighter controls on guns.

characters: Florida Coalition to Stop Gun Violence

798.text: GUN-9271 PRIMARY Council Seeks To Toughen Gun Controls In the aftermath of the shooting last month of Councilman James E. Davis, the City Council is pushing ahead with several changes in New York City's gun-control laws that would make the laws, already among the toughest in the nation, more restrictive. The Council will hold a committee hearing on Sept. 12 on at least half a dozen bills that, if approved, would seek not only to restrict how gun makers and dealers conduct business in New York City but also to counter a growing movement across the nation to roll back gun-control measures. The proposals include holding gun makers, dealers and importers liable for damages if their weapons are used to kill or injure people in the city, as well as prohibiting gun dealers from selling more than one firearm to the same person within 90 days. Another proposal would require gun owners in the city to obtain liability insurance. While these gun-control proposals are not new, many Council members say they have taken on new urgency since nearly all 51 members witnessed the shooting of one of their own at City Hall on July 23. Mr. Davis, 41, of Brooklyn, was gunned down by a political rival, Othniel Boaz Askew, in the Council chamber after Mr. Davis escorted him past City Hall metal detectors. "There was no urgency prior to the shooting," said Peter Vallone Jr., chairman of the Public Safety Committee, which is holding next month's hearing.

799.text: GUN-9632 PRIMARY BACK ON SALE: WEAPONS THAT CAN SHATTER CONCRETE Semiautomatic assault rifles have returned, and cops who face them know too well what they can do. THE COLT AR-15 semiautomatic assault rifle is long, black and sleek and shoots .223-caliber ammunition, or 2-inch long bullets. For 10 years, the sale and manufacture of the fearsome weapon was illegal anywhere in America. Last week, as some Southern stores resumed sale of the rifle, Police Officer Darrell Corti demonstrated its destructive power. Corti aimed the weapon at a cinderblock at the NYPD firing range at Rodman's Neck in the Bronx and pulled the trigger. The burst of gunfire rang loudly in the ears despite protective gear. The narrow bullet traveled at such a high velocity that it pierced a half-dollar-sized hole in the inch-thick side of the stone before shattering into shiny fragments. The round is capable of penetrating some bulletproof vests. "These are weapons of war, and to make them more readily available is certainly a concern for us," said Police Commissioner Raymond Kelly. "It is a concern for police officers because they're the ones at 2 a.m. who are most likely to confront these types of weapons." More than a decade ago, the AR-15 had been routinely used by dealers to guard drug dens. Now it can be legally sold in federally licensed gun shops in certain states, along with Uzis, Macs and Tec-9s, the sinister-looking pistols toted by thugs who devastated the city. With the expiration of the federal assault weapons ban two weeks ago, will the guns that held neighborhoods in the grip of violence in the late 1980s and early 1990s make a comeback?

803.text: NA-42852 PRIMARY Say Goodbye to the Assault Weapons Ban Fans of military-style assault weapons can stop worrying - their gun lobby has done its work, and all but assured that Congress will not pass a ban on their dangerous toys. Senate Democratic leaders have decided not to include the ban, proposed by Senator Dianne Feinstein of California, in the official gun bill that will reach the floor in the next few weeks. It was always a long shot, but now Democrats have officially given the ban the cold shoulder. Ms. Feinstein will probably manage to bring up the ban as a separate amendment, putting senators on record and letting the public know how they stand when given a chance to prohibit the kinds of guns used in so many massacres. As an independent measure, however, it's guaranteed to fail. And it's not even clear that an important piece of the ban, outlawing high-capacity ammunition magazines, will have enough support for a simple majority, let alone the 60 votes needed to get past a Republican filibuster. Forcing supporters of gun control to hold votes on separate amendments carries significant danger. If Democrats like Ms. Feinstein get to introduce amendments, then Republican senators will do so, too. And they will almost certainly submit proposals designed to put red-state Democrats on the spot, like new limits on the responsibilities of federal firearms agents, or a bill requiring every state to honor other's gun permits. If those kinds of measures pick up 15 Democratic votes, they could poison the entire gun package, making it unpalatable for gun-control supporters.

800.text: GUN-6658 PRIMARY Clinton will push to enforce gun laws Colo. implements own 'Project Exile' President Clinton is expected to rip a page out of the gun  lobby's playbook today, calling for stricter enforcement of gun  laws already on the books. He's not giving up on the sweeping new gun-control laws he  wants. But in a speech in Boston today, he is to announce that he  wants to spend $ 280 million to hire more federal agents to track  gun crimes and more prosecutors to try the people they catch. He even is expected to give a tip of the hat to the National  Rifle Association's favorite government program, Richmond, Va.'s  'Project Exile.' In that program, federal law-enforcement agencies  worked to impose maximum penalties on criminals who used guns. As pressure has mounted for tougher gun laws, gun groups such  as the NRA have attacked Clinton for doing a bad job of enforcing  existing gun laws. Gun-control groups have countered that Congress  has not given gun-enforcement agencies the resources they need. Clinton's plan would give them some of those resources. It also gives Clinton some political cover as he prepares to  roll out more gun-control proposals, Denver pollster Floyd Ciruli  said. 'The Democratic administration is sensitive to the argument -  and the effectiveness of the argument - that we have enough gun  laws and we need to enforce them,' Ciruli said. Ciruli noted that Clinton's U.S. attorney in Colorado, Tom  Strickland, already has picked up on that sentiment and rolled out  his own 'Project Exile' last fall. The Colorado version is more of  a team approach with local police and prosecutors.

808.text: NA-42205 PRIMARY The Buzz: Short survey shows growing support for control of assault weapons Stricter gun control is blowing in the wind here in East Contra Costa County. Supporters of the right to bear all kinds of weapons and the equally vocal Americans clamoring for stricter gun controls are frozen in their positions. But those in the middle seem to be thawing as a result of the recent massacres. A snap survey of East County voters indicates a growing change. Locals were asked if their feelings about gun control had altered in the past year. For some it hasn't. But for many others the time has come to tighten the gun laws. Cindi Tai of Antioch says the mood of the whole country is starting to shift. "I think Congress will be forced to act now. I expect we will see a ban on automatic weapons." Sandy Remington of Brentwood agrees. "My feelings have changed. The school shootings really got to me. The situation has gotten out of control and I've become more convinced that these killings must be stopped. We need to strengthen our gun control laws." Linda Stevens of Antioch has been a hunter. "I thoroughly approve of people being able to own guns, but the big automatic weapons that can fire unlimited numbers of bullets never made sense to me," she said. Tamara Waid of Oakley comes from a family of hunters. "I'm used to guns being safely held, but the climate has changed," she said. "I worry a lot more about my kids now, especially when they're away from home. Congress will do something, but unfortunately there always are some people who can figure out ways to get around the law."

806.text: GUN-6035 PRIMARY How G.O.P. Missed Mark on Gun Control Throughout the chaotic Senate struggle over gun control, Senator Larry E. Craig of Idaho, a champion of gun rights, played scrambling quarterback for his fellow Republicans. Mr. Craig, a board member of the National Rifle Association, turned his hideaway Capitol office into a place for huddles with the group's lobbyists. Yet no matter how they tried to turn back the Democrats on gun control, they failed. As disgruntled Republicans tried this weekend to sort out how they had allowed the Democrats to gain control of the Senate's agenda, they pointed to miscalculations by Senator Trent Lott, their majority leader, Mr. Craig, and other Republican leaders. Republicans say the party was drawn into a fight before it was ready, hastily put together flawed answers to Democratic proposals and had little sense of where its caucus actually stood. Some Republicans said they failed to recognize the image that would be created by allowing Mr. Craig to act as their most visible figure. And, most startling of all, they created a heady turn in the spotlight for Vice President Al Gore on Thursday as he cast a rare tie-breaking vote to pass a Democratic measure to require background checks at gun shows. "We were all hurrying," Mr. Craig said. "I like time to shape an issue, to know where my membership is, so that I have no surprises. We were simply not afforded that luxury."

805.text: GUN-9329 PRIMARY CHUCK FIRES AT GUN RUNNERS Saying I-95 is a "firearms freeway" used to flood New York with out-of-state guns, Sen. Chuck Schumer yesterday took aim at gun runners. Releasing a study by his office, based on 2001 data from the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, Schumer (D-N.Y.) said more than two-thirds of the guns used to commit crimes in New York came from out of state - mostly from Southern states with weaker gun control laws. "Criminals know the business, and they know where to find the weakest links in the chain, and they go there and buy the guns," Schumer said. Schumer said he will introduce legislation this week that would double sentences for interstate gun traffickers by prosecuting them under federal Racketeer Influenced and Corrupt Organizations laws. Under this legislation, the maximum prison term for anyone convicted of gun running would double to 20 years from 10 years for each offense. "Gun runners use our highways like firearm freeways to traffic illegal guns to criminals," said Schumer, citing Interstate 95 in particular. The study showed that in 2001, 3,662 New York crimes were committed with guns bought in another state. Of those crimes, 1,971 involved weapons from Florida, Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina and Virginia. But Gerald Stoudemire, president of Gun Owners of South Carolina, said he believed Schumer's data to be "trumped up and false." "We find that most of the guns [used in crimes] come from theft, and the theft could come from anywhere," Stoudemire said. "I do not believe that the guns are coming from South Carolina."

807.text: GUN-4886 PRIMARY GUN OWNERS' RESPONSIBILITY A July 13 editorial endorsed a bill before Congress that would make a gun owner liable for the illegal use of the owner's firearm by a third party (that third party being a minor). It seems to be a reasonable proposition, provided that the bill exempts those owners who have made an honest effort to secure their firearms. Also, the bill should not hold owners responsible who have had their guns stolen from environments that are normally secure. (A gun owner living alone shouldn't be expected to apply the same measure of security as the owner with additional household members.) But the editorial became abrasive when gun owners were characterized as having a "macho-man/victim dichotomy." It is this type of comparison that alarms the NRA and causes it be in opposition to many gun control proposals. I do believe that irresponsible gun owners should be made to be responsible, just as I believe that a motorist should be held responsible for his or her actions on the road, just as I believe that doctors or j udges should be held responsible for the ramifications of their actions. Accountability is an important thing but not by restricting reasonable access to the rights we already have entitlements to. The death of children at the hands of other children using guns is a matter that rightly should be receiving the attention of any individual with a sense of moral integrity. Too many children have unsupervised access to firearms. People about to purchase a gun should be educated as to the responsibility of their purchase and current owners should be reminded of theirs. Those who would take their responsibilities too lightly should be dealt with sternly.

809.text: GUN-248 PRIMARY The Perils of Protecting Police Representative Mario Biaggi of New York is properly dismayed to find the gun lobby triumphing even over the police: ''Earlier this year Congress voted to expand the death benefit program for police survivors and to memorialize police deaths. It seems only logical that somewhere along the line we would also try to prevent police deaths.'' The lawmakers came close to doing just that this year - until the collapse of a compromise worked out with the National Rifle Association. Police safety ought not to rest on such shaky politics. Mr. Biaggi, joined by Senator Moynihan and many other lawmakers, has been pleading for a Federal ban on handgun bullets made of certain alloys that are hard enough to penetrate a police officer's protective vest. Such ammunition has no legitimate law-enforcement or sporting purpose. James Huberty fired it at the police during his killing spree in a McDonald's restaurant in San Diego last summer. Yet the N.R.A. has for years opposed the ban, contending that it might open the way to Federal control of more conventional bullets. Officials of the Justice and Treasury Departments supported N.R.A. contentions that the armor-piercing bullets could not be defined precisely enough to prevent abuse of any law that banned them. This year, however, the issue was at the top of the lobbying agendas of police groups, and both the N.R.A. and the Administration saw a threat to their good relations with the law-enforcement community. Last spring they announced that they had found a way to write a bill after all. It carefully defined armor-piercing bullets and gave the Secretary of the Treasury some discretion to ban their manufacture and import.

804.text: GUN-4412 PRIMARY Fairfax Looks to New Gun-Control Fight; Safety Lock Plan and Wider Ban in Government Buildings Considered Fairfax County appears to be headed toward another battle in the General Assembly over gun control. Some supervisors said yesterday that handguns sold in Fairfax should be equipped with child-safety locks and also proposed broadening a ban on weapons in some government buildings. Either measure would require legislative approval. "This is about protecting kids and reducing violence," said Supervisor Robert B. Dix Jr. (R-Hunter Mill), who is backing a proposal to prohibit guns outside as well as inside teen and recreation centers. But before the day was over, a spokesman for the National Rifle Association said his organization was prepared to return to Richmond to combat the proposals. "Such legislation would only serve to ban the right to self-defense," said NRA spokesman Jim Manown." Fairfax has long been pushing legislation that would put limits on guns, with most of the effort focused on a bill intended to keep guns out of teen and recreation centers. During the last session, the General Assembly passed a bill allowing that ban, but Gov. George Allen vetoed it, saying the measure would do little to discourage criminals and instead just complicate the state's gun laws. Then-Attorney General James S. Gilmore III later issued an opinion stating that the county could issue its own regulations imposing such a ban, although it could not apply to the exterior grounds as the county had originally requested.

characters: Attorney General James S. Gilmore III

812.text: GUN-8109 PRIMARY Week 6: Gun control: Jim Hughes Individuals have the right to own guns. However, common sense and public safety dictate some regulation and oversight, and they are constitutionally permissible. The Second Amendment's language is quite limited. It says, "A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms shall not be infringed." We can't reasonably interpret this to say that private citizens can own guns of whatever type (and for whatever purposes) they choose; or that the public has no right to know the owner's identity or qualifications to use guns safely. A gun is no less dangerous than a car. Guns should be registered and their owners should be licensed. Anyone who wants a license should be required to have a record free of violent crime and medically diagnosed violent propensity, and demonstrate proficiency in gun use and knowledge of gun safety. In a similar vein, certain categories of guns should not be available to private citizens. Those include assault rifles and rapid-fire or large capacity guns. No person who uses guns for sport, self-protection or other legitimate purposes should object to any of these modest regulatory requirements. Some argue that only law-abiding citizens will obey registration and licensing requirements and criminals will ignore the process. Hogwash! Just because criminals break rules doesn't mean that we shouldn't have rules. Rules exist to encourage most people to avoid inappropriate behavior and to provide a basis for punishing those who don't.

810.text: GUN-5749 PRIMARY GOP-LED HOUSE SHOOTS DOWN CURBS ON GUNS WASHINGTON The Republican-led House scuttled the gun-control bill altogether yesterday, leaving President Clinton and New York Democrats fuming about the political muscle of the National Rifle Association. Democrats, angry that one of their own succeeded just before midnight Thursday in pushing through an NRA-backed amendment that watered down background checks on people who buy firearms at gun shows, chose to vote against the overall bill at 1:30 a.m. yesterday. The 280-to-147 rejection was an even bigger margin of victory for the NRA than what it won just before midnight, when the House voted 218 to 211 to okay an amendment by Rep. John Dingell (D-Mich.), a former NRA board member. "The American people will not stand for this," Clinton said in a statement issued in Europe. "Our nation is waiting for Washington to pass a real law that keeps guns away from children and out of the hands of criminals." Clinton also complained that GOP House leaders deliberately postponed yesterday's votes so that public outrage after the deadly April 20 Littleton, Colo., high school shooting could dissipate. Several of the guns used in the massacre that left 15 dead were purchased at a gun show. Dingell's amendment called for a 24-hour waiting period at gun shows the Senate and Clinton support a three-business-day delay but also watered down the definition of gun show. "The NRA played the tune, and the House danced to it," said Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.). "Champagne corks are popping at NRA headquarters."

811.text: GUN-937 PRIMARY Brady Bill, Senate Version THE SENATE is now ready to consider its own version of the Brady bill, which not only addresses some concerns voiced by opponents in the House but also enjoys the full support of law enforcement groups, Sarah and Jim Brady and those who have been backing them in their effort for a seven-day waiting period on handgun purchases. The new language, drafted by Senate Majority Leader George Mitchell and Sens. Herb Kohl of Wisconsin and Al Gore of Tennessee, adds to the House-passed bill a mandatory criminal records check that is tied to federal incentives for states to improve their records systems. In the House, one of the key complaints -- or so they said -- of National Rifle Association leaders and their congressional followers was that no background check was required. It would be in this version. So, will the leaders of the NRA join the forces of good sense and public safety this time around? The new bill also answers another complaint generated in the House by NRA lobbyists/leaders -- that the Brady bill could cause local law enforcement agencies to become "prime targets of opportunity for litigation." The Senate language would exempt police from liability on this point. Will the NRA leaders yield? Even if they won't, how about the senators who understand the overwhelming support shown for the Brady bill in poll after poll?

813.text: NA-43837 PRIMARY Why passing an assault weapons ban in Congress will be difficult President Obama is set to unveil a sweeping set of gun control proposals Wednesday, including a ban on assault weapons. The problem for gun control advocates who've long called for such a measure is that there are fresh signs that it could be stonewalled on Capitol Hill. For starters, two high-ranking congressional Democrats have expressed some degree of pessimism in recent days about the prospects of passing a ban in Congress. Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-Nev.), a gun rights advocate, said he was uncertain whether a ban could pass the Senate, and even less optimistic that it could clear the Republican-controlled House. "Is it something that can pass the Senate? Maybe. It is something that can pass the House? I doubt it," Reid said Friday on "Nevada Week in Review." Reid has received a "B" rating from the National Rifle Association. On Tuesday, House Minority Whip Steny Hoyer (D-Md.) did little to refute Reid's suggestion that an assault weapons ban would run into trouble in the House, telling reporters: "I don't have an assessment right now, I think that's been the case based on past history." While the outlook in Congress is murky, it's clear that most Americans favor such a ban. A Washington Post-ABC News poll released on Monday showed 58 percent of adults and 59 percent of registered voters support a nationwide ban on the sale of assault weapons. A Pew poll released the same day showed 55 percent of Americans support a ban.

819.text: GUN-7570 PRIMARY RINSING OFF FEAR, BOY GETS A CHANCE TO BE A BOY AGAIN THANKS TO A Long Island swimming pool and no thanks at all to the law, 11-year-old Noah of Brooklyn is finally back to being himself two months after another youngster robbed him at gunpoint "Smiling, cracking jokes," his mother says. "I hadn't realized how much of him was gone until it was back." Noah had ceased being Noah at 7:30 p.m. March 28 as he stood near his home on First St. in Park Slope, Brooklyn. The immediate cause was a 14-year-old who pulled a gun and robbed him. The trauma was then compounded by a twist in the law that taught him not even a child can always be assured of protection. The mother was left understanding how even a passionate gun control advocate, such as Rosie O'Donnell, could hire an armed guard for her kids. "If money were no object and I felt my child was being threatened, I would hire somebody," the mother says. The mother believes the hubbub over O'Donnell's armed guards should not distract us from the real issue. However hypocritical you think Rosie may or may not be, people in states with lax gun laws continue to load their car trunks with pistols and head for New York. "Kids who are 14 should not be walking around with a gun," the mother says. "That's what it's about." Noah's lesson in the law began several days after the robbery, when he peered through a two-way mirror in the 78th Precinct stationhouse. He saw four juveniles sitting with big numbers tucked under their chins.

818.text: GUN-11409 PRIMARY TAKING AIM Now a GOPer goes after guns Fights gangs' shared waeapons ALBANY - In a bid to steal some of the anti-gun thunder from Democrats, Senate Republicans are firing their own salvo against illegal weapons. Brooklyn Sen. Martin Golden, a former city cop, plans to introduce legislation stiffening penalties against people who plant so-called community guns - weapons hidden in key locations for use by groups of gang members and other criminals. "Today you have a person or a gang that will have one gun that all its members use and know where it is," Golden said. "This will now give us the ability to go after these people who facilitate guns." The bill comes as the debate over gun legislation in New York continued to heat up - with Democrats mostly taking the lead. Senate Democrats have introduced a package of gun legislation that would, among other things, ban assault weapons and require microstamping of shell casings. They blame Republicans for blocking those bills. "The Senate Republicans have to answer for the fact that our streets are not safe because they refuse to enact sensible gun laws," said Sen. Michael Gianaris (D-Queens). The Daily News reported last week that Gianaris planned a comprehensive package to cap the number of firearms a person can buy to one a month, enact a 10-day waiting period, improve record keeping on the sale of guns, and close background check loopholes.

815.text: GUN-30393 PRIMARY Editorial | A Purchase Limit; How many guns do you need? The National Rifle Association, when it sniffs a proposal to limit handgun purchases, responds with a version of this argument: "Limiting the sale of handguns deprives law-abiding Americans of their constitutional right to bear arms and defend themselves." Now, look at the photos around this text. You will see 26 handguns - the number a married couple could buy every year in Pennsylvania if House Bill 871, sponsored by State Rep. John Myers (D., Phila.), becomes law. The bill will be one of 98 proposals on the agenda of a special anticrime session of the House tomorrow. Laws can't be passed, but nonbinding votes will be taken. House members should stand up to the gun lobby - and for public safety - by supporting Myers' statewide limit in a nonbinding vote. Then, in a regular session, they should enact it. Myers' bill would allow an individual to buy one handgun every 30 days - 13 per year. Question: Aren't 13 handguns for one person, or 26 for a couple, enough to defend family and home from an attacker? The only people whom this bill would hamper are the illegal gun traffickers who use "straw buyers" to obtain guns in bulk from gun shops, then peddle them on the streets. This has made our city a capital of the illegal gun trade, exporting violence up and down the East Coast.

817.text: GUN-1199 PRIMARY Families Touched by Tragedy Plead for Handgun Bill; Bereaved Parents Urge Congress to Impose Week-Long Waiting Period on Purchases Edward M. Prince is one of the victims, the father of a 19-year-old Yale University student murdered last month by a thief with a handgun. As he told his son's story to a House subcommittee and pleaded for a bill to impose a waiting period on handgun purchases, Prince's strong voice broke and a shudder went through the crowded hearing room. "Our anguish and pain are enormous, our lives are permanently altered," said Prince, a Washington patent lawyer, as he burst into tears. "How many tragedies does it take to change political priorities?" Amid a nationwide surge of gun-related violence, proponents of the waiting-period bill yesterday brought forth their most powerful weapons -- a parade of gun victims to tell their painful stories and join what has become an annual battle with the National Rifle Association. Supporters this year are optimistic, but the Bush administration is still oppposed to the measure. A top Justice Department official said the week-long waiting period to allow local police checks of handgun purchasers would be "useless" in fighting gun-related crime. The namesake of the Brady Bill, former White House press secretary James Brady, told the House Judiciary crime subcommittee that a handgun had infringed upon his freedoms. "I no longer have the freedom of mobility, the freedom to play or chase after my son Scott who is now 12 . . . the freedom to dance with my wife Sarah," said Brady, crippled from the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan.

820.text: GUN-4144 PRIMARY N.J. LAW; Concealed Weapons: A Senator Says Their Time Has Come When the State Legislature banned the sale and possession of assault weapons in 1990, thousands of gun owners protested with seething letters and boisterous rallies.
