The issue has resurfaced this spring in the form of a proposal to allow citizens to carry concealed weapons. The proposal comes from State Senator Gerald Cardinale, a conservative Bergen County Republican who is a member of the National Rifle Association. Under the current law, New Jersey residents may carry a handgun only if they can convince a Superior Court judge that their safety depends on it. But Mr. Cardinale's proposal would allow people to carry weapons if they pass a background check and take eight hours of training. At least 28 other states have passed similar laws, many in the last few years. Senator Cardinale, a dentist from Demarest, said his wife gave him the idea for the proposal. She had read several newspaper accounts of carjackings, he said, and wanted to carry a gun in her car. "I had to disabuse her of the notion that she could get a permit to do that," he said last week as legislative lawyers were reviewing the final draft of his proposal, which he is calling the Crime Reduction Act. Senator Cardinale is backing up the proposal with statistics from states like Florida, which has allowed residents to carry firearms since 1987. Although violent crime has increased in Florida since then, the number of crimes involving handguns has dropped by 29 percent, according to the Federal Bureau of Investigation's Uniform Crime Report for 1993. Five percent of Florida residents -- about 300,000 people -- have obtained permits to carry weapons, Mr. Cardinale said.

814.text: GUN-665 PRIMARY ILLINOIS SENATE APPROVES BAN ON ASSAULT WEAPONS SPRINGFIELD, Ill. - After an emotional debate that included displays of assault weapons by state troopers, the Illinois Senate narrowly approved a limited ban on assault weapons.   The legislation, approved 32-25, would ban the import, use, sale or manufacture of semiautomatic pistols holding more than 15 rounds, all other semiautomatic weapons holding more than 10 rounds and shotguns holding more than six rounds.   Gov. James R. Thompson and Lt. Gov. George Ryan both lobbied Republicans on the floor of the Senate to support the bill.   ''This bill and the vote in the Senate today say, 'Hey, the sight of wackos in schoolyards with automatic and semiautomatic weapons killing kids is a sight America shouldn't tolerate,' '' Thompson said after the Senate approved the legislation and sent it to the House.   But he declined to predict the fate of the legislation in the House, where it is sure to be the object of strong opposition from the National Rifle Association.   Possession of an illegal ammunition magazine would be punishable by up to a year in jail and a $1,000 fine. The legislation exempts bona fide gun collectors from prohibitions against possession of such weapons if they register with the state police.   Sen. William Marovitz, D-Chicago and sponsor of the bill, said ''assault weapons' only purpose is to kill people quickly and efficiently.'' At one point in the Senate debate, two state troopers carrying assault weapons were allowed on the floor of the Senate at the invitation of Marovitz, who assured his colleagues that the weapons were not loaded. The troopers left a few minutes later after Sen. John Davidson, R-Springfield and an opponent of the legislation, objected to their presence.   Proponents of the legislation argued that regulation of the semiautomatic weapons was necessary because they are usually used by criminals. Some proponents also took aim at the NRA for opposing gun bans and using its powerful lobby to pressure legislators to vote against measures regulating guns. '' I hope we're more afraid of the damage these weapons do'' than of the NRA, Marovitz said.   But some legislators said that the proposal would endanger private citizens' ability to protect themselves and infringed on people's constitutional right to bear arms.   ''I will never deprive law-abiding citizens from protecting their homes with guns,'' said Sen. Adeline Jay Geo-Karis, R-Zion.   But Marovitz said that the legislation would not endanger people's right to protect themselves.   ''We are not banning anyone's right to own a weapon,'' he said.   And Sen. Joyce Holmberg, D-Rockford, said, ''When the founding fathers gave us the right to bear arms, I have the feeling they meant one musket per person.'' Three Metro East area legislators spoke out against the proposal.   ''This type of legislation is going to make felons out of thousands of people in my district,'' said Sen. Frank Watson, R-Greenville.   ''Banning possession, sale, manufacture or whatever it might be of these weapons is not going to solve the problem. The criminal is still going to have access, still have the weapon and the honest, law-abiding citizens of this state are the ones that are going to be punished,'' Watson said.   Sen. James Rea, D-Christopher, said, ''I know that people in the central part of the state and the southern part in particular are not asking for this legislation. They are asking, rather, that we punish the criminals that commit these crimes.'' Sen. Sam Vadalabene, D-Edwardsville, said that he opposed the legislation because it would hurt the ammunition industry in his area.   This story contains information from the Associated Press.   Geri Aston is a journalism student at Sangamon State University and an intern in the Post-Dispatch Springfield bureau.

824.text: GUN-10608 PRIMARY ABOUT OBAMA'S GUN GRAB... ; Never was a chance he'd disarm America. Does anyone have any idea when Barack Obama is going to take our guns away? Last year at this time, there were stories everywhere about gun shops with lines of people stocking up for the crisis. It looked like Cash for Clunkers, without trade-ins. Mr. Obama's election seemed to be funneling more money to guns than his predecessor's Troubled Asset Relief Program gave to wayward banks. Mr. Obama had merely to lay his hand on a Bible, and gun sales soared as if the shops were giving away 72 virgins in heaven with every Glock 17. The government, though, isn't kicking down doors to seize guns. It's time to wonder what that panic buying was all about. The fear was said to be that Mr. Obama wanted to take away our guns. It was a strange fear because there was no plausible answer to why and how he would do such a thing. The why, some people said, was because disarming Americans is part of a secret liberal agenda. Anybody who follows the adventures of the 18 or 19 liberals left in this country knows that they are not disciplined enough to agree on an agenda. And if they had a secret one, the last people to know would be the talking gasbags. The how is even murkier. There is no law that allows a president to take away guns. There is a Supreme Court decision saying, basically, that he can't. Even if you make the wild assumption that he could get the Army or the Marshal's Service to act illegally and unconstitutionally, why would any president want the widespread carnage that would be caused by trying to pry guns out of Americans' cold, dead hands in every city, village and farm?

816.text: GUN-8100 PRIMARY It may be time to extend background checks There used to be a time when the sale of conventional firearms  wasn't subject to much regulation. In the early 1960s, my dad opened a charge account for my  brother and me at an Albuquerque sporting-goods store where he  knew the manager and where we could charge the occasional rifle or  box of .22 ammo. That was then, before the assassinations of President John F.  Kennedy, his brother, Sen. Robert F. Kennedy, or the Rev. Dr.  Martin Luther King Jr. Gun-control laws were passed that  law-abiding gun-owners, myself included, didn't like. Then came a series of school shooting rampages, including the  worst of all, Columbine, and the so-called 'gun show loophole'  became a political issue. Tom Mauser, father of Daniel Mauser, who was murdered at  Columbine, took up the cause of SAFE Colorado to pass a state law  requiring that instant background checks (already necessary for  any sales made by federally licensed gun dealers) be extended to  private sales at gun shows. Three of the four guns used by teen killers Eric Harris and  Dylan Klebold were bought for the then-underage boys by Robyn  Anderson from an unlicensed vendor - with no background check - at  a local gun show. The fourth weapon had been bought several months  earlier by Mark Manes at a show, also in a private sale. Manes  later sold Harris and Klebold the semi-auto Intratec pistol.

characters: Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.

821.text: GUN-10942 PRIMARY NURSE WANTS TO TALK TIGHTER GUN CONTROL AFTER FATAL SHOOTINGS ST. PETERSBURG In the aftermath of the shootings that left two St. Petersburg police officers dead on Monday, most City Council members did not want to second-guess the department's tactics, procedures or policies. "It's time to mourn now," said council Chairman Jim Kennedy. "We will have time to evaluate later." But one council member, Karl Nurse, said now would be a good time to discuss what to do about all the guns being used in a spate of high-profile shootings lately. "It's remarkable that our system is so dysfunctional that we can't have a discussion about limiting the access that criminals and the mentally ill have to guns," Nurse said. He e-mailed the Buzza news release by Mayors Against Illegal Guns, a coalition of 550 mayors co-founded by New York City Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg, which calls for tightening U.S. gun control laws. The release points out that the Columbine killers used weapons bought at a gun show from an unlicensed seller; the Virginia Tech shooter got a gun he wouldn't have had if his records had been reported to the FBI gun background check system; and the suspect in Tucson, Ariz., got a gun because of lax federal regulations. The group, which lists Tampa Mayor Pam Iorio as a coalition member, seeks a list of those prohibited from buying guns and a new requirement that every gun sale be accompanied by a background check, including those at gun shows.

823.text: GUN-11621 PRIMARY Wis. prosecutor hits courtroom gun ban MILWAUKEE - A Wisconsin prosecutor asked the state's highest court on Tuesday to dismiss orders by local judges barring lawyers from bringing concealed weapons into courtrooms. Winnebago County District Attorney Christian A. Gossett argued in a petition to the Wisconsin Supreme Court that local judges cannot overrule a state law that took effect in November making Wisconsin the 49th state to allow qualified gun owners to carry a concealed weapon. The request comes after six Winnebago County judges, saying they want to have safe courtrooms, maintained their stance barring concealed weapons from the county courthouse. "The first step in fulfilling that obligation, and in assessing safety and security threats in a courtroom, is for judges and law enforcement to be aware of who is carrying a weapon during a proceeding," the judges said in a statement.

825.text: GUN-31368 PRIMARY Ruling reflects America's ambivalence on guns On hot-button cultural issues, the Supreme Court typically doesn't stray far from public opinion. So that's where the court found itself Thursday with its landmark Second Amendment ruling, right where the majority of Americans are on the issue: for gun ownership, but with limits. The court straddled that line. It said for the first time that the Constitution bars "the absolute prohibition of handguns held and used for self-defense in the home." At the same time, it left plenty of room for authorities to restrict how handguns are sold, who can buy them and where they can be carried. Fortunately, the 5-4 decision to strike down the District of Columbia's tough ban on handgun possession didn't go as far as gun-control advocates had feared or the gun lobby had hoped. The justices agreed that a right to individual ownership doesn't mean that anyone can own any weapon. That leaves in place reasonable restrictions or outright bans on firearms such as machine guns and sawed-off shotguns, and exotic weapons such as surface-to-air missiles. Thank goodness. The court also specified that felons and the mentally ill could continue to be barred from buying guns, that gun owners could be prohibited from carrying them in places such as schools and government buildings, and that authorities could restrict how guns are sold -- presumably meaning mandatory background checks are OK. And it said those limits shouldn't be interpreted as the only ones that would pass constitutional muster.

826.text: NA-42031 PRIMARY San Ramon Valley letters: Just restrict gun rights of Democrats, not Republicans Just restrict gun rights of Dems, not GOP Most crimes and murders are committed in the cities of America and by the constituency of the Democratic Party. The five most dangerous cities for crimes in 2011 were 1. Flint, Mich.; 2. Detroit; 3. St. Louis; 4. Oakland; and 5. Memphis, Tenn. These are FBI statistics measured as to numbers of violent crimes committed per 1,000 residents. In terms of dangerous cities, Oakland ranked 15th for murders, although it is 44th for population. For murders alone, New Orleans led the nation per capita, and Detroit ranked second. Note that all these cities are governed by Democrats, and all predominantly have Democratic voters. But lately, Democrats want law-abiding Republicans to give up their guns. A better idea, and one sure to solve most of the nation's crime problems, is for only Democrats to be restricted from possessing guns and ammo. They alone have proved over many years that they are incapable of living peacefully with anyone. John Morrow Alamo Other factors, not gun control, cut murder rate Your article that "Good Guys and Guns Don't Mix" manages to insult almost half the country, because almost half the country owns guns. You then choose a couple of incidents and decide that we should make national policy based on this. This is irresponsible. Tens of millions of guns have been purchased in this country in the last 20 years, and our crime and murder rate has dropped in half. The two are not necessarily related, but obviously, more gun ownership has not raised the crime and murder rate.

828.text: GUN-11277 PRIMARY During Super Bowl, for 30 Seconds, 2 Mayors Will Be on Same Side Never mind those Ferris Bueller and ''Star Wars''-themed Super Bowl ads. Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg has shot a Super Bowl ad, too, and he thinks it is pretty funny, though with a serious message. The half-minute ad features Mr. Bloomberg and Boston's mayor, Thomas M. Menino, ribbing each other as they cheer for their respective teams and cities. But then they turn to the purpose of their spot, declaring that one thing they agree on is the need for stricter federal gun control laws. The two mayors are longtime allies on gun control and founders of a group called Mayors Against Illegal Guns, which has pushed for a stronger federal background check system. Mr. Bloomberg and Mr. Menino shot the ad in New York early Tuesday. And, just as big Super Bowl advertisers like Honda and Volkswagen have been promoting their ads with previews on YouTube, Mr. Bloomberg publicized his at an appearance later Tuesday morning. ''It was very funny; you should take a look at it,'' he told reporters in a news conference at the Pershing Square Signature Center, the new home of the Signature Theater. Mr. Bloomberg then mentioned the ad on Twitter. ''We disagree on who'll win the #Superbowl, but @MayorTomMenino & I agree on fighting illegal guns. Tune in Sunday,'' the mayor (or one of his Twitter surrogates) wrote, attaching a photo of the shoot. In the picture, the mayors are sitting on a couch as though they are watching the game, with Mr. Bloomberg wearing a New York Giants jersey and Mr. Menino a New England Patriots jersey over their dress shirts and ties. (Mr. Bloomberg's tie, for the record, was blue.)

822.text: GUN-7427 PRIMARY IN BRIEF; At Kmart in Riverhead, A Debate on Gun Sales The Kmart store on Route 58 in Riverhead was the scene of protests last week, both in favor and against the sale of firearms in the sporting goods department. Similar concerns were raised when a Kmart opened at the Bridgehampton Commons Shopping Mall last March and offered BB guns and air rifles for sale. The corporation agreed to withdraw an application for a federal license to sell rifles and shotguns at that branch, under pressure from antigun groups and community leaders. But the Riverhead Kmart has made no such gesture, and representatives from the Suffolk County chapter of New Yorkers Against Gun Violence and from the Riverhead Sportsmen's Club and the National Rifle Association went head-to-head over a display of rifles and pistols kept under lock and key at the rear of the store. "They do not have the right to dictate to others how to live their life," said Carol M. Gebhart, a member of the Sportsmen's Association for Firearms Education (S.A.F.E.) and the N.R.A. "All law-abiding citizens should be able to purchase guns at Kmart." But Joyce Gorycka, whose husband, James, was killed in the Long Island Rail Road massacre in 1993, said the sale of guns in combination with ordinary household products sends a contradictory message to young people. "I don't think they should have guns in a family-oriented store with all the violence in schools," said Mrs. Gorycka, who is also a co-chairwoman for the county chapter of New Yorkers Against Gun Violence.   "I don't want anyone to go through what I went through."

831.text: NA-40435 PRIMARY YOUR VIEWS - LETTERS FROM OUR READERS OPINION 'Gun culture' is a modern invention I found it surprising to read that a professor of political science believes what protects us most from an oppressive, tyrannical government is our "gun culture" ("Why we need a gun culture," Jan. 2). I would have expected him to believe that it is our Constitution with its system of checks and balances and our long tradition of limited government dating to the Magna Carta in England that protects us from what Professor Brown calls the unthinkable. Brown also believes the Founding Fathers intended for the nation to have this "gun culture" we have today. I disagree, because if the Founders really felt this way they wouldn't have forgotten to include the "right to bear arms" in the Constitution when they wrote it in 1787. It took an amendment to add it to the Constitution, and that wasn't done until four years later. The "gun culture" with which Professor Brown seems so enamored is a modern invention created by gun manufactures and dealers. I don't think the Founders ever envisioned the kinds of weapons we have today where a single person can kill 26 people in a matter of minutes. Even our first constitution, the Articles of Confederation, makes no mention of the right to bear arms. And when the former 13 colonies decided to write their own state constitutions, only Massachusetts and Pennsylvania included provisions for the right to bear arms. It was many years later that more states added the right to their constitutions.

827.text: GUN-10752 PRIMARY Sources: Camden chief gave gun permit to councilman with record Camden Police Chief Scott Thomson signed off on two gun permits in July for a Camden city councilman with a criminal record that bars him from purchasing a firearm under state law, according to law enforcement sources and documents. Thomson, who has sole responsibility for approving gun permit applications from qualified city residents, declined to comment on Councilman Curtis Jenkins' application's approval, citing state confidentiality rules about applications and background investigations. Jenkins pleaded guilty to two counts of welfare fraud in June 1982, but did not disclose that fact on his application. A spokesman for the New Jersey State Police confirmed that its official corruption unit had launched an investigation of Jenkins. Two law enforcement sources said the inquiry began after a Camden police officer told state police that Thomson was made aware of Jenkins' record when he signed the permits. Thomson told the Camden County prosecutor that it was an administrative error, a spokesman for the prosecutor said. Jenkins, who received four years' probation and repaid $11,000 in goverment funds, said that because his conviction was for a nonviolent offense, "I didn't realize it was something I had to report. . . . Otherwise I would never have applied for" a permit. He said he never bought a gun with the permits and agreed to surrender the documents to the county prosecutor last week.

829.text: GUN-11828 PRIMARY Dec. 21 letters to the editor Let's use our power to end gun violence On Dec. 14, a few hours after learning about the senseless shooting rampage at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn., I was prompted to take a long walk in my small town of Piedmont. The walk provided much-needed silence and space to reflect on the horrific tragedy that has touched so many. On my way home, I walked past our town's relatively new and lovely elementary school, Frank C. Havens, and through an open window in the school's auditorium I could hear the clear and truly enchanting voices of children practicing a song for an upcoming Winter Concert. I paused and listened, as the singing voices made me smile and feel hope. Immense hope, in fact, that even in the face of random acts of unthinkable violence perpetrated against the most vulnerable in our communities, our children, that to be human is to love deeply and passionately -- and we have the power to bind together and collectively upend the interests of the few who gain so much in making weapons that eviscerate the innocent:www.bradycampaign.org . Denise Bostrom Piedmont Wrong interpretations of Second Amendment Adopted on Dec. 15, 1791, the Second Amendment to the Constitution under the Bill of Rights reads as follows, "A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed." Such legislation empowered the citizens of a fledgling democracy the right to protect and defend themselves.

833.text: GUN-6259 PRIMARY Letters, Faxes & E-Mail: We're killing ourselves; GUN CONTROL In the wake of the latest gun massacres, which now seem to be a commonplace event, it's important to remember that the NRA and its stooges in Congress just succeeded in killing another attempt at devising reasonable arms controls. It begs the question: Since when has the Second Amendment come to mean unregulated domestic arms trafficking? In our gun-obsessed culture it's important to remember that even at the height of anarchy known as the "Wild West," travelers were required to check their guns with the local sheriffs before they were permitted to stay in a town. By today's arguments put forth by the NRA, I guess all those sheriffs were representatives of a centralized government bent on achieving totalitarianism, black helicopters and all. Wake up, people! The United States, the world's No. 1 arms trafficker, is killing itself with its own products. WILLIAM KALLFELZ Kallfelz, of Atlanta, is a math instructor at Georgia State University.

832.text: GUN-10589 PRIMARY Councilman: Tougher gun sales law needed An Atlanta city councilman who claims membership in the National Rifle Association is pushing for a tougher state law on where guns can be sold. Councilman Michael Julian Bond's motivation for gun legislation comes after he learned a pawnshop might move to southwest Atlanta and sell guns. The proposed pawnshop site, the councilman said, is 1,000 feet from Washington High School. North Side Loan Office, the pawnshop owner, countered that Bond's concerns are unwarranted. Stephen Dell, the company's chief financial officer, said guns wouldn't be sold to anyone under 21 by his employees. "We are a very kosher business," Dell said. "We are very selective of [whom] we sell our firearms to." The loan office's building on Edgewood Avenue was recently sold and the business needs to move elsewhere, Dell said. Council members warned there's little chance the Republican-controlled Legislature will make any gun law changes. In recent years, state lawmakers have proposed several firearms measures to no avail. "I think we all know what's going to happen," Councilman Howard Shook said. Bond remains optimistic. "I think we can appeal to their better nature," he said. State law prohibits cities and counties from adopting zoning ordinances that regulate gun shop locations or businesses that sell or purchase firearms. Bond, who said he joined the NRA because "if you want to change something, you have to get involved" and describes himself as an expert marksman, emphasized he doesn't want to restrict gun owners' rights. He just doesn't want guns getting into the wrong hands.

834.text: GUN-3467 PRIMARY As Senate Debates Crime Bill, Weapons Ban Is the Focus The Senate opened debate on the crime bill today as Democrats warned that some Republicans were still trying to eliminate the bill's ban on assault weapons. Senator Phil Gramm, a Texas Republican who opposes the ban, said he would try to stall the $30.3 billion bill procedurally so he could add what he said were tougher sentences for criminals and to cut what he said was wasteful social spending. But Senator Joseph R. Biden Jr., the Delaware Democrat who heads the Senate Judiciary Committee, called the tactic a "smokescreen" by Republicans whose real goal was to do away with the bill's ban on assault weapons. As of tonight, it was not clear which side had the votes to prevail and how the much-bruised crime bill would fare in the Senate after its resurrection in the House on Sunday. The possibility that the ban was still in peril underscored the intense pressure being exerted by the National Rifle Association and its supporters. The gun lobby suffered a major loss on Sunday when the House, on a 235-to-195 vote, approved a crime bill that included a ban on 19 kinds of assault weapons and on models with similar characteristics. But the gun lobby and its supporters in the Senate continued a last-ditch effort to weaken or eliminate the ban. They constantly threatened the fragile compromise put together over the weekend in the House, coming forward at the last minute with a new bill that did not include the provision.

835.text: GUN-7618 PRIMARY NEW GUN LICENSING HURDLES ON TABLE The NYPD has sparked the ire of gun owners with proposed changes in handgun licensing, including an $85 jump in fees and a requirement that owners show their weapons to cops when they renew. The move comes amid a drop in the number of permits issued by the NYPD. There were 3,460 issued through April 30 - down from 4,898 a year ago. The new rules will be the focus of a hearing today at Police Headquarters. The biggest change will require handgun owners to show up at a police facility with their guns for inspection once every three years. "We want to make sure that when a license is renewed you still own the guns on your license," said Assistant Commissioner Karen Pakstis of the department's License Division. Renewals are now done every two years by mail. Checking the serial numbers to verify ownership would discourage gun owners from illegally selling off weapons. The fee will rise from $170 to $255 under the new plan. The department will seek advice at the hearing on how and where to implement the inspection, Pakstis said. Critics call the inspection unnecessary and say it will force many licensees to give up their guns because of the inconvenience. "There is no evidence that licensed gun owners are unlawfully disposing of their firearms," said Robey Newsom of the New York State Rifle and Pistol Association.

characters: New York State Rifle and Pistol Association

837.text: GUN-31250 PRIMARY Cuccinelli Sticks to His Guns in Statewide Race Gun rights advocates gathered outside the Virginia state Capitol for a rally in January, some of them wearing their weapons tucked beneath their clothes. Among the featured speakers was Sen. Ken Cuccinelli II, one of the most conservative members of the state legislature and a fixture in gun rights circles. Tall and lean, dressed in a black overcoat and buffered against the wind by an indigo scarf, he noted that his opposition to efforts to "roll back our right to protect ourselves" is not shared by many of his Northern Virginia constituents. "I have a district that votes four-to-one for gun control," he told the crowd in a speech that was circulated on YouTube. "But I know defending the Second Amendment is the right thing to do. And so I've led the charge year in and year out to do just that." That incongruity has been a hallmark of Cuccinelli's seven years as a GOP lawmaker from Fairfax County, where by his own admission, conservatives of his stripe are an "endangered species." The unusual blend of politics and geography will come into sharp focus, in ways favorable and not, when he runs statewide this year as the Republican candidate for attorney general. Critics and fans of Cuccinelli, 40, say he is a shrewd and hardworking campaigner with a record of winning in enemy territory and a loyal following among home-schoolers, antiabortion activists and others in the conservative wing of the party. That base helped push Cuccinelli over the top at the recent Republican convention, where he secured the nomination from among three competitors.

830.text: GUN-5365 PRIMARY TARGET: GUNS; AS IN THE TOBACCO CASES - EXCEPT PERHAPS MORE SO - ONE NEED NOT ADMIRE THE INDUSTRY, OR MINIMIZE THE TRAGIC TOLL OF VIOLENCE, TO BE APPALLED BY THESE ANTI-DEMOCRATIC TACTICS. The fusillade of lawsuits American cities and gun control advocates are filing against firearm manufacturers is a brazen attempt to circumvent the democratic process. Whatever one's attitude toward guns, this kind of ambush policymaking should be deeply disturbing. Like the coast-to-coast tobacco lawsuits that inspired them, these actions against gun makers have been triggered by the failure of zealous social reformers to achieve what they want through representative institutions. What gun crusaders want are severe restrictions on gun ownership and distribution. Firearm opponents, of course, haven't been wholly outgunned in the political system. They've won waiting periods and background checks for handgun purchases, bans on "assault weapons" and many other gun restrictions in federal, state and local laws. But gun owners and gun makers continue to use their considerable political firepower, not to mention their constitutional rights, to fight off more sweeping bans and regulations. So gun foes, like anti-tobacco crusaders before them, have turned to the courts. They've concocted legal theories under which gun manufacturers should be held liable for all the law enforcement and health care costs caused by gun crime and misuse. The aim, as in the tobacco offensive, is to concentrate ruinous legal fire on an industry, inflicting so many legal costs and risks that the industry will "voluntarily" accept drastic new restrictions. Thus, through imaginative lawsuits, reformers again hope to impose transformations in social policy they've never been able to secure through democratic means - and which might be unconstitutional if they weren't "voluntary." Not incidentally, the plaintiffs also hope to loot the target industry, as the tobacco plaintiffs did, enriching public coffers without the inconvenience of raising taxes.

838.text: NA-42682 PRIMARY California Senator Voices Anger Over Bill's Exclusion of an Assault Weapons Ban WASHINGTON -- Senator Dianne Feinstein, the California Democrat who 35 years ago discovered the bullet-riddled body of the gay activist Harvey Milk, reacted with anger on Tuesday that gun control legislation the Senate is to consider next month will not include the reinstatement of an assault weapons ban, a measure she had fought desperately to keep. ''How many assault weapons do you need circulating?'' Ms. Feinstein said to reporters, noting that her bill, which had almost no chance of a hearing in the House, exempted many weapons. '' To have these mass killings is such a blight on everything that America stands for.'' At a Senate hearing last week, Ms. Feinstein said that she still could not get out of her mind looking for the pulse of Mr. Milk, her colleague at the time on the San Francisco Board of Supervisors, and in the process ''putting my fingers in a bullet hole.'' Senate Democrats plan to introduce after the Easter recess a bill widely supported by both parties that would increase the penalties for people who buy guns for those barred from having them, known as straw purchasing. But Senator Harry Reid of Nevada, the majority leader, told Ms. Feinstein on Monday that her assault weapons ban would not be included in the bill. ''I tried my best,'' Ms. Feinstein said with obvious disappointment. '' My best, I guess, wasn't good enough.''

839.text: NA-46183 PRIMARY Common sense on guns Terrified by an intruder who had broken into her Walton County home this month, Melinda Herman grabbed a .38-caliber pistol to protect herself and her two children. As the intruder drew closer to their attic hiding spot, Herman called upon her handgun training, practice and protective instinct and fired six shots, five of which struck her target. The barrage drove the intruder out of her home, where he was later arrested. I do love a happy ending. You can debate the statistics about how often guns kept in the home are used for good or evil. You can point out the instances in which such weapons end up in the hands of children or criminals, or in which people mistake a loved one for an intruder. Those are facts that would-be gun owners ought to weigh in their decision-making. But for Herman, access to a firearm and the training to use it worked out well. Opponents of gun-safety laws have seized upon Herman's story as some sort of counterweight to the mass murder of children in Connecticut that is now driving a reassessment of national gun laws. But the connection is tenuous at best. No serious gun law proposal in the wake of the Connecticut tragedy --- and certainly no gun law that I would support --- would try to disarm Herman or deny her the right to protect her family.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, but not directly mentioned)

840.text: GUN-3575 PRIMARY EFFORT TO BLOCK RESEARCH; NRA SHOT IS OFF TARGET Reasonable people can and do differ over a variety of gun control issues. But trying to shoot down efforts to prevent gun injuries is paranoid and ludicrous at best. That's exactly what the National Rifle Association and its political friends want to do. Last month, NRA officials said the National Center for Injury Prevention, or NCIP - part of the federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention - had no business doing research to prevent gun injuries. Their argument: Such matters are criminal, not scientific. And, they say, the CDC has an "overtly, anti-gun bias." Therefore, the NRA is supporting an amendment that would strip all NCIP funds, including those targeted to prevent injuries from traffic, falls, fires and other causes. Adding insult to injury, last week a group of 10 U.S. senators, including presidential candidate Bob Dole, supported the amendment, saying they want to get politics out of the NCIP's work. According to the politicians, the center's research into accidental murders, suicides and injuries caused by guns is evidence of its "political agenda against gun ownership." Sure, there are those who want to ban guns, just as there are folks who favor unlimited permits to carry concealed handguns. But CDC research is designed to prevent the injuries; that means examining the psychological and sociological reasons behind the violence in an attempt to break the cycle.

836.text: NA-45947 PRIMARY Outrage inflates NRA, leader says; Membership swells to 5M as gun rights group is set to elect president The board of the National Rifle Association will elect as its president today a hard-line culture warrior who has worked for decades to make the NRA a more aggressive political force. The election of James Porter -- ensured after the endorsement of outgoing President David Keene last week -- is one of many defiant signals to come out of the NRA's annual meeting in Houston over the weekend. The organization vowed to continue to fight any compromise on gun-control legislation in Congress. "Revenge is what's motivating the president's unrelenting attacks on gun owners today," Porter told the group Saturday amid news that its membership had grown to a record 5 million. "Millions of Americans are becoming first-time gun owners. The media calls it fear. That's not it. It's a sense of natural outrage that's been building for quite some time." Porter, 64, is a Birmingham, Ala., lawyer who defends gun manufacturers.   His father, Irvine Porter, was NRA president  in 1959.   Porter told grass-roots organizers that they are on the front line of a "culture war." "He seems to come out of a mold that's much closer to the base than David Keene," said Josh Horwitz of the Coalition to Stop Gun Violence. Whereas Keene was a "steady hand" for the NRA amid controversy, Porter is "a complete wild card," he said. The weekend featured speeches from politicians such as Sen. Ted Cruz, R-Texas -- who challenged Vice President Biden to a debate on gun violence -- and the NRA's executive vice president, Wayne LaPierre.

842.text: NA-40100 PRIMARY I-4 FATALITY PROVES GUN OWNERSHIP RULES LAX It is probably safe to assume that a combination of morons, liquor, guns and strip clubs will pretty well guarantee that nothing much very good will result. And that's how it was that Fred Turner Jr. wound up dead - literally being the wrong man in the wrong place at the wrong time. All murders are senseless. But the tragic shooting death of Turner, 47, June 29 along a stretch of I-4 is only trumped perhaps by the abject stupidity of his alleged killer, Jerome Hayes, who quite likely will spend the rest of his life behind bars because he had too much ammunition and precious little brains. Hayes, 48, of St. Cloud and obviously a devotee of modern dance, found himself taking in the Tampa cultural scene that fateful Saturday at the Gold Club stripper saloon on Adamo Drive. At some point a dispute broke out between Hayes and another patron of the arts. Perhaps it was a disagreement about whether the dancers were influenced more by Twyla Tharp, or maybe Bob Fosse. Alas we'll never know for sure. What we do know is unpleasantries ensued and Hayes, along with a companion were bounced from the Gold Club so that the remaining customers could enjoy the two-bit gin joint chippies' performance in G-string minor in peace. According to law enforcement, Hayes waited in the parking lot for the chap who had offended his sensibilities to leave the Gold Club in order to conclude their differences of opinion when Turner happened to exit a nearby store and get into his car for the drive back home to Orlando.

845.text: GUN-9311 PRIMARY RALLY TO OPPOSE GUN PERMIT LAW Kim Stanley, a Bloomington mother of two young children, has organized a rally at 3 p.m. Sunday to generate support to repeal the state's new handgun permit law. The rally is scheduled for the Eagan Community Center on Central Parkway -- off Interstate 35E, one block north of the intersection of Yankee Doodle and Pilot Knob roads. Stanley said the decision to hold it in Eagan is symbolic because it's where Gov. Tim Pawlenty lives. Stanley teamed up with a handful of organizations that also oppose the law, which makes it easier for people to obtain permits to carry handguns in public. Speeches from Rep. Nora Slawik, DFL-Maplewood, a public school teacher and others are planned.

841.text: GUN-916 PRIMARY Senate stuck on gun control provision The Senate headed toward a showdown on gun control Thursday night as it labored to finish a crime bill laden with tougher sentences and fewer appeals for lawbreakers. Democratic and Republican leaders worked late to find a compromise that would remove gun owners' objections to the bill - and the political risk facing undecided senators. But like the House vote in early May, the Senate clash was shaping up to be a stark choice: - Gun-control proponents stuck by their plan for a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases and a ban on 14 semiautomatic weapons. - National Rifle Association backers united behind a plan to move toward an instant telephone check system, with financial aid for states that comply and penalties for those that do not. That system would not be operable for several years. ''We do hope that we will have a clear-cut vote,'' said Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, a leading gun-control opponent. The firearms issues were the last remaining hurdles for the Senate, which has voted to expand the federal death penalty to about 50 crimes, to restrict death-row appeals and to impose mandatory sentences for gun-related violations. The pending Democratic bill also would provide more than $ 3 billion to state and local governments for crime - if the money can be found in a tight $ 1.45 trillion federal budget.

843.text: GUN-11386 PRIMARY Contempt for the Mainstream TAMPA, Fla. - The campaign platform adopted by the Republican party this week became instantly notorious for its plunge to the right, deleting all memory of moderation in previous years. The document might be even more remarkable, however, for its tone of utter defiance. No one expected the party to soften its support for gun rights, even after the Aurora shooting. But despite the national horror at the deaths of 12 people and the injuries to 58 others, Republicans deliberately added a plank to this year's platform intended to inflame the gun debate. As the Associated Press reports, the platform contains this new line: "We oppose legislation that is intended to restrict our Second Amendment rights by limiting the capacity of clips or magazines." High-capacity magazines, which allow attackers to shoot more people quickly, without reloading, were used in both in Aurora and in the Tucson shooting that injured the former congresswoman Gabby Giffords and killed six. There is no Second Amendment right to shoot without reloading, and even many supporters of the right to bear arms oppose the easy availability of big clips, which used to be illegal. The platform also supports the "stand your ground" laws that played a role in the shooting of an unarmed black teenager in Florida earlier this year. Where the 2008 platform said that citizens have the right to a gun at home for self-defense, the new one adds a line supporting "the fundamental right to self-defense wherever a law-abiding citizen has a legal right to be."

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not explicitly mentioned)

844.text: GUN-4414 PRIMARY Ardent gun rights group loudly receptive to Barr Denver ---Rep. Bob Barr (R-Ga.) was in full-throated roar Saturday in front of one of the few groups in America where his only worry may be that he is too moderate. Speaking to the 12th annual Gun Rights Policy Conference, Barr said, "This administration either doesn't understand the Constitution or it does understand it and relies on the ignorance of the American people to completely disregard it, circumvent it or violate it every single day it is in power." This is the kind of language, along with his pro-gun record in Congress, that won Barr the conference's "Gun Rights Legislator of the Year" award, which he proudly accepted in a Denver hotel ballroom. Sponsored by the Second Amendment Foundation and the Citizens Committee for the Right to Keep and Bear Arms, two groups that believe the National Rifle Association does not go far enough in its defense of gun rights, the conference brought together 400 people who oppose any restrictions on gun ownership on the grounds that they violate the Constitution and will ultimately lead to the confiscation of their guns. One man's T-shirt advised, "When they come for your guns, make sure to give them your ammo first." The shirt did not spell out how that transfer would be accomplished. The giant banner that hung behind the speaker's podium read, "Fight for It," a call to oppose any of the gun control measures in state legislatures and the Congress.

characters: Citizens Committee for the Right to Keep and Bear Arms

849.text: GUN-5870 PRIMARY N.R.A. Is Using Adversity to Its Advantage After years of drift and disarray, the National Rifle Association finds itself right where many of its leaders want it to be: under attack. The organization has found new life, members and money through mass mailings that portray gun owners and sportsmen as being under siege. Two weeks after the last funeral for a victim in the high school shootings at Littleton, Colo., the N.R.A. sent letters to its 2.6 million members, warning that President Clinton would "demand that you pay the price for the insanity of the killers." With the threat of new gun-control measures being approved next week by the House of Representatives, checks are flowing into the organization, which for decades had been one of the most influential and feared single-issue advocacy groups. Its members have hit Congressional offices with a blizzard of E-mail messages and phone calls opposing new gun restrictions. At the state level, the rifle association is pushing legislation to prohibit municipal lawsuits against the gun industry. Since the early 1990's, the N.R.A. has been struggling with a heavy debt, rebellions within its ranks and declining membership and revenues. Its image was battered after the Oklahoma City bombing, when the group was accused of being a bastion for right-wing extremists, and again after a recent series of school shootings that have helped transform the gun-control debate into a children's safety issue. Its vaunted ability to win elections and oust enemies came into question when several of its closest Congressional allies lost races last year. And in April, the N.R.A. failed to persuade Missouri voters to approve a pro-gun ballot referendum, despite spending $3.7 million.

851.text: GUN-320 PRIMARY Topics; WITHDRAWAL SYMPTOMS; GUN CONTROL Ever since John Lennon died nearby from a gunshot wound, the New York Road Runners Club has used the blast of an air horn instead of a blank pistol to start its races in Central Park. But the horn is not a wholly satisfactory solution. It's imprecise - fine for a long race but not sharp enough for shorter events. Racing managers continue to experiment with gongs, chimes and flashing lights. Is the search for an alternative to the starter's pistol worth the effort? It seems an empty symbol, especially since the club will continue to rely on a cannon to dispatch the mammoth New York Marathon. Yet the members welcome the search with surprising enthusiasm. And as we've observed before, the proliferation of handguns in the next several years will depend more on fashion than law. So we'll take our fashions where we find them.

847.text: GUN-3187 PRIMARY Brady law's scorecard: 1 year later President Clinton says the year-old Brady law has stopped stalkers, felons, drug users and fugitives from acquiring handguns. Clinton today commemorates the law's anniversary and releases a federal study - obtained by USA TODAY - reviewing the law's effectiveness. The Treasury Department sampled 441,545 handgun applications and found 15,506 were denied - including those of 4,365 convicted felons, 945 fugitives, 97 people under indictment and 649 drug users. "The American people are safer" as a result of the law requiring up to a five-day wait for background checks, Clinton said in an interview Monday. "We did the right thing." But as the president celebrates in Washington, the law's effect on crime is still being debated across the nation. "It's a law that's broken," says Tanya Metaksa of the National Rifle Association. "It's broken in the whole concept that it's going to get the bad guys off the streets." "It was a feel-good law" that doesn't reduce crime, says gun expert Peter Alan Kasler. "It doesn't help get guns out of the wrong people's hands." Alfred Blumstein of the Heinz School of Public Policy and Management in Pittsburgh says the law "was important as a symbolic act more so than for whatever effectiveness it would have in limiting access to guns to undesirables." Critics see it as a symbol of an overreaching government. -- Sheriffs or citizens have filed 10 lawsuits in nine states challenging the constitutionality of the law.

848.text: GUN-312 PRIMARY WHITE HOUSE PLANNING TO KILL FIREARMS ENFORCEMENT UNIT The Reagan Administration has decided to eliminate the Treasury Department's Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms and shift its functions to other Federal law-enforcement agencies, Administration officials said today. The move was explained by the Administration as a budget-cutting measure. Advocates of gun control consider it to be a major victory for the National Rifle Association, which has been conducting a nationwide television campaign portraying the agency as ''jackbooted fascists'' who harass gun owners for technical law violations. Treasury officials said no decision had been made about the bureau's future. They said, however, that if they chose to eliminate it they could do so without consulting Congress. Other Administration officials confirmed that the agency would be abolished in the new round of budget cuts. Also today, the Administration was reported to be planning to request elimination of the Consumer Product Safety Commission. But a high-ranking Administration official said tonight that the idea had been dropped because enough money was saved when Congress cut the commission's budget in the fiscal year 1982 by 26 percent, from $44.6 million to $33 million. At the Firearms Bureau, one bitter employee said, ''They're wiping us out.'' And a secretary said, ''The N.R.A. has finally won.'' G. R. Dickerson, director of the bureau, which deals with those who violate firearms laws, smuggle cigarettes and produce illicit alcohol, left his office early today. He left instructions that he would be unavailable for comment, his office said.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

846.text: GUN-1382 PRIMARY POLITICAL NOTES; New Jersey Gun-Ban War Resumes Ending a late-summer cease-fire during which the fight over the attempt to repeal New Jersey's tough gun law briefly cooled, both sides in the debate returned to their fronts last week with new battle tactics. Gov. Jim Florio fired the first salvo on Wednesday when the Democratic State Committee chairman, Raymond J. Lesniak, announced a cable television advertising campaign supporting the Governor's veto of a Republican-sponsored bill that would repeal a state ban against the ownership and the sale of semiautomatic assault-style weapons. The fight escalated the following day when a platoon of police officials and beat patrol officers from across New Jersey paraded to Trenton to demonstrate their opposition to the state's semiautomatic-weapons ban. A three-hour rally held on the steps of the State House was organized by the Law Enforcement Alliance of America, a Virginia-based group of police officers supported by the National Rifle Association. Within an hour of that demonstration, a group calling itself New Jersey Citizens to Stop Gun Violence brought in its own contingent of supporters, including law-enforcement officers, to rally behind Mr. Florio. Meanwhile, the majority leaders in the Republican-controlled Legislature have put off any attempt at overriding Mr. Florio's veto because too many Republicans have defected on the issue. Will He, or Won't He? The status of Gov. Lowell P. Weicker Jr. of Connecticut as a third-party governor makes his endorsement in this year's Presidential race something less than the foregone conclusion it might be in other state capitols. And Mr. Weicker has milked it for all it is worth.

characters: New Jersey Citizens to Stop Gun Violence

854.text: GUN-7164 PRIMARY The Tyranny of Arms To the Editor: A March 27 letter writer rejects Richard Ford's call for reasonable gun control (Op-Ed, March 21) by asserting that "the Second Amendment was specifically intended as a hedge against tyrannical government." Any tyranny in the United States will be the result of the wishes of, if not a majority, at least a significant part of the American electorate. Thus, the handguns and assault rifles of citizens, if put to use as this hedge, would simply be a prescription for anarchy, the upshot of which would likely be to strengthen the hand of an abusive government. There is no bullet that will eliminate the possibility of domestic tyranny. Regulation of deadly weapons remains the province of government. SIMON TURKEL New York, March 27, 2000 http://www.nytimes.com

852.text: NA-43612 PRIMARY Prosecutor to look into TV incident The decision on whether anyone should be prosecuted after "Meet the Press" host David Gregory appeared to hold a high-capacity ammunition magazine on national television now belongs to the District's Office of the Attorney General, authorities said Tuesday. In an e-mail, a spokeswoman for D.C. Police Chief Cathy L. Lanier said her department has "completed the investigation into this matter, and the case has been presented to the OAG for a determination of the prosecutorial merit of the case." Possessing a magazine capable of holding 10 or more rounds of ammunition, even if empty, is a misdemeanor punishable by up to one year in jail and a $1,000 fine. The question of how to proceed now rests with the office of Attorney General Irvin B. Nathan, which oversees prosecutions of some low-level offenses. A spokesman for Nathan said the office would not comment until a decision was made. Gregory's televised actions mobilized gun rights advocates and others nationwide; thousands have signed a White House petition urging the NBC host's arrest. Many gun rights advocates pressed police to immediately arrest Gregory after he wielded the prop during a Dec. 23 interview with National Rifle Association chief executive Wayne LaPierre about the massacre in Newtown, Conn. Those advocates argued that not charging Gregory would show D.C. police to be hypocritical in enforcing gun laws its chief believes are necessary to curtailing violence.

855.text: GUN-5539 PRIMARY MANY FAMILIES WITH CHILDREN KEEP GUNS IN HOME, SURVEY FINDS * Gun-control group says findings should make parents vigilant about firearms safety. More than 40 percent of American households with children have guns - a figure that should make parents vigilant not only about guns in their own homes but in the homes of others, a gun-control group said Friday. The survey by the Center to Prevent Handgun Violence found that 43 percent of homes polled have both guns and children, and 23 percent of those keep the gun loaded. The survey found that 28 percent of the gun owners with children keep the gun hidden but not locked up. Still, 73 percent of parents think it is unlikely that their children would find or handle a gun without their permission, the survey showed. "Only when parents are as conscientious about guns in the home as they are about lesser dangers can we protect our children from these terrible and unnecessary tragedies," said Robert Walker, the center's president. Parents need to pay more attention to the possibility of guns in the homes their children visit, the group said. Sarah Brady, whose husband, James Brady, was seriously wounded in the assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan in March 1981, leads the group. The survey of 806 parents was conducted by Peter Hart Research Associates between Oct. 31 and Nov. 4. It has a margin of error of plus or minus 4 percentage points.

850.text: GUN-4907 PRIMARY The Right to Bear Tragedy Even the heartbroken mother of an 11-year-old who was shot in her Jonesboro schoolyard wasn't saying guns should be banned. Suzann Wilson, who kissed her daughter Britthney, goodbye on March 24 and never saw her alive again, said she did not care how many guns people have -- she just wants guns locked up. The president of the U.S. was standing beside her and he was of the same mind. "Guns are kept in the home for many purposes -- hunting or self-defense," he said, in case anyone would think he was proposing any radical domestic arms control measures. On the stage at the Executive Office Building were three Cabinet members, two senators, one congressman, the lieutenant governor of Maryland and a large contingent of Maryland state troopers, all of whom have apparently accepted the idea that nothing can be done about adults and their guns -- there are 192 million firearms in private hands in this country -- and are concentrating on keeping children and guns apart. The solemn meeting was convened around a stupendously modest new gun control initiative, a proposal by Sens. John Chafee (R-R.I.) and Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) to impose criminal penalties on an adult whose gun is used by a child to harm another person if the gun was not safely stored or did not have a safety lock. The bill is called the Child Access Prevention Act, and is also sponsored by Sen. Herb Kohl (D-Wis.).

860.text: GUN-7825 PRIMARY THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE VICE PRESIDENT; Gore Accuses Bush of Allowing Gun Lobby to Influence Policy Plans Vice President Al Gore continued his intensive assault against Gov. George W. Bush's policies today, saying his Republican rival had been inordinately influenced by the National Rifle Association and had proposed an inadequate health care plan that "offers too little for too few at the expense of too many." But when asked later by reporters about Mr. Bush's comments that Mr. Gore's attacks were having a corrosive influence on the political process, Mr. Gore refused to respond directly, saying the nation deserved a vigorous debate on the issues. Mr. Gore had intended to deliver a health care speech here, since he was addressing the inaugural meeting of the Association of Health Care Journalists, at Northwestern University Memorial Hospital. But his aides said he had been captivated by reports in the morning newspapers that suggested that the gun lobby was thrilled at the prospect of a Bush presidency, and Mr. Gore rewrote his speech as he flew here this morning from Washington to draw attention to those reports. The New York Times reported that gun manufacturers had broken off settlement talks with 31 cities and counties in hopes that a Bush administration would be more sympathetic than the Clinton administration has been. And The Washington Post reported that Kayne Robinson, first vice president of the N.R.A., had said in a speech to members in February that if Mr. Bush was elected, the gun lobby would expect to have "a president where we work out of their office."

859.text: GUN-7645 PRIMARY N.R.A. Planning Restaurant And Arcade in Times Square The National Rifle Association plans to open a theme restaurant and a game arcade in Times Square, a move that has outraged gun-control advocates and astonished redevelopment officials. At its national convention yesterday in Charlotte, N.C., the N.R.A. said it was negotiating to open a restaurant called NRASports Grille, featuring "a wild game menu and fresh mineral waters from around the world." The group said it also planned an adjacent electronic-game arcade and gift store called NRASports Blast, which it described as "an exciting total shooting sports and sporting goods experience." William Powers, the N.R.A.'s director of public affairs, was not specific about the plans for the Times Square project in a telephone interview. But he compared it to ventures by the World Wrestling Federation, which has a Times Square restaurant and gift store, and Planet Hollywood, the pop-culture theme restaurant on West 57th Street. Mr. Powers said the gift store would not sell guns. The N.R.A.'s plans quickly drew criticism. "This is ludicrous," said Kimberly Mariani, a spokeswoman for Handgun Control, a lobbying group in Washington. "The very New Yorkers who have worked to reduce their crime rate don't want the N.R.A. coming into Times Square and glorifying the gun culture. Now, not only their policies will leave a bad taste in your mouth, but their food as well." Brendan Sexton, president of the Times Square Business Improvement District, which provides business consulting, security and other services to businesses in Times Square, said he was surprised by the N.R.A.'s plans, which were first reported yesterday by The Daily News, and expressed mixed feelings.

857.text: GUN-4036 PRIMARY Clinton: Take handguns from domestic abusers President Clinton on Monday called on Congress to expand gun control laws so that anyone convicted of a domestic violence crime would be unable to lawfully buy a gun. Beginning the second day of his train trip through the Ohio valley, Clinton stood with crisply uniformed police officers in Columbus to endorse an add-on to the Brady Act that Congress passed in 1993. "I believe strongly in the right of Americans to own guns," Clinton told  a morning audience gathered under cloudless summer skies. "But make no  mistake, those who threaten the safety of others do not deserve our trust. "And if you commit an act of violence against your spouse or your child,  you shouldn't have a gun." The Brady Act has prevented more than 60,000 felons, fugitives and stalkers from buying handguns. It requires a five-day waiting period for gun purchases so the buyers' backgrounds can be checked. Clinton's proposal, which has stalled in Congress this year, is aimed at  the domestic violence cases that start out as felonies but are pleaded down in  the courts to misdemeanor convictions. Those defendants aren't covered by the  Brady Act ban and can still possess a firearm. The Clinton administration figures there are four times as many misdemeanor domestic violence cases as there are felony cases. In 1994, aides said, there were 88,500 incidents of domestic violence involving a firearm.

856.text: GUN-5681 PRIMARY A&E Explores 'Guns in America' The gun culture in the United States is examined and the firearms debate is taken to the street level, with views from police officers, trauma doctors, victims and their families rounding out a week-long A&E Network "Investigative Reports" focusing on "Guns in America," airing Monday through Friday at 9 p.m. "Investigative Reports," with anchor and executive producer Bill Kurtis, has won many laurels including a Peabody Award and Emmy. A&E has expanded the show, which now will air weeknights following the popular "Biography." The five-part "Guns in America" series launches the show's new time slot. The installments: Monday: "Gun Life," a look at guns across America and who uses them. Tuesday: "Inside the NRA," a look at how the pro-gun lobby of the National Rifle Association works. Wednesday: "The Gun Effect," a talk with people in the street. Thursday: "The Gun Makers," how and where the weapons are made and sold. Friday: "Young Guns," showing how children learn early to shoot and kill. Each part repeats at 1 a.m. Sunday on Showtime at 9 p.m., "Mean Streak," with Scott Bakula as a New York City cop and Leon as an FBI agent who track a white supremacist intent on killing a black baseball player before he breaks Joe DiMaggio's record 56-game hitting streak. Repeats: July 7, 12 and 15. Sunday on A&E Network at 9 p.m., "The Bunny Years," a two-hour profile of women who worked as bunnies during the 25-year history of Playboy Clubs. The program points out a number of bunnies' experiences, including those of feminist Gloria Steinem, model Lauren Hutton and a midwife who also was a beauty queen and nun. Repeats at 1 a.m.

862.text: GUN-2733 PRIMARY Brady law is working, in spite of gun lobby The good news is that since March 1, more than 23,000 felons have been prevented from buying handguns, thanks to the background checks required by the federally enacted Brady law. The bad news is that a lower-court judge in Montana this week overturned this simple, superb control measure. And the dismaying news is that on the same day, the Justice Department reported that handgun-related violent crime in 1992 hit an all-time high of nearly 1 million incidents. Nearly 30% of all felons get their firearms from retail outlets. Trying to stop those sales ought to make sense to even the most vociferous gun enthusiast. Yet the National Rifle Association, which says it supports background checks, is actually helping local officials challenge them in at least five states, including misguided Montana. To this end, the NRA argues that Congress has no right to order local law enforcement agencies to do federal work - in this case, background checks on gun sales. That's desperate and petty. Local agencies already perform some federal work. And beyond that, arguing technicalities while the carnage mounts does the nation a profound disservice. By opposing background checks, the NRA is guilty of unconscionable and blood-spattered hypocrisy. Americans are dying at war-zone rates thanks to the ongoing obstructions of the gun lobbyists. For every additional month they prevail, thousands more of us are imperiled and slain.

863.text: GUN-8396 PRIMARY Police: Caution key in using air gun law Alpharetta police expect to use a healthy dose of discretion in enforcing the city's controversial new air gun law. "We really don't want to influence the good kids that are using these," police spokesman Chris Lagerbloom said. "We want to be able to cease improper activity before actual damage occurs." Tuesday was the first day under the new ordinance, which makes it illegal for anyone under 18 to use BB or paintball guns without adult supervision. The law went into effect immediately after its approval Monday, but as of Tuesday afternoon, police had not had reason to use it, Lagerbloom said. When they do, charges likely would be made against youngsters in cases involving only injury, harassment or vandalism. Other kids will be sent home, temporarily stripped of their air gun or asked to put it up until a parent gets home, he said. Despite such assurances written into the law to protect legitimate use of air guns, critics still worry it will be misused. Councilman Doug DeRito said the law erodes parental rights, discriminates against youth and opens the city to possible constitutional challenges. The law was proposed after complaints of stray BBs and paintballs in the city's Windward community. It requires an adult to supervise the use of air guns, and subjects parents to fines up to $1,000 if they allow their children unsupervised use of the guns. City officials say that does not mean they must maintain constant visual contact.

858.text: NA-44658 PRIMARY Last American jurisdiction with a total handgun ban? The Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands is part of U.S. sovereign territory. It also (1) totally bans handguns, (2) bans carrying all guns, even in the home, (3) requires licenses for long guns, which are allegedly not granted for months on end, though theoretically they are supposed to be granted within 60 days, and (4) bans all ammunition except .22 cartridges, .223 cartridges, and .410 shotgun shells. Radich v. Guerrero (D.N.M.I. Sept. 5, 2014) argues this violates the Second Amendment, and surely that must be so; and before you go all Insular Cases on me, note that Â§ 501 of the CNMI Covenant expressly provides (emphasis added), To the extent that they are not applicable of their own force, the following provisions of the Constitution of the United States will be applicable within the Northern Mariana Islands as if the Northern Mariana Islands were one of the several States: Article I, Section 9, Clauses 2, 3, and 8; Article I, Section 10, Clauses 1 and 3; Article IV, Section 1 and Section 2, Clauses 1 and 2; Amendments 1 through 9, inclusive; Amendment 13; Amendment 14, Section 1; Amendment 15; Amendment 19; and Amendment 26; provided, however, that neither trial by jury nor indictment by grand jury shall be required in any civil action or criminal prosecution based on local law, except where required by local law.

861.text: GUN-10172 PRIMARY Friends, family recall music icon slain a year ago; The killing of John Whitehead, a top songwriter of the 1960s and '70s, remains unsolved. About 50 people crowded the sidewalk of the 1900 block of Dallas Road yesterday to celebrate the life of Philadelphia music legend John Whitehead, who was gunned down one year ago outside his West Oak Lane home in a slaying that remains unsolved. "Sometimes in my mind, it doesn't seem appropriate to use that word" celebrate, Elnor Whitehead said. "What happened to my husband was a tragedy, and we're here to turn a tragedy into a positive thing." Family and friends wore black T-shirts with a Whitehead's picture on the front. They listened to recordings of "Ain't No Stoppin' Us Now" and "Back Stabbers." And they heard Bilal Qayyum of Men United for a Better Philadelphia urge them to vote yes on a citywide question on Tuesday's ballot on whether Philadelphia should be able to make its own gun laws. (Currently, all such laws must be made at the state level, but city officials say that Philadelphia's gun crimes call for unique solutions. The question is nonbinding, meaning that before the city is in a position to draft its own gun laws, corresponding legislation would have to be approved in the state legislature.) Whitehead, who with Gene McFadden wrote a number of hits and in 1979 recorded the iconic hit "Ain't No Stoppin' Us Now," was killed May 11, 2004. He and his godson, Ocmidd Johnson, were working on an Isuzu in the rear of his home when at least one gunman opened fire, striking Whitehead and Johnson. Whitehead died at the scene of a neck wound.

characters: Men United for a Better Philadelphia

864.text: GUN-7136 PRIMARY SAFE seeks Clinton visit to support gun petition In an effort to boost support for its gun control initiative,  SAFE Colorado has invited President Clinton to Colorado the week  before the anniversary of the Columbine shootings. The group hopes that Clinton will support its  petition-proposed ballot measure to require criminal background  checks on sales at gun shows. SAFE has not received an answer from The White House, but one  could come as early as today. John Podesta, Clinton's chief of staff, will be on CBS' 'Face  the Nation' this morning at 9:30 on KCNC-Channel 4. The subject listed for the program is the Elian Gonzalez  case, but SAFE members hope Podesta will address their invitation  on the air. 'I would like to see an announcement that the president is  coming to endorse the initiative that we're proposing,' said John  Head, co-president of SAFE, which stands for Sane Alternatives to  the Firearms Epidemic. The group is a bipartisan organization formed in response to  the massacre at Columbine High School in Jefferson County. SAFE is  finalizing petition wording. The group will need more than 62,000 valid signatures to put  the measure up for a vote. If Clinton comes, SAFE will likely hold  a rally or event that would draw people from all sides of the  political spectrum, Head said. And if he says no? 'We'll proceed,' Head said. ' He's important, but not  critical, to our effort. We'll be disappointed, but we'll continue  with our plans.'

867.text: GUN-796 PRIMARY BAN ASSAULT RIFLES: ALL GUNS ARE NOT EQUAL WASHINGTON President Bush, balancing when he should be choosing, says he is seeking ''an accommodation between the police and the sporting interest'' on assault rifles. So much for the interest of ghettos, where such guns are often fired.   The poor are invisible while their betters plan their betterment. But the poor have a special interest in firearms policy. They would gain from a ban on assault rifles and suffer from handgun control.   The reasons for acting against the former are reasons for leaving the latter alone. Assault weapons are not yet prevalent and never will be needed for legitimate civilian use. Handguns are widely dispersed and necessary for some people. Criminologist Don Kates Jr. notes that New York's pioneering Sullivan law in 1911 was an example of gun control advocated by conservatives who associated handguns with foreign-born anarchists, labor agitators and criminals. Today, too, the gun-control argument has a class bias.   Literature from the National Coalition to Ban Handguns includes this gem: Q. Does the banning of handguns discriminate against minority members of society?   A. No. Handguns would be illegal in the hands of the total populace . . .   rich and poor alike.   Wonderful. It would impartially disarm white, safe Park Avenue and the South Bronx, a war zone.   Kates makes a disturbing but not unsupported argument as follows: Many urban Americans live where government cannot, or will not, enforce its proper monopoly on the use of force. Given this failure, or abdication, by government, it is unseemly for the safe majority to deny the endangered minority the handguns needed for self-help. Kates cites data that indicate that ''handgun-armed citizens actually thwart almost as many crimes annually as handgun-armed criminals succeed in committing.'' He says prison surveys show that criminals fear armed victims more than police and that fear of armed civilians deters criminals into non-confrontational crime. Burglars rarely encounter armed victims because burglars target unoccupied premises. Even so, more burglars meet armed resistance than are sent to prison.   About 50 million households have handguns. Prohibition of handguns would be a bigger failure than Prohibition. '' Handguns, unlike liquor,'' notes Kates, ''are reusable, and their continued use does not involve the visibility of perpetual illegal purchase.'' Assault rifles are different. Daryl Gates, Los Angeles police chief, says: ''A reasonable right to bear arms does not mandate that weapons . . . built for the express purpose of killing human beings on battlefields be made available to the . . . public.'' But assault rifles have enthusiasts. Hence the administration's position is: Assault weapons are misused by drug dealers, so let's not ban the guns; let's win ''the war'' on drugs.   Meanwhile, back on the non-metaphysical battlefield where many poor people live, the poor must be bemused by Bush's idea that his temporary ban on the importation of some rifles is a ''cooling-off'' period. It is not cooling their streets.   No wonder the National Rifle Association is so pleased by Bush's policy.   The policy is to cool off public opinion until some fresh menace appears, a menace that threatens substantial interests, like the serenity of the comfortable.

865.text: GUN-20175 PRIMARY Clinton: GOP bill 'ghostwritten by NRA' WASHINGTON -- President Clinton sharply chastised House Republican leaders Wednesday for what he described as back-door attempts to water down Senate-approved gun restrictions. Before a friendly audience of law enforcement officials and civil rights advocates, Clinton said House leaders were offering "a bill plainly ghostwritten by the NRA." But Rep. Henry Hyde, the primary author of the House bill and chairman of the Judiciary Committee, dismissed as "nonsense" the allegation that the NRA wrote the bill. The Illinois Republican said most of the measures he is proposing are identical to those passed by the Senate. He said he also has added two the Senate did not: criminal penalties for parents whose children misuse their guns and raising the age for handgun purchases from 18 to 21. Clinton and congressional Democrats focused their attacks on Hyde's proposal for mandatory background checks on all buyers at gun shows. They said it is weaker than the final Senate version, which the NRA has been lobbying against. Hyde's version defines gun shows as gatherings of at least 10 vendors. The Senate bill would require background checks at any event at which at least 50 guns are sold. Hyde's proposal would require that checks be completed within 72 hours. The Senate bill allows three business days, which could take longer because most gun shows occur on weekends. The House plans to vote on the Hyde proposal, which will be offered as an amendment to a juvenile justice bill, as early as Tuesday.

869.text: NA-45929 PRIMARY First lady, in a personal way, wades into gun debate Like  her predecessors, first lady Michelle Obama has tried not to get tangled up in contentious political issues. Until Wednesday. As she traveled to Chicago to take a stand against gun violence, she did it in a personal -- and emotional -- way, nearly coming to tears as she lamented the scores of youths gunned down in the streets of her hometown in recent months. She compared herself and her upbringing on Chicago's South Side to that of Hadiya Pendleton, 15, an honor student who was shot to death in a city park  a week after performing as a majorette in President Obama's inaugural parade. "Hadiya Pendleton was me, and I was her," Obama said, her voice  breaking, "but I got to grow up and go to Princeton and Harvard Law School and have a career and a family and the most blessed life I could ever imagine." Obama, in Chicago for a conference on youth violence, said  city leaders have a "moral obligation" to protect youths from gun violence. She also took a stand on new gun controls her husband has proposed in response to the Newtown, Conn., school massacre. "These reforms deserve a vote," she said. Previously, Obama had largely confined her public activities to fighting childhood obesity and supporting military families.

868.text: GUN-10464 PRIMARY Mr. CalderÃ3n doesn't need America's guns; Mexico's president could use Mr. Obama's help on assault weapons trafficking. MEXICAN PRESIDENT Felipe CalderÃ3n has shown courage in leading his government to take on the violent drug gangs that have claimed thousands of Mexican lives. On Thursday, he displayed a different kind of fortitude: standing before a joint meeting of Congress and asking for a revival of the U.S. assault weapons ban. The Obama administration, which has been largely absent in the fight against the illegal gun trade, should have such backbone. Mr. CalderÃ3n, who has been in Washington for a state visit, made a powerful case. Over the past three years, Mexican authorities have seized some 75,000 weapons used in crimes; more than 80 percent of those they were able to trace came from the United States. Mr. CalderÃ3n argued that the surge in violent, cartel-related crimes coincided with the 2004 repeal of the U.S. assault weapons ban. "I fully respect, I admire the American Constitution," Mr. CalderÃ3n said. "And I understand that the purpose of the Second Amendment is to guarantee good American citizens the ability to defend themselves and their nation. But believe me, many of these guns are not going to honest American hands." Mr. CalderÃ3n is right that the challenge to Mexico's security has "roots on both sides of the border." The Obama administration's newly unveiled national drug policy acknowledges as much and wisely emphasizes drug abuse prevention and rehabilitation to reduce the demand for illicit drugs. The White House also deserves credit for pushing forcefully to assist Mexican crime-fighting efforts. But it has been negligent in the battle against illegal guns, caving to the gun lobby on such issues as an assault weapons ban and a move to close a gun show loophole that allows some purchases without a background check.

871.text: GUN-2337 PRIMARY Let this be the year for true gun control Peace on Earth? For the new year, I have a wish less grand: Peace in our neighborhoods.
