Here's hoping that the American public will demand stricter gun regulations to quell the epidemic of violence brought on by our love affair with firearms. The ugliness of the past year has finally forced us to face the consequences of our romance with guns. The headlines are a blur of horror and heartache, but a few episodes still stand out. There was yet another killing at the post office. German tourists were issued warnings about the dangers of Miami. A madman opened fire in a California office building. Another madman opened fire on a Long Island commuter train. In between, of course, was the "routine" mayhem: drive-by shootings, children killed in crossfire, adolescent gunfights in school cafeterias. Schools installed weapons detectors and hired counselors to teach children to deal with their grief over dead schoolmates. In Washington, D.C., 11-year-old Jessica Bradford, convinced she will not liv e to adulthood in her violent neighborhood, discussed with reporters her plans for her own funeral. She told The Washington Post she wanted to be buried in her sixth-grade prom dress. Opinion surveys show that Americans already favor more stringent regulations governing the sale and use of firearms. A new USA Today- Gallup poll says that 67 percent of people favor stricter laws controlling the sale of firearms.

866.text: GUN-7577 PRIMARY Gun-control group rewards political candidates Bipartisan project gives checks to campaigns A bipartisan political action committee dedicated to  supporting candidates who support gun controls handed out its  first two checks at a kickoff rally Thursday. State Rep. Sue Windels, an Arvada Democrat, and Heidi Frey,  an Arapahoe County Republican, each got $ 1,000 from the newly  formed Colorado Gun Violence Accountability Project. 'We probably have given away half our treasury today,' said  Eileen McCarron, who chairs the organization. But Colorado GAP - it treats 'gun violence' as one word to  make the acronym - intends to raise more money to support other  candidates based on their positions on gun control, she said. Frey is challenging incumbent Rep. Lauri Clapp, R-Englewood,  in a Republican Party primary for the House District 37  nomination. Windels is hoping to unseat Sen. Jim Congrove,  R-Arvada, in the November general election. Clapp and Congrove are  strong gun-rights supporters. McCarron, who identified herself as a Democrat, said the  group filed its incorporation papers this week, spurred by the  Colorado legislature's failure to pass Republican Gov. Bill Owens'  package of gun controls. 'I'm sick and tired of the tyranny of the pro-gun activists,'  added Lisa Astrella, a Republican member of the group's steering  committee. The group's e-mail address is coloradogap@usa.net.

870.text: GUN-9344 PRIMARY GUN APPLICANTS PACK IRON WILLS THEY CITE PROTECTION, RIGHTS AS OTHERS POST SIGNS BANNING GUNS On the first day of Minnesota's new handgun permit law, some people said they needed guns for safety as others -- also citing safety -- put up signs banning guns. The permit applicants were mostly men, but from many walks of life -- a young military man, a retired railroad worker, a longtime social worker, an electrical engineer, a truck driver, a Northwest Airlines mechanic, a retired correctional officer. At 6-foot-4 and 300 pounds, Bruce Kraft was among the larger applicants Wednesday at the Ramsey County sheriff's office in downtown St. Paul. Kraft said he knew what some people might think when they saw him. "They're asking, 'Why does he need a gun to protect himself?' " said Kraft, 42, of Maplewood. "But I had a heart attack a few years ago, and I can't move as fast or as far as I could when I was 20 years old. Am I going to go looking for things? For problems? Hell, no. But when the eggs hit the fan, it's a lot better to do something besides standing there and curse at someone." Nobody got a gun permit Wednesday -- people only picked up applications from their local sheriff's office. The permits will come later, after background checks are completed and once fees are paid. Over the next three years, the number of permits in the state is expected to increase from 12,000 to about 90,000, according to legislative researchers and law enforcement officials.

872.text: GUN-6299 PRIMARY Hecklers mar ceremony for shooting heroes Hecklers disrupted the harmony of a ''Unity Rally'' held to honor the people who saved lives and provided comfort during last week's shootings at a Jewish community center. Gov. Gray Davis vowed Sunday to get assault weapons off the streets. Two men immediately shouted and booed the Democrat, who last month signed laws restricting gun purchases to one per month and tightening a ban on assault weapons. ''The Second Amendment is just as important,'' yelled Irv Rubin of the Jewish Defense League, referring to the right to bear arms. Last weekend Rubin and thousands of others successfully blocked a parade in the nation's capital by white supremacists, including Aryan Nations members. The men handed out fliers, which one woman tore up and threw at Rubin's feet. ''Don't leave yourself unarmed against those who want to murder you,'' the flier read. ''Gun control laws are only observed by decent people, and haters aren't decent.'' Another woman stood inches from the second man, who identified himself only as Louis, and said, ''We don't need you here,'' to which he replied, ''I don't need you anywhere.'' Davis responded by saying, as a Vietnam War veteran, he knows the destructive power of assault weapons. ''They have a place in a theater of war, but they have no place in a civilized society,'' he said. Davis, Attorney General Janet Reno and religious leaders spoke at the rally of about 500 people at California State University, Northridge, just a few miles from the site where Buford Furrow Jr. allegedly shot five people at a Jewish community center last Tuesday, then fatally shot a postal worker.

873.text: GUN-8284 PRIMARY GORE NEEDS TO UNHOLSTER GUN ISSUE The National Rifle Association has announced that it will run ads blasting the Democratic Party during the upcoming convention in Los Angeles. Al Gore's campaign aides must be thinking, Oh, no! Please7 don't throw us into that briar patch! So far, Vice President Gore has gotten no traction from an issue that should be one of the most hotly contested in this election: gun laws. The issue favors the Gore-Lieberman ticket. Most Americans support the Democrats' position, which calls for more stringent regulation of firearms. That is especially true of women. On Mother's Day, an estimated 750,000 women showed up at the Million Mom March in Washington to call for stronger gun laws. And polls turn up a decided gender gap on the issue. While 62 percent of men favor tighter handgun restrictions, according to a New York Times/CBS News poll, 78 percent of women do. The Texas-Wyoming ticket sneers at the concerns of those women. As governor of Texas, George W. Bush supported concealed-carry laws which allow Texans to carry handguns hidden in holsters (or purses) practically anywhere: in the mall, into a movie theater, even into church. And, during his decade in Congress, Dick Cheney managed a record on gun control somewhere to the right of Genghis Khan. He voted against a ban on armor-piercing bullets, called "cop-killers" because they pierce Kevlar vests; he also voted against a ban on plastic weaponry that would evade airport metal detectors. Even the NRA knew better than to go to bat for the plastic guns that are a weapon of choice among terrorists.

877.text: NA-40572 PRIMARY On tour, Giffords' actions speak Wounded former congresswoman travels country as a face for gun control. DOVER, N.H. o Thirty months after she was shot through the head, former Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, D-Ariz., sits in a New Hampshire restaurant facing parents of children killed in the nation's latest school shooting. They are here to talk political strategy, but Giffords doesn't say much. She doesn't have to. Giffords, 43, has become the face of the fight for gun control - a woman now known as much for her actions as her words as she recovers from a 2011 attack that forever changed her life and ended six others. Giffords traveled more than 8,000 miles last week, her husband, retired astronaut Mark Kelly, at her side, encouraging political leaders from Alaska to Maine to have the courage to defy the National Rifle Association. "I don't think any of us thought this was going to be easy," Kelly tells three parents of children killed in the Newton, Conn., school shootings, with Giffords next to him, nodding her agreement. "This is not going to be a quick fix. But we're trying." The couple were in the midst of a seven-states-in-seven-day tour across America, meeting with allies and opponents alike to generate momentum for federal legislation that would expand background checks on gun purchases. It's a scaled-back version of a broad legislative package to ban assault weapons and high-capacity magazines proposed in the aftermath of the Newtown shooting rampage that left 20 children dead. It was defeated in the Senate in April and has stalled in a divided Congress now preparing for its summer recess.

875.text: GUN-8198 PRIMARY Both Sides Slam Allen On Gun Ban Reversal; Move Questioned, Condemned Political friends and foes alike jumped on U.S. Senate candidate George Allen (R) today for abandoning his opposition to a federal assault weapons ban in the heat of the campaign. "It's a very bad strategic mistake," said Paul Moog, of Alexandria, president of the Virginia Citizens Defense League, a gun rights organization. "That kind of election-year conversion is insulting," added Mo Elleithee, campaign spokesman for Democratic U.S. Sen. Charles S. Robb, Allen's opponent. Allen, who has enjoyed the strong support of the National Rifle Association and related groups over the years, opposed restrictions on assault weapons until Wednesday, when he announced he would vote to extend the existing federal ban on those firearms when it expires in 2004. Allen's announcement followed a Robb campaign stop in Arlington, where the Democrat reiterated his strong support for the ban in a key battleground area that polls suggest favors such restrictions. In the past, Allen had criticized an assault weapons ban as impractical. As recently as May, Allen called the current law banning 19 specific assault weapons "toothless" legislation. Tim Murtaugh, Allen's campaign press secretary, said the GOP candidate made no flip-flop. Instead, Allen's announcement was consistent with the Republican's view that existing gun laws should be enforced--and was also his pragmatic response to a federal statute that is already on the books. "You can say one thing, but once you see [the law] in action, you can see that it's not doing any direct harm," Murtaugh said.

characters: Democratic U.S. Sen. Charles S. Robb

879.text: GUN-30333 PRIMARY BLOOMY, GET YER GUN. HEAT-HATING MIKE HIT WITH GIFT NRA MEMBERSHIP BALTIMORE - Mayor Bloomberg, who is leading a national crusade against illegal guns, said yesterday he's a member of the National Rifle Association - well, sort of. "They gave me a gift membership to the NRA. You can't make this up. It arrived four weeks ago," Bloomberg told a group of mayors and police gathered at a gun summit in Baltimore yesterday. "I'm just waiting for them to survey their membership," said Bloomberg, who vowed to give his powerful rivals an earful on gun violence. The NRA has publicly attacked Bloomberg for pushing the federal government to make more gun-tracing information available and for suing gun stores known for selling firearms used in city crimes. The association's magazine last April featured a cartoon of Bloomberg as a giant octopus on its cover with a headline that blared "Tentacles!" The article said the big city mayor was extending "his reach, and his illegal anti-gun tactics, across America." The one-year membership was given anonymously, but a spokesman for the mayor said Bloomberg planned to keep it. It was unclear if the gift was a gag. "We don't look a gift card in the mouth," said Bloomberg's spokesman Stu Loeser. NRA membership costs anywhere from $10 a year to $1,000 a lifetime. Bloomberg qualifies for "distinguished life membership," a $375-a-year slot for those older than 65. Bloomberg's 66th birthday is today.

876.text: GUN-5041 PRIMARY LETTERS TO THE EDITOR : EVENING THE ODDS FOR THE GOOD GUYS M.W. Guzy is against a concealed-weapon law (May 3 Commentary article). He says more guns out there will cause a return to the Wild West. But this is already happening between gangs in the ghetto. So the outlaws still have the guns, but what does the responsible law-abiding citizen have to defend himself when faced with death? With a gun, at least he has a chance. Guzy, a retired police officer has to know that the first ones to arrive at a shooting are not the police, but the murderer and the victim. Where's the level playing field here? As to the Second Amendment on the rights of citizens to bear arms, legal scholars have debated this issue, ad infintum and were forced to admit, citizens do have this right. Leo Kluesner, Olivette M.W. Guzy's article is right on one point: "The Second Amendment has nothing whatsoever to do with duck hunting" but that is about it. Guzy insults all Missourians by saying with a concealed carry law, "The possibilities for calamity appear boundless: the potential benefits, meager." These are the same scare tactics used in Florida and elsewhere before similar legislation was passed. For years, the Post-Dispatch has continually printed this old line about shoot-outs, blood in the streets, etc. The paper has not, however, been able to print anything about such laws being repealed. That is because they work and the citizens who take the time to get a permit are not the problem.

874.text: NA-41843 PRIMARY Rival gun rallies staying, so games have to go Morrisville, a Bucks County borough of 8,000 residents perched along the Delaware River, is a baseball town. More than 500 children play Little League there, according to league president Dan O'Connell, and in 1955, its squad of preteens won the Little League World Series. "You hear about that in this town with the same pride that people talk about the Phils," said Jim Martin, a Morrisville resident and parent of two Little Leaguers. So it comes with little surprise that some residents are up in arms about a disturbance to the baseball schedule Saturday. O'Connell, at the encouragement of state police, is moving, rescheduling, or canceling about 20 games to avoid two opposing gun-rights rallies to be staged around Williamson Park and its ball fields. The groups staging the rallies - one protesting gun violence, the other supporting gun ownership rights - continued to state Wednesday that they would not cancel or relocate their events. The Rev. Robert Moore, executive director of the Coalition for Peace Action, which is organizing the anti-gun-violence rally, said that it had been planned for weeks and there were no plans to move it now. "We went through all the proper channels," he said, emphasizing that the only intention is to hold a peaceful demonstration. The American Civil Liberties Union of Pennsylvania, CeaseFirePA, and Mayors Against Illegal Guns all announced Wednesday that they supported the coalition. Former Gov. Ed Rendell and State Rep. Steve Santarsiero (D., Bucks) are scheduled to speak at Saturday's event.

characters: American Civil Liberties Union of Pennsylvania

878.text: GUN-5883 PRIMARY For the Record From remarks by Sen. Tom Daschle (D-S.D.) at a press conference yesterday in Washington: Last month, the day before the Senate voted to close the gun show loophole, a prominent Republican senator made a prediction. He said it really didn't matter how the Senate voted, because the House would water down any gun restrictions that we passed. It's in the Congressional Record. The gun lobby and its supporters in the House have now made good on that prediction. But even though we were warned, we are still stunned. The Republican gun bill before the House is a sham. It's a fraud. Not only does it water down what we did in the Senate, it waters down gun laws that have been on the books for 30 years, since the assassinations of Martin Luther King and Sen. Robert Kennedy. The Republican House bill would make it easier for children and criminals to get guns, not harder. The gun lobby has already spent more than a million bucks to make sure the bill the Senate passed last month never becomes law. Its fingerprints are all over the House bill. And to make matters worse, our Republican colleagues on one of the Senate Appropriations subcommittees voted to eliminate the president's request to help communities hire new police officers. They've got their priorities backwards. We ought to be getting guns off the streets and putting more police on the streets.

882.text: GUN-8367 PRIMARY SHOULD I BUY A GUN, DESPITE STUDY'S FINDINGS? I've been thinking lately about purchasing a handgun. My gut instinct and the ripple effects of 9-11 tell me this state seems poised to loosen the concealed-carry law this year. I don't want to be left out. You know, homeland security, terrorists lurking about, and all that. But just when you are almost convinced of doing something, something else comes along to give you pause. The one thing I forgot or glossed over in my current packing-in-public state of mind is that whatever weapon I carry with me will ultimately go home with me. And that, according to a study of firearm-related deaths among kids ages 5 to 14 by the Harvard University School of Public Health, poses an increased risk to my children and others that I may not be willing to take. Firearm-related fatality is the third-leading cause of death among this age group in the United States after motor vehicle crashes and cancer. The study, which compared household gun ownership levels to firearm-related suicides, homicides and unintentional shootings between 1988 and 1997, concludes what may seem obvious: that in states and regions with higher levels of household firearm ownership, many more children are dying from homicide, suicide and gun accidents. When the study compared the death rates between the five states with the highest and lowest gun ownership levels, the difference was markedly pronounced. Children in that age group in high gun states -- Louisiana, Alabama, Mississippi, Arkansas and West Virginia -- were 16 times as likely to die from unintentional firearm injury, seven times as likely to die from firearm suicide, three times as likely to die from firearm homicide and, overall, twice as likely to die from suicide and homicide.

characters: Harvard University School of Public Health

886.text: GUN-2235 PRIMARY Letters to the Editor IN MY OPINION Anti-gun lobby won't rest until they have confiscated every firearm The Editors: It's time for a reality check on gun control. I will state from the start that I oppose gun control, but that doesn't mean that I'm not embarrassed by some of the things that the "gun lobby" says. The foremost sacred saying is "Guns don't kill people, people kill people." While technically true that a gun will not jump up and shoot someone without a person's assistance, it is intellectually dishonest. Can anyone believe that all of the murders committed each year would have been committed if no one possessed a gun? Would disputes among teenagers, which used to result in fist fights, end up as murder if guns were not present? Would there be drive-by knifings? Dishonesty, however, is not confined to the pro-gun side. The anti- gun people prominently displayed their hypocrisy recently after the passage of the Brady Bill, requiring a five-day waiting period for a handgun purchase. The supporters of the bill said repeatedly that this was not the "first step" to gun registration or confiscation. They said that this was just a reasonable way to control the sale of deadly weapons, but the ink wasn't even dry when the nut on the Long Island Railroad opened fire on innocent passengers. So, what was their response? "We need to license gun owners. They should be made to pass a test."

881.text: GUN-1289 PRIMARY GUNS, GUNS EVERYWHERE -- STRATEGIES FOR ARMS CONTROL; Trace Illegal Firearms Yesterday, in a special legislative hearing on illegal firearms held by the New York State Assembly Codes Committee, Mayor David Dinkins called on the New York City Council to ban the sale and possession of assault weapons in the city. Nine other witnesses who appeared before the committee in the daylong hearing sought to focus attention on lethal weapons and the trade in illegal firearms in the city. The Op-Ed page has asked six specialists for their suggestions for coping with the flood of guns on the streets. The first step in getting guns off the streets of New York City is to collect identifying information on the last 200 guns confiscated by the New York police. Through the serial numbers on the guns, Federal authorities can determine where and when these illegal guns were first sold at retail and to whom. Most of the guns confiscated in New York will have a readily identifiable history to the point of first retail purchase. This information can pinpoint where the major illegal gun supply pipelines are located, particularly for recently made guns. State and local police can assist in the search, but only the Federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms has the jurisdictional reach and investigative resources to seek out the major channels of illegal supply. Studies conducted over 25 years indicate that about 80 percent of the guns used for crime in New York came from out of state, and the great majority entered the city in violation of Federal law.

characters: New York State Assembly Codes Committee
Federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

883.text: GUN-2626 PRIMARY Senators pass youth gun bill The Florida Senate has decided to punish two unpopular groups: gun-wielding kids and politicians. Senators voted not only to build more facilities for juvenile offenders,  but also to pay for that plan by raiding a fund that would otherwise finance  state politicians' campaigns. The man behind that maneuver was Sen. Ander Crenshaw, R-Jacksonville, who  plans to run for governor. It's possible that the vote could take money out of  the hands of Gov. Lawton Chiles if he seeks re-election. The Senate bill contains $ 12.5-million in funding for juvenile treatment programs, a pot of cash that could eliminate the waiting list for young criminals who get sentenced to rehabilitation. Of that, about $ 2-million would come from the campaign fund. Now the bill goes to a committee of legislators who will try to reconcile differences between the Senate and House versions. The Senate bill is both tougher and weaker than the version the House passed Wednesday. Under the Senate bill, boys or girls who get caught with a gun would lose  their driver's licenses for a year and be sentenced to 100 hours of community service. On a second offense, the penalties would rise to two years license suspension and 250 hours of service.   Those sentences would be mandatory, unlike the House bill, which would allow  judges to impose lesser sentences. In the House version, youths would be tried as adults if they violated the  gun law three times, or used a gun while committing a felony.

885.text: GUN-5861 PRIMARY Whose 'Right to Bear Arms' Is It?; Militias Are Outdated To the Editor: William Safire is right when he says that the Framers did a poor job in drafting the Second Amendment (column, June 10). However, by limiting his proposal for repeal to the amendment's "ambiguous preamble," he does not go far enough. We should repeal the whole amendment. State militias are as anachronistic and needless as unfettered gun ownership is dangerous. The Constitution makes state armies unnecessary: states may not "enter into any treaty" or "engage in war, unless actually invaded, or in such imminent danger as will not admit of delay." It is the Federal Government that is required to protect the states against "invasion" and "domestic violence." JIM CHEN Dusseldorf, Germany, June 10, 1999 The writer is an associate professor of law at the University of Minnesota. http://www.nytimes.com

880.text: GUN-11174 PRIMARY 'This doesn't make any sense' The two youngest members of the Georgia House of Representatives --- reacting to last month's shooting death of a 17-year-old in Florida in a case that has riveted the nation --- promised to file legislation Tuesday that would repeal Georgia's "Stand Your Ground" law. At a rally Monday night that drew hundreds seeking justice for Trayvon Martin, Reps. Alisha Thomas Morgan, D-Austell, and Rashad Taylor, D-Atlanta, said, despite the fact that the 2012 legislative session is almost over, gun legislation has to be reined in. In the time remaining in the session, the General Assembly is debating a bill that would lower the age to carry a gun to 18. "We want to make a statement to get the conversation started," Taylor said. "So when we come back, we will have something. This doesn't make any sense. We should not have a society where guns are the norm." Morgan and Taylor's pledge came at the end of a long and emotional rally to bring attention to the Feb. 26 shooting death of Martin in the Orlando suburb of Sanford, and to call for the prosecution of George Zimmerman, who fatally shot Martin but has not been charged. One of the the march's organizers, Markel Hutchins, challenged lawmakers to push legislation challenging "Stand Your Ground." "If you look at all of these laws, you have to connect the dots," said Sen. Emanuel Jones,D-Decatur, head of the Legislative Black Caucus, adding that two gun bills are likely to be heard Tuesday. "This is vigilante justice and we don't need that. What happened to Trayvon could have happened to one of my sons."

884.text: NA-44507 PRIMARY D.C. to seek stay of gun ruling Judge NIXED ban on handguns in public Police are told not to enforce firearms law D.C. police were told Sunday not to arrest people for carrying handguns on the street in the wake of a judge's ruling that overturned the city's principal gun-control law. However, the D.C. attorney general's office said it would seek a stay of the ruling while the city decides whether to appeal. In an order approved by Police Chief Cathy L. Lanier, police were told that District residents are permitted to carry pistols if the weapons are registered. Those who had not registered their handguns could be charged on that ground, the instruction said. The number of registered pistols is thought to be low. Lanier's instructions to police also said that residents of other jurisdictions without felony records would not be charged under the ban on carrying pistols. Meanwhile, Ted Gest, the spokesman for the D.C. attorney general's office, which defended the handgun ban in court,  said it will "be seeking a stay shortly," so the order by U.S. District Judge Frederick J. Scullin Jr. may not be in effect for long. "Its time of effectiveness could be very short," Gest said. Legal experts have said that in many cases all parties in a lawsuit are given the opportunity to appeal a ruling before it takes effect. However, it was decided at some point Sunday that Scullin's ruling took immediate effect, and that set off  efforts to bring the city into compliance.

characters: U.S. District Judge Frederick J. Scullin Jr.

888.text: GUN-5369 PRIMARY Closing In on the Gun Makers Last week's Federal jury verdict against firearms manufacturers for negligent marketing and distribution practices marks an important turning point in the fight against gun violence. The Brooklyn jury found 15 of 25 gun manufacturers named in the lawsuit negligent and found that nine of them were collectively liable for three of seven shootings with illegally obtained handguns, even though it was not proved what brand of gun had been used in a particular shooting. In effect, the jury said the companies had allowed the guns to fall into the wrong hands, thereby putting the industry on notice that it can be held accountable for violence. The gun makers are claiming vindication because the jury imposed no damages for six of the seven shooting victims. But the industry knows it is in big trouble. There is growing evidence linking industry practices to illegal trafficking in guns, and five large cities have already filed suit against the industry to try to recover their considerable costs of mopping up after gun violence. These suits are based on the so-called "negligent-marketing theory" used in the Brooklyn case as well as a more conventional product-liability theory that argues that manufacturers have made their product "unreasonably dangerous" by failing to incorporate feasible safety devices. Even before the verdict, the gun lobby was so nervous it had begun appealing to gun-friendly state legislatures to pass measures aimed at blocking lawsuits. The industry has lavished millions on high-pressure lobbying tactics to preserve its unique exemption from government safety regulations -- all the while trying to prettify that exemption by calling it a constitutional right. The gun lobby knows that the political muscle and money it deploys to control lawmakers will not work in a courtroom. That is why it is trying so hard to shut courthouse doors.

892.text: GUN-1507 PRIMARY STARING DOWN THE BARREL If easy availability of guns provided safety, residents of Los Angeles would have little to fear. In the five years before the recent riots, 500,000 handguns were sold in Los Angeles County. And those were just the legal sales. Of course, that vast private arsental didn't make Los Angeles sage. The huge number of weapons on the street increased the toll. In Newsweek's list of 54 people killed during the riots, 39 were shot to death. But the image many people remember is of shopkeepers defending their stores and their lives with pistols and automatic weapons. That explains rising gun sales in California and elsewhere, including Florida. America has so many guns-- an estimated 200 million for a population of 280 million-- that even people who don't want weapons are deciding they have to arm themselves. this is the kind of ' ' sanity" that thrives in an insane situation. And it's the kind of sawed-off logic that, typically, the National Rifle Association already is exploiting. But the NRA will be silent when, in the coming year, many post-riot gun buyers or members of their families accidentally kill themselves, commit suicide or have their guns stolen. Any plan to solve the urban crisis has to include gun control. Waiting periods and background checks aren't enough. We have to get existing weapons off the streets, starting with short-barreled handguns and assault weapons.

887.text: GUN-11868 PRIMARY Supreme Court Gun Ruling Doesn't Block Proposed Controls, Experts Say WASHINGTON -- Despite the sweeping language of a 2008 Supreme Court decision that struck down parts of the District of Columbia's strict gun-control law, the decision appears perfectly consistent with many of the policy options being discussed after the shootings in Newtown, Conn. Legal experts say the decision in the case, District of Columbia v. Heller, has been of mainly symbolic importance so far. There have been more than 500 challenges to gun laws and gun prosecutions since Heller was decided, and vanishingly few of them have succeeded. The courts have upheld federal laws banning gun ownership by people convicted of felonies and some misdemeanors, by illegal immigrants and by drug addicts. They have upheld laws making it illegal to carry guns near schools or in post offices. They have upheld laws concerning unregistered weapons. And they have upheld laws banning machine guns and sawed-off shotguns. Nor does Heller impose any major hurdles to many of the most common legislative proposals in the wake of the Newtown shootings, said Adam Winkler, a law professor at the University of California, Los Angeles, and the author of ''Gunfight: The Battle Over the Right to Bear Arms in America.'' Among the responses that Heller allows, he said, are better background checks, enhanced mental health reporting and a ban on high-capacity ammunition clips. There is one major possible exception to the trend, and it is quite fresh. Last week, a divided three-judge panel of the United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit, in Chicago, struck down an Illinois law that banned carrying loaded guns in public. Judge Richard A. Posner, writing for the majority, said the ruling was required by the Heller decision.

894.text: GUN-30030 PRIMARY GIULIANI SHAMELESSLY KISSES UP TO GUN LOBBY In the most hypocritical speech of any presidential candidate so far, Rudy Giuliani told a National Rifle Association audience he no longer supports the tough gun-controls laws he once fought for as mayor of New York. Apparently that shameless denial of his past even embarrassed Giuliani, who interrupted his speech Friday to take a personal cell phone call from his wife. It was a bizarre moment, though not nearly as unsettling as watching a man who had once prided himself on his tough stance against urban gun violence now kissing up to the NRA. This was no mere waffle. This was an all-out retreat from his prior beliefs. He used the occasion to distance himself from a lawsuit he filed seven years ago to hold gun manufacturers liable for the violence done with their products. He didn't even wait for the issue to get its day in court. "That lawsuit has gone in a direction I probably don't agree with at this point," he said. The Republican tough guy even sounded contrite about his usually ballyhooed crackdown on violent crime in New York, when he became a national spokesman for sensible gun laws. In 1995, Giuliani called the NRA "extremists" and condemned the group's defense of assault weapons as "a terrible, terrible mistake." There was no such talk on Friday, when he offered up every sop he could think of to the powerful special-interest group.

891.text: NA-42262 PRIMARY Does U.S. Senate's rejection of gun control help or hinder California bills? The U.S. Senate's stunning rejection Wednesday of every proposed gun-control amendment -- including a bipartisan deal on expanded background checks -- might telegraph problems for California Democrats now pushing a gun agenda through the state Legislature. Already boasting some of the nation's toughest gun laws, the largely liberal Golden State is mulling over a raft of even stricter rules. Among the most controversial: banning all semi-automatic rifles that take detachable magazines of any size; requiring background checks for ammunition purchases; imposing ammunition taxes; and keeping ownership records for all guns. But just as the bipartisan Manchin-Toomey amendment to expand background checks to gun shows and Internet sales was sunk in Congress, in part by four red-state Democrats who probably feared for their jobs, so too might some state lawmakers from California's "purple" districts be unwilling to put their careers on the line. "That's a very legitimate question," said former state Senate President Pro Tem Don Perata, D-Oakland, author of several gun-control laws. Even if carried by the Senate's leader, he added, "that's never a guarantee on a controversial bill." Current Senate President Pro Tem Darrell Steinberg, D-Sacramento, said Thursday he still anticipates "strong support for most if not all of the measures in the package." "The message from Washington, D.C., is that states like California cannot wait: We have to do everything we can to protect the people of California and to hopefully over time affect the national debate in a positive way. ... The vast majority of the people are behind the kind of measures in our package -- and that's ultimately what matters."

893.text: GUN-4179 PRIMARY NRA ATTACKS CLINTON, PRAISES DOLE; GROUP'S LEADERS SAY RIGHT TO OWN GUN MAY BE AT STAKE IN ELECTION While avoiding making a formal political endorsement, leaders of the National Rifle Association assailed President Bill Clinton and complimented his putative opponent, Senate Majority Leader Bob Dole, Sunday. Speaking at the organization's annual convention here, NRA officials warned that their right to own a gun might be at stake in this year's elections. NRA President Marion Hammer said the group's 3 million members must be unified in their "determination to remove from office the politicians who try to outlaw our rights." A Florida woman who this year became the first female president in the NRA's 125-year history, Hammer said: "There is a message there for him (Clinton) and anybody else who wants to take away our right to defend ourselves. People are not buying it." The NRA's chief lobbyist, Tanya Metaksa, told convention delegates that "the Second Amendment is facing the greatest political challenge of this century." The constitutional clause grants citizens the right to bear arms. Clinton "refused to wear the uniform of his country," Metaksa said, referring to Clinton's avoiding the draft during the Vietnam War. Dole, R-Kan., "wears two Purple Hearts and a Bronze Star," she said. Metaksa said victories at the state level helped make this the most successful year in the 20-year history of the NRA Institute for Legislative Action, which she heads. Calling Clinton the "most anti-gun president ever," she said the White House now was using executive authority to shut off foreign imports. And she said Clinton was seeking "global gun control."

890.text: GUN-2070 PRIMARY Anti-gunners 'running a smokescreen' A recent letter writer's suggestion to ban bullets is not unique. In fact,  Sen. Patrick Moynihan has proposed the same thing. It seems as though the  members of the anti-gun movement have gone beyond the "first step" of a  background check, after promising that they would be satisfied with that  reasonable measure. It seems as though legal gun owners are the targets of a  virtual brainstorm of schemes designed to disarm them, ammo bans being one of  the latest. Needless to say, the crooks who misuse guns only care about their illegal gains. If, as the writer suggests, a black-market bullet would cost $ 6, we should expect smugglers to take up the challenge and switch from drugs to guns and bullets. Even at six bucks, a stickup man still stands to gain, especially if he doesn't suffer much chance of going to jail. Moreover, as the writer points out, sports men make their own ammo (actually reloads). What's to stop the crooks from doing the same, or even from making handguns? All this talk about banning everything associated with firearms distracts  us from the real problem. Researcher David Kopel has found that the only  country in the world to come even close to making illegal guns more than a  slight inconvenience to obtain is Japan, and Japan confiscated civilian  firearms 400 years ago. This whole argument about making it more difficult for crooks to get guns  by sacrificing my rights flies in the face of every principle upon which this nation was founded. Yet, the anti-gunners are always willing to run the anti-crime, anti-gun smokescreen.

889.text: GUN-31208 PRIMARY WEEK IN REVIEW; Sept. 30-Oct. 6 High Court's Review Sought in Gun Case  Ruling Could Affect Bans Across the Nation Both sides in a legal battle over the District's long-standing gun ban want the U.S. Supreme Court to review the case. A group of residents challenging the law wants a ruling by the high court that could endanger other gun bans across the nation, and the city wants to keep the law intact. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit ruled 2 to 1 in the spring that the District's law was unconstitutional. The law is a virtual ban on private handgun ownership and carries a requirement that rifles and shotguns be stored unloaded and disassembled, or outfitted with trigger locks. Fire Reveals Troubles With Water Mains Insufficient Water Pressure Found at NW Blaze A major fire at a condominium building in Adams Morgan exposed problems with aging water mains. D.C. Fire Chief Dennis L. Rubin said firefighters had to run 4,000 feet of hose to Connecticut Avenue and another 3,000 feet of hose to 16th Street and Columbia Road to hook into mains that could deliver sufficient water fast enough to combat the blaze. No one was hurt in the fire, which caused millions of dollars in damage to a 30-unit building in the 2600 block of Adams Mill Road. Rubin and other city officials faulted the D.C. Water and Sewer Authority, the quasi-independent agency responsible for the mains, for not providing information about water pressure on hydrants, saying that simple color-coding could have aided firefighters looking for ample water.

896.text: GUN-6337 PRIMARY CBI has busy weekend checking gun sales Bureau beefs up staffing for Tanner show buyers ADAMS COUNTY - The Colorado Bureau of Investigation's "InstaCheck" system had its busiest days over the weekend since its resurrection Aug. 1, boosted by hundreds of buyers at the Tanner Gun Show. At the close of business Sunday, the CBI had denied 78 of 1,187 potential gun buyers over the weekend. Saturday was the busiest of the two days, with CBI rejecting 47 of 787 bids for weapons. The figures represent calls to the CBI from across the state, and officials did not have a breakdown for the number of transactions from the Tanner show, held at the Denver Merchandise Mart in Adams County. But CBI spokeswoman Susan Kitchen said staffing was beefed up because the agency knew the Tanner show would bring in a lot of business. Ironically, the namesake of the show - Jimmie Tanner - sold his guns to customers without using the checks. Tanner, 72, is an "unlicensed vendor" and is not required to conduct the background checks because he sells from a personal collection. "I joke and tell them: 'You turn around. OK, I just did a background check,"' Tanner said while selling from behind his well-stocked booth. This weekend's Tanner show was the first held since CBI reinstated the "InstaCheck" system this month. The Colorado Legislature had allowed the checks to expire on April 1, leaving only a required federal check.

897.text: GUN-5442 PRIMARY The long and winding sale Brady Law change slows gun buyers COLORADO SPRINGS - Decisions made more than four years ago in Washington, D.C., now are being felt in gun shops across Colorado. Permanent provisions of the 1994 Brady Law went into effect recently, extending the sweep of the federal firearms measure. New requirements have pushed the previous "instant checks" of gun buyers' criminal backgrounds from a few minutes to up to three days. The delays have angered gun dealers and customers and forced the Colorado Bureau of Investigation, which monitors gun sales, to do a little fast-draw to keep the whole process moving. The basic problem: Background checks now are required for buyers of all firearms - not just handguns, as was the case previously. In Colorado, that has meant a tripling of background queries to 400 a day. In addition, buyers must have their names run through a new federal database maintained by the FBI. And the computers running that database aren't exactly spitting out results, officials say. The Brady Law gives states or the FBI three days to do all the required checking. Colorado gun buyers had gotten used to quicker service from the CBI, which does the work for the federal government. At best, said CBI Director Carl Whiteside, background checks are taking a full day, down from the three just after the expanded Brady Law took effect Nov. 30. Paul Paradis, owner of a Colorado Springs gun shop, said his customers are still waiting three days for the results to come back.

895.text: GUN-1885 PRIMARY KILLINGS JUST WAITING TO HAPPEN If St. Louis and St. Louis County needed more ammunition in their fight to be able to pass stricter gun-control laws than the rest of Missouri, they got it in the latest issue of The New England Journal of Medicine. After studying 420 killings in three urban areas, doctors reported that keeping a gun in your home nearly triples your chances of being killed but has little effect in protecting your home. Given such conclusions, longer waiting periods to buy handguns and stricter rules for gun dealers are no-cost, common-sense measures everyone should support. The study, funded by the federal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, looked at killings in the Memphis, Cleveland and Seattle areas.   For each death studied, researchers also looked at randomly selected households in the same neighborhood that included someone of the same sex, race, age range and economic circumstance as the victim. They found that people in homes with guns are 2.7 times as likely to be shot to death as people in homes without guns. In addition, the study found that in cases of forced entry, having a gun in the home provides little protection. In an earlier study, the same team of researchers had found that the risk of suicide with a gun in the house increases fivefold. The conclusions seem clear. The presence of a gun close at hand can be the spark that turns a potentially volatile situation into a deadly conflagration.   The study found that three-quarters of the victims studied were killed by a relative or someone they knew; half of the homicides occurred during a quarrel or romantic triangle. The offsetting potential seems small; the study found "no protective benefit of gun ownership in the home even in homicide cases that followed forced entry," according to Dr. Jerome P. Kassirer, editor of the journal.

characters: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

898.text: GUN-4783 PRIMARY Guns and Paperwork IT'S USUALLY the Second Amendment that is invoked by gun control opponents. But this morning in the Supreme Court, in a case challenging the Brady law, the focus will be shifted to the 10th Amendment. That constitutional provision, which has nothing to do with firearms, is general in nature and provides that the powers not specifically given to the federal government by the Constitution are reserved to the states or to the people. Although all but ignored by everyone but scholars, the 10th Amendment has been invoked recently by conservative justices to curb what the court found to be overreaching by Washington. In a series of cases, the high court found violations of the amendment in congressional directives to the states in areas as varied as hazardous waste regulation and Indian tribe litigation. And now gun control opponents are using it to attack the Brady law. Their argument is that the federal government cannot impose upon state and local officials the duty to perform background checks on gun purchasers so that those who have criminal records, a history of mental illness or other disqualifying records can be denied a gun. The responsibility imposed on local officials is temporary. By 1999, the federal government is to have in place a computer system of its own that can pull out the relevant information. The burden seems to us minimal, though the sheriffs who have brought this challenge say their deputies have better things to do. And when cooperation among state, local and federal law enforcement organizations is not only historically standard practice but absolutely necessary in this large and diverse country, balking at this request for assistance appears to be motivated more by opposition to gun control than concern for states' rights.

899.text: GUN-4519 PRIMARY Focus on ABUSERS WITH A BADGE Police officers who protect citizens from violence on the streets are sometimes the source of violence at home, where spouses and children become targets for their pent-up rage. A federal law prohibits those ever convicted of spouse or child abuse from carrying a gun. But Rep. Bob Barr; (R-Ga.) argues the law should be amended, and police groups worry about officers losing their jobs.; Barr works to end retroactive feature of gun law on abusers; He introduced a bill to change law so it applies only to those convicted since it took effect. Washington ---For Rep. Bob Barr, changing a new federal law to allow people previously convicted of misdemeanor spouse or child abuse to own guns is a simple matter of fairness. "You don't punish somebody after the fact," Barr said. The Cobb County Republican and former U.S. attorney in Atlanta introduced a bill earlier this year that would amend the law to apply only to people convicted since the law took effect Sept. 30. As it now stands, the law makes it illegal for anybody convicted of misdemeanor abuse crimes at any time to own or carry a gun. His legislation has remained stalled in the House Judiciary Committee, as Republicans have preferred to focus on tax and spending issues rather than tangle with a politically volatile topic that highlights the party's problems in attracting the support of women. "I'll continue trying to get it through," Barr said. Police groups worry that officers with a misdemeanor conviction on their record could lose their jobs because of their inability to carry a gun. They have challenged the law in federal court, contending that the gun ban should not apply to convictions obtained before the law was in place. Advocates for abuse victims argue that safety should be the primary concern and that keeping guns away from batterers should be paramount. But Barr said that since the new law covers only misdemeanors, it does not apply to violent offenders. Federal law has prevented felons from owning guns since 1968.

901.text: GUN-6247 PRIMARY LAW AND ORDER FULTON COUNTY Four indicted in illegal gun sales A federal grand jury Tuesday indicted four men for buying 143 firearms over a two-year period in the metro area and in Columbus, and then illegally selling them at inflated prices to buyers in Philadelphia. Jefferie Gaddie, Simon Kendrick, Sean Peeples and Gregory Ricks purchased most of their guns from Arrowhead Gun and Pawn in Jonesboro between November 1994 and November 1996, according to the indictment. The defendants falsely filled out their purchase forms and removed the serial numbers from some of the guns so they could not be traced, the indictment said. The men then mailed or took the guns on bus trips to Philadelphia, where the firearms were sold on the street. DOOLY COUNTY State helps pay for tornado costs The Dooly County school system has received $ 30,096 in state funds to pay insurance costs for buildings damaged by a tornado in April. School Superintendent Buford Hicks received a check Monday from the Georgia Emergency Management Agency. Tornadoes that swept through Dooly and Candler counties April 15 destroyed a storage building owned by the schools and damaged 22 vehicles at the system's bus maintenance shop. Most of the money will be used to pay insurance deductibles. The money will also pay for a shower installed at Vienna Elementary School, which was used as a shelter after the storm. No one died in the storm, but the tornadoes left more than 300 people homeless and caused an estimated $ 10 million in damage. The city of Vienna, 50 miles south of Macon, has received more than $ 1.6 million from the state for cleanup and road repairs.

902.text: GUN-7820 PRIMARY THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE GUN ISSUE; Gore and Bush Clash Further on Firearms The lines that divide the presidential candidates on the issue of gun control came into sharper focus today, as Vice President Al Gore vowed to veto any measure prohibiting cities from suing gun manufacturers and Gov. George W. Bush suggested he might support it. Gun manufacturers have been lobbying Federal lawmakers for such legislation; Mr. Gore, in an interview today, pledged to block that effort and questioned whether Mr. Bush would do likewise. "I challenge Governor Bush to make the same pledge," Mr. Gore said in an interview with Political Points, the Webcast of The New York Times and ABC News. "Will he veto any bill that lets gun manufacturers off the hook? Will he fight for the gun industry or for America's families?" When Mr. Bush was asked about that at a subsequent news conference here, he said, "It depends on what's in the legislation." But the Texas governor added, "You can get a feel for my position by looking at what I've done in office, and what I did in office was sign a bill that made it very difficult for local municipalities to sue manufacturers of a legal product." Mr. Bush was referring to a 1999 law that required lawsuits against gun makers to be filed, on behalf of Texas cities or counties, by the state attorney general, who might or might not support that course of action. The measure was similar to ones supported by other Republican governors, and reflected their general push to curb civil litigation in an array of areas.

900.text: GUN-7034 PRIMARY Gun 'straw purchase' bill faces rough road One of Gov. Bill Owens' last remaining gun-control bills is  under attack from the right and the left and may be in trouble in  the Senate. 'I'm going to have my hands full,' said Sen. Bryan Sullivant,  the Breckenridge Republican who is carrying the bill in the  Senate. ' The right says it goes too far. The left says it doesn't  go far enough.' The bill would allow local prosecutors to enforce federal law  banning 'straw purchases' of guns on behalf of criminals and kids. It barely made it out of the Senate Appropriations Committee  on Friday, on a 5-4 vote. Both pro-gun-rights Sen. Ken Chlouber,  R-Leadville, and pro-gun-control Sen. Bill Thiebaut, D-Pueblo,  voted against the bill. But moderate Republicans and Democrats  gave the bill the majority it needed to get out of committee. Debate has focused on how many innocent people could get  caught accidentally in the net cast by the bill. Gun-rights  supporters have warned that a wife could be charged for giving a  hunting rifle as a Christmas gift to her husband, not knowing he  had trouble with the law years ago. Gun-control supporters such as Thiebaut have argued that if  politicians want to get tough on gun crime, they should set a  legal standard that makes it easier for prosecutors to win gun  cases. House Bill 1214 is part of Owens' five-point gun plan  proposed in response to the Columbine High School massacre. Three  of those measures - background checks at gun shows, raising the  age for handgun sales at gun shows and 'safe storage' - already  have been defeated by his fellow Republicans in the Legislature.

903.text: GUN-700 PRIMARY Legislators dodge gun-control bills TALLAHASSEE - Faced with two emotional gun-control decisions    Tuesday, the Florida House ducked them both. After killing the Legislature's opportunity to establish a    seven-day waiting period between purchasing and picking up a handgun,    the lawmakers voted 80-36 to let voters make the decision in November    1990. And instead of voting on whether to ban semiautomatic assault    rifles, the House voted to establish a commission to study the issue    for a year. The legislators also voted to create a system requiring potential    buyers of all firearms to pay $ 10 to gun shop owners, who would    immediately check the buyer's criminal background by telephone. Whether    such a system is even workable was questioned by some lawmakers. For the National Rifle Association, which opposes waiting periods    and bans on semiautomatics, Tuesday was a good day. "They have not been led astray by emotionalism," Marion Hammer, a lobbyist for the NRA's Florida affiliate, said of the legislators.     "They've looked at the facts and taken some meaningful, responsible    steps." Gun control advocates remained hopeful that Floridians will get to    vote on a constitutional amendment requiring a statewide, seven-day    waiting period between buying and picking up handguns. They included    the wife of James Brady, the press secretary for President Reagan who    was wounded and disabled when John Hinkley attempted to assassinate    Reagan in 1981. "It's a wonderful step forward," said Sarah Brady. "It will do a    great deal to increase public safety in Florida."

904.text: GUN-3190 PRIMARY '95 Georgia Legislature EDITORIAL NRA's death grip on Georgia The single biggest threat to gun ownership in America is not Jim and Sarah Brady, or Handgun Control, or the U.S. Supreme Court. The single biggest threat to gun ownership in America is the National Rifle Association. By its extremism, the NRA makes all gun owners appear extremist. And by making rational gun-control legislation impossible, it threatens to make irrational legislation inevitable. Right now, the NRA is trying to weaken Georgia's gun-control laws, already among the weakest in the nation. Its effort has been thwarted for the moment in the state Senate, where Lt. Gov. Pierre Howard is presiding officer. Howard is under immense pressure from the NRA, and a critical vote on the question is expected Monday. It is absolutely essential that citizens opposed to the NRA's agenda make their feelings known to Howard and members of the Senate. On one level, the NRA's grip on the Georgia Legislature is difficult to understand, given the state's dire situation. This year alone, more than 500 Georgia citizens will be killed with firearms, and Georgia has been identified by federal law-enforcement authorities as a prime source state for illegal guns in other jurisdictions. Furthermore, polls show strong support across the state for tighter gun-control laws. So why does the NRA have so much influence? The answer is as much cultural as political. In much of Georgia, the NRA's support is the political equivalent of a Good Housekeeping seal of approval. To a legislator, particularly a Democratic legislator, an NRA endorsement offers protection from the charge of having gone liberal. After last fall's election, the perceived value of that kind of protection has increased mightily.

906.text: GUN-11651 PRIMARY FLORIDIANS DISLIKE RNC GUN BAN Floridians disagree with Tampa Mayor Bob Buckhorn's proposal to ban guns outside the Republican National Convention, a Quinnipiac University poll said. But opinions vary by region, political party and gender. For the ban: women, Democrats and residents of South Florida and the greater Tampa Bay area. Against it: Republicans, men and people who live in North Florida and the Panhandle. Florida law will allow gun owners with concealed weapons permits to carry their firearms outside the convention, and it prohibits local authorities from enacting any rules regulating guns or ammunition. (The Secret Service has said only police on duty will be allowed to carry guns inside the convention.) Buckhorn this month asked Gov. Rick Scott for an executive order allowing the city to ban carrying firearms in the city's convention "Event Zone," which will cover downtown, Ybor City, the northern part of Harbour Island and the area around the University of Tampa. Scott refused, saying a ban would be an unconstitutional violation of the Second Amendment. Statewide, those polled said 49 to 46 percent that there should be no ban during the convention, which will run from Aug. 27 to 30 at the Tampa Bay Times Forum. The poll of 1,722 registered Florida voters has a 2.4 percentage point margin of error. But in the seven counties around Tampa Bay, those polled took Buckhorn's position 52 to 46 percent. A majority of South Florida residents also supported a ban.

905.text: GUN-5279 PRIMARY Eaton keeps gun permits in town EATON - Eaton Town Board members have decided that the acting police chief can issue concealed weapons permits only to town residents, following a debate that lasted more than an hour. The 3-2 decision Thursday night will force the town's police department to refund 130 application fees that were collected March 6 by acting Police Chief Al Stutman at a Wheat Ridge gun shop. Voting in favor of keeping the permits in town were Scott Moser, Jeff Payne and Biff Yates. Voting against the measure were Gary Ruedin and Ralph Bretthauer. Keith McIntyre was absent. News that Stutman was soliciting concealed weapons permit applications outside the town limits sparked debate within Eaton and beyond. Stutman said he was acting in accordance with what he thought was town policy, but he admitted there was some misunderstanding between him and board members. According to several board members, it was a major misunderstanding. "It's not OK with me and it was unprofessional and unethical," Yates said. "I never even thought for a minute that it was going to be an action outside our jurisdiction." The Wheat Ridge police chief and city manager were so angered by Stutman's actions, a letter was sent to Eaton indicating that an investigation would be conducted. Wheat Ridge Police Chief Jack Hurst was miffed that Stutman didn't give him a courtesy call to tell him what was going to happen.

characters: Wheat Ridge Police Chief Jack Hurst

907.text: GUN-8684 PRIMARY Senators seek buyer checks at gun shows Washington --- Two powerful senators have joined forces to "close the gun-show loophole" and beef up enforcement of current gun laws. Republican John McCain of Arizona and Democrat Joseph Lieberman of Connecticut introduced a measure Tuesday that would mandate background checks for all buyers at gun shows where at least 75 guns are sold. "It is still far too easy for a convicted felon to buy firearms at a gun show," McCain said at a Capitol Hill news conference. "I believe law-abiding citizens have the right to own guns. . . . But with rights come responsibilities." The bill, touted as a compromise, also includes millions for hiring more federal agents and gun-crime prosecutors. "We all agree that the laws on the books should be enforced, and we all agree that convicted felons shouldn't be able to get guns," Lieberman said. "The bill we are introducing today would write both of those principles into law." The legislation targets unlicensed or private dealers at gun shows who are not subject to the Brady Act, which requires background checks. Under current law, all federally licensed vendors must use a national database to see if the purchaser has a criminal record or other disqualifying factors. The bill would allow as many as three business days for the vendor to complete a full background check. After three years, however, states could reduce the maximum time for some background checks to 24 hours.

910.text: GUN-3741 PRIMARY TO ARMS! TO ARMS! G. Gordon Liddy's misguided call for Americans to stage massive civil disobedience by violating gun laws spreads the national paranoia about government one step further, to no good end. The 1,000 or so people who gathered at the Lincoln Memorial in Washington on Sunday insist that their cause is not guns but liberty. Yet the focus of Liddy and other speakers makes that claim just as suspect as many of their other actions. About three dozen speakers addressed the rally, sponsored by the Committee of 1776, but the star was Liddy, the former Watergate burglar turned talk-show host. Since the federal building in Oklahoma City was blown up April 19, he has attracted a lot of attention - not always wanted - for his pointed remarks about the best way to kill federal agents who are thought to be threatening one's life. Now comes his call to arms at the Washington rally. "They want to disarm you," he told the adoring crowd, invoking a typically unspecified archenemy. "Refuse to be disarmed." Continuing the drumbeat against gun control and misrepresenting the Second Amendment's guarantees about a well-regulated militia, he added: "The Constitution is crystal clear. Any law which infringes on your right to keep and bear arms, any arm of your choice, is unconstitutional - just don't obey the damn law." Finally, he said anyone willing to violate gun-control laws need not fear being punished: "They're not going to put 150 million Americans in jail!"

908.text: GUN-31538 PRIMARY OUR OPINIONS: Gun bill's recoil can be disastrous Next time a driver cuts you off on I-285, think twice about rude gestures and nasty glares. He may feel threatened and shoot you. The state Legislature wants to give that irate driver what amounts to a license to kill. The "shoot first, ask questions later" bill reflects a national campaign by the National Rifle Association to sanction legal gunplay in the streets. The law encourages the use of deadly force, which should always be the last resort. These "stand your ground" laws, as the NRA describes them, expand what is known as the castle doctrine --- the right to use deadly force to defend your home --- to public places and eliminates any duty to retreat. "I think this bill has the potential to turn our streets into the OK Corral because it could be misinterpreted, misused and abused," said DeKalb District Attorney Gwen Keyes Fleming. Florida enacted its "shoot first" law last fall, despite the opposition of police. Miami Police Chief John Timoney warned that innocent people could die ---from kids playing pranks on neighbors to drunks who stumble into the wrong house. The Georgia Senate also ignored the concerns of police, passing Senate Bill 396 with no discussion about the wisdom of encouraging people to use deadly force when the option exists to flee or call police. Now, the bill goes to the House, which has never met an NRA-backed bill it didn't like.

characters: DeKalb District Attorney Gwen Keyes Fleming

913.text: GUN-5938 PRIMARY Clinton fires back at gun policy critics Defending his resolve to tighten gun controls, President Clinton said today he prefers sweeping restrictions --- such as a mandate that people ''register guns like they register cars'' --- but that could take years because he is hamstrung by a Republican Congress ''out of touch with the American people.'' The president and his wife, Hillary, aimed their anti-youth-violence campaign at family breakfast tables nationwide today, appearing on ABC's ''Good Morning America'' with 40 teenagers --- some from schools made infamous by shootings --- for a live brainstorming session. Anchor Charlie Gibson opened the broadcast from the White House's Roosevelt Room with a separate interview with the president. Gibson quoted an unnamed Clinton ally as saying the president, after the Littleton, Colo., school massacre, ''had a chance to roar and he meowed.'' Clinton narrowly won Senate passage last month of a limited bill requiring mandatory background checks for all transactions at gun shows, then lost a fight to have the House follow suit before its Memorial Day recess. He has --- as a matter of political practicality, he said --- deferred until later the battle for additional gun controls, such as background checks for explosives sales, increasing from 18 to 21 the legal age for owning handguns and assault weapons, and limiting gun purchases to one per month. ''Look, let's join the real world here,'' Clinton said to Gibson in response to the ''meowed'' characterization. His eyes narrowing and his finger pointing accusingly, Clinton continued:

909.text: GUN-8174 PRIMARY Gun control ads denounce 'dangerous candidates' Washington --- A leading gun control group released a "dangerous dozen" list of gun rights advocates Tuesday and said it planned a $ 4 million ad campaign to defeat congressional candidates this fall. The group reserved a special category for the Republican presidential ticket, Gov. George W. Bush of Texas and Dick Cheney, as the "most dangerous candidates for American families, neighborhoods and communities." Michael Barnes, president of Handgun Control Inc., criticized Bush for signing a bill blocking municipalities from suing gun makers and another allowing Texans to carry concealed weapons for the first time in 125 years. "When he signed that bill," Barnes said, "the police organizations in Texas said they were opposed to it. The educational organizations in Texas said they were opposed to it. The health care organizations in Texas said they were opposed to it. Who was for it? The gun lobby was for it." At a news conference, Barnes also attacked Cheney's record as a Wyoming congressman. While in the House, Cheney opposed a ban on easy-to-smuggle plastic weapons and another on so-called "cop-killer bullets," which can pierce bullet-resistent vests. Cheney has said he now would consider such bans. Ken Lisaius, a Bush spokesman, said "Texans are safer under Governor Bush than they have been previously." During Bush's tenure, violent crime has decreased 20 percent, overall crime is down 13 percent, and juvenile crime is down 7 percent, the first decline in more than a decade, he said.

911.text: GUN-5503 PRIMARY FBI CHECKS ARE TOO SLOW, GUN DEALERS SAY; FIRST DAY OF 'INSTANT' SYSTEM FOR EXPANDED BRADY LAW BRINGS DELAYS, COMPLAINTS; SOME STORE OWNERS EXPECT SLOWER BUSINESS AT FIRST Monday was the first time that Missourians who want to buy rifles or shotguns had to submit to federal criminal-background checks. Local gun dealers grumbled that the new system was all fouled up. Nothing changed for gun enthusiasts in Illinois, where state law already requires waiting periods for all firearms. But because Missouri only requires a wait for purchases of pistols, the state has come under the expanded reach of the federal Brady Handgun Violence Prevention Act of 1994. Unless a state has more stringent laws, the Brady law requires anyone who wants to buy a firearm of any kind from a gun dealership or a pawn shop to pass muster with an FBI records center in West Virginia. The FBI center is supposed to comb the nation's criminal-justice computers to determine whether a prospective gun buyer's past should prevent the sale. The FBI refers to them as "instant" checks. That's the first thing gun dealers object to. "There's nothing instant about it," said Mark Campbell, manager of the Shooting Systems gun shop in Affton. "It took me seven hours and maybe 100 times hitting the speed dial to finally get approval for one guy. It's ridiculous, but I guess it's to be expected." Campbell said his customer waited for three hours and had to leave for an out-of-town work assignment without getting word. Bill Carter, an FBI spokesman in Washington, said the new system was out of service for about 1 1/2 hours Monday, but promised things will get better.

912.text: GUN-6960 PRIMARY JUDGE THROWS OUT CINCINNATI'S LAWSUIT AGAINST GUN INDUSTRY, CALLING CLAIMS; VAGUE; ST. LOUIS ALSO PLANS SUIT OVER FIREARM VIOLENCE A judge's dismissal of Cincinnati's lawsuit against the firearms industry will be used to try to defeat similar lawsuits across the country, an attorney for gunmakers said Thursday. In the first dismissal of such a case, Hamilton County Common Pleas Judge Robert Ruehlman ruled Thursday that Cincinnati's claims were vague and unsupported by legal precedent, and the city was not entitled to recover damages for gun-related violence and its resulting costs. He said that people - not guns - should be held responsible for shootings. "Here, the nuisance is the criminal or reckless misuse of firearms by third parties who are beyond control of (gunmakers and distributors). Because (gunmakers and distributors) have no ability to control the misconduct of these third parties, nuisance does not apply," Ruehlman wrote in his order. Spokesmen for the industry hailed the decision. "It very clearly points out the weaknesses in the other cities' suits," said Jim Dorr, an attorney in Chicago for gunmakers Sturm, Ruger & Co. Inc. of Southport, Conn., and Smith & Wesson Corp. of Springfield, Mass. Dorr said Cincinnati's claims were similar to those advanced in 17 other lawsuits representing 27 city or county governments. "These lawsuits filed by the cities have been, in our opinion, nothing but smoke and mirrors, with no legal foundation to them," he said. St. Louis has announced plans to file a similar suit against gun manufacturers.

914.text: GUN-7389 PRIMARY PREZ RUSHES MEETING ON LAGGING GUN BILLS WASHINGTON - President Clinton said yesterday he will meet with congressional leaders next week at the White House to discuss gun-control legislation that has been languishing for more than six months. Clinton moved after a new round of gun violence this week, including a Michigan first-grader who fatally shot his 6-year-old classmate, had even House Judiciary Committee Chairman Henry Hyde (R-Ill.), a gun-control opponent, talking compromise. "I hope Congress will break the logjam, and I'm going to invite the conferees down here to do it," Clinton said of an eight-month-old House-Senate conference committee that has failed to agree on legislation that would require trigger locks on new guns and three days for gun show background checks. "If we had child trigger locks on all these guns, we could keep them alive," Clinton said. "Pure and simple, the stalled juvenile justice bill could have prevented the Michigan tragedy," said Rep. Carolyn McCarthy (D-L.I.). "The country wants legislation," Hyde agreed. "There's an old saying - if you can't get dinner, get a sandwich," he added. "I'm open to compromise." Hyde has been toeing the GOP leadership line in opposing the bill, which was passed by the Senate last year after the Columbine High School massacre in Colorado but lost steam in the House.

characters: House Judiciary Committee Chairman Henry Hyde

915.text: GUN-31256 PRIMARY Gunning for the District; For some gun advocates, securing the right to keep and bear arms at home wasn't enough. IF TOM PALMER and his fellow plaintiffs have their way, they'll soon be carrying loaded handguns through the streets of the nation's capital. Mr. Palmer, three other individuals and the Second Amendment Foundation sued the District last week, arguing that city laws that "ban registration of handguns to be carried for self-defense by law-abiding citizens" are unconstitutional. Mr. Palmer, a resident of the District, is asking Judge Henry Kennedy of the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia to strike down the prohibition. The lawsuit also asks Judge Kennedy to nullify laws that prohibit non-District residents from registering their weapons and obtaining handgun carry permits. The plaintiffs are represented by the same lawyer who argued successfully last year before the Supreme Court against District laws that essentially prevented residents from keeping functional firearms at home for self-defense. They now argue that the right to keep and carry firearms for self-defense extends beyond the home and that licensed owners can be prohibited only from carrying handguns into "sensitive places" such as schools or government buildings. This argument should be rejected as wrong on the law and wrong as a matter of public policy. In the landmark 2008 decision in District of Columbia v. Heller, the justices narrowly tailored the decision to recognize an individual right to keep and bear arms for self-protection in the home. Justice Antonin Scalia, who wrote the majority opinion, made clear that the decision to strike down the District's law pertaining to guns in the home should not be read to discredit all other gun regulation.

918.text: GUN-2860 PRIMARY Where Men Are Men, Guns Were for Movies To the Editor: I don't know where Michael Zirmo ("Gun Control Is Fine, Within Reason," April 3) went to school or how old he is, but I do know he's seen too many Clint Eastwood movies. I grew up "in the old days," as he says nostalgically in his Soapbox essay, and I went to a high school of 3,000 in Colorado (where men are men). Neither the high school nor the Y camp where I spent several summers had a riflery team or a plinking range. And far from guns being a "natural, routine" part of our lives, the only guns most of us saw were in movies about gangsters or ignorant hillbillies feuding in the backwoods. SUZANNE KISTLER Manhattan

917.text: GUN-30060 PRIMARY House panel passes NRA bill After weeks of delay, legislation asserting one's right to carry a firearm into a workplace parking lot was approved by a House committee Tuesday. The bill, however, is significantly weaker than first proposed and faces additional challenges. It took six tries for the House Judiciary Committee to pass the bill, which upholds the right to carry weapons in a locked vehicle but also states a business has the right to create policy banning them. That compromise was meant to settle a pitched fight between the National Rifle Association and business groups. On that score, it may have succeeded. But another section of HB 129 left few doubts the battle is over. The provision limits gun bans to when "reasonably necessary for the safety and welfare of employees, invitees, or the general public, or to safeguard business operations." NRA lobbyist Marion Hammer thinks that would include only sensitive areas, such as plants dealing with hazardous waste. Business interests, who insist property rights are paramount, complained the clause is vague and raises concern about litigation. "Reasonably necessary, what does that mean?" asked Mark Wilson, executive director of the Florida Chamber of Commerce. "Nobody on the committee could even define what it means. So how can an employer?" In a last-minute effort that threatened to derail the process, Rep. Juan-Carlos Planas, R-Miami, offered an amendment eliminating the reasonably necessary language.

916.text: NA-40540 PRIMARY Foul: Temper-Tantrum Caucus pitches a gun fit FOUL: The Temper-Tantrum Caucus of the Missouri Republican Party pitched a fit last week because the Missouri Highway Patrol shared a list of concealed-carry gun permit holders with the federal government as part of an investigation into fraud. Republicans, apparently convinced President Barack Obama will seize their guns as part of a Kenyan overthrow of the government, are indignant that their privacy rights allegedly are being violated. On Thursday, House Speaker Tim Jones, R-Eureka, and most of the House Republican caucus, stood on the steps outside Attorney General Chris Koster's office and demanded an investigation into this so-called violation of law. Never mind that Missouri's conceal-carry law already requires every permit holder to go through an FBI background check, including fingerprinting, meaning that their "private information" - most of which can be found on driver's licenses and tax forms - already has been reviewed by at least one branch of the federal government. The night before their protest, Mr. Jones and his caucus had a different view of privacy rights. They gave first-round approval to House Bill 343, which seeks to rein in alleged abuse of the temporary assistance for needy family program by allowing the following privacy violations of poor people: Drug testing without a warrant, based on a state worker's whim. Requiring them to submit up to two years of tax records if a state worker investigates fraud. Requiring them to prove their children are enrolled in school and attending regularly. Checking the income, including pay stubs if necessary, of all people living at a TANF recipient's home. Demanding copies of rental agreements and any court orders regarding custody.

924.text: GUN-2662 PRIMARY LOGIC BEHIND GUN CONTROL IS BACKWARD Besides guns, perhaps other potential weapons need to be controlled. How many people could a maniac with a baseball bat kill in a crowded mall? Sounds like assault bats need to be banned! Consider the number of fatalities if this maniac attacks with a vehicle. Let's ban assault cars. If just one life is saved, it will be worth it. Sound ridiculous? This is the logic being used by the proponents of gun control.
