Law-abiding citizens must pay for the lawlessness of a small percentage of the American population. Why not attack the cause of the problem, the criminal? Criminals need to be treated like criminals, not privileged individuals. Hard work in prison will do wonders rehabilitating them. If they refuse to work, they don't get fed. Let's try getting tough with the criminals instead of law-abiding citizens. Sam Sharpe Okeechobee

921.text: GUN-31109 PRIMARY Gun Registrations Off To Slow Start in D.C.; One Application Received on First Day Thirty-two years after banning most D.C. residents from possessing handguns, the District government opened its doors yesterday to applicants seeking permission to own revolvers, bowing to a recent U.S. Supreme Court ruling that declared the city's tough firearms restrictions unconstitutional. With the ban lifted after a momentous, years-long legal battle that led to the landmark high court decision last month, here's how many applications the city received by day's end: one. Bracing for a crowd at the registration office, at police headquarters on Indiana Avenue NW, officials set up a reception counter in the lobby and used portable metal railings to reserve one of the building's entrances for "gun registry applicants." Officers stood guard at the door, and a dozen reporters and TV cameras were waiting expectantly at 7 a.m., when the registration process was to begin. But in the eight hours that the office remained open, there was no crush of people eager to avail themselves of the newly affirmed right to own a revolver in the nation's capital. Police gave out 58 registration packets to people stopping by for the materials. But only two people showed up to apply to register handguns, and one was turned away by police officials because he didn't bring his weapon with him, as the registration rules require. "We didn't think there was going to be a big rush," Police Chief Cathy L. Lanier said. Still she added, "We'd always rather be overprepared than underprepared."

919.text: GUN-2422 PRIMARY Officials uncertain of gun law details Face deadline for enforcing waiting period Law enforcement leaders haven't a clue right now as to who will bear new administrative burdens, such as checking people's criminal backgrounds, when the Brady law ordering a five-day waiting period for guy buyers goes into effect Feb. 28. Paul Hetner, deputy director of the Georgia Crime Information Center (GCIC), said people in his office are "waiting to get a copy of the signed bill to look at it more closely" before deciding what its impact is going to be. However, he did not think the new law will present a problem for his agency. "It'll be a [computerized] name search, versus a fingerprint search like we do for a gun permit," he said. Regulations detailing such things as exactly who gun dealers are supposed to notify - the sheriff, the police chief, a local precinct commander - when someone wants to buy a gun are still being developed by a "work group" at the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF) in Washington. They hope to have their regulations ready by the Feb. 28 deadline. But ready or not, the law, with its delay provision, is effective that day. That means that even if the who, how, what and where are not known, at least a when is chiseled in stone: The local firearms retailer won't hand over that shiny new handgun for five days.

characters: federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF)

922.text: GUN-30822 PRIMARY Virginia and Guns To the Editor: I take issue with your May 12 editorial ''The Mayor Strikes a Nerve,'' about Virginia's new law related to firearm purchases. Requiring that investigations of gun dealers in Virginia must be done in cooperation with state or federal law enforcement does not hamper the investigation; it ensures that such activities do not interfere with investigations that may already be ongoing. What does Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg have to fear from cooperating with law enforcement if he has evidence that a crime is being committed? The editorial also scolds Gov. Tim Kaine of Virginia for shamefully warning activists not to use the Virginia Tech shootings as their ''hobby horse'' in pursuit of a political agenda immediately after the tragedy. What was shameful was the Brady Campaign to Prevent Handgun Violence sending out an e-mail message to supporters, referring to the shootings and asking for donations within 24 hours of the killings.   David Adams President Virginia Shooting Sports Association Richmond, Va., May 15, 2007

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Handgun Violence

920.text: GUN-30716 PRIMARY District of Columbia Set Back In Fight Over Its Gun Law A federal appeals court in Washington refused yesterday to revisit a March decision striking down parts of a gun control law there. The development brought the case one step closer to possible Supreme Court consideration of the Second Amendment's meaning. The earlier decision, by a divided three-judge panel, was the first federal appellate ruling in the nation's history to hold a gun control law unconstitutional on the ground that the Second Amendment's guarantee of a right to bear arms protects the rights of individuals, as opposed to the collective rights of state militias. Yesterday's decision was terse, indicating little more than that 4 of the 10 active judges on the court, the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit, would have granted the petition for rehearing. ''We are deeply disappointed that the court narrowly denied reconsideration,'' Mayor Adrian M. Fenty said in a statement. But Mr. Fenty and Washington's lawyers did not immediately vow to ask the Supreme Court to hear the case. '' Right now we're evaluating our options,'' said Linda Singer, Washington's attorney general. '' We haven't ruled anything in or out.'' Because the March decision's interpretation of the Second Amendment is in conflict with that of nine other federal appeals courts, a review by the Supreme Court is both warranted and likely should it be requested, said Michael C. Dorf, a Columbia law professor who is an authority on the Second Amendment.

923.text: GUN-7851 PRIMARY 6 bullets, 4 lives: Legacy of Reagan shooting; 20 years after the attempted assassination, key players have taken different paths, but most are doing well WASHINGTON - The pop, pop, pop of gunfire outside a Washington hotel 20 years ago sent the lives of those at the scene ricocheting in different directions. Secret Service agent Tim McCarthy, who took a bullet meant for President Reagan, is a police chief in a Chicago suburb. His heroism that day, March 30, 1981, underpinned a campaign for statewide political office a few years ago. Washington police Officer Thomas Delahanty, who was doing crowd control that day, was shot in the back of the neck and sent into immediate retirement at age 45, with enduring pain. Press secretary James Brady, shot in the head and permanently disabled, carries on his fight for gun control. He appeared in Washington this week to criticize the gun lobby yet again. And Reagan, who has Alzheimer's disease and broke his right hip in a fall at his California home in January, reached his 90th birthday on Feb. 6, a benchmark many people never achieve. "He is up and walking and has been since his birthday," Reagan spokeswoman Joanne Drake said Friday, the 20th anniversary of the shooting. "As for the Alzheimer's, he's doing as well as can be expected for a disease that's progressive." Reagan was walking out a side door of the Washington Hilton when John Hinckley Jr., a mentally disturbed young man trying to impress actress Jodie Foster, crouched and began firing a .22-caliber revolver.

926.text: GUN-6607 PRIMARY Republicans at odds over gun control After hours of hearings, statements and political maneuvers,  the only thing clear about the gun debate in the Colorado  Legislature is that nothing is clear. Most of Gov. Bill Owens' package of gun-control proposals is  on track, with one more proposal to be heard in committee next  week. But defiant members of his own Republican Party are pushing  several gun-rights bills, including one that would 'pre-empt' most  city and county gun rules, allowing people to carry guns in more  places. And House Republicans on Friday voted down a proposal to ban  concealed handguns from school grounds, prompting a veto threat  from Owens. 'I would veto the pre-emption bill if the situation with  concealed carry in schools remains as it is today,' Owens told  reporters Friday. ' Last year, I made the point that schools are  off-limits or face veto, and I'm still where I was last year.' Despite the energy going into the gun debate, it could stall  in the Senate. There, the committee handling gun bills has become  a black hole, where gun-control and gun-rights bills disappear. Under orders from Republican legislative leaders, lawmakers  are rushing to get all of the gun bills done by mid-February, so  they can move on to other issues, such as education and tax cuts. That has led to what Owens on Friday dubbed 'gun month' as  the General Assembly tries to sort through about 20 gun bills in  six weeks.

925.text: GUN-6395 PRIMARY YOUTH MOVEMENT; INSPIRED BY THE CIVIL RIGHTS ERA OF THE 1960S, ST. PAUL YOUNGSTERS MARCH AGAINST A PROBLEM OF THE 1990S. Re-creating the social action scene of the 1960s civil rights movement, more than 60 youngsters braved extreme heat and humidity Friday to march against gun violence through St. Paul's Summit-University area. Escorted by two police cars, the bedraggled but determined marchers carried banners that read "No more guns" and chanted slogans like "Stop the violence! Start the love!" as they tromped for 45 minutes from Mount Olivet Baptist Church to Jimmy Lee Recreation Center. "It's a more peaceful way to end gun violence," Brittani Fountain, 10, said of the march as she cooled off afterward in the shade outside the recreation center. Fellow marcher Samuel Butler, 8, agreed. "If people would stop gun violence and stuff like that, I think we would have a peaceful world and people wouldn't be dying so early." Brittani and Samuel are among the 70 African-American children attending the St. Paul Freedom School this summer. The six-week program is based on a national effort of the Children's Defense Fund and the Black Community Crusade for Children. The St. Paul Area Council of Churches is sponsoring this year's program at Mount Olivet. According to council spokeswoman Sandy Lucas, Freedom Schools are serving more than 3,600 children nationwide this summer in 23 cities, including two sites in Minneapolis and one in St. Paul. This is its first year at Mount Olivet. The program is inspired by the Freedom Schools that emerged during the civil rights movement in Mississippi in the summer of 1964. At that time, social action focused on getting African-Americans registered to vote. This year, for the St. Paul program, reading is the focus.

characters: St. Paul Area Council of Churches

930.text: GUN-7803 PRIMARY THE 2000 CAMPAIGN; Campaign Briefing THE ISSUES CUOMO SEEKS BUSH STAND ON GUN BILL Housing Secretary Andrew Cuomo added his voice yesterday to the chorus of Democrats who have called on Gov. George W. Bush of Texas to clarify his position on federal legislation that would prohibit lawsuits by local governments against gun manufacturers. Mr. Bush signed such legislation in Texas, and suggested last week that he would do the same in Washington. In a telephone news conference yesterday, Mr. Cuomo, who has tried to negotiate settlements between gun makers and cities that have sued them, called Mr. Bush's remarks vague. He asserted that the gun makers, who have broken off talks in recent months, would return to the table if they knew Mr. Bush opposed such legislation. Bush aides accused Mr. Cuomo of politicizing the issue. "Instead of spending their time distorting and misrepresenting Governor Bush's priorities, the Clinton-Gore administration ought to spend more time enforcing our existing gun laws," said Scott McClellan, a spokesman for Mr. Bush.   James Dao (NYT) THE REPUBLICANS ABORTION LOOMS AS PLATFORM ISSUE Various interests groups, especially those that support abortion rights, plan to have their voices heard when the Republican national convention this summer in Philadelphia assembles a platform for this year's nominee. Over the weekend, Gov. Tommy G. Thompson of Wisconsin, the recently selected chairman of the platform committee, offered them some thoughts. After all the hearings are held and all the testimony is heard, he said, the presidential candidate will have the last word, not the committee. "I'm certainly going to be listening to all sectors of the party," Mr. Thompson told The Philadelphia Inquirer. "But my ultimate client is George Bush, and what he feels best is what we will do." On the matter of abortion, Mr. Thompson said he would abide by Mr. Bush's promises to leave the current plank, which strongly opposes abortion.   Irvin Molotsky (NYT)

931.text: GUN-6500 PRIMARY Illinois man charged with gun sales to racist killer A man was charged Wednesday with illegally selling guns, including the weapons used by Benjamin Nathaniel Smith during his murderous rampage. Federal agents raided Donald Fiessinger's apartment July 1, one day before Smith set out on a three-day shooting rampage targeting blacks, Jews and Asians that ended with his apparent suicide Sunday. Smith is believed to have bought two handguns from Fiessinger days earlier. Two people were killed and nine injured by Smith. Fiessinger, 64, was not arrested immediately after the raid on his apartment in the Illinois town of Pekin, 12 miles south of Peoria. After reading about Smith's weekend killings, he called authorities Tuesday to tell them he sold Smith the handguns, according to a Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms affidavit. Records found in Fiessinger's apartment included the sales to Smith. Fiessinger said Smith told him he was going to use the guns for hunting. He also said he didn't know of Smith's white supremacist beliefs, according to the affidavit. Elsewhere . . . TEXAS: A man whose nickname for himself was "Evil" was executed by injection Wednesday for killing a 26-year-old woman during a burglary of her home. Tyrone Fuller, 35, was convicted of killing the woman who was stabbed 44 times and raped. A pathologist determined she likely lived for several hours after the 1988 attack but couldn't scream for help because one of the stab wounds severed her vocal cords.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

932.text: GUN-1879 PRIMARY In Heart of Gun Country, Utah Is Changing Its Mind Like starting to drive or date, owning a gun has long been a rite of passage for teen-agers in Utah, a state where many schools once closed during deer hunting season to accommodate the sport. But a recent wave of violence has horrified residents of this famously prim-and-proper city, where street gangs were unknown only a decade ago, and prompted what was traditionally scorned as downright unpatriotic: gun control. In a special session of the Utah Legislature, called this week to address gang violence that has more than doubled in the past year, lawmakers outlawed the sale of firearms to minors, unless accompanied by adults. And last month Salt Lake City imposed a five-day waiting period on the purchase of weapons by anyone 25 years of age or under. "Ten years ago everyone had a knee-jerk reaction to anything with the scent of gun control," said Gov. Mike Leavitt, a Republican. "But the insanity of 13-and 14-year-old kids running around with handguns has caused all of us to rethink some things." Two More Shot and Wounded Less than 12 hours after the Legislature adjourned, two teen-agers were shot and wounded today by gang members outside a Salt Lake City high school. The stricter measures in Utah, which follow a recent ban on possession of handguns by minors in Colorado, appear to signal a dramatic political change in the heart of gun country, the American West.

928.text: GUN-4363 PRIMARY WEST HOLLYWOOD GUN BAN MAKES NEIGHBORS TAKE NOTICE This tiny city has made a big move toward curbing access to cheap handguns, and its new ordinance has sparked a regional movement to ban the sale of "Saturday night specials" in other towns. Neighboring municipalities are considering sales bans similar to the one West Hollywood passed last week. Los Angeles is studying such a measure. Compton has given initial approval to such a ban, and Huntington Park seems close to doing the same. "I think there is going to be an absolute flood of cities passing ordinances like ours," said West Hollywood Councilman Paul Koretz, a co-author of the ban. "It's very exciting. It's exactly what we hoped would happen. Perhaps by 1997 . . . we can build the momentum to pass this statewide." The measure will take effect in West Hollywood, a city of 1.9 square miles, on Feb. 16. But the battle is far from won. The National Rifle Association and the 65,000-member California Rifle and Pistol Association say they are planning lawsuits against West Hollywood, claiming the city illegally seized the state's power to regulate firearm sales. In neighboring Los Angeles, the police department last year confiscated 1,437 Saturday night specials during criminal investigations. The inexpensive guns were found at the scenes of 64 murders, 56 robberies, 109 assaults and a host of other crimes. Twenty were taken to schools.

929.text: GUN-3772 PRIMARY THE NRA RELOADS ON GUN CONTROL WASHINGTON   The National Rifle Association took another angry swipe at President Clinton and gun control yesterday, saying the White House is trying to create a police state. "Before Bill Clinton pushes legislation that takes away from our freedoms and creates a police state, we've urged that members of Congress hold town meetings in their home districts to hear what you have to say first," the NRA told its members in a letter mailed last week. The missive drew immediate scorn from the White House and gun-control advocates coming so soon after NRA Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre retreated from an earlier money-seeking letter that called federal agents "jackbooted government thugs." "It's strange that one day they say they want to tone down their rhetoric and then the next they use such extreme language about our bill," White House spokeswoman Mary Ellen Glynn said. "This proves the NRA's word cannot be trusted," said Kristen Rand of the Violence Policy Center. "The NRA's apology to law enforcement was meaningless." President Clinton and former President George Bush, who resigned his lifetime NRA membership, have denounced repeatedly the group's inflammatory language. LaPierre last week issued a narrow apology before the NRA's national convention. But with the latest NRA screed in hand, Brooklyn Democrat Chuck Schumer fired off his own heated rhetoric at a news conference: "The NRA is pushing visions of Nazi Germany and jackbooted thugs."

933.text: GUN-10121 PRIMARY D.C. Residents Rally To Preserve Gun Bans; Some Speakers Defend Right to Arm Amid the rapid clip of anti-gun speeches last night, Eric Wright fired back. The five-year city resident stepped before a microphone and said he had been attacked three times by homeless people and shot at as he drove through Rock Creek Park. "I deserve the right to defend myself. I deserve the right to carry a weapon by my side," Wright said at an anti-gun town hall meeting at Shaw Junior High School in Northwest Washington. "I'd like to know why people believe I don't have that right." Del. Eleanor Holmes Norton (D-D.C.), a sponsor of the event, responded by questioning whether guns offer citizens much protection. And she wondered aloud whether Wright, if he had been armed, would have stopped his car and started firing back. More than 200 people, including Mayor Anthony A. Williams (D) and Police Chief Charles H. Ramsey, attended the meeting, designed to rally opposition to congressional efforts to abolish various D.C. gun laws. Overwhelmingly, residents, some of whom had lost loved ones to gun violence, delivered passionate speeches, speaking of a direct correlation between guns and crime. Among the speakers was the Rev. Anthony Black, the father of Antre-Vyn Mason-Black, an Iraq war veteran who was fatally shot last month in the District. But Wright's remarks, echoed by a few others in attendance, provided a rare public glimpse of the pro-gun contingent in the District, which politicians say represents a small minority. Some waved yellow signs urging repeal.

936.text: NA-42052 PRIMARY San Ramon Valley letters: Anti-gun rant was shallow Anti-gun rant was shallow A letter writer (May 30) promoted a vacuous, ludicrous notion that there is nothing inherently virtuous about gun ownership and usage. I can assure him that there is nothing inherently virtuous about opposing gun ownership and usage. A day hardly passes without news of lawlessness and crimes perpetrated by individuals and guns in virtually all areas of the United States. Some states are releasing convicts because of space considerations. Those who fail to recognize or are ignorant of the fact that attacks and other crimes against citizens and property are serious issues are potential sitting ducks naively awaiting attacks by the voracious beasts. Responsible gun ownership and proper usage of guns by citizens to repel the savage outlaws is "inherently virtuous" beyond description. The writer cites the "bullying tactics" of the NRA. What hogwash. The NRA conducts innumerable seminars nationwide on gun safety and responsibility. There will never be a "gun-safe society" because conscienceless criminals do not obey "gun control" laws. I wonder if the writer is intelligent and honest enough to acknowledge this irrefutable truth? Lanny Middings San Ramon Tax sodas, fight obesity Support SB 622, a bill designed to tax sugary drinks in the state of California! This bill is important because 17 percent of children and a quarter of the adult population in our state are obese. This bill targets sugary drinks that are a huge factor in the rise of obesity. A Harvard study found that soda taxes, like this one, can be expected to reduce consumption of the targeted drinks by 10 percent. This would save the state in adult health care costs, as obesity-related problems already cost our state $15.2 billion, according to the U.S. Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality, making this no longer a matter of personal responsibility but a social concern. I urge you to contact your state senators by calling or emailing their offices in support of this measure.

934.text: GUN-20190 PRIMARY GOP tax plan details end of the 'marriage penalty' Republican congressional leaders said they soon will name members of a panel to resolve differences in the House and Senate juvenile justice bills. House Majority Whip Tom DeLay, R-Texas, said the final legislation likely will contain a ban on the importation of high-capacity weapons clips and a requirement that handguns be sold with safety devices like trigger locks. These gun-control measures were passed after the shootings April 20 at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo. Senate Majority Leader Trent Lott, R-Miss., said the conference committee might not get started until after the August recess because Sen. Bob Smith of New Hampshire has vowed to block appointment of the conference members. Smith, a presidential candidate who renounced his Republican Party membership Tuesday to become an independent, opposes all gun control. Meanwhile, about 80 Colorado students, including some from Columbine, are due in Washington today for a gun-control lobbying campaign. They meet with President Clinton at the White House on Thursday. -- Kathy Kiely

938.text: GUN-20050 PRIMARY Justice weighing options on ruling on police gun ban WASHINGTON -- The Justice Department said Tuesday that it is deciding how to proceed after an appeals court struck down a controversial federal law that prohibited police officers with misdemeanor domestic abuse convictions from carrying a gun. The opinion, issued Friday by a three-judge panel of the federal appeals court for the District of Columbia, said that the Lautenberg Law was unconstitutional because it unfairly requires states to disarm police officers with misdemeanor domestic abuse convictions while specifying no punishment for officers who may have more serious felony convictions. The panel did not rule on whether a law banning firearms possession by anyone convicted of any domestic violence crime would be constitutional. Gregory King, a spokesman at the Justice Department, said that officials there were reviewing the ruling. The Department could drop the issue, ask for a rehearing before the full appeals court or appeal to the Supreme Court. The domestic-abuse gun ban of 1996 was initiated by Sen. Frank Lautenberg, D-N.J., as a way to close a loophole in the Gun Control Act of 1968. Though the 1968 law automatically barred convicted felons from owning firearms, most states considered domestic abuse offenses involving physical violence or the threatened use of a deadly weapon less-serious misdemeanors. The 1968 law also gave police a special exemption. No such exemption existed for the Lautenberg law. The Fraternal Order of Police, which represents 270,000 of the nation's 600,000 law enforcement officers, had appealed the lower court ruling. The group said the law unfairly punished officers because it endangered their jobs.

937.text: GUN-6992 PRIMARY And Now the Zoo THAT BULLETS could fly at the National Zoo on Easter Monday was horrifying but, in a country that lets its handguns do the talking, sadly unsurprising. This time, the bullets hit seven young people, one an 11-year-old boy shot in the head. Police said the shooter was part of a group of youths involved in a running scuffle with another group. Youths have always scuffled; but today, scuffles too often turn into tragedies that couldn't happen were guns not so accessible. The pro-gun lobbyists try to shift the subject away from the weapons to youth violence in general. They will say that to cite the shootings at the zoo in the nation's capital as more evidence of the need to control guns is to exploit the incident. We don't pretend for a minute that any new law will bring an immediate end to school shootings or church shootings or zoo shootings. Nor do we dismiss efforts to get at the root causes of extreme violence in youth. But nothing beats easily carried and concealed guns for efficiency when it comes to wounding and killing people quickly and in great numbers. It is true that the District of Columbia's ban on handgun sales and possession has not stopped the shootings here. Handguns travel; without effective, uniform federal controls in place, guns will be available. They will keep going off in the streets and in private homes, in the hands of criminals, unstable people, curious children and older kids in fights.

927.text: GUN-9982 PRIMARY Officials Ready to Trade Fire Over Assault Weapons Bill With the federal ban on assault weapons set to expire in September -- and little sign that the Republican-led Congress will extend it -- gun-control advocates are taking their case to Annapolis. Several Howard County officials are gearing up for leading roles in the debate on whether Maryland should adopt a statewide ban. Last week, dozens of local officials in Maryland, including three Democrats on the Howard County Council, sent a letter to House Speaker Michael E. Busch (D-Anne Arundel) and Senate President Thomas V. Mike Miller Jr. (D-Calvert) supporting a bill to outlaw the rapid-fire weapons in Maryland. "Assault weapons are the guns of choice for drug dealers and cop killers," the letter said, which was signed by council members Guy Guzzone (Southeast County), David A. Rakes (East Columbia) and Ken Ulman (West Columbia). The bill is being sponsored by Del. Neil F. Quinter (D-Howard), who has made it one of his top legislative priorities this year. Quinter said he expects Howard delegates Shane E. Pendergrass, Elizabeth Bobo and Frank S. Turner, all Democrats, to support his measure. Some Howard County Republicans, however, hold a different view. Del. Warren E. Miller said he plans to do all he can to defeat Quinter's measure. "I think Neil and a few others are trying to make a name for themselves" Miller said. "I don't think there is really a need for the law. I think the existing [gun] laws on the books in Maryland are very severe."

characters: Senate President Thomas V. Mike Miller Jr.

935.text: GUN-30727 PRIMARY Clause and Effect LAST month, the Supreme Court agreed to consider District of Columbia v. Heller, which struck down Washington's strict gun ordinance as a violation of the Second Amendment's ''right to keep and bear arms.'' This will be the first time in nearly 70 years that the court has considered the Second Amendment. The outcome of the case is difficult to handicap, mainly because so little is known about the justices' views on the lethal device at the center of the controversy: the comma. That's right, the ''small crooked point,'' as Richard Mulcaster described this punctuation upstart in 1582. The official version of the Second Amendment has three of the little blighters: A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed. The decision invalidating the district's gun ban, written by Judge Laurence H. Silberman of the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit, cites the second comma (the one after ''state'') as proof that the Second Amendment does not merely protect the ''collective'' right of states to maintain their militias, but endows each citizen with an ''individual'' right to carry a gun, regardless of membership in the local militia. How does a mere comma do that? According to the court, the second comma divides the amendment into two clauses: one ''prefatory'' and the other ''operative.'' On this reading, the bit about a well-regulated militia is just preliminary throat clearing; the framers don't really get down to business until they start talking about ''the right of the people ... shall not be infringed.''

939.text: GUN-5069 PRIMARY DON'T LOOK TO NRA TO EVER BE A LIFESAVER Here we go again - another shooting in which students are killed at school, this time in Arkansas. Recently, National Rifle Association President Marion Hammer complained about the new legislation being pushed to have background checks at Florida gun shows to show whether purchasers are felons or have been adjudicated by a court as being legally mentally deficient. This is the same Ms. Hammer who, when interviewed by John F. Kennedy Jr. for his George magazine last year, responded to the question, "How do you feel when you hear of children (one every two hours) in this country dying of gunshot wounds?" by giving a typical brainless NRA reply, "Children die from choking on Life Savers (approximately 60 per year), and no one bans Life Savers." When are we going to realize that the gun nuts' assertion that every idiot has the right to "bear arms" is a convoluted, special-interest statement the founders of this country would have fought, much as we should right now? When it comes time to vote this November, don't forget that the Republicans fought the Brady law and the ban on semiautomatic weapons. Madie Fillion Stuart

940.text: GUN-6759 PRIMARY Principal to discuss security Columbine High Principal Frank DeAngelis met this week with  nearly 30 parents concerned about security and communication  issues. The parents have circulated a letter asking administrators to  make sure doors are monitored more closely, students get tolerance  training and parents are more informed if threats occur. DeAngelis said Wednesday, after meeting with the parents the  night before, that one of their top concerns is communication. 'They want to make sure, as we do, that we provide the safest  environment for our students,' the principal said. It currently takes the school more than 30 hours to notify  the parents of all 2,000 students by voice mail of threats and  other emergencies. DeAngelis said the school is working on a system that allows  notification in four to five hours. Gun control a priority President Clinton, who has expressed frustration over  congressional inaction on gun legislation since the Columbine High  School massacre, plans to make gun control a top priority next  year, aides said Wednesday. A group of senior administration officials met at the White  House to discuss new legislation the president might seek from  Congress in January, as well as ways he could use his executive  authority to push for what officials have called 'common-sense'  gun-control measures. Asked what the president could do without congressional  approval, White House spokesman Joe Lockhart told reporters, 'It's  something that we're looking at now.' He said the administration  would provide more details next year.

942.text: GUN-30988 PRIMARY D.C. Tries to Finesse Gun Ruling Mayor Adrian Fenty and his feisty attorney general, Peter Nickles, stood on the steps of the Wilson Building this week ostensibly to announce how the District will comply with the Supreme Court's rejection of Washington's ban on handguns. But really, they were delivering very much the opposite message: With only the narrowest of exceptions, we're sticking with our gun ban. Don't like it? Sue us. "I am pretty confident that the people of the District of Columbia want us to err in the direction of trying to restrict guns," Fenty told me, smiling broadly at the suggestion that what he's really trying to do is make it as hard as possible for Washingtonians to keep a loaded gun at home. Fenty and Nickles reject any interpretation of the court's decision as a clear statement that Americans may, with very few exceptions, keep and bear what Justice Antonin Scalia called "the quintessential self-defense weapon," the handgun. Rather, the D.C. officials read the decision as an almost academic ruling that although there may be a constitutional right to bear arms to protect yourself, that right is pretty much limited to folks whose house is being broken into right this very second. The court ruled that there is "no doubt" that "the Second Amendment conferred an individual right to keep and bear arms." But Nickles, the acting attorney general, said that "it's clear the Supreme Court didn't intend for you to have a loaded gun around the house. I don't think the court thought this was going to become a Wild West scene."

943.text: GUN-7992 PRIMARY MORE LAWS WON'T STOP THE WORLD'S MCDERMOTTS In response to Thursday's editorial "Powerful guns require controls": Here we go again - the call by the media for knee-jerk policy-making regarding gun control. Except that the irony of this case is that the jurisdiction in which Michael McDermott's massacre occurred has some of the tightest gun-control laws, especially for the AK-47, which caused the most damage. It also has had gun-buyback programs. So what's your point? If we keep doing what we've always done (enacting useless gun-control laws), we'll get what we've been getting (people who find a way around them and still commit crimes). Every time horrific tragedies like this happen, the media and subsequently legislators wanting to placate the media and the public keep looking to the same ineffective answers. Instead of rehashing ineffective rhetoric, why don't the media lead the way in opening up new discourse and encourage people to think outside the same old tired box? BETH McELROY Jupiter

947.text: GUN-2975 PRIMARY No bill better than a bad bill These are tough days for state Sen. David Scott (D-Atlanta). For years, Scott has tried to persuade his fellow legislators to enact an instant background check. For most of those years, he was tilling barren soil. Scott's only ally in the beginning was common sense. But gun control is an emotional issue, and common sense wasn't enough. Then, as the toll taken by firearms began to grow, public sentiment also swung to Scott's side. But even that wasn't enough to overcome the grip of the National Rifle Association. Finally, this year, with passage of the Brady Act in Washington, Scott had logic, overwhelming public opinion and political momentum on his side. And even that may not be enough. The state House of Representatives is refusing to enact an instant background check unless other gun laws are weakened in return. Specifically, the House insists that the Legislature strip local governments of the right to impose waiting periods on gun purchasers. It is a deal Scott has refused to accept in the past. As a legislator representing an urban area, he has long understood the importance of giving local government the ability to respond to local conditions. Furthermore, these are strange times to weaken gun laws. Instead, Scott and his fellow senators on a House-Senate conference committee have offered a significant compromise: Wipe out waiting periods in the 10 localities where they exist, but allow local governments to institute "cooling-off periods" of up to five days. (Six of the 10 current waiting periods are for 15 days.)

944.text: GUN-6942 PRIMARY Council Passes Bill Requiring Use of Trigger Locks on Guns The City Council voted yesterday to approve legislation requiring all gun owners to use safety devices designed to prevent accidental shootings by locking a weapon's trigger. The measure, which passed unanimously at a meeting of the full Council yesterday, was sponsored by Councilman Sheldon S. Leffler of Queens and is supported by Mayor Rudolph W. Giuliani. The new law would broaden existing legislation that requires that safety devices be installed on all pistols that are sold or otherwise change hands in New York City. The existing law, passed in 1998, was called "Christopher's Law" in memory of Christopher Murphy, an 11-year-old from Staten Island who died from an accidental gunshot wound. Under the new legislation, the devices would be required for all weapons, and gun owners would be required to lock the triggers of all firearms whenever they are not in use. The locks can cost as little as $2 to $3, and are available from licensed gun dealers, officials said. Gov. Christine Todd Whitman signed a measure into law on Tuesday prohibiting the sale of any new handgun in New Jersey unless it is accompanied by a trigger lock. A commission appointed by Mr. Giuliani to change the City Charter has put forth a proposal that is virtually identical to the Council legislation. Mr. Leffler said the law would make the Charter change unnecessary. "We have the legislative authority to do just what the Charter proposal wants," he said.

941.text: GUN-9461 PRIMARY Ex-lobbyist to testify against gun industry A former gun lobbyist has agreed to testify for the NAACP in a lawsuit alleging that negligent marketing practices by gun-makers and distributors fuel street violence that victimizes minorities. It will be the first time Robert Ricker, once an attorney for the National Rifle Association, has testified for gun opponents since he switched allegiance. "He's a true insider," said Elisa Barnes, who will represent the NAACP at the federal trial, set to begin Monday. "He met and worked with the heads of all these companies." The lawsuit alleges that irresponsible marketing of handguns has "led to disproportionate numbers of injuries, deaths and other damages among those whose interests the [NAACP] represents." Unlike other gun-liability cases across the country, the civil-rights group seeks no monetary damages. Instead, it asks for injunctions that would place sweeping restrictions on buyers and sellers of handguns. Industry advocates deny allegations by Ricker that gun-makers knowingly sell their products to dealers who supply criminals. Lawrence Keane, general counsel of the National Shooting Sports Foundation, called Ricker's allegations "outrageous and highly offensive." "He doesn't scare me in the least," Keane said. Ricker, 52, made a living as an attorney for the NRA in the 1980s, and later as an executive director of the American Shooting Sports Council, a defunct trade group. He had a falling-out with industry executives in the late 1990s after advocating a series of voluntary gun-safety measures.

945.text: GUN-7846 PRIMARY School aid plans caught in cross fire Gun issues loaded into education debate WASHINGTON - Senate Democrats, angry that gun trigger locks  and other safety measures remain stalled in juvenile justice  legislation, now have a new vehicle big and popular enough to push  their cause for weeks: education. And, as debate begins today, federal school aid programs may  get caught in the cross hairs. 'You can't separate safety and security in schools from the  issue of the availability and the accessibility of guns,' Sen.  Edward Kennedy, D-Mass., said in an interview. ' The public  understands that school violence prevention is a complex issue.   The American people are increasingly demanding that we take action.' On Friday, the two sides set up 'war rooms' in the Capitol to  highlight their differences on who should control $ 20 billion in  federal K-12 grants. The Democrats replicated a classroom with  student desks and chairs to hold interviews and Internet chats  that coincide with President Clinton's Midwestern schools tour,  promoting class-size reduction and after-school funding. Republicans planned online chats and events to tout the  flexibility they say they're giving parents and schools with  voucher proposals and block grants. 'There is a clear difference between us on who should set the  priorities; people are going to want to know both sides,' said  Joseph Karpinski, a spokesman for Sen. James Jeffords, R-Vt.,  chairman of the Senate Health Education Labor and Pensions  Committee. Jeffords is bringing the main proposal to his  colleagues and will oversee the debate. Kennedy is the leading  Democrat on the panel.

946.text: GUN-365 PRIMARY The Region; JERSEY OPENS DEBATE ON GUN CONTROL BILL More than 500 people, most of them members of sportsmen's and gun clubs, crowded the Assembly chambers today to debate the merits of a bill that would ban the sale or importation of handguns in New Jersey. The measure was praised as a needed first step toward reducing gunrelated crimes by Patrick J. Murphy, the former New York City Police Commissioner, and attacked by gun enthusiasts. In Hartford, the judiciary committee of Connecticut's General Assembly released a bill calling for judges to state in writing his reasons for not imposing a sentence of at least one year on a person found with an unlicensed handgun. And in Albany, Governor Carey re-Special to the New York Times newed his call for national handgun control legislation. Merlino Sponsors Bill The actions in the New York area were given new meaning by the assassination attempt on President Reagan yesterday.

949.text: GUN-9604 PRIMARY CONGRESS CORRECT TO LET WEAPONS LAW SUNSET The Sept. 22 letter, "Republicans holstered as weapons ban expires," shows that you can have an opinion and not know anything about the subject. I wonder whether the writer realized that the ban did not take one of the millions of the 19 semiautomatics named in the law off the streets. All the law did was prevent the future manufacture of semiautomatics with certain features, such as the bayonet mount. I have to admit, there has been no drive-by bayoneting since 1994. The manufacturers didn't mind because they could crank out rifles faster by not having to machine-tool flash hiders and bayonet mounts. A real assault weapon is a military machine gun that has been restricted since the 1930s. The rifles covered by the ban were civilian models (semiautomatics) that were dressed up to look like the military counterparts. Sort of like putting a Rolls-Royce grille on a Volkswagen. It's still a Volkswagen. Congress was correct in not extending a useless law that did nothing but lose the Democrats their majority in Congress in 1994. Who says the House and Senate do not listen to public opinion? JAMES V. STUCCHI West Palm Beach

950.text: GUN-7780 PRIMARY Pawnshop hit with indictment 37 counts allege 'straw' deals on gun sales A federal grand jury slapped a 37-count indictment  Wednesday on two owners and an employee of an Aurora pawnshop,  charging that they illegally sold firearms to 'straw buyers,' then  falsified records of those sales. Named in all counts are Gregory Golyansky, 40, and his  brother Leonid Golyansky, 51, proprietors of ABC Loan on East  Colfax Avenue. Also charged are the Golyanskys' cousin, pawnshop  worker Dmitriy Baravik, 31, and the store itself. 'This indictment should send a message to all federally  licensed firearms dealers that the Brady background-check  requirements cannot be thwarted through sham or straw  transactions,' said U.S. Attorney Tom Strickland. ' Failure to comply with these requirements will subject these dealers to  severe consequences.' Gregory Golyansky said he was surprised by the indictment,  adding that he will try to keep the shop open under a federal law  protecting gun dealers facing criminal prosecution. He otherwise  refused to comment, lobbing questions to defense attorney Stephen  Peters. 'This is a sad day for justice,' Peters said. 'My clients are  law-abiding citizens who left the former Soviet Union to live in a  democratic society. They look forward to their day in court and to  vindicating their interest in this case.' The Denver Post highlighted ABC Loan in a December expose  revealing that the tiny pawnshop ranked among the nation's leading  sellers of inexpensive firearms - commonly identified as Saturday  night specials - to customers buying more than one handgun at a  time.

948.text: GUN-4120 PRIMARY SUPREME COURT TO REVIEW GUN AND SEX-OFFENDER LAWS< POLICE MUST CHECK OUT THOSE WHO WANT TO BUY HANDGUNS. < STATES CAN HOSPITALIZE SOME VIOLENT FELONS. The Supreme Court, building a docket of major issues for the term that begins in October, agreed yesterday to decide the constitutionality of laws aimed at potentially dangerous handgun buyers and at sex offenders. The justices said they would examine whether the Brady law, which requires local police to make background checks on people who want to buy handguns, is an improper federal invasion of state and local powers. At the same time, the high court accepted a constitutional test of state statutes that allow judges to confine "sexually violent offenders" to mental hospitals, even after they have served prison terms for sexual crimes. With the addition of the new cases, the 1996-97 court term is shaping up as an important one. The justices already have agreed to examine the validity of requiring English as a state's official language, and are considering cases on such issues as doctor-assisted suicide and affirmative action in college admissions. The current term is expected to end by July. The Brady law, which took effect in 1994 after bitter controversy in Congress and strong opposition from the National Rifle Association, was enacted to combat a nationwide "epidemic of gun violence." The law provides a five-day waiting period before a federally licensed gun dealer may transfer a handgun to a buyer. During the waiting period, local law enforcement officers must make a "reasonable" check of official records to find out whether the buyer is a felon, a drug addict, a fugitive, an illegal immigrant, a mental defective, a spouse under a court restraining order, or a dishonorably discharged member of the armed forces. Such a person is ineligible to receive a handgun.

951.text: GUN-2749 PRIMARY GUN FANATICS ARE STAINED WITH BLOOD In answer to the writer of the letter ''Rapid-fire gun story misfired on facts,'' why are people who are opposed to guns called ''anti-gun nuts'' and gun-toting machos are ''free Americans''? The writer says criminals are laughing about bringing the sale of guns to a halt. Yes, the real criminals are laughing: They are the gun manufacturers, the gun distributors, the National Rifle Association and the fanatics who think America must be the gun capital of the world. The advocates for guns have caused numerous deaths and injuries, and this is only the tip of the iceberg.   We are turning into a Wild West shoot-'em-up society. Gun-happy partisans should try looking into their mirrors, and they will clearly see America's real criminals. And that is not a laughing matter. N. Cohen Boynton Beach

956.text: GUN-3053 PRIMARY BULLET CONTROL' WON'T WORK, EITHER A recent letter stated that the 1993 federal handgun law, formerly known as the Brady bill, would not successfully keep guns out of the hands of hoodlums and that another bill was needed to keep bullets out of the hands of hoodlums. Good suggestion. But answer this: If the new law won't keep hoodlums from getting guns, how is the next bill going to keep hoodlums from getting bullets? The only way to keep hoodlums from using these illegal guns and bullets is by passing and enforcing stiff laws on violent crime and by making prisons uncomfortable places to be (elimination of entertainment, etc.). Jonathan J. Carroll Jupiter

953.text: GUN-445 PRIMARY Tame the White Panthers Once the White Panthers in San Francisco were peace marchers. Now a group of 20 have turned to wild ideas and predatory politics. They are stalking Mayor Dianne Feinstein because she sponsored an ordinance, since ruled invalid under state law, banning handguns. The Panthers have collected enough signatures to require a special election in April, which will cost the city $450,000. Like gun zealots of the right, the radical Panthers assert that the Second Amendment guarantees citizens the right to bear arms. It doesn't. As recently as this week, in an appeal involving the ban on handguns in Morton Grove, Ill., a Federal court ruled that the Second Amendment protects only the right of states to maintain a militia. The White Panthers apparently have their own reasons for opposing gun control: several members have had armed confrontations with the police. But they are on shaky ground when they claim to act on behalf of the poor, arguing that the poor need handguns because they get no protection from the police. Studies of crime do show that the poor are the most frequent victims of crime. But it's silly to think more guns will help them.   Using a gun to stop an armed criminal takes training and alertness.   Most civilians, whatever their income level, are likely to lack both, and their personal handguns are bound to end up in the hands of burglars, further expanding the criminal arsenal.

955.text: NA-43586 PRIMARY The link between gun rights and the Gospels? It's still missing. According to the startling results of a survey released last week by the Public Religion Research Institute, 57 percent of white evangelicals live in homes where someone owns a gun (compared, for example, with 31 percent of Catholics.) And more startling, even after 20  first-graders were slaughtered in Connecticut at the hands of a madman with an assault rifle, 59 percent of white evangelicals continue to oppose tighter restrictions on gun laws. An obvious question occurs in light of these results: How do such Christians reconcile their stalwart commitment to the Second Amendment with their belief in a gospel that preaches nonviolence? The Christian Lord allowed himself to be crucified rather than fight the injustice of the death sentence imposed on him. "If someone strikes you on the right cheek, turn to him the other also," he says, in the Gospel of Matthew. The Bible is mute on the matter of guns, of course, but it is impossible to imagine that Jesus would find anything good to say about them. With the Newtown tragedy so fresh and its victims so innocent, conservative Christian leaders are not falling over themselves to proclaim in public their pro-gun theologies. Neverthless, such arguments do exist. I will address some of them, moving generally from unpersuasive credos to more-convincing assertions of individual rights and responsibilities. The Second Amendment is approved by God. This, at least, appears to be the argument on the home page of the Christian Gun Owner Web site. It goes like this: The authors of the Constitution were acting under the guidance of God, therefore the Constitution is itself inspired by God. This argument is a subset of the bigger "American exceptionalism" worldview. God has special things in store for this country, and its founding documents bear the imprint of that specialness.

957.text: GUN-8535 PRIMARY Gun regulations Most notably, the Supreme Court in U.S. vs. Miller (1939) found no evidence that a sawed-off shotgun had "some reasonable relationship" to a well-regulated militia and the Second Amendment didn't convey the right to such a weapon. The Supreme Court confirmed its stance on the Second Amendment with Quilici vs. Village of Morton Grove (1982). Morton Grove's ban on possession of handguns was challenged on Second Amendment grounds. Appeals failed in district and federal appeals courts. The Supreme Court refused to hear an appeal, supporting the lower court's ruling. The Second Amendment does not prevent regulation of firearms, and most Americans want stricter gun laws. CONNIE PADGETT Chapel Hill

954.text: GUN-5791 PRIMARY House Votes to Weaken Senate Gun Show Checks The House last night approved a plan to weaken some of the existing rules for background checks at gun shows, in a vote that revealed the enduring power of pro-gun forces in Congress. The action signaled a tactical victory for the National Rifle Association, House Republicans and some conservative Democrats, who appeared to be succeeding in forestalling more aggressive proposals for regulating gun shows -- an idea that has gained momentum after the Littleton, Colo., school shootings. On a 218 to 211 vote, the House endorsed a proposal by Rep. John D. Dingell (D-Mich.) to water down a Senate measure requiring background checks for all weapons sales at gun shows. By 235 to 193, the House also shot down a competing proposal that more closely resembled the Senate plan, which is favored by gun control advocates. The Dingell plan would narrow the range of gun shows that are now subject to federal background check requirements. The amendment also would limit all background checks at gun shows to 24 hours, rather than the 72 hours the law now gives federally licensed dealers to make sure felons do not buy weapons. Dingell's plan would, however, close what people on both sides of the debate agree is a loophole in current law, requiring nonlicensed dealers to begin conducting such background checks. With more votes looming this morning, both on other gun amendments and on the overall gun bill, it remained possible that the House would adopt another plan or scrap the whole gun control enterprise altogether. Lawmakers also will consider a proposal to overturn the District's ban on handgun ownership.

959.text: GUN-4044 PRIMARY CONCEALED CARRY DETERS CRIME COMMENTARY COLUMN A new report on the impact of laws allowing persons to carry concealed guns has gun control advocates shooting wildly and missing their target by a mile. The report, by law school professor John Lott and economist John Mustard, both of the University of Chicago, concluded "allowing citizens to carry concealed weapons deters violent crimes and it appears to produce no increase in accidental deaths. If those states that did not have right-to-carry concealed gun provisions had adopted them in 1992, appro ximately 1,570 murders; 4,177 rapes; and more than 60,000 aggravated assaults would have been avoided yearly." Think of it; that's more murders than the combined total of 21 states last year. That's more than 4,000 women last year alone who were violently sexually assaulted. In percentage terms, the authors found that when state concealed handgun laws went into effect in a county, murders fell by 8.5 percent, and rapes and aggravated assaults fell by 5 percent and 7 percent, respectively. (Thirty-one states now have such laws). Lott and Mustard, however, probably feel like they've been assaulted after the smearing they received in much of the press. For example, the director of the Illinois Council Against Handgun Violence, Dan Kotowski, blasted the study as biased because it was funded by the Olin Corp., which owns Winchester Inc., a firearms manufacturer. An Associated Press story and that of individual newspapers aired the Kotowski allegations as fact, but they were wrong.

958.text: GUN-9988 PRIMARY Report Details Crime Gun Sales; Safety Group Traces 15% of Weapons to Fraction of Dealers A relatively tiny number of gundealers in 22 states including Maryland and Virginia sold nearly 15 percent of the firearms traced by police to crimes nationwide in the late 1990s, according to a new study. The report, by the Americans for Gun Safety Foundation, which advocates stricter enforcement of gun control laws, found that 120 of the nation's 83,000 licensed firearms dealers -- one-tenth of 1 percent -- sold about 55,000 of the 373,000 guns confiscated by police from 1996 to 2000. The guns either were seized after crimes or recovered after being stolen. Of those 120 dealers, 11 are in Maryland and 13 in Virginia, according to the study, released yesterday. "There are a small number of gun stores that regularly supply the criminal market, and yet the government has turned a blind eye to those that traffic in firearms and crime," said Deborah Barron, spokeswoman for the foundation. The 11 Maryland dealers cited in the study sold 4,362 of the guns, and the 13 Virginia dealers sold 5,628 of the weapons, the foundation reported. The study does not indicate whether a particular gun was sold by a store shortly before a crime with which it is associated or was sold years earlier. Gun dealers and gun-rights advocates criticized the study yesterday, saying that dealers with high sales volumes will inevitably sell many guns that end up in police custody.

960.text: GUN-7342 PRIMARY Once again, gun owners are being demonized Re: Latest school shooting incident in Flint, Mich.   The blame game being played by the gun-control crowd is an example of knee-jerk politics and demonization of gun owners in general. Despite the fact that the gun used unfortunately to kill the 6-year-old girl was stolen and possibly traded for drugs, the prohibitionist school of thought goes straight for the idea that more regulations on honest people are the way to prevent tragedies. Perhaps this is a means of getting revenge by way of collective guilt. Aside from that, does anyone honestly believe that a person so lacking in respect for the law and society as to deal drugs out of his home, to trade in stolen goods and so forth, is going to bother with trigger locks, not to mention registering his stolen weapons? As for the latter, the federal government, under the Constitution, has no basis for forcing the states to institute licensing or registration. This has nothing to do with stopping crime and is only a halfway measure toward confiscation. Nobody honestly believes that registering handguns will stop a single criminal from getting a gun. Furthermore, is Handgun Control Inc. going to fund the gun ranges, instructors and so forth needed to train people how to use weapons safely? Guess who has been doing that all along? The National Rifle Association. Of course, this "treat-guns-like-cars" talk is all baloney. We have the means to do "instant checks" at gun shops and gun shows. Burdening the public with more than that is simply punitive and unconstitutional.

952.text: GUN-10200 PRIMARY Metropolitan Area News in Brief Three critically hurt in Strawberry Mansion shooting Three men were in critical condition after being shot on a North Philadelphia street last night, police said. The three men, described as being in their 20s, were shot in the middle of Eyre Street in Strawberry Mansion about 9 p.m. Police said no other details were immediately available. The men were being treated at Temple University Hospital. - Stephanie L. Arnold Pa. House committee approves two handgun bills The state House Judiciary Committee yesterday approved two bills to strengthen Pennsylvania handgun laws. They are expected to come to a full vote in the House next month. One, sponsored by State Rep. Dwight Evans (D., Phila.), would require mandatory sentences for violent career criminals or drug traffickers who possess firearms. It would provide a mandatory five-year sentence for someone with two prior convictions for violent crimes or drug-trafficking offenses. A second bill, sponsored by State Rep. Jewell Williams (D., Phila.), would stiffen penalties for carrying a concealed weapon without a license. Evans predicted the bills will pass the full House when they come up for a vote next month. He said that both were supported by the Republican committee chairman, Rep. Dennis O'Brien of Philadelphia. - Nathan Gorenstein Motorist killed, another injured in Mayfair collision A head-on collision on Cottman Avenue in Mayfair early yesterday left a motorist fatally injured, police said. The cause of the accident is under investigation.

962.text: NA-42172 PRIMARY Berkeley legislator unveils bill to regulate California ammunition sales OAKLAND -- With calls for action resounding around the country after the Connecticut school shooting, a new bill to regulate and track ammunition sales could test just how far California is willing to go to tighten some of the nation's strictest gun control laws. While a national debate rages over whether to reinstate a federal assault weapons ban similar to California's, East Bay Assemblywoman Nancy Skinner wants the state to track every bullet and shotgun shell sold here. Other gun-control bills are being introduced in California in the wake of recent mass shootings, but AB 48 could be the most far-reaching, affecting every firearm owner in the Golden State. Law enforcement groups call the proposal overdue, but many hunters and gun-rights supporters call it silly. It's the kind of debate becoming familiar across the nation as lawmakers seize what many see as a watershed moment to advance tougher gun-control measures historically defeated by the powerful gun lobby. Noting that 2,800 Californians were slain by gunfire last year, Skinner, D-Berkeley, unveiled her bill Monday at a news conference in Oakland, a city plagued by gun violence. "We've had enough," she said. "Buying bullets should require the same scrutiny as buying guns." True Vine Ministries pastor Zachary Carey said his hometown of Oakland has seen 559 homicides in the past five years and almost 2,700 in the past 25 years, most of which involved guns. State data show San Jose had 165 homicides from 2008 through 2012, including 46 last year, the highest total in two decades.

964.text: GUN-2766 PRIMARY SIDES RELOAD AFTER VOTE ON WEAPONS BAN ALL'S QUIET ON THE GUN-CONTROL FRONT - BUT NOT FOR LONG. PROPONENTS PLAN TO SEEK FINGERPRINTING AND LICENSING. THE NRA SAYS IT HAS JUST BEGUN TO FIGHT. After Thursday's dramatic House vote to ban 19 types of assault weapons, both sides along the gun-control siege line are reloading for the next round. Flush with enthusiasm after the victory, gun-control proponents are hatching plans for follow-up measures - including proposals to require that all gun owners be licensed and fingerprinted. The National Rifle Association is promising continued efforts to fight the ban in the final crime bill - and threatening retaliation in November against legislators who crossed it on the bitterly contested vote. With only a single vote separating victory and defeat in Thursday's House showdown, NRA spokesman Bill McIntyre says the group is contemplating a renewed blitz to prevent inclusion of the assault-weapon ban in the final crime bill that emerges from a House-Senate conference committee. The Senate narrowly approved a nearly identical ban last September. "We are going to do what we can to make sure no gun ban becomes law," said McIntyre. "It's not over until it's over." White House officials say they take such threats seriously. "We're not done yet," said George Stephanopoulos, senior adviser to President Clinton. "We haven't gotten an assault-weapons ban yet. It would be wrong to be complacent." Even so, most observers believe that Congress, after all the agony of passing the ban, is unlikely to change course. "I think it is pretty much over," said Rep. Charles E. Schumer (D., N.Y.). "The House has spoken; the Senate has spoken; the public has spoken. It would take some pretty tricky legerdemain to (delete) it, and I don't think the leadership in the House or Senate will go along."

963.text: GUN-6221 PRIMARY DAVIS SIGNS BILLS TACKLING GUN SHOW SALES GUN CONTROL TIMES FIVE UNTIL now, gun shows have operated with near-impunity, mocking laws designed to keep firearms out of the wrong hands. Under-the-table sales have become as easy as drug deals at rock concerts -- a fast way to make a buck or to buy contraband weapons, no questions asked. That is about to end in California. Gun show regulations are among a quintet of gun-control laws that will enhance California's strictest-in-the-nation gun controls. Earlier this year Gov. Gray Davis signed measures outlawing assault weapons and limiting gun purchases to one a month. On Friday he signed the other three. Davis campaigned for two of them. One law will require that new firearms sold include trigger locks; it's intended to reduce accidental shootings, which killed 780 Californians and injured 8,000 others between 1991 and 1997. The other will ban cheap, unsafe handguns -- the "Saturday night specials -- by requiring safety standards and firing tests for all firearms sold in California. The third will give the attorney general's office and local officials more authority over gun shows. A companion bill would give the attorney general the money to hire 15 to 20 deputies for firearms enforcement, including policing gun shows. The gun-show bill contrasts with the approach taken last week by the supervisors in Los Angeles County, when they banned the sale of firearms on county property. Their aim is to deny continued access to the county fairgrounds to Great Western Shows, the nation's largest gun show, drawing as many as 50,000 people. Earlier this month, Alameda County supervisors passed a similar ordinance.

961.text: GUN-8946 PRIMARY Fear and loathing at 30,000 feet If the American anti-Second Amendment movement has achieved  anything at all, it has been the ability to malign entire groups  of people by painting them as unworthy to have access to legal  firearms. Decades of bombastic dialog with members of fanatic  gun-control organizations have impressed a basic truth on me. That is that members of the all-knowing anti-firearms  coalition don't believe any individual or group of Americans, from  sports hunters to police officers, are to be trusted with guns. Over the years, Americans have looked on in amazement while  anti-firearms campaigners like the Million Moms marchers have  coagulated in cities across our nation for what they believe to be  a good cause. No one can convince me their cause is anything but a  complete ban on every American's constitutional birthright to own  firearms. The newest group of people to fall under the propaganda  of fear and loathing so masterfully used by the anti-Second  Amendment clique are American commercial airline pilots. Desperate to keep legislation from becoming law that would  authorize airline pilots to arm themselves as the last line of  defense in the event of another Sept. 11-type attack, the  anti-firearms contingent has sought and gotten help from a  powerful benefactor: The New York Times. Carl Limbacher, writing for NewsMax.com, recently reported  a story under the tag 'The story behind the story.' It was  headlined, 'Times Continues Spin Against Arming Pilots.'

965.text: NA-42092 PRIMARY Sunnyvale voters to decide gun-control Measure C In one of the Bay Area's more disputed ballot battles of 2013, Sunnyvale voters on Tuesday might decide to go much further than state lawmakers and the governor did this year in imposing new gun controls. The Silicon Valley city of 146,000 is neither besieged by crime nor extremely liberal, yet it has become a new front in the national struggle over gun policy. With Congress paralyzed, gun safety measures "need to come from the ground up, not the top down," said Mayor Tony Spitaleri, Measure C's driving force. "We've got to start somewhere. We just can't accept the fact that this (gun deaths) is the norm now." Measure C would require gun owners to notify police within 48 hours of the loss or theft of their firearms; keep firearms locked up when not in the owner's immediate possession; and ammunition sellers to keep buyers' names for two years. It also would prohibit having ammunition magazines that hold more than 10 rounds. Each provision mirrors bills that failed this year in Sacramento. The National Rifle Association has vowed to sue if Measure C passes; the group also urges voters to consider the millions of dollars other cities have spent defending gun-control ordinances. The NRA is seeking cases it can take to the U.S. Supreme Court, and Measure C "presents a great opportunity for us," said Chuck Michel, the group's West Coast attorney.

966.text: GUN-20233 PRIMARY Rare alliance against guns NRA and its opponents back crackdown in Denver DENVER -- Opposite sides of the gun debate united in rare agreement Monday to endorse an aggressive push to prosecute gun crimes in federal court. Anti-handgun crusader Jim Brady and National Rifle Association Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre shared a stage with prosecutors, police and the father of a Columbine High School massacre victim to back Project Exile in Colorado, a new ground zero for gun control politics since last year's shootings in a Denver suburb. Colorado is at least the third state to adopt Project Exile, which combines vigorous prosecution of gun offenses with stark ads that threaten thugs with hard prison time. It was founded three years ago in Richmond, Va., where gun crimes have since dropped. A $ 1 million campaign on television, radio and billboards starts today in Denver with the tag line "Pack an illegal gun/Pack your bags for prison." "This will make your community a safer place," said Brady, who was shot in the attempt on President Reagan's life in 1981. LaPierre, who announced a $ 25,000 NRA donation to the Colorado project, said he wanted violent criminals and drug dealers "to know that the NRA is their worst enemy." He said that Project Exile's "magic" is that more criminals are choosing not to carry guns rather than risk five-year, no-parole federal prison terms. Tom Strickland, who was sworn in as U.S. attorney for Colorado the day after the Columbine shootings, said the campaign is "the one strategy that enjoys almost universal support." But hard-line gun advocates contended that law-abiding gun owners will be harassed while criminals go free.

967.text: GUN-7874 PRIMARY NEWS IN BRIEF Hoeffel will reintroduce legislation on antique guns Calling it a "common-sense extension" of federal gun-safety regulations, U.S. Rep. Joseph M. Hoeffel 3d announced yesterday that he was reintroducing legislation to require those buying antique guns to undergo the same background checks and waiting periods required for regular gun purchases. The Montgomery County Democrat introduced the legislation last term, pointing to a June 1999 hostage standoff at Norristown State Hospital in which a former employee killed a nursing supervisor and wounded a second with an antique firearm. But it never came to a vote in the House Judiciary Committee and died when the session ended. This time, with 15 Democratic cosponsors in the House, Hoeffel said the bill could perhaps piggyback legislation aimed at closing the gun-show loophole, which exempts buyers at gun shows and flea markets from waiting periods and background checks. Hoeffel said that while the measure "does pose an inconvenience for the lawful gun owner, I think that's something people can accept for a safer society." Cheltenham police seek help in search for missing woman WYNCOTE - Cheltenham Township police are asking for help in the search for an 83-year-old woman, who they said needs assistance to take daily medication for Alzheimer's disease and congestive heart failure. Marian D. Lawson, a resident of the Wyncote Church Home, was last seen about 10 a.m. Friday at the home, police said. Lawson, who sometimes uses her maiden name of Pizzullo, frequently takes trips on public transportation to Philadelphia, Atlantic City and Florida.

968.text: GUN-30933 PRIMARY Not Your 'Bud' or Ours A new report from Mayor Michael Bloomberg's office should end any debate about the need to close the lethal gap in federal law that allows purchasers at gun shows to buy weapons from unlicensed private dealers without submitting to a federal background check. Private investigators, hired by the Bloomberg administration, went to gun shows in Nevada, Ohio and Tennessee. Posing as buyers, they told the dealers that they probably could not pass a background check and secretly filmed the reaction. Unlicensed private dealers are not required to conduct checks, but they may not sell to people knowing their backgrounds are questionable. In almost three out of four instances, the investigators were still able to buy guns. In one video issued with the report, the dealer responds, smiling, that it does not matter, ''Because I wouldn't pass either, bud.'' In all, 19 of 30 private sellers failed the integrity test. No less troubling, 16 out of 17 federally licensed dealers -- who are required to conduct background checks -- willingly accepted straw purchases, in which sales are made to accomplices. These findings are consistent with a 300-page report published last month by the University of California at Davis Violence Prevention Research Program that documented the extensive problem of straw purchases and unrecorded private sales at gun shows. There has long been strong evidence that the gun show loophole has dire consequences for public safety. In 2000, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms found that nearly a third of the illegal guns recovered in gun trafficking investigations were originally purchased at a gun show.

969.text: GUN-4051 PRIMARY Sunday August 11, 1996: WEAPONS; Granny, Get Your Gun The National Rifle Association has to like Representative Gerald Solomon. During last spring's House debate over repealing the ban on assault weapons (the House voted to repeal; the Senate has yet to act), the New York Republican scolded a political opponent. "My wife lives alone five days a week in rural area in upstate New York," he said. "She has a right to defend herself when I'm not there. . . and don't you ever forget it." We're glad the Congressman's wife can handle herself, but the outburst also made us worry about the larger population of isolated rural women. When ruffians call, do they have the tools for adequate defense? Street Legal At present, our endangered lady - call her Granny - can legally get her hands on semiautomatic rifles. Each pull of the trigger fires single round; the rifle reloads automatically. (The assault ban forbids her from manufacturing semiautomatics with certain proscribed features.) Downside: As any N.R.A. member will tell you, criminals frequently tote fully automatic machine guns. Mano-a-granno, Granny goes down. The Upgrade If hard-liners get their way. Granny could obtain a fully automatic Uzi with a large clip, firing several hundred rounds per minute. Downside: Machine guns are difficult to control and aim - one slip and it's bye-bye to Granny's cherished collection of Lladro procelains. She might be better off with a perfectly legal .50-caliber rifle and scope. With practice, she could bag a meanie from a mile away.

972.text: GUN-8973 PRIMARY A straight shooter on gun-owner rights In addition to the undergraduate diploma and law degree, there is a large, framed certificate identifying Cliff Travis as a lifetime member of the National Rifle Association hanging on the wall of his downtown Inverness office. Travis received the certificate four days before he graduated from high school in 1976. "I've been an NRA member a lot longer than I've been a lawyer," Travis said, chuckling. Now the 47-year-old defense attorney has found a way to combine his knowledge of guns and the law. Travis is one of the only local lawyers who specializes in the law relating to guns and gun ownership. In fact, Travis recently attended the National Firearms Law Seminar in Reno, Nev., where lawyers throughout the country gathered to discuss topics ranging from constitutional rights for gunowners, product liability and legal ethics. The seminar, which is sponsored by the NRA, is designed for attorneys who represent clients who own, collect, sell, manufacture or import firearms, and those who wish to broaden their knowledge of the law as it relates to guns. Travis attended the seminar as part of his continuing legal education, which is required by the Florida Bar. "If you have to take time out of your office, would you rather sit through some sort of boring seminar on wills or trusts or something of interest?" Travis said. The conference in April was the second firearms seminar Travis attended. The first was held in Charlotte, N.C., in 2000.

975.text: GUN-3488 PRIMARY WILL GRIDLOCK WIN? COMMENTARY COLUMN The Washington gridlock Bill Clinton campaigned against may have cost him his anti-crime bill and threatens to take out health-care reform, too. If it manages both, the Clinton presidency will be effectively over. That, of course, has been the idea of the opposition all along, and never mind the merits of any legislation lost in the process. The gridlock that is being used against Clinton is compounded in about equal parts of special-interest lobbies and scorched-earth partisanship. In the case of the crime bill, the special interest, of course, was the gun lobby, led by the National Rifle Association. Fearful of the rising revulsion with firearms, the NRA pulled off a coup, enrolling the GOP as its ally against gun control. Thirty-eight Republicans who had voted to ban assault weapons turned tail under threat of losing party funding for their re-elections. Nearly two-score Democrats were cowed by the gunners, too, but as serious as that defection was, it was not the major cause of the crime bill's failure. Three out of four Democrats voted with the president. Only 11 Republicans lent themselves to it. The bill per se would be no great loss. It is far too punitive. Sixty or so new capital crimes, for heaven's sake. But this tough-on-crooks bill was just the kind that members of both parties usually rush to support, especially with the public in such a foul mood about crime.

971.text: GUN-4586 PRIMARY N.J. LAW; The Latest in Safety: 'Smart' Guns That Know Their Owners If two state senators have their way, all handguns sold in New Jersey may soon need to "recognize" their owner before a shot can be fired. They are called "smart guns," firearms that use radio waves and computer chips to forge an identity between gun owner and gun. In one prototype, the gun will not unlock its firing pin unless the owner is wearing a special magnetic ring.
