In another, the gun is useless unless the holder wears a wrist transponder emitting the right signal to an antenna imbedded in the handgrip. Manufacturers, owners and gun control advocates have praised the technology as a way to decrease accidental gun deaths and thwart thieves who steal guns and then use them in crimes. But they remain sharply divided over whether such guns should be mandated by law. "There are thousands of accidental gun deaths a year, there are teen suicides, there are guns stolen and used in crime," said Bryan Miller, executive director of Ceasefire NJ, a gun control lobby. "We believe this technology and this bill will save lives." But closely following the National Rifle Association view, David Caplan a gun owner in Madison, shot back that no one should be forced to buy personalized guns, adding that he would be afraid to depend on such a new technology, particularly one that would raise the cost of a firearm by $300 or more.

973.text: GUN-9016 PRIMARY Guns, a Health Threat To the Editor: Re "U.S., in a Shift, Tells Justices Citizens Have a Right to Guns" (front page, May 8): It shocks me how officials in the Bush administration -- Attorney General John Ashcroft and Solicitor General Theodore B. Olson, in this instance -- are using their personal feelings to change law and policies that have been in place for more than 60 years. The right to arm oneself has become a public health crisis in this country, not a referendum on freedom. How many more children, police officers and law-abiding citizens have to die because the gun lobby is a well-financed political machine? EMILY ROSENBERG Oakland, Calif., May 8, 2002

970.text: GUN-956 PRIMARY Rifle Group Fights Public Housing Gun Ban The National Rifle Association is challenging local public housing officials over a policy barring residents from keeping guns. The Chicago Housing Authority's gun ban has been on the books for 20 years but was not enforced until 1989. If a ban works in Chicago, "you'll probably find a lot of housing authorities jumping on it," said Marshall Kandell, a spokesman for the Housing Authority of the City of Los Angeles. The Philadelphia Housing Authority is considering such a ban, a spokeswoman said. The rifle association says law-abiding people who live in public housing have a constitutional right to bear arms, said Tom Wyld, a spokesman for the group in Washington. Banning guns has not stopped violence by people who own them illegally, he said. The rifle association protested the gun policy in Chicago in letter dated May 8 to its Housing Authority Director, Vince Lane. The group said the agency should not require people to waive their right to own a gun to gain public housing. More than 100 guns have been seized at Chicago housing projects in the last year. But the police counted 35 killings in which firearms were used last year in the Chicago projects, where 200,000 people live.

characters: Housing Authority of the City of Los Angeles

976.text: GUN-30702 PRIMARY Gun fans toss beanbags, eat corn and 'guess the bullet' at Glenwood City open-carry event The beanbag toss and corn on the cob were as well received as the "guess the bullet" game and a display of Wisconsin firearms laws during a Sunday picnic at the St. Croix County Fairground. Organizer Dave "Ole" Olson said crowd estimates were conservatively at about 350 for the "Open Carry Picnic" -- a mix of a typical outdoor barbecue and gun enthusiast rally. Spending well beyond his $2,000 budget and hoping to get some of that back from goodwill donations, Olson said he wanted to hold the event as a way to educate the public and gun owners about their rights. "Believe it or not, there's a lot of gun owners who don't know the laws," he said. UW-River Falls student and Ellsworth native Michael Morris, 21, admits he was one of those owners who didn't know all the permissions of Wisconsin's gun laws. "I didn't even know we could carry until I came here," he said at the picnic. He wore a holstered Heckler and Koch USP .40-caliber handgun, and his sister, Lauren, 19, attended the picnic with a .44-caliber Magnum revolver on her hip. "It's important to make people more aware," said Lauren Morris, also a UW-River Falls student. The two said some of Wisconsin's laws pose inconveniences to gun owners, namely how guns must remain unloaded and apart from ammunition while in a vehicle.

979.text: GUN-3718 PRIMARY Gun lobby has big deficit While waging battles against gun control on Capitol Hill, the National Rifle Association has been operating in the red for four years - and has been forced to deplete its cash reserves and investments drastically. Documents prepared for the NRA board of directors, and obtained by The Associated Press, show the powerful gun lobby has run cumulative operating deficits of $ 69 million between 1991 and 1993. An additional $ 3 million deficit is projected for last year. The largest expenditures, according to the documents, have been expensive fund-raising efforts and construction of a six-story headquarters in Fairfax, Va. NRA officials declined to comment. CABINET TRAVEL: Energy Secretary Hazel O'Leary generally spent more taxpayer money on travel than other Cabinet members, upgrading her flights and staying in four-star hotels, the Los Angeles Times reported. During her first two years as secretary, O'Leary spent $ 49,857 on 61 trips within the USA, excluding those on military or Energy Department aircraft. The median expense for such trips was $ 671 - 58% higher than for travel by Environmental Protection Agency administrator Carol Browner. O'Leary also travels with more aides than other Cabinet members do - at least two and as many as 10. She said her travel practices are appropriate, and she spent nearly $ 14,500 of her own money on official travel. O'Leary has announced plans to cut staff, close offices and reduce travel for her $ 17.5 billion agency.

characters: Environmental Protection Agency administrator Carol Browner

974.text: GUN-30916 PRIMARY The Deadly Myth of Gun Control in Electoral Politics In politics, conventional wisdom can be slow to die, even when the so-called wisdom is neither true nor wise. So I was reminded on a recent visit to Capitol Hill, when I asked several lawmakers and senior members of their staffs to explain the Democrats' timidity about standing up to the National Rifle Association by pressing needed measures to curb gun violence. House Speaker Nancy Pelosi and President Obama tossed cold water a few weeks back on Attorney General Eric Holder's well-founded enthusiasm for reviving the assault weapons ban that Congress and the Bush White House let expire in 2004. I was struck by a common thread in the responses I heard: Enactment of the original 1994 assault weapons ban cost Democrats control of Congress. In this season of successive mass shootings, that erroneous conventional wisdom should not go unchallenged. Michael Wolkowitz, board chairman of the Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence, calls the paralyzing myth of '94 ''the single biggest obstacle'' to tightening the nation's lax regulation of guns. The notion that gun control was responsible for the Democrats' debacle 15 years ago was floated by Richard Gephardt, the former Democratic House  leader, and other pols and commentators after the '94 election. But it was Bill Clinton who gave it current credence. '' The N.R.A. could rightly claim to have made Gingrich the House speaker,'' Mr. Clinton wrote in his 2004 autobiography, pumping up the gun lobby and, not incidentally, himself by attributing the body blow to his party to his principled leadership on guns.

characters: Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence

978.text: GUN-9584 PRIMARY Denver limits on weapons are reinstated A judge rules that city ordinances trump state laws that were passed to streamline gun rules. The city can continue to regulate assault weapons, "Saturday night specials" and the open carrying of guns despite two state laws to the contrary, a Denver judge ruled late Friday. Denver District Judge Joseph Meyer III said that since Denver is a home-rule city, Denver ordinances on these matters are not subject to the laws passed by the legislature in 2003. Meyer's ruling came in a Denver lawsuit filed against the state and Gov. Bill Owens. One state law, Senate Bill 24, requires county authorities to give gun permits to citizens meeting several basic standards. The other, Senate Bill 25, sets limits on the authority of local governments to regulate firearms. "I'm extremely pleased," said David Broadwell, the assistant Denver city attorney who argued on the city's behalf. "It is a big home-rule victory. It is just an outstanding win for the principle of home rule." Colorado cities can choose to have home-rule charters or to have governments defined by state statute. The major difference is that a statutory city can exercise only those powers granted by the legislature. A charter city defines its own powers so long as it doesn't conflict with the state constitution. Attorneys for the state of Colorado couldn't be reached Friday night. Denver's lawsuit received broad support from mayors of other metro area cities who felt the state laws hampered their ability to create safe public spaces. They also believed the laws were a "clear attack" on the rights of citizens and local officials to decide what is best for their communities.

980.text: NA-44689 PRIMARY Georgia law expands rights on carrying guns GEORGIA Law expands right to carry firearms Gov. Nathan Deal (R) signed a broad expansion of gun-carrying rights into law Wednesday, allowing legal gun owners to take weapons into bars, churches and government buildings under certain conditions. The measure, which will take effect July 1, also permits hunters to use silencers and authorizes schools to allow staff members to carry weapons on campus. Critics said the law gives gun owners too much leeway, but Deal said it will protect the constitutional right to bear arms for those with proper carry licenses. "This law gives added protections to those who have played by the rules - and who can protect themselves and others from those who don't play by the rules," he said in a statement. The Republican-controlled legislature, which passed the measure last month, opted not to allow gun owners to take weapons onto college campuses but approved firearms in bars unless the business owner objects and posts a sign at the door. Churches can allow worshipers to bring guns to services under the law but are not required to do so. Previous Georgia law banned firearms from churches and bars. - Reuters WASHINGTON Army opens hearing on Iraq slayings The two unarmed Iraqi brothers posed no threat as they herded cattle in a grove where a U.S. Army reconnaissance team was hidden one day seven years ago. But then-Staff Sgt. Michael Barbera took a knee, leveled his rifle and killed them anyway, a prosecutor said Wednesday as a preliminary hearing opened in the soldier's case at Joint Base Lewis-McChord, south of Seattle.

981.text: GUN-10300 PRIMARY Rendell calls bid to curb guns in Pennsylvania 'a lost cause' HARRISBURG - For eight years, Gov. Rendell has lobbied hard in the halls of the Capitol for what he calls "commonsense" handgun laws. He wanted to limit sales to individuals to one handgun a month. He wanted owners to report lost or stolen weapons. He wanted to close a loophole in state law that he believes lets criminals use gun permits obtained in other states. At each turn, the legislature - including some of his allies in the Democratic-controlled House - knocked down the proposals like so many slow-moving clay birds on a skeet-shooting range. On Monday, Rendell defended his weekend veto of a bill expanding gun owners' self-defense rights - but declared his efforts to reduce gun violence through legislation "an abject failure." "It's a lost cause in Pennsylvania," Rendell said in a conference call with reporters. He accused the General Assembly of kowtowing to the National Rifle Association. "The legislature proved consistently in my eight years that they are scared to death to buck the NRA," he said. "It's incredibly frustrating, the hold the NRA has over the legislature. It's embarrassing." The lame-duck governor spoke even as his Republican successor, Tom Corbett, was signaling he would sign the bill Rendell had just vetoed. Rendell's remarks about the NRA drew a swift rejoinder from the office of Senate Majority Leader Dominic Pileggi (R., Delaware). "I don't agree with the governor's conclusion," said Pileggi's spokesman, Erik Arneson. He said that NRA members have a right to air their views on legislation, and that lawmakers consider those views just as they do those of other interest groups.

982.text: GUN-6651 PRIMARY SENATE PANEL DELAYS VOTE ON FELONY GUN POSSESSION ; COMMITTEE APPROVES OTHER BILLS THAT REVIVE PARTS OF SAFE NEIGHBORHOODS ACT Members of an Illinois Senate committee voted Wednesday to reinstate much of the state's Safe Neighborhoods Act, with the notable exception of a measure that would have made it a felony to carry a loaded weapon. Sponsored by state Sen. Kirk Dillard, R-Downers Grove, the bills would reactivate the 1995 anti-gang law that was overturned by the Illinois Supreme Court on a technicality last month. The Executive Committee approved eight of the nine bills under consideration. Those bills will go to the Senate floor for a full vote. Gov. George Ryan, a Republican who supports gun control, and Senate President James "Pate" Philip, R-Wood Dale, have been at odds over the issue because Philip and other opponents believe a felony is too severe a punishment for a first-time offender, such as a licensed hunter, who is caught driving with a gun. Philip said after the hearing Wednesday that his position on the issue has not changed. He added that negotiations with Ryan, who was due back in Springfield on Wednesday, are in limbo. Gun control supporters have long alleged that Philip is playing parliamentary games with the gun control issue. Recent polls show overwhelming support by Illinois residents for Ryan's position on gun control. "The Senate continues to circumvent the will of the people (of Illinois)," said Dave Urbanek, spokesman for the governor. "I don't see what support it (the bill) will have outside of the Senate. One chamber can't do it alone."

986.text: GUN-31392 PRIMARY 'Gun-free zones' make vulnerable people targets It appears that malls, schools and businesses that post "gun-free" signs increasingly are becoming prime targets for people who want to cause harm to unarmed citizens ("Police seek link in Colorado attacks," News, Monday; "Lives, loves cut horribly short," News, Friday). When will those who pushed for gun-free zones realize that they are contributing to tragic situations? They are preventing legally armed citizens from interceding and preventing casualties. By the time law enforcement arrives, the shooter either commits suicide or escapes. Thankfully, New Life Church in Colorado Springs had armed security guards, and one shot the shooter, who then killed himself. This probably saved many lives. Arnie Krause Columbus, Ind. Fight for gun laws After the shootings at the Omaha mall and the church in Colorado Springs, some people are asking, "What could the mall personnel or the church staff have done to ensure safety?" This is not the question that they should be asking. They should be asking, "What could the government have done to make our cities safer?" I am appalled that there isn't a ban on assault weapons. In 2004, members of Congress let expire a 10-year-old ban on 19 types of assault weapons. This is a travesty. There are solutions to gun violence: *Americans can lobby Congress to ban military-style assault weapons and high-capacity ammunition clips that make it so easy to kill quickly.

983.text: GUN-31489 PRIMARY Legislature 2008: Panel slashes gun bill's scope A key Senate committee gutted a gun bill Tuesday that would have allowed Georgians to carry guns in their cars to work, in an attempt to diffuse a two-year battle between gun lobbyists and business owners. The vote came just a few hours after Gov. Sonny Perdue announced his opposition to the bill, which was backed by the National Rifle Association. The Senate Rules Committee, which sets the agenda for the chamber, drastically narrowed the scope of the bill that originally would have allowed any employee to keep a firearm in cars parked in company lots. The new version applies only to Georgians with state-issued concealed weapons permits, and only in limited circumstances. Originally, the bill would have applied to 9.5 million Georgia workers. The current version gives limited protection to the estimated 300,000 holders of state-issued concealed weapons permits. State Sen. Chip Rogers (R-Woodstock) sponsored the new version. "It's really so narrow there shouldn't be any opposition to it," he said. Though no representatives of the NRA spoke for or against the watered-down version, Rogers said the gun owners organization will support the legislation when it comes to the Senate floor on Thursday for a vote. Andrew Arulanandan, the NRA spokesman, said the bill preserves the integrity of Georgia's right to carry laws. However, he said, "if there is any further effort to tamper with the bill, it will be a nonstarter."

977.text: GUN-1045 PRIMARY SENATE PANEL OKS MAJOR GUN CONTROL BILL SPRINGFIELD, Ill. - A key Senate committee approved a sweeping package of gun-control legislation Tuesday that includes a seven-day waiting period to buy guns and a partial ban on semi-automatic weapons.   But members of the National Rifle Association vowed after the committee hearing to block the package and said they would punish at the polls legislators who supported it.  '' There's going to be a new (legislative map,'' said James Hayes, state liaison for the NRA. '' Many of the Senate districts are going to be competitive. And we're going to look at the vote to ban firearms as a key test vote on what to do in those elections.   ''If one sportsman goes to jail on a felony count for simply possessing a gun, then we're going to hold those senators accountable for that vote.'' The package, sponsored by Sen. William Marovitz, D-Chicago, would: Ban possession of semi-automatic weapons if the owner has a magazine or ammunition belt that holds more than 10 rounds.   Require a seven-day waiting period before the sale of any firearm regardless of its type or size.   Transfer 15- and 16-year-olds to adult courts if they are charged with shooting someone or possessing a silencer or sawed-off shotgun.   Make it a Class X felony to sell or transfer more than five firearms to people who do not have Firearm Owners Identification Cards. The legislation would also increase the fee for such cards to $10 from $5. The additional money would be used to pay for a state hot line that would owners of monitor identification cards to make sure that the cards are valid and that the person has not been charged or convicted of crimes. Prohibit a person released on bond from possessing a firearm, even if the person owned it before being arrested.   Require parents of small children to lock up firearms in their homes or face misdemeanor charges.   Chicago Police Superintendent LeRoy Martin and Cook County State's Attorney Jack O'Malley urged the Senate Judiciary II Committee to approve the package.   ''I know there is tremendous pressure from the rifle association to get you not to do the things that should be done,'' said Martin. '' But there is carnage in the streets.'' But opponents of the legislation said it would punish sportsmen, collectors and target-shooters and not the criminals it is aimed at.   James Valentino, president of the Illinois State Rifle Association, told the committee that ''no (gun collection would be complete without'' a semi-automatic weapon.   And Jay Simkin of Jews for the Preservation of Firearms Ownership said that calling semi-automatic firearms ''assault weapons'' was a ''Nazi term.'' ''We have in America so many firearms, there is nothing you can do,'' Simkin told the committee.

characters: Jews for the Preservation of Firearms Ownership

984.text: GUN-4117 PRIMARY Justices to review handgun waiting period WASHINGTON - The Supreme Court agreed yesterday to decide whether the federal government, through the Brady law, can require local law enforcement agencies to check the backgrounds of potential gun buyers. Under the law, people buying handguns must wait five days while law enforcement officials check for a criminal record or history of mental illness or drug abuse. The plaintiffs in the case accepted yesterday - sheriffs from Arizona and Montana - say the background checks would force their understaffed departments to take deputies away from other duties. They argued that as local officials they cannot be federally mandated to conduct the checks. "We've always said the Brady waiting period was unconstitutional, and now we'll get a chance to prove it in the nation's highest court," said Tom Wyld, a spokesman for the National Rifle Association. Recently, the high court has begun to limit inroads on the power of state and local governments. In March, the court curtailed federal lawsuits against states for alleged violations of federal laws. But gun-control advocates say the case is an attack on the Brady law, not a threat to states' rights. Still, the advocates say they are guardedly optimistic that the law - named after James Brady, the White House press secretary who was severly injured during the 1981 assassination attempt on President Reagan - will stand up in court.

987.text: GUN-6543 PRIMARY EDITORIAL A good gun compromise State legislators and Denver officials have long been at  loggerheads over a bill that would preempt county and municipal  ordinances regulating guns and replace them with state laws. We  commend Mayor Wellington Webb for working out a compromise that  protects law-abiding citizens, but doesn't let gun-toting thugs  off the hook, either. House Bill 1289, designed to do away with municipal  ordinances like the ones put into place in Denver following the  summer of violence in 1993, would say that Colorado local  governments may not enforce additional gun-control laws. The state  would thus preempt all municipal or county gun laws, saying statewide regulation is needed to ease confusion when Coloradans  travel over county lines. In a compromise that Webb's negotiators say will be  supported by Gov. Bill Owens, the bill now pending in the  Statehouse would be amended so that it would nullify only the  Denver ordinance that restricts when guns may legally be carried  in private vehicles. Denver's existing law is much more rigorous than the state's  and severely limits the situations in which guns may be carried in  cars. The state is more lenient, permitting Coloradans to carry  firearms in private vehicles for self protection during travel. The compromise would bring Denver into conformity with the  state law and stipulates that carrying for protection while  traveling is not an offense. Although the words 'travel' and 'self  protection' are difficult to define, the proposed amendment would  permit police to determine whether the person was truly traveling  between jurisdictions. The law would prohibit a gang member, for  example, from cruising around with a shotgun under the seat and  calling it travel.

985.text: GUN-1501 PRIMARY California Assault-Weapons Ban Is Upheld California's ban on the sale of semiautomatic assault weapons, enacted after five children were shot to death in a Stockton schoolyard in 1989, was upheld Friday by a Federal appeals court. The ruling, issued by a unanimous three-judge panel of the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit, rejected arguments by the National Rifle Association that the law, the first of its kind in the nation, violates the constitutional right to bear arms. Writing for the panel, Judge Pamela Ann Rymer said the Supreme Court, in an 1876 ruling that is still binding, declared that the Second Amendment's reference to a right to bear arms restricted actions only by Congress and not by the states. Judge Rymer also rejected the N.R.A.'s argument that the California law conflicted with a Federal law that established in 1924 a nationwide program of marksmanship competition. She said that although one of the rifles that can be used in the competition was prohibited by the state law, its use was not essential to the contest. The law forbids the sale of specified military-style rifles and pistols to private residents and requires Californians who already own such weapons to register them with the state. A lower Federal court upheld it in 1990.

characters: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

990.text: GUN-3686 PRIMARY July 2-8: Armed in the U.S.A.; Virginia Is Latest to Allow Concealed Weapons County police departments in Virginia began fingerprinting and photographing thousands of state residents last week. No lawbreakers, these average citizens were arming themselves. Virginia became the sixth state this year and the 25th overall to relax regulations for those seeking a permit to carry a concealed handgun. Under new procedures, any resident without a felony conviction or mental disability is virtually assured of getting the permit. But how all those additional handguns in cars and holsters might affect crime rates is unclear. Law-enforcement officers have mixed feelings. "Some officers feel this will only add to the proliferation of guns on the street," said Lieut. Shawn M. Barrett of the Fairfax County Police Department, as he watched people waiting on line. "Others feel it gives people the ability to protect themselves."    MICHAEL JANOFSKY

988.text: NA-42666 PRIMARY Advocates Gather in Albany to Protest New Gun Limits ALBANY -- Thousands of people gathered here on Thursday to protest New York State's new gun restrictions, denouncing Gov. Andrew M. Cuomo and lawmakers in one of the largest rallies at the State Capitol in recent years. The president of the National Rifle Association, David Keene, told the demonstrators that Mr. Cuomo had sacrificed the United States Constitution ''on the altar of his own ambition and on the ego of Michael Bloomberg of New York City.'' Mr. Keene vowed to help New York gun owners fight the measure in court and defeat legislators who supported it. ''We've lost battles before,'' Mr. Keene said. '' We'll not lose this war.'' Many of the gun owners traveled hours by bus from across upstate New York to attend the protest, organized by the New York State Rifle and Pistol Association. They stood in ankle-deep mud as lawmakers, mostly Republicans, criticized the new restrictions, as well as the swift process by which the Legislature adopted them. The new laws broaden an existing ban on assault weapons and reduce the permissible size of gun magazines to 7 rounds, from 10. The laws also make it harder for mentally ill people to obtain guns and impose more penalties on people who use guns during crimes. Mr. Cuomo, a Democrat, was the target of many of the protesters, who were predominantly male. A minister who offered an invocation at the rally said he was reading Mr. Cuomo ''the last rites for his political career,'' while many attendees held signs and banners that assailed the governor. (One resembled the cover of a how-to book and carried the title, ''Adolf Cuomo's Legislating for Dummies and Traitors.'')

characters: New York State Rifle and Pistol Association

993.text: NA-40453 PRIMARY NRA is confident in Congress Group estimates enough support to block new assault weapons ban. WASHINGTON o The powerful gun lobby is gauging enough support in Congress to block a law that would ban assault weapons, despite promises from the White House and senior lawmakers to make such a measure a reality. Senators plan to introduce a bill that would ban assault weapons and limit the size of ammunition magazines, like the one used in the December shooting massacre that killed 27 people, most of them children, in Newtown, Conn. Democratic Sen. Dianne Feinstein of California has promised to push for a renewal of expired legislation. The National Rifle Association has prevented passage of another assault weapons ban like the one that expired in 2004. But some lawmakers say the Newtown tragedy has transformed the country, and Americans are ready for stricter gun laws. President Barack Obama has made gun control a top priority. And on Tuesday Vice President Joe Biden is expected to give Obama a comprehensive package of recommendations for curbing gun violence. Still, the NRA has faith that Congress would prevent a new weapons ban. "When a president takes all the power of his office, if he's willing to expend political capital, you don't want to make predictions. You don't want to bet your house on the outcome. But I would say that the likelihood is that they are not going to be able to get an assault weapons ban through this Congress," NRA President David Keene told CNN's "State of the Union."

989.text: GUN-463 PRIMARY GUN CONTROL SPLITS THIS CHIEF, OFFICER Officer Tony Perez squats behind a trash bin, nervously fingers his weapon and mulls what he has just been told: the suspect has a crate of Uzis. "It doesn't make sense that cops have to go up against assault rifles," he says to himself. Nine minutes later, a shootout leaves a partner dead. Though Tony Perez is not real, increased police fears about criminals' firepower are. The shootout is a scene from a new novel by Joseph D. McNamara, the San Jose chief of police, who has waged a long and loud campaign against guns and their Washington lobby, the National Rifle Association. ELIOT NESS AND AL CAPONE Among those unconvinced is Leroy Pyle, one of the 1,100 officers under Chief McNamara's command, who was recently elected to the rifle association's national board. Officer Pyle calls his boss "a phony baloney plastic policeman" who is using a gun-control crusade to cover up his own failures to fight crime. "What kind of hero would Eliot Ness have been if he sat and sniveled about Al Capone's machine guns?" Officer Pyle said in a recent interview. "You don't snivel." The dispute between two men who have lived by the gun but have very different ideas about who should own one embodies much of the passion that has divided police groups from the NRA after decades of close ties and has altered the politics of gun control.

991.text: NA-40509 PRIMARY Colo Legislature OKs expansion of firearms checks DENVER o A landmark expansion of background checks on firearm purchases was approved Friday by lawmakers in Colorado, a politically moderate state that was the site of last year's mass shooting at a suburban Denver movie theater. The bill previously passed the state Senate and now heads to Democratic Gov. John Hickenlooper, who is expected to sign it into law. Earlier this week, Colorado lawmakers approved a 15-round limit on ammunition magazines. It is also awaiting the expected approval of the governor. The bill passed Friday expands cases when a $10 criminal background check would be required to legally transfer a gun. Republicans have opposed the bill, calling it an undue burden on law-abiding gun owners. "We know for a fact that whatever law we pass criminals won't care," said Republican Rep. Jerry Sonnenberg. Colorado is the first state outside the East Coast to significantly ratchet back gun rights after the theater and school shootings. Colorado's gun debate was being watched closely because it's considered a swing state with both a gun-loving frontier past and an unfortunate history of mass shootings, including the 1999 Columbine High School attack. "Are going to stop all criminals from getting guns? No," said Democratic Rep. Beth McCann, a sponsor of the background checks bill. "But are we are going to put a barrier there, make it more difficult for them? Yes."

994.text: NA-44468 PRIMARY Setting up potential appeal, D.C. asks federal judge to reinstate handgun ban; D.C. Attorney General Nathan: Second Amendment does not protect right to carry firearms in public Attorneys for the District formally asked U.S. District Judge Frederick J. Scullin on Monday to reconsider his July opinion striking down as unconstitutional the city's ban on carrying handguns in public. The District's attorneys argued that the move, which was anticipated, is a necessary step before the District decides whether to appeal Scullin's opinion or the judge considers lifting or doubling a 90-day stay, as the District has requested, to give city officials time to enact laws allowing residents with registered handguns to carry them on D.C. streets. In a 21-page legal filing, D.C. Attorney General Irvin B. Nathan and aides said that Scullin relied on opinions of "questionable logical and legal foundations" by a minority of courts, and said that no controlling court has found that the right to carry firearms outside the home is at the core of the Second Amendment. The D.C. attorney general argued that Scullin should at least stay his opinion to allow additional briefing on the District's defenses for the gun-control law, which is the strictest in the nation. In 2008, the District appealed and lost a landmark U.S. Supreme Court case, District of Columbia v. Heller, which ended the city's prohibition on keeping handguns in the home. spencer.hsu@washpost.com Get updates on your area delivered via e-mail

995.text: GUN-8696 PRIMARY Million Mom rally turnout dips Pro-gun hecklers blamed Attendance dipped at this year's Million Mom March rally, and  event organizers blamed a crowd of black-clad, pro-gun hecklers  for keeping potential supporters away. 'We're a little disappointed by the turnout,' said Marilee  Louis, co-president of the Denver chapter of the Million Mom  March. 'I think a lot of the women are a little nervous about  those guys.' She was referring to members of the Tyranny Response Team, a  pro-gun organization whose members regularly attend Million Mom  gun-control functions to shout their support for the Second  Amendment. About 250 people gathered for the Million Mom rally on the  steps of the state Capitol, while about 100 Tyranny Response Team  members stood below. Last year, the rally drew 3,000 to 5,000  people. Fearing a clash between the two groups, police cordoned off a  buffer zone between the Million Mom attendees and the Tyranny  Response Team members, who met on the sidewalk on Lincoln Avenue.   Forty Denver police officers and 10 State Patrol officers were  stationed around the Capitol. The Million Mom movement, whose slogan is 'Sensible gun laws,  safe kids,' began with a march that attracted 750,000 in  Washington, D.C., last Mother's Day, and has since added 240  chapters nationwide. This Mother's Day saw Million Mom rallies in  46 states. The goal this year, organizers said, is to make the issue of gun control less divisive.

997.text: GUN-7493 PRIMARY WHY GIVE GOVERNMENT A MONOPOLY ON GUNS? Apparently, the writer of Sunday's letter "Response to moms' march shows NRA is at war with society" believes that he represents society. I do not know who he thinks "the rest of us" are, but he obviously forgot about the 80 million legal gun owners in America (according to FBI data and survey research on gun ownership) and the millions of other citizens who believe in the right to own firearms. The Founders wrote the Second Amendment to ensure that the citizens' right to keep and bear arms remained a protected, fundamental means of preserving freedom, personal security and the capacity to resist tyranny, if necessary. Since the purpose of the nearly 4 million-member National Rifle Association is to support this inalienable right, it seems clear that "the rest of us" refers to those who naively believe that we should trust the government with a monopoly on our guns. DAN ROMERO Loxahatchee

999.text: GUN-3076 PRIMARY BILL TO BAN GUNS IS A TYPICAL QUICK FIX Once again we have some alarmists trying to make a name for themselves by riding the coattails of a current politically correct movement. Do Rep. Ron Klein, D-Boca Raton, and Sen. Howard Forman, D-Hollywood, have any regard for the Constitution and the rights of Americans? CS/HB 241 to ban guns is your typical quick-fix-type law. Violent crime is getting a lot of publicity, and a bill such as this may seem like popularity politics to Rep. Klein and Sen. Forman that might get them elected again. But how much thought has gone into this bill? How much time has been spent studying this subject before the bill was proposed? We don't have a gun problem, we have a criminal problem. Mark R. Anderson Lantana

996.text: GUN-11507 PRIMARY NAACP calls for new look at case Members of the state and local NAACP were in Marietta on Wednesday, rallying support for a Cobb County resident convicted of killing a man in his front yard and trying to link the case to Georgia's "stand your ground" law. The group is continuing its effort to free John McNeil, who shot and killed Brian Epp on the front lawn of McNeil's Kennesaw home in December 2005. McNeil claimed self defense, but was convicted of murder and sentenced to life in prison in 2006 by a Cobb jury. "If the framers of 'stand your ground' intended the law to apply equally, then [McNeil] shouldn't be in jail," Edward DuBose, president of the NAACP's state conference, said on the steps of the Cobb County Justice Center. "John McNeil did not have a duty to retreat." The group argued that McNeil, who is black, would not have been convicted if he had been white. Cobb District Attorney Pat Head was attending a conference Wednesday and unavailable for comment, but in previous statements to the media he has supported his office's prosecution of McNeil and denied any racial motivation. The shooting occurred prior to Georgia's 2006 implementation of the stand-your-ground law, which expanded self-defense laws and removed a person's obligation to retreat when threatened. But self-defense and defense-of-property laws were in place long before the incident, said McNeil's attorney, Mark Yurachek.

998.text: NA-43106 PRIMARY California Will Allow Family Members to Seek Seizure of Guns LOS ANGELES -- California will be the first state in the country to allow private citizens to ask a court to seize guns from family members who they believe pose a threat to themselves or the public, under a measure signed into law by Gov. Jerry Brown on Tuesday. The law will allow law enforcement officials, family members and some others to seek a gun restraining order from a judge. That order would authorize officials to temporarily seize any firearm owned by someone deemed potentially violent, who would also be placed on a list of people prohibited from purchasing weapons. ''This puts California in the leadership on efforts to stop gun violence, and it gives a very effective tool to law enforcement and families to intervene before a shooting tragedy occurs,'' said Nancy Skinner, the California assemblywoman who sponsored the bill. Several states have passed laws allowing law enforcement officials to petition to take firearms from people considered dangerous, but California is the first to allow family members to do so as well -- a provision that gun control advocates said would be crucial in preventing suicides as well as mass shootings. ''Family members are the ones who most acutely understand when their loved ones are in a dangerous situation,'' said Josh Horwitz, the executive director of the Coalition to Stop Gun Violence, which lobbies for stricter gun control across the country. '' Now, when they see dangerous behavior -- whether because of substance abuse or a mental health issue or a traumatic brain injury -- a court can act.''

1000.text: GUN-9689 PRIMARY KERRY FIRES AT BUSH ON ASSAULT-WEAPONS STANCE PRESIDENT'S CAMPAIGN SAYS CRITICISM SHOWS DEMOCRAT IS DESPERATE AFFTON, Mo. -- John Kerry accused President Bush on Friday of being beholden to the National Rifle Association and the gun lobby because he hasn't pressed Congress to extend the 1994 assault-weapons ban that expires Monday. Meanwhile, Bush, on a campaign tour through West Virginia and Ohio, slammed Kerry again for shifting his position on Iraq and said Saddam Hussein would still be in power if the Massachussetts senator were president. Kerry advocates renewing the assault-weapons ban and chose Missouri, a state with nearly half a million hunters, to issue his toughest indictment of Bush yet on the issue. He reiterated his point later in Pennsylvania, home to some 1 million hunters. During the 2000 presidential campaign, Bush backed an extension of the ban, but he hasn't actively encouraged the GOP-led Congress to renew the law. "I don't understand what the philosophy is that says that you make America safer when you take cops off the streets and put assault weapons back on," Kerry said. His remarks came two days after the NRA launched a $400,000 ad campaign in several states portraying Kerry as an opponent of the Second Amendment's right to bear arms. For his part, Bush included the Second Amendment in a list of things that he said he stands for when reciting his otherwise standard stump speech in Huntington, W.Va. His campaign charged that Kerry's criticism, coming as polls show him trailing Bush, was a desperate attempt to rally the Democratic base.

992.text: GUN-6555 PRIMARY Coalition takes up gun control Group aims to get measure on ballot The Colorado Legislature has failed to do anything to combat  gun violence, so a coalition of politicians, private citizens and  mothers will have to battle the pro-gun lobby at the ballot box. That was the message that went along with an unveiling Monday  of a wall with the names of 3,094 victims who have died by gun  violence between the April 20 attack on Columbine High School and  the end of 1999. Sponsored by the U.S. Conference of Mayors, the 8-foot-high  black wall was filled with names reported from 100 cities. The  youngest victim was 2, the oldest 96. Colorado has 36 names on the  wall, which will be up in the City and County Building this week. 'How many people have to die before we make any modest  changes?' said Mayor Wellington Webb. Tom Mauser, the father of Daniel Mauser, one of the 13 killed  at Columbine, said he believes American society has built a 'wall  of denial' about the number of deaths that can be attributed to  guns. 'That wall of denial was built from myths, cliches, rhetoric,  lies, campaign contributions, intimidation, selfishness,' Mauser  said. 'Especially here in Colorado, that wall of denial is  starting to crack.' Politicians and others gathered at a press conference -  including Denver District Attorney Bill Ritter, U.S. Attorney Tom  Strickland, state Rep. Ken Gordon, city Councilwoman Cathy  Reynolds and John Head of Sane Alternatives to the Firearms  Epidemic - said they had hope for gun control by placing an initiative on the November ballot.

characters: Sane Alternatives to the Firearms Epidemic

1004.text: GUN-31438 PRIMARY Gwinnett Opinions: READERS RESPOND Responses to "There's no reason to own a gun," AJC Gwinnett Opinions, Feb. 9. We find solutions to other problems; why not guns? When facing someone who defends the Second Amendment, I will state this: In 1998, three events occurred in which 11 children were trapped in automobile trunks. Unable to free themselves, they died. Now, there is a federal law stating that all cars manufactured must have glow-in-the-dark, internal trunk releases. But, three to five children, every day, die or are injured because of firearms (In some studies the number is much higher). Yet we do nothing. Why? I agree with AJC Gwinnett News blogger Woody Bass completely about firearms. Thanks to him for saying it. RICK POWELL, Lawrenceville Ideas on gun ownership are without merit I take particular exception with the Woody Bass blog regarding owning guns. I can usually find something to agree with in another's argument, but not here. He acknowledges a citizen's constitutional right to bear arms. While I personally feel having guns in the home and car for protection and their legal use in hunting should be allowed, no such right legally exists other then those granted citizens by their elected representatives. The only group granted such a constitutional privilege by the Second Amendment is "a well-regulated militia," which refers to the present day National Guard and organized reserves: not to individual Rambos. Should the NRA ever take this issue to the U.S. Supreme Court (and it won't), the resultant ruling will have the organization drinking its bath water for a week.

1005.text: GUN-859 PRIMARY MRS. BUSH WON'T CONTRADICT HUSBAND IN PUBLIC ANY MORE TOKYO (AP) - Barbara Bush has decided to keep any disagreements with her husband, the president, within the family after a public difference over military-style assault weapons, officials announced Thursday.   Under a new policy announced as Mrs. Bush arrived in Japan for the funeral of Emperor Hirohito, she no longer will talk about controversial issues such as gun control and abortion. '' They are not the issues where she wishes to have a focus,'' said Anna Perez, her press secretary.   ''It doesn't mean she doesn't have a right to an opinion. It means she has a right to discuss them or not,'' Perez said.   Mrs. Bush and her husband appeared at odds after she said in a recent interview that she ''absolutely'' favored a ban on military-style assault weapons, such as the one used in the recent schoolyard massacre in Stockton, Calif. Five children were killed and more than two dozen were wounded in the attack.   Questioned separately, the president said he did not favor a ban on semiautomatic weapons because they are used by sportsmen and hunters. A semiautomatic AK-47 was used in the Stockton incident.   Bush - a lifetime member of the National Rifle Association, the nation's largest gun lobby - said police should do more to enforce the laws.   Perez quoted Mrs. Bush as saying that ''there isn't that much of a difference of opinion'' between Bush and his wife over the issue of assault-style weapons.   ''If there is, they will discuss it among themselves,'' Perez said.

1002.text: GUN-2215 PRIMARY D.C., Va. Police Join U.S. Gun Effort; Tracking Movements of Illegal Firearms to Washington Is Goal Detectives from the District, Virginia and the U.S. Park Police have joined federal agents working to track down movements of illegal firearms from Virginia to Washington, a group of law enforcement officials announced yesterday. Patrick D. Hynes, special agent in charge of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF) office in Falls Church, said 35 ATF agents in Virginia are providing the bulk of the staffing in the effort. But he said the state and local investigators from Virginia and Washington would "accelerate the response" to reports of illegal firearms sales. The ATF agents were joined last week by a District detective, a Park Police investigator and three Virginia State Police detectives, he said. With additional detectives, Hynes said, the task force could coordinate continuing investigations between federal, state and local offices by avoiding duplication and matching computer databases. That coordination is especially important to the District, he said, because 40 percent of the firearms used in city crimes over the last 10 years have come from Virginia. The idea for the task force, Hynes said, came at a breakfast he attended several weeks ago with U.S. Attorney Richard Cullen and Virginia State Police Col. Carl R. Baker. Under the plan, announced yesterday at news conferences in Richmond and Washington, one Virginia state trooper will work at each of three ATF offices in Falls Church, Richmond and Norfolk. The District and Park Police detectives also will work in Falls Church.

1003.text: NA-43734 PRIMARY Obama to propose big steps on guns President Obama on Wednesday will formally announce the most aggressive and expansive national gun-control agenda in generations as he presses Congress to mandate background checks for all firearms buyers and prohibit assault weapons and high-capacity ammunition clips. The announcement will set off a fierce confrontation with Congress over an issue that has riven American society for decades. Obama's far-reaching firearms agenda has at best tepid support from his party leaders and puts him at loggerheads with Democratic centrists. Days before his second inauguration, Obama is seeking to drive the gun debate in a way that contrasts with the accommodating approach he often took during his first term. In the weeks ahead, he will attempt to rally popular support to bend the will of lawmakers to vote for what he considers the ideal, not merely the possible. "Yes, we can reduce gun violence, but it's something we have to do together," White House press secretary Jay Carney told reporters Tuesday. "It's something that cannot be done by a president alone. It can't be done by a single community alone or a mayor or a governor or by Congress alone. We all have to work together." Obama will begin this effort Wednesday in the presence of children who wrote him letters after last month's mass shooting at a grade school in Newtown, Conn., and who have been invited to Washington to attend the rollout.

characters: White House press secretary Jay Carney

1001.text: GUN-4158 PRIMARY SURPLUS WEAPONS; Foes say Army program aids NRA 373,000 outdated guns and 120 million rounds of ammunition are at issue under a defense provision dating back to 1903. A defense budget provision requiring the Pentagon to unload nearly 400,000 World War II-era guns is being criticized by gun control advocates as a sop to the National Rifle Association. The NRA and a member of Congress say the weapons - including vintage M-1 rifles - do not represent a windfall. At issue is a requirement that the Defense Department turn over outdated weapons to a nonprofit group that, in turn, sells them, with proceeds going to benefit marksmanship programs and an annual target- shooting tournament. The gun-control groups are crying foul because many of the target- shooting participants are NRA members, and the yearly competition, held at an Ohio Army camp, is managed by the NRA. "There's no justification for this program," complained Bob Walker, legislative director for Handgun Control Inc. The provision in the defense budget calls for the Army to transfer about $ 5 million in cash, $ 8 million worth of computers and other equipment, 120 million rounds of ammunition and 373,000 guns from its civilian marksmanship program to a new corporation. Estimates suggest only about 224,000 of the weapons are usable, but they could generate about $ 100 million in receipts, according to collectors. The marksmanship program dates to 1903, when it was started because military officials were startled by the number of soldiers in the Spanish- American War who couldn't accurately fire their weapons.

1007.text: GUN-12093 PRIMARY Camden County schedules gun buyback program After last year's groceries-for-guns program in Camden netted only 57 weapons, law enforcement officials are sweetening the pot in a new program by offering up to $250 in cash per firearm, up from $100 in vouchers. "I think upping the ante does help," Camden County Prosecutor Warren W. Faulk said in announcing the $100,000, two-day cash-for-guns program, along with state Attorney General Jeffrey S. Chiesa, other law enforcement officials, and members of the community. The event will take place between 8 a.m. and 8 p.m. next Friday and Dec. 15 at two city churches - Antioch Baptist Church at Seventh Street and Ferry Avenue, and Higher Ground Temple on Vine Street. Camden County residents can turn in up to three firearms each, no questions asked, said Chiesa. Payment will be on a sliding scale, with the highest amount paid for operable high-end weapons. Funding comes from forfeiture money recovered from drug dealers and other offenders, Chiesa said. "There are too many guns on the street and too many people dying as a result of gun violence," he said. Officials collected 700 guns in a buyback in Newark in 2009, he noted, though he could not say whether it affected gun violence in the city. "Common sense tells us that the less guns there are, the less likelihood of violence," he said. Homicides in Newark, a city of 277,000 people, rose from 80 in 2009 to 91 the next year, according to state police data.

1009.text: GUN-2949 PRIMARY MAKE IT COST TO SELL GUNS, BOSLEY URGES Mayor Freeman Bosley Jr. said Wednesday that there are too many licensed gun dealers in the metropolitan area - about 300 - and that access to guns is too easy. St. Louis had a record number of homicides last year. Many involved guns such as the Glock 9 mm, the AK-47 and the Uzi, he said. "These are not guns that are used for hunting; they are used for killing," he told a group of Realtors in Richmond Heights. Local governments should have the right to regulate who can sell firearms and ammunition, Bosley said. Charging $ 500 or $ 1,000 - instead of $ 60 - to purchase a firearms license would reduce the number of guns on the street, Bosley said. "The life you save might be your own," he said.

1010.text: GUN-1521 PRIMARY AFTER THE RIOTS; LOS ANGELES RIOTS SPURRING BIG RISE IN SALES OF GUNS In the aftermath of the Los Angeles riots, Californians are buying firearms at the highest rate since the state began keeping records 20 years ago, and other states are reporting similar surges in gun sales. In large part the rush to buy guns in California can be attributed to one of the more frightening messages to come out of the two days of arson, looting and violence in South-Central Los Angeles. That message, that fear, is that the police might not be able to defend people during an outbreak of civil unrest. While it may be too soon to tell how the image of Angelenos protecting their stores and homes with raised semiautomatic rifles will alter the gun-control debate in the United States, one thing seems certain: The riots have made gun owners out of people who used to shun guns. 50% Increase in Buying Californians bought 20,578 guns in the first 11 days of May, a 50 percent increase over the same period last year and a pace that will set a record for gun purchases in a one-month period if it continues. Although there is no way to account for gun purchases nationwide, some states in which sales are tracked through registrations report a similiar rise since the Los Angeles riots, which began April 29. Likewise, the National Rifle Association, the nation's most powerful gun lobby, reports an increase in requests for membership since four white Los Angeles police officers were acquitted in the beating of a black motorist, touching off the civil unrest.

1008.text: NA-45872 PRIMARY NRA toughens stance on background checks; Group president: Odds stacked against assault-weapons ban The powerful National Rifle Association will urge lawmakers to vote against mandating universal background checks for gun buyers, NRA President David Keene told USA TODAY on Wednesday. That raises questions about what gun-control measures have the prospect of enactment in the wake of last month's shootings in Newtown, Conn. The gun-show loophole allows some sales at gun shows  without  the buyer's name being checked against a federal database of convicted felons and the mentally ill. Closing it has emerged as one of the most widely supported proposals among congressional Democrats and some Republicans. In a Pew Research Center poll, 85% of Americans backed the idea. But Keene, in an interview, and NRA Executive Director Wayne LaPierre, appearing earlier before the Senate Judiciary Committee, said the background checks would do little to stop criminals from getting guns and would burden law-aiding citizens. That reflects a change from the NRA's position in 1999, when LaPierre called universal background checks "reasonable." Asked if the NRA would encourage members of Congress to vote against universal background checks, Keene said, "If it came up today, yes." He assessed the prospects the proposal would pass at less than 50-50. Still, he gave it better odds than an assault-weapons ban, which he said had a "very, very small" chance of passage. Keene was interviewed for USA TODAY's Capital Download. "I would suggest at the end of the day it's not going to pass, but let me give you one caveat," he said.

1006.text: GUN-1245 PRIMARY Speaker Puzzles House Tea-leaf readers in the House of Delegates are wondering if Speaker A.L. Philpott (D-Henry) might be considering retirement. They base their hunch on some recent peculiar actions by the 71-year-old Philpott, the senior member of the General Assembly who has represented rural Henry County in the House since 1958. Philpott is a staunch opponent of gun control -- witness that he cast the only negative vote in a 98 to 1 tally last week against expanding the instant background check of would-be gun purchasers to include all guns, a proposal that even the National Rifle Association favored. But this week, he turned around and abstained on a much more controversial proposal that could have required a three-day waiting period for gun purchasers. And before the vote, Philpott, in a rare compliment from the podium, praised Del. Jean W. Cunningham (D-Richmond), who sponsored what the NRA calls the "three-day harassment period," for delivering "one of the best presentations on the floor in all my years in the House." If that isn't puzzling enough, consider Philpott's unaccustomed fairness to minority Republicans this year. In filling vacancies on powerful committees created by the death of veteran Arlington Democratic Del. Warren G. Stambaugh, he bypassed senior Democrats and named Del. Jane H. Woods (R-Fairfax) to Finance and Del. Robert T. Andrews (R-McLean) to Courts of Justice. Those actions, and several others, have been enough of a signal for some delegates to start talking about a possible successor. For years, it was expected that it would be a two-way contest between House Majority Leader Thomas W. Moss Jr. (D-Norfolk) and Finance Chairman C. Richard Cranwell (D-Vinton). Now comes a boomlet for a possible compromise candidate in J. Samuel Glasscock (D-Suffolk).

characters: House Majority Leader Thomas W. Moss Jr.

1011.text: GUN-2804 PRIMARY Court OKs assault arms ban Denver's ban on assault weapons is constitutional, despite a lower court ruling to the contrary, the Colorado Supreme Court declared yesterday. In reversing the Denver judge who found the measure unconstitutional, Chief Justice Luis Rovira said the unique characteristics of assault weapons and their prevalent use in crimes show they pose a substantial threat to Denver citizens. "The city council expressly sought to promote the health, safety and security of citizens of Denver by enacting the ordinance," Rovira wrote. "More specifically, it declared that prohibiting the possession and sale of assault weapons was premised on the city's interest in curbing crime, particularly homicides." "A state has a very significant interest in preventing crime. There can be no doubt that an ordinance, intended to prevent crime, serves a legitimate governmental interest sufficiently strong to justify its enactment." Only one paragraph in the ordinance was declared unconstitutional and ordered deleted, said prosecutor Craig Silverman. That paragraph refers to rifles that are modified to become semi-automatic pistols. Silverman and Assistant City Attorney John Stoffel said the paragraph won't have any effect on the law, which prohibits about 40 types of weapons in Denver, including the MAC-10, MAC-11, Colt AR-15, and Beretta AR-70. Councilwoman Cathy Reynolds, who sponsored the weapons ban, was happy with the 6-1 ruling. "Wow! I think that's terrific," she said. "I'm just delighted. We now take the cloud away from whether we can enforce it. We have been trying through several mechanisms to have less weapons on the street. This is part of the whole package to make Denver a safer city. I feel very good about it."

1012.text: NA-42536 PRIMARY Gun-Rights Allies Rally in Hartford Against New Rules HARTFORD -- More than 1,000 supporters of gun rights, most wearing red stickers reading ''N.R.A. Stand and Fight,'' packed the atrium of the State Capitol on Monday as Connecticut's protracted negotiations on new gun laws seemed to be lurching toward a resolution. Many advocates for gun rights, who stood in the morning chill with signs reading ''We Will Not Comply'' and ''Dictators Prefer Unarmed Citizens,'' said the aggressive gun-control being pushed by Democrats would do nothing to keep residents safe while infringing on their rights. But many said that after the Dec. 14 massacre at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, they were expecting lawmakers to pass sweeping gun controls, perhaps including an expansion of the state's assault weapons ban and a prohibition on magazines with more than 10 rounds. ''I almost get the impression they're going to ignore us,'' said John Agnese, 22, who said he had been involved in shooting sports since he was 12. Leaders in the General Assembly met for the third day on Monday after a bipartisan subcommittee failed to come up with a unified agenda last week. The main sticking points were differences between Democrats and Republicans on which assault and assault-style weapons would be banned, and the maximum sizes of ammunition magazines. But Gov. Dannel P. Malloy and Democratic legislative leaders support expansive gun regulation, Democrats control both houses, and public opinion appears to favor tougher laws; as a result, many on each side of the issue said the only question on the legislation was when, not if.

1014.text: NA-40654 PRIMARY PEOPLE ENTERTAINMENT Wonder boycotts 'stand your ground' states Stevie Wonder said he won't perform in Florida and other states with a "stand your ground" law. In a video posted on YouTube, the singer, 63, said at a concert in Quebec City, Canada, on Sunday "that until the 'stand your ground' law is abolished in Florida, I will never perform there again." Wonder added: "Wherever I find that law exists, I will not perform in that state or in that part of the world." The "stand your ground" law allows people to use deadly force if they believe their life is in danger. George Zimmerman shot 17-year-old Trayvon Martin during a February 2012 confrontation in Sanford, Fla. Zimmerman said he fired his gun in self-defense. A six-member jury acquitted Zimmerman of second-degree murder and manslaughter charges on Saturday. Heroin, alcohol killed Monteith o Cory Monteith, the actor and "Glee" star found dead in a hotel room on Saturday, died of an overdose of heroin and alcohol, a British Columbia coroner said Tuesday. Monteith had battled addiction for years. Nas heads to Harvard o Nas has rapped his way to Harvard University. The Ivy League school announced Tuesday that the rapper, 39, is being honored with the Nasir Jones Hip-Hop Fellowship at its W.E.B. Du Bois Institute. It's a joint venture with Harvard's Hip-Hop Archive. The fellowship will assist students who excel in the arts "in connection with hip-hop."

1013.text: NA-44400 PRIMARY Newtown's legacy CONGRESS DISGRACED itself by refusing to toughen gun laws after the slaughter of children a year ago at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. , yawning in the face of a national tragedy. Meanwhile, the battle for reform in state capitals continued and intensified. Some states that already had lax gun laws, mainly in the South and Midwest, moved to relax them further, in the apparent belief that a Wild West approach would promote a safer citizenry. Montana enacted a measure barring health-care providers from asking patients whether they own guns; Tennessee cut the waiting period for people who want a handgun permit after they leave a drug or alcohol treatment program; and Alaska and Kansas nullified some federal firearms laws so that they will no longer apply in those states. According to an analysis by the New York Times of state legislation enacted since the Newtown massacre, about two-thirds of the 109 new firearms laws around the country loosen restrictions - for instance, by easing the rules governing concealed-carry permits or allowing guns to be brought into places of worship or schools. The irony and tragedy of such efforts is that many states with the most anemic gun laws already have the highest rates of gun deaths, according to a new report from the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence. By further relaxing restrictions on weapons, those states may invite even more gun violence.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1015.text: GUN-11264 PRIMARY Mike goes gunnin' for Congress MAYOR Bloomberg slammed Congress on Sunday for not providing federal funding to keep guns out of the wrong hands. Touting a new Super Bowl ad against illegal guns, the mayor said states don't get enough money to perform proper background checks on gun applicants. "Federal laws say you can't get a gun if you have a drug problem, psychiatric problem, criminal record or (are a) minor, and yet Congress doesn't give moneys to make sure that we can have a background check," Bloomberg said on "Meet the Press." Criminals seeking guns can take advantage of "too many loopholes," the mayor said. "The background database isn't up to date, private-sector sales of guns is something like 40%, and they don't do background checks," he said. Even with the city's tough gun laws, a Brooklyn thug with prior arrests allegedly obtained a pistol and shot Officer Kevin Brennan in the head last week, he said. "New York is now so much lower (in crime) than almost every big city, (but) we still had a cop shot last week with a gun that somebody had, even though the federal laws prohibited that person from having a gun," the mayor said, referring to accused gunman Luis (Baby) Ortiz. Bloomberg filmed the Super Bowl ad with his Boston counterpart, Mayor Thomas Menino. In the 30-second clip, the mayors, decked out in the gear of their respective cities' football teams, debate whose city is better but agree on tougher gun laws.

1020.text: NA-45832 PRIMARY No firearms at Starbucks a turnoff or draw for patrons? In an open letter to "fellow Americans," Starbucks CEO Howard Schultz requested customers not bring guns into Starbucks stores. Might as well put up a giant sign that says: "Criminals welcome! Feel free to rob us as we do not support the right to self-defense!" Jeffrey Ulatan How is this any different from "No shoes, no shirt, no service" or "jacket required"? I understand the constitutional right to bear arms, but can people just calm down and have a coffee? Nick Derrett The CEO said he was not against people's rights to bear arms. He is against them using his stores for their own agendas and making people uncomfortable by carrying them into the stores. I can't blame those customers. I would also be uncomfortable with some people carrying guns around. Look at all of the violence going on in the country. Seems like every day you hear of someone else shooting and killing people. John Ward Some people are uncomfortable with guns? Who cares? It is a basic right to keep and bear arms. I am more uncomfortable with foul language than a gun, but that doesn't mean I tell everyone out there with a dirty mouth to speak differently. Just because someone is "uncomfortable" with something doesn't mean it should be stopped. Freedom, and respecting others' freedoms and rights, mean that sometimes you will be uncomfortable.

1017.text: GUN-8066 PRIMARY Groups spending big on McCollum Several organizations are laying out thousands to support the Republican candidate for U.S. Senate. Republican U.S. Senate candidate Bill McCollum often reminds people that his staunch support of gun rights is based solely on principle, because he accepts no contributions from the National Rifle Association. But that's not stopping the nation's top gun rights group from lending McCollum a hand in the final stretch of his campaign. The NRA has spent more than $ 208,000 on McCollum's behalf during the past four weeks, according to federal campaign finance reports. That's not quite as much as Handgun Control Inc., the gun control advocacy group, spent in September on TV ads attacking McCollum's record and helping Democrat Bill Nelson. But in the last days of Florida's Senate race, McCollum so far is the main beneficiary of campaign spending by independent groups. The National Right to Life Committee, an anti-abortion group, has spent more than $ 89,000 on McCollum's behalf, including radio spots. Also, the National Right to Work Committee, a Virginia-based anti-union group, says it is spending about $ 300,000 blasting Nelson as a liberal pal of "union bosses" in direct mail, TV and newspaper ads. Marion Hammer, head of the NRA affiliate in Florida, said she was unaware of the organization's efforts on McCollum's behalf, and officials with the national NRA office did not return phone calls. The McCollum campaign said they also were unaware of the NRA's efforts.

1019.text: GUN-2546 PRIMARY Brady bill locked up a 3rd time The Brady bill suffered another setback Monday when Senate Republicans blocked it for a third time in four days. Senate Minority Leader Robert Dole, R-Kan., objected to the version of the bill that came out of a House-Senate conference committee. The House approved the new version 238-187; both call for a five-day handgun purchase waiting period. Senate Majority Leader George Mitchell, D-Maine, has said he'll bring senators back after the Thanksgiving holiday. Dole said Republicans acted in good faith last Saturday when they permitted the bill to come to a vote after blocking it twice Friday and, "We got zippo, zero," he said. The negotiated bill comes closer to the House version, ending the waiting period after five years, assuming an instant electronic background check will be operational by then. Until then, the five-day wait would allow background checks using available means. Sarah Brady, whose husband James is the bill's namesake, said, "I've never seen such obstructionism. This is gridlock at its worst." Also, the Senate moved to get a federal subpoena for Sen. Bob Packwood's diaries. Ethics committee investigators, after Packwood was accused of sexual misconduct, found possible criminal activity described in the diaries. When Packwood refused a committee subpoena, the full Senate gave permission to seek a court order.

1018.text: GUN-590 PRIMARY QUIT STALLING, BAN ASSAULT WEAPONS NOW About a year ago, 47-year-old Joseph Wesbecker, a former employee of Louisville's Standard Gravure Co., began stockpiling guns. Although diagnosed as mentally ill, he filled out the required gun registration form and bought high-powered semiautomatic assault rifles designed for military purposes, rifles that can shoot up to 75 rounds of ammunition a minute. The bullets of the AK-47, his weapon of choice, pierce metal and rip through brick walls. On Sept. 14, Wesbecker went to Standard Gravure, determined to get even with those he thought had "done him wrong." He gunned down 20 of his fellow workers, immediately killing five and critically wounding 15, before killing himself with a 9mm semiautomatic handgun. Three men died later, and the living suffer horrendous injuries from the impact of bone-shattering bullets. When I voted this June with other members of the U.S. Conference of Mayors to support gun control legislation, I had in mind the terrible day in Stockton, Calif., when a drifter, also armed with an AK-47, killed five children and injured scores of others. I never thought it would happen in my city. It is deeply disturbing to realize that it is not an isolated incident and that it can happen anywhere. WEAPON FOR CRIMINALS I grieve personally for the victims, their families and my city. I can help them best by joining the cry for legislation to eliminate the manufacture, sale and possession of semiautomatic weapons, such as the AK-47. No argument of a citizen's right to bear arms can logically justify the need for such a weapon of destruction.

1022.text: NA-40546 PRIMARY State stops scanning concealed gun permits JEFFERSON CITY o The Department of Revenue will stop keeping electronic copies of Missourians' concealed gun permits when they apply for drivers licenses, but Republican lawmakers say the policy reversal is only one step toward easing their concerns over how the state government collects private information. Republicans have spent several weeks crying foul over Missouri's new drivers license system, which requires the scanning and retention of personal documents, including birth certificates, marriage licenses and - up until Tuesday's announcement - concealed carry permits. "It has been determined that the scanning and retention of concealed carry certificates are not essential to the integrity of the license issuance process," Gov. Jay Nixon, a Democrat, said in a statement announcing the change. "We will continue to work with policymakers to ensure the security and privacy of our license issuance process." Senate President Pro Tem Tom Dempsey, R-St. Charles, said he sees the change as "a step in the right direction" but he stressed the need further reform. "To have the acknowledgement of this controversy over the procedure is the first step," he said. "I think there needs to be a bigger discussion about the scanning of source documents." Republicans repeatedly have characterized the new drivers license program as a way for Nixon's administration to share private information with the federal government, either through the federal Real ID Act or other means. The Missouri Legislature passed a law in 2009 requiring the state to opt out of the anti-terrorism initiative that, among other provisions, mandates the collection of documents for state licenses to meet federal standards required to board airplanes and enter federal courthouses.

characters: Senate President Pro Tem Tom Dempsey

1016.text: GUN-9620 PRIMARY Gun Rights Advocates Sound Alarm Over Ehrlich's Appointments When filling openings in his administration, Gov. Robert L. Ehrlich Jr. (R) has frequently turned to friends in the General Assembly -- with a little-noticed consequence, according to a gun rights publication. "Ehrlich Takes Out More Pro-Gun Delegates Than Sarah Brady Did Last Election," proclaims a headline in the September issue of Tripwire, referring to the well-known gun-control advocate. Her husband, James Brady, the press secretary to President Ronald Reagan, was shot and seriously wounded during the assassination attempt on Reagan. "Turnover . . . hits us hard," the gun-rights newsletter says, "as friends who've worked hard for us are replaced by junior appointees who may feel they owe our issue nothing." The most recently departed friend cited by the newsletter is former Del. Carmen Amedori (R-Carroll), whom Ehrlich named to the Maryland Parole Commission. Her appointment, Tripwire says, "came hard on the heels of another change" -- the naming of Del. George W. Owings III (D-Calvert) as Ehrlich's secretary of veterans affairs. "As majority whip for more than a decade, George was a friendly voice . . . (who) knew our issue and never minced words when he spoke forcefully for us, whether on the House floor or at community meetings in his district." Other departures cited by the publication include: Del. Thomas E. Tim Hutchins (R-Charles) who became superintendent of the Maryland State Police; Del. Robert L. Flanagan (R-Howard), who became transportation secretary; Del. Kenneth D. Schisler (R-Talbot), who left for the Public Service Commission; and Alfred W. Redmer Jr. (R-Baltimore County), who became insurance commissioner.

1021.text: GUN-6602 PRIMARY CLINTON'S BUDGET WILL INCLUDE MORE EFFORTS TO REDUCE GUN VIOLENCE; SOME FUNDS WOULD GO TO TEACHING FIREARMS SAFETY President Bill Clinton's budget proposal will include $ 30 million to help communities track gun violence and promote gun safety, says Housing and Urban Development Secretary Andrew Cuomo. Cuomo's department would administer the programs, which need c ongressional approval. He said Monday that the money would pay for local efforts to computerize the mapping of gun violence and to teach gun safety. The president will announce his budget proposal on Feb. 7. It's for the fiscal year that begins Oct. 1. Cuomo called the spending part of a three-track campaign against gun violence. The two others are legislative efforts and the possibility of a federal suit against gun manufacturers. On the legislative front, Clinton has proposed photo ID licenses for handgun purchases. He has also urged Congress to pass legislation that includes background checks for gun show purchases and child safety locks. He has also proposed $ 280 million to hire 500 more agents for the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms and 1,000 new prosecutors. In addition, the administration has threatened a national class-action suit by HUD's public housing projects against gunmakers. Cuomo said that suit would go forward unless the manufacturers settled suits already filed by 28 cities, including St. Louis. Those suits accuse the makers of negligently allowing firearms to fall into the hands of criminals.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1023.text: GUN-5491 PRIMARY N.R.A. Sues to Challenge New Database on Sale of Guns The National Rifle Association filed a lawsuit today in Federal District Court here in an effort to block a Federal database of gun purchases generated from new instant background checks of people buying guns. The group's suit seeks to stop the F.B.I. from keeping a record of gun transactions for six months to conduct audits to determine if gun dealers are following the law and to check for fraud and abuse of the system. The rifle association contends the Brady Law, under which the background checks are done, specifically bans the Federal Government from retaining records and keeping what the group contends is a central registry of gun purchases. The N.R.A. is joined in this assertion by some privacy groups. "I'm not a particularly big gun fan myself," said David Banisar, policy director of the Electronic Privacy Information Center. "I don't have one and don't intend to get one. But you are talking about something that is constitutionally protected and now you're talking about the F.B.I. collecting information on people exercising their constitutional rights. That is troublesome. If this were to happen in the First Amendment context, people would be up in arms." The new rules were mandated by the Brady Law, the Federal legislation named after James Brady, former President Ronald Reagan's press secretary who was critically wounded in a 1981 assassination attempt on Mr. Reagan. President Clinton has often called the measure one of his major crime-fighting achievements, saying it has prevented more than 250,000 handguns from being bought by felons.

1027.text: GUN-10029 PRIMARY Letters | Gun ownership not at heart of being American Re: the Nov. 29 Inquirer articles "About 500 mourn devoted young father," "Phila. man, 23, found fatally shot in Nicetown," and "Packing heat - and political punch." Guns are making a combat zone of our streets. Shootings wreak havoc from the inner city to the rural communities of Pennsylvania. As a medic with the Marines in Vietnam, I tried to save the victims of guns and other weapons.
