In "Packing heat - and political punch," I am appalled by Tammy Bruce's comment, "Owning a gun is at the heart of what it means to be an American." Many of us who have experienced the results of gun violence do not equate owning a gun with being an American. As a veteran, I want to see the dying stop. To stem this appalling violence, it is important that we have legislation, not influenced by the gun lobby, that addresses the plague of handguns in our streets. As a start, it is imperative that in Pennsylvania handgun purchases be limited to one per month. The death and injury caused by guns are unacceptable and unnecessary. We must do all we can to stop the carnage. Mike FelkerPhiladelphia The Revolution Center The Inquirer has done a public service by reporting on the unfortunate clash between the American Revolution Center and the National Park Service ("Revolution museum hits setback," Nov. 3). The Park Service seems to be ignoring the support the center has received from distinguished historians such as David McCullough and Don Higginbotham, and from thousands of other people who see the immense value of having, in a single building, the entire story of the American Revolution. It is almost unbelievable, but such a center does not exist anywhere in the United States.

1025.text: GUN-2792 PRIMARY Reject the gun lobby; ban assault weapons Today, members of Congress will be asked to choose between your personal safety and their political wallets. For some lawmakers, sad to say, it's a tough call. The exact question is: Should Congress ban the manufacture and sale of 19 assault-style firearms? It's a simple choice for thoughtful lawmakers. Many guns have legitimate sporting value, which is why the proposed ban excludes 650 firearms. But the guns it does prohibit - large-capacity semiautomatic shotguns and other vicious, shoot-from-the-hip firearms - are instruments of murder and nothing more. Banning them is the least Congress can do. Although 77% of all Americans favor such a ban, the vote is close, thanks largely to the gun lobby's low-blow politicking. -- On the public front, they lie. Pro-gun lobbyists say that the ban violates a Second Amendment right to bear arms, even though that claim has never been upheld in federal court. They say advocates of the assault-weapon ban want to ban all guns, even though most support gun ownership in the context of sensible restrictions. And they say assault-style weapons are blameless, even though federal law enforcement data show them to be the weapon of choice for many criminals. -- On the political front, they buy. In 1991 and 1992, the National Rifle Association's political action committee spent $ 1.1 million on direct and indirect campaign contributions to successful House candidates. The NRA spent another $ 1.1 million in "communication costs" to rally its own members in districts with important congressional contests.

1026.text: GUN-1390 PRIMARY Brady, yes; but not at all costs Once upon a time, George Bush declared that he would support the Brady Bill - which provides a five-day waiting period for the purchase of handguns - if it were part of a comprehensive crime bill. Such a bill has worked its way through both houses of Congress and through a conference committee, but it's being held up by a Republican filibuster in the Senate. The bill, which enjoys the support of national police organizations, is hardly a criminal-coddling measure. It thumps its chest by creating a federal death penalty for dozens of crimes, few of which are ever committed. It authorizes aid for a host of law-enforcement and crime- prevention programs. The bill also limits federal habeas corpus - the constitutional right under which defendants can go to federal court with claims that their constitutional rights have been violated in state court. And it makes an exception to the so-called exclusionary rule by permitting evidence gathered with a defective warrant to be introduced in court, providing the warrant was obtained in good faith. So why are the Republicans - at Mr. Bush's instance - holding the bill up? The answer is: the Brady Bill. The National Rifle Association has read Mr. Bush the riot act on this modest piece of gun-control legislation. He, not surprisingly, has caved. Today, the Senate will vote one more time to limit debate on the crime bill. In all likelihood, the vote will fail. After that, it could take up the Brady Bill as a separate measure.

1024.text: GUN-30501 PRIMARY Pa. black caucus: No gun laws, no budget; Angry Phila. legislators aim to hold up spending bill over crime relief. HARRISBURG - Anger about the lack of action on gun-control bills boiled over onto the House floor yesterday, with several African American Democrats from Philadelphia vowing to hold up passage of the state budget unless their bills are considered. With the city's death toll approaching 200 this year, six members of the Pennsylvania Legislative Black Caucus made impassioned pleas for help during an unexpected two-hour discussion about gun violence and many of its root causes: drugs, joblessness and poverty. They were not debating a bill; none of the 14 anticrime bills introduced this session has even made it out of a House committee. Amid a busy day filled with a slew of budget bills, Rep. Jewell Williams, chairman of the Philadelphia delegation, used a parliamentary procedure known as "unanimous consent" to voice his frustration over the stalled gun-control bills. "I am going to make it completely clear: no gun legislation, no votes for the budget," Williams said. "Philadelphia members are constantly asked to support legislation that helps other areas of the state as our pleas for help continue to fall on deaf ears. The Democrats hold a one-seat majority in the 102-member House. The caucus, with 17 members, could block the budget. Rep. Thomas W. Blackwell of Philadelphia told anti-gun-control rural lawmakers, who make up the majority of the House, that he had no intention of trying to restrict the rights of law-abiding citizens.

1029.text: GUN-6070 PRIMARY GEORGIA SCHOOL SHOOTING; "Oh, my God, he's got a gun! The difference between the Democratic and GOP bills restricting gun-show sales of firearms is, literally, a matter of hours. Republicans wanted to limit background checks at gun shows to 24 hours instead of three days, the current period. The measure approved Thursday also calls for a 90-day waiting period before law enforcement agencies can expunge background check records. The Senate vote and the Georgia shooting came a month to the day after the bloody massacre at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo. That episode left 15 people dead, including the two students who shot down 12 of their schoolmates and a beloved teacher. The perpetrators , members of Columbine's "Trenchcoat Mafia" of self-described outcasts, killed themselves at the end of their rampage. Unlike the young desperados at Columbine High School, the Georgia student accused of Thursday's shootings was reportedly distraught over breaking up with his girlfriend earlier in the week. According to one student, the youngster said, "I have no reason to live anymore. This from a 15-year-old kid who took out his frustration over a puppy love gone sour by spraying bullets on the last day of classes at his high school. The wonder is that no one was killed on the spot, and for that the wounded may be thankful their assailant had nothing more powerful than .22 caliber weapons. One of the weapons used at Columbine High School was a semiautomatic TEC-9 handgun, a veritable bullet pump compared to a .22 caliber rifle or revolver.

1028.text: GUN-3070 PRIMARY Justice Department gets more ammo to fight crime The Clinton administration is tackling crime, the nation's No. 1 issue, with a proposed Justice Department budget that increases spending by more than 24%. Part of the boost to more than $ 13 billion in fiscal year 1995 would come from a special anti-crime fund for hiring more local police, restricting handgun sales and tightening U.S. borders. At a time of belt-tightening, the Justice Department was one of the few agencies to get such a hefty increase, said Attorney General Janet Reno. But even at the Justice Department, some significant items were slashed, including a $ 375 million program for law enforcement aid to localities. "The bottom line is we will use our resources where they are needed most," Reno said. Highlights of the department's 1995 spending plan: $ 1.7 billion for grants to state and local governments for 50,000 police, and to expand community policing. Another $ 6 billion would be provided for 1996-99 to meet President Clinton's pledge to put 100,000 more police officers on the beat. $ 100 million to help states implement the Brady law, which calls for waiting periods on handgun purchases. $ 398 million to strengthen controls on illegal immigration. Proposed measures include expedited deportation of criminal immigrants and a crackdown on fraudulent documents. $ 450 million to add 9,673 beds and pay higher costs at crowded federal prisons.

1030.text: GUN-5128 PRIMARY Proposal would violate our right to bear firearms Re: Panel put gun control in the hands of voters, March 18. The Constitution Revision Commission has decided to abandon their  high-minded efforts to better the Florida Constitution by passing some of the  easy, politically correct issues and leaving the tough decisions for another  day and another commission. But, "despite hundreds of protesters and  thousands of faxes and e-mails insisting that Florida needs no further limits  on guns," they decided by a 24-11 vote that it is wise to reinstate local  government gun control and send the issue to the ballot with the hopes that it  would be taken out of the state Legislature's hands. In 1987, the Florida Legislature, as a result of abusive regulation at the hands of local governments, pre-empted the regulation of firearms and ammunition, reserving regulation for itself. Now the CRC wants to give it back to these same local governments. Has crime increased in Florida since 1987?   No, in fact, it has gone down. The passing of CRC 167 shows either their willingness to hand off the  state's responsibilities to someone else or it is a power grab by local  politicians. Who does the commission really represent, the people or local government? Should this amendment pass, it would allow county commissioners to create 67 different sets of rules in this state. A patchwork of local laws entrapping citizens for merely crossing county lines with no way of knowing all local laws.

1033.text: GUN-8260 PRIMARY Nelson lashes out at McCollum Moving his so far low-profile U.S. Senate campaign onto the offensive, Democrat Bill Nelson Monday jumped on Republican Bill McCollum as out of step with mainstream Floridians on guns, abortion and education. The insurance commissioner and former congressman told more than 75 women gathered in Ybor City that  those will be key issues for showing voters who truly represents most common sense Floridians. He told the crowd that as a fifth-generation Floridian, he grew up with guns, is an avid hunter, and is son-in-law to one of the world's most renowned trophy hunters. "But we don't need to be selling AK-47s," Nelson said, noting that McCollum not only voted against bans on assault weapons  but also against various bills that included waiting periods for handgun purchases, such as the Brady Bill. "I don't think that's reasonable. I don't think that's common sense," he said, standing on a stairwell in the ornate lobby of the Don Vicente de Ybor Historic Inn. In Congress, Nelson supported waiting periods. The sharp contrasts Nelson offered between him and McCollum appeared to signal the start of a more aggressive campaign mode. For months, McCollum has been crisscrossing the state for campaign appearances and flooding newspapers with near-daily faxes about McCollum's doings and Nelson's shortcomings. Nelson, comfortably leading in statewide polls, had kept a much lower profile but lately often had been  on the defensive against various criticisms and charges by the McCollum campaign.

1034.text: GUN-4580 PRIMARY 'Right to Carry' Gun Laws Help Deter Crime To the Editor: Your April 9 news article on Representative Charles E. Schumer's study showing that firearms from states with weak gun laws are often exported to other states and used in crimes was interesting but failed to ask the crucial question: Why do criminals take these guns to other states rather than use them where they obtained them? One possible answer is that criminals are hesitant to commit crimes in states with weak gun laws because they fear that their potential victims might also be armed. Four of the states mentioned in your article (Texas, Virginia, Florida and Georgia) have seen measurable reduction in rapes, homicides and assaults following legislation granting citizens the right to apply for concealed-weapon permits. The wild predictions of "Dodge City" have never materialized, and the nonparticipating majority get a free ride from the deterrent value of a small number of permit holders. Thirty-one states have enacted "right to carry" laws, with lower net incidents of violence as a result. Tragedies like the Long Island Rail Road and Empire State Building shootings have been prevented elsewhere by lawful gun owners on numerous occasions, and criminals know it. Someday mainstream America will realize that trying to reduce crime by keeping victims defenseless is a failed strategy and that the gun control emperor has no clothes. Until then, Mr. Schumer can continue to play the tailor.

1035.text: NA-42183 PRIMARY Jan. 16 letters to the editor Political courage is what's needed All members of Congress will soon be forced to make decisions on addressing the gun-control issue in the wake of the Newtown, Conn., tragedy. They all know several things can and must be done. They know, among other things, that the sale of assault weapons must be stopped, that magazine capacity of weapons must be diminished and that gun shows must adhere to the same standards as gun stores. They know new laws will require more personnel for enforcement and investigation. They also know 47 percent of American households possess firearms and that, in certain areas of this country, a great number of people are single-issue voters on gun control. Congressional members know very well that a vote that's politically wrong -- even though it's the right thing to do -- could be a great risk to their political future. I say to members of Congress, look in the mirror. Who are you serving -- your country, your state, your district or yourself? So what if you lose your next election. At least you have not lost your integrity. We know you have compassion and feel grief. What we are asking from you is to have political courage. Robert Cousins Walnut Creek There is a lack of community I applaud Marco Manriquez for his Jan. 9 letter, "Need neighborhood unity to save lives," because he hit the nail on the head.

1031.text: GUN-11826 PRIMARY Dec. 21 Readers' letters: Seeking solutions to mass shootings Restrict gun access by the mentally unstable I have hunted and shot guns more than 50 years and other than offending my libertarian streak, I couldn't care less if they limited the maximum capacity of all firearms to 10 rounds. That said, if our intent is to try to prevent or minimize these tragedies, limiting capacity or banning assault weapons would have prevented or even minimized the tragedies that occurred in Aurora or Newtown reflects no understanding of firearms. In both cases the shooters had at least three guns. The only way magazine capacity would be an issue is if you assume that one of the unarmed patrons in the theater or second-grade students would have rushed the shooter and succeeded in over powering him in the second or two while he reloaded (assuming of course he didn't simply shoot them with one of his other loaded guns). It seems to me that the answer is to make sure that mentally unstable people are promptly reported to law enforcement authorities and prevented from buying or having access to firearm. If we are really interested in preventing these terrible tragedies from happening we should enact legislation that has a reasonable chance of actually preventing them. Randy Creech San Jose Citizen militias prevent government tyranny Several letter writers recently wrote about repealing the Second Amendment. They must not understand why the Second Amendment was made part of the Bill of Rights. The Founding Fathers had just overthrown what they thought was the most tyrannical government in the world. Never, to the best of my knowledge, had such a group of men gathered together to write a document that protected the rights of citizens like our Constitution. They were intelligent men who knew from history that all governments eventually tend to tyranny. They also knew that standing armies are used to enforce that tyranny, and that the only counter to this was a well-trained and well-equipped militia that included all men ages 16 and 45.

1036.text: GUN-4833 PRIMARY Handguns In the Cross Hairs IN COUNTERPOINT to the opening of the National Firearms Museum, Handgun Control Inc. is sponsoring an anti-gun art show in the Target Gallery of the Torpedo Factory in Alexandria. The show originated in Pittsburgh and consists of artworks based on deactivated handguns acquired by that city's police department in a buyback program designed to get guns off the streets. The Alexandria show contains nearly half of the 65 submissions accepted, which range from pointed to ploddingly polemic. Oddly, the works that took the top places in the juried show are among the least engaging. The first-place winner, "Ascend and Remember" (undated, as are all the entries) by Baltimore's Kirsten Rook, is a multimedia assemblage centered on a revolver chamber. The three jurors, all avowed gun-haters, seem to have based their rankings largely on the artists' statements, which in Rook's case is, in part: "Random violence makes the ground we stand on thin as an eggshell. A branch from the tree of life can become a crown of thorns if we bottle up our tears while the flesh of our loved ones turns to ashes. . . . I too am afraid, but I am more afraid of owning a gun." Second place went to a riveted bronze "gun coffin" by Todd Noe of Philadelphia. "I have enclosed a gun in a seemingly impenetrable metal box," his statement says. "No light. No contact. Confined and alone. Encased and entombed. Forever." Unlike the other two winners, the piece is quite skillfully made.

1032.text: GUN-6283 PRIMARY Q&A ON THE NEWS Q: Q&A has printed contact information about the National Rifle Association. I know it's the principal organized gun advocacy group in the United States. However, we rarely hear about organizations advocating gun control. Do you know the name and address of such a group, and how one might make a donation? --- Larry Haskell, Acworth A: There are several gun control groups. One of the best known is the Center to Prevent Handgun Violence (and its lobbying arm, Handgun Control Inc. ), with Sarah Brady serving as its public chairwoman and prominent representative. She is the wife of James Brady, who was shot and severely disabled March 30, 1981, by John Hinckley Jr., President Ronald Reagan's would-be assassin. Donations can be sent to Center to Prevent Handgun Violence, 1225 Eye St. N.W., Suite 1100, Washington, DC 20005. Information can be obtained online at www.handguncontrol.org or you can call 202-898-0792. Another is the Coalition to Stop Gun Violence and its educational affiliate, the Educational Fund to End Handgun Violence. Donations can be sent to Coalition to Stop Gun Violence, 1000 16th St. N.W., Suite 603, Washington, DC 20036. Its Web site is www.gunfree.org or you can call 202-530-0340. An Atlanta-area organization with similar aims is Georgians Against Gun Violence Inc., P.O. Box 673732, Marietta, GA 30006-0063. Call 404-521-3605 for information, or check its Web site, www.gagv.org.

characters: Educational Fund to End Handgun Violence

1037.text: GUN-10984 PRIMARY Knee-jerk gun control in Congress What is as predictable as a threatened snowstorm bringing Atlanta to a standstill? Answer: gun control advocates taking advantage of every shooting incident as an excuse to further restrict Americans' Second Amendment rights. The recent shooting incident in Tucson, Ariz. --- involving a clearly deranged individual who shot Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, a federal judge and several other innocent people --- is no exception. Doctors had barely announced that Giffords had survived the shooting, than gun-control legislators at the nation's capital began trotting out their latest anti-gun schemes. Rep. Carolyn McCarthy, D-N.Y., was in the forefront. Close behind was her Empire State colleague, Republican Rep. Peter King. While most anti-firearms proposals emanating from the Congress are simply ineffective or unworkable, King's most recent proposal is utterly idiotic. In response to the Tucson shooting --- in which the gunman showed up at a public, outdoor town hall meeting hosted by Giffords in the parking lot of a local shopping center --- King has proposed what has to be one of the silliest pieces of federal legislation in many a year. He has suggested the federal criminal code be amended to incorporate a new provision making it illegal for a person to possess a firearm within 1,000 feet of a member of Congress. One might suppose that --- were King's proposal to actually become law --- members of Congress would have to walk around at all times with some obvious form of identification readily discernible to everyone within 333 1/3 yards of their presence; something that identifies them as a "Member of Congress." Perhaps it would be required that every member at all times wear a large hat with the congressional seal, or a distinctly colored sweatshirt with the words "MEMBER OF THE CONGRESS OF THE UNITED STATES" printed in a bright, contrasting color.

1039.text: GUN-20166 PRIMARY House gun-control foes try to slow Senate momentum WASHINGTON -- Even before the Senate gave final approval to gun-control legislation Thursday, the fight began to shift to the floor of the House of Representatives. With Democrats demanding a quick vote to press their newfound political advantage, Republican leaders scrambled to buy time to let passions subside over the high school shootings in Littleton, Colo., on April 20 and Conyers, Ga., on Thursday. The GOP leaders conceded that passage of some further restrictions on gun purchases, such as background checks at gun shows, seems inevitable. But gun-control opponents clearly are trying to slow some of the momentum that built up in the Senate, where Republican leaders at times seemed to lose control of the process. "We have adults running the House," said House Majority Whip Tom DeLay, R-Texas, his party's chief vote-counter. The Republican leadership's message to members, he added, is "Whoa, whoa, slow down." At the moment, "the members' nerves are on edge. Their emotions are running high," DeLay said in an interview after a bipartisan leadership meeting in which Republicans proposed holding a vote on gun control in mid-June. But Democratic gun-control advocates say they are prepared to engage in parliamentary guerrilla warfare to force the issue next week before House members leave town for a planned Memorial Day recess. Rep. Nita Lowey, D-N.Y., said she and other Democrats are prepared to seek "every opportunity" to attach gun-control amendments to legislation on the floor or in committee.

1041.text: GUN-11482 PRIMARY YES: Silence will end in more death It's time for a conversation about gun laws. Naysayers insist it's not the right time. But then, they never think it's the right time. That's because they, particularly the gun lobby, don't want to change the status quo. Coloradans deserve better. We're better than that. Who can blame many Coloradans for questioning civilian possession of military assault rifles, those weapons of mass destruction that fulfill the sick fantasies of disturbed people who want an easy means of mowing people down? Coloradans recognize that while the Aurora and Columbine killers had bombs, the victims were all shot, not killed or injured with bombs. Many Coloradans wonder why any law-abiding citizen -- let alone a mentally disturbed man -- needs a 100-round magazine. After all, we never hear of home invasions undertaken by 100 people. And they're shocked that mentally disturbed people have such unfettered access to 6,000 rounds of ammunition, with no background check or record-keeping. Coloradans are tired of the naysayers' simplistic responses to calls for changes in gun laws, such as "guns don't kill people, people kill people." These tired old clich\xE9s do nothing to solve problems; they're just a distraction from reasoned debates. Coloradans are tired of the naysayers' insistence that those calling for gun law reform are only interested in taking away all guns and in "punishing" law-abiding gun owners rather than criminals.

1040.text: GUN-20167 PRIMARY Landmark gun bill passes Senate vote comes just hours after school shooting in Ga. WASHINGTON -- A month after the school massacre in Littleton, Colo., the Senate broadly approved a juvenile crime bill Thursday night that contains the nation's most sweeping restrictions on gun possession in five years. Earlier in the day, Vice President Gore cast a dramatic tie-breaking vote on a Democratic amendment that would require background checks on all buyers at gun shows and pawn shops. The 51-50 victory reversed last week's 51-47 rejection of a similar proposal. The Senate action came as President Clinton met in Colorado with families of the victims of the Columbine High School tragedy and 12 hours after another school shooting in Conyers, Ga., that left six students injured. Senate Minority Leader Tom Daschle, D-S.D., said the Senate action represented a "sea change" in public and political attitudes toward gun control. "What you just saw was the NRA (National Rifle Association) losing its grip on the U.S. Senate at long last," he said. But Tom Korologos, a veteran lobbyist representing the NRA, gave the bill only a 20% chance of making it through the House of Representatives and onto the president's desk. "We took one on the chin today," Korologos said. He said the NRA cannot be counted out and will oppose the bill as "too big and too controversial." House Republican leaders agreed to allow a gun control vote next month after scrambling to avoid a showdown on the issue Thursday morning.

1042.text: GUN-2755 PRIMARY BARBER CHARGED WITH ILLEGAL GUN SALES A barber in University City has been accused of buying semi-automatic weapons as a private citizen and selling them illegally. Baxter C. Fort, 25, was arrested Tuesday and charged with selling firearms without a federal firearms license. Fort works as a barber at 8431 Olive Boulevard in University City and lives in the 1300 block of Leroy Avenue in Pagedale. The arrest followed a six-month joint investigation in which Fort sold a dozen semiautomatic weapons - mostly 9 mm pistols - to undercover detectives, authorities said. The federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms said Fort was involved in the transfer or sale of about 100 firearms during the six months. "Basically, you'd go to him and tell him what you wanted, and then he'd go out and buy the weapon as a private citizen and sell it back to you for a nice profit of a couple of hundred dollars," said Capt. Harry Hegger, commander of the St. Louis Police Department's intelligence unit. People dealing with Fort were people unable to purchase weapons on their own because of a criminal history or some other reason, police said.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1043.text: NA-43013 PRIMARY The Gun Report: October 17, 2013 A gun capable of determining whether it is being held by its owner could hit the market within 45 days, thanks to a German-based company called Armatix. And because of a 2003 law, New Jersey could be the first state in the nation to adopt smart-gun technology, NJ.com reported yesterday. The state's biometric gun law, signed by former Gov. James McGreevey, requires all guns sold in the state to be equipped with biometric technology that allows only their owners to fire them. But the law has remained unenforceable because, until now, no such guns have existed. Enter Armatix, which has developed a firearm that can determine, with 90 percent accuracy, whether a gun was being held by a person wearing a watch meant to pair with the firearm. But if Assemblyman Michael Patrick Carroll has its way, the law will never be enforced. Before a crowd at a V.F.W. in Whippany, N.J., Tuesday night, he said biometrics would make a simple technology complicated, unreliable and expensive, and doubted the smart gun's ability to fire only when pointed at its target. "When I'm talking about targets, I'm talking about someone who's about to rape my wife," said Carroll, who is also fighting to make it easier to grant concealed-carry permits in the state. "And I want to be sure that the gun goes off when I'm firing at that particular target."

1045.text: GUN-6578 PRIMARY NRA takes aim at Owens plan Letter urges members to push for defeat of background-check bill The National Rifle Association has declared political war on  Gov. Bill Owens' proposal to require background checks at gun  shows. The gun group has sent a letter to its 80,000 Colorado  members, urging them to call their state representatives and  senators and ask for a 'no' vote on the bill. Given the clout the NRA enjoys with the GOP, the group's  offensive will test Owens' ability to get his fellow Republicans  to support his gun-control plan. Their support is crucial, since  Republicans control both chambers of the Legislature. The NRA's objections stem not so much from the wording of the  bill, HB 1242, but from its concerns about the way the FBI handles  instant criminal-background checks. 'HB 1242 will expand the FBI's current illegal  gun-owner-registration scheme, could shut down gun shows as we  know them and lead us down a path of further government regulation  of private firearms sales between law-abiding individuals - even  your friends and family,' said the letter from Randy Kozuch, who  is in charge of the NRA's lobbying effort in statehouses. It was sent out during the weekend, and legislators started  getting calls Monday. Opponents say they respect the NRA's tactics, but they think  most Colorado residents support the background checks. 'This is good grass-roots lobbying,' said House Minority  Leader Ken Gordon, a Denver Democrat and sponsor of the gun-show  bill. ' This is what I want the people who feel the other way to  do.'

1044.text: NA-43036 PRIMARY The Gun Report: February 26, 2014 We have written before about gun sales transpiring on Instagram and Reddit, mostly without background checks. Now we can add Facebook, where photos of breastfeeding mothers are censored, but gun sales continue unabated. Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America recently created a spot styled after Facebook's recent 10th anniversary "A Look Back" videos-compiling the highlights of each user's activity since they joined the social network-which features gun adverts posted on users' profiles and gun sales groups where users congregate and peddle their wares. "Anyone can buy and sell guns without criminal background checks," the video warns. The organization is spearheading an effort to get gun sales banned from Facebook and Instagram, and a press rep at Mayors Against Illegal Guns told us that, so far, Facebook has been receptive to a discussion on how to fix the policy. Craigslist, eBay and Google have banned the buying, selling or trading of firearms on their sites without background checks. Last October, a 15-year-old boy brought a 9mm handgun to a Kentucky high school that he had purchased from a convicted felon on Facebook. The man, who lived in Ashland, Ohio, arranged the sale through the Portsmouth Pickers Facebook group. On Feb. 19, police caught 21-year-old William Kurtz, a convicted felon from Storm Lake, Iowa, attempting to acquire weapons via Facebook. In a transaction with an undercover officer, Kurtz allegedly exchanged an AR-15 assault rifle for a .45 caliber semi-automatic handgun.

characters: Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America

1046.text: GUN-6731 PRIMARY IN BRIEF; Trigger Locks Two top-level Westchester officials last week took steps to first encourage -- then mandate -- that trigger locks be used on all registered handguns in the county. County Clerk Leonard N. Spano, a Republican, will be distributing, within the next few months, free trigger locks to county residents with valid pistol permits. County Executive Andrew J. Spano, a Democrat and no relation to the county clerk, formally proposed a law that would require gun owners to install trigger locks on handguns and rifles. He also announced that he would sign an executive order on Jan. 2 requiring all county employees who have guns under county jurisdiction to use trigger locks when storing their weapons. "Children are irresistibly drawn to guns, not aware of the deadly consequences they can bring," Leonard Spano said. "But the tragedies of loaded firearms are avoidable, and a trigger lock is a common sense answer." The free trigger locks will be available after bids are received from sellers. Each trigger lock is made of sturdy die-cast metal and can be used on almost every type of handgun. The trigger locks come with a lock and key to prevent a child or any unauthorized person from using the gun. The locks, which would cost $4 to $5 each at stores, will be paid for with a $90,200 state grant obtained with the help of State Senator Guy J. Valella and $130,000 in drug forfeiture funds from the county's Department of Public Safety.

1048.text: GUN-2649 PRIMARY LETTERS Geewax off target in bashing the NRA I am one of the "fanatics and NRA extremists" described by Madame Geewax in her amusing little article on Wyatt Earp vs. the NRA, published in your paper July 15. For the record, I am neither a fanatic nor an extremist, but I am a member of the NRA, and I am proud of it. Her comments cannot go unanswered, and I raise the following points: 1. Ms. Geewax states that Wyatt Earp was "a hardened man with a reputation for being a horse thief . . . " I'm sorry, but when I went to school, a horse thief was a criminal. So we are forced to the inescapable conclusion that the city fathers of 1876 Dodge placed the entire safety of their city in the hands of a known criminal. And then sat back and allowed Earp to do what every criminal would like to do - trample all over the Second Amendment rights of everyone in the city. 2. Mr. Geewax states that the law-abiding citizens of Dodge "knew they needed guns to hunt and protect themselves on the range, but they weren't fanatical enough to think they had a constitutional right to swagger through the streets carrying weapons of war." Well, whether they "thought" they did or not, they actually did, and do, have that right, under the Second Amendment of the Constitution of the United States. In the same way that Ms. Geewax and her ilk have the right, under the First Amendment, to write arrant claptrap.

1038.text: GUN-889 PRIMARY GOOD GUN LAWS DEMAND COMPROMISE PUT LOGIC, NOT EMOTION, TO WORK TO AVOID; ANOTHER SCHOOLYARD MASSACRE The recent murder of five children in a schoolyard in Stockton, Calif., by a man wielding an assault rifle prompts renewed pleas to the California Legislature for the rapid outlawing of such weapons.   In my ninth year as a California state legislator, I can tell you that arguments on gun control rank among the most shrill and illogical to which a legislator can listen. They have much of the high emotional content of the debate over abortion, fraught with the same emotionalism that obscures practical and logical aspects of the problem.   During all my years as a public official there has existed a highly militant pressure group that would ban any and all personal possession of firearms.   This group is offset by others that fight for the right to possess any and all firearms. These two polarized factions have thus far been incapable of rational communication. I have lived on both sides of the fence. As a hunter, I have owned weapons designed for effective hunting. As a private citizen, I have owned handguns useful in defending my family from dangerous predatory human beings. I will not docilely tolerate any person or government interfering with my right to engage in lawful hunting or the lawful protection of my family. I have, on the other hand, spent 37 years as an officer, then police chief, in the Los Angeles Police Department watching all of the damage done by irresponsible people with guns and all of the carnage done by criminals in possession of guns. The idea of a total ban on fire arms or all handguns is abhorrent to me. We have too many defenseless citizens, often elderly, who are robbed at their places of business, on the streets and in their homes. Until we provide our police forces and courts with more funds to reform our criminal justice system and take these mad dogs out of the community, I'm going to maintain a firearm and I think every other law-abiding citizen should have that right.   I find the ''all or nothing'' approach of opponents on this issue disturbing. I don't want emotionalism on this issue; I want cold, logical arguments on the facts. With deliberate speed, the Legislature should, once all the facts are known, confront this problem by evaluating the type of weapon in question and the type of individual who commits such murders.   Does the type of rifle used in the killings in Stockton on Jan. 17 - an AK-47 - have any legitimate hunting or sporting use, or is it so easily converted into a machine gun that it should be banned, the way we banned the sawed-off shotgun and the machine gun? I don't yet know the answer, but I intend to listen dispassionately to both sides.   If we decide to restrict or ban such weapons, it should be done not by broad language, such as ''semi-automatic military weapons,'' but by a distinct identification of the AK-47 or other specific weapons we wish to restrict.   I will intently listen to police professionals on this issue as well as to abolitionists and members of the gun lobby. My years of experience in Sacramento have, however, convinced me that all three groups are equally uncompromising in their positions.   Most significant legislation comes about when both sides resolve enough of their differences to discover a synergistic approach. This happens too infrequently, and we have too many logjams in which powerful interests stymie constructive laws.   All too often the public falls prey to legislative impotence rather than reform. One example of a special-interest stalemate involves workers' compensation laws. Competing special interests have in effect assured that injured workers are inadequately compensated while employers pay increasingly high costs for workers' compensation insurance and medical and legal practitioners prosper from the process.   I do not damn the pressure groups, such as lawyers, trade unions and professional and business organizations. They are healthy to good government; without them individuals would be ineffective in having their voices heard in the far-off legislative bodies of Sacramento and Washington. The only evil is when these groups abuse their power by sitting like pigs in the middle of the road of progress to prevent any solution.   Let us break with this poor tradition so reasonable compromises may be achieved. We must convince the 1989 California Legislature that if everyone gives a little, solutions can be reached that protect all of our basic rights while dealing effectively with these issues. Most important, let us do everything in our power to minimize the possibility of another schoolyard massacre. Can we afford to do less? Ed Davis, who originated Neighborhood Watch when he was the Los Angeles police chief, is a state senator.   Los Angeles Times

1051.text: GUN-2580 PRIMARY Senate OKs ban on 19 assault guns Reflecting public fear of crime in the streets, the Senate voted Wednesday to ban the manufacture or sale of 19 assault-style weapons as part of a $ 22 billion crime package. As the Senate moved toward final passage of the crime bill, Minority Leader Robert Dole, R-Kan., continued to threaten to include a version of the Brady bill in an effort to exert leverage over its provisions. Late Wednesday, Senate leaders were trying to force an agreement to debate the handgun purchase waiting period as a separate measure. Gun control proponents want to keep the bill separate so it can move to the president's desk without the delays certain to come when the House and Senate reconcile vastly different anti-crime plans. Opponents also want a separate vote so the Brady bill can be derailed without jeopardizing favored elements of the package: billions of dollars for more police and prisons. As passed by the House last week, the Brady bill requires gun purchasers to wait five days to let authorities conduct criminal background checks. The waiting period would expire after five years on the assumption states would be able to conduct instant computer background checks by then. Dole hopes to include a provision pre-empting state waiting period laws once the instant check is up and running. The Brady bill is named after ex-White House spokesman James Brady, who was injured in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Reagan.

1047.text: GUN-10453 PRIMARY Editorial: Gun law preserved With most firearms regulations set by Harrisburg, there had been an element of civil disobedience in the widening movement of Pennsylvania cities and towns that are targeting gun trafficking with local ordinances that require reporting lost or stolen handguns. Well, not anymore. The state Supreme Court last week, in effect, approved the legal strategy. By refusing to hear a National Rifle Association challenge of a lower-court ruling, the court upheld Philadelphia's mandate that handgun owners must alert police soon after they discover a weapon missing. Philadelphia's ordinance - like those approved by Pittsburgh, Erie, Allentown, and two dozen other towns - is aimed at reducing the number of weapons purchased legally, then resold, often to criminals. Armed with these laws, police can go after "straw buyers" who claim that handguns were misplaced or stolen. Even though the local laws infringe on no one's right to own a legal handgun, they've been challenged in court by the NRA. While the NRA won't give an inch, the courts have been more favorable. For now, gun reporting fortunately remains the law in many of the communities suffering most from illegal handguns. The real import of the ruling, of course, is that it should keep up the pressure on Harrisburg to pass a statewide gun-reporting requirement that would help protect all Pennsylvanians from the scourge of handgun violence.

1049.text: GUN-1635 PRIMARY Gun Control, Privatized Last fall, Washington D.C.'s voters approved an innovative measure that employs product liability law to limit private possession of assault weapons where more direct efforts to ban them have failed. A similar measure before New York's State Legislature could give this new gun control tactic a welcome boost. The bill would subject manufacturers and sellers of assault weapons and unlicensed handguns used in New York to "strict liability." They would be responsible for damages caused by the guns they produce or distribute, even if they have no knowledge of the crime. There would be no strict liability for injuries caused by licensed guns, or for people who sustain gun wounds in the course of committing crimes. The law now recognizes such liability for many businesses that pursue "abnormally dangerous" activity for profit -- storers of explosives, transporters of gasoline, disposers of toxic waste. There's no denying the danger of casual gun sales. In New York and other cities, illegal handguns are a big factor in gun crimes. Assault weapons, often used to spray bullets indiscriminately in street shootouts, have killed or wounded innocent bystanders. As it has privatized regulation of other commercial activity that poses a clear threat to public safety, strict liability could privatize gun control. Minimally, the prospect of sizable tort claims would force gun makers and dealers to carry more insurance, thereby increasing the price of dangerous weapons to consumers while providing a new source of funds to compensate victims.

1053.text: GUN-8269 PRIMARY Gun control takes center stage as convention reaches climax; THE DEMOCRATIC CONVENTION Los Angeles --- The push for "common-sense gun control," a Democratic mantra in Washington, hit the City of Angels this week. On the podium, on the airwaves, and on political buttons, the issue of guns and children permeated the party's national convention, including a prime- time appearance by a parent who lost a child at last year's Columbine High School shooting in Littleton, Colo. At a forum on the subject Wednesday, Secretary of Housing and Urban Development Andrew Cuomo spoke with fervor about the need to regulate guns. There are about 30,000 homicides, suicides and fatal accidents attributed to guns each year, Cuomo said. "It is almost unbelievable that our country is letting this continue . . . when it is so unnecessary, so avoidable," he said. While there are safety measures for medicine bottles and garage-door openers, Cuomo said, "when it comes to guns, we say nothing." "How can this be? How can the Congress allow it to continue?" The forum, sponsored by Handgun Control Inc., a group that supports gun regulation, highlighted the Democratic hope that the issue will help Vice President Al Gore in November, especially with independent voters and women. "I think that issue will play very well," said Barbara Allen, Democratic Party chairwoman for North Carolina. "We've got to have some gun control. . . . Even in North Carolina, we're having too many children killed."

1052.text: GUN-7033 PRIMARY Panel rejects plate proposal Columbine parents to try elsewhere Two frustrated Columbine High School parents said Friday that  they would pursue creation of a 'Respect Life' license plate to  commemorate victims of the April 20 massacre and send a message of  nonviolence to the world after a legislative committee killed a  proposal that would have helped start the program. Dale Todd and Mark Schnurr also said they would solicit money  from the National Rifle Association or a gun-control group to help  pay for the plates. 'Lack of respect is what happened at Columbine,' said Todd,  father of Evan Todd, a Columbine student who sustained flesh  wounds in the back, neck and face last April. 'I might go to the  NRA or to SAFE because they understand what 'respect life' means.'   SAFE, which stands for Sane Alternatives to the Firearms Epidemic,  is a gun-control group. The House Appropriations Committee killed House Bill 1461 on  a 7-4 vote. Some lawmakers on the committee were direct in their  concerns about the anti-abortion implications of the 'Respect  Life' slogan, despite an explanation by Rep. Don Lee, R-Littleton,  the bill's sponsor, that the phrase was not related to the  abortion debate. Todd called the dispute 'an adult fight' that had nothing to do  with the intent of the bill, which he said was to raise money for  the students wounded last year and for future medical costs of  other school-age crime victims while sending an anti-violence  message. He also blamed the news media for starting the controversy.

characters: SAFE (Sane Alternatives to the Firearms Epidemic)

1055.text: GUN-11775 PRIMARY The Scourge of Guns, 1866 Edition Lest you think that the gun-control debate is a modern-day phenomenon, the following editorial appeared a mere 53,437 days ago, on page 4 of The New-York Times  of April 30, 1866, under the headline "Carrying Firearms." Homicide figures for 1865 and 1866 were not immediately available, but in 1868, according to the history tome "Gotham," there were 48 murders in New York City. Carrying Firearms Nearly every day we are reminded by the report of some murderous affray that many men among us go armed, for we constantly hear of pistols being dropped on the floor at balls, or being exploded in very inconvenient ways. It appears, too, that Colt, and Remington, and all of the rest of the pistol-makers, have enormous factories and orders in abundance. We have recently known that a boy of 12 has his pantaloons made with a pistol pocket behind, in which he carries a loaded pistol; and this at a boarding-school filled with boys, who, we suppose, do or wish to do the same thing. "Teaching the young idea [sic] how to shoot," we have heard quoted, but that boarding-schools had come to this, we had not, until this revelation, supposed. Do the teachers know of this thing, and do they connive in it? If they do not know it they ought to; and we would advise parents to look into it, and learn whether shooting is to be a part of the scholastic course which may be practised on their own boys; or else we advise them to see that their boys are properly armed with the most approved and deadly pistol, and that there may be an equal chance at least of their shooting as of being shot.

1050.text: GUN-30113 PRIMARY When corporate work rules collide with gun rights Pizza deliveryman case raises question of whether employer can limit right to bear arms. Missouri's firearm statutes gave the Domino's pizza deliveryman who shot and killed a would-be assailant Dec. 28 every right to use a legal weapon to protect himself and his property. By simply carrying the gun, however, the still unidentified employee violated a company policy prohibiting Domino workers from packing a weapon even if, as happened in Ferguson on the night of the shooting, the employee was using his own car. To some, the circumstances raise questions about where the right of Missouri citizens to legally keep and bear arms ends and the corporate right to limit liability begins. "I am not aware of a case quite like it," said Anders Walker, an assistant law professor at St. Louis University. "The legal riddle is whether Domino's can fire this guy because, if he agreed to work for them and signed a contract, he needs to abide by their policies." To borrow a law school term, the question is moot: The deliveryman quit shortly after the death of Brian Smith, 19, of Ferguson, in what authorities say was a robbery attempt. Another teenager alleged to be Smith's accomplice, Rodney Reese, 18, of University City, has been charged with second-degree murder, first-degree robbery and two counts of armed criminal action. The deliveryman has not been charged. Had he not resigned, a Domino's spokesman said this week, the company would have disciplined the deliveryman.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not directly mentioned)

1054.text: GUN-980 PRIMARY NRA DISTORTS THE INTENT OF THE SECOND AMENDMENT The National Rifle Association has established itself as the protector of our constitutional rights with its objection to a seven-day waiting period on gun purchases. The NRA insists that the right to handgun ownership under the Second Amendment to the Constitution of the United States is being threatened. Let us examine the truth. The Second Amendment reads as follows: "A well regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free state, the right of the people to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed." My right to keep and bear arms is not infringed in any manner by laws passed to restrict the proliferation of firearms. Our Founding Fathers, at the time of the Constitutional Congress, recognized the need for a regulated militia to be available in the event of national emergency. We have the reserve and National Guard units who back up our military today. Where would we have been in Desert Shield and Desert Storm without those support units? The NRA grossly distorts the intent of our Founding Fathers. William C. Corey Jr. Stuart OPINIONS ARE BECOMING ONE-SIDED The May 7 Palm Beach Post was a classic example of how to mold public opinion. The headline on The New York Times' Anthony Lewis column was "Our war stories were shameful." The only apparent shame was that our military, including equipment and personnel, worked magnificently. Mr. Lewis'

1057.text: GUN-11565 PRIMARY Hick: No law could stop him Colorado Gov. John Hickenlooper expressed skepticism Sunday that tougher gun laws would have stopped suspected gunman James Eagan Holmes from unleashing "terrorist" acts against 70 people in an Aurora movie theater. "This person, if there were no assault weapons available, if there were no this or no that, this guy's going to find something, right? He's going to know how to create a bomb. Who knows where his mind would have gone? Clearly a very intelligent individual, however twisted," Hickenlooper said during an interview on CNN's "State of the Union." The governor, who appeared on at least three Sunday-morning talk shows, said his administration "will try to create some checks and balances on these things, but it is an act of evil." "If it was not one weapon, it would have been another, and he was diabolical," he said. Hickenlooper, who referred to the alleged gunman as "warped," a "creature" and a "twisted, really delusional individual," said Holmes was not cooperating with police. Hickenlooper also said he had no insight into the motive of the alleged gunman who killed 12 and wounded 58 at the midnight premiere of "The Dark Knight Rises" early Friday. "So far, nothing that I've heard. Not an iota, nothing," the governor said on NBC's "Meet the Press." When the governor visited victims in area hospitals Saturday, he said he found many exhibiting "buoyancy and resilience."

1056.text: GUN-140 PRIMARY GOETZ CASE; N.Y.'S MIXED SUCCESS Law enforcement officials in New York City, armed with one of the toughest gun statutes in the country, have seen a rapid increase in gun crimes this year and are seizing sophisticated automatic weaponry that was rarely seen in criminal hands as recently as 1985. On the other hand, they say, the gun law, as revised in 1980, has made a difference. '' There are still tons of guns out there,'' said Kevin B. Frawley, the city's criminal justice coordinator. '' But there has been a change, if only in the types of people who have guns. I'd say it's been a qualified success'' in reducing gun crimes by people who are not habitual criminals. The 1980 law - an amendment to the 1911 Sullivan Law, the first gun registration statute in the country -made possession of an unlicensed weapon a serious felony with a mandatory jail sentence of at least a year, unless the judge found that ''unduly harsh.'' As a result, according to court officials, the police, and private law enforcement observers, a narrower range of people are willing to take the additional risk. And more criminals are sent to jail for gun violations. As they expected, officials say, the 1980 statute has little or no deterrent effect on professional criminals, and particularly not on the crack gangs that have emerged in the last few years, whose members often carry machine guns to intimidate would-be rivals.

1059.text: GUN-800 PRIMARY INCLUDE HANDGUNS IN ASSAULT ON RIFLES CHICAGO The gun-control debate has taken surprising twists lately, especially in the terms used. For example, why do we call modern military-style lightweight, rapid-fire rifles that come with clips of 20 or more bullets ''assault rifles''? Does that make the rest ''defense rifles''?   It seems that the difference between an assault rifle and a defense rifle depends pretty much on who aims first. But, no, the rest are usually called ''sport rifles.'' Which raises an interesting question: Can a sport rifle be used for assault? Maybe it depends on your definition of sport.   In many states, assault rifles are sold over the counter to people the National Rifle Association describes as ''serious collectors'' and ''responsible gun owners.'' But not every purchaser lives up to that vaunted definition. One used an AK-47 to kill five pupils and wound 29 other people in an elementary school in Stockton, Calif., a few months ago. Another used an Uzi to kill 21 people in a fast-food restaurant in San Ysidro, Calif., in 1984.   Suddenly the nation was shocked that just about any mope could walk into a gun shop in some states and buy one of these watermelon destroyers without a background check, and that with a little modification it could become an automatic rifle capable of wiping out a classroom in four seconds.   Suddenly assault rifles were a hot topic. And suddenly the sluggish wheels of government reform began to wheeze, grind and lurch forward. The California Assembly voted to ban the sale, possession and manufacture of 24 of the rapid-fire weapons. The Bush administration, in a policy reversal for NRA-member George Bush, banned imports of semiautomatic weapons indefinitely.   Colt Industries announced it would discontinue domestic sales of a semiautomatic version of the military M-16.   Unfortunately, these acts probably will be about as effective in fighting the nation's gun problems as a teaspoon would be in bailing out a rowboat.   The federal ban is temporary while the government studies whether the weapons are, as required by an astonishingly vague law, ''particularly suited for, or readily adaptable to, sporting purposes.'' Sporting purposes?   Legal sales of assault rifles tend to be limited to semiautomatic versions, which means each squeeze of the trigger fires only one shot, instead of the fully automatic military issue, which can empty an entire magazine with one sustained squeeze of the trigger. But it is not difficult to convert a semiautomatic assault rifle into a fully automatic version.   Ohio Sen. Howard Metzenbaum has introduced a bill that would ban assault rifles. Its time has come. A CBS poll released Thursday found 73 percent of Americans want to see a nationwide ban on ownership, manufacture and sale of assault weapons.   But it's kind of goofy to take action on assault rifles without doing something about handguns, too. Almost half of the nation's homicide victims in recent years have been killed by handguns, FBI statistics show. Rifles and shotguns accounted for only 11 percent. The Stockton deaths shocked the nation. But an average o f one child a day dies because of someone's poor use of a handgun.   Since we regulate everything from cars to drugs to driving privileges and barber colleges, it only makes sense that we also regulate guns.

1061.text: GUN-6640 PRIMARY NRA wins first round of gun debate Round one went to the National Rifle Association Monday in  the Colorado Legislature's gun debate. Republicans on the Senate Judiciary Committee defeated the  two Democratic gun-control bills they considered. And three  Democrats joined with a conservative Republican to weaken the  state's criminal background check - another NRA win. It was the first day in a week of marathon hearings that will  give an early hint of what the Legislature is going to do on guns  in the aftermath of the Columbine High School massacre. Today, the  committee is expected to look at gun-rights bills. 'So far, so good,' said Bill Dietrick, legislative director  for the Colorado State Shooting Association, the state chapter of  the NRA. But gun-control supporters and the administration of Gov.  Bill Owens say the battle is far from over. 'This was just an opening round,' said Tom Mauser, who  started lobbying for gun control after his son was killed at  Columbine. ' There's lots more to be done.' Mauser said he thinks the Republicans who control the  Legislature will not want to embarrass Owens, a fellow Republican.   Owens has put forward a package of middle-of-the-road gun  proposals aimed at keeping guns out of the hands of criminals and  children. Owens' point man on guns, his Department of Public Safety  chief, Ari Zavaras, said he's not worried. 'There's still a lot of bills to come,' Zavaras said. 'I  think the majority of legislators will see that what the governor  has proposed is sensible.'

1065.text: GUN-7490 PRIMARY Gun dealer list packs surprises Homes, mortuaries, even car wash licensed to sell A federal agency has licensed dozens of homes to sell  firearms in Colorado cities that claim to prohibit retail gun  sales in residential areas. Homeowners throughout the Denver area have registered  single-family houses on quiet streets as gun shops. None is  calling for background checks on large numbers of gun customers,  according to state records. But together, they have sold hundreds  of guns over the last two years. A Denver Post survey found that in Denver and four suburbs  alone, about 40 residences are licensed to deal guns, including a  house two doors from an elementary school playground in Aurora. Some residential gun dealers see no zoning violation in an  occasional visit from a customer to an unadvertised home business.   Others who have licensed their homes as gun shops say they sell  only at gun shows or have used their license merely to buy guns  for themselves. Officials in Denver and neighboring cities were surprised to  learn these businesses exist. Aside from some gunsmiths - a  permitted home occupation requiring a federal license - they say  they were unaware the U.S. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms  had approved gun businesses at any homes in their cities. 'It certainly looks bad,' Aurora assistant city attorney Rob  Werking said after reviewing a list of a dozen residential  locations in his city licensed to sell firearms. 'A home in a  neighborhood is not supposed to be Gart Brothers.'

1063.text: GUN-313 PRIMARY THE GUN LOBBY VS. THE GOVERNMENT When G.R. Dickerson became chief enforcer of the nation's firearms laws in early 1979, he raised a white flag and stepped gingerly into the sights of the National Rifle Association to talk peace. The pro-gun lobby held its fire briefly and, after hearing his plea for cooperation, sent Mr. Dickerson scurrying for cover.   ''I had thought I could be friendly with these people and work with them, being an N.R.A. member and hunter myself,'' said Mr. Dickerson, director of the Treasury Department's Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. Now, the bureau's budget has been cut, although the rifle association denies responsibility for that, and the two sides barely talk to each other. The bureau, which moves against violators of firearms and explosives laws, cigarette smugglers and illegal alcohol producers, is the target of one of the most powerful single-issue lobbies in Washington - the National Rifle Association. Model for One-Issue Lobbies With 1.9 million members and a multimillion-dollar war chest, the association has become a prototype for other one-issue lobbies. It contributes heavily to Congressional campaigns, produces films and brochures to discredit its opponents and, on short notice, can use its computerized mailing list to generate an extensive response to the slightest threat to its interests. For years now, the rifle association's chief goals have been repeal of the 1968 Gun Control Act and curbs on the enforcement powers of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1060.text: GUN-8703 PRIMARY Database Program Targets Illegal Gun Traffic FRIENDS and family have placed decorations around a tree at the corner of Princeton Street and Bennett Avenue in Hempstead as a makeshift memorial to Paget Belle, a 19-year-old who lived nearby and was shot to death at that spot on April 3. It is a neighborhood beset by gangs, and violent death is an occasional visitor. But there's a twist in the Belle case, according to the police: they say he was shot with an illegal pistol he had sold only a day earlier to his killer, a 16-year-old named Jermaine Grant. "It is ironic to get murdered with the same gun you just sold," said Detective William Fowlkes of the Hempstead Police Department. "You live by the sword, you die by the sword." The police say Jermaine Grant and Paget Belle were members of a local gang called the Outlaws, which Detective Fowlkes said was made up mostly of 14- to 18-year-olds, and that Mr. Belle arranged to sell Jermaine a .38-caliber revolver. According to Lt. Frank Guidice of the Nassau County Police Department homicide bureau, Jermaine paid $200 of the $300 sales price and was given the gun. When Mr. Belle encountered Jermaine on the street the next night and asked for the rest of his money, an argument developed, Lieutenant Guidice said. Jermaine allegedly shot Mr. Belle in the chest, was arrested a short time later with the gun in his waistband and was charged as an adult with second-degree murder.

1058.text: GUN-792 PRIMARY Federal Agency to Begin a Survey On Use of Semiautomatic Guns The Federal agency that enforces national gun laws will soon begin a survey to determine whether military-like semiautomatic rifles are used for target shooting and hunting. The study would provide evidence on whether the decision last week by the Bush Administration to suspend the importing of five kinds of such rifles indefinitely was correct and should be made permanent and whether other foreign guns should be added to the list. Stephen E. Higgins, director of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, which is an arm of the Treasury Department, said in an interview that bureau officials would question state fish and game departments, target shooting associations, hunting guides and outdoor writers and editors. Wider Inquiry This will widen the inquiry because the bureau had originally intended to ask only importers to furnish evidence on how the rifles are used. ''I can't see it being done in less than three months,'' Mr. Higgins said of the study. Theoretically, the study could lead to a reversal of the the decision last week, which affected applications to import more than 113,000 military look-alike semiautomatic rifles that fire one shot with each press of the trigger. Law-enforcement officials have been concerned that such semiautomatic rifles are getting into the hands of drug dealers and other criminals. The evaluation could also result in more rifles being added to the list of affected models, but this would have little practical effect since about 90 percent of such imported weapons are already covered by last week's ban.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1064.text: NA-40552 PRIMARY Missouri House tries to block U.S. firearms laws JEFFERSON CITY o The Missouri House passed legislation Wednesday that would make it a felony for U.S. authorities to enforce federal firearm laws in Missouri, despite concerns that the measure is probably unconstitutional. The legislation would make it a felony punishable by up to four years in prison for federal agents who try to enforce any federal laws or regulations relating to firearms and ammunition owned by Missouri residents. Democrats said the legislation probably violated the U.S. Constitution, which gives supremacy to federal laws over state laws. It's "pretty blatantly unconstitutional," said state Rep. Stacy Newman, D-St. Louis, and "a total waste of our time." But Republicans pressed forward anyway, asserting that President Barack Obama's gun policies and proposals infringe on the Second Amendment. The measure includes an emergency clause, which means it would take effect immediately upon the governor's signature - in the unlikely event it also passes the Senate and is agreed to by Democratic Gov. Jay Nixon. "We wanted the strongest possible language," said state Rep. Casey Guernsey, R-Bethany, the sponsor of the legislation. "We don't want to have further gun control enforceable in the state of Missouri." The legislation also states that private gun sales in Missouri involving people who are not licensed dealers shall be exempt from national instant criminal background checks. It also would lower the age to get a concealed gun permit in Missouri to 19 instead of the current minimum of 21.

1062.text: GUN-10751 PRIMARY Gun policy blamed in NYC parade deaths Mayor Michael Bloomberg on Tuesday blamed illegal handguns for a shooting that killed three people and wounded two police officers near the route of New York's West Indian Day Parade. Two gunmen  were killed Monday along with a bystander, Denise Gay, 56, who was shot while sitting on a stoop with her daughter. Bloomberg called Gay's death "another painful reminder I think of what happens when elected officials in Washington fail to take the problem of illegal guns seriously." The parade was also marred by fatal shootings in 2003 and 2005. Police Commissioner Raymond Kelly called the violence "unfortunate," but said ending the parade was unnecessary. Storm's remnants drench sodden East Heavy rain from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee rolled northeast, bringing threats of flooding, tornadoes and power outages. At least four people died because of the rough winds and drenching rains. Flood watches and warnings were in effect from Alabama and Tennessee  to Upstate New York, areas still recovering from  Tropical Storm Irene, the National Weather Service said. Tornado watches covered much of the Carolinas. Lee also churned up heavy surf that sent tar balls of oil washing onto Alabama's prime tourist beaches. California fire threatens 650 homes A California wildfire, started when a plane crashed, had grown to 20 square miles and threatened 650 homes, firefighters said. The winds died down Tuesday, and crews were aggressively attacking the blaze, said Daniel Berlant, spokesman for the California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection. The Canyon Fire started Sunday in Kern County when a single-engine Cessna crashed in the remote area, killing two people. Kern County Fire Department spokesman Victor Cruz said 1,255 firefighters six helicopters and seven air tankers were fighting the blaze, which had destroyed 12 homes.

characters: California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection

1066.text: GUN-11162 PRIMARY 'Stand Your Ground' - and shoot THE SHOOTING death of Trayvon Martinhas shone a light on a new breed of law that enforcers accurately predicted would lead to the kind of confrontations that took the life of the Florida teenager. Mr. Martin was killed in February by George Zimmerman, a neighborhood watch volunteer in his Sanford, Fla., townhouse community. Recordings of the 911 call made by Mr. Zimmerman to police show him stalking the 17-year-old, whom he described as suspicious and possibly high on drugs. Mr. Martin was unarmed when he was shot, but Mr. Zimmerman was not charged with any crime - in part because of Florida's "Stand Your Ground" law. Florida enacted the law in 2005, becoming the first of some 20 states to adopt such a measure. The law shields individuals from prosecution if police determine they used deadly force in self-defense. This much is relatively uncontroversial, given that individuals have always had a right to defend themselves against attack; these protections are particularly strong if one is attacked inside the home. What makes the Stand Your Ground law dangerous is that it absolves the individual of any responsibility to consider other ways to avoid harm. Before Stand Your Ground laws, police and law enforcement officials assessed whether a "reasonable person" would have resorted to the level of violence used to thwart an attack; the new law turns that standard on its head and immunizes an individual from criminal charges if he asserts he had a "reasonable" fear of grave harm. Florida's Stand Your Ground law applies wherever the person has a right to be - whether in his home, his car or his business or on a public sidewalk.

1070.text: GUN-606 PRIMARY Killings rekindle debate on guns // Move to expand ban gains steam WASHINGTON - A national debate over the future of assault weapons    that had simmered for much of the summer in Washington came to a full    boil again Thursday with news of the shootings in Louisville, Ky.        After emotional debate, the Senate Judiciary Committee voted in    July to send to the floor a bill banning nine families of assault    weapons from future manufacture, sale or possession. The measure - the most far-reaching gun control bill considered by Congress in 21 years - had faced an uncertain future. Thursday's    shootings, however, have prompted calls for a speedy debate on the    bill, sponsored by Sen. Dennis DeConcini, D-Ariz. "Unfortunately, real-life events are going to provide support for legislation of this type," said DeConcini. "It is a sad and costly    way to build support for legislation, but inevitable. "With the growing number of assault rifles in America, we cannot    escape the notion that these tragic events will continue to take    place," DeConcini said. Some Democrats have speculated the assault-gun bill may now be    tacked onto President Bush's drug-control policy measure as a way of    forcing the issue past the powerful opposition of the National Rifle    Association (NRA). The Bush administration has already banned 43 types of    foreign-produced assault weapons that are classified as five major    models. DeConcini's bill would add four others produced domestically,    including the MAC-11s found at the scene of the Louisville shootings.

1071.text: GUN-30572 PRIMARY Moms Against Guns given free billboards; They were donated by two local firms. The group backs election of foes of illegal guns. In April, Moms Against Guns had a humble request: one affordable billboard advertising its petition for stricter gun laws. Flash forward three months, and the Philadelphia-based advocacy group now has 27 billboards citywide, with at least six more on the way. The billboards will stay up through November's elections - just long enough, the group says, for voters to elect candidates willing to crack down on illegal guns. It's all part of what founder Lynne Honickman called a viral campaign. "Everybody has a power: the power to vote," Honickman said. "Not until the general public says 'enough' will the politicos do their duty." The group is working on getting 50,000 signatures for a petition on its Web site (http://www.momsagainstguns.org) urging state lawmakers to pass tougher gun laws. The petition has nearly 4,500 so far. The billboards come free from Clear Channel Outdoor Philadelphia and Interstate Outdoor Advertising, two locally based firms. "We didn't think a billboard was going to make a difference," said Drew Katz, chief executive officer of Interstate. "Thirty billboards might make a difference." The slew of billboards reflects a sense of urgency as gun violence continues to claim lives. As of yesterday, there were 175 homicides this year in the city. Of those, 138 were committed with handguns, two with rifles and four with shotguns, said Lt. Frank Vanore, a police spokesman.

1072.text: GUN-5852 PRIMARY Gun control: Issue splitting Georgia GOP's solid front In the weeks since the Senate passed new gun legislation, Rep. Johnny Isakson (R-Ga.) has heard an earful from his conservative constituents in suburban Marietta. Isakson has personally answered more than 500 letters. After a metro television report misrepresented his position on new gun measures, saying he favored blanket gun control, Isakson sat at his desk to field scores of irate calls. In each letter and call, Isakson said he has patiently explained that he favors legislation requiring safety locks to be sold with guns and requiring that all gun purchasers submit to criminal background checks at gun shows, where 20 percent to 40 percent of such sales are now exempted. "I have yet to have anyone tell me that these positions are inconsistent with a common-sense approach to guns," said Isakson. This week, Isakson and Georgia's 10 other House members will cast important votes on new gun control. For Isakson and a majority of the Georgia delegation, some of whom campaigned against Congress' adoption of the assault weapons ban in 1994, the votes will be their first on the controversial topic since joining Congress. Prompted by the deadly shooting in Littleton, Colo., and the near catastrophe at Heritage High School in Rockdale County, the House on Wednesday will take up a massive juvenile crime bill that now includes new restrictions on the sale of guns to juveniles, mandatory trigger locks on gun sales and background checks at gun shows.

1069.text: GUN-5155 PRIMARY A Year Later, Victim Forms A New Group To Fight Guns A year to the day after he was shot in a rampage atop the Empire State Building, Matthew Gross spoke publicly for the first time yesterday about the shooting that shattered his life, and announced the formation of a new anti-gun organization. Mr. Gross, a rock musician from Montclair, N.J., was left in a coma when a 69-year-old Palestinian teacher sprayed the observation deck of the Empire State Building with bullets from a semiautomatic handgun, one of which pierced Mr. Gross's brain. His friend and fellow band member, Christoffer Burmeister of Denmark, was killed, and five other people were wounded. "Last February 23d my friend Christoffer Burmeister was killed and I took a bullet in my head," Mr. Gross said at a midtown Manhattan office building, reading hurriedly from a handwritten note and appearing undaunted by a swirl of lights, cameras and questions. In announcing the formation of the anti-gun group, Mr. Gross was joined by several gun safety advocates and politicians, including the former White House press secretary, James S. Brady, who was shot in the assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan, and Representative Carolyn McCarthy, whose husband was killed in a 1993 shooting on the Long Island Rail Road. "It takes a lifetime to recover from a brain injury, but I'm not here to talk about me," Mr. Gross, 28, said in introducing the group, called Pax. "Hopefully Pax will keep what happened to Chris and me from happening to everyone else."

1067.text: GUN-10404 PRIMARY The Overturning of a City's Handgun Ban To the Editor: Re ''The Court and the Bill of Rights: Ignoring the Reality of Guns'' (editorial, June 29): If anyone is ''ignoring'' the reality of guns, it isn't the Supreme Court, but your editorial board. Crime rates across the country are steadily decreasing as firearms ownership soars to record levels. This week's Supreme Court ruling ensuring that all law-abiding citizens, whether they live in a big city like Chicago or in rural Wyoming, have the same American right to keep and bear arms is welcome, necessary and just. The Second Amendment is a right of individuals. Rights, by definition, are individual. You were right about one thing, however: this week's decision marks the beginning of a new era of civil rights litigation as laws and regulations that infringe upon and violate the individual right of law-abiding Americans to keep and bear arms, protected by the Second Amendment, are challenged. Lawrence G. Keane Newtown, Conn., June 30, 2010 The writer is senior vice president and general counsel for the National Shooting Sports Foundation, the trade association for the firearms industry. To the Editor: I am flummoxed by the Supreme Court's recent decision on gun control.
