The court acknowledges that restraints on gun ownership can be constitutionally imposed in certain spaces like courthouses or schools, but not in larger areas like the city of Chicago where gun violence is well documented.

1068.text: GUN-249 PRIMARY Fissures in the N.R.A. Bedrock For years it's been the bedrock policy of the National Rifle Association to oppose all attempts to keep guns from criminals as a threat to the rights of hunters, target shooters and collectors. It's nonsense, of course. In cities, regulating guns is as reasonable and necessary as regulating automobiles. Yet the well-heeled, well-organized gun lobby has managed to intimidate legislators of both parties and even Presidents. Now its grip may be weakening. That's good news for America, and perhaps for the N.R.A. too. The first fissure in the bedrock came in May, when the Senate Judiciary Committee took up the McClure-Volkmer Gun Control Act to ease restraints on interstate gun sales. Senator Kennedy suggested relaxing restrictions only for rifles and shotguns. Holding fast to curbs on handguns would recognize the interests of the sportsman without threatening urban safety. The committee agreed. Then the logic of that distinction forced the Reagan Administration and the N.R.A. to a dramatic reversal on the banning of armor-piercing bullets. For two years New York's Representative Biaggi and Senator Moynihan, supported by police groups, had pushed a bill to prohibit handgun ammunition capable of penetrating a police officer's protective vest. Neither hunters nor law enforcers have any use for such bullets. Their only purpose is to murder people wearing the vests. Yet the N.R.A. mounted a fierce campaign against the proposed ban. Armor-piercing handgun bullets couldn't be banned generically, it said, without also banning popular sporting ammunition. And Federal bureaucrats, it argued, couldn't be trusted with the power to outlaw the bullets by brand. The White House sided with the N.R.A.  Two weeks ago, however, the paranoia lifted. After dallying for months over a Congressional request to produce a generic definition, the Treasury and Justice Departments offered one that the N.R.A. pronounced acceptable. The group also said it would not oppose limited discretion for the Treasury Secretary to outlaw ammunition. Now the ban on manufacture and import of armor-piercing bullets is finally moving through Congress.   The immediate cause for the N.R.A.-White House reversal was their reluctance to keep alienating the police. Could the bill also mark the beginning of a broader compromise?   The N.R.A.'s advertising - portraying members as congenial Americans - suggests it's worried about image. The lobby's power thus far has been based mostly on fear. It could gain more positive respect by finally respecting the needs of city dwellers. If the armor-piercing bullet bill leads to that, it will do more than save police lives.

1075.text: GUN-8864 PRIMARY SUIT AGAINST GUN DISTRIBUTOR ILLOGICAL I was very disappointed to read of Pam Grunow suing the distributor of the gun that killed her husband two years ago ("Gun used to kill teacher is put on trial," Oct. 7). Being a mother and mother-in-law of two teachers, I was horrified when Barry Grunow was killed. I contributed to the fund set up for her and her children. I was delighted when the community and the school district came to her rescue financially. Why would she stoop so low as to sue a distributor that had nothing to do with the manufacture of the gun? They simply sold it. I am not for or against gun laws, but find this lawsuit ludicrous. Life can be cruel at times, but we must forge on. ELLIE LAFER Palm City

1073.text: NA-40936 PRIMARY Reckless gun bill still on target in Legislature The Florida Sheriffs Association is the latest victim of bullying by the National Rifle Association. I wrote last week about what happened to the Florida National Guard's lawyer who told a Florida Senate committee that it wasn't a good idea to allow all the state's legal gun owners -- most of whom haven't been trained to carry concealed weapons -- to carry concealed weapons after evacuating their homes during a state emergency, such as a hurricane. "People aren't thinking clearly," Army Capt. Terrence Gorman told the Florida Senate Military and Veterans Affairs, Space and Domestic Security Committee. "And when they aren't thinking clearly, they probably shouldn't have a weapon shoved into the back of their pants. Especially when you're talking about thousands of people that have to be evacuated." Gorman's testimony temporarily stalled the NRA-inspired Senate Bill 296, and angered its chief lobbyist, Marion Hammer, so much that she went straight to Gov. Rick Scott's office to complain about the lawyer's testimony. Scott's office responded smartly by saying the governor fully supported the bill and then dispatched Gorman's commander at the state Department of Military Affairs, Maj. Gen. Emmett Titshaw, to disavow the testimony of his own legal counsel. "Capt. Terrence Gorman is not authorized to speak for the Department of Military Affairs on legislative issues," Titshaw's letter to the legislators read. "Department of Military Affairs supports Senate Bill 296."

1077.text: GUN-2257 PRIMARY ID cards used to buy guns illegally Changes sought in way driver's licenses issued Inexpensive, easy-to-falsify Colorado identification cards regularly are used to illegally obtain handguns that aren't traceable to buyers, federal investigators say. Armed with that information, some law officers are calling for changes in the way ID cards - and driver's licenses - are issued by the state. If the process isn't tightened, they fear, the new Brady gun-control law could make the documents an even more popular tool for criminals. "I was stunned that it's so easy to get state-sponsored authentification of your identity," said Joe Gordon, southern Colorado agent-in-charge for the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. "In other words, the state of Colorado is telling gun dealers, 'We vouch for this person's identity and whereabouts.' That's how the gun dealer reacts to it." Authorities have known for years that bad-check writers and underage drinkers use fraudulent state-issued ID cards and driver's licenses, but they never have had any hard data on their widespread use by gun buyers. Gordon learned of the practice through a Colorado Springs gun-tracing program he initiated Oct. 1. So far, his "Project Young Guns" has identified 61 handguns - mostly semiautomatic pistols - purchased illicitly in Colorado Springs during the last three months. ATF determined that Colorado ID cards and driver's licenses, many with aliases or false addresses, were used in several instances. Gordon provided some examples but changed the names of the suspects because they still are being investigated:

1074.text: GUN-5846 PRIMARY Facing a Political Tripwire on Guns As the House of Representatives prepares to vote this week on gun-control legislation, the popularity of hunting and the nearly universal ownership of guns in many rural areas has put Congressmen from these areas at special political risk -- particularly Representative Bart Stupak, a Democrat whose district encompasses Michigan's northernmost reaches. Shootings in schools have alarmed millions of Americans, but here and in many other northern Michigan communities, the first day of deer hunting season each November is an official school holiday so that children as young as 5 years old can join their parents in the woods. While the minimum legal age for shooting a deer in Michigan is 15, the law is widely flouted, with boys and girls as young as 10 often bagging their first buck during their holiday. "Everybody hunts that day," said Jason Malner, a ninth grader who owns three rifles and two shotguns. "I use the .243 for deer, I use the 12-gauges for rabbits and partridge and I use the .22's for fooling around and shooting at targets," he said as he ate a steak at the Buck Inn Restaurant here with his mother, Pam, who confirmed his gun inventory. Mr. Stupak, a four-term Democrat whose wooded district is three times the size of New Jersey, shares his constituents' love of deer hunting, going out every year with his 19- and 16-year-old sons. A former police officer in Escanaba who also spent a decade as a Michigan State Police trooper, Mr. Stupak was one of about 30 Democrats who consistently crossed party lines to oppose gun-control legislation -- at least until this spring.

1076.text: GUN-2823 PRIMARY Getting Serious About Guns Congressional efforts at gun control have thus far been worthy but piecemeal. Advocates of gun control have concentrated on getting small pieces of legislation -- any legislation -- through Congress. That has meant choosing measures fashioned to seem most innocuous to those who fear the intimidation campaign of the National Rifle Association. So far, that tactic has proved its worth, most notably in getting the Brady law passed, which demands a five-day waiting period before buying a handgun, and in getting Congress accustomed to the notion that the gun trade is a fitting target of legislation. Some members think Congress is now ready for a broader approach. Late last year Representative Charles Schumer of New York and Senator Howard Metzenbaum of Ohio introduced a "kitchen-sink" bill that covered everything from licensing to lists of weapons to be prohibited. It proved politically ahead of its time. So this week Mr. Schumer, along with Senator Bill Bradley of New Jersey, introduced a more focused bill, aimed at stopping the illegal trafficking in guns. The two legislators note that almost all handguns on America's streets start as legal weapons. Then they are stolen, or sold through shady dealers, or bought by people without the proper documentation, or straw buyers who turn them over to illegal dealers with only one goal: to resell them on the street. The Schumer-Bradley bill aims to stop trafficking by a number of practical measures. The linchpin of the legislation is a proposal for a national handgun identity card, containing a photograph, a fingerprint and a magnetic strip to prevent fraudulent use. No one in America would be allowed to buy a handgun without such a card.

1079.text: GUN-6534 PRIMARY Million Mom March seeks to keep kids, guns apart Too many guns are finding their way into the wrong hands - the hands of children. Loopholes in gun laws allow handguns to pour into the illegal market. Young people can still buy guns on the street. According to data compiled by the U. S. General Accounting Office, about 10,000 Americans are unintentionally shot each year by young people 18 years and under. Research also indicates that 59 percent of sixth- to 12th-graders say they can get access to a handgun. Parents know that they should protect children from injury by locking up prescription medicines and requiring them to wear bicycle helmets and seat belts, yet many keep a loaded handgun on the bedside table. Safe gun storage needs greater promotion. Gun manufacturers should also have to produce guns with built-in locks and other devices such as loaded-chamber indicators. Limiting handgun sales to one per month per person can stop gun traffickers from spreading the disease of handgun violence across our community, our state and our nation. Gun traffickers buy large quantities of guns in states like North Carolina, which has weak gun laws, and then sell them on the streets of cities like Los Angeles, which has tough gun laws. Limiting handgun sales puts a stop to bulk handgun purchases. We are preparing to go to Washington on May 14 to implore Congress to get with our program or feel the backlash that is sure to come from a powerful voting bloc - America's moms. Public opinion is on our side. A 1998 Harris Poll found that 70 percent of adults favor stricter gun control laws. Even among gun owners, 57 percent favor stricter laws.

1078.text: GUN-9627 PRIMARY POLS VOW TO REVIVE GUN BAN Taking on assault rifles LOCAL LAWMAKERS vowed yesterday to work to reinstate the recently expired ban on assault weapons. U.S. Sen. Chuck Schumer said he hasn't given up trying to renew the ban, which fell by the wayside two weeks ago. "Not continuing the ban on assault weapons is one of the great disgraces this year," Schumer (D-N.Y.) said, promising that members of Congress will take up the cause during its next session. A story in yesterday's Daily News showed how the once-banned weapons can pierce through concrete and even some bulletproof vests. And some law enforcement officials fear the powerful guns, once used to guard drug dens during the height of the crack epidemic, could make their way back into New York City. "The Daily News talked about people having AR-15s," Schumer said. "Nobody needs an AR-15. These were designed as weapons of war. . . . They should be abolished." Mayor Bloomberg and Police Commissioner Raymond Kelly aggressively lobbied to keep the 10-year-old ban in place. This month, the Republican mayor took the unusual step of taking President Bush to task for his inaction. "I think every congressman and every senator has a responsibility to stand up," Bloomberg told reporters. "And I think the President can do more." Rep. Anthony Weiner (D-Queens, Brooklyn) accused the President of cozying up to the National Rifle Association. "Every police organization and even Commissioner Kelly said these will make the streets less safe," Weiner said. "In the showdown between the NRA and the overwhelming majority of the population and police officials, President Bush chose the NRA."

1080.text: NA-45890 PRIMARY Obama group plans ad blitz on gun plan; Online barrage to press reticent lawmakers President Obama's new grass-roots advocacy group will target more than a dozen members of Congress with online ads this week for failing to voice support for his plan to expand background checks to all gun buyers, organizers told USA TODAY. The advertising push is part of a so-called national day of action planned Friday by Obama's backers to rally support for background checks. It will mark the first large-scale test of Organizing for Action's ability to mobilize the president's army of 2.2 million campaign volunteers to press for legislative change. Obama and a group of his closest advisers launched Organizing for Action (OFA) in late January to transform his campaign operation into a tax-exempt group to advance his second-term priorities, including gun control, immigration and climate change. Organizers did not disclose which lawmakers they would target in the first round of advertising but say they could spend as much as $100,000. The goal is to expand criminal background checks on all gun purchasers. The current system applies only to federally licensed gun dealers and excludes guns sold privately. In addition, OFA is sponsoring more than 100 events  across the country to demand congressional action. "That's the strength the OFA will bring to supporting the president's agenda," said Jon Carson, the group's executive director and the former director of the White House office of public engagement. "We have voices in every corner of this country who are supporting his agenda and can make their neighbors understand where their members (of Congress) stand on these important issues."

1086.text: GUN-454 PRIMARY CAPITAL LINE Senate expected to OK crime bill today The Senate is expected to pass a sweeping anti-crime bill today that would ban nine semiautomatic assault weapons favored by drug dealers and expand to 34 the number of crimes punishable by the federal death penalty. Also in the bill: increased penalties for savings and loan fraud, including possible life sentences for ''S&L kingpins.'' The vote comes after the National Rifle Association staged a full-bore lobbying effort but failed to derail the gun ban. The gun control battle ''is over'' in the Senate, said Sen. Orrin Hatch, R-Utah. But the intense lobbying will begin again in the House, which is considering a gun control bill separately from a crime bill.

1084.text: GUN-236 PRIMARY Focusing on Guns Won't Alter Crime in the U.S. To the Editor: ''We're Killing One Another at a Great Rate,'' (letter, April 9) is a troubling example of sentiment, outrage and misinformation coupled to form an appealing but ineffective argument. Guns, violence, crime and death are too complicated to treat as the letter does. The statement that the ''statistics bear a significant message about government'' is naive. Empirical research on guns and violent crime has been inconclusive. Finding useful statistics is difficult; making meaningful inferences is harder. If mortality statistics dictated policy priorities, then other causes command greater concern. In 1981, automotive deaths outstripped firearm deaths almost 4 to 1. Deaths from cardiovascular diseases were 73 times higher than gun deaths the same year. Clearly, numbers are not the issue. The letter is incorrect about interstate sale and transport of firearms. They are regulated. It is illegal to sell a firearm to an out-of-state buyer and release it over the counter. According to the Firearms Control Act of 1968, any such gun must be transported dealer to dealer and picked up in the purchaser's home state in accordance with local regulations. There is also no legal definition of a Saturday night special in New York and many other states. Well-focused legislation for ''cop-killer bullets'' (I assume the reference is to KTW rounds that are available only to law- enforcement agencies) has been proposed by Senator Strom Thurmond (S-2766) and by Representative Jack Brooks (HR-5845). The terms may have emotional currency, but are of no use in formulating policy.

1081.text: NA-42438 PRIMARY APNewsBreak: Ill. Dems call for gov.'s gun verdict SPRINGFIELD, Ill. Some Illinois Democrats on Thursday urged the governor to act quickly on legislation allowing the carrying of concealed weapons in the state, saying they need him to accept or reject the measure so lawmakers can avert a "public safety and constitutional crisis" as they try to meet a court-ordered deadline. Illinois' General Assembly sent Gov. Pat Quinn a bill to end the state's holdout as last in the nation to prevent concealed carry after a federal appeals court ruled in December that the ban violates the U.S. Constitution's Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms. The Democratic governor has been tight-lipped about his intentions. In a letter to Quinn on Thursday, 23 Democrats in the state Senate, including Senate President John Cullerton, said the governor should "make his intentions known" so lawmakers have time to react before the court's July 9 deadline for implementing a plan. The letter, obtained by The Associated Press, notes that authorities in a dozen counties are already refusing to prosecute those who carry concealed weapons. Some counties are setting up their own rules to allow public possession of concealed firearms because the existing law has been invalidated, even if it's still on the books. "If you intend to issue a veto or amendatory veto, we ask that you do it quickly. The General Assembly will need time to respond. Every day we get closer to the federal court's July 9 deadline. Time is of the essence," the letter said. "We urge you to act on this legislation now. The last thing we need is a public safety and constitutional crisis."

1083.text: GUN-2018 PRIMARY Many gun dealers sell no weapons The applicant stated he wanted to become a federally-licensed "dealer in rifles, shotguns and pistols." He listed a house as his business location. He stated it would be open by appointment to the public seven days a week. He declared the statements on his application "are true, correct and  complete," and he signed his name. H. Norman Schwarzkopf. In the more than 2 1/2 years he has had his license, Schwarzkopf says he  has never sold a gun. His business location has not been an easy place to visit. Even as the  United States was gearing up for war with Iraq, he licensed his residence at  MacDill Air Force Base as a retail gun business. He later moved his license to his home in a walled and guarded community in Hillsborough County. Schwarzkopf is one of 12,000 licensed gun dealers in Florida. So many  people have these licenses that the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms,  which is responsible for enforcing gun control regulations, cannot possibly  keep up with what the dealers do. In the Tampa Bay area, doctors, lawyers, school officials and a judge have declared they intend to sell guns, listed a business location and business hours, obtained a federal dealer's license - and never sold a gun. Federal law requires a gun dealer to be "engaged in the business" and  empowers ATF to cancel the license of anyone who is not.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1082.text: GUN-6662 PRIMARY Clinton Plans Gun Initiative; President Seeks $280 Million for Enforcement of Existing Laws Preparing for a new round of skirmishes in Congress over gun control, President Clinton plans to announce a substantial increase in funding for enforcement of existing gun control laws today and will hail what administration officials described as a 25 percent increase in prosecutions for federal firearms law violations. Clinton will announce the $ 280 million initiative at a community center in Boston, the location of an aggressive federal, state and local law enforcement program targeted at crimes involving guns. Bruce Reed, Clinton's chief domestic policy adviser, said yesterday that the new money would add 300 firearms agents and 200 firearms inspectors to the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF). It would also fund the hiring of about 100 prosecutors in U.S. attorneys' offices across the country to concentrate on firearms violations and pay most of the cost of hiring 1,000 new state and local prosecutors, whose primary responsibility would be prosecuting gun-related crimes, Reed said. The new funding would be for the fiscal year that begins Oct. 1 and, according to Reed, would be "the largest national firearms enforcement initiative in history." The initiative also was the Clinton administration's response to gun control critics, who argued during last year's congressional debates that there was no need for new gun control laws because the administration was doing such a poor job of enforcing existing laws.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF)

1087.text: GUN-3079 PRIMARY NRA plays public for fools The National Rifle Association believes the people of Georgia, and the legislators who represent them, can be played for patsies. The next few weeks will determine whether the NRA is correct. The state House is expected to vote very soon, perhaps even today, on an amended version of Senate Bill 12. The bill is being sold to legislators and voters as a response to public demand for stricter gun- control laws. In reality, the bill as currently written would weaken gun-control laws in Georgia and make the people of the state more vulnerable to violent crime. And if Georgia voters don't understand that yet, they will by Election Day. As it passed the state Senate last year, SB 12 was a good bill. Every person who attempted to buy a firearm from a licensed dealer in Georgia would have been required to undergo an instantaneous, computerized background check. The check would have ensured that the purchaser was not a convicted felon and did not have a record of mental instability. The bill also left intact local laws in Atlanta and other metro areas requiring a waiting period before purchase of firearms. Unfortunately, the bill did not pass. The NRA blocked it, just as it had blocked the bill every year for the past decade or more. Last fall, however, something happened to change the NRA's attitude. Congress passed and President Clinton signed the Brady law, which mandates a national five-day waiting period before purchase of a handgun. But that law, which takes effect later this month, does not apply in states with an instant background check.

1088.text: GUN-3832 PRIMARY DA: Concealed weapons no panacea The fatal shootings at two Denver-area grocery stores in the past week probably could not have been prevented by armed citizens carrying concealed weapons, Denver's district attorney said yesterday. After attending a Denver "Summit on Gun Violence," District Attorney Bill Ritter told The Denver Post that the Albertson's and King Soopers tragedies should be carefully analyzed. In the aftermath of such incidents, Ritter said people should be very careful before urging the easing of gun restrictions in the belief that to do so would prevent similar incidents. Ritter, at the invitation of Handgun Control Inc., spoke to the "gun summit" at Trinity United Methodist Church about his concerns about handguns and the violence he sees on the street. Ritter said people planning these types of crimes probably wouldn't be deterred by the possibility that either the intended victim or a bystander might have a concealed weapon. "If we look at the crimes that have happened over the past few weeks, people should understand that easing concealed weapons restrictions is not going to make for a safer community," said Ritter. "Anyone who believes the carrying of a concealed weapon would have made a significant difference as it related to either the Jefferson County or the Aurora incidents should look closely at the details of those cases." Ritter said that he had heard one person comment that if patrons of the King Soopers store had concealed weapons, the outcome might have been different.

1089.text: GUN-2652 PRIMARY RECORD GUN SALES TIED TO FEAR OF CRIME Californians are buying guns at a record rate again this year, apparently because of a fear of crime, the Attorney General's Office said Wednesday. Californians legally purchased 332,088 firearms during the first half of 1994, a 6 percent increase over the same period last year. "There is no doubt that the fear of becoming a victim of crime has largely fueled this . . . trend," Attorney General Dan Lungren said. "I have always stood by the principle that law-abiding citizens have a constitutional right to possess firearms," Lungren said. "Yet I cannot believe that we will end the plague of crime by buying more guns and living in fear." Californians have set records for gun purchases each year since 1991. Last year they bought 642,197, according to sales reported to the Attorney General's Office by gun dealers. This year's sales include 224,415 handguns and 107,673 rifles and shotguns. "Based on these statistics, we can predict that total gun sales for 1994 will top the record set last year," Lungren said.

1090.text: GUN-30785 PRIMARY Metro Briefing  New York: Manhattan: Uniting Against Illegal Guns Six months after starting a national campaign against illegal guns, Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg said yesterday that 109 mayors from 44 states have joined his Mayors Against Illegal Guns coalition. At a news conference in Boston with that city's mayor, Thomas M. Menino, and the mayors of Providence and Yonkers, Mr. Bloomberg said regional conferences would be held in Atlanta, Boston and Chicago this fall for administrations involved, followed by a national summit in January in Washington, D.C. He said a Web site, www.mayorsagainstillegalguns.org, would track the group's progress.     DAMIEN CAVE (NYT)

1085.text: GUN-10434 PRIMARY Trigger lock Our view o Authority to promote gun safety remains very much alive. Second Amendment absolutists may be a little disappointed in the U.S. Supreme Court's decision Monday in the much-anticipated challenge to Chicago's strict gun laws. The court threw out Chicago's laws, but the court didn't throw out all limits on the right to bear arms. Had the 5-4 decision in McDonald v. City of Chicago been broader, it might have deepened the partisanship during the confirmation hearings that began later in the day for Elena Kagan, President Barack Obama's nominee to replace Justice John Paul Stevens on the high court. As it was, the opinion of the court's majority justices was a lot like a ceremonial 21-gun salute: It made a loud bang but lacked the live ammunition that gun-rights absolutists believe is every American's birthright. The decision is less of landmark than a kind of codicil to the court's decision two years ago in District of Columbia v. Heller. In that case, the court ended decades of speculation by legal scholars with a ruling that the Second Amendment to the U.S. Constitution confers a personal right "to keep and bear arms" but not some collective right regarding the organizing of state militias. The court thus struck down as unconstitutional an ordinance that narrowly restricted private ownership of handguns. There was some question about whether the Heller case also would limit the power of states and municipalities to regulate gun ownership. That was the central question in the McDonald case, and the court made it clear that Second Amendment rights apply with same force across the 50 states as they do in the unique federal enclave of Washington, D.C.

1094.text: NA-44558 PRIMARY Bipartisanship quickly gives way to a partisan battle This was going to be a quiet week in the U.S. Senate, one during which the majority Democrats would take the opportunity  to help a colleague facing a tough reelection race. But what was supposed to be an innocuous election-year proposal, designed to draw considerable bipartisan support, has become the first big fight over gun control in  Washington in more than a year - reopening old wounds for the very Democrats it was supposed to help. The measure would expand access to hunting and fishing on federal lands. Sen. Kay Hagan (D-N.C.) is the lead sponsor. She faces one of the most challenging reelections this year, so she persuaded 45 other senators - including 26 Republicans - to back the bill, making it one of the most popular legislative proposals debated by the Senate this year. Even in a badly fractured chamber where bipartisan agreement is rare, passage of the Hagan bill should be a slam dunk. But it's now caught up in something else entirely. Both gun rights proponents and gun-control advocates are looking at the bill as a way to score political points by adding amendments. "I want this debate. I want an opportunity to raise important issues about gun violence and gun safety in America," Sen. Richard J. Durbin (D-Ill.) said Wednesday. He is seeking to stiffen penalties for illegal straw purchasers, or people who buy guns for others who plan to use the firearms to commit a crime.

1091.text: GUN-8588 PRIMARY Arms conferees seek consensus amid U.S. dissent United Nations --- Facing a firm U.S. stand in defense of legal weapons sales and gun owners' rights, international negotiators scrambled late Friday to reach consensus on ways to stop the illegal and deadly small-arms trade. The two-week United Nations Conference on the illicit trade of weapons, ranging from AK-47 rifles to shoulder-fired rockets, was supposed to end by Friday. But delegates from the 189 U.N. member nations were reserving their rooms for one more night and facing the prospect that the conference could end in failure. They were stuck on details, such as nuances in language, as well as larger, tougher issues that some human-rights advocates conceded may make a meaningful global stance impossible to reach now. The conference "really raised the public consciousness," said Joost Hilterman of Human Rights Watch. "In many ways this conference came too early. The issue is not mature enough for some people. But it has been useful to mobilize a larger group of people." The U.N. hoped to produce an agreement sometime today that would outline standards for export controls, call for marking and tracing small arms and promote international cooperation to fight the proliferation of weapons. However, the document will not be binding, meaning whatever compromise is reached will have a questionable impact on the problem. Negotiations bogged down between weapons-supplying nations including the United States, China and Russia, and countries supporting greater gun controls, such as Canada and European nations. The United States and other countries were insisting that the document not refer to the legal gun trade or threaten the domestic right of civilians to bear arms.

1092.text: GUN-10411 PRIMARY TOO MANY GUNS ENDANGER OFFICERS Two Tampa officers shot and killed - June 30 I hereby indict the legislators who oppose gun control in the killing of two police officers in Tampa on Tuesday. Now, a mother will always remember her child's birthday as the day her husband died. It can't get more tragic than that. Our elected officials who take "campaign donations" from the NRA have allowed our police officers to be outgunned and in constant danger due to the amount of guns everywhere. People have been conned into thinking that if they carry a gun they are safe. Only the gun manufacturers and the NRA stand to benefit from the prolific sale of guns. How can you expect fine, intelligent men and women to want to become officers when you expose them to such risk? When will our legislators give up personal gain for public safety? My heart goes out to the families of these officers, their fellow officers and Tampa Mayor Pam Iorio. Such madness! Marilyn Weaver, Tarpon Springs * * * Lock them away As sad as it may seem, we almost are getting what we deserve. By putting human flotsam on the streets after they commit crime after crime, what can we expect? Does anyone believe this suspect would be gainfully employed with the criminal record he had compiled for his entire life? We make excuses of prison overcrowding, and give second, third, 20th chances. I could not care less if these violent people sleep four to a bunk. If they don't want Bubba as a bed partner, then don't be criminals.

1097.text: GUN-8827 PRIMARY PBIA TRAVELER CHARGED WITH TAKING GUN IN BAG Palm Beach County sheriff's deputies arrested a St. Petersburg man Wednesday after finding a loaded handgun in his carry-on luggage as it passed through a security checkpoint, according to an arrest report. Donald Lynn Fowler, an engineering manager, has a gun permit, according to the report. The gun, a 9mm Beretta, was loaded with three rounds of ammunition, according to the report. The gun was discovered in a zippered compartment during a hand search of his bag. The bag was searched after Fowler said he didn't have a weapon, although a security agent noticed the gun as the bag passed through an X-ray machine. Fowler, 42, was charged with the misdemeanor of violating his gun permit by carrying the weapon into an airport. Carrying a concealed weapon without a permit is a felony. Fowler was booked into the Palm Beach County Jail and released on $250 bond. antigone_barton@pbpost.com

1095.text: GUN-11136 PRIMARY Illinois' gun permit system is criticized Audit finds program for issuing, monitoring ID cards inadequate. SPRINGFIELD, ILL. - State oversight of gun ownership in Illinois is "limited" in its ability to safeguard the public because of poor follow-through, inadequate collection of mental health records and tardy action on permit applications by "overwhelmed" Illinois State Police officials, an audit Thursday reported. The State Police program for issuing and monitoring Firearm Owners Identification cards falls woefully short in gathering court records on potentially mentally ill gun owners, confiscates only 30 percent of the FOID cards that police revoke, and doesn't report critical information to federal authorities required by a criminal background database, the report found. Lawmakers ordered Auditor General William Holland's assessment last spring after the uproar caused by an Associated Press public-records request for names of the state's FOID-card holders. State Police denied the AP inquiry, but the attorney general said the records should be released, prompting a successful Illinois State Rifle Association lawsuit and pushing legislators to ban disclosure but calling for a study to ensure there is proper gun permit supervision. "Our audit concluded that the effectiveness of the FOID card program is limited in promoting and protecting the safety of the public," Holland wrote. Deficiencies in mental health reporting alone, he said, "seriously undermine the effectiveness of the FOID program." The State Police largely agreed with the findings and said it is making improvements. Spokeswoman Monique Bond pointed out that FOID applications topped record levels during the period audited, 2008-10 - about 300,000 a year.

1100.text: GUN-6626 PRIMARY U.S. MAYORS URGE CONGRESS TO TIGHTEN GUN LAWS; NAMES DISPLAYED OF 3,094 VICTIMS SINCE COLUMBINE Calling on Congress to pass more stringent gun laws, the nation's mayors displayed on Thursday a "wall of death," filled with the names of nearly 3,100 Americans fatally shot since the killings at Columbine High School. "Our hope is the tragic message of this wall will not be missed on Capitol Hill," Denver Mayor Wellington E. Webb said, standing before a black, 10-foot board with victims' names recorded in white and the dates of their deaths in red. "It's time for Congress to do its part." Webb, who is president of the U.S. Conference of Mayors meeting in Washington this week, called on Congress to renew its work on gun restrictions. Last spring, in consideration of a juvenile justice bill, the Senate passed new provisions requiring background checks at gun shows. But a similar set of proposals died in the House when Republicans complained the measures were too strong and some Democrats said they were too weak. Work on the gun legislation continues. The bloodshed didn't end with Columbine, Webb said, noting last year's other high-profile school and workplace shootings, which included the November slaying of seven at a Xerox office building in Honolulu. Mayors are on the front lines of the battle, New Orleans Mayor Marc Morial said in releasing a survey on gun deaths. The 3,094 victims' names and ages on the movable wall were reported from the group's survey of 100 cities, which recorded gun-related homicides from April 20, 1999 - the date of the high school shootings in Littleton, Colo. - through Dec. 31, 1999.

1098.text: GUN-11288 PRIMARY Inquirer Editorial: Take aim at illegal guns in antiviolence agenda In his inaugural address earlier this month, Mayor Nutter declared that reducing gun violence would be one of his top priorities. That task is all the more urgent now that the city has seen a spate of senseless killings, including a triple shooting in Juniata Park. The mayor has done a good job of expressing community outrage and empathizing with the survivors, but so far his antiviolence agenda is short on new specifics. More cops are coming, the mayor says, and he's promised a crackdown on illegal guns. He has also repeatedly called for closing the Florida loophole, which lets Pennsylvania residents with high-risk backgrounds obtain permits to carry a concealed weapon. Spokesman Mark McDonald says Mayor Nutter will offer "a more expansive description of where we're headed regarding violence" early next week. One key element should be a strict zero-tolerance policy for illegal guns. As law enforcement officials repeatedly point out, nearly every gun-related crime in the city is committed by someone who is not legally allowed to have one. The mayor should work with state and federal authorities to conduct stings and undercover operations that interrupt the pipeline of illegal guns. And when gun offenders are caught, they should face tough sentences - no handing out probation as if it were a minor offense. The mayor and District Attorney Seth Williams are trying to discourage overuse of costly, and often counterproductive, incarceration, and that's a worthy goal, but those who are caught with illegal weapons are high-risk offenders who deserve to be locked up.

1093.text: GUN-6478 PRIMARY NAACP Says It Will Sue Gunmakers; Strict Controls Sought On Selling Firearms The NAACP announced plans today to expand the wave of litigation targeting gunmakers by filing a federal lawsuit alleging that the industry recklessly distributes its product and asking the court to place broad controls on the sale of handguns. NAACP attorneys said the suit will follow the legal strategy used in many of the more than two dozen lawsuits brought recently by cities and individuals who argue that gunmakers foster the underground market in firearms through negligent distribution practices and deliberate oversupply. But rather than seeking monetary damages, this suit hopes to break new ground by asking the court to impose strict gun controls that in the past have been rejected by Congress and many state legislatures. Among the remedies the NAACP plans to seek in the suit are the elimination of sales at gun shows, a limit on the number of guns that can be sold in one transaction and unprecedented restrictions on who can sell guns. The planned lawsuit builds on the landmark verdict reached by a Brooklyn jury that in February found the gun industry legally at fault for not doing more to prevent criminals from buying guns. The jury in that case, brought by the survivors of homicide victims, found nine gun manufacturers negligent. Attorneys for the NAACP said at least 85 handgun manufacturers, distributors and importers will be named as defendants in the case, which they said would be filed in U.S. District Court in Brooklyn by Friday.

1096.text: NA-41829 PRIMARY Inquirer Editorial: The least they couldn't do There's no use passing laws against jaywalking, because some pedestrians will still jaywalk. There's no use passing laws against speeding, because motorists will still speed. There's no use making it a crime to rape or murder, because victims will still be assaulted. As dumb as those statements sound, they sum up the view of lawmakers who say there's no use passing tougher gun laws because criminals will break them. Despite all the carnage that has occurred in this country - not just the recent massacres, but also the daily toll of urban gun violence - they still don't get it. Because these lawmakers won't acknowledge that more restrictive gun laws can reduce violence even though they won't end it, the Senate on Wednesday killed a bill cosponsored by Sen. Pat Toomey (R., Pa.) to require background checks for most gun sales. It took one who knows Congress' ways, former Rep. Gabrielle Giffords (D., Ariz.), to accurately depict the Senate's moral malfeasance. In an essay for the New York Times, Giffords, who was disabled by a gunman in 2011, accused the senators of cowardice. "These senators made their decision based on political fear and on cold calculations about the money of special interests like the National Rifle Association, which in the last election cycle spent around $25 million on contributions, lobbying, and outside spending," she said.

1102.text: GUN-2379 PRIMARY Clinton Hunts, Making Point on Guns Setting out to prove that there is no inherent conflict between hunters who treasure their guns and politicians who seek to curb their sale, President Clinton bought a $41 Maryland hunting license in Annapolis today and spent two hours lying in wait for mallard ducks. Responding to an invitation extended several weeks ago by Representative Bill Brewster, an Oklahoma Democrat who is a board member of the National Rifle Association, Mr. Clinton rose well before dawn to renew his sportsman's credentials at the Fruit Hill Farm hunting lodge on Taylor's Island, Md. Mr. Clinton, who has campaigned for stronger controls on handguns while defending the right to own a firearm for sporting purposes, said he expected his excursion today would only "strengthen" his image as a gun control advocate. Who Shot the Duck? "It makes the point I've been making all along -- that it doesn't have anything to do with hunting," he said. "My policies don't have anything to do with hunting." For the record, the hunting party managed to fell only one duck in today's 16-degree cold. Mr. Clinton, who was carrying a borrowed 12-gauge Winchester shotgun and wearing camouflage and waders, said Mr. Brewster shot it; others said the duck was felled by the President's shot, the only one he took during the hunting trip. Mr. Clinton joins a long line of Presidential hunters, including George Bush, Jimmy Carter, Lyndon B. Johnson, Dwight D. Eisenhower and Theodore Roosevelt. Mr. Bush hunted quail during Christmastime trips to Beeville, Tex. Confronted on one occasion by animal rights advocates, Mr. Bush said: "These aren't animals. These are wild quail."

1099.text: GUN-10862 PRIMARY Chicago police decry conceal-carry bill PUBLIC SAFETY CHICAGO - Chicago police joined gun control groups Wednesday to voice concerns about flaws in Illinois' mental health screening system for would-be gun owners, problems that could be amplified by a bill to allow permits for carrying concealed firearms. A 2009 Illinois State Police report cited "significant information gaps" in the state's ability to detect and screen out people with serious mental illnesses. A State Police official testified last week that those gaps still exist. Illinois falls short in its reporting of psychiatric hospital admissions to the FBI as required by a federal law passed after the 2007 Virginia Tech mass shootings, according to the ISP report. Gaps also exist in reporting by health professionals and nursing homes of mentally ill people who should be disqualified from gun ownership under Illinois law. Last week, Illinois State Police Firearms Bureau Chief John Coffman told lawmakers that problems have caused him "some sleepless nights." "The concern is the name of a prohibited person would be found in a queue someplace after an event," Coffman said in an official audio recording of the April 14 hearing provided to The Associated Press. "We are concerned about the existence of some gaps and the potential tragedy that could occur as a result." Richard Pearson of the Illinois State Rifle Association said gaps in the reporting system are small and being fixed. He called the issue a maneuver by gun control advocates.

1101.text: GUN-7229 PRIMARY EDITORIAL Enforce existing laws Inaction, mistakes and convenient explanations seem to be  business as usual in some offices of the U.S. Bureau of Alcohol  Tobacco and Firearms. According to interviews and documents obtained by The Post,  federal regulators allowed two Colorado gun stores to stay in  business after investigators reported they had sold guns to  criminals. And there's more: Barely 2 percent of Colorado's licensed  firearms dealers are inspected annually to determine whether they  are in compliance with federal gun laws. We often hear the call - especially from gun-rights advocates  - for more enforcement of existing gun laws. Indeed, The Post's  Sunday stories seemed to describe a classic case of no - or, at  best, lax - enforcement of gun-control laws already on the books. Everything from straw purchases - in which a gun is bought by  a legal buyer then sold or given to a criminal - to penalizing  whistle-blowers has gone on with ATF knowledge. In the most glaring example, the ATF granted new firearms  licenses to a Lakewood dealer who had repeatedly sold guns to  people who couldn't pass criminal background checks. That same  dealer continued his business and kept his license after being  arrested for investigation of child abuse and domestic violence. ATF records indicate that a mere 21 of 1,300 dealers in  Colorado were inspected for compliance in the fiscal year ending  September 1997. There were 26 compliance inspections statewide in  1998, and 47 last year.

characters: U.S. Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco and Firearms

1104.text: GUN-5241 PRIMARY MISSOURI PROPOSITION B: CONCEALED WEAPONS : BOTH SIDES IN GUN DEBATE POINT TO; TEXAS TO MAKE THEIR CASES For a sneak preview of what life with concealed weapons might be like, Missourians can look to Texas. Both sides of the debate say the Texas experience bolsters their case. The Lone Star State legalized concealed weapons in 1996. Today, about 184,000 Texans have licenses to carry. "The law's been very successful and, clearly, the naysayers -- the ones who predicted shootouts at every four-way stop -- were wrong," said Jerry Patterson, a former Texas senator who sponsored his state's conceal-carry law. But an anti-gun group, the Washington-based Violence Policy Center, studied records and statistics supplied by the Texas Department of Public Safety and found that license holders have been arrested for 2,080 crimes since 1996. "When the gun lobby pushed the Texas law through, they promised it would not arm the bad guys," the study's author, Susan Glick, said Monday. "They now owe us over 2,000 explanations." The crimes ranged from felonies such as murder and kidnapping, which may or may not have involved the licensed concealed weapon, to misdemeanors such as driving while intoxicated. Gun-rights supporters say the arrests account for about 1 percent of the current number of Texas license holders. The law overall is working well, they say, and there has been no movement in Texas to repeal it. "The arrest rate of handgun license holders is tremendously low and lower than the rate for the total population," Patterson said.

1108.text: GUN-10366 PRIMARY The N.Y. mosque and religious freedom Reading the Aug. 23 front-page article about opposition to construction of an Islamic center in Murfreesboro, Tenn., ["Nowhere near Ground Zero, but no more welcome"] I thought about those who say they are for religious freedom but oppose an Islamic center in Lower Manhattan only because of its proposed location. If the opponents of Park51 in New York are concerned about the site because it reflects insensitivity to those who suffered from the Sept. 11, 2001, terrorist attacks, why are they not speaking out in favor of religious freedom in Murfreesboro and other communities where similar struggles are underway? Respect for the feelings of those victimized by the Sept. 11 attacks  is important, but are religious liberty and equal treatment of those of different religions less significant? Aryeh Neier, New York The writer is president of the Open Society Institute. -- In his Aug. 24 op-ed column, "The mosque cop-out," Richard Cohen claimed that one could not oppose the mosque being built near Ground Zero unless one believed that Islam as a whole attacked America on Sept. 11. Let me propose another scenario: If someone were to announce a plan to build a gun store close to Columbine High School, would Mr. Cohen defend such a move? The principle is the same. Our constitution protects religious freedom just as it protects the right to bear arms. One need not seek repeal of the Second Amendment to disagree with such a plan. Even if the person's intentions were noble, such as performing social outreach to show the world that the vast majority of gun owners are peaceful, law-abiding citizens who want nothing to do with the extremists who wreaked havoc nearby, would any of us not find such a move at best questionable, and at worst highly inappropriate?

1103.text: GUN-10691 PRIMARY Va. Democrats bear arms asa wedge issue While economic issues are dominating the debate from the presidential campaign down to most local contests, some Northern Virginia Democrats are hoping to make their races this year a referendum on another topic: guns. In the state Senate contest for the 31st District, Arlington County Board member Barbara A. Favola (D) and some sympathetic activists are making the case that businesswoman Caren Merrick (R) is far to the right of suburban voters on gun-control issues. Lawyer Pamela Danner (D) has made the same charge against Del. Barbara Comstock (R) in their 34th District House race. And a handful of other contests in Northern Virginia feature similarly stark splits, with Democrats who get an F grade from the National Rifle Association facing Republicans who scored A's. Virginia as a whole tends to support gun rights and elects politicians who do as well. But voters in the close-in Washington suburbs are often more liberal than their downstate neighbors on a host of social issues, including guns. The question in this year's races isn't just where Northern Virginia residents stand on gun issues; it's whether they care enough about the subject that it will affect their votes Nov. 8. Favola, in particular, hopes that they do. Running to succeed retiring state Sen. Mary Margaret Whipple (D) in a redrawn district that stretches from Arlington to eastern Loudoun County, Favola has leaned heavily on social issues to attack Merrick. She has been helped by gun-control advocates.

1106.text: GUN-11982 PRIMARY President calls for proposals on guns President Obama on Monday began the first serious push of his administration to attempt to reduce gun violence, directing Cabinet members to formulate a set of proposals that could include reinstating a ban on assault rifles. The effort will be led by Vice President Biden, according to two people outside the government who have spoken to senior administration officials since Friday, when a gunman killed his mother and rampaged through Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn., killing 20 children, six adults and himself. The tentative steps ended a paralyzing debate within the administration over how hard to pursue gun-control legislation, which has been a politically perilous issue for many Democrats. There were signs Monday, however, that such fear was abating on the Democratic side of the aisle. Democratic Sens. Harry M. Reid (Nev.), Joe Manchin (W.Va.) and Mark R. Warner (Va.) made clear that Congress should consider a range of options to address the issue; all three have been strong supporters of gun rights. Sen. Dianne Feinstein (Calif.) said she would introduce legislation that would reimpose the assault-rifle ban that lapsed in 2004. "We need to accept the reality that we're not doing enough to protect our citizens," Reid, the Senate majority leader, said after a moment of silence on the chamber's floor. "In the coming days and weeks, we'll engage in a meaningful conversation and proper debate about how to change laws and culture that allow this violence to continue to grow. . . . And every idea should be on the table."

1110.text: GUN-3505 PRIMARY JUDGE DENIES NORTH GUN PERMIT Oliver L. North can no longer pack a pistol because he is "not of good character," a judge ruled. The Republican U.S. Senate nominee had been allowed to carry up to three concealed handguns until March, when his permit expired. Clarke County Circuit Judge James L. Berry, the same judge who granted North his permit in 1992, rejected the application for a renewal last week. North's camp claimed the decision was politically motivated. Berry is a Democrat. "The only difference between 1992 and 1994 is that this year, Ollie North is running for the Senate." campaign spokesman Mark Merritt said. North was convicted in 1991 for his role in the Iran-contra arms-for- hostages deal, but the verdict was overturned on appeal. Berry acknowledged that North was competent to carry concealed weapons and has previously shown a need to have them: a terrorist threat while North worked in the Reagan administration. But the judge added that he was unable to ignore North's convictions "for crimes involving moral turpitude . . . even though his convictions were later overturned."

1109.text: NA-43860 PRIMARY Biden in Google hangout: It's gun safety, not gun control; Biden held a 'fireside hangout' on Thursday to address the issue of gun violence. Vice President Biden on Thursday continued to make the case for the Obama administration's new gun control agenda, imploring participants in a Google "fireside hangout" to reach out to their members of Congress on the issue. "Write your congressman. For or against, write your congressman," Biden said during the online conversation, which comes as Democratic members are reintroducing an assault-weapons ban on Capitol Hill. Biden was joined at Thursday's chat by moderator Hari Sreenivasan of PBS NewsHour, video blogger Philip DeFranco and Guy Kawasaki, the former Apple chief evangelist who has written a book on Google+. Asked about the renewed push for an assault weapons ban, Biden acknowledged that the "vast majority" of deaths due to gun violence do not involve the use of such weapons. But he framed the issue as a question of whether assault weapons have any "real utility" for gun users, arguing that fewer police officers were killed by such weapons while the previous ban was intact than after it expired. "Police organizations overwhelmingly support (an assault weapons ban), because they get outgunned," Biden said. "They are outgunned on the street by the bad guys and the proliferation of these weapons." He invoked the mass shootings in Tucson, Ariz., and Aurora, Colo., as examples of why high-capacity magazines should be banned. "When the guy had to swap out a new magazine, he fumbled," he said of the Tucson shooting at a constituent event for former Rep. Gabrielle Giffords (D-Ariz.), arguing that if alleged gunman Jared Lee Loughner had had a high-capacity magazine, "all of them would have been dead."

1107.text: GUN-10629 PRIMARY States loosen concealed carry gun laws; Several don't require permit; some allow on campuses A Mississippi resident who receives a concealed carry permit and takes an eight-hour course can now carry a gun on college campuses, in bars and in courthouses. As of this summer, Wyoming residents need no permits for concealed weapons. And in Indiana, private businesses must allow employees to keep firearms in their vehicles on company property. Those and other recent changes on the state level represent a growing shift toward loosening state gun regulations, according to University of Chicago professor Jens Ludwig. "When you look across the states, they are definitely moving in the direction of allowing concealed weapons in more locations," Ludwig said. Supporters of the trend see it as a boost for gun rights. The National Rifle Association tracks the legislation online and has praised the new state laws. Others say the trend could pose a threat to public safety. "The gun lobby won't stop," said Brian Malte, of the Washington, D.C.-based Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence. Malte described the way he sees the trend for the past 10 years: guns "anytime, any place and for anyone." Proponents of the shift say they are just trying to give law-abiding citizens a way to protect themselves. "Somebody who's disturbed or a crook -- they're not going to care (if it's illegal to carry a gun in certain locations)," said Mississippi state Rep. Greg Snowden, a Republican who was one of three authors of an amendment that paved the way for the new policy here.

characters: University of Chicago professor Jens Ludwig
Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1105.text: NA-43044 PRIMARY The Gun Report: March 17, 2014 Last week we wrote about how state gun control laws were strengthened in the aftermath of the Newtown massacre. But in some states, gun restrictions are quickly unravelling. A bill in the Georgia state legislature could soon allow firearms in bars, churches, airports and certain government buildings, and school employees could carry weapons on school grounds. It's been called the most extreme gun bill in America. Yesterday the Arizona Senate approved legislation allowing anyone with a gun, open or concealed, to ignore "no weapons" signs on public buildings if there are no available lockers in which to secure their firearms. A proposal to dismantle the state background check system is set for a committee vote in the Pennsylvania House today. The measure would bypass and defund the state background check system, which was established in 1998, in favor of the national background check system. Last Thursday, Indiana lawmakers approved an N.R.A.-supported bill to allow guns in parked cars on school property, despite objections from school groups. Last week, Idaho's governor signed a bill into law allowing students, staff and visitors to bring concealed guns onto college and university campuses. But the myriad new laws are following a national trend: America has more guns in private hands than ever before, even as crime rates fall. With no federal firearms prohibitions, the gun control debate will likely continue at the state level.

1111.text: NA-40411 PRIMARY Missouri Republicans outline new gun proposal JEFFERSON CITY o Missouri Republicans are considering a new approach to prevent federal agents from enforcing laws the state considers to be infringements on gun rights - barring them from future careers in state law enforcement agencies. The change marks the most recent version of Missouri's attempt to nullify some federal gun control laws. It was endorsed by a state Senate committee and is likely to reach the chamber floor. The employment ban is seen as a compromise designed to make the bill more palatable, while also making federal agents think twice before carrying out laws and policies that the measure's supporters consider unconstitutional. Republicans have been divided on how to punish potential violators of the legislation dubbed the "Second Amendment Preservation Act." The spectrum has ranged from possible jail time to allowing civil damages stemming from lawsuits filed by Missourians who believe an agent infringed on their gun rights. The version endorsed by the Senate General Laws Committee would leave the civil penalties on the table, in addition to the employment ban. "What we hope it accomplishes is for there to be a healthy degree of pause before anybody takes an action that could be unconstitutional," said Sen. Brian Nieves, R-Washington, the Senate bill's sponsor. Under the measure, federal employees who enforce or aid in a potential enforcement of certain gun control laws would be disqualified from any state and local law enforcement jobs in the future. Missourians could also sue if they think a law enforcement officer was employed after being involved in the implementation of a federal gun law. If the lawsuit is successful, the agent would be fired and the state or municipality would be required to pay the resident's court costs.

1112.text: GUN-8899 PRIMARY Ehrlich Backs Bill That Limits Suits Against Gunmakers; NARAL Criticizes Vote on Abortion Republican gubernatorial candidate Robert L. Ehrlich Jr. cast votes in Congress yesterday that would protect handgun manufacturers from certain lawsuits and allow hospitals to refuse to perform abortions, weighing in on two hot-button issues in Maryland politics. Ehrlich's campaign defended the votes as placing Ehrlich in the majority of members of Congress and the mainstream of public opinion. "These are not extreme positions," campaign spokesman Paul E. Schurick said. "They're consistent with his voting record in the past." But advocates and Democrats decried his positions as evidence that Ehrlich is not the moderate he claims to be. "This is a conservative -- make no mistake about it," said Peter Hamm, campaign spokesman for the Democratic candidate, Lt. Gov. Kathleen Kennedy Townsend. "He's not a moderate; he plays one on TV." At a House Energy and Commerce Committee hearing, Ehrlich voted for a bill that would limit lawsuits filed against gun manufacturers based on unsafe gun design. The bill, HR 2037, passed the committee by a vote of 30 to 16. Ehrlich also voted against an amendment proposed by Rep. Henry A. Waxman (D-Calif.) that would have imposed civil liability on people selling guns to terrorists. That amendment failed. Gun control advocates characterized the bill as the "gun industry protection act of 2002." "It's legislation that would provide about blanket immunity to gun manufacturers," said Mark Pertschuck, legislative director for the Coalition to Stop Handgun Violence.

1113.text: GUN-20192 PRIMARY Reno seeks state tests for gun owners WASHINGTON -- Prospective gun owners should be required to pass state-sanctioned proficiency tests before they obtain weapons, Attorney General Janet Reno said Thursday. Reno said applicants should have to pass both manual and written tests designed to measure basic knowledge of gun operation and safety. "I don't think anybody should have a weapon unless they demonstrate a proficiency to use it," Reno said. The attorney general's proposal is not binding on the states, and the Justice Department is not advocating federal legislation that would mandate proficiency tests. But Reno said the National Rifle Association offers the kind of training she thinks states should require. The NRA made it clear, however, that it would not support making such training mandatory. "The attorney general ought to spend as much time enforcing the law as she does cooking up more rules for law-abiding gun owners," said James Baker, the NRA's chief lobbyist. "What does she want to do next, tattoo every gun owner?" Reno's proposal comes as the Clinton administration continues to push for stronger gun laws in the aftermath of the Columbine High School massacre. Several Columbine students were with Reno and President Clinton at the White House on Thursday as Clinton continued his campaign for mandatory background checks at gun shows. The administration's proposal also would require child safety locks on handguns. "Don't allow the victims of Columbine to have died in vain," Clinton said. "We must not lose the urgency of our mission." The president also criticized Congress for stalling gun legislation.

1115.text: NA-42819 PRIMARY Armed in Kentucky When I went to a gun store in Lexington, Ky., recently to shoot a variety of guns, I knew I was in gun country. What I hadn't quite appreciated was the extent to which I had wound up in the beating heart of gun country. But after I got back to New York, my new friend Andrew Silton, a former financial consultant who writes a terrific blog, Meditations on Money Management - and whose analysis of Cerberus and the Freedom Group I quoted in my December 21 column - sent me some eye-opening statistics. He compiled a list of  the 50 states ranked according to how many background checks for firearm purchases had taken place in each state. At the very top is Kentucky, with 15 million checks over the last decade - in a state with only 4.4 million people. That is 3.41 searches per person. As you'll see in the chart below, no other state even comes close. Texas, for instance, saw 10 million searches in a state with 26 million people, for an average of 0.38 background checks per resident. My home state of Rhode Island had a mere 0.16 checks per resident. The country's average is 0.48 background checks per person. Any wonder why Kentucky gun laws are likely to remain lenient even as Connecticut and New York race to tighten theirs?

1114.text: GUN-1803 PRIMARY NRA TAKES AIM AT PRESIDENT OF FRATERNAL ORDER OF POLICE WASHINGTON - Leaders of the National Rifle Association are trying to topple the president of the Fraternal Order of Police, a strong supporter of gun control. The police group, with 226,000 members, is a major force in seeking controls on assault weapons and a waiting period for the buying of handguns. Dewey Stokes of Columbus, Ohio, president of the group, said Friday that the NRA was backing Tom Pussamato, a detective from Newark, N.J., to defeat him at the organization's elections Aug. 15 in Pittsburgh.   ''They've really gotten involved for the first time in our election process,'' said Stokes, who is seeking his third two-year term. '' They've put money and effort into Pussamato's campaign. They really have no business getting involved.'' Stokes said the NRA also was targeting the group's secretary, Ralph Orms of Louisville, Ky.   Pussamato said the NRA as an organization was not financing or backing him.   But he acknowledges that NRA magazines are running his campaign messages and that NRA board members support him.   Pussamato says he doesn't agree with the police group's position on gun control. Handgun Control Inc. has contributed more than $10,000 to the the police group while Stokes was president, he said.   The NRA campaign is being conducted through the Law Enforcement Alliance of America, a police group organized in October, according to Stokes. Leroy Pyle, executive director of the alliance, is on the NRA's board, and many other alliance members also belong to the NRA.   Pyle said the alliance backed Pussamato because Stokes had been too outspoken on political issues, including gun control.  '' He is not a friend of the NRA,'' Pyle said. '' I hope NRA is working against him. He should stay out of it (gun control issues.''

1116.text: GUN-31123 PRIMARY D.C. Legislation Would Remove More Gun Limits; Council Vote Near on Storage, Other Rules D.C. officials, coping with a U.S. Supreme Court ruling that threw out the city's handgun ban, have drafted legislation that would do away with several remaining firearms restrictions, including safe-storage requirements and a provision that bars ownership of semiautomatic pistols. The legislation could come up for a vote in the D.C. Council as early as Tuesday -- the same day the U.S. House of Representatives is expected to vote on a bill that would virtually end local handgun control in the District. The developments mark another step in what has become a dramatic shift in public policy regarding firearms in a city still plagued by gun violence. Thirty-two years after the first generation of elected D.C. officials under home rule enacted the nation's toughest gun control statutes, banning handguns entirely, the current leaders face a different political and legal climate and appear resigned to debating how best to have a less restrictive law. Addressing contentious issues that have arisen since the Supreme Court ruled in June that the city's handgun ban was unconstitutional, council member Phil Mendelson (D-At Large) said yesterday he has worked with the administration of Mayor Adrian M. Fenty (D) in drafting the legislation. The changes would go beyond emergency legislation passed by the council in the wake of the court ruling that allowed qualified residents to keep revolvers, but not semiautomatics, in their homes.

1117.text: GUN-9374 PRIMARY FEDS BULLETPROOF THE GUN INDUSTRY Government exists to protect the people it represents. But one bill marching through Congress would put Americans in danger. The Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act bans most lawsuits against gun makers and gun dealers, giving them immunity for selling weapons to criminals. U.S. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms statistics show that just 1.2% of the nation's firearms dealers sell 57% of the guns used in crimes. The feds and the firearms industry know who these traffickers are, but they do nothing about it. Blame the National Rifle Association - probably the most powerful lobbying operation in Washington. Congress won't pass general tort reform to derail excessive awards in civil suits, but it is willing to provide a security blanket for this bloody industry. Again, blame the NRA, which contributes prodigiously to campaigns and gets out the troops for elections and, therefore, has a gun to the politicians' heads. The fact that voters might want to hold irresponsible dealers and manufacturers accountable matters very little. Last fall, snipers terrorized the D.C. metro area for 23 days. Neither of the suspects, John Muhammad and Lee Malvo, was legally allowed to buy a gun - Muhammad because of his violent history, Malvo because of his age. Yet they were able to obtain a Bushmaster military-style assault rifle with a sniper grip from Bull's Eye Shooter Supply in Tacoma, Wash.

characters: U.S. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1119.text: GUN-3765 PRIMARY SALVATORE LEAVES PUZZLE IN SWITCH ON THE GUN BILL When Sen. Frank A. Salvatore stepped forward Monday to switch his vote and strip Philadelphia of a special gun-control law, his city colleagues were flabbergasted. "I have no idea why he did it," said Sen. Vincent J. Fumo (D., Phila.) . Salvatore's change of heart, to help break a 25-25 tie in the Senate, meant city residents would no longer have to demonstrate need before getting a permit to carry a concealed weapon. The city's six other state senators, all Democrats, voted for the tougher city law, which is supported by Mayor Rendell, Police Commissioner Richard Neal and District Attorney Lynne M. Abraham. Turns out, Salvatore voted for the tougher measure, too - in 1988. Back then, Salvatore, a Republican who represents Northeast Philadelphia, voted in favor of a bill that allowed the city police commissioner to retain extra powers over who can get permits. Not only did he support the bill, he was a cosponsor. Salvatore wouldn't comment Monday night on why this time he first voted "nay" on killing the Philadelphia provision, then 15 minutes later voted "aye." Yesterday, he was on his way to Italy and unavailable. Dick Shurilla, an aide, said the senator's flip-flop wasn't difficult to comprehend. Like others, including the National Rifle Association, Salvatore decided city residents should be treated the same as folks in Bucks, Berks, Potter or Erie Counties.

1118.text: GUN-11356 PRIMARY your world at a glance TALLAHASSEE County can sue Scott over gun law Palm Beach County commissioners will be allowed to move forward with a lawsuit filed against Gov. Rick Scott over a law that forced them to repeal several local gun restrictions, a Leon County judge ruled Wednesday. Circuit Judge John Cooper said Palm Beach County can pursue its action against the governor but that the state Legislature would be dropped from the case. The judge also agreed to allow Broward County to join Palm Beach County in the suit. Under a state law enacted last year, elected local officials face $5,000 fines and removal if they pass local gun laws more strict than state laws. As a result, Palm Beach County commissioners were forced to repeal several gun rules that had been on the books for years, including provisions that prevented people from taking guns into child-care facilities and that barred gun owners from firing weapons east of 20-Mile Bend. The county sued in the governor, House and Senate in December, calling the sanctions "political bullying that serves no governmental purpose." JENNIFER SORENTRUE TALLAHASSEE Nelson, Mack top $2 million mark Democratic U.S. Sen. Bill Nelson and Republican rival Connie Mack IV both topped the $2 million mark in fund-raising in the last full quarter before the Nov. 6 election, with both contenders saying Wednesday they are in good shape for the campaign's homestretch.

1121.text: GUN-10357 PRIMARY DRUNK AND PACKING? OK IF YOU'RE A COP! IT COULD SOON be a crime to get drunk while packing heat - unless you're a cop. A bill aired at a City Council hearing yesterday would subject licensed gun owners to a possible year in jail and $10,000 fine if they're over the limit and armed. Public Safety Committee Chairman Peter Vallone Jr. said state law prevents the city from including on-duty or off-duty cops in the measure. The bill has the support of the NYPD and Mayor Bloomberg, who failed to get a similar law passed in Albany that also exempted cops. Arkady Gerney, a mayoral criminal justice aide, testified that police officers are covered by departmental penalties for carrying their firearms and drinking even when off-duty. But nothing stops a civilian from doing it. "If automobiles are dangerous with a drunken driver behind the wheel, it should be obvious that the same principle applies when someone carries a gun while intoxicated," Gerney testified. The bill was blasted by the New York State Rifle and Pistol Association as a "new tactic" to undermine legal gun rights.

characters: New York State Rifle and Pistol Association

1120.text: NA-42068 PRIMARY Pleasant Hill considers regulating gun and ammunition dealers PLEASANT HILL -- In a surprise move, Mayor Michael Harris and Councilman David Durant on Monday presented a draft ordinance requiring a police permit for firearms and ammunition dealers. Under the proposal, gun shops could not locate within 150 feet of a residence, within 500 feet of parks, another gun dealer, massage parlor or adult entertainment venue or within 1,000 feet of a day care or school. Store owners and employees would have to pass a criminal-background check. Dealers also must install an alarm system and surveillance cameras. The permit must be renewed annually. Existing firearms dealers would be exempt from the location restrictions in the ordinance, but they would have to submit employees' background information to the Pleasant Hill police. The sole dealer in the city with a home occupancy permit to sell guns would be allowed to continue operating, but no new permits for residential sales would be granted if the council approves the ordinance. Durant and Harris said regulating gun and ammunition sales would protect residents' health, safety and welfare and provide for local control. The City Council voted 4-1 to direct the city attorney to review the proposed ordinance and make any changes necessary to conform with state and federal law by Sept. 9. The council is scheduled to discuss the ordinance Sept. 30. Police Chief John Moore said police have not had any problems with the four firearms dealers in the city -- City Arms, Diablo Valley Gun Works, Big 5 and a federally licensed gun dealer who operates an online business from his home. Dick's Sporting Goods, which is being built in the Crossroads Shopping Center, also is expected to sell guns.

1125.text: GUN-8296 PRIMARY Where Do Criminals Get Their Guns? To the Editor: The Journal of the American Medical Association study questioning the effectiveness of the Brady law in reducing homicide rates begins with a flawed premise: that criminals get guns from their own states (news article, Aug. 2). The study compared the 32 states that installed the Brady law in 1994 with the 18 states that already required buyer background checks and concluded that the law had limited effect. But gun-trace data show that a handful of states with weak gun laws are the source of most of the country's out-of-state crime guns. They also show that in states with tough gun laws, like New York and New Jersey, nearly three-fourths of all crime guns were bought in another state and trafficked over state lines illegally. Buried in the study is the acknowledgment that loopholes in the Brady law must be eliminated to allow it to work more effectively. If that were the result of this study, I could forgive its shortcomings. But it is more likely to set back the cause of gun control. CHARLES E. SCHUMER U.S. Senator from New York Washington, Aug. 4, 2000 http://www.nytimes.com

1124.text: GUN-7694 PRIMARY Anti-gun moms urged to follow MADD example Take on the NRA, Denver marchers told Columbine High School parent Tom Mauser asked thousands of  Colorado mothers Sunday to become a political and social force  like Mothers Against Drunk Driving. Instead of getting drunks out from behind steering wheels,  the new goal is to plug legal loopholes to keep criminals from  getting their hands on a gun, Mauser said. Mauser's son, Daniel, was killed during the Columbine High  School massacre. ' We need all of you to be advocates - just  look at MADD,' Mauser told about 12,000 at Civic Center. 'We need  the same effort by moms against gun violence.' Mauser was the keynote speaker of Colorado's version of the  Million Mom March for gun control.
