Passage of either bill would be a major defeat for the National Rifle Association, the powerful gun lobby that today is facing unprecedented challenges in state legislatures and biting criticism from politicians once considered friends. "Slowly, inexorably, the NRA is getting weaker," said Rep. Charles Schumer, D-N.Y., the House sponsor of the Brady bill. "It used to be that anything the NRA wanted, they got. Now, there's a fighting chance to beat them."

1174.text: GUN-7237 PRIMARY Governed by Extremes It's no surprise whatever to see gun-control advocates and their right-to-bear-arms opponents talking past one another. It's in the nature of things that those on opposite sides of highly charged issues will proceed from different definitions of the problem, different emphases, different realities. Still, there is something peculiarly nasty about the present argument between the Clinton administration and the National Rifle Association. One wonders what it is about. Let me be clear: While administration spokesmen have fired a few verbal salvos themselves, most of the nastiness is from the NRA. I first noticed it in that series of ads from actor Charlton Heston, who essentially calls President Clinton a liar for saying the NRA consistently has opposed gun-control legislation. But if Heston was over the top, what do you make of the charge by NRA Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre on Wednesday that the blood of Ricky Byrdsong, a black former coach at Northwestern University gunned down by a white supremacist on a shooting spree last July, is on Clinton's hands? Here is how he reaches this bizarre conclusion: The alleged gunman, Benjamin Nathaniel Smith, bought his guns from an unlicensed dealer after failing a background check initiated by a licensed seller. Clinton's failure? Although the background checks are explicitly a state function under the law, the federal government didn't arrest Smith for the attempt. If it had done so, he wouldn't have been free to buy weapons from the unlicensed dealer, and Byrdsong wouldn't have been shot. Ergo: Byrdsong's blood is on Clinton's hands.

1178.text: GUN-2872 PRIMARY ROBERTI DODGES RECALL CAMPAIGN BACKED BY POWERFUL GUN LOBBY State Sen. David Roberti, the powerful veteran California lawmaker, Tuesday easily dodged a recall campaign backed by the gun lobby. Roberti, 54, a liberal Democrat from the San Fernando Valley, survived the bitter and, for taxpayers, expensive effort to oust him from office just a few months before he has to leave anyway because of term limits. The recall failed Tuesday night by a margin of 59 percent to 41 percent, with 30,743 votes against ousting the veteran legislator and 21,276 in favor. The special election drew national attention because the recall was bankrolled in part by the National Rifle Association, which was angered by Roberti's authorship of an assault-rifle ban. "For years, the NRA has been perceived as having the power to make or break politicians," said Richard M. Aborn, president of Washington-based Handgun Control Inc., a Roberti backer. "It's not true. They just can't vote politicians out of office." Bill Powers, an NRA spokesman, reached by phone at his hotel room late Tuesday, said he had to catch a plane to Washington and didn't have time to comment. At his victory rally, Roberti claimed his survival proved that lawmakers across the country can vote their conscience on gun issues without fear of losing office. "They (NRA) wanted to take the credit for victory," said Roberti. "Now, they can take credit for a great big defeat."

1179.text: GUN-5830 PRIMARY House opts to vote separately on measures for gun control The House will vote separately on a series of gun control proposals, scuttling an earlier plan to vote on the measures as part of a juvenile crime bill, Republican leaders said Monday. The latest procedural move reflects the continued uncertainty and confusion within Republican ranks over how they should handle the most controversial elements of proposed gun restrictions, the first the House has considered since banning assault weapons in 1994. GOP leaders claimed that separating out the gun control provisions would provide a more honest and straightforward debate when the House takes up the juvenile crime measure either Wednesday or Thursday. But Democrats immediately denounced the move as a ploy to make it more difficult for some members to vote for gun control. Rep. Richard Gephardt (D- Mo.), the House minority leader, said it was easier for some members to vote for the 1994 assault weapons ban because it was included in a larger crime bill. Requiring members to vote exclusively on gun control this time around will make those votes politically more difficult, Gephardt said. "They don't want anybody to have any excuse to vote for this bill," he said. Rep. Bob Barr (R-Ga.), a board member of the National Rifle Association and a fierce opponent of any gun control, said he was pleased with the move. He said he hoped it would make it easier to defeat the measures.

1176.text: GUN-1509 PRIMARY Firearms Tax Proposed To Pay Treatment Costs An Illinois state legislator has added a new twist to the continuing battle between gun-control advocates and the National Rifle Association, proposing an apparently unique "user fee" in an attempt to force those who buy firearms to pay some of the cost of treating gunshot victims. Sponsored by Rep. Barbara Flynn Currie (D) of Chicago, the measure is given little chance of passage in the General Assembly, where the NRA's chief lobbyist said, "I'd love to have a roll call floor vote on it." But Currie and her allies, who include administrators of the few Chicago hospitals offering top-level trauma care, hope to use the debate to highlight what they say is the costly link between guns and financially troubled hospitals, especially in this and other major urban centers. The legislation would impose what Currie describes as a "user fee" and what the NRA calls a "tax on a constitutional right" of $ 20 on sale of all firearms statewide. The proceeds would be earmarked for the state's system of hospitals equipped with trauma centers, which provide the most sophisticated and expensive care for victims of gunshot wounds, automobile accidents and other severe injuries. Currie, a liberal anti-gun crusader, and Mark Karlin, head of a gun-control advocacy group here, got the idea from an unlikely source: Illinois Gov. Jim Edgar (R), who opposes new gun-control laws and has been supported by the NRA.

1173.text: GUN-4684 PRIMARY Two Md. Counties Aim to Put a Lock on Triggers; Montgomery, Pr. George's Want Handgun Owners to Buy Safety Device to Protect Children Taking a cue from President Clinton's State of the Union speech, political leaders in Montgomery and Prince George's counties will begin a campaign today to require all handgun owners in the two counties to buy trigger-locking devices for their weapons. Details of proposed county ordinances, which would go beyond present state law requirements, are to be disclosed at a joint news conference by Prince George's County Executive Wayne K. Curry (D) and Montgomery County Executive Douglas M. Duncan (D) in what they see as a major pitch to protect children from accidental shootings in the home. Joining the county executives will be the police chiefs of both counties, Maryland Attorney General J. Joseph Curran Jr., several members of both county councils and Sarah Brady, national head of Handgun Control Inc. Brady is the wife of former White House press secretary James S. Brady, who was severely wounded by gunfire in an assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan in 1981. The Bradys have crusaded across the country for limitations on handguns since the shooting. "I commend [Curry and Duncan] for taking a stand to protect Maryland's children from senseless gun injuries and deaths," Sarah Brady said in a statement. "You can't be too safe when it comes to children," said Vincent DeMarco, chairman of Marylanders Against Handgun Abuse, anticipating complaints by pro-gun groups that the ordinances would be onerous and ineffective.

characters: Attorney General J. Joseph Curran Jr.

1182.text: GUN-31556 PRIMARY YOUR OPINIONS; READERS WRITE Shootings in crossfire Responses to "Shootings are insane, but so is response,'" @issue, April 12. More of the same from anti-gun crowd Cynthia Tucker's Sunday column amounted to hardly anything more than a burp from another anti-gun scribe with an unfounded position to tout. We have all been challenged in some way by the economic recession. Unfortunately, the recent tragedies she cited serve as evidence that tough times also breed the type of despair that leads to violent, horrific crimes. Such events, combined with more outspoken anti-gun politicians in Washington, have contributed to the surge in applications for permits to carry a concealed weapon, the upswing in firearms sales and the shortage of ammunition. If Tucker calls for the curtailment of legal firearms possession, then she should enlighten us --- the supposedly "indifferent" public --- as to why we should be disarmed. She should carefully consider the police cutbacks and a marked uptick in violent crimes that have marred some of Atlanta's most attractive neighborhoods and driven average citizens to legally purchase at least one gun. BRIAN LUDVIGSEN, Atlanta What's the solution? What about rights? Cynthia Tucker's piece was not very clear on what she was proposing to end the shootings. And she ignored a sticky consideration: Gun ownership is a constitutionally guaranteed right. Guns and even bullets are illegal for most people to carry in D.C. But there are more gun-related crimes there than in the surrounding metro area where potential victims just might be armed.

1181.text: NA-42616 PRIMARY Closing Gun-Sale Loophole Rises on Agenda WASHINGTON -- As Senate Democrats grapple with how much of President Obama's broad gun control agenda is politically achievable, or even desirable, Senator Patrick J. Leahy of Vermont, chairman of the committee with jurisdiction over gun laws, said that moving to tighten background checks should be a top priority. ''One of the first things you want to do is close the gun-show loophole,'' said Mr. Leahy, in an interview for C-SPAN's ''Newsmakers,'' referring to a legal provision that permits unlicensed gun sellers to sell to people without conducting background checks. The loophole is a small piece of a much broader agenda introduced by Mr. Obama on Wednesday, one that faces considerable, though not insurmountable, odds on Capitol Hill. The view of Mr. Leahy, a Democrat with a history of supporting some gun rights, is crucial because the work of his Judiciary Committee will be central to advancing any new gun legislation. He declined to detail precisely how the committee, which will hold hearings on potential gun legislation this month, would proceed with Mr. Obama's request to push legislation that includes a renewal of an assault weapons ban, a limit on magazine size and universal background checks. Mr. Leahy's plans will also be closely watched by senators with their own gun control legislative agendas, lawmakers who may decide to push their own bills if the committee process does not yield the results they seek.

1180.text: NA-44089 PRIMARY Worst Week inWashington Look before you leap is a cliche. It's also true. Sens. Joe Manchin III (D-W.Va.) and Patrick J. Toomey (R-Pa.) found out just how true this past week when their attempt to pass a bipartisan gun deal imploded on the Senate floor. Manchin and Toomey spent much of the previous week being lauded for working together to craft a compromise to rescue the gun bill, which includes expanded background checks for gun purchases  and provides more money for school safety. However, as this past week dawned, it became clear that they were out on a limb that their colleagues were sawing off. Early in the week, Arizona's Jeff Flake (R), an undecided vote, came out against the bill's compromise for background checks on most gun sales. Richard Burr (R-N.C.) followed. And then Heidi Heitkamp (D-N.D.). By  Thursday's vote, even Manchin and Toomey had acknowledged that the 60 votes they needed just weren't there. The background-check proposal got 54 ayes, three fewer than an amendment offered by Sen. John Cornyn (R-Tex.) that would have allowed concealed weapons to be carried across state lines. Both men put a brave face on it. "No regrets ," Toomey told the Allentown Morning Call. Manchin insisted that the National Rifle Association had "made a big mistake " in opposing the amendment. But, when cutting a deal, it's kind of important to make sure you can deliver. Neither senator could do that.

1183.text: GUN-30890 PRIMARY Justices Will Weigh Challenges To Gun Laws The Supreme Court announced on Wednesday that it would decide whether state and local gun control laws may be challenged under the Second Amendment. The court also agreed to hear nine other cases from among those that had piled up over its summer break, including one concerning the constitutionality of an antiterrorism law that is a favorite tool of federal prosecutors. The Second Amendment case, McDonald v. Chicago, No. 08-1521, addresses a question that was left open last year when the court decided that the Second Amendment protects an individual right to own firearms rather than a collective right tied to state militias. Last year's decision, District of Columbia v. Heller, concerned only federal laws and struck down parts of the gun control law in the District of Columbia, a federal enclave. The court ruled that the law violated the Second Amendment by barring law-abiding people from keeping guns in their homes for self-defense. The new case was brought by residents of Chicago who say their city's handgun ban is identical to the one struck down in Heller. Several Supreme Court decisions, all more than a century old, have said that the Second Amendment does not apply to the states. In June, the United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit, in Chicago, affirmed the dismissal of the new case, saying it was up to the Supreme Court to overrule its own precedents if it wished to do so. Chief Judge Frank H. Easterbrook, writing for a unanimous three-judge panel of the appeals court, added that it was not certain whether and how the Supreme Court might apply the Second Amendment to the states.

1187.text: GUN-8821 PRIMARY Prevention Beats Prosecution THE COMMON REFRAIN of those who oppose limits on gun sales and gun ownership is that we must support vigorous prosecution of criminals who use guns. Don't limit the rights of law-abiding people who want guns for sport or protection, they say, but throw the book at people who abuse those rights. As Virginia, Maryland and federal authorities squabble over who gets to prosecute first in the sniper killings that terrified this region for three weeks, we can see this philosophy in action. We certainly support a vigorous prosecution of the two alleged assailants, if the evidence holds up (though not the frenzy to subject an apparent juvenile to the death penalty). But even convictions in each of the 10 murders and three woundings would be less satisfying than it would have been to prevent the shootings, by keeping guns away from potential criminals, or to solve them sooner, by maintaining better registries of gun owners and ballistic fingerprints. Being tough after the fact is only partial comfort. Ah, there they go again, the NRA will say -- exploiting a tragedy to make a political point. Well, yes. If trying to learn from sad experience and develop sound policies to make such experiences less likely in the future constitutes exploitation, we plead guilty. And we acknowledge that much remains unknown about this case. It seems that John Allen Muhammad was able to buy a gun illegally, exposing again the weaknesses in the nation's background-check system; he was under a court restraining order that should have barred the sale. It seems that a man who had been found in court to have illegally taken control of his children, who had threatened to kill his ex-wife, according to her allegation -- it seems that such a man had no trouble buying the civilian facsimile of an M-16 rifle and the scope to go with it, and apparently other guns as well. It seems that a national ballistics registry of the sort that only Maryland and New York have instituted might have given investigators a boost as they tried to solve these murders. But many details have yet to come out about this case, and some of these early impressions may not hold up.

1189.text: GUN-2418 PRIMARY LETTERS FROM THE PEOPLE Gun Restrictions Put U.S. Foot In The Door I have long ago decided that the position of the National Rifle Association in fighting any gun legislation was archaic and detrimental to the public good. I thought then and still do think that automatic weapons and armor-piercing bullets have no place in the hands of the public. But if I would bring this viewpoint up to an NRA member, he would always counter with, "If we let the foot of government in the door, they won't stop until every aspect of gun ownership has been controlled and restricted. "Surely not" I would argue, "and besides, we have nothing to fear from our government nowadays." Recent events are changing my mind. Passage of the benign Brady bill does not concern me, but it is eerie that less than a week after it was passed, the administration and legislators were promising that this is only the beginning and that there is need for a great deal more legislation. They now admit that the Brady bill will do little to solve the problem but that it is only "the foot in the door." Perhaps for the first time in my life, I was wrong again.   Paul E. Mohme Collinsville There are many reasons guns should not be licensed. The protection offered by Second Amendment to the Constitution is the main one. Another reason is that guns do not cause crime, and focusing on guns only allows us to evade reality. Focusing on guns is an easy way for politicians to make us believe that they are doing something about crime and violence.

1186.text: GUN-8412 PRIMARY WISCONSIN GROUP: 200 BOUGHT GUNS ILLEGALLY; IT BLAMES DELAY IN STATE BACKGROUND CHECKS FOR POTENTIAL GUN OWNERS MILWAUKEE -- Wisconsin's background check system for potential gun owners is only 65 percent automated, causing delays that have allowed more than 200 people to illegally buy firearms, an advocacy group said Wednesday. The Washington-based Americans for Gun Safety Foundation reports at least 216 people illegally bought guns in Wisconsin between December 1998 and June 2001 because of the delay caused by the state's failure to fully computerize its conviction records. "Wisconsin has done a better job than some states, but they're still not where they should be," said Jim Kessler, the study's author. "They might have their arrests automated, but they don't have the final disposition in the system." The foundation found almost 10,000 people illegally bought guns nationwide in the 30-month period. The data, culled from state and federal statistics, shows those people were able to get their guns before background checks were completed because records were not fully automated. Wisconsin's handgun laws predated the Brady gun control law, so the state performs checks on handgun sales. The FBI is responsible for looking into buyers of other guns. Wisconsin's handgun sales law requires potential buyers to wait a mandatory 48 hours before getting a gun. If the state does not complete the background check by that time, it will request an additional three-day waiting period. Under the Brady Law in Wisconsin, those interested in purchasing long guns -- which include rifles -- can get their guns automatically after a background check is performed. The gun dealer is allowed to sell the firearm to the customer if the check takes longer than three days.

1184.text: NA-42915 PRIMARY The Gun Report: June 13, 2013 A Senate Judiciary committee in Raleigh, North Carolina, approved a proposal Tuesday to eliminate a law that requires that people receive a permit from their county sheriff before they can buy a handgun. The bill also expands the places that weapons can be taken: all educational property, including private schools, and parades and funeral processions - unless, of course, they're expressly prohibited. "We're here to enhance our Second Amendment rights, which have been too long restricted by the previous majority," said Sen. Buck Newton, a Republican. But Gail Neely of North Carolinians Against Gun Violence told the Senate judiciary committee that fewer than 3 percent of people in the state have concealed-carry permits and that 90 percent of the state favors tougher restrictions. "This is ridiculous," she said. "There is no credible evidence this bill will make us safer in North Carolina." North Carolina would join more than 30 states that don't require a permit to purchase a gun; a handful of states also allow concealed carry without a permit. Here is today's report. -Jennifer Mascia A 16-year-old girl is recovering after being shot in the face while on her way to a Sweet 16 party in the Ezra Street neighborhood of Bridgeport, Conn. Kemoune Thomas, a sophomore at Central High School, was shot while standing with one of her friends. Police have strong leads on who may have shot her, but so far no arrests have been made.

1185.text: NA-41815 PRIMARY Toomey, Manchin announce deal on gun background checks WASHINGTON - Pennsylvania Sen. Pat Toomey was an unlikely figure to change the course of the nation's debate over gun laws. But there he was Wednesday, a Republican best known for his fiscal views, standing with a Democrat before a phalanx of cameras to present a bipartisan plan for expanding federal background checks to cover firearms purchases at gun shows and online. "Candidly, I don't consider criminal background checks to be gun control. I think it's common sense," said Toomey, a gun owner who previously received an "A" rating from the National Rifle Association. His compromise bill with Sen. Joe Manchin (D., W.Va.) could open the door to the first significant new gun law in two decades. It also prompted a storm of competing reaction. From the left, gun-control advocates such as President Obama, New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg and Philadelphia Mayor Nutter praised Toomey's leadership, after long seeing him as one of many Republican roadblocks. From the right, Toomey's erstwhile allies lashed him. The lobbying arm for the Heritage Foundation, the conservative think-tank led by Toomey friend and former Sen. Jim DeMint, put out a statement saying, "We expect more from Pat Toomey." The deal - crafted by senators who have each received strong NRA ratings - added new momentum to the push to strengthen gun laws in the aftermath of December's Newtown school shooting.

1191.text: GUN-3105 PRIMARY Punish Only the Guilty To the Editor: Re "Mr. Dole's Transparent Tactics" (editorial, March 21): You are reacting to a problem with demands for gun control. Your argument that assault (they aren't by definition) weapons should be banned because they were designed to kill is flawed logic. Ninety-nine percent of the owners of these weapons use them legitimately for hunting and target shooting. Instead of punishing them, severely punish anyone who commits a crime with one of these weapons. This won't happen because the legal industry won't let us punish the guilty the way they deserve. Crime is destroying this country, yet there is always someone who wants the guilty to get off lightly, and so society reacts with laws that punish us all. There are fighter plane races that use planes that were designed to kill. Do you want to ban that too? GLENN MAZZEI New London, Conn., March 22, 1995

1190.text: GUN-423 PRIMARY AROUND THE NATION; Vermont Town's Voters Back Gun-Owner Law A local ordinance encouraging residents to own guns was upheld by voters today, ending a three-month controversy that had divided this rural Vermont community. ''The first thing that Communists do is to make guns unlawful under penalty of death,'' a retired worker, Alfred Smith, warned his neighbors before the 201-to-90 vote supporting the ordinance. '' If anyone doesn't see the need for this ordinance then their sight isn't very good.'' Opponents had hoped their campaign would bring out enough of the town's 2,250 voters to defeat the ordinance at the special election. The ordinance was approved by the town's three selectmen in July. It begins as an affirmation of the constitutional right to bear arms, but also declares that every citizen ''has the responsiblity and duty to self, family and country to possess firearms.''

1188.text: GUN-8559 PRIMARY Gun-Control Groups Say Schundler Distorts McGreevey Stand New Jersey's leading gun-control advocates today denounced Bret D. Schundler, the Republican candidate for governor, for "turning the truth on its head" by asserting that his Democratic opponent, James E. McGreevey, had once supported the right to carry concealed assault weapons. In a news release last Wednesday, Mr. Schundler's campaign manager used a 26-word sentence fragment from a 1990 law to suggest that Mr. McGreevey had voted to allow individuals to carry concealed assault firearms and machine guns. In fact, the bill outlined the state's landmark assault weapons ban, which Mr. McGreevey voted for as a member of the Assembly. The Schundler campaign neglected to cite the provisions in the bill that make it all but impossible for anyone to obtain a license to own such a weapon, let alone to carry one concealed. Today's news conference was the first foray by supporters of gun control, a potent political force in past elections, into the New Jersey governor's race. "No citizen or voter of New Jersey is well served when a candidate distorts the facts, manipulates an issue or misrepresents the record or viewpoint of his opponent," said Debra Wachspress, president of the New Jersey affiliate of the Million Mom March organization. "We think Mr. Schundler owes it to the public to set the record straight and acknowledge that he twisted the record." Bryan Miller, executive director of Ceasefire New Jersey, a coalition of gun-control supporters in the state, called Mr. Schundler's news release shameful and said it was ironic that it had accused Mr. McGreevey of hypocrisy.

1195.text: GUN-20236 PRIMARY Fightin' words Wayne LaPierre, executive vice president of the National Rifle Association, said Sunday that he believes President Clinton is "willing to accept a certain level of killing to further his political agenda." Yet if any agenda requires a tolerance for killing, it's the one that calls for unregulated gun ownership. That's the NRA's position, not Clinton's. Still, LaPierre is welcome to his opinion and welcome to express it. The gun debate suffers from beside-the-point disputes over split-the-difference initiatives based on anecdotal atrocities. Does it really matter whether Congress imposes a 24-hour or a 72-hour waiting period on firearm sales at gun shows on the first anniversary of the Columbine massacre? If LaPierre's desperate rhetoric can reinvigorate the debate, let him rock. By Monday, an angry administration was lashing back. Vice President Gore demanded an apology, saying the remarks revealed "a kind of sickness at the very heart" of the NRA. Truth to tell, Clinton hasn't been entirely fair, either. He has accused the NRA of having a "knee-jerk reaction to any gun-safety measure." But the gun lobby actively promotes firearm safety. It has helped develop effective programs to deter criminal firearm use in cities such as Richmond, Va. And many NRA members disagree with LaPierre's crude tactics. At the same time, Clinton has been an inconstant champion of gun laws. The best solution to gun violence that also ensures gun privileges is licensing and registration, as is done for cars and drivers. Clinton has instead triangulated his way to modest proposals.

1192.text: GUN-5644 PRIMARY SPEAKERS ARGUE AGAINST PROPOSAL FOR GUN CONTROL OPPONENTS AT FORUM CALL PLAN 'BAD POLICY' In front of a standing-room-only crowd Wednesday, a series of speakers denounced a proposal by a committee of the Santa Clara County Violence Prevention Council to license and register firearms as a useless method for reducing violence. "These are the newest nibbles at the right to protect ourselves," said one North San Jose man who was among the more than 100 people at the meeting at Sunnyvale School District headquarters. The forum was the last of four being sponsored by the violence prevention council. The council was appointed in June 1997 by the county board of supervisors to create a program with the Public Health Department to develop strategies to combat violence and raise the public's awareness about it. The board is scheduled to act on the council's proposals Oct. 27. The draft recommendations of one of the council's committees, which has reviewed the issue of firearms and violence, were sharply criticized Wednesday by the audience, some of them National Rifle Association members. Among the committee's proposals: * Support state and federal legislation to require firearms to be registered and to make owners take a safety course to be licensed. * Make carrying an illegally concealed weapon a felony. * Enact a new sales tax on firearms and related items, such as ammunition, and use the revenue to fund community awareness and educational activities. "It's a bad policy and bad politics," said audience member Bruce Brady, who spoke out against limiting the ability of "law-abiding citizens" to obtain guns.

1194.text: GUN-3982 PRIMARY Md. Sending Troopers to Support P.G. Police; At School, Talk of Reinforcements in Fight Against Gang Crime, Efforts to Control Guns The State of Maryland will provide 20 state troopers to help support Prince George's County police battling a surge of violent, gang-related crime in the Suitland area, Gov. Parris N. Glendening announced yesterday. In addition, the governor said, Maryland will double the size of a statewide unit created last year to seize illegal firearms and coordinate other gun interdiction efforts. The governor made the announcements yesterday at Shadyside Elementary School, in Suitland, during a news conference attended by top political and law enforcement officials from Prince George's and the state. Glendening (D) said he chose an elementary school because "more than anything else, it is our children who are suffering from gun violence." Glendening used the news conference to draw attention to a broad-ranging gun law taking effect today in Maryland. The law, among other provisions, limits most handgun purchases to one a month. "Gun violence is robbing all of us of our future . . . robbing our children of their future," Glendening said. "It is our responsibility to en sure that once they're in school, violence does not stalk their hallways." County Executive Wayne K. Curry (D), who recently was critical of Glendening for allowing a drop in state dollars for county school construction, said he was pleased by the governor's announcements yesterday. He said it's important "to put resources behind the efforts to reduce crime in Prince George's County."

1196.text: GUN-6402 PRIMARY ALAMEDA COUNTY TAKES NEW TACK ON GUN SHOWS In a move to ban gun shows from the Pleasanton fairgrounds, the Alameda County Board of Supervisors approved an ordinance Tuesday that would bar gun possession on county property. The ordinance is tailored to skirt the constitutional issues that killed an outright ban on gun shows at the Santa Clara County Fairgrounds. Supervisor Mary King, who pushed for the ordinance despite objections from gun-control opponents, said the shows send the wrong message to residents about the county's position on guns, which she noted were used to kill nearly 900 county residents from 1990 to 1995. Supervisors approved Tuesday's first reading of the ordinance on a 4-1 vote, with Scott Haggerty dissenting. They'll consider final approval Aug. 17. The ordinance follows the board's 1997 decision to ban inexpensive handguns and require trigger locks on firearms.

1193.text: GUN-20104 PRIMARY NRA's influence is out of proportion USA TODAY's editorial debate on gun laws shows the hypocrisy that the National Rifle Association has exhibited for many years ("D.C. sniper case highlights NRA's duplicity on guns," Our View, Firearms regulations debate, Tuesday). The debate includes a fact box showing that the NRA has 2.8 million members. The total population of the United States is about 285 million. Why then do such a small number of people have so much power to negatively affect our daily lives with their selfishness? It seems to me that there are many groups in this country that want more gun control and that number far more than 2.8 million. How about these: * Relatives of police officers killed by a gun in the line of duty. * Teachers who work in a school where a gun has been found in a locker or confiscated from a student. * Volunteer youth coaches who have had a child in their local sports league injured or killed by a gun. * Military veterans wounded in battle who, after many years, still suffer the effects of being shot. * Emergency room nurses and doctors who have treated a child who has been shot. * Morticians and cemetery workers who have witnessed firsthand the impact of gun violence on the surviving family members. The problem is that many of these people don't have a formal and powerful political organization like the NRA.

1198.text: GUN-3709 PRIMARY White House Shows Clinton Campaign Ads; TV Messages Promote Assault Weapons Ban The White House yesterday previewed three campaign ads -- a year before the general election contest is in full swing -- designed to show that President Clinton is tough on crime. The television ads promoting the assault weapons ban will cost $ 2.6 million and will be carried in 18 to 20 markets over the next few weeks. The campaign runs against the conventional wisdom of presidential political advertising: Americans pay little attention to political advertising in the summer; fighting a problem you don't have offers the potential of creating the problem; and turning a president into a candidate with campaign ads this early politicizes the presidency, making it harder to govern. Ignoring all that, White House press secretary Michael McCurry showed the 30-second ads to reporters at the White House yesterday and said "the spots were prepared not as direct campaign spots" but to protect the assault weapons ban from attacks by congressional Republicans and the National Rifle Association. McCurry cited a pledge made to the NRA by Senate Majority Leader Robert J. Dole (R-Kan.) that he would try to repeal the assault weapons ban this summer. But Dole has not put the ban on the legislative schedule, and the ads make no effort to get viewers to lobby Congress, the way ads aimed at legislative outcomes do. Instead, the ads, two of which feature police officers as narrators, speak about crimes committed with assault weapons and portray the president as fighting to keep the weapons off the street and to put more police on the street. All three ads note that Clinton supports an expanded death penalty.

characters: Senate Majority Leader Robert J. Dole

1197.text: GUN-570 PRIMARY A courageous gun-control stand WASHINGTON - Twenty-one years ago, when Congress was considering the    first federal gun-control legislation in more than 20 years in the    immediate wake of Sen. Robert F. Kennedy's assassination, then-Sen. Eugene McCarthy, a Kennedy foe for the 1968 Democratic presidential nomination, was asked for his view. McCarthy said he didn't believe legislation should be considered "under panic conditions" in the heat    of that emotional moment. Congress passed it anyway. McCarthy's comment came to mind the other day when former Reagan    White House press secretary James Brady testified before a Senate    Judiciary subcommittee in support of the so-called Brady bill requiring    a seven-day waiting period on the purchase of any handgun, so that the    prospective purchaser could be checked out by law-enforcement    officials. Brady's appearance, the first before Congress since he was shot in    the head and partially paralyzed in John Hinckley's 1981 assassination    attempt on President Ronald Reagan, was not only emotional but    unsparingly hard-hitting. He called members of Congress "cowardly    lions" for failing to stand up to the anti-gun control lobbying of the    National Rifle Association and suggested that those who oppose the    seven-day waiting period to "try being in my wheels for just one    day." Brady, speaking from a wheelchair with clear articulation that    measured his remarkable progress in an eight-year recovery, scolded    Congress. "Are you willing and ready to cast a vote for a common-sense    public safety issue endorsed by (law enforcement) experts," he asked,    "or are you going to pander to the special interests that whine about    a little inconvenience and other such lamebrain foolishness?"

1202.text: GUN-7197 PRIMARY CITY SHERIFF SAYS HE'LL ACCEPT FREE GUN LOCKS HARMON REJECTED; MAYOR SAYS HE REJECTED OFFER BECAUSE OF PENDING LITIGATION; 5,000 DEVICES WILL BE ISSUED St. Louis Sheriff Jim Murphy will accept the 5,000 free safety locks from the gun manufacturing industry that Mayor Clarence Harmon turned down. "If you can just save one child's life by having a gun lock in their home, it's well worth it," Murphy said Thursday at a news conference with Aldermanic President Francis Slay. Murphy, whose office issues new gun ownership permits in the city, said he plans to hand out the locks to future applicants once the devices are supplied by the gun group later this spring. He pointed out that a city ordinance requires applicants for permits to show proof they've bought a lock themselves. Harmon said Wednesday that he had rejected an offer in January from the gun group, the National Shooting Sports Foundation. The mayor said he believed doing so could hurt the city's lawsuit against the industry. On Thursday, the mayor said he had no objections to the sheriff's office distributing the locks because Murphy is an independent elected official and not a plaintiff in the city's case. In fact, he said "it's a great idea" for someone else to distribute the free locks. "When you're in litigation with an industry, you don't just jump out and accept the first offer they make to you," Harmon said, referring to his administration. Slay said he supports Harmon's lawsuit against the industry but that the mayor had made a mistake in turning down the January offer.

1204.text: GUN-431 PRIMARY CONTROLS ON GUNS SUPPORTED IN POLL A majority of Americans favor stricter controls on pistol sales but stop short of calling for an outright ban, according to a Gallup Poll released yesterday. The researchers reported that 59 percent favored stricter laws on the sales. Only 44 percent, however, favored a ban on the sale and posession of pistols, while 48 percent opposed such a ban. In Gallup surveys dating to 1975, a majority have always favored stricter controls. The results over the years have been uneven, however. In 1975 the survey found 69 percent favoring stricter controls; in 1980 it was 59 percent and in 1982 it was 65 percent. Few Want to Ease Laws A very small minority of those questioned has favored less strict controls. This year that figure was 4 percent. Thirty-one percent said the laws should be kept as is, while 6 percent offered no opinion. The 4 percent who felt that gun control laws should be less strict said that people needed guns to protect themselves and their property. They said that there was a Constitutional right to bear arms and that controls were not effective. Some said they wanted to keep their guns because they were hunters. President Reagan has consistently said that pistol controls have not been effective. After John W. Hinckley Jr. wounded him in a 1981 assassination attempt with a pistol, Mr. Reagan said the laws had been no help in deterring the attack.

1201.text: GUN-2410 PRIMARY License and Restrict Handguns Handgun Control Inc. -- the Brady bunch -- puts out an annual poster that makes the case for controlling handguns in one picture and about 33 words. It shows a revolver painted with the stars and stripes of the American flag. The caption reads: In 1990, HANDGUNS KILLED 22 PEOPLE IN GREAT BRITAIN 13 IN SWEDEN 91 IN SWITZERLAND 87 IN JAPAN 10 IN AUSTRALIA 68 IN CANADA AND 10,567 IN THE UNITED STATES. GOD BLESS AMERICA. But despite the recommendations of several presidential commissions for the strict licensing of handguns, the number of handguns and other repeating weapons in the country has risen from an estimated 25 million in 1968 to an estimated 66 million today -- a 150 percent increase over 25 years. We now have one handgun for every two American males, little boys included. And we manufacture or import an additional 2.5 million per year. The Brady law will not do much to stem this rising tide. But its passage -- supported by 90 percent of Americans in recent polls -- shows that handgun control measures can be passed despite the lobbying power of the National Rifle Association. Here is a list of desirable additional measures we should tackle next, listed in rough order of political feasibility and real-world effectiveness: (1) Finance the necessary research to perfect a radioactive or chemical tracer that can be placed in all handguns or in all handgun ammunition, so that the police, using Geiger counters and similar devices, can readily detect the presence of a loaded and concealed handgun on the street or in a vehicle. Carrying concealed weapons is already illegal almost everywhere. Even the NRA favors such laws. The catch is how to detect a concealed weapon. Our military-industrial complex builds satellites capable of reading the headline of a newspaper lying on a Moscow street as well as smart missiles that can hit a Baghdad bridge from launch sites hundreds of miles away. Our engineers are almost certainly capable of finding effective ways to detect a concealed firearm on the street or in a vehicle. But we have never devoted the time or the money to try.

1199.text: GUN-898 PRIMARY EROSION OF CONSTITUTIONAL RIGHTS In response to a Jan. 3 letter writer's fear of assault from concealed handguns, a long coat can easily conceal a high-powered rifle, which requires no purchase permit or waiting period to obtain. So why single out handguns for derision? The National Rifle Association may have spent $4 million in an attempt to defeat a total handgun ban in Maryland, but what was the total amount spent by the state to assure passage? The citizens of Maryland were the real losers. They abrogated their Second Amendment rights. The tilt toward totalitarianism was a great loss. State-sponsored advertising for gun opponents and the use of police-bully tactics against gun proponents signal an alarming disrespect for constitutional democracy and an inclination by those in power to use the police to promote their political agenda. The campaign waged by gun opponents bore the weight of duplicity and desperation.   If the aim of the legislation were to curtail access to Saturday Night Specials, why isn't language describing such weapons contained in the legislation? Why wouldn't the local media print the referendum in whole instead of an edited version? Why did Maryland's governor, attorney general, superintendent of state police, the Baltimore Archdiocese and the media use all the power at their disposal to ban handguns in the state? To stop the use of handguns in sui cides, accidents and family quarrels? To stop criminals from obtaining handguns?   A ban on handguns will not stop the violent resolution of social problems nor will it deter criminals from obtaining weapons. It does restrict the law-abiding citizens' access to sporting weapons for use in defending their lives and personal property.   Kevin Lacostelo St. Louis

1206.text: GUN-11131 PRIMARY The Law of the Gun in Florida Florida leads the pack in passing bills written by the gun lobby that block any sensible attempt to control the purchase and use of firearms. The dangerous folly of these laws was on display in the Trayvon Martin shooting, and will again be on display when Republicans gather for their presidential convention in Tampa this August. The City Council is sensibly preparing tight security precautions for the downtown area by temporarily banning clubs, hatchets, switchblades, pepper spray, slingshots, chains, shovels and all manner of guns that shoot water, paint or air. But not handguns that shoot actual bullets. In other words, someone outside the convention hall will be entitled to pack a handgun, but not a squirt gun. This down-the-rabbit-hole situation is the result of a law passed last year by state legislators at the behest of the gun lobby. It ended longstanding local gun controls, prohibiting city governments from enacting any ordinances on the sale, possession or use of firearms. Tampa officials wanted to ban handguns outside the convention hall (the Secret Service has undisputed power to ban weapons inside the hall) but came up against the state law, which imposes $100,000 fines on local governments that try to meet such obvious public-safety needs. This lethal parody of gun control should be repealed, like the notorious Stand Your Ground law. But voters cannot expect common sense from the Republican-controlled Legislature, which is on a leash held by the gun lobby.

1200.text: GUN-30580 PRIMARY D.A. candidates on gun violence in unique forum; The debate included three journalists and a panel of victims' relatives. Striving to sound smart - and hard-nosed - about the city's most pressing crime problem, the six candidates for Philadelphia district attorney offered strategies to combat gun violence at a unique forum hosted by CeaseFire PA, Pennsylvania's largest gun-control group. In a city where some neighborhoods are awash in illegal handguns, crimes with guns are the ultimate challenge to law and order, the candidates agreed last night at the Community College of Philadelphia. But the five Democrats - Seth Williams, Michael Turner, Dan McCaffery, Brian Grady, and Dan McElhatton - and the lone Republican, Michael Untermeyer, also differed in substantive ways. Turner said past efforts to defeat gun violence had failed because proponents of gun controls used the "failed paradigm" that pits public safety against the Second Amendment's right to keep and bear arms. State law prohibits cities from enacting their gun laws. Philadelphia, where more than 80 percent of homicide victims are African American or Latino, has had to conform to the political will of mostly rural Pennsylvania, where hunting is popular and most of the lawmakers in Harrisburg are white. Turner said the next district attorney needed to take a "civil-rights" approach to the argument that Philadelphia should be able "to enact its own gun laws. "What made the event unusual as political theater was its format. In addition to three journalists asking questions, there was a panel of four people whose lives were forever altered by gun violence, including Larry McDonald, father of slain Philadelphia Police Sgt.

1203.text: GUN-9347 PRIMARY EDITORIAL Guns: One hit, one miss The city of Denver misfired in the legal challenge it filed Friday  against the state's new law establishing uniform standards for  issuing concealed weapon permits. But it was squarely on target  with the second part of its suit, aimed at preserving gun  ordinances with purely local effect. During the 12-year tenure of Mayor Wellington Webb, Denver has  automatically denied virtually all requests for concealed carry  permits - even those from victims of violent crimes. When The Post  checked last summer, the city had issued just six permits - all to  employees of the city and county, five to city building inspectors  and one to a county judge. Thus, in a city of more than half a  million people, not one ordinary citizen received a concealed-carry permit. Denver defends its policy as a valid exercise of its rights as a  home-rule city. But the right to keep and bear arms is guaranteed  by both the state and federal constitutions, with the state  declaring: 'The right of no person to keep and bear arms in  defense of his home, person and property, or in aid of the civil  power when thereto legally summoned, shall be called in question; but nothing herein contained shall be construed to justify the  practice of carrying concealed weapons.' That language clearly allows for regulation of concealed weapons,  however. The real question is whether that regulation should be a  uniform statewide standard or the crazy quilt of local rules that  existed prior to passage of Senate Bill 24, sponsored by Sen. Ken  Chlouber, R-Leadville.

1205.text: GUN-1014 PRIMARY NRA vows 'aggressive' fight on bill The National Rifle Association vows an intensified fight against a seven- day waiting period for handgun purchases as the battle shifts to the Senate. Stung by Wednesday's 239-186 House vote for the Brady bill, the gun lobby plans to refine its message and mobilize its 2.6 million members. ''I'm not going to let them stick us with a black-hat image,'' NRA executive vice president Wayne LaPierre said Thursday. He pledged to ''take our case to the American public as aggressively as possible.'' Handgun control advocates acknowledge the Senate skirmish will be more difficult: - Rural states have equal clout in the Senate. In the House, more than half the votes for the Brady bill came from just seven states - California, New York, Illinois, New Jersey, Michigan, Massachusetts and Ohio. - Senate rules allow more chances to stall legislation. Senate Judiciary Committee chairman Joseph Biden, D-Del., Thursday predicted the waiting period could pass only as part of a broader anti-crime bill sought by President Bush. With a Bush veto threat, Biden said ''chances are zero'' the Brady bill can pass alone. In the House, 41 members switched sides since 1988, when the bill was defeated 228-182. LaPierre promised the NRA ''will be active, very active, in the (1992) elections.''

1208.text: GUN-4631 PRIMARY Virginia to Issue Weapon Permits Without FBI Check Virginia judges will have to issue concealed-weapon permits without knowing whether applicants have criminal records, because the FBI has told police departments that they no longer can use its system for making instant background checks. Instead of running applicants' names through an FBI computer databank, police now must rely on an FBI check of their fingerprints, according to a state police directive issued in late February. It takes at least 60 days to get the results of a background check under that system, police said. And that has put them in a bind, because under state law judges must grant or deny concealed-weapon permits within 45 days. Felons are barred from carrying guns. Police can still check Virginia criminal records instantly. But an applicant "could be a known felon in California and that wouldn't show up," said Charles Bailey, head of the Alexandria police section that handles the checks. "It's kind of scary." Several other local law enforcement officials agreed that the time lag is a problem, even though a weapon permit can be revoked after it has been issued. "You have some borderline people who get [gun permits]. It's an accident waiting to happen," said Fairfax Commonwealth's Attorney Robert F. Horan Jr. "If it becomes compounded by not doing an FBI check . . . that makes an accident more likely." An FBI spokeswoman said yesterday that she did not have enough information to comment on the bureau's policy and that other FBI officials were unavailable.

1207.text: GUN-30907 PRIMARY Now Alabama In spraying bullets from military-style, semiautomatic assault rifles, a deranged 28-year-old gunman in Alabama this week killed 10 people and wounded 6 others before fatally shooting himself. In a sane world, this bloody rampage -- the latest in a string of mass homicides, including the gun massacres at Columbine High School in 1999 and Virginia Tech in 2007 -- would at last persuade Congress to reinstate, in tightened form, the national assault weapons ban that it let expire in 2004. But this is not a sane world, at least not on Capitol Hill, where the National Rifle Association still wields far too much power for the country's good. In February, the Senate approved an amendment that would abolish not only an assault-weapons ban, but other gun control laws already on the books in the District of Columbia -- including reasonable rules on gun registration and trigger locks. This dangerous provision was attached to an overdue bill that would give the District of Columbia a voting member in the House of Representatives. The N.R.A. has now moved its formidable lobbying campaign to the House. Steny Hoyer, the Democratic majority leader, said last week that he did not have the votes to bring the District of Columbia bill to the floor without opening the possibility that the weapons amendment would be added. That was before the murders in Alabama. At this sad point, surely, Mr. Hoyer and House Speaker Nancy Pelosi ought to be able to muster enough votes to stand up to the gun lobby and its threat to the safety and democratic rights of the citizens of the nation's capital. Surely they can provide that leadership.

1209.text: GUN-10028 PRIMARY CITY FIGHTS HUGE FLOW OF ARMS NEW YORK has some of the toughest gun laws in the nation - but weak federal legislation often blows a hole in the local safeguards, top city officials and gun-control advocates said yesterday. Two days after a thug allegedly killed NYPD Officer Dillon Stewart with a stolen handgun, an emotional Mayor Bloomberg called on federal lawmakers to "stop this craziness" because guns are flooding city streets faster than cops can seize them. Police Commissioner Raymond Kelly said many of the illegal guns are coming from Georgia, Florida, Virginia, West Virginia and Ohio. "You can jump on a bus, buy five guns for $1,000 and sell them for $8,000 to $10,000," Kelly said. He said 5% more illegal guns have been taken off the streets this year when compared with the same period in 2004. In a bid to seize even more guns, Kelly told the Daily News Editorial Board he will bolster the NYPD's Operation Impact initiative next month. The strategy floods high-crime areas with cops, many of them rookies. "When we had our last graduating class, I had to cut down on the size of Operation Impact because the department has thousands fewer cops than it did in 2000," Kelly said. "After the next class in January, Impact will be more robust with 1,100 to 1,200 cops, up from about 600." Gun-control experts say there's little reason to believe Congress will help the NYPD by putting teeth into federal firearm laws because of the nation's powerful gun lobby.

1210.text: GUN-9039 PRIMARY Web Winners: For ammo in the debate over guns, hit these sites Our gun-totin' country can always do with a little more education on the divisive subject of firearms. Here are some places to start. Gun laws. Several law schools collaborate on producing this site that watches for court cases, news stories, and lobbying efforts by both sides in the gun debate. Handy links will get you the latest news releases from the National Rifle Association and Handgun Control Inc. http://jurist.law.pitt.edu/gunlaw.htm Gun talk. "Straight-shooting talk show host" Tom Gresham does a weekly call-in program about guns. Woo-hoo. Click on one of the bullets on the screen for more info. www.guntalk.com Pax. This site promotes campaigns urging parents to ask neighbors if there is a gun where their children play, and encouraging children to speak up if they hear of a gun or threat of violence in school. www.paxusa.org Human target. "Many people are predisposed to fall down when shot," and other fatal factoids are examined coldly on this document attributed to the Federal Bureau of Investigation. www.firearmstactical.com/hwfe.htm

1212.text: GUN-12074 PRIMARY Senior GOP congresswoman: 'We have to be careful' about new gun laws The most senior Republican woman in Congress said that lawmakers can hold hearings and investigate the mass shooting at an elementary school in Connecticut and "try to better understand exactly what happened," but she would not say whether Congress should review the nation's gun laws. "We need to find out what happened and what drove this individual to this place," said Rep. Cathy McMorris Rodgers (R-Wash.), the incoming chairman of the House Republican Conference. "I think we have to be careful about new, suggesting new gun laws. We need to look at what drives a crazy person to do these kind of actions and make sure that we're enforcing the laws that are currently on the books. And yes, definitely, we need to do everything possible to make sure that something like this never happens again." Read the whole story on 2chambers.

1211.text: GUN-10612 PRIMARY Should Georgia allow concealed weapons at public gatherings? Yes Restore gun owner rights and make state law consistent. The time is now for Georgia's residents to be given back their rights as protected by the Second Amendment and the Constitution of Georgia. Georgians have, since our founding, been allowed under state law to possess and carry firearms. In recent times, more and more restrictions have been placed upon our lawful residents for many different and various reasons. Well-intended legislators, responding to single incidents, attempted to fix problems, real and perceived, with a patchwork of conflicting laws and confusion. My proposed House Bill 615 is an attempt to clarify and simplify Georgia law on the issue of individual gun rights, and allow a Georgian to finally go to one code section to see clearly where it is illegal to carry a weapon in this state. My legislation will clarify that lawful adults may possess legal firearms all places where the property owners allow. The Second Amendment and the Georgia Constitution protect our rights against government restrictions, not individual wishes. Opponents emotionally exclaim that if you allow lawful residents to possess firearms on public transportation and in establishments where alcohol is served, there will be mass carnage. HB 89, passed in 2008, allows just that and no mass carnage has taken place. Lawful residents are just that, lawful. We cannot, and will not, continue to strip the rights of law-abiding people because of the actions of criminals.

1213.text: GUN-4210 PRIMARY Clinton Defends His Criticism of a New York Judge's Ruling President Clinton today vigorously asserted his right to criticize the ruling of a Federal judge, saying the independence of the judiciary did not mean that a judge "should be entitled not only to lifetime tenure but a gag rule on everyone else." Speaking a day after Judge Harold Baer Jr. of Federal District Court in Manhattan reversed a widely criticized decision suppressing evidence in a Washington Heights drug case, Mr. Clinton also used the debate over Judge Baer's ruling to go on the offensive against Republicans on the issue of crime. "I think it's obvious what's going on here," Mr. Clinton said at a joint news conference with Italy's President, Oscar Scalfaro, where he was asked about Judge Baer, whom he appointed in 1994. "The people on the other side are sort of embarrassed about their crime record." Mr. Clinton cited legislative struggles over the Brady bill, which created a waiting period for handgun purchases, and over a Federal anti-crime package that passed narrowly in 1994, which included financing for 100,000 new police officers and banned 19 semiautomatic weapons and high-capacity ammunition clips. Last month, the House of Representatives voted to repeal the ban. "They fought the crime bill," he said of the Republicans. "They fought the 100,000 police. They fought the Brady bill. They fought the assault weapons ban." Now, he said, "They think they can sort of get well by making some outrageous claims about the judges I have appointed."

1214.text: GUN-9389 PRIMARY GUN-BUYING BILL GETS SECOND BREATH; SENATE SPONSORS REVIVE MEASURE TO LIMIT HANDGUN PURCHASES TO ONE MONTH; NRA OPPOSES LEGISLATION A bill to limit people to buying one handgun a month, which failed once before, headed to the Senate floor Wednesday after passing in a different committee. The Health and Human Services Committee approved the measure on a partisan 7-4 vote, two months after two Southern Illinois Democrats helped defeat the same bill in the Judiciary Committee. The proposal, amended to a House bill, attempts to stop so-called "straw" purchases - people legally buying firearms in the suburbs and reselling them to criminals in cities that ban handguns, such as Chicago. "You can make sure it's against the law to take a gun and illegally transfer it to someone who's not entitled to have it," said the bill's sponsor, Sen. John Cullerton, D-Chicago. "But if you limit the ability to make the straw purchase in the first place, it will be more effective." Cullerton said the measure would not affect hunters' shotguns and rifles and would still allow two adults in a household to buy 24 handguns a year. Those who want more guns, or need to replace a lost or stolen gun, could get permission from the Illinois State Police. Opponents said the bill would violate the Second Amendment right to bear arms. "This is a rationing of a constitutional right," said the National Rifle Association's Todd Vandermyde. "The problem is not the 1.5 million law-abiding gun owners in this state who have to get a FOID (firearm registration) card, who have to go through the background checks just to be able to purchase or possess firearms or ammunition," Vandermyde said. "The problem is a very small minority of recidivists."

1217.text: NA-41697 PRIMARY Inquirer Editorial: Inquirer Editorial: Zimmerman's acquittal shouldn't lead to riches It would be a shame if the unnecessary death of 17-year-old Trayvon Martin produced more paydays for the man who fatally shot the unarmed black teenager in the heart during a scuffle. But George Zimmerman, the Sanford, Fla., neighborhood watch captain who has already received hundreds of thousands of dollars from gun-rights advocates and other supporters, is likely to be offered millions through lucrative book and movie deals. Zimmerman, 29, was acquitted of second-degree murder and manslaughter charges late Saturday night by a sequestered jury that had deliberated about 16 hours over two days. The five-week-trial had included more than 50 witnesses and 60 pieces of evidence. That a verdict was returned at night almost seemed by design to reduce the possibility of sparking protests that might turn violent. But heated arguments stemming from Martin's death at the hands of the white Hispanic defendant 17 months ago will likely continue for a long time. People remain bitterly divided not only by race, but their attitudes toward Florida's controversial Stand Your Ground law. The eight-year-old law, which has been mimicked in more than 20 other states, is an abomination. It extends the so-called Castle Doctrine - which gives a person the legal right to use deadly force in his home - to anywhere, anytime. Zimmerman's lawyers contended that Florida's law, which does not require a person to use deadly force only as a last resort, gave their client the right to shoot Martin. They presented testimony suggesting that Zimmerman, though he outweighed Martin, was unable to protect himself otherwise in their fight.

1216.text: NA-45994 PRIMARY A sad legacy: Newtown changed my life Maybe it's because I grew up in New York, a city that seemed to have one tragic headline after another, day after day, in the local tabloids. For decades, the magnitude of gun violence throughout the country didn't register. I would quickly turn the newspaper page to move past a story about a shooting or hit the remote to switch to another channel when fatal bullets were fired. Now, though, every shooting -- no matter where it happens in America -- registers immediately. Even talk or legislation about gun control and gun rights -- such as the law signed Wednesday by Georgia Gov. Nathan Deal allowing licensed guns in bars, schools, churches and some government buildings -- instantly captures my attention. And it makes me wonder when the bloodshed will cease. My mind-set changed on the morning of Dec. 14, 2012. Twenty-seven people, including 20 young children, lay dead from a single shooter, who then fired a gun to kill himself at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, where I live. A friend from Vancouver, British Columbia, called and asked, "What's going on in your country?" He wanted to know why so many Americans were dying in a hail of bullets, and I didn't have an answer. I could only tell him I rushed that December morning to the horrific, chaotic scene, a place I knew best from coaching youth basketball in the school gymnasium and youth baseball on the field outside.

1219.text: GUN-9739 PRIMARY Clock Ticks On Extension Of Gun Ban During his campaign for the presidency in 2000, George W. Bush took a dramatic step: a vow to support the ban on assault weapons, enacted in 1994 for a 10-year period. ''It makes no sense for assault weapons to be around our society,'' Mr. Bush declared. Since then, the president's advisers have repeatedly affirmed his support for the ban, a position that has helped him appeal to moderate voters and demonstrate independence from the powerful gun lobby. But in the middle of a presidential election year, and with only five working days left in Congress before the law expires at midnight on Sept. 13, the issue has become a hot potato that no one in the Bush administration seems eager to touch. Mr. Bush has said he would sign legislation extending the ban if the Republican-controlled Congress passed it, and White House officials say he stands by that pledge. But Congress has already begun a six-week recess, and Republicans there say they have yet to hear him declare that he actually wants the legislation to move. All the while, the clock keeps ticking, leaving legislators of both parties predicting that the ban, which for a decade has barred production and sale of 19 kinds of assault weapons, will lapse. The lawmakers do not return to work until Sept. 7, leaving too little time, many feel, for legislation extending the measure to pass both houses of Congress.

1222.text: GUN-9797 PRIMARY A promise to keep There won't be a million moms from the Million Mom March on the National Mall today, but there will be enough to make their point: Congress should renew the ban on assault weapons, due to expire Sept. 13. As usual, we are proud to say, a delegation of Durham moms will be on the Mall with thousands of others from across the United States. The 1994 Federal Assault Weapons Act was a landmark event for gun control in this country. The law bans the manufacture of semi-automatic rifles, handguns and shotguns with features common to military-style assault weapons. President Bush said during the 2000 presidential campaign that he would sign a renewal of the ban, which is credited with substantially reducing the criminal use of Uzis, AK-47s and other assault-style weapons. The president should follow through on his pledge as the Senate prepares to vote on renewal of the ban. On this Mother's Day, President Bush and Congress are getting a vocal reminder that a promise made is a promise kept.

1215.text: GUN-7251 PRIMARY Bill on gun buys faces new battle Senator wants measure toughened up Allowing local police and prosecutors to go after people who  buy guns for crooks and kids once seemed like a 'no-brainer.' It  looked like something gun-control and gun-rights advocates at the  Legislature could both support. But a fight is brewing over how tightly this law enforcement  web should be woven. The legislation will be back before the  Senate Judiciary Committee on Monday. The committee endorsed it earlier this month, but now committee  chairwoman Sen. Dottie Wham, R-Denver, would like to see the bill  strengthened, and she's counting on a fight from the gun lobby. 'It didn't do anything the way we passed it,' Wham said. Such 'straw purchases' of guns are already illegal under  federal law. Supporters of the bill say there aren't enough  federal agents in Colorado to prosecute every case, so they want  to bring in local authorities. But the Colorado District Attorneys Council says that the way  the legislation is drafted, it would be too difficult to prosecute  someone for giving a gun to a criminal or a child. The bill says prosecutors would have to prove that the defendant  knew the person shouldn't have a gun. That gets into proving what someone is thinking, prosecutors  say. They say they should have to prove only that the defendant  'knew or should have known.' One legislator who felt the bill was too weak complained,  'This is not a straw-purchase bill. It's a straw bill.'

1218.text: GUN-429 PRIMARY CUOMO VETOES RESTRICTION ON LOCAL GUN LAWS A bill that would have curbed New York City's power to regulate handguns was vetoed yesterday by Governor Cuomo.   ''I want to discourage the profligate use of weapons,'' Mr. Cuomo said at a news conference at the World Trade Center. '' The fewer weapons the better.'' The gun-control bill, sponsored by Assemblyman Robin L.  Schimminger, a Buffalo Democrat, and Senator Jay P. Rolison Jr., Republican of Poughkeepsie, attracted little attention as it moved through the Legislature. The Assembly approved it May 11, and the Senate on June 20. Under the legislation, municipalities would have been prevented from issuing regulations more restrictive than those set forth in the state's penal code. New York City's Police Commissioner, Robert J.  McGuire, had said after the measure's passage that it could block regulations governing the carrying of guns on city streets and limit his department's ability to screen applicants for gun permits. The Assembly and the Senate have recessed, and the upper house is scheduled to return in July to take up a limited agenda.   At the Governor's news conference, he also took these actions: - Joined Attorney General Robert Abrams in announcing a lawsuit to make the Federal Government provide $19 million to clean the Hudson River of polychlorinated biphenyls, or PCB's. Mr. Abrams said the state had been subjected to ''bureacratic flimflam'' by Federal officials. - Disclosed that $12.1 million in state and Federal funds would be spent to supplement the diets of ''nutritionally at-risk low-income women, infants and children.'' This would provide aid to a total of 266,714 New Yorkers, out of an estimated 500,000 who are in need, he said.

1221.text: GUN-8243 PRIMARY TUCKER DISTORTS TRUTH ON GUN LAWS BUSH BACKS Cynthia Tucker's Aug. 14 column "Gore needs to unholster gun issue" distorts the truth. As governor of Texas, George W. Bush supported concealed-carry laws, but she doesn't mention that the states experiencing the largest reductions in crime are those with newly enacted right-to-carry laws and the fastest-growing rates of gun ownership. And, yes, Dick Cheney did vote against a poorly worded bill that would have banned 85 percent of all hunting-rifle ammunition. But Ms. Tucker does not tell you that he did vote for the National Rifle Association-rewritten bill that banned the Teflon-tipped bullets that were a concern. Ms. Tucker doesn't realize that the reason why people are flocking to the GOP is they are waking up to the fear and deception tactics used by politicians and writers such as herself. Prosecuting criminals, allowing law-abiding citizens to carry concealed weapons, child gun-safety training and responsible parenting will continue to help reduce the number of firearm deaths nationwide. KEITH FAUCI Riviera Beach

1223.text: GUN-2491 PRIMARY For Bradys, brief rest then 'new beginning' Don't expect Sarah and Jim Brady to declare victory and retreat to normal life. To celebrate enactment of the Brady law, the couple will take a three-day vacation in New Orleans to listen to jazz and eat great food. Then, it's back to Washington, fighting for more restrictions on gun sales. "The signing of the bill means closure in one sense," says Sarah Brady. "But in another way, it's a new beginning. We can finally get to all the other solutions we feel must be addressed." Their life was forever changed on March 30, 1981, when mentally troubled John Hinckley tried to assassinate President Reagan. A bullet struck Jim Brady, Reagan's press secretary, in the head. Now forced to use a wheelchair, Brady Tuesday said the signing marked "the end of unchecked madness and the commencement of a heartfelt crusade for a safer and saner country." Brady, who has said he's financally dependant on his wife, also receives government benefits and support from a foundation established after the shooting by his friends. After the White House signing ceremony, the Bradys took their son, Scott, 14, to the airport to return to prep school. Sarah Brady says the next project for Handgun Control Inc. is a broad proposal for new gun control laws that will be unveiled later this month. It calls for a crackdown on black market gun sales, tightening rules on licenses for gun dealers and mandatory safety training for gun owners.

1224.text: GUN-31087 PRIMARY The Supremes Make a Mean 'To Duel' List Justice Antonin Scalia was the first to pull out the dueling words: Justice John Paul Stevens used "faulty" analysis,  Scalia wrote in the Supreme Court decision that  struck down the District's handgun ban.   Stevens composed the dissenting opinion; Scalia called his colleague's reasoning "worthy of a mad hatter," adding that Stevens "flatly misreads the historical record." Oh, it is on, my good man. It is on. Stevens replied in kind: "The Court stakes its holding on a strained and unpersuasive reading" of the Second Amendment, he wrote in his opinion, and "fundamentally fails to grasp the point" of sentence analysis. The word "feeble" was used. One envisions a kid glove being struck repeatedly about the face. Thwap, thwap, thwap. In the time before gun bans, this sort of dialogue might have devolved into a pair of single-shot pistols in a velvet-lined case. It has happened before. Some of our finest forefathers resolved their verbal altercations through gentlemanly and regulated gunplay. Missouri legislator Charles Lucas became infuriated in 1817 when Sen. Thomas Hart Benton referred to him as a "puppy." Their first duel resulted only in knee and throat wounds, which would seem sufficient for the scurrilous p-word, but the men decided to go at it again until Lucas died. True, "feeble" is no "puppy." But these escalations do happen. After all, District of Columbia v. Heller is a monumental court case that centered on  a comma, opposing parties arguing that the punctuation mark determined who could carry weapons.

1225.text: GUN-5158 PRIMARY A Hit From the NRA Last November the NRA put a hit on handgun-safety forces in Washington State that says a lot about that organization. Here's the story. Initiative 676 called for trigger locks to accompany handguns at the point of sale and for handgun owners to pass a test or complete a safety course. It was supported by the state medical and education associations, the State League of Women Voters, the Association of Churches, parent-teacher groups around the state, C. Everett Koop and the American Bar Association, among others. The organized opposition consisted almost solely of the gun lobby and the largest police union in the state. Six weeks before the election, support for Initiative 676 was over 60 percent, but on Election Day it was crushed 70 percent to 30 percent. How did this happen? Three reasons: The NRA, its ability to raise and spend millions (the gun lobby spent more than $ 3 million opposing I-676, most from out of state and well over the cost of the last winning gubernatorial campaign) and its willingness to spend that money telling lies and half-truths. In Washington State we take enormous pride in eclipsing other states with our natural beauty and in out-selling the world in software, airplanes and coffee. One category we'd just as soon ignore: firearms violence. In Washington (population, 5.5 million), we murder more of our own people with handguns than the entire countries of Canada (population 26.9 million) and Great Britain (population 57.5 million) combined. Moreover, between 1990 and 1996, 241 of our children and teenagers were killed or hospitalized as a result of accidental firearms violence. In the past two years, more people here have been killed by firearms violence than by automobile accidents.

1220.text: GUN-11491 PRIMARY DONATIONS BLAST MIKE' STANCE ALBANY - Mayor Bloomberg has been all over the airwaves calling for better gun control following the Colorado massacre - but he doesn't always put his money where his mouth is. Even as he's pushed for tougher gun laws, he's showered donations on lawmakers who drew high marks from the gun lobby over the past decade, campaign finance records show. And, his office said Thursday he'll host an Aug. 15 fund-raiser for Massachusetts Sen. Scott Brown in the Bay State despite Brown's long history of supporting gun rights. State Conservative Party Chairman Michael Long said the donations show that Bloomberg "is not a purist." "If you're really, really hell-bent on gun control, which he appears to be on the outside, then you wouldn't give money to people who deviate from your position," Long said. Hizzoner gave $2,500 each last year to Sens. Olympia Snowe (R-Maine) and Orrin Hatch (R-Utah). Both have strong pro-gun records, with Hatch having received an "A" rating from the National Rifle Association. In 2007, Bloomberg gave $4,600 to Sen. Susan Collins (R-Maine), who received a "B+" from the NRA. And he gave to former Staten Island GOP Rep. Vito Fossella, Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.) and Sen. Richard Shelby (R-Ala.) - and all had gun-industry backing. In New York, the mayor is the biggest donor to the state Senate GOP, which in recent years has blocked his push for bullet microstamping, which law enforcement officials say would help solve gun crimes.

characters: State Conservative Party Chairman Michael Long

1229.text: GUN-235 PRIMARY FOCUSING ON GUNS WON'T ALTER CRIME IN THE U.S. To the Editor: Your April 9 letter writer asserts that the Supreme Court in 1983 rejected a claim of constitutional protection for handguns. Nothing of the kind. The Court merely denied certiorari review, which means only - as the Court has repeatedly explained - that the time was not ripe for the High Court's review of such a claim. On the one hand, it is true that certain people use small handguns - so- called Saturday night specials - for improper purposes. But it is also true that other people use handguns of all kinds - successfully about 600 times a day across the country - for proper purposes of defense and protection against violent predatory attacks. A similar proper purpose exists for armor-piercing ammunition - so- called ''cop-killer'' bullets: What if a burglar breaks into your home at night wearing a bullet-resistant vest?
