Moreover, it is not rational, in attempting to curb improper use of handguns, to pursue the Congressional handgun legislation approach that the letter urges; that sort of legislation inevitably impedes law- abiding citizens much more than career criminal predators: the latter can always get as many guns as they want, the same as drugs, regardless of Federal (or state) legislation - by smuggling, hijacking and surreptitious manufacture. The basic trouble with the gun-control approach is that it proverbially throws out the baby with the bathwater. And the Goetz affair supplies a stark example, demonstrating that gun control is not criminal control or crime control. Gun control is victim control.

1228.text: GUN-3874 PRIMARY HIGH COURT KILLS LAW BANNING GUNS IN A SCHOOL ZONE The Supreme Court today dealt a stinging blow to the Federal Government's ability to move into the realm of local law enforcement, ruling in a bitterly divided 5-to-4 decision that Congress acted beyond its constitutional authority five years ago when it made possession of a gun within 1,000 feet of a school a Federal crime. The decision, with a majority opinion by Chief Justice William H. Rehnquist, was based on the Court's interpretation of the authority of Congress to regulate interstate commerce. While not overturning any precedents, the decision marked a sharp departure from the modern Supreme Court's expansive view of Congressional power to regulate commerce. Justice Stephen G. Breyer, in a dissenting opinion, said the decision "threatens legal uncertainty in an area of law that, until this case, seemed reasonably well settled." The Constitution's grant of authority to Congress to regulate interstate commerce has been the basis for the development of the Federal Government in many of its most familiar aspects, particularly since the Supreme Court reversed course in 1937 and began to uphold the regulatory laws at the heart of the New Deal. The last time the Court overturned a Federal law on the ground that it exceeded the Congressional commerce authority was 1936, when it struck down minimum-wage and maximum-hour requirements in the coal industry. The ruling today, declaring unconstitutional the Gun-Free School Zones Act of 1990, cast doubt on the ability of Congress to exercise jurisdiction over a range of activities it has recently defined as Federal crimes, including car jacking, drive-by shootings and violent demonstrations at abortion clinics.

1226.text: GUN-7696 PRIMARY The mother of all gun protests Coloradans join massive rally in D.C. WASHINGTON - It would have been easier to have stayed in  Denver, they said, to have marched at the Civic Center on behalf  of gun control. But the 50 Colorado women who joined with tens of thousands  on the National Mall on Sunday to demand congressional action on  guns said they were so troubled by the Columbine High School  shootings that they wanted to do more. 'I wanted to put my body where my feelings are,' said Bee  Adams, the mother of a 16-year-old Columbine junior who escaped  the shootings unharmed. So Adams came to Washington for the second  time since the massacre to press for gun legislation. This time Adams and the other Coloradans joined the 'Million  Mom March,' a massive Mother's Day rally that its sponsors hope  will awaken Congress to the belief that the public wants new  restrictions on gun sales. At the national rally here that  attracted a mostly female crowd, and in 64 other cities including  Denver, speakers pounded that theme, sometimes with anguish, but always with bluntness. 'Politicians, take heed. We are watching you. The hands that  rock the cradles rule the world,' said Dawn Anna of Littleton,  whose daughter, Lauren Townsend, died in the Columbine shootings.   'The mothers of the world are angry. And you never, never tick off  a mother.' 'I'm outraged that in the year since the Columbine tragedy,  Congress has done nothing to protect kids from violence. Nothing,'  said Patti Nielson, the Columbine art teacher who recalled how she  was coldly gunned down by the two students who fired 'weapons of  war' at her and their classmates.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not directly mentioned)

1227.text: GUN-3787 PRIMARY CLINTON TELLS NRA TO KICK IN President Clinton said yesterday that NRA members should put "their money where their mouth is" and give up the $ 1 million they raised by tarring federal agents as "jack-booted government thugs." "They ought to give up the ill-gotten gains from their bogus fund-raising letter," the President said, referring to a National Rifle Association campaign to raise money with a letter that scalded police as the enemy. Clinton said the money should go to families of law enforcement agents who died in the line of duty. But the NRA was having none of it, issuing a terse one-line response: "We thank the President for his suggestion." Members at the gun group's convention opening in Phoenix were more candid. "I think Clinton should give back the money he solicited for his legal defense fund," said Budd Schroeder, an NRA board member from Buffalo. "Clinton is just trying to cash in on the national panic and horror over the Oklahoma bombing," said Patricia Lack. "He's trying to turn this into a bonanza for his party." Charles Gullett of the Arizona Rifle and Pistol Association said the gun lobby already gives to worthy causes such as programs that teach women how to defend themselves, gun safety projects for kids and "a handicapped shooters" program that helps the disabled. Clinton leveled his latest broadside against the gun lobby while surrounded by leaders of major law enforcement agencies, possibly hoping to get in a few licks before the NRA gears up to try to repeal the assault weapons ban.

1230.text: NA-45821 PRIMARY Justices reject young-adult gun limits appeals The Supreme Court refused Monday to review any  expansion of gun rights. The decision does not preclude the court agreeing to consider the next big legal issue in the national debate over guns: whether the right to keep a gun at home extends to public places. In fact, a federal appeals court panel's divided ruling this month in a California case makes it more likely that the question  will  head to the high court soon. The petitions denied Monday included challenges to a federal and a state law restricting access to young adults. The federal law blocks those ages 18 to 20 from buying guns from licensed dealers. The Texas law doesn't allow them to carry guns in public.

1235.text: NA-45952 PRIMARY Rush of gun legislation yields mixed results; States have tightened and loosened access to firearms In the six months since the Sandy Hook Elementary School shootings, lawmakers in four key states have approved significant restrictions on access to firearms. Elsewhere in the USA, the picture is far from clear. A USA TODAY analysis of the 86 state laws passed since Dec. 14 shows that states have both tightened and loosened access to guns. Lawmakers in many states used the spotlight the shootings created to broaden both who can carry a gun and where they can carry it. States including Colorado and Maryland tightened access to guns, Arkansas and Mississippi eased restrictions, and many other states issued rules whose impact could be debated either way. A U.S. Senate vote April 17 blocked a plan to expand background checks for gun purchases. The attack by gunman Adam Lanza killed 20 children and six staff members at the Newtown, Conn., school. Since then, the national death toll from guns has topped 5,000, according to a crowd-sourced data initiative by online magazine Slate and the Twitter feed @gundeaths. There are about 13,000 to 14,000 murder and non-negligent manslaughter victims each year, based on FBI data. In each of the past five years, guns have been used in roughly two-thirds of all homicides. That means about 5,000 deaths in the first six months of the year is typical. The incidents since Newtown include 10 mass shootings that killed 44 people. The most recent came last week at Santa Monica College in California, in which a gunman killed five people.   The FBI defines a mass shooting as one in which four or more people die, not including the perpetrator.

1234.text: GUN-8233 PRIMARY BUSH'S RECORD ON GUNS PROTECTS CITIZENS' RIGHTS In her Aug. 14 column, "Gore needs to unholster gun issue," Cynthia Tucker accuses Republican presidential candidate George W. Bush of sneering at the concerns of the women in the Million Moms March by supporting concealed-carry laws for law-abiding citizens. Ms. Tucker is obviously unaware that violent crime rates have decreased in almost every state that has passed such laws, as criminals are less likely to assault potential victims who might be armed. She is also unaware that Washington, D.C., which has had the most restrictive gun laws in the nation for many years, correspondingly also has one of the nation's highest violent-crime rates, probably because restrictive gun laws keep guns only out of the hands of people who obey laws, not away from criminals. I do not believe either Mr. Bush or his running mate, Dick Cheney, sneers at the concerns of any American. I am tempted, however, to sneer at the ignorance of Ms. Tucker regarding the "gun issue" and her eagerness to deprive us of the right to bear arms. H. DONALD LAMBE West Palm Beach

1236.text: GUN-10083 PRIMARY Antigun ads take aim at Florida's tourism-based economy Re: The shoot first state? Ads warn about new gun law, Sept. 29. The Brady Campaign's characterization of Florida law is so twisted and self-serving that the propaganda it is spewing should be a crime. The Web site, press releases and fliers produced by this group completely mischaracterize and misstate Florida law, with the sole purpose of scaring away tourists to put pressure on the Florida government and Gov. Jeb Bush. I hope that people outside of Florida understand that "nervous and frightened" Florida citizens cannot start shooting tourists walking our streets. A person has to be in legitimate fear for his life or the life of another before deadly force can be used, and only then by a person who has lawful possession of a means to deliver such deadly force. Law abiding tourists, even those with short tempers, have nothing to fear in Florida. Armed and violent criminals, however, should consider going to areas where firearms are more restricted, so that they can prey upon their victims with less fear of lawful self-defense. Make no mistake that this is an attack on Florida and its main business - tourism. The Brady Campaign hopes to scare off tourists and hurt Florida's business owners and citizens, all in the hopes of furthering its own political agenda. The "Shoot First Law" campaign of fear-mongering and false information is irresponsible, and designed to hurt Florida. Shame on them.

1232.text: GUN-30397 PRIMARY Blame criminals, not the guns that stop them Re the Dec. 27 commentary, "Too many weapons: Control guns to stop the tide of death," by John D. Kelly IV: Dr. Kelly obviously is committed to the medical profession. However, his negative position on firearms ownership is an emotional response that avoids the true factors in violent acts. He blames availability of guns and concealed-carry permits as the cause of 380 homicides in Philadelphia in 2005, noting that 208 of these deaths were over disputes. Dr. Kelly seems to purport that holding individuals accountable for their actions is unrealistic, and, therefore, that eliminating the rights in the Second Amendment is the only plausible solution to violent crime. Economist Lawrence Southwick Jr., in his 1999 paper, "Guns and Justifiable Homicide: Deterrence and Defense," said 800,000 to two million violent crimes were prevented each year as a result of gun ownership and use by civilians. In addition, armed civilians stop 1.5 million to 2.5 million crimes. This clearly demonstrates that the Second Amendment provides citizens with a means to protect themselves against violence. Measures that prevent law-abiding citizens from exercising their Second Amendment rights will only empower criminals and increase the vulnerability of law-abiding citizens. The answer to violent crime is stiffer penalties for people whose criminal actions place society in danger. Ultimately, we must stop blaming guns for violence, as they are simply tools that are used regularly to aid the innocent against vicious attacks.

1231.text: GUN-2713 PRIMARY Ban on importing guns costly to firms in Atlanta with ties to Chinese military Three Atlanta-based companies with close ties to the Chinese military's booming international arms trade will be hit hard by President Clinton's ban on guns and ammunition from China. The ban, one of the few sanctions levied by Clinton as he reversed his threat on trade penalties against China, will dry up a vast private market that Chinese gun manufacturers were only just discovering. More than 1 million rifles, handguns and shotguns were imported into the United States last year, according to the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms - nearly a tenfold increase over the previous year. The three Atlanta companies - Keng's Firearms Specialty, PTK International Inc. and Poly USA Inc. - are major importers or brokers of weapons and ammunition produced by companies run by the Chinese People's Liberation Army. Da Keng, owner of Keng's Firearms Specialty, said the ban will severely hurt his company. "That's probably 80 percent of our business," he said. Keng, who has an offic e in an industrial park in southwest Atlanta, is the local importer for China North Industries, also known as Norinco. While nominally separate from the People's Liberation Army, Norinco oversees China's defense industries. PTK International and Poly USA are located at the same address on East Paces Ferry Road in Buckhead. They are local brokers for Poly Group, which is run by the general staff of the People's Liberation Army and lists as its president He Ping, the son-in-law of supreme leader Deng Xiaoping.

1233.text: GUN-30489 PRIMARY City to sue state over gun laws Fed up with foot-dragging in Harrisburg over gun control, Philadelphia is now taking its case to court. City Councilman Darrell L. Clarke said last night that the city plans to file a lawsuit today in Philadelphia Common Pleas Court alleging that the General Assembly has failed in its duty to protect the residents of the city. "It is becoming increasingly clear to me that the General Assembly is unwilling or unable to act," Clarke said in a telephone interview last night. "We have no choice but to go to court." Straw purchases have proliferated dramatically because the state has failed to enact laws that rein them in, he said. In a straw purchase, somebody legally acquires a firearm and then sells it to somebody else who is not eligible to own one, such as a convicted felon on parole or probation. Critics say that straw purchases are responsible for much of the firepower on the city's streets. In addition to authorizing the suit today, Council intends to approve eight gun-control measures that have been languishing in Council for more than a year, Clarke said. Among other things, the bills call for limiting handgun purchases to one a month, and for owners to report any guns that are lost or stolen, Clark said. David Kairys, a professor at the Beasley School of Law at Temple University, said that the laws Council is expected to enact today should be valid because of the city's Home Rule Charter. But the charter's power is diminishing, he said.

1237.text: NA-40520 PRIMARY NRA study suggests trained, armed school staffers WASHINGTON o With the Senate gun control debate on the near horizon, a National Rifle Association-sponsored report on Tuesday proposed a program for schools to train selected staff members as armed security officers. The former Republican congressman who headed the study suggested at least one protector with firearms for every school, saying it would speed responses to attacks. The report's release served as the gun-rights group's answer to improving school safety after the gruesome December slayings of 20 first-graders and six adults at an elementary school in Newtown, Conn. And it showed the organization giving little ground in its fight with President Barack Obama over curbing firearms. Obama's chief proposals include broader background checks for gun buyers and bans on assault weapons and high-capacity ammunition magazines - both of which the NRA opposes. The study - unveiled at a news conference watched over by several burly, NRA-provided guards - made eight recommendations, including easing state laws that might bar a trained school staff member from carrying firearms and improving school coordination with law enforcement agencies. But drawing the most attention was its suggested 40- to 60-hour training for school employees who pass background checks to also provide armed protection while at work. "The presence of an armed security personnel in a school adds a layer of security and diminishes the response time that is beneficial to the overall security," said Asa Hutchinson, a GOP former congressman from Arkansas who directed the study.

1238.text: NA-43677 PRIMARY Both sides on gun control are turning to women In the increasingly confrontational debate over the nation's gun laws, two female archetypes have emerged. There's the grieving mother whose child was killed in a shooting and whose pleas for stricter regulation seem unassailable. And there's the flinty mother who wants maximum firepower to take matters into her own hands to protect her brood. As Congress weighs President Obama's agenda to toughen gun legislation, powerful lobbies on both sides of the issue are turning to women as leading spokespeople and symbols. In television advertisements and op-ed articles, speeches at rallies and testimonials before legislators, both types are stoking emotion and fear in an attempt to sway public opinion. In Newtown, Conn., one mother after another testified last week about losing her child in the Sandy Hook Elementary School massacre or about the guilt that haunts her because her child survived. The military-style AR-15 rifle the shooter used, one mother told a legislative panel, was "a death machine." On the same day in Washington, another woman, Gayle Trotter of the Independent Women's Forum, testified just the opposite. She said an assault rifle in the hands of a mother defending her children and her home against violent intruders offers "peace of mind." The AR-15, Trotter told a Senate panel, is "a defense weapon." Advocates for stricter firearm restrictions are employing mothers of shooting victims in their public relations push, calculating that when they speak out against gun violence they are hard to dismiss. Hundreds of thousands of moms who began organizing on Facebook after December's Newtown shootings are staging rallies nationwide and lobbying lawmakers to pass President Obama's gun-control proposals.

1239.text: GUN-632 PRIMARY THE SENATE'S GOOD START ON GUN CONTROL By a single vote, the Senate JudiciaryCommittee has approved a sensible bill by Arizona S en. Dennis DeConcini to ban the sale of 11 types of military-style assault weapons. While not as sweeping as some might like, this welcome action is Congress' first serious attempt to limit the spread of such dangerous weapons.   However, the 7-6 vote also shows that the powerful National Rifle Association will fight even the most limited gun legislation. Senate and House passage of the bill is uncertain.   But even the NRA should be able to support the DeConcini bill. Central to it is something the NRA and other gun groups have said they wanted for years: tougher penalties for criminals who use guns. A criminal caught using a semiautomatic assault weapon would get an additional 10 years in prison.   Penalties for gun smuggling by drug dealers would also be increased.   Nor should the NRA oppose the DeConcini bill because it would ''disarm law-abiding Americans.'' This measure is very narrowly drawn. It targets the 11 foreign and domestic assault weapons that are the most popular among criminals and drug gangs.   Of the four American-made weapons, one of them, Colt's AR-15, has already been removed from the open market by its manufacturer. The other three have no legitimate hunting or sporting role. The four U.S. weapons account for nearly half of all assault weapon-related crimes.   While the DeConcini bill may not accomplish everything, it is a good start and it should be quickly passed.

1241.text: GUN-11495 PRIMARY Seeking Common Ground on Gun Rights To the Editor: Re ''A Way Out of the Gun Stalemate,'' by Craig R. Whitney (Op-Ed, July 25): One way for liberals to understand those who insist on gun rights might be to think of the Second Amendment as conservatives' First Amendment. Many liberals (me included) see societal harm from rights claimed by Second Amendment supporters and would like to see more restrictions. Likewise, many conservatives see societal harm caused by rights claimed by First Amendment supporters (too easy access to pornographic and violent media content, for example) and would like to see more restrictions. The acknowledgment that Second Amendment rights have equal standing to those of the First might set the stage for a less rancorous discussion. A good starting point would be the premise that neither is absolute. Most First Amendment supporters concede that falsely shouting fire in a crowded theater is not protected speech. Maybe a more mutually respectful discussion could lead Second Amendment supporters to concede that easy access to guns allowing one to shoot people in a crowded theater should not be protected either. PAUL MANY Ottawa Hills, Ohio, July 25, 2012 To the Editor: Craig R. Whitney says, ''Liberals have to deprive the National Rifle Association of its core argument, that the real aim of all gun control measures is to strip Americans of their right to have and use firearms.''

1243.text: GUN-7916 PRIMARY THE OPEN FORUM CONCEAL-CARRY BILL Thanks to the 'D' senators Re: 'Concealed-carry permit bill killed,' Feb. 13. My heartfelt thanks to the 'D' Colorado senate for killing  the concealed weapons bill. I've noticed that all the bills that  are for doing away with gun controls are always brought by  senators with 'R' before their names. Could it be that they do not want to remember Columbine? Could  it be that they don't listen to the will of the people who are in  the majority, who voted to close the loopholes that these 'R'  people wanted to pass? I also lament the editorial ('Revive uniform gun bill') that  supported these bills. The Post gives several reasons for their  support, one being that law-abiding citizens need to carry  concealed weapons. It was concealed weapons that killed presidents from Lincoln to  Kennedy. It was a concealed weapon carried by a previously  law-abiding citizen who took a shot at Reagan. Even law-abiding  citizens break the law when they have access to weapons and  manufacture a reason to shoot. Remember 'guns don't kill people, people with guns kill people.' HOWARD M. BOTNICK Denver Thanks for editorial Re: 'Revive uniform gun bill,' Feb. 13 editorial. As I've chastised you in the past over firearms issues, I also  need to say 'thank you' when you run a favorable editorial. So  thank you for supporting this issue in your editorial. I really do  appreciate that.

1245.text: GUN-1898 PRIMARY Study: Gun at home not much protection A study of the relationship between gun ownership and homicide published today shows "no protective benefit" in having a gun in the home - and nearly 3-1 odds that a gun was kept in a house where a slaying took place. The study, headed by Dr. Arthur Kellermann of Emory University, appears in The New England Journal of Medicine. Kellermann and nine other doctors based their conclusions on case studies of 1,860 slayings over five years in Shelby County, Tenn., where Memphis is located; King County (Seattle), Wash.; and Cuyahoga County (Cleveland), Ohio. Their final statistics, however, were based on killings in 316 homes occupied by residents for whom fairly exact statistical counterparts could be found in the community. The counterparts, researchers said, served as controls, matching the statistics for age, sex and race. Researchers also questioned members in control households about previous arrests, drug or alcohol use, and prior violence in their households. Comparison of those cases, the study said, showed odds of 2.7 to 1 that somebody kept a gun in a house where a slaying took place. Kellermann said, however, that this cannot be interpreted to mean that gun owners are three times more likely to be killed than those who do not keep guns at home.   Other findings showed that households where a gunshot homicide occurred were: 2.5 times more likely to have a resident who had been arrested.

1244.text: GUN-858 PRIMARY Councilman urged to alter gun ordinance ST. PETERSBURG - Council member Bill Griswold ran into some hard legal    facts Thursday and had to tone down his proposal that the city ban the    sale and possession of assault weapons. City Attorney Mike Davis explained to the council that the state    had reserved for itself all authority for regulating firearms. Therefore, Davis said, Griswold's proposed ordinance would be    unenforceable and could subject the city to costly lawsuits. "I really think that your only options are to try to change the    (state) law," Davis said. Ultimately, that's what the council did, by passing a resolution    urging the League of Municipalities, the Urban Partnership and the    Police Benevolent Association to work for a statewide ban on assault    weapons or relinquishing control of those firearms to local    governments. Griswold's proposed ordinance was modeled after a similar ordinance recently passed in Los Angeles. That ordinance was in response to a    shooting at a Stockton, Calif., school in which a man armed with an    AK-47 killed five children and himself and wounded 29 other children    and a teacher. That kind of gun and others are available over the counter at some    local gun shops and Griswold worries about their use. "What I would like to do today is take some kind of stand on assault-type weapons," Griswold said. "They are war weapons. A    sportsman wouldn't take them hunting . . . They have no business being    sold. A good portion of these weapons are in the hands of teen-age    groups . . . Our police officers are outgunned."

1240.text: GUN-823 PRIMARY NO DOMESTIC ASSAULT-GUN BAN PLANNED The Bush administration has no plans to curtail sales of semiautomatic assault guns that are produced in the United States even while suspending imports of similar weapons, the White House said Wednesday. Furthermore, President Bush, despite the move against imports announced Tuesday, still does not support a ban on the weapons themselves, his press secretary, Marlin Fitzwater, said. Fitzwater was asked by reporters if the president would expand the review of the uses of the weapons to domestic models. "There are no plans of that kind anticipated," he said. "Right now we don't see any need for any review or any other changes at all." He said the problem of imports was highlighted in discussions between Bush's new drug policy coordinator, William Bennett, and Stephen Higgins, director of the Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco and Firearms. "It did prompt a review of them. . . . At this point we are not willing to extend that," Fitzwater said. Several domestic models perform the same functions and have the same characteristics as the imported models affected by Tuesday's suspension. One U.S. manufacturer, Colt, Wednesday announced a voluntary suspension of sales of its AR15.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco and Firearms

1242.text: GUN-9788 PRIMARY REVIVED BILL WOULD PROTECT THOSE SHOOTING IN SELF-DEFENSE/ GOVERNOR REMAINS OPPOSED TO MEASURE State legislators revived legislation Thursday that would give legal protection to gun owners who fire in self-defense, even though Gov. Rod Blagojevich adamantly opposes the bill. The bill was a response to the case of Hale DeMar, a Wilmette restaurant owner who shot a burglar who broke into his home twice. Prosecutors declined to press charges against DeMar, but Wilmette officials charged him with breaking the city's ban on handguns. Versions of the measure cleared both chambers of the Legislature this spring but stalled after concerns they would let gun owners carry and use their weapons on the streets and escape punishment. Supporters were able to get the measure back on track by changing it to offer only self-defense protection when gun owners fire in their homes, businesses or on their own property. "I think it's reasonable and logical, and people are going to do it anyway," said Rep. Willie Delgado, a Chicago Democrat who sent the original bill to a House subcommittee to bury it but removed his opposition after the change. "Now we just have to see how it plays itself out. It really is about protecting your loved ones." The Illinois House approved it 90-25, but as the bill heads to the Senate, there is still vocal opposition -- led by the governor. Blagojevich vowed in March to veto the measure and other gun-friendly proposals unless lawmakers approve a statewide ban on assault weapons. He reiterated that commitment Thursday, saying the vote shows "strong support for those who are urging the agenda of the gun lobby."

1248.text: GUN-11711 PRIMARY From Apocalypse To Dystopia WASHINGTON WE'RE a little overwrought now. The N.R.A. understands that. It's as patient with us as a husband with a tremulous pregnant wife prone to crying jags. This is just a passing meltdown. We'll get ourselves back under control soon and things will return to normal. For decades, when the public has grown more sympathetic to gun control after an attempted assassination or a spike in gun murders or a harrowing school shooting, Wayne LaPierre and his fellow N.R.A. officials have hunkered down to wait for the ''emotional period'' or ''hysteria,'' as they call it, to pass. They rule in the back rooms on Capitol Hill and rein in panicked senators and congressmen who fret that they should support some measly legislation to pretend they are not pawns of the gun lobby. They defend anyone owning anything with a trigger, reiterating that military-style semiautomatics are just uglier hunting guns. While there were more heartbreaking funerals in Newtown, Conn., with long hearses carrying small bodies, LaPierre stepped to the microphone in Washington on Friday to present the latest variation of his Orwellian creed: Guns don't kill people. Media kill people. ''Rather than face their own moral failings,'' he said in high dudgeon, ''the media demonize gun owners, amplify their cries for more laws, and fill the national media with misinformation and dishonest thinking that only delay meaningful action, and all but guarantee that the next atrocity is only a news cycle away.''

1246.text: GUN-975 PRIMARY LETTERLINE Freedom by the gun: The Brady bill is a liberal political conspiracy and the first step in the total disarmament of the American populace. Our local and federal governments cannot protect the honest citizenry. The criminal elements control the streets, and our political hacks are legislating against gun owners. Guns made us free and guns will keep us free. William D. Nueske Honolulu, Hawaii A little respect: I was really offended by Hal Linden's comment that ''when the NRA lost Reagan, it ran out of ammo.'' Reagan may have been president at one time, but, in reality, he is just another man with an opinion. I am an active NRA member and want to say that we are nowhere out of ammo! Hal's opinion is fine and even welcomed, but in the future, I'd advise him to watch his P's and Q's. I hope he takes my opinion into consideration and gives the NRA the respect it deserves. Lauren Peipelman Bozeman, Mont. Alternatives: The Brady bill is the kind of worthless placebo America gets when it allows extremist groups like Handgun Control Inc. and the NRA to control national debate. There are compromise solutions which would both protect our Second Amendment rights and make our streets safer: Mandatory training and certification before any firearms purchase. Major rewriting of sentencing, parole and probation laws to keep violent felons in jail longer. Additions instead of cuts in funds for rehabilitation, job training, education and mental health programs. Marc J. Johnson Syracuse, N.Y.

1247.text: GUN-6408 PRIMARY Teens keep focus on gun control Winkler delays college to push for moderation Spurred on by last week's high-publicity lobbying trip to the nation's capital, David Winkler says he's got better things to do over the next year than attend Tufts University. So the Denver teenager has deferred his admission and will stay close to home pushing for gun-control legislation. "There is just so much work to be done," Winkler says. "Colorado has the chance to lead the nation in reasonable gun control." Although Winkler emerged as a spokesman for the group of 90 high school students while they were in Washington, D.C., the aspiring political prodigy didn't fly solo. Winkler and good friend Ben Gelt, 18, both helped organize the two-day visit to Washington with SAFE Colorado. The bipartisan group, which stands for Sane Alternatives to the Firearms Epidemic, formed immediately after the Columbine High shootings. In the spotlight More than any other teen on the trip, 17-year-old Winkler was the most-televised and most-often-quoted. He even gave a speech at the White House standing next to President Clinton and appeared on "Good Morning, America" the next day. "He was superb, confident and eloquent," says SAFE Colorado co-founder Arnie Grossman. "He has a natural gift." Winkler graduated from George Washington High in the spring. He took honors classes and played varsity baseball but credits the debate team with polishing his speaking skills. Though Winkler was the one in the spotlight last week, Grossman says it was the way Winkler and Gelt worked so well together that made the trip a success.

1249.text: NA-42994 PRIMARY Sane Gun Laws To the Editor: Re '' 'Smart' Firearm Draws Wrath of the Gun Lobby'' (news article, April 28): Belinda Padilla, who is trying to market a handgun that can recognize authorized users, is another in a long line of those who are on the receiving end of the wrath caused by the all-too-powerful gun lobby. The National Rifle Association and other gun groups are out to destroy the business and reputation of anyone who dares to attempt to administer sanity into the debate over gun control. It seems so simple, having a weapon that can only be used by its rightful, authorized owner. It seems simple, too, that background checks should be mandatory before anyone can purchase a gun. It seems that gun owners would want laws put into effect that might stop some of the carnage we read about on a daily basis, because those tragedies only further reinforce the belief of non-gun-owners that this has to stop. The Second Amendment has nothing to do with protecting innocent people, many of them children, against the mounting numbers of deaths caused by guns in the hands of those who should never have them. Enacting sane laws to save lives will not endanger anyone's constitutional rights. It might endanger some of the huge profits that gun manufacturers are making, by keeping weapons out of the reach of criminals.

1250.text: GUN-3040 PRIMARY EXPLOSIVE POLITICS Everybody knows it can be politically dangerous for politicians to introduce gun control legislation. But nobody said such a stance would ever be physically dangerous. Two members of the Legislature from Palm Beach County, however, have received bomb threats because they support a bill that would restrict the sale and possession of assault weapons. Reps. Ron Klein, D-Boca Raton, and Carol Hanson, R-Boca Raton, say they won't change their position. The two lawmakers probably have nothing to worry about anyway. After all, bombs don't kill people. Only bombs in the hands of gun nuts kill people.

1253.text: GUN-31297 PRIMARY The D.C. Right on the Line; It's voting, not guns -- and the House should make that clear. THE HOUSE of Representatives will soon have the opportunity -- indeed, the privilege -- to right a 200-year-old wrong. Action is set this week on a measure to give the District of Columbia a voting member of Congress. Nothing is more heady than bringing democracy to a people, and House members should not sully this noble effort with matters that are irrelevant or politics that are petty. Strong support exists in the Democrat-controlled House for the D.C. voting rights bill. The measure would give long-disenfranchised D.C. residents a voting representative in the House under an arrangement that would permanently expand that body by two seats. The other seat would go to the state next in line to pick up an additional representative, with Utah benefiting for the next few years. The measure won approval in the House in 2007, only to be blocked in the Senate. Last week, the Senate approved the measure, albeit with a controversial gun amendment that would strip the D.C. government of its ability to regulate guns. The question now is whether that Senate amendment will become part of the House legislation. Predictably, the National Rifle Association is applying pressure. Similar legislation to gut the District's gun restrictions actually passed the House last year, with significant Democratic support, only to die in the Senate. We hope the House leadership insists on rules that limit amendments to subjects that are germane to the bill, such as whether there should be a provision for expedited court review.

1251.text: GUN-31410 PRIMARY Guns at political events stir up a volatile brew When a man with a gun strapped to his leg showed up outside a presidential town hall meeting in Portsmouth, N.H., this month, and a dozen protesters -- including one man with an AR-15 rifle slung over his shoulder -- repeated the performance in Phoenix, shrill and overly simplistic rhetoric ricocheted around the nation for days. Those on the left decried the incidents, many suggesting that anyone who would carry a gun to a presidential event must be nuts, potentially violent or both. Conservatives parried that this was nothing more than Second Amendment advocates asserting their rights to self-protection. But a funny thing happened amid the all-too-predictable din. A couple of advocates -- polar opposites in the gun debate -- found a kernel of common ground that holds a ton of common sense. Their bottom line: Carrying guns openly outside presidential events may be legal in many states, but it sure isn't smart. Press both advocates and their reasoning gets very different, very fast.   Alan Gottlieb, of the Second Amendment Foundation, argues that openly carrying a gun in this situation is politically foolish. It intimidates people and turns them off to the gun-rights cause. Paul Helmke, president of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence, argues that loaded firearms in such situations stifle debate and endanger everyone involved. The fact is, all are solid reasons to leave guns at home when going to presidential venues, or political events of any kind. And on websites and Internet discussion groups, many gun owners made that point.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1252.text: GUN-4872 PRIMARY REGISTRATION OF OLD ASSAULT GUNS HALTED RULING: JUDGE ORDERS ATTORNEY GENERAL LUNGREN TO STOP ALLOWING ANY FIREARMS MADE ILLEGAL BY 1989 BAN. A San Francisco judge Wednesday ordered state Attorney General Dan Lungren to stop allowing gun owners to register assault weapons they've possessed since before 1989, when such firearms were banned in California. At issue in the case, filed by the pro-gun-control group Handgun Control Inc., is whether Lungren is violating state law by continuing to register weapons after the Legislature passed a second law: a March 1992 registration deadline for grandfathered weapons. San Francisco Superior Court Judge Raymond Williamson signed an order finding that Lungren's "continued registration thus permits illegal assault weapons to remain in the state, posing significant risk to the safety and welfare of the public, contrary to the expressed intent of the Legislature." Possession of an unregistered gun on the state's list of banned assault weapons is a crime. While Wednesday's ruling prohibits future registration, it is unclear whether registrations made since March 1992 are invalid. After an education speech in San Jose, Lungren refused to comment on the ruling, saying he hadn't yet read it. His spokesman, Rob Stutzman, said the attorney general plans to seek a stay while he appeals the decision. "The law is written to be interpreted a couple of different ways," Stutzman said. About 16,000 assault weapons have been registered since March 1992, the majority of them shortly after the deadline, Stutzman said. A lawyer representing the attorney general had argued in court that continued registration is necessary because there is a provision of law that allows late registrants to reduce charges of owning an unregistered pre-ban weapon from a misdemeanor to an infraction.

1256.text: GUN-354 PRIMARY RODINO OFFERS A BILL TO CURB PISTOLS Legislation to ban the sale of socalled Saturday night specials and tighten controls on the sale of other pistols has been introduced by Representative Peter Rodino Jr., Democrat of New Jersey. Representative Rodino, the chairman of the House Judiciary Committee, said today that enactment of the bill would save lives and ''reduce the climate of violence in America.'' He introduced similar legislation two years ago, but the measure never emerged from a subcommittee on crime.   A Rodino aide said there have been about 30 gun-control bills introduced so far this session, ranging from measures to outlaw pistol to ones that would impose mandatory sentences for crimes committed with a pistols. Hoping for a 'People's Lobby' Mr. Rodino said he hoped a ''people's lobby'' of those outraged by the wounding of President Reagan and three others last week would actively support his bill ''so that the National Rifle Association isn't the only one sending out mimeographed messages to members of Congress.'' The rifle association has opposed measures to outlaw gun ownership as an unconstitutional infringement on the right of citizens to bear arms. Mr. Rodino said Senator Edward M. Kennedy, Democrat of Massachusetts, planned to introduce similar legislation in the Senate. Representative Rodino's bill would ban the manufacture, importation, assembly or sale of Saturday night specials, closing a loophole in the Federal Gun Control Act of 1968 that permitted importation of parts for such inexpensive and easily concealed weapons. It would also require a 21-day waiting period before the purchase of any pistol to permit a background check and mandate prison terms of at least two years for the first conviction on a charge of carrying a pistol while committing a felony and five years for subsequent offenses.

1258.text: GUN-7587 PRIMARY Rosie a Hypocrite? Rosie O'Donnell, keynote speaker of the Million Mom March, comes to Washington to call for a ban on handguns and to rail against concealed weapons permits. But she has no problem with her bodyguard applying for a concealed weapon permit so that he can pack heat when he drops her kids off for school [Reliable Source, May 26]. This is hypocrisy and elitism at its worst. When it comes to Ms. O'Donnell's loved ones, a handgun is an essential tool of personal protection, should be carried concealed so as to be readily available in the event of danger and is perfectly safe when kept around children and toted into school zones. Those of us who cannot afford to hire bodyguards, though, should turn in our guns and depend on the police to protect our families. Perhaps the Second Amendment should be revised to read, "The right of the elite to hire persons to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed." BRAD BENNETT Arlington

1255.text: GUN-6792 PRIMARY BILL WOULD BAN SUITS AGAINST GUNMAKERS PA. MUNICIPALITIES COULD NOT SUE FOR DAMAGES UNDER A BILL SENT TO GOV. RIDGE. IT WOULD REQUIRE TRIGGER LOCKS. A proposal to ban Philadelphia and other cities from suing gun manufacturers received final legislative approval yesterday and was sent to Gov. Ridge, who is expected to sign it into law in the coming weeks. The bill would prevent Pennsylvania counties and municipalities from joining Boston, Chicago, Camden and 25 other cities that are moving to sue gunmakers to recover costs related to gun violence. The proposal contains other provisions, including a requirement that all handguns be sold with trigger locks. Gun buyers who purchase the locks would receive immunity from civil litigation, although the law would not require them to actually use the locks. Gov. Ridge, who opposes most gun control, called the bill a "very effective piece of legislation." Because harsher restrictions on gun use had not made it to the final version of the bill, its passage was hailed as a victory for gun-rights advocates - and a bitter defeat for those looking for tighter controls. "I wish that many young people who are now paralyzed or in the grave as the result of gun violence had the kind of protections we are giving gun manufacturers," said Rep. Curtis Thomas (D., Phila.). Thirteen states prohibit lawsuits against gunmakers. Pennsylvania's bill was passed by the Senate Monday and cleared the House, 122-75, yesterday after a fierce floor debate. Most of the action focused on the ban, which would prevent Philadelphia's mayor-elect, John F. Street, from fulfilling his campaign promise to join in the suits.

1257.text: GUN-6972 PRIMARY IN BRIEF; Statewide Gun Control Group Endorses Assembly Candidates The gun control group Ceasefire New Jersey has announced its endorsement of 52 candidates for state Assembly. Twenty-four of those endorsed are incumbents, and of the incumbents, 20 are Democrats and 4 are Republicans. "A vote for any of these candidates is a vote for sanity," said Bryan Miller, executive director of Ceasefire. Mr. Miller said the endorsements were partly based on a survey to which 85 of the 160 Assembly candidates responded. The survey asked how the candidates planned to vote on an Assembly bill mandating childproof gun technology and another easing restrictions on carrying concealed handguns. Ceasefire made its final decision based on the responses and the incumbents' voting records. The New Jersey Association of Rifle and Pistol Clubs, a gun rights advocate, had no comment on Ceasefire list, according to Nancy Ross, a spokeswoman, who said they had not viewed it. The association has ranked all the Assembly candidates and is endorsing 20 incumbents, Ms. Ross said.    LISA SUHAY http://www.nytimes.com

characters: New Jersey Association of Rifle and Pistol Clubs

1260.text: GUN-154 PRIMARY Metro Matters; Gun Possession: Lighter Penalty Than Advertised The television commercial made it seem open and shut. Striding down the corridor of an empty Rikers Island cell block, Mayor Koch delivered this stern warning: ''If you get caught carrying an illegal handgun, you'll go to jail for one year. And there's no way around it.'' With cell doors clanging closed in the background, Mr. Koch declared: ''If you've got the gun, we've got the room.'' So much for truth in advertising. Even when the commercial was broadcast in 1980, Rikers was overcrowded. But, more significantly, there have been ways around the gun law. In New York City, people convicted of criminal possession of a handgun face just over a one-in-three chance of being sentenced to a full year. Indeed, nearly half of those convicted last year did not go to jail at all. This is why the so-called mandatory one-year term has barely been mentioned in connection with the sentencing of Bernhard H. Goetz, scheduled for Sept. 4. A Manhattan jury acquitted Mr. Goetz last week of attempted murder charges in the shooting of four youths. He was convicted only of criminal possession of a weapon under a 1980 law that, in the section on sentencing, never mentions the word ''mandatory'' although the law was always promoted as providing little leeway. Instead, the controlling ''m-word'' in the legislation was ''mitigation,'' which allows a judge discretion in waiving the minimum one-year penalty.

1254.text: NA-42842 PRIMARY Senate Judiciary Committee Set to Consider Gun Legislation The Senate Judiciary Committee will begin to consider four separate gun safety measures on Thursday, although the process of narrowing actual legislative language will most likely dribble into next week. Among the measures to be pondered is one that would reinstate the expired assault weapons ban and place limits on high-capacity magazines, offered by Senator Dianne Feinstein, Democrat of California. The notion of such a ban has been radioactive among Republicans and many Democrats, especially those up for re-election in conservative states in 2014. Also under consideration is a bill sponsored by Senator Patrick J. Leahy, Democrat of Vermont and chairman of the committee, which would address the problem of illegal gun trafficking; a measure offered by Senator Barbara Boxer, Democrat of California, that would improve school safety; and a "placeholder" proposal concerning universal background checks written by Senator Charles E. Schumer, Democrat of New York, as he attempts to work out a bipartisan version with Senator Tom Coburn, Republican of Oklahoma and a staunch Second Amendment supporter.   The talks between Mr. Schumer and Mr. Coburn have stalled over a provision that would require records to be kept of private gun sales. President Obama has asked Congress to take up measures that would create a universal background check system for gun sales and enhanced tools to stem "straw purchases" of guns for criminals who cannot pass background checks. He has also called for reinstating the assault weapons ban, which expired in 2004, and tightening limits on the capacity of some ammunition magazines, which, as its own measure, enjoys more support from lawmakers than an outright ban of assault weapons.

1259.text: NA-42912 PRIMARY The Gun Report: June 6, 2013 As our colleague Charles Blow pointed out in his column yesterday, George Zimmerman is going on trial for the killing of Trayvon Martin-a killing, of course, that Zimmerman first got a pass on by invoking Florida's Stand Your Ground law. What we've come to realize in compiling the Gun Report each day is that more and more people feel completely justified to use a gun to settle an argument, to shoot first and ask questions later-even when the person who is killed is a drunk 16-year-old who accidentally climbs through a neighbor's window instead of his own-and to "defend" themselves even when no defense is required. Stand Your Gun laws-as well as concealed carry laws, and laws that allow people to brandish weapons in public-are laws that provoke gun violence rather than prevent it, and help return us to the ethos of the wild west, where guns were the court of first resort. I would be nice to think that Zimmerman's trial might change that, just a little, but we're not holding our breath. Here is today's report. -Joe Nocera A Florida couple faces child neglect charges after their 4-year-old son shot himself in the hand with a handgun he found wedged in the couch. The child told deputies he knew where the gun was because he saw his father, Donald Greeson, a felon, put it there the night before. The child had his left ring finger removed.

1263.text: GUN-7219 PRIMARY Reader responses: GUN LAWS; Place blame on the person who pulls weapon's trigger Has the editorial board lost its collective mind? Not once in the editorial "NRA ignores the facts" (March 15) did it blame the party actually responsible for the recent shootings. Millions of children grow up in homes where guns are present and, remarkably, never shoot anyone! In truth, it is a tiny minority of young people who choose to commit crimes with guns. In almost every one of the cases where a child commits a horrible act with a gun, the child comes from horrible parents! I know this may be a sobering realization for the liberal media, but bad parents turn out bad kids. Yes, even children as young as 6, if they come from a rotten home, are capable of murder. It's time to put the blame where the blame belongs --- on the person who pulls the trigger. SHANE MILLER Miller, of Lawrenceville, is a teacher. Educate public on regulations I thought The Atlanta Constitution finally got it right when I read Cynthia Tucker's column, "Buckhead crime: Targeting gun offenders reduces violence" (March 15). Full enforcement of existing laws, coupled with effective prosecution and incarceration, is the only proven approach to curbing gun violence --- a position strongly advocated by the NRA's leadership and 3 million members. Then, on the same day and page, I read the inflammatory editorial "NRA ignores the facts" and realized that Tucker must have had only a temporary attack of sanity. Claiming we live in a country where guns are "virtually unregulated" shows a lack of basic understanding of existing laws regulating firearms.

1261.text: GUN-20246 PRIMARY Clinton urges on Colo. gun-control fight 'Don't quit until you win' on issue of sales loophole DENVER -- President Clinton invoked the memory of the Columbine High School tragedy Wednesday as he told gun-control activists in Colorado not to give up their effort to tighten gun-sale laws despite stiff opposition from the gun lobby. As several hundred protesters chanted outside, Clinton told backers of the proposal not to be discouraged by setbacks since the Columbine shooting nearly a year ago. He urged them to work for a state ballot initiative under which a majority of voters could close the so-called gun-show loophole, which allows gun sales without background checks of buyers. Supporters are collecting signatures to get the proposal on the November ballot. "I want you to understand that the country is looking very closely at Colorado," said Clinton, who is trying to get lawmakers in Washington to approve a similar provision. The state still has "a broken heart over Columbine," Clinton said, referring to the school where two 17-year-olds slaughtered 12 fellow students and a teacher last April 20 before taking their own lives. The shooters, too young to buy guns legally, had a friend, 18, buy three of the weapons at a gun show. Clinton noted that a group called SAFE Colorado, which stands for Sane Alternatives to the Firearms Epidemic, has formed since the attack in nearby Littleton to seek stricter gun laws. He told the crowd: "Other states will follow your lead. I hope and pray Congress will follow your lead as well."

1264.text: GUN-9816 PRIMARY N.R.A. Opens an All-Out Drive for Bush and Its Views When the National Rifle Association opens its annual meeting here on Friday, it will do more than celebrate hunting, weaponry and the Second Amendment. It will also kick off a vigorous campaign to whip up support among its nearly four million members for President Bush's re-election. Before tens of thousands of gun owners at the Pittsburgh Convention Center, the association's leadership plans to label Mr. Bush's likely Democratic opponent, Senator John Kerry of Massachusetts, as a liberal threat to gun ownership. It is a message they will repeat again and again until Election Day, using the Internet, mailings, television advertising and their formidable nationwide network of gun clubs. ''What you see in John Kerry,'' Wayne LaPierre, the association's executive vice president said in an interview this week, ''is a politician that spent his life voting against the Second Amendment. What I see is the same thing I saw in Michael Dukakis and Al Gore. It's an elitist arrogance.'' It is no accident, N.R.A. officials said, that this year's convention is being held in Pittsburgh. Two-thirds of the attendees are expected to come from within a 100-mile radius that spans three battleground states: Pennsylvania, which voted for Mr. Gore in 2000, and Ohio and West Virginia, which voted for Mr. Bush. ''These are states where the N.R.A. can make a difference,'' said G. Terry Madonna, director of the Keystone Poll at Franklin & Marshall College in Lancaster, Pa.

1265.text: GUN-1205 PRIMARY CAN PITY, RAGE DEFEAT DOGGED GUN LOBBY? WASHINGTON What will it take to pass the Brady bill? If pity would do it, the seven-day wait to buy a gun would be on the books today. Who could look at Jim and Sarah Brady, 10 years after John Hinckley blew their lives apart with a $29 handgun, watch them lobbying to save others from comparable catastrophe and not be moved?   Well, the National Rifle Association, for one.   The Bush administration, for another.   Ten years ago, Jim Brady was Ronald Reagan's press secretary, a big Washington player. He had a genial disposition and a dry wit. Ten years of surgery, therapy and all the painful, demeaning days and nights that go with traumatic injury have shortened his temper, but his humor miraculously remains. Sarah Brady is the ultimate wife: stylish, upbeat, tirelessly but not tiresomely solicitous. She steers her husband's wheelchair to the very spot at the witness table. She turns the pages of his script as he laboriously testifies about the need to make people hold their horses for a week while the police check their eligibility to become gun owners.   It does n't seem a lot to ask, but three years ago, the Brady bill lost on the House floor by 46 votes. Since then the Bradys have criss-crossed the country and have spoken to state legislatures, police chiefs and PTAs.   Polls say 95 percent of the American people are in favor of the Brady bill.   So if pity won't do it, how about rage?   Louis Sullivan, secretary of health and human services, made a speech the other night about guns and what they are doing to people. He related how gun-related homicides of black teen-agers went up 38 percent in 1988. Gun-related deaths for all male teens exceeded the number of deaths from all natural causes. Black male teen-agers were 11 times more likely to be killed by a gun than white contemporaries.   The secretary did not mention gun control. Says Rep. Charles Schumer, a Brady bill backer and chair of the Subcommittee on Criminal Justice: ''Sullivan may not have meant to, but he helps build the case.'' He will hold hearings next week.   Gun control is not popular in an administration whose boss is a member of the NRA, a rich and paranoid organization that goes to war every time any meddler tries to interpose an obstacle between a gun and a would-be buyer. In the crime bill he intends as the legislative heritage of Desert Storm, Bush ignores the Brady bill.   This year the NRA is running ads in which it admits felons do get guns. The solution: an instant-check system similar to one in operation in Virginia where a call to an 800 number makes available the criminal record of would-be gun owners.   The Bush administration is all for this high-tech approach. Gun controllers call it Star Wars. Attorney General Dick Thornburgh has told Congress that the system is extremely expensive and years away; even so, advocates urge it as the fair-minded and reasonable way to keep America armed.   Speaker Tom Foley, who kept the Brady bill off the floor last session - he has the Westerner's allergy to gun control - promises an early vote.   If pity and rage can't carry the day, maybe a simple realization that the time has come will bring the Brady bill home.   Copyright 1991 Universal Press Syndicate

1266.text: GUN-8374 PRIMARY EDITORIAL 'The check is in the mail' Gun sales in Colorado halted Tuesday because the Federal Bureau  of Alcohol,Tobacco and Firearms failed to get dealers new forms  for doing background checks on purchasers. The ATF had mailed a letter last October telling dealers a  new Form 4473 was contemplated but didn't say when the switch  would occur. Surprise: Businesses such as Dave's Guns in Aurora,  the state's biggest dealer, learned the change took effect Tuesday  when the Colorado Bureau of Investigation refused to do instant  background checks on the old forms. Owner Dave Anver was miffed because only two weeks ago, he  ordered another 5,000 forms and received the old version. 'The  first inkling we got,' Anver said, 'was with our first customer  transaction (Tuesday) when the CBI said,'If you don't have the new  form, this conversation is over.' ' After frantic telephone calls, Anver finally obtained a faxed  copy of the new form from the offices of U.S. Rep. Scott McInnis,  R-Grand Junction. The only difference in the forms, as far as Anver could tell,  is that purchasers used to write in their race but now check one  of six boxes labeled with different ethnic and racial  designations. Also, there is a place to indicate if the sale is  made at a gun show, although dealers had to make such notations on  the old forms, he said. Denver gun dealer Bob French learned of the Catch-22 from a  customer. 'I called the CBI, and they said if it's not on the new  form, they can't do the background check.' French said he called  the ATF and asked that new forms be mailed to him.

1267.text: GUN-11938 PRIMARY How Should We Prevent Future Mass Shootings? In the wake of the shootings at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn., that left 20 children and 6 school staff members dead, President Obama stated that "we're going to have to come together and take meaningful action to prevent more tragedies like this, regardless of the politics." However, he did not provide any detailed proposals for what meaningful action should look like. So, we're asking you: What kind of meaningful action do you think the United States should take to prevent future mass shootings? The Op-Ed columnist Nicholas D. Kristof raises many questions in his piece "Do We Have the Courage to Stop This?" Mr. Kristof writes: In the harrowing aftermath of the school shooting in Connecticut, one thought wells in my mind: Why can't we regulate guns as seriously as we do cars? The fundamental reason kids are dying in massacres like this one is not that we have lunatics or criminals - all countries have them - but that we suffer from a political failure to regulate guns. Children ages 5 to 14 in America are 13 times as likely to be murdered with guns as children in other industrialized countries, according to David Hemenway, a public health specialist at Harvard who has written an excellent book on gun violence. So let's treat firearms rationally as the center of a public health crisis that claims one life every 20 minutes. The United States realistically isn't going to ban guns, but we can take steps to reduce the carnage.

1262.text: NA-42123 PRIMARY Pleasant Hill defends gun dealer ordinance PLEASANT HILL -- The city denies that its firearms ordinance was improperly approved and violates the Constitution, as alleged in a December lawsuit brought by gun advocates. In a response filed Feb. 6 in Contra Costa Superior Court, the city also argues that the National Shooting Sports Foundation, a gun industry trade group based in Newtown, Conn., lacks standing to sue. Furthermore, Pleasant Hill maintains the lawsuit is premature because existing gun dealers have until Nov. 1 to comply with the ordinance. "It's a standard response," City Attorney Janet Coleson said. "It's the very beginning stages of this litigation." The Law Center to Prevent Gun Violence, a national advocacy group, arranged for San Francisco law firm Keker & Van Nest to defend the lawsuit pro bono for the city. Coincidentally, Laura Cutilletta, a staff attorney with the Law Center, and Laurie Mims, the attorney handling the case, both live in Pleasant Hill. On Feb. 27, a judge will set the schedule for the case. The firearms ordinance requires a police permit for gun dealers. The law also prohibits gun stores from locating within 150 feet of a residence; within 500 feet of a park, another gun dealer, a massage parlor or an adult entertainment venue; or within 1,000 feet of a day care center or school. Existing firearms dealers are exempt from the new location restrictions, but they must submit employees' background information to the Pleasant Hill Police Department. Dealers also must install an alarm system and surveillance cameras and submit an annual report to the police chief detailing compliance with the regulations.

characters: Law Center to Prevent Gun Violence

1272.text: GUN-30359 PRIMARY OH, SHOOT, MAYOR'S A BOBBLEHEAD MAYOR BLOOMBERG'S anti-gun crusade has won him a new mantle in the Second Amendment world, as a pistol-packing bobblehead doll. "It's an instant laugh to anybody that looks at it," said Steve Lauer, the Wisconsin gun dealer who commissioned the doll after years as the target of Bloomberg's ire. "I never stop laughing about it." Lauer stands on one side of the doll holding an AK-47 automatic rifle, while Bloomberg stands next to him with a red Glock handgun and a fake yellow Beretta. It's not a great likeness of the mayor, but it matches the pose he struck when declaring war on Lauer's firm in 2006 for selling kits to paint guns in bright colors. Bloomberg said cops and kids could mistakenly assume that neon-colored firearms are just toys, with deadly results. His remark is emblazoned at the base of his figure: "People will do a lot of twisted things to make a buck." Lauer thinks the mayor's position is hogwash, and fired back by marketing a line of bright "Bloomberg Collection" colors and a blood-red shade called "Furious Mike." The publicity juiced sales for his Lauer Custom Weaponry mail-order business, and spurred him to set up a tribute to Bloomberg in a corner of his gun store. He said he ordered 500 of the dolls and has sold about 100 for $19.99 each - and given away some at a recent national firearms show, where it was a huge hit.

1269.text: GUN-2905 PRIMARY CONTROVERSY PROMPTS SHIFT IN GUNFIRE BILL St. Charles County Council President Carl Bearden has modified his bill aimed at controlling the discharge of firearms in unincorporated parts of the county, after the bill took a few potshots. Bearden says he believed that he had taken care of the most controversial parts of the bill by reducing the age to 12 from 16 for anyone to discharge a gun without supervision, and by making it unlawful for anyone to fire a gun within 100 yards of an inhabitable structure without first getting permission. The bill had barred firing a gun within 300 yards of a residential property line. The bill could be up for final passage at the council's meeting this week. Bearden, R-7th District, and Sheriff Ray Runyon held a news conference Friday to explain that despite their many statements that the bill concerned public safety - and not gun control - somehow the anti-gun-control reaction had been triggered. Runyon said that most of the people, including a spokesman for several duck and hunting clubs, who called him had never read the bill. Steven Mobley, 27, who lives near Cottleville, picketed the county's Courts Administration building last week because of the bill. Bearden said he and other council members had received many, many telephone calls about his bill. Bearden added that many of his constituents, who live in urban areas, were in favor of it.

1271.text: NA-41181 PRIMARY Gun foes, Schumer fight on DAYS AFTER the stinging defeat of gun control measures in the Senate, Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and gun-safety advocates rallied to show continued support for cracking down on guns. About 100 people gathered outside Schumer's Manhattan office to vow to push on despite the defeat of a measure to expand background checks for gun purchases. "I've been fighting for gun safety for 25 years," said Schumer, one of the architects of the compromise measure that was voted down. "This is the first time we have a crowd on our side. "We are aroused, we are fighting, and we're not going to stop until we win," he said. Schumer blamed the NRA for distorting the contents of the gun control bill. "They can't win the argument on truth. They can only win on spreading fear and lies." Lauren O'Neill, 21, the cousin of first-grader Daniel Barden, who was killed in the Newtown massacre, said she was taken aback when the bill went down. "I was disappointed and upset," she said. "It makes you question who the senators represent." David Devaughn, 28, of Manhattan, said it should have been "easy" to pass the background-check bill. "Senators are afraid of their NRA ratings," he said. "Every day, this person or that person is shot. Every day you hear about a weapon being used. There are daily reminders - it's hard to forget."

1270.text: GUN-20098 PRIMARY Plan to arm pilots is taking off WASHINGTON -- Since the Senate voted overwhelmingly last month to arm up to 85,000 airline pilots, carriers and safety advocates have raised numerous concerns about cost, security and safety. So far, no one appears to be listening. Despite renewed criticism from airline executives and private worries from some government officials, the measure seems certain to become law. The politics of guns has overwhelmed the slower-moving aviation world, according to officials, lobbyists and Capitol Hill insiders. Vigorous lobbying by pilots and gun rights groups, who say concerns about the proposal have been overblown, eroded most objections from Congress. Proposals to make airline pilots eligible to carry guns passed the House of Representatives in July and the Senate in September. Groups such as the National Rifle Association urged lawmakers to vote for the measure, and few wanted to oppose it in an election year. Even some liberal lawmakers who generally favor gun control embraced the idea. But the House and Senate versions, which must be merged before being sent to the president, do not specify how pilots would be trained, how weapons would be transported between flights or how the measure would be paid for. A fraction of the 5,000 to 10,000 airline pilots who fly each day are expected to volunteer to carry a gun. The Bush administration initially opposed arming pilots, but that position weakened under intense lobbying. James Loy, the acting head of the Transportation Security Administration, says he will follow Congress' wishes, though he would prefer to begin with a small test program instead of allowing all pilots to participate.

1268.text: NA-42981 PRIMARY With Move to Limit Gun Sales, Facebook Is Caught in Debate Facebook wants to unite the world so everyone can talk about everything. One of the big things people want to talk about, it seems, is guns. The social network is one of the world's largest marketplaces for guns. A DoubleStar AR-15 is offered for $650. A raspberry-colored Taurus pistol can be had for $239.95, a Bushmaster M4 ''fresh from the box'' for $1,200. '' We've got over 550 guns and we need buyers!'' posts a Louisiana seller. Under pressure from law enforcement and advocacy groups, Facebook took steps Wednesday to regulate gun sales on its site as well as on its photo-sharing app Instagram. Pages advertising guns for sale, for instance, will be shielded from minors. Facebook does not want its growing prominence as a private gun mall to alienate users. Nor does it want to squelch free speech. But if the company hoped its announcement would satisfy everyone and make the issue disappear, the plan backfired. Gun control groups applauded the changes. So did Michael R. Bloomberg, who is making gun control one of the most visible elements of his career after serving as New York mayor. But the National Rifle Association said the changes were so insignificant that Mr. Bloomberg had ''failed.'' And Daniel Gross, president of one of the largest gun control groups, the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence, said little had been achieved.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1275.text: NA-44624 PRIMARY Five things to know about the D.C. handgun ruling; What just changed about the city's main gun law? Can residents carry guns today? What might stop them? A federal judge has overturned the District's signature gun law, and while the city gears up to fight the ruling, D.C. police now say that they will not arrest people carrying registered handguns on the streets. Before you walk out your front door with your gun in your hand - or you start keeping a wary eye out for guns in your neighborhood - here's what you need to know. What was the law that was overturned by this decision? The law forbade carrying handguns in public places. A little bit of history: All the way from 1976, shortly after the District gained home rule, until 2008, handguns were banned entirely in the District. Then in a case called District of Columbia v. Heller, the Supreme Court ruled that individuals had a right to own handguns. The District came up with a new law that said people could own the guns, but not carry them in public. Now a federal judge has ruled that the ban on carrying handguns is also unconstitutional. I'm a District resident. Can I carry a gun in public now? If you own a registered gun, you can carry it. But that's not as likely in D.C. as in most states because existing D.C. laws makes it very hard for residents to obtain gun ownership licenses. The process is involved, time-consuming and expensive. Police officials said that since 2008, there have been 3,078 handguns registered in the District.

1274.text: GUN-6835 PRIMARY "I CAN'T BELIEVE I SHOT HER" GUN CONTROL THE nation got to see the National Rifle Association's much-touted conceal-carry law at work last week in the upper middle-class Birmingham, Ala., suburb of Alabaster. Gena Foster, 34, and Shirley Henson, 40 had been battling each other for four miles this particular Monday afternoon, jousting for position in heavy rush-hour traffic. After Ms. Foster cut off Ms. Henson, the two women played a cat-and-mouse game of tailgating, lane-changing and slamming on brakes until they got off at the same exit. Ms. Foster, the mother of three, was racing to pick up her 4-year-old daughter at an after-school program for children with cerebral palsy. Ms. Henson, a former Cub Scout den mother, was headed to her home in a quiet cul de sac. When the two cars came to a stop at a traffic light at the exit ramp, Ms. Foster jumped out and started toward Ms. Henson's vehicle. As Ms. Foster approached, Ms. Henson, lowered the window about halfway and reached for .38-caliber revolver she kept in the console. She had permission to carry the weapon under Alabama's conceal-carry law. Ms. Henson fired a single shot, striking Ms. Foster in the left cheek. Ms. Foster crumpled to the pavement and died. As Ms. Henson realized what she'd done, she said, "Oh my God, I can't believe I shot her." Law enforcement officials estimate that at least half of all motorists in the area around Birmingham keep firearms in their cars. "I expect there'll be more situations such as this because of heavy traffic and the guns being so prevalent," Alabaster Police Chief Larry Rollan said.

1276.text: NA-46154 PRIMARY Cagle backs 'opt in' on campus-carry guns bill Lt. Gov. Casey Cagle said Wednesday he will support giving college leaders a say on whether students may carry guns on campus. With a new version of a controversial gun bill expected to be filed Friday, Cagle sat down with The Atlanta Journal-Constitution to talk about why he favors an "opt in" approach to guns, how quickly he wants to privatize parts of Georgia's troubled foster care system and how to properly honor the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. On efforts to expand gun rights in Georgia: "I have had a strong view of the protection of the Second Amendment and the right to bear arms. I don't really back away from that. I think it's a fundamental right and we're proud of our record. The two most controversial issues (in the gun proposal) are those of 'campus carry' along with (allowing guns in) churches. Provided that there are the appropriate measures built around safety and the well-being and the concern of all, I could probably be comfortable with a campus carry approach. If an individual (such as a college or a church) wants to allow someone to be granted the right to carry under a concealed weapon permit, they should have that right. If they choose not to, then obviously the carry permit holder would have to respect that right. There's discussion of the 'opt in' or 'opt out' provisions, and I think giving that school or that university or that church that ability is something that has some merit."

1273.text: GUN-1369 PRIMARY Individual dealers cited in sales Jim Craig fought the gun-control law and the law won. For 18 years Craig and his family ran a thriving gun shop in Alamogordo, N.M. Then the government started passing gun-control laws and the news media started reporting how dangerous guns can be. ''People started bringing in guns for resale before the government comes and takes them,'' says Craig. The upshot: Craig and his 79-year-old mother, Sue - known for packing a pistol at their $ 750,000-a-year business - hung up their holsters and closed shop July 1. Now he runs an appliance brokerage. Craig and other gun shop owners say individual dealers, not shops, sell many of the weapons used in crimes. There are 30,000 storefront dealers licensed by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, compared with 240,000 licensed individuals who often work gun shows and flea markets across the USA. ''Why does any moron that can fill out that form immediately receive a license that makes him a federal firearms dealer?'' asks Bill Bridgewater, president of the National Alliance of Stocking Gun Dealers. ''Gun-control measures, such as a seven-day waiting period, only apply to legitimate store front dealers ... and push most of the gun trade into the black market,'' he says. The bureau's Dan Black agrees a federal gun license isn't difficult to obtain, but he says independents are not a major problem.

characters: National Alliance of Stocking Gun Dealers
Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1279.text: GUN-6889 PRIMARY With Eye on Charter, Mayor Refuses to Sign a Gun Bill In a political maneuver aimed at encouraging voters to approve changes to the City Charter in today's ballot referendum, Mayor Rudolph W. Giuliani refused yesterday to sign City Council legislation that would require gun owners in New York City to use safety-locking devices even though he believes the devices are important tools in preventing accidental shootings. Mr. Giuliani said he took the unusual step of withholding his signature from a bill that he essentially supported because it was nearly identical to one of the 14 changes proposed by his Charter Revision Commission. The Charter proposal would also make the so-called trigger locks mandatory, but it would provide for slightly stiffer penalties than the bill passed last month by the City Council. Under the Council bill and the Charter proposal, any first-time violator could face up to 10 days in jail and a fine up to $250. For a second-time offender, the Council bill calls for a maximum jail sentence of 30days and a fine up to $1,000, while the Charter proposal allows a sentence of six months and a fine of $5,000. For a third-time offender, the Charter proposal calls for a maximum jail sentence of a year and a fine up to $10,000.
