The Charter proposal would also make a violator of the law subject to a civil penalty up to $5,000. The Council bill does not provide another penalty level for third-time offenders or any civil penalty.

1280.text: GUN-7281 PRIMARY 46 GOPERS JOIN DEM PUSH FOR GUN CONTROL WASHINGTON - More than 40 House Republicans yesterday bucked pressure from the National Rifle Association and joined Democrats in urging Congress to hammer out a gun control bill. Hours before the vote was taken, President Clinton convened about three dozen members of Congress for a White House pep rally that fired up - and sometimes choked up - members from both parties. "Once again, the gun lobby and their allies in the leadership of the Congress are standing in the way of real progress. And once again, we battle not just for the safety of our families, but for the soundness of our democracy," Clinton said. Suzann Wilson, whose daughter was killed in a 1993 Jonesboro, Ark., shooting spree, said, "We're being held hostage by the NRA." Rep. Carolyn McCarthy (D-Nassau), whose husband was killed and son wounded on the Long Island Rail Road six years ago, said, "The moms of this country have had it. I'm tired of the lies. . . . We represent the people of the United States. The NRA does not." Clinton has been jousting all week with the NRA after its top lobbyist accused him of accepting a level of gun violence for political gain. But despite the NRA's strong-arm tactics, the House voted 218 to 205 for the bipartisan House and Senate Conference Committee to meet within the next two weeks to resolve differences on stalled gun control legislation.

1281.text: GUN-1238 PRIMARY Don't Trade a Good Law for Federal Money I was having considerable trouble attempting to follow the logic in The Post's Jan. 26 editorial "Gun Liability -- D.C. Liability." First, The Post calls the recent D.C. Assault Weapons Liability Act a "dud" and asks for its repeal partly on the basis of the national impact the law would have. Then, without missing a beat, The Post declares the necessity of gun laws with a national impact, the only stipulation being that these laws be enacted by Congress, which ignores that for the past 20 years, Congress has been anything but eager to pass gun laws. Even if scrapping the weapons bill would lead to more federal money, the idea is a dud. Trading District laws that some in Congress don't like for federal money makes no sense. What would be next? Should we outlaw abortion? How about bringing back the death penalty? Those two alone should be worth enough from Congress to ensure fiscal stability for years to come. Maybe if we got rid of rent control we'd be rich. Where would it end? Still, the most bizarre point The Post makes is when it voices its concern for Rep. Thomas J. Bliley's (R-Va.) constituents. It is true that some of the people he represents, the gun dealers who sell the majority of weapons used in District crimes, would be adversely effected by this law. Who cares? The Post and the city government should be more concerned about constituents in the District who are getting shot than they are about those across the river who are selling the murder weapons.

1284.text: GUN-12036 PRIMARY Mercury News editorial: Newtown tragedy inspires calls for stricter gun laws The horror of the Sept. 11 attacks on the World Trade Center did not quickly fade away -- still has not faded, really. America changed the way it protects itself after those attacks. And while Homeland Security sometimes overreaches, at least nobody can say we watched the twin towers fall and did nothing. In Newtown, Conn., and among parents of schoolchildren across the country, comparisons to 9/11 keep coming up. The wrenching pain of Friday's execution of 20 innocent children and of brave adults trying to protect them inspires the same transformative fear, a sense that no one is really safe. It's not just Newtown's child-survivors who will never see the world the same way again. Will Americans let this pass as they did Aurora, Oikos, Virginia Tech and all the other pointless slaughters of recent years without at least trying to do something to deter them? Will we again dry our tears, swallow our empty words and do nothing, absolutely nothing, to even try to temper the gun violence that horrifies the rest of the civilized world? In gun carnage, America truly is No. 1. This is the time to talk about it. Ignore the cynical posturing about a time of mourning. There is no better way to honor the dead of Sandy Hook school than to introduce legislation and begin a conversation about common sense regulation.

1277.text: GUN-2216 PRIMARY People, not guns, made America what it is Re: Message to the "Brady bunch," letter to the editor, Oct. 25. The letter writer not only has his wires crossed, he has them badly  tangled. The Brady bill, Gun Control Inc., or Handgun Control will in no way alter  the Second Amendment, although NRA leaders (one of the most subversive groups  in the United States) submit this view as their only opposition to gun  control. Basic logic would indicate lack of gun control does not protect U.S.  citizens from our government or armed forces - this is achieved in a much more  civilized manner. I refer the letter writer to The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich for an understanding of how and why Nazi Germany came into being - it certainly was not because German citizens did not have guns. Along the same vein, I suggest, the easy availability of automatic and combat-type weapons to subversive  groups (i.e., paramilitary, white supremacy, etc.) in this country could be detrimental to the quality of life in these United States, much more so than gun control. The murder rate in this country is approximately 20 times the rate in  Western European countries, England and Japan - countries where guns are not  as readily available. While no one believes gun control will eliminate crime  or keep criminals from having guns, they would be a limiting factor in this  area, as well as a positive step toward reducing accidental shootings,  moment-of-anger shootings and group killings by the mentally disturbed. Deaths  from these three types of shootings exceed 10,000 annually in the United  States.

1278.text: GUN-12154 PRIMARY Lawmakers Look to Boost Guns in School While the federal government and some states ponder more rigorous gun control laws after the shootings at a Connecticut elementary school, some Texas legislators have taken the opposite approach. They have emphasized what they see as a need to make firearms more available, and their discussions have included increasing access to guns for teachers and other school personnel. Ahead of the 83rd legislative session, bills have been proposed to support that mission. The efforts have drawn the praise of statewide elected officials, including Gov. Rick Perry. But even lawmakers who support arming school personnel disagree on how best to achieve it, and other critics say the debate is a distraction from a more difficult issue, state support for mental health, which could go farther toward improving school security. Currently, under the federal Gun-Free Schools Act and state law, Texas school boards may grant permission to anyone, including employees, to carry firearms on campus. Two Republicans, Representative-elect Jason Villalba of Dallas and Representative Debbie Riddle of Tomball, want to expand those rights. Mr. Villalba announced last week that he would file legislation modeled on the federal air marshal program that would deputize school employees with special training access to firearms as the last line of defense during an attack. He said it had been ''mischaracterized'' as a teacher-carry bill when in fact it provided a ''narrow and tailored solution'' that imposed harsh restrictions on a deputy's weapon-use authority. '' There's a significant difference from saying anyone who has a C.H.L. license can carry concealed in a classroom versus what I'm proposing,'' he said, referring to concealed handgun licenses.

1282.text: GUN-7688 PRIMARY Moms demand gun controls; In Washington and around the nation, demonstrators say 'we've had enough.' Washington --- Picking power over pampering for this Mother's Day, tens of thousands of moms and their families gathered in the nation's capital Sunday and demanded that Congress enact "common sense" gun control legislation. Pushing strollers, waving placards, prodding husbands, tending children and wearing T-shirts proclaiming their Million Mom March, a sea of women surged over several blocks between the Capitol and the Washington Monument. Organizers said the crowd numbered 500,000, but official estimates were unavailable. Recruited through car pools, play groups and over the Internet, the mothers' movement is pushing for tougher laws aimed at gun safety after a series of highly publicized shootings at schools across the country. The mostly suburban crowd came equipped with cell phones, sunscreen, backpacks and catchy slogans: "No child's life should end with a bang," declared one sign. "Real men don't need guns," read a popular button. "We have had enough," said Rosie O'Donnell, the television talk show host who emceed the rally. She said voting moms will overcome the influential gun lobby led by the National Rifle Association. "Politicians, take heed. We are watching you," warned Dawn Anna, mother of Lauren Townsend, a student killed in the Columbine High School shooting last year. "The hands that rock the cradles rule the world." Supporters held similar rallies for gun control in scores of cities around the country. For many activists, though, the Washington march became a Mother's Day outing. Under bright sunshine and a cloudless blue sky, three generations of some families called for tighter controls on firearms.

1283.text: NA-41071 PRIMARY Defeat NRA or become its prey THERE IS A MOVIE out right now about New York politics called "Broken City," starring Russell Crowe and Mark Wahlberg, at a time when we all know the real broken city is Washington, D.C., know that despite all the ceremonies of the inauguration, and despite all the brave talk from the President about his second term. You saw it with guns even as the second term officially began. Already you see members of the President's own party running away from a ban on large-capacity magazines and assault weapons when that should be the easy part of this, for any politician with real guts, that is. Somehow at a time when every poll involving reasonable people and not mouth-breathing gun nuts shows that the country wants more gun control, some Democrats act as if they're the ones staring down the barrel of a gun. It is why you wonder if Washington will ever catch up with New York on this issue. The governor of the state, Andrew Cuomo, is trying to show the way on guns the way he did on marriage equality, as he continues to be the most effective political leader in this country at this time. "We have to be in the business of getting these changes (on gun control) done," Cuomo is saying late Saturday afternoon, five days after he has signed the most sweeping gun control measures in America into law, five weeks after the slaughter of 20 children at an elementary school in Newtown, Conn.

1285.text: GUN-11720 PRIMARY Investments in gun makers under review Some public-worker retirement funds consider divesting after Connecticut massacre. PUBLIC SAFETY ALBANY, N.Y. o From California to New York, teacher and public-worker retirement funds are reconsidering their investments in gun makers and confronting an uncomfortable fact: Their pensions have supported the manufacture of deadly weapons, in some cases the same type of gun used in the Connecticut school shooting. For years, the gun industry has been a reliable investment, attracting tens of millions of dollars from some of the nation's largest retirement funds. The firearms business has been strong, driven by relaxed laws for carrying concealed handguns and by buyers who feared that tighter gun restrictions were more likely under President Barack Obama. But after the bloodbath in Connecticut, the practice is under review in at least four states, including two of the most populous, California and New York. Although the amount of money involved is relatively small compared with the size of the pension funds, it has raised questions about the social responsibilities of huge retirement systems that invest on behalf of millions of American workers. "It's a bad investment to put money behind companies that put military-grade weapons on our streets and refuse to take responsibility for the outcome," said New York City Public Advocate Bill de Blasio. "We should not be giving capital to an industry that is responsible for the deaths of thousands of Americans each year. ... It's our moral responsibility."

1287.text: NA-46164 PRIMARY Felons' gun rights shrouded in secrecy by parole board When Georgia's parole board considers restoring a convicted felon's firearms rights, it doesn't discuss the case in public. Or in private. Each board member votes in isolation, and in secrecy. The five members rarely, if ever, deliberate as a body over restoring offenders' gun rights. They keep no public record of their votes. And they give neither public notice nor public explanation of decisions that enable felons to re-arm --- not even to the felons' victims. Georgia's process for restoring firearms rights to convicted felons, even those who used guns to commit violent crimes, is among the most secretive in the nation, almost entirely unregulated by the state's open government laws, an investigation by The Atlanta Journal-Constitution has found. The Board of Pardons and Paroles asserts that virtually all information in such cases is a "confidential state secret." Why? The board won't say. Through a spokesman, board members declined to be interviewed. They also directed senior staff members not to comment. Some former board members, however, suggest that the process is overly secretive and open to abuse. "There needs to be scrutiny," said a former board member who spoke on condition of anonymity to avoid harming relationships with current members. "There needs to be an opportunity to see if the information they're relying on is accurate. What is the rationale for not releasing that information?"

1289.text: NA-45992 PRIMARY Make us safer or get sent home; If Congress won't change gun laws, we'll have to change Congress one vote at a time A year ago today, the United States Senate failed us. It was last April -- mere months after the senseless slaughter of 20 children at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. -- that a minority of senators blocked legislation that would have made it harder for criminals and people with dangerous mental illnesses to get their hands on deadly firearms. Given the opportunity to stand up to the gun lobby by supporting legislation favored by two-thirds of Americans, these senators did what all politicians do when they fear upsetting powerful interests: They voted for the status quo. These were some of the same senators who had sat across the table from the grieving parents of children massacred at Sandy Hook or who looked me in the eye as I told them what it is like to learn to walk and talk again after a bullet from a madman's gun nearly took my life. Long fight ahead I say now what I said then: There is more work to do. So today, I am not wasting my time focusing on this single setback in what we have always known would be a long, hard haul. I am looking to a safer future: I was in Mobile, Ala., for the keel-laying ceremony of the USS Gabrielle Giffords on Wednesday, a Navy vessel that, once completed, will help protect our country. It will be tough, steely and ready to do the hard things, the same qualities that we should expect from our leaders in Washington.

1286.text: GUN-1750 PRIMARY Congress Still Torn on Gun Control by Complex Regional Divisions The gun control debate on Capitol Hill has evolved into an annual political fight that is a more reliable barometer of the divisions between urban and rural interests than of those between Republicans and Democrats. This week, the murders of 23 people in Killeen, Tex., cast a new pall over the debate, as members of the House agonized aloud over gun provisions in a sweeping anticrime bill that would have banned the manufacture of certain semiautomatic firearms. "For me, suddenly all the old arguments ring hollow," said Representative Chet Edwards, Democrat of Texas. Mr. Edwards, whose district includes Killeen, said he had always opposed gun control. But on Thursday, he voted in favor of a ban on the sale and ownership of some semiautomatic weapons and the type of multiple-bullet gun clips that were used in Wednesday's rampage in the Killeen cafeteria. Complex Regional Divisions The House, torn by more complex regional divisions, rejected the provision, in a 247-to-177 vote that reflected all of the shadings of the dispute over gun control. "A smart elected official knows that, except for the community and the family that has to live with it, that event is in political terms is ephemeral," said Representative Charles E. Schumer, Democrat of Brooklyn, referring to the Killeen shootings. "Whereas the N.R.A. is going to be there every day until September." The fight is a different one on the state and local level. All 50 states have placed some limitations on the sale or manufacture of weapons over the years, but they vary widely in their effect.

1288.text: GUN-10481 PRIMARY Bill opens Atlanta airport to firearms Lawmakers in Georgia have approved a bill that would allow gun owners to carry their licensed firearms at parts of Atlanta Hartsfield, despite the airport's vigorous opposition. The legislation, which is waiting for Gov. Sonny Perdue's signature, would permit carrying of firearms in areas that are not controlled by the federal government, such as terminals and parking lots. It expands on a state law passed in 2008 that allows Georgia residents with firearm licenses to bring concealed weapons onto public transportation, in parks and recreational areas and into restaurants that serve alcohol. Gun advocates have since been lobbying to expand the law to include the airport. The case is being closely watched by airport officials nationwide. *The head of the agency that runs New York LaGuardia called for demolishing the airport, which serves about 26 million travelers in one of the busiest air markets in the country. Port Authority of New York & New Jersey Executive Director Chris Ward said last week that the airport's facilities are too old and its current capital plan calls for only modest upgrades that aren't sufficient, according to Reuters. The Port Authority confirmed Ward's statements. The airport offers a "chaotic" experience and should be rebuilt when money is available, he said. "LaGuardia should not be the gateway for fliers into New York City. It should fundamentally be torn down and rebuilt again," he said.

characters: Port Authority of New York & New Jersey

1290.text: GUN-8164 PRIMARY Clinton announces 'Net checks for gun dealers WASHINGTON - President Clinton, acting to curb illegal gun  sales, said Saturday the government is turning to the Internet to  make it harder to acquire weapons via computer or mail by using  fake firearms licenses. 'Unfortunately, the Internet, despite all its benefits, is  making it easier for guns to fall into the wrong hands. There are  now 4,000 firearm sales-related sites on the Internet, and there  are 80 sites where you can actually buy a gun at an auction,'  Clinton said in his weekly radio address, broadcast before he left  for a political trip in California. 'Clearly, we must do more to ensure that every sale over the  Internet is legal and that no one uses the anonymity of cyberspace  to evade our nation's gun laws.' The government has set up a Web site, known as eZ Check, for  online federal firearms license authentication. Under current law, licensed dealers can ship firearms only to  other licensed dealers. Sellers are required to obtain a certified copy of the federal  firearms license of the individual buying the guns. Criminals,  however, have gotten around this requirement by using forged  licenses. The Web site, created by the Treasury Department and its  Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, will allow licensed gun  dealers to quickly verify the validity of licenses presented for  purchase or shipment of guns. Over the past several years, ATF has documented a sizable  illegal market in guns fed by corrupt federally licensed dealers,  straw purchasers and unlicensed gun sellers, Deputy Treasury  Secretary Stuart Eizenstat said.

1291.text: GUN-8866 PRIMARY A Mix of Fear and Fascination; For Gun Fans, Sniper Also Raises Threat of New Controls Dave Onks III, standing a few yards away from a paper target that he had nicely peppered with bullet holes, said he knows that his .38-caliber Smith & Wesson revolver is no match for a rifle in the hands of sniper who can zero in on him from much farther away. But Onks wants a pistol at his side just the same. Because of the deadly string of shootings, Onks has decided to apply for a license to carry a concealed weapon. "I think carrying a gun . . . in general makes me feel safer," said Onks, 21. Onks was taking target practice Friday at The Range in Stafford County, just off Route 1 and not far from the scene of that day's fatal shooting in Spotsylvania County of a Philadelphia man as he was pumping gas. Onks said he signed up recently for a training course as a step toward obtaining the permit. In sporting goods shops and shooting ranges, talk inevitably has turned to whether more people will acquire weapons. But gun enthusiasts are also fascinated by the sniper: his marksmanship, the weapon he uses and what it might take to stop him -- or them. They even wonder whether he has been there to buy ammunition. They also said they worry that the sniper shootings would renew calls for tighter gun control.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not explicitly mentioned)

1293.text: GUN-11364 PRIMARY The Human Cost of the Second Amendment Wisconsin, Aurora, Virginia Tech, Columbine. We all know these place names and what happened there. By the time this column appears, there may well be a new locale to add to the list. Such is the state of enabled and murderous mayhem in the United States. With the hope of presenting the issue of guns in America in a novel way, I'm going to look at it from an unusual vantage point: the eyes of a nurse. By that I mean looking at guns in America in terms of the suffering they cause, because to really understand the human cost of guns in the United States we need to focus on gun-related pain and death. Every day 80 Americans die from gunshots and an additional 120 are wounded, according to a 2006 article in The Journal of Policy Analysis and Management. Those 80 Americans left their homes in the morning and went to work, or to school, or to a movie, or for a walk in their own neighborhood, and never returned. Whether they were dead on arrival or died later on in the hospital, 80 people's normal day ended on a slab in the morgue, and there's nothing any of us can do to get those people back. In a way that few others do, I became aware early on that nurses deal with death on a daily basis. The first unretouched dead bodies I ever saw were the two cadavers we studied in anatomy lab. One man, one woman, both donated their bodies for dissection, and I learned amazing things from them: the sponginess of lung tissue, the surprising lightness of a human heart, the fabulous intricacy of veins, arteries, tendons and nerves that keep all of us moving and alive.

1294.text: GUN-3862 PRIMARY NIXON SEEKS TO OUTLAW GUNS IN SCHOOLYARDS Jay Nixon, the Missouri attorney general, is asking state legislators to quickly pass a law making it illegal to have a gun in a schoolyard. The law is needed, Nixon said Friday, because of Wednesday's U.S. Supreme Court decision striking down the 1990 Gun-Free School Zones Act. The act had made it a federal crime to have a gun within 1,000 feet of a school. Missouri law makes it illegal to take a gun into a school, but it does not prohibit a weapon on the playground, bus or parking lot, Nixon said. Nixon said a new law could "strike a balance between the need to know our children are not in harm's way at school and the right of law-abiding citizens to bear firearms."

1298.text: NA-41849 PRIMARY Toomey is open to reviving background-check bill WASHINGTON - Sen. Pat Toomey (R., Pa.) hasn't given up on his bill to expand background checks for gun purchases, he said Thursday, signaling more willingness to return to the plan than he had publicly shown before. "If we could find a way to bring some more folks on board, then of course it would be worth bringing it up again," Toomey said in an interview. While he has largely returned his focus to the fiscal issues he has long been associated with, he said he still occasionally talked privately with fellow senators about the background-check bill that was defeated last month, and about potential changes that might win new support. But he has not heard of any opponents willing to switch their votes, he said, leaving the bill stuck short. "I've had occasional conversations with my colleagues really ever since the vote," Toomey said. "There's nobody that I'm aware of that has indicated that they would change their vote." Toomey stepped to the center of the charged debate on guns in April, when he cosponsored the plan to expand background checks only to see the bill fall six votes short of the 60 needed to overcome a filibuster threat. "I would like to be able to persuade enough senators to change their votes to get to the 60 votes," Toomey said, "but I'm not aware of any senator that voted no and would now change that vote and vote yes."

1295.text: GUN-3304 PRIMARY WILL CARTER'S NEW PAL GIVE SUNDAY SERMON? I will be forever grateful to Jimmy Carter for clarifying something that has been troubling me for quite some time now. I admit that I was at a loss to fathom how human beings could treat each other so abysmally as to engage in ethnic cleansing, concentration camps and the mass slaughter of innocents. Now I see that the Serbs were simply "misunderstood" here in the States. I suppose we always overlook the simple answers. I recall that during his mission to Haiti, Carter found Gen. Raoul Cedras -- the overseer of 5,000 civilian deaths -- morally worthy of an invitation to teach Sunday school. Surely Radovan Karadzic will be delivering the Sunday sermon at Carter's church sometime in the near future. -- Paul George Palo Alto Pro-law enforcement equals pro-gun control The recent articles on the public perception of crime in Santa Clara County indicated that the greatest factor contributing to people's fear was the availability of guns. The public is right. As a deputy district attorney, I have seen countless cases of violent crime that would not have occurred if a handgun had not been easily available. It is standard wisdom in the law enforcement community that the level of violent crime would be significantly lowered if we could control handguns. Conversely, buying a gun is not the smart response to the fear of crime. Law enforcement experience shows us that such guns are very rarely used for protection. A bad result such as an accidental shooting, suicide, gun theft or actual use in committing a crime is far more likely to happen.

1296.text: GUN-8497 PRIMARY Seattle Scrambles to Find a Prosecutor's Assassin As a prosecutor in the United States attorney's office here for 18 years, specializing in fraud and white-collar crime, and as president of a statewide gun-control group, Thomas C. Wales certainly made some enemies. But so far, even as the police and federal authorities comb through his case files and other details of his life, none of it has helped answer the question, Who gunned down Tom Wales? Mr. Wales, 49, was killed by a sniper's gun late Thursday night as he worked in an office at his home on a quiet street in Seattle's upscale Queen Anne neighborhood. The police say robbery was not the motive. The shots were fired from outside his window; a neighbor said she saw a man running away and then speeding off in a car. The contract-style killing has shocked people here and set off a hunt for clues that is consuming both the local police chief, who was a friend of Mr. Wales's, and F.B.I. officials. So far, the authorities are "pleased with the progress that detectives are making on the case," Duane Fish, a police spokesman, said today, but are "not going to comment on the direction it's going in." In the absence of suspects or evidence disclosed to the public, there is only speculation about possible motives involving Mr. Wales's work as a prosecutor and gun-control advocate. (Though the prosecutor was involved in a minor accident with a rental limousine last summer, and was issued a traffic citation for leaving the scene, investigators said they strongly doubted the incident had any connection to the shooting.)

1297.text: GUN-4687 PRIMARY CONCEALED WEAPONS AND THE CRIME RATE For the third or fourth year in a row, gun nuts in the General Assembly are pushing a bill to put more guns on our streets. Almost certainly they will defend this outrage by claiming "law-abiding citizens" need guns to protect themselves from . . . well, from something not apparent to the rest of us. Normal citizens don't believe the dangers of modern society are so great everyone needs a gun, but gun nuts do. Allowing them to force their delusions on the community would be a grave mistake. The perennial campaign to allow gun-carrying by common citizens is the product of nothing other than irrationality. By any reasoned analysis, even gun nuts would have to admit they are at greater risk from the guns they love than from any injury their guns prevent. Maybe they're 43 times more likely to be killed by the gun they keep than they are to use it to kill an attacker, as one study found, or maybe it's not that much. No matter. More guns mean more gun deaths, and international comparisons prove it. In the industrialized world, countries with permissive gun laws have higher rates of gun death, and countries with restrictive gun laws experience lower rates of gun death. The conclusion: We don't need more guns. According to gun nuts, everyone is in danger of deadly assault anywhere, anytime. The danger may come from the drug-crazed mugger they believe lurks around every corner, a conspiracy of federal law-enforcement officers to oppress "patriotic" Americans or an invasion by the Russian, Chinese and/or European forces of the New World Order, 3 million of which, by the way, are said to be massing in Canada at this very moment. According to gun nuts, real Americans must always have their guns handy to resist these and any other threat they may face.

1299.text: GUN-11481 PRIMARY NO: State gun laws do the job After Columbine, Colorado enacted eight gun law reforms. All are founded on the principle that guns in the wrong hands are very dangerous, and guns in the right hands protect public safety. One of the new laws forbids adults to transfer guns to minors without parental consent. Another prohibits straw purchases (a legal buyer obtaining a gun on behalf of a prohibited person). Today, guns are the most stringently regulated consumer product in the United States, the only product for which every single purchase from a store requires permission from the FBI or its state counterpart, such as the Colorado Bureau of Investigation. In 2000, Colorado went even further, enacting special rules for background checks at gun shows. The post-Columbine laws that aim to keep guns out of the wrong hands are complemented by new laws that protect the self-defense rights of the law-abiding: repealing an old law which had authorized the governor to ban gun sales during an emergency (when guns would be needed most); preventing localities from interfering with the transportation of guns in automobiles; limiting localities' power to enact anti-gun laws; and forbidding suits against firearms manufacturers because of gun misuse by criminals. The most important reform was the 2003 Concealed Carry Act. The act provides for issuance of handgun carry permits to adults who pass a 10-point fingerprint background check and a safety training class. The law affirms a sheriff's discretion to deny a permit to someone who has a clean record but whose documented conduct shows that he would be a danger to himself or others.

1300.text: GUN-8521 PRIMARY Judge Rejects Montgomery Gun Show Restriction A U.S. District Court judge yesterday derailed Montgomery County's efforts to end gun shows held for the past decade at fairgrounds in Gaithersburg, ruling that the county cannot enforce its new gun show law within that city's borders. County Executive Douglas M. Duncan (D) plans to appeal the ruling, spokesman David Weaver said. "We are going to defend our right to regulate firearms," Weaver said. In his ruling, U.S. District Court Judge Marvin J. Garbis did not address broader issues about whether the county's law violated the right to assemble people of similar viewpoints or to engage in commercial speech protected by the U.S. Constitution. He did, however, say the county and gun show promoter Frank Krasner had raised "serious questions" -- questions that could be taken up in the appeal. For 10 years, Krasner's Silverado Promotions has held gun shows at the Montgomery County Fairgrounds Agricultural Center in Gaithersburg. After an outcry from gun control advocates, the County Council passed legislation in May restricting where gun shows could be held and imposing new regulations on gun show promoters. But because state law prohibits the county from imposing such regulations on municipalities such as Gaithersburg, the County Council added another provision it hoped would end Krasner's shows. The fairgrounds are privately owned but have received grants from the county. To pressure the fairgrounds' board, the council cut off county funding for any group that allows the display and sale of guns on its property.

1304.text: GUN-5078 PRIMARY NRA'S STOCK ANSWERS FALL SHORT THIS TIME Iwonder how Charlton Heston and his National Rifle Association buddies feel about the shooting in Arkansas. They oppose any gun control legislation, saying, ''Don't take our guns - take them from the hands of criminals,'' yet they oppose background checks. They have programs teaching children how to shoot and handle guns and are proud of it. Would they let their children drive their family car? The manufacturers of guns and the NRA are particularly to blame for the tragic killings at the school in Arkansas. If we had stricter gun control laws, the killings of those innocent children may never have happened. Today, we have road rage involving guns; disgruntled employees shooting their bosses; unhappy wives, girlfriends, husbands and boyfriends shooting each other; students taking guns to school to shoot other students and teachers; even people unhappy with our government shooting at presidents. Many of them were not criminals until they did their shooting. Does the NRA have any answers as to how we can stop this bloodshed? Vincent Lombardo Boynton Beach

1292.text: GUN-46 PRIMARY Internatl Assn of Police Chiefs meeting, Anaheim, Calif, devotes seminars to rising incidence of violence in sts and killing of policemen by 'senseless and unprovoked' gunfire; their concern underscored by FBI rept showing while violent crimes in nation had increased 11% in '71, killing of law enforcement officers had risen 20%; in their inquiries into 'psychological climate that accepts guns and violence,' police officials agree on strong corrective measures but divide on proposal for tighter Fed gun control laws, including regis; instead, they criticize leniency of cts, prosecutors and probation officers, failure of prison rehabilitation, growing lack of pub understanding and support and Chief J R Shrylock's attack on news media for their portraying of criminals as heroes; Shrylock is assn's pres; NYC Police Comr Murphy, in appeal for more stringent gun controls as means of denying weapons to criminals, outlines proposal for Fed gun regis; proposal detailed; would outlaw importation, manufacture, sale or possession of cheap 'Saturday night special' pistols, put Fed quotas on domestic manufacture and import of all handguns, require demonstration of 'good cause' before purchase of such weapon and establish natl regis of handguns in addition to machine guns, sawed off shotguns and rifles as required under '68 Fed Gun Control Act; Repr Murphy offers new bill for Fed gun permits and regis

1301.text: GUN-2061 PRIMARY BRADY TOUTS GUN LIMITS IN VISIT HERE; FORMER REAGAN AIDE SEEKS SENATORS' HELP Gun control activist James S. Brady joined St. Louis area police and emergency-room workers Thursday in urging Missouri's U.S. senators to support the gun legislation that carries Brady's name.   It could be a tough sell, because Sens. John C. Danforth and Christopher S.  Bond, both Republicans, have voted against the measure.   At a news conference at the St. Louis County Police Academy in Wellston, Brady said co-sponsoring the bill would be "the very best thing that senators Danforth and Bond can do for the families of slain citizens in St. Louis." The Brady Bill would require background checks of handgun purchasers within five working days. Missouri already has a waiting period, but 24 states do not. Bond and Danforth have opposed further federal restrictions on handguns and prefer tougher penalties for criminals.   A record number of Congress members - 99 in the House and 31 in the Senate - have signed on as co-sponsors of the bill that was reintroduced this year.   President Bill Clinton said in February that he would sign it.   Brady, former White House press secretary, was shot in the head in 1981 in an assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan. He remains physically impaired by brain damage.   Diana G. Fendya, trauma nurse specialist at Cardinal Glennon Children's Hospital, said the number of children treated at the hospital for gun-related injuries has increased 100 percent each year for the past three years.   "How can we say we live in a civilized society when we allow young children to be maimed and killed by the muzzle of a gun?" she asked. "The Brady Bill offers a beginning to control a situation that has gotten out of hand." One young man in the audience, Reginald Montgomery, shook Brady's hand and wished him luck.   Montgomery, 17, and a few other students from Eskridge High School in Wellston were at the press conference.   Montgomery says he knows one boy who died and another whose face was partly paralyzed from shootings.   "It's getting very bad," he said. "It's getting tougher and tougher for people to walk to the stores these days."

1303.text: GUN-8122 PRIMARY Gun-check issue to stay on ballot High court backs judge's decision The initiative to close the so-called gun show loophole will  stay on the ballot, the Colorado Supreme Court ruled Tuesday. The court's decision means the initiative that asks voters to  require background checks for anyone buying a gun at a gun show  now has survived six legal challenges from gun rights groups. All  have been dismissed by the courts. 'The gun lobby uses lawsuits the way a carpenter uses a  hammer,' said John Head, co-president of SAFE Colorado, the group  that placed the initiative on the Nov. 7 ballot. ' Except every  time they take a swing, they miss the nail.' The state's gun lobby has run out of challenges it can make  to the ballot question before Election Day, SAFE Colorado attorney  Mark Grueskin said. 'They're all out,' he said. 'It's a dead end.' The most recent challenge came from Ari Armstrong, a member  of the Rocky Mountain Gun Owners. He claimed the initiative should  not be on the ballot because most of the signatures required to  place it there were collected illegally. A Denver district judge ruled against Armstrong on Sept. 14,  and he appealed to the state's highest court. 'This is disappointing but not surprising,' Armstrong said. About 90,000 of SAFE Colorado's 110,000 signatures shouldn't  count, Armstrong said. He arrived at his figure by subtracting all the signatures  gathered before July 3, the day the Supreme Court ruled against  earlier challenges filed by his organization.

1302.text: GUN-10897 PRIMARY Inquirer Editorial: Gun-safety talk is cheap It will take more than the talk therapy proposed by President Obama if gun-safety reforms are ever going to result from the Arizona shooting that left six people dead and 13 wounded, including Tucson-area Congresswoman Gabrielle Giffords. In a commentary article published Sunday in an Arizona newspaper, the president called for "the beginning of a new discussion" on gun-safety measures in the wake of the tragedy. Jared Lee Loughner, 22, stands accused in the January shootings. Before even a day had passed, though, the National Rifle Association snubbed the president's invitation to sit down with gun-control advocates, whom NRA chief Wayne LaPierre described as having spent "a lifetime trying to destroy the Second Amendment." The NRA head did acknowledge that there was a need to have "a dialogue about dangerous people." Indeed, the president described that as the main challenge posed by horrific shootings like those in Tucson: to crack down on gun sales to the mentally unstable, and people with violent and criminal pasts. Obama's approach could work: He would provide incentives to states to improve their reporting of data on individuals who would be a risk if armed, especially the mentally ill. Ten states haven't submitted any information on unstable individuals, and 28 have added fewer than 100 mental-health records to a federal database. As a result, gun buyers who later prove violent are able to secure weapons despite troubled pasts.

1305.text: GUN-2251 PRIMARY Session on Guns Produces Little but Discord in Albany There was plenty of stagecraft: angry hunters, a table of menacing assault weapons, a legislator who flew from the Caribbean to snowbound Albany to cast his vote. But there was little action today as the Legislature met, in a session ordered by Gov. Mario M. Cuomo, to consider laws against gun violence on Martin Luther King Jr. Day. The Governor and lawmakers in both parties did not get far beyond accusing each other of playing politics, with the crime issue as well as with the memory of Dr. King. The Assembly, led by the Democrats, passed eight bills, including legislation that would sharply restrict the rapid-firing assault weapons. It was the second year in a row the Assembly passed an assault-weapons bill. But the Republican-controlled Senate did not act on any anti-crime legislation, deflating hopes for a negotiated agreement today. As each side sought to shift the blame, party leaders met this morning for nearly an hour with Mr. Cuomo at his mansion, legislative aides said. The Governor was described by Republicans and Democrats alike as seeming eager for compromise on an assault-weapons bill. Aides on both sides said any break on the issue would have to come from Mr. Cuomo, who has his own political stake because he has pushed so hard for anti-crime legislation this election year. Looking to the Governor "How successful we are now in getting a tough assault-weapons ban through both houses is dependent on the leadership the Governor shows with the Senate," said an Assembly aide, who spoke on the condition of anonymity.

1307.text: GUN-5964 PRIMARY Concealed weapon applicants rise ; OK with police: Law enforcement officials say gun owners who play by rules seldom a problem, deter some criminals. Even as the national gun debate intensifies after the school shootings in Colorado and Rockdale County, the approaching millennium is apparently fueling an increase in applications for permits to carry concealed handguns in Georgia. Tens of thousands of Georgians already have licenses to carry, and for the most part law enforcement authorities don't see it as a problem.   "The people that go through all the effort to get a pistol permit seldom get into any trouble with the police," Fulton County Police Maj. Terry Mulkey said. Probate court clerks, who take applications for those interested in carrying concealed guns, say that the demand for them is increasing in the metro area --- and the most common reason they're hearing is Y2K. Melinda Carter, who handles the applications in Cobb County probate court, said she processes between 3,500 and 4,000 applications a year. "We're real busy with them right now," she said. "Quite a few people who come in are talking about the millennium." In DeKalb, Probate Court Clerk Mary Partain said her office handled 2,826 new applications and renewals in 1998. She said the deputy clerk who handles the applications "is telling me every day that it's busier, and a lot of people are telling her they thought they ought to be prepared for Y2K." Gun dealers say the approaching century mark has boosted sales somewhat, but not to the extent of the current gun control furor in Congress.

1311.text: GUN-11226 PRIMARY Let's have informed consent on guns Regarding the Feb. 29 Metro article "Virginia Senate passes modified ultrasound bill" and the Feb. 29 Local Digest item about the governor signing a bill ending the one-a-month handgun restriction: Too bad that before voting to repeal the one-handgun-a-month law, members of the Virginia General Assembly weren't subjected to the same "informed consent" they want to impose upon women seeking abortions. It's a shame the legislators weren't required to sit face to face with the parents of young men and women who'd been gunned down on the street, in a school or university, or during a drive-by. The legislators should have been made to listen as the mothers and fathers recounted the moment they learned they'd never see their children's faces again and how they've endured the birthdays, holidays and memories ever since. Both pieces of legislation make one thing abundantly clear: Virginia's lawmakers really care about children. That is, until they're born. Corinne Dufka, Rockville

1306.text: GUN-30453 PRIMARY Aboard the buses from Harrisburg, a sense that their message was heard; "I think they know they will be out of office if they don't do something soon," said one rider. On one of the many buses returning to Philadelphia early yesterday evening from the antiviolence rally in Harrisburg, the 49 passengers agreed their message had grabbed the attention of state lawmakers. Enough is enough. Lance Haver, the city's consumer advocate, told the riders, many of whom had arisen before sunrise to board one of 35 buses for the round trip to the state Capitol: "This rally was larger than the one protesting legislative pay raises. This was a success, and you should be proud." Jennifer Perry, 49, a homemaker from North Philadelphia, said the rally, the speakers, and the show of solidarity by more than 2,000 antiviolence activists "opened my eyes, because I have two teenage boys. I'll tell them, 'When you get angry, you don't have to pick up a gun.' " Asked whether she thought the message that gun violence was killing and wounding young people in Philadelphia would resonate with lawmakers, Perry said, "I believe so. I really do." Yesterday's event was organized by the Pennsylvania Coalition to End Handgun Violence. The group's mission is to stop the illegal flow of handguns and to urge legislators to enact more effective laws to curb gun crime. Mayor Street, along with New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg and mayors from cities across Pennsylvania, called on lawmakers to change current gun laws, to stop straw purchases and to allow only one gun purchase a month.

characters: Pennsylvania Coalition to End Handgun Violence

1309.text: GUN-6670 PRIMARY Top firm quitting gun shows Ruger wants to confine firearm sales to stores The nation's largest gun manufacturer is saying no to gun  shows. In year-2000 contracts with its gun distributors, Sturm,  Ruger & Co. has stipulated that its shotguns, rifles and handguns  should be supplied only 'to federally licensed firearms dealers  selling exclusively from their regular place of business.' The policy drew praise Friday from a New York gun distributor  who has notified about 5,000 firearms dealers that Ruger no longer  wants its guns sold at the nation's weekend gun shows. But the  restriction was denounced by Colorado's largest firearms dealer,  who questioned why customers who pass background checks should be  treated differently at gun shows than at gun stores. Sturm, Ruger has made and sold more than 13 million guns  since it was founded 50 years ago in Southport, Conn., and it  ranked as the top gun manufacturer in the United States last year. The company's new gun-show policy provides fresh evidence  that even as gun-control issues remain stalled in Congress,  manufacturers are changing their practices in response to public  pressures and the risks of litigation. Davis Industries, a California manufacturer that specialized  in cheap handguns, filed for bankruptcy protection last summer in  response to municipal lawsuits against the industry. Colt  Industries announced last fall that it was sharply curtailing its  production of handguns. Now, Ruger is believed to be the first gun  manufacturer to discourage sales of its products at gun shows.

1308.text: GUN-5119 PRIMARY IN AREA WHERE 5 DIED, GUNS AREN'T THE ISSUE / THE SCHOOL-YARD AMBUSH HASN'T SHAKEN THE BELIEF IN THE RIGHT TO OWN FIREARMS. On Tuesday, when 11-year-old Andrew Golden wanted some guns, he broke into his grandfather's house and took three rifles, four handguns, and several boxes of ammunition. The boy, charged along with 13-year-old Mitchell Johnson in the deadly shooting at Westside Middle School, could have gotten similar weaponry from almost any home here. In this community, guns are as common as Bibles. And the deadly school attack, in which four students and a teacher were killed, has not shaken Jonesboro's belief in the right to own firearms. "This is a part of the country where it's unusual if a child doesn't grow up going out with dad and granddad to go hunting, and maybe using some very powerful weapons," said Bill Sadler, an Arkansas State Police spokesman. "People enjoy their hunting privileges, and they say they don't want something like this to ruin those privileges." Here, father-son deer hunts are a rite of passage, with children too young to drive toting shotguns into the woods. Many people own not one gun, but five or six. And they don't blame guns for the tragedy that has left them planning five funerals.. "The problem is not guns," said local criminal-defense attorney David Rees, dismissing suggestions that tighter firearms laws could have prevented the killings. "That's a real easy answer." Even the parents and relatives of the four girls and one teacher killed Tuesday had little to say about gun control at a meeting with prosecutors Wednesday evening.

1312.text: GUN-9774 PRIMARY Police group endorses McCollum One of Florida's largest police organizations is endorsing former U.S. Rep. Bill McCollum in the wide-open Republican primary for U.S. Senate. Leaders of the Fraternal Order of Police said Wednesday they like McCollum because of his 20 years of experience in Congress, including the chairmanship of the House subcommittee on crime, and his willingness to listen to police concerns. "He has the experience, he has the knowledge, he has the wisdom of a junior senator," said Ron Bartholome, FOP first vice president. With more than 23,000 members, the state FOP says it's the largest group in Florida representing sworn officers. The rival Police Benevolent Association makes the same claim and says it has more than 30,000 members statewide. The PBA represents police and correctional officers in the prison system, who are not sworn law enforcement officers. McCollum, 59, is one of eight Republicans seeking the nomination to succeed Democrat Bob Graham, who is retiring after three terms. The FOP supports McCollum despite a significant difference on a gun control issue. The FOP  favors extending a federal ban on certain assault weapons such as Uzis and AK-47s. The ban was enacted in 1994 and is due to end in September. Many gun control groups and police chiefs are lobbying for it to be extended. McCollum told reporters the assault weapons ban got caught up in politics and in definitions over what constituted an assault weapon.

1313.text: GUN-5649 PRIMARY Saturday Talk; ONLINE In last week's Conversation Sarter, we asked readers what they think about tougher gun policies in metro Atlanta schools. Here are some responses. You can have your say online at http://www.accessatlanta.com/community/talk Here are excerpts from online responses Are tough gun policies warranted at schools? Allowing school districts to focus on education is a good start. Eliminating guns from school is the quickest and cheapest way to lower the rate of violent crime in school. I find it upsetting that many conservatives and NRA supporters will protest this policy in order to support the ability of a 12-year-old to have target practice during his lunch period. Marc Ross, Atlanta I definitely agree with Cobb County's decision to expel permanently a student who brings a gun to school. It is time to have zero tolerance on some things, and this is one. Today's permissive climate must be reversed. Too many people blame their actions on other circumstances and have no respect for rules, regulations or authority. If people can't handle the responsibility, then they should be punished accordingly or lose their freedom. Carl N. Umstead, DeKalb County To all those who think expelling students who bring guns to school has anything to do with Second Amendment rights: Please think again. Georgia law states that people the age of our high school students are forbidden (without supervision of an adult) from possessing a firearm . . . in or out of school. Enacting a law that seeks to expel these law-breakers is both in the spirit and in the letter of the Bill of Rights.

1310.text: GUN-10016 PRIMARY Letters | Gun advocates fueled by irrational emotion Thanks to Lawrence Braico of the Association of New Jersey Rifle and Pistol Clubs ("Gun law's flaws," Dec. 5) for finally clarifying why pro-gunners are so vehement in opposition to limits on packing heat at will. Gun-lobby rhetoric is awash in false patriotism and incorrect reading of constitutional principle, and Braico didn't spare us either. But in his rambling and parental letter taking Gov. Rendell to task for signing legislation enabling law enforcement to remove guns from domestic abusers, Braico defined what really motivates pro-gunners to fight against every limit on gun possession or use: emotion. He wrote that emotion was key to "how much said firearms are valued" by their owners. So now we know. It's less about so-called rights, and more about the emotional attraction pro-gunners feel toward guns. That would explain the irrationality in opposing, as Braico's organization did, requiring that handguns be childproofed in the future so kids can't unintentionally (or intentionally) fire them. And it would explain why it wants anyone to be able to carry concealed handguns in public. What is really telling is how Braico makes clear that he and his ilk value guns over people, as his ire is directed not at gun-toting abusers who put women and children at risk, but at Rendell for signing legislation to protect those innocents. Bryan Miller Executive director Ceasefire NJ

characters: Association of New Jersey Rifle and Pistol Clubs

1314.text: GUN-1175 PRIMARY Mr. Reagan Finally Leads on Guns The gun control campaign conducted by James Brady, who was wounded in the 1981 Presidential assassination attempt, has touched millions of Americans. But until yesterday, one prominent American remained oddly silent. Ronald Reagan, target of the bullet Mr. Brady stopped, seemed oblivious to his former press secretary's cause. In a speech yesterday and an article on today's Op-Ed page, Mr. Reagan finally breaks his silence with a resounding endorsement of the so-called Brady bill. His welcome support adds heft to growing pressure for the bill and puts President Bush, who continues to oppose it, on the spot. The bill, introduced by Representative Edward Feighan, Democrat of Ohio, would impose a national seven-day waiting period between purchase and delivery of a handgun so police could check the purchaser's background. Similar laws in several states, including Mr. Reagan's native California, block thousands of illegal gun sales each year. The National Rifle Association resists the national bill and argues that few criminals go to gun shops to purchase guns. That's true -- but beside the point. Street criminals typically purchase guns from neighborhood entrepreneurs who realize handsome profits smuggling guns from shops in states with lax laws for street-corner sales in cities with strict ones. A national waiting period could severely inhibit that deadly black market. Beyond honoring his friendship with Mr. Brady, Mr. Reagan, a member of the N.R.A., makes clear that no one need be bullied by the big gun lobby when it strays from common sense. That's an act of leadership for President Bush to follow.

1315.text: GUN-2602 PRIMARY House passes Brady bill The House of Representatives has approved the Brady bill, requiring a nationwide waiting period of five business days on handgun purchases. The waiting period would give local police time to check the background of prospective handgun purchasers. House members said they were responding to public demands that the government act to reduce the pervasive threat of violent crime. The measure is called the Brady bill after former White House press  secretary James Brady, who was wounded in the 1981 assassination attempt on  President Reagan. It passed the House 238-189 Wednesday and now goes to the  Senate, which plans to consider similar legislation after completing action on  a broader crime bill. The Brady bill was first proposed in 1987 and has become a regular in  biennial debates on anti-crime legislation in Congress. The House also approved Brady two years ago only to see it fail after being attached to a larger crime bill blocked by Senate Republicans. This time, it  is being kept separate in the hope the Senate will send it to President  Clinton, who has promised to sign it. The bill's waiting period provision would end once a national computerized system was in place to check the criminal records of gun purchasers. The Senate is scheduled to vote on its version of the bill next week, and senators have said they expect it to pass. But the Senate remained mired in a dispute over how to dispose of scores of amendments to its $ 22-billion, five-year anti-crime bill, including proposed constraints on semiautomatic assault weapons that were tentatively approved by a 51-49 vote Nov. 2.

1316.text: NA-43828 PRIMARY Sen. Joe Manchin: Stand-alone assault weapons ban 'will not go anywhere' Sen. Joe Manchin (D-W.Va.) said Sunday that a stand-alone ban on assault weapons would not win passage in Congress, and that the effort to curb mass shootings must include a broader discussion involving the entertainment industry and mental health issues. "An assault weapons stand-alone ban on just guns alone, in the political reality we have, will not go anywhere. It has to be comprehensive," Manchin said on CNN's "State of the Union." Manchin, a conservative Democrat who has received high marks from the NRA, said in the wake of the mass shooting at a school in Newtown, Conn., that it was time for a new discussion about regulations on assault weapons. He later wrote in a Washington Post op-ed that the problem of mass shootings must be addressed in a comprehensive manner, by looking at guns, security, the entertainment industry and mental health issues. He reiterated that view Sunday.

1319.text: GUN-9200 PRIMARY KILMER SAYS ROLLING STONE MISFIRES WITH GUN ARTICLE Val Kilmer says he sang New Mexico's praises during an interview with Rolling Stone magazine and was misquoted. "I didn't say I live in the homicide capital of the Southwest," Kilmer said. He said another comment that he carries a gun in his car while driving with his children because there are so many drunken drivers in the area "doesn't make sense." The magazine's Oct. 16 issue features an article headlined "Mr. Difficult" that quotes him as saying he lives in the "homicide capital of the Southwest" and 80 percent "of the people in my county are drunk." The actor, who owns a ranch in San Miguel County south of Pecos, said he'll ask Rolling Stone to apologize for the article. "Rolling Stone stands by the article," Rolling Stone spokeswoman Claudia Diromauldo said. WEAPONS LAWS HOLD UP ELVIS EXHIBIT: A British museum specializing in memorabilia has hit a snag with plans for a new exhibit. Strict gun-control laws in the United Kingdom are holding up a planned exhibit at the Fingerprints of Elvis museum in Liverpool of firearms once owned by Elvis Presley. Britain's Home Office wants to know how the guns will be transported from the United States and how they will be guarded. The collection includes a World War II-era Colt pistol, a Golden Beretta pistol and a derringer pistol Presley used to carry on stage with him, tucked into one of his boots.

1317.text: NA-40071 PRIMARY STATES' GUN MEASURES SPARK RUN ON AMMO News of new weapons legislation in Connecticut and elsewhere has touched off a buying frenzy for ammunition, making it hard for stores nationwide to keep shelves stocked and even affecting some local law enforcement departments. Merchants say the run started in November with President Barack Obama's re-election and has not abated. The nation's 100 million firearms owners are driving the market for some 10 billion rounds annually, with demand increasing the past several months, said Lawrence Keane, whose National Shooting Sports Foundation is in Newtown, Conn. Major U.S. manufacturers are working around the clock keep up, he said. But runs on ammo are nothing new to Michael Sfakianos, who manages Bill Jackson's Shop for Adventure in Pinellas Park. He said Thursday that the shortage began when the gun control debate first heated up. "We're out of a lot right now," he said, noting that the store puts strict limits on how much people can buy. He added that politicians' talk of gun control drives buyers. "They just caused 300 times more people to buy guns."

1322.text: GUN-1296 PRIMARY Gun registration law has low response Despite a last-minute registration crush, California state officials estimated that thousands of military assault rifles were not registered by Monday's deadline leading to possible criminal charges against their owners. State officials still are uncertain about how many of the estimated 300,000 assault weapons in California will be registered, but have increased their estimate from fewer than 10,000 a few weeks ago to as many as 15,000. Under the terms of the 1989 law that banned the sale and manufacture of 55 specific types of military-style semiautomatic weapons in California, people who lawfully owned them before June 1, 1989, were allowed to keep them, provided they registered them with the Department of Justice by Monday. Failure to register a weapon is an infraction, similar to a traffic ticket, punishable by a $ 350 fine on the first offense if the weapon is found on the gun owner's own property or a licensed shooting range. Boston transit system to test for drugs BOSTON Transportation officials said Monday they had fired a driver whose blood-alcohol level tested above the legal limit after the trolley he was operating rammed into another rail car last week, injuring 33 people. The Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority (MTBA) also will start testing its employees at random for alcohol and drugs in the wake of the Friday morning accident, MBTA officials said. Two dispatchers who allegedly were slow to react to a passenger's warning about the driver's behavior that morning have been reassigned, they said. "We dropped the ball," Thomas P. Glynn, the MBTA's general manager, said at a news conference. "We did not act in time to avert this accident." Dartmouth paper judged non-prejudiced MANCHESTER, N.H.   The chairman of New Hampshire's Human Rights Commission said Monday he found no evidence of discrimination against blacks, women, homosexuals or Jews in an off-campus Dartmouth College newspaper. Barry Palmer said he reached his conclusion after reading two years of the Dartmouth Review. Criticizing blacks, women, homosexuals or Jews is not prejudice as long as the criticism is aimed at performance, and not sexual or racial status, Palmer said. He said he conducted the study as an individual, not on behalf of the commission. The Review has been vilified for years for its views on women, homosexuals, blacks and Indians. Kevin Pritchett, editor of the weekly newspaper, said that "from the very start, the Review has criticized people, not because of their race or sex (but) because of the way they performed." Air Force plans to proceed on the B-2 WASHINGTON The Air Force said Monday it intended to go ahead with plans to build all 75 B-2 "stealth" bombers approved by Defense Secretary Dick Cheney. "The B-2 continues to enjoy the confidence of the Air Force and remains its highest priority. We are strongly committed to pursuing the full production run of 75 aircraft approved by the secretary of defense," the Air Force said in a statement. The comment came in response to a Wall Street Journal report that the service was dropping plans to build more B-2 bombers in 1991 and would use money provided by Congress for planes already approved for production.

1318.text: GUN-10277 PRIMARY COUNCIL LOCKS, LOADS GUN BILL A BILL THAT HAS the gun industry up in arms won approval yesterday in the City Council with only two dissenting votes. Passed by a 43-to-2 vote at the Council's first session of the year, the measure is intended to make it easier for victims of gun crimes in the city, or their survivors, to sue out-of-state gun makers and dealers for injuries or deaths caused by their weapons. Gun industry representatives have denounced the bill as an infringement of interstate commerce and predicted it will be shot down by the courts. "It's like blaming Budweiser for drunken driving," said Lawrence Keane of the National Shooting Sports Foundation, the industry's trade association. "It will be struck down." Backers of the bill hope it will help curb the flood of illegal guns into the city from states with lax regulations. Each year, there are about 400 gun-related deaths and 1,300 injuries in the city. Gun violence is an especially poignant issue for members of the Council. One of their own - James Davis of Brooklyn - was shot and killed during a session Aug. 23, 2003. His killer, a disturbed political opponent who had been visiting Davis, was shot and killed by a security police officer. Davis' brother Geoffrey praised the bill before the vote, and said of the gun industry, "We're ready to take them on if they want to sue."

1321.text: GUN-7681 PRIMARY On Guns, 'Enough Is Enough' To the Editor: Re "Mothers Rally to Assail Gun Violence" (front page, May 15): I attended the Million Mom March in Washington. I had not planned to, and I knew no one who was going, but the day before I surfed the Web and noticed there were eight buses leaving from a local shopping center at 6 a.m. All seats were reserved, but I went stand-by. The first bus was full; the second had one no-show. I am a registered Republican who votes by issues, and my presence at the march was not intended as a political statement. It was a moral stance. To the National Rifle Association, I say, along with the other marchers, "Enough is enough." I intend to write my representative and senators in support of child-safety locks, the registration of all handguns and the licensing of all handgun owners. Which candidate, George W. Bush or Al Gore, will be the first to support sensible gun laws? That candidate will earn my vote. LAUREL HARVEY Princeton, N.J., May 15, 2000 http://www.nytimes.com

1320.text: NA-40441 PRIMARY Protect families, not criminals As chiefs of police, every day we send our officers into dangerous situations and ask them to put their lives at risk. So we take it very seriously when laws are proposed that could make it harder for police officers to do their job - especially when those laws would also make our communities less safe. That is exactly what the poorly titled Second Amendment Preservation Act does. Don't let the name fool you. This legislation is not about gun rights for law-abiding citizens. The only gun owners who stand to benefit from this bill are criminals. The Second Amendment right to bear arms goes hand in hand with common-sense measures that help keep guns out of the hands of dangerous people. That's why we have laws on the books that bar criminals like murderers, rapists and domestic abusers from owning guns. There are compelling public safety reasons for these kinds of laws. For example, we know that people with history of domestic violence - a conviction or a restraining order - are more likely to murder an intimate partner; and we know that women are five times more likely to be murdered when their abuser has a gun. Put simply: laws that bar domestic abusers from possessing guns save women's lives and make children, families and communities safer. But the legislation being considered in Jefferson City - versions of which have already been approved by the House and Senate - would make it illegal for police officers to enforce these laws. What's more, any local, state, or federal law enforcement official could actually be sued in civil court by a convicted domestic abuser they arrest for possessing a gun in violation of federal law.

1323.text: GUN-30929 PRIMARY Good Sense in Tennessee Safety-minded localities in gun-friendly Tennessee have delivered a blunt and very welcome lesson in gun control to the National Rifle Association. While Tennessee's ever-obeisant Legislature has enacted a law permitting handguns in all state and local parks, about 70 cities and counties have voted to opt out of this latest lock-and-load obsession from the N.R.A. This is common-sense evidence that statehouse bullying tactics by the gun lobby do not necessarily translate to the local level, not when people have a chance to disinvite guns into areas designed for adult relaxation and children's play. A local option to ban guns was written as a boilerplate clause for the new law, which extends gun packing to hundreds of parks -- including public playgrounds, campgrounds, greenways and nature trails. Few state politicians anticipated there would be local resistance. But bans were voted by Nashville and Memphis and dozens of suburban counties and rural communities. The result is a crazy-quilt pattern of gun safety and risk that is prompting one county to ban student field trips and track meets to areas that did not opt to ban guns. The gun lobby is not giving up, of course. It is already marshaling lobbyists and cash to ensure the next Legislature strips localities of their right to just say no. Before they make that mistake, politicians who have spent years ignoring their consciences and taking the N.R.A.'s orders should listen closely to Tennessee's citizens. As one demanded at a local hearing this summer: ''Are you going to hear the voices of the unarmed?''

1327.text: GUN-8613 PRIMARY BRIEFING: LAW ENFORCEMENT; GUN PERMITS INCREASE New Jersey is one of only three states of the 19 that require background checks for gun purchases that reported an increase last year in the number of applications for gun permits. During 2000, 77,211 applications to buy handguns or rifles were submitted to state or local law enforcement agencies, a 1.1 percent increase over 1999, according to a study released last week by the federal Bureau of Justice Statistics. Colorado and Maryland also reported increases. In the other states, the F.B.I. rather than state officials does background checks and statistics are not released on a state-by-state basis. Nationally, requests to buy guns dropped 11 percent from 8.6 million to 7.7 million between 1999 and 2000. The number of applications rejected also declined from 204,000 to 153,000.    Karen DeMasters

1326.text: GUN-8739 PRIMARY METRO; In Brief MARYLAND The Montgomery County Council postponed yesterday a vote on legislation aimed at restricting gun shows at the county fairgrounds, but a compromise version appears likely to pass when the council takes up the measure again in two weeks. Council President Blair G. Ewing's bill would extend the county's "gun-free zones" to include "multipurpose exhibition facilities" like the Gaithersburg fairgrounds. It would also cut off county funding to any group that allows the display or sale of guns on its property. Extending the county's gun-free zones would not affect the privately owned fairgrounds because it is in Gaithersburg, a municipality that would have to adopt its own version of the ban. The fairgrounds has, however, received government grants. With the council closely divided, member Isiah Leggett (D-At Large) is considered the swing vote. He said yesterday that his primary concern is that the bill is too broad. While he supports cutting off public funds to groups that host gun shows, he said, extending the county's gun-free zones could effectively ban gun shows in too much of the county. If that language can be fixed, he said he will support the bill. The state's second-highest court has dismissed a lawsuit seeking to block the planned fluoridation of Cumberland's water supply. City officials said cavity-fighting fluoride could be added to the water this spring, a year after voters approved it.

1324.text: GUN-9928 PRIMARY Blame Gun Makers for Crime? To the Editor: Re ''Lawyers, Guns and Mayors,'' by Michael R. Bloomberg, Richard M. Daley, James K. Hahn and Scott L. King (Op-Ed, Feb. 24): The four big-city mayors want to be able to sue gun makers. Since cars also kill people, why don't they sue car makers? Gun ownership carries at least as many responsibilities as car ownership. People who want to own guns should pay for the privilege and prove to an insurer that they are capable. Shift the burden to where it belongs; the makers may be venal, but they are not liable. MARSHALL L. SMITH Rockville, Md., Feb. 24, 2004

1328.text: GUN-11686 PRIMARY We are not helpless against violence Newtown aftermath o Support ban on assault weapons, more background checks on gun sales. OTHER VIEWS As a nation, we are all saddened and disheartened by the tragedy at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. This was how we felt after the mass shootings at Columbine, Virginia Tech, Aurora and Tucson. Each time we hoped those events would finally be a catalyst for real change - yet here we are, once again. We can no longer brace for the next terrible event. Now is the time to finally say enough is enough and demand action, no matter our political persuasion. Often, as individuals, we feel helpless to do anything to stem the violence in our society. But we are not helpless. We owe it to our families and those we love to act. My daughter, Melissa, lost her life to gun violence in 1995. I have spent the years since her murder learning about research-based practices to prevent violence from to some of the foremost experts at universities in the U.S. and Canada. These caring and committed individuals work in the areas of family violence prevention, bullying prevention, suicide prevention, school violence prevention, etc. The sad fact is that we spend more on incarceration of offenders then we do on mental health services and prevention methods. There are many ways to reduce the incidence of violence, but they will require changes in our priorities. There is something we can do immediately by imploring our elected officials to do the following:

1325.text: GUN-31124 PRIMARY Congress's Power Grab I was taken aback to learn of the legislation before Congress that would strip the District of its power to regulate firearms [Metro, Sept. 10]. It is outlandish, offensive and presumptuous that members of Congress would act against the will of local leaders such as D.C. Police Chief Cathy L. Lanier and our elected representative, Del. Eleanor Holmes Norton (D-D.C.). It is nothing short of comical that the bill is primarily supported by Republican lawmakers, those who most often scoff at the notion of government interference and who champion state sovereignty. It is ironic that Rep. Virginia Foxx (R-N.C.) would call Chief Lanier's remark that D.C. residents can already protect themselves at home by legally owning shotguns or pistols "arrogant." The true arrogance lies in Ms. Foxx's actions, which would make our city less safe and put at risk the lives of law enforcement officers  who face danger every day to protect us. JEFF WURZBURG Washington

characters: D.C. Police Chief Cathy L. Lanier

1329.text: NA-45945 PRIMARY GE won't make loans to buy guns; Move follows Newtown tragedy General Electric has stopped financing gun purchases, highlighting the wide-ranging impact of the Sandy Hook school shooting in Newtown, Conn., in December and the recent debate in Washington over restricting firearm sales. GE Capital Finance ceased providing consumer financing for new gun-shop customers in 2008, and recently extended the policy to existing customers. The decision affects fewer than 75 retailers, which GE says is about 0.001% of all gun retailers. The company implemented the new policy "in light of industry changes, new legislation and tragic events that have caused widespread re-examination of policies on firearms," GE Capital spokesman Russell Wilkerson said in an e-mail. "As a responsible lender, we regularly review our lending policies and products to meet changing conditions and requirements," he said. Loans to gun customers is "an immaterial part of our sales volume," Wilkerson added. Some of the nation's largest lenders don't finance gun purchases. Wells Fargo stopped the practice in 2004 for business reasons, company spokeswoman Lisa Westermann said. Citigroup doesn't finance firearm loans, says spokeswoman Liz Fogarty. Bank of America would not comment on whether it provides consumer financing for firearms. GE Capital's new policy affects only retailers that sell firearms exclusively and not general merchandise stores, such as Walmart, that sell guns and other products. Some stores have changed their product mix in recent years to just firearms, and the new policy will cut off financing at those shops, Wilkerson says.

1330.text: GUN-2781 PRIMARY Assault weapons ban OK'd A ban on 19 assault-style weapons is headed for almost certain congressional approval after an anguished House vote Thursday and intense lobbying by President Clinton. Despite a feverish offensive by the National Rifle Association and other pro-gun activists, the House narrowly approved the ban 216-214. Clinton called it "a sea change in the crime debate." A similar measure was endorsed by the Senate last November. A reconciled version is expected to become part of a massive anti-crime bill Congress considers later this year.
