One person waved a green sign that read, ''Ban Russo, Not Handguns.'' 'Infringing on My Rights' ''He's infringing on my rights as a citizen,'' said Steve McCoy, 23 years old, of Toms River. ''I consider shooting targets not a criminal offense.'' The legislation sponsored by Mr. Russo, a Democrat from Toms River, would ban the future sale, ownership and possession of most handguns by private citizens. The measure has been criticized by many New Jersey law-enforcement officers as well as the Fraternal Order of Police and the National Rifle Association. Police Chief William Osterman of Elmer called the bill a ''misguided'' effort by Senator Russo that would fail to curb crime. '' It's going to remove handguns from legitimate, law-abiding citizens,'' he said.

1377.text: GUN-10162 PRIMARY Gun panel fails to back key measures; A Rendell commission didn't endorse a one-per-month limit on purchases or allowing cities the right to OK local gun laws. A special commission formed by Gov. Rendell to combat a surge in gun violence statewide failed to support two key gun-control initiatives backed by Mayor Street and other city officials. In a report issued yesterday, the divided commission recommended the legislature commit more funding for gun violence prevention programs, enact tougher penalties for those who violate state gun laws, and create better tools for prosecutors to pursue gun traffickers. But the commission failed to endorse the most controversial proposals: limiting an individual's gun purchases to one a month, and allowing Philadelphia and other municipalities the right to approve local gun laws. Gun-control advocates said they were disappointed in the report's recommendations, which they said failed to address any substantive prevention measures. "It's very unfortunate that no consensus was able to be reached on any steps that really qualify as gun violence prevention," said Peter Hamm, communications director for the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence. "The commission's recommendations have gun violence prevention starting in the emergency room." In a 2004 report, the Brady Campaign gave the state a D+ for its gun-control laws. In issuing the report, the group said a state law prohibiting cities from enacting their own tougher gun-control laws was reprehensible. Mayor Street's spokeswoman, Deborah Bolling, said the mayor was generally supportive of the report. "He thought it was good work, a lot in a little time."

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1382.text: GUN-5036 PRIMARY Right to Bear Arms To the Editor: Maureen Dowd, surely one of the two or three best columnists in the nation, stole my heart with her writing from the beginning. Now she's broken it with her flawed attack on the Second Amendment (column, May 6). Barbra Streisand should be forgiven for her shaky acquaintance with the Bill of Rights, but a professional journalist should know better. The Founders' intent in framing the Second Amendment is perfectly clear and undeniable. Thomas Jefferson wrote, "No man shall ever be debarred the use of arms." Some anti-gun elitists declare this notion outdated. However, many constitutional scholars from this country's most prestigious universities agree that the Founders' intent is clear and irreversible: To "keep and bear arms" is a right for all law-abiding citizens. I'll continue to read Maureen Dowd's well-written columns, but as often happened in my films, I've lost my true love. CHARLTON HESTON Beverly Hills, Calif., May 8, 1998

1389.text: GUN-8406 PRIMARY METRO ONLINE Hartsfield sitting pretty While Hartsfield International Airport is Atlanta's main economic engine, the Federal Aviation Administration regulations prevent the city from using money generated by the airport for other city departments. At a time when the city searches for ways to balance its budget, Hartsfield seems to suffer an embarrassment of riches, embarking on a $5.4 billion expansion. For more information about Hartsfield, including how to do business with the airport, visit Hartsfield's Web site at: www.atlanta-airport.com/homepage3.htm --- Richard Whitt Guns slipping through A national study of gun purchase background checks found loopholes in Georgia's system. Georgia was one of eight states not recording sales to prohibited buyers. The records lapses earned Georgia a C-. The study found that Georgia's background check system doesn't include domestic violence records and has only spotty information about persons involuntarily committed to mental hospitals. The survey of gun sales records in all states over a 30-month period found nearly 10,000 ineligible people were allowed to buy guns. For more information, including a full text of the study, "Broken Records: How America's Faulty Background Check System Allows Criminals to Get Guns," go to the Americans for Gun Safety Foundation Web site at: www.agsfoundation.com --- Don Plummer

1387.text: NA-42119 PRIMARY Harvey Weinstein fighting a losing battle over gun control Hollywood producer Harvey Weinstein announced recently that he's going after the National Rifle Association   a group that he told Howard Stern is a "disaster area"   by producing a new film about which he said "they're going to wish they weren't alive after I'm done with them." That's some bold talk. He's wrong, but that's some bold talk. Oh, his intent isn't wrong. Wanting to curb gun violence is one of the most noble ideas there is. It's how to do it that is so problematic. Gun control advocates say fewer guns would result in fewer killings, while gun rights advocates say more guns would make criminals think twice before pulling a piece, out of fear that someone could shoot back. Political filmmaking is touch and go. Sometimes filmmakers with a big point get their desired results   see "Blackfish," the film exploring captive killer whales that has people seriously reconsidering the idea of holding orcas in amusement parks for exhibition. The same goes for any number of Michael Moore's films, from "Roger and Me" to "Fahrenheit 9/11," which had people questioning the actions of the Bush administration after Sept. 11. Of course, Moore also made "Bowling For Columbine," which explored the nation's supposed love of guns and attempted to change positive public opinion about gun ownership. It didn't work. Not even close. We can decide other countries are reprehensible, thanks to political documentaries (see "The Cove," which, no doubt, raised awareness about a small Japanese village's annual slaughter of dolphins, which Caroline Kennedy, American ambassador to Japan, spoke out against this week in what could easily be considered a diplomatic no-no). We can question the Bush administration's post Sept. 11 actions in Moore's "Fahrenheit 9/11"c and have constructive conversations on what we may or may not have learned.

1386.text: GUN-8685 PRIMARY GUN STORAGE BILL MAY BE SHOT DOWN ALBANY - Legislation that would slap criminal penalties on gun owners who don't store their weapons safely is once again bogged down in partisan bickering. Members of the Manhattan-based New Yorkers Against Gun Violence group came to the Capitol to lobby for the bill and other gun-control measures. For seven years, the Assembly has passed a gun storage bill, but it has always died in the state Senate. Assembly Democrats are expected to pass the measure again shortly. Assembly Speaker Sheldon Silver (D-Manhattan) said it makes sense to "give people liability if they fail to protect children." Asked about the legislation later, state Senate Majority Leader Joe Bruno (R-Rensselaer) said Democrats were using the issue to distract the public from the late state budget, which he said was "the most important thing right now going on in the lives of the people in this state."

1388.text: GUN-10471 PRIMARY ATTACKING A BASIC AMERICAN RIGHT Congress: Terrorists have gun rights too - May 8 Gail Collins' column and the Lautenberg bill it supports are as fatally flawed as the FBI's terrorist watch list. The Terrorist Screening Center has been and still is adding names to that list with no investigation beyond those names coming up in uncorroborated, uninvestigated reports of suspected terrorism supporters, sympathizers and participants. The list now contains more than 1.1 million names, many with multiple entries and misspellings, and almost all are completely innocent of any crime. It has included 4-year-olds and nationally known figures, and it took years for those victims to get their names off the list. Nelson Mandela and his family were not removed until 2008 after intervention by members of Congress. Not only is the list grotesquely inaccurate, but real terrorists are often allowed to fly because of the inaccuracies and the delays in updating by the FBI. The Times Square bomber was allowed to board an aircraft because of the airline delay in reading the latest list revision. Fortunately he was removed before takeoff. The removal of a basic civil right by faceless bureaucrats without legal process or easy recourse to appeal is unfortunately not unprecedented. But the last time this occurred was during the McCarthy era. I suggest that Gail Collins and Sen. Frank Lautenberg at least have the honesty to admit their goal, to repeal the Second Amendment and ban all firearms, instead of trying to subvert and corrupt the Constitution.

1390.text: GUN-7660 PRIMARY ONE MAN'S VOTE GUN CONTROL ON a tellingly contentious day on the Senate floor, Illinois Sen. Peter Fitzgerald took a stern scolding from his Republican party leaders for siding with Democrats and more than half a million marchers, encouraging action on gun control legislation left for dead in committee for nearly a year. Wednesday's nonbinding Senate resolution calls for congressional action by Memorial Day. It passed by a single vote. An outraged Senate majority leader, Trent Lott of Mississippi, fumed over the time wasted on a symbolic gesture that he says will accomplish "zero." If it accomplishes nothing, then why was Mr. Lott knocked so far out of his calm demeanor? Was Mr. Lott really so anguished about one day's delay in considering budget bills, as he states? Or was he sensing a critical shift in the mood of the public and Congress? Is Mr. Lott perhaps more concerned about explaining the break in party ranks by Mr. Fitzgerald and six other Republicans when the National Rifle Association opens its convention today in Charlotte, N.C.? The NRA did, after all, donate $ 478,100 in campaign soft money last year - every penny of it to Republicans. Were the 100 percent-loyal gun disciples assuming similar loyalty in return? Commanding the more genteel governing chamber, was Mr. Lott more concerned about Republicans having been forced by newly emboldened Democrats to move on a tough issue? Polls, marches and other hard-to-quantify expressions of public sentiment suggest a had-it-up-to-here anger over Congress' refusal to do anything about the epidemic of gun violence. Is this what had Mr. Lott in a knot?

1391.text: GUN-1262 PRIMARY Soft on Crime in Trenton These days, most state legislators consider toughness on crime as important a political posture as reluctance to tax. That's why New Jersey's lawmakers last year enacted a strict ban on semiautomatic assault weapons. Such guns, designed for military use and often easily converted into fully automatic machine guns, are the favorite armament of drug gangsters and other street criminals. They have made casual gunplay and stray bullets a common hazard in many urban neighborhoods. The New Jersey law bans new sales of the guns and requires most people who own them now to get rid of them or render them inoperable by May 31. Only members of target-shooting clubs authorized by the state's Attorney General would be exempt. But the National Rifle Association sniffed an opportunity and intervened to help defeat a candidate in a special election for state senator last fall. Now the gun lobby threatens to press the issue in this year's legislative elections. Intimidated legislators, eager to defuse a gun-control fight at a time when they are under fire for raising taxes, are introducing bills to emasculate the existing law. One recently approved by a Senate committee would exempt the most popular assault weapons from the current list of those banned and eliminate the requirement to surrender all guns purchased before last May 31. This isn't the first time the N.R.A. has lobbied against measures designed to reduce violent crime. But lawmakers who go along ought to be reminded that when they do so, they destroy their own credibility on law enforcement and crime.

1385.text: GUN-8016 PRIMARY Years After Sale, Handgun Haunts Ex-Owner in Court On the day in October when a police officer was shot to death here, Terry Walker was 70 miles away. But Mr. Walker, a 49-year-old cook, has been jailed on charges of involuntary manslaughter, even though there is no evidence that he knew the killer, Ljeka Juncaj, who shot himself to death after the slaying of the police officer, Chris Wouters. The charges stem from Mr. Walker's failure to file the legal forms several years ago when he sold the handgun that wound up in the hands of the killer. Criminal justice experts say the case is an extraordinary attempt to hold a gun seller responsible for the ultimate use of the gun, even if the weapon changed hands several times before it reached the killer. Macomb County prosecutors say the charge against Mr. Walker should send a message to gun owners about the dangers of improperly transferring weapons. But gun rights groups and other conservatives say the prosecution is an unfair use of gun laws to blame a man who had no real connection to the shooting. "This is a real stretch," said Mitch Pearlstein, the president of the Center of the American Experiment, a conservative research organization in Minneapolis. "It reminds me of these cases we're seeing now of landlords being held responsible for the misbehavior of their tenants." Advocates of gun control, meanwhile, say the case demonstrates the importance of the laws on transferring guns. If the gun had been properly registered, they say, it would be less likely that the weapon would have fallen into dangerous hands.

1393.text: GUN-4699 PRIMARY Gun control under the guise of a noble cause The law says it is illegal for anyone convicted of a domestic-violence crime to own or possess a firearm. This includes any misdemeanor conviction resulting from a domestic situation (felons are already prohibited from owning firearms). Not only that, the law is retroactive: Anyone who has ever been convicted of a domestic-violence crime may not own or possess a firearm after Sept. 30, 1996. This single piece of gun-control legislation is the largest assault on the Second Amendment in my lifetime. People convicted of certain misdemeanors, rightly or wrongly, will be prohibited for the rest of their lives from defending themselves with a firearm -- this under the noble guise of crime control. This law will deny thousands of American citizens the God-given right under the Constitution to defend themselves with a firearm (felons, remember, are not citizens.) All this is simply the latest attempt by the anti-gun movement to disarm the people of this country. I am not condoning domestic violence, but what's next, prohibiting convicted speeders from owning firearms? How about property owners delinquent in their tax payments? This will not stand. It's time for the people of this country who have any inkling of what the word freedom means to get up and do something. Write your representative and your senator: demand that this despicable law be repealed. And pray that they listen to you.

1392.text: GUN-10944 PRIMARY MORE GUNS MEAN LESS CRIME More madness in gun-happy Florida - Jan. 21, Daniel Ruth column Daniel Ruth's basic premise that expanded firearm rights have had a negative effect on Florida is false. It began in 1987 when Republican Gov. Bob Martinez signed Florida's concealed-carry bill, repeatedly passed by the Legislature but vetoed time and again by Democratic Gov. Bob Graham. Since then, Florida's murder and violent crime rates have plummeted, according to statistics from the Florida Department of Law Enforcement. Since 1987, the number of licensed concealed-carry citizens has mushroomed to more than 780,000. These facts support the concept of "more guns, less crime," in direct opposition to Ruth's rhetorical and emotional "common sense" approach of more and more gun control laws. Each of the proposed new firearm-related laws is based in reality and protects gun owners from local government intrusion on their constitutional rights, including protection from unwarranted medical-related intrusion on their privacy, allowing concealed weapon license holders to move their pistols from inside their shirts to the outside of their clothing, and to allow on-campus carry to give all licensed law-abiding students, faculty and staff more equal footing in the event of criminal campus attacks. The right to self-defense and the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms are the central issues here. The NRA has got it right, Marion Hammer has got it right and, hopefully, the Legislature and Gov. Rick Scott will also get it right. Right on target. Sorry, Daniel, you missed the target altogether.

1395.text: GUN-7668 PRIMARY Senate Halts As Democrats Force Votes On Gun Issues Seizing the moment after Sunday's Million Mom March on Washington against gun violence, Democrats brought the Senate to a halt today until the majority Republicans promised to allow votes on two gun control measures on Wednesday. "We will not do anything else," the minority leader, Senator Tom Daschle of South Dakota, said as he tied up the Senate in hours of procedural maneuvers. "Maybe the silence will get the message through." The Democrats, increasingly frustrated about inaction on gun control, made it clear that they were going to try to put the issue atop the Senate's agenda and do their best to keep it there. Unable to continue working on the pending military spending bill, the majority leader, Senator Trent Lott of Mississippi, struck a deal with Mr. Daschle early this evening on two symbolic gun measures. One deals with a Republican amendment that stresses tougher enforcement and prosecution of gun-related crimes. It also restates the right of a law-abiding citizen to own a gun. The measure will probably pass easily, with some Democratic voting for it. The second is a Democratic amendment that praises the Mother's Day march for "common-sense gun control," and calls on the Senate to enact the gun provisions it passed after last year's Columbine High School massacre in Colorado. That package has since languished in negotiations with the House, which rejected similar gun legislation.

1398.text: GUN-7986 PRIMARY Tragedy, Then Action; Michael Price Works for Gun Safety We interviewed Michael Price just before he attended the Million Mom March in Washington. That was a gathering of people who believe there would be fewer deaths and injuries from guns if there were there were more laws that made guns harder for criminals and children to get. In 1998, Michael's older brother John walked down his Carroll County street to play at a neighbor's home and never returned. A child in the house found a gun and accidentally fired it, killing John, who was 13 at the time. The tragedy led Michael's family to believe that more gun laws are needed. One law they supported, which was approved in Maryland, would require all guns made in the state to have locking devices, so that children could not fire them. Michael told us: "My brother would be alive if we had that law here." Michael's family joined the gun control movement, and Michael attended rallies and hearings. He also endured some mean remarks, especially when his family protested a gun raffle held near their home. Since attending the 750,000-person march, Michael has not been to many gun control-related events. He's gotten more involved in sports. As a fifth-grader this fall, he played noseguard for his school football team, the North Carroll Panthers. "We went five and six," 10-year-old Michael reported proudly. "I kind of lead the team in tackles."

1397.text: GUN-614 PRIMARY LOSING THE WAR ON DRUGS The decision by the Convention Center Commission to ban gun and knife shows atthe Cervantes Convention Center is another distressing example of criminal activity affecting American society and individual liberties.   Recent reports of armed street gangs enforcing their own territorial justice have brought frantic cries for gun-control legislation - ranging from registration to an outright ban on gun ownership. The concern is understandable, for no one wants to face the business end of a weapon in the hands of a felon with good aim and no conscience.   The problem with gun control is that it is a Band-Aid solution to a deep wound in the soul of society. The combined forces of the Coast Guard and Navy, along with federal, state and local law enforcement agencies, have not been able to stem the flood of drugs poisoning our country. How a law restricting the lawful ownership of firearms can prevent their possession and use in criminal activity is beyond comprehension. Gun control is mere lip service to the crisis our country faces, not the solution.   If we're serious about reducing gang activity, drug usage and violence in our cities, we have to solve problems like poverty, teen-age unemployment and the hopelessness people in ghettoes must face every day. Youths are offered employment and protection by BMW-driving drug dealers. What society offers in return is unemployment and welfare. It's not hard to understand why we're losing the war on drugs. Kids can see through empty slogans spouted by a society that isn't willing to share its wealth with those trapped in poverty. Raymond T. Kyle Florissant Who's The Cave Man? I am writing in reference to Robert Maynard's Aug. 4 column comparing Jesse Helms with Neanderthal man. I take great exception to his column, due to its inaccuracies. First, Neanderthal man had a cranial capacity larger than the Cro-Magnon man, from whom we are descended. Second, he certainly had a culture rich in artistic effort, as documented by his cultural artifacts. Lastly, he also demonstrated great sensitivity to his fellow man and had great sorrow due to the mystery of death, as evidenced by discoveries of his graves covered with flowers at the time of the burial. He represented a sophisticated and enlightened individual.   Maynard's column did not refer to any of these achievements; therefore, I believe he did a great disservice to Neanderthal man by comparing him with Helms, who has not achieved any of these efforts, despite many opportunities during his myopic career.   Furthermore, it is not polite to kick a man when he is down, but when he is extinct?   J. William Campbell, M.D. St. Louis Up On The Levee As the mayor of Valley Park, I would be remiss if I didn't challenge the incorrect impression caused by an Aug. 7 article on this city's and U.S.  Rep.  Jack Buechner's efforts on the levee project. Let me say it clearly: All Valley Park officials and Buechner have worked extremely hard on this project and look forward to the day when this very complicated and involved venture can be brought to a successful conclusion.   Trying to compare two flood-control projects as different as Cape Girardeau and Valley Park is difficult, and it's virtually impossible in the space of one article to evaluate the effort to secure funding.   From the outset, the Cape Gira rdeau project met all the criteria for inclusion in the president's budget; it simply didn't make it on the basis of priority. This wasn't the case with our project, which was mistakenly evaluated, and therefore, initially rejected.   Buechner took every step possible to correct this very critical deficiency.   Moreover, Buechner recognized that testifying before an appropriations committee that had been supplied with a defective analysis was counterproductive. It was more important to ensure that the misinformation was corrected.   There's more I could add, but I think the point has been made. This levee-funding project is complicated, moving far beyond a simple request and approval. It takes hard work and commitment to obtain federal funds, and my colleagues and Buechner are doing exactly what's necessary.   The proof can be seen in the most recent development. Buechner has now been assured that the Valley Park levee project does meet federal criteria, thus making it an excellent candidate for inclusion in the 1991 budget and eventual funding.   Fred L. Palmer Jr.  Valley Park Closed-Door Policy An Aug. 21 article told of a Jewish emigrant from Germany whose mother and father made it to the United States ''later, before the Nazis closed the gates to Jewish emigration.'' In the interest of historical accuracy, I would like to point out that the Germans didn't cut off emigration to the United States until a state of war was declared in December 1941.   Until then, those gates were effectively closed by the U.S. government, which rigorously enforced and applied immigration quotas in effect since the early part of the century. Germany had only 500,000 Jews to begin with, and we have probably taken in more refugees than that from Latin American and Vietnam alone in the past few years. I am not absolving the Germans from killing my parents, but the fact is that they would have survived if they could have gotten a U.S. visa as late as 1941. Even though both my brother and I were living here then, it couldn't be done. Rudolf Kaufman University City Jobs Wanted The first step in solving the homeless situation is personal recovery from any and all drug or alcohol problems the homeless person faces. The next step is a reintegration into the work force. And, finally, the person gets to be independent and moves out into society.   The problem I am having is in the second step - getting the person a job.   Earlier this month, I spent nearly $500 on a very impressive ad in your newspaper letting employers know who I am and what I am trying to do. Basically, I offer as much as $2,000 to an employer who is willing to try one of my clients out in an on-the-job-training situation. That's a $2,000 gift!   The ''help wanted'' section lists many pages of employers needing help, but, apparently, no one needs $2,000. I only received two calls over the 15 days, and both calls were from people looking for a job rather than from someone seeking information on how to obtain this gift.   Did I do something wrong? Bobby L. Jones Salvation Army Homeless Veterans Reintegration Project St. Louis Read His Lips Columnist Jerry Berger's expose of the exorbitant fees charged by downtown parking lots indicates that we need a site-value tax in lieu of more sales taxes.   Tom Lehmann Alton

1394.text: NA-41107 PRIMARY NRA courts disaster The National Rifle Association is fighting like hell to stop Congress from imposing universal background checks on gun buyers. Its man on Long Island personifies why the NRA must be defeated. NRA field representative Richard D'Alauro is living the nightmare that the group's leaders have spun for all America: The government forced him to surrender more than 39 rifles and handguns, plus a cache of ammunition. Why? Because his estranged wife, Maribeth, who walks with a cane due to multiple sclerosis, accused her husband in a 2010 domestic incident. The Suffolk County district attorney filed misdemeanor charges of assault, endangering the welfare of a child and violating an order of protection. D'Alauro settled the case in 2012 by pleading guilty to harassment, an offense that's categorized lower than a misdemeanor. "Did you intend to harass annoy or alarm (Mrs. D'Alauro) by subjecting her to physical contact and you had no legitimate purpose to do so?" Suffolk County District Court Judge Toni Bean asked. After D'Alauro responded in the affirmative, Bean issued a fresh order of protection for his wife, ordered him to hand all his weapons over to police and prohibited him from buying more. The restrictions extend until October, at which time D'Alauro intends to lock and load again. Federal and New York State laws bar convicted domestic abusers from purchasing guns. Their records should turn up when dealers order background checks, and they should be turned away.

1396.text: NA-44244 PRIMARY Joe Manchin's support dips in new GOP poll A Republican poll finds that support for Sen. Joe Manchin (D-W.Va.) has fallen since his high-profile push for new gun legislation, but the survey gives little evidence that the decline is related to that proposal. Mark Blankenship's MBE Voter Opinion Poll has Manchin's approval rating dropping 7 points from March, to a still-high 63 percent. However, the poll also finds that 67 percent of voters support Manchin's recent attempt to expand background checks for gun purchases, legislation he co-authored with Republican Sen. Pat Toomey (R-Pa.). Forty-three percent of Republicans and 34 percent of independents oppose the plan, compared with19 percent of Democrats. In the wake of the legislation's defeat, Manchin has continued to talk to lawmakers and has expressed optimism about another vote. But gun control is not likely to come up again any time soon. The poll also looked at the race to replace retiring Sen. Jay Rockefeller (D). Rep. Shelley Moore Capito (R) has a 20-point lead over Democratic state Supreme Court Justice Robin Davis in a potential matchup. But Democratic primary voters prefer W.Va. Secretary of State Natalie Tennant to Davis or any other potential candidate, with 40 percent of voters choosing her. Scott Clement contributed to this report.

1399.text: GUN-11806 PRIMARY 32K VOICES SCREAM TO D.C....ENOUGH! THIRTY-TWO thousand concerned Americans have signed the Daily News petition urging government action on gun control - an indication how far the pendulum has swung on the issue, said a member of Congress. "People have had enough," said Rep. Carolyn McCarthy (D-L.I.). "They're clearly fed up with gun violence and the Daily News is performing a great public service by giving them a voice." McCarthy said she will sponsor the House version of the new assault weapons ban legislation that is being introduced in the Senate in January. "The (petition signers) are sending Washington a strong message that we will not let the gun lobby win in the fight to save innocent lives in America," said McCarthy, whose husband was a victim of the 1993 LIRR gun rampage. Daily News petition signers were even more blunt. "Stop the murders now Congress or you are complicit," said Diane Wiener, from Ozone Park, Queens. "If our government fails to ban assault rifles then they will have failed us and more importantly our children," added Danny DeBalsi of Norwalk, Conn.

1401.text: GUN-1915 PRIMARY In Shift, Massachusetts Governor Backs Gun Law With voters growing increasingly fearful of gunfire on the streets, Gov. William F. Weld of Massachusetts reversed course this week and proposed some of the most stringent gun control laws in the country. Mr. Weld, a Republican who will run for re-election next year, called for a statewide ban on assault weapons -- a proposal he opposed during his 1990 campaign -- as well as a waiting period for buying handguns and a prohibition on handgun ownership by anyone under 21. His proposed legislation would also limit the number of handguns an individual could buy and would impose tough penalties for illegal gun sales and gun-related crimes. "The purpose of this common sense legislation is to remove deadly guns from our streets and to take weapons out of the hands of many teens who themselves are becoming deadly killers," the Governor said. As a candidate three years ago, Mr. Weld's position on gun control had won him the endorsement of the Gun Owners' Action League, a local lobbying group affiliated with the National Rifle Association. And while many law enforcement officials this week praised his turnaround, his critics charged that the Governor was acting out of political expediency. "I'm glad that he's changed his mind and his position, but this is just totally contrary to everything that he has said for the last three years," said Attorney General Scott Harshbarger, a Democrat, who is considering a run for Governor next year.

1400.text: GUN-2043 PRIMARY CONN. SENATE APPROVES ASSAULT-WEAPONS BAN Fresh from recent victories in New Jersey and Virginia, the gun-control lobby appears to be headed toward another breakthrough in a most unlikely place: Connecticut, a state that since the Revolutionary War has supplied the nation with guns. Thursday, the Connecticut Senate voted 19-18 in favor of a bill that would ban the sale of all assault weapons except the Colt Sporter, which is manufactured in the state. Action by the Senate virtually guaranteed that an assault-weapons ban will be enacted into law in Connecticut. Proponents of the bill said the Connecticut ban would have symbolic as well as real importance in the national gun-control debate because this state has been responsible for manufacturing some of the country's most popular guns: the Colt revolver, the Remington rifle and the Winchester rifle. By voting 20-16 to exempt the Sporter from the bill, the Senate chose to protect the state government's own financial interest and about 1,000 jobs at the bankrupt gun factory. The House is known to be favorable to the bill, which Gov. Lowell Weicker Jr. has pledged to sign into law when it reaches his desk.

1404.text: GUN-6005 PRIMARY Too Many Guns, Too Many Laws To the Editor: Re "Only a Start on Gun Control" (editorial, May 21): I am neither a gun owner nor a member of the National Rifle Association. It is obvious there are too many guns available in America. However, it is equally obvious there are too many gun laws -- and, particularly in the last six years, not enough enforcement of the laws on the books. The Clinton Administration has chosen to play politics with the tragedies in Littleton, Colo., and Conyers, Ga., looking to pass more laws that it will then choose not to enforce. But when you ignore the nonenforcement of existing restrictions on gun purchase and ownership while urging additional restrictions, you lose credibility. Passing all the laws we want but never insuring they are followed does not make anyone safer. MARC ALTMAN Chadds Ford, Pa., May 21, 1999 http://www.nytimes.com

1402.text: GUN-4019 PRIMARY SLAIN MAN'S SHOES MARCH IN THE WAR AGAINST GUNS He was the life of the family, always getting everyone together and inspiring them to laughter. Now, Leon Pickett - shot down in front of two daughters during a botched robbery attempt last year - will help inspire others as his family donates a pair of his shoes for a national protest against gun violence. "He was always a giving person, always willing to help anyone," said his sister, Stephanie Dildy, 28. "Maybe this will help wake some people up." Pickett's shoes will be among the 40,000 pairs organizers hope to collect to place around the Reflecting Pond in Washington, D.C., in what is being billed as the Silent March, a Sept. 30 protest against gun violence. The shoes will symbolize the nearly 40,000 who died from gunshot wounds in 1993, the most recent year for which statistics are available. "We want to make these victims visible to fight politically," said Debra Bari, the event's Florida coordinator, who hopes to get 2,292 pairs of shoes to represent everyone from this state who died from gun violence in 1993. "We need better laws about who gets guns." Pickett's family couldn't agree more. The 43-year-old father and grandfather was killed on Dec. 14 while waiting outside the Publix supermarket at Military Trail and Woolbright Road west of Boynton Beach, where he worked as an assistant manager. His two daughters, arriving to drive him home, witnessed the killing. Celestine Pickett, 22, stopped going to counseling because another victim's relative looked too much like her father.

1403.text: GUN-31194 PRIMARY Criminals' One-Stop Shopping?; Many Weapons Used in Crimes Are Traced to a Few Gun Stores When criminals need guns, they have plenty of options in a country with nearly 100,000 licensed gun stores. But drug dealers and other crooks don't shop just anywhere. They have their favorites. In Compton, Calif., gangsters preferred Boulevard Sales & Service, a shop police describe as so felon-friendly that some salesmen offered tips on how to buy a gun despite a criminal record. In Philadelphia, shady gun buyers sent girlfriends to a suburban pawnshop, Lou's Loan, where the staff wouldn't raise a fuss if a young woman came by a few times a month to buy  cheap handguns. And on the outskirts of New Orleans, killers-to-be armed themselves at Elliot's Gun Shop. Over the past five years, the store was the source of 2,300 weapons later linked to crime, including 125 homicides, according to the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives. In fact, government figures show that a small number of gun shops account for a spectacularly large number of weapons used in crimes. Such stores have long occupied protected territory. The products they sell are legal. Congress has sheltered them from lawsuits and limited the power of regulators. It can take years for ATF to revoke a dealer's license. But there are signs that scrutiny is on the rise. Over the past three years, ATF agents have cracked down on some of the stores most notorious for selling large numbers of weapons used in street crime. In 2005 and 2006, about  220 firearms dealers had their licenses revoked, 20 more than in the previous eight years combined.

1407.text: GUN-89 PRIMARY Police Lift Ban on Gun Ward Carries, a Glock A day after it was disclosed that Police Commissioner Benjamin Ward had been licensed to carry an Austrian-made handgun that is banned in New York City, the Police Department announced that it had dropped its prohibition and would allow officers and private citizens to purchase and carry the pistol. The 9 millimeter 17-shot pistol, called a Glock 17, is made partly of plastic. The police banned the Glock, a semiautomatic, four years ago when it was first introduced on the American market, saying research was needed to determine whether its plastic parts made it undetectable by magnetic and X-ray security devices like those in airports and courtrooms. But late yesterday evening, Robert Goldman, deputy police commissioner for legal matters, announced that ''the Glock will no longer be prohibited from being a licensed weapon,'' saying that a long period of research had demonstrated that the gun ''could not be at this time defined as an undetectable weapon, and in fact can be detected with today's present technology in the security field.'' Mr. Goldman, asked whether the publicity over Mr. Ward's weapon had prompted the decision, told reporters: ''It played no part other than the timing.'' Without the publicity, ''it's a decision I would have made next week,'' he said. On Wednesday, The Associated Press reported that Mr. Ward had obtained a license to carry a Glock, which weighs less than similar pistols.

1406.text: GUN-525 PRIMARY Congress Didn't Want to See. . . I wheeled my way up to both houses of Congress in recent months to testify in support of legislation bearing my name: the Brady bill. It would require a national seven-day waiting period for handgun sales from gun dealers. The bill is intended to give police departments time to run a background check on the purchaser, to deny instant access of a concealable weapon to a known felon or to another John W. Hinckley Jr. I believe that if the bill had been law in March 1981, Mr. Hinckley would not have been able to purchase the handgun he used against President Ronald Reagan, and I would not have been wounded and disabled. It was surprising that Representatives who made a point of voting against the bill last time were conspicuously absent when I put in my two cents at the hearing. Perhaps they didn't want to see what a handgun wound can do to a human being. I'm sure it's easier to deal with the gun issue when you're talking about abstract statistics or contributions from political-action committees. The Brady bill, still pending in both houses, is a political winner. Few pieces of legislation are endorsed by every major law enforcement group in America and backed by the overwhelming majority of voters. The point left unsaid was that voting against the bill could make the legislator a loser in November.

1405.text: GUN-1350 PRIMARY THE RIGHT TO BEAR ARMS In his Sept. 24 letter, R.M. Parks says he wishes NRA gun lovers would get thewording of the Second Amendment correct. Parks should read the Second Amendment again and notice that it does not say ''the right of the militia'' or ''the right of the armed forces.'' It says ''the right of the people, '' as in ''We, the people of the United States.'' The phrase, ''the right of the people,'' is found in the First Amendment: ''the right of the people peaceably to assemble''; in the Second Amendment: ''the right of the people to keep and bear arms''; and in the Fourth Amendment: ''The right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers and effects.'' A variation of this wording is found in the Third, Fifth, Sixth, Ninth and 10th Amendments. The first 10 amendments to the Constitution are called the Bill of Rights. They are all guarantees of the rights of sovereign, individual people and are limits on the power and authority of government.   My dictionary defines ''militia'' as ''all able-bodied males of each state between 18 and 45 years of age considered eligible for military service.'' In light of the 14th Amendment: ''nor shall any State . . . deny to any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws.'' This includes just about everyone, doesn't it?   We've got it right, Mr. Parks. We've had it right for the 199 years since the Bill of Rights was ratified. Daniel B. January St. Charles I was very impressed by Greg Freeman's Sept. 14 column on the problem of gun violence in St. Louis and the United States. I am glad to see leaders in the black community calling attention to this tragic spiral of handgun deaths, which has caused a public health crisis all over the country.   I hope that black leadership will accept Freeman's challenge to push for controls on handguns and assault weapons. The immediate hurdle for the congressional leadership is to schedule the bill for a floor vote.   Unfortunately, this bill may never come up for a vote because an element in the House leadership is a pawn for the National Rifle Association.   The NRA is desperately trying to sabotage this bill for reasons unknown to anyone with common sense. This bill does not threaten responsible gun owners.   But the NRA is only one problem. The other problem is the apathy of many Americans, who are fed up with killing and violence but are not letting their representatives know how they feel.   This is the real tragedy because this apathy may defeat the bill. All concerned St. Louisans should call their representative, as well as call House Majority Leader Dick Gephardt, who supports the bill. They should also call House Speaker Tom Foley. If the Brady bill does not come up for a vote in this session, it will be delayed until at least early January and perhaps a year longer. By January, the death toll from handgun killings will be about 5, 600 people. In one year, the same holocaust will claim an estimated 22,000 victims.   Carol Rose University City Let us juxtapose two recently reported events. In New York, an unarmed man is knifed and killed in the subway while attempting to protect his father and mother from a gang of robbers. The mayor of New York subsequently pleads for the citizenry to come out from behind locked doors and take back the streets from the criminals, presumably, with their bare hands.   Some days later, in Miami, the mayor of Miami uses a handgun to scare off two robbers who had threatened to kill his wife if she didn't hand over her valuables.   These events demonstrate two disparate approaches to dealing with urban crime. Based on the facts, we can conclude that a citizen who is armed and willing to defend himself is less likely to have himself or his family be a victim of violent crime than one who does not.   Missouri lawmakers have made it a crime for a citizen to carry a concealed weapon. Police, in general, oppose the possession or carrying of weapons by otherwise law-abiding citizens. This is understandable. It is more comfortable for the police to have to deal with only two classes of citizens: criminals and victims. There is no convenient place in their schema for an armed citizen who acts to prevent himself or herself from becoming a victim.   Nevertheless, it is time for citizens to assess what works, as opposed to what is convenient for police and lawmakers. Armed resistance by citizens in the face of crime works. Political pronouncements to ''take back the streets,'' don't. It is time that the law was changed to permit law-abiding citizens to possess and carry the means to defend themselves.   Anthony C. Lange St. Louis

1408.text: NA-43631 PRIMARY Leahy could slow Obama's efforts on gun control President Obama has promised quick action to get a series of gun-control proposals through Congress and has been moving purposefully to that end. But one of his key allies on Capitol Hill, Sen. Patrick J. Leahy (D-Vt.), could prove to be an early obstacle to the president's swift pace. Leahy, who chairs the Senate Judiciary Committee and will be at the White House on Wednesday when Obama unveils proposals from Vice President Biden to limit gun violence, said he wants to hear from more than just the administration. Leahy said he plans to field ideas from a broad range of voices before advancing any legislation through the committee. "I want everybody to be heard," Leahy said in an interview Tuesday, despite potential pressure from the White House to act quickly. "I have some ideas of things that can be done, and if there are things in the vice president's proposal that the committee agrees with, then they'll be included in the legislation. If not, they won't be." Leahy's decision to remain at the helm of the Judiciary Committee, instead of seeking the powerful Appropriations Committee gavel, was greeted with disappointment by some gun-control advocates, who worry his centrist views on gun issues will make him an insufficient advocate for bold action. Advocates blame Leahy for stalling efforts to push new restrictions after the shooting rampage two years ago in which then-Rep. Gabrielle Giffords (D-Ariz.) was wounded.

1409.text: GUN-31102 PRIMARY Chicago Ban May Test Ruling; Challenges Follow Justices' Decision on D.C. Gun-Control Law One small reflection of Chicago's bloody year is a sign outside a South Side school that says, "Congratulations Class of 2008. Stop the Violence." The school is not a college or a high school, but Carnegie Elementary in Woodlawn. In a city where homicide rates have risen by 13 percent over the same period last year and 26 students were killed by gunfire in the past school year,  Mayor Richard M. Daley (D) thinks  the Supreme Court majority that overturned the District's  gun ban last month is detached from urban reality. "If they think that's the answer, then they're greatly mistaken," Daley protested after hearing that Chicago's 26-year-old gun law is  at risk. "Then why don't we do away with the court system and go back to the Old West? You have a gun and I have a gun, and we'll settle in the streets." Chicago officials say they have reason to be concerned about the high court's decision. The city looks likely to provide the next critical test of the justices' ruling as courts decide how far the decision extends to other cities and the 50 states. Within hours of the 5 to 4 decision written by Justice Antonin Scalia, gun rights groups filed fresh challenges to Chicago's restrictions. Scalia, who spent five years as a law professor at the University of Chicago, close to some of the city's most violent precincts, made clear that some restrictions would be permitted, but the majority opinion left unclear what standards courts should use to assess them.

1414.text: GUN-6704 PRIMARY Second Amendment ruse Their thinking is, Don't confuse me with facts. So all of you self-appointed constitutional lawyers, answer this question: Why is it that the NRA never challenges gun control laws on Second Amendment grounds? The answer is simple: They know that they would lose. So it serves their purpose better to spread lies to the gullible, the ignorant, and those too lazy to look up the truth. The NRA used to be a respectable organization of sportsmen, but it was changed into a group of extremists who refuse to recognize the need for personal responsibility in firearms ownership. The NRA has blocked sensible firearms control in our country for years in spite of the fact that about 168 Americans die every other day by gunshot. Lawyers should get back to the question and answer it for all of us who want to slow down this national slaughter with a step in the right direction: Where's the Second Amendment gun control challenge? LaMOTTE AKIN Chapel Hill

1411.text: NA-42452 PRIMARY Lots of questions As expected, the murder rampage in Santa Barbara, California, by Elliot Rodger brought an immediate avalanche of heartrending appeals for more draconian gun laws. But that knee-jerk reaction overlooks the fact that of the six people Rodger killed, three were stabbed to death. Yet where are the corresponding cries for knife laws? After all, according to 2012 FBI statistics, 1,589 murders were committed with knives and other cutting instruments, nearly six times as many as were killed by rifles of all types (of which the gun control extremists' favorite target, the improperly labeled "assault rifle," is just a subset). Furthermore, virtually all these murderous rampages in recent years were done by individuals taking or withdrawing from psychiatric drugs. Therefore, it was no surprise when the Santa Barbara County sheriff said that Rodgers had received mental health treatment and counseling. But, unfortunately, his condition apparently had not reached the point where he would have been prohibited to purchase firearms. The fact is we don't need more gun laws; the existing background check system would have screened out Rodger IF criteria for disqualifying him had been breached. And that brings us to the crux of the issue: Where does society draw a line in the sand with regard to mental health? Should any use of psychiatric drugs, past or present, disqualify you from acquiring firearms? Should a patient who has successfully completed a mental care regimen have his/her firearm prohibition rescinded? Is there a clearcut point at which mental health care professionals should be required to breach doctor-patient confidentiality? Lots of questions to which I doubt that there are infallible answers. Plus, I'm concerned that one consequence of establishing firm thresholds in the mental health arena for gun ownership is that people who genuinely need mental health care will decide, instead, to forgo such care. What will that mean in terms of future such incidents?

1412.text: GUN-1842 PRIMARY BRADY GUN BILL EXPECTED TO PASS AS SEPARATE LAW A House subcommittee is expected to move the Brady Bill onto a fast track to passage today after Democratic leaders this week abandoned any hopes of passing the gun control measure within a comprehensive crime bill. Left in the slow lane will be such divisive proposals as expanding the death penalty and limiting appeals for death-row inmates - two provisions that have helped defeat three previous comprehensive crime bills. ``Once and for all, the Brady Bill will be on the president's desk before we get out of here in November,'' said Rep. Charles Schumer, D-N.Y., during Thursday's meeting of the House Judiciary Committee. In a letter to the committee, White House budget director Leon Panetta said President Clinton also wants the bill passed before the winter adjournment. ``It is time for us to act without delay,'' Panetta said. The committee Thursday passed Clinton's $3.4 billion plan to hire up to 50,000 new police officers in the next six years. It will now go to the House floor, where it is expected to pass. The Brady Bill would impose a national five-day waiting period on handgun purchases. Police would be urged, but not required, to make a criminal background check of gun buyers to ensure none is a felon. In about five years, most states are expected to have computerized their criminal records, and the waiting period would be replaced with an instant criminal background check for handgun purchases.

characters: White House budget director Leon Panetta

1410.text: GUN-5152 PRIMARY COUNCIL TO EXPLORE HOW TO LIMIT ACCESS TO FIREARMS / ALARMED BY THE TOLL AMONG THE YOUNG, COUNCIL CALLED FOR A HEARING. A GROUP PLANS A PR CAMPAIGN, TOO. Alarmed at the toll of gun violence on city youngsters, City Council plans to explore what the state and city government can do to limit access to firearms. A Council resolution, sponsored by Councilwoman Happy Fernandez and approved unanimously Thursday, calls for a hearing to be held, probably in late April. The session is to include testimony from prosecutors of gun traffickers; federal firearms agents; city police; academic experts; and social workers and physicians who work with gunshot victims. Fernandez (D., at-large) said yesterday that the session would also explore whether state lawmakers should approve a law limiting sales to one gun per month per applicant. Such laws have recently gone into effect in Virginia, Maryland and South Carolina, but Fernandez acknowledged that such a restriction would likely face a cool reception in Harrisburg, where many politicians oppose gun control. "We need a two-pronged attack," Fernandez said. "Yes, we do have more control over what happens here, but we would be in error if we didn't at least address some of the problems at the state level." In another action targeted against guns, the Father's Day Rally Committee - a community organization that has long been working to curb violence - has begun a public-relations campaign in the African American community with the slogan, "Brothers are going to work it out. Let us use our arms to embrace each other, not erase each other."

1416.text: GUN-2207 PRIMARY WHITE FLAG FROM THE NRA Facing a deadline for appealing to the U.S. Supreme Court to overturn California's ban on semiautomatic assault weapons, the National Rifle Association decided to come out with its hands up. NRA spokesmen tried to put the best possible face on this surrender, saying it will continue to keep the issue in its sights. But the withdrawal is one more sign that the group once universally considered the high-caliber gun lobby may be losing some of its firepower.   The case began when the nation was shocked by the killing of five children and the wounding of 30 more in a playground at a Stockton, Calif., school by a man with an AK-47 rifle. The state quickly enacted the Assault Weapons Control Act of 1989, which bans the sale and possession of the 21 types of rifles, eight types of pistols and three kinds of shotguns unless a permit is issued.   The NRA, which has consistently fought such laws on the basis of the Second Amendment right to bear arms, lost its case in federal district court and on appeal as well.   The deadline to file a further appeal with the Supreme Court was Nov. 12, but the NRA allowed the date to pass. Richard Gardiner, legislative counsel for the 2.5 million-member group, said that if the NRA had filed an appeal and the case was not accepted by the court, gun-control advocates would have portrayed such a decision as a big defeat. Instead of leaving themselves open for such a public relations setback, he said, NRA officials decided to leave this battle behind and concentrate on fighting the law in California state courts. "This doesn't mean we are dropping the issue by any means," he said. But such bobbing and weaving can't disguise the NRA's reduced clout. Last year, it failed to win Supreme Court review on another high-profile issue, the federal ban on the civilian sale of fully automatic machine guns. Now, it has fired and fallen back on the California law as well. No one is accusing the NRA of only shooting blanks. But as public acceptance grows for the notion that certain high-powered weapons have no place on the street, the lobby's once-intimidating big guns sound more like popguns.

1417.text: GUN-3953 PRIMARY LETTERS FROM THE PEOPLE 2nd Amendment Vs. Safety The recent ban by the British government on handguns is a lead that the American government should follow. In 1994, 13,673 Americans were killed by firearms. How may more deaths is it going to take before the American people and government combat this problem with both legislation and personal responsibility? I understand the fears of many gun owners that gun control will be yet another opportunity for the government to extend its authority into their lives. But aren't the lives of more than 13,000 Americans worth a few limits on our freedom? Gun owners argue that they need their guns for protection. But how much protection do they really offer when statistics show that the chances of being shot rise sharply in homes with firearms? The American government needs to implement effective gun-control laws. The Brady Law is a significant step, but it will do nothing to prevent the senseless deaths of those killed by guns in the home. If keeping guns is that important to licensed gun owners, they should do as the British now do: Keep them in safes at gun clubs and spare the lives of their family members. Julie Fogarty - Sunset Hills * Get a grip, Post-Dispatch! You have been chanting the same tired ban-the-guns mantra for years with total disregard for the truth, the facts and the safety of your readers and your community.

1415.text: GUN-587 PRIMARY First arrest made under new gun law TALLAHASSEE - A Tallahassee grandfather apparently became the first    person in the state to be arrested for violating a new gun safety law    designed to keep loaded guns out of the hands of children. Leon County sheriff's deputies charged Willie W. Green, 60, with violating the new gun access law after his 8-year-old granddaughter was wounded with a loaded .32-caliber automatic she found in a closet. In a separate incident, a 9-year-old accidentally shot and killed a playmate in Palatka with a .22-caliber rifle he took down from a gun    rack in his home. Police say an adult could be charged under the new    gun safety law in that case also. The new law took effect Oct. 1 and makes it a third-degree felony    for adults to leave loaded guns in an unsecured area if children get    them and cause injury or death to themselves or others. Convictions    under the law could result in a maximum five-year prison sentence and a    fine of $ 5,000. Dick Simpson, spokesman for the Leon County Sheriff's Department,    said Green's granddaughter, Shelia Owens , also of Tallahassee, and her    mother were visiting Green's home with several other relatives when the    incident occurred. A bullet the girl fired from the gun went through a portion of her thumb, through a bedroom door, a bookcase and onto the floor of the    living room, where other family members were gathered, Simpson said.

1413.text: GUN-3893 PRIMARY Around the South; REGION IN BRIEF; Louisiana may allow concealed guns Allowing Louisiana residents to carry concealed weapons is a bad idea, Gov. Edwin Edwards says, but he adds that vetoing the popular legislation would probably be useless. A concealed gun measure passed overwhelmingly in the Senate last week and is considered a sure bet to pass the House as early as this week. Edwards said he believes support is so strong that the House and Senate would override a veto. Though he's an avid hunter and gun collector, Edwards said the average person is not emotionally or physically qualified to be walking around with a concealed pistol. "People don't realize that it's different shooting snakes with a revolver than it is shooting people," he said. Adults without criminal charges, mental problems or alcoholism could get annual permits for concealed guns under the bill. -FISH STORIES: Charlie Moore, supervisor of the finfish program for the South Carolina Natural Resources Department, says he agrees with fishermen who gave federal regulators an earful about red tape at a Charleston, S.C., hearing. When he started with the department in 1977, he said, there were four laws regulating saltwater fishing. Now there are regulations for 76 species, all with size and take limits. "A fisherman called the other day and said, 'I don't think I'm smart enough to go fishing anymore,' " Moore said. SAY WHAT? "Never go swimming in a creek and always watch out for alligators." - Advice from 7-year-old Tyee Hammons of Gainesville, Fla., who suffered deep gashes in his shoulder and side after being attacked by an 8 1/2-foot alligator earlier this week.

1418.text: GUN-1476 PRIMARY There's Plenty Wrong With Gun Registration To the Editor: Re "Treat Guns Like Cars" (editorial, May 16): For you to disparage as "a simplistic view of safety" those Los Angeles citizens who, in the absence of effective police protection, arm themselves against the potential of mob violence to person and property is disturbingly elitist and out of touch. Your proposal for universal gun licensing is equally so. How could universal licensing, for example, stop the black market, from which the vast majority of criminals get their guns? And how would it prevent gun smuggling from other countries? You say a fear of registration as a precursor to confiscation makes no sense. But your own city is using registration records of so-called assault weapons to confiscate them, despite promises to the contrary when registration was imposed. The District of Columbia used registration records to enforce its ban on private ownership of firearms, again, despite assurances that they would never be used for that purpose. The tired old analogy of auto registration as a model for gun control is patently false. Vehicle operation is a governmentally dispensed privilege, not a constitutionally protected right. A better analogy would be the requirement of tests for sanity, criminal history, drugs, American history and literacy before one could exercise the right to vote. Besides, in crimes where cars are concerned, we don't impose drug tests, waiting periods and bans on models used; nor do we allow the police absolute and arbitrary control over who can and cannot get licenses to drive.

1420.text: GUN-10323 PRIMARY New gun store opens in Sunnyvale amid concerns from neighbors Neighbors concerned about the opening of a new gun store in Sunnyvale turned out unannounced to a Sept. 28 city council meeting, hoping the council could prevent the opening despite the store securing all the appropriate licenses and permits. The council did not have the authority to prevent the opening, however, as the site in question is zoned for gun sales and the issue was not on the meeting's agenda. Residents' concerns were heard during a period of oral requests. U.S. Firearms Supply Co., 590 S. Mary Ave., opened its doors on Oct. 2 with little fanfare and no protests, despite residents expressing displeasure to the council over the shop's location. "This is not an issue of banning gun sales or restricting people's Second Amendment rights; it's simply in regard to the location of this business," Sunnyvale resident Casey Miller said. "I'm sure there are many locations where this type of business would be appropriate, but this is not one of them." More than a dozen residents turned out to support Miller's assertion and asked the council to reconsider allowing the store to open. Another dozen or so turned out to support the store. U.S. Firearms is located a block and a half from the Sunnyvale Christian School and backs up against residential housing. When Vice Mayor Chris Moylan informed the crowd that the site in question was zoned to allow firearm sales, the store owner had completed all the appropriate applications and forms, and the council had no jurisdiction over whether a business could open in a properly zoned location, residents began asking the council to consider changing the zoning of the site to ban firearm sales.

1419.text: GUN-9363 PRIMARY GUNS BANNED IN COURT AREAS JUDGES ISSUE ORDERS THAT VARY BY COUNTY Minnesota judges are using their authority to do something many other government officials wish they could do: Ban guns in their workplaces. This week, judges issued orders that explain where guns are forbidden in courthouses and other county offices. Ramsey County appears to have one of the broader bans -- prohibiting guns everywhere from the city council/county board chambers to a court area in Regions Hospital. In Hastings, however, the ban doesn't extend to the Dakota County boardroom, located in a building connected by a tunnel to the courthouse. The bans are in response to a state law that takes effect Wednesday that will allow more people to obtain handgun permits. Supporters of the "conceal and carry" law say some judges may be extending their authority too far and setting themselves up for legal challenges. "As soon as a judge attempts to set regulations outside the doors to his or her courtroom we will have a problem," said John Caile, spokesman for Minnesota Concealed Carry Reform, Now! Last week, the Conference of Chief Judges, which represents the court districts in Minnesota, decided each chief judge will issue an order "prohibiting possession of firearms within courtrooms, adjacent hallways and other areas attendant to the court functions, as designated by the order." The bans generally don't apply to on-duty law enforcement or when the guns are needed as evidence in court. Violators could be found in contempt of court and be ordered to leave the building or face jail time.

1422.text: GUN-5180 PRIMARY For Law-Abiding Gun Owners, Safety Comes First Although its objective was sarcasm, your editorial was actually correct in stating that it should be a "great source of comfort" that Fairfax County's illegal attempt to restrict the Second Amendment rights of its citizens was overturned ["Pistol-Packers in County Buildings," Dec. 27]. I agreed to be one of the five plaintiffs in this case because for me, it was an issue of public safety. I have no desire to see unauthorized persons bringing weapons into county buildings. But ample laws already exist to prosecute those who would bring a concealed weapon or dangerous device into county buildings -- or any public buildings for that matter. Only one group of citizens was affected by this ban, and that was those of us who have a court-issued permit to carry a concealed handgun. In my case, I began using a county recreation center earlier this year as a major element in my rehabilitation and fitness program after open-heart surgery. I am grateful that the Fairfax County Parks Authority has such a splendid facility to offer. But these facilities have a higher-than-average crime rate, not crimes of violence but crimes against property. In the past, I have had my vehicle broken into on two separate occasions at a recreation center. Since I occasionally legally carry a handgun in my vehicle, I had to make a decision in the interest of public safety when using this county facility. Do I leave the weapon locked in my vehicle, where it would have a higher-than-average probability of being stolen; or do I take it in with me and secure it in a locker with the rest of my belongings? I concluded that the interest of public safety was best served by my taking the weapon in with me. The ban, however, forced me to put this weapon at higher risk by having to leave it in my vehicle.

1421.text: GUN-20198 PRIMARY California targets loopholes in ban on illegal Uzis California is considered a state with some of the toughest gun laws in the country. However, Uzis, such as the one reportedly used in Tuesday's shooting in Los Angeles, can be legal. Last month, Gov. Gray Davis signed an assault weapons ban that prohibits particular types of weapons and features. The list includes guns with detachable, large-capacity magazines, second handgrips and threaded barrels to which silencers can be attached. Although the law goes into effect Jan. 1, owners of the outlawed weapons have until Dec. 31, 2000, to register the guns. Initially, possession of an unregistered weapon will bring a $ 500 fine. Starting in 2001, the crime will carry a maximum penalty of one year in prison. The law addresses loopholes in the state assault weapons ban of 1989, which outlawed several weapons identified by brand names. Gunmakers skirted the law by marketing similar weapons under new names. The Roberti-Roos Assault Weapon Control Act of 1989 was the result of a shooting in a Stockton, Calif., schoolyard in which five children were killed. The law outlawed all versions of the Uzi, an Israeli-made weapon that comes in rifle and pistol forms. However, owners of weapons sold before June 1, 1989, were given until March 1992 to register the weapons. The state attorney general continued to allow owners to register Uzis and other assault weapons until 1997, when Handgun Control Inc. challenged the practice in court. A San Francisco superior court judge ruled last year that the registrations had to stop.

1423.text: GUN-10899 PRIMARY No guts on gun reform "My administration has not curtailed the rights of gun owners," the president boasted, "it has expanded them, including allowing people to carry their guns in national parks and wildlife refuges." No, that wasn't George W. Bush. It was President Obama in an op-ed piece published Sunday in the Arizona Daily Star that practically begged the gun lobby to support modest reforms of our lax gun laws. He wasn't even asking for a ban on those large gun magazines, which would almost certainly have saved lives on that January day when six people were killed and Rep. Gabrielle Giffords was among 13 wounded by gunfire just outside Tucson. What Obama endorsed were, well, baby steps toward strengthening background checks to keep guns out of the hands of "dangerous criminals and fugitives" and those who are "unbalanced." That's a fine idea, though his specific proposals - "enforcing laws that are already on the books," "reward the states that provide the best data," "make the system faster and nimbler" - were hardly the stuff of political courage. But if Obama lacked audacity, he was full of hope for a dialogue. "I'm willing to bet," he wrote, "that responsible, law-abiding gun owners agree that we should be able to keep an irresponsible, law-breaking few . . . from getting their hands on a gun in the first place." And the president's tender approach was promptly met with a slap in the face from the National Rifle Association's chief executive, Wayne LaPierre: "Why should I or the NRA go sit down with a group of people that have spent a lifetime trying to destroy the Second Amendment in the United States?"

1426.text: NA-41304 PRIMARY RISING, BLOODY TIDE FBI FIGURES An FBI report issued earlier this year concluded that the frequency of U.S. mass shootings more than doubled between 2000 and 2013. There were roughly six attacks per year between 2000-2006. From 2007-2013, there were almost 16 annually. A total of 1,043 people were killed and wounded in 160 "active shooter incidents" over the 13-year period. Roughly 25% of the incidents took place in schools. NYC SHOOTING NUMBERS Through Dec. 7, 2014 1,299 up 5.5% from same period last year. For all of 2013 1,299 people shot. For all of 2012 1,624 people shot. OTHER STATISTICS 52% of Americans favor "gun rights" 46% of Americans favor "gun control" Every day, 86 Americans are killed by gun violence. There were at least 94 school shootings in the last two years, according to the gun control group -Everytown for Gun Safety. *PEW RESEARCH CENTER SURVEY A list of major shooting incidents since the Dec. 14, 2012, massacre at Sandy Hook Elementary School Dec. 12, 2014: Three students wounded outside an alternative high school in Portland, Ore., in an apparent gang dispute. Oct. 24, 2014: Four students killed and one wounded by a classmate at Marysville-Pilchuck High School in Washington state. June 10, 2014: A 15-year-old student at Reynolds High School in Troutdale, Ore., murdered a freshman student and wounded a gym teacher before killing himself.

1425.text: GUN-30378 PRIMARY SHOOT IT DOWN The pistols-for-everyone-everywhere crowd in Washington has engineered diabolical legislation that would blow gaping holes in New York's gun control laws. The measure must be stopped. Republican Sen. John Thune wants Americans across the country to be able to carry concealed weapons, including Americans who live in, work in or visit this city and state. That's apparently the way of life, and death, in his beloved South Dakota. But what flies in Sioux Falls, population 150,000, or capital city Pierre, population 14,000, does not make it in the world's safest big city, population 8.4 million, where the police have saved thousands from gun deaths by vigorously enforcing weapons laws. Thoroughly distorting the legislative process, Thune has attached a pro-gun amendment to a defense authorization bill. It would let anyone granted a concealed carry permit by any state carry a concealed handgun in any other state that issues such permits. Which New York does. But not all concealed carry permits are created equal. New York has some of the strictest rules in the country, and it doesn't have reciprocal agreements with other states that allow visitors to bring guns across the state line. The Thune amendment would gut those safeguards. Anyone meeting the laxest standards of federal law - including people with misdemeanor convictions, long arrest records or alcohol abuse problems - would be able to walk the city streets packing heat. The NYPD could do nothing.

1424.text: GUN-6978 PRIMARY GUN-LOCK MEASURE GOES TO GOVERNOR THE N.J. SENATE UNANIMOUSLY PASSED A BILL REQUIRING TRIGGER LOCKS OR GUN SAFES TO BE SOLD WITH EACH NEW HANDGUN. Anxious to show voters that they are taking steps to control guns, state senators yesterday unanimously passed a measure that would require trigger locks or gun safes to be sold with every handgun purchase made at stores and gun shows. But few like the bill. Antigun lobbyists and most Democrats say it does too little. Gun-rights advocates say it is unnecessary. The measure has already passed the state Assembly and now heads to the governor's desk for consideration. Trigger locks immobilize the trigger of the gun. They come in different forms. Some are operated with a key, others with a combination lock. The action brings the Republican Legislature one step closer to setting itself up for the fall Assembly elections with a gun-safety measure in their campaign arsenal. Democrats have vowed to make gun control a campaign issue. Senate Democratic Leader Richard Cody tried - but failed - to get the Senate to table the trigger lock bill, S-1686, yesterday and have the Senate consider his tougher gun-control measure, S-1711. "The American public, the New Jersey public, wants serious, meaningful gun control," said Codey. "Trigger locks alone are nothing more than a Band-Aid when you need a tourniquet." Codey's "smart gun" bill would have required all guns be sold with technology that allows them to be fired only by their owners. But his motion to have that bill considered in lieu of the trigger-lock measure failed by a vote of 17-19. Two Republicans - Sens. Peter Inverso (R., Mercer) and Joseph Palaia (R., Monmouth) - voted with Democrats for the smart-gun bill.

1428.text: GUN-195 PRIMARY GUN REFORM MUST BE A BALANCE OF INTERESTS To the Editor: ''Will Handgun Foes Be Over a Barrel?'' by Peter W. Rodino Jr. (Op-Ed, March 28) presents a deliberate distortion of the provisions of the McClure-Volkmer bill. Representative Rodino asserts that if this much-needed gun-control-reform bill is passed, ''it would be easier for anyone - criminals, drug addicts, mental incompetents - to buy a handgun.'' To the contrary, the bill specifically prohibits people in these groups from buying or carrying a handgun and has mandatory and stiff penalties. With millions of handguns and easily converted shotguns and rifles in circulation in this country, restrictive gun-control laws of the type supported by Mr. Rodino have no effect on the criminal, yet make handgun ownership and use by responsible law-abiding citizens difficult or impossible. Ironically, in those areas where crime rates are highest and people have the most need to defend themselves against criminals, unreasonable ''strict'' gun control laws have the effect of allowing only criminals, drugs addicts and mental incompetents to carry handguns. If we are truly serious about protecting every American from senseless violence, we must do two things. First, we must make the use of any weapon during a crime a felony with mandatory and significant penalties. Second, we must eliminate laws that prevent Americans from defending themselves. The McClure-Volkmer bill, as passed by the Senate, does both. KIRK P. LINDVIG Malvern, Pa., March 29, 1986

1430.text: NA-44725 PRIMARY Two years later, Newtown has meant almost nothing to the gun debate The tendency of all political analysts -- The Fix included -- amid a major news story is to over-estimate its impact on the political zeitgeist. And that's especially the case these days, with an increasingly polarized public and an intransigent Congress that can rarely agree on anything. Perhaps no event has driven this home like the 2012 school shootings in Newtown, Conn. After 26 died, Democrats and the White House made an all-out push for new gun laws. They wound up focusing on increased background checks -- something polls showed as many as nine in 10 Americans supported (in theory). A few Republican senators even jumped on board. But it failed. With the two-year anniversary of Newtown this weekend, support for gun control has reached a new low. A Pew poll shows, for the first time, a majority of Americans prioritize gun rights (52 percent) over gun control (46 percent). As recently as 1999, two-thirds of Americans picked gun control over gun rights. After Newtown, it was 51-46 in favor of gun control. The progression has been remarkably steady. Compared to after Newtown, people are also significantly more likely to say the guns protect people rather than endanger them. While the numbers are striking, this isn't really all that new. Polls have long shown this trend toward gun rights over gun control. Here's Gallup's version. In 2000, 62 percent wanted stricter gun control. Today, it's 47 percent, and 52 percent either want gun laws kept as their are or scaled back.

1429.text: GUN-30793 PRIMARY Does Owning Guns Prevent Crime? To the Editor: Re ''A Rifle in Every Pot'' (Op-Ed, Jan. 16): Glenn Reynolds may be right in his contention that mandatory firearm ownership helps maintain lower crime rates in some communities, yet the resolution of the question of gun control is not furthered by simply tallying the benefits that accrue to specific groups as a result of ready access to these weapons.
