The widespread availability of guns in this country brings with it benefits to some and costs to others. Because the commerce in firearms is hardly impeded by state boundaries, lowered crimes rates in towns like Greenleaf, Idaho, have to be considered with respect to increased homicide rates in cities like Atlanta. This weighing of the pluses and minuses of an activity and determining an appropriate level of regulation is a legitimate role for government. Gun control is no exception. Marc Merlin Atlanta, Jan. 16, 2007 To the Editor: Glenn Reynolds argues that America's relatively high rate of gun ownership compared with that of other developed countries leads us to have a lower rate of ''hot'' burglaries (burglaries of occupied homes). This claim rests on very fragile evidence, as Prof. Philip J. Cook of Duke University and I demonstrated in a 2003 study published by the Brookings Institution. In fact, we show that within the United States, counties with higher rates of household gun ownership may, if anything, have higher burglary rates.

1431.text: GUN-2690 PRIMARY Soliloquy in the shower Recently, I have been interviewing political candidates seeking endorsements for the July primary election. In these interviews, my job is to gather information, not argue. Still, after the day is done, it's hard to not engage in mental debates with the candidates. In the shower, I can respond to the one who opposed gun control, saying: "If we take away guns, criminals will kill people with ropes or knives or fingernail files." Obviously, that assertion is true. Criminals were killing people long before gunpowder was invented. If we banned all guns today, criminals would continue to kill. But what does that have to do with the need for sensible gun laws? I don't want assault rifles sold easily because mentally unstable people will buy them. Criminals don't worry me as much as guys such as Dean Mellberg, who carried an assault rifle with a 75-round drum into Fairchild Air Force Base near Spokane, Wash. In just minutes, Mellberg was able to fire at least 50 shots, slaughtering four people and wounding 23, before being shot down enough. Despite his well-documented history of mental illness, he had no trouble buying an incredibly dangerous weapon from a gun dealer. If he had been using a fingernail file during his rampage, no massacre would have occurred. Another topic that comes up during endorsement interviews is the treatment of gays. Often, candidates will say: "I oppose 'special' rights for gays. I think everyone should be treated equally."

1435.text: GUN-10819 PRIMARY AL QAEDA AT THE GUN SHOW A chilling Internet video confirms what has long been suspected: Al Qaeda terrorists are eager to launch a gun-fueled rampage against Americans - and they know just how to do it. Spotted on jihadist forums, the video shows American-born Al Qaeda fighter Adam Gadahn almost cheerily urging followers to action: "America is absolutely awash with easily obtainable firearms. You can go down to a gun show at the local convention center and come away with a fully automatic assault rifle without a background check and most likely without having to show an identification card. So what are you waiting for?" We don't say this often about Al Qaeda types, but: He's right on the facts. Gadahn owes thanks to the National Rifle Association, whose political muscle has kept open two indefensible gun-rights loopholes that all but invite terrorists to buy weapons. Unlike those who purchase guns from a dealer, people who buy firearms from private sellers at gun shows don't have to pass a background check. The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms says 30% of illegally trafficked weapons are linked to gun shows. In fact, an undercover investigation of private sellers at gun shows found that nearly two-thirds broke the law by selling to people who they thought could not pass a background check. Then there's the so-called terror gap, which prevents gun sellers from checking purchasers against terrorism watch lists. More than 1,450 people on terror watch lists tried to buy guns and explosives from February 2004 to December 2010; on 1,321 occasions, 91% of all tries, the FBI was powerless to stop the sale.

1427.text: NA-40126 PRIMARY 'INSANE' GUN BILL DIES IN SENATE It's not often that a bill backed by the National Rifle Association dies in the Florida Legislature. But that's what happened Thursday, thanks to the lobbying force of the Florida Sheriff's Association and the legislative acumen of Sen. Jack Latvala, R-Clearwater, who for the second straight day helped kill a controversial bill. SB 296 would have allowed those "in lawful possession" of guns to conceal weapons without a permit during mandatory evacuations and local emergencies, such as riots. That's not the same as a gun owner, and could apply to adult children or spouses of gun owners with clean criminal records who are found carrying guns. The bill, which passed the House last month 80-36, was called "insane" by Pinellas County Sheriff Bob Gualtieri. He worked with Latvala on an amendment that would limit the exemption for concealed weapons permits to 24 hours. It also removed the exemption once the carrier of the weapon reached a destination upon evacuation. "It just provides a reasonable limit," Latvala said. The amendment was adopted on a 23-15 board vote, with the support of Tampa Bay Sens. Latvala, John Legg, R-Trinity, and Arthenia Joyner, D-Tampa. Tom Lee, R-Brandon, Wilton Simpson, R-Trilby, Bill Galvano, R-Bradenton, and the sponsor of the bill, Jeff Brandes, R-St. Petersburg, voted against the amendment. Afterward, Brandes pulled the bill and said he isn't going to bring it back this session.

1436.text: GUN-2407 PRIMARY GUN DEALER FEE MAY INCREASE The Clinton administration will propose a sharp increase in the federal licensing fee for gun dealers in an effort to reduce the number of people selling weapons, Treasury Secretary Lloyd Bentsen said Monday. ``You are going to see an increase in the licensing fees for the selling of guns,'' Bentsen said in an interview with The Associated Press. Since 1968, gun dealers have paid $30 for a three-year license, or $10 per year. The recently enacted Brady law increases that figure to $200 for three years for new licenses and sets the fee for renewal of a valid license at $90 for three years. Bentsen said the administration is studying a larger increase as part of a broad crackdown on violent crime. Any increase would have to be approved by Congress. ``It makes no sense at all to have over 250,000 dealers in guns today and to have a very minimum payment where you have people even operating out of their kitchens,'' the Treasury secretary said. He said a higher fee would reduce the number of dealers and make it easier for Treasury's Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms to do its job of regulating gun dealers. ``There is no way with over 250,000 (registered gun dealers) for us to be able to supervise them and see that they are abiding by the laws,'' Bentsen said.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1432.text: NA-41704 PRIMARY Christie signs anti-gun-violence measures TRENTON - Gov. Christie signed 10 bills Thursday targeting gun violence in New Jersey, including measures requiring that certain mental-health records be submitted to the federal background-check database and increasing penalties for gun trafficking. Christie did not take action on several other measures passed by the Legislature after the December massacre in Newtown, Conn., including a proposal to ban .50-caliber guns and another requiring that gun-permit information be encoded on driver's licenses. The driver's license proposal, sponsored by Senate President Stephen Sweeney (D., Gloucester), would overhaul the state's system for gun purchases, requiring the creation of a database that would allow gun dealers to instantly check whether a person is qualified to buy a firearm. Gun owners also would have to complete a firearms safety course. Those proposals have drawn opposition from gun-rights groups, including a New Hampshire organization that recently urged supporters to ask Christie to veto the bills. "Tell him you're watching with 2016 in mind," said the group, Pro-Gun New Hampshire, referring to a possible presidential bid by Christie. On its website, the Association of New Jersey Pistol and Rifle Clubs described the bills still awaiting action from Christie - including one requiring law enforcement agencies to report data on unlawfully used or possessed guns - as "horrific antigun bills" and "the worst of the bills that made it to the governor's desk."

characters: Association of New Jersey Pistol and Rifle Clubs

1434.text: GUN-11191 PRIMARY Fla. shooting reignites 'Stand Your Ground' debate The shooting of unarmed teen Trayvon Martin in Florida last week has sparked a national debate over "Stand Your Ground" laws. But in many states, fights over the controversial legislation have been going on for years without garnering much attention from anyone other than prosecutors and gun-rights activists. Although George Zimmerman admits to shooting Martin, he says he acted in self-defense and has not been arrested. Under a 2005 Florida law, a citizen who uses deadly force is immune from prosecution when "he or she reasonably believes that such force is necessary to prevent imminent death or great bodily harm to himself or herself or another." Backed by the National Rifle Association, first in Florida and then across the country, state legislators have pushed for expanding the right to use deadly force. Twenty-one states have laws giving citizens wide latitude to use deadly force without first attempting to retreat. Here are some states where "Stand Your Ground" was recently passed or is up for debate: l A bill passed by the legislature in Minnesota was just vetoed by Gov. Mark Dayton (D). l In Pennsylvania, former governor Ed Rendell (D) vetoed a "Stand Your Ground" bill; it was signed last year by Gov. Tom Corbett (R). l Democratic lawmakers staged a walkout in Iowa recently to avoid a vote on the legislation. It will probably be blocked on procedural grounds this year, but state Rep. Matt Windschitl (R) plans to reintroduce it next year.

1433.text: GUN-9870 PRIMARY PENNSYLVANIA COUNTY STOPS TAKING CONCEALED GUN APPLICATIONS FROM HERE * The sheriff has received more than 650 mailed requests for a permit from Missourians. Applications for concealed weapon permits are pouring into Centre County here from Missouri after a court challenge there, apparently because a gun enthusiast Web site and media accounts referenced the county's permitting process. The Sheriff's Department has received 650 mailed applications and more than 500 telephone calls from Missourians since Monday, officials said. Sheriff Denny Nau decided Thursday not to process applications postmarked after that day, citing legal concerns and a lack of manpower. His announcement came just hours after a mail carrier delivered hundreds of letters bearing Missouri postmarks. Their number topped the 256 that had arrived the day before, the carrier told a disbelieving Corrine Peters, the office secretary. The phone calls alone were straining the office's resources, especially because an older person who sometimes volunteers was on vacation. "I called my mother-in-law," Peters said. "She's coming in tomorrow." Missouri Attorney General Jay Nixon recommended last week that counties wait to begin issuing permits until lawmakers could take action after a complex Supreme Court ruling. The Supreme Court upheld the right of the Legislature to legalize concealed guns but said the law could illegally impose new duties on sheriffs without properly compensating them for handling the permits. A few Missouri sheriffs have begun taking concealed gun permit applications, but many others are waiting for clarification. Meanwhile, other parts of the law are in effect, including provisions allowing concealed guns in the passenger area of vehicles without the need of a permit and recognizing concealed-carry permits from other states.

1437.text: NA-44249 PRIMARY Harsh new ad hits Flake for gun control vote Gun control supporters are continuing to target Sen. Jeff Flake, with a group led by New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg (I) launching a new ad buy against the Arizona Republican. The Mayors Against Illegal Guns ad features the mother of a victim of the movie theater shootings in Aurora, Colo., Caren Teaves. She criticizes Flake for voting against the Senate's background checks legislation after telling her in a personal letter than he supported expanding them. "The issue isn't just background checks; it's keeping your promise," Teaves says, "and Senator Flake didn't." A spokeswoman for MAIG said the ad buy is six figures but wouldn't specify beyond that. Flake has admitted that he has noticed the criticisms of his vote. "Nothing like waking up to a poll saying you're the nation's least popular senator," he wrote on Facebook shortly after the background checks vote. "Given the public's dim view of Congress in general, that probably puts me somewhere just below pond scum." Gun control advocates have focused their efforts on recent weeks on Flake and another GOP senator, Kelly Ayotte (N.H.), whose numbers have also dipped since the vote. Their hope is that Ayotte and Flake may support a renewed effort on background checks. Flake, for what it's worth, was just elected to his first term and won't face reelection until 2018.

1438.text: NA-41253 PRIMARY Guns = death A gun rights article of faith holds that if everyone went around armed with a gun, then everyone would be a whole lot safer. No, what you get where guns are plentiful is Michael David Dunn killing unarmed 17-year-old Jordan Davis. Dunn, a 47-year-old computer programmer, encountered Davis at a gas station in Florida, one of America's most heavily armed states. They argued over rap music emanating from an SUV carrying Davis and three friends. Dunn, who is white, fired 10 shots into the fleeing SUV, killing Davis, who was black. Dunn later said that he saw what he took to be the tip of a shotgun barrel. No such weapon was recovered. While in custody, Dunn described himself as the "f------ victim." As a defense, he invoked Florida's "stand your ground law," a statute that lets people open fire, without trying to avoid doing so, if they feel threatened. The same law helped George Zimmerman escape conviction in the death of Trayvon Martin. Dunn's story won him a hung jury on the charge of murdering Davis but secured his conviction for attempting to murder Davis' companions because he fired as they were taking off. Facing 60 years in prison, Dunn asked his lawyer, "How is this happening?" The answer, sir, is that you had a gun and you used it in an atmosphere in which anyone around you might be armed - in a state that empowers gun owners to shoot when they're afraid of someone else who may have a gun. That, or you were just a hotheaded jerk with a chip on his shoulder and a gun in his hand.

1439.text: NA-42968 PRIMARY The Year After Newtown Chris Murphy, a freshman senator from Connecticut, made his maiden speech on the Senate floor on April 10. It was the day the Senate was set to vote on a bill that would make background checks mandatory for anyone who wanted to purchase a gun. ''I didn't expect this,'' he told his colleagues, meaning that when he was running for office he never expected that his first Senate speech would be about gun violence. But then came Dec. 14, 2012, when Adam Lanza barged into the Sandy Hook Elementary School and killed 20 children and six adults in the space of about 10 minutes. Murphy was a senator-elect by then. He was standing on the train platform in Bridgeport, Conn., planning to take his family to see the Christmas tree in Rockefeller Center, when he got the news. He instead made his way to Newtown and was there when the parents were informed that their children were dead. '' I wish I'd never had to see that,'' he told me recently. Newtown galvanized Murphy. By the time he made his speech last April, the gun issue had become his passion. '' When a school group asked what I wanted to see done in my first term, I told them I wanted to see an anti-gun-violence bill pass,'' he said. Toward the end of that April speech, Murphy displayed photographs of some of the children who had died at Newtown. This child had loved to play outside, he said. That child was happiest when he was singing. Murphy visibly choked up as he spoke.

1444.text: NA-41084 PRIMARY BAM! Skeet-shooting photo shows Prez not totally gun-shy GUN CONTROL is more than a political issue for President Obama. The commander-in-chief, in a picture snapped last August, showed his mastery of a shotgun while skeet shooting during a Camp David getaway. The White House released the photo Saturday, possibly to muzzle critics who suggested Obama was posturing when he talked about target shooting in an interview with The New Republic magazine. The picture of Obama shows him with a literally smoking gun, its barrel pressed against his left shoulder as he pulls the trigger. It was taken on his 51st birthday. Obama spokesman Jay Carney had no comment Saturday on the timing of the photo's release, just two days before Obama heads to Minneapolis to discuss his gun control proposals with local law enforcement officials. But critics, including the National Rifle Association, charged Obama used the interview to convince gun owners that the President was a kindred spirit despite his gun control initiative. Rep. Marsha Blackburn (R-Tenn.) had suggested the President's claim of skeet shooting was way off target. "I think he should invite me to Camp David, and I'll go skeet shooting with him and I bet I'll beat him," she said. But the President - in jeans, ear protectors, sunglasses and a blue golf shirt - shows good form in the Aug. 4 handout picture. "Up at Camp David, we do skeet shooting all the time," Obama said in the interview that started the flap.

1443.text: NA-43113 PRIMARY The N.R.A.'s Instant Classic Attack Ads We've written a lot lately on the Republicans' expert use of fear - whether about Ebola or Guantánamo prisoners or general chaos - to drive voters to the right. But the grand master of this technique is the National Rifle Association, which thrives on putting guns in nervous hands. It has been active this year with several instant classics. On the cover of the new election issue of the N.R.A.'s magazine is an Islamic State fighter (waving an automatic rifle, of course) over the headline: "Chaos at our Door? A Dangerous World is Closing In." Inside the issue, as Media Matters for America noted, Wayne LaPierre, the N.R.A.'s chief executive, explained the relevance of this far-away group to Second Amendment issues. "It's just a matter of time before the U.S. faces some kind of terrorist attack," he wrote, and when that happens, Americans will want their guns at their sides because "the government can't - or won't - protect you." Building the hysteria, he concludes: "We will not be left defenseless. We will not give up our freedoms. We will not surrender our safety to the lies of leaders who promise everything and deliver nothing. On Election Day, Tuesday, Nov. 4, we will defend our right to defend ourselves because we have no other choice. We will vote our guns! We will vote our freedom! And we will prevail!"

1441.text: GUN-3651 PRIMARY NRA MEMBERS JUST LIKE ANYONE ELSE EXCEPT SMARTER Contrary to the letter ''Assault-weapons stand injures NRA,'' which was in response to the recent article ''Faces of the NRA,'' most Americans are not against guns. The National Safety Council reports that far more children drown in pools or die playing with matches or are run over in driveways at home. Your fine article by Ron Wiggins was a breath of fresh air, especially from a newspaper that's traditionally been anti-gun. Congratulations. The article shows that the National Rifle Association is made up of people just like your next-door neighbors. I have a question for anti-gun people such as the letter writer: If you're so against guns, why don't you put up signs in your front yard proclaiming, ''There are no guns in this house''? Bill F. Stegkemper, member National Rifle Association board of directors Okeechobee

1446.text: NA-43758 PRIMARY From tragedies, activists are born The mass shootings that have rocked communities across the country in recent years - from Blacksburg, Va., to Tucson to Aurora, Colo., to Oak Creek, Wis., to Newtown, Conn. - have left a well-documented trail of carnage and grief. But those tragedies and others like them also have produced what could prove to be the most formidable, fervent advocates in the looming fight over U.S. gun-control policy: survivors who know what it feels like to be in the crosshairs of a mass murderer and outspoken families who have lost a loved one to gun violence. Stephen Barton is among them. He had planned to spend much of 2013 in Russia, teaching English on a prestigious Fulbright grant. "I was expecting to be freezing in Siberia right now, drinking vodka," joked Barton, 23, who graduated from Syracuse University last spring with a triple major in economics, international relations and Russian studies. Instead, after surviving a shotgun blast last summer inside a movie theater in Aurora and undergoing emergency surgery and the physical therapy that followed, he decided on a different line of work. Six months after the shooting, he is a full-time outreach and policy associate for Mayors Against Illegal Guns, a nationwide coalition that pushes for stricter gun laws. "I said to them, 'I'll do anything and everything. I just want to help out,'aÌ?[#x20ac][#x2c6]" said Barton, who has filmed commercials on behalf of the group and crisscrossed the country from Colorado to Connecticut, speaking with lawmakers and gathering public support for stricter gun control. "It's been empowering and, in a way, therapeutic to do this sort of work."

1442.text: GUN-3547 PRIMARY SUPREME COURT NARROWS USE OF GUN LAW IN DRUG CASES The Supreme Court made it harder Wednesday to lengthen the prison sentence of federally convicted drug dealers who carry guns. In a unanimous decision, the court ruled that the prosecution must show "active employment of the firearm" to invoke a federal law that adds five years to the sentence of a convicted drug dealer who carried a gun while dealing drugs. The question arose in two cases in the District of Columbia. In one, the federal court there tacked five years on the sentence of Roland J. Bailey because a loaded gun was found in his locked car trunk at the same time he had 30 grams of cocaine (a bit under an ounce) in the glove compartment. The evidence was found when Bailey was pulled over in 1988 for a traffic violation. He was sentenced to 51 months for cocaine possession with intent to distribute and gun-possession, and an additional five years for gun use. In the other case, Candisha Robinson, convicted in 1991 for selling crack cocaine out of her apartment to an undercover agent, got an added five years for having an unloaded .22-caliber pistol locked in a trunk in her bedroom closet. Robinson was sentenced to eight years and one month on the selling charge, plus five years for the gun. Justice Sandra Day O'Connor wrote that the interpretation of the law upheld by the lower courts "provides almost no limitation on the kind of possession that would be criminalized."

1440.text: NA-46212 PRIMARY 'Campus carry' stops gun bill Efforts to expand access to guns across Georgia failed Thursday on the state Legislature's last day, as negotiations floundered on a single issue: Whether to allow firearms on the more than 50 campuses of the state's university and technical college systems. The so-called "campus carry" provision in a sweeping gun bill before lawmakers this year has long stymied pro-gun advocacy groups pushing to broaden firearms access statewide. They thought it was within their grasp this year right up until Thursday, until Senate negotiators appeared to dig in their heels and stopped their momentum. Senate Bill 101 would also have let churches allow guns in sanctuaries, school boards to arm school administrators and military veterans younger than 21 to carry weapons. The debate comes as many states and Congress are wrestling with gun laws --- especially whether to tighten restrictions in the wake of mass shootings such as December's school massacre in Newtown, Conn. Lawmakers acting as negotiators from both the House and Senate had been working on a compromise all week. But they arrived at loggerheads Thursday afternoon over whether to include campuses in the bill. Rep. Alan Powell, R-Hartwell, said the two sides had an apparent agreement on Wednesday but said the Senate moved the goal posts the next morning. "We thought we had a deal," an exasperated Powell said. His comments surprised Sen. Frank Ginn, R-Danielsville, S.B. 101's original sponsor. Talks on the bill during the session's last few days went on in fits and starts. Given that the state Legislature works on a two-year cycle, next year will likely see a repeat of the effort to come to some sort of agreement.

1445.text: GUN-8569 PRIMARY Fight Ashcroft's plan for gun files Attorney General John Ashcroft's irresponsible and dangerous decision to restrict law enforcement agencies to retaining background checks on gun buyers for a mere 24 hours ought to be stopped cold in the U.S. Senate. Sens. Ted Kennedy (D-Mass.) and Charles Schumer (D-N.Y.) recently introduced legislation to block the policy that will cripple police efforts to fight crime and prevent fraudulent gun sales. The Democrats should use their newly won majority in the Senate to strike down this proposal. A lifelong NRA member, Ashcroft appears determined to gut the nation's gun laws single-handedly. Along with rescinding Ashcroft's 24-hour rule, the legislation would require that he release all memos and documents pertaining to the decision. Democrats want to see whether Ashcroft approved the policy change over the objections of FBI officials. "Purging these files after only 24 hours is at best reckless policy," said Schumer. The New York senator characterized the policy change as "the Bush administration's way of saying 'thank you' for the National Rifle Association's get-out-the-vote efforts last fall." The NRA vehemently opposes any retention of records of gun purchases. It looks upon the records as the first step in a national gun registry. And Ashcroft insists that a day is adequate for all checks to be completed. But proponents of background checks maintain that records must be maintained for 90 days to allow police to chase down corrupt gun dealers and uncover abuses.

1447.text: GUN-352 PRIMARY America's Average Kind Of Day ''The day the President was shot,'' says an ad of Handgun Control Inc. in newpapers across the country, ''was an average kind of day'' - meaning that more than 50 Americans were killed with handguns. The gun lobby, led by the National Rifle Association, says people kill, not guns. Polls show that most people disagree, but not enough care to argue. So the nation fails to respond to an epidemic of handgun deaths, unlike its strong reaction to the rash of deaths from toxic shock syndrome and Legionnaires' disease. Handguns accounted for almost half the homicides in the United States in 1979. In fact, murder is the only cause of death in America that is increasing, according to Reynolds Farley of the University of Michigan's Population Studies Center, who recently completed a study of homicide. The United States can slow down or prevent deaths from cancer, heart disease and tuberculosis, but it cannot seem to do anything about the rising rate at which people kill each other. The rising homicide rate has many causes, but according to Professor Farley the major one is simply the increasing availability of firearms. The problem feeds on itself. As more and more people grow fearful of violence, they buy handguns for protection. The Treasury Department said there were 55 million in civilian hands in 1978. With more than two million added each year, the number today is probably close to 60 million.

1450.text: NA-40127 PRIMARY U.S. GUN VIOLENCE GOES 'OFF THE CHARTS' President Barack Obama spoke for the nation this week in lamenting the senseless gun violence in America. Coming the same week that students and police officers were murdered in the latest spasms of violence, the president struck an appropriate tone, mindful of the pain for this new batch of families as Congress and state legislatures remain under the thumb of the National Rifle Association. Hours after a teenage gunman killed a student and wounded a teacher before fatally shooting himself, Obama said his failure to tighten the gun laws was the "biggest frustration" of his presidency. He decried that school shootings had become "the norm," blasted America's gun violence as "off the charts" and dismissed the argument by the gun-rights lobby that new laws should focus on improving access to mental health services instead of restricting access to weapons. "The United States," he said, "does not have a monopoly on crazy people." Obama's appeal to common sense and urgency captured the helplessness Americans feel every time a new mass shooting breaks across the television screen. Since the 2012 school attack in Newtown, Conn., which killed 20 students and six staffers, there have been at least 74 shootings on school campuses alone - and 13 in the past eight weeks. Seven people were killed across the country in high-profile shootings this past week. In the Tampa Bay area, five people have been shot to death in the first 11 days of June, including a Tampa man who was murdered Wednesday.

1453.text: GUN-4692 PRIMARY Va. Legislator Intends to Unseat Judge Who Pulled North's Gun Permit A Republican state senator has moved to unseat a Clarke County Circuit Court judge who revoked Oliver L. North's gun permit during his U.S. Senate campaign three years ago. In 1994, shortly after North won the GOP nomination, Judge James L. Berry rescinded North's concealed-weapon permit, asserting the Iran-contra figure lacked "good character." Republican Sen. H. Russell Potts Jr., of Winchester, called that action capricious and political today and said he will not renominate Berry this year. In Virginia, circuit court judges are appointed by the General Assembly, but recently the Senate has deferred to individual lawmakers from each judicial district and accepted their nominations. "Every senator has a right to put up who they want to to be the judge. These guys are not appointed for life. They're appointed for eight years," said Potts, 57, a two-term senator and sports promoter. Potts said he had received nearly 500 complaints about Berry, and he pointed out that Berry was the one who approved North's gun permit in the first place, two years before he pulled it. North "had the character to get the permit the first time, but once he got the Republican nomination for the Senate, his character changed? That's a coincidence," Potts said. Berry, 63, released a statement from his court in Winchester today that said in part, "I believe this to be a serious attack on our concept of a separation of powers and the maintenance of an independent judiciary not beholden to the legislative and executive branches of government."

characters: Republican Sen. H. Russell Potts Jr.

1448.text: GUN-3665 PRIMARY COUNCILWOMAN PROPOSES STRICT RULES FOR GUN SELLERS Saying the city needs to be more vigilant in tracking its 391 gun dealers, a San Jose city councilwoman is proposing that police regularly investigate every gun retailer. Vice Mayor Trixie Johnson, who is planning a run for the state Assembly next year, says she was surprised to learn that only 10 of the city's 391 federally licensed dealers have a city permit. Under current city law, sellers of concealable handguns need a permit and dealers cannot operate in residential neighborhoods. Federal officials send police their list of dealers every year. But that list does not indicate who deals in concealable handguns, and local police do only spot checks. Johnson's proposal calls for police to make on-site inspections every time a dealer applies for or renews a federal license. And, as a condition of that license, she is proposing that dealers must equip every gun with a trigger lock, buy liability insurance and prove the business is in a properly zoned location and that the guns are protected with an alarm system. She said she plans to bring a package of new rules to the council for its consideration in mid-August. Anti-gun control lobbyists and some local business people already plan to oppose the measures. "Restrictions are pretty tough now," said Mike Fournier, owner of the Gun Exchange in San Jose. "We have - let me count them - nine licenses. Whenever politicians want to get their name in the paper, they propose a new gun law."

1454.text: GUN-3705 PRIMARY PREZ' NEW ADS ARE AIMING TO DEFEND WEAPONS BAN WASHINGTON  President Clinton hits the airwaves today with three campaign ads targeted at a GOP bid to repeal his assault weapons ban. New Yorkers won't see the 30-second spots, because the White House thinks the city's "citizens well understand the dangers posed by assault weapons," said presidential spokesman Mike McCurry. The $ 2.4 million for the television ads will be better spent in states where members of Congress might well prove to be swing votes, McCurry said. He listed Florida, California and Oregon as examples. States like Texas, considered a lost cause on the issue, won't see the ads either. Two of the spots feature police officers one from Houston, another from Dayton, Ohio who pulled over drivers toting rapid-fire assault weapons. The Dayton ad shows photos of paramedics trying to revive one of the officers and tearful cops walking arm in arm. Lt. Randy Beale recalls what happened when he and his partner approached the car in Houston. "As quick as he could pull the trigger, that's how quick the bullets were coming. I never heard a weapon that loud," Beale says, recalling how his partner died. The third ad shows victims a mother, a school girl and a female police officer murdered with assault weapons. Then Clinton appears, speaking from the Oval Office. "Deadly assault weapons off our streets. One hundred thousand more police on the streets. Expand the death penalty. That's how we'll protect America," the President says.

1451.text: NA-45833 PRIMARY Supreme Court may rule on guns outside the home; Three cases are up for consideration at Friday conference Guns will be on the table Friday when the Supreme Court meets in private conference to consider potential cases for next fall's docket. Just figuratively, of course. The petitions set for possible discussion include three that challenge state or federal gun-control laws. None of those cases directly raises the next big legal issue in the national debate over guns: whether the right to keep a gun at home for self-defense extends to public places. The justices could choose to take on that issue now, but it would be a stretch. However, a federal appeals court panel's divided ruling last week in a California case makes it more likely that the question of guns outside the home will be heading to the high court soon. Ever since Justice Antonin Scalia wrote for a divided court in 2008 that the Second Amendment to the Constitution protects the right to possess guns at home, the question of public places has been looming. Many states impose restrictions, such as requiring a demonstrated need to carry a gun, whether concealed or in plain sight. Most lower courts have upheld those restrictions. Friday's decision by a three-judge panel of the 9th Circuit Court of Appeals changes the equation. The 2-1 opinion struck down San Diego County's restrictions as a violation of Second Amendment rights. If the ruling holds, it would represent a clear split with other appeals court rulings that have upheld restrictions.

1452.text: GUN-5219 PRIMARY Gun Importers Are Rushing For Permits, Eye on Clinton Amid indications that President Clinton intends to clamp down on imports of assault weapons, gun dealers have obtained permits to bring in thousands of these firearms before a deadline bars them from doing so. In one week in late October, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms granted 150,000 permits for importing modified assault weapons, a huge proportion of the 600,000 permits issued over the entire year. Each permit allows the import of one weapon, and the unusually high number, first reported today in The Los Angeles Times, followed the Clinton Administration's ordering a policy review prompted by gun-control supporters in Congress. Rahm Emanuel, a senior adviser to the President, said today that Mr. Clinton intended to issue an executive order in the next few days to place restrictions on the import of firearms that are altered to skirt Congress's 1994 ban on 19 specific types of assault weapons. Administration officials would say nothing about the nature of the planned restrictions. Brian Burns, a spokesman for the firearms bureau, said that contrary to the published report today, processing of applications from gun importers had not been accelerated and that the licenses had simply been approved in the "normal course of business." Wayne LaPierre Jr., head of the National Rifle Association, said that when gun importers learned earlier this fall that Mr. Clinton intended to curb the modified assault weapons allowed in from abroad, they stepped up their applications for licenses.

1449.text: GUN-10871 PRIMARY Inquirer Editorial: Gunslinger law Once more, the allure of the Wild West is exerting an irresistible pull on Harrisburg. In a dangerous policy decision, the state Senate and House have revived legislation wisely vetoed by former Gov. Edward G. Rendell that would expand gun owners' rights to blast away any time they feel threatened. By extending the "castle doctrine," which gives armed homeowners the right to shoot an intruder, lawmakers are risking deadly confrontations in any public setting. More road-rage incidents could prove lethal, with armed motorists claiming they were legally defending themselves against a perceived threat. Criminals caught up in drug-related gun battles could mount a defense under the castle doctrine, state prosecutors have said. At the same time, there's no evidence of a compelling reason to let armed citizens draw their weapons in public. As Rendell said, the measure embraces a "shoot first, ask questions later" mentality. It poses particular risks in heavily populated city and suburban communities. In a state that had refused to surrender policymaking to the National Rifle Association, majority Republican leaders in the Senate should pay a political price in their suburban-Philadelphia districts. The House vote Tuesday was just as lopsided in its support by suburban lawmakers. Gov. Corbett may be an NRA devotee, but he owes it to all Pennsylvanians to veto this measure that will threaten public safety as much as this expansion of the castle doctrine does.

1456.text: GUN-5388 PRIMARY LETTERS; IN MY OPINION; Ban the sale of all handguns The Editors: Should cities or states sue gun manufacturers because of the deaths and injuries they cause? Gun violence is an escalating social ill in America, but are lawsuits the appropriate weapon to fight this menace? I see several problems in this approach. The predominant mindset is that if I can produce a legal product, I should have the right to sell it. That is not going to change. Second, how can a lawsuit against a gun manufacturer succeed when the weapons involved have been altered or customized after leaving the factory?   What about the difficulties of suing overseas gunmakers? Also, suits against gun manufacturers differ from those against cigarette makers because of the addiction factor. Every gun purchase is an act of free will, whereas purchases of cigarettes are dictated by addiction rather than free will. The solution I see is a most radical one: The U.S. government should ban the sale of all handguns to anyone except law enforcement agencies and the military. "But," you are saying, "the Constitution guarantees us the right to buy and keep firearms." No, the Constitution guarantees this right only to a "well-regulated Militia," that is, a military force or national guard. This was the interpretation of the U.S. Supreme Court in U.S. v. Miller, 307 U.S. 174 (1939) and U.S. v. Hale, 978 F.2d 1016 (8th Cir. 1992), as well as more than 30 cases in lower federal and state courts.

1461.text: NA-44665 PRIMARY NRA rep on new D.C. gun law: 'We're obviously going to continue to fight this'; The D.C. Log Cabin Republicans couldn't have chosen better timing for their  on Wednesday night - coming a day after the D.C. Council passed a bill giving gun owners the ability to apply to carry a concealed firearm in the city for the first time in decades. The featured speaker was scheduled to be none other than Dick Heller, the retired security guard who became the lead plaintiff in the federal court case that ended the city's 33-year-old handgun ban and who is now challenging the restrictive registration regime that replaced it. Alas, Heller did not show up to share his thoughts on the D.C. government's latest response to a federal court ruling on firearms. But also invited was Robert Melvin, grass-roots coordinator for the National Rifle Association's Institute for Legislative Action, who did attend the meeting of several dozen gay Republicans at a 16th Street NW apartment house and addressed city lawmakers' move to institute a tightly regulated system for issuing permits. "We're obviously going to continue to fight this," Melvin said. He took particular aim at the "may issue" standard under which the city plans to offer carry permits at the police chief's discretion: "'May issue' means pretty much you can go through the entire process and still get denied, and ... all of the rules can be changed based off of what the police chief thinks could hurt the city and could hurt residents. It's unfortunate that that's the case."

1458.text: GUN-4159 PRIMARY Up in Arms Over Rifle Giveaway; Gun-Control Advocates Call Army Weapons a Subsidy for NRA A provision of the defense budget that went into effect earlier this year requires the Pentagon to give away 373,000 old rifles from World War II and the Korean War, spurring protests from gun-control advocates who believe the government shouldn't add to gun commerce. The little-noticed measure was promoted by the National Rifle Association and the congressional delegation in Ohio, home to an annual marksmanship competition that will be financed by the sale of the venerable M-1 rifles and other aged guns with a resale value of about $ 100 million. The heavy, nine-pound M-1s are unlikely to be used in street crimes such as drug killings, the program's advocates say, because the main buyers have been and likely will continue to be gun collectors who must be trained in shooting rifles and pass a stringent background investigation. But critics say the recent congressional action is in effect a subsidy to the NRA. It requires the Army to transfer control over the rifles for free to a new nonprofit corporation. The corporation will sell them to benefit marksmanship programs and the yearly target tournament in Camp Perry, Ohio, which is managed by the NRA. The old Army-administered program also co-sponsored the annual Ohio tournament with the NRA, and over the years the NRA used its close relationship with the project to market itself, critics of the group said.

1455.text: GUN-1840 PRIMARY Legislation to Delay Handgun Purchases Advances in House The Brady bill, the long-delayed effort to use a five-day waiting period to stop felons and the mentally ill from buying handguns, took a step toward early Congressional approval today. The House Judiciary subcommittee on crime endorsed the measure, 10 to 3. After the vote, its chairman, Representative Charles E. Schumer, Democrat of Brooklyn, said: "The Brady bill is a hard, solid, shining part of our fight against crime. The long wait for the waiting period is coming to a close." Supporters of the bill said public anger over a rise in gun-related violence insures the votes needed to send the bill to President Clinton's desk this year, perhaps as early as Thanksgiving. But opponents said the bill's requirement of a waiting period before the purchase of a handgun would do little or nothing to end violence but would interfere with the right of the public to buy guns for recreation and self-defense. The House subcommittee brushed aside a long series of Republican amendments before sending the measure to the full Judiciary Committee, which is expected to act next week. The waiting period is intended to give local law-enforcement agencies time to conduct background checks to weed out convicted felons and the mentally ill. It would be waived when computer records compiled by states become sophisticated and extensive enough to allow for instant checks anywhere in the country.

1460.text: GUN-4818 PRIMARY NATION/WORLD BRIEFS NATION WASHINGTON Court revives lawsuit over police, domestic violence A federal appeals court reinstated a suit brought by a police organization challenging a gun-control law that forbids anyone convicted of domestic violence, including police officers, to own a gun. The ruling Friday by the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia reverses a lower court's dismissal of the suit by the Fraternal Order of Police. At issue is an amendment to the 1968 Gun Control Act. In 1996, the prohibition against gun ownership or possession by convicted felons was extended to anyone ever convicted of domestic violence. The law made no exception for police and, as a result, officers in some cities were fired after being stripped of their guns. Advocates for victims of domestic violence praised the amendment. But law enforcement groups said its retroactive provisions were unfair and unconstitutional. WASHINGTON U.S. moves to deport man accused of Nazi massacre Federal authorities began deportation proceedings this month against an elderly man from Illinois who is accused of taking part in a massacre of Jews at a Nazi slave labor camp, the Justice Department said Friday. The man, Bronislaw Hajda, lost his U.S. citizenship this year when a federal appeals court affirmed a lower court's finding that Hajda served as an armed guard at SS Training Camp Trawniki and the Treblinka labor camp in Nazi-occupied Poland from 1943 to 1945.

1459.text: GUN-8771 PRIMARY Supreme Court, 9-0, Rebuffs a Gun-Seeking Felon Federal judges do not have authority to disregard the will of Congress and restore gun ownership rights to convicted felons, the Supreme Court ruled unanimously today. The case, a dispute over the effect of a 10-year-old Congressional ban on the restoration of felons' gun privileges by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, did not directly raise the question of whether the Second Amendment grants an individual right to bear arms. But because this was as close as the court is likely to come to that issue in the near future, the case received more attention than its straightforward questions of administrative law would otherwise have warranted. The Federal Firearms Act makes it a crime for a convicted felon to own guns or ammunition. The law also authorizes the secretary of the Treasury to lift the "firearms disability" on the basis of an individual's circumstances; the secretary delegates this authority to a Treasury Department agency, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. The law also provides that appeals from denials of relief by the bureau can be heard in federal court. The Violence Policy Center, a gun-control group here, studied the issue in the early 1990's and learned that the bureau was spending millions of dollars in administrative costs to review applications and grant relief to thousands of felons, some of whom used their restored gun privileges to commit new crimes. In response, Congress in 1992 passed an appropriations bill to bar the bureau from spending any money on the relief program. The ban has been renewed annually since then.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1462.text: GUN-163 PRIMARY WASHINGTON TALK: WORKING PROFILE; Fighting the Fight On Gun Control: Sarah Brady A determined-looking woman gazes from a poster being circulated by Handgun Control Inc. ''Help me fight the National Rifle Association,'' the poster proclaims. The woman is Sarah Brady, wife of James S. Brady, the White House press secretary who was shot and left partly disabled in the assassination attempt on President Reagan in 1981. Handgun Control is a citizens' lobby that was established in 1974 and now reports almost a million members. It is seeking Federal controls on pistols and waging an educational campaign to promote the safe handling of guns. A Powerful Nemesis Mrs. Brady, now vice chairman of the organization, has worked with it for 18 months. She lobbies Congress, makes speeches, writes Op-Ed page articles, appears in forums and town meetings and shows up on such television programs as ''Nightline'' and the Phil Donahue show. As often as possible she matches wits and debating points with members of the National Rifle Association, one of Washington's most potent and feared lobbies. Last week she participated in a Capitol Hill news conference with Senator Howard M. Metzenbaum and Representative Edward F. Feighan, Ohio Democrats who are sponsoring a new gun control bill. It would hold up delivery of a pistol to a purchaser for seven days, giving the authorities a week to look over the purchaser's application. '' Jim's shooting could have been prevented if legislation such as this had been in force,'' Mrs. Brady said. '' John Hinckley walked into a Dallas pawnshop and in minutes purchased a handgun. He committed a felony when he used a false address, but it went unnoticed because there was no requirement in Texas that his statements be verified.''

1457.text: GUN-5776 PRIMARY House OKs weaker gun checks Version backed by NRA passes in narrow vote WASHINGTON - In a jolt to President Clinton and gun-control advocates in the Democratic Party, the House late Thursday night narrowly approved a provision to regulate sales at gun shows that is substantially weaker than legislation passed by the Senate last month. The measure, sponsored by Rep. John Dingell, a Michigan Democrat working in conjunction with Republicans, eked through by a vote of 218 to 211. It drew the support of 173 Republicans and 45 Democrats, though most Democrats stuck with the president. The House then rejected 235-193 early this morning a stricter gun-show measure, sponsored by Rep. Carolyn McCarthy, a New York Democrat whose husband was killed by a gunman on the Long Island Rail Road in 1993. The McCarthy proposal included the limits that the Democrats unexpectedly pushed through the Senate after the shootings at Columbine High School. The National Rifle Association has been working for weeks to see that the House does not back similar gun regulations. In emotional debate Thursday night, Rep. Dick Gephardt, the House minority leader, pleaded for the House to take a strong stand on gun shows. He ticked off the places where deadly gunfire had erupted in schools. "When you have Littleton and you have Georgia and you have Arkansas and you have Oregon and you have Kentucky," he said, "and you have kids killing kids in high schools, not just in inner cities but in suburbs all across this country, you have a national crisis."

1465.text: NA-40014 PRIMARY MARCO RUBIO SAYS SECOND AMENDMENT IS UNIQUE IN SPEECH TO NRA "No other country has a constitutional right" like the Second Amendment. Marco Rubio on Friday, April 25th, 2014 in a speech to the NRA * * * THE RULING: MOSTLY TRUE Sen. Marco Rubio, R-Fla., went all in for gun owners in a speech to the NRA as the potential presidential candidate sought to squelch any doubts about his commitment to gun rights. Rubio was one of a few 2016 Republican presidential hopefuls who spoke at the NRA's annual convention in Indianapolis on April 25. He described the right to own a gun as part of the American Dream, highlighted the "futility" of existing gun laws and chastised the media for stigmatizing gun owners. At one point, Rubio described the Constitution of the United States as unique. "I'm always amused that those who come up to me and say no other country has a constitutional right like this," he said. "As if to imply that there is something wrong with us. But we say no other country has a constitutional right like this not with scorn, but with pride." Wayne LaPierre, the NRA's executive vice president and CEO, made a similar statement during his own speech to the annual meeting, saying, "Our Second Amendment freedom separates us from every other country on earth." Is it correct that the United States is the only country that gives residents a constitutional right to bear arms?

1466.text: GUN-7114 PRIMARY Students at the State House Listen, Learn and Speak Out Itching to have her say in the debate over Gov. Parris N. Glendening's controversial smart-gun proposal, Merci McKinley could hold it in no longer. "I'm just so irate," McKinley said to two friends, standing in the foyer of the House of Delegates Office Building in Annapolis last month. "I was at a hearing and this lady sitting next to me was mocking the smart-gun legislation. I did some research and found out she was from the NRA. She said, 'Over the last five years, only 2,200 people have died from handguns.' I was like, 'That's way too many!' " "Yeah," agreed Star Johnson. "Even if it was just two, that's two too many." Then they hustled out to attend another boisterous hearing on the matter in the Senate chambers. But McKinley, 17, and Johnson, 16, are not politicians. They're not professional lobbyists, citizen activists, concerned parents or State House staffers. They're students at Prince George's Potomac High School who spent six days tracking bills, running errands for the county's senators and delegates and even testifying at committee hearings. It's part of Potomac's 10-year-old Academy of Law and Public Policy. Instead of sitting through routine class lectures, these students get hands-on experience. "Think about the difference of learning from a textbook and doing this," said Rhonda Hanson, a government teacher in charge of Potomac's academy. "Kids who are asked to memorize things from a textbook forget them in two weeks. But this is something they're going to remember."

1467.text: GUN-201 PRIMARY House Leaders Put Off Vote on Gun Measures House leaders decided today to put off a vote on two bills that would make major revisions in the Federal law on gun control. The votes, originally due this week, are now scheduled for early April. One measure, being supported by the National Rifle Association, would weaken the Gun Control Act of 1968 and ease interstate buying of pistols. A version of the legislation was adopted by the Senate last year. The other bill, passed unanimously by the House Judiciary Committee and endorsed by several police groups, would prohibit most interstate sales of pistols but allow rifles and shotguns to be sold across state lines. Both bills would reduce the record-keeping restrictions for gun dealers, but the legislation supported by the police groups would require dealers to inform local police departments of the names of all purchasers of pistols.

1463.text: GUN-4745 PRIMARY Police awaiting gun restrictions; Federal law forbids spouse abusers from carrying firearms Metro Atlanta police officials are struggling to determine how to comply with a new federal law that bars gun ownership by cops or anyone else convicted of any crime of domestic violence. "It was a surprise to everybody," said Bob Hightower, Cobb's director of public safety. "Right now, we have no idea if any officers would be affected. We are making sure that they are aware of it and the ramifications of it." The new law, intended to prevent spouse abusers from having guns, was approved Sept. 30. It prohibits anyone - including police officers, federal agents and soldiers - from owning or carrying a gun after conviction of any crime involving domestic abuse, including misdemeanors such as simple assault. Although some officers in Denver, Los Angeles and Detroit reportedly have turned in their guns because of the law, some metro Atlanta agencies say they have no officers who fall under the law and others are still trying to determine the law's reach. Atlanta Police Chief Beverly Harvard, for example, asked that the city seek direction from the U.S. attorney and the U.S. attorney general on what course to follow. The law's potential impact on law enforcement agencies generally was overlooked by police until the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms issued a memo Nov. 26 saying officers convicted of "a qualifying misdemeanor" would "not be able to lawfully possess or receive firearms or ammunition for any purpose, including performing their official duties."

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1464.text: NA-44629 PRIMARY Licensed handgun carry now legal in District of Columbia: Palmer v. DC On Saturday afternoon, the United States District Court for the District of Columbia ruled unconstitutional the District's absolute prohibition on the carrying of handguns outside the home for lawful self-defense, in the case of Palmer v. District of Columbia. The District Court did not issue a stay, but the D.C. Attorney General's Office announced on Sunday that it would seek a stay. As reported by the Washington Post, D.C. Metropolitan Police Chief Cathy Lanier has approved an order which allows District residents to carry a handgun IF AND ONLY IF the handgun has been properly registered with the District Police, pursuant to the District's handgun registration ordinance. What about non-residents? According to the Post article, "Lanier's instructions to police also said that residents of other jurisdictions without felony records would not be charged under the ban on carrying pistols." Fox5 DC reporter Emily Miller reported the non-resident rules slightly differently on her Twitter feed late Sunday night: "STUNNING DEVELOPMENT: DC Police Chief Lanier just told force not to arrest a person who can legally carry a gun in DC or any state." "More - DC police chief using guidance from AG - grants full reciprocity for all open and concealed carry from others states." "Only gun arrests now in DC can be DC residents with unregistered guns and non-residents who are prohibited under federal laws."

characters: D.C. Metropolitan Police Chief Cathy Lanier
United States District Court for the District of Columbia

1469.text: NA-40928 PRIMARY Will Florida follow Iowa in arming the blind? Florida has been outflanked by Iowa when it comes to permissive gun laws. I know, it sounds crazy that any state can be more gun- happy than the "Gunshine State." After all, we're the state that tried to prohibit pediatricians from giving firearms safety warnings to new parents. We pioneered the shoot-'em-up "stand your ground" law for the rest of America, and we're now petitioning the U.S. Supreme Court to scrap the federal law that prohibits licensed dealers from selling handguns and ammunition to teenagers. We have more concealed weapons permits in Florida than any other state, and we give these permits out so freely that people from other states have circumvented their own states' gun laws by getting a Florida permit in the mail. So the idea that Florida could be less permissive about guns than another state sounds absurd. And yet, we have Iowa, a state that issues concealed weapons permits to the blind, who can get the licenses online without a shooting accuracy requirement. This happened when legislators in Iowa rewrote that state's concealed weapons law three years ago by removing the discretion of the state's 99 sheriffs in granting gun permits, while at the same time failing to list any physical disqualifiers for a permit. As a result, the blind in Iowa can walk the streets with loaded handguns, and some of them are, according to a Des Moines Register story published last weekend that caused a national stir.

1470.text: GUN-4558 PRIMARY 3 in home raid face charges Illegal arsenal found, documents say AURORA - Three men whose Aurora home was raided by federal agents Thursday had stockpiled an arsenal of illegal firearms and explosives and slept with their guns at the ready in case of attack by law enforcement officers, according to court documents. The three - Ronald David Cole, 27, Kevin Terry, 24, and Wallace Stanley Kennett, 33 - made their first appearance Friday in U.S. District Court on charges of illegal possession of a pipe bomb. They face preliminary hearings on Tuesday. Federal officials said more charges are possible as the investigation progresses. Cole claimed to be the leader and the others members of the Colorado First Light Infantry, a militia unit, according to the court documents. The men also had their home rigged with explosive booby traps to alert them to intruders, the documents said. They dressed in fatigues for regular target practice in Lefthand Canyon, and Cole claimed to network with terrorist organizations around the world on the Internet, the court affidavits said. But Cole's impact may not have been as far-reaching as he hoped. As far as the FBI can ascertain, Cole's militia group had only three members - those arrested. "So far as we can tell     there's nobody else that we're looking for, so it might have been a very light infantry," said John H. Kundts, FBI supervisory agent. Kundts wouldn't say specifically what items were found in the house at 2439 S. Kittredge Way or if rocket fuel was among them. However, The Associated Press reported that agents had seized six fully automatic AK-47s, three land mines, 75 pounds of rocket fuel, a pipe bomb and large amounts of ammunition.

1471.text: GUN-242 PRIMARY GOETZ, AT HEARING, TOLD OF ANGER WITH SYSTEM Asserting that ''you cannot ask me to defend New York City's incompetence,'' Bernhard Hugo Goetz told a police hearing in 1982 of his frustration with the criminal justice system after he was mugged in a subway station the year before. At the hearing, at which he unsuccessfully sought to obtain a police permit to carry a gun, Mr. Goetz described his work as self-employed electronics specialist, how he regularly carried large sums of money and expensive equipment, and how he was confronted by three men on Jan. 26, 1981, in the West Side IRT subway station at Canal Street.   The 40-page transcript of the hearing, which was held at Police Headquarters on Jan. 18, 1982, was made available by the department yesterday in response to requests from reporters.   Mr. Goetz told the police in Concord, N.H., on Monday that he shot four teen-agers on a Manhattan subway train last month after they approached him and asked him for $5. He is to be charged this week in New York with four counts of attempted murder, the city police said.   The incident took place on the same subway line where the 1981 mugging occurred. Cross-Complaint Filed According to the transcript of the 1982 hearing, Mr. Goetz said that a man arrested in connection with the 1981 mugging filed a cross-complaint. The complaint charged that Mr. Goetz had started the incident.   As a result, the matter was apparently at one point treated as a noncriminal dispute and an attempt was made to resolve it through mediation.   Referring to the charge by the other man, Mr. Goetz, who is 37 years old, said, it was ''ridiculous'' for anyone to consider that he would start a fight with three men in the subway. The mediation process, a noncriminal proceeding outside the court system, is frequently used in situations, usually of a nonviolent nature, where there are conflicting complaints. All parties must agree to the procedure, which is intended to reduce the caseload of the criminal courts.

1468.text: GUN-8314 PRIMARY Brady law too lenient, article says There is no evidence that the Brady law requiring background  checks of handgun buyers reduced homicide rates after it went into  effect in 1994, according to a study published today in the  Journal of the American Medical Association. The authors of the study - Philip Cook, a professor of public  policy at Duke University, and Jens Ludwig, a professor of public  policy at Georgetown University - suggest that the reason the  Brady law has not reduced homicides, most of which are committed  with guns, is it does not go far enough. The law requires background checks only for those who bought  handguns from federally licensed firearms dealers and does not  cover the unregulated market involving 30 to 40 percent of all  sales, such as gun shows and private transactions, where criminals  and juveniles often buy handguns. But the study's conclusion was immediately challenged by the  Clinton administration and the Center to Prevent Handgun Violence,  the gun control group that helped push for the Brady law. James Johnson, an undersecretary for enforcement at the  Treasury Department, said Justice Department figures have shown  that crimes committed with handguns fell 52 percent between 1993  and 1998, which is twice the rate of crimes overall. The Brady  law, new uses of crime-gun tracing by the Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco and Firearms, and other efforts led to the decline,  Johnson said. ' We think there has been a significant contribution  by Brady,' he said

characters: Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco and Firearms

1475.text: NA-42761 PRIMARY More Guns In the Sky Confidential. For N.R.A. internal. Now that the Senate has voted down new gun control laws and we have the president on the defensive -- never thought I'd say it, but God bless the I.R.S.! -- we have a real opportunity to guide Congress along a common-sense path that will further ensure the freedoms of peaceful, law-abiding citizens. Herewith, talking points on the next crucial phase of our work. Please distribute to all concerned elected officials who share our love of liberty and public safety. Objective: We aim to pass laws that will allow passengers to carry guns on commercial airplanes; this will make business class the safest place in America. Talking Points: Experts say, ''This is the most progressive means of preventing hijacking in aeronautic history.'' Air Marshals are good people, but on a modern aircraft anyone can nod off. Our plan mitigates snoozing risks. And consider the budget savings when every flyer becomes a Citizen Air Marshal (CAM). CAMs eliminate the need for uniformed personnel patrolling our nation's terminals. Now trained personnel can be deployed to the domestic and international hot spots, including war zones and schools. Duty-free ammo sold on international flights is good for the American economy. Airport gun marts are another surefire economic success story. Metal detectors are costly; we make them instantly obsolete. The appealing smell of well-oiled metal provides a fragrant antidote to exotic French cheeses in carry-on bags.

1473.text: NA-44066 PRIMARY States move to restrict gun magazines With President Obama's push for limits on high-capacity ammunition magazines stalled, some states are restricting their size in a shift that authorities say could dramatically reduce deaths in future mass shootings. Congress is set to debate expanded background checks for gun purchasers in the coming days, but Obama's proposals to ban some assault-style weapons and impose a 10-round limit on magazines are unlikely to reach a vote. Governors and legislatures across the country, however, are seizing the magazine issue. Rhode Island on Tuesday became the latest state to propose restrictions on high-capacity magazines. Connecticut and Maryland have banned the sale of magazines that hold more than 10 rounds of ammunition. California prohibits the sale of magazines holding more than 10 rounds and has proposed a ban on their possession. New York and Colorado also have adopted new restrictions. Firearms experts say restrictions on magazines - detachable ammunition storage and feeding devices -might not prevent a fatal shooting but could prevent a shooting from turning into a massacre. "They will prevent a killer from becoming a killing machine," said David Chipman, a former Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives official who is a senior policy adviser for Mayors Against Illegal Guns. As a special agent arresting criminals, Chipman said, he routinely carried a firearm with a magazine that was limited to 15 rounds. Gun rights advocates oppose the limits, saying Americans need the increased firepower to defend themselves against criminals.

1474.text: GUN-11887 PRIMARY California legislators seek new gun restrictions SACRAMENTO -- Seemingly awakened by the mass shooting rampage in Newton, Conn., that killed 20 children and seven adults, legislators around the country are taking a fresh look at gun laws. And though California has the toughest gun laws in the nation, a handful of legislators have proposed new restrictions. Here is a summary of planned measures expected to be taken up when the Legislature returns to session next month. Sen. Leland Yee, D-San Francisco, SB47: Would prohibit semi-automatic weapons like AR-15s and AK-47s from having devices known as "bullet buttons," which allow easy reloading of multi-bullet ammunition clips. Sen. Kevin De Leon, D-Los Angeles (no bill yet): Wants to require annual permits for ammunition purchases. Sen. Ted Lieu, D-Torrance (no bill yet): Wants to mandate school safety plans. Sen. Ted Gaines, R-Roseville (no bill yet): Wants to deny weapons to mentally ill people Contact Steven Harmon at 916-441-2101. Follow him atTwitter.com/ssharmon . Read the Political Blotter atIBAbuzz.com/politics .

1476.text: GUN-6083 PRIMARY POLL FINDS SUPPORT FOR GUN CONTROL; TWO-THIRDS IN SURVEY BACK MORE RESTRICTIONS In a new poll, two-thirds of those questioned, including three out of four women, said that increasing restrictions on guns is more important than protecting the rights of Americans to own them. Support for gun control in the poll by the Pew Research Center for the People & the Press was slightly higher than in a Pew poll in December 1993. The percentage of those who feel television news contains too much violence also grew. The latest Pew poll was taken before Thursday's school shootings in Conyers, Ga. The poll reported that almost two-thirds of those surveyed said television news contains too much violence, compared with half who felt that way in 1993. Seven of 10 said TV entertainment programs have too much violence, the same level as the 1993 Pew poll and a 1971 Harris poll. The Pew poll showed an 8-point increase in support for gun control since December 1993. An ABC-Washington Post poll released earlier this week reported that two-thirds of those questioned supported tougher gun controls, about the same as the percentage when ABC asked that question in March 1993. Associated Press polls taken just before and after the Littleton, Colo., shooting a month ago showed a 9-point increase in the percentage of those who believe tougher gun controls are a better deterrent than stricter enforcement of current laws. In the Pew poll, almost all demographic groups showed increased support for giving priority to gun controls, with a shift of more than 10 percentage points among women, people over age 65 and those with some college education. Men were more evenly divided, with 53 percent saying restrictions were more important and 42 percent saying protection of gun rights was more important.

1472.text: GUN-2886 PRIMARY Silent but deadly statistic in gun-control debate: Suicide In the gun control debate, one fact often is overlooked: More Americans take their own lives with guns than have their lives taken from them. In 1991, 18,526 of the nation's 38,317 gun deaths were suicides - outnumbering the 17,746 firearm homicides. Although some gun suicides make headlines - such as Clinton adviser Vince Foster and comedian Freddie Prinze - the toll gets scant attention compared with murder. But public health researchers increasingly call suicide a key factor in the gun control debate, arguing that some gun suicides could be prevented. And many families and friends of those who used guns to kill themselves have joined the campaign. "Having a gun in your home that a family member uses to kill himself adds an element of responsibility to an already guilt-ridden loss," says LaRita Archibald of Colorado Springs, whose son, Roger, 24, used a family hunting gun to commit suicide in 1978. "If there hadn't been a gun, maybe he would have waited." The 1991 numbers - the most recent available - aren't a fluke. Since 1981, gun suicides have outnumbered gun homicides every year, the National Center for Health Statistics has found. Yet gun suicide largely has been ignored - until now. Lloyd Potter, head of suicide prevention for the Centers for Disease Control, says firearm suicides are of growing concern because although the overall suicide rate was stable in the 1980s, a growing proportion of all suicides each year are committed with guns.

1479.text: GUN-5700 PRIMARY CLINTON OFFERS AID TO PARTY LAWMAKERS WHO SUPPORT GUN CONTROL / THE PRESIDENT SAID HE WOULD RAISE MONEY TO ENSURE THAT / MEMBERS CAN DEFEND THEIR VOTES IN THE 2000 ELECTIONS. President Clinton will lend political aid to Democratic lawmakers who risk their seats to vote for gun control, his chief of staff said yesterday as the White House prepared to turn its focus to domestic issues such as crime and prescription drugs for the elderly. "We'll go out and do anything we can to help people who vote the right way," John Podesta said in an interview with the Associated Press. On the drug plan, White House officials have said they would give senior citizens prescription benefits for the first time, requiring them to pay a monthly premium to help cover the costs. Revealing a new wrinkle, Podesta said the poorest people would not have to pay the full premium. "Some people will get help with the premium at the lower end of the scale, but the premium will be fixed so that it's universal," he said. He did not say how much the plan would cost or how the White House wants to pay for it. As Clinton wrapped up his weeklong European trip, Podesta said the administration would shift its attention to domestic policy. On gun control, some Democrats, particularly from rural and Southern districts, fear that a vote against the National Rifle Association will unleash massive advertising and organizing campaigns to defeat them in the next election. Democrats believe gun control was a factor that helped Republicans take control of the House in 1994.

1477.text: GUN-8001 PRIMARY Gunfire at the Office THE MAN accused of killing seven co-workers at a software company in Massachusetts Tuesday had all the deadly tools he needed--and many more at home, in his office cubicle and in his locker. His weapons of choice, pulled out along with a gym bag full of ammunition from his desk and locker, were an AK-47-style semiautomatic rifle and a 12-gauge shotgun. For good measure, he had a .32-caliber pistol in his right pocket and, back in the locker, a .460 magnum rifle with sniper scope. In a matter of minutes, he gunned down everyone he saw, firing 37 rifle rounds and dozens of shotgun shells. How did he come to possess such an arsenal? Details to come. But in America, it is too easy to come by such equipment--even in a state such as Massachusetts, which has some of the strictest laws in the country. Michael McDermott, charged with the killings, had no state-required permit for the weapons he brought to work, no special state permit to carry a handgun; according to police, he had no prior criminal record. And because gun laws vary sharply from state to state, the looser laws in New Hampshire and Vermont attract traffic from Massachusetts. More than two-thirds of the guns linked to crimes in Boston come from out of state, most from along the East Coast. Still other buys can be made at gun shows, where loopholes in the law allow all sorts of transactions. Internet sales are brisk and barely monitored; gun-runners can still rely on "straw purchases" to stock up in states that have no limits on purchases by individuals.

1480.text: GUN-9936 PRIMARY AFTER 5 YEARS, CRITICISM OF 10-20-LIFE CONTINUES Five years after the state passed its strict 10-20-Life gun law, defense attorneys are calling it a disaster, judges are complaining that it robs them of their constitutionally granted powers and even some prosecutors, who critics say benefit the most from the law, admit parts of the law are "Draconian." So with virtually every side of the legal community crying foul over what some perceive as an inflexible law that gives prosecutors too much power and forces judges to hand out sentences that are too long in some cases, what are legislators doing to change the law? Apparently, nothing. The 10-20-Life law, Gov. Jeb Bush's first major piece of legislation after being elected, was touted as an answer to the growing trend of gun violence in Florida. It orders mandatory prison sentences for crimes committed with a gun: 10 years if a person is carrying a gun during the commission of a crime; 20 years if the gun is fired; and 25 years to life if a person is seriously injured or killed with the bullet. A recent report by the Florida Department of Corrections credits the law for reducing violent gun crime by 25 percent since it was passed in February 1999. But those numbers seem to follow a national trend, where gun crime rates are falling from coast to coast. The same criteria used by the state are not available through the FBI, which is the crime statistic clearinghouse for law enforcement agencies across the country.

1481.text: NA-42911 PRIMARY The Gun Report: June 5, 2013 A month after the Newtown shooting, President Obama issued 23 presidential actions regarding gun regulation, one of which was to restore government funding to research gun violence in the United States. (If you'll recall, heavy N.R.A. lobbying caused research funding to dry up in 1996, and the Centers for Disease Control has reportedly cut firearms safety research by 96 percent since then.) This morning, the first result of those presidential actions was made public, as a new report from the Institute of Medicine and National Research Council proposed a research agenda to improve understanding of the public health aspects of gun violence. From the Council: The report said significant progress can be achieved in three to five years through a research program that addresses five high-priority areas: the characteristics of gun violence, risk and protective factors, prevention and other interventions, gun safety technology, and the influence of video games and other media. It's worth noting that this basic, common-sense approach - research - is being hailed as a breakthrough, nearly two decades after funding for it dried up. Here is today's report. -Jennifer Mascia A shooting at a Miramar Ranch, Calif., residence left a 10-year-old boy dead Tuesday afternoon.
