A resident who heard a gunshot from inside a next-door home found the child suffering from a chest wound shortly before 4:30 p.m. It was not immediately clear if the boy shot himself accidentally or if another person was responsible.

1482.text: GUN-8059 PRIMARY THE 2000 CAMPAIGN: THE GUN LOBBY; Rallying Voters and Relishing a Leading Role The curtains have not quite been drawn around Charlton Heston's holding room backstage at the big National Rifle Association rally here, so thatin the moment before entering, one sees the old actor seated there, alone. He is wearing a gray suit and those cloth slippers the airlines hand out and is looking straight ahead, his expression not sad, exactly, but not anything -- still in a way not often glimpsed in the cell phone century, at least not on the campaign trail in the final days of a close presidential race. Then there are introductions, and he is on, in full command of that beautiful voice. Tonight's event, a get-out-the-vote rally for Gov. George W. Bush and other Republicans, is part of a 7-day, 16-city swing through key states in an intense effort to mobilize the gun owners who are convinced that Vice President Al Gore wants to confiscate their firearms. But it is also, as Mr. Heston makes clear, a production by a national touring company, a final starring role. "I've been a public performer most of my life, and I've never seen a crowd response like this," he says, pausing for just the perfect two beats before adding, "Ah, well, maybe at the Academy Awards." "Just wait, you'll see women holding up 4-month-old babies. The other day a woman came up with a boy and said, 'Meet my son, Charlton!' Now, that was really neat!" he says, slapping his thighs for emphasis.

1484.text: GUN-11749 PRIMARY Defiant NRA: Arm every school Proposal: Police, NRA members would guard students. LaPierre says: "The only thing that stops a bad guy with a gun is a good guy with a gun." WASHINGTON o Guns and police officers in all American schools are what's needed to stop the next killer "waiting in the wings," the National Rifle Association declared Friday, taking a no-retreat stance in the face of growing calls for gun control after the Connecticut shootings that claimed the lives of 26 children and school staff . "The only thing that stops a bad guy with a gun is a good guy with a gun," said Wayne LaPierre, the group's chief executive officer. Some members of Congress who had long scored at gun-control proposals have begun to suggest some concessions could be made, and a fierce debate over legislation seems likely next month. President Barack Obama has demanded "real action, right now." The nation's largest gun-rights lobby broke its weeklong silence on the shooting rampage at Sandy Hook Elementary School with a defiant presentation. The event was billed as a news conference, but NRA leaders took no questions. Twice, they were interrupted by banner-waving protesters, who were removed by security. Some had predicted that after the slaughter of a score of elementary schoolchildren by a man using a semiautomatic rifle, the group might soften its stance, at least slightly. Instead, LaPierre delivered a 25-minute tirade against the notion that another gun law would stop killings in a culture where children are exposed daily to violence in video games, movies and music videos. He argued that guns are the solution, not the problem.

1478.text: GUN-9459 PRIMARY Trying Again to Make Gun Makers Liable for Shootings Jury selection is to begin tomorrow in Brooklynfederal court in a lawsuit that gun control advocates say could bring a landmark ruling against the firearms industry. Firearms makers, however, call the suit a defamatory attack by radical anti-gun forces. The suit, filed by the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People, has drawn national attention because the N.A.A.C.P. has gained access to previously sealed government data tracing sales of guns that may have been used in crimes. The group is also expected to call a former firearms industry lobbyist who would be the first important whistleblower against the gun industry. The N.A.A.C.P contends in the suit, filed in 1999, that its members have been disproportionately injured by gun makers and dealers, and claims that their distribution policies permit guns to be channeled to criminals. More than 80 manufacturers and distributors, including all the major companies, are defendants. "It's going to be the first time the manufacturers are going to have to face the full array of the proof against them," said David Kairys, a professor at Temple University Law School in Philadelphia who is an adviser to many municipalities in other gun cases around the country. The lawsuit, like more than 30 others around the country, claims that the practices of the gun industry amount to a "public nuisance" because they permit guns to fall into the hands of criminals.

characters: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (N.A.A.C.P.)

1483.text: NA-43773 PRIMARY Jon Stewart on gun control: Awkward Jon Stewart shot himself in the foot on Monday's "Daily Show" when he interviewed Bob Costas about the gun control comments the sportscaster had made during a football game, landing Costas in hot water last month. During Costas's appearance on the Comedy Central show - whose advertisers include violent films - Stewart wondered what it was like to be at the epicenter of the gun control argument. Costas set off a firestorm during his halftime slot of an NBC Sunday football broadcast in early December when he discussed the previous day's suicide of Jovan Belcher; the Kansas City Chiefs linebacker shot himself Dec. 1 after killing the mother of his baby daughter, using his legally registered gun. Among other comments last month, Costas read from a column by Fox Sports columnist Jason Whitlock: "If Jovan Belcher didn't possess a gun, he and Kasandra Perkins would both be alive today." Much hoo-ha followed, in which some industry navel-lint pickers debated whether NBC should sack Costas. Costas told Stewart that he'd been let off the hook when "Newtown happened." He added that the slaughter of 20 elementary school children and six adults, "as horribly tragic as that was, if it did redirect the debate - and people are now at least somewhat more willing to think about this rationally and compassionately - then that is a good thing."

1486.text: NA-45876 PRIMARY Obama visit highlights strides on guns; Minneapolis leaders push legislation This city has managed to shed the moniker of "Murder-apolis," the ugly nickname it was tagged with during the worst days of the crack and gang wars of the 1990s, when it had a homicide rate that rivaled New York City's. The progress Minneapolis made reducing gun violence in the past few years has caught the attention of President Obama, who will visit the city today for his first trip outside the Beltway to tout his plan to overhaul the nation's gun laws. Even as Minneapolis is being held up by the White House as a community that's been ahead of the curve in pushing the conversation on gun violence, city leaders here are clamoring for Congress and state lawmakers to implement tougher gun laws. They say it is a necessity to further reduce the epidemic of gun violence in their city. "We have done so much, and we're committed to doing more," said Minneapolis Mayor R.T. Rybak, a Democrat. "But there is also only so much you can do at this level to prevent illegal guns from getting into the hands of kids, the mentally ill and people who shouldn't have them." The White House has praised Minneapolis for a youth violence initiative the city launched in 2008 that has had success. And a bipartisan push by sheriffs in Minnesota to improve the state's background check system for gun buyers -- including speeding up the input of felony and drug convictions, along with mental-health court orders -- has also piqued administration interest.

1485.text: GUN-5542 PRIMARY CHICAGO SUES GUN-MAKERS AND RETAILERS / THE LAWSUIT SAYS FIREARMS CREATE A DEADLY PUBLIC NUISANCE. IT FOLLOWS A RECENT FILING BY NEW ORLEANS. The City of Chicago and Cook County yesterday filed a $433 million lawsuit against 38 retailers, distributors and manufacturers of firearms on the grounds that the guns create a massive and deadly public nuisance. So far this year, 471 people have died in Chicago as a result of gun violence. "You can't expect the status quo on businesses which make money and then have no responsibility to us as citizens," Mayor Richard M. Daley said in announcing the lawsuit, the second broad antigun suit filed by a major city. "We are going to hit them where it hurts, in the wallet." In Philadelphia, Mayor Rendell had a similar lawsuit drafted earlier this year, then decided to hold off filing it to allow time for a group of cities including Philadelphia to negotiate with gun manufacturers about possible restrictions on gun sales. Shortly after Rendell announced that decision, Daley said Chicago would go ahead and file a lawsuit of its own, which it did yesterday. An industry spokesman dismissed Chicago's suit as groundless, noting that gun manufacturers and distributors were part of a federal program that requires gun purchasers to pass background checks. "Whose responsibility is that when someone purchases a firearm illegally in the city?" asked Robert Delfay, president of the Sporting Arms and Ammunition Manufacturers Institute in Newtown, Conn. "That can't go back to the manufacturer."

1489.text: NA-40599 PRIMARY Gun magazine supplier expands in Iowa college town GRINNELL, Iowa o An Iowa college known for liberal activism is welcoming an unlikely neighbor: one of the nation's largest suppliers of high-capacity ammunition magazines and accessories for assault rifles and other guns. With demand for gun products booming, Brownells Inc. is building a large distribution and retail center along Interstate 80 in Grinnell. The central Iowa city of 9,100 is home to Grinnell College, a selective liberal arts institution proud of its commitment to social justice. Critics have attacked the company for profiting from accessories for military-style weapons, but the city and college are rolling out the welcome mat. It's an example of how left-leaning politics and gun rights can coexist cheerfully, particularly when economic benefit enters the equation. "I think it is going to be fantastic for the community," said Juli Sanford, owner of Juli's Natural Foods, which sells organic food and supplements a block from the college. She said the project would bring jobs, income and visitors to Grinnell, about 55 miles east of Des Moines. But the development is galling to gun-control advocates, who have watched cities and states continue to compete for firearms-related businesses despite mass shootings advocates hoped would bring more criticism for the industry. "Grinnell has been synonymous with high-quality education, with creative and even brilliant contributions to education," Cheryl Thomas, a spokeswoman for Iowans for Gun Safety, said. "This is not a fit."

1488.text: GUN-6837 PRIMARY N.C. school gun raffle stirs storm over safety; Firearms advocates snapping up tickets Gun advocates are buying tickets by the hundreds to show their support for a private school gun raffle that has drawn criticism from school safety advocates. Five hunting rifles and shotguns are being raffled Dec. 6-10 by Hobgood Academy in Halifax County, N.C. The school plans to use the money to buy equipment for the its Future Farmers of America group. In light of the April shooting at Columbine High in Littleton, Colo. --- where two students killed 13 people before committing suicide --- as well as other school shootings nationwide, the raffle drew media attention and criticism from school safety advocates. Then buyers surfaced from all over the country, saying they wanted to register their disapproval of anti-gun groups by purchasing the $ 10 tickets. Many said they learned of the raffle from Internet newsgroups and newspaper articles, Headmaster John Hardison said. ''I'm sure we're getting this interest because of the publicity,'' he said. '' We're not insensitive to gun violence; we're a school, too." The school, a kindergarten through 12th-grade campus that has operated for 30 years, is still tabulating the number of tickets sold. The number has reached into the hundreds, far surpassing anticipated sales. Word of the raffle reached Fred Bonner a couple of weeks ago. The syndicated columnist and editor of Carolina Adventure, a Raleigh hunting, fishing and boating publication, posted information about the raffle on a few Internet gun advocacy newsgroups and e-mailed it to hunting friends across the country.

1487.text: GUN-3468 PRIMARY DINGELL PUT CRIME BILL AHEAD OF HIS OPPOSITION TO GUN CONTROL IT WAS A TOUGH CHOICE FOR THE MICHIGAN DEMOCRAT. HE RESIGNED FROM THE NATIONAL RIFLE ASSOCIATION BOARD. For passionate pro-gun House members, it was easy to vote against an anti- crime bill that bans military-style assault weapons. But for John D. Dingell, whose power in Congress is legendary, it was agonizing. He voted for the bill and resigned from the board of the National Rifle Association. Dingell (D., Mich.), a veteran of 39 years in the House, has not changed his longtime opposition to gun control. In his resignation letter, he wished the NRA board "continued success." "After a great deal of thought, I deeply regret that I find that the conflict between my responsibilities as a member of Congress and my duties as a board member of the National Rifle Association is irreconcilable," Dingell said in a letter to NRA president Tom Washington. Dingell would not discuss his decision. But in his surprise speech Sunday on the House floor, delivered in dramatic fashion just before the vote that passed the bill, Dingell said he found the weapons ban "offensive, obnoxious, and contrary to the rights guaranteed to all Americans under the Second Amendment." However, he added, he could not vote against a bill that would hire more police officers, build additional prisons, and take a tough stance against terrorists, drug kingpins, illegal immigrants, sexual predators and killers of police. Sarah Brady, chairwoman of Handgun Control Inc., said, "John Dingell did a fine thing. I assume that he was in conflict there. I just find it a highly ethical thing for him to have done."

1491.text: NA-44826 PRIMARY Marijuana, gun background checks qualify for Nevada votes Nevada voters are likely to have the chance to decide whether to legalize marijuana for recreational use, and whether to require stricter background checks on gun purchases after petitioners turned in hundreds of thousands of signatures last month. Supporters of the measure to legalize marijuana turned in 138,453 valid signatures, according to Secretary of State Ross Miller's (D) office, far above the approximately 100,000 valid signatures necessary to qualify an initiative. That measure, backed by the Marijuana Policy Project and its local affiliate, the Coalition to Regulate Marijuana Like Alcohol, will allow consumers over 21 years of age to purchase and possess up to one ounce of the drug, and up to 12 marijuana plants. Tax revenue from marijuana sales would be dedicated to public education and enforcement under the proposal, which is modeled closely on ballot measures that passed this year in Oregon, Alaska and Washington, D.C. Supporters of stronger gun control measures turned in 166,779 valid signatures, Miller's office said. That proposal would close loopholes in state background check laws, similar to a measure that passed by a wide margin in Washington state in November. That measure was advanced by Nevadans for Background Checks, a group funded by former New York City mayor Michael Bloomberg, who also helped fund the Washington measure. It exempts transfers of antique firearms, transfers between immediate family members and transfers for self-defense, hunting and target shooting from requiring background checks.

characters: Coalition to Regulate Marijuana Like Alcohol

1493.text: NA-43869 PRIMARY Kelly: 'This time must be different' Former astronaut Mark Kelly, the husband of former Rep. Gabrielle Giffords (D-Ariz.), told a Senate committee on Wednesday morning that "this time must be different," calling for stronger gun laws in the wake of a string of mass shootings. Kelly, a retired Navy officer who flew four times in space, has become the country's best-known spokesman for gun control, following the January 8, 2011, shooting that nearly killed his wife. Giffords was shot in the head as she held a constituent event in a supermarket parking lot in Tucson, Ariz. Six others were killed, before alleged gunman Jared Loughner was stopped as he sought to re-load. Kelly spoke after a brief opening statement from Giffords, who he said is still partially blind, and struggles to walk. He took her hand to help lead her from the room, and then returned a few minutes later to be a witness himself. In Kelly's statement to the Senate Judiciary Committee, he took pains to cast himself and his wife as political moderates, and longtime gun owners. "We take that right very, very seriously, and we would never, ever give it up," Kelly said. But Kelly urged the committee to seek to fix the "matrix of failure and inadequacy" that underlies every gun death. He suggested three potential actions: Kelly, who founded a group called Americans for Responsible Solutions with Giffords, had previously said he wanted a new ban on assault weapons and high-capacity magazines. In this hearing, however, he stopped short of calling explicitly for those measures. Instead, Kelly called for a "careful and civil conversation" about whether those items should face greater regulation.

1494.text: GUN-8281 PRIMARY HIS WEAPONS WERE LEGAL Police brass yesterday blasted one of the biggest loopholes of gun control - the reality that shotguns and rifles can be bought by virtually anybody. For a handgun, there's a waiting period, a police permit to obtain, a background check to pass - a whole gamut of restrictions in gun-tough New York state. For the so-called long guns favored by hunters, it's a whole different world. Simply put your money down, and you can take a rifle or shotgun home. In New York City, you must report shotgun purchases at your local precinct stationhouse. But there is no oversight. That's how Arthur Alalouf, the mentally unbalanced Brooklyn man who wounded four officers, obtained his rifles and shotguns - legally. Chief of Detectives William Allee said common sense should have kept the gun out of Alalouf's hands. Allee said that the Brooklyn gun shop owner who sold Alalouf the Mossberg 12-gauge shotgun he used a day before the rampage should have thought better. "If a drunk goes into a bar, you shouldn't sell him a drink," Allee said. Alalouf also was issued a target permit in the mid-1980s, a police source said. Because Alalouf, a former correction officer, didn't have a criminal record and nobody apparently complained to police about his behavior, he appears to have repeatedly had his target permit renewed, sources said.

1495.text: GUN-770 PRIMARY SECOND AMENDMENT DOESN'T BAR GUN LAWS WASHINGTON President Bush is catching up, as it were, with Ronald Reagan. It's still too early to tell whether this is a course adjustment or a 180-degree turn.   But Bush is at least seeking a ''reasonable'' solution, and one that is ''constitutionally sound.'' The two criteria pose different problems, one of which is wholly imaginary.   The real problem is the National Rifle Association, which on matters of firearms regulation is as difficult to reason with as the legendary Iranian moderates. The imaginary problem is devising regulations that are, in the president's words, constitutionally sound. It will be a cinch to do so if Bush will only recall a bygone that isn't quite a bygone from last autumn's campaign: When the Pledge of Allegiance issue arose, Bush took the position that the Supreme Court's ban on coerced recitals of the pledge didn't apply to a recent Massachusetts law that required schoolteachers to lead their students in the pledge. In the absence of a specific ban, he argued, the law could be presumed constitutional. Bush would be on even firmer ground now if he presumed that Congress and the states have all the constitutional authority they need to ban the importation and sale of deadly weapons of any sort - so long as the regulations are reasonably related to a compelling public purpose.   Not for a very long time have the courts challenged congressional regulatory power under the Commerce Clause. That power has been invoked to justify far more stringent restrictions on private behavior than curbing high-powered weapons that, in the hands of thugs and crazies, maim and kill law-enforcement officers and even helpless children playing in a school yard. The National Rifle Association is nonetheless certain to spout its usual drivel about the Second Amendment: ''A well-regulated militia being necessary to the security of a free state, the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed.'' NRA literature usually amputates or plays down that prefatory dependent clause - for good reason. It explains the historic purpose of the Second Amendment: that it was not adopted to license madmen, fools and criminals to buy and use deadly weapons of their choosing, but to assure the survival of the state militias. There has been no quarrel over standing armies in America. But when Congress wrote the amendment, that historic apprehension was still fresh.  '' What, sir,'' asked Elbridge Gerry during the debate on the amendment, ''is the use of a militia? It is to prevent the establishment of a standing army, the bane of liberty.'' There is nothing in the history, or prior judicial interpretation, of the Second Amendment to prevent any reasonable regulation of semiautomatic weapons. There are, to the contrary, precedents, including a Supreme Court decision upholding the 1934 ban on sawed-off shotguns, that would support such regulation.   But if his real concern is to be constitutionally kosher, the standard the president announced last autumn in the Pledge debate will do very nicely: What the Supreme Court hasn't specifically banned is presumably permissible. If that makes his NRA chums unhappy, Bush can invite them in the friendliest way to bring a test case.

1490.text: GUN-31157 PRIMARY Fenty, Groups Rally Against Court Ruling; Leaders, Youth Activists Decry Bid to Weaken Restrictions Standing before about three dozen students and young adults, Ron Moten, the co-founder of the advocacy group Peaceoholics, painted a grim picture yesterday to illustrate why he hopes to save the District ban on handguns. Moten, speaking from the steps of the John A. Wilson Building, gestured to the young people, many of whom have been affected by gun violence. Any of them could be a victim in the future, he said. "People whose brains get splattered on the streets look like these people up here," Moten said. "We must change the mentality of the people. Guns are not the answer." Peaceoholics, along with Reaching Out to Others Together and other civic groups, sponsored the event to draw attention to a recent U.S. Court of Appeals ruling easing the ban. D.C. Mayor Adrian M. Fenty (D) attended, along with Council members Marion Barry (D-Ward 8), Phil Mendelson (D-At Large) and Kwame R. Brown (D-Ward 7) and Paul Strauss, D.C. shadow senator. "We know the answer to the amount of violence in the District of Columbia is not to have more guns on the streets," Fenty said. "That's why we decry the decision of the judges." A federal appeals court overturned the District's tough gun-control law earlier this month, citing the Second Amendment, which grants the right to possess firearms. The court ruling was the result of a challenge by six city residents.

1496.text: GUN-2862 PRIMARY NRA Fires an Ad Attack At Schumer, Crime Bill The National Rifle Association went on the attack last week against Rep. Charles E. Schumer (D-N.Y.), a leading sponsor of the House crime bill, calling him "the criminal's best friend in Congress." A full-page ad in USA Today said the $ 15 billion legislation that Schumer helped draft as chairman of the House Judiciary subcommittee on crime and criminal justice put too much money into crime prevention programs and not enough into more prisons. Disparaging the $ 7 billion for prevention efforts, the NRA ad said Schumer would "squander all those tax dollars on programs like 'diversion' training, money to 'increase the self-esteem' of young criminals and to pay for midnight basketball leagues." The text urged readers to inform Schumer that "America needs to build prisons, not build friendships with criminals." Schumer went to the House floor and accused the gun lobby of "sinking to the cheap shot, the smear and the big lie" and of trying to scare Americans. The Brooklyn Democrat defended the House crime bill, saying it "hits violent criminals with a ton of bricks" and "cuts violent crime off at the roots." The legislation, on which House leaders hope to complete action this week, would authorize $ 3 billion to help states build prisons and boost the number of federal crimes punishable by death from two to 65. Schumer also maintained the NRA attack was "really about gun control" issues not covered in the crime bill. He was a leading sponsor of the Brady handgun control law and figures to have a big role in upcoming legislation to ban assault weapons and curb large-scale gun dealing.

1492.text: NA-43094 PRIMARY Gun Deaths Versus Car Deaths A report out on Tuesday from the Violence Policy Center confirms yet again the lunacy of America's loose gun policies. The report contains the striking finding that gun deaths exceeded motor vehicle deaths in 14 states and the District of Columbia in 2011, the latest year for which the relevant data are available from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. That's up from 12 states in 2010 and 9 states in 2009. The 2011 states are Alaska, Arizona, Colorado, Illinois, Louisiana, Maryland, Michigan, Nevada, Ohio, Oregon, Utah, Vermont, Virginia and Washington State, as well as the District of Columbia. In Alaska, for example, there were 126 gun deaths and 87 motor vehicle deaths. The numbers in Ohio were 1,227 gun deaths and 1,178 motor vehicle deaths. Nationwide, the number of motor vehicle deaths still exceeded gun deaths: 35,543 to 32,351.   But consider that in 2009 the spread was much larger - 42,624 motor vehicles deaths nationwide and 28,874 gun deaths. Overall, the C.D.C. numbers indicate that gun deaths are trending somewhat upward as motor vehicle deaths continue on a steady decline, thanks in large measure to serious government safety regulation aimed at reducing fatalities and injuries that is notably missing in the sphere of firearms. In fact, guns remain the only consumer product not regulated at the federal level for health and safety, in keeping with the wishes of the gun industry and compliant lawmakers.

characters: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

1497.text: GUN-5570 PRIMARY GUN CONTROL ADVOCATES GAINING FIREPOWER FOR REVISION; COUNTY AUTHORITY OVER GUN SHOWS WOULD CAUSE CONFUSION, ACCORDING TO THE NRA In the chilly back room of the Piccadilly Cafeteria off Military Trail, the board members of the local chapter of the National Rifle Association have gathered for their last board meeting before Tuesday's election. Foremost on their minds is the constitutional revision that would grant counties power over gun sales at gun shows. ''You go to gun shows and they don't sell guns to everyone who walks in,'' grouses Jorge Adan, a new recruit whose wanderings on the Internet led him to the Piccadilly meeting in suburban West Palm Beach. ''All these laws and regulations don't do a damn thing for criminals because criminals don't obey laws,'' President George Russum complains. Says Vice President Lee Coleman: ''Imagine how different history would be if all the Jews in Germany had guns.'' And so it goes. The nine men assembled at a table around the corner from the chow line early on a Wednesday night, who say they stand for Joe Public and his pursuit of life, liberty and happiness, have a long list of reasons why gun sales at gun shows should not be regulated. But in an increasingly violent state, theirs is a losing battle. It is an anachronistic view that no longer works in a state of more than 14 million people, gun control advocates say. On Tuesday, the issue comes to a vote with Revision 12 to the Florida Constitution. Public support for the amendment has been overwhelming. A statewide poll by Handgun Control Inc. showed 75 percent of those questioned supported it. A poll by the Constitution Revision Commission found 78 percent endorsed it.

1499.text: GUN-3678 PRIMARY Williams misleads Williams also neglects to tell his readers that the rather liberal Swiss have an extensive social welfare system and an extremely low unemployment rate: in some years less than 1 percent. On the other hand, the U.S. rate is around 6 percent. We have more unemployed than the Swiss have citizens. The reason Switzerland has a low crime rate is that everyone either has a job or gets government help, which makes crime unprofitable. Williams' preposterous, unsubstantiated and incorrect assertion that the Swiss have low crime because of high gun ownership is an insult to his readers. Sweden, Switzerland, Japan, Canada, Great Britain and Australia are all advanced, wealthy, modern, stable democracies with strict handgun laws. Their combined population is just shy of ours, but in 1992 they had a total of 394 handgun homicides while the U.S. had 13,220, according to the FBI. Those countries have less than 3 percent of our death rate. Williams either deceitfully or ignorantly compared apples to oranges (South Africa, Mexico, and Israel to the U.S.). Once you account for variables like a nation's wealth and military service requirements, we see that countries with more gun control have much lower death rates. MATTHEW CLEMENT

1498.text: GUN-10753 PRIMARY WE ARE A STATE WITH NO FIREARMS CONTROLS The Sept. 1 front page article, Gun laws come under fire, explains in detail why local lawmakers can't, in any way, control how guns are used anywhere, including at this new super gun range proposed by Commissioner Henry Wilson. If lawmakers did, his new firearms preemption law explains the jail time and fines the NRA would impose. This latest firearms preemption law is just a cleanup law for the original preemption law passed in 1987. For sure, we are now a state with absolutely no controls on firearms, a longtime goal of the NRA and, as usual, supported 100 percent by our Pasco legislative delegation. Ever wonder what it costs in treasury and lives to run a state with no controls on firearms? Arthur C. Hayhoe, director Florida Coalition to Stop Gun Violence Inc. * * * Top of the class Aug. 31 A brief moment to toot a marching band's horn I think it is great that you have made people aware of how hard our marching band kids work. However, I was disappointed that The Marching Mustangs from Mitchell High were not mentioned. We have the biggest band in the county, 130 kids. They also went through two weeks of band camp in this grueling heat and have three-hour practices twice a week after school. They work so hard, and the halftime show that they provide will knock your socks off.

characters: Florida Coalition to Stop Gun Violence Inc.

1503.text: GUN-3600 PRIMARY Machine gun ban appealed Donald Wright just wanted to bury his two machine guns and three pipe bombs in the ground and remove them if and when a government insurrection occurred, but he was arrested before he had the chance. "The whole time I was praying I would never have to use it," Wright said of his stash of heavy ordnance found by federal agents at his Marietta home in August 1994. "The only time I would have pulled them out of the ground was in case of a holocaust or invasion or something." Wright, 39, pleaded guilty to possession of a machine gun and possession of a pipe bomb. He is serving a 41-month sentence. His appeal, to be argued today in Atlanta, challenges a federal law banning machine gun possession. If he wins, it could throw into question a host of federal gun laws, including the section of the omnibus 1994 crime bill banning 19 semiautomatic assault rifles. Wright's appeal is led by his federal defender, Greg Smith, who is no fan of the types of weapons his client harbored and doesn't own a gun. "But the issue here is what the Constitution allows," Smith said. "I think the Second Amendment, if it means anything, means that the federal government cannot ban the general citizenry's mere possession of militia- style weapons." Bill Rankin's report, B/04

1500.text: GUN-31080 PRIMARY 2007 Increase Percentage increase in number of concealed-weapon permits issued in Virginia from 2006 to 2007: Statewide: 60.6% (43,927 permits issued in 2007) Alexandria: 34% (256 issued in 2007) Arlington County: 46.6% (349 issued in 2007) Fairfax County: 64.4% (2,471 issued in 2007) Loudoun County: 52.4% (962 issued in 2007) Prince William County: 58.7% (1,636 issued in 2007) Total number of valid concealed-carry permits statewide as of today: 152,691 Sources: Virginia Supreme Court, Virginia State Police

1502.text: GUN-11841 PRIMARY Obama calls for measures to restrict weapons access President Obama on Wednesday urged Congress to vote on measures banning the sale of assault weapons and high-capacity ammunition magazines and requiring background checks before any firearm sale, part of an emerging White House response to a massacre last week at a Connecticut elementary school. The potential gun-control measures, which will be the focus of a working group led by Vice President Biden, mark the most specific proposals to date from Obama to deal with what he called a gun violence epidemic plaguing the United States. The move pleased gun-control advocates and many Democrats who have been disappointed with Obama's inaction on the issue during his first term, which has included four mass shootings of civilians. Obama told reporters at the White House that he expected the group, which will feature key Cabinet secretaries, to bring him recommendations in January. He said he intends to discuss the issue in his State of the Union address. With the anger and sadness over Friday's massacre at a Newtown, Conn., school driving the issue at a busy moment in Washington, Obama vowed to act on concrete proposals "without delay" as several Hill Democrats urged Congress to do the same. "This time, the words need to lead to action," Obama said. He added:  "We know this is a complex issue that stirs deeply held passions and political divides. . . . But the fact that this problem is complex can no longer be an excuse for doing nothing."

1501.text: NA-45923 PRIMARY Obama lauds Colo. as model on guns; President pushes own legislation as Senate prepares to debate issue President Obama is looking to rally gun-control backers in Washington by trumpeting the success of allies in states such as Colorado and Connecticut. Facing a tough congressional fight in the coming weeks, Obama praised a new set of gun laws in Colorado on Wednesday and said they are in line with what he wants to do nationally. "I've come to Denver today in particular because Colorado is proving a model of what's possible," Obama said during a visit to the Denver Police Academy. The president, who has a similar trip to Connecticut planned next week, said Colorado is showing that it's possible to enact "tougher background checks that won't infringe on the rights of responsible gun owners, but will help keep guns out of the hands of dangerous people." The president's trip came as Congress prepares to return from its Easter recess next week, with gun control on the Senate agenda. In the wake of a series of mass shootings -- particularly the December attack that killed 20 schoolchildren in Connecticut -- Obama and aides are pushing legislation that includes improved background checks for gun buyers, a renewed assault weapons ban and restrictions on the capacities of ammunition clips. Obama is also proposing programs to improve school safety and mental health treatment. "The president wants to sign the strongest gun bill he can," White House adviser Dan Pfeiffer said.

1505.text: GUN-11669 PRIMARY What's Good for Gun Manufacturers... If President Obama wins re-election, the 2nd Amendment is dead! Gun owners will be rounded up and herded into Marxist re-education camps! If guns are outlawed, only deer, elk, geese and quail will own guns! That's only a slight exaggeration of the sort of doomsday propaganda spun out by the National Rifle Association and its supporters in Congress, as part of the campaign to paint Mr. Obama as a bleeding-heart liberal on guns. It's effective. Crain's Detroit Business reported this week that gun sales are surging, and attributed the "boom" (Crain's unintended pun, not mine) to people who fear Mr. Obama will confiscate their Ruger handguns and their AK-47 knock-offs. That's sort of interesting, because it suggests they think he's going to win. It's also ridiculous. Mr. Obama has not been a gun-control zealot. He agreed to sign a bill - on credit cards of all things - that included a measure allowing people to carry loaded guns into the national parks, without much of a fight. He's kept silent-too silent, in my opinion-on gun violence over the last 3-plus years. Now the administration wants to relax rules on the export of firearms so that gun manufacturers can make more money, because apparently the weapons-making business is just not profitable enough. The Wall Street Journal reported that foreign sales of all American-made weapons were more than $34 billion last year, but industry officials believe they could make tens of billions of dollars more with relaxed export controls.

1504.text: GUN-6935 PRIMARY Don't resell guns; Saturday Talk I am shocked that certain law enforcement agencies sell guns confiscated from drug dealers back into circulation ("Cobb sheriff under fire for selling guns," Oct. 9). Most of the guns noted on the published list are not normally used by legitimate gun owners for either hunting or protection. Hubert Williams, president of Police Foundation (a Washington-based organization that promotes improving law enforcement), states "police agencies have found that some guns they sold lawfully later were used to commit crimes." It would seem that the $ 4,979 gained by the Cobb County sheriff doesn't justify the possible loss of one life related to his selling of 58 confiscated guns, even though the buyers were "licensed gun dealers." With 58 guns involved, the possibility of more than one life being lost is highly likely. For Cobb Sheriff Bill Hutson to blame criticism on "anti-gun advocates" is a travesty. Anti-gun advocates are committed to protecting the public by making it more difficult for children, insane people and criminals to obtain firearms. DR. HOWARD BROWN Brown, of Atlanta, is a semiretired thoracic surgeon.

1510.text: GUN-1268 PRIMARY County Commission again dodges gun control, saying it's a state issue The Gwinnett County Commission decided Tuesday that gun control is a responsibility of the General Assembly, and urged the members of a citizens task force to meet with the local delegation. "It's not a county issue," Commissioner Renee S. Unterman said. "It belongs downtown." While the eight members of the task force agreed to meet with local legislators, several were critical of the way their group handled the issue and charged that the anti-gun-control faction railroaded through its recommendations. Martha Stephenson, one of three members to oppose the group's final recommendations, said that while meeting with the legislators would be productive, philosophical divisions within the task force are so deep that further work together would be fruitless. The group, established by the County Commission last year, is made up of four members who generally oppose gun control on constitutional grounds and four members who favor it. The task force did recommend that the state adopt a driver's license that would use magnetic strips or special seals to signal any criminal background the driver might have. Felons are not allowed to purchase guns. When asked by task force Chairman Richard Reber whether they thought the state is responsible for imposing gun control, Commissioners Lillian H. Webb and W.J. Dodd and Mrs. Unterman said yes. Although they didn't respond to the question, Commissioners J. Curtis McGill and Douglas E. Williamson have come out against gun control ordinances at the county level.

1507.text: GUN-30808 PRIMARY Mental Health and Guns: Do Background Checks Do Enough? Revelations about Cho Seung-Hui's 2005 stay in a psychiatric center under a magistrate's order have raised questions about whether background checks adequately scrutinize the mental health history of potential gun buyers. Federal law prohibits those who have been ''adjudicated as a mental defective'' by a court, as well as those who have been involuntarily committed to a mental health facility, from buying a firearm. Only 17 states, however, submit such mental health information to the background check systems they use on gun purchases, said Dennis Henigan, legal director of the Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence. Although Virginia has some of the most permissive gun laws in the country, the state does, in fact, include such mental health records in the background checks it conducts when someone tries to buy a handgun, said Donna Tate, program manager in the firearms transaction center for the Virginia State Police. ''We determined Mr. Cho to be lawfully eligible to purchase a gun on two occasions,'' Ms. Tate said, referring to the Glock 9-millimeter handgun and .22-caliber pistol recovered after the shootings. A magistrate ordered that Mr. Cho be evaluated at Carilion Saint Albans Behavioral Health Center on Dec. 13, 2005, after two women who were students complained about his behavior. The next day, a special justice approved outpatient treatment for Mr. Cho and he was free to go, which may explain why no red flags were raised by the state's background check system when he bought the weapons this year.

1506.text: GUN-6527 PRIMARY Group to march in D.C. SAFE Colorado seeks 'responsible' gun laws It's not yet an official political action committee, but later this month SAFE Colorado will take political action in Washington, D.C. Sane Alternatives to the Firearms Epidemic plans to fly 100 students to the nation's capital to lobby for what it calls "responsible" gun legislation. The bipartisan group was born out of the Columbine High School massacre to counter the National Rifle Association's political clout. "We are taking a plane full of students to the capital and will talk to anyone that will listen," said David Winkler, who graduated from George Washington High School in Denver this spring. Winkler last month accompanied a friend to the Tanner Gun Show where they bought a TEC-9 assault weapon to expose what gun-control supporters call a loophole in gun-buying laws. "We need gun laws that people can understand and comply with," Winkler said Wednesday. "Right now the tax code is more complicated than the gun code." SAFE's founders say the group believes in the right to bear arms but advocates stricter gun regulations. In Washington, the group plans to march from the Washington Monument to the Capitol, where participants will try to meet with Colorado's legislative delegation, Democratic and Republican party leaders, and President Clinton. SAFE co-founder Arnie Grossman said that while the group is not an official PAC, it plans to keep raising money and donate to various political campaigns. The group already has raised $ 30,000 for the trip to Washington and for advertising.   "Right now we hope to be informational yet influential and get something done before another school year starts," Grossman said.

1508.text: GUN-1563 PRIMARY Weapons Show in Howard County Attracts High-Caliber Gun Enthusiasts They came in pickup trucks and they came in Volvos, some wearing camouflage and others wearing corduroys from Britches of Georgetowne. They came from all over and probably had little in common beyond their belief that "the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed." That's from the Second Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. Gun enthusiasts tend to talk a lot about the Second Amendment. And at the Howard County Fairgrounds yesterday, where hundreds attended a firearms show, the mantra was everywhere, engraved on watch faces and heavy brass belt buckles, silk-screened on T-shirts, even tattooed on a fellow's forearm. In this age of violent street death, gun enthusiasts have acquired a bad name in some circles, a name that many show-goers yesterday said is unjustified. Several said the media have wrongly portrayed them as crazies, yahoos or both. "Look around," said Courtney Smith, a dealer from Richmond who was selling antique rifles and pistols inside a building the size of an aircraft hangar. "There are all types of people of here, and they're law-abiding people, good people, people who like guns. They're not the people you see on TV, people being arrested for murdering people in the street." One of Smith's guns, a gold-plated, single-action revolver, was one of just 50 delivered by the Colt Patent Firearms Manufacturing Co. to the J.P. Moores & Sons store in New York on May 3, 1876. Not many are left. Smith wants $ 12,500 for it.

1509.text: GUN-11012 PRIMARY Jan. 14 Readers letters Memorial really was just a political rally I was thoroughly disgusted by the political rally under the guise of a memorial service that took place in Tucson. Having conducted and spoken in several memorial services myself, I understand their purpose. That is to pay respects to those deceased and give comfort to their loved ones left behind. Some of what was said should be spoken in other appropriate venues but not in a memorial service. Moralizing and condemning others do not in any way represent the purpose of a memorial gathering. In such an environment it can only be seen as trying to draw attention to the speaker. Cheering during a true memorial gathering is grossly offensive. Lyle Topham Saratoga Second Amendment intent has changed I fully agree with Ed Jacklitch's thesis (Letters, Jan. 13): The proliferation of firearms is a direct consequence of the Second Amendment, whose original intent was to safeguard the citizens' rights and freedoms against depredations of oppressive governments. It made perfect sense in the times of the Revolutionary War, when a spirited militia, armed with no more than pitchforks and shotguns, could successfully challenge government forces. Alas, nowadays governments possess vast, powerful and ultra-sophisticated arsenals for which pistols and handguns are no match. I therefore fully expect the NRA, following the logic of the Second Amendment, to push for the right of citizens to own cannons, tanks and surface-to-air missiles.

1511.text: GUN-2770 PRIMARY HOUSE BACKERS OF GUN BAN AWAIT NRA BACKLASH POWERFUL LOBBY VOWS CAMPAIGN House supporters of a ban on combat-style semiautomatic weapons carefully pondered the wrath of the National Rifle Association on Friday, a day after the chamber narrowly approved the prohibition. "The NRA has a lot of members who are very enthusiastic in their beliefs," said Rep. Henry Hyde, R-Ill., who voted for the ban. "You don't disappoint or turn your back on people who feel intensely about issues without considering the consequences." The come-from-behind 216-214 approval of the bill -- which would ban 19 types of military-style weapons and their look-alikes -- was a rare defeat for the NRA. The organization has 3.4 million members nationwide and a political action committee that has contributed more money to congressional candidates than almost any other PAC. Wayne LaPierre, the NRA's executive vice president, said Friday that representatives who supported the bill should expect to see the organization resort more to activity by its members than with its money. He said the NRA would inform its members how lawmakers voted, but he declined to discuss specific strategies. "Our strength is in our people," LaPierre said. "We'll be in districts throughout the country at the grass-roots level, showing people what a sham this crime control was." "They'll make sure all the people who share their view know exactly how their member of Congress voted," said Rep. Gerald Solomon, R-N.Y., a foe of the ban and himself an NRA member. "And there's nothing wrong with that."

1513.text: GUN-5277 PRIMARY Letters, Faxes & E-Mail; Atlanta gun suit unnecessary to prevent gun deaths Atlanta Mayor Bill Campbell and his administration should know exactly what policy will cut gun murders and save lives. If they do know, they have ignored it. Anti-gun allies can't explain why, according to them, last year 300,000 felons, drug dealers and fugitives who were turned away by gun dealers weren't arrested. They weren't prosecuted because they don't exist. Every felon caught carrying or attempting to purchase a gun gets five years in federal prison. This policy has cut gun murders 65 percent because of prompt indictments and no pretrial release and because 85 percent of a given felon's sentence must be served in distant prisons, with resultant decreased access to family and friends. Campbell is suing gun manufacturers but denies it's because Atlanta is "cash-strapped." It's time to quit playing politics with numbers and money and to enforce criminal laws, period. KEN COBB Cobb, of Atlanta, works in the entertainment business.

1512.text: GUN-4444 PRIMARY Protecting Children From Guns Once again, Congressional Republicans are blocking sensible gun-control legislation. The measure involves child safety, making the G.O.P.'s doctrinal alliance with the National Rifle Association especially offensive. Seeking to prevent the tragedies that frequently occur when children get their hands on guns, President Clinton included in his juvenile justice bill a provision that would require firearms dealers to sell a safety lock with every gun. But the provision got omitted from a version of the bill approved in May by the Republican-controlled House. Republican leaders, anxious to please the N.R.A., successfully maneuvered to avoid an open vote on the House floor. The N.R.A. argued that the safety lock might prevent gun owners from protecting their families in an emergency, like a robbery. But the child lock is easily removable. Besides, as studies have shown, unlocked guns in the home pose more of a threat to family safety than outside intruders do. These child safety locks are an important step, and one that has wide support in the law enforcement and medical communities. Even gun manufacturers are beginning to get the message. In August, the world's largest handgun manufacturer, Smith & Wesson, announced that it would voluntarily begin providing a trigger lock with every handgun sold to the public. A number of states -- not including New York -- have already passed laws imposing severe penalties on gun owners who leave unsecured weapons around the premises for children to find and use.

1517.text: GUN-30659 PRIMARY Court rejects D.C. ban on guns; Tough controls had barred handguns not registered before 1976 WASHINGTON -- A U.S. appeals court on Friday struck down a strict ban on owning firearms in the nation's capital, setting the stage for the Supreme Court to rule on the scope of the Second Amendment and whether it expressly protects a person's right to own a gun. Lawyers on both sides of the debate called the decision significant -- it was the first time a federal appeals court has voided a gun law on the basis of the Second Amendment, they said. For that reason, the case is expected to reach the nation's highest court. If the justices were to agree with the lower court, the ruling would not likely sweep aside the many laws that regulate guns and gun ownership. However, it could cast doubt on measures that forbid law-abiding residents from possessing weapons. Although the Second Amendment is among the best-known provisions of the Constitution, it has had remarkably little effect on the law. Gun owners, led by the National Rifle Association, have spotlighted the amendment's reference to "the right of the people to keep and bear Arms" in waging political battles against efforts to limit the availability of weapons. But in the Supreme Court's only major ruling that interpreted the amendment, it focused on the measure's opening words, which speak of "a well-regulated Militia" being "necessary to the security of a free State."

1515.text: GUN-2928 PRIMARY GROWTH SPARKS BILL TO CURB FIRING OF GUNS; 'NOT GUN CONTROL,' BEARDEN SAYS; DEALS WILL 'COMMON-SENSE ISSUES' The encroachment of new subdivisions and businesses on what once was open space in St. Charles County has prompted the introduction of a bill to regulate the discharge of firearms in unincorporated parts of the county. The bill has been endorsed by the Sheriff's Department. Council Chairman Carl Bearden, R-7th District, says the measure "is not gun control" but deals with "common-sense issues." The bill was introduced March 14 and could be up for final passage at the council's meeting March 29. Capt. Tom Neer and Maj. John C. Bush of the Sheriff's Department said in a letter to Bearden that the bill "addresses all of the problem areas we have experienced through the past years with firearms complaints." They offered the opinion that a majority of residents in the outlying areas will appreciate the new restrictions, "especially during dove season and deer season when hunters tend to saturate the woods and fields." The officers recognized that some of the county's long-time residents "may be offended, and even angered, with an ordinance restricting the discharge of firearms in places formerly considered uninhabited." But, they say the proposal is intended "to preserve the peace and ensure the safety of all residents and visitors of our county." The bill defines pellet and BB guns as firearms as well as rifles and shotguns from which shot or bullets are propelled by exploding gunpowder.

1514.text: GUN-11274 PRIMARY Milwaukee: Customer who shot robbery suspect won't be charged The Aldi customer who shot an armed robber in the store Monday won't face criminal charges, prosecutors confirmed Friday. Milwaukee County District Attorney John Chisholm said the law allows use of reasonable force in threatening situations, regardless of whether the shooter has a concealed-weapons permit, which the customer did. "He disrupted an act that potentially exposed himself and others to great bodily harm," Chisholm said. Video footage from the store showed "he acted reasonably and in a controlled manner during the encounter," Chisholm said. It was a best-case scenario for gun-rights advocates who say such incidents will dissuade criminals from using guns. Critics of Wisconsin's new concealed carry law, however, say things easily could have gone worse. "Obviously, we're glad things turned out the way they did," said Jeri Bonavia, of the Wisconsin Anti-Violence Effort. "But we believe a robbery is bad enough and that adding this grocery store shoot-'em-up just increases the risk there will be a bad outcome." The customer, Nazir Al-Mujaahid, 35, of Milwaukee, called the incident nothing to brag about, but that "sometimes you have to do what you have to do." At a news conference Friday, Al-Mujaahid said he and his wife stopped at the store, 7601 W. Fond du Lac Ave., for some last-minute dinner items. They'd never shopped there before, he said. As they were in line to check out, he noticed the suspect pointing a shotgun at a cashier, demanding money in a very agitated way. He said he feared for the safety of himself and others so he unholstered his semiautomatic 9mm Steyr handgun, cocked it and kept it down at his side as he motioned another customer behind the robber to move away.

1518.text: GUN-11692 PRIMARY Quinn gives 'stop!' sign NEWTOWN MASSACRE CITY COUNCIL Speaker Christine Quinn became the latest NewYorker to sign the Daily News petition to ban assault weapons. Quinn put her John Hancock on the petition Sunday morning ? and urged other New Yorkers to join the newspaper?s campaign for banning assault weapons and high-capacity magazines. "I?m very grateful for the Daily News for all the attention they?re bringing to the desperate need for the assault weapons ban to be repassed," she said. "I wanted to make sure, as an elected official, I sent not just a message of support of what the paper was doing, but I hoped I could urge other New Yorkers to sign the petition," she added. ?I wanted to make sure they don?t let 2012 end without putting their name on it." More than 66,500 people have signed the petition since it was first printed in The News last week. Quinn said she was further inspired to join the effort on Sunday morning after hearing NRA head Wayne LaPierre on "Meet the Press" double-down on his comments resisting any new gun restrictions and calling for armedsecurity in schools. "It was the most shocking disconnect from reality I think I have ever seen, and it was just callous," she said. "I hope we have millions and millions of people sign this petition," Quinn said. "There are families in Connecticut without children to wrap presents for.

1516.text: GUN-5804 PRIMARY To Make All Americans Safer Last month, the Senate came together in a spirit of bipartisanship and passed serious gun legislation that will help keep dangerous weapons out of the hands of fugitives, felons and our children. At that time, I expressed my hope that the House would follow suit. The House now has an opportunity to do just that. Will it join the Senate in passing sensible measures that require background checks for all gun sales at gun shows? Or will it pass a bill that not only leaves a loophole for gun shows but weakens one of the most effective and important gun control measures ever passed -- the Brady law? Both the Hyde-McCollum bill and Rep. John D. Dingell's alternative proposal have big problems. While both measure supposedly provide for background checks at gun shows, they create new loopholes and weaken current protections. Under the Hyde-McCollum bill, the definition of gun show is narrowed to exclude many events where large numbers of guns are sold, such as flea markets. Sellers at these events won't be required to perform background checks, leaving a gaping hole in the law. And even for the events that it does cover, the Hyde-McCollum bill weakens the Brady law by cutting down the amount of time law enforcement has to complete pre-sale background checks, thus making it easier for criminals to buy guns. But changing this law won't benefit law-abiding buyers. Under the FBI's instant check system, 73 percent of all buyers are allowed to buy within minutes, and 95 percent of all buyers have their check completed within two hours. Meanwhile, more than 400,000 people who should not have gotten guns have been stopped from buying them since the law took effect five years ago.

1521.text: GUN-1875 PRIMARY Armed and Dangerous (Cont'd) Oren E. Stembel's Oct. 9 letter contained a comparison that stymies me to no end: "There are reportedly more gun dealers than gas stations in the United States." I would greatly appreciate it if Mr. Stembel or his astute bedfellow, Handgun Control Inc., would explain the significance of comparing apples and oranges. His offering more gun laws in addition to the 20,000 already on the books as a means of curbing crime translates into an admission of gun law ineffectiveness. For some unknown reason, Mr. Stembel and people of his ilk can't seem to accept the fact that felons and 13-year-old murderers don't make their purchases at the neighborhood gun store. Much like crack cocaine, there's a black market supplying the tools of death to the lawless. It's simple economics: supply and demand. As for his assertion that gun owners should be certified marksmen -- get real! In 1988 motor vehicles claimed the lives of 48,024 people in the United States. Using his logic, that number may well have been zero if all car owners were certified by the National Association for Stock Car Auto Racing. Mr. Stembel is in dire need of a reality check.   MATTHEW P. KELLY Vienna I am no apologist for the National Rifle Association. In fact, I believe that outside of law enforcement and the military, automatic and large-caliber weapons should be banned while private ownership of nearly all firearms should be controlled by strict (but fair) licensing procedures. But it is simplistic and naive to think that banning guns would significantly impact the level of gun violence. Much more is required, including better family, public and community educational programs; meaningful citizen involvement in countering crime; improved and realistic programs to counter the causes of drugs, crime and violence; increased attention to closely supervising and working with juvenile offenders when they first start down the road to criminal activities; and stricter sentencing and longer terms for repeat offenders and those convicted of especially violent and callous crimes.

1520.text: GUN-6423 PRIMARY CALIFORNIA ENACTS THE TOUGHEST BAN ON ASSAULT GUNS Six years ago this month a disgruntled businessman named Gian Luigi Ferri took two TEC-9 semiautomatic pistols into the office tower at 101 California Street here and, in less than 15 minutes, killed eight people and wounded six others before turning a gun on himself and committing suicide. Ever since, those shots have helped inspire a gun-control debate that reached a milestone today when Gov. Gray Davis signed into law the nation's toughest and most comprehensive ban on assault-style weapons. "My friends, guns do kill people," Mr. Davis said in an elaborate signing ceremony at the San Francisco Hall of Justice, surrounded by a sea of law-enforcement officers and relatives of the victims of the 101 California shootings. "In short, assault weapons on our street are an assault on our common values. Today, California fights back." To be sure, California has not been alone in fighting back, or at least trying to. Amid the fierce Congressional gun-control deadlock that followed the Columbine High School shootings this spring, state legislatures around the country have been grappling with a variety of proposals to restrict firearms. But no other measure yet has gone so far as California's, which passed both houses of the Legislature by 2 to 1 last week after years of partisan feuding and the veto of a similar bill last year by Mr. Davis's Republican predecessor, Pete Wilson.

1522.text: GUN-6843 PRIMARY DEATHS AND INJURIES FROM FIREARMS FELL SHARPLY FROM 1993 TO 1997 The rate of gun deaths in the United States dropped 21 percent from 1993 to 1997 to the lowest level in more than 30 years, and firearm-related injuries fell 41 percent, the government said yesterday. Experts cited such reasons as tougher gun-control laws, a booming economy, better police work, and gun-safety courses. The study by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention looked at all gunshot wounds reported at emergency rooms, whether they were intentional, accidental or self-inflicted. The number of fatalities dropped from 39,595 - 15.4 gun deaths per 100,000 people - in 1993, to 32,436 - 12.1 per 100,000 - in 1997. The rate "is the lowest it's been since the mid-'60s," said J. Lee Annest, a CDC statistician. "This progress is really encouraging and really says that joint prevention efforts of public health officials, legislators and law enforcement should continue." The drop was not unexpected: Homicide rates in the 1990s have fallen to levels not seen since the 1960s, and about two-thirds of all homicides are committed with guns. But the latest figures also include suicides and accidental deaths. Moreover, nonfatal shootings fell from 104,390 to 64,207 in the same period, or from 40.5 per 100,000 to 24.0. Bill Manown, a spokesman for the National Rifle Association, said the numbers proved that more gun laws were not needed and that existing laws needed to be enforced.

1523.text: GUN-30893 PRIMARY The Gun Lobby's Loss The gun lobby has long intimidated politicians with its war chest and its trumpeted ability to deliver single-issue voters, especially in tight races. After this year's election, those politicians should be far less afraid and far more willing to vote for sensible gun-control laws. The National Rifle Association directed much money and bile against Barack Obama. In false, misleading and, fortunately, ineffective ads, fliers, mailers and Web postings, the group said that Mr. Obama posed a ''clear and present danger'' to Second Amendment rights and that his election would mean a gun ban. Despite that harsh barrage, Mr. Obama won states with heavy gun ownership, including Virginia, Ohio and Pennsylvania. That success should send a signal to other politicians: consistency matters. In fact, Mr. Obama has long been a supporter of the argument, disputed by this page, that the Second Amendment bestows an individual right to bear arms unrelated to raising a militia. But Mr. Obama did not abandon his support for reasonable gun-control laws. '' Don't tell me we can't uphold the Second Amendment while keeping AK-47s out of the hands of criminals,'' he declared at the Democratic convention. In Congressional races, the N.R.A. endorsed candidates in 20 of the 25 races where Democrats picked up seats from Republicans. We will not miss Florida's Tom Feeney and Ric Keller, Idaho's Bill Sali, Michigan's Joe Knollenberg, Ohio's Steve Chabot, Colorado's Marilyn Musgrave and Pennsylvania's Phil English -- willing champions of an extreme agenda.

1519.text: GUN-10081 PRIMARY Catering to NRA's bidding creates absurd laws Re: Zephyrhills law conflicts with state gun rules, Oct. 4 letter. Editor: Daniel W. Burgess Jr. of the Zephyrhills City Council is certainly correct that state law does supersede local gun control laws. In 1998, the National Rifle Association pushed through what was called a preemption law that in effect canceled all city and county gun control laws and elevated that right exclusively to the state. No gun control laws on the local or even the state level have been passed since that time. Miami tried to pass some gun control laws to calm the gun violence rampant there. However, the Florida Supreme Court threw the laws out. It would be difficult to count the many lives and injuries this preemption law has cost this state. The NRA thoroughly dominates this Legislature and governor,  and no gun control law of any kind has been passed since 1988. However, this legislature and governor continue to support and pass NRA-sponsored laws that have loosened what few controls we do have on firearms. The latest is the new shoot-first law effective Oct. 1 that sped to the governor for signature with the votes of Sens. Mike Fasano and Victor Crist and, as always, Rep. Ken Littlefield. In addition, the two legislators who wrote the shoot-first law for Marion Hammer of the NRA have just introduced another bill that will force business owners to allow guns in cars in company parking lots in Florida. We think Disney just might have some objections. But it does make it easier for someone who wants to go postal to obtain his gun and act out his anger on fellow employees.

1524.text: GUN-7494 PRIMARY ASSAULT-GUN BAN STANDS, STATE'S HIGH COURT RULES 6-1 DECISION CALLED SIGNIFICANT DEFEAT FOR WEAPONS LOBBY Providing strong legal insulation to California's sweeping gun-control laws, the state Supreme Court on Thursday upheld the constitutionality of a 1989 assault-weapons ban and left little room for the gun lobby to challenge broader restrictions enacted by the Legislature last year. The justices, by a 6-1 vote, concluded that the Roberti-Roos Assault Weapons Control Act -- enacted in the months after a Stockton schoolyard massacre 11 years ago -- does not violate the rights of gun owners and manufacturers and is a legal legislative response to gun violence. The ruling upholds one of the strongest gun-control measures in the nation. "We reject any suggestion that the regulations at issue here impermissibly infringe on the right to defend life or protect property guaranteed by the California Constitution," Justice Janice Rogers Brown wrote for the majority. The ruling reversed a previous decision by the Sacramento-based 3rd District Court of Appeal. The appeals court had struck down the assault-weapons ban on the grounds that it was vague, violated equal-protection laws because it focused on a narrow group of weapons, and improperly vested power in the courts to decide which guns fall under the scope of the law. But the California Supreme Court disagreed on all arguments, sending a symbolic message to gun enthusiasts that restrictions on certain weapons in the state may be here to stay. As a result of the ruling, dozens of versions of military-style semiautomatic rifles, pistols and shotguns will be outlawed in the state under the 1989 law, as well as copycat assault weapons banned under related legislation that went into effect Jan. 1 of this year.

1528.text: GUN-7005 PRIMARY AMERICANS FAVOR TOUGHER GUN CONTROLS, POLL FINDS Most Americans say they want tougher gun controls, including a big majority who favor a law requiring that guns be sold with trigger locks, an Associated Press poll finds. But people are split on the best way to reduce violence: better enforcement of current laws or passage of tougher ones. The poll found that 42 percent thought stricter enforcement was more likely to cut gun violence, while 33 percent said enacting tougher gun laws was a better approach. Neither option was best for one-fifth of those in the poll conducted for Associated Press by ICR of Media, Pa. The survey reflected a shift from an AP poll taken immediately after the Columbine High School shootings in Littleton, Colo., a year ago Thursday when more people said tougher laws were the answer. The earlier poll showed just over half of Americans said more gun laws would be more effective, while four in 10 picked tougher enforcement. But by late August, people had shifted to thinking stricter enforcement was a better way to reduce violence.

1526.text: GUN-10015 PRIMARY State Democrats and Republicans Duel Over Methods to Decrease Illegal Guns From Amber alerts to Kendra's law, new policies often stem from high-profile crimes. So it is with the recent shooting deaths of two New York City police officers, which have reignited an increasingly bitter debate in Albany about how best to keep illegal guns off the streets and deter violence against police officers. There have been dueling editorials from Gov. George E. Pataki, a Republican, and Assembly Speaker Sheldon Silver, a Democrat. Republicans say Assembly Democrats have blocked legislation to toughen sentences for people who use or sell illegal guns. Mr. Silver says he sees irony in the Republican Party's effort to portray itself as the party with the better record on gun control. The governor and Senate Republicans, he said, have turned away from such Assembly legislation as a ban on .50-caliber rifles, and are beholden to the National Rifle Association. But the politician caught most in the cross-fire has been Mr. Silver himself, for comments in The New York Post last week in which he said, ''I don't go to cops' funerals.'' After days on the defensive after those remarks, Mr. Silver showed up today at the wake of Daniel Enchautegui, an off-duty police officer who was fatally shot over the weekend. In an interview shortly before the wake, the speaker sought to clarify his comments, saying he did not see funerals as a place for a photo op.

1525.text: GUN-133 PRIMARY Campaign Trail Allegiance to Firearms Eight years ago John B. Anderson made headlines by telling a convention of the Gun Owners of New Hampshire that he was in favor of gun control. None of this year's Republican candidates took that approach Tuesday night. Instead, the current crop competed to prove their allegiance to - and even possession of - firearms. At one point Vice President Bush pulled a tiny translucent pistol out of his pocket to underscore a point on which he, the other candidates and the audience all seemed to agree: steps were needed to control such guns, which can elude detection devices. ''I'm making a pitch here for reason'' in balancing the needs of law enforcement against the rights of gun owners, he said, asserting that to refuse to seek a balance would play into the hands of terrorists. Previously, none of the Republican candidates had indicated that they were inclined to yield to gun control of any sort. And in New Hampshire, whose license plates read ''Live Free or Die,'' many hold that the Constitution's right to keep and bear arms is absolute and not to be tampered with. An Eventful Visit When a member of Richard A. Gephardt's staff recently asked Gary and Nona Reyhons if the candidate might stop by for coffee at their farm in Mount Vernon, Iowa, they said that would be fine. But according to The Sun, the local weekly newspaper, the visit proved to be a lot more eventful than expected.

1531.text: GUN-10255 PRIMARY Loopy Laws And Loopholes Nothing better defines the illogic that governs much of politics than this: In the post-Sept. 11 hysteria, metal detectors were installed at the entrances to Virginia's  Capitol and General Assembly building. Yet individuals in the commonwealth have the right to carry guns. So when people packing heat approached the metal detectors, they handed their guns to the attendants, walked through the detectors, retrieved their weapons and proceeded inside. What were the metal detectors supposed to protect against -- car keys and loose change? Now, at least the machines serve a purpose. The legislative committee in charge of Capitol Square recently decided that only individuals with concealed-weapon permits may bring guns into the buildings. This move has infuriated some gun owners, who see it as an infringement of their Second Amendment rights and vow to see the ban rescinded. Anyone defending the Constitution has an ally in this corner, but these people are missing the bigger picture: What justifies blocking the entrances to these buildings in the first place? We have no reason to believe that terrorists have Virginia lawmakers in their sights, and, if some did, they certainly would be shrewd enough to obtain  concealed-weapon permits. Virginians had open access to their Capitol until recently, and that was a freedom that represented no danger. Gunfire was exchanged in the Capitol only once -- in 1866, and it was between reporters.

1529.text: GUN-4898 PRIMARY IN BRIEF; Gun Database In an effort to monitor pistol ownership, the county will share its computer database on handgun permits with local law-enforcement agencies -- including the County Departments of Public Safety and Probation and District Attorney Jeanine Pirro's Office -- County Clerk Leonard N. Spano announced. "Connecting these agencies to our system is a major piece in our overall plan for handgun accountability in this county," Mr. Spano said. "Very often, the safety of the public may depend upon knowing who has these handguns, where they are and how many weapons they have. I have insured that law enforcement in this county has access to this information when they need it." The County Clerk said the need for making such information available to law-enforcement agencies was clearly shown during recent police standoffs in Dobbs Ferry and Tarrytown. In Dobbs Ferry last month, a 34-year-old man held the police at bay for 15 hours, and officials later found a high-powered assault rifle and 12-gauge pump shotgun in his apartment. In Tarrytown, also last month, a retired police officer held the police at bay after killing his wife and daughter and injuring the daughter's boyfriend. . The police said the suspect had two registered handguns in his possession. The recently enacted Westchester County Handgun Record Keeping Accountability Act requires that all current pistol licenses be registered with the Clerk's Office by April 1, 2000. The information would help the police know the weapons they might encounter in situations like the two last month, the County Clerk said.

1527.text: GUN-5613 PRIMARY METRO IN BRIEF VIRGINIA Gilmore Wants to Toughen Gun Penalties Gov. James S. Gilmore III (R) said yesterday that he will ask the General Assembly to approve a statewide version of Project Exile, a federal program that imposes harsher sentences for gun-related crimes in Richmond and Hampton Roads. Gilmore said his Virginia Exile initiative would impose minimum, mandatory three-year prison terms for possession of an illegal gun in a school, possession of an illegal gun by a felon, and possession of an illegal gun and illegal drugs at the same time. Under current state law, punishment for those offenses can range from no jail time to five years in prison, depending on the offender's record and other factors. A minimum, mandatory three-year term is considered tougher because it eliminates the possibility of lighter sentences. The federal Project Exile requires a minimum, mandatory sentence of five years for most federal gun convictions. Court Won't Hear Appeal on Checks of Jurors The Virginia Supreme Court has declined to hear an appeal of an order blocking Loudoun County prosecutors from running criminal background checks on potential jurors. A three-judge panel ruled Friday that the court does not have jurisdiction to hear the case, which stems from a June order by Loudoun Chief Circuit Court Judge James H. Chamblin. The background checks can reveal such information as felony arrests and convictions, traffic charges and involuntary commitments for mental health reasons. The checks are used by prosecutors in several other Virginia jurisdictions, including Prince William and Arlington counties, to screen prospective jurors in serious cases.

1532.text: NA-43029 PRIMARY Obama Announces Gun Control Actions HONOLULU - President Obama on Friday announced two new executive actions that will help further his gun control agenda. The actions will make it easier for states to provide information about mentally ill individuals to the federal background check system. The Department of Justice is proposing to clarify that the term "committed to a mental institution" includes involuntary inpatient as well as outpatient commitments. The clarification would help states determine what information should be made accessible to the federal background check system. The Department of Health and Human Services relaxed some privacy protections in order to help identify in the background check system those individuals who, under federal law, should be prohibited from owning a gun for mental health reasons. The department proposed a rule to give certain entities covered by the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act permission to submit to the background check system the "limited" information required to prevent mentally ill people from obtaining firearms. "Too many Americans have been severely injured or lost their lives as a result of gun violence," the White House said in an email statement.
