"While the vast majority of Americans who experience a mental illness are not violent, in some cases when persons with a mental illness do not receive the treatment they need, the result can be tragedies such as homicide or suicide." The White House added, "The federal background check system is the most effective way to assure that such individuals are not able to purchase a firearm from a licensed gun dealer."

characters: Department of Health and Human Services

1530.text: GUN-6137 PRIMARY Gun Control's Limited Aim; Bills Would Not Have Stopped Recent Killings None of the gun control legislation under discussion in Congress would have prevented the purchase of weapons by shooters in a recent spate of firearms violence, including last week's massacre at a Texas church, gun control supporters and opponents agree. People at both ends of the spectrum in the gun control debate say the provisions before a House-Senate conference committee would have done nothing to save the scores of people who died in those attacks. In that string of violence, all of the killers had either bought their guns legally or found an easy way to get around state and federal laws. The provisions now on the table, from child-safety locks to stricter regulation of gun shows, would not have stopped the sales. "The whole gun control debate is on the fringe of the problem," said Kristen Rand, director of federal policy at the Violence Policy Center, a gun control advocacy group. "You can't pretend by plugging loopholes here and there that you're going to have an effect on the crux of American gun violence." John Velleco, a spokesman for Gun Owners of America, voiced a similar conclusion for different reasons. "Not only would the provisions being discussed do nothing to prevent those shootings, neither do the thousands of gun laws on the books today," he said. The Senate version of the juvenile justice bill would impose new restrictions on weapons sales by unlicensed dealers at gun shows, requiring them to conduct criminal background checks on buyers. It also would outlaw the import of ammunition clips of more than 10 bullets, require child safety locks on all new handguns and prohibit juveniles from obtaining assault weapons.

1536.text: NA-44393 PRIMARY 7 guns taken at Leesburg restaurant owned by 2nd Amendment advocate Burglars  recently took seven guns from a restaurant in Virginia whose owner has drawn attention for his advocacy of the right to bear arms - and to carry guns into his establishment. The burglary took place Nov. 23 at the Cajun Experience  in Leesburg, town police said. About 12:45 a.m., they said, two people forced their way inside, broke into a padlocked closet and stole seven long guns described as rifles and shotguns. "It's just my luck," owner Bryan Crosswhite said Saturday night. "We don't keep guns there" routinely, he added. He said the guns t were left there overnight by a friend after a hunting trip as a matter of convenience and were to be taken to the person's house in Virginia, where they are usually kept. But within hours of the guns being locked away in the restaurant, the burglars struck, he said.    The closet was off a private room and not where diners are generally served. The burglary was not noticed until hours  later, he said. The restaurant drew attention after Crosswhite began offering a discount to diners for every weapon carried. A Nov. 20 posting on the restaurant's Facebook page read: "Today we celebrate the 2nd Amendment! Stop in for Open Carry Wednesday and get 10% off for exercising" the right to bear arms. The idea of "open carry" generally applies to sidearms, such as pistols and revolvers, and not long guns, such as rifles and shotguns.

1534.text: GUN-30091 PRIMARY BILIRAKIS DISTORTS REPORT'S FINDING The statement It is "the Department of Homeland Security's view that returning military veterans and gun owners are likely to commit terrorist acts." Rep. Gus Bilirakis, R-Palm Harbor, on Wednesday in a news release The ruling: BARELY TRUE Conservative groups have been sounding the alarm about a leaked report from the Department of Homeland Security that they say portrays veterans and gun owners as likely terrorists. The report in question is part of a series of assessments issued by a Homeland Security division that works with the FBI to analyze threats in the United States. It begins by stating that it has "no specific information that domestic right-wing terrorists are currently planning acts of violence, but right-wing extremists may be gaining new recruits." A few sections down, the report says: "The possible passage of new restrictions on firearms and the return of military veterans facing significant challenges reintegrating into their communities could lead to the potential emergence of terrorist groups or lone wolf extremists capable of carrying out violent attacks." The report says that "right-wing extremists will attempt to recruit and radicalize returning veterans in order to exploit their skills and knowledge derived from military training and combat." On gun control, the report states that "a correlation may exist between the potential passage of gun control legislation and increased hoarding of ammunition, weapons stockpiling, and paramilitary training among right-wing extremists." But it also notes that "law-abiding Americans" may also make bulk purchases of ammunition. The department issued a similar report on left-wing groups a few months ago.

1533.text: GUN-7677 PRIMARY GEORGIA'S GUNS 0N N.Y.'S MIND Probers link attacks to flood of pisto ls from South The city's illegal gun market is booming, and authorities blame an "iron pipeline" from northwestern Georgia that enabled just one group of gunrunners to bring as many as 100 weapons here. Seventeen of those guns have been traced to crimes, including three murders, a cop shooting and three assaults. Three men, Charles Chapman and Walstene Butts of Georgia, and Jeffrey Smith of Brooklyn, were indicted by the Brooklyn U.S. attorney's office on May 5 as partners in the gun-trafficking ring behind the crimes. Others are being sought in connection with the ring. Georgia's lax gun laws have long been an issue with New York law enforcement authorities. Georgia does not restrict the number of guns in a single purchase and also hosts the most gun shows, which tend to have more lax enforcement of federal gun laws. Two hundred sixty-two guns recovered in crimes here last year came from just one corner of Cobb County, Ga., the same locale where Chapman, Butts and Smith allegedly purchased some of their firearms. "That is 3.3% of the 7,000 New York crime guns we traced," said Joseph Green, spokesman for the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. "That's an enormous amount of guns from a very small area. It shows Georgia is a top gun-source state." "Fifty percent of all the guns recovered in Brooklyn last year came from Georgia, Florida and Virginia," said Dennis Hawkins of the Brooklyn district attorney's office. "There has to be a way to stem the flow."

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1535.text: GUN-9700 PRIMARY Assault-weapons ban expires Monday; no vote planned Congress will not vote on an assault-weapons ban that is due to expire Monday, Republican leaders said yesterday, rejecting a last-ditch effort by supporters to renew it. "I think the will of the American people is consistent with letting it expire, so it will expire," Senate Majority Leader Bill Frist (R., Tenn.) said. The 10-year ban, signed by President Bill Clinton in 1994, outlawed 19 types of military-style assault weapons. A clause directed that the ban expire unless Congress specifically reauthorized it. Some Democrats and several police leaders said President Bush should try to persuade Congress to renew the ban. Bush has said he would sign such a bill if Congress passed it. "If the President asked me, it'd still be no . . . because we don't have the votes to pass an assault-weapons ban, and it will expire Monday and that's that," House Majority Leader Tom DeLay (R., Texas) said later. DeLay called the ban "a feel-good piece of legislation" that he said did nothing to keep weapons out of the hands of criminals. Appearing at a news conference, police chiefs from the District of Columbia, Los Angeles, Atlanta and Seattle predicted an increase in violent gun crimes if the ban expired. "Our streets, our homes, our citizens, and our police officers will face great danger unless the federal ban on assault weapons is renewed," said Charles H. Ramsey, police chief in the nation's capital.

1540.text: GUN-10785 PRIMARY Lower age for concealed carry Nixon signs bill setting minimum age at 21; it had been 23. JEFFERSON CITY - Missouri Gov. Jay Nixon has signed legislation lowering the minimum age for obtaining a concealed weapon permit to 21. The minimum age had been 23 - said by the National Rifle Association to be the highest in the country - since Missouri adopted its concealed weapons law in 2003. The legislation signed Friday also revises one of the training requirements for getting a concealed gun permit. Those seeking permits will have to shoot 50 rounds each with a revolver and a semiautomatic pistol instead of 50 rounds with any handgun. Nixon also vetoed two measures Friday. One would have barred local governments from prohibiting billboards, although they could still have set rules on the size, height, lighting and spacing of the signs. The governor said in a letter the measure would have limited efforts by local officials to preserve the character of their communities. Nixon also vetoed a wide-ranging bill dealing with vacancies in state offices, local elections and Missouri's presidential primary. In a message explaining the veto, he cited language requiring special elections to fill vacancies in elected statewide offices rather than having the governor fill them. He also objected to a section on local elections that he says could have cancelled municipal elections and barred write-in candidates in hundreds of smaller communities. The bill would also have delayed Missouri's presidential primary. Nixon says he supports a later primary, but his veto of the entire bill tossed that portion out, too.

1542.text: GUN-6069 PRIMARY LETTERS, FAXES & E-MAIL Guns as pacifiers The May 14 "Colorado Voices" column ("Gun control isn't the answer to reducing violence" by J.B. Churchill) nicely pointed out the crux of the gun issue: male Rambo urges in a minority but free-spending NRA-led male population. This article touted that the answer to reducing gun carnage is for everyone, especially teachers, to all carry weapons. I'm picturing this gun tit-for-tat society: American towns and cities begin to look like Mogadishu or some of those hapless Soviet ex-republics where life is cheap and miserable, but males of all ages swagger around with automatic rifles and ammunition belts slung over their shoulders. It's time for this minority of males to let go of the gun pacifier for the benefit of the greater good. ROLAND DUPREE Littleton   Not absolute All efforts to discuss controlling handguns are derailed into discussions of the Second Amendment's prohibition against infringing the right to bear arms. That prohibition is not now, and never was, considered absolute. Arms are not equivalent to handguns. Arms include rocks, sharp sticks, bows and arrows, hatchets, crossbows, smoothbore muskets, muzzle-loading rifles, bolt and lever action rifles, automatic rifles, revolvers, automatic handguns, assault pistols, assault rifles, light and heavy machine-guns, bazookas, rocket launchers, mortars, light artillery, heavy artillery, ship-mounted artillery, conventional explosive bombs, tactical-nuclear weapons and strategic-nuclear weapons. It is evident that we already have prohibitions against private ownership of most arms above a certain threshold on this continuum. The only subject under discussion is where that threshold is to be set.

1539.text: GUN-10220 PRIMARY GUN CONTROL NOT IN 'CULTURE OF LIFE' CRITICS QUESTION BUSH'S RESPONSE TO TWO HIGH-PROFILE NEWS EVENTS WASHINGTON -- Does the "culture of life" extend to the victims of gun violence? That's the question critics are pointedly asking after President Bush's contrasting responses to the two events dominating national attention this week. While Bush made a special trip back to Washington from vacation to sign legislation offering a new federal right of appeal to Terri Schiavo, the brain-damaged Florida woman, the president and his aides have said almost nothing about the mass shooting in Red Lake, Minn. -- the most deadly outbreak of school violence since the 1999 massacre in Columbine, Colo. The Minnesota tragedy has increased alarm among some school safety professionals about Bush's efforts to eliminate federal funding for two major programs meant to prevent classroom violence, including a Clinton administration initiative to help schools hire more police officers. "It makes absolutely no sense that at a time when we are talking about better protecting bridges, monuments, dams and even the hallways of Congress, that we are going backward in protecting the hallways of our schools," said Kenneth Trump, president of National School Safety and Security Services, an independent consulting firm. Bush's responses to the Schiavo case and the school shootings track with the preferences of two of his core constituencies. Conservative Christians pressed Bush to intervene for Schiavo, while the National Rifle Association and other gun-owner groups generally look to minimize the relevance of political responses to mass shootings.

1537.text: GUN-31544 PRIMARY Appeals court upholds ban on guns at Atlanta airport; Firearm-rights backer pledges fight will go on A federal appeals court on Thursday ruled licensed firearms owners may not carry guns into parts of Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport. GeorgiaCarry.org, a gun rights group, and state Rep. Timothy Bearden (R-Villa Rica) challenged the airport's ban on guns. They contended a law enacted last year overrode a long-standing policy prohibiting airport visitors from carrying guns in non-secure areas. The law, which took effect in July, allows owners of licensed firearms to carry guns on public transportation and in parks and restaurants. In September, Senior U.S. District Judge Marvin Shoob threw out the suit. Shoob said public transit systems are not the same as airports and airline passengers are not allowed to carry firearms onboard. On Thursday, the 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals agreed with Shoob and called arguments raised by GeorgiaCarry.org and Bearden "meritless." Airport general manager Ben DeCosta, who declared the airport a "gun-free" zone, applauded the ruling. "We are pleased ... that Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport will continue to remain a safe, secure, gun-free environment for its 90 million passengers a year," he said. In a statement, Jonathan Lowy of the Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence called the ruling "right on the law and sound policy." Bearden expressed disappointment. "I know what the legislative intent was," he said, noting he was the author of the legislation. "We will either continue to handle this through the judiciary process ... or we could just go ahead and fix this legislatively and spell it out."

characters: Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence

1538.text: GUN-10094 PRIMARY Courting a new audience on gun case High school students and members of the public can witness a potentially precedent-setting case on Dec. 7 before the Colorado Supreme Court. That day, the seven justices will hold court at Arapahoe High School, as part of the courts-in-the-community program, to hear arguments on the city of Denver's right to adopt stricter gun laws than the rest of the state. Assistant City Attorney David Broadwell will base his argument on the city's home-rule authority. The case is being closely watched by other metro cities whose gun-control ordinances, like Denver's, were wiped off the books by a 2003 law. Denver sued the state after Gov. Bill Owens signed the controversial law pre-empting gun-control laws that go beyond state or federal controls. The state law, for instance, tossed aside ordinances banning assault weapons and open carrying of firearms. A district court judge last year ruled that Denver could continue to regulate assault weapons, handguns known as "Saturday night specials" and the open carrying of guns. The state appealed. Broadwell said that since the state law passed, there have been several incidents in Denver that should have involved arrests. But Denver police are holding off enforcement pending the conclusion of the case. When a man paraded around the City and County Building sporting a loaded gun and holster, police simply monitored, he said. "Once the court rules, we should know the extent of our municipal power," Broadwell said. The court could issue a ruling by early 2006.

1541.text: GUN-7845 PRIMARY Governor's name to lead off gun-control petition Gov. Bill Owens will be the first one to sign up to 'close  the gun-show loophole.' On Wednesday he's kicking off SAFE Colorado's petition drive  to require background checks at gun shows. His signature will be the first of more than 62,000 that the  gun-control group will need to get the measure on the November  ballot. He is to be joined by Attorney General Ken Salazar on the  west steps of the Capitol at the 10 a.m. event. The National Rifle Association and others are challenging the  petition before the state Supreme Court, but SAFE has decided to  go ahead and collect signatures. If the Supreme Court rejects  SAFE's petition, any signatures gathered up to that point will be  invalid. SAFE, which stands for Sane Alternatives to the Firearms  Epidemic, decided to run the petition after the Legislature  refused to require background checks on private sales at gun shows. Instant criminal background checks already are required on  gun purchases at stores. Licensed dealers who sell at gun shows  also are required to do such checks. But many vendors at gun shows don't have to do background  checks because they're not licensed dealers. They're considered  private sellers offering their personal collection for sale. Gun-control supporters say that 'loophole' makes gun shows a  great place for criminals and children to get guns. Gun-rights  supporters say the government should stay out of private sales.

1544.text: NA-42944 PRIMARY The Gun Report: May 8, 2014 The attack on prospective smart-gun purveyors continues. In February, The Washington Post wrote about how the Oak Tree Gun Club in southern California was stocking the country's first smart gun, the Armatix iP1, which only fires if it is within range of a wristwatch worn by the gun's owner. Touted as "the iPhone of guns," the technology is intended to reduce gun violence. Three weeks later, Oak Tree, which one of the largest gun stores and shooting ranges­ in the state, pulled the gun from its shelves after the article unleashed a tidal wave of backlash from the gun-rights crowd. They fear that the introduction of the iP1 would trigger a New Jersey law mandating that all handguns in the state be personalized within three years of a smart gun going on sale anywhere in the country, thus infringing on their right to bear arms. Oddly, the store then denied ever offering the gun, despite evidence to the contrary. A couple of months later, a gun store owner in Rockville, Md., decided that he would be the first in the U.S. to offer the new technology. Andy Raymond, the owner of Engage Armament, said he was willing to risk the backlash because the state, with its strict gun-control laws, "has already essentially put us out of business." He planned to sell the German-made guns, he said, because he believed in the right of gun ownership: "You have freedom. It shouldn't be compromised."

1547.text: GUN-6380 PRIMARY Following The Guns One was built in Miami, another crafted by an Ohio family business; the final two were too old to trace. On April 20, all four were used by Eric Harris and Dylan Klebold Dylan Klebold walked into his high school cafeteria carrying an assault pistol originally designed as a submachine gun for the white South African government. Eric Harris wielded a black semiautomatic rifle produced by an Ohio company whose owner says he just wanted to make guns for the average working man. The two Columbine High seniors also had a couple of old shotguns that could have come from any hunter's closet - except both had been sawed off, making them concealable and able to spray ammunition over a wider area. At lunchtime on April 20, they opened fire. In the next hour, they killed or wounded 35 people and reignited the national debate over the manufacture, distribution and sporadically regulated trade of guns in America. To examine that trade, The Denver Post followed the trail of the guns used in the worst school shooting in U.S. history. The journey led from a European gun designer in South Africa to a handgun seller whose mother belongs to Handgun Control Inc., from a mom-and-pop factory that made its first rifles in 1996 to the Longmont gun shop of a retired police sergeant, from the 1994 assault-weapons ban to the 1999 assault-weapons market. Along the way, parents, police officers, gun makers and firearms dealers continue to wrestle with the gun-control questions Columbine spawned. Should all gun sales be regulated? Why can juveniles buy semiautomatic rifles? In a nation with enough guns to arm almost every man, woman and child, could any set of laws prevent such massacres?

1543.text: GUN-7009 PRIMARY Gun control fades as campaign issue Thousands of Tennesseans visit the weekend gun show that Dave Goodman stages at the state fairgrounds here each month, and the anger that many express over Al Gore's gun policies is helping put a political safety lock on the issue in this year's presidential campaign. "Angry? I'm damn angry," added Goodman. "They're trying to turn a right into a privilege. They're trying to stigmatize law-abiding Americans. And they're trying to put us out of business." Goodman holds up to 80 gun shows a year, mostly in Tennessee, Kentucky, Ohio, Indiana and Michigan. And everywhere he goes gun owners are "fed up" with the Clinton-Gore administration, Goodman said. That kind of anger is having an unexpected effect on the gun control debate, which was supposed to favor Democrats this fall --- especially Vice President Gore's expected race against Texas Gov. George W. Bush. During the Democratic primaries, Gore's anti-gun rhetoric was directed at Bush rather than his challenger, former Sen. Bill Bradley of New Jersey, who advocated registration of all firearms. But since securing the nomination, Gore has said little about Bush's position on guns. That suggests that gun control, once a centerpiece of Gore's campaign strategy against Bush, may not emerge as a defining issue this fall. As public opinion polls show Americans increasingly skeptical of the need for more gun laws, Gore and Democratic congressional candidates are beginning to speak of gun "safety" rather than gun "control."

1549.text: GUN-6170 PRIMARY The Nation; The Face of Guns: Who Owns Them and Why THERE are about 240 million guns in America, according to Federal estimates, a total that gives the country one of the highest per-capita rates of gun ownership in the world. For gun-rights proponents, the large numbers of guns are a reflection of a fundamental freedom and a sign of national strength. Gun-control advocates view the statistic as a cause for national shame, the easy availability a factor in the 750,000 deaths by firearms since 1960 and in violent episodes like the killings earlier this year at Columbine High School in Colorado and a Jewish community center in Los Angeles. The debate over gun rights continues in Congress, in statehouses and in the Federal courts, where a Texas case may well wind its way to the Supreme Court as a landmark test of whether the Second Amendment to the Constitution specifically gives individuals, and not just state militias, the right to bear arms. But the fight over the guns, the numbers and the laws often overlooks some surprisingly basic questions: Who owns all these guns, and why? Some criminals, of course, steal guns or buy them illegally with the intent to intimidate or to kill. This gallery of portraits, assembled by Sarah Weissman, picture editor of the Week in Review, reflects gun owners (all those pictured say their guns are legal) who offer a range of other reasons for owning a weapon: for sport, for hunting, for collecting, for self-protection (against rattlesnakes as well as human intruders), for sentimental reasons and as a financial investment.

1545.text: NA-41221 PRIMARY Congress shoots blanks After the murder of 20 first graders - and no federal action to curb ready access to firearms, even by the deranged - what's another dozen victims cut down in cold blood a stone's throw from the Capitol? That, apparently, is the attitude of Congress, which - despite overwhelming popular support for tightening background checks, building a nationwide database of deranged people prohibited from buying firearms and banning high-capacity magazines - refuses to inconvenience anyone who wants to get his hands on a weapon. "We don't have the votes," came the feeble explanation Tuesday from Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid. More relevant is why the votes aren't there - namely, because of the outsized influence of one deep-pocketed lobby, so dominant that Republicans and even some Democrats dare not cross it. Many are the lessons yet to be learned from Aaron Alexis' Navy Yard rampage. But there are already plenty that just might make it a little harder for the next maniac to shoot up a workplace or school or movie theater or house of worship. Or, for that matter, make it a little harder for the next thug to cut someone down on the streets of Brownsville or the South Bronx. Alexis had a long, documented history of insanity. He had been treated by the Veterans Administration for a slew of psychiatric problems, including paranoia and a sleep disorder.

1546.text: GUN-1791 PRIMARY CHRONICLE DEWEY STOKES, a 50-year-old police officer from Columbus, Ohio, and a vocal supporter of gun control, was re-elected president of the National Fraternal Order of Police, the nation's largest police organization. The election took place at the group's 50th convention, which ended yesterday in Pittsburgh. JAMES BRADY, the press secretary who was shot in the head in the 1981 assassination attempt on President RONALD REAGAN, spoke at the convention, which is held every two years. He praised Mr. Stokes and the 225,000-member organization for their support of the gun-control measure bearing Mr. Brady's name. "Without your lobbying, it would not have passed," Mr. Brady said on Wednesday. The measure, which would require a seven-day waiting period before a handgun may be bought, has passed the House of Representatives. A Senate version calls for a five-day waiting period. Mr. Stokes, a 23-year police veteran, criticized the National Rifle Association for what he described as its attempt to influence the vote against him, saying "no outside force should be involved in politics here."

1550.text: GUN-1276 PRIMARY A Right to Machine Guns? THE SUPREME Court in the past has found no constitutional bar to legislation requiring the registration of handguns or prohibiting the sale of firearms to convicted felons, the mentally incapacitated and other groups thought to be especially dangerous when armed. But when Congress prohibited future private ownership of machine guns in 1986, the National Rifle Association thought it had a stronger case to bring before the justices. That law, says the NRA, is the first ban in American history on firearms possession by law-abiding citizens, and because it is such a sweeping restriction, it violates the Second Amendment. Yesterday the Supreme Court declined to consider that argument. It's not easy to get any kind of gun control legislation through Congress -- or through most state legislatures, for that matter -- but private ownership of machine guns was hard to defend. No one uses them for hunting, for target practice or for protecting home and family, and not even the NRA could convince lawmakers that every American has an inalienable right to keep this kind of artillery in the hall closet. So while gun control advocates suffered a number of defeats during the debate on the 1986 law, they were able to get the machine gun prohibition accepted on a voice vote. It is an indication of the self-confidence of the gun lobby that even this concession to good sense and nonviolence was challenged in the courts.

1548.text: GUN-4771 PRIMARY States' rights at forefront of gun law debate Supreme Court hears Brady Act arguments WASHINGTON - The oral arguments yesterday were over the Brady handgun law, but the Supreme Court quickly moved to broader questions of how Congress treats the states. Justice Antonin Scalia dismissed the Clinton administration's defense of the Brady Act, which forces sheriffs to check the backgrounds of would-be gun buyers, saying the rationale would make states "dance like marionettes on the fingers of the federal government." But Justice Stephen Breyer questioned why it was not "more respectful" of states' rights for Congress to have imposed "minor reporting duties" on the states rather than a big bureaucratic gun-control system. And Justice Anthony Kennedy, a potential swing vote in this dispute, observed that each level of government makes policy choices basedon costs. "If Congress makes a choice," he said, "it pays for it." In short, the session brought to the fore the enduring but delicate balance between federal and state powers - a true constitutional debate in the justices' white marble and burgundy-draped courtroom. A decision in the combined cases of Printz vs. United States and Mack vs. United States is expected by next summer. The law, named for James Brady, the former press secretary who was disabled in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan, requires gun dealers to give the names of potential buyers to the sheriff or other chief law enforcement officer in the community. The sheriff has five days to check crime records and inform the dealer if the buyer is a convicted felon or otherwise barred from buying a handgun.

1553.text: GUN-319 PRIMARY FOLLOW-UP ON THE NEWS; Outlawing Pistols When the Board of Village Trustees in Morton Grove, Ill., voted last June 8 to ban both the sale and individual possession of handguns, officials of the Chicago suburb said they expected a constitutional battle with the National Rifle Association. ''A lawsuit was filed the very next day to enjoin the village from enforcing that law,'' notes Morton Grove's Corporation Counsel, Martin Ashman. The plaintiff, Victor D. Quilici, a lawyer who lives in the village, charges that the ordinances violate the Federal and State Constitutions. Both the National Rifle Association in Washington, D.C., and the Second Amendment Foundation in Bellevue, Wash., are helping to pay for the lawsuit, Mr. Quilici says. Last week five more Morton Grove citizens sued on the same grounds, supported by the Second Amendment Foundation. All cases are before the Federal District Court in Chicago. Meanwhile residents may still sell and own a pistol in Morton Grove. The bans do not take effect until Sept. 6. And even when they become law, the Corporation Counsel notes, they allow residents to store handguns in any licensed gun club in town.

1555.text: GUN-10949 PRIMARY Dayton opposes ending gun permits Gov. Mark Dayton says he doesn't want to get rid of state permits that provide "reasonable" background checks for gun buyers. Dayton took his first public position Thursday on a Republican move to repeal state gun permits. He made the comments in a speech to a newspaper convention in Bloomington. A House panel approved legislation Wednesday that would abolish Minnesota's permits for gun buyers. Supporters say the state requirement duplicates federal laws. The Democratic governor said the background checks required for the state permits balance gun rights with public safety. He said current law doesn't prevent law-abiding citizens from owning guns. -- Associated Press

1557.text: GUN-9177 PRIMARY U.S. DIGEST PHILADELPHIA Police chiefs group seeks to keep assault rifle ban A group of police chiefs from across the nation called on Congress Wednesday to renew the federal law outlawing military-style assault weapons. The 10-year-old ban expires next September, and groups including the powerful National Rifle Association have been trying to persuade Congress to let the law die. "The idea that 10 years later that we're even debating (it) is insanity," said William Bratton, now police chief in Los Angeles and former police commissioner in New York. Bratton was one of five big-city chiefs who addressed the subject in Philadelphia, where 15,000 law-enforcement officials from around the world are gathering for the annual conference of the International Association of Chiefs of Police. HAMILTON, Wash. Residents return home as floodwater recedes Residents returned home as rain-swollen rivers started to drop below flood stage Wednesday, but were told to boil water and wear gloves to protect themselves from contaminated floodwaters. Only two of the 10 flooded rivers in western Washington were still over flood stage Wednesday. The Skagit and Skokomish rivers were expected to recede later this week. Determined sandbagging helped limit damage in Mount Vernon as the Skagit River crested at 36.2 feet, more than 8 feet above flood stage. "It was like a carnival" Tuesday as townspeople erected sandbag walls along the river, said Leon Torrey, whose restaurant was sheltered by sandbags at the back door. "There were thousands of people out here."

1556.text: GUN-80 PRIMARY PLASTIC, X-RAY EVADING GUNS BANNED BY HOUSE The House approved Thursday a ban on firearms capable of evading detection by X-ray or metal detection devices. "You can just imagine the consequences if terrorists or other criminals could board airplanes or walk into courtrooms or prisons carrying firearms that can escape detection," said Rep. William Hughes, D-N.J., as the bill won approval on a voice vote. The bill, which would outlaw the manufacture, import, sale or possession of so-called plastic guns, goes to the Senate. A similar measure is part of the major drug bill in a House-Senate conference committee, but press secretary Mark Brown said Hughes wanted to keep a separate version moving "simply to keep both irons in the fire." Violators would be subject to $250,000 in fines for individuals and $1 million for organizations. The bill also calls for prison terms of up to 5 years for violations. The measure would not affect existing firearms because they do not fall below the minimum levels of detection devices. Exemptions for the military and intelligence agencies would be provided by the legislation. The White House has indicated that President Reagan will sign it. The bill is aimed at preventing terrorists and others from using plastic weapons to evade detection by metal detectors or X-ray machines at airports and public buildings. Such weapons are expected to be feasible soon. Hughes' bill prohibits the manufacture, importation, sale, shipment, delivery, possession or transfer of any firearm that, after removal of parts and magazines, cannot be detected by a metal detector calibrated to discover a weapon with 3.7 ounces of stainless steel.

1552.text: GUN-3570 PRIMARY HEALTH WORKERS ORGANIZE AGAINST GUNS HOSPITAL ACTIVISTS: EPIDEMIC OF VIOLENCE SPURS PROFESSIONALS INTO ACTION. They have silently watched patients rushed into emergency rooms, splattered in blood. They have silently watched them die on operating tables, or be rolled out in wheelchairs. But as the epidemic of violence keeps growing, physicians and health workers are increasingly supplementing their stethoscopes with placards, and their scalpels with calls to representatives in Congress. To critics - like the powerful National Rifle Association - who say they should stick to anatomy, the physicians defend their growing involvement in public policy: "Doctors," said Jerome Kassirer, editor of the prestigious New England Journal of Medicine, "have to deal with the consequences of the violence associated with handguns." HELP organization Jerome Paulson, a primary care pediatrician at George Washington University Hospital here, added, "I don't think the criminal justice system is going to solve the problem." This week, Kassirer, Paulson and other public health experts met here to try to put handgun violence on the national political agenda. The conference was organized by the Handgun Epidemic Lowering Plan (HELP), a national group of public health workers. Just as the best way of fighting diphtheria or whooping cough is by preventing the diseases from happening, Paulson said, doctors must try to prevent gun violence. And they may be ideally placed to intervene in the area where such violence seems to be growing fastest: Among young people. "The kid who is a victim today is very likely to be a perpetrator tomorrow," said Mary Ann Fenley, communications officer at the National Center for Injury Prevention and Control at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in Atlanta.

1554.text: GUN-11276 PRIMARY Va. expected to lift gun limit Virginia is poised to lift a 19-year-old limit on handgun purchases, with the Republican-controlled state Senate expected to do away with the one-gun-per-month cap in a final vote Friday. With the purchase limit likely headed for extinction, Richmond appears to have grown friendlier to gun rights since _blankRepublicans took control of the evenly divided Senatelast month, pro-gun and gun-control activists agree. This week, the Senate passed a bill prohibiting localities from requiring that people seeking permits for concealed handguns submit fingerprints as part of their applications. The House passed a bill allowing government employees to store guns and ammunition in personal cars parked in workplace lots, including those at child-care centers and parks. That is not to say the gun lobby will get everything it wants from this _blankGeneral Assembly session, even with the GOP in control of the House, Senate and governor's mansion. A Senate committee has killed two high-profile gun-rights proposals that would have done away with state background checks and prevented colleges from banning firearms on campus. "It's going pretty well," said Philip Van Cleave, president of the Virginia Citizens Defense League. "It's by no means perfect. There are some good bills that aren't making it, but there are good bills that are. And I think we're seeing much more progress than we've seen the last several years." Lori Haas, a gun-control activist whose daughter, Emily, was injured in the 2007 mass _blankshooting at Virginia Tech, said she thinks the General Assembly is moving in the wrong direction this year.

1558.text: GUN-7462 PRIMARY REGISTERING ALL GUNS IS NO ANTIDOTE TO CRIME Nancy Hwa, spokeswoman for Handgun Control Inc., must be wearing extra-strong rose colored glasses if she thinks, (quoting from Monday's front-page article "Owners fret over licensing of guns") that "(Licensing and registration) would make sure the wrong people don't get hold of guns." The majority of guns used in criminal activity are stolen and have been sold or swapped on the streets numerous times without benefit of licensing, registration or background checks. I have been a victim of residential burglary and, as a responsible, law-abiding gun owner, was able to furnish serial numbers for identification when and if it is ever found. Licensing my gun would not have prevented this theft, nor will it assist in finding the weapon. Child-safety locks are a great tool for parents, but this should be the decision of the gun owner and not used to cripple a homeowner's right to defend himself or herself. We have adequate gun laws. Deter gun thefts and crimes with stronger court sentencing. BARBARA LEE SCHURTER Royal Palm Beach

1559.text: GUN-4620 PRIMARY GUNS IN LAW-ABIDING HANDS CUT CRIME I must respond to the letter"Only a gun ban can stop this mayhem," although I am not a member of the National Rifle Association or any other pro-gun group. I believe only the facts, and here are some rarely seen in the media. From the FBI statistics: Since Florida's concealed weapons law was passed, the homicide rate has fallen 22 percent while the national average rose 14 percent. Of the 258,000 Floridians who have received permits to carry concealed weapons, only 18 have used their guns to commit a crime, a minuscule percentage. In 1976, Georgia and Wisconsin tried different approaches to fighting crime. Georgia enacted laws making it easier for citizens to carry guns for self-defense, while Wisconsin opted for a 48-hour waiting period before the purchase of a handgun. During the ensuing years, Georgia's homicide rate dropped by 21 percent while Wisconsin's rose by 33 percent. Ernest W. Bergeron Royal Palm Beach

1560.text: GUN-5135 PRIMARY PROPOSAL TO SLICE UP SCHOOL DISTRICTS DIES Gun control and Cabinet reform proposals are headed for next fall's ballot as constitutional amendments, but voters will not get the chance to break up big school districts like Palm Beach County's. In the most decisive day of a once-every-20-year round of meetings, the Constitution Revision Commission voted 24-11 Tuesday to let voters decide whether counties should regulate gun shows. That angered the National Rifle Association's national president in her own hometown. ''I live here,'' NRA President Marian Hammer said. '' My kids live here. My grandkids live her. Any time some politician wants to take away the rights of law-abiding gun owners, you bet it's personal.'' The measure would allow counties to impose a three-to-five day waiting period and require background checks at gun shows. Because of a loophole in existing law, such shows are virtually unregulated now, amendment supporters said. It was one of 36 measures that passed out of a field of 45 proposals the commission considered Tuesday. They will be combined into perhaps fewer than a dozen ballot proposals in a final commission meeting next week. During the ''grouping'' votes, the gun-control measure could still be defeated - and Hammer vowed to try to do so. Voters will decide in November whether to put the proposals into Florida's Constitution. One of the proposals that failed was a provision to break up big counties into several school districts. South Bay mayor Clarence Anthony appealed to fellow commission members that it would hurt less affluent areas such as his own and possibly reinforce segregation.

1562.text: GUN-8620 PRIMARY GUN PROFICIENCY TEST REQUIREMENT ADVANCES One of the most significant gun-control measures of the year in California, a bill requiring new handgun owners to pass a proficiency test cleared a state Senate committee Tuesday despite opposition from the gun lobby. The bill by Assemblyman Kevin Shelley, a San Francisco Democrat, moved out of the Senate Public Safety Committee on a 5-1 vote. It was contested by the National Rifle Association, which argued the test would discriminate against non-English speakers and subject prospective gun buyers to extra obstacles in order to buy a gun. Under the bill, new handgun buyers would have to attend a two- to four-hour course on handgun law and safety, then take a written test and, finally, fire a pistol in the presence of a Department of Justice official.

1551.text: NA-42073 PRIMARY Highlights from the California Legislature's final week Here are just a few of the controversial or noteworthy bills that did or didn't reach Gov. Jerry Brown's desk this session. PASSED Gun bills SB299 by Sen. Mark DeSaulnier, D-Concord -- would require gun owners to report a gun theft or loss to police within seven days of knowing about it SB374 by Senate President Pro Tem Darrell Steinberg, D-Sacramento -- would add all semi-automatic rifles with detachable magazines to the state's list of banned assault weapons SB475 by Sen. Mark Leno, D-San Francisco -- would essentially ban gun shows at the Cow Palace by requiring approval from San Francisco and San Mateo supervisors for such shows SB567 by Sen. Hannah Beth Jackson, D-Santa Barbara -- would update the definition of an illegal shotgun to include a shotgun with a revolving cylinder and a rifled bore SB683 by Sen. Marty Block, D-San Diego -- would require owners of long guns to earn safety certificates like those already required of handgun owners SB755 by Sen. Lois Wolk, D-Vacaville -- would expand list of convicts who can't legally own guns to include those with multiple drug or alcohol crimes, street gang members and others AB48 by Assemblywoman Nancy Skinner, D-Berkeley -- would ban conversion kits that allow people to turn regular magazines into high-capacity magazines AB169 by Assemblyman Roger Dickinson, D-Sacramento -- would tighten exemptions to the law prohibiting purchase of handguns that haven't been tested and deemed safe by the state

characters: Senate President Pro Tem Darrell Steinberg

1564.text: GUN-8227 PRIMARY Million Mom March modeled after MADD A year ago, a New Jersey stay-at-home mother was transfixed in  horror by what she saw on her television screen. Heavily armed  police were leading a daisy chain of tiny children out of a Los  Angeles Jewish Community Center preschool where a gunman had just  opened fire. Donna Dees Thomas' daughters went to preschool. They even  went to a Jewish Community Center preschool. It could have been  them. The next day, Thomas began working to launch a march on  Washington demanding stricter gun control in this country. It  would be on Mother's Day. It would be called the Million Mom March. 'What has this world become when we need security for  children to finger paint?' she was quoted as saying. In San Francisco, a professional organizer of non-profit  groups named Andrew McGuire already was working on the same cause.   In 1998, he had been given $ 4.5 million to create a national  grassroots organization for gun control. 'They said we need a MADD for guns,' McGuire said. McGuire knew a little something about the success of Mothers  Against Drunk Drivers. He was one of its original board members.   He saw public opinion about drunk driving change. He saw laws get  changed. McGuire's new foundation was to be called the Bell Campaign,  to symbolize the tolling of a bell for every person lost to gun  violence. The organization was to kick off in late May of 1999.

1561.text: GUN-10907 PRIMARY WHY DO WE KEEP ARMING KILLERS? SHOOT DOWN EXTRA-AMMO CLIP Every country has crazy people, but few let them buy guns and ammo as easily as ours. Jared Loughner was trying to reload after killing six people in January when three heroes jumped him and 61-year-old Patricia Maisch grabbed the high-capacity bullet clip he'd dropped. Loughner had already squeezed off 30 shots in 15 seconds - also wounding 14 people, including Arizona Rep. Gabrielle Giffords - and that second magazine would have given him 30 more shots. God only knows what the death toll would have been if he'd been able to reload. Guess what? That extra-ammunition clip is perfectly legal. "It took us five years to pass the Brady Bill and another year to get the automatic weapons and high-capacity magazine ban in 1994," says Richard Aborn, the former Manhattan prosecutor who ran the pro-gun control Brady organization and now heads the Citizens Crime Commission. "But it had a 10-year 'sunset' provision, and President Bush and the Republican Congress let the ban expire in 2004." Since then, nearly every single mass murderer in America has used these clips, which can hold as many as 100 bullets, to help them kill: 32 dead at Virginia Tech; 13 people slaughtered at Ft. Hood; 8 killed at a Hartford, Conn., beer distributorship; 13 slain in Binghamton. "This is not about Republicans or Democrats," Aborn says. "It's about cops, and how we're going to protect them, and in turn, ourselves. There is no purpose in having these magazines in civilian hands."

1567.text: GUN-10704 PRIMARY Walker OK's rules for concealed guns Gov. Scott Walker on Friday approved the state Justice Department's rules for implementing Wisconsin's new concealed-weapons law despite the National Rifle Association's concerns that they go too far. The rules mandate that anyone who applies for a permit to carry a concealed weapon must undergo at least four hours of training. They also require that an instructor sign a completion certificate. The NRA contends the concealed-carry law doesn't require minimum training standards and doesn't give the Justice Department the right to dictate a certificate's format. The Justice Department drafted the rules on an emergency basis because the law goes into effect Nov. 1. Walker said he had no choice but to approve the rules in such a short time frame but hopes DOJ will improve the permanent rules substantially. -- Associated Press

1563.text: GUN-3128 PRIMARY MILITIA GROUPS ON RISE IN STATE; LEADERS SAY FOCUS IS RIGHTS, NOT BIGOTRY Their basic weapons are the rifle and the computer. They fear that the federal government is trying to curb their right to own guns. And some worry we are headed for global tyranny and one-world rule. They welcome all races and creeds. But some militia watchers fear these groups could end up under the influence of racist, anti-Semitic survivalists. They are the Missouri Militia - part of a growing nationwide movement that has two meetings planned for this week in the St. Louis area. The state has at least four groups: the 1st Missouri Volunteers in St. Louis and St. Louis County; the 51st Missouri, in Kansas City, and the Northern and Southern Missouri Regional Militia. Organizations that track the militia trend say the movement in this area is new, and little information exists about its leadership and practices. The militia watchers say the movement began with people concerned about government efforts to control gun ownership and to relax search-and-seizure laws. The groups also fear that the government, by  force at times, wants to strip away constitutional rights. Militia members often cite two examples. The first is the siege in 1993 at Waco, Texas, atDavid Koresh's cult compound, in which more than 70 people were killed. The other is the shootout in 1992 in Idaho between federal authorities and white supremacist Randy Weaver. A federal marshal, Weaver's wife and his 13-year-old son were killed. Who The Militiamen Are

1565.text: GUN-2592 PRIMARY LETTERS FROM THE PEOPLE Last week, as the Senate debated new anti-crime legislation, Sen. Orrin Hatch addressed his colleagues. His message, or rather warning: A ban on assault weapons will kill the proposed anti-crime bill. Hatch and others sympathetic to the gun lobby believe gun control is tantamount to an infringement of citizens' constitutional rights.   Implicit in this thinking is the belief that the right to bear arms is absolute. But is it? Constitutional rights have limits. Few, for instance, argue that the First Amendment's guarantee of free speech is unqualified. Most of us, no doubt Hatch too, understand the necessity of limiting our speech rights. Laws prohibiting "harmful" speech like libel and misleading advertising meet little opposition. If we can ban fighting words, why then can we not ban assault weapons? As with speech, we must balance the benefit of limiting the exercise of the constitutional right with the harm such a restriction would impose. In the case of assault weapons, the scale tips heavily toward a ban. While I hesitate to create a hierarchy of constitutional rights, I realize such a hierarchy may be necessary. Neither the National Rifle Association nor Hatch, however, has explained why the Second Amendment is more important than the First. Hatch's stance is constitutionally inconsistent. Further, his warning sends the nation a strange, if unintended message: We need to be tough on crime, but not too tough.   Marc J. Boxerman Creve Coeur The Great Debate?

1568.text: GUN-5755 PRIMARY GUNS AND SCHOOLS: THE OVERVIEW; GUN-CONTROL BILL REJECTED IN HOUSE IN BIPARTISAN VOTE The House concluded a bitter, wide-ranging debate today over the seeds of violence among American children by rejecting, 280 to 147, a package of new restrictions on firearms that was significantly weaker than legislation approved by the Senate last month. An odd coalition of liberal Democrats, moderate Republicans and conservative opponents of gun control in both parties banded together to defeat the House measures, which struck many lawmakers as too little and many others as too much. In the end, 82 Republicans joined with 197 Democrats and 1 independent in opposition to the legislation, while only 10 Democrats sided with 137 Republicans in favor of it. That outcome seriously clouded the prospects for the passage of modest new gun-control measures in Congress this year and brought to a close -- at least for now -- the long, intense weeks of political wrangling and legislative soul-searching touched off by the shootings at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo., on April 20. A House juvenile-crime bill that was passed on Thursday, before the companion legislation on gun control was considered, will proceed to a conference committee to be reconciled with a Senate juvenile-crime bill that includes modest new restrictions on firearms. But that could be many weeks away, and the absence of gun control in the House version makes it unclear how much, if any, of the Senate measures will survive. House Democrats vowed to push their cause in other ways and perhaps use parliamentary maneuvers to bring back to the House floor stronger gun-control provisions than the ones they opposed today.

1566.text: GUN-11499 PRIMARY Letters to the Editor Why assault weapons available Why is a 24-year-old graduate student allowed to build up an arsenal of assault weapons? I expect our military and our police to have such weapons, but I see no reason that every random person should be allowed to purchase them. We have the National Rifle Association to thank for making military-style weapons so easily available, as well as our elected officials who don't speak out about this. Thank you, E.J. Dionne, for saying so articulately what I feel ("A debate gun lobbyists don't want," Sunday). I am so saddened for the families of the victims in Colorado, as well as the victims of all the other needless massacres that take place in our country more and more frequently. My heart breaks for those lives cut short in such a horrible way. Julie Vick, Haddonfield Ignore gun-control lobby There is another act of violence, and the gun-control lobby and its allies in the media are predictably complaining about the availability of guns. It's time we stop listening to them. In 1993, the gun-control lobby swore that the Brady Law would "keep guns out of the wrong hands." It has been a total failure. In 2004, the gun-control lobby swore that the expiration of the assault-weapon ban would result in a massive increase in violence. Instead, the national homicide rate has declined about 10 percent since then.

1570.text: NA-43709 PRIMARY The path forward on gun violence The human carnage attributable to gun violence in America is undeniable. Our shared experience with the Virginia Tech shootings, the Aurora, Colo., theater massacre and the recent tragedy in Newtown, Conn., have most Americans shouting "Enough!" Perhaps the political inertia that has long stalled intelligent legislation to curb gun violence can finally be overcome. Perhaps the political calculus regarding gun safety laws has at last changed from "How can I possibly vote to support such legislation?" to "How can I not support such legislation?" Since 1994, the conventional wisdom has been that acting to restrict gun rights can put a politician on a fast track to a new career. That's part of the reason why Congress and the Maryland General Assembly allowed their assault weapons bans to expire in 2004 and 2006, respectively, and why weak gun controls are treated like an untouchable third rail in swing states such as Virginia. After the horror of Newtown, however, many people are asking if progress is again possible. One should be careful not to underestimate the power of the opposition. It's true that the National Rifle Association (NRA) poured money into last year's election only to see President Obama - and many other NRA targets - prevail. But rumors of the NRA's demise are greatly exaggerated. It remains a potent force, with strong grass-roots organizations across the country and big campaign coffers that it will continue to use in state and federal elections. Calls for leaders to show "political courage" in the face of this strength are not enough. Unless we create a counterweight, gun rights advocates trying to ride out outrage over Newtown may succeed.

1569.text: GUN-4664 PRIMARY RAMPAGE AT THE EMPIRE STATE BUILDING: THE WEAPON; Calls for Stricter Gun Control Laws Increase in Aftermath of Shooting The chorus of public leaders and private individuals calling for stricter gun control laws after the Empire State Building shootings grew louder yesterday, as Clinton Administration officials began a review of Federal gun control policies and the relatives of a Montclair, N.J., man who was critically injured pleaded for tougher regulations and enforcement. At a brief news conference at Bellevue Hospital Center in Manhattan, Daniel Gross spoke of his brother, the wounded man, Matthew Gross, 27, and of his friend Chris Burmeister, the same age, who was killed in the shooting. Mr. Gross said, "One thing we know Matthew and Chris would want is for us all to help make this country a safer place so that tragedies like this will never happen again. Senior Clinton Administration officials said they were considering such policy changes as new wording on gun sale forms to remind dealers that people who have not been in the United States for at least 90 days are prohibited by Federal regulations from purchasing a firearm. Ali Abu Kamal, the gunman who killed Mr. Burmeister and wounded Mr. Gross and five others atop the Empire State Building on Sunday, did not meet that criterion, but was able to buy a .380 caliber Beretta semiautomatic handgun in Florida. Administration officials also said they were exploring ways to possibly combine data from the Immigration and Naturalization Service with data from the Federal Bureau of Investigation, so that when gun dealers run criminal checks on customers, customers who fail to meet the residency requirement will be flagged.

1571.text: GUN-5126 PRIMARY SCHOOL SHOOTINGS BRING CRY FOR TOUGHER GUN LAWS Gov. Lawton Chiles called for tougher gun laws Wednesday in the wake of the Arkansas school shootings. Those killings ''showed the problem with the argument that guns don't kill people, people do,'' Chiles said. '' Are we going to put these kids in the electric chair? Give them a lethal injection? We've got to do something to get guns out of the hands of young people.'' Chiles wants a law passed this spring to close what he calls a loophole that allows unregulated sales at gun shows. A proposed constitutional amendment headed for the November ballot would give counties the option to do the same thing. Chiles said he supports both measures but does not want to wait until November to act. Though there is no evidence the alleged teen shooters in Arkansas bought the weapons at gun shows, the point is to reduce the unregulated flow of guns that could leave them in kids' hands, Chiles said. Not everyone shared the governor's sense of urgency. ''Before we go overboard, I'd like to see figures on how many people commit crimes with guns they got at gun shows,'' said Sen. Charlie Bronson, R-Indian Harbor Beach. '' I believe in public safety. I just don't think we need to overreact.'' Rep. Les Miller, D-Tampa, whose son was shot less than a year ago, is sponsoring a bill to make a three-day waiting period at gun shows mandatory statewide and allow for background checks at certain shows. Similar restrictions already exist for retail gun sales.

1573.text: GUN-8255 PRIMARY High schoolers favor gun curbs But NRA calls survey flawed WASHINGTON - High school youths overwhelmingly support the  gun control measures Congress has refused to enact, but they also  strongly support the constitutional right of Americans to own  firearms, a new national poll showed Monday. The poll, conducted by Hamilton College students in  cooperation with a major polling company, showed 'almost  universal' support among high school students for handgun  registration, a mandatory five-day waiting period for handgun purchases, background checks for all gun buyers and licensing of  all gun purchasers. It also showed high support for requiring  trigger locks, requiring gun purchasers to pass safety courses and  holding adults responsible for failing to store guns properly if  someone is harmed by their gun. High school attitudes on guns have been the subject of much  attention since the Columbine High School shootings more than a  year ago, but the new survey was said to be the first to provide a  national view of youth attitudes on gun issues. The students' solidarity on those proposals stunned Hamilton  sociology professor Dennis Gilbert, whose students conducted the  poll. Gilbert said he had never seen any age group so united on a  national issue that has sharply divided older Americans. That could be a warning sign for politicians, indicating that  future voters will be more supportive of gun control measures than  current voters, Gilbert said. Polls conducted by national news  organizations have found that 75 percent to 80 percent of adults  support handgun registration compared with 96 percent of the high  school students surveyed, he noted.

1572.text: GUN-3565 PRIMARY SHOTGUN WEDDING PA. SENATE CHOOSES TO BOLSTER THE FREE(-FIRE) MARKET. You've got to hand it to the 36 Pennsylvania state senators who voted Tuesday to water down Pennsylvania's new gun-control law. In one bold stroke, they earned themselves a new loyal constituency: criminals, especially young ones. Surely, if the House goes along, young toughs will find some way to thank the lawmakers who gave them a two-year window of opportunity to go on a gun- buying spree. Now that the mandatory criminal background checks for buyers of rifles and shotguns have been delayed by two years, it will be 1997 before young (and old) muggers and rapists will have to worry about getting flagged by a background check when they try to pick up some added firepower at the Kmart counter. The delay undermines a good feature of Sen. Vincent Fumo's original law - expanding the list of those who can't legally buy guns to include those who as teenagers ran afoul of the law. This juvenile background check is unique to Pennsylvania. People who as teens were adjudicated for, or convicted of, any of 32 offenses, including possessing a gun on school property, are barred by this law from owning guns until 15 years after their offense, or age 30. Any teen adjudicated for, or convicted of, more serious offenses - rape, assault or homicide - is permanently forbidden to own any gun. The way violent youth crimes have been surging, by 1997 the indulgent senators may have collected a huge cadre of grateful, gun-toting young constituents.

1578.text: GUN-10331 PRIMARY OLDIES BUT GOODIES: N.R.A.'s Big Spending Plans All this talk of newfangled outside groups receiving money from anonymous donors has overshadowed some of the more traditional players. But one of them, the National Rifle Association, is coming in big. All told, the N.R.A. says it plans to spend between $15 million and $20 million this election cycle. As of the end of this week it will have spent $6.7 million so far including mail. So, doing the math, that leaves the last two weeks before Election Day to make up the lion's share of its action, though group officials cautioned their final spending decisions could change with the political environment. Today, the association unveiled a new advertisement tagging Representative Joe Sestak of Pennsylvania - the Democrat and former Navy vice admiral who is running for the United States Senate seat of Arlen Specter - as an anti-gun, big government liberal. And it started a new mail campaign supporting Dino Rossi, the Republican running to take Patty Murray's Senate seat in Washington State. The mailer takes a page out of the pro-choice movement's playbook by referring to the 5-4 Supreme Court decision earlier this year upholding gun rights - more particularly, the Second Amendment's primacy over state and local law -- in the McDonald V. Chicago case (''Our Second Amendment rights were saved by only one vote,'' reads the mailer, to be sent to 600,000 voters -- N.R.A. and non-N.R.A. -- in Washington).

1574.text: GUN-4230 PRIMARY Md. Panel Approves Gun Control Plan Maryland Gov. Parris N. Glendening's handgun control measures cleared another hurdle yesterday when a legislative committee approved the strongest components of the plan, including a limit on individual handgun purchases to one a month. The House of Delegates Judiciary Committee approved the legislation on a 13 to 7 vote. The panel joins the state Senate's Judicial Proceedings Committee, which approved the legislation last week. The measure goes to a vote by the full Senate tomorrow, and legislators predict that it is almost certain to pass. In addition to the restriction on handgun purchases, the bill would regulate private or "secondary" gun sales and prohibit anyone who is the subject of a protective order involving allegations of domestic abuse from purchasing a gun. If the measure is approved, Maryland would join two other states -- Virginia and South Carolina -- that restrict handgun purchases to one a month. The federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms said that its most recent statistics show that Maryland has surpassed Virginia as the primary supplier of handguns used in violent crimes in the District since Virginia adopted the one-gun-a-month restriction in July 1993. Glendening (D) says that the bill will curb the flow of firearms into illegal hands in Maryland.

characters: federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1575.text: GUN-5460 PRIMARY COUNTY PROPOSES TO PLUG STATE GUN LAW LOOPHOLE Palm Beach County government lawyers on Monday released a proposed gun control law made possible by a state constitutional amendment taking effect today. The voter-approved amendment enables counties to close a loophole that allowed gun buyers to skip three-day waiting periods by purchasing firearms from unlicensed sellers at gun shows and flea markets. Since the amendment, four counties - Miami-Dade, Broward, Hillsborough and Orange - have passed gun control ordinances. Santa Rosa County has passed a resolution criticizing the state law. Palm Beach County commissioners will discuss the proposed gun ordinance for the first time on Feb. 2; a final vote is set for Feb. 23. Martin and St. Lucie counties are not preparing such measures. A copy of Palm Beach County's proposal was sent Monday to the county's League of Cities for review. The law - which is opposed by the National Rifle Association - would apply in both municipalities and unincorporated areas. The county's proposed ordinance would require that unlicensed sellers obtain national criminal background checks of potential buyers through the Florida Department of Law Enforcement. But FDLE will do checks only for licensed dealers, manufacturers or importers - a Catch-22 not contemplated during the statewide campaign last fall for the constitutional amendment. It would then be up to unlicensed sellers to work out an arrangement with licensed dealers to run the checks for them, said Assistant County Attorney Laura Beebe.

1576.text: GUN-366 PRIMARY NEW PUSH FOR ANTIGUN LAW IS EXPECTED The attempted assassination of President Reagan by a suspect carrying a .22-caliber revolver today was expected to bring another flurry of activity in Congress for passage of a strong national law for control of pistols. Proponents of gun control said that today's shootings would increase the chances of such legislation in a Congress that had appeared to be opposed to any tightening of controls. But John M. Snyder, chief lobbyist for the Citizens Committee for the Right to Keep and Bear Arms, predicted that the expected rush of activity would be nothing more than that and that, after the excitement was over, Congress, if anything, would be more likely to pass a bill sponsored by the National Rifle Association to weaken the enforcement power of the Government. History and the strong sentiment against control registered by members of Congress and Mr. Reagan himself before today's shootings seemed to be on Mr. Snyder's side, according to several officials on Capitol Hill. Reagan a 'Pro-Gun President' ''After all, Mr. Reagan is the most pro-gun President we have had in many years,'' Mr. Snyder said.   On the other side, Nelson T. Shields 3d, chairman of Handgun Control Inc., said that public pressure for such legislation had been mounting since the murder of John Lennon, the singer, in Manhattan last December in part because ''the 60's generation has come out of the closet'' and has become politically active against the proliferation of handguns.

characters: Citizens Committee for the Right to Keep and Bear Arms

1579.text: GUN-238 PRIMARY ACCIDENT LEADS TO PRISON FOR VICTIM WITH RECORD On May 31, 1984, Daniel Garcia of Manhattan was struck by a speeding taxicab near Park Avenue South and 22d Street. The impact knocked him out and left him, in the words of one witness, ''a bloody mess.'' A few hours later, however, he was to learn that the taxi was by no means the worst thing to hit him that day. Shortly before Mr. Garcia underwent surgery at a nearby hospital for internal bleeding, a loaded revolver fell from the pocket of his trousers. When he regained consciousness 18 hours later, the first person he saw was not a doctor, but a police officer, who arrested him and took him to Police Headquarters, where he was charged with illegal possession of a firearm, a felony. Mr. Garcia later said he was carrying the gun, which he said he had found several months earlier in Central Park, because he was scared. It was payday, he said, and he had been mugged several times. Furthermore, his apartment had been broken into, and there had been knife fights in the lobby of the building, he said. A Previous Conviction ''I wasn't shooting anybody, I wasn't mugging anybody,'' Mr. Garcia, who is 46 years old, said in a recent interview. '' I just know those people, and they're crazy. They'll kill you for nothing.'' The discovery of the gun was particularly unfortunate for Mr. Garcia. Ten years earlier, he had been convicted of robbery. His record had been clean since then, but as a prior felon, he came under one of New York State's mandatory-sentencing statutes.

1580.text: GUN-6256 PRIMARY JUNK-GUN BAN GETS OK STATE BILL AIMS TO DISPOSE OF 'SATURDAY NIGHT SPECIALS' The Senate on Monday sent a bill banning cheap "Saturday night special" handguns to a governor who has said he supports it, as California continues its drive to create the strongest gun controls in the nation. Gov. Gray Davis already has signed into law two of this year's four high-profile measures: one closes loopholes in the state's assault-weapons ban, and the other limits gun buyers to one purchase a month. The final bill, requiring child safety locks on new guns, is expected to clear the Legislature soon. "This is the grand slam of gun control in California," said Luis Tolley, western region director for Handgun Control, an advocacy group. Approved by the Senate on a 24-13 vote Monday, the bill by Sen. Richard Polanco, D-Los Angeles, is aimed at ridding the state of "junk" handguns, often called "Saturday night specials." The bill will make it a crime to sell a handgun that is not equipped with safety devices and has not passed firing and drop-safety tests, beginning in 2001. The measure almost stalled on the Assembly floor last week. But late-minute lobbying by Davis aides helped shore up the support, and the bill passed 43-26. The Senate's approval was expected. Opponents of SB 15 said the measure will do little to remove unsafe handguns from the street. "If the governor signs this bill, he will increase the black market of new guns sales and increase the liability for law enforcement by exempting these 'unsafe guns' for their use," said Steve Helsley, Sacramento lobbyist for the National Rifle Association's Institute for Legislative Action. "(Davis) has thrown policy out the window in favor of playing political patty-cake with people's lives by promising them safety when none exists."

1577.text: GUN-1301 PRIMARY NATION WASHINGTON SMOKING BAN ON BUSES The Interstate Commerce Commission voted unanimously on Tuesday to ban smokingon all regularly scheduled interstate buses in the United States.   Bus companies were given the option of allowing charter passengers to smoke if the chartering group agrees it should be permitted.   The ban will take effect soon after the issuance of the commission's decision in writing and its publication in the Federal Register, according to ICC spokesman A. Dennis Watson.   Amtrak recently banned smoking in the last four rows of its Superliner lounge cars and in most of its train stations. There are still smoking cars on trains. Smoking is now banned on almost all domestic airline flights.   AP GUN CONTROL Strictest Bill Approved The District of Columbia has approved the nation's most far-reaching handgun control bill, a measure to make manufacturers and dealers liable for deaths and injuries caused by semiautomatic weapons.   Mayor Marion Barry Jr. said Tuesday that he had signed the bill, which cannot become law until reviewed by Congress.   The Associated Press erroneously reported Monday that the City Council was expected to give final approval on Tuesday. Final passage actually came last week. Congress has 30 days from Jan. 23, when it resumes legislative business, to invalidate the law.   mm AP WORLD SOUTH AFRICA De Klerk Warns ANC President F.W de Klerk warned the African National Congress on Tuesday that the South African government would not capitulate to radical pressure or allow anarchy.   He said the ANC had reverted to outdated polemics and policies at its conference last weekend. '' The time has come for the ANC to decide what they want,'' de Klerk said Tuesday night in Johannesburg. He said its choices were whether to follow the commitments its leadership had made to the government in May and August or to revert to what he called ''the confrontation and conflict of the past.'' NY Times ISRAEL Koch Struck In Israel Former New York City Mayor Edward I. Koch became a casualty of the Palestinian uprising Tuesday when a rock struck and scratched his head while he toured Jerusalem's walled Old City.   ''I know the stone wasn't meant for me because everyone likes me,'' Koch joked, then continued his tour alongside Jerusalem Mayor Teddy Kollek.   ta There were no reports of arrests.   AP COLOMBIA Drug Lord Surrenders The youngest of three brothers believed to help run the Medellin cocaine cartel surrendered Tuesday under a Colombian government offer not to extradite drug trafficking suspects, President Cesar Gaviria said in Bogota.   The man, Fabio Ochoa Vasquez, became the first cartel leader to turn himself in under the 3 1/2-month-old offer, which also promises reduced jail sentences for drug suspects who surrender and confess.   c c AP INDIA Riots Lead To Curfew More than half of Agra, the city that is home to the Taj Mahal, was under curfew Tuesday after three days of riots between Hindus and Moslems. The Taj Mahal, the 17th century architectural masterpiece, reopened to tourists.   At least 11 people were killed and 49 injured in Agra in riots that were the latest in a wave of sectarian clashes to have swept several cities across India, claiming at least 307 lives in 12 days. s AP ETHIOPIA Port Opened For Food The Ethiopian government and Eritrean rebels have agreed to reopen the rebel-held Red Sea port of Masawa for the first time in 10 months to take food aid to millions of drought victims, a U.N. relief agency said Tuesday in Nairobi, Kenya.   A U.N.-flagged ship would start ferrying supplies to Masawa from Djibouti, further south on the Red Sea, in early January, the World Food Program said.   Masawa is the main port for Eritrea and the most efficient route for supplies to the drought-hit northern province. It has been closed since it was captured February by the Eritrean People's Liberation Front.   Reuters

1581.text: NA-42245 PRIMARY California Legislature takes first steps toward 'ammo control' SACRAMENTO -- Nearly four months after the massacre in Newtown, Conn., and even as gun control legislation appears stalled in Congress, California's Legislature took its first steps toward further tightening its already strong firearm laws Tuesday, when a panel approved a bill to restrict ammunition sales. Under a measure carried by Assemblywoman Nancy Skinner, D-Berkeley, people would have to buy ammunition from a licensed dealer and provide identification. The bill, AB48, was approved by the Democratic-controlled Public Safety Committee on a party-line 5-2 vote. It was the first of nearly two dozen bills legislators will soon address to combat gun violence. Sales of ammunition would also have to be reported to the state Department of Justice, which would check the names against a registry of prohibited gun owners -- a tool to allow authorities to seize guns from those who are banned from possessing them because of criminal or mental health histories. In addition, local law enforcement agencies would be alerted to anyone purchasing more than 3,000 rounds of ammunition within a five-day period. Also prohibited would be the sale of kits that gun owners use to convert 10-round magazines into high-capacity magazines. Unlike most states, California already prohibits gun owners from possessing magazines that have more than 10 rounds. Large-capacity magazines are the "instrument of choice" for those involved in rampage shooting, an Assembly analysis said.

1583.text: GUN-3240 PRIMARY COLIN CAMPBELL'S DIARY Quick-draw legislation, or, gutting gun control Something splendid or sneaky or downright grotesque is always afoot over at the state Capitol around this time of year, and so on Wednesday I took a sunny walk over to the golden dome, prowled the marble halls awhile and eventually slipped into a meeting of the House Public Safety Committee. Year after year, the Public Safety Committee talks about guns. There are handguns and assault rifles to be talked about; also jail terms, background checks, the Second Amendment, the rights of hunters, computer files, cooling-off periods - the whole ball of wax. These are lively subjects; also deadly subjects. But it adds a special pungency that the committee meets in Atlanta, one of the most deeply homicidal cities in the Free World, and also that Atlanta is the capital of Georgia, which is the nation's chief source of illegal handguns.
