The committee was meeting in Room 216, and Ralph Twiggs of Hiawassee, the committee's courtly white-haired chairman, began whispering up witnesses to describe the various bills they wanted transformed into laws. Twiggs spoke most cordially but so softly you had to strain to hear. Everybody else spoke rather quietly and respectfully in return, and pretty soon bills were passing left and right. Then Twiggs invited Rep. Charlie Watts, from Paulding Coutnty, to describe a bill Watts had introduced that would provide for instant telephonic background checks on prospective gun buyers.

1582.text: NA-42041 PRIMARY April 19 Readers' letters: The Senate vote on background checks and the Audrie Pott tragedy Gutless senators voted to protect powerful A fine day for democracy. A minority in the U.S. Senate blocks a watered-down background check bill. What a pathetic, gutless group of politicians. Every poll says the American public overwhelmingly believes background checks should be strengthened. Putting the interests of a small minority of NRA backed wing nuts and a $32 billion gun industry and ignoring the ''well regulated" clause of the Second Amendment over the wishes and safety of the American people is despicable. Our system of government is seriously broken and our politicians are morally bankrupt. To quote Gabrielle Gifford: "Our democracy's history is littered with names we neither remember nor celebrate -- people who stood in the way of progress while protecting the powerful. On Wednesday, a number of senators voted to join that list." Thomas Braucht Campbell GOP is against what a majority of U.S. wants After the Senate vote on Wednesday concerning background checks for gun purchasers at gun shows and stiffer penalties for gun traffickers, which is actually supported by the NRA, we now know what the GOP is against. They are not against restrictions on Second Amendment rights; they are against what the majority of Americans want as well as anything that makes the Democrats look like they are accomplishing something. They are for violent, mentally ill persons purchasing guns, and they are for people being able to buy guns and reselling them to criminals. The American people need to work with Gabrielle Giffords, the Newtown and Aurora families, and others to enact sensible and responsible gun control legislation nationwide.

1584.text: GUN-635 PRIMARY MAINE FELONS RETAIN RIGHT TO OWN GUNS, JUDGE RULES A 1987 state constitutional amendment broadening the right to bear arms means that even convicted felons may own guns, a judge ruled. Cumberland County Superior Court Justice Stephen Perkins Friday dismissed a charge of possession of a firearm by a felon against Edward Brown of Cumberland. Prosecutors had argued that the amendment's backers did not intend to allow felons to own guns. "If Maine legislators and citizens wanted to restrict or qualify the right to keep and bear arms, they could have enacted a constitutional provision that contained the desired restrictions," he wrote. "Maine's right to keep and bear arms amendment is the most broad and least restrictive of any of the 43 similar state amendments," he wrote. Attorney General James Tierney said Saturday that the case would be appealed. "With all due respect to Justice Perkins, we think he is wrong." The Maine Constitution used to guaranteed the right to keep and bear arms "for the common defense." But a 1987 amendment declared, "Every citizen has a right to keep and bear arms, and this right shall never be questioned." Brown was accused of criminal threatening in 1988, as well as with illegal possession of a gun.

1589.text: NA-41737 PRIMARY At 6th District debate, Trivedi chides Costello for taking money from NRA At a Monday night debate, Democrat Manan Trivedi lashed out at his competitor in the Sixth District congressional race for taking money from the National Rifle Association, saying the group is "clouding our common sense" on gun control. Republican Ryan Costello, the only candidate in the area to receive a donation from the gun owners' group, said the $10,000 gift had no strings attached. "I have an independent mind," Costello responded in front of about 300 people in an auditorium at West Chester University. "I think for myself. I don't take orders from anybody." The debate was the second and final between Costello, a lawyer and Chester County commissioner, and Trivedi, a doctor and Iraq war veteran, in their race to replace U.S. Rep. Jim Gerlach. The race, originally considered a toss-up by political forecasters, is now being called a likely win for Costello. The question about gun control elicited the only pointed exchange between the two men. Twice during the debate, Costello was not compelled to expand on his positions. When asked whether the Department of Defense should provide military equipment to police departments, he simply answered, "No." Asked whether he supports access to birth control, he said yes and paused before adding: "I think you should be able to go to Walgreens or CVS." Trivedi said police departments need to be provided for but not "over-militarized." He also spoke out against companies being able to limit access to contraception for their employees.

1587.text: GUN-7686 PRIMARY Reader responses: MILLION MOM MARCH: Guns provide security Once again Cynthia Tucker has written one of her predictable editorials urging more gun control ("Gun showdown: Million Moms are mighty nemesis for NRA," May 10). This time she doesn't really say much more than Million Mom March "good" and NRA "bad." Never mind that crime in general is down, violent crime is down and accidental shootings are down. Never mind that there is a dramatic difference in these statistics between states where a law-abiding citizen can legally carry a handgun and states having very restrictive laws. Academic studies show that everyone is safer when people who are not criminals can carry guns. The reason is clear: Would you wear a name tag that said, "Hi, I'm John Doe and I'm unarmed," or would you rather the criminals wonder about it? ROBERT CAMP Camp is a retired federal law enforcement officer.

1585.text: GUN-10599 PRIMARY Gun-rights group plans to protest proposed CSU gun ban A gun-rights group says it will fight Colorado State University's proposal to ban concealed weapons on the Fort Collins and Pueblo campuses. Rocky Mountain Gun Owners, which is based in Fort Collins, claims the gun ban is a clear violation of Colorado law. On Monday, the group will announce its protest plans, which may include legal action. "We are certainly going to pursue every opportunity to not let CSU wag its little finger and do whatever they want under state law," executive director Dudley Brown said. Larimer County Sheriff Jim Alderden said he will attend the announcement Monday in Fort Collins. "I am opposed to the ban, and I don't agree with the policy of trying to ram this down everyone's throat," he said. In February, the CSU Board of Governors is expected to vote on the ban of weapons on campus, including those held by people with concealed weapons permits. Exceptions include law enforcement and some military personnel. School officials - including some professors - say banning weapons leads to a safer environment on campus. Weapons already are prohibited in residence halls. Brown said CSU is trying to implement an overly broad gun-control policy to appease its liberal faculty. "What problem are we trying to address here?" Brown said. "Nobody is running around CSU shooting up the campus. "It's political correctness running amuck." But CSU spokesman Brad Bohlander - in a prepared statement - said that under Colorado law, the university has every right to regulate gun use.

1588.text: GUN-6980 PRIMARY THE LOADED QUESTION Does crime cause gun control or vice versa? Children typically need parental permission to play sports or  attend sex education classes at school but, in many states, those  same children don't need anything to possess a rifle or shotgun. In Texas, for example, there is no minimum age requirement  for gun possession. In most states, it's legal to buy semiautomatic assault-style  weapons. The majority of states require no licensure or  registration of guns. Forty-six states have no limit on the number of guns one  person can purchase at one time, which means only four states  impose limits as a precaution against illegal gun trafficking. Most of the states in the union have tougher restrictions on  vehicles, gardening equipment and children's toys than they do for  guns, yet gun-related violence and accidents kill thousands of  people each year. A new study that compared the firearm laws among the 50  states shows no uniformity in gun control and tough restrictions  in only a couple of the states. The study was performed by the Center on Crime, Communities &  Culture, which is a project of the Open Society Institute, a  nonprofit foundation that works to find public safety solutions.   It compares gun laws by scoring them on 30 weighted criteria  grouped into three categories.     Although federal gun laws are  important and there has been a national outcry to enforce existing  federal laws, most gun measures are state laws.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not explicitly mentioned)

1592.text: GUN-31107 PRIMARY Gun Registration D.C. police will start the gun registration process at 7 a.m. tomorrow, when the department opens an office at police headquarters at 300 Indiana Ave. NW. It is the start of the 180-day amnesty period in which residents may register handguns they have had illegally, or guns from other states. An officer from the gun unit will meet the applicant at the door and take temporary possession of the gun to ensure safety at headquarters. Officers will tag the gun and conduct ballistics tests before returning the gun to the owner. Paperwork indicating that registration is in process will be provided. About 14 days later, after an FBI background check, the gun will be officially registered.

1590.text: GUN-6373 PRIMARY Regulars worry gun shows under fire CHARLOTTE - In the wake of school shootings from Colorado to Arkansas and gun violence last week in Atlanta and Charlotte, gun-show regulars are worried that collectors' shows might get swept away in anti-gun sentiment. "We're feeling very threatened," said Scott Woller, director of the N.C. Gun Collector's show, held this weekend at the Merchandise Mart. Critics of gun shows, including sponsors of stricter gun-show legislation, say the shows provide a convenient place for buyers and sellers to get around federal laws requiring background checks. Gun-show advocates argue that a great majority of dealers at gun shows are licensed and responsible, and that those who are unlicensed are collectors selling expensive guns that wouldn't be used in crimes. Those hobbyists, Woller says, make up the vast majority of the dealers at his show. Most of the firearms sold there this weekend are guns not to be fired, he says, but items to be saved, talked about, and appreciated. "This is our hobby, even if it is repulsive to other people," he said. At a gun show in Fayetteville Saturday, vendors and buyers said they feared losing their right to legally trade weapons. Bob Ashby, who has traded rifles, shotguns and pistols at the show for three years, said restrictions chip away at his Constitutional rights. "Every gun law on the books since the Second Amendment was passed is illegal, unconstitutional and should be repealed," he said.

1591.text: GUN-9895 PRIMARY D.C. in the Cross Hairs A SENATE DEBATE on the gun liability bill scheduled for today could take a disastrous turn for D.C. residents if, as reported, an amendment is offered to repeal the District's ban on handguns. Besides representing an outrageous attempt to overturn gun and ammunition control protections that city leaders put on the books 28 years ago, the proposal, if adopted, would turn the capital into an armed camp. That is about the last thing needed by law enforcement officers on the front line. We hope Sen. Orrin G. Hatch (R-Utah), who in the past has expressed a desire to override the city's handgun ban, would abandon his plan and discourage any of his colleagues who might favor vigilantism in the District. A city that has had more than its share of gang shootouts and children shooting children doesn't need to load up with more lethal firepower. Late last night Senate leaders played down the possibility of pushing the repeal measure. We hope that is the case. That the District already has an excess of weaponry is not at issue.   D.C. police have recovered  385 illegal firearms so far this year. In 2003, law enforcement took 1,982 illegal firearms off the street. As Police Chief Charles H. Ramsey said yesterday, the greatest threat in the District is not al Qaeda but our home-grown terrorists with weapons. But putting more guns in District homes, stores,  coat pockets and purses for criminals to steal or buy will not make the city safer.

1596.text: GUN-1449 PRIMARY State's Big Stake in Colt Clouds Debate on Guns A long shadow fell across the debate over gun control in the State Legislature this week. The shadow belonged to Colt's Manufacturing Company, the famed gunmaker in the onion-domed warehouse that has turned out revolvers, pistols and assault rifles here since the 1850's. Two years ago Connecticut invested $25 million in state pension funds to help revive Colt, which had been brought to its knees by a four-year strike. The state, which owns 47 percent of the company, has since made loans and loan guarantees as well, but none of this really helped: in March, the company, which has sales of about $100 million a year, declared bankruptcy. Mindful of the state's investment, and the more than 900 jobs that Colt represents, several legislators balked when asked to support a bill that would have outlawed cheap handguns known as Saturday night specials, and another that would have banned assault rifles. The handgun measure was voted down in the Senate today but could be brought back in the House of Representatives. Such attitudes illustrate the moral and financial quandary that this relationship with Colt holds for Connecticut. In a state where guns and military suppliers have been principal industries for decades -- even the state library features a permanent display of Colt weapons -- lawmakers lately find themselves having to choose between saving jobs or saving lives.

1594.text: GUN-11684 PRIMARY Some in Newtown couple activism with mourning Connecticut residents and others work to channel power of grief toward gun control laws, mental health care. NEWTOWN, CONN. o People drawn to Newtown to share in its mourning brought cards and handmade snowflakes to town Monday while residents prepared to observe Christmas less than two weeks after a gunman killed 20 children and six educators at an elementary school. On Christmas Eve, residents lighted luminaries outside their homes in memory of the victims. Tiny empty Christmas stockings with the victims' names on them hung from trees near where the children were shot. "We know that they'll feel loved. They'll feel that somebody actually cares," said Treyvon Smalls, a 15-year-old from a few towns away who arrived at town hall with hundreds of cards and paper snowflakes collected from around the state. Organizers said they wanted to let the families of victims know they are not alone while also giving Connecticut children a chance to express their feelings about the shootings at Sandy Hook Elementary School. Police say Adam Lanza, 20, killed his mother in her bed before his Dec. 14 rampage and committed suicide as he heard officers arriving. Authorities have yet to give a theory about his motive. Some residents are urging political activism in the wake of the tragedy. A grass-roots group called Newtown United has been meeting at the library to talk about issues ranging from gun control, to increasing mental health services to the types of memorials that could be erected for the victims. Some clergy members have said they also intend to push for change.

1595.text: GUN-8615 PRIMARY Ashcroft trying doggedly to go backward on guns Despite his role as chief law enforcement officer, Attorney General John Ashcroft appears determined to undo the nation's gun laws rather than uphold them. Even after a new Justice Department report confirms the potency of the landmark Brady Act in blocking hundreds of thousands of felons from getting guns, Ashcroft is seeking to dilute the law. He wants millions of background-check records now kept by the FBI for 90 days to be destroyed after only one day. His explanation --- that the one-day limit will improve the instant-background check system required under the Brady Act --- defies credibility. Drastic reductions in the time that the FBI can maintain records will only make it less likely that mistakes and fraud will be caught. "Without the ability to retain records, the FBI would be unable to monitor the performance of the system, such as determining whether criminals are using false identification to pass the name-based check, as well as to make sure that the system is not being used for unauthorized purposes," said Michael D. Barnes, president of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence, formerly Handgun Control. In his oath of office, Ashcroft swore to protect the public, but his actions show he's more interested in shielding a longtime contributor, the National Rifle Association. A lifetime NRA member, Ashcroft began his tenure as attorney general with a chummy letter reassuring the NRA that he shared its view that the U.S. Constitution guaranteed personal arsenals.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1599.text: GUN-30041 PRIMARY GUN BILL WILL MAKE PUBLIC SAFER Gun zealots put Floridians at risk - April 11, editorial Unfortunately, you got it wrong, yet one more time. The right of a duly licensed gun owner to be able to legally keep a locked gun in a locked car will no more end with blood in the streets or Wild West shootouts than did the "stand your ground" law which doesn't obligate someone with a gun to retreat from an imminent threat. This new bill allows someone to legally keep his/her gun accessible before and after work without having to first go home. It doesn't allow a person to carry at work or to use a gun illegally at work. Period. This law doesn't make us feel safer, it makes us safer. If someone is disgruntled at their place of employment, no law will prevent them from illegally carrying a gun to work and (illegally) killing someone. Conversely, legal gun owners may stop the crazy person, because they possess the means of defending themselves. The right to defend yourself trumps property rights. This is as it should be. William W. Nelson, St. Petersburg A veto is in order I am ashamed to say I am a Republican voter. I cannot believe in the wake of all the shootings in these last several years, that our lawmakers would consider such a bill. There are so many other pressing issues for the state of Florida, why would they waste their time on a bill that would only put more guns out there?

1597.text: GUN-20250 PRIMARY Gun violence triggers action -- but not enough It's been a full year since two heavily armed teenagers walked into Columbine High School and shot to death 12 students and a teacher, wounded 23 others and killed themselves. The event capped a 27-month cluster of random schoolyard shootings in which 32 died and 82 were wounded. And it so galvanized the nation that, according to the Pew Research Center, the shooting ranks sixth among all major news stories since 1986, measured by public interest. That's ahead of the Gulf War homecoming and the Oklahoma City bombing. With so much attention, the moment should have been ripe for a new, more effective generation of gun controls. Similarly bloody events inspired such revolutionary reforms as the Gun Control Act of 1968, enacted after the back-to-back assassinations of Sen. Robert Kennedy and Martin Luther King Jr. in the spring of that year. Twelve months after Columbine, though, the new opportunity for substantial change is at risk of being wasted. Popular support for tighter gun laws, always high, jumped higher in the months after Columbine. And for the first time in more than 30 years, comprehensive gun law reform entered the presidential debate in substantive ways. In Washington, Congress began work on a juvenile justice bill that included new limits on gun sales. And last January, President Clinton used Columbine as a reference for a surprise State of the Union proposal to license handguns.

1593.text: GUN-9118 PRIMARY Dealer Charged With Enlisting College Students in Gun Scheme The United States attorney's office unsealed a conspiracy indictment on Thursday charging an Ohio gun dealer with aiding the illegal transport of guns to a New Jersey street gang with the help of Ohio college students who acted as "straw buyers." According to the indictment, 76 guns made their way from the gun store, Hole in the Wall, in Xenia, Ohio, about 20 miles southeast of Dayton, to members of the Double II Bloods street gang in Newark, including the gang leader, who is in prison for manslaughter. At a news conference in Washington, Attorney General John Ashcroft; Christopher J. Christie, the United States attorney for New Jersey; and Gregory Lockhart, the United States attorney for Ohio's southern district, announced the indictment of the store owner, James Dillard, and three East Orange gang members, Quadree Smith, Michael Harris and Daniel Alvarez. The four men were charged with conspiring to deal in firearms without having a license and with shipping, transporting and receiving firearms in interstate commerce. "This is the first time in history that one federal district has charged a licensed gun dealer in another state in an interstate gun-running conspiracy," Mr. Christie said. According to the indictment, among Mr. Dillard's sales were two large ones, one involving 16 firearms and one involving 15, to two straw buyers in April. Five days later, he sold 25 guns to the same buyers.

1598.text: GUN-9570 PRIMARY A Gun Analogy Misfires In his Nov. 24 op-ed column, "Bytes and Bullets," Lawrence Lessig took a shot at equating copyright protection with gun control. He missed the target. The legislation at issue would control theft of intellectual property, and it is limited to systems that are commercially viable only by facilitating copyright infringement. If guns were used only for legal purposes, their manufacture would remain a viable industry. If  peer-to-peer file-sharing networks were used only for non-copyrighted material, they would not. Ironically,  Mr. Lessig invokes the Constitution to defend his premise. But it is the Constitution that protects creators' exclusive rights to their work. Congress understands the rationale for this; as illegal file sharing increases, artists, authors and other creators have less incentive to create, and we as a society are poorer for it. And that -- to stay with   Mr. Lessig's gun analogy -- would be shooting ourselves in the foot. NEIL PORTNOW President The Recording Academy Santa Monica, Calif.

1600.text: NA-40568 PRIMARY Quinn proposes rewrite of gun bill Move gives lawmakers only one day to react; Illinois must allow concealed carry by Tuesday. CONCEALED CARRY SHOWDOWN Illinois Gov. Pat Quinn offered on Tuesday a gun-control advocate's vision of what concealed carry should look like: limits on numbers of guns and bullets, bans in restaurants and workplaces and a default assumption that gun-carriers aren't welcome in any business without signs inviting them in. With a federal court deadline looming for Illinois to legalize concealed carry, Quinn issued an "amendatory veto" of the bill before him, writing in restrictions that would produce one of the nation's most stringent systems. "Let me be clear," Quinn wrote about the court order that forced the concealed-carry bill to his desk. "I do not agree with this ruling. However, I am duty-bound" to address the issue. In addressing it by rewriting the bill, Quinn sets up a showdown. The Legislature now has three choices: Accept Quinn's changes by a simple-majority vote; override him by a three-fifths vote to put the original bill into law; or do neither and let the bill die. If it dies, the state will miss the July 9 court-ordered deadline to have a concealed-carry law in place. If so, Quinn could face a gun-control advocate's nightmare: unfettered concealed carry, legalized by the court but unregulated by the state. Illinois is the last state in the U.S. where concealed firearms are banned. Chicago's 7th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled in December that the ban is unconstitutional, and ordered the state to pass a concealed-carry law.

1602.text: GUN-2965 PRIMARY Mighty NRA revels in its death grip According to a recent poll, 91 percent of Georgians would like to see an instant background check enacted in their state. They're not going to get one. Negotiations have ended between members of the House and Senate trying to work out an agreement on an instant background check. No additional talks are scheduled. And with only two official working days remaining in the session, time is running short. Blame for the failure lies clearly and absolutely with the House. At a time of great public concern about violent crime, at a time when innocent people are dying in the streets, in schools, in homes and in shopping malls, the House is actually trying to weaken gun-control laws in Georgia. Specifically, the House demands that in return for allowing an instant background check, all local gun-control laws in Georgia must be wiped off the books. The House also insists that local government must be permanently barred from enacting any future gun-related laws. And that shouldn't happen. The Legislature's inability to deal responsibly with even the most basic of gun-control laws, a background check, demonstrates perfectly why local government must retain its ability to act on its own. Furthermore, with a five-day waiting period now in effect in Georgia thanks to the federal Brady Act, there's no need to cave in to the House's blackmail. In many cases, the Brady waiting period is giving local law enforcement enough time to run its own background checks of would-be handgun buyers.

1604.text: GUN-7538 PRIMARY LETTERS TO THE EDITOR Time for More Gun Regulation As a society, we regulate potentially harmful products and actions. We regulate food, water, cars, we even regulate toys. So why is it so hard for us to regulate guns? The Second Amendment specifies a well-regulated militia. A regulated gun is not a banned or a confiscated gun. Of course, existing gun laws should be enforced, but that does not eliminate the need for responsible, reasonable regulations. It is reasonable to limit gun purchases to one a month, insist on built-in gun locks and impose a 24-hour waiting period for purchases. The hundreds of thousands of participants at the Million Mom March represent millions of people who want legislation passed to make our citizens safer. Confiscation of weapons is a phantom threat; gun deaths are a tragic reality in our society. Janie M. Jones Columbia Neighborliness Takes Two We do indeed "live in times where people are so busy and wrapped up with their lives, they detach themselves and neglect the welfare of others," but there may not have been anyone in hearing distance as Rhonda Spero cried out in pain after injuring herself while walking her dog ["An Un-Neighborly Response," Letters, June 1]. If it was evening, even though her neighbors were home, they may well not have heard a (perhaps fainter than she realized) cry for help over the noise of TV, phone calls and children's voices. If the community is used to a lot of noise and disturbances, it's even more likely that no one heard or noticed her. When I see letters such as Ms. Spero's, I feel badly for the victim, but I also wonder what kind of neighbor the individual is herself/himself.

1601.text: GUN-20281 PRIMARY Some gun shops see business jump after ban's end WASHINGTON -- The expiration of the 10-year-old ban on 19 types of assault weapons Monday drove up business at some gun stores and set off sparks in the political world. John Kerry, the Democratic presidential nominee, said President Bush had failed tests of character and leadership. But the National Rifle Association lauded the demise of what it called a "misguided law, which had no effect on the actions of criminals, but penalized law-abiding citizens." Bush said in the 2000 campaign that he would sign an extension of the 10-year ban on the semiautomatic weapons. However, he did not press Congress to send him such a bill, and its Republican leaders never did. "Why didn't you ask, Mr. President?" Kerry asked, surrounded by police officers and gun violence victims at a community center here. "Why didn't you fight for it?" The answer, Kerry said, is that Bush caved to the NRA. "It is a test of character," he said. "In a secret deal, he chose his powerful friends in the gun lobby over the police officers and families that he promised to protect." He said Bush has made it easier for terrorists to do their jobs and harder for police to do theirs. White House spokesman Scott McClellan said Monday that Bush "does not set the legislative timetable." Asked whether it was disingenuous for Bush to say he supported the ban and then do nothing to extend it, McClellan said, "His position has always been well-known." He said the best way to prevent gun violence is to better enforce current laws. Since Bush took office, McClellan said, prosecutions for crimes committed with guns are up 68%.

1606.text: GUN-2611 PRIMARY Showdown on the Brady Bill There's a chance today for the House of Representatives to pass the simple, fair Brady handgun bill and take a big step toward the safety from uncontrolled weapons sales the public yearns for. Auspiciously, the Senate is committed to a vote on the bill and for the first time a President is eager to sign it. House members widely support this bill, yet friends of the National Rifle Association in their ranks threaten to cripple it with damaging amendments. The House must stare down these challenges and avoid changes that will complicate quick final passage in both chambers. The bill simply seeks to prevent handgun purchases by people the law already declares ineligible to own them: felons, fugitives, the mentally incompetent, minors, drug and alcohol abusers and illegal immigrants. Only half the states have significant regulation of sales, and their citizens are prey to criminals who travel from states where handguns are easy to buy with few questions asked. Under the bill a buyer would be required to wait five working days while the dealer checked for eligibility. Such a modest requirement might have saved Jim Brady, the former White House press secretary for whom the bill is named. Mr. Brady was wounded along with President Reagan in 1981, and Mr. Brady was left paralyzed. He and his wife, Sarah, are convinced that even a superficial check would have warned a Texas pawnshop owner that there was something funny about his customer John Hinckley, the obsessed stalker and assassin. Mr. Hinckley, who used an outdated driver's license as proof of residence, had recently been arrested for trying to carry a shotgun aboard an airliner.

1605.text: NA-40460 PRIMARY 'This is how we will be judged' Our View o President takes gun debate to theAmerican people while some extremists play political games. OPINION In the minds of 62 Missouri Republicans, nothing President Barack Obama said Wednesday about reducing gun-related violence mattered. Not one word. Not a single proposal. Those families of the 20 child victims of the Newtown, Conn., school shooting who appeared with the president? To these 62 House members, they're just props in the president's war on guns. The senseless 62 want to make criminals of any state and federal officials who would, as required by law, try to implement any of the 23 executive actions signed by the president in his effort to protect Americans from a continuing epidemic of gun violence. Let's be clear: Those House Republicans, all co-sponsors of Rep. Casey Guernsey's House Bill 170, would make it a Class D felony for police officers in Missouri to do their jobs. Tuesday's incident at the Stevens Institute of Business & Arts, in which a student is accused of shooting a financial aid officer and himself, puts the madness of such a proposal in perspective. According to court records, the student, Sean Johnson, 34, has a history of mental illness. He's a felon, having pleaded guilty in 2011 to second-degree assault and unlawful use of a weapon. City police are trying to figure out how he obtained the Kel-Tec 9-millimeter semi-automatic pistol used in the shooting. Perhaps he lied on a background check. Maybe he stole it. He might have done nothing wrong in obtaining the weapon. The answer could be helpful in determining what happened in this case of violence.

1603.text: GUN-3020 PRIMARY CUSTOMS ERROR ALLOWED IMPORT OF ILLEGAL RIFLES Thousands of illegal semiautomatic weapons have been allowed into the United States in the past few months because of a mistake by Customs agents who thought the guns were legal. The illegal MAK-90 guns look just like the legal semiautomatic version except for a tiny exterior detail near the trigger guard, U.S. Treasury and U.S. Customs officials said Friday. But the illegal guns, made in China for military use, can easily be converted into machine guns that fire as many as 600 rounds a minute, instead of one round each time the trigger is pulled. The Soviet-designed guns were mixed in with batches of legal rifles shipped to U.S. gun importers from the manufacturer, said Jack Killorin, chief spokesman for the U.S. Treasury's Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms in Washington. "Right now, we consider them contraband. Having them is the same under the law as having a kilo of cocaine," Killorin said. The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms is working with the U.S. importers of the MAK-90 to get them recalled from retailers and returned to their manufacturer. Authorities are still not sure how many thousands of illegal guns were let into the United States. Customs and Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms officials believe that many of the illegal rifles were shipped through Los Angeles, one of 300 ports of entry into the United States.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1607.text: NA-43684 PRIMARY Guns vs. abortions Bob Tripp of Reston asked, "Why this imbalance of coverage?" when The Post's coverage of the March on Washington for Gun Control appeared below the fold on the Jan. 27 Metro section cover a day after the antiabortion rally topped the front page with a large color photo ["A tale of two marches," Free for All, Feb. 2]. Perhaps it was because of the imbalance between the number of firearms- related deaths (about 30,000 a year) and abortions performed each year in the United States (more than 750,000). There are approximately 0.0001 deaths per firearm and 0.0127 abortions per child-bearing womb each year, so do we need gun control in this country, or womb control? Patrick McGinn, California, Md.

characters: March on Washington for Gun Control

1608.text: GUN-2868 PRIMARY ROBERTI SURVIVES RECALL, REITERATES STANCE ON GUNS State Sen. David Roberti easily survived a bitter recall Tuesday night in a special election that became a nationally watched referendum on gun control. With partial returns showing Roberti, D-Van Nuys, surviving the recall by a 2-to-1 ratio, the lawmaker declared victory less than three hours after the polls closed. "Let these results inspire all lawmakers to vote for gun control," Roberti said, declaring victory. "I will go to Washington and show them that I am here and can survive this. We are going to get tough gun control legislation passed in California." The recall gained national attention because it was heavily backed by gun advocates, including the National Rifle Association. While recall leaders insisted the issues went far beyond gun control, Roberti steered the debate back to guns, portraying the recall leaders as gun "extremists and crazies." Recall leaders defended their decision to go after the veteran legislator. "I think we made a point that the citizens do count, that their voice must be heard," said recall leader Kevin Washburn. "Special interests have to be challenged, especially when there's corruption in government." Roberti avoided becoming the first state legislator recalled by voters in the last 80 years. Some recall officials openly admitted they were out to punish Roberti, co- author of the state's ban on semiautomatic weapons after the 1989 Stockton schoolyard massacre in which five children died and 29 other people were wounded.

1610.text: GUN-4358 PRIMARY COUNTY SUPERVISORS' VOTE TO BAN GUN SHOWS WAS A SHOT IN THE DARK Although I agreed with the County Board of Supervisors' vote to ban gun shows at the Santa Clara County Fairgrounds I was surprised to see that not one of them had ever attended a gun show. It reminded me of attempts by school boards to ban books, also unread. Decision making from a position of ignorance seems to be an unfortunate specialty of governing bodies. In anticipation of this upcoming vote at least one of our county supervisors should have shown some professionalism by attending a gun show, which are held frequently in the Bay Area and nearly constantly in Vallejo. - Jim Thurber Mountain View

1586.text: GUN-540 PRIMARY States and gun laws Lawmakers in 18 states introduced bills last year to place restrictions on the AK-47 and similar semiautomatic weapons. Alabama and Virginia approved restrictions, and California enacted a ban. Cities and counties in nine states approved some type of restriction. In February, the U.S. Senate is expected to consider a bill to ban the sale of 14 models of assault weapons nationwide, and efforts are under way in several states to outlaw the weapons. Where does your state stand? - The following have no restrictions on ownership of semiautomatic weapons: Alaska, Arizona, Arkansas, Hawaii, Idaho, Iowa, Kansas, Maine, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nevada, North Dakota, Oklahoma, South Carolina, South Dakota, Utah, Vermont, West Virginia, Wyoming. - Some states considered legislation last year, but it failed to pass. Repeat attempts or first-time bills are expected to be introduced this year in: Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Florida, Georgia, Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Louisiana, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, Nebraska, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, Ohio, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Tennessee, Texas, Washington, Wisconsin. Source: USA TODAY research

1611.text: NA-40135 PRIMARY CLOSE LOOPHOLES FOR GUN PURCHASES A national group of law enforcement leaders meeting in Orlando this week renewed the reasonable call for mandatory background checks on all gun purchases, and it's time public officials in Tallahassee and Washington listened to them rather than the National Rife Association. The law enforcement groups don't write big campaign checks like the NRA, but their voices should be heard as they continue to plead for common sense in closing loopholes in screening gun buyers. Universal background checks will not wipe out gun violence, but they would be an important tool in limiting access to guns by those who already are not legally supposed to have firearms. Twenty years ago, the federal Brady Handgun Violence Prevention Act went into effect. The law requires background checks for guns purchased through federally licensed dealers. But screening of potential gun owners is not required for private sales, including transactions conducted online or at gun shows. Both Congress and the Florida Legislature have rebuffed every prudent attempt to close the loopholes. Members of the National Law Enforcement Partnership to Prevent Gun Violence said on Monday that expanded background checks are needed to keep guns out of dangerous hands. The group held a news conference at the International Association of Chiefs of Police annual conference in Orlando and cited a host of grim statistics about gun deaths as evidence of the need for expanded background checks. Guns are the leading cause of deaths for officers in the line of duty, and 41 officers have been shot and killed so far this year, an increase of 64 percent over 2013. Gun deaths in the United States rose to 91 per day in 2012, up from 82 per day a decade earlier.

characters: National Law Enforcement Partnership to Prevent Gun Violence
International Association of Chiefs of Police

1613.text: GUN-601 PRIMARY KENTUCKY MASSACRE SPURS CALLS FOR GUN CONTROL LAWS The killing of seven people in Louisville, Ky., by a deranged man firing an AK-47 semiautomatic rifle has spurred new momentum among state legislators across the country to ban such weapons, gun-control experts and advocates say. "Lawmakers from many states who have been working on this legislation have been calling in and asking for the latest data that we can give them so they can argue the case in their own state," said Bernie Horn, the state legislative director of Handgun Control Inc., an advocacy group in Washington. "Each time there is a tragedy like this, people are re-energized." A spate of new state gun-control bills followed the killing of five schoolchildren in Stockton, Calif., in January, but few of the measures became law. Lawmakers in 18 states introduced bills; passage was achieved in three. "We'll start seeing the action on gun issues again in January and February when most of the legislatures reconvene," said Chris Pattarozzi, a policy associate with the National Conference of State Legislatures. Asked what effect the Louisville shootings would have on federal legislation, Horn said, "There is no way to know." "Each time there is an assault-weapon tragedy like this, there is increased pressure on the administration to take the next step," he said. But Horn noted that his organization's position is that "the president has done very well so far" on such issues.

1609.text: NA-40626 PRIMARY St. Louis-area superintendents say guns in their schools not likely Many St. Louis-area school superintendents say they don't want guns in their schools regardless of a new state law that allows designated teachers to carry them. The law lets school districts appoint teachers or administrators as "school safety officers" who would be allowed to carry concealed weapons after training. Gov. Jay Nixon vetoed this bill in July. But on Wednesday, the Legislature overrode his veto of gun rights expansion legislation that also allows residents with concealed weapons permits to openly carry guns in public. Parents contacted the Clayton School District late last week concerned that teachers could soon be carrying weapons through the hallways. "There's the perception that school districts are required to do this, rather than allowed to do this," district spokesman Chris Tennill said. The district's position was posted on its website Friday afternoon. "There are few, if any, situations that would require the District to consider appointing 'school protection officers' given the level of and proximity to law enforcement resources already in place," it stated. Most superintendents and school district representatives who were contacted on Friday said their school systems already have police working in buildings as school resource officers, and response time from law enforcement is fast enough not to warrant arming school staff. St. Louis Public Schools Superintendent Kelvin Adams said his staff needs to review the legislation, but at this time there are no plans to designate any teacher to carry a firearm into school buildings.

characters: St. Louis Public Schools Superintendent Kelvin Adams

1612.text: GUN-5338 PRIMARY MISSOURI'S VOTE ON CONCEALED WEAPONS APRIL 6 WILL DRAW NATIONAL ATTENTION; OKLAHOMA AND INDIANA OFFER VERY DIFFERENT PICTURES OF LIFE WITH SUCH GUN LAWS The eyes of the nation are turning to Missouri, which on April 6 will become the first state where voters decide whether people should be allowed to carry concealed weapons. The National Rifle Association, which supports Proposition B, says it can't stress enough the importance of the Missouri election. If Proposition B is approved, county sheriffs and the St. Louis County chief of police would issue permits to nonfelons who are at least 21 years old and have passed a 12-hour handgun safety course. State Auditor Claire McCaskill estimates that if the referendum passes, 62,000 Missouri residents will get the permits. That will greatly increase the chances that people you encounter in everyday life will be armed. What would Missouri be like if citizens could carry guns in public places like restaurants and on the highways? How will police cope with the fact that a greater number of people they meet could be carrying a pistol? The experiences of Oklahoma and Indiana, which each have concealed weapons laws, may offer some answers. Oklahoma's three-year-old law is one of the strictest in the nation. Indiana's law, one of the oldest in the country, makes it easy to get a concealed weapons permit. Missouri's proposed statute falls somewhere in between. Reporters Kim Bell and Terry Ganey traveled to Oklahoma and Indiana to research how concealed weapons have worked. Page A5.

1614.text: GUN-139 PRIMARY Washington Talk: Briefing; Two Views of Hate The National Rifle Association has decided that its major opponent, Handgun Control Inc., is a hate group. The N.R.A. is annoyed by ads featuring Handgun Control's vice chairwoman, Sarah Brady, whose husband, James S. Brady, was shot and paralyzed in the 1981 assassination attempt on the President. But what really irked the N.R.A. is an ad in which the police chief of San Jose, Calif., Joseph McNamara, says: ''Cop-killer bullets, mail-order handguns, machine guns. Has the N.R.A. gone off the deep end?'' In response, the N.R.A. is mailing a letter in which its legislative affairs director, Wayne LaPierre, says: ''The only political groups doing well in America right now are those preaching hate. And the No. 1 group on the list that is selling hate to raise funds is Handgun Control Inc.''

1617.text: GUN-6599 PRIMARY Gun Violence: A Turning Point To the Editor: Re your Jan. 29 front-page news analysis about President Clinton's proposal to license all new handgun buyers: The school shootings and daily headlines recounting gun violence have struck a chord in this country. We have reached a turning point; things must change, if not with this Congress, then with a new Congress that listens to the majority of the people. That is why I, along with many other moms, am taking my family to the Million Mom March in Washington on Mother's Day. We will send a strong message to Congress that we demand common-sense gun control to protect our children and that we will vote for what we want. You can be sure that change is coming, with or without this Congress. HILARY WENDEL New York, Jan. 29, 2000 http://www.nytimes.com

1618.text: GUN-1257 PRIMARY THE 1991 LEGISLATIVE SESSION; Virginia gun law does a bang-up job; Similar system aim of many in Georgia NORFOLK, Va. - When Virginia began considering a mandatory background check on handgun buyers, Robert Markus saw it as another bit of interference in the family business. To his surprise, that wasn't the case. "It has not been the nightmare most bureaucracies turn out to be," said Mr. Markus, owner of Bob's Sporting Goods in downtown Norfolk. "The check truly does only take a minute and a half." If Georgia sets up a system similar to Virginia's - as many legislators are inclined to do - gun dealers and enthusiasts shouldn't worry, according to Mr. Markus and other firearms advocates in the state. "Of all the systems that we've seen, Virginia's is probably the fairest to the law-abiding, tax-paying citizen," said John Copeland, vice president of the Virginia Firearms Dealers Association. Virginia requires buyers of guns with barrels shorter than five inches to submit to a criminal background check. Gun dealers call a toll-free state police number, and officers run a potential buyer's name through computerized state and federal crime records. If the buyer has been convicted of a felony in the United States, the purchase is refused. Since the system started in November 1989, 1,195 people have been denied the right to buy a gun, while 72,000 others were approved. What is most unusual about the Virginia system is that activists on both sides of the contentious gun-control debate find it acceptable.

1615.text: GUN-4245 PRIMARY TEMPERS FLARE AS HOUSE VOTES TO REPEAL BAN ON ASSAULT GUNS< THE HOUSE LEADERSHIP'S VICTORY MAY PROVE HOLLOW. < CLINTON VOWS A VETO, AND DOLE DOESN'T WANT A BILL. Republican leaders kept their promise to gun enthusiasts yesterday by steering a repeal of the assault-weapons ban through the House, but it was a victory without a conclusive result. The 239-173 vote to end the 18-month-old ban fell 48 votes shy of the two-thirds majority that would be needed to overcome a promised veto by President Clinton. And the bill is not likely to get out of the Senate. Still, the legislation will be a source of controversy and campaign contributions in the fall congressional elections. As is usual in gun-control debates, emotions ran high, leading in one instance to a challenge by House Rules Committee Chairman Gerald B.H. Solomon (R., N.Y.) to freshman Rep. Patrick J. Kennedy (D., R.I.) to take their disagreement outside. The 28-year-old Kennedy ignored the 65-year-old Solomon's taunt. Although Senate Majority Leader Bob Dole (R., Kan.) had earlier promised the gun lobby that he would bring a similar repeal bill to the Senate floor, it appears now that the measure will not see the light of day in that chamber. Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D., Calif.) the principal sponsor of the original ban on 19 models of semiautomatic assault weapons, said yesterday that she counted 50 votes in the Senate against any move to lift it. And Dole has passed the word to Judiciary Committee Chairman Orrin G. Hatch (R., Utah) not to bother reporting a repeal bill from the panel.

characters: House Rules Committee Chairman Gerald B.H. Solomon
Judiciary Committee Chairman Orrin G. Hatch

1616.text: GUN-31436 PRIMARY Gun rights group wants airport restraining order The gun rights group suing Atlanta and its airport over restrictions on firearms in the terminal asked a federal judge Tuesday for a temporary order stopping officials from arresting anyone caught with a weapon. GeorgiaCarry.org filed a federal lawsuit July 1, the day a new gun law took effect in Georgia, when airport officials threatened to arrest a state legislator who had announced he planned to bring a handgun to the airport when he went there to pick up visitors. On July 1, Georgia law changed to allow permitted guns in state parks, restaurants that serve alcohol and on mass transit. Atlanta Mayor Shirley Franklin and Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport General Manager Ben DeCosta said guns in the airport would violate another state law that bans firearms at "public gatherings," and said they would have police arrest anyone bringing a firearm to the airport. In the request for a temporary restraining order, attorney John Monroe said state Rep. Tim Bearden's Fourth Amendment protection from illegal searches and seizures would be violated if he were arrested for exercising his Second Amendment right to carry a gun inside nonsecured areas of the airport. The motion for a temporary restraining order said Bearden, a Villa Rica Republican, would be harmed if the airport carried out its threat to arrest him while the airport would not suffer harm waiting until there is a decision on the suit.

1620.text: GUN-30624 PRIMARY Giuliani stakes middle ground at NRA meeting WASHINGTON - Republican presidential candidate Rudy Giuliani, who as New York mayor backed gun control and sued firearms manufacturers, sought a middle ground Friday with skeptical gun-rights activists. But his remarks left many at the National Rifle Association's "Celebration of American Values" conference uneasy, especially after he struggled to answer whether he still thought gun makers should be held liable for criminals' actions. Several other Republican presidential hopefuls also addressed the group, most with stronger gun-rights records than the former mayor, and directed digs at him and at one another. Arizona Sen. John McCain chided "big-city mayors (who) decided it was more important to blame the manufacturers of a legal product than it was to control crime in their own cities." McCain also went after Mitt Romney, a former Massachusetts governor, saying that candidates are wrong when they think "if you show your bona fides by hunting ducks or varmints or quail, it makes up for support for gun control." Romney embarrassed himself earlier this year by claiming he'd been a lifelong hunter, only to have his campaign acknowledge that he'd been on just two hunting trips. Romney later said he'd hunted "small varmints" more than twice. "I will say the same things I've been saying since 1994," actor and former Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson told the group, contrasting his strong NRA rating with the more nuanced positions of Giuliani and Romney.

1619.text: GUN-30757 PRIMARY Bloomberg Bolsters Gun Drive in Ohio and Kentucky Broadening his national effort to reduce the use of illegal guns, Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg of New York announced the addition of 14 mayors from Ohio and Kentucky to his coalition on Thursday and unveiled a campaign to pressure Congress to allow cities to share information about the source of guns used in crimes. The campaign, to include Internet and television advertisements as well as a new Web site, is intended to pressure Congress into rescinding the so-called Tiahrt amendment, a measure attached to spending bills each year since 2003 that bans the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives from releasing gun-trace data, except to police officials and prosecutors working on a particular crime. The provision prevents the data from being used in civil lawsuits against gun dealers or manufacturers, but also keeps law enforcement officials, many say, from being able to see a full picture of gun trafficking in and around their communities. ''I am tired of my people having to face illegal guns,'' said Scott M. Knight, who appears in the advertisement and is police chief in Chaska, Minn., a city of 24,000 just south of Minneapolis. '' I am just tired of roadblocks, barriers, legislation being put up that gets in our way of doing our job.'' In making the announcement at the police academy here, Mr. Bloomberg was accompanied by Mayor Mark Mallory of Cincinnati and other mayors from the region who have recently joined Mr. Bloomberg's national coalition on illegal guns, which law enforcement officials say are contributing to a rise in violent crime.

1621.text: GUN-6595 PRIMARY New Pressure for Gun Control President Clinton has moved the nation's gun control debate an important step forward with his State of the Union proposal for a national system of photo licensing and safety testing for handgun buyers. The proposal puts fresh political pressures on an indifferent Republican Congress, which has yet to approve the more modest gun control measures the administration called for last spring following the Colorado school shootings. Mr. Clinton's plan -- identical to Vice President Al Gore's campaign proposal -- would essentially treat handgun ownership as seriously as society treats the licensing of drivers. The president could make a good plan even better by requiring not only licensing of buyers but also the registration of all handguns themselves, including the tens of millions now in circulation. That is the course urged by Mr. Gore's Democratic opponent, Bill Bradley. No one expects this Congress to approve a dramatic overhaul of existing regulations. But the Republicans should at least be willing to hold hearings on the Clinton plan's common-sense provisions. One would require buyers to show that they can handle a weapon safely. Buyers would also have to show that they are aware of their legal responsibility to keep loaded guns away from children. Mr. Clinton has also requested $280 million to better enforce existing gun laws -- a deft response to complaints from the gun lobby that too little is done to enforce laws already on the books.

1623.text: GUN-1456 PRIMARY Cuomo Proposes a Crackdown To Stem Flood of Illegal Guns Releasing new statistics showing the steep rise in illegal gun use in New York State, particularly among children and teen-agers, Gov. Mario M. Cuomo today proposed a multipronged assault on the sale and distribution of illegal weapons. The proposal calls for stiffer penalties for the illegal sale or possession of guns; greater cooperation with states where most illegal guns are sold, in an effort to stop the flow of guns into New York, and new enforcement strategies by the state police and local police departments to detect and disarm people carrying illegal weapons. Governor Cuomo said in a statement that the Federal Government's failure to develop an effective anti-gun strategy had left the states with no choice but to develop their own. "New York must work cooperatively with other states to develop our own comprehensive action plan to get the guns off the streets and stop the bloodshed," Mr. Cuomo said. The program would cost the state no new money, said Richard H. Girgenti, New York's Commissioner of Criminal Justice Services, but would involve a redeployment of existing resources and, in some cases, increased responsibilities of law-enforcement officials who now focus primarily on narcotics investigations. Tracing Trafficking Patterns The plan calls for New York State to work with the five states that are the source of 80 percent of the illegal guns seized in New York State -- Virginia, Florida, Ohio, Texas and Georgia -- to strengthen enforcement of gun laws there and to trace weapon trafficking patterns.

1625.text: GUN-2185 PRIMARY ATTACK ROOT PROBLEMS, NOT GUNS COMMENTARY COLUMN For two decades, the National Rifle Association has argued that "guns don't kill - people do." Now, a major study by the National Academy of Sciences (NAS) suggests that the NRA may be right, even if their proposed solution - longer jail terms - is flawed. The academy's report, "Understanding and Preventing Violence," notes that five out of every six guns used in crime are obtained illegally. In other words, more restrictions on legal gun ownership are unlikely to substantially change the violence equation. If gun control is not a panacea for violent crime, the "silver bullet" of longer jail sentences is not much better. The NAS notes that despite a 300 percent increase in average time spent in prison by inmates since 1975, violent crime has significantly declined as a consequence. But if neither gun control nor long-term mass incarceration are "quick fixes" to violent crime, what does work? The NAS would base solutions on intervention, not legislation. Among them: Intervene to disrupt illegal gun markets, where so many of the weapons used in violent crime are bought and sold. A comprehensive program of buy-and-bust and sell-and-bust arrests, which have been shown to have little impact on street drug markets, could have a real effect on reducing violence by crippling illegal street gun markets.   Because the act of buying a gun is not driven by the kind of physical dependence common to addiction, buy-and bust and sell-and-bust techniques are likely to be particularly effective in combating illegal gun sales.

1622.text: NA-40380 PRIMARY Why we need a gun culture The tragedy in Newtown, Conn., in which a mentally ill gunman went on a shooting spree in an elementary school, has escalated the gun-control debate and brought out some good legislative ideas. But it has also brought out the worst extremes on both sides of the debate - from Wayne LaPierre's unworkable (and perhaps even cynical) proposal to place an armed guard in every single school in America, to Piers Morgan calling his interviewee, gun-rights advocate Larry Pratt, "unbelievably stupid" on the air. What Morgan and the rest of the world do not seem to understand is that the American "gun culture" that is so derided by the global left was actually intended by our Founding Fathers from the beginning. Although the framers of the Constitution probably did not intend (or foresee) the decline in American morality that has led to so much violence in this country, it's clear from the text of the Constitution itself that the framers intended the population to be well-armed. Hence the inclusion of the "right to bear arms" in the Bill of Rights. So why should a gun culture still be considered desirable, even in the wake of a string of tragedies from Columbine to Newtown? Because a well-armed population is the last bastion against the imposition of tyranny. Let us step back for a moment and contemplate the unthinkable: Suppose that a totalitarian party gains political power (could be Nazi, communist, or some other extreme ideology), by constitutional or extra-constitutional means. Let's now imagine that they start dismantling all of our civil rights - freedoms of expression and religion, rights of the accused, right to due process, and everything else you can name. You might be thinking to yourself, "it couldn't happen here." The liberal Germans of the 1920s and the liberal Egyptians of the 1960s probably thought the same, and yet the Nazis were elected into power in 1933 and the Muslim Brotherhood in 2012.

1624.text: GUN-6064 PRIMARY Senate approves gun checks With Vice President Al Gore casting the tie-breaking vote, the Senate voted Thursday to require background checks for the sale of all firearms at gun shows, dealing a stinging defeat to the powerful gun lobby. The background check provision, part of a $ 1 billion juvenile crime bill, would affect 2.5 million gun sales, about half of all guns sold in the United States every year. The Senate passed the overall juvenile justice bill 73-25 Thursday night after more than a week of tumultuous debate over gun control. Sen. Max Cleland (D-Ga.) voted in favor, while Sen. Paul Coverdell (R-Ga.) voted against. The passage sent the measure to the House, where Speaker Dennis Hastert (R- Ill.) has signaled his support for it. The earlier 51-50 vote requiring background checks at gun shows was a setback for Senate Republicans, who sought to derail the Democratic-sponsored initiative with a less restrictive proposal of their own. Strategists on both sides of the aisle said Thursday's shooting in Conyers changed no votes. But the latest incident of school violence, coming on the one-month anniversary of the deadly school shootings in Littleton, Colo., hung heavy in the Senate chamber as senators prepared to cast their votes. "If anyone woke up this morning wavering . . . they got a huge wake-up call in Georgia," said Sen. Barbara Boxer (D-Calif.). "This morning's news was yet another reminder of gun violence in our society."

1632.text: GUN-5708 PRIMARY Gun vote just salvo in campaign season NO DOUBT about it: The vote that killed a gun control bill in the House last week was driven almost entirely by politics. That shouldn't be surprising, of course. Virtually everything that happens in Washington for the next 16 months is going to be part of the political gamesmanship leading up to the elections for president and congressional seats. The defeat of the bill on Friday was particularly crass, however. Democrats, who supplied most of the "no" votes, want more than anything to have a campaign issue to use against Republicans, and they are willing to sacrifice things they say they believe in to get it. In this case, they sacrificed several new controls on firearms, including a requirement for safety locks on guns, a ban on some ammunition clips and a measure to forbid anyone under 18 from owning assault weapons. They also sacrificed the real possibility that a House-Senate conference committee could have undone changes introduced by Republicans regarding background checks at gun shows. That's not likely to happen now because the House-passed juvenile justice law that will go into conference contains nothing on the issue, so there's nothing to use for bargaining with the Senate's gun-control language. It must be said, however, that House Republicans weren't very smart in handing this political issue to the opposition --- nor were they operating entirely on principle. They had shown some sensitivity to public opinion by accepting the need to plug a loophole that allows people in some cases to buy weapons without a background check at gun shows. Then they ruined the effort by actually reducing, to 24 hours from the current three days, the existing period for a background check when the firearms are purchased at gun shows from licensed dealers.

1627.text: NA-42995 PRIMARY No Comment Necessary: Gun Permits for the Legally Blind The Des Moines Register reported over the weekend that "Iowa is granting permits to acquire or carry guns in public to people who are legally or completely blind." Polk County officials say they've issued weapons permits to at least three people who can't legally drive and were unable to read the application forms or had difficulty doing so because of visual impairments. And sheriffs in three other counties - Jasper, Kossuth and Delaware - say they have granted permits to residents who they believe have severe visual impairments. "I'm not an expert in vision," Delaware Sheriff John LeClere said. "At what point do vision problems have a detrimental effect to fire a firearm? If you see nothing but a blurry mass in front of you, then I would say you probably shouldn't be shooting something." It is not clear exactly how many Iowans with visual impairments are legally permitted to pack heat. ·    Jesus, Freedom and Guns

1626.text: GUN-8815 PRIMARY Townsend Proposes Assault Rifle Rule; Ehrlich Suggests Consulting With Law Enforcement on Need for New Gun Law Lt. Gov. Kathleen Kennedy Townsend (D) called yesterday for new restrictions on the sale of assault weapons in Maryland, the latest sign of how the Washington area sniper attacks are driving the governor's race. At a news conference in Rockville, Townsend said the Bushmaster semiautomatic rifle allegedly used by the sniper suspects to kill 10 and wound three is not regulated for sale in Maryland because of a loophole in state law. Townsend said she wanted to close the loophole by enacting a law that would grant the Maryland Handgun Roster Board the authority to approve or bar the sale of certain models of semiautomatic rifles commonly known as assault weapons. The board's powers are now limited to handguns. Republican Robert L. Ehrlich Jr., who drew criticism last month for questioning the effectiveness of the gun board, spoke cautiously yesterday on an issue that has hurt him in the polls. "We're willing to expand plans that work," he said, adding that he wanted to consult with law enforcement agencies for their views. "The police, not public officials, should take a leading role." Ehrlich appeared in Montgomery County yesterday as well, alluding to the sniper attacks as he received an endorsement from the county's Fraternal Order of Police. "We don't know what you've been through in the last month," Ehrlich said to a small group of officers who turned out for the rainy-day event. "Thank God, those snipers were found."

1629.text: GUN-513 PRIMARY NELSON SUPPORTS GUN LIMITS; AIDE BOUGHT RIFLE WITH LITTLE TROUBLE Waving a newly purchased AK-47 rifle above his head, gubernatorial candidate Bill Nelson said Thursday it is easier to buy an assault weapon in Florida than it is to rent a car. Nelson said it took a campaign aide less than 10 minutes to buy the weapon, along with two 30-round clips and other ammunition, at a gun shop in Miami Wednesday night. "All you need to show is a driver's license and the cash to purchase it with," Nelson told the Kings Point Democratic Club. He said it takes longer to rent a car. Nelson also said that buying a gun requires no more documentation than that required to buy a six pack of beer. "As governor, I'm going to put a stop to that," said Nelson, a congressman from Melbourne who is facing former U.S. Sen. Lawton Chiles in the Sept. 4 Democratic primary. Nelson spokesman Don Pride said it took 25-year-old campaign aide Brian May just nine minutes and 27 seconds and $873 in cash to buy the weapon and fill out a store form of six questions. The aide used campaign money to buy the weapon, Pride said. Customers of Florida gun shops include Colombian drug lords, Nelson said. He said federal officials found that 54 percent of the weapons confiscated from drug traffickers in Colombia had been bought in Florida.

1630.text: NA-44636 PRIMARY The man who fought D.C.'s gun laws; With the help of skilled lawyers, Tom Palmer took on the city's handgun ban. Now he's on his way to beating D.C.'s carry law, too. To legal scholars, the case that promises to end the District's ban on carrying handguns in public is known as Palmer v. District of Columbia. Tom G. Palmer himself is hardly an abstraction, however. A Oxford-trained, libertarian political ethicist who has lived in the city for more than two decades, Palmer tells The Washington Post's Spencer Hsu that he's a "firearms-owning peacenik" who just wants to allow vulnerable people to defend themselves. In response to Palmer's victory in federal district court, the city is now pondering whether to appeal and/or how to rewrite its gun laws to comply with the ruling. Said Palmer: "It's not like they have to sit down in a philosopher's chair and dream something up. ... If they decide to make it so onerous that they seem to comply in form, but in fact make it impossible to exercise, they will see us in court again." In other news: Jim Brady becomes D.C.'s 71st murder of 2014 after Virginia medical examiner rules death a homicide (Post, WRC-TV, AP, Homicide Watch) Streetcar operators in training learn that driver ignorance may be their biggest challenge (Post) Courtland Milloy rides a bike downtown, gains a modicum of empathy for urban cyclists (Post column) The highly-regarded Rocketship charter-school chain lands in Anacostia (Post) Colby King calls out Jim Graham, Muriel Bowser and Vincent Orange for poor oversight (Post column)

1631.text: GUN-3153 PRIMARY KILLINGS UP AFTER CONCEALED-GUN LAWS, STUDY CONCLUDES MORE PEOPLE were killed with guns after concealed-gun laws were relaxed in four of five urban areas studied by University of Maryland researchers. The university's Violence Research Group examined homicides by gun and other means before and after new, relaxed concealed-gun laws took effect in Jacksonville, Miami and Tampa, Fla., Jackson, Miss., and Portland, Ore. Average monthly homicides by gun increased 74 percent in Jacksonville, 43 percent in Jackson, 22 percent in Tampa and 3 percent in Miami. Portland had a 12 percent decrease, the researchers announced Monday. They also found that although homicides by gun increased after the less restrictive laws were adopted, homicides by other means remained steady. "While advocates of these relaxed laws argue that they will prevent crime, and suggest that they have reduced homicides in areas that adopted them, we strongly suggest caution," said University of Maryland criminologist David McDowall. "When states weaken limits on concealed weapons, they may be giving up a simple and effective method of preventing firearm deaths." Concealed-weapons supporters in Missouri disputed the study's conclusions. "The study doesn't prove what they say it proves," said Sen. Harold Caskey, D-Butler. Caskey said the study lumped all homicides by guns together, instead of separating those committed with firearms other than handguns. He said it also failed to pinpoint how many deaths were caused by concealed-gun permit holders and whether self-defense was involved.

1628.text: GUN-695 PRIMARY GUNS: GIVE VOTERS SAY; COOLING-OFF PERIOD SHOULD BE AN ELECTION ISSUE Our crime-fighting Legislature has once again demonstrated that talking tough is a lot easier than being tough. One last attempt to pass a seven-day cooling-off period for the purchase of handguns was defeated by the House. One last attempt to ban six of the most deadly assault weapons also was defeated. Apparently it didn't matter to our lawmakers that the folks back home overwhelmingly support the cooling-off period and a ban on assault weapons. What a disgrace. Clearly, there is only one way to take this issue away from the National Rifle Association and other opponents of gun control. It is time to make this issue an election issue. Legislators who continue to ignore the need for cooling-off periods, bans on assault weapons and other meaningful gun-control legislation should be held accountable on election day. Palm Beach County and Treasure Coast legislators whose votes favored sensible gun control were Reps. Lois Frankel, D-West Palm Beach; Carol Hanson, R-Boca Raton; Ed Healey, D-West Palm Beach; Marian Lewis, R-North Palm Beach; Ray Liberti, D-West Palm Beach, and Steve Press, D-Delray Beach. Voting with the gun lobby were Reps. Jim Hill, R-Jupiter; Frank Messersmith, R-Lake Worth, and Charles Nergard, R-Port St. Lucie. Florida is now one of the easiest places in the nation to buy a gun. We've also become one of the deadliest states. Is this just a coincidence? Of course not.

1636.text: GUN-6888 PRIMARY Letters, Faxes & E-Mail: GUN CONTROL; Punish the criminals Apparently, Robert Parham feels that opposition to gun control and morality are mutually exclusive ("Religious right takes wrong side," Viewpoints, Oct. 27). I'm a Southern Baptist and make use of something God blessed me with that many who get involved in the issue seem to be lacking: Common sense. Guns are inanimate objects and it makes no sense to place blame on them. The answer is not restricting honest gun owners, but enforcing current laws and punishing criminals to the maximum extent. And what's wrong with religious leaders defending guns? The vast majority of them are never used in a crime. Our right to bear arms is guaranteed in the Constitution, and I sleep better at night knowing I own one that may ensure my family's safety. None of that seems wrong to me. JOHN STANLEY Stanley, of Lawrenceville, is an analyst for a federal government agency.

1634.text: NA-44483 PRIMARY Santorum's misguided view of gun control in Wild West BOB SCHIEFFER:"Does it bother you or does it worry you that we may be going backwards, that we're going back to the day of the O.K. Corral and the Old West, where everybody carried a gun? Is that where we're headed here?" FORMER SENATOR RICK SANTORUM (R-Pa.): "You know, everybody romanticizes the O.K. Corral and all of the things that happened. But gun crimes were not very prevalent back then. Why? Because people carry guns." - Exchange on CBS's "Face the Nation," April 27, 2014 The Hollywood version of the Wild West is at the core of this exchange on "Face the Nation," so perhaps it's time for a history lesson. One-time presidential candidate Rick Santorum asserted that gun crimes were low in the  late 19th century because people had the right to carry guns. But he actually has the story backward. The Facts The 1881 gunfight in Tombstone, Ariz., was actually sparked by an effort to enforce the town's Ordinance No. 9:  "It is hereby declared unlawful to carry in the hand or upon the person or otherwise any deadly weapon within the limits of said city of Tombstone, without first obtaining a permit in writing." That's right, City Marshal Virgil Earp and his brother Wyatt were attempting to enforce a gun-control law that cowboys were evading - a law that was rather common in the West, according to historians.

1635.text: NA-43827 PRIMARY NRA President: Assault weapons ban not likely to pass Congress National Rifle Association President David Keene said Sunday that he does not believe a ban on assault weapons and high-capacity magazines will win passage with the current Congress. "I would say that the likelihood is that they are not going to be able to get an assault weapons ban through this Congress," Keene said on CNN's "State Of The Union." In the wake of the mass shooting at a Newtown, Conn., school last month, an Obama administration task force is set to offer policy recommendations Tuesday about how to curb gun violence. President Obama has endorsed renewing an expired ban on assault weapons and high-capacity magazines, and gun control advocates in Congress have said they will get behind such a measure. The NRA met last week with the Vice President Biden, who heads the Obama administration task force. After the meeting, the group said it was "disappointed with how little this meeting had to do with keeping our children safe and how much it had to do with an agenda to attack the Second Amendment." Appearing on the same program as Keene, Sen. Chris Murphy (D-Conn.), a gun control advocate, said he disagreed with the NRA president, and believes an assault weapons ban can win passage on Capitol Hill. "Newtown fundamentally changed things. And the NRA just fundamentally doesn't get this," Murphy said.

1633.text: GUN-7630 PRIMARY Online movements gather momentum; Internet allowing activists, such as the Million Moms, to get out their messages and recruit new supporters. Back in January, four months after the first news stories about the Million Mom March, Jeannie Marklin got an e-mail message from a friend: It was time to organize. ''We put flyers in schools. We tacked posters in grocery stores. But the real task of enlisting folks fell to the Internet,'' Marklin, 28, a social worker from Silver Spring, Md., said as hundreds of thousands of like-minded people were gathering last Sunday under a bright sun near the U.S. Capitol to press for more gun control. As the May 14 march approached, the grass-roots effort mushroomed into a massive virtual community, energized by a multifaceted Web site that drew 6 million hits. Many in the huge Mother's Day crowd on the National Mall wore shirts and caps advertising the Web address. Meantime, a group of female gun-control critics, calling themselves the ''Second Amendment Sisters Armed Informed Mothers March,'' put up their own Web site to promote a modest-sized counterassembly. It carried links to hundreds of affinity groups, from the Gun Owners of America to the Tyranny Response Team. The rival online recruiting drives were but the latest example of how cyberspace is helping ever larger numbers of people organize to voice common concerns. These virtual communities leap barriers of time and space while crossing social, cultural and racial lines. Just as church groups and labor unions played a key role in forging the 1963 Civil Rights March on Washington, led by the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr., e-mail and Web pages are changing the organizing landscape for today's social and political movements.
