1637.text: GUN-4084 PRIMARY Clinton tells NAAC: We'll battle 'hatred,' wave of church arson President Clinton told delegates to the NAACP's annual convention Wednesday that America must stop "the fires of hatred and bigotry" or lose its ability to fight for freedom around the world. "If we are going to continue to be a force against terrorism, against hatred for the rest of the world, we have to continue to purge ourselves of it," Clinton said in an address focused on the recent rash of church fires. Clinton used the occasion to contrast his position on gun control with that of Republican rival Bob Dole, declaring that he would veto any bill repealing a ban on assault weapons. "We are prepared to stand by the ban on 19 deadly assault weapons. It is the right thing to do," Clinton said. Dole on Tuesday appeared to back away from his commitment to make repeal of the assault weapons ban a priority. He said the nation had "moved beyond" that issue and suggested that most banned weapons are back on the market in modified form anyway. Dole raised the ire of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People this week when he declined to address the convention. The campaigning president's speech drew a standing ovation and shouts of "four more years." Clinton said Vice President Al Gore will meet today with insurance industry representatives to talk about rebuilding burned churches and to discuss complaints that some of the churches have had their insurance canceled.

characters: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People

1638.text: GUN-1835 PRIMARY POLICE IGNORING HARD-TO-ENFORCE GUN LAW Police anguished in May when 11-year-old Jimmy Hinojosa, using a gun he found in his house, accidentally shot his next-door neighbor, age 12, through the head and neck. The victim was Jimmy's best friend for nine years. Now police were faced with having to arrest Jimmy's brother-in-law, who had become his surrogate father when Jimmy's father died two months earlier. But police in Florida are routinely ignoring a law passed in 1989 that makes it a third-degree felony for adults to leave guns where children find and use them to cause injury or death, a Florida Department of Law Enforcement spokeswoman said. "Understand the controversy here," FDLE spokeswoman Linda Booz said. "Is the sheriff or police chief really going to come and arrest someone in the family where a child has killed or been killed and everyone is consumed by the grief?" FDLE statisticians track arrests for every state criminal law. After the handgun law took effect Oct. 1, 1989, FDLE reviewed eight months of arrest reports and found only three arrests statewide. That prompted an FDLE decision to quit counting because police obviously are not enforcing this law, Booz said. In the Boynton Beach case, the victim lived. The decision not to arrest Jimmy's brother-in-law for a crime punishable by five years in prison was relatively easy. The 11-year-old admitted to picking the locked door of a bedroom.

1639.text: NA-43582 PRIMARY Sweeping bill on gun limits advances Maryland Senate Democrats passed sweeping gun-control legislation Thursday that would make the Free State the first in nearly 20 years to require residents to submit to fingerprinting, training and background checks to obtain a license to buy a firearm. The licensing provision is the centerpiece of Gov. Martin O'Malley's response to the December school shooting in Newtown, Conn. - and one of the most ambitious legislative attempts nationwide this year on gun control. Over objections from Republicans and some conservative Democrats who warned that fingerprinting would trample on Second Amendment rights, the bill passed the Senate 28 to 19 after 17 hours of sometimes-boisterous debate this week. It now heads to the House of Delegates, where it could again face a blizzard of amendments and more heated debate. A "rally to prevent gun violence" is scheduled at the State House in Annapolis on Friday, and gun-rights activists are expected to descend on the capital as well. In addition to licensing and fingerprinting measures, the Senate bill includes provisions that would ban assault weapons and ammunition magazines that hold more than 10 bullets in Maryland. Similar efforts by Democrats to reinstate a federal assault-weapons ban have been stymied in Congress so far. On mental-health issues, the original bill by O'Malley (D) would have increased the likelihood that someone committed involuntarily would be banned from making subsequent firearm purchases. But the Senate took it a step further, banning guns for anyone admitted against his will for any length of time. In addition, Marylanders who voluntarily admit themselves for mental-health treatment after visiting an emergency room for such issues could be banned if doctors determine that they pose a risk.

1643.text: GUN-743 PRIMARY Rep. Silver dodges bullets to lead fight for gun control TALLAHASSEE - Ron Silver, the Legislature's leading gun-control    advocate, laughed as he showed off his newest toy: a lime green Uzi    squirt gun. "It's a crazy world," he said. Silver presented the squirt gun to Marion Hammer, the lobbyist for    the Florida affiliate of the National Rifle Association, shortly before    the two staged a debate last week. The diminutive Hammer promptly    emptied her water glass into the gun and prepared to squirt Silver in    the face. "She's filling it up," Silver shouted. "I knew she would. Oh    boy, wait until after I speak. Then you'll have plenty of reason." Silver laughed again. The popular North Miami Beach lawmaker's laugh regularly spills out    in loud, machine gunlike bursts. Even as he prepares this spring for    what could be his most visible accomplishment in the Legislature and a    launching pad to greater political heights, Silver finds plenty of time    to laugh. "Right now I'm in the position of not really caring what people    think," he said. "I just be myself. If nobody likes it, they know    what they can do." What Silver would like his fellow lawmakers to do is vote for his    bill that calls for a statewide, seven-day waiting period between    purchasing and picking up handguns, or agree to put the issue on the    1990 ballot as a constitutional amendment. If they don't, he said, he    will rekindle his "Cool It  Florida" petition drive for 350,000    signatures to get the waiting period before the voters.

1641.text: NA-42709 PRIMARY Gun Limits Are Signed Into Law By Malloy HARTFORD (AP) -- Gov. Dannel P. Malloy of Connecticut signed into law on Thursday sweeping new restrictions on weapons and ammunition magazines similar to the ones used by the man who fatally shot 20 children and 6 educators at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown. Alongside family members of some of the victims of the shooting, Mr. Malloy signed the bill hours after the General Assembly approved the measure, which lawmakers said gave the state some of the toughest gun laws in the country. ''This is a profoundly emotional day for everyone in this room,'' said Mr. Malloy, a Democrat. '' We have come together in a way that few places in the nation have demonstrated the ability to do.'' The legislation adds more than 100 firearms to the state's assault weapons ban and creates what officials have called the nation's first dangerous weapon offender registry as well as eligibility rules for buying ammunition. Some parts of the bill took effect immediately after Mr. Malloy's signature, including background checks for all firearms sales. The bill passed 26 to 10 in the Senate on Wednesday night, and 105 to 44 in the House around 2:30 a.m. on Thursday. In the General Assembly, which Democrats control, leaders waited to unveil gun legislation until they struck a bipartisan deal that they say shows how the parties can work together in Connecticut and elsewhere. They said the package was a comprehensive response to the killings that also addressed mental health and school security measures.

1640.text: GUN-1036 PRIMARY Toy Gun Lobby Aims High Once again I know you'll think I made this up -- but I didn't. It was in the newspapers. The U.S. Customs Service has banned the importation of toy M-16 rifles because they look so much like the real thing. Apparently, as far as the government is concerned, some authentic guns are not as much of a threat to the citizenry as toy ones. The reason behind the ban is that the fake M-16 looks so similar to a bona fide one that police can't tell the difference. Officers have been shooting people carrying the toys. Also, fake guns are being used more and more in "armed" robberies by criminals who can't afford real guns. Clyde Heston, president of the National Toy Gun Association and Charlton Heston's third cousin, was indignant over the Customs order. "The Constitution provides that every child has a right to bear toy arms. When you confiscate toy guns at Customs, you are preventing law-abiding children from owning them. The bad kids are going to buy them in the schoolyard, so you won't stop any toy-gun stickups." "Is the NTGA going to give PAC money to Congress to fight the ban?" "You better believe it. We even have the NRA on our side because they know if Customs seizes toy guns today, it will grab real guns tomorrow. I've asked my cousin Charlton to do the commercials," Heston said.

1646.text: GUN-9893 PRIMARY Legislation crucial for gun owners, law enforcement Editor: The NRA-backed bills regarding the protection of shooting ranges and illegal gun registration by police and government agencies are important. It is in the public interest for law-abiding firearm owners to have a place to shoot and practice gun safety, and bill HB149/SB1156 provides a good balance of environmental protection in preventing overzealous governmental agencies from forcefully taking ranges and bankrupting their owners.   If we allow our gun ranges to be arbitrarily shut down, there will be no place for law-biding citizens to learn and/or maintain their shooting and safety skills. Our Second Amendment and state Constitutional right to keep and bear arms will be seriously infringed. Bill HB155/SB1152 is designed to prevent government agencies from compiling databases that include arbitrary lists of law-abiding gun owners who have committed no crime, and that amounts to illegal gun registration. Federal law (Firearms Owners Protection Act) prohibits the establishment of "any system of registration of firearms, firearm owners, or firearm transactions or dispositions." The bill exempts lists and records of firearms that are necessary for legitimate law enforcement and criminal investigations and cases involving gun crimes. These two bills are pro-law enforcement, pro-public safety, pro-environment, pro-pollution control, pro-Second Amendment, pro-federal and state Constitution and law, and pro-freedom from undue governmental interference and procedures. Urge your representatives to pass these important bills for Gov. Bush's signature. Lee Hanson, Hudson

1645.text: NA-42596 PRIMARY New York Has Gun Deal, With Focus on Mental Ills; Obama's Plan to Be Broad: Willingness to Use Executive Orders to Take Action WASHINGTON -- President Obama this week will embrace a comprehensive plan to reduce gun violence that will call for major legislation to expand background checks for gun purchases and lay out 19 separate actions the president could take by invoking the power of his office, lawmakers who were briefed on the plan said Monday. Lawmakers and other officials said that the president could use a public event as soon as Wednesday to signal his intention to engage in the biggest Congressional fight over guns in nearly two decades, focusing on the heightened background checks and including efforts to ban assault weapons and their high-capacity clips. But given the difficulty of pushing new rules through a bitterly divided Congress, Mr. Obama will also promise to act on his own to reduce gun violence wherever possible. Actions the president could take on his own are likely to include imposing new limits on guns imported from overseas, compelling federal agencies to improve sharing of mental health records and directing the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to conduct research on gun violence, according to those briefed on the effort. White House aides believe Mr. Obama can also ratchet up enforcement of existing laws, including tougher prosecution of people who lie on their background checks. At a news conference on Monday, exactly one month after the school massacre in Newtown, Conn., Mr. Obama said a task force led by Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr. had ''presented me now with a list of sensible, common-sense steps that can be taken to make sure that the kinds of violence we saw at Newtown doesn't happen again. He added: ''My starting point is not to worry about the politics. My starting point is to focus on what makes sense, what works.''

characters: Vice President Joseph R. Biden Jr.
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

1642.text: GUN-301 PRIMARY GUN-CONTROL ADVOCATES ARE FEELING SURROUNDED One of the darkest hours for handgun control advocates was the election of Ronald Reagan. Suddenly, with Mr. Reagan's inauguration, the battle shifted from winning passage of stiffer handgun control legislation to trying to keep the conservative tide in Congress from sweeping away laws already on the books. Gun control, like abortion and busing, is one of those emotional issues that Senate Republican leaders managed to keep off the legislative agenda in 1981. The New Right and its Congressional allies did not press these issues during Mr. Reagan's first year, lest they divert attention from the President's economic program. But in 1982 they will be demanding action on their priorities - including gun control. For his part, Mr. Reagan, a friend and member of the National Rifle Association, has remained steadfast in his opposition to handgun control legislation despite the unsuccessful assassination attempt in March. He announced plans to abolish the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, the enforcer of Federal firearms laws, and endorsed a proposal to weaken the 1968 Gun Control Act. Still, the gloom pervading the handgun control movement seemed to lift recently, when the Senate Judiciary Committee held hearings on the McClure-Volkmer bill to repeal key provisions of the 1968 law - the nation's major legislative attempt to control guns, passed after the assassinations of the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. and Senator Robert F. Kennedy. They lifted because the hearings proved to be a re-run of an old show.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1644.text: GUN-9702 PRIMARY POPULAR GUN BAN SET TO END MONDAY More than two-thirds of Americans support it, according to a recent poll. Major law enforcement organizations back it. A majority in the U.S. Senate and, some say, in the House of Representatives would vote for it. President Bush and his opponent, Sen. John Kerry, both favor it. But despite the widespread support, the 10-year-old federal ban on the manufacture and importation of semiautomatic assault weapons appears certain to expire at midnight Monday. Gun-control advocates, including the ban's main sponsor, Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., say there's one reason why: the National Rifle Association. The powerful gun-owners lobbying group, with 4 million members, calls the ban ineffective. Ending the assault-weapons ban is the NRA's top priority this year, and the organization is waiting until after it expires to announce whether it will endorse Bush for re-election. The group showed its power on this issue after the ban first passed in 1994, helping to oust several lawmakers who supported it. "To see the special interests, the NRA, with its selfishness, win this is very hard because I know that people are going to die," Feinstein said in an interview. Wednesday, Feinstein joined more than two dozen police officials, assault-weapon victims and gun-control advocates like James Brady -- the former press secretary to President Reagan who was paralyzed by a gunshot -- to make a last-ditch appeal to Bush to force Republican congressional leaders to bring the reauthorization of the ban up for a vote.

1648.text: GUN-104 PRIMARY TIME TO COOL; LONGER WAITING PERIOD FOR GUNS A GOOD IDEA "Whatever we can do to strengthen our gun regulations, we should do it," Palm Beach Commissioner Carol Roberts said as the commission raised the waiting period to buy a handgun from two days to three days. She is exactly right. The longer cooling-off period is good news because it may save a few lives. The possibility will be enhanced if Martin County's Commission takes the same action. The farther a Palm Beach resident bent on shooting someone has to drive to buy a handgun, the better the chance is that he or she will cool off before using it. Congress is considering a national seven-day waiting period on handgun sales-- the so-called Brady amendment, after Jim Brady, the White House press secretary who was permanently disabled by a shot fired by John Hinckley Jr. in 1981. While waiting periods don't keep guns out of the hands of criminals, they do serve as a deterrent against impulsive domestic shootings. Americans want a seven-day cooling-off period and they should be allowed to have it.

1649.text: GUN-4196 PRIMARY DISARMING WAYS; ADVOCATING HANDGUN CONTROLS, SARAH BRADY FINDS IRONY IN CONTROVERSY THAT SHADOWS HER Handgun-control advocate Sarah Brady said Wednesday in Edwardsville that it's confusing and somewhat ironic that security has to be increased almost everywhere she speaks. "Who'd ever think a middle-aged mother and housewife would be so controversial?" Brady asked at a news conference at the St. Louis police headquarters. She spoke before a small group of reporters in a closed room that had been swept by three security officers and a police dog a few minutes earlier. She also was to speak before the public Wednesday night at SIUE, where campus police were on heightened alert. The cause for concern was Brady's well-known view that guns should be more tightly controlled in America. Some of her more vocal opponents have said she actually promotes crime by wanting to restrict access to weapons for self-defense by law-abiding citizens. Hogwash, she said. Even with a state law that allows most of its residents to carry concealed weapons, "Florida remains the single most violent state in the nation," Brady said. Easy access to guns doesn't stem crime and causes many innocent people to be killed or injured, she said. Her husband, Jim Brady, was shot in the head and seriously wounded in the assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan in 1981. At the time, Jim Brady was Reagan's press secretary. Brady was shot with an illegally obtained handgun. Jim and Sarah Brady became handgun-control activists after the shooting.

1653.text: GUN-11909 PRIMARY Beating the NRAat its own game Like so many other people these days, I regain my composure only to see it crumble in an instant. At the piercing sight of a photograph: Daniel Barden with his impish smile and missing front teeth. At the devastating power of a simple sentence, about Charlotte Bacon's Girl Scout troop: "There were 10 girls in the group. Only five are left." This national wallowing serves a purpose - not only to grieve but also to summon the resolve for change. There is talk, finally, belatedly, of reinstating the long-lapsed ban on assault weapons. This is an admittedly imperfect, certainly porous solution (existing weapons would remain untouched; look-alike guns would be produced), but a useful step nonetheless. Even better would be a companion measure - which had also lapsed with the expiration of the assault weapons ban - to prohibit the manufacture of magazines of more than 10 rounds. It is testament to our political fecklessness that these restrictions were not reimposed in the aftermath of Fort Hood, or Tucson, or Aurora. And we need to ask whether changes in technology offer other solutions. For example, requiring guns to be stored in safes with fingerprint-recognition locks, or equipped with trigger locks in the form of fingerprint readers. This technology saved James Bond in "Skyfall." Could it have saved Daniel Barden and Charlotte Bacon? There are two additional areas we must confront to respond effectively to Newtown: first, the power of money in politics; second, the role of the courts.

1647.text: GUN-1116 PRIMARY Bush gives ground on gun control issue A national waiting period for handgun purchases and a ban on some semiautomatic weapons would be ''favorably reviewed'' by President Bush if included in a broad anti-crime bill, Attorney General Dick Thornburgh said Thursday. The concession on the two major gun control proposals pending in Congress prompted lawmakers to predict swift action on the legislation that eluded them last year. But Sen. Joseph Biden Jr., D-Del., chairman of the Senate Judiciary Committee, said several stumbling blocks remain, including disputes over the application of the death penalty. Appearing before Biden's panel, Thornburgh cited the administration's ''strong objections'' to the so-called Brady bill. It establishes a seven-day wait before handgun purchases and another measure banning 14 domestic assault weapons. The bill is named for former White House press secretary James Brady, paralyzed in the 1981 attack that also wounded former President Reagan. Both Bush and Biden are pushing broad crime bills. Bush emphasizes procedures to help prosecutors and protect victims. Biden wants the assault weapons ban and $ 1 billion more for police agencies. House Democrats, meanwhile, have made the Brady bill their top anti-crime priority. The measure is likely to be approved by the House Judiciary Committee Tuesday. Bush opposes both the waiting period and the semiautomatic ban, as do the National Rifle Association and some congressional leaders. But their appeal to lawmakers makes it likely Bush will be forced to swallow gun control.

1652.text: GUN-4352 PRIMARY BREAK NRA'S DEADLY GRIP ON OUR NATION For a cure of the rampant crime in our cities, suburbs and, sadly, in our schools, the uncontrollable presence in our society of guns must be dealt with. A ban on assault weapons is a beginning but not enough. In this gun-happy society, being run by the National Rifle Association gun lobby, if we're to get a grip on safe and peaceful existence before we all go up in smoke, desperate measures are mandatory. Living in peace shouldn't be dependent upon having a gun at hand. How did we become so degraded in our quest for normal existence? Even former President George Bush finally saw the light and gave up his membership in the NRA. Now if we can get Charlton Heston and other big shots to do the same and eventually return our nation to its citizens, and eliminate the NRA lobby and other enemies of our government from within, then perhaps the United States will become a peaceful and safe country for our children to live in! Martell L. Folb Lake Worth

1650.text: GUN-10356 PRIMARY Letters to the Editor Let them buy guns, but restrict ammo Re: "Accused shooter had Fla. permit," Thursday: This is an example of how cities and towns across the country are constantly subverted in their efforts to control the proliferation of gun violence. The excuse mostly cited is the constitutional right of citizens to bear arms, which has frustrated any attempt to regulate the sale of guns. Has it ever occurred to anyone that while the Constitution allows virtually anyone to buy or own a gun, it does not prohibit banning the sale, control, or regulation of ammunition? Instead of trying to pass laws prohibiting people from gun ownership, pass laws that severely limit access to ammo. Without bullets, a gun is no more able to harm anyone than a stick, and certainly less dangerous than a knife. Forget about gun control and concentrate on ammunition control. Jon Tucker Philadelphia catman19116@yahoo.com Corbett is anything but an outsider Calling Republican gubernatorial candidate Tom Corbett a political outsider is astounding in the face of the very political actions he has taken as attorney general. Suing a municipality not once but twice for its ordinances restricting the use of industrial sludge on agricultural fields is hardly apolitical. East Brunswick Township in Schuylkill County found out the hard way that Corbett holds business interests above local health standards. In support of agribusiness interests, Corbett has also sued Packer Township in Carbon County to force it to allow industrial sludge placement. If anything, Corbett fits right in with "business as usual."

1654.text: GUN-3779 PRIMARY BILL TO BAN GUN RANGES IS ADVANCED; ALDERMEN AIM TO QUIET FUROR BY OUTLAWING SHOOTING IN CITY Fed up with the seemingly unresolvable problems of shooting matches, the Pacific Board of Alderman has given preliminary approval to a bill to ban gun ranges in town. Alderman Donna Bocato, 1st Ward, sponsored the bill, which would repeal all references to shooting matches and make any discharge of a gun illegal within city limits. Shooting ranges have been a hot topic in Pacific since the American Legion opened a range last fall in a residential neighborhood on the city's south side. Residents and Legionnaires have sparred over the range, which the residents contend is unsafe and noisy. The Legion decided in December to hold off on the matches until the board decided whether the ranges were legal. Last year, the Legion got a permit to hold the matches for one year. Another group, the Eagles, also has a shooting range in town. The ordinance would close both of these ranges. Mayor Hebert Adams said last week the bill was necessary because the noise problem has never been addressed. "This is the only way that I know to get everyone on a level playing field," he said. "You tell me how we have shooting matches and keep the peace of the residents - I want to see that," he said. In other action, the board moved on some items from its Contract with Pacific, a pledge by all city officials to work to fulfill their campaign promises within 120 days.

1651.text: GUN-3116 PRIMARY Mr. Dole's Transparent Tactics Senator Bob Dole's calculations are pretty easy to figure out. To secure the Republican Presidential nomination, he has to overcome his longstanding reputation as a moderate. So he is running hard to the right, offering some slice of himself to every conservative faction. He is against abortion. He is against affirmative action. Now he wants to revoke the ban on assault weapons passed as part of last year's crime bill. Four-fifths of the American people and the police officers who protect them favor that ban. But Mr. Dole has a smaller audience in mind. He is tailoring his position to please the 3.5 million members of the National Rifle Association. Mr. Dole is afraid that if he does not obey the N.R.A., its political contributions and support will go to someone else in the 1996 Presidential primaries. Pandering to a party's most militant ideological factions is a venerable tactic in nomination fights. Former Vice President Walter Mondale used it to win the Democratic nomination in 1984. There was hardly a Democratic interest group that did not get to nail a plank into his platform. Mr. Dole seems bent on demonstrating an equal malleability. Hence his shamelessly sycophantic letter to the N.R.A.'s executive director, Tanya Metaksa, last week. Mr. Dole pledged to make it a legislative priority to repeal the ban on assault weapons that was passed as part of the crime bill last year.

1655.text: GUN-8938 PRIMARY Pilots, NRA echo over cockpit gun debate; Senate resists what backer calls 'emergency equipment' Washington --- The National Rifle Association and the nation's airline pilots are waging parallel but equally aggressive campaigns to bring guns into the cockpit. Uniformed pilots are roaming Capitol Hill, visiting lawmakers and their staffs, dismantling what they say are the myths about guns in airplanes. One pilot union has devised a slogan for the effort: "Qualified to Fly. Qualified to Defend." The phrase is featured on posters, along with a pilot's silhouette against an American flag. The NRA is doing what it does best --- using the power of its nearly 4 million members. The gun rights group has sent letters and e-mails across the country urging the rank and file to contact members of Congress and urge them to support arming pilots. The NRA's magazine has focused on the issue for months, and the group has placed newspaper ads in the Washington area urging Congress to act. "We have been besieged with calls from commercial airline pilots asking for our help," said Wayne LaPierre, NRA executive vice president. "I don't know how you look at what happened on Sept. 11 and you don't wish that those pilots had a firearm to give them a chance." LaPierre said he has appeared on 35 radio shows and several television programs to promote arming pilots. House and Senate votes on arming pilots will surely be on the NRA "report card" mailed to members before the fall elections as a voting guide, he said.

1656.text: GUN-7326 PRIMARY Clinton Fires Back at NRA Attack; Truthfulness at Issue In Gun Control Debate President Clinton and the National Rifle Association yesterday exchanged acrimonious charges over truth and consequences in gun control. The first rounds were actually fired last week when the NRA aired tough television advertisements in which its president, Charlton Heston, all but called Clinton a liar for blaming the gun lobby for blocking gun control legislation in Congress. The 30-second spots also targeted what the gun organization characterized as the administration's unimpressive record of prosecuting violations of existing firearms laws. "Mr. Clinton, when what you say is wrong, it's a mistake. When you know it's wrong, that's a lie," Heston says at the end of each spot. Clinton, in an interview taped Friday and aired yesterday on ABC's "This Week," denounced the ads as "wounded rhetoric" from a "ruthlessly brutal" lobby that had driven a number of gun control supporters from Congress in 1994. "These crocodile tears--I don't think it will wash with the voters, even with Moses reading the script," Clinton said, refering to the biblical figure Heston portrayed in the movie "The Ten Commandments." Caught in the middle was Congress, which Clinton has urged to act on stalled gun control legislation before the first anniversary next month of the fatal shootings at Columbine High School in suburban Denver, in which 15 people died. The House and Senate last year passed bills in the shooting's aftermath, but Republican congressional leaders have yet to convene a joint committee to work out differences between the two versions--mainly because the Senate GOP leadership no longer supports the tougher version its chamber approved, and which Clinton supports.

1659.text: GUN-1814 PRIMARY Senators give final approval to crime bill WASHINGTON - The Senate on Thursday night approved its wide-ranging crime control bill that would expand the federal death penalty, limit death row appeals, create a waiting period for handgun purchases and ban certain types of assault-style weapons. The lawmakers voted 71-26 for the $ 3.3 billion bill, which seeks to apply more money to the fight against the drug trade, gang violence and terrorism. Congress has passed anti-crime legislation over the past several years in an effort to appear responsive to public concern over the rising rate of violent crime. Senators voting against the bill were an unlikely combination of conservatives opposed to the bill's gun control proposals and liberals unhappy with the death penalty provisions. Sen. Steve Symms (R-Idaho), a gun control opponent, complained that the bill "is not going to control crime, it's going to control law- abiding gun owners." Senate leaders worked in closed meetings throughout the afternoon and evening to settle final details of the measure after three weeks of often combative debate over amendments. The anti-crime bill would create a five-day waiting period for handgun purchases and require criminal background checks for would-be buyers. The House in May approved the so-called Brady Bill for a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases. The House has yet to consider its anti-crime bill.

1657.text: GUN-2881 PRIMARY EDGAR, MADIGAN DUEL OVER GUN DEAL; BAN ON ASSAULT WEAPONS IN QUESTION In the chess game that is Illinois politics, it was check, but not mate, Thursday morning for House Speaker Michael J. Madigan, D-Chicago. Madigan has decided to offer a helping hand because Gov. Jim Edgar is at odds with fellow Republican James "Pate" Philip, the Senate president, over a ban on assault weapons. But a sputtering James Reilly, the governor's chief of staff, marched into a news conference to say that Madigan's help wasn't needed. "Political games are not new, but what the speaker did today is beyond comparison in political gamesmanship," Reilly said. Reilly's impromptu tirade came minutes after one of Madigan's committees approved a 200-page bill promoting safe neighborhoods in Chicago. The committee killed a part of the bill that would have banned assault weapons, and Reilly accused Madigan of pulling the strings. Reilly said Madigan torpedoed a deal that Edgar's office had been working on with Chicago Mayor Richard M. Daley and members of the House Judiciary II Committee. The agreement modified Edgar's proposed ban on some assault weapons so the plan would be more palatable to Senate Republicans, who approved a watered-down version last week. The deal, Reilly said, was a compromise between what Edgar proposed in his State of the State speech in January and the weak Senate bill. But an aide to Madigan, Steve Brown, responded coolly that the speaker was just giving the governor his wish and would deliver on a promise to have the House vote on Edgar's original proposal.

1661.text: GUN-5012 PRIMARY Md. Handgun Sales Down 25 Percent; Drop Comes Year After One-a-Month Buying Limit Was Imposed Handgun sales in Maryland dropped more than 25 percent last year, the first full year of sales since a new state law barred most residents from buying more than one handgun a month. Gun dealers in the state sold about 21,500 handguns in 1997, down from 29,281 the year before and fewer than in any year since 1986, according to Maryland State Police. Officials attributed much of the decrease to an 80 percent drop in the number of handguns sold in batches of two or more -- exactly the type of sales that the law was intended to curtail. "This bill is working, and we are proud of the results we have seen from it over the past year," Gov. Parris N. Glendening (D) said last week. "This one-gun-per-month provision has been a highly effective tool in reducing the numbers of illegal handguns on the streets and in our neighborhoods." That view is disputed by gun dealers and other opponents of the law, who say the drop in handgun sales results from a routine business downturn. They also say there's no proof the law has kept criminals from getting weapons, and officials report signs that lawbreakers may be turning to rifles and shotguns in place of handguns. For Glendening, who campaigned four years ago as a strong advocate of gun control, the bill limiting handgun sales was a centerpiece of his crime policy. The General Assembly approved the measure in 1996.

1658.text: GUN-5291 PRIMARY CONCEALED WEAPONS BILL UNLIKELY TO BE HEARD Minnesota's concealed weapons law, regarded as overly strict by gun-rights groups but supported by police and gun-control advocates, will not be eased this year, a key House committee chairman said on Tuesday. Rep. Rich Stanek, R-Maple Grove, chairman of the House Crime Prevention Committee, who had been trying to find a middle-ground approach to this contentious subject, said he will not be hearing a concealed-weapons bill this year. He said he hopes to have public hearings on the issue before next year's session. "I don't think there's enough time within this Legislative session to address some of the concerns of the public, pro and con," Stanek said. "Rather than forcing something through, I think it's better to wait." The decision was supported by House Speaker Steve Sviggum, R-Kenyon, who said the new Republican majority in the House wants to be known instead for its work on tax cuts and other economic issues. In recent years, a majority of the states have loosened their concealed weapons laws under pressure from the National Rifle Association. The laws allow citizens without serious criminal records or mental health problems to obtain permits to carry loaded weapons in public. Minnesota's law requires those seeking permits to convince the local sheriff or police chief that they have a specific safety threat or occupational need for the weapon. That requirement gives considerable discretion to local authorities and reduces the number of permits issued, particularly in urban areas.

1660.text: GUN-5138 PRIMARY GUN-CONTROL AMENDMENT IS A SCATTERSHOT APPROACH Firearms kill and wound so many Americans that doctors consider gunshot wounds an epidemic. The desire to stop that bloodshed is sincere, but a gun-control amendment that may be proposed for the Florida Constitution would not be effective. The constitution now requires a background check and three-day waiting period for the purchase of handguns. People who are not licensed gun dealers are exempt if they are selling their "personal inventory," even when the sales take place at public gun shows. The amendment would try to close that loophole. Rather than make improvements on a statewide basis, however, the amendment would allow individual counties to adopt gun-control ordinances more restrictive than state law. Currently, the Legislature doesn't allow counties to do that. The Constitution Revision Commission will vote on the amendment Tuesday. At the commission's last vote, the amendment got 20 votes. It needs 22 to make the November ballot. Specifically, the amendment would allow counties to increase the waiting period up to a total of five days. Local ordinances could regulate the sale of rifles and shotguns - not just handguns - and the loophole for "personal inventory" could be closed by allowing the regulation of any sale conducted in a place to which the public has access. The new amendment would exempt anyone who holds a Florida concealed weapons permit. Those would be solid, reasonable reforms - if they were enacted nationwide. As reforms drop to governmental levels below that, they become less effective. New York's gun-control laws don't stop people from buying weapons in Miami and using them in Manhattan.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, not explicitly mentioned)

1664.text: GUN-1804 PRIMARY Assault-Weapon Ban Passes The City Council passed a bill, 28 to 4, yesterday banning assault weapons in New York City. Mayor David N. Dinkins said he would sign the bill as soon as possible. Owners of registered guns had fought the proposal. It defines an assault weapon as a semiautomatic or automatic firearm with such military-type features as a folding stock, bayonet mount, grenade launcher, flash suppressor or modifications of them. A shotgun with a revolving cylinder magazine also falls under that category. Owners have 90 days to get rid of the weapons once the bill is signed. Violators could be fined up to $5,000.

1663.text: GUN-30809 PRIMARY The Portrait of a Gunman, and of a Society To the Editor: Re ''In Words and Silence, Hints of Anger and Isolation'' (front page, April 18): The tragedy at Virginia Tech and discussions of Cho Seung-Hui's behavior as a student make it clear that, as a society, we cannot accurately identify and predict who will commit murder, nor are we able to effectively intervene in advance. The obvious solution is strict controls on handguns so that individuals do not have the means to commit such atrocities. This requires legislators to have the courage to stand up to the powerful pro-gun organizations, which -- through a combination of propaganda, financial and political influence -- made it possible for a man to simply walk into a gun store and buy a 9-millimeter handgun on the spot.   Wendy M. Geringer Croton-on-Hudson, N.Y. April 18, 2007  To the Editor: Cho Seung-Hui did not emerge in our world full grown with guns blazing. He has been with us a long time. He was a child in our school systems. He was a person in our community, in our mental health system. He was part of a family. Instead of rushing to fight a gun lobby that will not move one inch, what about finding ways to help the young, desperate, angry, isolated and miserable among us? That means money for early childhood education, parent education, mental health and school counselors. Ways that we can intervene in the lives of the disassociated young before it is so tragically too late.   Susan K. Finkel Seattle, April 18, 2007   To the Editor:

1662.text: NA-41193 PRIMARY Cold gun bigs fire up Conn. GUNMAKERS SLOUGHING off their responsibility for preventing weapons from getting into the hands of maniacs landed like a thud in Newtown, Conn., Tuesday. "What we have learned since 12/14 is eye opening," said Monte Frank of the Newtown Action Alliance, referring to the December date of the massacre at Sandy Hook Elementary School. "The gun industry profits immensely from the proliferation of illegal guns, and they have lobbied hard to keep the spigot on." In depositions obtained by The New York Times taken while gunmakers were successfully fighting a spate of liability lawsuits, gun execs claimed not to know how much damage their products were doing - or insisted the monitoring of gun dealers wasn't their problem. Mayor Bloomberg said he's not surprised by the gunmakers' unvarnished testimony. He said they've been bankrolling the National Rifle Association and the fight against sensible gun control for years. "The bottom line is, the organizations that fight those common-sense measures that the public is overwhelmingly in favor of, do get funded by the gun manufacturers," he said. Jon Lowy, of the Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence, called it "blood money." "This evidence shows that the gun industry has known for years that its irresponsible practices supplied the criminal market," he said. The massacre of 20 first-graders and six staffers at the Connecticut school by a crazed gunman armed with a legally-purchased Bushmaster rifle has renewed drives for increased gun control. The efforts have had some success in Connecticut but were stymied in Congress last month by pols fearing the power of the gun lobby.

characters: Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence

1668.text: NA-40068 PRIMARY BLOOMBERG, LAPIERRE TRADE TRUTHS, HALF-TRUTHS For decades, the National Rifle Association was the one holding the megaphone. Leaders David Keene and Wayne LaPierre voiced vigorous defenses of the Second Amendment and rallied members to resist new gun laws, often with great success. Now, they have an opponent. New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg wields the power of public office and his own personal fortune, and he's using both to push for legislation that puts new limits on firearms. This week the battle came to NBC's Meet the Press. Bloomberg and LaPierre never came face to face on the set, but the two men had plenty of sharp words about each other. LaPierre said Bloomberg was trying to "buy America" by pouring $12 million of his own money into gun-control advocacy. Bloomberg said he has an obligation to make sure the NRA isn't the only voice in the debate. Both made factual claims, too, which got the needle buzzing on the Truth-O-Meter. Here's a tally: - Bloomberg accused LaPierre of flip-flopping on the primary policy change being considered by Congress: expanded background checks on gun purchases. "If you go back to 1999, Wayne LaPierre testified on behalf of the NRA that background checks were appropriate and should be done," Bloomberg told host David Gregory. We found that in an appearance before a Senate subcommittee in the wake of the 1999 Columbine High School shooting, LaPierre said, "We think it's reasonable to provide mandatory instant criminal background checks for every sale at every gun show. No loopholes anywhere for anyone." We rated Bloomberg's claim True.

1666.text: GUN-6061 PRIMARY Editorials; We must say 'no more'; How many more children will die before we pass gun-safety laws? To many parents around the country, it had seemed inevitable, like the rumble of far-off thunder signaling a violent storm headed their way. Pearl, Miss., West Paducah, Ky., Jonesboro, Ark., Springfield, Ore., Littleton, Colo. . . . And now Conyers, Ga. The storm has hit home. The shootings at Heritage High School, 25 miles east of downtown Atlanta, refreshed all of those emotions --- fear, shock, outrage --- that had begun to ease a month after the tragedy at Columbine High School. And today, or next week, it could happen again at another school, in another region. But things are changing. As shots were being fired at Heritage High, senators in Washington were preparing to debate whether to require background checks on gun sales at firearms shows. Sen. Larry Craig, an Idaho Republican and member of the board of the National Rifle Association, had bragged just a day earlier that he and his colleagues would make no more concessions to those advocating tighter gun- safety laws. They were drawing the line. The Senate erased Craig's line, voting overwhelmingly for a Republican measure requiring background checks on all transactions at gun shows and at pawnshops. Craig was one of 21 senators, all Republican, who voted against the measure. Sen. Paul Coverdell of Georgia joined him in that vote. Later, the Senate approved an even stricter amendment, thanks to a vote by Vice President Al Gore, who broke a 50-50 tie. Coverdell voted against that bill as well.

1669.text: GUN-2867 PRIMARY FREMONT TO RESTRICT FIREARM SALES LAW TO BAN NEW LICENSES FOR RESIDENTIAL-AREA DEALERS Joining a growing movement among Bay Area cities, Fremont will ban the sale of firearms from people's houses. The city council is expected next week to approve a moratorium on issuing new business licenses for gun dealing in residential areas. That will be followed sometime within the next four months by an ordinance -- which the council late Tuesday night ordered the city attorney's office to draft -- that will ban firearm sales in residential areas and require all gun dealers, regardless of location, to obtain $1 million liability insurance. Some residential dealers will be allowed to phase out their businesses, but the council hasn't decided how long the phase-out will last. Council members said they wanted to give that consideration to the 33 dealers who have all the required federal, state and local licenses. The other 86 dealers have violated state law by failing to obtain a city business license, police said. Angry gun dealers who attended the Tuesday night council meeting charged that the pending ban is the first step toward gun control in the Bay Area's fourth-largest city, but council members characterized the move as proper land-use planning. "It's the politically correct thing to do," said Walter Schoenmann, one of the 127 federally licensed gun dealers now in business in residential areas. Schoenmann and other gun dealers say police have yet to document any complaints or problems attributable to them.

1665.text: GUN-1133 PRIMARY Gun-Control Bill Passes Its First Hurdle In the first test of national gun control legislation since President Ronald Reagan's endorsement last month, a House subcommittee approved a measure today to create a seven-day waiting period to buy a handgun. At the same time, the Bush Administration suggested in its strongest terms so far that President Bush would back the measure if it was combined with his anti-crime package. Before a 9-to-4 vote to approve the measure, taken by the House Judiciary Committee's subcommittee on crime and criminal justice, Representative Charles E. Schumer, the Brooklyn Democrat who is the panel's chairman, read from a letter sent by Attorney General Dick Thornburgh. Support for Identifying Felons While stopping short of endorsing the handgun bill, the Attorney General said, "The President supports effective measures to identify felons attempting to purchase firearms." Mr. Thornburgh warned that, even so, the President's advisers would urge him to veto any bill that is not part of the Administration's anti-crime package, a set of proposals, including a Federal death penalty, that many Democrats oppose as inadequate to deter crime. Mr. Thornburgh said that he had concerns about a waiting period and that he would favor a system in which gun sellers could immediately identify buyers. But even Administration officials have conceded that it could be years before such a system could be effectively put into use. Brushing aside the veto threat, the bill's supporters described the letter as an important concession by the Administration. Saying that the letter marked a "shift in position," Mr. Schumer asserted it "indicated for the first time that the President is not opposed to the concept of the bill per se."

1671.text: GUN-6209 PRIMARY THE SECOND AMENDMENT COULD USE 1990S CLARIFICATION In order to receive a weapon permit in Great Britain, a person must have a very convincing reason. Even the police are unarmed. This is not because the government is authoritarian or tyrannical; it is because the democratic government and the people do not want angry lovers, playful children or madmen to be able to get their hands on a murder weapon with ease. Their low crime-fatality figures show that this works. The writer of the Aug. 18 letter ''Even if a gun ban passed, it would fail'' would do well to read all of the Second Amendment, especially ''A well-regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free state . . '' This is the qualifying part that comes before ''the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed.'' The drafters of this amendment wanted the members of a well-regulated militia to have their weapons at hand at all times to defend the state. We now have local and state police, not to mention the National Guard, that fulfill the functions of a militia. It is dangerous for citizens and to the state to have unregulated groups and individuals running around with hatred in their hearts and powerful guns in their hands. Thus, the Constitution should be amended to permit only non-automatic hunting weapons and sports target guns in safety-regulated settings.

1672.text: GUN-7093 PRIMARY Disarming Massachusetts may once again have fired the shot heard round the world. This time, it's the gun control world, and words were the weapons, not colonial muskets. The Bay State has laid down regulations that comprise the strictest gun control measures in the country. They went into effect immediately when Attorney General Thomas Reilly announced them. Now, all guns in Massachusetts have to have trigger locks, tamper-proof serial numbers and devices that show whether they are loaded. And Saturday night specials, the handguns favored by criminals, are banned. The tough measures are already court-tested. After two years of litigation brought by pro-gun groups, the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court ruled that the attorney general can lay down the law where firearms are concerned. The gun control community, stymied by the National Rifle Association in its congressional battles, is delighted. The genius of the Massachusetts solution is that it simply bypasses all the obstacles the gun lobby habitually throws in the way of attempts to limit gun sales. The Second Amendment, which contains the lethal phrase "the right of the people to . . . bear Arms" has no place in the discussion. The NRA is still engaged in trying to persuade people that the redcoats and the Indians--who were on the Founders' minds when they spoke of the importance of "a well-regulated militia"--are still around. Massachusetts ignored all that by decreeing that guns are consumer products like all others, and must meet certain safety standards.

1670.text: GUN-3924 PRIMARY SCRAP GUN VOTE, PANEL URGED Backers of a bill on concealed weapons urged a House committee Tuesday to scrap plans for a state referendum on the gun issue. An election would cost millions of dollars and is unneeded, judging from the popularity of concealed-weapons laws in other states, the backers said. "We've elected you people to pass laws for us," said Lee Koury, a supporter of the bill. Koury, a former U.S. marshal in Kansas City, added, "Don't pass it back to the citizens." Opponents found that position illogical. "You don't think people have enough sense to vote, but you want them to interject with guns" to deter crimes, said Rep. Tim Harlan, D-Columbia. The Senate has passed a bill letting voters decide whether sheriffs should issue concealed-weapons permits to adults who pass background checks. The bill, which calls for an election in April 1996, is now before the House Judiciary and Ethics Committee. The committee chairman, Rep. Gary Witt, D-Platte City, said he supported the concealed-weapons concept but plans to fine-tune it and present a draft, possibly by next week. He said he might raise the $ 50 application fee and tighten the requirement that applicants demonstrate gun safety or take a training course. Witt said he had no strong feelings about the referendum but believes backers would be smart to keep it. "Without a referendum, the bill will die," Witt predicted. The leading opponent, Senate Majority Leader J.B. "Jet" Banks, D-St. Louis, "will kill it or the governor will veto it," Witt said.

1676.text: GUN-8101 PRIMARY No threat of confiscation from gun legislation The legislation is meant to combat illegal gun trafficking by registering handguns to allow law enforcement to more easily trace handguns used in crimes. It also puts in place tougher penalties for those who sell guns to criminals. It is a common sense approach that 76 percent of Americans support. Yet a small minority have used the inflammatory argument that registration will lead to confiscation to oppose the bill. For more than 60 years law-abiding citizens have purchased and registered machine guns, grenades and short-barreled shotguns, and there has been no effort to seize these weapons. The registration of handguns will not lead to confiscation: It will mean that more criminals who use handguns and those who sell guns to criminals will go to jail. Handgun registration is desperately needed in America, and I applaud Sen. Reed for introducing this legislation. And I invite others to sign Sen. Reed's online petition in support of handgun registration at www.gunregistration.org. BERNADETTE PAGE

1667.text: NA-43054 PRIMARY Weekend Gun Report: April 25-27, 2014 "Gun control supporters have the blood of little children on their hands." That quote, by Larry Pratt, the executive director of Gun Owners of America, opens an ad by a group called Everytown for Gun Safety, a new organization combining Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America and Mayors Against Illegal Guns. Pratt issued the quote the day after the Newtown massacre, and the ad, juxtaposed with images of people who have lost family members to gun violence and survivors revealing scars from bullet wounds, is jarring. The ad was timed to the N.R.A.'s 143rd annual meeting, which was held this weekend in Indianapolis, a city grappling with a spate of gun violence. More than 75,000 people showed up to hear Wayne LaPierre, Sarah Palin and Oliver North talk about the Second Amendment. "Yeah, I carry a gun because a cop is too heavy," Palin said amid cheers. Everytown staged a protest near the convention center and held a press conference during which they released a 21-page report, "Not Your Grandparents' N.R.A." "The N.R.A. that was created after the Civil War to promote hunting, marksmanship, and safety training - and that supported common sense gun laws - no longer exists," the report reads. The N.R.A.'s leadership fights for the interests of gun manufacturers under the guise of defending citizens' constitutional freedoms, and makes it easier for felons to get guns, according to the report. The N.R.A. even backed a measure in Indiana that would have expanded Stand Your Ground to include using lethal force against uniformed police officers, the group says.

characters: Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America

1673.text: NA-41852 PRIMARY N.J. Senate passes package of gun measures TRENTON - New Jersey has some of the toughest gun laws in the nation, and they are likely to get tougher still. The state Senate on Thursday gave final passage to a bill banning the sale of .50-caliber rifles, high-powered weapons that are accurate to more than one mile and popular with some firearms enthusiasts. The measure was included in a package of gun measures crafted by Senate Democrats after the massacre at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn., on Dec. 14. It passed largely along party lines. The Senate also passed a bill calling for encoding firearms permit information on driver's licenses and requiring gun purchasers to take a firearms safety course. "This is a far-reaching plan to reduce gun violence that will serve as a national model for gun safety," Senate President Stephen M. Sweeney (D., Gloucester) said of the package of gun bills. "We are accomplishing here in New Jersey what couldn't get done in Washington." Gov. Christie has his own plan, including measures that would make it a crime to provide a weapon to a convicted criminal, bolster penalties for illegal gun trafficking, and make it easier to confine and treat psychiatrically disturbed people who pose a risk of gun violence. Christie has voiced support for a ban on .50-caliber rifles. But a spokesman said Thursday that the governor had not committed to any specific measures proposed by the Democrats.

characters: National Rifle Association (implied, but not directly mentioned)

1675.text: GUN-5122 PRIMARY Editorial; Tragic duo: Guns, kids An angry seventh-grader warned friends in Jonesboro, Ark., that he "had a lot of killing to do." Tragically, the 13-year-old suspect may have had a lot of guns with which to do it. Accused of opening fire Tuesday on classmates at Westside Middle School, he and his 11-year-old cousin, also a suspect, were apparently able sharpshooters. Four girls and an English teacher who shielded a student died in the ambush. Eleven others were wounded. Once again, a nation mourns the senseless loss of innocent lives. The shooting was at least the third fatal attack in a school in the past five months. As with the deadly rampages in schools in West Paducah, Ky., and Pearl, Miss., the first question is: "Why?" Experts point to a generation of damaged and dangerous children who are underexposed to caring adults, overexposed to guns and incubated in a culture of violence. As the Georgia Legislature demonstrated only last week, our national fixation on guns and the political power of the gun lobby combine to make it hard to take even the most basic step in trying to keep firearms out of the hands of children. A bill to require parents to secure their guns from children went down in fiery defeat, and the National Rifle Association went home happy. The pro-gun camp will respond to the Arkansas massacre with bumper sticker patter: "Guns don't kill people. People kill people." But how many more children would be alive today in Jonesboro if two children didn't have access to an arsenal?

1677.text: GUN-9699 PRIMARY Effort to Renew Weapons Ban Falters on Hill Despite widespread popular support, the federal law banning the sale of 19 kinds of semiautomatic assault weapons is almost certain to expire on Monday, the result of intense lobbying by the National Rifle Association and the complicated election-year politics of Washington. While President Bush has expressed support for legislation extending the ban and has said he would sign it into law, he has not pressured lawmakers to act, leading critics to accuse him of trying to have it both ways. Efforts to renew the ban, which polls show is supported by at least two-thirds of Americans, have faltered this year on Capitol Hill. Democrats are well aware that they lost control of the House of Representatives in 1994, the year President Bill Clinton signed the original legislation, and have shied away from the issue of gun control, while Republican leaders have opposed the ban. ''I think the will of the American people is consistent with letting it expire, so it will expire,'' Senator Bill Frist of Tennessee, the majority leader, said on Wednesday. The House majority leader, Representative Tom DeLay of Texas, dismissed the ban as ''a feel-good piece of legislation'' and said flatly that it would expire Monday, even if Mr. Bush made an effort to renew it. ''If the president asked me, it would still be no,'' Mr. DeLay said. '' He knows, because we don't have the votes to pass the assault weapons ban. It will expire Monday, and that's that.''

1674.text: GUN-4233 PRIMARY Gun Alert for Annapolis THOUGH IMPORTANT relief seems to be on the way for Marylanders concerned about guns in the hands of criminals, watch out: The modified anti-gunrunner bill now backed by the governor and the state Senate still could be ambushed in the House of Delegates this week. Any change in the language of the bill at this point would explode the agreement that was struck to get it through the state Senate. The House Judiciary Committee is scheduled to take up the bill today while the Senate is set for a final floor vote Tuesday. If the measure clears these checkpoints, it will up to Speaker Taylor and the rest of the House leaders to protect the bill from lethal amendments. There is no good reason to change the measure. If any lawmaker has other ideas for protecting the public from armed criminals, fine; save them for another bill. This one is strong enough on its own to win the support of Sen. Walter Baker, chairman of the Senate committee that has killed countless handgun bills over the years. Sen. Baker has concluded that a one-handgun-a-month purchase limit and provisions to guard against sales by private gun owners to criminals as well as to prohibit straw purchases are effective moves against handgun traffickers who supply criminals. That's what Sen. Baker and Gov. Glendening agreed to, with an understanding that if there were any subsequent change in the package that Sen. Baker could not accept, the governor would veto it. The result is an important public safety bill -- not a gun ban, not a confiscation proposal and not a change in anything to do with rifles and other long guns used for sport.

1678.text: GUN-517 PRIMARY Automatic Sentences For All Gun Crimes To the Editor: If James S. Brady's zeal in ''Congress Didn't Want to See'' (Op-Ed, April 3) is to curtail the criminal, not the law-abiding citizen, why not propose a Federal law that automatically sentences a person caught committing a felony with a firearm - automatic, semiautomatic, single or double-action revolver, rifle or shotgun - to a 10-year prison term without parole? More than 70 percent of command officers in state, county and local police agencies responding to a postal survey last year by the National Association of Chiefs of Police do not believe a waiting period to purchase a firearm will have any effect on criminals getting firearms; more than 55 percent do not believe a seven-day handgun purchase waiting period is enough time to conduct an investigation; more than 62 percent believe waiting periods should be regulated by states; more than 68 percent believe that law-abiding citizens have the right to purchase any type of firearm for sport or self-defense, and more than 86 percent do not believe banning semiautomatic rifles or shotguns would hamper criminals in obtaining them. ROBERT J. PHILHOWER Hawthorne, N.J., April 14, 1990

characters: National Association of Chiefs of Police

1680.text: GUN-11393 PRIMARY CU: Guns won't cancel class BOULDER University of Colorado Chancellor Phil DiStefano notified the Boulder campus faculty Tuesday afternoon that professors "do not have the right to shut down a class or refuse to teach" should they learn that one of their students is lawfully carrying a gun under a concealed-carry permit. And, DiStefano added, any faculty members who do so will be in violation of their contracts and face disciplinary action. DiStefano's message comes a day after Professor Jerry Peterson, chairman of the Boulder Faculty Assembly, told the Camera that, under his own "personal policy," he plans to cancel class if he ever learns any of his students are carrying firearms. A Colorado Supreme Court ruling this spring overturned CU's Boulder campus gun ban, and university officials say that students with concealed-carry permits are allowed to bring guns into classrooms and labs. "I have the utmost respect for Professor Peterson, who is an old friend and valued colleague, but I want to make clear that if the student carrying the weapon has a concealed-carry permit, the position implied by Professor Peterson's comments directly violates Colorado law and the operating principles of the campus," DiStefano wrote in the e-mail to faculty. Peterson stood by his classroom policy Tuesday and said he's never had to cancel a class. The physics professor -- who has said he simply wants his students to feel safe to engage in discussions that could become controversial -- reiterated that the presence of guns in his classroom "would destroy the learning environment."

1684.text: NA-43973 PRIMARY Obama returns to Connecticut to press Congress on guns WEST HARTFORD, Conn. - With his proposed gun regulations in serious jeopardy, President Obama delivered an impassioned and urgent plea here Monday evening for swift action, telling a state still shaken by the Newtown school massacre, "We've got to expect more from Congress." The call for stricter gun laws - his most forceful yet - came in a campaign-style event in front of several thousand people packed into the University of Hartford's basketball arena. Obama recalled December's slaughter of 20 small children and six adults at Sandy Hook Elementary School as the toughest day of his presidency. "If we don't respond to this," he added, "that will be a tough day for me, too." "We've got to expect more from ourselves," he continued. "And we've got to expect more from Congress. We've got to believe that, you know, well, every once in a while we set politics aside and we just do what's right." Obama, who met before his speech with parents of slain children, also vowed not to forget the Connecticut slayings. He wore a green bracelet, while many in the audience wore green ribbons, honoring the school's colors. "Newtown, we want you to know that we're here with you," Obama said. "We will not walk away from the promises we've made. We are as determined as ever to do what must be done."

1679.text: GUN-31252 PRIMARY Senate Narrowly Defeats Conceal-Carry Measure An amendment that would have allowed gun owners to carry their weapons across state lines fell just short of passage Wednesday in a vote that revealed deep divisions among the Senate's Democrats. Supporters included all but two Republicans and 20 Democrats, but the vote of 58 to 39 in favor fell two short of the 60 needed to defeat a filibuster. Despite its defeat, the amendment, sponsored by Sen. John Thune (R-S.D.), demonstrated the continuing power of the National Rifle Association and the gun rights issue in Congress. Rather than a setback, those backing the effort consider the vote a sign of strength for the Second Amendment and are planning more gun-related amendments to other legislation throughout the year. Afterward, Thune said he hopes the Senate will "reconsider this important issue" later this year. It split not only Democrats, many of whom got to the Senate by supporting gun rights, but also the caucus's leadership: Majority Leader Harry M. Reid (Nev.), campaigning for reelection in 2010, voted yes, while his top lieutenants, Sens. Richard J. Durbin (Ill.) and Charles E. Schumer (N.Y.), led the push by liberal Democrats against the measure. Offered as an amendment to the annual defense authorization bill, the legislation would have allowed people to carry concealed firearms across state lines, provided they "have a valid permit or if, under their state of residence" they "are entitled to do so." It was considered one of the most far-reaching federal efforts ever proposed to expand gun-permitting laws.

1685.text: GUN-11492 PRIMARY Shooting blanks Hope rose when President Obama spoke for the first time about gun control since the Aurora massacre. With dead aim, he told the National Urban League: "I believe the Second Amendment guarantees an individual the right to bear arms, but I also believe that a lot of gun owners would agree that AK-47s belong in the hands of soldiers, not on the streets of our cities." The President then went on to say, "We should check someone's criminal record before they can check out a gun seller, that a mentally unbalanced individual should not be able to get his hands on a gun so easily." Then the White House clarified that Obama would not - repeat, not - seek new gun laws. Not when he's in a tight reelection fight with Mitt Romney, another summer soldier who has bowed to the National Rifle Association. So callow have both men been that movie producer Harvey Weinstein told the Huffington Post that "it's time to put up or shut up about gun control for both parties. Mitt Romney better outline where he stands, and people know that I'm a firm supporter of the President and I believe he's got to do the same." Good for Weinstein because America needs a broad campaign to win legislation that would not in any way infringe on Second Amendment rights. Weinstein also called for a Hollywood summit on movie violence - and who could be against toning down bloodshed whether or not it can spark real-life mayhem?

1682.text: GUN-8275 PRIMARY Guns Took Unusual Route to Newark; Ex-Manager of Warehouse Is Charged Most illegal guns reach the streets of New Jersey after being bought legally in Southern states with relaxed gun laws, then smuggled north by car, federal officials say. But up to 120 9-millimeter and .40-caliber handguns took a more direct route, shipped via United Parcel Service from a warehouse in Virginia to homes in Newark by the former manager of the warehouse, according to charges filed this week in Federal District Court here. The manager, Mario Guzman Jr., 26, of Newark, was arrested on Tuesday on federal charges that he shipped the guns from a warehouse owned by the gun manufacturer, Heckler & Koch, in Sterling, Va. He was ordered held in lieu of $100,000 bail today, after pleading not guilty before Judge Susan D. Davis in federal court. Judge Davis allowed Mr. Guzman's release on the condition that court officials certify that no guns, legal or otherwise, be kept at the Newark home of his mother, Luvinda Guzman. Mr. Guzman's lawyer said his client planned to stay with his mother after his release. Mr. Guzman has no prior arrest record, said his lawyer, John D. Caruso. The investigation began after Heckler & Koch conducted a routine inventory of the warehouse last winter and found that one box of 40 weapons was missing. Officials said Mr. Guzman was asked to account for it, and told the company, falsely, that it had been found.

1683.text: NA-44451 PRIMARY D.C. should control its gun laws I would like to remind Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) that the U.S. Capitol building is in the District of Columbia and therefore should be subject to the laws of the District ["House passes measure to gut city's gun laws," Metro, July 17]. If Massie, who offered the amendment preventing the city from spending money to enforce gun laws, thinks that gun-control laws "prevent good people from being able to protect themselves" and that people should be able to own assault rifles and carry guns everywhere, shouldn't that apply to everyone, in every place? If he truly believes that carrying a gun makes one safer, then members of Congress, their staff and all visitors to the Capitol should be able to carry guns. Get rid of the metal detectors and bag searches. To do otherwise is the height of hypocrisy. Melissa Yorks , Gaithersburg l As a supporter both of the Second Amendment's right to bear arms and of voting rights for the citizens of the District of Columbia, I found it disheartening to read about congressional Republicans' misguided attempts to impose their public policy preferences on the District ["Firearms-related amendment to D.C. budget riles city officials," Metro, July 18]. If Congress believes that Second Amendment rights are threatened and only federal gun control laws should exist, then it should pass legislation that applies to the entire country. Absent nationwide legislation, self-defense advocates resemble oppressors, not persuaders.

1681.text: GUN-51 PRIMARY US Sup Ct agrees to decide if key provision of '68 Fed Firearms Control Law violates Const's privilege against self incrimination; issue comes to Ct as result of Los Angeles Fed Dist Judge Ferguson's ruling that threw out indictments against D Freed and S J Sutherland who had been charged with having attempted to purchase hand grenades from undercover police agents; Ferguson declared unconst sec of firearms control law that requires venders of such dangerous devices as hand grenades, bazookas and machine guns to record name and addresses of all purchasers; held that this could force purchasers to give information that could incriminate them under state and Fed gun control legis; Justice Dept, in asking Ct to rev case, notes that if ruling is allowed to stand it could cast doubt on principle that individuals may constitutionally be punished for possessing weapons that were obtained without compliance with law, which is crucial to all gun control legis; US Sup Ct grants hearing to Madison Av art dealer S Radich, who was convicted under NYS's law against flag desecration after he exhibited M Morrell sculptures, Dec '66, in which flag was used to depict male sex organ and express antiwar themes; ACLU asks ct to declare that use of flag in artistic objects is protected by 1st amendment

1691.text: NA-45953 PRIMARY After Newtown, how to reduce gun violence? Six months after the shootings in Newtown, Conn., we asked our Twitter followers: What's the best way to address gun violence? Members of Congress and states have done exactly what their voters wanted on the issue of guns. It differs by state. @MaggioMatt Lawmakers should allow everyone who passes a background check and a qualified gun-training course to own a gun. @JAZZYUSA More gun control is not the solution. People are thinking with their emotions. We need to think with our brains. @gregoryakoch There will always be crazy people, and there will always be illegal guns. Time is better spent defending yourself. @KandisBonnet To reduce gun deaths,  measure sentences for violent felonies in decades, not years or months. @billytheimpaler For more of this discussion, follow @USATOpinion or #tellusatoday on Twitter.

1687.text: GUN-30340 PRIMARY DON'T NEED HIZZONER ON STAND, GUN SUIT JUDGE SEZ A BROOKLYN judge yesterday booted Mayor Bloomberg off the witness list of the city's upcoming trial against a Georgia gun dealer. "I'm not going to permit the case to turn into a media show for either side," federal Judge Jack Weinstein said. "I see no reason to have the mayor here. From the city's point of view, he doesn't add a thing to your case." The judge's decision caught both sides by surprise. Neither had objected to the star witness taking the stand on June 3, after opening arguments in the civil trial. Bloomberg sued 27 gun dealers in 2006, accusing them of contributing to violence in New York City by facilitating straw gun sales. The owner of Adventure Outdoors in Smyrna, Ga., is fighting the allegations, while most of the other gun shops have settled with the city. The mayor was to testify about attending police officers' funerals and consoling their widows as a result of illegal gun violence, city attorney Kenneth Taber said. John Renzulli, the lawyer for Adventure Outdoors owner Jay Wallace, said he was "unbelievably disappointed" he wouldn't get the chance to cross-examine Bloomberg in front of the jury. "At the end of the day, the jury would have seen what Mayor Bloomberg has said is not reality," Renzulli said, who grilled the mayor for over seven hours at a deposition.

1686.text: GUN-7151 PRIMARY Something for Everyone to Criticize in Gun Bill It began as a plan to require that only high-tech, personalized smart guns be sold in Maryland. But now, stripped of that proposal, Gov. Parris N. Glendening's gun control bill is being nibbled at the edges by both opponents and supporters as it faces a critical House committee vote today. Some don't like the legislation because it includes mandatory sentences for gun violations. Others are critical of provisions they say could open juvenile criminal records now sealed by law. Still others say the proposal won't do what Glendening (D) says it will: require built-in locks on all new handguns sold in Maryland beginning in 2003. And the House vote was supposed to be the easy one. The governor removed the personalized gun requirement from his proposal to win approval in the Senate, where the legislation was in jeopardy of being voted down. House Speaker Casper R. Taylor Jr. (D-Allegany) asked the Senate to take up the legislation first, figuring that the House would approve whatever passed muster there. But now, even some gun control proponents want to make changes to Glendening's bill. If they do, the legislation would go back to the Senate, where opponents would get another chance to filibuster or kill it as the General Assembly enters the final week of this year's session. Del. Sharon Grosfeld (D-Montgomery), who serves on the House Judiciary Committee, which considers the bill today, will urge her colleagues not to amend the bill. "It will serve the greater good as it is drafted," she said. "Any amendments attempting to make the bill perfect would defeat the greater good."

characters: House Speaker Casper R. Taylor Jr.

1690.text: GUN-10747 PRIMARY "Castle-doctrine" defense arises in deadly North Philly shooting When Gov. Corbett signed a law June 28 expanding the right to use deadly force outside the home, gun-control proponents predicted every thug would have a new defense to pulling the trigger. It didn't take long. Just eight days after the new "castle doctrine" law took effect, it has been raised in the defense of a North Philadelphia man charged with killing a neighbor over $100 owed in the purchase of a pit bull puppy. To be sure, there are questions about whether Lydell Johnson, 23, would qualify to use the defense in the shooting of Shane McCreery Jr., 32. First, there is the Aug. 3 date - after Corbett signed the law but before it took effect at the end of August. Steve Miskin, spokesman for state House Majority Leader Mike Turzai (R., Allegheny), said the 60-day waiting period was firm, but he acknowledged that lawyers could argue the defense in court. In Pennsylvania and most other states, the law traditionally held that people's homes were their castles and that they might use deadly force to defend it against an intruder.
