At least two dozen states go further, not imposing a "duty to retreat" inside the house before firing. Pennsylvania's law goes further still, giving people the right to use deadly force if confronted in their cars, on the street, or anywhere they "have the legal right to be."

1689.text: GUN-2189 PRIMARY Virginia Aims to Shed Image as a 'Handgun Supermarket' A few months ago the Federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms undertook a study of gunrunning on the East Coast, focusing mainly on tracing the origins of handguns seized at crime scenes. The findings startled even the most hardened law-enforcement officials in Virginia. In New York City, the investigators found, one of every four guns whose origins could be determined had been bought in Virginia stores. And in Washington, often called the murder capital of the nation, one in three traceable guns had been bought in Virginia. Concerned and embarrassed by the study's findings, Gov. L. Douglas Wilder and some Virginia legislators and law-enforcement officers are moving to tighten regulations on buying guns. The 'Grim Reaper State' "Virginia is the No. 1 source state for handguns on the East Coast," the Governor said in a recent speech, "and we must stop the trafficking or become known as the 'Grim Reaper State.' " Arguing that Virginia laws permit gunrunners to buy weapons literally by the car load, Mr. Wilder has called for a law that would limit sales to one gun a month for each customer. He has also asked for laws to tighten record-keeping on handgun sales. State law permits any Virginia resident who has not been convicted of a felony to buy an unlimited number of guns as often as desired. To find out whether a prospective buyer has a criminal record, gun dealers rely on a computerized system operated by the state police. Virginia was one of the first states to set up such a system.

1688.text: NA-46217 PRIMARY It's tough to live up to 'Mayberry' The headline says it all: "Moving to Nelson? Pack a gun." The Atlanta Journal-Constitution ran that front-page story this month about the small town of Nelson, Ga., located 54 miles north of Atlanta. The story chronicled how the town's council recently passed a law requiring the head of every household to own a gun and bullets. Local leaders say the law was meant as a security measure. But it also made a strong statement about possible federal gun restrictions. In discussing the town and his support of the new law, Nelson's police chief, B. Heath Mitchell, noted that Nelson was a place full of good, law-abiding folks. In fact, "we're quieter than Mayberry," he said. OK, OK, the chief was using a bit of poetic license here. But since he compared his town to one of the best-known locales on the planet --- even if it is just a too-good-to-be-true TV town --- we thought we'd go ahead and give it a spin in the AJC Truth-O-Meter. And anyway, a fact check that requires watching episodes of "The Andy Griffith Show" is one we couldn't pass up. Nelson has become a media magnet ever since the town passed the gun law. Requests for comment came from as far as Germany and the United Kingdom. Nelson's new law is similar to a 1982 law passed in Kennesaw, Ga. Like Kennesaw, Nelson ultimately has no intention of enforcing the law, but hopes to send a message to possible criminals, council members said.

1693.text: GUN-10966 PRIMARY Big foes await gun,E-Verify bills ; The tourism and farm industries see potential for economic losses. From the farm fields of Belle Glade to the nightspots of Clematis Street, Palm Beach County business owners are expressing concern about bills being considered in Tallahassee. Two proposals in particular -- one dealing with gun rights and the other with an Arizona-style immigration law -- have made entrepreneurs in tourism and agriculture uncomfortable. And because those are the two largest industries in Florida -- a state besieged by economic problems -- the sponsors can expect an earful from businessmen. One concerned businessman is Jim Brew. Brew has the perfect name for a guy who manages a popular Clematis Street bar and restaurant specializing in microbrewery beer. Despite the recession, trade was brisk at his place of business -- Grease Burger Bar -- during a recent lunch hour. But he was afraid trouble could be brewing up in Tallahassee, not only for him but for other businesses that depend on sidewalk traffic and tourist trade. The problem as he sees it is a bill proposed by Sen. Greg Evers, R-Crestfield, that would allow licensed gun owners to carry their weapons on their hips in public, otherwise known as "open carry." Brew would be able to keep gun toters out of his barroom, but he also does good business at his sidewalk tables and that's where he sees issues: people with guns in full view passing close by.

1694.text: GUN-4204 PRIMARY Vigils focus on gun violence Activists gather at shooting sites JEFFERSON COUNTY - To sound the alarm about gun violence, activists held Good Friday vigils yesterday at fatal shooting sites throughout the metro area - including the Albertson's store at South Kipling Street and West Bowles Avenue where Albert Petrosky allegedly shot and killed three people. "This was a terrible tragedy and one of the reasons we decided to end our journey here," said the Rev. Bruce Inglis, a minister at Washington Park United Church of Christ. This is the second year the group has held Good Friday vigils at fatal shooting sites, and it is the first year they gathered outside of Denver. Violence in suburbs, too "We wanted to come to the suburbs this year because we wanted to highlight the fact that violence is here as well as in the urban areas," Inglis said. "It is a suburban and rural problem." The activists' views vary - from a desire that all guns be banned to wanting stricter gun-control laws. The vigils are called Stations of the Cross in honor of Christ's 14 Stations of the Cross, which correspond to 14 events that happened to Christ on Good Friday, Inglis said. Visited six sites More than 50 gun-control advocates visited six sites yesterday, including the Chuck E Cheese's in Aurora where Nathan Dunlap shot four people to death. Many pro-gun advocates characterize perpetrators of gun violence as being hardened criminals, Inglis said. "It's not the so-called thugs that are problems; it's all of us."

1695.text: GUN-2548 PRIMARY For Bradys, A Gamble On Gun Bill Pays Off On the morning after, James Brady slept in. But his wife, Sarah, was up and reveling in the late-night Saturday turnaround victory in the Senate for the gun-control measure that she and her husband had labored to pass for more than five grueling years. "It was like a Dewey-Truman story," Mrs. Brady said in a telephone interview this morning. Mrs. Brady's reference to Harry S. Truman's remarkable eleventh-hour comeback victory over Thomas E. Dewey in the 1948 Presidential election was apt. The bill had been pronounced dead on Friday -- and again as late as Saturday afternoon -- by both lawmakers and analysts. Then it suddenly was revived in a legislative and emotional roller-coaster ride for the Bradys. The signs for the legislation had been positive this year, and the bill passed the House on Nov. 11. But the Bradys said that all last week, while awaiting Senate action, they had been had been feeling "lurkey," a Bradyism that rhymes with "turkey" and means an ice-water feeling in the heart. A Tumultuous Week "It's just been a week of so many highs and lows," Mrs. Brady, who heads Handgun Control Inc., a national organization of gun-control advocates, said this morning. Her husband was still asleep, recuperating from a late-night news conference with Senator Howard O. Metzenbaum, a strong supporter of the Brady bill. The bill is named for Mr. Brady, the former Presidential press secretary who was critically wounded in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan. He has had to use a wheelchair ever since.

1697.text: GUN-11673 PRIMARY SCOTT DENIES GUN BAN FOR RNC Saying he will not allow Tampa to disarm citizens throughout its downtown, Gov. Rick Scott shot down the city's request to ban concealed weapons outside the Republican National Convention. Mayor Bob Buckhorn asked Scott on Tuesday for an executive order authorizing the ban. In a two-page response dated the same day, Scott said no, that's too much. Already, he said, the Secret Service will ban guns inside the convention's security perimeter. "You are now requesting that citizens be disarmed in all of downtown Tampa," Scott wrote. The governor said he shares Buckhorn's concerns about the dangers of violent antigovernment protests during the Aug. 27-30 convention in Tampa. "But it is unclear how disarming law-abiding citizens would better protect them from the dangers and threats posed by those who would flout the law," he said. "It is at just such times that the constitutional right to self-defense is most precious and must be protected from government overreach." On Wednesday, the mayor said he was disappointed but not surprised. "He's made no bones about where he stands on Second Amendment issues," Buckhorn said. Also disappointed was City Council member Lisa Montelione, who is not ready to give up. Montelione said she will urge her colleagues to ask the Secret Service to use its authority to ban concealed weapons around the convention, as well as inside it. "To me, that's the only hope we have left of reducing the number of firearms in that general vicinity," she said.

1696.text: GUN-2198 PRIMARY Report Criticizes U.S. Licensing of Gun Dealers A Washington research group on violence called the federal system for licensing gun dealers yesterday "a public safety scandal" and said the U.S. government makes only cursory checks of applicants and routinely licenses people who are criminals or who sell guns to criminals. The Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF) has licensed in excess of 200,000 gun dealers, more than the number of gasoline stations in the country, said officials at the Violence Policy Center in a 138-page report released yesterday. The study, published less than two weeks after a Washington Post series probed the same issue, said disreputable gun dealers often obtain guns wholesale through the mail, then sell the weapons without adhering to local and state laws, some of which require waiting periods and background checks of buyers. In addition, the study said, those with felony records have received gun dealer licenses by getting relatives and associates to apply for them. The licensing system "is a public scandal created by the very agency charged with enforcing federal firearms laws," said Josh Sugarmann, executive director of the center, a national education foundation that conducts research on firearms violence and supports gun control. "By giving a federal gun-dealing license to virtually anyone who can come up with $ 30 and isn't a convicted felon, ATF has put criminals in the business of selling guns."

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF)

1698.text: NA-41048 PRIMARY NEWS READERS: DUMP THE GUNS Nearly 127K sign petition Schumer lauds 'overwheliming response' NEARLY 127,000 outraged Daily News readers have united their voices to deliver a simple message: Enough is enough. The concerned people - now totaling 126,952 from all 50 states - signed The News' petition in the wake of the deadly Newtown, Conn., school shooting rampage to demand lawmakers finally toughen gun control laws. They want to be heard, from Albany to Washington. They want assault weapons banned. They want high-capacity magazines outlawed. And they want it now! "Stop the murderers, Congress, or you are complicit!" Diane Wiener, of Ozone Park Park, Queens, wrote on The News' online petition form. "Right NOW, President and Congress!" added Ethan Odin of Fairfield, Conn. "I have lost family and friends to gun violence," wrote Juan Coleman, of Prospect Lefferts Gardens, Brooklyn. "Thank you for doing something." For many, the horror that engulfed a tiny, picturesque New England town was the final straw. On Dec. 14 - exactly one month ago - 20-year-old Adam Lanza burst into Sandy Hook Elementary School brandishing a Bushmaster XM-15, a 10-mm. Glock handgun and a 9-mm. SIG Sauer handgun. He stalked the halls, shooting anyone in his path. He coldly walked into classrooms, places of enrichment and learning, and transformed them into killing fields. He opened fire on innocent young children and veteran teachers, all of whom never stood a chance. By the time he turned the gun on himself, 20 students and six adults were dead.

1700.text: NA-40541 PRIMARY Gun debate revives questions about self-defense The beam from the intruder's flashlight pierced the blackness of the bedroom at 4:45 a.m., sweeping across the down comforter and into Eric Martin's eyes. Outside, the streets of his Utah subdivision lay still and silent. But as Martin rolled to the floor, reached into the nightstand drawer and drew out his 9 mm pistol, the 46-year-old executive's mind raced with calculation: Would this man harm Martin's fiancée or her son? Was an accomplice outside waiting? What if he pulled the trigger and hit the sleeping 8-year-old across the hall? In the months since the Connecticut school massacre, some of the most intense debate has swirled around how to keep guns from criminals without infringing on the ability of lawful gun owners, like Martin, to protect themselves and their families. Indeed, self-defense is now the top reason gun owners cite for having a firearm, a new survey shows, a figure that has nearly doubled since 1999. But even after years of study, there is little clarity on how, exactly, Americans use guns to protect themselves in moments of jeopardy - or how often. Researchers known for sharp disagreement on the self-defense riddle say the answers may be shifting dramatically because of a steep drop in crime, an increase in guns and state laws giving owners more leeway to wield them. Determining the absolute value of guns for self-defense is clouded by that complex dynamic of policy, judgment and circumstance. Still, advocates of gun rights and of gun control understand the issue's importance in shaping the debate.

1699.text: NA-42941 PRIMARY The Gun Report: February 14, 2014 On Thursday, a federal appeals court in California struck down rules permitting counties to restrict the right to carry a concealed weapon in public, saying that such restrictions violate the Second Amendment. The decision was hailed by gun rights advocates, while gun control supporters described the ruling as aberrant and reckless. The decision will affect populated areas like Los Angeles, San Diego and San Francisco, but the the rules will remain in effect pending appeals. California generally prohibits carrying guns, loaded or not, in public locations. Before the ban was enacted, state courts routinely upheld restrictions on carrying concealed weapons. Tens of thousands of Connecticut residents appear to be refusing to register powerful assault weapons and high-capacity magazines. A law requiring registration was enacted last April in response to the Sandy Hook massacre, but Dan Haar at The Courant reported that state police had received only 47,916 completed registration forms by the end of last year, just 15 percent of what it should be. Haar estimates that 100,000 gun owners are flouting the law. Mike Lawlor, a criminal justice official, said an aggressive crackdown is unlikely, but those who haven't yet registered may receive a letter. Tony Guglielmo, a Republican state senator on the public safety committee, said most of his friends with military-style rifles such as AR-15s had not come forward. "He made the analogy to prohibition," Guglielmo said of one particular friend. "I said, 'You're talking about civil disobedience,' and he said yes."

1701.text: GUN-31349 PRIMARY Target: Illegal guns; Smart proposals that could deny weapons to possible terrorists MAYORS AGAINST Illegal Guns, a bipartisan coalition of roughly 500 U.S. mayors, has been pushing for smart and sensible law enforcement solutions to reduce the number of illegal guns obtained by criminals or would-be criminals. Last month the mayors renewed their call for adoption of two relatively modest but potentially powerful proposals. The first aims to close the so-called "terror gap" in existing gun laws by prohibiting any one on the country's "no fly" list from being allowed to purchase a gun. Such a prohibition would allow the FBI to stop "people who are too dangerous to get on a plane from buying guns and explosives," said New York Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg, a co-founder of the mayors group. This proposal is a no-brainer. A 2009 report by the General Accountability Office found 865 instances between 2004 and February 2009 where those on the terrorist watch list were able to buy a gun. The law currently bars convicted felons and immigrants who are in this country illegally, among others, from purchasing guns, but no mention is made of a suspected terrorist. Legislation to close this loophole, introduced by Sen. Frank Lautenberg (D-N.J.) and Rep. Carolyn McCarthy (D-N.Y.), has stalled. The mayors are also arguing for elimination of a provision that forces the FBI to destroy within 24 hours any record of a background check performed on someone who passes the screening. The provision is part of the Tiahrt Amendment, a series of measures that place high hurdles in the way of law enforcement officials trying to discern where and when guns used in crimes were sold. Before the Tiahrt Amendment was passed, the FBI would routinely keep records of background checks on file for six months. Had this remained the practice, law enforcement agents who were tracking the activities of Maj. Nidal M. Hasan, the alleged gunman in the Fort Hood massacre, would have had the opportunity to see that he had purchased a weapon in August.

1702.text: GUN-10398 PRIMARY Beyond media hype on Second Amendment Now that the U.S. Supreme Court has decided that the Second Amendment to the Constitution means that individual Americans have a right to bear arms, what can we expect? Those who have no confidence in ordinary Americans may expect a bloodbath, as the benighted masses start shooting each other, now that they can no longer be denied guns by their betters. People who think we shouldn't be allowed to make our own medical decisions, or decisions about which schools our children attend, certainly are not likely to be happy with the idea that we can make our own decisions about how to defend ourselves. When you stop and think about it, there is no obvious reason why issues like gun control should be ideological issues in the first place. It is ultimately an empirical question whether allowing ordinary citizens to have firearms will increase or decrease the amount of violence. Many people who are opposed to gun laws that place severe restrictions on ordinary citizens owning firearms have based themselves on the Second Amendment to the Constitution. But, while the Supreme Court must make the Second Amendment the basis of its rulings on gun control laws, there is no reason why the Second Amendment should be the last word for the voting public. If the end of gun control leads to a bloodbath of runaway shootings, then the Second Amendment can be repealed, just as other constitutional amendments have been repealed. Laws exist for people, not people for laws.

1703.text: GUN-7021 PRIMARY Government, not NRA, enforces gun laws As a long-standing member of the National Rifle Association, I would be remiss to fail to reply to Peter G. Aitken's letter to the editor of April 2, in which he stated that the NRA has "quietly worked behind the scenes, as they have for years, to cripple gun law enforcement" ("NRA lobbying thwarts enforcing gun laws"). He said, "The NRA has been instrumental in limiting the number of ATF agents, and in preventing the agency from modernizing by computerizing its records." Where does Aitken get this misinformation? I am not aware of such activities. On the contrary, I have been aware that the NRA has backed Project Exile, which was adopted in Richmond, Va. Crime has dropped as a result of Project Exile, which requires a minimum number of years in jail for just the possession of a firearm while committing a crime. Criminals interviewed there have said it is dangerous for them to carry a gun. I have heard President Clinton asking for draconian gun laws, but have you heard of the prosecution of any of the hundreds of thousands of felons he said attempted to buy a firearm? Such an attempt has been illegal since 1968. If the government fails to enforce existing laws, why do we need more laws that will not be enforced? Our government has not been effective in enforcing gun laws. Why? Why do law-abiding citizens need to give up the right to protect themselves against criminals? If we are trying to find persons at fault, we should make sure that we blame the right parties. The NRA is not the one to blame.

1705.text: GUN-5975 PRIMARY Gun show loophole is target again One more Tampa Bay community appears ready to tighten the loophole that exempts gun shows from background checks and waiting periods. In November, Florida voters amended the state Constitution to authorize counties to require criminal background checks and a three-day waiting period for firearm sales at gun shows. So far, Hillsborough, Pinellas and Citrus counties have adopted such ordinances. Pasco County officials have indicated they will as well. Now add Hernando County to the list. "I expect to definitely support it," Hernando County Commission Chairwoman Pat Novy said Monday, noting that Hernando voters approved the statewide amendment by a 3-to-1 ratio. Commissioners Chris Kingsley and Paul Sullivan have said they support the change, yielding the necessary three votes. A public hearing is expected by early July. Fire chief: Mapping out schools may fight violence OLDSMAR - Gun laws aren't the only reaction to school shootings in Littleton, Colo., Conyers, Ga., Springfield, Ore., and other suburban and rural communities around the country. In Oldsmar, the Fire Department plans to assemble aerial photographs, site maps and floor plans of the city's three schools and of commercial buildings that house large numbers of children. The information may be invaluable to police in case the unthinkable happens, said fire Chief Scott McGuff. The Fire Department already has emergency plans for responding to fires or hazardous material leaks at schools and businesses. But the plans McGuff would like to create would be more in-depth and would be handed over to law enforcement officers responding to a call about a shooting or other violence at a school.

1707.text: GUN-10945 PRIMARY The Open Forum Gun-clip capacity and the availability of firearms Re: "Clamp down on gun-clip overkill," Jan. 24 editorial. Here we go again! If 33 rounds are too many, how many is enough? Are two too many? Three? Ten? Fifteen? As an example, go back to the assault weapons ban that Congress let die in 2004. If 10 rounds is not too many, why is 11 too many? All you can do is pick an arbitrary number, and those who want to ban all guns will not be happy and those who want no guns laws will not be happy. It's a silly suggestion. If I were bent on doing harm to a large number of people, I could carry three, four or 10 10-round magazines. You can't justify limiting the number of rounds in a handgun magazine, whether it's one, 10 or 33. William P. Schmitt, Buena Vista Re: "The Tucson shooting and the availability of guns," Jan. 18 letter to the editor. Letter-writer Luanne Patton tries to downplay the role of the easy access of guns, claiming guns were just as easy to obtain in the 1960s. But she ignores the fact it was uncommon for people to purchase military assault weapons then. She instead cites the real problems as mental illness and glorified violence. But our easy access enables people with serious mental illnesses to get guns. The gun lobby has fought any attempts to restrict access to anyone other than those who are extremely mentally ill. The Tucson shooter hadn't yet met that definition.

1704.text: GUN-30920 PRIMARY Politics of the Gun Congress has shamefully caved in, yet again, to the gun lobby and abandoned the effort to grant the  long-suffering District of Columbia a voting representative in the House. Hopes for passage were high this year, until the historic measure was poisoned in the Senate with an amendment to strip the district's government of its power to enact responsible gun control laws. Sadly, the district's need for strong controls was dramatized Wednesday when a man the authorities identified as a white supremacist opened fire with a rifle and killed a guard at the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum. The gun lobby galvanized anti-gun control Republicans and timorous Democrats in both houses to stop the representation bill in its tracks. Pleas from district officials about public safety drew no interest from the Senate majority leader, Harry Reid. House Speaker Nancy Pelosi likewise failed to sway Democrats too cowardly to stand up to the gun lobby in next year's elections. Most disappointing, President Obama showed no appetite to confront the gun lobby and campaign for a clean bill. He had already signaled his weakness when he signed a credit card reform law that included another senseless gun lobby diktat -- to allow people to carry loaded guns in national parks. Representative Steny Hoyer conceded on Tuesday that the votes for passage had evaporated. He noted disagreement among city officials. Some wanted acceptance of the amended measure because the city's chance for a voting representative was unlikely to occur again soon.

1708.text: GUN-10377 PRIMARY NRA GIVES SCOTT EDGE OVER MCCOLLUM Bill McCollum, seeking to regain his mantle as the only tried-and-true conservative Republican running for governor, got some bad news Tuesday when the National Rifle Association gave his rival, Rick Scott, a higher ranking. Scott got an A and McCollum a B according to the NRA's grading system. The grades came after what NRA lobbyist Marion Hammer called a "lengthy and thorough" analysis of candidates' records, statements and responses to a questionnaire. Neither man won the influential gun-rights group's outright endorsement, which amounts to good news for McCollum, the state attorney general. An endorsement might have come with paid advertising to amplify the NRA decision. But Scott got bragging rights, no small feat in a statewide Republican primary where gun rights is an important test of conservatism, particularly in North and Central Florida. Hammer said McCollum, while serving in Congress, cast a number of antigun votes, including for the Brady Bill on final passage and against an amendment that would have allowed District of Columbia residents to keep guns in their homes. "Some folks say they will and don't and some folks say they won't and do. McCollum has done some of both," said Hammer. "You can't unring a bell. Bad votes don't go away." McCollum's campaign says the NRA was wrong about his vote on the Brady Bill, and provided documentation of his "no" vote.

1706.text: GUN-1702 PRIMARY Harlon B. Carter, Longtime Head Of Rifle Association, Dies at 78 Harlon B. Carter, who became the nation's foremost opponent of gun controls in leading the National Rifle Association to its greatest expansion in membership and political influence, died Tuesday at his home in rural Green Valley, Ariz. He was 78 years old and died of lung cancer, his family said. In his tenure from 1977 to 1985 as executive vice president, the rifle association's chief operating position, the group's membership more than tripled, to three million, and its annual budget multiplied severalfold, to $66 million. The organization's influence with elected officials also soared, to the dismay of gun control advocates. The association promotes a right to own and use firearms for protection, sport and hunting. Mr. Carter, adamant against restrictions, once testified before Congress that the misuse of guns was "a price we pay for freedom." N.R.A.'s 'Fiercest Warrior' Nelson T. Shields, a founder of an opposing organization, Handgun Control Inc., once called Mr. Carter "Mr. N.R.A." And yesterday Wayne R. LaPierre, the rifle association's current chief officer, said Mr. Carter "was our champion and fiercest warrior." Mr. Carter held 44 national shooting records with pistol, military rifle and small-bore rifle. He was one of 53 civilians who have received a special Army award for marksmanship. He served on the rifle association's board from 1951 to 1969 and was its president, a part-time position, from 1965 to 1967. He was the first director of its lobbying arm, the Institute for Legislative Action, from 1975 to 1976. In a bitter battle for control in 1977, he won an election ousting the association's executive vice president, Maxwell E. Rich. Later he was re-elected by a unanimous vote and continued in the post until he retired in 1985.

1711.text: GUN-3579 PRIMARY A sensible gun law At least 26 states now have super-liberal concealed weapons laws, which in general allow anyone who is not certifiably insane or a felon to pocket a pistol. Advocates say these laws will deter crime because stick-up artists and burglars will be afraid of picking out a victim who's armed. They also say the laws will deter general violence because citizens won't be so quick to give each other grief if they think the whole world is packing. Unfortunately, at least one early study, by researchers at the University of Maryland, says otherwise. In four out of five cities in three states, gun-related homicide rose after easy-permit laws were enacted. Still, these laws aren't going away soon. So the question is: If states are going to let their citizens carry hidden firearms, is there a way to do it right? They're giving it a shot, so to speak, in Texas, where relaxed gun-permit laws take effect in January. Texans who want to carry a concealed weapon must show familiarity with relevant state laws; prove their proficiency with firearm handling; and attend classes in conflict resolution. Several other states have pre-permit requirements, but none to this degree. There's no way of knowing if the Texas plan will work. The whole point of carrying a concealed firearm is to surprise your opponent, which is a dangerous game no matter what.

1709.text: GUN-471 PRIMARY 'Loophole' hid Fla. killer's past Revelations of the criminal background of a man whose suicidal rampage Monday claimed nine lives are dramatically focusing Florida's gun-control debate. Police say the killer, James Pough, 42, was charged with manslaughter in 1971, but pleaded to a lower charge of aggravated assault. Pough's criminal record was expunged after he served five years on probation, thus permitting him to buy the semiautomatic rifle used in his attack at a General Motors Acceptance Corp. office and the pistol he used to take his life. Pough used the rifle to kill two others Sunday, police said. Pough had a permit to carry the .38-caliber pistol, despite his past. '' It's a loophole we need to plug up,'' says Jacksonville Sheriff Jim McMillan. The murders come as the USA grapples with gun control: - Congress has before it a bill that would ban the sale of most semiautomatic rifles. - A Florida ballot referendum this fall calls for a three-day, cooling- off period before a gun purchase is approved. Handgun Control Inc. is spending $ 8,000 on an ad Thursday in Jacksonville's Florida Times-Union promoting a federal bill requiring a seven- day wait to check records of handgun buyers. ''We certainly hope it doesn't take a massacre of this type in every congressional district to get action,'' says Handgun Control's Gwen Fitzgerald. She says 22 states already have a waiting period or permit requirement to own handguns.

1710.text: NA-44622 PRIMARY Shaneen Allen, race and gun control Last October, Shaneen Allen, 27, was pulled over in Atlantic County, N.J. The officer who pulled her over says she made an unsafe lane change. During the stop, Allen informed the officer that she was a resident of Pennsylvania and had a conceal carry permit in her home state. She also had a handgun in her car. Had she been in Pennsylvania, having the gun in the car would have been perfectly legal. But Allen was pulled over in New Jersey, home to some of the strictest gun control laws in the United States. Allen is a black single mother. She has two kids. She has no prior criminal record. Before her arrest, she worked as a phlebobotomist. After she was robbed two times in the span of about a year, she purchased the gun to protect herself and her family. There is zero evidence that Allen intended to use the gun for any other purpose. Yet Allen was arrested. She spent 40 days in jail before she was released on bail. She's now facing a felony charge that, if convicted, would bring a three-year mandatory minimum prison term. At first blush, much about Allen's case seems counterintuitive. When we think about the gun control debate, we typically picture progressive pundits, politicians and activists arguing with white, conservative activists and politicians representing rural interests. When I first posted her story to Twitter, a couple of progressive responders predicted that because Allen is a black single mother, the gun rights community would all but ignore her. But that hasn't been true at all. In fact, Allen has become something of a rallying point for gun rights activists. She is being represented by Evan Nappen, an attorney who specializes in gun cases and is a gun rights activist himself. Some conservatives have similarly accused progressives of ignoring Allen's case because she stands accused of a gun crime. It's certainly true that her case has received much more attention from the right than the left. But Nappen says he has seen plenty of support for her from racial justice groups, too.

1692.text: GUN-2363 PRIMARY NRA leads gun-lobby spending Political action committees on both sides of the guns issue contributed $ 3,302,844 to Senate and House candidates between Jan. 1, 1991, and June 30, 1993, according to a USA TODAY computer analysis of Federal Election Commission records. The biggest spending was by the pro-firearm National Rifle Association - 88% of the total. A breakdown of the spending (including direct, in-kind and independent expenditures for and against candidates): Pro-gun PACs    $ 3,015,659 Anti-gun PACs     $ 287,185 Total amount spent National Rifle Association (pro-gun)       $ 2,917,525 Handgun Control Inc. (anti-gun)              $ 287,185 Safari Club International (pro-gun)           $ 81,850 Gun Owners of America (pro-gun)               $ 16,105 Safari Club/Detroit Chapter (pro-gun)            $ 179 PRO-GUN PACS Senators given most support (top 5 of 33): Paul Coverdell, R-Ga.            $ 95,806 Alfonse D'Amato, R-N.Y.          $ 20,486 Lauch Faircloth, R-N.C.          $ 17,973 Arlen Specter, R-Pa.             $ 16,146 Kay Bailey Hutchison, R-Texas    $ 11,509 Spent to oppose senator (only one): Harris Wofford, D-Pa.             $ 40,292 House members given most support (top 5 of 203): Gene Green, D-Texas               $ 157,640 Patricia Danner, D-Mo.             $ 35,806 Pete Geren, D-Texas                $ 25,267 James Talent, R-Mo.                $ 21,636 Thomas Foley, D-Wash. (Speaker)    $ 21,067 Spent to oppose House members (all 5): Mike Synar, D-Okla.               $ 136,521 Dan Glickman, D-Kan.               $ 52,080 Vic Fazio, D-Calif.                $ 15,318 Herbert Bateman, R-Va.              $ 6,598 Wayne Gilchrist, R-Md.              $ 1,020 ANTI-GUN PACs Senators given most support (all 6): Barbara Boxer, D-Calif.           $ 4,500 Russell Feingold, D-Wis.          $ 4,000 John Glenn, D-Ohio                $ 3,000 Carol Moseley-Braun, D-Ill.       $ 1,000 Bob Graham, D-Fla.                $ 1,000 Barbara Mikulski, D-Md.             $ 500

1714.text: GUN-10641 PRIMARY Herhold: The logic of gun permits Editor's Note: Unable to curb Mr. Herhold's bent for satire, we have to warn the irony-challenged that what follows is not meant to be taken as scripture. A story on Sunday highlighted the range of folks getting concealed weapons permits from Santa Clara County Sheriff Laurie Smith. For the most part, their reasons for needing a gun did not offer much surprise: They were transporting gold and silver, dealing with disgruntled baseball fans, stealing kidneys, etc. But two of the rationales from permit holders, Santa Clara Valley Water District board chairman Don Gage and Cisco CEO John Chambers, offer a compelling road map for efficiency and safety, a kind of "best practices" template for government and business. Consider Gage, who first began packing as Gilroy mayor and continues to find danger in his part-time gig on the Golden Spigot's board. He says the board got threats over its decision to add fluoride to the drinking water. "There's a lot of crazy people out there," Gage said. "I think I have a valid reason, because I'm in the public all the time." Frankly, I'm astonished that more elected officials don't see the wisdom in Gage's view, and not just because of vigilante packs of flossers. Every public official ought to pack heat. In this era of polarized politics, it's just too perilous not to. Take that ugly business of the Occupy San Jose folks protesting at City Manager Deb Figone's home. Would that have happened if they knew she was packing? Of course not.

1712.text: GUN-9934 PRIMARY Senate to Vote on Shielding Gun Makers Backers of a bipartisan bill that would shield firearms dealers and manufacturers from lawsuits are pushing for a vote this week in the Senate, injecting the volatile issue of gun control into this year's presidential and Congressional elections. The bill, which would grant legal immunity to gun makers and dealers so long as they did not sell defective weapons or violate any law, has the support of the White House and 55 sponsors in the Senate, including the Democratic and Republican leaders. It has already passed the House and could come up for consideration in the Senate as early as Wednesday. ''The American people are on the side of what we're doing,'' said Senator Larry E. Craig, Republican of Idaho, the bill's chief sponsor. He said he had the 60 votes needed for passage, adding, ''When the dust settles in the Senate, you will have a substantial number of Democrats voting for this.'' But the dust may take time to settle, because other senators are pushing to tack two other gun-related measures onto the bill -- a move that could complicate its chances for passage. Some opponents, meanwhile, led by Senator Jack Reed, Democrat of Rhode Island, are vowing a filibuster. ''Things are very fluid,'' said Tony Orza, chief lobbyist for the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence, an advocacy group. '' We're looking to a fierce fight.''

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1713.text: GUN-751 PRIMARY SHOTGUN APPROACH TO GUN-CONTROL LAW WASHINGTON Lunacy breeds lunac y. Emotion overwhelms reason. On Jan. 17, Patrick James Purdy murdered five children in a schoolyard in Stockton, Calif. It was a terrible crime, but for the past three months presumably intelligent persons have ceased to blame Purdy. They are blaming the kind of rifle he used instead. It is like blaming the pot for burning the beans.   The campaign to ban assault weapons makes no sense. It is a manifestation of the panicky demand often made upon lawmakers: ''Don't just sit there! Do something!'' At times it is wiser simply to sit there, and one of those times is at hand.   The U.S. Senate is praying over a bill, S.386, sponsored by Howard Metzenbaum of Ohio. It is a bill ''to control the sale and use of assault weapons.'' Companion legislation is pending in the House. At least 13 state legislatures are considering similar bills. The sponsors may be credited with possessing good intentions, but they may equally be criticized for fostering bad legislation.   On superficial inspection, the Metzenbaum bill appears to take a reasonable approach. It would ban the sale or possession of certain named semiautomatic firearms, such as the Kalashnikov and the Uzi. One may fairly inquire: Who needs these for hunting? The deer that is struck by a 20-round burst would hit the ground as venison stew.   On closer examination, the Metzenbaum bill loses any semblance of merit. It is so loosely drafted that it could affect millions of semiautomatic firearms that are owned and used by law-abiding citizens throughout the nation. The bill would place dangerous and unprecedented power in the hands of the secretary of the treasury. None of its provisions would have any significant impact upon drug traffickers or crazies. Perhaps careful draftsmen - some one who know something about firearms - could put together a useful bill, but S.386 isn't it.   Sponsors of the bills run into trouble at the threshold: Exactly what is an ''assault weapon''? Metzenbaum's definition is the kind of definition beloved by Humpty-Dumpty. An assault weapon is any weapon the secretary of the treasury says is an assault weapon. This won't do. Millions of law-abiding sportsmen own semiautomatic weapons that are ''substantially identical'' to the weapons specifically banned by the Metzenbaum bill.   An additional objection to the pending bills may lie in the Second Amendment to the Constitution. '' A well-regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free state, the right of the people to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed.'' I am not certain what the amendment means, and neither is the U.S. Supreme Court, but obviously it means something. The amendment does not establish an absolute right to keep and bear arms any more than the First Amendment secures an absolute right to engage in free speech.   Constitutionally speaking, a working machine gun and child pornography are in the same class: Neither is protected. All the same, it would stagger the Founding Fathers to learn that in a free society, no person has a right to own an ordinary repeating shotgun.   Banning weapons like the weapon used by Purdy would accomplish little. The legislative goal should be to prevent loonies like Purdy from getting such weapons in the first place.

1716.text: GUN-4529 PRIMARY Massachusetts enacts strict rules for handguns Regulations issued under consumer laws BOSTON - Safety warnings, child-proof features and trigger locks will be on all handguns sold in Massachusetts next year, under an unprecedented set of handgun regulations enacted Wednesday. "Today we're doing what no other state has done, but what every state should do," Massachusetts Attorney General Scott Harshbarger said. Meanwhile, California's Senate voted Wednesday to impose a statewide ban on cheap, easily hidden handguns known as Saturday night specials. The bill now goes to the state assembly for approval. In Massachusetts, Harshbarger is enacting the rules, the first of their kind in the nation, without legislative approval because they are state consumer protection laws, which he enforces. Starting Oct. 1, safety warnings and instructions will have to accompany all handguns sold in the state. And by next June at the latest, all handguns must: Meet minimum quality standards for such things as metal strength. This measure has been passed in other states as well. Have a built-in trigger lock or detachable key-activated lock. Have mechanisms to keep them from being fired by children, including increased trigger resistance or an oversized grip. Have serial numbers in their barrels, to make it easier to trace them. Come with a personal demonstration for their safe use. The rules should ban the sale of 30 brands of cheap handguns in the state, mostly inexpensive .22- or .25-caliber weapons, officials say, and may also cut down on accidental deaths caused by the improper use of handguns.

1717.text: GUN-7707 PRIMARY A WEAPON FOR CHANGE GUN-CONTROL ADVOCATES GATHER BY THE THOUSANDS AROUND THE NATION WASHINGTON: POLITICIANS, CELEBRITIES SPEAK OUT ON MOTHER'S DAY FOR STRONGER LAWS. America's fervent debate about guns took center stage on Mother's Day, as more than 100,000 moms, children and their allies called Sunday for stronger national gun-control laws at Million Mom March rallies in Washington and about 70 cities nationwide, including Oakland. Gun-control advocates hoped their Washington rally, born in anguish over recent high-profile shootings of children -- often by other children, such as last year's massacre at Columbine High School in Colorado -- will prove to be a turning point in their battle against gun enthusiasts led by the National Rifle Association. "This is like reliving my son's funeral," said Debra Davis of Lansing, Mich., whose 19-year-old son was shot to death in 1997. "There are so many mothers here like me who have suffered." "Are you listening, Congress? Senators?" shouted talk-show host Rosie O'Donnell, who presided over the ceremonies, with the gleaming white Capitol rising behind her against a bright blue sky. "We vote, and we will be watching you!" Throngs of people, many pushing strollers and carrying signs with anti-gunmessages, crowded the east end of the National Mall, a wide, open grassy strip that runs from the Capitol to the Washington Monument. Speakers claimed a turnout of 500,000 to 750,000 people, but the number could not be independently confirmed as the federal Park Police no longer gives crowd estimates, after years of controversy over how many people attend events like these. At least 100,000 people appeared to be present.

1715.text: GUN-11193 PRIMARY Questioning the Castle Doctrine Trayvon Martin's death may or may not have been about race. It was definitely about guns. Quick primer: On Feb. 26, Martin, a black, 17-year-old high school senior, was walking through a gated community, carrying snacks on his way to a friend's house. George Zimmerman, a Hispanic, 28-year-old Neighborhood Watch volunteer, shot and killed him. There were no witnesses on site. A 16-year-old girl who was on the phone with Martin before he was shot told police that Martin told her that a man was following him; she advised Martin to run. She heard a scuffle, and the call ended. When police arrived, Zimmerman, who had a bloody nose and a wound on the back of his head, invoked Florida's "Stand Your Ground" law. Like North Carolina's "Castle Doctrine," Florida's law permits the use of deadly force in defense of oneself, without fear of prosecution. North Carolina's Castle Doctrine, which became law last December, expanded the right to use deadly force beyond a person's home, to include businesses and vehicles, and added "a person is justified in the use of deadly force and does not have a duty to retreat in any place he or she has the lawful right to be if ... [h]e or she reasonably believes that such force is necessary to prevent imminent death or great bodily harm to himself or herself or another. "A lot of North Carolinians, including this editorial board, argued that these sloppily written laws attempt to circumvent basic American justice. Self-defense has been a successful defense for as long as court records have been kept; these new laws seem designed to avoid the courthouse altogether. Worse, it's not just the courthouse. These laws seem designed to relieve people from applying their own basic judgment, let along anyone else's. Zimmerman apparently thought he was one of the good guys, a white hat, but since the Stand Your Ground law might allow him to escape the scrutiny of a courtroom, how can he know?Isn't it reasonable that the fear of a deadly threat increases with the likelihood that others have the power to harm us - a possibility that increases as gun laws are relaxed? What is the goal? To spiral ever downward until everyone is armed because everyone else is armed, quick on the trigger because any confrontation could become a deadly assault?But, as seems to be the case with gun-toting Zimmerman and unarmed Martin, a person's sense of his or her own fear of threat is not the best measure of his or her actual danger. Trayvon Martin's death is a bloody illustration that these sloppy gun-rights laws are no substitute for the justice system - in Florida, in North Carolina, or anywhere else.

1719.text: NA-46247 PRIMARY Religious leaders decry gun proposal A move to expand access to guns across Georgia drew more high-profile opponents Wednesday when some of the state's top religious leaders spoke out against the effort, despite its support by some of the state's top lawmakers. House Bill 875 received its first hearing in committee just hours after a spokesman for Catholic Archbishop Wilton D. Gregory, Rabbi Peter Berg of The Temple synagogue in Atlanta and more than a dozen pastors and priests gathered in a church sanctuary across the street from the Capitol to denounce the legislation. The bill would lift restrictions on guns in churches and bars, allow school boards to arm employees and make an end run around a ban on licensed owners carrying guns on college campuses by making it no longer a crime. "We don't want to be in a position where we have to post a giant sign in front of the church or synagogue that says 'no guns allowed,' " said Berg, whose synagogue is the largest in Georgia, with more than 1,500 families. The bill, he said, "introduces weapons of destruction into houses of worship, one of the few places in the world people can go for a sense of serenity. ... Nothing sets the tone for meaningful prayer like a picture with a gun on it." Rep. Rick Jasperse, R-Jasper, the primary sponsor of HB 875, testified later Wednesday before the House Public Safety Committee and said his bill helps protect the rights of those who follow the law. The committee will continue testimony on the bill at 4 p.m. Thursday in Room 606 of the legislative office building across from the Capitol.

1721.text: GUN-6549 PRIMARY CBI's gun background check reauthorized The Colorado Bureau of Investigation will keep doing its own  instant criminal background checks on gun purchases in the state. A bill reauthorizing the $ 1 million-a-year state program got  final legislative approval Tuesday and is on its way to Gov. Bill  Owens, who calls it the most important gun bill of this  legislative session. If the state didn't reauthorize the check, background checks  in Colorado would have been done by the FBI. But the Colorado  check is more comprehensive. Colorado blocks gun sales to people  under restraining orders and to those whose criminal records show  arrests but not convictions. The Colorado check blocks about 6 percent of gun sales, while  the federal check blocks 1 percent. To get the bill passed, Owens had to overcome the opposition  of the National Rifle Association, which backed him in his 1998  election, and many conservatives in his own Republican Party. 'Gov. Owens is ecstatic that the most important piece of his  common-sense gun package is now going to be permanent law,' said  Dick Wadhams, Owens' spokesman. Wadhams said to expect a very public signing ceremony when the  bill gets to Owens' desk. ' He will want to herald this,' Wadhams  said. Colorado's instant computer background check was created in  1994, so Colorado could avoid the five-day waiting period for a  gun purchase that otherwise would have been imposed by the federal  Brady law.

1720.text: GUN-4003 PRIMARY WITHOUT A WORD, PANEL VOTES TO FIRE TEACHER WITH GUN Enforcing its zero-tolerance policy against guns on campus, the Palm Beach County School Board voted without a word Wednesday to fire high school teacher Roberto Alonso Jr. Nearly 10 friends and parents testified that Alonso, 33, is a good teacher and role model who just made a mistake. And teachers' union President Frank Williams blasted board members for "potentially destroying a career." But parent David Morrow of Royal Palm Beach, whose son was expelled last year for computer hacking and is in another school this year, said the board should stick to its zero-tolerance stance. "We need to speak loudly to the students and this community - zero tolerance means zero tolerance," Morrow said. The board suspended Alonso without pay for 30 days from his job as a special education teacher at John I. Leonard High School in Greenacres. The firing takes effect after the suspension expires, but Alonso has requested a special one-hour hearing before the school board. He also wants an administrative hearing before a state hearing officer. If his appeal is successful, the four-year teacher and junior varsity baseball coach will be reinstated with back pay. Superintendent Joan Kowal said Alonso clearly and admittedly violated the district's zero-tolerance policy on firearms possession by employees. The policy carries an automatic termination, regardless of whether the employee intended to break the law. At least one other district teacher has been fired for gun possession since the policy began in January 1995.

1718.text: GUN-10256 PRIMARY Dinner with a .38 on the side? Critics call it the "Rambos in the restaurant" bill. Supporters say it embraces constitutional freedoms. House Bill 193 would allow anyone with a concealed weapons permit to take a handgun inside most restaurants in Georgia. For years, the state has banned weapons at restaurants that serve alcohol, at football games, in churches and at other public gatherings. "What if someone comes to Atlanta to stay at the Ritz-Carlton, and they have to walk a couple of blocks to a restaurant?" said state Rep. Bobby Franklin (R-Marietta). "They'd like to be able to carry that gun in downtown Atlanta at night. Right now, even if they have a permit and have passed the background checks, they can't." Gun control activists have attacked Franklin's proposal, which would apply to any restaurant that generates more than 50 percent of its income from food. "Can you imagine being in JavaMonkey knowing the patrons are carrying concealed weapons?" Decatur resident Mary Jane Mahan wrote to her state representative, referring to a local coffeehouse and wine bar. "Please do not support this." Atlanta officials, who plan to announce today that crime downtown dropped in 2004, bristled at Franklin's remarks. "We should invite Rep. Bobby Franklin down to see what a nice walk it is when you walk out of the Ritz-Carlton, day or night, to show that you don't need a gun," said A.J. Robinson, president of Central Atlanta Progress and the Atlanta Downtown Improvement District.

1722.text: GUN-2601 PRIMARY HOUSE PASSES BRADY BILL, BUT ADDS EXPIRATION DATE The House of Representatives on Wednesday approved legislation that would require a buyer of a handgun to wait five business days before taking possession of it, but only after adding an amendment that would cause the requirement to lapse after five years. The legislation, known as the Brady bill, was first introduced six years ago. It has since passed in the House twice and been killed twice in the Senate, but the emergence of crime as a central issue in elections this fall has improved chances the measure will survive. ``Five years is better than no years,'' Rep. Charles Rangel, D-N.Y., who strongly supported the bill, said of the amendment tacked on Wednesday by a coalition of Westerners, Southerners and lawmakers leery of burning all their bridges in relations with the National Rifle Association. Rangel predicted that ``if we show it's working, we'll get broader support'' to extend the five-day requirement in future years. Congress has not enacted a major gun-control bill since banning mail-order sales of rifles, shotguns and ammunition in 1968, after the assassinations of the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and Sen. Robert F. Kennedy. But the tide appeared to turn even before the vote Wednesday, as the Senate on Tuesday passed a measure that would prohibit the sale of handguns to minors. That measure is part of a $22 billion anti-crime package that is now moving toward passage in the Senate. Leaders in that chamber had hoped to pass the package Wednesday, but it got bottled up in negotiations over contentious proposals such as a strict limit on federal appeals by Death Row prisoners and a ban on assault rifles.

characters: Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.

1724.text: GUN-7655 PRIMARY A FREE PEOPLE CAN'T CEDE THEIR RIGHT TO BEAR ARMS Current advocacy for federal gun-control legislation manifests a staggering apathy to the rudiments of American liberty. The right of self-defense is just as essential to freedom as the right to articulate one's convictions or pray to one's god. Our constitutional heritage recognizes all of these rights. James Madison boasted of being armed as a right "Americans possess over the people of almost every other nation." Instead of exploiting the anguish of mothers, political elites should withdraw support of a policy that rebuffs the law and dovetails with the worst regimes of the modern age. MYLES KANTOR Boynton Beach

1723.text: GUN-912 PRIMARY Victim: Handgun laws could have thwarted attacker; Peachtree gunman remains unidentified A lawyer wounded by apparently random gunfire Wednesday outside his Downtown office blamed the attack on the lack of gun control laws. Wayne Shortridge, 53, said in an interview Thursday from his hospital bed at Georgia Baptist Medical Center that he originally thought the gunman, who was still at large, was firing blanks. "I had been working a lot lately and was hoping to go home early to play with my daughter, and after that I knew I wouldn't be able to make it home early," he said with a slight smile. The corporate lawyer for Powell Goldstein Frazer & Murphy has four children, including 21-month-old Kelly. "This could have happened anywhere," said Mr. Shortridge. "I've always been against handguns other than for law enforcement. This would not have happened if he had not had a handgun." Mr. Shortridge was struck once in the back as he returned to his office in the new 191 Peachtree Tower, next to the Ritz-Carlton Hotel, about 3 p.m. after getting his hair cut. He was in stable condition Thursday. Atlanta Police Capt. Calvin Wardlaw said Thursday police still had no suspect or motive for the shooting spree. "We can only canvas the Downtown area and see if this is a person who hangs out down there," he said. John Hill, 21, a messenger for another Downtown law firm, said a bullet narrowly missed his head and lodged in a revolving door frame behind him.

1725.text: GUN-7074 PRIMARY TV NOTES; The Gun Debate Tom Brokaw and NBC will take on the roiling question of gun control in a series of programs on MSNBC and NBC today and tonight. At 3 p.m. on MSNBC, Mr. Brokaw will moderate a town hall meeting in Denver, beginning with an interview with President Clinton, who will be attending the meeting. Portions of the interview will be played on Mr. Brokaw's "Nightly News" tonight. That town hall meeting will also be replayed on MSNBC at 10 p.m. followed at 11 by a second town hall meeting, also moderated by Mr. Brokaw. That one will include the Colorado governor, Bill Owens, and numerous advocates and opponents of gun control. The connecting point is the shooting tragedy at Columbine high school, which is nearing its first anniversary. "Colorado has become ground zero in the gun debate," Mr. Brokaw said in a telephone interview yesterday. He noted that his own background, growing up in the West where boys learned to use guns with their fathers in a "male ethos" that continues today, has helped shape his own view that the gun control debate has become too polarized. "I don't think the middle on this issue has been heard," he said. Mr. Brokaw, who still spends vacations at his ranch in Montana, said, "I live among gun owners," adding that many of them have "totally the right attitude" about how they should be used and regulated.

1729.text: GUN-10947 PRIMARY Utah's Gun Appreciation Day This week in Washington, Senator Frank Lautenberg of New Jersey introduced three very modest gun regulation bills, including one making it more difficult to sell guns to people on the terror watch list. Meanwhile, in Salt Lake City, the State Legislature is considering a bill to honor the Browning M1911 pistol by making it the official state firearm. Guess which idea has the better chance of passage? Can I see a show of hands? Oh, you cynics, you! Yes, a committee in the Utah House of Representatives voted 9 to 2 this week to approve a bill that would add the Browning pistol to the pantheon of official state things, along with the bird (seagull), rock (coal) and dance (square). Also, although it really has nothing to do with this discussion, I have to mention that the Utah Legislature has provided its citizens with an official state cooking pot, and it is the Dutch oven. ''This firearm is Utah,'' Representative Carl Wimmer, the Browning bill's sponsor, told The Salt Lake Tribune. He is an energetic-looking guy with a huge forehead who has only been in office four years yet has, according to one of his videos, ''sponsored and passed some of the most significant pieces of legislation in Utah history.'' Capitol observers say the Browning bill has an excellent chance of becoming law. Meanwhile, Lautenberg will be lucky to get a hearing. The terror of the National Rifle Association is so pervasive that President Obama did not want to poison the mood of his State of the Union address by suggesting that when somebody on the terror watch list tries to buy a gun, maybe we should do an extra check.

1728.text: GUN-2188 PRIMARY WHO'S POLICING THE GUN DEALERS? With homicide rates reaching record levels in recent years, some cities have pushed to get tough on gun-toting street gangs and violent criminals. But who is policing the gun dealers from whom at least some of the weapons are obtained? The agency that is supposed to enforce federal gun-control laws, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms (ATF), has done very little to keep track of gun dealers. In the absence of more frequent and timely ATF audits of the dealers, gun-control laws are virtually useless.   The ATF force assigned to police gun dealers has been cut by 13 percent in a decade of rising gun-related homicides and a rapidly expanding gun-dealership business. In two decades, the number of federally licensed gun dealers has risen to 276,000 in 1990 from 138,000 in 1970. In the past five years alone, roughly 60,000 people have been killed by guns. Dealers aren't even required to report to ATF the loss or thefts of their weapons.   ATF focuses mainly on processing applications for gun dealerships; by law, they must be processed within 45 days or the applicant can sue. Yet the agency has been less than diligent in screening out those whose criminal records or business practices show that they should not be dealers. A recent series in The Washington Post showed how easy it is to get a gun-dealer permit and how much less likely it is for any dealer to face a federal inspection. All it takes to obtain a permit to sell rifles, shotguns and handguns is completion of a two-page form, along with a $30 fee. The applicant isn't likely to be interviewed by an ATF agent and can expect to be inspected only once every 20 years, if at all. The newspaper found, for example, that over a dozen federally licensed dealers in Detroit alone had been charged with providing 2,000 firearms to criminals.   ATF needs to focus more on keeping guns out of the hands of criminals and other undesirables. That means, among other things, more policing of gun dealers. Their lack of accountability is probably one reason guns are so plentiful and the murder rate continues to soar.

1727.text: NA-42060 PRIMARY Mercury News editorial: Gun ban in parks thankfully upheld in San Mateo County Although more than 20 states have passed stand-your-ground laws that encourage people to use firearms when they feel threatened, sanity prevails in places like the Bay Area -- and got a nice boost in San Mateo County this week, when a state appeals court upheld the county's reasonable ban on guns in county parks. "Our parks are a refuge not only for wildlife but for visitors," former county sheriff and current Supervisor Don Horsley said in a statement Tuesday. "The presence of guns ramps up the chances of deadly accidents, and no one should have to worry that just around the corner some hiker with a gun is going to mistake you for a mountain lion." Or, we would add, see a real mountain lion, fire wide in panic and instead hit a Girl Scout on the next switchback. The Calguns Foundation in San Carlos, supported by the National Rifle Association, argued that state law authorizing concealed weapon permits should include taking guns to parks. But the court found that the county ban is sufficiently narrow as to bear "no resemblance" to broader gun bans that have been struck down. Gun proponents don't take such insults lightly, so a further appeal is likely. But it's great that San Mateo -- armed with .45-caliber common sense -- is standing its ground.

1726.text: GUN-447 PRIMARY AN EXPLOSIVE MISTAKE Last year, responding to demands from gun-control advocates and police, President Bush banned the importing of foreign-made assault rifles. The ban was a timid approach to a serious problem, since Mr. Bush chose to ignore the sale of U.S.-made assault rifles. But even that weak effort was blasted by the National Rifle Association, whose members have an acute fear of common sense. Now, even the ban on foreign assault weapons is being thwarted by gun manufacturers who are abusing gun import rules. After removing pistol grips, large magazines and bayonet attachments, gun manufacturers are calling these assault weapons "sport" rifles. Unfortunately, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms has agreed that these minor modifications are enough to allow such weapons into the country. What the gun manufacturers don't say, and what the bureau has chosen to ignore, is that these minor modifications can easily be changed so that the gun buyer-- criminal or otherwise-- can convert the weapon back to its original lethal character. Congress must finally pass legislation permanently banning the sale and possession of all semiautomatic assault rifles. In the House, the Judiciary Committee has approved a bill that would ban many domestic and foreign-made semiautomatic assault rifles. The committee also plans to consider a bill that would impose a seven-day waiting period for anyone seeking to purchase a firearm. The Senate passed a much weaker bill that bans nine types of assault rifles for just three years. The time limit is pointless. The Senate should adopt the House plan.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1730.text: GUN-10445 PRIMARY letters to the editor Anti-gun laws wrong answer in Florida First, in response to the letter advocating more gun laws, I thank the writer for his service as a prosecutor in New York state. But he does not have to worry about The Post not covering gun-related crimes. They will be headlined each and every time, real or not. My point is that you cannot compare Long Island to Palm Beach County. They may be similar in population, but otherwise the demographics are different. Anti-gun laws simply don't work. The writer's emotion won't change that. The best thing that has happened here is our concealed carry laws. Now the bad guys stay away, or at least think twice about a personal attack. Only folks with a really clean record get a license to carry, and they carry only under certain rules. It works well. That's why more than 34 states now reciprocate in one way or another. Here in Florida, we teach firearm safety, lawful use and proper storage, in order to obtain your license. With respect, the writer is not in New York anymore. My NRA instructor colleagues and I use our experience to introduce safe, responsible, armed citizens with legal concealed carry permits that may one day save your life. The writer should consider it for himself and his family. MARK LEAMING North Palm Beach Pretty weak tea for state's party 'leaders'

1732.text: GUN-10720 PRIMARY Guns in church argued in court A grass-roots gun rights group had its argument turned on them Thursday as they tried to convince three judges that a year-old Georgia law infringes on gun owners' freedom of religion by putting restrictions on bringing their firearms with them to worship. They were before a panel for the 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals asking them to stop the enforcement of a law that "bans" firearms at places of worship. They claim the law forces gun owners to choose between two constitutional rights --- the First Amendment protection of individual religious choices and the Second Amendment that guarantees the right to bear arms. But Assistant Attorney General Laura Lones said GeorgiaCarry.org was misreading the law. She said guns can be brought into churches, synagogues and mosques with the approval of leaders of those institutions and as long as they secure their firearms, if it is requested. The law lists eight places that guns cannot be brought --- government buildings, prisons and jails, nuclear power plants, bars without the owner's permission, state mental institutions, within 150 feet of a polling place and places of worship. Lones said that law, in another section, allows for guns to be brought into churches, synagogues and mosques if approved by leaders at those places. "They have repeatedly referred to this as a ban. It's not a ban," Lones said.

1731.text: GUN-4629 PRIMARY Childproof-gun rules favored Poll: Better safety laws needed WASHINGTON - Nearly nine out of 10 Americans surveyed favor a requirement that all new handguns be childproofed and nearly seven out of 10 want the guns "personalized" so they could be fired only by an authorized user, a new national poll showed Thursday. Sponsors of the survey said it documented - for the first time - strong public demand that handguns be treated like other consumer products. Three out of four Americans believe the government should regulate the safety design of guns. "It's bizarre to think that a 4-year-old, who can't open an aspirin bottle, can operate a loaded pistol," said Stephen Teret, director of the Center for Gun Policy and Research at Johns Hopkins School of Public Health. Teret's center and the University of Chicago's National Opinion Research Center jointly released the results of a telephone survey of 1,200 people nationwide on a range of gun-related issues. The survey, with a sampling variation of plus or minus 3 percent, was funded by the Joyce Foundation of Chicago. Among its major findings: 86 percent of those surveyed support laws requiring all new handguns to be childproofed. 82 percent favored mandatory registration of handguns. 81 percent want handgun sales to an individual limited to one per month. 72 percent say private gun sales not involving a licensed dealer should be subject to the same background checks as those required for dealer sales.

characters: Center for Gun Policy and Research
Johns Hopkins School of Public Health
University of Chicago's National Opinion Research Center

1733.text: GUN-30948 PRIMARY Adventures in New York Politics At the press conference announcing her appointment on Friday, Senator-designate Kirsten Gillibrand acknowledged that she is still an unknown to most New Yorkers. It is a blank slate that could serve her well. As she moves from representing a conservative upstate district into Hillary Rodham Clinton's office, Ms. Gillibrand needs to move beyond some narrower views suited to her old constituents and embrace her role as a senator of a powerful, diverse and complicated state. Ms. Gillibrand certainly seems to have the capacity to make that large political leap. She comes from hearty political stock -- her grandmother and father were powerful forces in Albany's city and state politics. She was an expert securities lawyer, a useful background these days. And she has managed two skillful campaigns, raising a considerable amount of money to win what was considered an unassailable Republican seat. Ms. Gillibrand, 42, has solid credentials in the fight for women's issues, including abortion rights, and she has bravely endorsed gay marriage -- moves that would normally be considered political suicide in her district. What is the most disappointing about Ms. Gillibrand's record is her extreme opposition to reasonable gun control laws. Her opposition to new efforts to trace illegal guns and support for rolling back gun control laws in the District of Columbia go well beyond her declared support for hunters' rights. She earned a top rating and vigorous campaign support from the National Rifle Association. Her jarring views on guns could cost her a bitter Democratic primary fight next year for re-election if gun-control advocates like Representative Carolyn McCarthy of Long Island challenge her on this issue.

1734.text: GUN-3251 PRIMARY HOUSE REPUBLICANS DELAY ASSAULT WEAPONS QUESTION House Republicans will delay a drive to repeal last year's ban on assault weapons until May and separate it from overall revisions to the crime bill passed last year, the chairman of a crime subcommittee said Saturday. Rep. Bill McCollum, R-Fla., appearing on CNN's "Newsmaker Saturday," said House Republican leaders have decided to reconsider the assault weapons after the House has dealt with the legislation stemming from its so-called Contract With America. "We're going to address it because a lot of our members want to," said McCollum, chairman of the Judiciary subcommittee on crime. But he emphasized that it won't be done as part of the crime section of the Contract With America. "We're going to produce legislation very soon on the floor of the House that's going to end the endless appeals of death row inmates, change the rules of evidence on search and seizure, beef up our prison systems, do something about criminal aliens, and merge the cops-on-the-streets program and the prevention programs into a local community block grant program," McCollum said.

1735.text: GUN-6691 PRIMARY Predators just need to be free The Colorado Legislature is expected to waste much of this  year's session arguing about gun control - what sellers must do to  legally peddle guns, who can buy guns, how owners must store guns,  where guns can be carried, when guns must be registered, why  people should own guns at all. The story line conforms nicely to the old formula taught to  journalism students about the essential elements of a news  article, known as the 'Five W's' - who, what, when, where and why? Meanwhile, Denver police are trying to solve one of the most  intriguing murders in recent Colorado history, the death of an  all-American schoolteacher. The problem is that she wasn't shot to death. She was beaten.   Her killer didn't use a gun. He used raw force. Her killer didn't pass a background check, didn't need a  concealed-carry permit for a murder weapon, didn't register his  anger with the FBI. Instead, the only apparent suspect in Emily Johnson's horrid  murder was a man who was walking free on the streets because  Colorado is more concerned over gun control than criminal control. Robert Davis is a career criminal. His resume lists his  considerable talents: arrests for attempted murder, forgery, drug  trafficking, sexual assault - but no time behind prison bars. His life has been that of a predator, the kind of person who  has little regard for the rules of civility, the kind of person  who hasn't demonstrated any respect for a work ethic, the kind of  person who drives up the cost paid by honest, hard-working  taxpayers for police and courts and social workers.

1739.text: GUN-11436 PRIMARY Letters to the editor Unpredictable outcome Having grown up in rural West Virginia, virtually every family I knew owned firearms and I cannot recall their being used irresponsibly. But when Rick Willis stated, in his July 27 letter regarding the Aurora, Colo., theater shooting, that "a well-armed crowd is much more able to defend itself," I was dumbfounded. Whether for or against gun rights, people seem only to think in terms of best case scenarios. In this case: Crowd sees gunman, pull their weapons, and end the bloodshed. Willis' assumption that the crowd would immediately understand what was happening and use their weapons effectively is not guaranteed. What if the first people in the crowd who drew their weapons were perceived to be accomplices of the gunman? A situation could result whereby innocent people were being killed, not by the gunman, but by the well-armed citizenry; the bloodshed could have been greater. And since Willis is in favor of citizens being able to possess "whatever kind of weapon (they) choose," the bloodshed could have been much greater. Whether for or against gun ownership, please understand that people do not always act the way we would wish them to, especially in high pressure situations. Michael Williams El Cerrito Prop. 34 could mean savings Many thanks to the Times for its Aug. 1 editorial, a thoughtful endorsement of Proposition 34 to replace the death penalty in California with life in prison without possibility of parole.

1740.text: GUN-10135 PRIMARY READERS WRITE ASK isn't anti-gun, it is pro-children Bob Barr's dismissal of the ASK program ("Anti-gun initiative full of blanks," @issue, June 29) is paranoid bluster. As a parent who asks, I can attest that I couldn't care less if someone owns guns, enjoys shooting or is a National Rifle Association member. I simply won't let my children become a statistic by playing unsupervised in a home where guns are present. Further, every parent of whom I've ever asked this awkward question has been candid and appreciative. The ASK program isn't stealth anti-gunism; it's pragmatism in action. It avoids infringement on Second Amendment rights and concentrates instead on preventing the too-common tragedy of dead or wounded children. I wonder if Barr has ever considered that accidental gun deaths of children might be at an all-time low because parents are asking. ANDREW McALISTER, Decatur Many moral, ethical people are atheists Sadie Fields is simply wrong in saying morality is derived from religion ("Can't have morality without religion," Letters, June 28). Some of the most moral and ethical people I know are atheists. Fields uses the religious right's mantra "acknowledgment of God" to justify putting copies of the Ten Commandments on public property. God is acknowledged with our lives, not with our lips. The Rev. BO TURNER Turner, of Clarkesville, is pastor of Tallulah Falls Baptist Church. Panhandling: Responses to Cynthia Tucker's column "Panhandling a racial issue? I beg to differ," @issue, June 29

1737.text: GUN-8783 PRIMARY COPS AND SMUGGLERS GUN CONTROL IN the shady world of gun smuggling, it isn't always easy to know who's the bad guy. Last month, federal agents disclosed a seizure of 1,500 assault rifles from several places in Missouri, Kentucky and Ohio. The shocking number of banned, fully automatic weapons and the relative ease with which they were acquired mean that Congress and federal agencies must do more to close loopholes that made this smuggling possible. One of the smugglers was James Carmi of Lincoln County, Mo. Police found a stockpile of 800 weapons, mortars and cannons on his property. Carmi is a convicted felon, and as such was not supposed to have even one gun, let alone 800 assault rifles. The other smuggler was Keith B.
