Baranski of Hopewell, Ohio, from whose cache police seized 372 machine guns. The rest of the weapons were found in a warehouse in Kentucky. Carmi pleaded guilty last year and testified for the government in the trial against Baranski, who was convicted in federal court here last month. The third bad guy wore a badge. He is Jeff Knipp, the former police chief of Farber, Mo., who helped the smugglers. The case is especially worrisome because it shows the virtually effortless way that criminals can get their hands on weapons that are supposed to be banned except for use by the military, law enforcement, manufacturers, dealers and collectors. All Carmi and Baranski had to do was bribe a police agency to write a letter, or forge a letter themselves, saying that the agency wanted to see or buy the weapons. Knipp and other small-town police chiefs obliged. Their letters gave the men the green light to import hundreds of machine guns from Bulgaria, Poland and Russia between 1999 and 2000.

1736.text: GUN-3814 PRIMARY Is the gun lobby losing its grip? You know who really touched off the Oklahoma City bombing? Congress did, when it banned assault-weapons last year. Other responsible parties include the "murderous" FBI and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. That and other strange theories are laid out in a remarkable letter to Georgia legislators and congressmen from the Georgia Sports Shooting Association, the state affiliate of the National Rifle Association. "If last year's gun ban had not been enacted, and/or if the BATF and FBI assaults on the Branch Davidians at Waco, Texas or the Weaver family at Ruby Ridge, Idaho had not occurred . . . the Oklahoma City bombing would, in all likelihood, not have occurred," the letter states. "The murderous assault on the Branch Davidians and the Weaver family had the effect of killing more children than the criminal bombing of the Oklahoma City federal building," the letter continues. (That offensive comparison ignores the fact that the killer fires at Waco were set by the Branch Davidians themselves, in a bizarre act of mass suicide.) The letter also rejects criticism of the NRA for likening federal law-enforcement officers to Nazis and "jackbooted government thugs." Former President George Bush this week resigned from the NRA in protest of such language, saying it "deeply offends my own sense of decency and honor." The Georgia Sports Shooting Association, however, worries about what might have happened had the NRA kept its mouth shut.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1741.text: GUN-11984 PRIMARY HEAT'S ON BAM Angry Mike: Gun control must be 'No. 1' priority MAYOR Bloomberg demanded Sunday that President Obama make cracking down on guns his "No. 1" agenda - even as Democratic governors of two states where carnage has occurred failed to back him up. "It's unbelievable, and it only happens in America, and it happens again and again," Bloomberg said on "Meet the Press," two days after one of the deadliest mass killings in American history took place at the Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. "We kill people in schools, we kill them in hospitals, we kill them in religious organizations, we kill them when they're young, we kill them when they're old, and we've just got to stop this," Bloomberg said. The mayor's comments came as Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) said on the same show that she would introduce a renewed assault weapons ban on the first day of the new Congress in January. Rep. Carolyn McCarthy (D-L.I.), whose husband was killed in a mass shooting on the Long Island Rail Road in 1993, also cited legislation she had introduced in the House to improve the background check system for guns, as well as a bill to reduce the capacity of ammo clips. But Bloomberg grabbed the spotlight by striking a more confrontational tone. He accused Obama - who has said he supports an assault weapons ban - of failing to act. "It's time for the President, I think, to stand up and lead," he said. "This should be his No. 1 agenda. He's President of the United States, and if he does nothing during his second term, something like 48,000 Americans will be killed with illegal guns. His job isn't just to be well-meaning, his job is to perform.

1738.text: GUN-1398 PRIMARY Assault Weapons Are Designed for Murder To the Editor: Gary Kleck sees the good-versus-evil attitude of gun control advocates as "only oratory" aimed at the National Rifle Association ("Assault Weapons Aren't the Problem," Op-Ed, Sept. 1). He has nothing to say about the oratory generated by the N.R.A. that is so often peppered with distortions and false claims. That is understandable, as his entire argument reflects the kind of approach found in any issue of the American Rifleman or American Hunter, the two magazines published monthly by the National Rifle Association. And like so many of the articles in those journals, Mr. Kleck's analysis of the gun control problem distorts the evidence on some key points. His opening salvo is aimed at destroying the concept of assault weapons as lethal, military-style instruments. Semiautomatic firearms that "fire only one shot with each trigger pull" he describes as having only "the cosmetic appearance of a military gun." Why does he think so many of those guns are included in the internationally known reference work, Jane's Infantry Weapons? The reason is precisely because they were designed for the military. In the face of that fact, Mr. Kleck's statement that semiautomatic assault weapons are not designed to be "especially lethal" is simply ludicrous. Let him try that explanation on the parents of the children who were killed or maimed in the Stockton, Calif., massacre, or any of the other multiple killings by assault rifles.

characters: victims of the Stockton, Calif., massacre

1742.text: GUN-8299 PRIMARY Don't Give Up on Brady THE NATIONAL Rifle Association apparently is pleased with a new study that finds no evidence that the Brady handgun law has reduced gun deaths in America. The study, the NRA's James Jay Baker told The Post, shows that "schemes like the Brady waiting period have nothing to do with reducing criminal behavior." Coming, as it does, in the middle of an election year in which gun control is an issue, the study--published last week by the Journal of the American Medical Association--seems like a splash of cold water on the premise that controlling access to handguns can affect homicide and suicide rates. The reality, however, is more complicated. The study examined firearms deaths before and after the Brady background check requirements went into effect both in states that already had similar laws and in states that did not. The authors reasoned that if Brady worked, shooting deaths would decline faster in the states that had to make changes to comply with the new law. Yet, though firearms homicides declined nationwide, the study found no difference between the rate of decline in the 32 states that made changes under Brady and elsewhere--though it did find such a difference with respect to handgun suicides among the elderly. The data are interesting because they suggest that Brady's effects on handgun murders have been subtle. But the study does not prove, as the NRA wishes, that gun control has failed.

characters: Journal of the American Medical Association

1744.text: GUN-1747 PRIMARY Coming Back; Get Guns Off the Street Last year, gunfire killed 1,559 New Yorkers, including numerous innocents hit by bullets meant for others. The gunfire did something else, just as destructive in its own way: It raised the fear level among seven million New Yorkers. Hoodlums with guns have long been part of New York City. But suddenly the guns are more ubiquitous and powerful, the hoodlums younger and more volatile. Citizens who might once have challenged someone smoking on the subway hesitate now, for fear of a lethal response. Guns are drawn over aggressive driving, casual insults, petty thefts. Removing illegal guns from the streets would dramatically enhance safety and reduce fear. New strategies for gun control are acutely needed; most can be pursued within current budgets, if the Mayor and Police Commissioner will focus on the need. The gun problem has assumed emergency proportions. It demands an emergency response. New York City's Sullivan law, one of the nation's strictest, licenses only people who show clear need to carry guns. But tough licensing is not enough when social and economic forces expand the trade in illegal guns as a deadly side effect of drug traffic. Crack dealers, younger and wilder than the old-line gangsters who managed the heroin trade, consider 9-millimeter semiautomatic handguns, sawed-off shotguns and military assault rifles essential. As the crack culture grew more entrenched, gun toting became something even for "good" kids to emulate. No one knows how many illegal guns are on the streets, but homicide statistics confirm the alarming trend: In 1990, guns were used in 69 percent of homicides; in 1980, it was 58 percent.

1745.text: GUN-7483 PRIMARY Gun-show initiative passes test Colorado Supreme Court rejects challenges to fall ballot measure The Colorado Supreme Court cleared the way Monday for a  citizen initiative to require background checks at gun shows. If the measure reaches the November ballot, Colorado voters  will be the first in the nation to decide whether every firearm  sale at a gun show must be preceded by a call to check the  customer's criminal record. Proponents expect a high-stakes battle with the National  Rifle Association, which has already begun shopping for a Colorado  political consultant to help defeat the initiative. In legal challenges, four groups of gun owners tried to keep  the initiative off the ballot. They contended that its language  was misleading, that it contained more than one subject and that  it failed to adequately assess its likely cost to taxpayers. Supreme Court justices rejected those challenges in a  unanimous opinion. They held that the initiative has a single  purpose, that the law enforcement and incarceration costs of  regulating all gun-show sales were adequately described as  undetermined, and that the citizen initiative is a fundamental constitutional right in Colorado. The ruling was a victory for SAFE Colorado, a group formed  after four guns sold privately at gun shows were used in the 1999  Columbine High School massacre. The sponsors announced at a news  conference Monday that they had collected signatures from 63,424  people - enough to put the initiative on the ballot - but are  continuing to circulate petitions to make sure enough registered voters have signed.

1743.text: NA-42993 PRIMARY 'Smart' Firearm Draws Wrath of the Gun Lobby BEVERLY HILLS, Calif. -- Belinda Padilla does not pick up unknown calls anymore, not since someone posted her cellphone number on an online forum for gun enthusiasts. A few fuming-mad voice mail messages and heavy breathers were all it took. Then someone snapped pictures of the address where she has a P.O. box and put those online, too. In a crude, cartoonish scrawl, this person drew an arrow to the blurred image of a woman passing through the photo frame. '' Belinda?'' the person wrote. '' Is that you?'' Her offense? Trying to market and sell a new .22-caliber handgun that uses a radio frequency-enabled stopwatch to identify the authorized user so no one else can fire it. Ms. Padilla and the manufacturer she works for, Armatix, intended to make the weapon the first ''smart gun'' for sale in the United States. But shortly after Armatix went public with its plans to start selling in Southern California, Ms. Padilla, a fast-talking, hard-charging Beverly Hills businesswoman who leads the company's fledgling American division, encountered the same uproar that has stopped gun control advocates, Congress, President Obama and lawmakers across the country as they seek to pass tougher laws and promote new technologies they contend will lead to fewer firearms deaths. Lately, there has been little standing in the way of the muscle of the gun lobby, whose advocates recently derailed Mr. Obama's nominee for surgeon general, Vivek Murthy, a Boston doctor who has expressed alarm about the frequency of shooting deaths.

1747.text: GUN-3309 PRIMARY PISTOL-PACKING BY WOMEN MAY BE OVERSTATED Gun manufacturers say she's out there. So do gun groups such as the NRA, and the media have jumped on the story. But the pistol-packing female may be greatly exaggerated. According to a new study by the University of Chicago's National Opinion Research Center (NORC), female gun ownership has remained virtually unchanged in America since 1980. "There's absolutely no indication that women are more likely to own guns or handguns in the last 14 years," said Tom Smith, director of NORC's General Social Survey and author of a study analyzing media reports about the alleged gun craze among women. The whole thing was "fabricated by the gun groups" because the male market became saturated, gun sales dropped and gun manufacturers wanted to mine American women as a potential new market, according to Smith's study released recently by the anti-gun Violence Policy Center in Washington. The gun groups, including the National Rifle Association and other industry representatives, deny fabricating anything. They cannot disprove NORC's data, they said, but they find it hard to believe. "Why do we claim more women are owning guns? Because we make a line of guns, the Ladysmith line, and we have sold quite a number over the years. And I don't think men are buying those guns," said Ken Jorgensen, director of communications at Smith & Wesson, which pioneered handguns for women in the late 1980s.

1746.text: GUN-2388 PRIMARY HEAVY NEW SALES TAX SUGGESTED FOR GUNS; LEVY COULD HELP FINANCE HEALTH-CARE PLAN Linking the toll of gun violence with spiraling health-care costs, some lawmakers are calling for new taxes on handguns, assault weapons and ammunition. "If we're going to stem the flow of dangerous weapons flooding our streets, we need to make consumers think twice before they buy," said Sen. Bill Bradley, D-N.J. He has proposed a 30 percent sales tax and an increase in the federal licensing fee for gun dealers from $ 10 a year to $ 3,000 every three years. Bradley is a member of the Senate Finance Committee, which will help craft the health-care reform bill. But convincing a majority of lawmakers that a tax increase is appropriate won't be easy, a fact that gun control advocates acknowledge. The proposals already have raised the ire of the National Rifle Association and its allies, who dismiss a tax increase as both unconstitutional and ineffective. "That concept is simply a diversion and a distraction from the real issues . . . of the violent crime problem," said Rep. Bill McCollum, R-Fla. Even some of the most outspoken gun control supporters in Congress concede that the tax proposals might do little to reduce crime. "I don't have a problem with the taxes," said Rep. Charles E. Schumer, D-N.Y., sponsor of Brady bill legislation. "But people who want to get a bullet are not going to not buy a bullet because it costs 25 cents more."

1748.text: NA-40606 PRIMARY The NRA is (gulp) right Our view o The twisted tale of how the gun lobby opposed the mother of all pro-gun bills. Note to Missouri Republicans: When you are so far out there on a gun bill that the National Rifle Association has to pull back, you're doing it even wronger than usual. Such is the case with this year's version of the gun nullification bill sponsored by Sen. Brian Nieves, R-Washington. The Senate gave initial approval to the bill last week, but now the NRA is urging the same Republicans who passed it to kill it because of an amendment the gun lobby doesn't like. That's quite the 24-hour turnaround on a piece of legislation that Senate Republicans obviously think is more important than schools, or jobs, or any of the other important matters they could have been talking about. How does this happen? Let's hit rewind. The bill, which Mr. Nieves calls the Second Amendment Preservation Act, actually preserves nothing but Mr. Nieves' solid reputation for legislative irrelevance. It's a slightly watered down version of the same legislation passed by the Legislature last year and then vetoed by Gov. Jay Nixon. The bill is, to use the words of state Rep. Jay Barnes, R-Jefferson City, "plainly unconstitutional." Mr. Barnes is an attorney. Mr. Nixon's veto was sustained after two key Senate leaders - Sen. Tom Dempsey, R-St. Charles, and Sen. Ron Richards, R-Joplin - found some intestinal fortitude and voted against it during last September's veto session. Mr. Dempsey, the Senate president pro tem, and Mr. Richard, the majority floor leader, never should have allowed such a piece of unconstitutional nonsense to the floor to begin with.

1752.text: GUN-11718 PRIMARY Dec. 24 letters to the editor Heroic teachers at Sandy Hook The next time someone tells me teachers are overpaid, I will remind them of the Sandy Hook massacre. Gil Stratton Livermore Simply must alter the Constitution Destruction was brought upon vulnerable elementary school children in Newtown. Those children didn't bear arms and their parents now don't want to be anywhere near weapons. Even though I live across the country, in Richmond, my parents still feel the impact of letting me go to school. The Second Amendment, which states citizens have the right to bear arms, is no longer relevant in today's world. Though we can't blame the Founding Fathers for not having foreseen such tragedies caused by weapons they could never have imagined, we should now alter this amendment and adjust it to ensure the security of our nation's citizens. Lesly Sanchez Richmond Lesly is a student at Leadership Public School in Richmond. Stricter gun laws urgently needed I implore President Barack Obama and my Congress members to take action on gun restrictions. The Newtown massacre is beyond description. It's incomprehensible to imagine what was in the deranged mind of this young, near-child himself. His actions are nearly unspeakable. But we must speak about the insanity regarding the weapons industry and flood of small arms filling homes nationwide. We've come through a very divisive election that left some people feeling excluded, disappointed and angry. That's reflected in Congress with the level of goodwill and cooperation at an all-time low.

1751.text: GUN-7323 PRIMARY CLINTON AND NRA TAKE FIGHT TO TV AFTER BEING ACCUSED OF "KNEE-JERK" REACTIONS, THE NRA ACCUSED THE PRESIDENT OF EXPLOITING GUN DEATHS. Accusations of dishonesty and scare tactics flew yesterday in a televised brawl between President Clinton and the National Rifle Association over tougher gun laws. In a mocking broadside, Clinton tore into the group for its "knee-jerk reaction to any gun-safety measure." Clinton's accusations on ABC's This Week brought an incendiary rebuttal from NRA executive vice president Wayne LaPierre, who said the President exploited gun deaths for political purposes. "I've come to believe that he needs a certain level of violence in this country," LaPierre said. "He's willing to accept a certain level of killing to further his political agenda - and his vice president, too." At issue was a new advertising campaign in which NRA president Charlton Heston all but accuses Clinton of lying in his characterizations of the group as an impediment to sensible laws and public safety. But the sparring was more broadly over the President's two-track effort to use his final year in office to win some of the gun controls that have eluded him so far and to inject the subject into the presidential campaign pitting Vice President Gore against Republican Texas Gov. George W. Bush. Clinton said the NRA was "ruthlessly brutal" in helping to defeat members of Congress who gave the nation such laws as the Brady Bill waiting period for gun purchases and the ban on assault-type weapons.

1749.text: GUN-11555 PRIMARY Yes, blame gun laws for Aurora massacre With frightening, rapid-fire frequency, gunmen wielding combat-style weapons keep forcing Americans to face the consequences of our irrational acceptance of the nation's too-easy access to guns. How many mass murderers will it take before lawmakers from Congress on down break the National Rifle Association's grip? A masked man turned an Aurora, Colo., movie theater into a horror show after midnight Friday, killing 12 and injuring 58. The massacre followed more than a decade of mass shootings that have claimed dozens of lives, including the 1999 killings in nearby Columbine. Yet the suspected gunman, former doctoral student James E. Holmes, 24, hadn't been in custody a day before politicians, pundits, pollsters, and even some gun-control advocates predicted the carnage wouldn't lead to more sensible gun-control laws. Both President Obama and Republican challenger Mitt Romney offered heartfelt condolences to victims and their families, but said not a word about gun control. Philadelphia Police Commissioner Charles H. Ramsey said too many policymakers "lack the courage to do anything." But it wouldn't be hard to keep guns out of the hands of disturbed persons without revoking law-abiding citizens' right to hunt, collect guns, or carry one for self-defense. To start, restore the ban on assault weapons and limit large-capacity ammunition magazines. Next, impose a monthly limit on gun purchases to thwart anyone like Holmes, whom police say legally purchased his four-gun arsenal within weeks. Such steps could limit the killing power of assailants when other measures fail to keep guns out of their hands.

characters: Philadelphia Police Commissioner Charles H. Ramsey

1750.text: GUN-2616 PRIMARY Sabotage on the Brady Bill YOU KNEW they would be back with a vengeance when it came time for a vote on the Brady bill, and now, with a House vote set tomorrow, the handguns-are-great lobbyists are pushing a new plan to sabotage the waiting period proposal. Because an overwhelming number of Americans -- including 68 percent of the National Rifle Association's members, according to a Harris poll -- now support the Brady bill as a matter of public safety, the handgun-pushers on the Hill are peddling some amendments that would not only wreck the proposal but also undo laws already enacted in various states. One amendment the NRA Washington lobbyists are pressing on their paid-subsidiary House members calls for the Brady bill to end after a certain period of time regardless of whether a national check system is in place. Experts hired by the Justice Department have said that even a five-year date for establishing any kind of efficient national check system is probably unrealistic. So what's the point of enacting an automatic end to the Brady bill no matter what? Most thinking members of Congress can figure this one out -- and if they do, they may catch up to their constituents. Another amendment would wipe out all existing state waiting periods and other safeguards once any instant check system took effect. It also would bar states from enacting any measure beyond a telephone check system. For members of Congress who can't read through this one, here are just a few of the laws around the country that would be blown away: Virginia's one-handgun-per-month limit aimed at gun-runners; waiting periods of varying durations up to 15 days in Maryland, California, Oregon, Florida, Indiana and Connecticut; and permit-for-purchase systems in New York, North Carolina, New Jersey and Michigan.

1753.text: GUN-856 PRIMARY LIKE, WELL, YOU KNOW, Like, Well, You Know, (REST) That Bushspeak Thing Tom Wicker COLUMN NEW YORK Asked about his refusal to support a ban on semi-automatic rifles, President Bush promised to take ''a hard look.'' Beyond that, he said little and said it badly: ''But I also want to have - be the president that protects the rights of, of people to, to have arms. And that - so you don't go so far that the legitimate rights on some legislation are, are, you know, impinged on.'' It was discouraging enough to hear the president expressing banalities (''I am in the mode of being deeply concerned and would like to be a part of finding a national answer'') on one of the deadliest problems Americans face; or reaching for political advantage (''I'd like to find a way to be supportive of the police who are out there on the line all the time'') when he has been unwilling even to endorse a ban on cop-killer bullets.   But could he not express himself at least in, like, maybe, you know, sixth- or seventh-grade English, rather than speaking as if he were Dan Quayle trying to explain the Holocaust? Bush also can say nothing at numbing length - this, for example, on whether or not his support for former President Reagan's Strategic Defense Initiative was conditional on a 90-day review: ''I'm not ruling anything in or not. I have stated my support for the principle of SDI. I have not favored what some would call premature deployment, but on the other hand I will be very interested in seeing what this overall review comes up with. And I'm not going to close any doors or open any in regards to this or any other systems. We're going to have to make some tough choices on defense. I'm aware of that and so let's wait and see what the review produces.'' The president may be saying he might favor ''premature deployment'' if the ''overall review'' ''comes up with'' such a proposal. You betcha.   On his way to Japan, at a stopover in Alaska, Bush was in good form. Asked at a news conference if he would present new initiatives in Tokyo, he replied, or something: ''We're ready to roll, yeah.'' Let them figure that one out at the KGB. If, however, yeah meant that Bush indeed was ready with ''new initiatives,'' he was confounding his national security adviser, Brent Scowcroft. Scowcroft had said that ''the substance will not be extensive'' in the president's talks with other leaders.   Most Americans talk about as Bush has been talking. Almost everyone lapses into cliches, leaves sentences hanging in the confidence that listeners will understand, adds meaning to utterance with eyebrows and hands, and relies on, like, you know, the vernacular and slang. But Bush is not ''most Americans.'' He is the president and needs to speak as well as look like a president.   For one thing, he should set a needed example, in precision of speech as well as clarity of thought, neither of which distinguishes his constituency nor many of his predecessors. Bush will find, moreover, that Americans may be briefly charmed by a president who talks as they do, but that the charm will wear off; remember Jimmy Carter's sweater. Finally, a president can't afford to be imprecise, in speech or tho ught, when his every word is weighed in the capitals of the world.   Andover, where are you when we need you? Eli Yale, speak to your favored son.

1755.text: GUN-3212 PRIMARY 'Make Neighbor's Day' law OK'd by committee A House committee yesterday approved expanding Colorado's pioneering "Make My Day" law into something akin to "Make My Neighbor's Day," passing a bill that would let anyone shoot an armed felon, even if the shooter isn't directly threatened or if the culprit is fleeing. HB 1250 by Rep. Jeanne Faatz, R-Denver, passed the House State Affairs Committee 6-5, despite the opposition of committee chairman Bill Jerke, who recounted his own dramatic story of shooting a fleeing felon in 1987. A second, similar bill, HB 1173 by Rep. Jim Congrove, R-Arvada, was approved, 8-5, by the House Judiciary Committee after a hearing that went late into the evening. Congrove's bill would protect from criminal prosecution or civil liability anyone who uses deadly force to protect a third party from the threat of violence or a felonious act. The Faatz bill was prompted by the shooting of a burglar by a private investigator last year. It would allow citizens to use the same deadly force as police against any armed felons they encounter, not just in their own homes. "For very serious crimes - crimes of violence, burglary or robbery - a person can use deadly force in order to arrest the individual, to bring the person into the criminal-justice system," Faatz said. But opponents fear the measure could spark vigilantism, and that many gun owners - unlike police officers - aren't trained in making the decision to shoot.

1756.text: GUN-11553 PRIMARY The Gun Rights Tilt President Obama has staked out some surprisingly liberal positions during this campaign season. From gay marriage to illegal immigration, the White House has deliberately created stark contrasts with the Republican Party on ground where Democratic politicians have often been on defense. But not on gun control. As with previous tragedies, last Friday's massacre in Aurora, Colo., inspired a groundswell of op-eds and blog posts making the case for stricter gun laws. But the president conspicuously failed to embrace this argument. The White House's official statement on the issue, offered by Jay Carney, Obama's press secretary, reaffirmed the president's support for the "Second Amendment rights of the American people" while urging more effective enforcement of gun laws as they exist today. This caution is not surprising, given the public's distinctly anti-gun control tilt. The tilt itself, though, has not been adequately explained. The American love affair with guns and gun culture is deep and enduring, but 20 years ago it coexisted with broad support for more restrictions on how and when and whether weapons could be bought and sold. In 1990, Gallup reported almost 80 percent support for the proposition that gun laws should become "more strict." By 2010, though, just 44 percent of Americans wanted stricter gun laws, compared to 54 percent who opposed them. That's a striking swing for such a short time period.

1754.text: GUN-5458 PRIMARY Gun Marketing Is Issue in Trial Against Makers On the night of July 27, 1993, Njuzi Ray, 17, was fatally shot in a dispute on a Brooklyn street. A suspect was later acquitted, and nobody else has been arrested for the killing. Normally a case like this would have long disappeared from public awareness, lost in the city's crime statistics and recalled only by the victim's family and friends. But the slaying in Bedford-Stuyvesant is once more in the spotlight, as a subject of a potentially precedent-setting lawsuit against the gun industry. Opening arguments in the suit could begin today in United States District Court in Brooklyn. In the case, Mr. Ray's mother, Freddie Hamilton, and the relatives of five other people killed in separate shootings in New York City and Yonkers in recent years are seeking to hold the gun industry collectively liable for the deaths, just as many recent suits have attacked the tobacco industry as responsible for deaths and illnesses from smoking. At the heart of the claims is the argument that more than 30 gun manufacturers and distributors have marketed and distributed handguns so negligently that dealers in states with lax gun control laws are oversupplied with them. Many of those guns, in turn, end up in the hands of criminals and youths in states with tough gun laws, like New York, the suit contends. The suit is considered potentially precedent-setting because it invokes the theory of "negligent distribution" of guns, rather than charging liability on the basis of the product's inherent harmfulness, said David Leebron, the dean of Columbia University Law School and an expert in tort litigation.

1757.text: GUN-30164 PRIMARY NRA backs deadly force measure JEFFERSON CITY * The National Rifle Association wants Missouri to join 25 states that have made it easier for people to use deadly force to defend themselves. The association calls its proposal the "castle doctrine," after a concept in English common law. The idea is that people who are threatened in their homes have no duty to retreat before using deadly force. "Put the law back on the side of the victim, where it belongs," John C. Sigler, the NRA's first vice president, urged Thursday at a gun-rights rally in the Capitol Rotunda. Bills backed by the group are pending in House and Senate committees. One is scheduled for a hearing next week. Critics say the proposals are unnecessary and overly broad. Under current law, a homicide can be considered justified if a person fears imminent death or serious physical injury. "That seems to cover it just fine," St. Louis County Prosecuting Attorney Robert McCulloch said in an interview. "You don't have to retreat if you're defending yourself. The law's very adequate on self-defense." Gun-rights activists say courts have interpreted the law too narrowly. Kevin Jamison, a lawyer in Gladstone, Mo., has written a book on Missouri's weapons and self-defense law. The bright orange paperback, which he distributes to legislators and reporters, says that a Missouri Supreme Court decision in 1962 allowed deadly force against an intruder only if the intruder was killed while breaking in.

1758.text: GUN-4097 PRIMARY DOLE SHOOTS DOWN REPEAL OF BAN WASHINGTON Bob Dole is backing off his pledge to repeal the assault weapons ban, saying it's so ineffective it's not worth repealing. But Democrats crowed that Dole, in an election-year conversion, is trying a "ludicrous" middle-road approach to appeal to moderates and his friends at the National Rifle Association. Dole, campaigning in Richmond, Va., yesterday at a police academy, said it's time to quit debating which weapons to ban and move to nationwide instant computer checks for gun buyers. A year ago, Dole told the NRA he'd make repealing the assault weapons ban a priority but when he had the chance this year as Senate majority leader he didn't schedule a vote. "Let's be realistic," Dole said yesterday. "Of the 17 weapons that were specifically outlawed, 11 are already back on the market in some other form. . . . Instead of endlessly debating which guns to ban, we ought to be emphasizing what works." The NRA insisted that Dole's comments were not a turnabout saying he merely was agreeing that Clinton's gun ban did not work. But the President said he was baffled by Dole's latest comments. "I fought for and passed the Brady bill, the assault weapons ban, the 100,000 police. If he now believes that we were right on that, then I applaud that," he said.

1760.text: GUN-4061 PRIMARY SCHUMER ZAPS NRA ON RACE REPORT Rep. Charles Schumer yesterday took aim at a National Rifle Association report that says race plays a bigger role in rising murder rates than the easy availability of guns. "The NRA has consistently refused to admit the obvious: The number of guns on our streets increase the number of murders of police, children and others," Schumer said. "Now they are going to a new extreme. To say it's not guns, but the genetics of race, is a tawdry and evil form of race-baiting," he said. But NRA spokesman Chip Walker said the report simply echoes earlier findings that young black males in inner cities are more likely to be homicide victims than other groups, and that shooting deaths are often highest in cities with strict gun laws. Walker admitted that the study's author, the NRA's Paul Blackman, is a longtime pro-gun advocate whose Ph. D. in government may not make him an expert in such areas. The NRA report prompted Schumer to call on Sen. Arlen Specter (R-Pa.), chairman of the appropriations subcommittee for Health and Human Services, to restore funding for federal studies on gun violence. Earlier this month the House, under NRA pressure, axed money for firearms research at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. "Not only do NRA partisans want to muzzle reputable research on firearms, they apparently want to replace it with a substitute explanation that emphasizes race," Schumer wrote to Specter. "This irresponsible agenda proves again why the CDC's impartial science is so important."

1761.text: GUN-2248 PRIMARY GUN OWNERS NOT EQUAL IN ALAMEDA COUNTY I am writing this letter to express my outrage upon learning that Alameda County Supervisor Don Perata holds a permit to carry a concealed weapon (" 'Zero tolerance' author vows to return gun permit," Local News, Jan. 12). The supervisor has made repeated calls for Draconian gun control measures and now for stepped-up enforcement against citizens with his "Zero Tolerance" resolution. We have here a privileged member of the community seeking to dramatically increase punishment of others for an act he regularly commits, namely the possession of a concealed weapon for personal protection. One may legitimately argue that citizens must obtain a permit for this privilege (as the supervisor has done). It is, however, virtually impossible for a non-politico citizen to obtain the privilege in Alameda County. Real need, a clean record, documented training and community ties do not help. Perhaps it is a luxury enjoyed only by those whom our system chooses to allow to protect themselves. But if the danger is sufficient that supervisors need personally accept responsibility for their own protection, why is it not sufficient for the rest of us to do the same? Are only our leaders' lives of value? One wonders whether the Board of Supervisors was aware of Supervisor Perata's concealed weapon permit when they voted for his "Zero Tolerance" resolution. How could they support his resolution, given this situation? Would they embarrass themselves with the same votes today?

1762.text: GUN-295 PRIMARY Briefing T HE National Rifle Association's ''open letter to all Polish Americans,'' a newspaper advertisement in which martial law in Poland was construed as a lesson in the American public's right and need to bear arms, has backfired with at least one leading Polish-American. ''I don't appreciate what they're trying to do,'' said Aloysius Mazewski, president of the Polish-American Congress and Polish National Alliance. '' It sounds great to talk of the Polish people revolting, to talk of other people spilling their blood, but this isn't the time for that kind of talk.'' In sympathizing with the Polish public, the N.R.A. ad contrasted the loss of freedom under martial law with the Bill of Rights in the United States, noting that the Polish authorities had confiscated firearms. '' Poland has precisely the firearms laws that the N.R.A.  has been opposing in the United States,'' the rifle association contended, hailing the Second Amendment's gun guarantee as ''the ultimate protection'' of all other constitutional rights. Mr.  Mazewsk i was particularly upset by the lobbying group's argument that the Polish people were willing to fight but that ''the authorities have all the guns.'' ''I don't think the right to bear arms is the same thing as starting a bloody revolution,'' he said. '' It's easy to talk that way, but I don't appreciate it.'' S WIFT as the Sugarplum Fairy, a rubber-faced Ronald Reagan suddenly flitted from the wings at the Kennedy Center as the American Ballet Theater ad libbed some fun New Year's Eve. The Reagan mask had been donned quickly in an offstage instant by a dancer who popped back onto center stage without missing a beat. The permanently earnest Reagan grin and latex pompadour weathered each pirouette famously, as the audience cheered still another doll-face coming to life in ''The Nutcracker.''

1765.text: GUN-1500 PRIMARY Guns Are Result, Not Cause, of Crime To the Editor: "If Troops, Then Guns" (letter, May 13) rightly points out the tragedy of illegal firearms, specifically as used during the Los Angeles riots. Nevertheless, James Rudolph seems to assume that regulating sale and possession of such weapons will solve the problem. That is no solution. Like the drug trade, handguns are the result, not the cause, of a larger problem: the decay of our cities because of a lack of education and employment of the urban poor. As a result, the natural urge is to turn to a life of crime, especially drugs, which naturally involves guns. And like drugs, there is no effective law that will prevent their purchase and use. Therefore, to speak of the need for laws to curb gunfire is to offer a false solution and to ignore the real problem. JOHN WEISL Weston, Conn., May 14, 1992

1759.text: GUN-7516 PRIMARY GOP PUSHES BILLS TO BLOCK CLINTON FROM USING COURTS AIM IS TO CUT OFF FUNDS FOR FIGHT AGAINST GUN, TOBACCO INDUSTRIES As President Clinton and Republicans in Congress slog through another season of dispute over spending priorities, they also are locked in a power struggle over Clinton's use of the courts to achieve victories deniedhim by his political enemies on Capitol Hill. Taking advantage of their superior authority over the details of the spending bills that fuel the government, Republicans are pushing several proposals to cut off the flow of money to Clinton's legal attacks on the gun and tobacco industries. After failing to get Congress to back his plan to tighten federal controls on handgun sales and cigarette marketing, the Clinton administration, in its waning days, has pursued an aggressive legal strategy of suing or threatening to sue the industries to extract concessions from them. Earlier this year, one of the nation's largest handgun makers, Smith & Wesson, agreed to a number of concessions, including child-safety locks and stepped-up oversight of retailers. Those concessions came only after Clinton threatened to join some 30 cities and counties that have sued several gun companies to recoup the public costs of gun-related crime. In September, the Justice Department sued nine tobacco companies for allegedly deceiving the American public into believing its products were safe and for marketing smoking products to children. The government says the industry should pay it billions of dollars for the cost of treating military personnel, veterans and Medicare beneficiaries for smoking-related disease.

1764.text: NA-43728 PRIMARY Bloomberg wants change in the GOP NEW YORK - Michael Bloomberg, America's most prominent and deep-pocketed advocate for gun control, would rather rehabilitate Republicans than oust them. "Somebody got them the way they are now," the mayor of New York said in a recent interview as he sat in the bullpen offices of City Hall, surrounded by a buzzing staff, blinking Bloomberg terminals and clocks telling the same time in each of the five boroughs. "Why can't you change them?" On Monday, Bloomberg will headline a summit on guns at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health in Baltimore, another opportunity for the outspoken mayor to deliver an indictment of Washington's failure to do anything meaningful on the issue. Although the Democrat-turned-Republican- turned-independent says he practices a "noble and practical" brand of post-partisan politics, when it comes to gun laws, he is more aligned with one party than the other. Democrats in the White House and in Congress are working closely with his advocacy group, Mayors Against Illegal Guns, to enact his gun-control agenda. And Republicans, especially those in the House, don't seem the least bit interested. "Oh sure," Bloomberg said, he would blame Republicans if they blocked new gun-control legislation in the House. "But having said that, I won't let the Democrats off the hook." He added that when Democrats "were in power, they didn't do it," and President Obama "campaigned on an assault-weapons ban and he didn't do it, so spare me."

1763.text: GUN-7755 PRIMARY AS MILLION MOM MARCH NEARS, CLINTON AND BUSH TALK GUN SAFETY * Bush says Texas will give away free trigger locks. The president promotes "smart gun" technology. Gore says he used to vote with the NRA, but they parted ways. Two days before the Million Mom March for gun control, George W. Bush said Friday that he will distribute free trigger locks in Texas while President Bill Clinton announced grants to develop "smart gun" technology. Filling out the election-year debate, Vice President Al Gore acknowledged that he often voted with the National Rifle Association during his early years in Congress. But he said they parted ways as he watched "the flood of cheap handguns and the wave of gun violence sweep across our country." A second group of women - calling themselves Second Amendment Sisters-Armed Informed Mothers - says it opposes stricter gun control laws and plans to stage a counterdemonstration. Bush has drawn fire for signing a bill allowing Texans to carry con cealed handguns with a permit. He said in a TV interview Friday that he welcomed Sunday's march. Gore's office said he would stay home Sunday to baby-sit his grandson while his wife, Tipper, and eldest daughter, Karenna, join the march. Clinton dismissed Bush's trigger-lock giveaways, saying the presumed Republican presidential nominee was simply trying to hide the fact that "the NRA will control policy" in the White House if Bush wins. Bush said, "There's only going to be one person in the Oval Office, and that's going to be me. And I'm going to make judgments based upon how I think America can be a safer place."

1767.text: GUN-6284 PRIMARY NATION IN BRIEF Gun sales on county property may be barred Gun and ammunition sales on Los Angeles County property would be banned under a proposed ordinance backed by a majority of the Board of Supervisors. Such a ban would affect four gun shows held each year at Fairplex in Pomona. Earlier this month, California officials placed a moratorium on scheduling new gun shows at all state fairgrounds. The shows already scheduled at the 54 state fairgrounds will still be held. Also in California, the state attorney general is dropping an appeal of a lawsuit over gun registration, clearing the way for the state to order owners of about 1,600 assault weapons to give them to law enforcement. Attorney General Bill Lockyer will ask the Legislature to provide funding to compensate owners who turn in the weapons, valued at $ 750 to $ 1,500 each, spokesman Nathan Barankin said Tuesday. A 1989 state law prohibited the sale and possession of 62 models, but it let gun owners keep them if they registered them with the state by March 30, 1992.   Nearly 50,000 assault weapons were registered by the deadline. A judge ruled in a 1997 lawsuit that registration after March 1992 was invalid. Refusal to display Ten Commandments upheld A California appeals court has upheld a school district's refusal to display the Ten Commandments on a ball field fence. Downey High School was soliciting ads to pay for new uniforms in 1995 when it refused to put up a local engineering company's sign made up with the Ten Commandments under a caption inviting readers to ''meditate on these principles to live by.'' When the Downey Unified School District instead removed all ads from the fence in 1996 and ended the fund-raiser, Edward DiLoreto, chief executive of the engineering firm who was represented by the conservative Individual Rights Foundation, sued the district, claiming violation of free speech and freedom of religion. Superior Court Judge Thomas McKnew ruled against him and was upheld by the 2nd District Court of Appeal on Tuesday.

1766.text: NA-44831 PRIMARY Two years after Sandy Hook, the gun control movement has new energy Washington State voters last month defied the gun lobby and approved a measure extending firearm background checks private transfers. For the gun reform movement, the victory was not only a policy success but also potentially a strategic one, offering a roadmap for activism to come. That gun reformers see a path forward is significant, because for at least two decades they have lost much ground to their better-funded and better-organized opponents. Firearms laws at the national level and in many states have loosened; the public has grown more supportive of guns in the home and more skeptical about gun regulation; grassroots cadres have arisen to normalize firearms in public life; and political partisans have hardened in a way that makes compromise on the issue very difficult. Against these strong headwinds, the gun control movement struggled to keep moving. But after a spate of mass shootings - Virginia Tech, Tucson, Aurora, and Sandy Hook, among others - pro-reform groups are mobilizing on a scale not seen in more than a decade and doing so with resources and tools that their predecessors lacked. As the mighty gun lobby meets a resurgent gun control movement, now is a good time to take stock of the seismic pro-gun shift that happened largely under the radar in every branch of government and at every level - and how gun reformers are taking lessons from their opponents in how to change laws and culture.

1768.text: GUN-9397 PRIMARY OVERHEARD Among friends While Gov. Tim Pawlenty and House Speaker Steve Sviggum are best friends, they can't resist taking verbal shots at each other. During a pep talk to Republican legislators last week, Pawlenty noted that even Sviggum, a 26-year House veteran, will have to leave the Legislature some day. "You look better when I can't see you," Sviggum interjected. "You sound better when you aren't talking," Pawlenty fired back. Vanilla or semi-automatic? I scream, you scream ... about guns and ice cream? If you are armed with a handgun -- or an ice cream cone -- you had better stay on the sidewalk outside of Peddler's Landing gift shop in downtown Hastings. In the past, business owner Daryl Donath has posted a sign on the door of her store, forbidding shoppers with ice cream from strolling through. This year, she asked her husband to whip up a sign prohibiting guns as well in response to the recently passed law that will allow more Minnesotans to obtain gun permits. "Absolutely No Guns or Ice Cream! Even if you are my Best friend!" reads the fluorescent lime-green sign on the door of her shop. Although Donath chuckles when asked about her sign, she isn't laughing about the new law -- saying she's worried about innocent bystanders being shot, and the possibility of more guns in domestic disputes and road rage incidents.

1769.text: GUN-4877 PRIMARY DURBIN SEEMS TO RELISH BEING AN NRA TARGET OVER BRADY LAW The sign on Sen. Dick Durbin's bedroom door reads: "DICK DURBIN REPEATEDLY TRIED TO TAKE YOUR GUNS AWAY." The message was used by the gun lobby in its unsuccessful attempt to defeat Durbin's bid for re-election to the House of Representatives in 1 994, the year that Congress passed the Brady bill, which requires a waiting period for handgun purchases. The negative campaign did not work then against Durbin, D-Ill; neither did a similar effort two years ago, when Durbin won his Senate seat. But although Durbin prevailed in 1994, many of his Democratic colleagues did not, and his party lost its majority in the House. More than a few analysts attributed the defeat in part to the Brady law's energizing effect on gun enthusiasts, who streamed to the polls to vote for Republicans. The law expires Nov. 30, and Durbin is helping to lead an effort to make the waiting period permanent. He says the law has successfully kept guns out of the hands of thousands of felons by giving police time to do thorough background checks on gun buyers. He says it also has saved lives by imposing, in effect, a "cooling-off" period for hotheads who rush to gun stores with violence on their minds. "This isn't about more gun laws; it's about fewer gun victims," he said at a news conference last week to announce the legislation.

1772.text: GUN-30331 PRIMARY MIKE'S ADS TARGET GUNS - AND POLS MAYOR BLOOMBERG, who has repeatedly said he's not running for President, yesterday placed full-page advertisements in newspapers in states hosting the nation's first presidential contests. Bloomberg funded two full-page ads in The Des Moines Register in Iowa and the New Hampshire Union Leader to promote his anti-gun coalition. The ads cost the billionaire mayor $22,203. They feature him and a host of other mayors in his nonprofit group, Mayors Against Illegal Guns. "Where do the presidential candidates stand on gun control?" the ads ask, featuring excerpts from a questionnaire that the gun coalition released on Dec. 9, asking for the candidates' responses by Jan. 2. The questions in the survey include: "Do you support increasing the maximum penalty for illegal firearms possession from 10 years to 15 years?" No answers have been turned in so far. A spokesman for Bloomberg said neither the mayor nor other members of his coalition plan to visit either state before Iowa's Jan. 3 caucus and New Hampshire's Jan. 8 primary. The coalition plans to post responses to its questionnaire on its Web site, but won't endorse any of the candidates.

1770.text: NA-44406 PRIMARY Sticks and guns at the Virginia Capitol: One is banned, the other not Regarding the Jan. 28 front-page article "Mall shooter gave no hint of what was to come": Here is a young man, polite and presentable, and yet he kills. He has a gun in his backpack. What is the difference between him and another polite young man I encountered? Last week, I saw with my own eyes a gun sticking out of the latter's backpack, and I was scared. It happened inside the Virginia Capitol in Richmond, where I went with others to celebrate Martin Luther King Jr. Day and to talk to delegates and senators about the importance of background checks for gun purchases, banning the firing of guns in celebration and keeping guns out of the hands of domestic abusers. We were shocked to find that firearms were permitted inside the building. Why, then, the masquerade of going through security, being frisked and wanded and having our bags X-rayed? Even young mothers carrying babies were wanded. Ironically, during the celebration outside the Capitol, police told people holding a flag or a sign on a short stick to surrender their sticks "because they can be used as weapons." Even more ironically, that morning, on the same spot, a group of gun-rights advocates gathered for their own celebration, most of them visibly carrying guns. Yet the police found that behavior perfectly acceptable. Sometimes life in Virginia is stranger than fiction.

1773.text: NA-44116 PRIMARY Obama to Congress: Finish the job on gun control Three months after an elementary school massacre in Connecticut reignited a debate over the nation's gun laws, President Obama is urging Congress to "join me in finishing the job" by taking swift action on gun-control legislation. Obama, in his weekly radio address released on Saturday, noted the preliminary steps taken in the Senate to advance such measures as universal background checks for all gun buyers and a federal crack-down on gun trafficking. But he chastised Congress for the slow pace of progress, contrasting their indecision with the widespread popular support across the country for strengthening the background check system and other proposals. "Today there is still genuine disagreement among well-meaning people about what steps we should take to reduce the epidemic of gun violence in this country," Obama said. "But you   the American people   have spoken. You've made it clear that it's time to do something." Before departing on his trip to Israel and Jordan, Obama spoke with lawmakers from both parties about the gun measures under consideration, according to a White House official. The president plans to continue pressuring Congress on the issue, including additional travel outside of Washington designed to mobilize public support, said the official, who spoke only on the condition of anonymity. Obama's remarks come after a difficult week for his gun violence agenda. After a ban on assault weapons -- supported by Obama and authored by Sen. Dianne Feinstein (D-Calif.) -- did not gain enough bipartisan traction on Capitol Hill, Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid (D-Nev.) decided not to include it as part of a bill encompassing several other gun-control proposals that the Senate is scheduled to begin debating in early April. Reid's decision severely diminished any prospect of passing a prohibition on hundreds of specific so-called assault weapons, although Feinstein still can introduce the assault weapons ban as an amendment to the full bill.

characters: Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid

1771.text: NA-42057 PRIMARY Illinois is last U.S. state to allow concealed carry of guns CHICAGO   Illinois adopted a law on Tuesday allowing residents to carry concealed guns, becoming the last state in the nation to permit some form of possession of guns in public. The Illinois action was a significant political victory for the gun rights lobby, the National Rifle Association, which strongly supported the new law and lobbied hard for its passage. Illinois was the last holdout among the states in prohibiting residents from carrying guns in public. Gun control has been a hot national issue since 20 children and six adults were killed at an elementary school in Newtown, Connecticut, last December, prompting President Barack Obama to push Congress for tighter gun laws. However, Congress failed to enact a law supported by Obama that would have strengthened background checks for gun purchases. In the state Obama considers home and where he served in the state Senate, the legislature voted to expand gun rights. Democratic Governor Pat Quinn had objected to major parts of the concealed carry proposal. But both chambers of the state legislature voted on Tuesday to override his veto, allowing concealed carry to become the law. The new law says that Illinois state police "shall issue" a permit to carry concealed guns to any applicant who passes a background check, takes 16 hours of required firearms training and meets other conditions. The bill would ban guns in some public places such as bars, schools and hospitals.

1774.text: GUN-4476 PRIMARY Fairfax to End Fingerprinting Of Applicants for Gun Permits The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors decided yesterday to end the practice of fingerprinting applicants for concealed weapon permits, against the recommendation of the county's police chief. An ordinance needed to continue the program, in which applicants' fingerprints are sent to the FBI for checks against prints of known criminals, failed to win passage in a 5 to 5 vote. A state law passed this year required Virginia's local governments to adopt such ordinances if they were to continue using the FBI fingerprint service. The vote in Fairfax occurred two weeks after the Prince William Board of County Supervisors took a similar action. But the Prince William supervisors plan to revisit the issue at a public hearing July 22. In both counties, opponents of fingerprinting argued that the practice didn't make much sense and wasn't worth the money. Even without the fingerprinting, gun permit applicants must show identification and undergo a criminal background check. The only time a fingerprint check helps is if an applicant gives a fake name in an attempt to get around the law, they argued. What's more, said Fairfax Supervisor Michael R. Frey (R-Sully), a concealed weapon permit isn't required for a handgun purchase, so people intent on breaking the law aren't likely to apply for one. "Someone who is a criminal or who has criminal intent is not going to apply for a permit," Frye said.

characters: Prince William Board of County Supervisors

1776.text: NA-41819 PRIMARY Senate clears way for debate on Toomey-Manchin gun bill WASHINGTON -- It was a procedural vote, part of the Senate's arcane process, but it brought tears to the eyes of family members of those killed in Newtown in December. The Senate voted Thursday morning to take the first step toward considering the background-check bill sponsored by Pennsylvania Sen. Pat Toomey, a Republican, and West Virginia's Joe Manchin, a Democrat. While the vote will only allow debate to move ahead, it opens the door to up-or-down votes on the background check bill as well contentious plans to ban assault weapons and high-capacity gun magazines. Those debates are expected in the coming weeks. Needing 60 votes to overcome a Republican filibuster threat, the Senate voted 68-31 to move ahead -- with 16 GOP senators joining Democrats to overcome the potential procedural blockade. Contact Jonathan Tamari at jtamari@phillynews.com, or follow on Twitter @JonathanTamari. Read his blog, "Capitol Inq," at www.philly.com/CapitolInq.

1775.text: GUN-6587 PRIMARY Law ending turkey shoot may get boot Gwinnett County is considering repealing a gun law that was used to end a nearly 50-year tradition of turkey shoots near Duluth. County Attorney Karen Thomas said the law department is "looking at it . . . but at this point (it's) on the books." Repealing or changing a county ordinance requires a vote by the Board of Commissioners. Since police used the law in October to shut down the annual turkey shoots hosted by an American Legion post on Ga. 120 (Duluth Highway), Police Chief Bill Dean has told his officers to stop enforcing it. Once the Legion learned of Dean's memo issuing the order during a court battle over the issue, the veterans resumed their turkey shoots in January. Then earlier this week, the county Solicitor's Office dropped its case against the Legion member charged with violating the county law, the action which had abruptly ended the fall tradition in October. The reason given was " insufficient evidence to prove guilt beyond a reasonable doubt." That, even though the man charged, James David Sarrio, had fired a gun well within 500 feet of a house in the presence of a Gwinnett police officer. The action had been agreed to beforehand, as a way of working out a dispute between the Legion and neighbors in the Woodbridge subdivision just behind the shooting range. While the case proceeded against Sarrio, Legion attorney Walt Britt filed a lawsuit claiming the county's ordinance is unconstitutional. The law is superseded by a more lenient state law that only prohibits gunfire within 50 feet of a road, Britt said.

1779.text: GUN-8442 PRIMARY OUR OPINIONS: Ashcroft's pro-gun ideology is put ahead of U.S. security In John Ashcroft's America, it is perfectly permissible to eavesdrop on private conversations between suspected terrorists and their lawyers, because the potential threat to public safety is so great. And under the circumstances, a lot of Americans would agree with that assessment. However, in Ashcroft's America, it is not permissible to study federal records of gun purchases to ascertain if any of the 1,200 people detained in connection with possible terrorism had purchased weapons recently. That, the attorney general says, would violate the suspected terrorist's right to privacy. In practice, Ashcroft's decision means that if one of the people detained in connection with Sept. 11 has been accumulating an arsenal of several dozen semiautomatic assault weapons, information about that arsenal may now be sitting unaccessed in a government computer. According to Ashcroft, the law creating the instant background check forbids him from using the information for any purpose other than to check the accuracy of the system. But if you look at the law, the legal point is at best debatable. For example, among those forbidden by law to purchase guns are illegal immigrants or legal immigrants admitted on a non-immigrant visa, such as a student or visitor visa. Many of the 1,200 detainees fall into one of those categories; surely it would be possible to access the data to see if they violated that provision.

1777.text: NA-45911 PRIMARY Get tough on illegal gun purchases A Senate panel has OK'd gun legislation in response to December's school shooting in Newtown, Conn. Measures include cracking down on "straw purchases," improving school safety and banning assault weapons. GUN TRAFFICKING Simply put: If someone purchases a gun for someone illegally, they both should be punished. If it is used in a crime, they both should be punished the same. Travis Roundtree A proposed bill "explicitly makes straw purchases a federal crime and provides penalties of up to 25 years in prison." Twenty-five years in prison for buying a gun for a friend? No. That's the problem: crazy sentences that are totally out of line with the actual crime. You can get less time for rape, robbery or murder. Mathew Andresen Is 25 years a "crazy" penalty for buying a gun for a neighbor who turns around and kills 10 people? I don't think so. Sorry. Michael Anthony Shea We place far too many people in prisons for far too long. Not only does this waste vast amounts of money, but it compromises the public safety when the wrong people need to be released. James Oerichbauer ASSAULT WEAPONS BAN I think the Democrats recognized gun control as a political loser, and they were content to leave it alone. However, the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting might have taken the loser out of gun control.

1778.text: GUN-5375 PRIMARY Verdict Against Gun Makers Is Likely to Prompt More Suits A landmark verdict in Federal District Court in Brooklyn Thursday holding gun makers liable for shootings because of negligent marketing practices is likely to encourage many more lawsuits using the same legal approach. While industry executives expressed confidence that the Brooklyn verdict would be overturned, the plaintiffs' success, in the first gun trial using the novel claim of negligent marketing, poses a serious threat to the gun makers as more suits are filed. The success against gun makers came much more swiftly than in suits against the tobacco industry that pioneered the negligent marketing approach. And the threat to the gun industry is especially serious because gun makers do not have the enormous resources of the tobacco companies, with total sales in 1997 of only $1.4 billion compared with $48 billion in sales for the cigarette makers. As an indication of the seriousness of the stakes, the National Rifle Association has used its influence to rush through a law in Georgia barring cities in that state from suing the gun industry. Similar bills are pending in Louisiana, Alabama and Wyoming. While the Brooklyn suit was brought by victims of gunfire or their survivors, there are several suits pending in other states in which cities have sued the gun manufacturers. The jury's verdict, holding 15 of the 25 firearms makers named in the suit negligent, caught people on both sides by surprise, given the gun industry's undefeated record until now and the long history of litigation against the tobacco companies.

1781.text: NA-43081 PRIMARY After Attack Near Campus, California Weighs Gun Bill LOS ANGELES -- Just days after a 22-year-old killed six college students and himself near the campus of the University of California, Santa Barbara, state lawmakers are championing legislation that would permit law enforcement officials and private individuals to seek a restraining order from a judge that would keep people with a potential propensity for violence from buying or owning a gun. The process would be similar to the one currently used for restraining orders in cases of domestic violence. The legislation is being introduced this week in response to the attack on Friday by Elliot O. Rodger, who was able to buy three guns and go on a rampage despite warnings from his family and mental health professionals that he was unstable and possibly dangerous. It is unclear, however, if the measures contained in the bills could have prevented his actions if they had been law. Although mass shootings have not translated into stricter gun control laws nationally, they have prompted changes on the state level -- largely limiting access to guns, but in some cases loosening existing laws, which gun advocates say give people more leeway to arm themselves against criminals. After a mass shooting at a school in Newtown, Conn., Connecticut and New York passed bans on assault rifles and created stricter background checks. But California, which already has some of the strictest gun control laws in the country, could go even further. The legislation, known as a gun violence restraining order, would allow people to notify courts or law enforcement officials if they are concerned that a family member or friend is at risk of committing violence. Gun control advocates have recently started pushing for such restraining orders in statehouses across the nation, expanding on similar laws that have passed in Connecticut, Indiana and Texas.

1783.text: GUN-11196 PRIMARY ARTHENIA JOYNER SAYS THERE WERE WARNINGS ON 'STAND YOUR GROUND' LAW "When we passed (the stand your ground law), we said it portends horrific events when people's lives were put into these situations." Arthenia Joyner on Wednesday, March 21st, 2012 in an interview * * * THE RULING: TRUE The possibility that Florida's "stand your ground" law could protect a neighborhood watchman who shot and killed an unarmed teen last month has lawmakers who opposed the measure saying told you so. "When we passed the law, we said it portends horrific events when people's lives were put into these situations, and my worst fears came to fruition," Sen. Arthenia Joyner, D-Tampa, said March 20, 2012. "A young life was snuffed out." Joyner was in the state House when "stand your ground" passed in 2005 and fears the law - which allows people to use deadly force in cases of self-defense when they believe their life is at risk - could save 28-year-old shooter George Zimmerman from prosecution. Zimmerman killed 17-year-old Trayvon Martin in the Orlando suburb of Sanford on Feb. 26, police say. As the investigation unfolds, it's worthwhile to look back to the origins of the "stand your ground" law and the concerns legislators like Joyner had at the time. The measure passed the Senate 39-0 and the House 94-20. It was signed into law by Gov. Jeb Bush. Joyner and 19 other House Democrats voted against the bill.

1785.text: NA-40448 PRIMARY YOUR VIEWS - LETTERS FROM OUR READERS OPINION Acceptance of the gun culture can be changed As a lifelong liberal Democrat and charter member of the Slackers Guild International, I usually agree with pretty much everything Bill McClellan writes. Have to disagree, however, with his column titled "Time for honesty in gun debate" (Jan. 7). It should have been called "Time for pathetic, weak-kneed surrender in gun control debate." I'm sick to death of fellow liberals wringing their hands and whimpering about gun violence in one breath then conceding that nothing can be done in the next. We heard these same tired arguments against reforms involving wearing seat belts, smoking in public, school yard bullying, date rape and a host of other destructive behaviors that had been accepted for decades in our culture. Those arguments include: It's a right that has to be respected, it's too widespread to be stopped, the forces against reform are too strong or too well-funded. Those patterns of abuse were not addressed by laws alone, but a law is a strong statement that society will no longer tolerate certain behaviors. When was the last time we heard a public official make a racist remark without paying a heavy price? Societal attitudes and norms can be changed, and acceptance of the gun culture is no exception. The lives of our children and of all our citizens who are killed and wounded needlessly by gun violence and accidents every year are far more precious than any perceived absolute right to own whatever type of firearm someone may desire.

1780.text: GUN-5085 PRIMARY Letters; IRS should settle for tighing The Editors: Many Americans are preparing to celebrate Easter, and all American taxpayers face the looming IRS filing deadline. If the tithe (10 percent of income) was good enough for Jesus Christ, 10 percent should be good enough for the federal government. BOB ANDREWS, Smyrna Guns too easy The Editors: The shootings in Jonesboro, Ark., are a tragedy for which all in that community should bear responsibility. It seems the whole community takes pride in hunting. If guns were inaccessible to those involved in the shooting, the most they could have done was throw stones. SAMPATH PARTHASARATHY, Dunwoody Guaranteed right The Editors: In "Guns are for killing: That stops me dead" (Living, March 31), columnist Michael Skube quoted the spokesman for the Arkansas state police, who correctly described hunting as a "privilege." Skube then correlated that if hunting is a privilege and not a right, then so the private ownership of firearms must also be a privilege and not a right. One need not be a constitutional scholar to understand the Second Amendment. That many people use firearms while hunting has no bearing on the right to own firearms. Nothing in the U.S. Constitution remotely addresses hunting. Until this privilege infringes on my individual rights, I'll live in happy co-existence with those who choose to hunt. I will not debate hunting. I will, however, stand up for the rights guaranteed by our Constitution. The Second Amendment represents the teeth of the Constitution. Without it in full force, the other rights are in jeopardy. M. CRAIG COLE, Norcross Owners in control The Editors: Michael Skube drew on the tragedy in Jonesboro, Ark., to portray American gun owners in general as enthralled, in an "alien and creepy" way, with the lethal beauty of their possessions. No one with half a heart could fail to be sickened by the news of children lying dead, allegedly at the hands of their classmates. But law-abiding citizens are no more deserving of vilification for owning firearms than they are for owning cars when others choose to drive drunk. The vast majority of American gun owners are sensible, disciplined people. If the millions of legally owned guns in this country had the hypnotic sway over their bearers that Skube suggested, bodies would be piled so high we couldn't bury them fast enough. MICHAEL S. KEILEY, Atlanta Societal message The Editors: The question shouldn't be, "What is wrong with our kids," but, "What is wrong with parents, guardians and society's leaders?" Kids will model what they learn at home. Other pressures in society will add to that. If kids are raised in a nonviolent home where they're loved and taught respect and love, that is what they'll reflect. However, if they are raised in a home where violence is their main dish every day, then that is how the child will turn out. It's that simple. MAGGIE MONTANA, Acworth Still sinking The Editors: I am appalled that House Majority Leader Dick Armey called President Clinton "shameless" (Page One, Tuesday). Armey continues to sink the Republican Party to new lows and reinforce my theory of a GOP-involved conspiracy to oust the president. STEVE GLODECK, Norcross King of fools The Editors: I was struck by the report that President Clinton was so stunned by word of the Paula Jones' ruling that he asked whether it was an April Fools' joke. The narcissistic Clinton continues to amaze even himself with what he is able get away with. ROBERT G. KNIGHT, Rome Danger to others The Editors: The article "High-quality day care not harmful, study finds" (Living, April 3) stated that child care is not a danger to children's social development, it does not make them more aggressive, and the children are not more unruly than those of stay-at-home mothers. I've been doing unofficial research for quite a few years. My studies show that if you see a day care van at a playground, restaurant or any child-directed entertainment, find somewhere else to go. I can't count the times my children have had to sit out or leave something fun they had been looking forward to because of unruly, unmonitored behavior of children with a day care group.

1782.text: GUN-5788 PRIMARY TEN COMMANDMENTS GET HOUSE BACKING; POSTING GUIDELINES IN SCHOOLS BECOMES ISSUE IN GUN CONTROL; CRITICS OF VOTE CITE CONSTITUTION After an impassioned debate about God, guns, and schoolhouse murderers, the House endorsed on Thursday public display of the Ten Commandments in schools and other state-owned facilities. The vote marked the beginning of a showdown over gun control. The Ten Commandments vote was a victory for conservatives, who have rejected enactment of new gun laws in the wake of the spate of school shootings. Instead the conservatives blame youth violence on the collapse of traditional values and discipline. The long-awaited debate on new gun measures was to start Thursday night. Republican leader Dick Armey said the House might vote late Thursday night on a gun-show proposal by Michigan Democrat John Dingell. Dingell is a longtime ally and former board member of the National Rifle Association. His NRA-backed plan requires background checks at gun shows but allows 24 hours to run them, not the three business days in a bill the Senate passed last month. The Senate's bill also had a broader definition of a gun show. The House will also consider other gun-control measures, such as banning imports of high capacity ammunition clips. The measure to display the Ten Commandments passed by a 248-180 vote and was sponsored by Rep. Robert Aderholt, R-Ala. Critics charged that the provision was unconstitutional, but Aderholt argued that his measure would "promote morality and work toward an end of children killing children."

1787.text: GUN-3599 PRIMARY Marietta man challenges machine-gun ban today; Issue likely headed to high court A Marietta man's challenge to a federal ban on machine guns is ideally suited to let the Supreme Court hear its first serious case on the right to bear arms in more than a half-century, legal experts say. Donald W. Wright's case will be considered today by a three-judge panel of the 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals in Atlanta, a federal court one rung below the Supreme Court. As a member of a North Georgia militia group, Wright asserts he had a right under the Second Amendment to possess the two machine guns and three pipe bombs that federal agents seized from him in August 1994. Wright also says Congress did not have authority to ban machine guns under the commerce clause because it had nothing to do with interstate commerce. Wright's commerce clause attack is bolstered by a landmark Supreme Court decision issued this year. In this case, U.S. vs. Lopez, the court held in a 5-4 vote that Congress had no power to punish someone possessing a firearm within 1,000 feet of a school. The high court said this criminal issue falls under state police powers. Legal top gun to argue case "This is the single best Second Amendment and commerce clause challenge to a federal statute that I've seen in a long, long time," said William Van Alstyne, a Second Amendment scholar and professor at the Duke University School of Law. "It's virtually ideal in that regard."

1788.text: NA-41708 PRIMARY After shootings, new gun-law action unlikely in Congress WASHINGTON - The shootings at the Navy Yard here brought renewed calls on Capitol Hill for tougher gun laws, including the background-check plan cosponsored by Sen. Pat Toomey (R., Pa.). But with the pleas came acknowledgments that the measures still don't have enough votes. With congressional politics against them, neither Toomey nor his cosponsor, Sen. Joe Manchin (D., W.Va.), was eager to bring the bill up, even as other lawmakers urged its passage. "The Senate spoke on this issue and we came up five votes short," Toomey said Tuesday in a statement. "It is unclear if yesterday's tragedy changes the atmosphere sufficiently to yield a different outcome." Manchin also indicated that a renewed push was unlikely. Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid (D., Nev.) said bluntly, "We don't have the votes." Reid doesn't plan on bringing the bills back up without sufficient support.
