Exactly five months earlier, on April 17, the Senate blocked several gun bills, including the Toomey-Manchin plan to expand background checks to cover all commercial gun purchases. (Officially, it fell six votes short of defeating a filibuster threat, though Reid's "no" was for procedural reasons only.) In light of that defeat, coming just months after 26 people including 20 children were massacred at a school in Newtown, Conn., gun-control advocates doubted that the killings at the Navy Yard would change the equation in Congress.

1786.text: GUN-2283 PRIMARY TOURISM OFFICIALS BACK BILL Tourism officials joined police leaders Monday in a call for state gun control, but they failed to stop a proposal to ship 4,000 confiscated guns to Tallahassee next year if anti-gun legislation fails next month. The county's Criminal Justice Commission wants guns confiscated by police in Palm Beach County this year to be dumped on the capitol lawn a year from now. But tourism officials oppose the gimmick, saying the media will scare away tourists with photographs of Palm Beach County's huge pile of guns. The two sides met Monday and agreed to join forces in support of a proposed law by Rep. Ron Klein, D-Boca Raton, to ban 37 types of assault weapons. If this happens, there won't be the need for such a publicity stunt,'' County Commission Chairman Mary McCarty said of the unified political lobbying before the Legislature convenes this February. Former justice commission chairman Monroe Al'' Coogler, who proposed the Guns-to-Tallahassee stunt, encouraged members of the county's Tourist Development Council to tell lawmakers that if they don't pass Klein's bill this year, they are risking embarrassment because of the gun pile planned for 1995. Tell them, If this doesn't pass, we're going to have every restaurant owner in Florida in that parade when it approaches Tallahassee next year,' '' Coogler said at Monday's meeting.   Tell them, If somebody shoots one more tourist, we can stop the flights in.' ''

1784.text: GUN-1569 PRIMARY FATHER OF SLAIN BOY PROTESTS CONCEALED-GUN BILL ... POLICE JOIN CONCERNED; PARENTS IN LOBBYING EFFORT AT CAPITOL On the playground where his son was shot, Michael Neal said in nearly a whisper that a bloodbath would come if Missouri lawmakers let people carry concealed weapons.   ''I'm really hurt and devastated by the loss of my son,'' Neal, a Wellston police sergeant, said Wednesday. His 11-year-old son, Maurice, was fatally shot March 25. '' If this bill passes, there will be a lot more hurt people.'' Neal was among dozens of people who spoke out Wednesday here and in Jefferson City against a proposal that would allow people 21 and older to carry concealed weapons if they have permits from their local sheriff. The measure, an amendment to an anti-crime bill, was overwhelmingly approved by the House last month. It is pending; no vote was taken Wednesday.   Although the bill is aimed at those over 21, opponents argue that the bill would put more guns on the street and that guns eventually would find their way into the hands of children. '' When you break into my home and steal my gun, who gets the gun - the children,'' said C.V. Smith, pastor at Original Friendship Baptist Church. Smith and other community leaders spoke at a press conference at the Salvation Army playground where M aurice Neal was shot by a juvenile.   Melvin Watson, a radio-station employee, said his niece was killed in September 1989. The girl, Natasha Monique Watson, was 4 when she was struck by a bullet intended for a man. '' I have a son that's 20 now, and I don't want him carrying a concealed weapon next year,'' Watson said.   Watson is helping organize a bus trip by Wellston residents to Jefferson City on April 22 to lobby against the concealed-weapons bill.   Neal, who said he has never gone to the state Capitol to talk with legislators about anything, plans to go. The measure, he said, is a ''slap in the face'' of his son and his fellow officers.   Under the bill, a sheriff could issue a permit if the applicant met criteria including taking a safety course offered by the National Rifle Association or by another firearms training school. Neal said that is not enough.   ''I had to have 640 hours of training to carry a gun,'' he said. '' And this bill is telling me those 640 hours don't mean diddly.'' Meanwhile, in Jefferson City, police chiefs and gun-control advocates walked the Capitol's hallways and buttonholed legislators Wednesday in an attempt to derail the bill.   ''It's a crazy idea,'' said Frank Barrow, a detective with the St. Ann Police Department. He said those who take the law into their own hands could get hurt when someone grabs their weapon and uses it against them.   Barrow was among about 40 officers, mainly from the St. Louis area, who spent the morning lobbying against the measure. The police officers were joined in the hallways by about 20 people from the Missouri Council to Control Handguns.   ''No Dodge City,'' read a brightly colored placard held by Jeanette Culpepper of St. Louis. She said her son, Curtis Johnson Jr., 22, was shot to death last May.   Besides lobbying against the concealed-weapon proposal, the handgun-control group was working on behalf of a measure that would require safe storage of guns in homes where children are present. Legislators pushing the concealed-gun proposal predicted the lobbying effort would have little impact. Rep. Joe Driskill, D-Doniphan, who sponsored the amendment, said ''constituents and average citizens'' support the idea.   A similar reaction came from Rep. Chet Boeke, R-Overland. '' My constituents have been 99 percent in favor of this by phone calls and letters,'' Boeke said. He estimated he had heard from at least 50 people.   The amendment was attached to an anti-crime bill sponsored by Rep. Doug Harpool, D-Springfield. Harpool said he would strip the amendment if it damaged his bill's chances. '' But at this point, it's garnering more support than opposition,'' he said.   Under the amendment, the Missouri Highway Patrol would issue regulations on who could get concealed-gun permits. Applicants would have to complete firearms training and prove they were in ''actual physical danger'' or needed a gun to protect their property. Some of those with criminal records, mental problems and drinking problems would be ineligible.

1789.text: GUN-20071 PRIMARY Money is ammo in gun fight WASHINGTON -- For years, Rep. Bart Stupak, D-Mich., was a staunch gun-rights supporter who enjoyed generous quarterly contributions from the National Rifle Association. That was before Littleton. After the school shootings April 20 in Colorado, Stupak joined with most Democrats in opposing an NRA-sponsored amendment. And the money stopped flowing. "I don't expect to receive any contributions from them for a while," says Stupak, who has been heckled repeatedly by gun-rights supporters since his vote. His is a classic Washington tale of how special-interest groups reward friends and punish foes in their efforts to influence policy. With gun-control issues pending before a committee of House and Senate negotiators, groups on both sides are using campaign contributions to improve their leverage. The NRA has traditionally been the heavyweight in political donations. In the first six months of this year, its political action committee contributed $ 251,750 to candidates for federal office. The PAC set up by Handgun Control Inc., a leading gun-control group, gave $ 5,500 during the same period, but Handgun Control is trying to catch up. It plans a $ 2 million fund-raising drive for next year's elections, although it has raised only about $ 200,000 so far. "We love our friends and pick our enemies, like every else," Handgun Control spokeswoman Naomi Paiss says. The NRA's chief lobbyist, James Jay Baker, says his group follows a similar approach. "Stupak's an obvious (target)," he says. "It would be highly unlikely we'd give him a PAC donation, or that he'd accept it."

1791.text: GUN-2814 PRIMARY RAPID-FIRE GUN STORY MISFIRED ON FACTS About the recent article Victims disagree over rapid-fire gun ban,'' let's get the facts straight: Assault weapons are fully automatic weapons, which require one pull of the trigger to release all the ammunition in a clip. (The military and police own them.) The AR-15 is not designed for the battlefield and is not an automatic. A five-round limit on ammunition clips added to the crime bill sponsored by Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., will affect only the honest citizen, not the criminal. If Texas had not had a ridiculous concealed-weapon law, the killer in Killeen would have been dead before his first magazine change. Anti-gun nuts want free Americans subservient, and the criminal is still laughing. K. Bond Pace Stuart

1792.text: GUN-30089 PRIMARY U.S. GUNS FUELING DRUG WARS America's lax gun laws are killing Mexico. Drug cartels are using the easy access to guns on this side of the border to ramp up their war against the Mexican government. The United States cannot allow its neighbor and ally to lose more ground to the narcoterrorists. It needs to crack down on dealers in the States and help stanch the flow of weapons across the border. Guns bought in the United States and carried on foot or by car across the border were involved in many of the more than 6,000 murders in Mexico's drug warfare last year. Authorities in Mexico and the United States say 90 to 95 percent of the thousands of weapons seized before or after attacks in Mexico have been traced to dealers in the United States. U.S. gun laws allow for the sale of automatic and high-powered weapons. Mexico limits most high-caliber weapons to military and police forces. Mexico also inspects few vehicles or people crossing the border. Authorities say they have no means to determine how many guns have been smuggled into Mexico, and they note that nearly 7,000 licensed gun dealers operate in the four U.S. border states. Both nations are threatened by a pipeline that has drugs flowing north and cash and guns flowing south. Straw buyers buy guns on the U.S. side, at both shops and gun shows, and then pass those weapons off to smugglers who carry them to the drug cartels. The traffickers' firepower has become a serious challenge to Mexico's security services, which do not have the manpower or money to stop the smuggling.

1793.text: NA-42719 PRIMARY Threat to Block Debate on Guns Appears to Fade WASHINGTON -- Several Senate Republicans said Tuesday that they would not participate in a filibuster of the first major gun control bill since 1993, as Democrats appeared on the verge of overcoming a blockade threatened by a group of conservatives before a word of debate on the measure was uttered. Senator Harry Reid of Nevada, the majority leader, said he would schedule an initial showdown vote for Thursday. If backers of the measure can corral at least 60 votes, the Senate will begin consideration of a series of gun safety proposals -- strongly supported by President Obama -- that would still face a long and difficult journey across the Senate floor. The bill, which would increase penalties for illegal gun purchase and greatly expand background checks on gun buyers, would again need 60 votes to end the ensuing debate after consideration of contentious amendments, including a renewal of the assault weapons ban. Should it cross that very high hurdle, 51 votes would be needed to get to final passage. Even with Democrats controlling 55 seats, no majority was assured given the resistance of some Democrats from more conservative states who face re-election campaigns next year. Even as Mr. Reid scheduled a vote, Senators Joe Manchin III, Democrat of West Virginia, and Patrick J. Toomey, Republican of Pennsylvania were very near a deal that would most likely serve as an amendment replacing the background check piece of the measure that Mr. Reid is seeking a vote on. Their measure, which would almost certainly appeal to a broader base of members than the one now at the heart of the debate, would include fewer gun buyers in newly expanded checks, but allow for the record keeping that many Republicans have opposed. The two were expected to announce a deal Wednesday. Mr. Manchin briefed Mr. Reid late Tuesday.

1790.text: NA-45868 PRIMARY Obama, police chiefs discuss ways to halt mass shootings Lobbying Congress to pass meaningful gun control, President Obama sought Monday to build public support through local law enforcement officials, including police chiefs from cities that saw mass killings in 2012. "If law enforcement officials who are dealing with this stuff every single day can come to some basic consensus in terms of steps that we need to take, Congress is going to be paying attention to them," Obama said. The president spoke as he and Vice President Biden met at the White House with the police chiefs of Newtown, Conn.; Aurora, Colo.; and Oak Creek, Wis. The Dec. 14 shooting in Newtown killed 20 children and six adults at an elementary school and prompted the new gun push by Obama and Biden. The president's proposal calls for a renewed assault weapons ban, universal background checks and restrictions of the sizes of ammunition magazines, as well as new school safety and mental health programs. Aurora, Colo., is where 12 people died in a mass shooting at a movie theater in July; Oak Creek, Wis., is the site of the Sikh temple where six people died in an August  shooting. Obama's plan  received an endorsement Monday from another group of local officials: the U.S. Conference of Mayors. Philadelphia Mayor Michael Nutter, the conference president, called for "a national commitment to reduce the culture of violence, stop the easy access to guns by those who should not legally possess them and increase access to badly needed mental health services."

1795.text: GUN-5966 PRIMARY Dealers: Talk of gun control boosts sales For gun-show organizer Ron Martin, the congressional push to tighten firearms sale restrictions has been great for business. "Every time they (politicians) talk about new laws to limit the Second Amendment, it actually increases business," said Martin. "The last couple of shows --- Kennesaw and the (Atlanta) Farmers Market --- have been great. People worry that this may be their last chance to buy guns." Gun dealers at Sunday's show in a Gwinnett County Fairgrounds exhibition building agreed sales were steady, as a stream of people moved up and down the aisles, examining handguns and rifles. Several hundred people attended the show, one of several held monthly in the Atlanta area. Although the proposed federal laws may boost sales, the dealers could not contain their contempt for the restrictions. The Clinton administration and the Senate want gun-show purchases to be as tightly regulated as gun-store sales. That already is the rule in Georgia, the dealers argued. "The same rules apply here that apply in the shop," said Jimmy Gill of Woodbury. He accused Washington of attempting to make gun-show dealers a scapegoat in the intensifying debate over gun violence. "We already have to fill out the same federal forms and call the GBI and do instant background checks on the purchasers," said Kenny Powell of Lindale, pointing to the phone in his booth at the show. "It isn't going to change anything we do."

1794.text: NA-40887 PRIMARY Fla. Senate panel agrees to modest changes in 'stand your ground' law; Even law's foes laud tweaks, but bill faces long odds in the House. In a hearing marking the first time the Republican-ruled Florida Legislature has revisited the state's controversial "stand your ground law," a Senate committee agreed Tuesday to make modest changes to the 2005 law, which has been under heightened national scrutiny since George Zimmerman fatally shot teenager Trayvon Martin in 2012. The Judiciary Committee voted 7-2 to endorse a bill (CS/SB 130) that would require law enforcement agencies to fully investigate shootings involving self-defense claims based on the law and to develop guidelines for neighborhood watch groups. Senators hailed the bipartisan work that yielded Tuesday's legislation, even though any changes still face long odds in the state House. "This has truly been an effort where we have taken this issue ... and reached a consensus so far," said Sen. David Simmons, R-Altamonte Springs, a drafter of the original law who is now sponsoring the fix. "If there's more we can do later, we will do it." Similar proposals to revisit stand your ground went nowhere this year in the Legislature. But Zimmerman's acquittal in July led to nationwide protests and a 31-day sit-in at the Florida Capitol by civil rights activists calling for the law's repeal. Opponents of the NRA-backed law say it puts minorities more at risk of being shot or stabbed when encountering someone who might feel threatened. Zimmerman was acquitted of second-degree murder and manslaughter charges in the 2012 shooting death of the unarmed Martin in the Central Florida city of Sanford.

1796.text: NA-43730 PRIMARY Some Md. lawmakers want O'Malley to go further on gun control If Maryland Gov. Martin O'Malley is willing to put his political clout behind instituting a new licensing requirement for gun owners, might he also support a 50 percent tax on bullets? How about mandating that gun owners purchase insurance on their weapons or giving the state police powers like those of federal agents to raid Maryland gun shops? O'Malley's announcement  Monday that he would seek a sweeping package of gun-control legislation - almost a week before the bills are actually due to go before the legislature - has many liberal Maryland lawmakers hoping to include even more restrictive gun-control measures. And if they can't sway O'Malley (D), a handful of senior Democrats say they will introduce measures of their own and seek to amend the governor's gun-control package as it moves through the Democratic-controlled General Assembly. "The governor's package will be the heart of the action, and my focus will be on passing the governor's plan," Sen. Jamie B. Raskin (D-Montgomery) said. "But there will be a number of other bills out there." In one of the most expansive legislative responses proposed to last month's school shooting in Newtown, Conn., O'Malley said Monday that he will seek to institute some of the nation's strictest gun-licensing requirements, ban assault weapons and restrict visitor access to schools. Perhaps most controversially, O'Malley wants a new gun-licensing requirement that would force prospective gun owners to provide fingerprints to state police, complete a hands-on weapon-familiarization and gun-safety course, and undergo a background check before beginning the existing process of seeking a permit to purchase a weapon.

1797.text: GUN-20240 PRIMARY Gore urges Bush to help push gun-control measures WASHINGTON -- Vice President Gore called upon Texas Gov. George W. Bush Thursday to put aside their political differences and join him in backing three gun-control proposals now before Congress. Gore, in an interview with USA TODAY, asked his Republican rival for the White House to join him in seeking enactment of a law requiring mandatory child-safety trigger locks for new firearms, ending a loophole that exempts purchasers of guns at gun shows from background checks, and banning cheap handguns known as Saturday Night Specials. "I would like to reach out to Gov. Bush, not in a partisan or combative way at all but in a genuine offer to join together in urging the Congress to pass common-sense safety measures related to guns," Gore said. The Democratic presidential candidate said members of the House and Senate should pass the proposals before next month's first anniversary of the shooting attack at Columbine High School near Denver. Gore's challenge was an attempt to put Bush on the spot on an issue the vice president is making a central part of his campaign. Bush's response was on the whole positive, but he did not embrace any of Gore's proposals. Gore said he hoped to avoid partisan politics on gun control, an issue that has sparked angry exchanges this week between the White House and the National Rifle Association.

1799.text: NA-44328 PRIMARY Gun control, RIP Gun control, RIP. Response to Navy Yard shooting is nail in the coffin for firearms reform. Why can't conservatives just take the win on gun rights? On Monday morning, President Obama didn't even try to use the massacre at the Washington Navy Yard to revive the gun- control debate. He praised the "patriots" who were targeted by the gunman, offered the requisite thoughts and prayers, and, without any overt call for gun restrictions, moved on to Syria, the economic recovery and his budget fight with Republicans. Rather than accept this surrender on gun control, Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus accused Obama of a "bizarre response" to the shootings, and House Speaker John Boehner complained the president didn't "rise above partisanship." "President Obama delivered only brief condolences for the victims of the shooting at the Navy Yard in Washington, D.C., Monday morning, before quickly pivoting to a scheduled attack on Republicans," protested the conservative Daily Caller. Of course, conservatives would have been even more indignant had Obama used the occasion to talk about gun control, as he did after the Newtown, Conn., massacre. His response was really a tacit acknowledgment that there is no hope of reviving even the modest gun measure that failed in the Senate in April. If 20 slain first-graders didn't move Congress, the killing of a dozen adults - a depressingly ordinary event in this violence-numb nation - wasn't about to change the equation.

1800.text: GUN-8957 PRIMARY New Wal-Mart Policy Stiffens Requirements for Gun Sales Wal-Mart Stores Inc., the world's largest retailer, has toughened its gun-sales policy and taken what it calls an "extra step" beyond what the federal government requires for background checks. In a memo to store managers, Wal-Mart banned employees from selling a firearm until the potential buyer is approved by the appropriate federal or state agency. Current law allows a gun sale to proceed if the government fails to complete a background check on the buyer within three business days. The new policy took effect on May 31 but was never announced by Wal-Mart, which sells firearms in most of its 2,700 stores. Wal-Mart does not break out sales numbers on its guns. "We will not proceed with any gun sale if we do not have an approved background check," Jessica Moser Eldred, a Wal-Mart spokeswoman, said yesterday. The move was immediately criticized by the National Rifle Association as infringing on the rights of law-abiding citizens who want to buy their guns in a timely manner. But one retail group defended Wal-Mart's tougher stance as a common-sense approach. Retailers often get enmeshed in litigation tied to guns they sell that are then used in crimes. "A retailer can refuse or delay a sale based on the need for information, as they do in the case of minors," said Edie Clark, a spokeswoman for the International Mass Retail Association.

1798.text: GUN-9259 PRIMARY QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS ON NEW GUN LAW Missourians will be eligible to carry concealed weapons under the bill enacted by the Legislature on Thursday. Some of the basics of the new law: Q: Where can I apply for a permit to carry a concealed weapon? A. In most counties and in St. Louis, you should apply at the sheriff's office. In St. Louis County, apply at the St. Louis County Police Department. Q. When can I apply? A. The law takes effect Oct. 11, a Saturday, so the earliest date to apply would apparently be Monday, Oct. 13. Q. What are the qualifications to get a permit? A. You must be at least 23 years old and have lived in the state at least six months or be a member of the armed forces or a soldier's spouse. You must pass a background check and a gun safety course. Q. How much does a permit cost? A. $100. After three years, there can be a $50 renewal fee. Q. How is the background check conducted? A. You will be fingerprinted, and the prints will be sent to the Missouri Highway Patrol. The patrol will run the prints through its criminal records database and forward them electronically to the FBI. Q. What type of criminal record will disqualify me? A. You cannot have been convicted of or pleaded guilty of a felony. Within the last five years, you also cannot have had two or more DWI or drug possession convictions or a record of misdemeanors that includes unspecified "crimes of violence."

1802.text: GUN-11304 PRIMARY Gun violence unlikely to prompt any changes in federal law WASHINGTON -- At an early learning center in Eatonville, Wash., on Sunday, mourners will light candles to honor Margaret Anderson, the 34-year-old ranger and mother of two toddlers who was shot and killed on New Year's Day while she tried to set up a roadblock in Mount Rainier National Park. That same evening, at the mall of the University of Arizona in her hometown of Tucson, Democratic Rep. Gabrielle Giffords will hold a glow stick and listen to a symphony orchestra at a vigil to recognize the anniversary of the shooting that critically wounded her and killed six others. In ceremonies from New York to Seattle, candlelight vigils are planned in more than 30 cities to remember the thousands of Americans who are killed in the United States each year, most of them with guns. For gun-control advocates, it will be a day to "light a candle against the darkness of gun violence" and to demand that Congress tighten the nation's gun laws. After attack on Giffords Congress did nothing of the sort after the Giffords shooting last year, and the odds are good that nothing will happen this year. U.S. Rep. Norm Dicks, D-Wash., a gun-control proponent who gets failing grades from the National Rifle Association, said it's just a matter of political reality on Capitol Hill. He wants Congress to overturn a law that took effect in 2010 that allows loaded guns in national parks, but he's not optimistic.

1801.text: GUN-30711 PRIMARY Court Decides Strict Gun Law Violates Rights Interpreting the Second Amendment broadly, a federal appeals court in Washington yesterday struck down a gun control law in the District of Columbia that bars residents from keeping handguns in their homes. The decision was the first from a federal appeals court to hold a gun control law unconstitutional on the ground that the Second Amendment protects the rights of individuals, as opposed to the collective rights of state militias. Nine other federal appeals courts around the nation have rejected that interpretation. Linda Singer, the District's acting attorney general, said the decision was ''a huge setback.'' ''We've been making progress on bringing down crime and gun violence,'' Ms. Singer said, ''and this sends us in a different direction.'' By contrast, advocates of gun rights praised the decision, by the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit, saying it raised the prospect of a national re-evaluation of the meaning of the Second Amendment and the rights of gun owners. They said the District of Columbia would have to begin procedures to allow handgun possession in private homes unless yesterday's decision was stayed. Lawyers on both sides of the case said it had created a conflict among the federal courts of appeal on a significant constitutional issue, making review by the Supreme Court likely. The Supreme Court last considered the issue in 1939, and there are only scattered hints about how the current justices might rule.

1804.text: GUN-4364 PRIMARY Hopes for Gun Sanity in Annapolis THERE MAY be new, important help in Annapolis this year for the growing number of Marylanders seeking more protection against the all-too-free flow of handguns to the wrong hands. Sen. Walter Baker, chairman of the state Senate committee where countless handgun proposals have gone to their death with his considerable help over the years, has concluded that certain measures should be taken against handgun trafficking that supplies criminals. Though Sen. Baker's latest statements shocked and disappointed the fringe groups that fight any and all attempts to bring some sanity to the firearms market, the senator's position in no way jeopardizes gun ownership for all the recognized uses of firearms that Marylanders have long enjoyed. What Sen. Baker says is that he is ready to support a limit on handgun sales of one or two a month per person and provisions to help make sure that private gun owners do not sell handguns to criminals. These are not only sensible proposals but also key provisions of a gun safety package proposed by Gov. Glendening. The governor is calling for a one-a-month handgun purchase limit, and for the inclusion of private sales of handguns under the law requiring a criminal background check of buyers before a gun can be sold. The governor also seeks a requirement that handgun owners be licensed, which Sen. Baker strongly opposes. There's room here for a significant breakthrough, for legislation that could go a long way in protecting citizens from the all-too-open marketing of concealable weapons to criminals. That's the point here -- not the disarming of law-abiding citizens, but more protection for them and for highly endangered police who have been pleading for this help.

1803.text: GUN-1056 PRIMARY Lawmaker Is Target on Gun Issue Wallace Coon came to Representative Les AuCoin's town hall meeting carrying copies of National Rifle Association magazines in which he had highlighted articles about gun control legislation with a neon orange marker. "Incarcerate the criminals; inconvenience those guys," Mr. Coon, a 32-year-old systems analyst programmer, told the Democratic Congressman. "Don't impose legislation on us law-abiding citizens who are not committing any crimes." Mr. Coon was more polite than most. At the first meeting Mr. AuCoin has held in his home district since he decided to vote for a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases, the mood in the Beaverton church hall ranged from tense to fractious. On a sunny Saturday afternoon, more than 150 residents chose to come tell Mr. AuCoin just how angry they were. In March, citing a rise in handgun-related violence, the eight-term Congressman announced that he was reversing his longstanding opposition to gun control. The measure he will support, called the Brady bill after James S. Brady, the Presidential aide who was injured in the 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan, is scheduled to go to the House floor for a vote Wednesday. Gun-control advocates have expressed confidence that they have changed enough minds to win approval. But a number of last-minute maneuvers have endangered the bill's chances in the face of a competing measure supported by the National Rifle Association. That amendment, sponsored by Representative Harley O. Staggers Jr., Democrat of West Virginia, would require states to set up point-of-sale criminal background checks for handgun purchases within six months.

1806.text: GUN-30923 PRIMARY When Fear And Fury Drive Gun Sales At the Bullet Hole, an  Essex County gun store, and at Gun for Hire,across the street, where Anthony Colandro offers courses in firearm use, martial arts and personal safety, it's the best of times and the worst of times. Worst of times? Just ask Manny Cerca, who for the past 27 years has run the Bullet Hole, a family-owned shop and firing range about five miles from Newark. Inside, there are deer heads on the walls, more than 300 handguns for sale and the pop of pistols from the firing range. Outside is a patriotic and Second Amendment tableau -- flags, Washington, Jefferson, Teddy Roosevelt and pheasant hunters on the pebbled concrete exterior. Mr. Cerca is less impressed with today's Democrats. ''Their agenda is to toughen the gun laws to the point that it's going to be nearly impossible for the average citizen to buy a gun for recreational target purposes or hunting or so on and so forth,'' he said. '' Whatever else they say is just sweetening their little ultimate agenda.'' Best of times? Well, the gun sales and the training business may be the only things still prospering. Last year, Mr. Colandro sold two gift certificates for Father's Day. This year, he sold 17. Gun dealers across the nation have reported robust sales since  Barack Obama's election. In New Jersey, applications for pistol permits have soared -- to roughly double last year's totals, according to The Star-Ledger of Newark. There's a shortage of ammunition, and many types are almost unavailable. The Bullet Hole limits ammunition sales to two boxes a customer for the firing range and one if you're taking it home.

1808.text: GUN-5540 PRIMARY Gilmore Defends Appearance in NRA Ad; Spots Promote Richmond Crime Project Virginia Gov. James S. Gilmore III (R) today defended his appearance in an NRA advertising campaign picturing him holding a shotgun and touting a Richmond-based federal program designed to get illegal guns off the street. The full-page ad, in the Nov. 16 issue of U.S. News and World Report and in other magazines, features a beaming Gilmore with the skeet-shooting gun of his son, Jay, 15. It promotes the Richmond-based Project Exile, a nationally publicized program endorsed by the National Rifle Association that guarantees five-year prison terms for felons caught carrying a gun. The governor has embraced a plan to spread Project Exile statewide. "If you're a felon caught carrying a gun, you get five years in prison, no exceptions," Gilmore says in the ad. "The NRA has long advocated this approach and is leading the fight for Project Exile as a model nationwide." Some credit the program with lowering Richmond's homicide rate by more than half. It had ranked among the highest in the country recently. Gilmore said he did the ad because of its strong anti-crime message. "I'm thinking about it in terms of pushing a strong program that all people can come together on," Gilmore said in an interview. "This should not be a divisive issue." Bill Powers, a spokesman for NRA, which is based in Fairfax County, said this week that the Gilmore spot is the latest in an "I'm the NRA" campaign that started 15 years ago. It also has featured such figures as thriller writer Tom Clancy, actor and NRA President Charlton Heston and sports greats Steve Largent and Nolan Ryan.

1809.text: GUN-8085 PRIMARY EDITORIAL DENVER POST ENDORSEMENTS Close gun-show loophole It should come as no surprise that The Denver Post is  wholeheartedly supporting Amendment 22, the ballot initiative that  would close the gun-show loophole and require all purchases of  firearms at such events to be subject to a background check. After all, the legislature - under heavy pressure from the  powerful gun lobby - failed to pass an array of reasonable  gun-control bills, so the course left was to turn to voters. Current law requires only licensed dealers to perform  background checks when they sell their wares at gun shows. But  unlicensed vendors claiming to sell 'private collections' may make  sales at the shows without appropriate checks. Three of the guns used in the Columbine High School massacre,  for example, were purchased specifically for Eric Harris and Dylan  Klebold at gun shows without background checks. Even the fourth  weapon, bought privately, had been obtained by its original owner  at an earlier show without a background check. Amendment 22 would force those private sellers to have a  background check performed on the buyer before the sale of any  firearm. Nothing about Amendment 22 should be objectionable to  law-abiding citizens. And, no, it's not a prelude to registration  and licensing of firearms. It's just a proactive law that closes  an existing loophole. Voters shouldn't be fooled by initiative opponents or  gun-rights defenders who contend this is a sinister law. It's not.   We reiterate: People who are legally eligible to own guns for  hunting, target practice and even home security have nothing to  fear from Amendment 22.

1805.text: GUN-2628 PRIMARY FEDERAL GUN LAWS USED TO HOLD VIOLENT OFFENDERS John R. Reece is just the person federal authorities and St. Louis police detectives had in mind when they hit upon a new way to clear the streets of gunmen. Until this week, Reece had been free even though witnesses told detectives that he fatally shot Shonta Hogans, 16, on July 25 outside a house in the 4500 block of Aldine Avenue, authorities said. Authorities did not press the case when the witnesses later refused to talk. Reece, 22, of Beverly Hills was arrested this week - the first person charged through the work of the newly formed Homicide Task Force. The group includes St. Louis homicide detectives and two agents from the federal Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. Reece was charged in a federal indictment Thursday with being a felon in illegal possession of a firearm. Federal prosecutors want him held without bond. "That's basically the aim - to get these violent offenders off the street," said Larry A. Washington, spokesman for the ATF office here. "There is an outcry from the community to get something done about these attacks, these drive-by shootings and this street violence." Assistant U.S. Attorney Thomas Mehan, who is prosecuting Reece, said he hoped that witnesses in Shonta's killing now will feel safe enough to tell authorities what they saw. Reece was convicted in 1991 of attempted armed robbery in St. Louis County.   He got a seven-year sentence. He was on parole in April when St. Louis police raided his home in the 5400 block of Cabanne Avenue.

1807.text: GUN-7730 PRIMARY CLINTON SAYS MARCH MAY PROD CONGRESS TO ENACT GUN BILLS OTHERS EXPRESSED DOUBT ABOUT WHETHER THE LEGISLATION'S CONTROLS ON GUNS WOULD CURB VIOLENCE. The gun lobby is "no match for America's moms" pressing for gun controls, President Clinton said yesterday on the eve of Mother's Day rallies around the country for stronger laws. "If the moms stick with it, they will succeed," Clinton said in his weekly radio address, broadcast before today's "Million Mom March" on the National Mall and events in at least 62 communities nationwide expected to draw up to one million participants. He pointed to grass-roots campaigns that helped enact stricter gun laws in California, Maryland and Massachusetts. "They're letting the gun lobby know it is no match for America's moms," he said. "But our nationwide fight won't be over tomorrow, no matter how many march. We have so much work still to do." Clinton said the march is sending a clear message to the Republican Congress to act on gun legislation that has been before lawmakers since shortly after the April 1999 shootings at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo. Those bills would require child safety locks with all new handguns, close a loophole that permits firearms purchases at gun shows without background checks, and ban the import of large-capacity ammunition clips. Clinton said that every day nearly a dozen children are killed by guns, either intentionally or accidentally, leaving families with "a wound that never heals." "Congress must pass common-sense gun legislation to protect our children without constraining the rights of legitimate gun owners," the President said.

1813.text: GUN-424 PRIMARY VICTORY WITH NO SHOTS FIRED THE Supreme Court gave gun control advocates a victory last week by deciding not to hear a case. It let stand an appeals court ruling that a ban on private possession of handguns in Morton Grove, Ill., is constitutional. Three groups from the Chicago suburb, with support from the National Rifle Association, challenged the 1981 ordinance on the ground that it violated Second Amendment guarantees of the right to bear arms. The High Court has issued only two interpretations of the amendment, ruling in 1886 that it only limits Federal legislation and in 1939 that it does not block prohibition of interstate commerce in sawed-off shotguns. The action not to hear the case creates no legal precedent, and therefore may be of limited significance outside Morton Grove. Even in Illinois, the State Supreme Court has yet to decide a challenge based on the state constitution. Indeed, rifle association attorneys played down the High Court's action, saying the issue was primarily a matter of state law. Last year, a California appeals court overturned a San Francisco law that generally banned the possession of pistols, ruling that the city had no authority to enact it. With numerous other jurisdictions considering similar bans, however, a Supreme Court decision to review the Morton Grove case could have thrown gun control efforts into temporary limbo. A Gallup survey earlier this year indicated that a majority of Americans favor stricter controls on pistol sales, but stop short of calling for an outright ban. In general, the poll found that residents of cities with populations over 50,000 favored bans, while those living in smaller cities and rural areas did not. President Reagan has consistently said that pistol controls are ineffective.

1812.text: GUN-20037 PRIMARY California should douse 'Ring of Fire' An oft-repeated axiom says: "As goes the state of California, so goes the nation." So developments this week in Sacramento may well affect the entire nation. On Tuesday, the California Legislature sent a historic gun-control bill to Republican Gov. Pete Wilson that would regulate the manufacture and sale of "Saturday night specials" throughout the state. Under normal circumstances, curtailing the production of these cheaply made guns would have bearing only on Californians. But it just so happens that 80% of these guns are manufactured by six companies in an area popularly known as "the Ring of Fire," which extends from Los Angeles to Orange County. Interestingly, these guns, according to a recent study conducted by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms, are the "guns of choice" used by juvenile delinquents in our major urban centers in the commission of burglaries, robberies and murder. As recently as 1994, juveniles were being murdered at the rate of seven per day. And as chilling as these statistics are, without some drastic changes things could get worse before they get better. By 2005, demographers predict a 14% increase in the number of teen-agers between the ages of 14 and 17 -- the children who buy and use these guns. Ironically, the kids killed with these cheap guns will mirror the diversity of the community where they were made. The groups that will grow the most among teen-agers are blacks and Latinos. It is those two groups that will bear the brunt of the violence.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1810.text: GUN-10119 PRIMARY No Immunity for the Gun Industry At a time when Congress is grappling with critical measures, including military and energy issues, the Senate majority leader, Bill Frist, has seen fit to catapult a special interest bill for the gun lobby to the head of the legislative queue. The bill would grant gun manufacturers, distributors and sellers an unreasonable degree of immunity from civil suits by families or communities harmed by gun violence. It would even require that lawsuits already filed be dismissed. Although the firearms industry argues that it should not be held liable for the criminal acts of those who buy or steal guns, all too often the dealers, distributors or manufacturers contribute to the problem by failing to safeguard their inventories or police their own sales responsibly. The victims of their negligence deserve the right to sue. Most Americans would surely applaud the legal settlement made in the Washington-area sniper case. The dealer that ''lost'' the sniper's assault rifle, and some 200 other guns as well, and the rifle's manufacturer paid $2.5 million to two surviving victims and the families of six victims who died. Yet the pending bill, according to legal experts, is so restrictive that if it had been in effect, this lawsuit would have been barred. A similar bill cleared the House last year, but it was withdrawn in the Senate when the National Rifle Association objected to the attachment of gun-control amendments. Republicans have since gained four Senate seats, and Democrats have grown more fearful of opposing the gun lobby. When this misguided immunity bill comes up for a vote, responsible senators must find a way to head it off or to summon the courage to vote no.

1811.text: GUN-5888 PRIMARY Clinton decries NRA role in gun bill President Clinton and congressional Democrats accused House Republicans on Wednesday of buckling in to the National Rifle Association and gutting proposed gun control provisions scheduled for a House vote next week. "It is a bill, plainly, ghostwritten by the NRA," said Clinton, speaking at a Justice Department conference. "I think it is wrong to let the NRA call the shots on this issue. They've been calling the shots on this issue for decades now, and we have failed to do what is manifestly in the interest of our children and our communities." Clinton angrily invoked the shootings of 12 students and a teacher at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo. "If we can still remember Littleton --- it hasn't even been two months --- then we ought to speak up and be heard," Clinton said. "This is a classic, horrible example of how Washington is out of touch with the rest of America." Clinton delivered his remarks as the first details of the gun control proposals drafted by Judiciary Committee Chairman Henry Hyde (R-Ill.) and Rep.  Bill McCollum (R-Fla.) began circulating among both gun control advocates and the gun lobby. The proposed language by Hyde and McCollum, to be offered as an amendment to a massive juvenile crime bill, would soften some of the requirements for criminal background checks conducted at gun shows. Closing the so-called gun show loophole was a major flash point in the Senate, which three weeks ago adopted strict language to govern when and on whom the checks must be conducted.

1815.text: GUN-2519 PRIMARY THE SENATE TALKS TOUGH The congressional debate over the Brady bill generated more publicity, but the real battle in Washington is likely to come in January, when the House and the Senate try to reconcile their differing versions of omnibus crime legislation. With only four negative votes - Sen. Paul Simon being one of them - the Senate approved last week a far-reaching $ 22 billion anti-crime package that expands the number of federal crimes that carry the death penalty, calls for building new prisons and imposes tougher sentences for the prisoners who will fill them. The House chose a wiser, more limited approach, concentrating on putting more police officers on the street and fighting drug abuse. In past years, contention over the Brady bill had held up passage of any crime legislation. This year, congressional leaders agreed to separate it from other crime-related measures, letting each stand on its own merits. The House passed a version of the Brady bill's provisions for a waiting period for the purchase of handguns, giving local authorities the opportunity to make a background check on prospective buyers. The Senate passed a different version.   After more than 24 hours of debate and contention, the Senate accepted something much closer to the House version and sent the measure on to President Bill Clinton for his signature. Society has a right to keep guns out of the hands of felons or mentally unstable people, and the Brady bill will help do so. The minor inconvenience it will impose on gun buyers will be worth the benefits.

1814.text: NA-42284 PRIMARY Feb. 20 letters to the editor Interpreting the 2nd Amendment I've smiled as several readers have interpreted the "true" meaning of the Second Amendment. One writer stated the Second Amendment was drafted to provide citizens with arms only in "common defense" situations against foreign enemies. Read the history on the amendment; it also pertains to "domestic" enemies. Another writer said, "Ditch the M-16s and high-capacity magazines." California has had, for some time, some of the most stringent gun-control laws: no automatic weapons, no high-capacity magazines (more than 10 rounds) and background checks with a waiting period to purchase a firearm. Instead of declaring war on those illegally possessing a firearm, politicians continue to go after law-abiding citizens. They believe outlawing everything -- from BB guns to Nerf shooters -- will keep everyone safe. The problem is those who don't obey laws don't care about those laws. Check Chicago or Washington, D.C.'s crime statistics, where it is virtually impossible for a law-abiding citizen to possess a firearm. To those interpreting the Second Amendment for the rest of us: What's your explanation for what our forefathers meant when they wrote, "the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed?" Gabby Talkington Richmond Colleges getting dumbed down The abysmal pass rate (to avoid taking remedial or "bonehead English") at Contra Costa community colleges, recently highlighted in a column by Tom Barnidge, is only the tip of the iceberg.

1816.text: GUN-11895 PRIMARY Preventing massacre is not a left-right issue Gun debate o Country must work to find common ground, not stand divided. OTHER VIEWS In her landmark studies of the terminally ill, psychiatrist Elizabeth Kubler-Ross suggested there are five stages of grief. In no particular sequence, she said, they are anger, denial, bargaining, depression and acceptance. On Monday, talking with Mark Reardon on his radio show on KMOX about the slaughter Friday at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn., I didn't get much past anger. Talk-show hosts know that shades of gray don't play well on radio, so everything is painted as up vs. down, black vs. white, left vs. right. Mr. Reardon suggested that in the gun debate, everyone on the left wants to seize all the guns. Those on the right stand for the Second Amendment. That's bunk and I called him on it. I went straight to anger as I explained my grief over Sandy Hook. On top of being an American, a human being, a father and grandfather of children the age of the 20 who were shot to death, two days before the tragedy I'd been thinking about what might happen in a real-life school shooting in my community. I'd taken my wife to lunch. She works at an early learning center. She sits at the front desk, where she stares at a photo of a man who isn't allowed to enter the building. On her desk is a phone that she's supposed to dial if the guy shows up. The police are notified. The building goes on lockdown.

1817.text: NA-44630 PRIMARY More guns, less crime? Not exactly. Over the weekend, a federal judge struck down the District's ban on carrying handguns in public, the last such law in the country to fall as gun-rights advocates have been accumulating victories. Shortly afterward, Alan Gura, the lawyer representing the plaintiffs in the case, told The Post this: I believe the city is absolutely safer. Make no mistake about it. This is a fantastic improvement in public safety. Yes, we have a problem in America with gun violence. But no, that problem is not the result of law-abiding people carrying guns. That prediction - Washington will be safer without the gun ban - stems from what's known as the "more guns, less crime" hypothesis. It argues that violent crime declines in places where more legal gun owners carry the weapons in public, both because those people are capable of self-defense, and because would-be criminals know they're out there. "More guns, less crime" has been an incredibly potent idea in local policy debates over gun laws. But is there evidence that it's true? The District, which is considering an appeal of the decision, isn't buying it. The theory has largely been fueled by a deeply contested 1997 paper by economists John Lott and David Mustard, who concluded that "concealed handguns are the most cost-effective method of reducing crime thus far analyzed by economists." If states without concealed-carry laws had them back in 1992, Lott and Mustard calculated, that year they could have avoided hundreds of murders, thousands of rapes and tens of thousands of assaults.

1818.text: GUN-918 PRIMARY Senate Rejects Instant Checks on Handgun Buyers The Senate early today signaled its intention to approve a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases as it rejected an alternative backed by the gun lobby that called for instant, computerized checks to screen out felons. The alternative was defeated, 54 to 44, as the Senate struggled to clear the way for passage of an omnibus anti-crime bill before it adjourns this weekend for the Fourth of July recess. Although both sides characterized the vote as the key test for the waiting-period proposal in the Senate, the intense battle over it is not necessarily over. Senate leaders laid the groundwork for a cloture vote Saturday, if necessary, to break a filibuster if one is launched by foes of the seven-day wait. Compromise remains a possibility, especially if there is a filibuster and it is impossible to muster the 60 votes needed to break it. But the vote margin was wider than expected, making any last-ditch attempt to overturn it more difficult. The vote was largely but not entirely along party lines, with most Democrats voting for it and most Republicans opposed. Nine Democrats, mostly from southern and western states, voted for the alternative, while eight Republicans, mostly moderates, voted against it. All Washington-area senators voted against the alternative. The showdown came after several days of talks between groups led by Majority Leader George J. Mitchell (D-Maine) and Minority Leader Robert J. Dole (R-Kan.) aimed at reaching a compromise on the contentious gun-control issue.

1822.text: NA-45820 PRIMARY Gun law battle may take spotlight in 2014; Groups to put millions into midterm races, state law challenges Both sides of the gun law debate have geared up to wage war across the country in 2014 using millions of dollars, midterm elections, opposing messaging strategies and dueling grass-roots campaigns. This year, groups will focus on pouring money into candidates that support their ideals and changing or upholding laws in hot spots such as Colorado, Washington and Illinois. On one side, gun rights activists plan to challenge laws in states such as New Jersey and California that they believe overly restrict law-abiding firearms owners. Advocates of tougher laws say they plan to build stronger state infrastructures and work on issues such as prohibiting domestic-violence offenders from having weapons. "We have tens of millions of people across the country who support the Second Amendment and who will go out and listen to the NRA and vote the way the NRA tells them to," said Andrew Arulanandam, director of public affairs for the National Rifle Association. Arulanandam said the NRA will focus much of its $200million-$250million annual budget on educating its members about candidates and on making sure that laws don't infringe on rights -- and have an effect on criminals. An integral part of that plan means being active on the federal level, and in Colorado, where some state lawmakers were recalled after voting to require universal background checks for gun purchases and to ban large-capacity ammunition magazines, Arulanandam said.

1823.text: GUN-1668 PRIMARY MURDERS IN CAPITAL RANDOM BUT ROUTINE; DESPITE GUN-CONTROL LAWS, POLICE IN WASHINGTON EXPECT 1991 MURDERS; TO EXCEED THE 483 RECORD SET LAST YEAR. At a recent symposium of the Amerian Psychological Association, 20 children from Washington brought in pictures they had drawn to depict their daily lives. All 20 drawings had guns in them. Such is life in the nation's capital. Last weekend, 34-year-old Keith Kellums trucked up a load of Christmas trees from around his hometown of Owensboro, Ky., to sell in Washington. Somebody promptly shot him in the head and robbed him. Thus far, the hospitalized Kellums has survived, though, and has not been added to the 1991 murder total that stood at 468 on Wednesday. Police said they expect to break the 1990 record of 483. Although the numbers are about the same, there's a difference in the type of killings between this year and last, said officer Ed Wilson, a spokesman for the District Police. "There's less of the gangland type of violence and more of the argument category. Only 34 percent of the homicides this year are drug-related." Indeed, there has been a frightening randomness to murder in the capital lately. Newlyweds Patricia and Freddie Lexie were riding on the Anacostia Freeway -- one of the District's major thoroughfares-- last month when a car pulled beside them and a stranger shot into their car from the next lane. Mrs. Lexie, riding on the passenger side, was killed. The suspected killer, Henry James, 19, said he "just felt like bustin' somebody," the Washington Post reported.

1821.text: GUN-11602 PRIMARY Law silencing docs on guns tossed ; Physicians can ask patients about weapons, judge rules. TALLAHASSEE -- A federal judge has permanently blocked a Florida law barring doctors from asking their patients about gun ownership, ruling the law unconstitutionally violates physicians' freedom of speech. In issuing a permanent injunction Friday, U.S. District Judge Marcia Cooke rejected the state's argument that the law was aimed at protecting gun owners from discrimination. Cooke had temporarily put the law on hold last year, after three groups of doctors sued Gov. Rick Scott within days after he signed the National Rifle Association-backed bill passed by the GOP-dominated legislature. "This law chills practitioners' speech in a way that impairs the provision of medical care and may ultimately harm the patient," Cooke wrote in her 25-page ruling. "The state, through this law, inserts itself in the doctor-patient relationship, prohibiting and burdening speech necessary to the proper practice of preventive medicine, thereby preventing patients from receiving truthful, non-misleading information. ... This it cannot do." Scott spokesman Lane Wright said the governor was considering his options. Rep. Jason Brodeur, R-Sanford, the bill's sponsor, and the Florida Senate's general counsel, Craig Meyer, said they believed Scott would appeal. Mobeen Rathore, president of the Florida Chapter of the American Academy of Pediatrics, said he hoped the case would be quickly resolved, for patients' sake, "so we can continue our efforts to get them the best information, specifically preventive information to families for their children on this and all risk issues."

1824.text: GUN-1140 PRIMARY Legally Bought Guns Do Plenty of Mayhem To the Editor: In television interviews on PBS and ABC last week, Attorney General Dick Thornburgh said that one of his reasons for not supporting the bill requiring a seven-day waiting period for gun purchase is that only one-sixth of all murders committed with firearms involve guns legally purchased from an established dealer. He did not say that more than 12,000 murders are committed annually with firearms, and that one-sixth is equivalent to no fewer than 2,000 murders a year, or six every day. To this must be added the larger number of people injured by legally purchased firearms, with President Reagan and James Brady only the most prominent. Maybe if Mr. Thornburgh received every day a list of the six human beings killed with legally purchased guns, he would stop saying "only one-sixth." WALTER A. STRAUSS Forest Hills, Queens, March 25, 1991

1819.text: NA-43766 PRIMARY Mental health clouding gun effort Virginia, home to the National Rifle Association's headquarters, the Nation's Gun Show and a conservative legislature, is by many measures a more gun-friendly state than Maryland. But in one aspect of gun control, Virginia is more restrictive than its neighbor across the Potomac. Residents of the commonwealth are almost three times as likely to be banned from owning a firearm for mental health reasons as residents of Maryland, which has been reluctant to restrict the rights of the mentally ill. For the Democratic-controlled Maryland General Assembly and Gov. Martin O'Malley (D), this poses a dilemma. Their determination to pass one of the nation's toughest gun-control policies in the current legislative session is bumping headlong into the desire to protect the rights and privacy of people with mental illness. After last month's school shooting in Newtown, Conn., O'Malley was quick to join several governors, Democrats in Congress and President Obama in proposing measures that would restrict access to firearms. O'Malley's plan would ban assault weapons, institute strict gun-licensing requirements - and help him cement a progressive legacy as he eyes a possible presidential run in 2016. The package is also notable for what it does not do: O'Malley has deliberately sought to steer clear of what he considers overly expansive restrictions when it comes to mental health, saying that going too far could scare people from seeking treatment.

1820.text: GUN-9639 PRIMARY Senator Abandons D.C. Gun Proposal; Action by House On Ban Awaited Idaho Republican Sen. Larry E. Craig announced yesterday that he has abandoned a bid to seek a repeal of D.C. gun laws in a Senate committee, and Senate GOP leaders indicated that they did not have time to get bogged down in a debate over gun limits in the weeks before Congress adjourns for the fall elections. Craig dropped his effort as opponents of the proposed repeal, including parents of District residents killed by gunfire, lobbied senators and held a news conference on Capitol Hill. A similar proposal has been introduced in the House and has more than enough co-sponsors to pass that chamber. "If the U.S. Capitol can be handgun-free, why can't we?" asked Hannah Hawkins, director of the Children of Mine Center, a youth services agency in Anacostia. She was joined at the news conference at the Dirksen Senate Office Building by other youth advocates, parents, the Metropolitan Washington Council of the AFL-CIO and business groups led by the Greater Washington Board of Trade. "There is nothing honest, there is nothing just, there is nothing compassionate in these bills," said Lori Kaplan, executive director of the Latin American Youth Center in the District. "They are a blatant attack by people who do not live in this city, and who do not care about the quality of life in this city, on the self-determination for those of us who do."

characters: Metropolitan Washington Council of the AFL-CIO

1825.text: NA-42984 PRIMARY The Gun Lobby's Latest Bizarre Crusade The National Rifle Association has mounted an outrageous campaign to torpedo President Obama's nomination of an outstanding young doctor to be the next surgeon general of the United States because of his attitudes on gun control. The sad part is, the campaign is causing some nervous Democrats whose votes may be needed for Senate confirmation to consider breaking with the president to bolster their own chances for re-election in states where the gun lobby is powerful. The nominee is Dr. Vivek Murthy, an internist, who has impeccable credentials for the job. He currently works at Brigham and Women's Hospital in Boston, an outstanding teaching hospital affiliated with Harvard Medical School, where he also teaches. He started a software technology company to improve the efficiency of clinical trials around the world and currently is president of Doctors for America, the successor to Doctors for Obama, whose 16,000 members support the health care reforms and access to affordable health care. The job of surgeon general plays an important role advocating for healthier lifestyles. Some former surgeons general have reshaped the nation's thinking on the dangers of smoking and the urgency of combating AIDS. Dr. Murthy said at his confirmation hearing last month that he intended to focus on preventing obesity, tobacco-related disease, mental illness and community-driven public health initiatives -- all subjects worthy of attention. In answer to a Republican question, he said he would not use his office as ''a bully pulpit for gun control.''

1831.text: GUN-9765 PRIMARY Senate sends weapons bill to president Off-duty and retired police officers would have the right to carry concealed weapons in any state under legislation the Senate approved by voice vote Wednesday and sent to the president. The bill would override some states' prohibitions on carrying concealed firearms. Many states do not recognize other states' concealed weapons permits. "This legislation will allow thousands of equipped, trained and certified officers to continually serve and protect our communities regardless of jurisdiction and at no cost to taxpayers," the House's chief sponsor, Rep. Randy "Duke" Cunningham, R-Calif., said when the House approved it by voice vote June 23. The White House endorsed the measure in a statement, saying, "This legislation will better protect the nation from danger by ensuring that officers are ready to handle an emergency regardless of their location and duty status."

1827.text: NA-42882 PRIMARY Harry Reid's Conscience Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid generally isn't thought of as a conviction politician. He's a vote-counter; a highly cautious pragmatist who not long ago ducked an opportunity to reform the filibuster, and with it the increasingly slow-to-non-existent legislative process. So it was surprising to hear him invoke his conscience on Wednesday.   Even as it became clear that the Senate would not pass a post-Sandy Hook gun safety bill-not even a provision to expand background checks that's highly popular with the American people-he urged his colleagues to "say yes." "Today I choose to vote my conscience, not only is Harry Reid a United States senator but also a husband, a father, a grandfather and I hope friend of lots and lots of people, I choose to vote my conscience because, if tragedy strikes again, I'm sorry to say, Mr. President, it will, if innocents are gunned down in a classroom, theater or restaurant, I would have trouble living with myself as a senator, as a husband, a fathers, or grandfather and friend, knowing that I didn't do everything in my power to prevent that incident." Mr. Reid, no doubt providing fodder for future political attack ads, also announced his support for Senator Diane Feinstein's assault weapons ban. He said that, before deciding to support the ban, he called a gun-advocate friend to ask his advice - and found that friend's reasoning "absurd."

1828.text: GUN-30521 PRIMARY Warning for the 'bad guys' TRENTON - Citing New Jersey's soaring prison population and violent-crime upswing, Gov. Corzine yesterday unveiled the first elements of a major crime-fighting policy that takes aim at gang activity and gun crimes. Corzine outlined the "enforcement" aspects of a three-part "Strategy for Safe Streets and Neighborhoods" during a midday news conference on the front steps of the State House. Additional appearances by the governor are scheduled for today in Newark and tomorrow in Camden. Standing amid dozens of police officers, mayors and state officials, Corzine outlined a wish list of tougher laws and improved police computer and data systems that he deems essential to the goal of attacking a sinister trio in New Jersey: Gangs. Guns. And violent crime. Framed by a silent cohort of uniformed police behind him, Corzine calmly uttered a warning: "The bad guys should know," he said, "if you're a violent gang member, we're going to arrest you. If you carry illegal guns, sell or use those guns, you're going to go to jail. And you're going to stay there. "If you intimidate a witness, your prison stay's going to be longer," Corzine said. The Corzine plan is in response to the growing problem of gang violence across New Jersey, which is inextricably linked with homicides and illegal gun trafficking, officials said. The Department of Law and Public Safety is expected to piggyback on Corzine's three-day media blitz by releasing its own, long-awaited report on gang activity in New Jersey later this week - the first in three years.

characters: Department of Law and Public Safety

1829.text: GUN-10344 PRIMARY SELF-DEFENSE SHOULDN'T BE LICENSE TO KILL It took Hillsborough County deputies two days to arrest Trevor Dooley, the school bus driver accused of shooting and killing a Valrico Air Force veteran on a basketball court. The arrest on manslaughter charges may have been complicated by the state's "stand your ground" law, which allows the use of lethal force if a person feels threatened by another with great bodily harm. The law makes it more difficult to make arrests and prosecute assailants when there has been a fight. It has been five years since the Florida Legislature disregarded the advice of prosecutors and law enforcement and passed the controversial "stand your ground" law at the behest of the powerful National Rifle Association. Under the law, a person has no duty to attempt to retreat from a threat of violence even if that is a safe option. Florida has not become a "Wild West" with gunfights in the streets as many feared, but the law has interfered with prosecutions. In numerous cases, serious charges were not pursued because it appeared that the killer might technically be protected by the "stand your ground" law. In one Miami case last year, occupants of two cars exchanged gunfire during a high-speed chase and one man was killed. The gunman received a three-year sentence in a plea agreement. Prosecutors worried about potential "stand your ground" immunity. In Dooley's case in Hillsborough, the Sheriff's Department says he carried a gun as he confronted a boy riding a skateboard on a basketball court. The episode ended with 41-year-old David James shot dead. He had been playing basketball with his 8-year-old daughter when he and Dooley argued over the skateboarding.

1826.text: GUN-11337 PRIMARY Court backs U.S. curb on under-21 gun sales The United States may ban federally licensed firearms dealers from selling handguns to people under 21 years of age, an appellate court ruled Thursday in a defeat for the National Rifle Association. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the 5th Circuit in Houston rejected the NRA's argument that 18-to-20-year-olds have a right to buy the guns under the Second Amendment to the Constitution, as well as the equal-protection clause of the Fifth Amendment. A unanimous three-judge panel said Congress, in a law dating from 1968, adopted the sales ban to help curb violent crime. It also said that the nation's founders and 19th-century courts and commentators believed that disarming specific groups did not trample on the right to bear arms. "Congress was focused on a particular problem: young persons under 21, who are immature and prone to violence, easily accessing handguns," mainly from licensed dealers, Judge Edward Prado wrote for the panel. Thursday's decision upheld a September 2011 ruling by U.S. District Judge Sam Cummings in Lubbock, Tex. The case had been brought a year earlier by the NRA, firearms dealers and individuals against the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, which is part of the Justice Department. The 5th Circuit said it was the first federal appeals court to address the ban since the Supreme Court announced a broad Second Amendment right for individuals to keep and bear arms, in the 2008 case District of Columbia v. Heller.

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives

1830.text: GUN-31233 PRIMARY Senate Approves Bill To Insure Same-Sex Partners The Senate  passed a bill yesterday that would allow local governments to expand health insurance to more members of their employees' households, including same-sex partners. The legislation passed 23 to 17, with two Republicans siding with all 21 Democrats to advance it. A similar bill passed the Senate last year but failed in the House of Delegates. That year, it passed by a much greater margin. Sen. Mary Margaret Whipple (D-Arlington) introduced the bill at  the request of the Arlington County government. -- Sandhya Somashekhar House Rejects Plan to Restrict Guns at Virginia Capitol The House of Delegates overwhelmingly rejected a proposal yesterday to restrict the possession of guns at the Virginia Capitol. The House voted 77 to 18 to reject  a change in House rules proposed by Del. Lionel Spruill Sr. (D-Chesapeake). The rule would have prohibited guns  in areas of Capitol Square controlled by the House, which has no say over space controlled by the Senate. House Republican Leader H. Morgan Griffith (Salem) said that was a major flaw in the proposal. He also said that  although the proposal exempted police officers, visiting dignitaries would be unable to bring their armed bodyguards. Spruill said some visitors to the Capitol this week said they felt unsafe because gun rights activists were carrying their firearms on the grounds. -- Associated Press Senate Passes Bill to Let Officers Stop Unbelted Drivers

characters: House Republican Leader H. Morgan Griffith

1835.text: GUN-2106 PRIMARY The N.R.A., Wounded For the second time in a matter of weeks, a Governor fed up with the National Rifle Association's bullying seems likely to rub the arrogant lobby's nose in the dirt, perhaps crushing its invincible image once and for all. A vote today in New Jersey's Senate is almost certain to uphold Gov. Jim Florio's firm position on gun control -- thus signifying a historic transformation in the national gun control debate. A bill before the Senate would override Mr. Florio's veto of an earlier measure that repealed a law severely limiting assault weapons. The law, passed in 1990, is the toughest in America to restrict military style guns that have no legitimate civilian purpose and are favored by drug gangs and other organized criminals. After the law survived court challenges, the gun lobby sought to emasculate it in the Legislature. Last summer, that effort succeeded: even though a large public majority supported the ban, both houses approved a bill repealing it for all but the AK-47 and Uzi, two of the more popular guns. It was the sort of compromise the politicians might once have swallowed to get the N.R.A. off their backs. But it enraged Governor Florio, who vetoed the bill and called for a popular referendum to settle the issue once and for all. Campaigning aggressively around the state, he managed to seize the initiative on the issue and build public support in an election year. Republicans who engineered the repeal began to waver.

1834.text: NA-42129 PRIMARY Facebook wins praise in illegal gun sales crackdown MENLO PARK, Calif.    Gun control groups praised Facebook on Wednesday for changing its policies to block potentially illegal firearm sales online. The social networking giant agreed to delete posts reported by members where a seller agrees to forego background checks, or where a buyer tries to avoid one. Posts about sales looking to cross state lines will also be deleted. The same policy will apply to Facebook's photo sharing subsidiary, Instagram. Facebook is relying on members to report pages that could potentially facilitate illegal gun sales, and members under 18 years old won't be able to view posts flagged by other members, or pages where guns are sold or traded. Facebook, based in Menlo Park, Calif., will remind private sellers to follow relevant laws, including those requiring background checks, and will block users who don't acknowledge the reminder.cAnd when someone searches on Instagram for a hashtag related to gun offers   like "#guns4sale," which turned up 133 posts Wednesday morning   they'll be required to acknowledge the relevant laws that apply to them before they see search results. "We will not permit people to post offers to sell regulated items that indicate a willingness to evade or help others evade the law," Monika Bickert, Facebook's head of global policy management, said in a news release. "We believe these collective efforts represent the right approach in balancing people's desire to express themselves while promoting a safe, responsible community."

1832.text: GUN-8754 PRIMARY Truth About Guns In his April 2 letter, James E. Atwood presents a clear and needed defense of gun control legislation. His dedication to the gun control movement is commendable. His dedication to facts, however, is somewhat less so. He says he trusts "that Republican of old (Abraham Lincoln) who said, 'I'd rather lose in a cause that will ultimately win than to win in a cause that will ultimately fail.' " Those words were spoken by Woodrow Wilson, a Democrat, while fighting for the League of Nations. JAMES R. GOODWIN Herndon * James E. Atwood makes a series of misleading assertions in his April 2 letter. He said 30,000 Americans die by "gun violence" every year. This figure, a favorite of the anti-gun movement, typically includes deaths by suicide, law-enforcement actions and legal self-defense by private citizens. Mr. Atwood also said American "kids are 12 times more likely to die from a firearm than in the next 25 industrialized nations combined." This statement is based on selective and unreliable figures that have been debunked by researcher John Lott of Yale University Law School. Mr. Atwood also suggests falsely that in "other nations" the same conditions of parenting and media violence exist as in the United States. The news and entertainment media in nearly all other countries are more subject to censorship than here. In Japan, for example, almost no material attacking the existing social order is seen in the popular media marketed to young people.

1836.text: GUN-217 PRIMARY Gun Sellers Liable, Court Says People who make or sell small, cheap handguns should know that they are used mainly by criminals, and therefore the makers or sellers can be sued by victims shot in criminal attacks with them, Maryland's highest court ruled today. The unanimous decision by the Court of Appeals was the first in the nation to hold that the manufacturer or seller of cheap pistols, often called Saturday night specials, is liable for damages if the weapon is eventually used by a criminal to wound or kill. Olen J. Kelley of Silver Spring, Md., who was wounded in a 1981 holdup of a supermarket, sued Rolm Gesellschaft, a West German concern that designed and sold the gun used to shoot him. The Federal District Court in Baltimore where Mr. Kelley's suit was brought asked the state court for a ruling on whether the manufacturer could be held liable under Maryland law. The state opinion, by Judge John C. Eldridge, said that because of their poor quality such guns were ''virtually useless'' for ''legimate purposes,'' and that ''the manufacturer or marketer of a Saturday Night Special knows or ought to know that he is making or selling a product principally to be used in criminal activity.'' The ruling applies only in Maryland.

1833.text: GUN-2666 PRIMARY Connecticut Tightens Restrictions on Gun Owners Through hours of debate on a bill restricting the sale of handguns, one legislator after another rose in the Capitol here to say that more gun control will not stop the violence in Connecticut's cities that has become a huge concern among voters and politicians. The poverty and social problems in the cities were too deep, they said, the hold of the drug trade and street gangs too tight, for the General Assembly to legislate guns out of the shooters' hands.
