It was a familiar argument from gun-control opponents, another angle on their "Guns Don't Kill, People Do" bumper stickers. And this time, even many of the bill's supporters agreed. But in a year when teen-agers have been killed on courthouse steps and small children shot to death on city streets, gun control was something the legislators could seize on as a possible remedy. And so the House and Senate voted late Wednesday and early today to approve new restrictions, and Gov. Lowell P. Weicker Jr. quickly signed them into law. "This is a small step toward getting guns off our streets," said Rep. Robert Farr, a Republican from West Hartford who split from his party to vote for the bill. "If this saves only one or two lives, it's worth it. We can't stand by and watch children kill children." Higher Black-Market Prices? Explaining his opposition, Rep. Richard O. Belden, a Republican from Shelton, said: "This will not take one gun out of the hands of a single criminal." Its main effect would be to drive up black-market prices, he said, "because it will dry up the supply of guns, not the demand."

1837.text: GUN-2778 PRIMARY HOUSE BANS 19 ASSAULT WEAPONS IN CLOSE VOTE In a remarkable defeat for the National Rifle Association and its allies, the House voted Thursday to ban the manufacture, future sale and possession of 19 brands of combat-style weapons and their look-alikes. The 216-214 vote, coming on the heels of a similar bill that Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., crafted and squeezed through the Senate last fall, virtually ensures that the ban will soon become law as part of broader crime legislation. The outcome clearly reflected growing concern of Americans about violent crime in their communities. It came just days after gun-control opponents appeared headed for defeat of the bill by 15 or 20 votes. In voting for the bill, many lawmakers defied the politically potent NRA for the first time. The vote marked the second big defeat in Congress for the NRA, which last year failed by a much larger margin to block the "Brady bill," a new law that imposes a five-day waiting period on handgun purchases so police can do background checks on prospective buyers. President Clinton, who lobbied hard for the assault-weapons ban, called the vote "a sea change in the crime debate." The victory came in a moment of high drama. With tension rising in the closing moments and opponents ahead 214-213, Rep. Andy Jacobs, D-Ind., switched his vote, putting the ban ahead by a single vote. Rep. Austin Murphy, D-Pa., and Rep. Doug Applegate, D-Ohio, who had not voted yet, were besieged by colleagues on both sides of the issue pleading for support. Murphy voted "no" and Applegate voted "yes," while another foot- dragging member, Rep. Sanford Bishop, D-Ga., also voted for the bill to produce the final result, 216-214.

1838.text: GUN-31406 PRIMARY Four states considering open-carry gun laws; Among last holdouts in U.S., debate invokes Wild West, deterring crime Four  states -- Texas, South Carolina, Oklahoma and Arkansas -- are considering legislation that would allow people to carry handguns openly in a holster. These generally Second Amendment-friendly states are among the last six holdouts against open carrying of guns. Openly carrying handguns is legal in most states, even those that ban concealed firearms. New York and Florida also prohibit carrying handguns openly. The four other states that ban so-called open carry "are extremely gun-friendly. They understand the individual-rights aspect. Yet for whatever reason, the carry laws in these states are restrictive," says John Pierce, a co-founder of OpenCarry.org, which promotes gun rights. Most states have strict laws governing concealed weapons. Illinois and Wisconsin ban carrying them entirely, according to the National Rifle Association. Concealing a weapon "was seen in the early days of our nation as something of an unwholesome act. People would bear arms openly," Pierce says. Says Paul Helmke, president of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence, which opposes open-carry laws: "We don't want more people carrying guns either openly or concealed because the more guns you have in a situation, the more likely you are to get gun violence." Grass-roots movements supporting open carry have emerged via Internet and e-mail campaigns, Pierce says. The online Texas petition now has more than 55,000 signatures. OpenCarry.org raised $25,000 through online donations to pay for advertising in Texas, says OpenCarry.org co-founder Mike Stollenwerk.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1839.text: GUN-747 PRIMARY DEFINITIONS SHOOT HOLES IN GUN BILL A vigorous attempt by Florida lawmakers to ban military-style semiautomatic assault weapons is teetering on the brink of collapse because legislators can't decide how to define them. Spurred by arguments from the National Rifle Association that hunting rifles are identical in function and design to the controversial high-powered weapons, members of a House subcommittee last week indefinitely tabled a plan to ban 18 paramilitary weapons listed by name, manufacturer and function. Nonetheless, a majority of members of the House Criminal Justice Committee, which is reviewing the legislation, believes the definitions could be construed by courts and law enforcement to apply to semiautomatic shotguns and rifles used by hunters and sportsmen. "There is no such thing as an assault weapon," said Marion Hammer, lobbyist for the NRA's Florida chapter. "It's a new term that's been created and has not yet been defined. All semiautomatic firearms are functionally the same. They only look different." Hammer showed the committee a video supplied by the NRA showing that Uzis, MAC 10s, AK-47s and other "paramilitary" or "assault" weapons, as they are known, operate exactly like a wide variety of semiautomatic shotguns, handguns and rifles. Each gun fires a single shot with each pull of the trigger and automatically reloads. Their distinquishing characteristic is their appearance. Assault weapons are designed to look like military combat weapons, unlike target and hunting rifles.

1842.text: GUN-10898 PRIMARY SHERIFFS WORRY WE'LL BE GUNSLINGER STATE Fast-forward to Gasparilla 2012. Hillsborough County Sheriff David Gee envisions gunslinging paradegoers, free to drink alcohol and openly display pistols. Now picture Florida's beaches. Pinellas County Sheriff Jim Coats sees no reason why, starting this July, visitors couldn't pack their own heat under the sun. On Wednesday, local sheriffs joined others statewide to voice trepidation over a bill in the Florida Legislature that would allow people with concealed weapons permits to openly carry guns. Both the Senate and House versions of the bill passed their criminal justice committees this week. If enacted into law, it would take effect July 1. Supporters say the bill is meant to protect permit holders whose guns are accidentally made visible. Marion Hammer, former president of the National Rifle Association, has testified that permit holders have been unfairly punished in cases where, for example, a strong wind blows a shirt up and exposes a gun. The Senate bill is sponsored by Greg Evers, R-Baker, and the House's version is co-sponsored by eight Republicans. Sheriffs across the Tampa Bay region say that the "accidental exposure" argument is ludicrous. They're worried that criminals might steal exposed weapons and possibly use them against gun owners. A visible weapon could provoke crimes of opportunity, and concealed weapon permit holders - unlike deputies - don't have to pass physical fitness tests, said Polk County Sheriff Grady Judd.

1841.text: GUN-5242 PRIMARY Background checks by state for gun purchases about to end As Colorado legislators work to let more people carry more guns in more places, they're dropping a program that's proven effective at keeping guns out of the hands of criminals. The Colorado Bureau of Investigation's 12-member instant background check unit will officially disband Wednesday. After that, people buying guns in Colorado will be subject only to the FBI's background check, which even the FBI admits is flawed. That has some Colorado law enforcement officials worried about more guns getting in the wrong hands. "The net effect is that there will be more guns that go to people who previously would have been denied," said El Paso County Sheriff John Anderson, a Republican and a leader in issuing concealed weapons permits. "I've felt more comfortable when we're doing the CBI check on top of the federal check." The state system generally has rejected about 6 percent of people trying to buy guns because of criminal problems. The federal system, often called the "Brady check," rejects many fewer, about 1 percent. The 5 percentage point difference, those in law enforcement say, comes because the federal check is less comprehensive and errs on the side of giving the gun to the buyer. Under the state system, if an instant background check showed that a potential gun buyer had an arrest record, he or she couldn't buy the weapon. The person then could appeal by showing proof that he or she was found innocent or the charges were dismissed.

characters: El Paso County Sheriff John Anderson

1843.text: GUN-2849 PRIMARY WELL-REGULATED LUNACY Santa Rosa County in the Florida Panhandle has its own militia. In fact, every able-bodied person is a member. So residents can sleep well. (Unless militia members have to respond to reveille.) The county commission created the militia to preserve county residents' right to bear arms. Taking that rather paranoid step actually is a goof. For years, gun control advocates have said that the Second Amendment to the Constitution applies only to members of a well-regulated militia.'' The county commission acts as though it buys that argument (which, by the way, is valid). But Santa Rosa still hasn't fulfilled constitutional requirements. The militia there isn't well-regulated. Not only is there no reveille, there are no drills or exercises. It is, County Attorney Tom Dannheisser says, strictly symbolic. Great. In that respect, the Santa Rosa Militia is very much like most efforts to reduce violence caused by guns.

1845.text: GUN-30947 PRIMARY Guns Are Not the Problem To the Editor: Re ''Price of Lax Gun Laws'' (editorial, Dec. 23): You cite a study by Mayors Against Illegal Guns as support for the proposition that there is a link between ''weak gun laws'' and gun violence. While the study correlates background checks at gun shows and strong state gun laws to a reduction in the use of guns in crimes, the antigun lobby is far from satisfied with such measures. For example, gun control advocates seek to impose ''one gun per month'' laws on law-abiding citizens who have already been subjected to and passed background checks. If background checks work as well as the study suggests, why are additional restrictions necessary? The problem is not weak gun laws. Rather, the problem is an unwillingness on both sides of the gun control debate to accept limited gun laws that are, in the words of the mayors' study, ''designed to keep firearms away from the criminal minority without infringing on the rights of the law-abiding majority.'' Paul Malek Teaneck, N.J., Dec. 24, 2008

1840.text: NA-41806 PRIMARY Area events call for increased gun control laws Mayor Nutter chatted with seven children on spring break at the Widener Branch of the Free Library on Thursday afternoon, talking to them about gun violence in Philadelphia and across the nation. During the exchange, a 10-year-old boy said he knew twins who were shot in the head. "I didn't know anybody who was shot when I was 10 years old," said Nutter, who demanded stringent gun safety laws. "It's time for Congress to act." Nutter's plea at an event at the North Philadelphia library was part of Thursday's National Day to Demand Action, which had more than 100 events across the state and country, including one in Norristown. In Washington, President Obama urged Congress to enact tougher gun control measures. Nutter said the Dec. 14 massacre at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn., sparked an increased sense of urgency to pass comprehensive laws to reduce gun violence. In the Sandy Hook incident, 20 students and six adults were shot and killed by a gunman who earlier that day killed his mother and who later committed suicide. Nutter called for federal laws that would require criminal background checks for every gun sale. "Background checks are the only systematic way to stop felons, domestic abusers, and people with severe mental illnesses from buying firearms," he said. Deputy Police Commissioner Kevin Bethel said background checks were already required for sales at licensed gun shops and asked, "Why can't we hold private sellers to the same standards? The guns they sell are no less deadly than the guns sold by licensed dealers."

1846.text: GUN-3033 PRIMARY A GRAPHIC ARGUMENT AGAINST GUN CONTROL Your articles 1 dead, 1 hurt, 1 arrested in attempted robbery'' and Addict begged for help hours before fatal holdup,'' about the store employee at Marshall's Stereo who shot to death two would-be burglars, is another example of why Americans should oppose gun control. Had store employee Peter Glynn not had a gun, the store's owner, Marshall Howard, an innocent, hard-working small-business owner, could very well be dead. Though it is tragic when any human life is taken prematurely, I think what occurred was justified. I am not a gun owner, but I believe that if a person wants to own a gun for self-protection and has the proper training to use the gun, then the government should not interfere. We cannot expect the police to be everywhere to protect us, and we do not live in a perfect world in which gun laws will rid our society of guns. Gun laws will not stop criminals from obtaining guns, they will only thwart law-abiding citizens. Larry Kerr Greenacres

1847.text: GUN-2392 PRIMARY Wal-Mart to End Sales of Handguns in Stores Wal-Mart, the nation's largest retailer, said yesterday that it would stop selling handguns in its 2,000 stores because its marketing surveys indicated a majority of customers now feel "uncomfortable" shopping for lingerie, sofas, lawn rakes and teddy bears in the same place where revolvers and semiautomatic pistols are stocked. "They don't want to be around them," said Don Shinkle, a spokesman for Wal-Mart, with headquarters in Bentonville, Ark. While the company said it would no longer stock handguns in its stores, it also said it would continue to stock rifles and shotguns and that any customer who wanted to purchase a revolver or automatic pistol could do so by ordering through a catalogue at the store. The huge chain's decision, which takes effect on Feb. 1, came at a time when gun sales are surging around the country. Given Wal-Mart's highly regarded marketing savvy, gun-control advocates hailed the move as a major victory in their fight against firearm proliferation. 'A Sea Change Under Way' "When Wal-Mart, the family store of Middle America, does this, you know there's a sea change under way in the gun-selling business," said Representative Charles E. Schumer, a Brooklyn Democrat who has been in the forefront of gun-control efforts on Capitol Hill. The decision also came at a time when some retailers, including Wal-Mart, are being sued for selling guns to customers who were reportedly not qualified to buy them and who used the guns in fatal shooting incidents.

1844.text: GUN-9799 PRIMARY In W. Phila., Jesse Jackson rallies city against gun deaths; The civil-rights activist visited a barbershop to tout a May 9 march in Washington that will focus on assault weapons. Acknowledging barbershops as forums for frank discussion, the Rev. Jesse Jackson yesterday couldn't resist an invitation to visit one in West Philadelphia to spread the word about gun violence. As he sat in a chair getting sheared at Woodward's Barbershop, Jackson decried the loss of lives in Philadelphia and across the country. "We sell the most guns. We're the most violent country on Earth, and it doesn't have to be that way," the noted civil-rights activist and former Democratic presidential candidate said as media crews, community activists and curious neighbors watched. Jackson was promoting the Mother's Day March to Halt the Assault, a national rally against gun violence to be held May 9 in Washington. Jackson agreed to visit the barbershop after the owner, Robert Woodward, called Jackson's syndicated Sunday morning radio show on WDAS-FM (105.3). "I called his show and talked with him, and he said he would come by," said Woodward, who has organized antiviolence efforts through his Barbershop Talk Human Family Foundation. The foundation promotes cultural diversity and racial harmony. Jackson said that while there is controversy about showing photos of coffins bearing the remains of U.S. soldiers killed in Iraq, the carnage of gun violence continues to grow in American cities and towns. "It is a tragedy and it is painful" that our young soldiers are dying, Jackson said. "We have lost 50,000 in our streets at home" since the war started, he added. "Eighty Americans are killed a day by gun violence. Nine children a day are killed."

1849.text: GUN-2322 PRIMARY S.F. GUN-CONTROL PROPOSALS FOCUS CHIEFLY ON DEALERS San Francisco officials Tuesday said they're taking aim at crime with a new set of laws making it tougher to sell firearms. Three members of the board of supervisors and District Attorney Arlo Smith said the proposed laws are directed mostly at gun dealers. "Each day we don't restrict weapons sales is another day of weapons flowing readily onto our streets. We must stop that flow," said Supervisor Kevin Shelley. The proposal came the same day Treasury Secretary Lloyd Bentsen proposed legislation to charge gun dealers $600 a year for a federal firearms license. The current annual fee is $66. Of the 207 San Francisco residents who hold federal firearms dealer licenses, only 13 are authorized to sell in the city, said Supervisor Carole Migden. She proposed the city require all firearm dealers to pay a 3 percent tax on payroll and gross receipts. Currently, all businesses pay 1 1/2 percent of payroll and gross receipts. The proposals "send the message that San Francisco does not want to be user friendly to the gun trade," Migden said. Shelley proposed that firearm dealers should: * Obtain a city license to sell firearms along with their federal license. * Have an established place of business rather than sell guns at home or out of the trunk of a car. The business must be at least 1,000 feet from a residential district and 1,500 feet from a school or day care center.

1848.text: GUN-9244 PRIMARY FENTON IS URGED TO BAN HIDDEN GUNS IN CITY BUILDINGS; ATTORNEY SAYS LAW ALLOWS BANS IN SPECIFIC BUILDINGS; HE CALLS FOR IMMEDIATE ACTION Fenton officials want to approve legislation banning concealed weapons from municipal buildings. A new state law allows licensed gun owners to apply for permits to carry hidden guns. Under the new law, concealed weapons are banned in some government buildings such as courtrooms and police stations, as well as schools and gambling establishments. Fenton Assistant City Attorney Stanley Wallach said that would mean concealed weapons would be allowed in city halls, libraries and recreational facilities. Wallach said the statute also allowed municipalities to pass bills banning concealed weapons in specific buildings. Wallach recommended that the municipality begin drafting a bill immediately because gun owners could begin applying for concealed-weapon permits on Oct. 11, 30 days after the Senate overrode the governor's veto of the gun bill. Wallach said that even with an ordinance in effect, the consequences for improperly carrying a concealed weapon into a designated building would be minimal. "We can make an ordinance, but the only penalty is that the person will be asked to leave," he said. "If they don't leave, then a series of penalties would kick in including a $100 fine." When Alderman Mark Sartors, 3rd Ward, asked whether Fenton could also ban concealed weapons in areas such as parks, Wallach said the law specified buildings. "We'll have to put more buildings in our parks, then," Sartors quipped. Wallach said the municipality would watch closely to see how other municipalities handled the concealed-weapons issue.

1853.text: GUN-7015 PRIMARY Tell truth about weapons The study on gun laws in Georgia says exactly what I would have hoped it would say --- that we don't have as many idiots here as they do in New York (" Study: State gun laws lacking," April 13). This study was done by and for gun control advocates. Since 1968, you had to be 21 to buy a handgun and 18 to buy a rifle or shotgun. That's a federal law. So what's the big deal? How about publishing the findings of a study showing how crime has decreased since the enactment of concealed-carry laws that make it easier for people to get their permits? I remember when that idea was being discussed, and the newspaper was full of quotes from people saying there would be more shootings of police by civilians, more shootings overall and, in general, rampant gunslinging. Well, where is it? JEFF JOHNSON Johnson is an information technology consultant living in Morrow.

1855.text: GUN-11360 PRIMARY Aurora Victim Pushes Gun Issue With New Ad Stephen Barton was supposed to spend the fall teaching English in Russia on a Fulbright fellowship. But shortly after midnight on July 20, a gunman in a movie theater in Aurora, Colo., derailed those plans. Still recovering from the wounds he sustained when the gunman opened fire that night, killing 12 and injuring dozens more, Mr. Barton has decided to devote his energies this fall to something entirely different: Trying to get the presidential candidates to address the touchy issue of guns and gun violence. In a television advertisement to begin airing on Monday, Mr. Barton, seated in an empty movie theater, tells viewers that despite the injuries from 25 shotgun pellets that embedded themselves in his face and neck, he was lucky. "In the next four years, 48,000 Americans won't be so lucky, because they'll be murdered with guns in the next president's term, enough to fill over 200 theaters," Mr. Barton, 22, says in the advertisement. "So when you watch the presidential debates, ask yourself, 'Who has a plan to stop gun violence?'" The advertisement will appear in Colorado and on cable stations in Washington and other cities across the country, said Mark Glaze, director of Mayors Against Illegal Guns, a bipartisan coalition of more than 725 mayors that is sponsoring the ad as part of its "Demand a Plan" campaign. After the shooting spree in Aurora, both presidential candidates offered their condolences to the victims and their families. President Obama traveled to Aurora to visit the injured. Mitt Romney said, "Our hearts break with the sadness of this unspeakable tragedy."

1850.text: NA-46144 PRIMARY Little consensus on gun proposal WASHINGTON --- President Barack Obama's sweeping gun-law proposals and executive orders Wednesday brought a range of reactions from Georgia lawmakers in Washington, a reminder of the heated politics of guns. The executive actions ranged from streamlined sharing of mental health information to reviewing standards on gun safes. But many conservatives balked at any such action. "Americans have as much right to defend their families as President Obama does his," said Rep. Austin Scott, a Republican from Tifton. "I am concerned that some of the president's executive actions will ultimately impede their ability to do that. I do not understand what part of 'shall not be infringed' is so confusing to him." He was referring to the Second Amendment, which says "the right of the people to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed." The National Rifle Association also invoked Obama's family in an advertisement this week accusing the president of being an "elitist hypocrite" for surrounding his children with armed guards but not supporting the NRA's proposal to put armed guards in every school. DeKalb County Democratic Rep. Hank Johnson saw racism in the powerful gun lobby's attacks on the president. "I think they have invoked racist sensitivities," Johnson said. "They are certainly not free of those kinds of tactics to win their battle. They will gladly confuse people. They will gladly divide people. And so whatever it takes for them to accomplish their objective, which is no limits on firearm use and possession whatsoever."

characters: DeKalb County Democratic Rep. Hank Johnson

1851.text: NA-40037 PRIMARY COOL HEADS PREVAIL ON SCHOOL GUN PROPOSAL To be perfectly fair, you have to have a bit of sympathy for Hillsborough school superintendent MaryEllen Elia. There you are running the eighth-largest school system in the country - close to 200,000 students with 14,000 teachers and some armed-to-the-gills lunatic has just murdered 20 children and six school employees in Newtown, Conn. People expect you to do something. So it's perfectly understandable why Elia opted for Knee-jerk Reaction 101. Clearly we needed to put more people with more guns in more schools. But for all her good intentions, Elia didn't count on her bosses - the Hillsborough County School Board - rejecting the notion that more guns necessarily translates into more security. Elia didn't count on her plan being undermined by something so rarely found in large governmental bureaucracies - good, old-fashioned common sense. The board voted to reject Elia's "a-gun-in-every-pot" approach requiring the hiring of 130 new security personnel at a cost of $4.1 million in its first full year. In the end, the optics were always against Elia. By advocating turning the system's elementary schools into armed enclaves, the superintendent, perhaps unwittingly, appeared to be kowtowing to the harrumphing shills of the gun industry at the National Rifle Association, whose sole response to Newtown was to lock and load school administrators and teachers in the nation's schools. Body armor optional? You would have an easier time reasoning with a bobblehead doll than expecting any thoughtful approach to the nation's culture of gun violence from the NRA.

1854.text: NA-41119 PRIMARY 'OUTRAGE' Residents rip pols for beding to NRA THE NEWS HIT Newtown like another death in its extended family. The decision by Senate Democrats to abandon the assault weapon ban left residents of the still-mourning Connecticut town angry, disillusioned and disgusted with Washington. "This is an outrage," said Jean Haddon, 72, inside the Blue Colony Diner in Newtown. "This is horrible. . . . People here feel resentment, as if Congress wishes this issue will blow over. "It's not. We need better gun control." Fellow diner Harold Berglund, 88, said the nation's politicians should be ashamed by their refusal to challenge the powerful National Rifle Association lobby. "They are doing what the NRA wants," said Berglund, whose kids attended Sandy Hook Elementary School decades earlier. "They have too much money and lobbying power. We are going to have to get rid of the NRA." Berglund then ripped the nation's elected officials: "What Congress did was dumb. It makes me angry." The decision to drop the ban from proposed gun control legislation came barely three months after 20 first-graders and six staffers died in a hail of bullets from a popular brand of assault rifle. Dan and Jane Smith were shaking their heads Wednesday as they left the diner just a few miles from the school building-turned-crime scene. "What do regular people need automatic weapons for?" asked Dan, 61. "I can't believe the assault weapons are not part of that bill. Nobody needs to hunt deer with that many bullets. They should have let the public vote, not Congress."

1852.text: GUN-5287 PRIMARY LAW & ORDER 2 more are charged in "straw" gun purchases Two more people were charged Wednesday in the case of a federally licensed firearms dealer accused of falsifying applications for gun sales. Charged in a federal complaint were Joseph R. Hamm, 28, and Robert G. Englert, 57, both associates of Henry J. Cernicek, owner of Marshal Gun Shop in Dellwood, the U.S. attorney's office said. Cernicek, 71, was charged Tuesday. All are free on bond. The U.S. attorney's office alleges the defendants sold and delivered guns in "straw" deals. In such sales, the actual buyer uses someone else to complete a government form showing the straw buyer as the real buyer. GRANITE CITY Child starts fire; baby dies A 22-month-old Granite City girl died Wednesday morning in a house fire started by her brother, who was playing with matches or a lighter, authorities said. Cheyenne Brown, who lived in the 2400 block of Logan Street, died of smoke inhalation when a couch caught on fire about 7:30 a.m. She was in the living room with her 4-year-old brother and 2-month-old sister when the blaze started. Her mother, Nicole Martin, 27, was in another room, said Granite City Fire Capt. Tim Connolly. Martin told her son to get out of the house, then she grabbed the youngest child and ran out. Brown was left on the love seat next to the couch, where she died, Connolly said.

1856.text: GUN-10610 PRIMARY Your views Responsible owners are no threat to safety The commentary "Haven't we had enough?" (Jan. 12) by Judy Larsen criticized guns in our society. Aside from the usual anti-gun bantering, her first sentence said it all, "I don't like guns." The right to keep and bear arms is a fundamental right guaranteed to us by the United States of America. The author said, "I've never handled a gun. I was not raised in a family that hunted. I disagree with bumper stickers that say, 'Guns don't kill people. People kill people.' My response has always been people with guns kill people." While people with guns may and do kill people, so do people with knives, scissors, bats, hands, automobiles and a thousand other things. I have guns. My family has guns. I have hundreds (maybe thousands) of friends or acquaintances whose guns haven't killed anyone, and they never hurt anyone either. The author mentioned the National Rifle Association as the big bad gun lobby. The NRA has been and always will be about gun safety, sensible gun laws and strict enforcement of our gun laws. The major obstacle that gun owners face is the people who just don't like guns. Responsible gun owners want those who are not allowed to be in possession of a firearm to not have them. We want strict and swift punishment for those who choose to use any weapon in a manner that is illegal or dangerous.

1857.text: GUN-2756 PRIMARY Vote for assault-gun ban doesn't trigger backlash The newspapers are blasting him, and the NRA is promising revenge come election time, but U.S. Rep. Don Johnson had an easy ride Monday stumping in his 10th District for the first time since the House voted to ban assault weapons. Johnson breezed through a meeting of the Retired Military Officers Association in Lavonia and a tour of the Carrier Transicold plant in Athens without a single person mentioning Thursday's 216-214 House vote to ban 19 types of assault weapons. "I haven't heard anybody talking about it,'' said Dwight Bunn of Habersham, who attended the Lavonia meeting. "Personally, I don't think it's a big deal." Johnson's district director, Jane V. Kidd, said the 15 to 20 calls she received Monday morning were all positive. But University of Georgia political science professor Charles S. Bullock said Johnson, who angered some conservative constituents by voting for President Clinton's budget, probably will be getting some static soon. "He's clearly going to be asked about this," Bullock said. "It may not show up until the general election campaign, when he is going to have to debate or come across the Republican nominee." The clouds already are gathering in the largely rural, conservative district. His three Republican challengers - dentist Charles Norwood, former legislator Bill Jackson and businessman Ralph Hudgens - jumped on the news quickly. Hudgens, who lost to Johnson two years ago, sent out a news release saying the congressman "sold out the 10th District to the wishes of the liberal leadership in Washington again." Norwood was out with picketers at Johnson's Athens office the day before the House vote.

1858.text: GUN-5314 PRIMARY Barr acts to block gun suits; His bill would bar cities from seeking to hold weapons makers responsible for violence. Rep. Bob Barr (R-Ga.) plans to unveil legislation today that would block cities and states from suing gun makers, saying they are fast becoming victims of a rash of "unfair" lawsuits. Barr, a board member of the National Rifle Association, said he was prompted to introduce the legislation after watching Atlanta and four other cities file lawsuits seeking to hold firearms manufacturers accountable for gun violence. "These lawsuits are a frontal assault on lawful manufacturing of products in America," Barr said. "This is a shameless effort by certain cash-strapped cities to grab a bag of money." A spokesman for Atlanta Mayor Bill Campbell fired off a response Monday: "Atlanta is not a cash-strapped city, and Mayor Campbell did not file this suit for monetary gain," said Nick Gold. "The purpose of the suit was to get gun makers to make their products safer. We feel there is technology available today where that could be achieved," the mayor's spokesman said. Groups advocating gun control say Barr's bill would offer the industry special legal protection and that it is time for gun makers to be responsible for the damage caused by their product. "This amounts to political obstruction of justice," said Bob Walker, president of Handgun Control in Washington. "Barr is trying to use political means to stop lawsuits. If these cases are so frivolous, let the courts throw them out."

1859.text: GUN-92 PRIMARY Trial Opens for Rowan in Shooting of Trespasser Carl T. Rowan, the columnist and outspoken advocate of gun control, went on trial here today for possessing the unregistered pistol with which he shot a teen-age intruder at his home June 14. The case, which touched off a furor that continues in the capital, catapulted the gun control issue once more into the spotlight and revived debate about what is acceptable and moral in dealing with an intruder at one's home. At issue are the events surrounding a post-midnight swim by 18-year-old Benjamin Smith of Chevy Chase, Md., and several companions who came, uninvited, to the pool at Mr. Rowan's home in Northwest Washington. ''Pool hopping,'' as it is called, is a popular pastime among young people in the Washington area. Shot Called Attempted Warning The 63-year-old Mr. Rowan, who has said that his life had been threatened in the past, has also said that he was confronted by Mr. Smith. He said that he warned the youth that he had a gun, but that the youth lunged at him. He fired what he said was a warning shot, which struck Mr. Smith in the wrist. Jury selection in the trial was completed today in District of Columbia Superior Court. Testimony is to begin Tuesday in the trial, which is expected to last for two or three days. Some Washingtonians have argued that Mr. Rowan, acting out of real fear, was within his rights in trying to defend himself. Others have called the incident an ironic, powerful argument for the strict gun control for which the syndicated columnist and television commentator had long argued. It was lucky, they say, that Mr. Smith was not killed.

1863.text: GUN-11336 PRIMARY Why do you own a gun? Katherine Whitney / Ruger LCP .380 Katherine Whitney says she grew up in a "fantasy land" before moving to Boulder to attend law school. That's when she bought her first gun. Whitney, who is from Kansas, began attending criminal trials as part of her law studies at the University of Colorado and discovered another side to seemingly peace-loving Boulder. "I became more and more aware of the violence around me," she said. "I started thinking how I would defend myself. Hit someone in the nose or groin? How close would I have to get to use Mace or pepper spray?" "Look at me," the petite 27-year-old said. "I'm not that hard to subdue." She heard plenty of people tell her that violent crime in Boulder is "exceedingly rare." But after attending a murder trial of a student killed off-campus, she doesn't find that argument convincing. "I don't think telling the family of the murder victim that crime in Boulder is exceedingly rare is a lot of comfort," she said. So she applied for a concealed- carry permit and attended an intensive two-day course. Whitney -- who has been an advocate for concealed-carry permits on campus -- said those who apply must be age 21 and are carefully scrutinized before they are allowed their permits. "Someone who holds a concealed-carry permit is five times less likely to engage in criminal behavior," Whitney said. "We are the least likely of people on campus who will cause any problem."

1861.text: GUN-9540 PRIMARY Pilot arrested at New York airport for packing illegal gun A Northwest Airlines pilot scheduled to work a Detroit-bound flight was arrested this morning at LaGuardia Airport after a loaded handgun was found in his carry-on luggage, authorities said. Robert Donaldson, 43, was charged with three state counts of criminal possession of a weapon, and could face up to 15 years in prison, said the office of Queens District Attorney Richard A. Brown. According to authorities, Donaldson was walking through a terminal used for luggage screening of passengers and crew of both Northwest and Delta Airlines. Officials discovered the Taurus 9 mm semiautomatic handgun in his carry-on luggage. Donaldson was scheduled as co-pilot on Flight 1191 from New York to Detroit, departing at 6 a.m. He had a permit to carry the gun in Michigan. Brian Turmail, spokesman for the federal Transportation Security Administration, said screeners identified a handgun while examining a carry-on bag and referred the owner to local law enforcement. Police of the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey, the agency that runs the airport, took him to a secure area, Turmail said. No disciplinary action was immediately taken against Donaldson, who was in custody pending arraignment. ''We are cooperating with local and federal authorities,'' said Mary Beth Schubert, spokeswoman for Northwest. A spokesman for Northwest's pilot union, the Air Line Pilots Association, said the group was aware of the incident. '' We definitely don't want to speculate at this time,'' said Will Holman.

characters: Port Authority of New York and New Jersey

1860.text: NA-44266 PRIMARY Newtown parents mark six months since shooting It's been six months since a gunman killed 20 schoolchildren and six adults at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Conn. In hopes of keeping attention on the issue of gun control, several parents of the youngest victims, and children of the educators killed, returned to Washington this week to meet with lawmakers and President Obama. On Wednesday, a group of parents met with Sen. Joe Manchin III (D-W. Va.), lead sponsor on a Senate gun bill defeated in April: On Thursday, the family members held a news conference with lawmakers, including Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid (D-Nev.), who once again mentioned how his father killed himself with a gun, as he discussed his hope that the Senate will reconsider gun legislation: The families met Thursday at the White House with President Obama and Vice President Biden. On Friday several family members launched a bus tour in Newtown to raise awareness about gun crimes and to continue pressing lawmakers to take action.

characters: Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid

1862.text: GUN-300 PRIMARY Bar Association Report Urges Stiffer Gun Laws Stronger gun control laws and better programs to control juvenile criminals are needed to help reduce crime in the United States, according to a report released today by the American Bar Association. The report from the association's Task Force on Crime disagreed with the proposal of the Attorney General's Violent Crime Task Force, which said that more prisons were needed to reduce violence. ''There is no evidence to support the conclusion that sending more convicted offenders to prison for longer periods of time will deter others from committing crime,'' the bar group's report said. ''The Task Force does not advocate a strict ban on the ownership of all firearms or handguns,'' the report said. '' It does, however, endorse those laws which prohibit the carrying of handguns outside the home or business.'' The report has not yet been submitted to the association's policymaking House of Delegates for approval.   The Task Force said laws should be passed to prohibit juveniles, convicted felons, mental defectives, drug addicts and alcoholics from getting guns. It recommended severe penalties be imposed for possession or use of firearms in the commission of a crime.

characters: Attorney General's Violent Crime Task Force

1864.text: GUN-8879 PRIMARY Lautenberg Plays Offense With Attacks On Forrester After months of answering questions about Senator Robert G. Torricelli's ethics and a week fending off legal challenges to prevent Frank R. Lautenberg from replacing him on the ballot, Democrats finally found themselves in a position today to play offense in New Jersey's United States Senate race. Mr. Lautenberg clearly relished the opportunity, accusing his Republican opponent, Douglas R. Forrester, of using "character assassination" and legal obfuscation to distract voters from his conservative positions on issues like environmental protection, abortion rights and privatization of Social Security. And when Mr. Forrester appeared on a radio program this morning and said that no gun control law could have prevented the random sniper attacks that have killed six and wounded two others in the Washington area in the past week, Mr. Lautenberg wasted little time adding gun control to the list. By midafternoon, Mr. Lautenberg was standing on the steps of the New Jersey State House, surrounded by gun control advocates, telling reporters at a hastily arranged news conference that Mr. Forrester was a pawn of the National Rifle Association who was out of step with state voters on the issue of gun violence. "Doug Forrester doesn't understand New Jersey," Mr. Lautenberg said. "He doesn't understand the value we place on our families and our children." Mr. Forrester's campaign disputed that characterization and, continuing the aggressive tone he has adopted since Mr. Torricelli withdrew last week, hammered away at Mr. Lautenberg's record on military affairs and defense issues. As Mr. Forrester toured the state, meeting with several veterans' groups, he criticized Mr. Lautenberg for opposing several military pay increases and defense budget increases, and for voting against the gulf war resolution in 1991, which authorized the invasion of Iraq.

1867.text: GUN-5212 PRIMARY Critics Say N.R.A. Uses Safety Campaign to Lure Children Faced with a slump in gun sales and a raft of negative publicity, the National Rifle Association is trying to cultivate a new generation of gun owners by using some of the same strategies to hook children on guns that the tobacco industry has used to hook them on cigarettes, according to a new study by an anti-gun group. The rifle association strongly disputed the findings by the Violence Policy Center, saying its educational programs are designed to promote safety and have no connection to the gun industry. Marion Hammer, president of the National Rifle Association, said the study was so "ludicrous" that the organization, which has spent $10 million to teach gun safety to more than 10 million children, intended to sue the Violence Policy Center for defamation. The rifle association uses cartoon characters, including one named Eddie Eagle, to teach children to handle guns safely. In videos shown in thousands of school districts to children in preschool through sixth grade, the animated feathered mascot, in sing-song style, urges children not to touch a gun if they find one and to report it to an adult. A spokesman for the rifle association said that the chief difference between Eddie Eagle and the Joe Camel mascot who sold cigarettes is that Joe Camel smoked; Eddie Eagle does not pick up a weapon. But in a 144-page analysis to be released on Wednesday, the Violence Policy Center, a nonprofit organization based in Washington, asserts that the decade-old program puts a friendly face on a hazardous product that cannot legally be sold to children and is designed to draw children into the gun culture. The ultimate goal, the group claims, is to protect the financial future of the gun industry.

1865.text: GUN-20105 PRIMARY We don't need more gun-control laws I agree that the public screams for tougher gun control whenever crime sprees occur ("D.C. sniper case highlights NRA's duplicity on guns," Our View, Firearms regulation debate, Tuesday). There are two important questions that should be answered: * Have gun-control laws decreased crimes involving firearms? I have yet to see that. * How many crimes involve guns that are purchased illegally? Better surveillance of gun dealers would help monitor the purchase of firearms, and whether they are legal or illegal. We need to stop relying on the government to protect us from our neighbors. Taking a stand that uses common sense will go a long way toward improving our country's security as well as personal security. Michael Patrick Mullock Columbia, S.C. NRA's 'Wild West' logic The argument by NRA Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre, that his organization "fiercely supports" plans to get the "bad guys" without getting the "good guys," exudes the NRA's own 19th-century, Wild West logic ("Gun-control logic fails," Opposing View). What would be the harm if law-abiding citizens -- the good guys -- had to register their weapons, just as they undoubtedly register their cars and pets? Of course, criminals wouldn't comply; that's why they're labeled the bad guys. Gun registration and ballistic fingerprinting are simply safeguards to protect those citizens who are the good guys. Neil S. Friedman Brooklyn, N.Y. Gun critic misses mark

1866.text: GUN-11814 PRIMARY Carrie Nation at the New Yorker Here's Adam Gopnik's thought experiment on guns: We live, let's imagine, in a city where children are dying of a ravaging infection. The good news is that its cause is well understood and its cure, an antibiotic, easily at hand. The bad news is that our city council has been taken over by a faith-healing cult that will go to any lengths to keep the antibiotic from the kids. Some citizens would doubtless point out meekly that faith healing has an ancient history in our city, and we must regard the faith healers with respect-to do otherwise would show a lack of respect for their freedom to faith-heal. (The faith healers' proposition is that if there were a faith healer praying in every kindergarten the kids wouldn't get infections in the first place.) A few Tartuffes would see the children writhe and heave in pain and then wring their hands in self-congratulatory piety and wonder why a good God would send such a terrible affliction on the innocent-surely he must have a plan! Most of us-every sane person in the city, actually-would tell the faith healers to go to hell, put off worrying about the Problem of Evil till Friday or Saturday or Sunday, and do everything we could to get as much penicillin to the kids as quickly we could. We do live in such a city. Five thousand seven hundred and forty children and teens died from gunfire in the United States, just in 2008 and 2009. Twenty more, including Olivia Engel, who was seven, and Jesse Lewis, who was six, were killed just last week. Some reports say their bodies weren't shown to their grief-stricken parents to identify them; just their pictures. The overwhelming majority of those children would have been saved with effective gun control. We know that this is so, because, in societies that have effective gun control, children rarely, rarely, rarely die of gunshots. Let's worry tomorrow about the problem of Evil. Let's worry more about making sure that when the Problem of Evil appears in a first-grade classroom, it is armed with a penknife.

1871.text: NA-46157 PRIMARY Heavy issues going down to the wire Sit tight, Georgia. Today may be a bumpy ride as state lawmakers wrap up this year's legislative session with contentious issues still in play, including child welfare, medical marijuana and guns. The final day of the 2014 legislative session is also the last of the General Assembly's two-year cycle, meaning it's do-or-die time for dozens of bills. Anything that doesn't pass this year is officially dead and must restart the process in 2015. Lawmakers must finish before the clock strikes midnight. Many big issues could come down to the session's final hours, something leaders said early this year that they did not want. Among the biggest is House Bill 60, which would be one of the broadest expansions of gun rights in the state's recent history. The Senate made changes and sent the bill back to the House just before midnight Tuesday, seemingly aiming to provoke a conference committee in which three members from each chamber would try to negotiate a final compromise. "Is this not an example of the upper chamber leading?" asked Senate Majority Whip Cecil Staton, R-Macon. "Not name-calling ... but working together" to make the bill better? The bill, as changed, would allow guns in churches but make the provision an "opt in" --- meaning church leaders don't have to act unless they want to allow guns in their houses of worship. Original wording by the House would have lifted the state's ban of guns in churches unilaterally unless leaders vote to prohibit them on individual church properties.

1869.text: GUN-9781 PRIMARY HEARING IS SET ON COUNTY CHALLENGE TO GUN LAW * The lawuit says the concealed guns law is unconstitutional because it mandates that St. Louis County provide a new service without full state financing as required by Missouri's Constitution. Cole County Circuit Judge Thomas Brown III has scheduled a hearing for June 8 on St. Louis County's challenge to the law requiring county sheriffs to issue concealed weapons permits. The suit, filed by the county and County Executive Charlie A. Dooley, contends that the $100 fee that sheriffs may charge for processing permit applications is insufficient. The law requires that proceeds from the fees be used only for equipment and training. Because proceeds from the fee cannot be used for processing applications, St. Louis County's suit says the county will incur costs to its general revenue fund of $73 for each request submitted. According to the suit, the state has required the county to provide a new service without providing full state financing as required by Missouri's Constitution. The suit also says that the state's unconstitutional acts will require St. Louis County "to provide activities, services and expenditures of general revenue funds not justified according to law and an expenditure of taxpayer funds not justified according to law." The suit asks Brown to declare the concealed weapons law unconstitutional. This year, the state Supreme Court said the Legislature could enact a system that allows citizens to carry concealed weapons. But t he court's decision also said the Legislature's funding mechanism could force counties to carry out unfunded mandates. The Legislature failed to address the problem before adjourning May 14.

characters: Cole County Circuit Judge Thomas Brown III

1868.text: GUN-5243 PRIMARY EDITORIALS Conceal guns, not truth The debate over concealed weapons now convulsing the Colorado General Assembly is far less important than such truly vital issues as education and transportation. But what the gun debate lacks in substance, it has more than made up in bitterness. Two major gun bills have passed the House and are now in the Senate. Some of their provisions overlap, and they should either be merged into one bill or at least carefully reconciled to avoid conflicting provisions. HB 1316, by Rep. Doug Dean, R-Colorado Springs, and Sen. Ken Chlouber, R-Leadville, would establish a uniform state standard for issuing gun permits, replacing Colorado's present crazy quilt of local regulations ranging from the ultra-liberal, like El Paso County's, to the ultra-restrictive, like Denver's. Citizens could get a permit if they have passed an approved gun safety course, have no criminal record and can pass a background check designed to weed out substance abusers, the mentally ill and felons.   Gov. Bill Owens has said he will sign a bill if it incorporates these safeguards - and also keeps guns out of schools and school yards. That's a wise precaution, especially in view of the fact that current Colorado law allows holders of gun permits to carry them into school buildings. Dean already amended HB 1316 to ban guns in school buildings unless they are authorized by the principal for security purposes, and a similar safeguard on school grounds should be added.

1875.text: GUN-11805 PRIMARY ALBANY EYES WEAPON-BAN DEAL ALBANY - In the wake of the Connecticut shooting rampage, Gov. Cuomo and legislative leaders are quietly negotiating a package of tough new gun laws that they hope can be voted on within days, the Daily News has learned. At the center of the talks is a proposal to expand the state assault weapons ban to include all firearms with clips of more than seven bullets, sources said. That proposal would have outlawed at least one of the assault weapons that Adam Lanza used to kill 20 children and six adults at Sandy Hook Elementary School last Friday. Lawmakers have been told to be prepared to return to the Capitol as soon as Thursday if a deal can be pieced together, sources said. They also have been told to be available Friday or the week after Christmas - days when lawmakers are usually far from Albany. Cuomo - a potential Democratic presidential candidate - is believed to be eager to show progress on gun control while Washington faces weeks of partisan wrangling before enacting any new federal gun laws - if it can enact any reforms at all. A Cuomo source insisted Wednesday night that negotiators in Albany are still far apart on a final deal. The source said it's more likely that a full proposal will be unveiled in Cuomo's State of the State speech Jan. 9.

1872.text: GUN-6583 PRIMARY EDITORIAL Don't disarm Denver A perky cheerleading squad for House Bill 1289 - a bill that  wipes out county and municipal ordinances regulating guns - The  Denver Post is not. But we don't want to throw the entire idea  out, either. The bill that passed the House on a 38-26 vote and is now in  the Senate does create some uniformity that protects law-abiding  people. Granted, there is a widespread inconsistency in firearm  regulations among Colorado jurisdictions. This patchwork of laws  confuses people, who can unwittingly cross jurisdictional lines  without knowing they are breaking the law. To resolve this, the  proposed new law would preempt all municipal or county gun laws,  saying such regulation is exclusively a matter of statewide concern. This seems like a good idea on the surface. But while  it's one thing to curb Denver's confiscations of cars that pass  through the city bearing guns, it's quite another to tell  municipalities they can't regulate assault weapons, enact  safe-storage requirements and ban guns from their own school yards. Most of Denver's strict gun laws were in response to the 1993  'Summer of Violence.' Denver authorities say those laws were key  to helping police fight crime and regain control of their city's  streets. Because of Denver's gun laws, many guns designed to kill have  been confiscated - taken off Denver streets - as were many of the  street thugs who possessed them. Yes, you heard us right, street  thugs - not law-abiding hunters who just drove into the city after  shooting rabbits up in the hills.

1870.text: GUN-6966 PRIMARY NO PARTY LINE ON GUN-CONTROL VOTES If members of Congress can't figure out how to enact sensible, bipartisan gun control they can ask the Palm Beach County Commission for help. Mary McCarty, who has raised lots of money around the state for Republicans, voted last week for Florida's toughest local gun-control measure. Burt Aaronson, a Democrat to the core, was the ordinance's most vocal supporter. Along with Republican Tony Masilotti and Democrats Maude Ford Lee and Carol Roberts, they increased the county's waiting period to five from three days and applied the waiting period to rifles as well as handguns. As might be expected, the National Rifle Association has threatened to sue. The move toward gun control is not, as the NRA keeps insisting, a government attempt to disarm and repress its citizens. It's an attempt to find practical ways to reduce gun violence and accidental deaths. While county commissioners were being reasonable, some who spoke against the new law weren't. For example, one man declared that gun owners are an oppressed minority and quoted Martin Luther King to commissioners, saying, "A right delayed is a right denied." Waiting an extra two days to buy a gun is in the same league with basic voting rights? And where does a gun nut get off quoting Martin Luther King? Dr. King is America's most revered advocate of non-violence. And since a rifle shot killed Dr. King, it was strange indeed to hear someone cite the civil rights leader to oppose extending a waiting period to rifles. Commissioner Aaronson bluntly called the guy's pitch racist.

1873.text: GUN-9505 PRIMARY Safer gun laws There have been several letters of late touting the theory that more guns mean less crime. John Lott, the researcher supporting this position, has been strongly criticized lately for inventing a chat-room personality to defend his research. Questions have arisen about whether he even invented the research itself. There have been scholarly criticisms of his methodology for years. Whatever Lott's actions, other research has shown the opposite - more guns, more crime. Regions of the United States that have the highest rates of handgun ownership have higher suicide rates. The South and Mountain regions have the highest suicide rates. The states in these areas also have high rates of handgun ownership. These findings were reported by David Hemenway at Harvard University. The 13 states that have adopted right-to-carry concealed gun laws since 1992 have had sharp increases in murder and violent crime rates, according to research by Stanford University's John Donohue. We need sensible gun laws that stop gun trafficking. We need gun laws that keep guns out of the hands of criminals, children, and mentally incompetent people. When we have these laws, then we'll all be safer. CONNIE PADGETT Chapel Hill

1874.text: GUN-6257 PRIMARY The House vs. D.C. on Guns SIX WEEKS before the District launched its guns-for-cash exchange program to get illegal weapons off the streets, the House voted during debate on proposed national gun-control legislation to allow D.C. residents to carry concealed weapons and to keep handguns in their homes. The vote was another encroachment on the right of District residents to make their own decisions. The House went a giant step further, however, by interposing a shocking idea on a city sickened by gun violence. By 213 to 208, the House voted to override the District's 23-year-old gun ban and allow citizens to arm themselves in their homes as well as carry unloaded concealed guns through the streets. The provision, sponsored by Rep. Duncan Hunter (R-Calif.), was the answer to a criminal's prayer. No more prowling the streets to find a gun to buy or steal. Under the Hunter amendment, a crook in want of a gun need look no further than the closest home. The measure was also a clear threat to any D.C. household with a troubled teen. Fortunately, the bill carrying the Hunter amendment was scuttled, thus leaving the city's anti-gun law intact. Still, a House majority was recorded in favor of letting the District arm to the teeth. That makes it all the more important for Operation Gun Tip, the gun buy-back program launched by the D.C. police yesterday, to succeed. The goal is to purchase 1,000 handguns, assault weapons or other illegal firearms turned in at the city's seven police district headquarters. A guns-for-cash exchange program run earlier this month in Northeast was considered by police to be an unqualified success. An ecstatic D.C. Police Chief Charles Ramsey said of the 6th District program, which netted about 600 weapons, "Any time you get guns off the street, several people's lives have been saved."

1878.text: GUN-30145 PRIMARY Three years after concealed-carry law was passed, officials say: Streets are neither safer nor more dangerous No examples of shootouts on the street. No reports of permit holders bringing guns into restricted areas. It's permit renewal time for a lot of private citizens in Missouri who carry weapons, and the effect of the controversial concealed-carry law remains what it's been from the start: nil. Police say it hasn't increased crime, nor reduced it. People don't seem to be shooting others, or themselves, by accident in any greater numbers. Perhaps most telling is that the St. Louis County Police Department has no record of ever responding to a single call that someone had carried a gun into one of the many restricted locations. City police keep no record of that but didn't remember any. "When they were debating this, one side was saying it was going to reduce crime and another was saying it was going to cause gunfights in the streets," Franklin County Sheriff Gary Toelke said. "I really haven't seen either. It's really a nonissue right now. You're not having fights in the streets, but it's not saving the world either." His office received 853 initial applications for the three-year permits and rejected about 20 based on the required criminal background check. Missouri's first permits were issued in 2004, although because of litigation, they were not available everywhere that soon. St. Louis and St. Louis County did not begin issuing them until 2005. Toelke's department has received about 220 renewal applications with more coming in. Two of them have been rejected so far.

1877.text: GUN-30969 PRIMARY Among the Scattershot Arguments in the D.C. Gun Ban Case, Which Ones Will Hit the Mark? Grammar, history, intent, the District's unique role in the Constitution and crime in contemporary society could all play major parts in today's historic U.S. Supreme Court session on Washington's handgun ban. You might need a scorecard to keep tabs on the 75 minutes of argument. First question: Will the lawyers and justices get hung up on the grammar question that has divided gun rights supporters and opponents for decades -- what is the true meaning of the preamble to the Second Amendment, "A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State"? If this becomes a linguistics seminar, the focus will be on what those words imply about whether the right to keep and bear arms is an individual or collective right. That debate has raged since before the 1939 Supreme Court decision in which the justices said the amendment is really about ensuring that armed civilian militias be available to defend the people. Much may turn on whether the justices get into dueling ideas about the amendment's roots in early American history. Both sides emphasize that the amendment's meaning lies in how it came to be, but they choose different periods to make their case. Gun advocates lean on the Colonial period, offering a stirring, profoundly radical story about how the aggrieved American colonists created "extralegal" militias and fought hard against the British authorities' efforts to suppress those armed civilians. Given that these militias were "critical" to the success of the Revolution, the original plaintiffs in the case say it's essential that Americans today have the right to take up arms once more should their government fall into tyrannical ways.

1876.text: NA-41121 PRIMARY Paging all cowards on the Hill CONFRONTED by a Daily News front page calling them "Gutless Cowards" for chickening out on an assault weapons ban, Capitol Hill lawmakers shot back with bipartisan defensiveness. Sen. Dick Durbin of Illinois, the No. 2 Democrat in the Senate, stuck up for himself and his colleagues as he was shown The News' Thursday edition. Not only are they not cowards, he said, "I think there's been a lot of courage shown on gun safety and I hope more courage is shown when [gun control legislation] comes to the floor," said Durbin. He was referring to Democrats from rural gun-loving states like Sen. Joe Manchin (D-W.Va.) who have gone out on a limb by supporting an assault weapons ban. Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid has taken heat for stripping a ban on assault weapons and high capacity magazines from the gun bill that's going to the full Senate next month because the measure doesn't have enough support. The assault weapons ban instead will be offered as an amendment that is expected to fail. "It's not in the bill but there's going to be an effort to add it," said Sen. Carl Levin (D-Mich.), when shown The News in the Capitol building. On the Republican side of the aisle, lawmakers approached by the Daily News stared icily at a reporter and ducked into a senators-only elevator at the Capitol building. Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-Ky.) didn't even break stride or glance at the headline as a reporter thrust it in his face.

1879.text: GUN-2228 PRIMARY New York Republicans Offer Two-Fisted Gun-Violence Answer To the Editor: "Bipartisan Pistol Denial" (editorial, Jan. 23), on growing handgun violence, is right on the mark. You note that handguns were responsible for an overwhelming majority of the murders committed in New York State in 1992 (1,653 out of 2,394 homicides). But so-called assault weapons were involved in fewer than 20 homicides, according to statistics compiled by Gov. Mario M. Cuomo's criminal justice agencies. New Yorkers kill one another with handguns more often than do those in any other major state. And virtually all of these weapons were brought in illegally, violating the country's toughest handgun-control laws. Yet the Governor and Assembly Democrats make an assault-weapons ban the symbolic centerpiece of their new tough-on-crime stance. By contrast, the sweeping, anticrime plan unveiled by State Senate Republicans contains a two-fisted solution to gun violence that includes tougher penalties for the use of any firearm in a crime and backs stronger Federal laws to curb availability of illegal weapons. Even the Governor concedes that the best way to battle gun violence is through better Federal regulation. In his annual message to the Legislature, he notes that, "Since 90 percent of the illegal weapons seized in New York originate in other states, effective gun control depends on comprehensive Federal action." The Governor added: "Without tighter restrictions elsewhere, it is difficult to control the flow of illegal guns into New York."

1883.text: GUN-2354 PRIMARY Retailers resent being target of control efforts The Kittery Trading Post is the Baskin-Robbins of guns. Only it has a lot more than 31 flavors. Beneath signs saying "Treat every gun as if it were loaded" and "Never climb a tree or fence with a loaded firearm," you have your choice of: -- 472 kinds of rifles. -- 289 shotguns. -- 459 handguns. -- 861 grades of ammunition. Located at the southern tip of Maine, the Trading Post draws customers from four states. It caters to hunters; a sign on the door says, "Deer Hides Taken in Trade, Nov. 1 - Dec. 31." If you want an Uzi, go elsewhere. "We don't go into the paramilitary," co-owner Gary Adams says. "That's not our type of gun shop." Adams' father bought the Trading Post in 1938, when it wasn't much more than a shed. Today it has 43,000 square feet of selling space, more than 300 employees and annual revenue of $ 25 million. In many respects, business couldn't be better for gun retailers. The value of guns and ammunition bought in the USA from manufacturers here and abroad nearly doubled to $ 7 billion in 1992 from $ 3.7 billion in 1987 and is estimated at $ 9 billion this year. National sales figures at the retail level don't exist. Gun sellers are secretive, and the federal government is prohibited by the Gun Control Act of 1968 from keeping records on gun sales. Individual retailers say 1993 was a good year, though. At Kittery, gun sales were up 17% over 1992.

1881.text: GUN-1104 PRIMARY Brady Handgun Bill Sent to House Floor for Vote; Panel Approves 7-Day Waiting Period, 23 to 11; NRA-Backed Substitute Defeated The House Judiciary Committee yesterday approved a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases, clearing the way for a House floor showdown on the issue in two weeks. The 23 to 11 vote came amid further signs that the waiting period measure, known as the "Brady bill" after former White House press secretary James S. Brady, who was crippled in a 1981 assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan, was gaining momentum in the House. Reps. Michael A. Andrews (D-Tex.) and Butler Derrick (D-S.C.), longtime supporters of the National Rifle Association (NRA), which opposes the Brady bill, have announced they were switching their positions and would vote for the measure because of their concerns about gun-related violence. "Just like every American, I pick up the papers every morning and see the mayhem," said Derrick, who became the first member of the South Carolina delegation to announce support. "I don't know how you can argue with a check to find out if somebody has a criminal record or is mentally unstable before you sell them a handgun." The Brady bill has engendered some of the most emotional debate and is one of the most fiercely lobbied measures of this congressional session. Backers of the bill acknowledged yesterday that, despite the recent switches and the endorsement last month by Reagan, nose counts for the House show an extremely close vote.

1880.text: GUN-4867 PRIMARY Shots Reopen but Are Unlikely to Reshape Gun Control Debate As Rep. Randy "Duke" Cunningham (R-Calif.) was walking across the Capitol plaza Friday in the aftermath of the shooting deaths of two Capitol police officers inside the building, a reporter asked whether the deaths would influence the congressional debate on gun control. "After an incident like this, I'm sure there's going to be some reflection," said Cunningham, an opponent of gun control laws. He agreed there were "far too many guns on the streets," but said, "that doesn't mean we should take guns out of the hands of law-abiding citizens." At about the same time, gun control advocate Sen. Robert G. Torricelli (D-N.J.) was circulating a news release saying "this tragedy shows once again the need for responsible gun control measures," and calling on those "who regularly obstruct our efforts" to "finally agree." Each time the nation suffers a high-profile shooting incident -- from the attempted assassination of President Ronald Reagan in 1981 and the schoolyard deaths in recent months to Friday's killings -- the debate over gun control is joined anew. And each time, with a few rare exceptions, consensus eludes the lawmakers. The Friday incident occurred just days after the Senate handily defeated two measures intended to make it more difficult for children to obtain weapons. One, sponsored by Sens. Barbara Boxer (D-Calif.) and Herb Kohl (D-Wis.), would have required new guns to have trigger-lock mechanisms. That amendment to the Commerce, State, Justice appropriations bill garnered 39 supporters.

1884.text: NA-44480 PRIMARY High court bolsters gun ban tied to cases of domestic violence The Supreme Court on Wednesday unanimously bolstered the federal law that bars those convicted of domestic violence from possessing a firearm. The court gave a wide interpretation of the law's requirement of "physical force." The federal government said that was important because in some states, misdemeanor domestic-violence laws are not specific about the force required. The case was brought by James Alvin Castleman, who was charged with selling firearms on the black market. He was charged with violating the federal firearms law because he had pleaded guilty in Tennessee to misdemeanor domestic violence, meaning he had "intentionally or knowingly caused bodily injury" to the mother of his child. Because the federal law requires the "use or attempted use of physical force" and Tennessee's misdemeanor domestic-violence law does not, a district judge agreed with Castleman that the federal charges should be dismissed. The judge said the federal law requires "violent contact with the victim." An appeals court agreed that violent physical force was necessary to prosecute under the federal law. But the court, in an opinion written by Justice Sonia Sotomayor, said that was too literal a definition. "Minor uses of force may not constitute 'violence' in the generic sense," Sotomayor wrote. But even squeezing someone's arm to the point of causing bruises "is easy to describe as 'domestic violence,' when the accumulation of such acts over time can subject one intimate partner to the other's control."

1882.text: GUN-5948 PRIMARY IS YOUR KID WORTH $ 11? GUN CONTROL THE question for all of us is whether Dominic Johnson's life was worth $ 11. Dominic, 6, was killed Tuesday when he found a .38 caliber pistol hidden inside a radio in his grandmother's upstairs bedroom and accidentally shot himself in the head. If the owner of the gun had put an $ 11 trigger lock on that weapon, Dominic would be in kindergarten class this morning. His aunt and grandmother, who were downstairs when he shot himself, wouldn't today be reliving that horrible moment. Every three days a Missouri child or youth is killed by a gun, according to the Children's Defense Fund. In Illinois, where Dominic lived, a child is killed nearly every day by a gun. Trigger locks could change those numbers dramatically. Trigger locks could have stopped Thomas Solomon, who used weapons he took from his stepfather's gun cabinet to go on a shooting spree at Heritage High School in Conyers, Ga. They might have stopped Mitchell Johnson, 13, and Drew Golden, 11, who used weapons stolen from the home of Golden's grandfather to kill four girls and a teacher at their school in Jonesboro, Ark. At the very least, trigger locks can save the lives of many curious children who accidentally shoot themselves or other children every year. The St. Louis Police Department thinks so, too. That's why last year, at the request of Officers Richard Simpler, director of the St. Louis Police Academy, and Bill Burnett, the department Armorer, the St. Louis Police Department bought 1,800 trigger locks to be used by every officer i n the department. They did so even though every police officer receives 100 hours of training in the use and safety of their weapons. Because the simple truth is that, "no matter what you do, if kids are determined, they will find that weapon," Officer Simpler says.

1885.text: GUN-4032 PRIMARY DEMOCRATS' OPENING SALVO TARGETS GUNS Democrats opened their 42nd national convention Monday with an anti-gun message designed to bolster President Clinton's standing with suburban swing voters and to erase the kinder, gentler image the Republicans projected at their convention. The convention, as tightly scripted for television as the GOP gathering in San Diego two weeks ago, also sought to appeal to the hearts of these critical voters with a poignant prime-time speech by Christopher Reeve, the wheelchair-confined actor who achieved stardom as Superman. The president, wending slowly toward Chicago across the vote-rich Midwest aboard a special campaign train, stopped at a police academy in Columbus, Ohio, to spread the day's unified anti-gun message. Clinton proposed a new law that would extend federal handgun control to people convicted of misdemeanor spouse abuse, a category that could include tens of thousands of persons. Although Clinton and his entourage are not scheduled to arrive in Chicago until Wednesday, the president's speech was timed to magnify the impact of one of the convention's main events Monday: an appearance by gun-control crusader Sarah Brady and her husband, James, who was grievously wounded in the 1981 assassination attempt on then-President Reagan, and after whom the existing federal handgun control law - the Brady bill - is named. "Those who threaten the safety of others do not deserve our trust," Clinton said. " ... If you're stalking or harassing women or children, you shouldn't have a gun. And if you commit an act of violence against your spouse or your child, you shouldn't have a gun."

1890.text: GUN-11778 PRIMARY In Phila. and elsewhere, gun sales boom For Fred Delia, a shop owner in Northeast Philadelphia, business has been brisk the last few days, and it has nothing to do with Christmas. Delia runs a namesake store on Torresdale Avenue in Wissinoming. It's a gun shop. "The traffic has been huge," Delia said Thursday afternoon. "I can't stop to get a breath." Gun stores in Philadelphia, the region, and nationally are reporting a sharp increase in weapons sales as a result of the mass killings at the elementary school in Newtown, Conn., a week ago. The buyers are telling store owners, who say sales have shot up 25 percent or more in the past week, that they are rushing to purchase guns before Congress can enact new restrictions on assault weapons. Delia said he has sold about 30 AR-15 military-style weapons in the past week to customers who are concerned that Congress will ban them. An assault-style rifle was used in the slaying of 20 children aged 6 and 7, and six adult staffers at the Sandy Hook Elementary School. "They are getting scared as far as the Obama administration banning guns," Delia explained. With President Obama vowing to seek new restrictions on the sales of military-style rifles, purchases of those and other types of guns has been fevered. This kind of rush to buy arms that might be banned has happened in the wake of other national tragedies.

1886.text: NA-42802 PRIMARY Guns, Abortion and Mistrust Before his closed-door meeting with gun-control advocates on Wednesday, Vice President Joe Biden chatted briefly with reporters. What he told them incensed right-wing news sources. The Drudge Report splashed headshots of Hitler and Stalin.   The Examiner  ran a story claiming that President Obama would "act like a dictator" by taking legally owned firearms from "nonviolent Americans" and that he would "ban guns." What Mr. Biden actually told reporters was: "The president is going to act. There are executive orders, executive action that can be taken. We haven't decided what that is yet, but we're compiling it all." He made no mention of bans, or confiscation, which the White House-besides-could not accomplish through executive order, even if it wanted to. Operating without Congressional approval, President Obama could appoint a new ATF director or require federal agencies to report mental health records, but he could not enact an assault weapons ban, let alone an outright ban. The distance between what Mr. Biden said and what The Examiner reported gets at why it's so difficult to conduct a national conversation on the regulation of firearms.
