If the gun-control camp mentions restrictions the anti-gun-control camp hears bans. If the former mentions a ban on certain kinds of guns, the latter hears all guns, plus confiscation. Many gun-rights activists, moreover, seem to suspect that the other side argues in bad faith. In public, gun-control advocates may sound reasonable, proposing only limited regulations, but what they really want is to repeal the Second Amendment, or to overturn Heller, and force the complete disarmament of the civilian population. First they'll come for our Bushmasters, then they'll come for our hunting rifles.

1888.text: GUN-549 PRIMARY FIREARM RESTRICTIONS HAVE PROVED TOTALLY INEFFECTIVE Brian Crowley gives the impression that the only thing standing between effective, lifesaving legislation and what we now have is the National Rifle Association. Much research has been done on how effective firearm restriction laws really are. In 1975, California increased the waiting period for handgun purchases from five to 15 days. According to the information provided by the California Bureau of Criminal Statistics, the homicide rate rose from 9.3 per 100,000 in 1974 to 14.3 per 100,000 in 1980. The homicide rate has not dropped below the 1974 level since. In fact, the highly touted handgun ban enacted by Morton Grove, Ill., for largely symbolic reasons has produced some even more symbolic results: Morton Grove has had more homicides in the years following the ban than the years preceding it, and the crime rate rose 15.7 percent in 1988. By comparison, the crime rate rose a mere 3 percent in 1988 for all of suburban Cook County. The Department of Justice was ordered to find a means of screening firearms purchasers. It said that there is no means to do this since the states don't keep accurate enough records. Many states only record the arrest, not the conviction. That would mean a person who might be innocent of a crime would not be able to have a firearm. The Department of Justice said it would cost more than $1 billion to set the system up and that since so few criminals purchase firearms legally, it would be very cost-ineffective.

1887.text: GUN-31012 PRIMARY Gun-Ban Foes Seek Review by High Court; Both Sides Want Rulings on D.C. Law Reconciled The D.C. residents who successfully challenged the District's handgun ban urged the U.S. Supreme Court yesterday to review the case, saying it presents a "unique opportunity" for the court to recognize that the Second Amendment guarantees an individual's right to "keep and bear arms." It is rare for the winning side to agree with the losing side that its legal victory should be reviewed, but the residents and their attorneys have a greater goal: a ruling by the justices that would allow courts across the country to strike gun prohibitions and gun control measures that they think infringe on an individual's rights. "Unfortunately, a majority of federal circuit courts of appeal fail to acknowledge the requisite first element of Second Amendment analysis: the existence of an individual right," wrote the residents' attorneys, led by Alan Gura. "Clarifying that the Second Amendment secures individual rights would enable courts to properly analyze firearms regulations, even if the unusual facts of this case are unlikely to be repeated." The city's law is one of the strictest in the country -- a virtual ban on private handgun ownership and a requirement that rifles and shotguns be stored unloaded and disassembled or outfitted with a trigger lock. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit ruled 2 to 1 in the spring that the law was unconstitutional. That panel of judges found that the Second Amendment protected an individual's right to bear arms. Most federal appeals courts have said the amendment protects only a collective right, to maintain a militia.

characters: U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit

1889.text: GUN-2393 PRIMARY Court Upholds U.S. Gun Law A Federal law banning guns within 1,000 feet of schools has been upheld by a Federal appeals court but may be headed for the United States Supreme Court. Ruling in the case of a teen-ager arrested with two rifles in a school parking lot, the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit in San Francisco on Tuesday upheld the 1990 law providing up to five years in prison for possessing a gun near a school. But three months ago, the New Orleans-based United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit declared the same law unconstitutional, ruling that Congress had failed to justify a Federal law on an issue traditionally left to the states. The Supreme Court often steps in to resolve such conflicts between apellate courts. In Tuesday's 3-to-0 ruling, Judge Arthur Alarcon said that Congress, which has the authority to regulate interstate commerce, was entitled to conclude that "the violence created through the possession of firearms adversely affects the national economy." The appeals court in New Orleans had said Congress failed to explain how the power to regulate interstate commerce extended to possession of a gun near a school. The ruling came in the case of Ray Harold Edwards 3d, 19, who was arrested in 1991 after the police searched his car.

1893.text: GUN-4757 PRIMARY FLAWS STYMIE ENFORCEMENT OF GUN LAW It sounds like a great idea: pass a federal law making it illegal for anyone ever convicted of domestic violence - even if the crime is a misdemeanor, such as slapping one's wife - to possess a firearm. But 2 1/2 months after the law took effect, problems are beginning to surface: The law failed to stop an Iowa man from shooting his wife in the chest last month. William Smith, who on Wednesday became the first man in the country to be charged under the new law, had been convicted two years ago on the misdemeanor charge of pushing his wife, Lauralee, grabbing her neck and throwing her to the ground. Nevertheless, he obtained a permit to buy a handgun before the law went into effect and bought the gun Nov. 15, two days before shooting his wife. The application form with his purchase didn't include the question: "Have you ever been convicted of a misdemeanor sexual assault?" Even if the question had been on the form, authorities acknowledge, there's no guarantee Smith would have answered truthfully. In Minnesota, the law is causing concern among the state's many hunters who wonder why a conviction of misdemeanor domestic assault 20 years ago should affect their right to hunt today. Potentially dozens of deer hunters with assault convictions who bagged bucks during deer-hunting season last month were breaking the law.

1892.text: GUN-1825 PRIMARY Senate Limits Anti-Crime Debate The Senate voted last night to limit debate on its omnibus anti-crime bill, clearing the way for approval of the measure after senators deal with proposals from Sen. Jesse Helms (R-N.C.) involving penalties for child pornography and transmission of AIDS by medical personnel. Cloture was invoked on the second try, by a vote of 71 to 27, after approval of amendments aimed at addressing complaints of many of the senators who opposed cloture on the first try -- when it fell short by four votes. Among the amendments approved were an increase in penalties for drug use in prison and expansion of wire-fraud stat-utes to make it easier to use them to convict public officials of wrongdoing. Still voting against cloture were senators opposed to gun-control provisions in the bill, including a five-day waiting period for handgun purchases and curbs on the sale of semiautomatic assault weapons. But, with a post-cloture agreement last night to limit further debate to no more than 10 hours, it appeared that final passage of the bill might come as early as today, ending debate that began three weeks ago. The final hurdle for the bill came in the form of two controversial proposals from Helms, who employed a parliamentary device to force votes on the politically sensitive issues. If Helms follows through on his threats, votes will occur today on his proposal to make it a federal crime, punishable by a minimum 10-year sentence, for a doctor, dentist, nurse or other health care worker who has AIDS and knows it to perform invasive medical procedures without informing the patient. Helms's other proposal would instruct the U.S. sentencing commission to increase penalties for child pornography.

1894.text: GUN-9575 PRIMARY Illinois high court throws out two suits against gun-makers The Illinois Supreme Court yesterday threw out two lawsuits that accused gun-makers of knowingly letting weapons fall into the hands of gang members and other criminals. The court ruled that the manufacturers could not legally be blamed for street violence. Both rulings were unanimous, but five of the seven justices were so disturbed by allegations raised in the case that they wrote a separate opinion urging the legislature to create tougher gun regulations. The lawsuits, filed by Chicago officials and victims of shootings, argued that the defendants had created a public nuisance by pouring guns into the Chicago area that are used to kill. "The mere fact that defendants' conduct in their plants, offices and stores puts guns into the stream of commerce does not state a claim for public nuisance," the court said. "It is the presence and use of the guns within the city of Chicago that constitutes the alleged nuisance." The city sought $433 million, the amount it says it paid in law enforcement and emergency medical treatment for gun violence over four years. The families sought unspecified damages. Similar lawsuits had been filed around the country. An earlier wave of product-liability lawsuits, alleging that guns are unreasonably dangerous, failed. Chicago's lawsuit cited a sting in which undercover officers bought guns at suburban shops even after plainly telling the sellers that they were gang members, were buying the guns for gang members, or were taking the guns to Chicago, where handguns are banned.

1891.text: GUN-31404 PRIMARY NRA suits push cities to drop gun bans; Some locales resist after federal ruling CHICAGO -- Some communities have moved quickly to repeal handgun bans since the U.S. Supreme Court in June established an individual right to gun ownership, while others plan to defend their bans in court battles with the National Rifle Association. The NRA filed at least five lawsuits after a 5-4 Supreme Court ruling struck down a handgun ban in Washington, D.C., and said for the first time that the Constitution's Second Amendment gives individuals the right to keep guns at home for self-defense. Washington is a federal district, so the pending cases will determine whether the Supreme Court decision also applies to state and local governments, says Wayne LaPierre, the NRA's executive vice president. The ruling was "the opening salvo of a step-by-step process to bring this freedom to citizens throughout the country," he says, and the NRA will "continue to force this issue into the courts." Chicago, Oak Park and Evanston, Ill., and the San Francisco Housing Authority are being sued by the NRA. Dennis Henigan, vice president of law and policy at the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence, says the Supreme Court also left open questions about what restrictions can be placed on gun ownership. "There is going to be a lot of litigation on this," he says. Most other handgun bans are in Illinois because it's one of just three states where state laws don't automatically pre-empt local ordinances. The others are Hawaii and Nebraska.

1897.text: GUN-3585 PRIMARY BOND WANTS TO END GUN-VIOLENCE STUDY; 9 SENATORS SAY RESEARCH PROJECT IS POLITICAL Sen. Christopher S. Bond, R-Mo., is one of nine senators pressing to cut a federal research project on gun violence, which they called a "political agenda against gun ownership." A letter Oct. 19 from Bond and the other senators - eight Republicans and one Democrat - to Sen. Arlen Specter, R-Pa., say they are sympathetic to a move to eliminate funding for the National Center for Injury Prevention and Control. Specter heads an appropriations committee that recommends funding for the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, of which the injury center is a part. The injury center is a favorite target of the National Rifle Association, which alleges that some of the agency's $ 2 million in annual federal grant money has paid for literature urging people to support gun control. "Taxpayer dollars are supporting unadulterated anti-gun advocacy," Tanya Metaksa, the NRA's chief lobbyist, said Friday. "We don't think it's an appropriate use." The letter says the senators are "absolutely committed to getting the politics out" of the injury center's work. It cites as evidence studies that are designed and used to "promote a campaign to reduce lawful firearms ownership in America." The letter quotes the injury center's director, Dr. Mark Rosenberg, as saying that he "envisions a long-term campaign, similar to tobacco use and auto safety, to convince Americans that guns are, first and foremost, a public-health menace."

characters: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

1899.text: GUN-5981 PRIMARY LIMIT ONE AMENDMENT, LIMIT THEM ALL Under the incorrect premise that the Founding Fathers would not have enacted a right to bear arms if they had foreseen modern firearm technology, the writer of the May 18 letter ''Would Second Amendment pass today?'' misses key points. If this incorrect premise were applied equally (and as courts have ruled, all precepts in the Bill of Rights are to be applied equally), then the First Amendment would protect only the quill, ink and parchment paper - not computers, cell phones, the Internet, pornography, profane language disguised as comedic routines and certainly not the gratuitous violence that passes for entertainment in movies and videos. The writer seems to believe only in the rights he agrees with. The Founding Fathers knew full well the overwhelming power that government and its agents could exert if a document guaranteeing rights was not issued. , a document of the debates on the issues on the Constitution and Bill of Rights, itthat would be clear. The Bill of Rights - a complete package, not just what you choose to accept - was created specifically because of the belief that rights were granted in nature and that government must guarantee these rights. James A. Settembre Lake Worth

1898.text: GUN-3653 PRIMARY Assembly bullish on bearing arms RALEIGH -- Responding to voter concerns about public safety and the need to crack down on criminals, state lawmakers held a special legislative session last year to pass tougher laws and spend more money on building prisons. This year, the General Assembly decided that what voters really needed to feel safe were guns in their pockets. Beginning Dec. 1, N.C. residents may apply for permits to carry concealed handguns. A gun owner must pass a background check and a training course that spells out where and when a potential crime victim legally may shoot to kill before a sheriff issues a permit. Those wanting concealed-carry permits also must pass criminal background checks and sign affidavits stating they have no history of mental problems or substance abuse. The permit will allow a gun owner to carry a pistol under a car seat, in a pocket or strapped into a holster. Dozens of police chiefs warned that the law will increase the number of shootings. Several lawmakers -- including Rep. Arlene Pulley, R-Wake, and Rep. Michael Decker, R-Forsyth -- admitted that they already carried guns with them, even though they knew they were breaking the law. The concealed weapons bill was the top priority of the National Rifle Association, which pushed four issues this year. Another priority -- overriding local gun laws with statewide regulations -- will wait until next year, said Jeff Freeman, the chief NRA lobbyist in North Carolina.

1896.text: GUN-1179 PRIMARY Reagan gives Brady bill backers a boost Former president Ronald Reagan's support for a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases gives gun control advocates a big boost as the countdown to a full House vote begins. ''The Brady Bill would have passed this year even without President Reagan's endorsement,'' said Rep. Edward Feighan, D-Ohio, author of the bill. '' With it, our victory is assured.'' In 1988, the bill was defeated in the House by 36 votes. Last year, House Speaker Thomas Foley blocked the bill from the floor, and a second vote. Supporters said this year they have Foley's assurances of a floor vote, which they expect before Memorial Day. A Senate fight still looms. '' Right now it's an uphill battle. After this endorsement, that could change,'' said Sen. Paul Simon, D-Ill. Gun control opponents, meanwhile, minimized the impact of Reagan's endorsement. ''It seems he doesn't understand how the bill will operate,'' said Larry Pratt, executive director of the Gun Owners of America. Although the bill calls for a seven-day waiting period: - It does not apply to handgun purchases in states that impose their own waiting periods of at least seven days. - Local police could waive the seven-day wait in special cases. Jim Spurling, an aide to Rep. Harold Volkmer, D-Mo., a leading opponent of the bill, said the endorsement won't sway many votes. The bill's status is ''too close to call,'' he said.

1900.text: GUN-11457 PRIMARY Philadelphia man with Florida gun permit convicted in killing A Philadelphia man who received a Florida permit to carry a gun after Philadelphia police stripped him of his Pennsylvania gun permit was sentenced to prison Thursday in the shooting death of a teen in the city's Olney section. Marqus Hill, 29, was sentenced by Common Pleas Court Judge Glenn B. Bronson to eight to 20 years in prison in the September 2010 killing of 18-year-old Irving Santana. Santana was allegedly one of three teens who broke into Hill's car in the 300 block of East Gale Street. He was shot 13 times. Hill, of Hunting Park, who was convicted of third-degree murder and possession of an instrument of crime, had drawn criticism from police and others for using a loophole that allowed him to carry a gun after police had stripped him of his Pennsylvania permit to carry a firearm. Hill, who had a previous charge of attempted murder dismissed, was denied a permit to carry a gun in Philadelphia in 2008. The case highlighted what police and others said was a loophole that allows nonresidents of three states - Florida, Utah, and New Hampshire - to get gun permits even if their home states have denied or revoked permits for them. Hill lost his Philadelphia-issued permit in 2005 after what authorities described as "significant contact" with police. He appealed the revocation of his gun permit in 2008, but it was upheld.

1895.text: GUN-10368 PRIMARY Church gun ban, challenge still alive; Judge asks sides for further arguments. ; Gun rights advocates say churches should get say on guns on premises. Gun rights advocates have gained ground in their effort to permit Georgians to carry guns in more and more public places, and a federal judge on Monday kept alive their effort to let people carry firearms into churches. U.S. District Judge C. Ashley Royal declined Monday to grant a preliminary injunction to allow guns in houses of worship, but he set deadlines for each side to submit the written arguments on which he will base his final decision. Royal said in Monday's hearing that the "places of worship" reference in the law being challenged is vague. He asked the parties to further define whether that description pertained to the sanctuary, classrooms and administrative office. "A very plausible argument can be made ... [that] this statute deprived this man of being able to protect himself at work," the judge said. The lawsuit was filed against the state in July in Upson County Superior Court on behalf of the Rev. Jonathan Wilkins, head of the Baptist Tabernacle of Thomaston. He wants to arm himself while working alone at the church and during services "for the protection of his flock, his family and himself," according to the lawsuit. The case has since been moved to the U.S. District Court in nearby Macon. Wilkins partnered with gun-rights group GeorgiaCarry.org to challenge a law the state Legislature passed this year that revised the prohibition of guns in public places.

characters: U.S. District Judge C. Ashley Royal

1901.text: GUN-877 PRIMARY STOCKTON CITY COUNCIL BANS ASSAULT WEAPONS STOCKTON, Calif. (AP) - Councilmen in the city where five children were shot to death and 30 people were wounded in a schoolyard Jan. 17 have unanimously adopted an ordinance outlawing assault weapons such as the AK-47 used in the attack. '' No one on this council is naive enough to believe that the passage of this ordinance could have prevented the tragedy, but it sends a strong message to the kooks of this land, and more importantly a strong message to the Legislature, that we want to get tough,'' Vice Mayor Ron Coale said.   Gun owners packed the City Council meeting Monday to protest that the ordinance was unconstitutional and could lead to the elimination of all weapons.   The ordinance makes possession or sale of an assault weapon a misdemeanor punishable by up to six months in jail and a $500 fine.

1902.text: GUN-2203 PRIMARY Gov. Wilder's Run at the Guns GOV. WILDER is arming for an all-out battle next year that we hope he wins hands down. He's about to take on the gunrunners who have made Virginia a major source of firearms used in East Coast crimes. Seizing recommendations submitted by a bipartisan commission he appointed to study violent crime, the governor says he'll make the measures a centerpiece of his legislative agenda for '93. One proposal would limit handgun purchases to one every 30 days. As you could expect, NRA big shots already are blasting the idea of any limit. They claim that it would amount to gun rationing for ordinary citizens but would do nothing to reduce crime. Come on. Do they seriously believe that 12 guns a year won't do it for the average Virginian? In fact, the NRA's guns-are-great lobbyists can't even live with a limit of 24 a year -- which was in a bill they worked to kill during the last General Assembly session. That's why Virginia is now the Arsenal of the East, the purveyor of choice for world-class gunrunners. In one 18-month period, 40 percent of the illegal handguns seized by police in New York City were found to have been purchased in Virginia. The state also is the number one source of handguns in the District. Says the governor: "On the East Coast, Virginia is pitiful" in this regard, adding that unless the state acts, "we'll be looked on as almost the Grim Reaper."

1903.text: GUN-1637 PRIMARY Government is not the answer The Editors: I have been reading with interest the opposing views as to whether or not gun control should be implemented. I am appalled by the number of readers who seem to be convinced that more government with more control is the answer to any of our problems. An individual with any degree of intelligence should be able to reflect upon our social progress during this century and discover that more and more government does not work, but simply adds to the escalating problems. It seems that more and more people have the mindset of cattle and follow wherever they are led. We now live in a nation bogged down by a mindless bureaucracy that has little if any concern about our Constitution. Now, many of the same people who have worked for bigger and better government, are proposing additional controls in the name of gun control. They relate statistics that are available as recently published by the New England Journal of Medicine in regard to the fact that homicides in Washington, D.C., have decreased as a result of gun control. Aren't statistics great? They can be used to prove a point regardless of the side taken. In this particular instance, one should also be aware of the fact that the population of Washington, D.C., has decreased, but it's too convenient to ignore that fact when one is trying to prove a point. Accordingly, the per-capita rate of homicides has risen in Washington, D.C., and if we truly want to base our decisions upon facts, I would suggest that gun control advocates stick to facts rather than statistics that have been purposely manipulated to prove their point.

1907.text: GUN-3245 PRIMARY ABOUT-FACE! GOV. RIDGE'S ON-AGAIN, OFF-AGAIN SUPPORT FOR A MILD GUN-CONTROL BILL GIVES HIM NO PROFILE IN COURAGE. Perhaps he misspoke. Or maybe he was misunderstood. Or maybe he caved in to pressure from the National Rifle Association. Whatever, Gov. Ridge's back-and-forth position on a bill mandating a 48- hour waiting period for gun purchases represents the first big waffle of his young administration. The governor has received fairly good reviews for his other politically popular anti-crime positions - a toughening of the death penalty, 20-year sentences for three-time violent offenders, a reasonable police notification rule for released sex offenders. The missing element in his crime package was any attempt to discourage crime, except for his courageous endorsement of the gun-control bill drafted by the Select Committee on Gun Violence chaired by state Sen. Vincent Fumo (D., Philadelphia). Courageous, in that the NRA has mounted an all-out war to kill the 48-hour waiting period and some other sound gun-control measures in the Fumo bill. (Note that levels of courage are purely relative in Pennsylvania, since Sen. Fumo, a loyal NRA member, last year helped scotch laws to ban assault weapons in Philadelphia and Pittsburgh.) Courage can also be fleeting in politics. This is how fast it came and went in Mr. Ridge's case. At a Jan. 18 press conference, Gov. Ridge noted that the state's requirement for a waiting period dated back to 1931 - "and I favor keeping it," he said. The provision in the Fumo bill makes the state waiting period permanent even after the federal Brady law's five-day waiting period expires. The point is not to dissolve the waiting period once computerized instant background checks are available. It's to maintain a cooling-off period, to discourage homicides and suicides committed often by friends or family members in anger or depression.

1905.text: NA-42108 PRIMARY Sunnyvale: Gun-control measure triggers lawsuit from gun industry, dealer SUNNYVALE -- A gun store owner and a national gun industry trade group have sued to block enforcement of parts of Sunnyvale's new gun control ordinance, claiming that it clashes with state and federal laws and tramples on constitutional rights. The National Rifle Association plans to file its own lawsuit early next week. But Sunnyvale announced Tuesday that the San Francisco law firm of Farella Braun + Martel will offer its services for free to defend the ordinance against all challenges. "Our community spoke loud and clear that we want to do what we can to prevent gun violence," said Mayor Tony Spitaleri. "The threats of lawsuits and defense costs didn't stop them from doing what they felt was right, and now we have one of the best law firms in the country prepared to defend us." Sunnyvale's Measure C, approved by 67 percent of voters last month, requires gun owners to notify police within 48 hours of the loss or theft of a firearm, as well as to keep firearms locked up when not in the owner's immediate possession. It also requires ammunition sellers to log and keep buyers' names for two years and prohibits possession of ammunition magazines that hold more than 10 rounds. A Santa Clara County Superior Court lawsuit filed Monday by U.S. Firearms; its owner, Eric Fisher; and the National Shooting Sports Foundation claims the ammo sales provision is pre-empted by state law and illegally shares customers' data with police -- and that the loss or theft reporting provision conflicts with state and federal laws.

1909.text: GUN-10733 PRIMARY Bring guns. Don't get comfy ; Mayor Muoio correct to comply with law and maintain safety. West Palm Beach's mayor doesn't want guns in city hall, and who can blame her? Allowing firearms into public buildings that serve as an arena for political and legal conflict invites trouble. Unfortunately, it's also the law. That the law is amply clear seemed for a while not to matter. This month, Mayor Jeri Muoio vowed to continue banning firearms in the building after Oct. 1, when a new state law imposes stiff fines on local governments that continue to enforce their own firearm regulations. Though her attitude was understandable, threatening to buck state law in all but name was a mistake. Now Mayor Muoio and her staff have devised a much wiser policy -- allowing people to enter city hall with guns but requiring them to be accompanied by a police escort during their entire visit. Thus the city complies with the law and maintains safety. Legislators in Tallahassee argue that West Palm Beach and scores of municipal governments have been violating state law for years by creating their own gun regulations. These regulations, though, were rarely, if ever, challenged. Now that penalties -- including $5,000 fines and potential removal from office -- are being attached, governments are taking note. The Palm Beach County Commission recently repealed ordinances that have kept guns out of day-care centers and parks. West Palm Beach and other local cities took similar steps.

1906.text: GUN-2769 PRIMARY THE GUN TIDE SHIFTS AFTER YEARS OF FOLLOWING THE GUN LOBBY LINE, CONGRESS SEEMS TO BE TAKING A SANER ROUTE Texas Rep. Chet Edwards' district includes lots of gun owners in and around Killeen. Nevertheless, during Thursday's remarkable debate on the House floor, he explained what had prompted him to vote for an assault-weapons ban in 1991: A gunman had killed 23 people in a Killeen cafeteria earlier that year. And how was the Democratic congressman rewarded? By re-election in 1992 with 66 percent of the vote. That story should warm the hearts of such Republican congressmen as Pennsylvania GOP gubernatorial candidate Tom Ridge of Erie and James Saxton of Burlington, N.J. Both men kept everyone guessing for days last week by saying they could not decide how to vote on a House bill to ban 19 of the world's nastiest semiautomatic weapons. Mr. Ridge's indecision was particularly perplexing since he publicly supports a statewide ban on assault weapons. Both deserve credit for deserting their previous congressional positions on this critical issue - they voted no in 1991 - and for having the guts to join the slim majority that passed this bill 216-214. By contrast, congressmen such as Rick Santorum of Allegheny County - who is seeking the GOP nomination for U.S. Senate - and Curt Weldon of Delaware County deserve to be remembered for voting wrong on this issue. For clearly, Thursday's vote was not so much a blow to crime as a signal of the slow but steady change in American sensibilities over government's role in limiting the proliferation of weapons.

1908.text: NA-40563 PRIMARY Illinois towns get nudge about assault weapons Cities who want ban must act before new law kicks in, Simon warns. CHICAGO o Lt. Gov. Sheila Simon is urging Illinois communities to consider banning assault-style weapons before legislation is signed that could prohibit local governments from doing so in the future. The Legislature approved a measure last month that would end Illinois' last-in-the-nation prohibition on the concealed carrying of firearms. The legislation, prompted by a federal court ruling that found Illinois' law unconstitutional, also prohibits future bans of assault-style weapons. It allows current bans, such as one in Chicago, to remain. Gov. Pat Quinn hasn't said whether he will sign the proposal, which the 7th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals has given him until July 9 to enact. Simon said that if the bill is signed into law as written, cities with so-called "home rule" decision-making authority would have just 10 days to prohibit assault-style weapons. "We have seen the tragic results assault weapons have had on our streets, in our schools, movie theaters and more," Simon, D-Carbondale, said in a statement to be issued Sunday. "The clock is ticking, so I encourage mayors and local officials to act now to ban assault weapons and retain local control over this important issue." The firearms legislation was a compromise between supporters of gun rights across Illinois and advocates for gun control trying to address violence in Chicago. It would prohibit the possession of guns in places such as parks, bars and schools, but would allow a firearm to be securely stored in a vehicle. The measure also would require the Illinois State Police to issue a concealed carry permit to any gun owner who passes a background check, undergoes 16 hours of training - the most required by any state - and who pays a $150 fee and has a valid Firearm Owners Identification card.

1910.text: GUN-10482 PRIMARY Legislature briefs Measure OK'd to allow guns in parts of airports The Georgia House gave final passage late Tuesday to a bill that would expand licensed gun-holders' rights to carry in parts of airports. The bill, sponsored by state Sen. David Shafer (R-Duluth), has strong support from the National Rifle Association, said Rep. Katie Dempsey (R-Rome), who shepherded the bill through the House. The bill, which the House approved 120-37, would allow gun permit holders to carry their weapons in nonsecure areas of an airport, Dempsey said. Other provisions also would prohibit firearm seizure and registration by the federal government, she said. Before the bill passed, Rep. Stephanie Benfield (D-Atlanta) asked: "How are citizens safer by allowing firearms in airports?" NANCY BADERTSCHER 'In God We Trust' could get spot on license tags The Senate voted 44-1 Tuesday to allow drivers to affix an "In God We Trust" decal on their license plate. The decal would go in the space where the county sticker currently is. Sen. Emanuel Jones (D-Decatur) cast the lone vote against the bill. Jones, who owns several car dealerships, said county names on tags are crucial to law enforcement. The bill now goes back to the House, since the amendment was approved. The Senate also voted 49-0 to allow a wider circle of family members to buy Gold Star license plates to honor soldiers killed in action. The bill would allow siblings, children and stepparents to buy the plates. Currently, spouses and the legal parents are given one free plate each and are allowed to buy more. ERNIE SUGGS

1904.text: GUN-5456 PRIMARY Lawyers for Shooting Victims Clash With Top Gun Makers Lawyers for major gun makers and wholesalers and for the families of six people killed with illegally possessed handguns clashed sharply in a Brooklyn courtroom yesterday over whether the companies were liable for the slayings. In opening arguments in a civil suit that is being closely watched by the firearms industry and gun-control advocates, a lawyer for the families maintained that the companies shared responsibility for the homicides because of a negligent, "head in the sand" distribution system that led to widespread illegal possession of guns in New York State by young people and criminals. "The defendants here, the manufacturers and distributors of handguns, knowingly failed to take the most basic precautions and actions to minimize the likelihood that their guns would cause injuries and loss at the hands of individuals who, in New York State, we say are not permitted to have guns," said the lawyer, Elisa Barnes. Addressing a jury in Federal District Court in Brooklyn, she said that the gun industry's distribution system "knowingly saturated" states having weak gun control laws with more new handguns than the legitimate market for the guns in those states. That, she said, led to an excess of handguns being sold to gun traffickers and others who then sold the guns illegally to people in states with strong gun laws, like New York. As a result, she said, almost half the guns used in crimes in New York State in recent years have come from Georgia, Florida, Virginia and North and South Carolina.

1912.text: GUN-372 PRIMARY RECORD NUMBER ASK GUN PERMITS IN NEW YORK CITY In the seven months since the New York State Legislature enacted a tougher handgun law, a record number of New York City residents have applied for licenses to own guns - for their homes and businesses, for target shooting or to carry on the street. Between Aug. 12, when the law went into effect, and March 5, the police received 5,758 applications for handgun licenses, almost twice as many as were filed in the same period a year earlier. The percentages of applications granted are the same as in the past, the police say: approximately 93 percent of those for a gun at a home or business or for use at a gun range, and 55 percent of those to carry a gun. Fear and the New Law The police attribute the increase in applications to a fear of rising crime and to a provision in the new law that is designed to speed the processing of applications. This provision mandates that the Police Department process applications within six months. Previously, it had often taken a year or more because of the paperwork involved and because of what the police said was a shortage of manpower. The provision allocated $625,000 to help the department pay overtime for the handling of the applications.   In the first few months after the law took effect, many who applied had already been on a waiting list to do so. Even so, the police said, applications are still being filed at a rate 30 to 40 percent above past years.

1913.text: GUN-272 PRIMARY IN THE NATION; Taking Aim at Handguns This city's bold new ban on ownership of concealable handguns probably won't stand up to court challenge.   Nevertheless, polls suggest that California may vote this fall to extend and tighten statewide handgun controls. In the nation's most populous state - a Western state at that, where firearms have long been commonplace -that can conceivably set off a national trend. So a knock-down, drag-out fight can be expected before Californians vote in November on the handgun initiative. The San Francisco ordinance, passed last month, requires all handguns now in the city to be turned over to the police, or otherwise disposed of, within a 90-day period ending in late October. Some of these weapons are already being turned in. But the ordinance appears to be in impermissible conflict with state law in at least two ways. First, a large body of California legislation concerning firearms apparently means that the state has pre-empted that field, so that the city of San Francisco is not entitled to legislate in it; and San Francisco's District Attorney, Arlo Smith, so advised Mayor Dianne Feinstein in the City Council before they passed the handgun ordinance. Second, Mr. Smith said in an interview, one provision of California's firearms laws states that nothing in those laws is to be interpreted as prohibiting gun ownership in the owner's home or place of business - as the city ordinance attempts to do.

1911.text: GUN-2685 PRIMARY Pistol-packing fever side effect of Brady Act Permit requests increase sharply in response to legislation, crime Apparently scared by federal gun control legislation and concerned about crime, hundreds more Gwinnett residents than usual have applied for permits to carry handguns, Gwinnett County Probate Court records show. The number of people who applied for permits during the first quarter of this year alone exceeded half of the number of permits granted during all of 1993, said Gwinnett Probate Judge Walter "Jim" Clarke. "It was just incredible, the amount of people applying for handgun permits," Clarke said. "We had them in lines, waiting for permits. "They were afraid the Brady Bill would somehow restrict their ownership," he said. "I think they're afraid they're not going to be able to get them later." The Brady Act, which Congress passed in November, mandated a federal five-day waiting period and background check for prospective handgun buyers. The law was named for James Brady, the press secretary under President Reagan, who was shot and wounded during an assassination attempt. Under Georgia law, the names of people who seek and receive such permits are not public records, and thus the names remain secret. But, said Clarke: "You'd be surprised who's carrying one." Many tote handguns in briefcases or purses, he said. Authorities say that most people don't need to carry handguns. "There are very few people who really have a compelling reason to carry a gun," said District Attorney Daniel J. Porter, adding that anyone who does should realize the consequences for anyone who points a gun at another person, much less shoots at them. Further, anyone who owns a gun should seek adequate training, Porter said.

1915.text: GUN-1692 PRIMARY Bush Threatens to Veto Crime Bill, Saying It's Too Soft on Defendants President Bush vowed today to veto an anti-crime package that would extend the death penalty to many drug-related crimes and impose a waiting period on the sale of handguns, arguing that the legislation was not tough enough on criminals. The veto threat came only hours before the House of Representatives was scheduled to vote on the bill, a compromise between competing House and Senate versions that was approved Sunday evening along partisan lines, the Democrats in favor and the Republicans opposed. But Mr. Bush's action, combined with intense pressure from the gun lobby and groups opposed to the death penalty, forced a delay on the vote until at least Tuesday because Democratic leaders feared they might not have enough votes to pass the bill tonight. Hopes for Tougher Rules Democrats accused the President of trying to thwart the strongest anti-crime bill to advance through the Congress in the last decade, and they said that the threatened veto would set the stage for crime as a major issue in the 1992 campaign. Although the bill included a number of tough measures he had sought, Mr. Bush had hoped the legislation would include tighter restrictions on habeas corpus appeals, the hearings in Federal courts in which prisoners raise constitutional challenges to their sentences or convictions in lower courts, particularly for death-row inmates facing execution. Mr. Bush had also favored a provision in an earlier version that had allowed some evidence gathered without a search warrant to be introduced in trials.

1914.text: GUN-6973 PRIMARY Tide turns on gun control In wake of Columbine, lawmakers expect more proposals on table Before Columbine, it was about more guns. Now, it's about  fewer. The April 20 Columbine High School massacre has shifted the  focus of Colorado's legislators to finding ways to keep guns out  of the hands of criminals and children. That's a big change from earlier this year. Before the worst  school shooting in U.S. history took place in suburban Denver, the  Colorado Legislature was focused on letting more people carry guns  in more places. House Majority Leader Doug Dean, R-Colorado Springs,  acknowledges the tide has turned on guns. 'In this session, we will not be focusing on liberalizing  laws in areas like carrying concealed weapons and other areas,'  Dean said. 'This session we'll focus on making sure we prosecute  felons and other people who shouldn't have guns.' Dean had led the high-profile fight in the House to allow  more people to carry concealed handguns but won't this year.   Instead, he held a news conference Friday with several other House  Republicans to talk about how much gun control they can stand. Tracking closely with fellow Republican Gov. Bill Owens, they  talked about introducing 'safe storage' legislation holding  parents legally accountable if children find guns in the house.   They talked about raising the legal age for buying a handgun. They  expressed openness to requiring criminal background checks at gun  shows. Democrats applauded the change in focus, but say they will  push Republicans to go further.

1917.text: GUN-8584 PRIMARY Nations Agree To Limit Sales Of Illicit Arms More than 170 nations reached consensus today on a watered-down plan to combat illegal small-arms trafficking after the United States threatened to block agreement if language to restrict civilian gun ownership and limit weapon sales was included. The conference president, Camilo Reyes of Colombia, announced the agreement early in the morning, ending a two-week conference that went into overtime today. Exhausted delegates left for a few hours of sleep and were expected to return at noon to approve the agreement formally. The United States made clear from the outset that it would oppose any plan that interfered with citizens' rights to own guns and would reject any measure barring governments from supplying small arms to "nonstate actors," such as rebel groups. In the final make-or-break negotiations, the United States said it could not support consensus unless language calling for governments "to seriously consider legal restrictions on unrestricted trade in and ownership of small arms and light weapons" was dropped. The United States also stood as a lone voice in demanding elimination of language calling for small arms to be transferred only to governments or government-approved entities. African nations were especially vocal in demanding that this language remain in the text. But the African nations dropped their demand to achieve consensus for the plan, and the United States prevailed. Many African and European delegates who backed them were visibly angry.

1916.text: GUN-11486 PRIMARY Congress will never act on gun control. Right? Dozens of college students murdered in their classrooms; a member of Congress shot at point-blank range; innocents gunned down in a movie theater. Then, in the aftermath of a mass gun crime, the same ritual: national shock and anger, traumatized communities asking how this could happen, followed by . . . nothing. At least, no progress on gun safety.           In a  speech to the Urban League on Wednesday , President Obama called for a conversation on youth violence and more steps to keep guns away from criminals and the mentally ill. But everyone, including Obama, has been pretty frank about it: No major new gun laws will result. However, in Washington, nothing is ever as immovable as it seems. The key is to understand how intractable problems are ultimately dislodged - not by a single, seismic event, but by a slow shift in politics. Consider three categories of such issues and the forces that have gotten them unstuck. When the problem gets too big One reason for paralysis is that politicians fear the consequences of taking on powerful, well-financed organizations such as, when it comes to guns, the National Rifle Association. The gun lobby dramatically outspends gun-control advocates and has a long record of successfully opposing politicians who cross it. Still, even a heavyweight institution can be sidelined when a problem becomes too large. The AARP has long been regarded as an invincible opponent of Social Security reform. But in the early 1980s, Social Security was nearing collapse. In 1981, its trustees  issued a report warning that the system would be "unable to make benefit payments on time beginning in the latter half of 1982" and that it would run out of money by the middle of 1983. A bipartisan group of lawmakers began to explore the idea of making changes to Social Security, but the AARP stood firm.

1919.text: GUN-5973 PRIMARY Got a Cause? Get a Celebrity; Let Them Run To the Editor: Re "Keep Politics Out of Reach of Celebrities," (The Big City column, May 27): Rosie O'Donnell's criticism of Tom Selleck's gun-control views shifted her talk show a step away from its light-hearted premise of noncontroversial banter and product pitching. However, the more serious mistake she and other outspoken celebrities have made is not differentiating between a solution and a law. Unfortunately, since the tragedy in Littleton, Colo., no matter how a discussion begins on stemming youth violence, it inevitably ends with a debate on politics. Laws are not always solutions, and the current tide of anti-gun sentiment is similar to painting a new coat without scraping off the old paint. It's time we find something more than sound-bite solutions. One of the first steps is to allow celebrities' influence on our opinions to wane, at least until we elect them to office. PAUL R. WALKER New York, May 27, 1999 http://www.nytimes.com

1920.text: GUN-7233 PRIMARY N.R.A. Stands By Criticism Of President In the face of criticism from political friends and foes alike, the National Rifle Association refused today to back off the supercharged rhetoric of the group's executive vice president, Wayne R. LaPierre Jr., who last week labeled President Clinton as an accessory to murder for not enforcing the nation's gun laws. Mr. LaPierre did decline to repeat the inflammatory line that the president has blood on his hands but he nonetheless stood by his remarks in a series of television appearances today. "I know I used some strong language," Mr. LaPierre said on NBC's "Meet the Press." "But I did it to center the issue on the fact that this administration, over the last seven years, has dropped federal enforcement of existing federal gun laws against the bad guys by 50 percent. And that's why I used the language, to center the debate." Within the N.R.A. itself, Mr. LaPierre's tough talk won accolades from some hard-liners who have regarded him as too moderate in his defense of gun owners' rights. Turning up the heat on Mr. Clinton, N.R.A. officials say, has also resulted in an increase in the organization's membership. "When he said what he did I thought, 'It's about time someone says it like it is,' " said David Edgar, who builds swimming pools in Houston and keeps guns for self-defense. Mr. Edgar logged onto his computer and joined the N.R.A. last week after hearing Mr. LaPierre lambaste the president for allowing a certain level of violence in the country for political gain.

1918.text: GUN-8140 PRIMARY POLITICS; With a Poll-Tested Message, NRA Aims to Counter Labor Efforts in Midwest The National Rifle Association plans an aggressive campaign of phone calls, letters and television commercials in the midwestern battleground states to counter an anticipated onslaught by organized labor against Texas Gov. George W. Bush. The NRA is formulating its message using data from a new poll showing a large majority of voters across the country--including Democrats and union members--want enforcement of existing firearms laws, not new gun regulation. Bush echoes those ideas, unlike Vice President Gore, who for years has called for more gun controls. But in recent months Gore has remained conspicuously silent about the issue, apparently in an effort to avoid inflaming the Midwest's generally pro-gun white working-class voters. The Tarrance Group poll of 1,000 likely voters last month shows 72 percent favor stronger enforcement of existing gun laws, while 19 percent want more gun legislation. About 79 percent of union members are for tougher enforcement. "We have the poll numbers, and I'm sure Gore and Bush have it, too," NRA Executive Vice President Wayne LaPierre said. "The gateway to the presidency turns out to be Michigan, Missouri and Ohio," where he said NRA membership has risen 36 percent, 43 percent and 49 percent respectively in the last year. Up to one-fifth of union members in those states are in the NRA, group officials said. In interviews arranged by the group, NRA members in Michigan and Pennsylvania who also belong to unions say their union leaders are spreading the word that Gore strongly endorses gun owners' rights.

1921.text: GUN-4595 PRIMARY Concealed weapons trigger no extra violence; None of permits linked to firearms used for crimes GASTONIA -- The end of the state's century-old ban on concealed weapons apparently has not sparked new gun-related violence, but police chiefs and gun control advocates still oppose the new law. Opponents had dire warnings of increased bloodshed when the new law allowing citizens to apply for permits to carry concealed handguns took effect in December 1995. But according to State Bureau of Investigation figures, not one gun covered under the 20,082 permits issued was used to commit a crime. And police revoked only 22 permits -- about one-10th of 1 percent of those issued, the Gaston Gazette reported. ``I'm actually pleased that there have been no more problems than there have been,'' said Gerald Galloway, past president of the state's Association of Police Chiefs. In 1995, Galloway told a state Senate committee: ``We believe to be secretly armed is bad public policy.'' Despite the good news from the permit statistics, Galloway still believes the law is not the way to calm fears about crime. ``Concealed weapons should not be the answer to making people safe on the streets,'' he said. ``We need to deal with it from the side of catching criminals and convicting them so citizens don't have to feel unsafe.'' The legislation still has forced police officers to change their approach when faced with people carrying weapons. They now have to ask to see a permit card before nabbing the person, said John Crone, who is also an association past president.

1924.text: GUN-6030 PRIMARY Clinton wants gun law 'before school lets out'; Law: Republicans support move, but also seek solution to 'cultural state of emergency.' President Clinton on Saturday pressed the House for swift passage of gun- control legislation and prepared to propose that states be allowed to spend unemployment funds on paid leave for parents so they can spend more time with their very young children. In the wake of April's fatal shootings at a Littleton, Colo., high school, senators approved legislation last week that would require safety locks on new handguns, ban violent juveniles from purchasing a firearm, and stop the import of large ammunition clips. "I hope the House of Representatives will pass every one of these common- sense efforts that the Senate has passed to protect our children from guns," Clinton said in his weekly radio address. "And I hope they'll do it before school lets out." One Republican, Rep. J.C. Watts of Oklahoma, said in response to Clinton's remarks that "some of the steps" taken by the Senate "have some merit" and the House "will move to consider them as soon as possible." He urged the administration to step up enforcement of gun laws and offer "a much more comprehensive solution to this cultural state of emergency." Rep. Henry Hyde (R-Ill.), chairman of the House Judiciary Committee, said later Saturday that his panel will hold hearings on the Senate legislation on Thursday, but that no House vote is likely until after the Memorial Day recess.

1927.text: GUN-5715 PRIMARY Senate Democrats Stall Spending Bills With HMO, Gun Amendments Senate Democrats yesterday stalled a farm spending bill by tacking on their so-called patients' bill of rights, vowing to attach it to every possible piece of legislation until they are promised a vote on their proposed HMO reforms. Majority Leader Trent Lott (R-Miss.) had to temporarily withdraw the $ 60.7 billion appropriations bill for the Agriculture Department shortly after Sen. Byron L. Dorgan (D-N.D.) offered the bill on health maintenance organizations as an amendment. Lott warned that the Democrats' action would hurt farmers, but Democrats said they will keep holding up unrelated bills as long as GOP leaders stall their health plan. At the same time, House Democrats led by Rep. Steny H. Hoyer (D-Md.) have threatened to load another spending bill with gun control provisions that failed last week unless congressional negotiators agree to include them as part of juvenile justice legislation. The provisions, which include new rules for background checks at gun shows, a ban on the importation of large-capacity ammunition clips and a requirement that safety locks be sold with new guns, would be offered as amendments to the fiscal 2000 spending bill for the Treasury and general government agencies. Fearing a reprise of last week's divisive gun debate, House Appropriations Committee leaders are likely to put off consideration of the Treasury bill until after the July 4 recess. HMO reform and gun control are the latest in a series of controversies that have complicated the annual appropriations process and enhanced prospects for another legislative train wreck this fall. House conservative activists, led by Rep. Tom Coburn (R-Okla.), added weeks to the process by demanding and getting modest reductions in next year's agriculture and legislative branch spending, and conservatives are expected to demand even larger cuts in a transportation spending bill this week.

1928.text: GUN-6797 PRIMARY Judge Allows 3 Families To Sue Firearms Industry A decision by an Illinois state judge last week to continue a private lawsuit that accused the gun industry of knowingly creating an underground market in handguns has encouraged 28 cities and counties suing the firearms companies and may suggest that judges have become more receptive to innovative claims against gun makers. The decision, by Judge Jennifer Duncan-Brice of Cook County Circuit Court in Chicago, was in a case brought by the families of three people killed by handguns and not in a better known lawsuit by the city of Chicago. But lawyers involved in the municipal lawsuits said the claims made by the families -- that the firearms industry negligently distributed its products and created a public nuisance -- are similar to cases brought by Chicago and other cities and offer some indication of how judges may regard this new approach to attempting to hold the gun companies responsible for urban violence. Judge Duncan-Brice denied a motion by the gun companies to dismiss the lawsuit, saying individuals, not just a governmental entity, could bring a claim that the firearms industry as a whole had created a public nuisance. The judge also said it "is very clear to this court" that gun companies knowingly violated Chicago's strict antihandgun laws by oversupplying gunshops in the suburbs, where the laws are weaker, "creating an underground market" in Chicago.

1925.text: GUN-11210 PRIMARY California gun shows caught in the crossfire Before the end of this year, Russell and Sallie Nordyke will set up shop for at least five gun shows at the Santa Clara County fairgrounds, providing a gathering spot for thousands of gun enthusiasts to buy and sell rifles, pistols and other weapons. For the Glenn County couple, the South Bay is a small island amid a sea of hostility toward their TS Gun Shows. Bay Area counties from Alameda and Marin to San Mateo have enacted laws that forbid the sale or possession of guns on government property, effectively banning gun shows at some of the best spots to hold them. The Nordykes believe those laws are unconstitutional -- and on Monday, a federal appeals court will once again take up their 12-year quest to strike down the regulations. The case offers another crucial test of Second Amendment rights that could have repercussions for California's sweeping slate of state and local gun control laws. Specifically, an 11-judge 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals panel is to hear arguments in the Nordykes' legal challenge to Alameda County's ordinance, which has outlawed gun shows at the fairgrounds in Pleasanton since 1999. "It has impacted our lives tremendously," Sallie Nordyke said. "We used to be able to have gun shows in a lot of other places." With gun rights groups such as the National Rifle Association on one side and gun control advocates such as the Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence on the other, the Nordyke case is being closely watched across the country. The showdown is considered a barometer of how far local and state governments can go in regulating guns following U.S. Supreme Court rulings expanding constitutional protections for the right to bear arms.

characters: Brady Center to Prevent Gun Violence

1929.text: GUN-709 PRIMARY GUN LAWS MISGUIDED; ATTACK REAL PROBLEMS Regarding your editorial headline: "Ban assault weapons; Everyone is for it except of course the NRA." I don't recall you asking me if I was for it. I have had it up to my gunsights with your unknowledgeable attitude toward the use and ownership of firearms. You fail to accept the underlying problem: America has been breeding a nation of unresponsible people. Then we distort the problems by attempting to deal with them by simply passing laws, believing that this will solve the problems. These root problems will not go away with legislation. Wayne LaPierre of the National Rifle Association states, "We cannot legislate against objects and hope to control the insanity of the criminal mind." Neither can we hope to control the unresponsible mind. DO WE BAN AUTOMOBILES? A local woman was convicted and jailed for vehicular homicide. The automobile she was driving while intoxicated contained sufficient mass and velocity to cause the death of two men in the other automobile. Did we blame the deaths on the automobile, or did we place blame on the operator of the automobile? The automobile is a lifeless hunk of inanimate material that is dependent on a human force before it can do anything. The car by itself did not cause the deaths of two men. The cause was the unresponsible behavior of the human operator. Did we put the car on trial or in prison? Did we ban the sale and use of other automobiles? Of course not. We placed the blame right where it belonged.

1923.text: GUN-11882 PRIMARY Corporate dominoes falling over firearms Retailers, investors and Hollywood are reacting to the massacre of 20 schoolchildren in Newtown, Conn., in what gun control advocates are hoping will be a domino effect. Dick's Sporting Goods, a chain with more than 500 stores, suspended sales of "modern sporting rifles" nationwide and pulled guns off the shelves at its store closest to Newtown, calling the Friday shootings "an unspeakable tragedy." Wal-Mart, one of the country's leading gun sellers and the nation's largest retailer, yanked from its website the information page on the Bushmaster .223, the semi-automatic rifle police said gunman Adam Lanza used in Friday's rampage. Private equity firm Cerberus Capital Management announced Tuesday it will sell Freedom Group, the company that manufactures the Bushmaster line of firearms and the nation's largest gun maker. And TV networks are airing disclaimers before violent shows and swapping some programs for more subdued fare while Hollywood movie studios have canceled some film openings in response to the shootings at Sandy Hook Elementary School. "What this represents is a sea change in the politics on this issue. The dominoes keep falling at this point," said Coalition to Stop Gun Violence spokesman Ladd Everitt. Also on Tuesday, Michigan Gov. Rick Snyder, a Republican, vetoed a bill that would have allowed gun owners with special training to take their weapons into schools, day care centers, stadiums and churches.

1930.text: GUN-9933 PRIMARY Lawyers, Guns and Mayors Few problems facing American cities are more serious than gun violence. Guns destroy thousands of lives every year and spread fear in our neighborhoods. It is much too easy for criminals to get firearms. And when a handgun is readily available, a minor argument or domestic dispute can quickly escalate into a homicide. We've worked hard to get illegal guns off the streets, through tough law enforcement, by supporting reasonable gun laws -- and by suing gun manufacturers and dealers to get them to take responsibility for their actions. Some two dozen American cities and counties have filed similar lawsuits. But Congress is on the verge of passing legislation that would undercut the ability of local governments to hold the gun industry accountable for its role in flooding our cities with guns. The Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act would shield irresponsible firearms manufacturers, wholesalers, dealers and trade associations from any form of civil liability in cases in which they recklessly or negligently supply firearms to criminals. The bill, which was approved by the House last April, is now being considered in the Senate. We do not advocate suing manufacturers in all instances when an incident involving a gun causes harm or injury. But shouldn't we be able to sue manufacturers and suppliers when they act with wanton disregard for the safety of our neighborhoods?

1926.text: NA-43042 PRIMARY Weekend Gun Report: March 7-9, 2014 For some reason, Facebook's recent announcement that it was taking steps to curtail illegal gun sales on its social network has the N.R.A. declaring victory. "The N.R.A. enjoys 150 times more support on Facebook than Michael Bloomberg's Mayors Against Illegal Guns," Chris Cox, the executive director of the gun-rights organization, said in a press release. "That's why Bloomberg and the gun control groups he funds tried to pressure Facebook into shutting down discussion of Second Amendment issues on its social media platforms. Bloomberg failed." Some have criticized the social network's attempt at shutting down pages on which guns are being illegally sold as superficial, as the effort relies largely on gun control advocacy groups to police content and report it to Facebook. Shannon Watts, the founder of Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America, said online purchases are nearly impossible to track. "To not know what's happening out there is truly frightening," she said. Here is today's report. -Jennifer Mascia Friday: A 13-year-old girl was shot in the head with a 177-caliber pellet fired from a pistol or a rifle near Springfield High School in Springfield, Ore., Thursday evening. Matthew Bolton, a 15-year-old high school student, shot and killed his ex-girlfriend, 16-year-old Anastasia "Ana" Greer, before turning the gun on himself in Phoenix, Ariz., Friday night. Rajean George, 16, was found shot to death along with 22-year-old Nikko Taylor in a home in Shreveport, La., Friday. George had been reported missing in January.

characters: Moms Demand Action for Gun Sense in America

1934.text: GUN-3391 PRIMARY COUNTY OKS WARNING-LABEL LAW FOR GUNS Beginning next month, guns sold in Fulton County will join cigarettes and plastic bags in being required to bear a label warning of a potential health hazard. County commissioners voted unanimously Wednesday to require licensed gun dealers to label weapons as a leading cause of murder and suicide and to post bulletins bearing the same message. It is the first such ordinance in the nation, said Paul Blackman of the National Rifle Association in Washington. "This is not gun control," said Commissioner John O'Callaghan, sponsor of the bill. "This helps gun buyers make informed decisions and, if they do buy, to treat the gun with respect and take the required safety precautions." Bill Davis of the Georgia Gunowners Association, an NRA affiliate, said the labeling will be of little use. "It's a feel-good effort that isn't going to tell anyone anything he didn't already know," he said. Failure to comply with the ordinance, which goes into effect Nov. 1, is a misdemeanor with a maximum penalty of $500 and 60 days in jail.

1931.text: GUN-919 PRIMARY Alaska, Guns and Logic As the Senate considers imposing a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases, many senators from urban states see it as a way to reduce gun violence on the streets, while some from rural states fear it could lead to curbs on hunting rifles. Then there is Sen. Ted Stevens (R-Alaska), who had this to say during debate last week: "I had somebody tell me, 'Why do you Alaskans need handguns anyway?' The next time you come up, Mr. President, . . .   you can go fishing with me out in the river. And stand there, catch a nice big salmon, and have a brown bear standing on the other side and decide he wants your salmon -- and maybe you too. You cannot very well carry a shotgun or rifle over your shoulder and use your pole, but that is what people want you to do."

1935.text: GUN-7410 PRIMARY GUN-LAW DEBATE STALEMATED The public outrage sparked by back-to-back shootings in Michigan and Pennsylvania got the attention of the White House and members of Congress on Wednesday, but a sympathetic ear is about all the federal government has to offer. The shooting death Tuesday of 6-year-old Kayla Rolland, a first-grader in Mount Morris Township, Mich., by one of her classmates, in particular prompted renewed calls on Capitol Hill for stricter gun laws. Before the echoes of those words had faded, the shootings Wednesday in Wilkinsburg, Pa., that killed two people left the political establishment in Washington as bewildered as ever about how to stop gun violence. Just as they did after the shocking events at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo., almost a year ago, gun-control advocates are calling for passage of gun legislation that is bottled up in Congress. Meanwhile, opponents argue that putting more gun laws on the books won't solve the social problems that usually precipitate gun violence. The result is political stalemate, just like last year's after President Clinton and Congress failed to agree on legislative solutions in the wake of Littleton. Election year "I don't think the Republican leadership wants to put its members in a position of voting on gun control in an election year," said Marie Carbone, director of congressional relations for Handgun Control Inc. "I think they will run from it as fast as they can."

1932.text: GUN-963 PRIMARY Sen. Mitchell and the Brady Bill BRADY BILL time is coming soon to the U.S. Senate, where there are some encouraging moves afoot to answer the strong public calls for protection against unchecked sales of handguns. If the senators should have learned anything from the solid bipartisan House vote for the measure earlier this month, it's that Americans everywhere -- gun owners included -- want an effective federal waiting period/background check on purchasers of handguns. The key to a good response by the Senate could be the work of Majority Leader George Mitchell of Maine. Once seen as a principal obstacle to passage in the Senate, he is now talking about how the House version could be improved with several more provisions that Brady bill advocates generally support. If his proposal, which he considers a compromise, is drafted as described, Brady bill supporters say it could improve both the measure itself and its prospects for passage. The senator's proposal would include the Brady bill's seven-day waiting period and would add a requirement that police conduct criminal background checks before a handgun sale is made. This requirement was not part of the House version, which Brady bill opponents there used as an argument against it. Such a requirement is fine -- and all those NRA-goaded crocodile tear-shedders who lamented its absence should welcome this improvement. The Mitchell proposal also would provide federal incentives to states to computerize and share criminal records and conduct background checks. Here again it was the NRA that made such a big -- and empty -- deal about computerized checks, knowing full well that any system would be years in the making. Their sham bill, sponsored by Rep. Harley O. Staggers Jr. (D-W.Va.), specifically excluded any seven-day waiting period; it was meant only to kill the Brady bill. Sen. Mitchell's plan would be aimed at speeding the establishment of a computerized system while keeping the Brady waiting period in place. Again, this is good.

1936.text: GUN-182 PRIMARY CHIEF IN SAN JOSE BATTLES GUN LOBBY Joseph D. McNamara, Chief of Police in this hub of high technology, is at war again, this time with the National Rifle Association. Chief McNamara, a Manhattan-born, Harvard-educated former New York City street cop and part-time novelist, was warned recently by the association that it would sue him if he continued to use what it called untruthful assertions to criticize its lobbying against gun control. ''I was a little surprised,'' Chief McNamara said of the letter, ''because it's so stupid. You don't threaten an Irish cop.'' And then, in an interview sandwiched between negotiations for the sale of hard-cover rights to his second novel and movie rights to his first one, the 51-year-old Police Chief repeated his criticism of the nation's largest gun owners' organization, saying he was not worried about a lawsuit over his characterizations of the group's membership and positions. Chief McNamara has been one of the most prominent spokesmen recently among law-enforcement officials who favor tighter Federal and state regulation of firearms, especially pistols and machine guns, and who oppose the N.R.A.'s efforts to give greater latitude to interstate sales of firearms. The Chief and other advocates of tighter gun controls were dealt a setback last month when President Reagan signed legislation relaxing Federal controls on the transportation of firearms across state lines. A Few Complain About Him Chief McNamara, who had a controversial tenure in the same post in Kansas City, Mo., appears frequently on national television shows, and a few present and former police officials complain that he is egotistic and often agressively seeks personal publicity. Still, morale among the 1,010 officers here seems high, perhaps partly because they are among the highest paid in the world, with a starting salary of $30,000 a year and a top scale of $42,000.

1933.text: NA-42727 PRIMARY As the Senate Agrees to Consider Gun Limits To the Editor: Re ''Senator's Search for Ally Keeps Gun Bill Alive'' (front page, April 11): The Senate compromise on background checks represents a clear victory for the National Rifle Association's policy of delay-delay-delay. Instead of agreeing that every transfer of a firearm should be preceded by a background check -- that is, a true universal background check system supported by the overwhelming majority of Americans -- the Senate deal exempts all person-to-person private sales from the background check requirement. I can't legally sell a single oxycodone tablet to someone I meet in a bar, but I can sell him an assault rifle, even if he is a felon or someone barred from gun ownership because of a history of domestic violence. There is no rational justification for not requiring that all transfers of firearms ownership take place through a licensed firearms dealer. With guns that can kill so many so quickly, can anyone really tell me with a straight face that it is not worth taking a few days to sell a gun if it means keeping it out of the hands of dangerous people? ROBERT CHECCHIO Dunellen, N.J., April 11, 2013 To the Editor: The Senate may be on the verge of extending background checks, but that alone may not be enough. The New York County Lawyers' Association recently did an extensive study of federal gun control legislative proposals and published a report that makes recommendations as to what should be enacted. Virginia Tech teaches us that the checks are only as good as the information provided by the states. Congress should provide states with incentives to provide full information to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System.

1937.text: NA-43103 PRIMARY An Attack on Guns Tests Good Will Toward Giffords Gabrielle Giffords is about to find out just what sort of leeway being the nation's most recognizable - and well liked - gunshot victim gives you when it comes to political attack ads. Ms. Giffords, a former Democratic congresswoman from Arizona who was shot in the head at a 2011 campaign event, has worked tirelessly to advance stricter gun laws. But now, some are saying her gun control "super PAC," Americans for Responsible Solutions, may have gone too far. The group is running ads to help 2014 candidates who support stricter gun laws, including Representative Ron Barber, an Arizona Democrat who worked for Ms. Giffords and was also injured in the shooting before replacing her in Congress. In perhaps the most emotional ad, a mother named Vicki, puffy-eyed and tearful, recounts how her daughter and her husband became victims of gun violence. "My daughter was just 19 when she told her boyfriend their relationship was over," said Vicki, choking up. "And he got a gun and shot her and my husband." [Video:  Watch on YouTube.] Vicki then turns her attention to Mr. Barber's Republican opponent, Martha McSally, a fierce proponent of gun rights. "Martha McSally opposes making it harder for stalkers to get a gun," she says. On Tuesday, Politico weighed in with an article headlined "Gabby Giffords Gets Mean," chronicling Ms. Giffords's emergence as a "ruthless attack dog."

1938.text: GUN-6166 PRIMARY PROTECTING 'PRECIOUS FREEDOMS'? HARDLY It seems strange that in the gun-control battle, no one, especially columnists, ever discusses what ought to be the most salient issue - the effect that wide accessibility to guns has had on everyone's personal freedom. Immediately after the shooting at Columbine High, we were treated to a sermon from National Rifle Association mouthpiece Charlton Heston about the need to protect "our precious freedoms," meaning his constituency's unfettered right to buy and use guns. OK. So what has that "precious freedom" cost us? Schools that are run almost like maximum-security prisons, where millions of dollars are spent on high-tech surveillance systems, ID cards and armed guards instead of education. Students whose first lesson in democracy is to submit to random searches and who are punished, suspended and even jailed for making smart remarks or wearing clothing that adults view as threatening. And now, in a bizarre case here in Palm Beach County, a school board attorney generates front-page headlines for a casual comment about "going postal." In their selfish zeal to propagate a bogus interpretation of the Second Amendment, the gun lobby has created an America where the real freedoms guaranteed by the First and Fourth are constantly trampled as a bureaucratic excuse to keep us safe from guns and their liberty-loving owners. Sorry, Charlie, but that's not my idea of freedom. George English Boynton Beach

1940.text: GUN-3675 PRIMARY NRA ITSELF BRINGS ON GUN RESTRICTIONS The board member of the National Rifle Association who wrote the recent letter ''Distrust of government isn't unique to the NRA'' stated: ''Many people in America deeply distrust the government for restricting their gun rights.'' We should all distrust the NRA for trying to hoodwink us by misquoting our Constitution. The Second Amendment does not state that ''the right of the people to keep and bear arms shall not be infringed.'' It specifically states, ''A well-regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free state, the right of the people to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed.'' Isn't it interesting how the NRA intentionally omits the first and most important part of the amendment? The Constitution says nothing about the right of criminals and crackpots to own the now more than 200 million guns in America. The NRA has also claimed that ''guns don't kill people; people kill people.'' Try telling that to the parents of a 5-year-old who has just been killed by a stray bullet. David Ross Lake Worth

1939.text: GUN-1452 PRIMARY Amendments Stall Gun-Control Measure in Hartford Senate A special session of the General Assembly that was expected to act swiftly on a package of gun-control laws has instead turned into a complicated political minuet.
