On Monday, after almost 10 hours of debate, the House of Representatives passed a pair of bills that had been vastly transformed from the original proposals. The package moved to the Senate late today, where the bills passed, but the fate of one of them became uncertain after a key amendment, banning the sale of cheap hanguns known as Saturday night specials, was defeated. At the same time, the Senate approved an amendment that would make it easier to apply the death penalty, a combination that one of the bill's sponsors, Senator George Jepsen, a Democrat from Stamford, said would prove deadly because Gov. Lowell P. Weicker Jr. opposed the death penalty and was likely to veto the measure. "If the death penalty stays on it, without the handgun ban, it's in big trouble," Mr. Jepsen said before the vote. That bill, which makes it a felony to sell or otherwise provide firearms to juveniles, now goes back to the House, which returns to Hartford on Monday. "It's very disappointing," Mr. Jepsen said. "One problem when you're dealing with a special session is that time is very limited." He said he would try to bring back the measure in the regular session.

1942.text: GUN-824 PRIMARY Violence the spark behind gun bans Outrage has limits. '' It's not against guns per se, but automatic weapons - weapons clearly associated with crime, not sports,'' says Clemson University criminologist Mervin White. Monday, California's Assembly backed a ban on 40 semiautomatic assault rifles. Tuesday, the Bush administration stopped imports of assault rifles. Wednesday, spokesman Marlin Fitzwater said Bush has not banned the guns, only stopped imports while uses of the rifles are studied. Net outcome for gun shops: a run on the weapons. ''We were selling five a week; now it's 20, at whatever price we want. Our buyer got on the phone to the East Coast at 6 this morning and couldn't find any guns,'' says Jabie Gray at Dunn's Discount Gun Mart, San Diego. ''If you said red Volkswagens were hard to get there'd be a run on red Volkswagens,'' says Woody Seals at San Diego's A&W Sporting Goods. Says customer Nick Gonzales, 55, at Turner's Outdoorsman in Norwalk, Calif.: ''If a terrorist comes here and you don't have a semiautomatic, you're at a real disadvantage.'' Gun control opponents are mounting their own efforts. In Stockton, Calif. - where Patrick Purdy gunned down five schoolchildren with an AK-47 assault rifle Jan. 17 - a new group of gun control opponents seeks to repeal a gun ban instituted after Purdy's crime. ''We're going to start doing something. Up to this point, they've been doing it to us,'' says Bob Banks of Citizens for a Better Stockton.

1944.text: GUN-6111 PRIMARY Guns Galore Why isn't the tragedy in the Fort Worth Baptist church being treated as a "hate crime?" If this crime had been perpetrated against homosexuals, women or minorities it would be instantly labeled as an unqualified hate crime. ERIC LARSON Chula Vista, Calif. The Sept. 19 article "Gun Control's Limited Aim" [front page] missed the point when it proclaims in a subheadline: "Bills Would Not Have Stopped Recent Killings." Gunmen such as the Texas shooter can find ways to get around gun laws, but most deaths related to guns do not happen in high-profile cases such as Fort Worth or Littleton. The Post's front-page article creates an impression that because we cannot stop these "mentally unstable" people, we cannot stop the death caused by an overabundance of firearms on our streets and in our homes. But according to the Centers for Disease Control, 13 people under the age of 19 are killed in gun homicides, suicides and unintentional shootings each day. And most of these deaths occur not because of rampages but because guns were easily available during a heated argument or child's play. Communications wizards at the National Rifle Association are sure to exploit incidents such as the Texas shooting by spinning their message that "gun control won't really stop the killing." But with nearly 200 million privately owned firearms circulating in the United States, it's past time to pass laws to get some of those guns off the street.

1941.text: GUN-10833 PRIMARY Doctors shouldn't ask about gun ownership It was good to read that three states are considering laws that would penalize doctors and other health care providers for asking patients or their parents whether they have a gun at home. All of the other states should do likewise ("Measures would ban physicians' gun talk," News, Friday). In regard to firearm-related injuries, the American Academy of Pediatrics states, "The absence of guns from children's homes and communities is the most reliable and effective measure to prevent firearm-related injuries" to them. This position statement clearly indicates the hoplophobia (irrational fear of firearms), and the anti-gun, anti-gun ownership,  anti-Second Amendment bias and bigotry of that organization. There is no place for, and no reason for, snooping by pediatricians, other doctors, or other health care providers into whether constitutional rights by law-abiding citizens are exercised, and especially not by extracting this information from children. David R. Russell Berkeley, Calif. Medical industry at fault Opposing view writer Avik Roy  is out of touch with  middle-class Americans when he says the  main reason health insurance is so expensive in America is because it is easy for us to waste other people's money ("Allow states more freedom," Health care debate, Friday). As a retired federal employee, I can shop (choose a plan), but I have no choice about Medicare. I pay half of my insurance premium plus part B in Medicare. I have no control over the program's  ever-increasing costs and how it wastes my money.

1922.text: GUN-2564 PRIMARY Senate Roll-Call On Brady Bill Following is the second roll-call vote by which the Senate today failed to limit debate on the Brady bill. The vote was 57 to 41. A "yes" vote was a vote to limit debate. X denotes those not voting. Voting "yes" were 48 Democrats and 9 Republicans. Voting "no" were 7 Democrats and 34 Republicans. New Hampshire: Gregg (R) No; Smith (R) No. New Jersey: Bradley (D) Yes; Lautenberg (D) Yes. New Mexico: Bingaman (D) Yes; Domenici (R) No. New York: D'Amato (R) No; Moynihan (D) Yes. North Carolina: Faircloth (R) No; Helms (R) X. North Dakota: Conrad (D) Yes; Dorgan (D) X. Ohio: Glenn (D) Yes; Metzenbaum (D) Yes. Oklahoma: Boren (D) Yes; Nickles (R) No. Oregon: Hatfield (R) Yes; Packwood (R) No. Pennsylvania: Specter (R) No; Wofford (D) Yes. Rhode Island: Chafee (R) Yes; Pell (D) Yes. South Carolina: Hollings (D) No; Thurmond (R) No. South Dakota: Daschle (D) Yes; Pressler (R) No. Tennessee: Mathews (D) Yes; Sasser (D) Yes. Texas: Gramm (R) No; Hutchison (R) No. Utah: Bennett (R) No; Hatch (R) No. Vermont: Jeffords (R) Yes; Leahy (D) Yes. Virginia: Robb (D) Yes; Warner (R) Yes. Washington: Gorton (R) Yes; Murray (D) Yes. West Virginia: Byrd (D) Yes; Rockefeller (D) Yes. Wisconsin: Feingold (D) Yes; Kohl (D) Yes. Wyoming: Simpson (R) No; Wallop (R) No.

1943.text: GUN-11427 PRIMARY Broun misfires on U.N. gun claim U.S. Rep. Paul Broun wants you to reach for your checkbook. A Second Amendment rights group needs money to keep the United Nations from coming for your guns. The Athens Republican made his pitch in a YouTube video posted by the National Association for Gun Rights. "If passed by the U.N. and ratified by the U.S. Senate, the U.N. Small Arms Treaty would almost certainly force the United States to ... create an international gun registry, setting the stage for full-scale gun confiscation," Broun said during the video posted July 10. Treaty negotiations fell apart in late July, but advocates are hopeful that a version will pass soon. We therefore took a closer look. PolitiFact Georgia asked Broun for more information, but his spokeswoman refused repeated requests for comment. We soldiered on. PolitiFact Georgia interviewed gun rights experts, especially those who, like Broun, are critical of gun control. We also consulted past PolitiFact coverage of the arms treaty. The U.N. has mulled over a treaty to regulate the global arms trade for years. Backers say it would curtail mass killings and terrorism, and keep dictators from killing their own people. (Small arms are not the treaty's sole focus, as Broun's description suggests. Conventional weapons, including guns, missiles and attack helicopters, are.) The George W. Bush administration rebuffed the effort, but the Obama administration said in 2009 that it is open to negotiations.

1947.text: NA-44535 PRIMARY A reactionary gun lobby EVERY YEAR guns are used to kill thousands of people, and not always the bad guys. For decades, doctors and other public health experts have argued that preventing gun deaths, particularly from accidents and suicides, should be a priority - like curtailing car fatalities or tobacco use. For their sense, the National Rifle Association (NRA) and others on the pro-gun side have pilloried them, ruining careers and stopping needed research with the help of a cowed Congress. Their latest victim is Vivek Hallegere Murthy, President Obama's pick to be surgeon general, whose nomination is languishing in the Senate after the NRA and various conservative commentators attacked him. The NRA is wrong, and senators who know better should find some backbone. Mr. Murthy is a Yale-trained physician, an instructor at Boston's prestigious Brigham and Women's Hospital and a health technology entrepreneur. As Mr. Obama's nominee for surgeon general, it's also no shock that he supports a variety of the president's policies, including on gun control. Among his sins, the NRA explained in a letter to Senate leaders, is past support for meek gun regulations such as licensing and waiting periods. He also once dared to claim that "guns are a health care issue," a fact that any doctor with experience in the emergency room knows well. Mr. Murthy, the NRA fumed, would remove the ban on physicians asking patients whether they keep guns in the home and lift restrictions on gun death research at the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

1946.text: NA-44626 PRIMARY Judge's handgun ruling has D.C. officials, police scrambling; D.C. police ordered not to make arrests on charge of "carrying a pistol without a license" The District's top lawyer on Monday sought to temporarily halt a federal court ruling allowing thousands of city residents with registered handguns - as well as legal gun owners from other states - to carry firearms on the streets of the nation's capital. The ruling, which upended the city's decades-old restrictions on carrying handguns in public, created confusion and concern as officials grappled with its implications. The law was declared unconstitutional in an opinion released over the weekend, leaving police scrambling to give clear direction to officers. D.C. Police Chief Cathy L. Lanier told her officers Monday that they could no longer stop someone simply for carrying a gun in public; they would need suspicion that a crime had been committed. For visitors who carry guns into the city, police will need to determine whether they are following the laws of their home state. "We understand that the immediate implementation of this ruling creates safety concerns for our members and our community," Lanier wrote in a memo to police officers Monday. She instructed them to "continue to serve the best interest of public safety while respecting individual rights." Police urged officers to contact a department lawyer, on call around the clock, with questions. D.C. officials have argued that the gun law - among the strictest in the nation - has been a pillar of their public safety strategy in a city that has historically been beset by gun violence. Mayor Vincent C. Gray (D) said Monday that he was "very troubled" by the decision. He appealed to people who live elsewhere to refrain from bringing guns into the city.

characters: D.C. Police Chief Cathy L. Lanier

1945.text: GUN-8451 PRIMARY EDITORIAL Beware of false savings Saddling Colorado gun buyers with all the costs of criminal  background checks may sound attractive to some members of the  legislature's Joint Budget Committee, especially with dwindling  state revenues, but we think it's a counterproductive proposal. Running instant background checks on prospective gun buyers  was turned back to the Colorado Bureau of Investigation after the  normal FBI Brady Act checks required under federal law failed to  turn up some local information that might have prevented some  ghastly killings. Gov. Owens reinstuted the CBI instant checks, and the  legislature later formalized his action and provided funding for  the program. Currently, those who buy guns at licensed gun stores  don't pay for the Brady checks. The instant checks, however, covered only sales by licensed  dealers, not private transactions at gun shows. After the  revelation that Columbine killers Eric Harris and Dylan Klebold  got three of their four firearms at gun shows, widespread public  pressure led to passage of Amendment 22, an initiated statute that  extended the criminal background check requirement to private  sales at gun shows. When two private parties agree on a gun sale, for example,  they go to a licensed gun dealer at the show, and the buyer pays a  $ 10 fee that supposedly compensates the dealer for doing required  paperwork and submitting an inquiry to the CBI. The Joint Budget Committee is looking at saving about $ 1.8  million yearly by having gun buyers pay all the costs - about  $ 14.80 per transaction by some estimates. The CBI hasn't had as  large a volume of instant checks as it had staffed up for.

1949.text: GUN-20178 PRIMARY Gun control advances, but what comes next? Historically, gun control laws are reactions, not precautions. After a rash of gangland killings, for instance, Congress passed the National Firearms Act in 1934, restricting the sale and ownership of machine guns. After a rash of assassinations, Congress passed the 1968 Gun Control Act, restricting mail-order sales and gun ownership by felons and the mentally ill. After a rash of gun-related violence, starting with the attempted assassination of President Reagan, Congress passed the 1993 Brady law, imposing background checks. Now, a rash of school killings is inspiring Congress to debate a new packet of firearm laws. The main goal is to close a loophole that allows people to buy weapons at gun shows without a background check. The Senate passed such a proposal last month amid much sound and fury; the House is girding itself for that fight this week. Any such laws are welcome, but Congress' episodic approach, echoed in the states, has left a patchwork of thousands of laws. More ominously, the lack of a binding and politically appealing theme threatens to squander the support for gun controls now blossoming across the nation for a comprehensive solution that balances the need for regulation against the demand for unrestricted individual privileges. Among women, a key voting bloc, support for tougher gun controls jumped from two-thirds to three-fourths in the week after the Columbine High School killings in Littleton, Colo., according to a recent Associated Press poll. And for the first time, a majority of Americans believes that tougher laws, which tend to forestall gun violence, are more desirable than tougher enforcement of existing laws, which is after the fact.

1951.text: GUN-10159 PRIMARY Mayor Rebukes Senators Fighting D.C. Gun Ban A U.S. senator from Texas revived legislation yesterday to repeal the District's ban on handguns, a proposal that provoked a heated response from Mayor Anthony A. Williams, who described it as an "assault" on the District's right to govern itself. Sen. Kay Bailey Hutchison, the Republican sponsoring the legislation, said the repeal is needed to allow District residents to protect themselves in their homes, where the law bars them from keeping guns. "It's a matter of self-defense," Hutchison said at a news conference at the U.S. Capitol, where she was joined by fellow GOP Sens. George Allen (Va.) and John Cornyn (Tex.). In a statement, Williams (D) described the proposal as "a vicious attack on representative democracy for District of Columbia residents" and urged Congress to take no further action. "I am incensed by any proposal that is an insult to the memory of the people who have died in this city due to gun violence  --  in particular the three children who have died from gun violence this year," he said. Citing a 17 percent decline in homicides since last year, the mayor said: "We're on the right track toward reducing crime in the District. We're working to get guns off the street, and we're doing that successfully." In September, the House passed a similar bill to repeal the District's ban on handguns and semiautomatic weapons, enacted in 1976. Sen. Larry E. Craig (R-Idaho) abandoned his legislation when it appeared that there was insufficient support.

1956.text: GUN-2656 PRIMARY GUN CONTROL WILL NEVER PREVENT CRIME It may make sense that the pictorial article Boynton buyback nets 95 guns'' merits a front-page spread in a recent Local News section, in keeping with the current hysteria your paper is so willing to maintain and feed. The text ridiculously addresses some of the reasons accidents may be avoided if there's no gun available (as though this were a reason for not having guns!), but it conveniently fails to identify how crimes may be prevented by disarming the citizenry! Neither does it address the fact that no criminals came to give their guns away, or would. Did you notice how effectively the lack of access to a gun kept Nicole Brown Simpson's barbarous assassin from doing his work? What will you do after all guns are turned in? (Which is, of course, impossible!) Ban kitchen knives and baseball bats? How are your ridiculous measures going to stop crime? That's what you should address in your gun control articles, the real problem - crime prevention. Manuel Gutierrez Boynton Beach

1950.text: GUN-3003 PRIMARY Brady law goes into effect today Named for Ronald Reagan's press secretary Jim Brady, who was disabled by an assassin's bullet in 1981, the Brady law is designed primarily to prevent convicted felons from shopping for weaponry in gun stores, said Bobby Browning of the Atlanta office of the U.S. Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms. "The old adage that criminals don't buy guns from gun stores is not true," Browning said. A 1991 Justice Department study found that 28 percent of prison inmates admitted going to a gun store first when they wanted a gun, according to the ATF. "I believe some of the licensed gun dealers and possibly some of the chief law enforcement officers are going to be surprised at the number of prohibited persons that try to buy guns after Feb. 28," Browning said. Criminal background checks won't "interfere greatly with rights of law-abiding citizens," said ATF spokesman Jack Killorin. "[But] it will interfere in the process where dangerous convicts can go get guns at the easiest place, with the biggest selection."

1953.text: GUN-11020 PRIMARY Mass shootings are a tragic fact of life in America There's nothing that prompts a livelier debate about gun control than a deadly mass shooting. And when the shooting victims -- slain or surviving -- include someone as prominent as a member of Congress or a federal judge, advocates on both sides of the debate seize the opportunity to advance their agenda. No sooner had the gun smoke cleared after Saturday's tragic massacre at a shopping plaza in Tucson, than a duel of words and statistics commenced pitting gun control and gun rights advocates. Folks such as Paul Helmke, president of the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence,  pointed out that Arizona ranks near the bottom in terms of state gun restrictions in a country that ranks near the bottom among nations of the world. Yet, people like John Lott, author of More Guns Less Crime,  dismissed the episode as an unfortunate aberration to the downward trending murder rate in Arizona and nationally. More frustrating than suffering the endless and unproductive tug of war over the gun issue is the sad truth that nothing short of the complete and impossible disarmament of America will eliminate the risk of the kind of incident that stunned the nation so deeply that President Obama called for a collective moment of silence to remember the dead and honor the wounded.   Notwithstanding changes over the years in regulations pertaining to background checks for gun purchasers, the temporary requirement for waiting periods, the passage and subsequence expiration of the assault weapons ban,  and the spread of concealed carry laws, the number of mass shootings in the USA -- averaging 20 per year -- has remained relatively stable over decades.

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence

1955.text: GUN-20083 PRIMARY The changing politics of guns -- Democrats back off on firearms WASHINGTON -- With little fanfare, the Bush administration is undoing or ignoring gun-control measures that were pursued aggressively by the Clinton administration. And to the delight of gun-rights activists, the Democratic opposition in Congress hasn't responded with the expressions of outrage and demands for redress that have met President Bush's actions on such issues as the Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty and environmental protection. The politics of guns are changing. Some Democratic leaders and key strategists are worried that a perception of Democrats as anti-gun is costing the party's candidates dearly among white men, rural residents and Southern voters. More than any other issue, some analysts say, unease about gun control helped defeat presidential candidate Al Gore in several traditionally Democratic Southern and border states -- any one of which would have been enough to put him in the White House. "We lost a number of voters who on almost every other issue realized they'd be better off with Al Gore," Connecticut Sen. Joe Lieberman, Gore's running mate, says of the gun issue. "They were anxious . . .   about what would happen if Al was elected. This one matters a lot to people who otherwise want to vote for us." Frustrated gun-control advocates argue that Democrats are overreacting. They note that gun control was a powerful issue that boosted Democratic Senate candidates and hurt Republican incumbents last fall in Missouri, Michigan and Washington state. The Democratic challengers in all three states won.

1948.text: GUN-10781 PRIMARY Letters to the editor Nixon is congratulated for signing new gun law Thanks to Gov. Jay Nixon for signing a bill that lowers the age for a concealed carry permit from 23 to 21. Some people predictably will belly ache about the new law, as they do about everything related to firearms and their lawful ownership and use. But the bill Mr. Nixon signed was supported overwhelmingly by both chambers of the Missouri Legislature, whose bipartisan membership has had plenty of experience and discussion on these issues. The naysayers have to like the important part of the new law that makes it a crime to knowingly solicit, persuade, encourage or entice any dealer or private seller to transfer a firearm or ammunition in an unlawful manner. No law will stop all illegal purchases, but scofflaw buyers and their straw parties now may have to fear more than a slap on the wrist. Robert Hutton - Chesterfield First, recover Regarding "Tech tricks lead to thief's door" (July 9): I have a question for St. Louis Metropolitan Police Chief Dan Isom: Isn't it possible to examine whether a particular theft or burglary is part of a crime pattern after the goods have been recovered? William Klopfenstein - Edwardsville Police need to catch up "Tech tricks lead to thief's door" (July 9), which described how the police ignored Alex Van Almsick's successful electronic sleuthing that led him to his stolen Macintosh, belies the public pronouncements about the police wanting the public's assistance in catching criminals.

characters: St. Louis Metropolitan Police Chief Dan Isom

1952.text: GUN-10571 PRIMARY 2 CSU campuses face different rules on weapons ban PUEBLO - Stun guns, Tasers and pepper spray would still be allowed in purses and backpacks at Colorado State University-Pueblo under a new weapons ban passed by the Board of Governors on Tuesday. However, students at CSU's Fort Collins campus won't have that option under a stricter version of a policy that officials stretched to meet the needs of individual campuses. "The goal of the board is to have avoided having a policy where one size fits all," said CSU spokesman Brad Bohlander. Still, the policy that drew support of the board and faculty at both the Fort Collins and Pueblo campuses is almost sure to attract the ire of gun-rights groups. "I think the race will be on to the courthouse," said Bradley Deats, a CSU parent and a certified National Rifle Association instructor. Rocky Mountain Gun Owners has threatened to lead the legal charge against the gun policy, which bans people from carrying licensed concealed weapons on both campuses. State Sen. Greg Brophy, R-Wray, has also said he will craft legislation to nullify the CSU policy, which he claims violates the state's concealed-weapons law. The Board of Governors on Tuesday made no comment on the passage of the policy, which will be enacted Aug. 1. The vote was 8-0, with one member - Scott C. Johnson - absent. CSU is one of the few universities in the country without a concealed-weapons ban. The university - in its new policy - said it concurs with a statement by the International Association of Campus Law Enforcement Administrators that says concealed weapons do not reduce violence.

characters: International Association of Campus Law Enforcement Administrators

1954.text: GUN-10203 PRIMARY Congress Fetches for the Gun Lobby Americans stunned at recent sprees of gun mayhem in churches, schools and courthouses should know that Congress is gearing up for action on this issue. No, lawmakers are not rushing through legislation to cut down on the 29,000 gun deaths a year. Rather, the Senate is preparing to revive a craven proposal to shield irresponsible gun manufacturers and dealers from accountability. The proposal, which failed narrowly last year, is now estimated to have majority support from lawmakers who have been either cowed or bolstered by the gun lobby. It would have stopped such lawsuits as the one that led to a $2.5 million settlement for the families of victims in the Washington sniper shootings. That rifle was traced to a West Coast dealer who claimed that it was one of 238 weapons suddenly missing from inventory. The Senate proposal would shelter such transparently harmful dealers and manufacturers and snuff out more than a dozen other suits now in the courts. The bill, offered this year by Senator Larry Craig, an Idaho Republican, goes further, undermining not just lawsuits but also administrative proceedings to revoke abusive dealers' licenses. Under it, gun dealers couldn't be disciplined for black-market deals involving illegal weapons unless it could be proved that they had engaged in them knowingly -- as if general ineptitude or a lack of interest were acceptable. Last year, Congress joined President Bush in letting lapse the 1994 law restricting the availability of battlefield assault rifles. Congress also mandated the destruction of gun-sale records within 24 hours. Lawmakers were only making things easier for potential terrorists and other sociopaths.

1957.text: GUN-11944 PRIMARY Supreme Court real culprit in tragedy Much will be written about Sandy Hook Elementary School attempting to make sense out of what is beyond any sense or rational thought. What motivated someone to murder little children in cold blood? Why wasn't there timely intervention? Weren't there signs that indicated the shooter was going to act? Unfortunately, the real culprit is the Supreme Court; specifically, the five justices who have made a virtual laughingstock of legislation designed to keep firearms out of the hands of irresponsible people. In 2008 and 2010, by 5-4 decisions, the Roberts court ruled that the Second Amendment guarantees the right to own firearms for self-defense. The court's argument was that "law-abiding" citizens have this guarantee. All of this jurisprudence sounds good on paper, but it doesn't help justify what occurred in Connecticut in the real world. When innocent children and educators are the victims of someone who could easily claim that he was only exercising his court-sanctioned constitutional rights, injustice is the victor. CARL LYLE Boynton Beach It's time to amend Second Amendment When our society gets over its obsession with violence and guns, works to eliminate violence in TV and movies and stops buying computer games that are violent, then we may change. When our elected officials stop dancing to the tune of the National Rifle Association, then we may change. We know a gun doesn't by itself kill people; someone pulls the trigger. But the accessibility of all kinds of guns creates an atmosphere in which people find it easier to express their anger by using guns. Allowing guns to be carried in all public places puts me and everyone else at risk. Hunting, sport or home protection? OK. But multiple guns?

1962.text: GUN-10766 PRIMARY Don't seal the lips of my doctor, gun lobby Excuse me, but when I'm seeing my doctor, could the gun lobby kindly step out of the examining room? Thanks so much. Yes, I know the National Rifle Association is perfectly comfortable striding about Tallahassee poking lawmakers in the chests and telling them what to do. (Always wondered: Do they have photos involving Barbies, or goats, or what?) But when it comes to what's between me and my doctor - and you and yours - the gun lobby should have no place at the examining table or anywhere else in the mix. And some doctors are hoping a federal judge will soon agree and stop the first-of-its-kind Florida doctor gag law. A legislative session that surely set a record for different ways to punk the people of this state brought us a new law that says a doctor or other health care worker may not ask a patient whether there are guns in the home unless the doctor has a "relevant" reason. (Next, the beef industry convinces legislators that doctors have some nerve asking how many weekly cheeseburgers you consume.) Now, I don't know what kind of doctor you go to, but mine doesn't waste a lot of chatty time on things he finds "irrelevant." Doctors ask gun and other safety-related questions - are potentially dangerous household products safely stored? - confidentially and with an eye toward prevention. They are, after all, the ones who deal with the aftermath of accidents and tragedies including gunshots. Pediatricians ask about guns as they would pools or bike helmets.

1959.text: GUN-30420 PRIMARY Assembly supports anti-gang measures; They would toughen gun laws and offer protection to witnesses. A sponsor called the effort "triage." A package of bills aimed at curtailing gang and gun violence throughout New Jersey advanced through the Assembly yesterday. The 17 bills would toughen gun laws, offer more protection to crime witnesses, and educate children about the perils of street gangs. Majority Leader Bonnie Watson Coleman (D., Mercer), who guided the bills through the Assembly, called them "triage" for a gang violence epidemic that involves an estimated 17,000 members and was responsible for 1,900 criminal cases last year. "Together, this package seeks to protect our communities, protect our children, protect our witnesses, and give the law enforcement community additional tools to crack down on some of this," she said. "This is a triage package. It is responding to the crisis we're experiencing not just in our urban centers, but across the state of New Jersey." Among other things, the measures would increase penalties for possessing assault weapons and giving guns to minors, regulate the sale of ammunition, and require elementary-school seminars on preventing gun violence. The bills would also add gang-crime information to Uniform Crime Report data and prohibit disclosure of addresses and other personal information about grand jury witnesses. The bills head to the Senate Law, Public Safety and Veterans Affairs Committee. Its chairman, State Sen. John Girgenti (D., Hawthorne), has long had an interest in curbing the influence of gangs. The measures had bipartisan support in the Assembly, but it was not unanimous.

1960.text: GUN-4701 PRIMARY Union to judge: Quit letting cops lose jobs to new gun law; Four Fulton deputies have been fired because anyone guilty of domestic violence cannot carry firearms. Fearing that dozens of Fulton County deputies soon may lose their jobs, a police union has asked a federal judge to strike down a law banning the possession of firearms by anyone convicted of domestic violence. In recent weeks, four Fulton deputies have been fired because of the law, said Chip Warren of the International Brotherhood of Police Officers. "We've been told between 35 and 40 more qualify" out of the 950- member Fulton County Sheriff's Department, he said. A federal lawsuit was filed Friday by former Deputy William S. Hiley, who was fired Jan. 14. In August 1995, Hiley, who worked at the jail, pleaded no contest to domestic violence charges and was sentenced to a year's probation. Hiley, 31, twice has been charged with domestic violence, according to court records. He pleaded no contest to battery charges stemming from both incidents, in January and March 1995. He also was fined $ 300. Hiley and the police union have asked U.S. District Judge Richard Freeman in Atlanta to issue an injunction against Fulton County and stop it from firing any other deputies who may qualify under the law. The law, enacted in September, prohibits gun use by people convicted of any crimes of domestic abuse, including misdemeanors such as simple assault or harassment. Although some police departments across the nation have interpreted the law to mean officers may carry guns on the job but not off duty, the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms has said an officer convicted of domestic abuse cannot possess a gun "for any purpose."

characters: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms

1963.text: GUN-836 PRIMARY EX-AGENT URGES ASSAULT GUN BAN; SECURITY TIGHT AS WEAPONS DISPLAYED AT HOUSE HEARING A former FBI agent, wounded and outgunned by the firepower of semi-automatic rifles in a 1986 Miami shoot-out, told a House committee Wednesday to ban assault weapons because "enough is enough." "You've got to do something," John Hanlon, one of five agents shot during a battle with two bank robbery suspects in April 1986, told the House Criminal Justice Committee. The two suspects were killed after they had shot two FBI agents to death. The committee took unusual security precautions for the hearing, including posting armed guards and metal detectors at the entrances to the committee room. Committee Chairman Ron Silver, D-North Miami Beach, said the precautions were because of death threats made to Rep. David Flagg, who has announced plans to file a bill that would ban automatic weapons. The committee also wanted to avoid problems arising from the display of a dozen semi-automatic rifles and submachine guns in the committee room, said Silver's aide, Robin Hassler. The beefed up security and rumored threats were dismissed as potential political manuevering by Marion Hammer, the National Rifle Association's Florida lobbyist. "Why didn't you have metal detectors here when my life was threatened," she asked Silver after the meeting. She said later that it's a "distinct possibility" supporters of the assault weapon ban threatened Flagg's life "to make it appear it was us." At the hearing, Attorney General Bob Butterworth testified that the proliferation of assault weapons in South Florida have turned emergency rooms to "urban M*A*S*H units where doctors and medical personnel are treating wounds once only seen on the battlefield."

1961.text: GUN-1075 PRIMARY Washington Talk; Gun Measure Is Lesson in Geography The Brady bill is the first tough domestic issue the House will face this year, and the whole issue of gun control has stirred intense ideological passions. But next week's vote will not be an occasion for rounding up the usual stereotypes of the 101st Congress. The fight over the Brady bill, which would impose a seven-day waiting period on handgun purchases, is not really a fight between liberals and conservatives or Republicans and Democrats. Neither party's leadership is demanding, or even counting, votes. This issue is intensely individual and representative. There are divisions that offer a better clue to how the roll-calls will break than the traditional political analyses; they are geographical. Like the major battles of the last century, over railroads and banks, this one pits the East against the South and the West and, most of all, divides the House on rural-versus-urban lines. Consider two outspoken Wisconsin Congressmen. Representative David R. Obey is one of the most dogged liberal Democrats in the House, and he is leaning strongly against the Brady bill. Representative F. James Sensenbrenner Jr. is one of the House's most conservative Republicans. National Journal, the Washington weekly of government, found that last year he voted more conservatively than 89 percent of the House on social issues and 98 percent on economic issues. Although rarely found backing any measure that can be attacked as increasing the power of government over the individual, he is a supporter of this bill.

1964.text: GUN-9383 PRIMARY Hastert, DeLay Disagree on Assault Weapons Vote In a rare split with his top deputy, Speaker J. Dennis Hastert (R-Ill.) said yesterday that House Republican leaders have not made a final decision on whether to allow the 1994 ban on semiautomatic assault weapons to expire next year. Two days after House Majority Leader Tom DeLay (R-Tex.) infuriated gun control advocates by saying the GOP-led House would let the assault weapons ban expire next year, Hastert raised the possibility of a future vote to extend it. Hastert said he wants to talk with President Bush, who supports extending the ban, before making a final call. "I am not ready to make that decision," Hastert said. Hastert and other GOP leaders were concerned that DeLay was making unilateral decisions without consulting them, said several Republican aides, and attracting attention to the politically sensitive topic of banning certain semiautomatic firearms 16 months before the law expires. Still, Hastert stopped short of promising a vote, and two GOP leadership aides said they doubted one will take place. At issue is a 1994 law that made it illegal to import, "manufacture, transfer or possess" 19 types of semiautomatic weapons. The law does not apply to the sale or possession of the weapons if they were legally held before the ban took effect. Unless extended by the House and Senate, the law will expire on Sept. 13, 2004. Bush has supported an extension since the 2000 presidential campaign, so the National Rifle Association and other gun rights activists are counting on the Republican-controlled House to end the prohibition. On Tuesday, DeLay told reporters that most House members oppose the ban, and his spokesman, Stuart Roy, said Republicans would not bring up legislation to extend it. The NRA cheered the statement, but Senate Democrats denounced it.

1965.text: GUN-7786 PRIMARY Gun Debate Pits County's Past, Present; Hunters, Suburbs Collide On Proposed Restrictions When Alison Trent heard the sharp crack of gunfire near her yard last year, she rushed out of her Loudoun County home to find a neighbor plinking away at a target against a makeshift wooden backstop. Fearful of stray bullets, she rounded up her children, took them inside, then went into action. Trent gathered signatures from dozens of her neighbors in the remote Goose Creek Farms subdivision off Lime Kiln Road west of Route 15, an area known for its lush and rugged landscape. In November, she delivered the signatures to her supervisor, Jim Burton (I-Mercer), who proposed a new countywide ordinance that would ban the discharge of firearms within 150 yards of any building, road or public land. "I haven't been hit yet. I kind of want to avoid that; that's why I approached Mr. Burton," Trent said. But in a county where hunting geese, deer, doves and turkeys is a longtime tradition, the proposed restrictions have come under fire, pointing up the gulf between suburban and country values in the fast-changing county. The debate is taking place as neighboring Fauquier County is considering a gun control initiative that would require fingerprinting for those who apply to carry a concealed weapon. It also comes as the Million Mom March anti-gun rally is gearing up in Washington this weekend. Opponents say Burton's proposal is misguided.

1958.text: GUN-1775 PRIMARY HOUSE PANEL PASSES MAJOR ANTI-CRIME BILL VOICE VOTE ADVANCES $1.1 BILLION; PACKAGE WASHINGTON - A sweeping anti-crime bill that would expand the death penalty and ban certain assault-style weapons cleared the House Judiciary Committee on Thursday as Democratic leaders promised House action this fall.   The $1.1 bill ion package - which would help state and local governments put more police on the streets, improve programs for youthful offenders and give more prisoners drug treatment - was passed on a voice vote after 3 1/2 days of debate.   House Speaker Thomas S. Foley, D-Wash., said the full House would debate the measure next month.   Rep. Charles E. Schumer, D-N.Y., said he was optimistic that the fights over the death penalty and other issues that blocked passage last year of major crime legislation would be overcome this year.   ''The old ideological fights are losing their strength,'' said Schumer, who heads the panel's crime subcommittee.   But Republicans are expected to wage floor fights as they did last year to make it easier to impose the death penalty and further limit the ability of state prisoners to challenge their sentences in federal court.   A floor fight on the assault-weapon ban also is expected, although gun-control backers scored a big victory last spring when the House approved a seven-day waiting period for handgun purchases.   The legislation, known as the Brady bill, is named after former White House press secretary James S. Brady. He was left disabled when he was shot in the head when a gunman shot President Ronald Reagan in 1981. The Senate's crime bill includes a modified version of the Brady bill. The Senate bill requires a five-day waiting period for handgun purchases while local police check criminal histories of prospective buyers.   President George Bush, who chided lawmakers for their inability to pass a major crime bill last year, has demanded enactment of several controversial provisions as a condition for signing gun-control legislation.   Bush has sought an expanded federal death penalty, wider latitude for police to conduct searches and more limits on habeas corpus petitions used by inmates on death row to delay their executions.   Bush's administration praised the bill that passed the Senate in July, even though it did not contain all the provisions Bush sought.   The House bill would authorize executions for 48 federal crimes, including terrorism, espionage, treason and political assassinations.   But unlike the president's proposal that the Senate accepted, it generally would require prosecutors to prove the defendant intended to kill the murder victim to obtain a death sentence.

1969.text: GUN-10626 PRIMARY GUN SALES; Firearms on more wish lists this year Little Ralphie from the classic holiday movie "A Christmas Story" might have been onto something with his dreams of unwrapping a shiny new Red Ryder BB gun. Turns out, many Americans share the bespectacled, tow-headed boy's desire for firepower. And they have made out their wish lists for Santa accordingly. FBI personal background checks, which are required before an individual can buy guns, hit an all-time high of 129,166 on Black Friday. That figure shattered the previous record of 97,848, set on Black Friday of 2008, by 32 percent. In Georgia, FBI personal background checks recorded during the month of November are at a three-year high of 33,484. December figures were not yet available for comparison with last year. But if November's uptick continued, it's likely that plenty of people will be testing their trigger fingers come Sunday. Jeremy Powell, general manager at the Bulls-Eye Indoor Range & Gun Shop in Lawrenceville, said his store has seen sales climb every year since it opened in 1991. And this Christmas season sales are up about 10 percent over last year. Previous spikes in sales came in 1999 during the Y2K scare and in 2008, after the election of President Barack Obama, when many would-be gun owners feared that the Democratic leader would usher in tough anti-gun laws, Powell said. The majority of Bulls-Eye's business this year has been in handguns and firearms used more for personal protection. Powell said compact guns that fit in a pocket or a purse are especially hot sellers.

1968.text: NA-43894 PRIMARY McCain: Background check package can likely win support of most senators Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.) expressed optimism Sunday that a measure to expand background checks on gun purchases could win support from most senators. "There [are] some senators, bipartisan again, Sen. [Tom] Coburn (R-Okla.) and Sen. [Joe] Manchin (D-W.Va.) and some others who are working on package that I think that most of us will be able to support," McCain said on NBC News' "Meet The Press." The group McCain referred to has been working to develop a proposal to expand background check requirements on gun purchases. Under current law, transactions involving licensed gun dealers require background checks, while others do not. There is a growing consensus on Capitol Hill that a law requiring universal background checks stands a better chance of winning passage than a proposed ban on assault-style weapons.

1967.text: GUN-5916 PRIMARY GUN CONTROL MAY FALL TO PARTISAN INFIGHTING LEGISLATORS SAY THAT THE BILL PASSED BY THE SENATE COULD FAIL IN THE HOUSE AS BOTH PARTIES MANEUVER. The post-Littleton gun-control proposals that barely survived the Senate now face such intense partisan infighting in the House that some legislators are saying that the measures could be doomed. House Republicans are trying to water down the Senate-passed gun provisions while pushing Democrats to accept tougher juvenile-crime penalties in exchange for new limits on firearms sales. But some Democrats are warning that any changes in what had been a delicately balanced juvenile-justice bill could draw Democratic opposition, and perhaps even spoil chances to enact any legislation - gun restrictions and all. "If they put stuff in the bill that we don't agree with, it could endanger its passage," Rep. Steny Hoyer (D., Md.) said. At the center of the debate is a Senate provision that would make it more difficult for minors, felons, and the mentally ill to buy weapons at gun shows with no questions asked. Some GOP House members plan to move to dilute, but not remove, this provision. Democrats have said that they will strive to keep the Senate version intact. The dispute is playing itself out before the House Judiciary Committee, which is set to write new juvenile crime legislation this week, legislation that has become, in the wake of Littleton, a vehicle for new gun rules. The Democrats also are expected to resist a Republican move to toughen the punitive features of the House's version of the juvenile-justice bill by requiring longer mandatory minimum sentences for juveniles convicted of gun crimes. Democrats prefer that local judges be given more case-by-case leeway in meting out punishment for such offenses.

1970.text: GUN-3113 PRIMARY LETTERS; THE BATTLE OVER GUN LAWS; Voting members The statistics in the most recent anti-NRA editorial are meaningless. The pro-gunners also have bunches of numbers to prove their points, and they are meaningless, too. The NRA is an open lobby, meaning anyone can join. More than 3 million members pay dues to assure that their voice will be heard in Washington. These are not industry people with special financial interests, but voting Americans who believe that free people should be allowed to responsibly own guns. Fighting back MARJORIE H. DAVIS, Decatur Polls show overwhelming support for some kind of gun control; so what happens in Georgia? A heavily financed, well-organized NRA controls both the state Legislature and the governor. What can we do? We can keep track of those who voted for the bill in both the Senate and the House, and let our votes tell them what we think. We can also do what the NRA does: Develop a strong group of supporters. We must support groups like Georgians Against Gun Violence with our dollars and our volunteer time. No resistance SUZAN SATTERFIELD, Norcross I'd like to be able to explain to my children why our Georgia legislators have voted for some of the most liberal gun un-control laws in the country. Try as I might, I can't see what's so awful about waiting five days before owning a deadly weapon. An "instant" background check sounds great - until you remember all the times your own bank can't even access your records because their "computers are down." (I'm sure the government system will be much more efficient, though.)

1966.text: GUN-8136 PRIMARY McCain ads urge end to gun-show loophole WASHINGTON - John McCain is bringing his maverick appeal to  the Colorado campaign to close the so-called gun-show loophole. The Republican senator from Arizona is appearing in  television advertisements, asking voters to approve a state ballot  initiative requiring background checks at gun shows. The ads  started Wednesday and will swamp the airwaves by this weekend. 'I think that if the Congress won't act, the least I can do  is support the initiative in states where it's on the ballot,'  McCain said in an interview. The former presidential contender was recruited by New York  dot-com billionaire Andrew McKelvey to bring star power to a new  $ 8 million national gun-control campaign. The McCain ad is the  public kickoff for McKelvey's Americans for Gun Safety. The organization will spend about $ 500,000 in Colorado,  including $ 150,000 it is contributing directly to initiative  sponsor SAFE Colorado, said president Jon Cowan. It plans to spend  another $ 500,000 in support of a similar gun-show initiative in  Oregon. The ad was filmed recently in Washington, when McCain was  recovering from skin-cancer surgery. In it, he complains that  criminals have been able to buy guns at gun shows because  background checks aren't required. Licensed dealers must run  background checks at shows or their stores, but at shows, some vendors are unlicensed, private sellers who don't run background  checks. Opponents of the initiative say that only a small number of  guns used in crimes come from gun shows and that the government  should stay out of private gun sales.

1971.text: GUN-465 PRIMARY HOUSE SUBCOMMITTEE BACKS WAITING-PERIOD HANDGUN BILL WASHINGTON - A House Judiciary subcommittee handed the National Rifle Association a legislative defeat on Tuesday by approving a bill to establish a seven-day waiting period for the purchase of pistols.   The bill is intended to give local law-enforcement authorities time to conduct background checks on buyers of pistols to determine if they may legally buy the weapon. Former White House press secretary James Brady, after whom the bill is named, smiled broadly from his wheelchair in the packed hearing room after the 6-4 vote. Brady, who was shot in the head during an assassination attempt on President Ronald Reagan, had lobbied for the bill.   The bill must still be approved by the Judiciary Committee and the full House.   The NRA has lobbied against the bill, arguing that it amounts to de facto registration of pistols and that it does little to curb possession of pistols by criminals.

1972.text: GUN-2083 PRIMARY The problem of increasing violence is all too real Re: Citizens do not need guns in 1993, letter to the editor. Don't they? The writer claims she "just doesn't remember the last news story about a  murder by a criminal." This is either a case of selective reading or  selective memory. Hardly a day passes without at least one hair-raising tale  of car-jacking, home invasion or rape - often fatal to the victims.   Overpopulation and a revolving-door penal system, among other factors, have  made a means of self-defense much more necessary today than it ever was in the  past. Even George F. Will - certainly no friend of the NRA - in his column on  March 28, was forced to admit the validity of the NRA's contention that gun  controls, in society as it exists today, merely disarm potential victims. He also quotes the street-smart saying: "We'd rather the police catch us  with it (a gun) than the other guys catch us without it." This vividly  illustrates another adverse effect of strict gun laws. They create a whole new  class of "criminal"; those who feel bound to obey the only real "higher  law": the law of self-preservation. No, it isn't "the guns, stupid." The incidence of increasing violence is  all too real, but a significant part of the problem is the pressure of those self-anointed sociologists whose simplistic answer to every issue is: "There ought to be a law!"

1973.text: GUN-30735 PRIMARY Challenges to Bans On Handguns Begin Using the new judicial muscle provided by the Supreme Court's affirmation of the right to bear arms, the National Rifle Association and another pro-gun group sued San Francisco and its housing authority on Friday to invalidate a ban on handguns in public housing. The lawsuit said the ban violated the Second Amendment and ''renders responsible, law-abiding adult public housing residents especially vulnerable.'' But officials here and in other cities where gun restrictions are now being challenged took a defiant stance. As the lawsuit was being filed, San Francisco officials held a news conference in the city's hardscrabble Western Addition neighborhood to announce a series of antigun measures. Mayor Gavin Newsom said the timing was coincidental, but apt. Mr. Newsom, who said he suspected that the rifle association might also sue to overturn a local ordinance requiring trigger-locks, challenged N.R.A. officials to come to his city and spend time in public housing developments, which he said were often overrun with weapons. ''We don't happen to believe that it's good public policy in public housing sites where guns and violence is the highest in our city and, for that matter, respectively, in cities across America, to say 'Hey, come on in; let's everybody get guns,' '' said Mr. Newsom, a Democrat. In an interesting turn in a city known for its embrace of gay rights, the chief plaintiff in the suit against the city is a gay  man living in a public housing development, owned by the federal government, who wants to have a gun to protect himself from potential hate crimes.

1976.text: GUN-2281 PRIMARY 'ZERO TOLERANCE' AUTHOR VOWS TO RETURN GUN PERMIT Supervisor Don Perata, the author of Alameda County's "zero tolerance" policy on guns, said Tuesday he is returning his concealed weapons permit. Perata said many people had accused him of being a hypocrite for carrying a gun while wanting to send to jail anyone who illegally uses a gun. "It looks like I'm a privileged class, and I'm asking for safety other people can't have," said Perata, who is running for state controller. "It looks like a contradiction, and I don't want to be part of a contradiction." Perata said he got the permit about 12 years ago when he received a death threat. Although he does not carry the gun at all times, he felt he needed it because he still receives threats. Last week, Perata said, someone telephoned him at 7:48 a.m. one day and said, "All you that think you can take our guns will die trying. You better be real careful if you go outside." Perata said he will mail the permit back sometime this week.

1974.text: GUN-8788 PRIMARY 'Smart gun' bill clears N.J. hurdle; The Assembly voted to require technology that blocks unauthorized users. Senate reapproval is expected. The Assembly yesterday approved a bill requiring that new handguns sold in New Jersey contain technology that would prevent unauthorized users from firing them. The bill, however, would not restrict gun sales until three years after the state attorney general determined such technology was commercially available. When Gov. McGreevey signs the "smart gun" bill - as he has said he will - New Jersey will become the first state to legislate the use of this technology as a method of gun control. Because it has been modified, the bill first must return to the Senate, which had approved the old measure, 29-3. Senate leaders have indicated their support. The Assembly debated the bill for nearly two hours, mostly dividing along party lines. Many opponents abstained from voting so they would not be characterized as foes of gun control. The result was 47 votes in favor, 11 votes in opposition, and 19 abstentions. Republican opponents and a single Democrat, Deputy Majority Leader Neil M. Cohen of Union County, contended it was premature to pass a law requiring a technology that is still being developed. Nine Republicans voted in favor of the bill. No Democrats voted against it, although four abstained. "Why is there a need to rush this through the legislature today?" Assemblyman Richard A. Merkt (R., Morris) asked. "There is no way to know when this technology will be reliable or even available as a consumer product. Counting on future technology is like buying a pig in a poke."

1978.text: NA-42794 PRIMARY Watershed: Teaching About Gun Control After Newtown Overview | What should happen in response to the shootings at Sandy Hook Elementary School?   How should the nation try to prevent similar tragedies from occurring in the future? In this lesson, students will explore the political process that was set in motion by the Dec. 14 school attack that killed 20 children and 6 staff members.   They will research the issues and history behind gun control in the United States and try to predict what sort of bill might eventually emerge from Congress. Materials | Computers for individual students or groups to read articles from The New York Times online, or copies of articles as needed. Warm-Up | Tell students: By now you have all heard about the school shootings in Newtown, Conn. Twenty-eight people, including 20 children, were killed by a man carrying four of his mother's guns. And President Obama said he was determined to introduce new laws to make sure this cannot happen again. But what exactly does that mean? What changes to the law could prevent such a terrible thing from happening? Any ideas? Write student brainstorming ideas on the board under the heading "possible laws." Do you think this will be easy, or are there reasons this might be either challenging or a bad idea? Write brainstorming ideas under the heading "possible obstacles." Over the next few days we are going to look at these issues and see if we might be able to predict the sort of law that the president might introduce and Congress might be able to pass. But first, let's read an article about what happened in Newtown before the shootings, when residents tried to pass local laws restricting gun use.

1975.text: GUN-2647 PRIMARY SENATE CONFEREES BACK ASSAULT-GUN BAN The much-debated ban on military-style assault weapons appeared destined for final approval after Senate negotiators voted yesterday to keep it in the crime bill, and a leading House member said House conferees were expected to do the same today. The ban was approved, 5-4, by the Senate conferees as the long-delayed crime legislation moved toward final congressional approval. "There isn't any reason why semiautomatic assault weapons should be on the streets of America," said Sen. Howard M. Metzenbaum (D., Ohio). Without the ban, he said, the crime bill "isn't worth a tinker's damn." Sen. Orrin G. Hatch (R., Utah) called the provision "just a game" and "a misleading substitute for fighting crime." He said that less than 1 percent of violent crimes were committed with guns that the provision would ban. These firearms are singled out for criticism, he said, because they "look sinister." On the House side, Rep. Henry J. Hyde (R., Ill.) said he would provide the key vote today for the ban, which would prohibit the future possession or sale of 19 military-style weapons and copycat models. As of yesterday, House negotiators appeared poised to approve the ban on a 6-4 vote. Similar to measures previously passed by both the House and Senate, the provision also would limit magazine capacity to 10 rounds. It would, however, exempt 650 named firearms and all guns legally owned when the law took effect.

1980.text: GUN-31423 PRIMARY READERS WRITE Protecting gun rights: Bill introduced after Va. Tech shooting The Virginia Tech shootings were tragic. As a father of three daughters, my thoughts and prayers go to those who lost loved ones on that day. However, the opinion column "Sensible gun laws needed" (@issue, Jan. 10), made false claims against me in relation to this national tragedy. The bill mentioned in the column, which I opposed, was not introduced in Congress until June 11, 2007, almost two months after the Virginia Tech shooting. This makes it impossible for that bill to have somehow prevented the shootings from occurring. The bill, in fact, would not have prevented the shootings because it does not change existing law that should have required the Virginia Tech killer, Seung-Hui Cho, to have been included on the National Instant Criminal Background Check System. Virginia has already changed the way it reports individuals committed to a mental institution for treatment as a result of its oversight. My objection to the bill was based upon the threat it posed to the Second Amendment rights of veterans and other law-abiding citizens. I was able to address these concerns with Sen. Charles Schumer (D-N.Y.), after which the bill was quickly approved. It is important that even in the aftermath of tragedy, Congress not rush to pass well-intentioned bills without considering the full impact, such as limiting the rights of innocents.

1981.text: GUN-7550 PRIMARY O'DONNELL STICKING TO HER GUNS Rosie O'Donnell, a staunch gun-control advocate, is defending the concealed-weapon permit her son's bodyguard applied for. "Whether or not my family is in need of armed guards, that doesn't change my position on gun control," the talk show host told People magazine. "It's not inconsistent." National Rifle Association officials and others have accused O'Donnell of hypocrisy since the Greenwich Time, her local paper in Connecticut, reported May 25 that a bodyguard who will accompany her 5-year-old son to kindergarten has applied for a gun permit. O'Donnell, the master of ceremonies at the recent Million Mom March, says the bodyguard will not carry the gun to school.

1983.text: GUN-9565 PRIMARY Pushing Ill-Fated Bills on Principle Alone Among the 3,000 bills filed before the Virginia General Assembly every January are two carried year after year by Fairfax County Democrats: local ordinances banning firearms in government buildings and recreation centers and adding sexual orientation to the county's anti-discrimination laws. The bills die quick deaths in GOP-controlled legislative committees, receiving little or no debate as Democratic lawmakers resign themselves to the will of their Republican colleagues. While gun restrictions and protections for gays are popular in large swaths of the Washington suburbs, they are anathema to many social conservatives in Virginia's rural corners. And such views increasingly dominate the legislature's GOP majority. So why keep pushing bills that don't have a prayer of becoming law? Two Republican county supervisors, Michael R. Frey (Sully) and Elaine N. McConnell (Springfield), chided their Democratic colleagues last week for resurrecting the two measures in the board's package of legislative priorities for the coming session in Richmond. "I don't think that we are helping ourselves by pushing those two issues," Frey said, threatening to oppose the package. The Board of Supervisors then voted on the gun and anti-discrimination measures separately. McConnell later said the board should "save our guns for big battles"  -- such as securing more state road money and authority to raise some local taxes to spread out the burden of real estate taxes. The seven Democratic supervisors who outnumber McConnell and Frey say that folding on principles they and their constituents believe in would be tantamount to giving up on the civil rights movement because it was unpopular at the time.

1979.text: GUN-10303 PRIMARY Letters to the Editor Using murder to promote ideology Your audacity in blaming the death of wildlife conservation officer David Grove on lax gun laws ("Blame lax gun laws," Wednesday) to suit your antigun philosophy is immeasurable. Using the young officer's murder to suit your needs at a time when his family and the entire Pennsylvania Game Commission are grieving is just plain wrong. Almost assuredly the alleged murderer shot and killed Grove because he knew that a strong anticrime law regarding firearms possessed illegally by a felon would put him in jail for the third time - pretty much automatically. That hardly seems like a lax law. Spout your antigun propaganda, but don't make a bad situation worse for Grove's family and coworkers. John W. McGonigle West Chester Mcg4guns@aol.com Show necessity for reasonable gun laws We the people must show Harrisburg lawmakers the absolute necessity for reasonable gun laws to cut down on the more than 1,200 Pennsylvanians who die every year in gun-related incidents ("Blame lax gun laws," Wednesday). To do this, we must expand the coalition of those backing sensible gun-safety regulations to include younger voters, who I believe are underrepresented in the movement. Younger people must also understand they are touched by gun violence, and every death brings horrific pain. If enough young people join the movement, more legislators will see their jobs depend on doing the right thing.

1977.text: GUN-498 PRIMARY N.J. gov set to sign toughest weapon ban Disbelieving New Jersey gun enthusiasts and store owners are stunned and seething as Gov. James Florio prepares to sign the nation's toughest ban on assault weapons. Many doubted that Florio could overcome opposition mounted by the National Rifle Association and gun owners. But even as 3,000 demonstrators rallied last week at the Statehouse, the Legislature passed the ban. It requires owners of assault weapons to turn them in within a year. There are an estimated 300,000 assault weapons in the state, officials say. Assemblyman Michael Adubato says the law ''sends a message throughout the country: We can't allow any group, no matter how well-intentioned, to dictate to the majority.'' Gun enthusiasts are quick to point out state statistics that show assault weapons weren't used in any crimes last year. Counters Steve Perskie, Florio's chief of staff: ''I don't think we're required to wait for a tragedy to occur.'' He says Florio was simply following up on a campaign promise and on what voters want. Polls during 1989's Democratic primary race showed 4-to-1 support for a ban. Store owners say the law will devastate their business. David Delph, 46, of the Gun Exchange in Palisades Park, says more than one- third of his sales are in the banned guns. '' I won't survive it,'' he says. Some, like systems analyst Martin Dodt, 30, of Hopatcong, say they won't comply. The owner of several semiautomatic weapons he uses for target shooting says: ''It's going to make me a criminal.''

1985.text: GUN-4096 PRIMARY Editorials: Health; Responsible gun policies save lives This is a fact: American children are killing and being killed with guns. Since 1983, gun homicides by juveniles have nearly tripled while homicides using other weapons have declined, according to a recent report for the U.S. Department of Justice. Today, more than three-quarters of killings by youths are committed with firearms. Guns are also involved in the vast majority of killings of 10-to 17- year-olds, explaining in part why homicide is now the leading cause of death of Fulton County youths. Few would disagree: Putting a gun in the hand of a teenager is like putting a drunken driver behind the wheel of a car a dangerous and dumb. President Clinton's announcement this week of a plan to curb the illegal sale of guns to youths is therefore good news, whether politically inspired or not. Under a pilot program in 17 cities, including Atlanta, local and federal law enforcement will join forces to trace the sale of guns used by juveniles. As U.S. Attorney Kent Alexander pointed out: "Guns don't fall from the sky into kids' arms. They come from adults." Even gun lobbyists are endorsing the program, which they say appropriately targets those who illegally sell guns to juveniles while vindicating law-abiding gun dealers. Their support is a responsible reaction. By contrast, congressional Republicans are acting irresponsibly in their renewed assault against legitimate gun research by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Last month, the House Appropriations Committee stripped the $ 2.6 million in the CDC's budget for gun research, dictating that the money be used instead to recruit rural doctors. This proves that members remain firmly in the hold of the gun lobby.

characters: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

1982.text: GUN-2013 PRIMARY Building Stronger Cases in Gun Trials There is no question that on the night of Dec. 13, 1992, at least one of two men arrested in East Flatbush, Brooklyn, violated state gun laws. Lester Smith and Orson Grosse were pulled over by Police Officer Michael Dalton and his partner for a routine traffic violation. In the car the police found a loaded 10-millimeter Glock semiautomatic, a $600 gun. Neither man had a permit. Even if one did, the gun is supposed to be transported unloaded, in a locked box in the trunk. A Brooklyn assistant district attorney, Kyle Reeves, called it a "stronger than average" gun case. His first offer to the 21-year-old Mr. Grosse was 1 to 3 years. (The driver of the car disappeared; there is a bench warrant for his arrest.) The prosecutor's final offer was 30 days, again refused. Mr. Grosse probably would have been offered probation, except that illegal guns are such a menace that the Brooklyn District Attorney, Charles J. Hynes, has implemented a policy of seeking prison time in all gun cases. More Complex Than It Seemed But this apparently simple case, which went to trial last month, turned out to be not so simple, and its resolution has much to say about the complexities of law enforcement in New York City. It mirrors the forces at work in both the high-profile, racially charged Crown Heights murder trial last year and in the daily grind of Brooklyn Supreme Court.

1988.text: GUN-8928 PRIMARY LETTERS TO THE EDITOR Mishap doesn't show need for gun laws The Aug. 13 editorial regarding a pistol fired in Rep. Bob Barr's presence has a clear anti-gun agenda. Barr's friend foolishly stored it loaded, failed to empty it before presenting it for inspection and probably fired it himself. The 1908 Colt .380 will fire only when the grip safety is firmly squeezed. A fumble won't do it. So why does the Post-Dispatch call for more unnecessary gun legislation? Contrary to bogus set-up "surveys" by one director of the American Medical Association, most gun dealers are eager to help anyone who requests or clearly needs advice on selection or safe handling. Abundant safety information is free for the asking and is included in the box with any new gun. Sorry, a free brain transplant doesn't come with motorcycle, firearm or automobile sales. Bob Hutton Sr. Chesterfield Your editorial about Rep. Bob Barr, R-Ga. accidentally discharging an old pistol was totally inappropriate. You used this unfortunate accident, in which no one was injured, as yet another excuse to rant and rave for more gun control laws. Firearms safety is the responsibility of the person handling the weapon. Barr made an error by accepting a firearm from someone who apparently did not know much about firearms safety. And the gun went off because of both persons' carelessness. But immediately the Post-Dispatch jumped on the anti-gun bandwagon, first by calling for more laws to protect children from loaded-gun accidents, and then by attacking firearms dealers (the most knowledgeable group of gun safety experts) for not providing safety information to gun owners.

1984.text: GUN-805 PRIMARY BUSH SEEKS SOLUTION TO GUN-CONTROL ISSUE WASHINGTON - President George Bush, admitting that he is stymied in the searchfor an appropriate policy on gun control, said Monday that he believed the answer to growing questions about access to powerful firearms ''is going to be found in various ways out in the states.'' ''The country needs to know that there is some answer to this,'' Bush said.  '' And I don't yet know what it is.'' White House spokesman Marlin Fitzwater said that no presidential decisions on gun control were likely until a review of the issue by the administration is completed.   Drug czar William J. Bennett has said he will deliver recommendations to the president within two weeks. But Fitzwater said Monday that the review, of which Bennett's recommendations are a part, might not be completed for 90 days.   Also Monday, Fitzwater said the administration would continue to allow certain semiautomatic assault-type weapons - similar to those banned last week - into the country until Bennett completes his review.   Gun experts say that at least four types of assault-type semiautomatic weapons - from Germany, Israel, Italy and South Korea - were not included among the imported firearms suspended last week.   The weapons suspended were the AKS-type weapons: the Chinese-made AK-47, Uzi carbines, FN/FAL-type weapons, FN/FNC-type weapons and Steyr Aug semiautomatic weapons.   Similar weapons not covered by the ban, according to the National Association of Licensed Firearms Dealers, include the Heckler and Koch models 91 and 93, from West Germany; the Galil semiautomatic, from Israel; the Beretta Model 70, from Italy; and Dae Woo's version of the AR-15, from South Korea. '' They look like, shoot like, load like and do everything like'' the suspended AKS rifles, said Bob Lesmeister, the managing editor of the association publication, American Firearms Industry. Bush made his comments while speaking to a group of state legislators at the White House.   He said, ''We can find accommodation between the legitimate interests of the sportsmen and the interests of the police chiefs in protecting their people who put their lives on the line every single day.'' Law enforcement officials nationwide have been pressing for restrictions on semiautomatic assault rifles by noting that they are the weapon of choice for drug dealers. Their argument has been getting growing support as violence connected to drug trafficking has risen markedly in several major cities.   The National Rifle Association has been trying to head off efforts to limit public access to the weapons. It asserts that such weapons have legitimate sporting uses and that the rights of all citizens should not be limited because of the crimes of a minority.   The issue has been particularly touchy for Bush because he is a longtime member of the rifle association and enjoys the political support of many of its members.   ''I am convinced that reasonable men and women can work together to find an answer to the problem of these automated weapons,'' Bush said in his speech.  '' And I think we've already had some signs in the sporting community that there is support for what we've done. But we're going to keep working it.''

1989.text: GUN-4897 PRIMARY 5-Day Wait to Buy Guns May Become Election Issue The law requiring a five-day waiting period to buy a handgun is scheduled to expire in November, and gun-control advocates are girding for a bruising late-summer fight with the National Rifle Association and its supporters in Congress over whether to extend it. The law, part of the Brady bill that Congress approved in 1993, requires gun buyers in many states to wait as long as five working days before taking new handguns home, giving state and local authorities time to conduct background checks to weed out convicted criminals, fugitives and others not eligible to buy guns. The bill was named for James S. Brady, Ronald Reagan's press secretary, who was seriously wounded in the 1981 attempt on Mr. Reagan's life and whose wife, Sarah, is a leading lobbyist for extending the waiting-period law. Under the Brady law, the waiting-period requirement is to expire on Nov. 30, when a criminal data base allowing almost instantaneous background checks is scheduled to go on line. Though the issue does not cut precisely along party lines, the emerging debate has taken on a sharply partisan edge, and officials in both major parties predict that it will spill into Congressional races in several states. On one side, Republican Party officials and members of Congress have begun attacking the waiting period as an intrusive, big-government policy that has done nothing to stem violent crime. The Republican leadership in both houses is expected to try to block Democratic legislation that would extend the law.

1990.text: NA-40456 PRIMARY Mix of measures will be pushed to curb gun violence Some of the proposals Obama is expected to announce: WOULD REQUIRE CONGRESSIONAL ACTION o Banning assault weapons. Obama has spoken in support of such a measure. Congress passed a 10-year ban on high-grade, military-style weapons in 1994, signed into law by President Bill Clinton. But supporters didn't have the votes to renew the law when it expired in 2004. Last week, Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid, D-Nev., predicted that such a ban might clear the Senate this time around, but he doubted it could get through the House. o Limiting high-capacity ammunition magazines. Obama also has spoken in support of limiting access to these items, like the ones used by the Newtown gunman. o Requiring background checks for all gun buyers. Obama has spoken in support of broader background checks. Currently, people who buy guns at trade shows and through some other private purchases, such as over the Internet, are not required to submit to background checks. Gun control advocates long have wanted Congress to close the "gun-show loophole." The Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence has urged Obama to make broader background checks his top priority, believing it has the best chance to win congressional approval. o Installing a permanent director of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives. The agency has had an acting director for some time. CAN BE ACCOMPLISHED THROUGH EXECUTIVE ORDER

characters: Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence
Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives
